blocks:
Ayahs

الٓمٓ (١)
Alif, Lam, Mim. (1)
ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ لَا رَيْبَ ۛ فِيهِ ۛ هُدًى لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (٢)
This is the Book, there is no doubt in it, a guidance to the Godwary, (2)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَيُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (٣)
who believe in the Unseen, maintain the prayer, and spend out of what We have provided for them; (3)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلِكَ وَبِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ (٤)
and who believe in what has been sent down to you and what was sent down before you, and are certain of the Hereafter. (4)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَلَىٰ هُدًى مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٥)
Those follow their Lord’s guidance and it is they who are the felicitous. (5)

(Commentary)

Since the Surah was revealed in stages (نجوماً), it is not unified by a single specific purpose, except that the majority of it points toward one ultimate objective: the clarification that the right of Allah’s worship (العبادة) — the act of absolute submission and devotion to the Creator — is that His servant must believe in everything He has revealed through the tongues of His messengers, without distinguishing between one revelation and another, or between one messenger and another, or any other such division. This is followed by a reprimand of the disbelievers (الكافرين) and the hypocrites (المنافقين), and a censure of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) for the innovations they introduced by creating divisions in the religion of Allah and separating His messengers. The Surah then transitions to the clarification of several legal rulings, such as the changing of the Qiblah (تحويل القبلة), and the regulations concerning Hajj, inheritance, fasting, and other matters.

His saying, the Exalted: الم (Alif, Lam, Mim). Some discussion regarding the disconnected letters (الحروف المقطعة) at the beginning of the Surahs will follow at the beginning of Surah al-Shura, Allah willing. Likewise, the discussion regarding the meaning of the Quran’s guidance (هداية) and the meaning of it being a "Book" (كتاباً) will be addressed there.

And His saying, the Exalted: هُدًى لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ (a guidance for the God-conscious, who believe...). The God-conscious (المتقون) are the believers (المؤمنون). Godwariness (التقوى) — the inner state of being protective of one's soul against what displeases Allah — is not a specific attribute belonging to a particular class among them—meaning a specific rank among the ranks of faith (الإيمان) such that it would be a station (مقام) among its stations, similar to excellence (الإحسان), humble submission (الإخبات), or sincerity (الخلوص). Rather, it is a comprehensive attribute (صفة مجامعة) for all ranks of faith when faith is clothed in the garment of actualization (التحقق). The evidence for this is that the Exalted does not restrict His description of it to a specific group of believers despite their varying levels and degrees.

The attributes that the Exalted has taken as defining characteristics of godwariness (التقوى) in these nineteen verses—wherein He explains the state of the believers, the disbelievers, and the hypocrites—are five: belief in the Unseen (الغيب), the establishment of prayer (الصلوة), spending from what Allah has provided, belief in what He revealed to His prophets, and certainty (الإيقان) regarding the Hereafter. He has described them as being "upon guidance from their Lord," which indicates that their being characterized by these noble attributes is due to their being clothed in the garment of guidance (الهداية) from Allah, the Glorified. Thus, they only became God-conscious (متقين) possessing these attributes through a guidance from Him, the Exalted.

Then, He described the Book as being a guidance for these God-conscious ones by His saying, the Exalted: ذَٰلِكَ الْكِتَابُ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ هُدًى لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ "This is the Book, there is no doubt in it, a guidance to the God-conscious." From this, we learn that one guidance is distinct from the other, and that these individuals, while being God-conscious, are encompassed by two guidances: an initial guidance by which they became God-conscious, and a second guidance with which Allah, the Glorified, honored them after their godwariness.

By this, the contrast between the God-conscious and the disbelievers and hypocrites becomes valid. For the Glorified places the latter in their description between two misguidances and two blindnesses: an initial misguidance (ضلال) — the straying from the path of truth — which necessitates their foul attributes of disbelief (الكفر) and hypocrisy (النفاق), and a second misguidance by which their first misguidance is reinforced, and which they are described with after the actualization of disbelief and hypocrisy. This is as He, the Exalted, says regarding the disbelievers: خَتَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَعَلَىٰ سَمْعِهِمْ وَعَلَىٰ أَبْصَارِهِمْ غِشَاوَةٌ "Allah has set a seal on their hearts and their hearing, and there is a blindfold on their sight" (Al-Baqarah: 7). Thus, He attributed the "sealing" (الختم) to Himself and the "blindfold" (الغشاوة) to themselves. Similarly, He says regarding the hypocrites: فِي قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ فَزَادَهُمُ اللَّهُ مَرَضًا "In their hearts is a sickness, and Allah has increased their sickness" (Al-Baqarah: 10). He attributed the first sickness to them and the second sickness to Himself, in accordance with what is understood from His saying, the Exalted: يُضِلُّ بِهِ كَثِيرًا وَيَهْدِي بِهِ كَثِيرًا وَمَا يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفَاسِقِينَ "He misleads many thereby, and He guides many thereby; and He misleads no one thereby except the transgressors" (Al-Baqarah: 26), and His saying: فَلَمَّا زَاغُوا أَزَاغَ اللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ "So when they swerved, Allah made their hearts swerve" (As-Saff: 5).

In short, the God-conscious are situated between two guidances, just as the disbelievers and hypocrites are situated between two misguidances.

Furthermore, since the second guidance is through the Quran, the first guidance must precede the Quran and occurs by virtue of the soundness of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being. For if the primordial nature remains sound, it cannot fail to be alerted to witness its own poverty and need for a reality outside of itself. Likewise, it recognizes the need of everything else—whether perceived by sense, imagination, or intellect—for an external reality where the chain of needs terminates. Thus, the primordial nature believes in and submits to the existence of a Being hidden from the senses, from Whom everything begins and to Whom everything ends and returns. It recognizes that just as He did not neglect a single detail of what creation requires, He likewise does not neglect the guidance of humanity toward that which saves them from destructive actions and character traits. This constitutes the submission to monotheism (التوحيد), prophethood (النبوة), and the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and final judgment — which are the foundations of religion (أصول الدين). This necessitates the practice of submission to Him in His Lordship (الربوبية), and the utilization of whatever is within a person's capacity—wealth, status, knowledge, or virtue—to revive and spread this matter. These two are prayer (الصلاة) and spending (الإنفاق).

From here it is known that the attributes the Glorified has taken for them are those dictated by the primordial nature when it is sound, and that the Glorified promised them that He would pour upon them a matter He called "guidance." Thus, these pure deeds of theirs are intermediate between two guidances, as you have learned: a preceding guidance and a subsequent guidance. Between the two guidances lie the truthfulness of belief and the righteousness of action. Among the proofs that this second guidance from Allah is a branch of the first are many verses, such as His saying: يُثَبِّتُ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ "Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world and in the Hereafter" (Ibrahim: 27); and His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَآمِنُوا بِرَسُولِهِ يُؤْتِكُمْ كِفْلَيْنِ مِن رَّحْمَتِهِ وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ نُورًا تَمْشُونَ بِهِ "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and have faith in His Apostle. He will grant you a double share of His mercy and give you a light to walk by" (Al-Hadid: 28); and His saying: إِن تَنصُرُوا اللَّهَ يَنصُرْكُمْ وَيُثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَكُمْ "If you help Allah, He will help you and make your feet steady" (Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: 7); and His saying: وَاللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ "And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot" (As-Saff: 7); and His saying: وَاللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفَاسِقِينَ "And Allah does not guide the transgressing lot" (As-Saff: 5); among other verses.

The matter regarding the misguidance of the disbelievers and hypocrites is analogous to that of the God-conscious, as will follow, Allah willing.

In these verses, there is an indication of another life for the human being, latent and hidden beneath this worldly life. It is the life by which the human lives in this abode, after death, and at the time of Resurrection. The Exalted said: أَوَمَن كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَاهُ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُ نُورًا يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَن مَّثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُمَاتِ لَيْسَ بِخَارِجٍ مِّنْهَا "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people, like one who is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (Al-An'am: 122). Discussion on this will follow, Allah willing.

And His saying, the Glorified: يُؤْمِنُونَ (they believe). Faith (الإيمان) is the firm establishment of belief in the heart. It is derived from "security" (الأمن), as if the believer grants the object of his faith security from doubt and skepticism, which are the maladies of belief. Faith, as previously mentioned, is a concept possessing ranks (مراتب), for submission (الاذعان) may relate to the thing itself, resulting only in its primary effect; or it may intensify somewhat and relate to some of its necessary implications; or it may relate to all of its implications. From this, it is concluded that the believers have classes according to the ranks of their faith.

And His saying, the Glorified: بِالْغَيْبِ (in the Unseen). The Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — is the opposite of the manifest (الشهادة). It applies to that which is not perceived by the senses, namely Allah, the Glorified, and His greatest signs that are hidden from our senses. Among these is revelation (الوحي), which is referred to in His saying: وَالَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِمَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ وَمَا أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلِكَ "and who believe in what has been sent down to you and what was sent down before you." Thus, what is intended by "belief in the Unseen," in contrast to belief in revelation and certainty in the Hereafter, is belief in Allah, the Exalted, so that belief in the three foundations of religion is completed. The Quran emphasizes the point of not being restricted to the senses alone and is keen on following the sound intellect (سليم العقل) and the pure core (خالص اللب).

And His saying, the Glorified: وَبِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ (and are certain of the Hereafter). The shift specifically regarding the submission to the Hereafter from "faith" (الإيمان) to "certainty" (الإيقان) — the state of conviction that leaves no room for doubt — is perhaps to indicate that godwariness is not complete except with certainty in the Hereafter, which is incompatible with forgetting it, unlike mere faith. For a person may believe in something yet be heedless of some of its implications, thus committing what contradicts it. However, if he possesses knowledge and remembrance of a Day in which he will be held accountable for the significant and the trivial of his deeds, he will never plunge into destructive sins nor hover around the prohibitions of Allah, the Glorified. The Exalted said: وَلَا تَتَّبِعِ الْهَوَىٰ فَيُضِلَّكَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِمَا نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسَابِ "and do not follow desire, or it will lead you astray from the way of Allah. Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah will have a severe punishment because they forgot the Day of Reckoning" (Sad: 26). Thus, the Exalted clarified that straying from the path of Allah is only due to forgetting the Day of Reckoning; therefore, remembering it and having certainty in it produces godwariness.

And His saying, the Exalted: أُولَٰئِكَ عَلَىٰ هُدًى مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ (Those are on a guidance from their Lord). All guidance is from Allah, the Glorified; it is never attributed to anyone else except metaphorically, as will follow, Allah willing. Since Allah has described them with guidance, and He has said in its description: فَمَن يُرِدِ اللَّهُ أَن يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ "Whomever Allah desires to guide, He opens his breast..." (Al-An'am: 125)—and the opening of the breast (شرح الصدر) is its expansion, and this expansion repels from it all constriction and greed (شحّ)—and the Exalted has said: وَمَن يُوقَ شُحَّ نَفْسِهِ فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ "and those who are saved from their own greed—it is they who are the felicitous" (Al-Hashr: 9), He, the Glorified, has also followed His saying here, "Those are on a guidance from their Lord," with His saying: وَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ "and it is they who are the felicitous."

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Ma'ani, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْغَيْبِ (who believe in the Unseen), he said: "Whoever believes in the rising of the Qaim (peace be upon him), that it is the truth."

I say: This meaning is narrated in other than this report, and it is of the nature of application (الجري) — the application of a verse to a specific instance or individual that fits its general meaning.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَاهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (and spend out of what We have provided them), he said: "And from what We have taught them, they disseminate."

In al-Ma'ani, from him (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "And from what We have taught them, they disseminate, and of what We have taught them of the Quran, they recite."

I say: The two narrations are based on interpreting "spending" (الإنفاق) in a sense broader than the spending of wealth, as we have mentioned.

(Philosophical Discussion)

Is it permissible to rely on rational concepts (المعاني العقلية) other than sensory perceptions (الادراكات الحسية)? This issue is a battlefield of opinions among modern Westerners, although the majority of the ancients and the philosophers of Islam maintained the permissibility of relying on both the senses and the intellect together. In fact, they mentioned that scientific demonstration (البرهان العلمي) does not encompass the sensible (المحسوس) insofar as it is sensible. Nevertheless, Westerners have differed on this, and the majority of them—especially natural scientists—do not rely on anything other than the senses. They have argued that pure rational matters (العقليات المحضة) are prone to frequent error and mistake, with no means to distinguish truth from error, which is the role of the senses and experience (التجربة) that are in contact with particulars (الجزئيات). This is in contrast to sensory perceptions; for when we perceive something with one of the senses, we follow it with experience by repeating similar instances, and we continue to repeat until we establish the required property in the external world, after which no doubt occurs.

However, this argument is flawed and invalid:

First: All the premises used in it are rational (عقلية) and not sensory. Thus, it is an argument for the invalidity of relying on rational premises using rational premises, which means the validity of the argument necessitates its own corruption.

Second: Error in the senses is no less frequent than error in rational matters, as indicated by the researches they have presented regarding optics and other sensibles. If the mere occurrence of error in a field were a reason to close it and drop reliance upon it, then closing the door of the senses would be more necessary and mandatory.

Third: Distinguishing between error and truth is necessary in all perceptions. However, experience—which is the repetition of sensory perception—is not the tool for that distinction. Rather, the experimental proposition becomes one of the premises of a syllogism (قياس) used to argue for the objective. For when we perceive a specific property with the senses and then follow it with experience by repeating instances, a syllogism is actually formed in this shape: "This property is permanent or predominant for this subject; and if it were a property of something other than this subject, it would not be permanent or predominant; but it is permanent or predominant." This syllogism, as you see, consists of rational premises that are neither sensory nor experimental.

Fourth: Suppose that all sensory sciences are supported by experience in the realm of practice. It is clear that the validity of experience itself is not established by another experience, and so on ad infinitum. Rather, the knowledge of its validity comes through a path other than the senses. Thus, relying on the senses and experience is, by necessity, a reliance on rational knowledge (العلم العقلي).

Fifth: The senses only apprehend the changing particular (الجزئي المتغير), while sciences do not conclude or utilize anything other than universal propositions (القضايا الكلية), which are neither sensible nor experimental. For example, anatomy only apprehends a few or many individuals of humans, giving the senses the observation that this human has a heart and a liver, for instance. From the repetition of this, a number of observations—few or many—is obtained. But that is not the universal judgment (الحكم الكلي) in our saying: "Every human has a heart or a liver." If we were to restrict ourselves to relying only on what is benefited from the senses and experience without leaning on rational matters at all, no universal perception, theoretical thought, or scientific research would be completed for us. Therefore, just as it is possible or necessary to rely on the senses in their specific domain, it is likewise necessary to rely on what pertains to the rational faculty. By "intellect" (العقل), we mean the principle for these universal assents (التصديقات الكلية) and the perceiver of these general judgments. There is no doubt that the human being possesses something whose function is this. How can it be conceived that there exists, by way of creation and fashioning, something whose function is absolute error? Or that it can err in the very function for which creation specifically designated it? Creation only designates an existent for an action after establishing an external link (رابطة خارجية) between them. How can a link be established between an existent and a non-existent—that is, error and mistake?

As for the occurrence of error in the sciences or the senses, the clarification of its reality belongs to another place to which one should refer, and Allah is the Guide.

(Another Philosophical Discussion)

The simple human being in the early stages of his development, when he first treads the path of life, sees nothing of himself except that he attains the external realities (أعيانها الخارجية) of things, without being alerted to the fact that he mediates the attribute of knowledge (العلم) between himself and them. He remains in this state until he encounters doubt (الشك) or opinion (الظن) in some of his situations. At that point, he becomes aware that in his vital journey and worldly living, he never ceases to employ knowledge, especially since he sometimes errs and mistakes in his distinctions. Error and mistake have no way into the external realities themselves. He then becomes certain of the existence of the attribute of knowledge—which is the perception (الادراك) that precludes the contradictory—within himself.

Furthermore, exhaustive research leads us to this same result. Our judgmental perceptions (ادراكاتنا التصديقية) are analyzed back to the "primary principle" (قضية أول الأوائل) — the law of non-contradiction — which is that affirmation and negation cannot both be true and cannot both be false at the same time. There is no self-evident (بديهية) or theoretical (نظرية) proposition except that it requires this primary self-evident proposition for the completion of its assent. Even if we were to suppose ourselves doubting it, we would find that the supposed doubt is incompatible with its own falsity, which is already supposed. Once this proposition is established in its self-evidence, a vast number of scientific assents are established according to the need for their proof, and upon them the human relies in his views and actions.

There is no scientific position or practical event except that the human relies therein upon knowledge, to the extent that he only identifies his doubt by his knowledge that it is a doubt, and likewise his opinion, imagination, or ignorance by what he knows to be an opinion, imagination, or ignorance.

In the era of the Greeks, a group arose called the Sophists who denied the existence of knowledge. They would express doubt in everything, even in themselves and in their own doubt. They were followed by others called the Skeptics, who were close to them in approach; they denied the existence of knowledge regarding what is external to themselves and their thoughts (perceptions), and they perhaps fabricated arguments for this.

Among them: That the strongest of sciences and perceptions—those obtained through the senses—are full of error and mistake, so what of others? With this description, how can one rely on any of the sciences and assents related to what is external to us?

And among them: Whenever we intend to attain any of the external things, we only attain at that moment the knowledge of it, not the thing itself. So how can the attainment of any of the things be possible? Among other such arguments.

The answer to the first: This argument invalidates itself. If it were not permissible to rely on any assents, it would not be permissible to rely on the premises taken in the argument itself. Furthermore, the admission of the existence of error and its frequency is an admission of the existence of truth in a measure equal to or greater than the error. Moreover, the one who affirms the existence of knowledge does not claim the correctness of every assent, but only claims it in general (في الجملة). In other words, he claims a "particular affirmative" (الإيجاب الجزئي) in contrast to a "universal negative" (السلب الكلي), and the argument is insufficient to negate that.

The answer to the second: The subject of dispute—which is knowledge (العلم)—has as its reality the disclosure (الكشف) of what is beyond it. If we suppose that whenever we intend an external thing we find the knowledge of it, we have admitted that we have disclosed it at that time. We only claim the existence of this disclosure in general. No one, regarding the existence of knowledge, has claimed that we find the reality itself and attain the external essence (عين الخارج) rather than its disclosure. These people are refuted by what their souls admit by necessity in the voluntary actions of life and others. For they move toward food and water upon sensing the pain of hunger and thirst, and likewise toward every sought object when seeking it, not upon its mere empty conception (تصوره الخالي). They flee from every avoided danger upon the knowledge of its existence, not upon its mere conception. In short, every psychological need that their sensations have inspired in them, they create an external movement to fulfill; but when they conceive that need without the nature requiring it, they do not move toward fulfilling it. There is inevitably a difference between the two conceptions: one of the two knowledges is created by the human by his choice and from himself, while the other is only created in the human by the creation of an external matter affecting him, which is what the knowledge discloses. Therefore, knowledge exists, and that is what we intended.

Know that regarding the existence of knowledge, there is a strong doubt from another perspective, which is the basis upon which materialist sciences are founded today: the negation of fixed knowledge (العلم الثابت) — knowledge that remains true regardless of time or change. Its explanation: Scientific research proves in the world of nature the system of transformation and evolution (التكامل). Every part of the parts of the natural world is situated in the path of motion, directed toward perfection. There is no thing except that in the second instant of its existence, it is other than what it was in the first instant. There is no doubt that thought and perception are among the properties of the brain; thus, they are a material property of a material compound. They are inevitably subject to the law of transformation and evolution. Therefore, these perceptions—including the perception called "knowledge"—are situated in change and transformation. Thus, there is no meaning to the existence of a fixed, enduring knowledge; rather, it is relative (نسبي). Some assents are more enduring in stay, longer in life, or more hidden in contradiction and refutation than others, and that is what is called "knowledge" when it is found.

The answer to it: The argument is built upon knowledge being material (مادياً) and not immaterial (مجرد) in its existence, and that is neither self-evident nor proven. Rather, the truth is that knowledge is not material at all, because the attributes and properties of matter do not apply to it.

(1) For material things share in the acceptance of division (الانقسام), while knowledge, qua knowledge, does not accept division at all.

(2) Material things are spatial (مكانية) and temporal (زمانية), while knowledge, qua knowledge, accepts neither space nor time. The proof for this is the possibility of conceiving a particular event (الحادثة الجزئية) that occurred in a specific place and time, in every place and every time, while preserving its identity (العينية).

(3) All material things are situated under the mastery of general motion; thus, change (التغير) is a general property in them. Yet knowledge, qua knowledge, does not change. For the aspect of knowledge in its essence (بالذات) contradicts the aspect of change and transformation, which is apparent to the contemplative.

(4) If knowledge were something that changed according to its essence like material things, it would not be possible to conceive one single thing or one single event at two different times together, nor to remember a previous thing or event in a subsequent time. For the changing thing in the second instant is other than what it was in the first instant.

These points and their likes indicate that knowledge, qua knowledge, is not material at all. As for what occurs in the sensory organ or the brain of the actualization of a natural process, that is not our discussion at all, and there is no proof that it is the knowledge itself. The mere actualization of a process upon the actualization of a matter does not indicate that they are one and the same thing. Further depth in this research should be sought from its proper place.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٦)
As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith. (6)
خَتَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَعَلَىٰ سَمْعِهِمْ ۖ وَعَلَىٰٓ أَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ غِشَـٰوَةٌ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (٧)
Allah has set a seal on their hearts and their hearing, and there is a blindfold on their sight, and there is a great punishment for them. (7)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (Indeed, those who disbelieve)—these are a people who have become entrenched in disbelief (الكفر) — the rejection of faith and the denial of truth — and whose hearts have been overtaken by obstinate denial (الجحود). This is indicated by the description of their state as being one where warning them or not warning them is equal. It is not improbable that those intended by "those who disbelieve" are the disbelievers (الكفار) among the chieftains of the Quraysh and the leaders of Mecca who showed enmity and persisted in their opposition to the matter of religion (الدين), sparing no effort in doing so, and did not believe until Allah destroyed them to the last man at Badr and elsewhere. This is supported by the fact that this expression—namely His saying: سَوَاءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith)—cannot be generalized to all disbelievers; otherwise, the door of guidance (الهدى) would be closed, whereas the Quran proclaims the opposite. Furthermore, this expression occurs only in Surah Ya-Sin (which is Meccan) and in this Surah (which is Surah al-Baqarah, the first Surah revealed in Medina), having been revealed before the Battle of Badr had taken place. Thus, it is most likely that the intended meaning of "those who disbelieve," here and in other instances of His speech, the Exalted, refers to the disbelievers of Mecca at the beginning of the Prophetic mission (البعثة), unless there is evidence (قرينة) to the contrary. This is similar to what will follow regarding His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (those who believe); where it is used in the Quran without a specific context, it refers to the foremost among the early Muslims, who were singled out with this address as a mark of honor (تشريفاً).

And His saying, the Exalted: خَتَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَعَلَىٰ سَمْعِهِمْ (Allah has set a seal on their hearts and their hearing...) [2:7]. The change in the grammatical flow (السياق)—where He attributes the sealing (الختم) to Himself, the Exalted, while attributing the veil (الغشاوة) to them—suggests that there is a barrier within themselves against the Truth, and a barrier from Allah, the Exalted, following their disbelief and transgression (الفسوق). Thus, their deeds are situated between two barriers: one from their own essence (الذات) and one from Allah, the Exalted. Some matters related to this topic will follow regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَسْتَحْيِي أَن يَضْرِبَ مَثَلًا (Indeed Allah does not disdain to draw a parable) [2:26].

Know that disbelief (الكفر), like faith (الإيمان), is an attribute capable of intensity (الشدة) and weakness (الضعف); thus, it has different levels (مراتب) with varying effects, just like faith.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, narrated from al-Zubayri, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: I said to him, "Inform me about the aspects of disbelief (الكفر) in the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic." He (peace be upon him) said: الكفر في كتاب الله على خمسة أوجه ، فمنها كفر الجحود ، والجحود على وجهين ، والكفر بترك ما أمر الله ، وكفر البرائة ، وكفر النعم . فأما كفر الجحود فهو الجحود بالربوبية وهو قول من يقول : لا رب ولا جنة ولا نار ، وهو قول صنفين من الزنادقة يقال لهم الدهرية وهم الذين يقولون وما يهلكنا إلا الدهر وهو دين وضعوه لأنفسهم بالإستحسان منهم ولا تحقيق لشيء مما يقولون : قال عز وجل : ان هم إلا يظنون ، أن ذلك كما يقولون ، وقال : ان الذين كفروا سواء عليهم أأنذرتهم أم لم تنذرهم لا يؤمنون ، يعني بتوحيد الله ، فهذا أحد وجوه الكفر . — "Disbelief in the Book of Allah is of five types: among them is the disbelief of obstinate denial (الجحود), and denial is of two kinds; the disbelief of abandoning what Allah has commanded; the disbelief of dissociation (البراءة); and the disbelief of [denying] blessings (النعم). As for the disbelief of obstinate denial, it is the denial of Lordship (الربوبية), which is the claim of those who say: 'There is no Lord, no Paradise, and no Fire.' This is the claim of two groups of heretics (الزنادقة) called the Materialists (الدهرية), who are those who say: 'Nothing destroys us but time.' This is a religion they have fashioned for themselves based on their own personal preference (الاستحسان), without any verification for anything they say. The Mighty and Majestic said: إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَظُنُّونَ (they do nothing but conjecture) [45:24], that it is as they say. And He said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَوَاءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith) [2:6], meaning in the monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — of Allah. This is one of the aspects of disbelief."

وأما الوجه الآخر فهو الجحود على معرفة ، وهو ان يجحد الجاحد وهو يعلم أنه حق قد استقر عنده ، وقد قال الله عز وجل : وَجَحَدُوا بِهَا وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْهَا أَنفُسُهُمْ ظُلْمًا وَعُلُوًّا ، وقال الله عز وجل : وكانوا من قبل يستفتحون على الذين كفروا فلما جاءهم ما عرفوا كفروا به فلعنة الله على الكافرين ، فهذا تفسير وجهي الجحود ، والوجه الثالث من الكفر كفر النعم وذلك قوله سبحانه يحكي قول سليمان : هذا من فضل ربي ليبلوني أأشكر أم أكفر ومن يشكر فإنما يشكر لنفسه ومن يكفر فإن الله غني كريم ، وقال : لئن شكرتم لأزيدنكم ولئن كفرتم إن عذابي لشديد ، وقال : فاذكروني أذكركم واشكروا لي ولا تكفرون . — "As for the other aspect, it is denial despite knowledge (الجحود على معرفة); it is when the denier denies while knowing that it is the truth established within him. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has said: وَجَحَدُوا بِهَا وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْهَا أَنفُسُهُمْ ظُلْمًا وَعُلُوًّا (They impugned them—though they were convinced of them in their hearts—out of injustice and pride) [27:14]. And Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: وَكَانُوا مِن قَبْلُ يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَلَمَّا جَاءَهُم مَّا عَرَفُوا كَفَرُوا بِهِ ۚ فَلَعْنَةُ اللَّهِ عَلَى الْكَافِرِينَ (previously they would pray for victory over the faithless, but when there came to them what they recognized, they denied it. So may the curse of Allah be on the faithless) [2:89]. This is the explanation of the two aspects of denial. The third aspect of disbelief is the disbelief of [denying] blessings (كفر النعم), and that is the saying of the Glorified, recounting the words of Solomon: هَٰذَا مِن فَضْلِ رَبِّي لِيَبْلُوَنِي أَأَشْكُرُ أَمْ أَكْفُرُ ۖ وَمَن شَكَرَ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِ ۖ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ رَبِّي غَنِيٌّ كَرِيمٌ (This is by the grace of my Lord, to test me whether I will give thanks or be ungrateful. Whoever gives thanks, gives thanks only for his own sake. And whoever is ungrateful [knows that] my Lord is indeed all-sufficient, all-generous) [27:40]. And He said: لَئِن شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ ۖ وَلَئِن كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذَابِي لَشَدِيدٌ (If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing], but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe) [14:7]. And He said: فَاذْكُرُونِي أَذْكُرْكُمْ وَاشْكُرُوا لِي وَلَا تَكْفُرُونِ (So remember Me, and I will remember you, and be grateful to Me, and do not be ungrateful to Me) [2:152]."

والوجه الرابع من الكفر ترك ما أمر الله عز وجل به ، وهو قول عز وجل : وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَاقَكُمْ لَا تَسْفِكُونَ دِمَاءَكُمْ وَلَا تُخْرِجُونَ أَنفُسَكُم مِّن دِيَارِكُمْ ثُمَّ أَقْرَرْتُمْ وَأَنتُمْ تَشْهَدُونَ ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ هَـٰؤُلَاءِ تَقْتُلُونَ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَتُخْرِجُونَ فَرِيقًا مِّنكُم مِّن دِيَارِهِمْ تَظَاهَرُونَ عَلَيْهِم بِالْإِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوَانِ وَإِن يَأْتُوكُمْ أُسَارَىٰ تُفَادُوهُمْ وَهُوَ مُحَرَّمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ إِخْرَاجُهُمْ أَفَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِبَعْضِ الْكِتَابِ وَتَكْفُرُونَ بِبَعْضٍ فكفّرهم بترك ما أمر الله عز وجل به ونسبهم الى الايمان ولم يقبله منهم ولم ينفعهم عنده فقال : فما جزاء من يفعل ذلك منكم إلا خزي في الحيوة الدنيا ويوم القيمة يردون الى أشد العذاب وما الله بغافل عما تعملون . — "The fourth aspect of disbelief is the abandonment of what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has commanded. This is the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَاقَكُمْ لَا تَسْفِكُونَ دِمَاءَكُمْ وَلَا تُخْرِجُونَ أَنفُسَكُم مِّن دِيَارِكُمْ ثُمَّ أَقْرَرْتُمْ وَأَنتُمْ تَشْهَدُونَ * ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ هَٰؤُلَاءِ تَقْتُلُونَ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَتُخْرِجُونَ فَرِيقًا مِّنكُم مِّن دِيَارِهِمْ تَظَاهَرُونَ عَلَيْهِم بِالْإِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوَانِ وَإِن يَأْتُوكُمْ أُسَارَىٰ تُفَادُوهُمْ وَهُوَ مُحَرَّمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ إِخْرَاجُهُمْ ۚ أَفَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِبَعْضِ الْكِتَابِ وَتَكْفُرُونَ بِبَعْضٍ (And when We took a pledge from you: ‘You shall not shed your own blood, and you shall not expel your own people from your homes,’ you pledged, and you are witnesses [to it]. Then there you are, killing your own people and expelling a part of your own people from their homes, backing one another against them in sin and aggression; and if they come to you as captives, you ransom them, though their expulsion itself was forbidden you. What! Do you believe in part of the Book and deny another part?) [2:84-85]. Thus, He charged them with disbelief for abandoning what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, had commanded, and He attributed faith to them, yet He did not accept it from them, nor did it benefit them with Him. He said: فَمَا جَزَاءُ مَن يَفْعَلُ ذَٰلِكَ مِنكُمْ إِلَّا خِزْيٌ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا ۖ وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ يُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ أَشَدِّ الْعَذَابِ ۗ وَمَا اللَّهُ بِغَافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (So what is the recompense of those of you who do that except disgrace in the life of this world, and on the Day of Resurrection they shall be consigned to the severest punishment? And Allah is not oblivious of what you do) [2:85]."

والوجه الخامس من الكفر كفر البرائة وذلك قول الله عز وجل يحكي قول ابراهيم : وكفرنا بكم وبدأ بيننا وبينكم العداوة والبغضاء حتى تؤمنوا بالله وحده ، يعني تبرأنا منكم ، وقال : ( يذكر ابليس وتبريه من أوليائه من الإنس يوم القيامة ) إني كفرت بما أشركتمون من قبل ، وقال : انما اتخذتم من دون الله اوثاناً مودة بينكم في الحيوة الدنيا ثم يوم القيمة يكفر بعضكم ببعض ويلعن بعضكم بعضاً ، يعني يتبرأ بعضكم من بعض . — "The fifth aspect of disbelief is the disbelief of dissociation (كفر البراءة). This is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, recounting the words of Abraham: وَكَفَرْنَا بِكُمْ وَبَدَا بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمُ الْعَدَاوَةُ وَالْبَغْضَاءُ أَبَدًا حَتَّىٰ تُؤْمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَحْدَهُ (We disown you, and enmity and hatred have appeared between us and you for ever, until you believe in Allah alone) [60:4], meaning: 'We dissociate ourselves from you.' And He said—mentioning Iblis and his dissociation from his followers among mankind on the Day of Resurrection: إِنِّي كَفَرْتُ بِمَا أَشْرَكْتُمُونِ مِن قَبْلُ (Indeed I disavow your having taken me as a partner [of Allah] before) [14:22]. And He said: إِنَّمَا اتَّخَذْتُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ أَوْثَانًا مَّوَدَّةَ بَيْنِكُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا ۖ ثُمَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ يَكْفُرُ بَعْضُكُم بِبَعْضٍ وَيَلْعَنُ بَعْضُكُم بَعْضًا (You have only taken idols [for worship] besides Allah because of [mutual] affection between you in the life of the world. Then on the Day of Resurrection you will disown one another and curse one another) [24:25], meaning: 'You will dissociate from one another.'"

I say: This narration is in explanation of how disbelief (الكفر) accepts intensity (الشدة) and weakness (الضعف), as previously mentioned.

Ayahs

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَقُولُ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَبِٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَمَا هُم بِمُؤْمِنِينَ (٨)
Among the people are those who say, ‘We have faith in Allah and the Last Day,’ but they have no faith. (8)
يُخَـٰدِعُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَمَا يَخْدَعُونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٩)
They seek to deceive Allah and those who have faith, yet they deceive no one but themselves, but they are not aware. (9)
فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ فَزَادَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مَرَضًا ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْذِبُونَ (١٠)
There is a sickness in their hearts; then Allah increased their sickness, and there is a painful punishment for them because of the lies they used to tell. (10)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ لَا تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ مُصْلِحُونَ (١١)
When they are told, ‘Do not cause corruption on the earth,’ they say, ‘We are only reformers!’ (11)
أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْسِدُونَ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا يَشْعُرُونَ (١٢)
Behold! They are themselves the agents of corruption, but they are not aware. (12)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ ءَامِنُوا۟ كَمَآ ءَامَنَ ٱلنَّاسُ قَالُوٓا۟ أَنُؤْمِنُ كَمَآ ءَامَنَ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٣)
And when they are told, ‘Believe like the people who have believed,’ they say, ‘Shall we believe like the fools who have believed?’ Behold! They are themselves the fools, but they do not know. (13)
وَإِذَا لَقُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَإِذَا خَلَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ شَيَـٰطِينِهِمْ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مَعَكُمْ إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ مُسْتَهْزِءُونَ (١٤)
When they meet the faithful, they say, ‘We believe,’ but when they are alone with their devils, they say, ‘We are with you; we were only deriding [them].’ (14)
ٱللَّهُ يَسْتَهْزِئُ بِهِمْ وَيَمُدُّهُمْ فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (١٥)
It is Allah who derides them, and leaves them bewildered in their rebellion. (15)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةَ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ فَمَا رَبِحَت تِّجَـٰرَتُهُمْ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُهْتَدِينَ (١٦)
They are the ones who bought error for guidance, so their trade did not profit them, nor were they guided. (16)
مَثَلُهُمْ كَمَثَلِ ٱلَّذِى ٱسْتَوْقَدَ نَارًا فَلَمَّآ أَضَآءَتْ مَا حَوْلَهُۥ ذَهَبَ ٱللَّهُ بِنُورِهِمْ وَتَرَكَهُمْ فِى ظُلُمَـٰتٍ لَّا يُبْصِرُونَ (١٧)
Their parable is that of one who lighted a torch, and when it had lit up all around him, Allah took away their light, and left them sightless in a manifold darkness. (17)
صُمٌّۢ بُكْمٌ عُمْىٌ فَهُمْ لَا يَرْجِعُونَ (١٨)
Deaf, dumb, and blind, they will not come back. (18)
أَوْ كَصَيِّبٍ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فِيهِ ظُلُمَـٰتٌ وَرَعْدٌ وَبَرْقٌ يَجْعَلُونَ أَصَـٰبِعَهُمْ فِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِم مِّنَ ٱلصَّوَٰعِقِ حَذَرَ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ مُحِيطٌۢ بِٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٩)
Or that of a rainstorm from the sky, wherein is darkness, thunder and lightning: they put their fingers in their ears due to the thunderclaps, apprehensive of death; and Allah besieges the faithless. (19)
يَكَادُ ٱلْبَرْقُ يَخْطَفُ أَبْصَـٰرَهُمْ ۖ كُلَّمَآ أَضَآءَ لَهُم مَّشَوْا۟ فِيهِ وَإِذَآ أَظْلَمَ عَلَيْهِمْ قَامُوا۟ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَذَهَبَ بِسَمْعِهِمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٢٠)
The lightning almost snatches away their sight: whenever it shines for them, they walk in it, and when the darkness falls upon them, they stand. Had Allah willed, He would have taken away their hearing and sight. Indeed Allah has power over all things. (20)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَن يَقُولُ (And among the people are those who say...) until the end of the verses. Deception (الخدعة) is a type of cunning (المكر), and the devil (الشيطان) is the wicked one (الشرير); for this reason, Iblis was named Shaytan.

In these verses is an exposition of the state of the hypocrites (المنافقين) — those who outwardly profess faith while concealing disbelief — and, if Allah wills, a detailed discussion regarding them will follow in Surah al-Munafiqun and elsewhere.

And His saying, the Exalted: مَثَلُهُمْ كَمَثَلِ الَّذِي اسْتَوْقَدَ نَارًا — Their parable is that of one who lighted a torch, and when it illuminated around him, Allah took away their light and left them in a manifold darkness in which they cannot see. (2:17). This is a parable (مثل) struck to describe their condition: they are like one who has fallen into a blind darkness (ظلمة عمياء) where good cannot be distinguished from evil, nor the beneficial from the harmful. Thus, he seeks to dispel it by some means of illumination, such as a fire he kindles to see his surroundings. Yet, once it is kindled and illuminates what is around him, Allah extinguishes it through some cause, such as wind, rain, or the like, leaving him as he was before.

He remains in darkness and becomes entangled between two darknesses: the darkness he was originally in, and the darkness of confusion (الحيرة) and the futility of the means he sought.

This is the state of the hypocrite; he manifests faith (الإيمان) and thereby benefits from some of the advantages of the religion (الدين) by sharing with the believers in their inheritances, marriages, and other matters. However, when the time of death arrives—which is the moment when the full benefit of faith should be realized—Allah takes away his light, nullifies what he has done, and leaves him in a darkness where he perceives nothing. He falls between his original darkness and the darkness he brought upon himself by his own deeds.

And His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ كَصَيِّبٍ مِّنَ السَّمَاءِ — Or like a rainstorm from the sky, wherein is darkness, thunder and lightning. They put their fingers in their ears against the thunderclaps, being afraid of death; and Allah besets the faithless. (2:19). The rainstorm (الصيب) is heavy rain; lightning (البرق) is well-known; thunder (الرعد) is the sound produced from the clouds during lightning; and the thunderbolt (الصاعقة) is that which descends from the lightning.

This is a second parable struck to describe the state of the hypocrites in their outward display of faith. They are like one caught in a heavy rainstorm from the sky, accompanied by a darkness that robs him of sight and discernment. The rainstorm compels him to flee and seek escape, yet the darkness prevents him from doing so. Terrifying elements like thunder and thunderbolts surround him, so he finds no recourse but to make use of the lightning and its glow. However, this light is neither permanent nor continuous; whenever it shines for him, he walks, and when it turns dark over him, he stands still.

Such is the state of the hypocrite: he does not love faith, yet he finds no alternative but to manifest it. Due to the lack of correspondence (المواطأة) between his heart and his tongue, his path is not fully illuminated for him. Consequently, he continues to stumble time and again, and trips repeatedly; he walks a little and stands still a little. Allah exposes him through this, and had Allah willed, He would have taken away his hearing and his sight, exposing him from the very first day.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُمْ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (٢١)
O mankind! Worship your Lord, who created you and those who were before you, so that you may be Godwary (21)
ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ فِرَٰشًا وَٱلسَّمَآءَ بِنَآءً وَأَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَخْرَجَ بِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ رِزْقًا لَّكُمْ ۖ فَلَا تَجْعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ أَندَادًا وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٢٢)
—He who made the earth a place of repose for you, and the sky a canopy, and He sends down water from the sky and with it brings forth crops for your sustenance. So do not set up equals to Allah, while you know. (22)
وَإِن كُنتُمْ فِى رَيْبٍ مِّمَّا نَزَّلْنَا عَلَىٰ عَبْدِنَا فَأْتُوا۟ بِسُورَةٍ مِّن مِّثْلِهِۦ وَٱدْعُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٢٣)
And if you are in doubt concerning what We have sent down to Our servant, then bring a surah like it, and invoke your helpers besides Allah, if you are truthful. (23)
فَإِن لَّمْ تَفْعَلُوا۟ وَلَن تَفْعَلُوا۟ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلنَّارَ ٱلَّتِى وَقُودُهَا ٱلنَّاسُ وَٱلْحِجَارَةُ ۖ أُعِدَّتْ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٢٤)
But if you do not—and you will not—then beware the Fire whose fuel will be humans and stones, prepared for the faithless. (24)
وَبَشِّرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ كُلَّمَا رُزِقُوا۟ مِنْهَا مِن ثَمَرَةٍ رِّزْقًا ۙ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى رُزِقْنَا مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَأُتُوا۟ بِهِۦ مُتَشَـٰبِهًا ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِيهَآ أَزْوَٰجٌ مُّطَهَّرَةٌ ۖ وَهُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٥)
And give good news to those who have faith and do righteous deeds, that for them shall be gardens with streams running in them: whenever they are provided with their fruit for nourishment, they will say, ‘This is what we were provided before,’ and they were given something resembling it. There will be chaste mates for them, and they will remain therein [forever]. (25)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: يا أيها الناس اعبدوا (O mankind, worship...) [2:21] etc. After the Glorified has explained the state of the three groups—the God-conscious (المتقين), the disbelievers (الكافرين), and the hypocrites (المنافقين)—and that the God-conscious are upon guidance from their Lord and the Quran is a guidance for them, while the disbelievers have seals upon their hearts and their hearing, and over their vision is a veil, and the hypocrites are diseased and Allah has increased them in disease, being deaf, dumb, and blind (this spanning a total of nineteen verses), He, the Exalted, branched out from that by calling mankind to His worship (العبادة) and to join the ranks of the God-conscious rather than the disbelievers and the hypocrites. This call continues through these five verses until His saying: خالدون (abiding [therein] forever) [2:25]. This context indicates that His saying: لعلكم تتقون (so that you may be God-conscious) [2:21] is connected to His saying: اعبدوا (worship), rather than His saying: خلقكم (created you), even though the meaning is correct under both interpretations.

And His saying, the Exalted: فلا تجعلوا لله انداداً وانتم تعلمون (so do not coin any equals for Allah while you know) [2:22]. The word equals (الأنداد) is the plural of nidd (ند), which is like mithl (مثل)—a peer or likeness—in both weight and meaning. The fact that His saying: وانتم تعلمون (while you know) is not restricted by a specific object and is positioned as a circumstantial clause (حال) for His saying: فلا تجعلوا (so do not coin) implies an extreme emphasis in the prohibition. It suggests that a human being, possessing any form of knowledge whatsoever, is not permitted to take equals for Allah, the Glorified, while the reality is that He is the One who created them and those before them, then organized the cosmic system for their sustenance and survival.

And His saying, the Exalted: فأتوا بسورة من مثله (then bring a surah like it) [2:23] is a challenge intended to demonstrate incapacity (أمر تعجيزي) in order to manifest the miraculous nature (إعجاز) of the Quran—that it is a Book sent down from Allah in which there is no doubt—a miracle (إعجاز) that remains throughout the passage of ages and the succession of centuries.

This challenge has been repeated in His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying: قُل لَّئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنسُ وَالْجِنُّ عَلَىٰ أَن يَأْتُوا بِمِثْلِ هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنِ لَا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ وَلَوْ كَانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيرًا — "Say, ‘Should entire mankind and the jinn muster to produce the like of this Quran, they will not produce the like of it, even if they should back each other up.’" (Al-Isra: 88), and His saying: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرَاهُ قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِّثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَيَاتٍ وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ — "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated, and invoke whomever you can, besides Allah, should you be truthful.’" (Hud: 13). Based on this, the pronoun in "like it" (مثله) refers back to His saying: مما نزلنا (of what We have sent down), making it a challenge based on the Quran itself and the novelty of its style and expression.

It is also possible that the pronoun refers back to His saying: عبدنا (Our servant), in which case it would be a challenge regarding the Quran from the perspective that the one who brought it was an unlettered man (رجل امي) who never learned from a teacher nor received any of these precious, lofty insights and exquisite, precise expressions from any human being. Thus, the verse would follow the course of His saying: قُل لَّوْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ مَا تَلَوْتُهُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَا أَدْرَاكُم بِهِ فَقَدْ لَبِثْتُ فِيكُمْ عُمُرًا مِّن قَبْلِهِ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ — "Say, ‘Had Allah wished, I would not have recited it to you, nor would He have made it known to you. I have certainly lived out a lifetime among you before it. Do you not exercise your reason?’" (Yunus: 16). Both interpretations have been mentioned together in some narrations (الأخبار).

Know that this verse, like its counterparts, establishes the miraculous nature (إعجاز) of even the shortest surah of the Quran, such as Surat al-Kawthar or Surat al-Asr, for example. The possibility that the pronoun in "like it" (مثله) refers to the specific surah itself—such as Surat al-Baqarah or Surat al-Yunus—is rejected by an understanding accustomed to the styles of speech. For whoever accuses the Quran of being a fabrication against Allah, the Exalted, accuses all of it and does not restrict such a claim to one surah over another. Therefore, there would be no meaning in rebutting them by challenging them with Surat al-Baqarah or Surat al-Yunus, as the meaning would then revert to something like saying: "If you are in doubt regarding Surat al-Kawthar or al-Ikhlas, then bring a surah like Surat al-Baqarah," and the absurdity of this is evident.

(The Miracle and Its Essence)

Know: that the Quran's claim of being a miraculous sign (آية معجزة) through this challenge presented by this verse actually resolves into two claims: the claim of the reality of the miracle (الإعجاز) itself and the breaking of the habitual course of nature (خرق العادة الجارية), and the claim that the Quran is an instance (مصداق) of such miracles. It is well known that the second claim, once established, proves the first. The Quran contents itself with this mode of explanation and challenges by its own self, thereby concluding both results. However, there remains the discussion on how the miracle (الإعجاز) is realized despite its inclusion of things that the habitual course of nature (العادة الجارية) does not affirm—namely, the attribution of effects (المسببات) to their well-known, specific causes (أسباب) without exception in the law of causality (السببية) or failure and variation in the law of causation (العلية). The Quran explains the reality of the matter and removes the doubt therein.

The Quran elaborates on this matter from two perspectives:

First: That the miracle (الإعجاز) is a reality, and the Quran is one of its instances, proving the principle of the miracle and its own status as one through the challenge.

Second: What is the essence of the miracle (الإعجاز), and how can an event occur in nature that breaks its habit and negates its universality?

(The Miraculous Nature of the Quran)

There is no doubt that the Quran challenges with its miraculous nature (الإعجاز) in many different verses, both Meccan and Medinan, all of which indicate that the Quran is a miraculous, habit-breaking sign (آية معجزة خارقة). Even the preceding verse—namely His saying: وَإِن كُنتُمْ فِي رَيْبٍ مِّمَّا نَزَّلْنَا عَلَىٰ عَبْدِنَا فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِّن مِّثْلِهِ — "And if you are in doubt concerning what We have sent down to Our servant, then bring a surah like it" (2:23), meaning from the like of the Prophet (ص)—is an argument for the Quran being a miracle (معجزة) by challenging them to bring a surah similar to a surah from the like of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). It is not a direct argument for prophethood (النبوة) without an intermediary. The proof for this is His saying at its beginning: "And if you are in doubt concerning what We have sent down to Our servant," and He did not say, "And if you are in doubt regarding the message (رسالة) of Our servant." Thus, all the challenges occurring in the Quran are arguments for the Quran being a habit-breaking miracle (معجزة خارقة) from Allah. The verses containing the challenge vary in their generality and specificity. Among the most general in challenge is His saying: قُل لَّئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنسُ وَالْجِنُّ عَلَىٰ أَن يَأْتُوا بِمِثْلِ هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنِ لَا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ وَلَوْ كَانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيرًا — "Say, ‘Should entire mankind and the jinn muster to produce the like of this Quran, they will not produce the like of it, even if they should back each other up.’" (Al-Isra: 88). This verse is Meccan, and its generality in challenge is beyond doubt for anyone with discernment.

If the challenge were only regarding the eloquence (بلاغة) of the Quran's expression and the grandeur of its style, the challenge would not have extended beyond a specific people—namely, the pure Arabs (العرب العرباء) of the pre-Islamic era and the Mukhadramun (those who lived in both the pre-Islamic and Islamic eras) before the language became mixed and corrupted. Yet the verse struck the ears of both mankind and jinn.

Similarly, besides eloquence and grandeur, every specific attribute the Quran contains—such as the true insights (المعارف الحقيقية), virtuous ethics (الأخلاق الفاضلة), legislative rulings (الأحكام التشريعية), reports of the unseen (الأخبار المغيبة), and other knowledges whose veil humanity had not yet lifted at the time of revelation—each of these is something known to some of the two heavy groups (الثقلين) [mankind and jinn] but not all of them. Therefore, the absolute challenge to both groups is only regarding everything in which excellence in attributes is possible.

Thus, the Quran is a sign (آية) for the eloquent in his eloquence and oratory, for the sage (الحكيم) in his wisdom, for the scholar (العالم) in his knowledge, for the sociologist in his sociology, for the legislators in their legislation, for the politicians in their politics, for the rulers in their governance, and for all the worlds in that which they cannot collectively attain, such as the Unseen (الغيب) and the absence of discrepancy in judgment, knowledge, and expression.

From here it becomes clear that the Quran claims the universality of its miraculous nature (إعجاز) from all aspects, in that it is a miracle for every individual of mankind and jinn, whether common or elite, scholar or ignorant, man or woman, a person of卓越 excellence or one of lesser status, provided they possess an intellect that perceives speech. For the human being is created with a primordial nature (الفطرة) to perceive excellence and to recognize increase or decrease therein. Every person can reflect upon the excellence they recognize in themselves or in others and then measure what they have perceived against what the Quran contains, thereby judging with truth and fairness. Is it possible for human power to invent demonstrated divine knowledges that rival what the Quran has brought and equal it in reality? Can it bring ethics built upon the foundation of truths that match what the Quran has brought in purity and virtue? Can it legislate complete jurisprudential rulings (أحكام فقهية) that encompass all human actions without discrepancy leading to contradiction, while preserving the spirit of monotheism (التوحيد) and the word of godwariness (التقوى) in every ruling and its result, and the flow of purity in its root and branch? Can this wondrous encompassment and strange precision originate from an unlettered man (رجل امي) who was raised only in the lap of a people whose share of humanity—despite its countless merits and limitless perfections—was to subsist on raids, battles, and the looting of wealth; to bury daughters alive and kill children for fear of poverty; to boast of ancestors and marry mothers; to pride themselves on immorality and dispraise knowledge while flaunting ignorance? And despite their false pride and zeal, they were humiliated by every oppressor and a prey for every predator—one day for Yemen, one day for Abyssinia, one day for Rome, and one day for Persia. This was the state of the Arabs of the Hijaz in the pre-Islamic era (الجاهلية).

Would any rational person dare to bring a book claiming it to be a guidance for all the worlds, then deposit within it reports of the Unseen (الغيب) from the past and the future, regarding nations that have passed and those yet to come—not just one or two, but in various chapters of stories, epics, and future unseen events—and then not a single one of them deviates from the path of truth?

Can a human being—who is but one part of the realm of material nature (نشأة الطبيعة المادية), where the domain is one of transformation and evolution (التحول والتكامل)—intervene in every affair of the human world and present to the world knowledges, sciences, laws, wisdoms, exhortations, parables, and stories in everything small and large, and then his state does not vary in any of them in terms of perfection or deficiency, even though they were brought into existence gradually and delivered separately, including that which appeared and was then repeated, and branches derived from their roots? This is despite what we see: that no human being remains in one state regarding the perfection or deficiency of their work.

Therefore, the intelligent person capable of conceptualizing these meanings does not doubt that these universal merits and others contained in the Noble Quran are all above human power and beyond material, natural means. And if one is not capable of that, they have not lost their way in their humanity nor forgotten what their primordial conscience (وجدانه الفطري) dictates: to refer to the experts in matters they cannot well test and whose sources they do not know.

If you say: What is the benefit in expanding the challenge to the common folk and exceeding the domain of the elite? For the common folk are quick to be influenced by a call and to respond to any artifice, and they have submitted to the likes of the Bab, the Baha, the Qadiani, and Musaylimah, even though what they brought and argued with was more akin to nonsense and delirium than to speech.

I say: This is the way for the universality of the miracle (الإعجاز) and the possible path for distinguishing perfection and precedence in a matter where competition and excellence occur. For human understandings differ by necessity, as do perfections. The necessary result of these two premises is that the one with high understanding and sound vision perceives, and the one below them in understanding and vision refers to the former. The primordial nature (الفطرة) is the judge, and instinct is the arbiter.

Nothing that a human attains through their perceptive powers and reaches through their understanding can accept universality and encompassment for every individual in every time and place—in terms of access, reach, and survival—except that which is of the nature of knowledge and insight (العلم والمعرفة) according to the mentioned method. For every other supposed miraculous sign (آية معجزة) besides knowledge and insight is a natural entity or a sensory event (حادث حسي) governed by the laws of matter and limited by time and space. Thus, it is not witnessed except by some individuals to the exclusion of others. Even if we assume—as an impossibility or near impossibility—its universality for every individual, it would only be possible in one place rather than all places. And if we assume its expansion to every place, it would not be possible for it to expand to all times and periods.

This, then, is what the Quran challenged with—a universal challenge to every individual in every place and every time.

(Its Challenge through Knowledge)

It specifically challenged through knowledge and insight (العلم والمعرفة) with His saying, the Exalted: وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ تِبْيَانًا لِّكُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "And We have sent down the Book to you as an exposition of all things" (Al-Nahl: 89), and His saying: وَلَا رَطْبٍ وَلَا يَابِسٍ إِلَّا فِي كِتَابٍ مُّبِينٍ — "nor anything fresh or withered, but it is in a manifest Book" (Al-An'am: 59), among other verses. For Islam—as known and recognized by anyone who has traversed the core of its teachings—consists of universals provided by the Quran and particulars referred back to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) through such verses as: مَا آتَاكُمُ الرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَمَا نَهَاكُمْ عَنْهُ فَانتَهُوا — "Take whatever the Apostle gives you, and relinquish whatever he forbids you" (Al-Hashr: 7), and His saying, the Exalted: لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِمَا أَرَاكَ اللَّهُ — "that you may judge between people by what Allah has shown you" (Al-Nisa: 105). It addresses both the grand and the subtle of divine "philosophical" knowledges, virtuous ethics, and secondary religious laws—including acts of worship (عبادات), transactions (معاملات), politics, and social affairs—and everything that touches upon human action and work. All of this is based on the primordial nature (الفطرة) and the principle of monotheism (التوحيد), such that the details return to the principle of monotheism through analysis (التحليل), and the principle returns to the details through synthesis (التركيب).

He has explained the permanence of all of them and their applicability to human welfare through the passage and recurrence of ages by His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتَابٌ عَزِيزٌ لَّا يَأْتِيهِ الْبَاطِلُ مِن بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلَا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ تَنزِيلٌ مِّنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ — "Indeed it is an august Book: falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it, [it is] a successive revelation from One All-wise, All-laudable" (Fussilat: 41-42), and His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحَافِظُونَ — "Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it" (Al-Hijr: 9). Thus, it is a Book over which the judge of abrogation (النسخ) does not rule, nor does the law of transformation and evolution (التحول والتكامل) pass judgment.

If you say: The views of researchers in sociology and scholars of legislation today have settled on the necessity of the transformation of positive social laws (القوانين الوضعية) according to the transformation of society and their variation with the difference of times and periods and the progress of civilization and culture.

I say: The discussion on this matter and the answer to the doubt will come in the commentary (تفسير) of His saying, the Exalted: كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً — "Mankind were but a single community..." (Al-Baqarah: 213).

The gist of the matter and its summary is that the Quran builds the foundation of legislation upon primordial monotheism (التوحيد الفطري) and instinctive virtuous ethics, claiming that legislation must grow from the seed of creation and existence (التكوين والوجود). These researchers, however, build their view upon the transformation of society while discarding spiritualities—namely the knowledges of monotheism and the virtues of ethics. Thus, their word is frozen upon the course of material social evolution which lacks the virtue of the spirit, while the word of Allah is the highest.

(The Challenge Regarding the One to Whom the Quran was Revealed)

It has also challenged through the unlettered Prophet (النبي الأمي) who brought the Quran—miraculous in its wording and meaning—without having learned from a teacher or been raised by a mentor, by His saying, the Exalted: قُل لَّوْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ مَا تَلَوْتُهُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَا أَدْرَاكُم بِهِ فَقَدْ لَبِثْتُ فِيكُمْ عُمُرًا مِّن قَبْلِهِ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ — "Say, ‘Had Allah wished, I would not have recited it to you, nor would He have made it known to you. I have certainly lived out a lifetime among you before it. Do you not exercise your reason?’" (Yunus: 16). He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) was among them as one of them, not excelling in any virtue nor speaking with knowledge, to the point that he did not produce anything of poetry or prose for about forty years—which is two-thirds of his life—possessing no precedence nor undertaking any great feat of excellence. Then he brought what he brought suddenly, producing that which their champions were incapable of and before which the tongues of their eloquent ones were exhausted. Then he spread it to the corners of the earth, and no opponent—whether scholar, person of virtue, or one of intellect and cleverness—dared to oppose it.

The most they could take against him was that he traveled to Syria for trade and learned these stories from the monks there. Yet his travels to Syria were only with his uncle Abu Talib before his maturity, and with Maysarah, the servant of Khadijah, when he was twenty-five years old, while being accompanied by those who were with him night and day. Even if we assume that as an impossibility, what are these knowledges and sciences? From where did these wisdoms and truths come? From whom did this eloquence in expression originate, to which necks have bowed and before which eloquent tongues have faltered?

Another thing they took against him was that he used to stand by a blacksmith in Mecca from the people of Rome who used to make swords and sell them. So Allah, the Glorified, sent down: وَلَقَد نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ لِّسَانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجَمِيٌّ وَهَٰذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُّبِينٌ — "We certainly know that they say, ‘It is only a human that teaches him.’ The tongue of him they point to is foreign, while this is a clear Arabic tongue." (Al-Nahl: 103).

They also said about him that he learned some of what he learned from Salman the Persian, who was among the scholars of the Persians, knowledgeable in sects and religions. However, Salman only believed in him in Medina, while most of the Quran was revealed in Mecca, containing all the universal knowledges and stories that were revealed in Medina, or even more. So what did the faith of Salman and his companionship add to him?

Furthermore, whoever reads the Two Testaments and reflects on what is in them, then returns to what the Quran narrates of the histories of the past prophets and their nations, will see that the history is not the same history and the story is not the same story. In them are slips and sins attributed to the righteous prophets of Allah from which the primordial nature (الفطرة) recoils and refuses to attribute even to ordinary righteous and rational people. The Quran exonerates them from these. In them are other matters that do not relate to any true knowledge or ethical virtue, and the Quran mentioned nothing of them except what benefits people in their knowledges and ethics, leaving the rest, which is the majority.

(The Quran's Challenge through Reporting the Unseen)

It has challenged through reporting the Unseen (الإخبار عن الغيب) in many verses. Among them is its reporting of the stories of the past prophets and their nations, such as His saying, the Exalted: تِلْكَ مِنْ أَنبَاءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهَا إِلَيْكَ مَا كُنتَ تَعْلَمُهَا أَنتَ وَلَا قَوْمُكَ مِن قَبْلِ هَٰذَا — "These are accounts of the Unseen which We reveal to you. Neither you nor your people knew them before this." (Hud: 49), and His saying, the Exalted, after the story of Joseph: ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنبَاءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ أَجْمَعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَمْكُرُونَ — "That is from the accounts of the Unseen which We reveal to you, for you were not with them when they conspired and as they were plotting." (Yusuf: 102), and His saying, the Exalted, in the story of Mary: ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنبَاءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يُلْقُونَ أَقْلَامَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ يَكْفُلُ مَرْيَمَ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ — "That is from the accounts of the Unseen which We reveal to you, for you were not with them when they were casting lots [to see] which of them would take charge of Mary, nor were you with them when they were contending." (Al-Imran: 44), and His saying, the Exalted: ذَٰلِكَ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ قَوْلَ الْحَقِّ الَّذِي فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ — "That is Jesus, son of Mary, a statement of the truth concerning which they are in doubt." (Maryam: 34), among other verses.

Among them is reporting about future events, such as His saying, the Exalted: غُلِبَتِ الرُّومُ فِي أَدْنَى الْأَرْضِ وَهُم مِّن بعد غَلَبِهِمْ سَيَغْلِبُونَ فِي بِضْعِ سِنِينَ — "The Romans have been defeated in a nearby territory, but after their defeat they will be victorious in a few years." (Al-Rum: 2-4), and His saying, the Exalted, regarding the Prophet's return to Mecca after the Hijrah: إِنَّ الَّذِي فَرَضَ عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لَرَادُّكَ إِلَىٰ مَعَادٍ — "Indeed He who has revealed to you the Quran will surely restore you to the destination." (Al-Qasas: 85), and His saying, the Exalted: لَتَدْخُلُنَّ الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرَامَ إِن شَاءَ اللَّهُ آمِنِينَ مُحَلِّقِينَ رُءُوسَكُمْ وَمُقَصِّرِينَ لَا تَخَافُونَ — "You will surely enter the Holy Mosque, if Allah wishes, in safety, with your heads shaven or hair cut short, and without any fear." (Al-Fath: 27), and His saying, the Exalted: سَيَقُولُ الْمُخَلَّفُونَ إِذَا انطَلَقْتُمْ إِلَىٰ مَغَانِمَ لِتَأْخُذُوهَا ذَرُونَا نَتَّبِعْكُمْ — "The stay-behinds will say, when you set out to take the spoils, ‘Let us follow you.’" (Al-Fath: 15), and His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ — "and Allah will protect you from the people." (Al-Ma'idah: 70), and His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحَافِظُونَ — "Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it" (Al-Hijr: 9), and many other verses regarding the promise to the believers and the threat to the disbelievers and polytheists of Mecca.

From this category are other verses regarding epics (الملاحم), similar to His saying, the Exalted: وَحَرَامٌ عَلَىٰ قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْنَاهَا أَنَّهُمْ لَا يَرْجِعُونَ حَتَّىٰ إِذَا فُتِحَتْ يَأْجُوجُ وَمَأْجُوجُ وَهُم مِّن كُلِّ حَدَبٍ يَنسِلُونَ وَاقْتَرَبَ الْوَعْدُ الْحَقُّ فَإِذَا هِيَ شَاخِصَةٌ أَبْصَارُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يَا وَيْلَنَا قَدْ كُنَّا فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَٰذَا بَلْ كُنَّا ظَالِمِينَ — "It is forbidden for [the people of] any town that We have destroyed that they shall return, until when Gog and Magog are let loose and they race down from every slope, and the true promise draws near, behold, the eyes of the faithless will be glazed [as they say], ‘Woe to us! We were certainly oblivious of this. Rather, we were wrongdoers.’" (Al-Anbiya: 95-97), and His saying, the Exalted: وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ — "Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth" (Al-Nur: 55), and His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هُوَ الْقَادِرُ عَلَىٰ أَن يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابًا مِّن فَوْقِكُمْ — "Say, ‘He is able to send upon you a punishment from above you...’" (Al-An'am: 65). Also from this category is His saying, the Exalted: وَأَرْسَلْنَا الرِّيَاحَ لَوَاقِحَ — "And We send the fertilizing winds" (Al-Hijr: 22), and His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ شَيْءٍ مَّوْزُونٍ — "and We caused to grow in it every matter in due balance" (Al-Hijr: 19), and His saying, the Exalted: وَالْجِبَالَ أَوْتَادًا — "and the mountains as pegs?" (Al-Naba: 7), the reality of which is based on scientific truths unknown at the time of revelation until the veil was lifted from them by the scientific research that humanity has been granted in these eras.

From this category—and it is among the specialties of this commentary (تفسير) which researches the verses of the Quran by making some speak to others and using some as witnesses for others—is what is in Surat al-Ma'idah in His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَن يَرْتَدَّ مِنكُمْ عَن دِينِهِ فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِي اللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُ — "O you who have faith! Should any of you desert his religion, Allah will soon bring a people whom He loves and who love Him..." (Al-Ma'idah: 54), and what is in Surat al-Yunus in His saying, the Exalted: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَّسُولٌ فَإِذَا جَاءَ رَسُولُهم قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُم بِالْقِسْطِ — "There is an apostle for every community; so when their apostle comes, judgment is made between them with justice..." (Yunus: 47), and what is in Surat al-Rum in His saying, the Exalted: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا — "So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind..." (Al-Rum: 30), among other verses that inform of the great events that will face the Islamic nation or the world in general after the era of the Quran's revelation. We shall, Allah willing, present a portion of them in the discussion of Surat al-Isra.

The Quran's Challenge through the Absence of Discrepancy within It

It has also challenged through the absence of discrepancy (الاختلاف) within it. The Exalted said: أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ وَلَوْ كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ غَيْرِ اللَّهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلَافًا كَثِيرًا — "Do they not contemplate the Quran? Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would have surely found much discrepancy in it." (Al-Nisa: 82). For it is necessary that the realm is one of material nature (نشأة المادة) and the law governing it is the law of transformation and evolution (التحول والتكامل). There is no existing thing among the entities that are parts of this world except that it is gradual in its existence, moving from weakness to strength and from deficiency to perfection in its essence and all the consequences and appendages of its essence, such as actions and effects. Among these is the human being, who continues to transform and evolve in his existence, actions, and effects, including those effects he reaches through thought and perception. There is not one of us except that he sees himself every day more perfect than yesterday, and he continues to discover in the second moment slips in his actions and errors in his words that originated from him in the first moment. This is a matter no conscious human denies in himself.

Yet this Book was brought by the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) in installments (نجوماً) and he recited it to the people piece by piece over a period of twenty-three years under different circumstances and varying conditions—in Mecca and Medina, by night and day, at home and while traveling, in war and peace, on the day of hardship and the day of victory, the day of safety and the day of fear. It was for the purpose of delivering divine knowledges, teaching virtuous ethics, and legislating religious rulings in all areas of need. Yet there is not found in it the slightest discrepancy in its similar composition—a Book consistent (متشابهاً) and oft-repeated (مثانى). No discrepancy has occurred in the knowledges it delivered and the principles it provided that would contradict one another, nor does any part of it negate another. Thus, the verse explains the verse, and one part clarifies the other, and the whole confirms the whole, as Ali (peace be upon him) said: ينطق بعضه ببعض ويشهد بعضه على بعض — "One part of it speaks through another, and one part of it bears witness to another." (Nahj al-Balaghah). Had it been from other than Allah, the composition would have differed in beauty and splendor, the speech in eloquence and oratory, and the meaning in terms of corruption and correctness, and in terms of precision and firmness.

If you say: This is merely a claim that does not rely on proof, for contradictions and numerous objections have been taken against the Quran, and perhaps works have been authored regarding them.
