I say: In some narrations, the purity is generalized to include freedom from all defects and dislikes.

Ayahs

۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَسْتَحْىِۦٓ أَن يَضْرِبَ مَثَلًا مَّا بَعُوضَةً فَمَا فَوْقَهَا ۚ فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ ۖ وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَيَقُولُونَ مَاذَآ أَرَادَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَـٰذَا مَثَلًا ۘ يُضِلُّ بِهِۦ كَثِيرًا وَيَهْدِى بِهِۦ كَثِيرًا ۚ وَمَا يُضِلُّ بِهِۦٓ إِلَّا ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٢٦)
Indeed Allah is not ashamed to draw a parable whether it is that of a gnat or something above it. As for those who have faith, they know it is the truth from their Lord; and as for the faithless, they say, ‘What did Allah mean by this parable?’ Thereby He leads many astray, and thereby He guides many; and He leads no one astray thereby except the transgressors (26)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَ ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مِيثَـٰقِهِۦ وَيَقْطَعُونَ مَآ أَمَرَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦٓ أَن يُوصَلَ وَيُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (٢٧)
—those who break the covenant made with Allah after having pledged it solemnly, and sever what Allah has commanded to be joined, and cause corruption on the earth—it is they who are the losers. (27)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: إن الله لا يستحيي أن يضرب (Indeed Allah does not disdain to set forth [a parable]). The gnat (البعوضة) is a well-known insect and is among the smallest perceptible animals. This verse and the one following it are counterparts to what is found in Surah al-Ra‘d: أَفَمَن يَعْلَمُ أَنَّمَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ الْحَقُّ كَمَنْ هُوَ أَعْمَىٰ إِنَّمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ . الَّذِينَ يُوفُونَ بِعَهْدِ اللَّهِ وَلَا يَنقُضُونَ الْمِيثَاقَ . وَالَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ مَا أَمَرَ اللَّهُ بِهِ أَن يُوصَلَ — "Is he who knows that what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth like one who is blind? Only those who possess intellect take admonition. Those who fulfill Allah’s covenant and do not break the pledge. And those who join what Allah has commanded to be joined" (13:19-21). In any case, the verse testifies that there is a type of misguidance and blindness that afflicts a human being as a consequence of his evil deeds, distinct from the misguidance and blindness he possesses within himself and from himself. This is because the Exalted says: وما يضلّ به إلا الفاسقين (and He misleads none by it except the transgressors); thus, He placed His misleading (إضلاله) as a consequence of transgression (الفسق), not preceding it.

Furthermore, guidance (الهداية) and misleading (الإضلال) are two comprehensive terms for all types of honor (الكرامة) and abandonment (الخذلان) that proceed from the Exalted toward His servants—the felicitous and the wretched. Allah, the Exalted, has described in His speech the state of the felicitous among His servants by stating that He gives them a good life, strengthens them with a spirit of faith, brings them out of darkness into light, and grants them a light by which they walk. He is their Guardian (ولي); they shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve. He is with them, responding to them when they call upon Him and remembering them when they remember Him, while the angels descend upon them with glad tidings and peace, among other such descriptions.

Conversely, He described the state of the wretched among His servants by stating that He misleads them, brings them out of light into darkness, and sets a seal upon their hearts and their hearing, and over their vision is a veil. He effaces their faces and turns them backward, and places shackles around their necks reaching to their chins so that their heads are forced up. He places a barrier before them and a barrier behind them, enveloping them so they cannot see. He assigns to them devils as companions who mislead them from the path while they reckon they are guided. They adorn their deeds for them, and they are their guardians. Allah leads them on gradually from where they do not perceive, and He grants them respite, for His scheme is firm; He outwits them and leaves them to wander blindly in their rebellion.

This is a brief summary of what He, the Glorified, has mentioned regarding the state of the two groups. Its apparent meaning is that for the human being in this world, beyond the life he lives therein, there is another life—either felicitous or wretched—possessing its own roots and origins by which he lives. He will become aware of it and encounter it when the means are severed and the veil is lifted.

It also appears from His speech, the Exalted, that the human being has another life prior to his worldly life, which he follows in this world just as he follows the pattern of his worldly life in what succeeds it. In other words, the human being has a life before this worldly life and a life after it. The third life follows the decree of the second, and the second follows the decree of the first. Thus, while the human being is in this world, he is situated between two lives: a preceding one and a succeeding one. This is what the apparent sense of the Quran dictates.

However, the majority of commentators have interpreted the first category of verses—those describing the prior life—as a figurative expression of the "language of the state" (لسان الحال) and the requirements of innate potential (إقتضاء الاستعداد). They interpreted the second category—those describing the succeeding life—as various forms of metaphor (المجاز) and allegory (الاستعارة). Nevertheless, the apparent meanings of many verses refute this. As for the first category, which are the verses of the Primordial Covenant (الذر) and the Pledge (الميثاق), they will be discussed in their respective places. As for the second category, many verses indicate that the recompense on the Day of Recompense is the very deeds themselves, such as His saying, the Exalted: لَا تَعْتَذِرُوا الْيَوْمَ إِنَّمَا تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — "Do not make any excuses today. You are only being requited for what you used to do" (66:7); and His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ تُوَفَّىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا كَسَبَتْ — "then every soul shall be recompensed fully for what it has earned" (2:281); and His saying, the Exalted: فَاتَّقُوا النَّارَ الَّتِي وَقُودُهَا النَّاسُ وَالْحِجَارَةُ — "then beware of the Fire whose fuel is humans and stones" (2:24); and His saying, the Exalted: فَلْيَدْعُ نَادِيَهُ سَنَدْعُ الزَّبَانِيَةَ — "Then let him call out his gang! We shall call the guards of hell" (96:17-18); and His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُّحْضَرًا وَمَا عَمِلَتْ مِن سُوءٍ — "The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil, it has done" (3:30); and His saying, the Exalted: مَا يَأْكُلُونَ فِي بُطُونِهِمْ إِلَّا النَّارَ — "they eat nothing into their bellies but fire" (2:174); and His saying: إِنَّمَا يَأْكُلُونَ فِي بُطُونِهِمْ نَارًا — "they only eat fire into their bellies" (4:10), among other verses.

By my life, if there were nothing in the Book of Allah, the Exalted, except His saying: لَّقَدْ كُنتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَٰذَا فَكَشَفْنَا عَنكَ غِطَاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ — "You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today" (50:22), it would be sufficient. This is because "obliviousness" (الغفلة) can only be regarding something known and present, and the "removal of the veil" (كشف الغطاء) is only valid regarding something covered and existing. If that which the human being witnesses on the Day of Resurrection were not already existing and present beforehand, it would not be correct to say to him that these were matters of which he was oblivious and which were hidden from him, and that today the veil has been removed from them and the obliviousness dispelled.

By my life, if you were to ask your own soul to guide you to an explanation that fulfills these meanings in reality without metaphor, it would not answer you except with these very explanations and descriptions with which the Noble Quran was revealed.

The summary of the matter is that His speech, the Exalted, is structured upon two aspects:

First: The aspect of requital (المجازاة) through reward and punishment. A vast number of verses follow this, conveying that the good or evil the human being will face—such as Paradise or the Fire—is a recompense for the deeds he performed in the world.

Second: The aspect of the embodiment of deeds (تجسم الأعمال). Another set of verses follows this, indicating that the deeds themselves—either directly or through their necessary implications and effects—prepare desirable or undesirable matters (i.e., good or evil) which the human being will encounter on the day the "shin is bared." Beware of imagining that these two aspects are contradictory, for realities are only brought closer to understanding through the parables that are set forth, as the Quran explicitly states.

And His saying, the Exalted: إلا الفاسقين (except the transgressors). Transgression (الفسق), as has been said, is among the terms whose usage the Quran innovated in its well-known meaning. It is derived from the phrase "the date has transgressed" (فسقت التمرة) when it emerges from its husk and skin. For this reason, it is explained immediately after by His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَ اللَّهِ مِن بَعْدِ مِيثَاقِهِ — "those who break the covenant of Allah after its pledge" (2:27). Breaking (النقض) only occurs after a firm binding (إبرام). For this reason also, He described the transgressors at the end of the verse as the losers (الخاسرين), and a human being only loses (يخسر) in what he possessed in some way. The Exalted said: إِنَّ الْخَاسِرِينَ الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنفُسَهُمْ وَأَهْلِيهِمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ — "The losers are those who lose themselves and their families on the Day of Resurrection" (42:45).

Beware of regarding these attributes that He, the Glorified, has established in His Book for the felicitous among His servants—such as the "brought near" (المقرّبين), the "sincere" (المخلصين), the "humble" (المخبتين), the "righteous" (الصالحين), the "purified" (المطهّرين), and others—or for the wretched—such as the "wrongdoers" (الظالمين), the "transgressors" (الفاسقين), the "losers" (الخاسرين), the "deviant" (الغاوين), the "misguided" (الضالّين), and their like—as commonplace descriptions or as being employed merely for linguistic adornment. If you do so, your intuition will falter in understanding His speech, the Exalted, and you will treat them all as being in one category, taking them as common epithets or simple, mundane talk. Rather, they are attributes that reveal spiritual realities (حقائق روحية) and metaphysical stations (مقامات معنوية) on the paths of felicity and wretchedness. Each one of them is, in itself, a principle for specific effects and a source for particular, defined rulings, just as the stages of age, the specificities of faculties, and the conditions of physical creation in a human being are each a source for specific rulings and effects. We cannot seek one of them from a source or origin other than its own. If you were to contemplate their occurrences in His speech, the Exalted, and examine them deeply, you would find the truth of what we have claimed.

Discussion on Compulsion and Delegation

Know that His explanation, the Exalted, that misleading (الإضلال) only pertains to the transgressors (الفاسقين), clarifies the nature of His influence, the Exalted, upon the deeds of the servants and their results (which is what is sought to be resolved in the discussion of compulsion (الجبر) and delegation (التفويض)).

The explanation of this is that He, the Exalted, said: لِّلَّهِ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ — "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" (2:284); and He said: لَّهُ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth" (57:5); and He said: لَهُ الْمُلْكُ وَلَهُ الْحَمْدُ — "To Him belongs all sovereignty and to Him belongs all praise" (64:1). In these and similar verses, He established ownership (الملك) for Himself over the world in the sense that He, the Exalted, is the Owner in an absolute sense. It is not that He owns in some respects and not in others. This is unlike an individual human who owns a slave or something else in a way that accords with the views of rational people regarding his actions; as for foolish actions, he does not "own" the right to them. Similarly, the world is owned by Allah, the Exalted, with an absolute ownership (مملوكية على الإطلاق), not like our ownership of some parts of the world, for our ownership is deficient and only validates some actions but not all. For instance, a person who owns a donkey only owns the right to use it for carrying loads or riding; as for killing it through thirst or hunger, or burning it with fire without a justifying cause, rational people do not see that he has the right to do so. That is, every ownership in this human society is a weak ownership that only validates some conceivable actions regarding the owned object, not every possible action. This is in contrast to His ownership, the Exalted, of things, for they have no lord (رب) besides Allah who owns them, and they do not possess for themselves any benefit or harm, nor death, nor life, nor resurrection. Thus, every conceivable action regarding them belongs to Him, the Exalted. Whatever action He takes regarding His servants and His creation, He has the right to it, without it entailing any ugliness (قبح), blame (ذم), or reproach (لوم). This is because an action among actions is only deemed ugly or blameworthy when the actor does not own the right to it, because rational people do not grant him that right. Thus, the ownership of such an actor is limited and directed toward actions permissible according to reason. As for Him, the Exalted, every action He takes is the action of an Owner and an action upon what is owned; thus, there is no ugliness, no blame, and no such thing.

He has supported this reality by preventing anyone else from any action in His kingdom except what He wills or permits. He is the Questioner and the Accountant, not the questioned or the one held to account. The Exalted said: مَن ذَا الَّذِي يَشْفَعُ عِندَهُ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ — "Who is it that may intercede with Him except with His permission?" (2:255); and He, the Exalted, said: مَا مِن شَفِيعٍ إِلَّا مِن بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِ — "there is no intercessor, except after His leave" (10:3); and He, the Exalted, said: لَّوْ يَشَاءُ اللَّهُ لَهَدَى النَّاسَ جَمِيعًا — "had Allah wished, He would have guided mankind all together" (13:31); and He said: يُضِلُّ مَن يَشَاءُ وَيَهْدِي مَن يَشَاءُ — "He leads astray whomever He wishes and guides whomever He wishes" (16:93); and He, the Exalted, said: وَمَا تَشَاءُونَ إِلَّا أَن يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ — "But you do not wish unless it is wished by Allah" (76:30); and He, the Exalted, said: لَا يُسْأَلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ وَهُمْ يُسْأَلُونَ — "He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned" (21:23). Thus, Allah is the Actor and the One who acts in His kingdom, and nothing else has any share in that except by His permission and His will. This is what His Lordship (ربوبيته) necessitates.

Furthermore, we see that He has placed Himself in the position of legislation (التشريع) and has proceeded therein according to what rational people proceed upon in human society—regarding the approval of the good and the praise and gratitude for it, and the disapproval of the ugly and the blame for it. As the Exalted said: إِن تُبْدُوا الصَّدَقَاتِ فَنِعِمَّا هِيَ — "If you disclose your charities, that is well" (2:271); and He said: بِئْسَ الِاسْمُ الْفُسُوقُ — "Evil is the name of transgression" (49:11). He mentioned that His legislations take into account the interests (مصالح) and harms (مفاسد) of the human being, observing what is most beneficial to remedy human deficiency. The Exalted said: إِذَا دَعَاكُمْ لِمَا يُحْيِيكُمْ — "when He summons you to that which will give you life" (8:24); and He, the Exalted, said: ذَٰلِكُمْ خير لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ — "That is better for you, should you know" (61:11); and He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسَانِ ... وَيَنْهَىٰ عَنِ الْفَحْشَاءِ وَالْمُنكَرِ وَالْبَغْيِ — "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness... and He forbids indecency, wrongdoing, and aggression" (16:90); and He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشَاءِ — "Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecency" (7:28). The verses in this regard are numerous.

In this, there is an endorsement of the method of rational people in society. This means that these circulating concepts among the rational—such as beauty and ugliness, interest and harm, command and prohibition, reward and punishment, or praise and blame, and so on—and the rulings related to them, such as their saying: "The good must be preferred" and "The beautiful must be done" and "The ugly must be avoided," just as they are the foundation for general rational rulings, so too are the religious rulings (الأحكام الشرعية) that Allah has legislated for His servants; they observe these principles. It is the way of rational people that their actions must be motivated by rational goals and interests. Among their actions are their legislations and their establishing of rulings and laws, and among them is the establishing of recompense and the requiting of good with good and evil with evil if they so wish. All of these are motivated by interests and righteous goals. If there were not, in a particular command or prohibition among the rational commands, that which contains the welfare of society in a way that applies to the case, rational people would not undertake such a thing. All recompense is only based on the correspondence (المسانخة) between the recompense and the original deed in its goodness or evilness, and in a measure and manner that is appropriate. Among their rulings is that command, prohibition, and every legislative ruling are only directed toward the one who has choice (المختار), not the one who is compelled (المضطر) or forced (المجبر) to act. Also, that good or bad recompense—meaning reward and punishment—only pertains to voluntary action (الفعل الإختياري), except where the loss of choice and falling into compulsion is based on a prior bad choice, such as one who places himself in a state of compelled disobedience; in such a case, rational people do not see his punishment as ugly, and they pay no heed to the story of his compulsion.

Therefore, if He, the Glorified, were to compel His servants toward acts of obedience or acts of disobedience, the recompense of the obedient with Paradise and the disobedient with the Fire would be nothing but caprice (جزافاً) in the case of the obedient, and injustice (ظلماً) in the case of the disobedient. Caprice and injustice are both ugly according to the rational, and it would necessitate "preferring one side without a preponderant factor" (الترجيح من غير مرجح), which is also ugly according to them. There is no proof (حجة) in what is ugly, and the Exalted has said: لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَى اللَّهِ حُجَّةٌ بَعْدَ الرُّسُلِ — "so that mankind may not have any argument against Allah, after the [sending of the] apostles" (4:165); and He, the Exalted, said: لِّيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَن بَيِّنَةٍ وَيَحْيَىٰ مَنْ حَيَّ عَن بَيِّنَةٍ — "so that he who perishes might perish by a manifest proof, and he who lives might live by a manifest proof" (8:42). Thus, several matters have become clear through the preceding explanation:

First: That legislation (التشريع) is not built upon the foundation of compulsion (الإجبار) in actions. Thus, religious obligations are established in accordance with the interests of the servants in their worldly life and their hereafter, firstly; and they are directed toward the servants insofar as they have choice in acting or refraining, secondly. Those obligated are only rewarded or punished for what their hands have earned of good or evil by choice.

Second: That what the Quran attributes to Him, the Exalted—such as misleading, deception, outwitting, aiding in rebellion, giving authority to the devil and making him a guardian over the human being, assigning a companion, and the like—all of these are attributed to Him, the Exalted, in a manner that suits the sanctuary of His holiness and His transcendence from the stains of deficiency, ugliness, and evil. For all these meanings ultimately return to misleading (الإضلال) and its branches and types. Not every misleading—even initial misleading (الإضلال البدوي) or by way of making one heedless—is attributed to Him or is worthy of His presence. Rather, what is established for Him is misleading as a recompense and abandonment (خذلانا) for one who, by his own bad choice, invites that upon himself. As the Exalted said: يُضِلُّ بِهِ كَثِيرًا وَيَهْدِي بِهِ كَثِيرًا وَمَا يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ — "He misleads many by it and guides many by it; and He misleads none by it except the transgressors" (2:26); and He said: فَلَمَّا زَاغُوا أَزَاغَ اللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ — "So when they deviated, Allah made their hearts deviate" (61:5); and He, the Exalted, said: كَذَٰلِكَ يُضِلُّ اللَّهُ مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ مُّرْتَابٌ — "Thus does Allah lead astray him who is a profligate, a doubter" (40:34).

Third: That the Divine Decree (القضاء) does not pertain to the deeds of the servants insofar as they are attributed to the actors by "active attribution" (الإنتساب الفعلي) rather than "existential attribution" (الإنتساب الوجودي). Further clarification of this statement will come in the following supplement and in the discussion on Decree and Destiny (القضاء والقدر), Allah willing.

Fourth: That legislation, just as it does not suit compulsion (الجبر), likewise does not suit delegation (التفويض). This is because there is no meaning to masterly command and prohibition in that which the Master does not own in any way. Additionally, delegation cannot be completed except by stripping the absolute ownership from Him, the Exalted, over some of what is in His kingdom.

(Narrative Discussion)

Narrations have reached the level of being widely transmitted (إستفاضت) from the Imams of the People of the House (peace be upon them) that they said: لا جبر ولا تفويض بل أمر بين أمرين — "There is neither compulsion nor delegation, but a matter between the two matters."

In al-Uyun, through several chains: When the Commander of the Faithful, ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him), returned from Siffin, an old man who had witnessed the battle with him stood up and said: "O Commander of the Faithful, inform us about this journey of ours: was it by the decree (قضاء) and destiny (قدر) of Allah?" The Commander of the Faithful said to him: أجل يا شيخ فوالله ما علوتم تلعة ولا هبطتم بطن واد الا بقضاء من الله وقدر — "Yes, O old man! By Allah, you did not ascend a hill nor descend into the heart of a valley except by the decree and destiny of Allah." The old man said: "I seek from Allah the reward for my hardship, O Commander of the Faithful." He (peace be upon him) replied: مهلا يا شيخ لعلك تظن قضاء حتما وقدراً لازماً ، لو كان كذلك لبطل الثواب والعقاب والأمر والنهي والزجر ، ولسقط معنى الوعد والوعيد ، ولم تكن على مسيء لائمة ولا لمحسن محمدة ، ولكان المحسن أولى باللائمة من المذنب والمذنب أولى بالإحسان من المحسن ، تلك مقالة عبدة الأوثان وخصماء الرحمن وقدرية هذه الامة ومجوسها . يا شيخ إن الله كلف تخييراً ونهى تحذيرا ، وأعطى على القليل كثيرا ولم يعص مغلوباً ، ولم يطع مكروهاً ولم يخلق السموات والأرض وما بينهما باطلا . ذلك ظن الذين كفروا فويل للذين كفروا من النار — "Wait, O old man! Perhaps you think it was a deterministic decree and a necessary destiny. If it were so, reward and punishment, command and prohibition, and deterrence would be nullified. The meaning of the promise and the threat would fall away. There would be no blame for the evildoer nor praise for the doer of good. Indeed, the doer of good would be more deserving of blame than the sinner, and the sinner more deserving of kindness than the doer of good. That is the saying of the idol-worshippers, the adversaries of the All-Beneficent, and the fatalists (القدرية) of this nation and its Magians. O old man! Indeed, Allah has given obligations while granting choice, and He has forbidden while giving warning. He has given much for little. He is not disobeyed by being overcome, nor is He obeyed by being coerced. He did not create the heavens and the earth and what is between them in vain. That is the thought of those who disbelieve; so woe to those who disbelieve from the Fire."

I say: His saying: "by the decree and destiny of Allah" until his saying: "I seek from Allah the reward for my hardship." Let it be known that among the oldest discussions that occurred in Islam as a subject of debate and conflicting views are the issue of theology (الكلام) and the issue of Decree and Destiny (القضاء والقدر). When they conceptualized the meaning of Decree and Destiny and deduced its conclusion, it was that the eternal Divine Will (الإرادة الإلهية الأزلية) pertained to everything in the world. Thus, nothing in the world exists with the quality of possibility (الإمكان); rather, if it exists, it is by necessity (بالضرورة), due to the attachment of the Will to it and the impossibility of His willed object failing to match His Will. If it is non-existent, it is by impossibility (بالإمتناع), due to the lack of the Will's attachment to it; otherwise, it would have existed. When this rule was applied to all existents, the problem arose regarding the voluntary actions (الأفعال الإختيارية) proceeding from us. We see at first glance that the ratio of these actions—in existence and non-existence—to us is equal, and only one of the two sides is determined by the attachment of the will to it after choosing that side; thus, our actions are voluntary, and the will is influential in its realization and a cause for its origination.

However, the assumption that the eternal Divine Will—which cannot fail—attaches to the deed nullifies the voluntariness of the deed, firstly, and the influence of our will in the existence of the deed, secondly. At that point, there would be no meaning to power (القدرة) over the deed before the deed, nor any meaning to religious obligation (التكليف) due to the lack of power before the deed—especially in the case of opposition and rebellion, for it would be an obligation beyond one's capacity (تكليفاً بما لا يطاق). There would be no meaning to rewarding the obedient through compulsion, for it would be ugly caprice, nor any meaning to punishing the sinner through compulsion, for it would be ugly injustice, among other such consequences. All these researchers committed themselves to this and said: Power does not exist before the deed, and beauty and ugliness are not real matters such that His actions, the Exalted, must be restricted by them; rather, whatever He does is good, and His action is not described as ugly. Thus, there is no barrier there to "preferring without a preponderant factor," nor to capricious will, nor to obligation beyond capacity, nor to punishing the sinner even if the deficiency was not from his side, among other consequences—exalted is He far above that.

In short, the belief in Decree and Destiny in the first century was synonymous with the removal of beauty and ugliness and recompense based on merit. Therefore, when the old man heard from him (peace be upon him) that the journey was by decree and destiny, he said in a state of distress and despair: "I seek from Allah the reward for my hardship," meaning: "My journey and my will are devoid of utility since the Divine Will has attached to them; nothing remains for me but the hardship and fatigue of the action, so I seek its reward from my Lord, for He is the one who fatigued me with it." The Imam (peace be upon him) answered him by saying: "If it were so, reward and punishment would be nullified..." which is an appeal to the rational principles upon which the foundation of legislation is built. He (peace be upon him) argued at the end of his speech with His saying: "He did not create the heavens and the earth and what is between them in vain..." This is because the validity of a capricious will—which is among the consequences of the removal of choice—necessitates the possibility of an action occurring without a goal or purpose. This necessitates the possibility of the removal of the goal from creation and origination. This possibility is synonymous with necessity; thus, on this assumption, there would be no goal for creation and origination, and that would be "creating the heavens and the earth and what is between them in vain." In that lies the nullification of the Return (المعاد) and every other peril. His saying: "He is not disobeyed by being overcome, nor is He obeyed by being coerced" likely means: He is not disobeyed while the sinner is overcome by compulsion, nor is He obeyed while the obedience is coerced for the obedient.

In al-Tawhid and al-Uyun, from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him): Compulsion and delegation were mentioned in his presence, so he said: ألا أعلمكم في هذا أصلا لا تختلفون فيه ولا يخاصمكم عليه أحد إلا كسرتموه ؟ قلنا إن رأيت ذلك ، فقال إن الله عز وجل لم يطع بإكراه ، ولم يعص بغلبة ، ولم يهمل العباد في ملكه ، هو المالك لما ملّكهم ، والقادر على ما أقدرهم عليه فان إئتمر العباد بطاعته لم يكن الله منها صادّاً ، ولا منها مانعاً وان إئتمروا بمعصيته فشاء أن يحول بينهم وبين ذلك فعل ، وان لم يحل فعلوه فليس هو الذي أدخلهم فيه — "Shall I not teach you a principle in this regarding which you will not differ, and no one will argue with you over it except that you will break him?" We said: "If you see fit." He said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is not obeyed by coercion, nor is He disobeyed by being overcome, nor has He neglected the servants in His kingdom. He is the Owner of what He has given them to own, and the Powerful over what He has empowered them to do. If the servants resolve upon His obedience, Allah is not one to block them from it nor prevent them. And if they resolve upon His disobedience, and He wishes to intervene between them and that, He does so; and if He does not intervene and they do it, He is not the one who made them enter into it." Then he (peace be upon him) said: "Whoever masters the boundaries of this speech has defeated whoever opposes him."

I say: You have realized that what forced the proponents of compulsion (المجبّرة) to say what they said was the discussion on Decree and Destiny and the deduction of necessity and inevitability therein. This discussion is correct, and the result is also a correct result; however, they erred in its application. The matter of realities (الحقائق) and mental constructs (الإعتباريات) became confused for them, and necessity (الوجوج) and possibility (الإمكان) became mixed. To clarify: Decree and Destiny, on the assumption of their being established, result in things in the system of origination and creation being characterized by necessity and inevitability. Every existent among existents and every state among the states of an existent is measured and limited with Allah, the Glorified; all that is with it of existence, its modes, and its states are determined for it, neither failing nor differing. It is clear that necessity and inevitability are among the properties of the cause (العلة); for the complete cause (العلة التامة) is that which, when a thing is measured against it, becomes characterized by the quality of necessity, and when measured against anything else, it is only characterized by possibility. Thus, the spreading of Destiny and Decree in the world is the flow of complete causality and effectuality in the world in its entirety.

This does not contradict the flow of the rule of potentiality and possibility in the world from another perspective and another view. The voluntary action proceeding from a human being by his will, if assumed to be attributed to all that it needs for its existence—such as knowledge, will, sound tools, the matter to which the action pertains, and other temporal and spatial conditions—would be of necessary existence (ضروري الوجود), and it is that to which the eternal Divine Will has attached. However, the action being necessary in relation to all parts of its complete cause and from its perspective does not necessitate its being necessary when measured against some parts of its complete cause. For instance, if the action is measured against the actor alone, without the rest of the parts of its complete cause, it does not exceed the limit of possibility and never reaches the limit of necessity. Thus, there is no meaning to what they claimed—that the universality of the Decree and the attachment of the Divine Will to the action necessitates the disappearance of power and the removal of choice. Rather, the Divine Will has only attached to the action with all its existential modes and specificities, among which are its connections to its causes and the conditions of its existence.

In other words, the Divine Will has attached to the action proceeding from Zayd, for example, not absolutely, but insofar as it is a voluntary action proceeding from such an actor at such a time and such a place. Therefore, the influence of the Divine Will on the action necessitates the action being voluntary; otherwise, the object of the Divine Will would have failed to match it. Thus, the influence of the Divine Will in making the action necessary necessitates the action being voluntary—that is, the action being necessary in relation to the Divine Will and possible and voluntary in relation to the human, active will. Thus, the Will is in the longitudinal line (في طول) of the will, not in the horizontal line (في عرض) with it such that they would conflict, and such that the influence of the Divine Will would necessitate the nullification of the influence of the human will. It has become clear that the basis of the error of the proponents of compulsion lies in their failure to distinguish the nature of the Divine Will's attachment to the action, their failure to differentiate between two longitudinal wills and two horizontal wills, and their judgment that the influence of the servant's will in the action is nullified due to the attachment of Allah's will to it.

The Mu‘tazilites, although they opposed the proponents of compulsion regarding the voluntariness of the servant's actions and other consequences, followed a path in proving it that was no less corrupt than the statement of the proponents of compulsion. This is because they conceded to the proponents of compulsion that the attachment of Allah's will to the action necessitates the nullification of choice. From another side, they insisted on the voluntariness of voluntary actions; thus, they ultimately denied the attachment of the Divine Will to the actions. This forced them to establish another creator for the actions, which is the human being, just as the creator of other things is Allah, the Glorified. Thus, the peril of dualism (الثنوية) became necessary for them. Then they fell into other perils more severe than those the proponents of compulsion fell into, as he (peace be upon him) said: مساكين القدرية أرادوا أن يصفوا الله بعدله فأخرجوه من قدرته وسلطانه — "Poor are the Qadariyya (proponents of delegation); they wanted to describe Allah by His justice, so they removed Him from His power and His sovereignty."

An example of this is like a master among conventional masters who chooses one of his servants and marries him to one of his young women, then allocates for him a piece of land and designates for him a house, furniture, and other things a human needs in his life for a limited time and a specified term. If we say that the master, although he gave to his servant what he gave and made him own what he owned, still the servant does not own anything—and where is the servant from ownership?—that would be the statement of the proponents of compulsion. If we say that the master, by giving the property to his servant and making him own it, has made him the owner and has himself been removed from ownership, and the owner is the servant, that would be the statement of the Mu‘tazilites. But if we combine the two ownerships by preserving the two levels and say: the master has his station in mastership and the servant has his station in servitude, and the servant only owns within the ownership of the master—so the master is the owner at the same time that the servant is the owner, thus here is ownership upon ownership—that would be the true statement held by the Imams of the People of the House (peace be upon them), and for which the proof stands.

In al-Ihtijaj, regarding what ‘Abayah ibn Rab‘i al-Asadi asked the Commander of the Faithful, ‘Ali (peace be upon him), concerning the meaning of capacity (الإستطاعة). The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said: تملكها من دون الله أو مع الله ؟ — "Do you own it apart from Allah or with Allah?" ‘Abayah ibn Rab‘i remained silent. He said to him: "Speak, O ‘Abayah." He said: "And what shall I say, O Commander of the Faithful?" He said: تقول تملكها بالله الذي يملكها من دونك فإن ملككها كان ذلك من عطائه وإن سلبكها كان ذلك من بلائه وهو المالك لما ملكك والقادر على ما عليه أقدرك — "You say: you own it through Allah who owns it apart from you. If He gives you ownership of it, that is from His gift; and if He strips you of it, that is from His trial. He is the Owner of what He has given you to own, and the Powerful over what He has empowered you to do."

I say: The meaning of the narration is clear from what we have explained just now.

In Sharh al-Aqa'id by al-Mufid: It has been narrated from Abu al-Hasan the Third (peace be upon him) that he was asked about the deeds of the servants: are they created by Allah, the Exalted? He (peace be upon him) said: لو كان خالقاً لها لما تبرأ منها وقد قال سبحانه : إن الله بريء من المشركين ولم يرد البراءة من خلق ذواتهم وإنما تبرأ من شركهم وقبائحهم — "If He were their creator, He would not have disassociated Himself from them, while He, the Glorified, has said: 'Indeed Allah is disassociated from the polytheists.' He did not mean disassociation from the creation of their essences; He only disassociated Himself from their polytheism and their ugliness."

I say: For actions, there are two aspects: the aspect of subsistence and existence, and the aspect of attribution to the actor. This second aspect is that by which actions are characterized as being obedience or disobedience, or good or evil. For marriage and adultery—there is no difference between them from the aspect of subsistence and realization; the only distinguishing difference is that marriage conforms to the command of Allah, the Exalted, while adultery lacks the aforementioned conformity. Likewise, killing a soul for a soul versus killing a soul without a soul, and striking an orphan for discipline versus striking him out of injustice. Thus, acts of disobedience lack an aspect of the aspects of righteousness, or conformity to the command, or the social goal, unlike others.

Allah, the Exalted, has said: اللَّهُ خَالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "Allah is the creator of all things" (39:62), and the action is a "thing" by its subsistence and existence. He (peace be upon him) said: كل ما وقع عليه اسم شيء فهو مخلوق ما خلا الله — "Everything upon which the name 'thing' falls is created, except Allah." Then the Exalted said: الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ — "who perfected everything He created" (32:7). Thus, it becomes clear that everything, just as it is created, is beautiful in its being created. Creation (الخلقة) and beauty (الحسن) are inseparable companions; one never detaches from the other at all.

Furthermore, He, the Exalted, named some actions as "evil" (سيئة), saying: مَن جَاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثَالِهَا وَمَن جَاءَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ فَلَا يُجْزَىٰ إِلَّا مِثْلَهَا — "Whoever brings a good deed shall receive ten times its like, and whoever brings an evil deed shall be requited only with its like" (6:160). These are the acts of disobedience performed by the human being, as evidenced by the recompense. We know from this that, insofar as they are acts of disobedience, they are non-existential (عدمية) and not created; otherwise, they would have been beautiful.

The Exalted said: مَا أَصَابَ مِن مُّصِيبَةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِي أَنفُسِكُمْ إِلَّا فِي كِتَابٍ مِّن قَبْلِ أَن نَّبْرَأَهَا — "No affliction visits the earth or your souls but it is in a Book before We bring it about" (57:22); and He said: مَا أَصَابَ مِن مُّصِيبَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ وَمَن يُؤْمِن بِاللَّهِ يَهْدِ قَلْبَهُ — "No affliction visits [anyone] except by Allah’s leave. Whoever has faith in Allah, He guides his heart" (64:11); and He said: وَمَا أَصَابَكُم مِّن مُّصِيبَةٍ فَبِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُو عَن كَثِيرٍ — "Whatever affliction that may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]" (42:30); and He said: مَّا أَصَابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ اللَّهِ وَمَا أَصَابَكَ مِن سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِن نَّفْسِكَ — "Whatever good may happen to you is from Allah, and whatever evil may happen to you is from yourself" (4:79); and He said: وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ حَسَنَةٌ يَقُولُوا هَٰذِهِ مِنْ عِندِ اللَّهِ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَقُولُوا هَٰذِهِ مِنْ عِندِكَ ، قُلْ كُلٌّ مِّنْ عِندِ اللَّهِ فَمَالِ هَٰؤُلَاءِ الْقَوْمِ لَا يَكَادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ حَدِيثًا — "If a good fortune comes their way, they say, ‘This is from Allah;’ and if an ill fortune befalls them, they say, ‘This is from you.’ Say, ‘All is from Allah.’ What is the matter with these people that they do not seem to understand any matter?" (4:78).

We know from this that these afflictions are only "evil" in a relative sense (سيئات نسبية). This means that a human being who is blessed with one of Allah's blessings—such as security, safety, health, or wealth—is considered a possessor; if he loses it due to the descent of a calamity or the occurrence of an affliction, the calamity is considered evil in relation to him because it is coupled with a loss and a non-existence. Thus, every calamity is from Allah and is not evil from this perspective; it is only relatively evil in relation to the human being who was a possessor. Therefore, every evil is a non-existential matter that is not attributed from this perspective to Allah, the Glorified, at all, even if from another perspective it is attributed to Him, the Exalted, by way of permission (الإذن) and the like.

In Qurb al-Isnad, from al-Bazanti: I said to Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him): "Some of our companions believe in compulsion (الجبر) and some in capacity (الاستطاعة)." He said to me: أكتب ، قال الله تبارك وتعالى يا بن آدم بمشيّتي كنت أنت الذي تشاء لنفسك ما تشاء وبقوّتي أدّيت إليّ فرائضي وبنعمتي قوّيت على معصيتي جعلتك سميعاً بصيراً قوياً ، ما أصابك من حسنة فمن الله ، وما أصابك من سيئة فمن نفسك ، وذلك اني أولى بحسناتك منك وأنت أولى بسيئاتك مني ، وذلك أني لا أسأل عما أفعل وهم يسألون ، فقد نظمت لك كل شيء تريد — "Write: Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, said: 'O son of Adam! By My will, you were the one who wills for yourself what you will; and by My power, you performed My obligations to Me; and by My blessing, you were strengthened for My disobedience. I made you hearing, seeing, and strong. Whatever good happens to you is from Allah, and whatever evil happens to you is from yourself. That is because I am more worthy of your good deeds than you, and you are more worthy of your evil deeds than Me. That is because I am not questioned about what I do, but they are questioned. I have organized for you everything you desire.'" This, or something close to it, is narrated through other Sunni and Shi'a chains. In short, the actions that are not attributed to Allah, the Glorified, are the acts of disobedience from the perspective that they are specifically acts of disobedience. By this, the meaning of his (peace be upon him) saying in the previous narration is known: "If He were their creator, He would not have disassociated Himself from them... He only disassociated Himself from their polytheism and their ugliness."

In al-Tawhid: From Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them): قالا : إن الله عزّ وجلّ أرحم بخلقه من أن يجبر خلقه على الذنوب ثم يعذبهم عليها ، والله أعزّ من أن يريد أمراً فلا يكون — "They both said: 'Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is more merciful to His creation than to compel His creation to sins and then punish them for them; and Allah is more mighty than to will a matter and it not be.'" He said: They (peace be upon them) were asked: "Is there a third station between compulsion and destiny (الجبر والقدر)?" They said: "Yes, wider than what is between the heaven and the earth."

In al-Tawhid, from Muhammad ibn ‘Ajlan: I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "Did Allah delegate (فوّض) the matter to the servants?" He said: الله أكرم من أن يفوّض إليهم — "Allah is more generous than to delegate to them." I said: "Then did Allah compel the servants to their actions?" He said: الله أعدل من أن يجبر عبداً على فعل ثم يعذّبه عليه — "Allah is more just than to compel a servant to an action and then punish him for it."

In al-Tawhid also, from Mahzam: Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Inform me about that regarding which those you left behind of our followers differ." I said: "Regarding compulsion and delegation (الجبر والتفويض)." He said: "Then ask me." I said: "Did Allah compel the servants to acts of disobedience?" He said: الله أقهر لهم من ذلك — "Allah is more dominant (أقهر) over them than that." I said: "Then did He delegate to them?" He said: "Allah is more powerful (أقدر) over them than that." I said: "Then what is this thing, may Allah set you right?" He turned his hand twice or thrice and then said: لو أجبتك فيه لكفرت — "If I were to answer you regarding it, you would disbelieve."

I say: His saying (peace be upon him): "Allah is more dominant over them than that" means that compulsion (الجبر) is only through a dominance from the compeller by which the resistance of the active power is nullified. More dominant and stronger than that is for the Willer to will the occurrence of the voluntary action from its actor through the course of his choice, so he performs it without his will and choice being nullified, and without the will of the actor contending with the will of the Commander.

In al-Tawhid also, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): قال : رسول الله : من زعم أن الله يأمر بالسوء والفحشاء فقد كذب على الله ، ومن زعم أن الخير والشر بغير مشيّة الله فقد أخرج الله من سلطانه — "The Messenger of Allah said: 'Whoever claims that Allah enjoins evil and indecency has lied against Allah; and whoever claims that good and evil are without the will of Allah has removed Allah from His sovereignty.'"

In al-Tara'if: It is narrated that al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf wrote to al-Hasan al-Basri, ‘Amr ibn ‘Ubayd, Wasil ibn ‘Ata', and ‘Amir al-Sha‘bi, asking them to mention what they had and what had reached them regarding Decree and Destiny. Al-Hasan al-Basri wrote to him: "The best of what has reached me is what I heard from the Commander of the Faithful, ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him), that he said: أتظن ان الذي نهاك دهاك ؟ وإنما دهاك أسفلك وأعلاك ، والله بريء من ذاك — 'Do you think that He who forbade you is the one who afflicted you? Rather, what afflicted you is your lower and your upper parts, and Allah is disassociated from that.'" ‘Amr ibn ‘Ubayd wrote to him: "The best I have heard regarding Decree and Destiny is the saying of the Commander of the Faithful, ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him): لو كان الزور في الأصل محتوماً لكان المزوّر في القصاص مظلوماً — 'If falsehood were originally decreed, then the one who was falsified against would be oppressed in the retaliation.'" Wasil ibn ‘Ata' wrote to him: "The best I have heard regarding Decree and Destiny is the saying of the Commander of the Faithful, ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him): أيدلك على الطريق ويأخذ عليك المضيق ؟ — 'Does He guide you to the path and then block the narrow pass against you?'" Al-Sha‘bi wrote to him: "The best I have heard regarding Decree and Destiny is the saying of the Commander of the Faithful, ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him): كلما استغفرت الله منه فهو منك ، وكلما حمدت الله عليه فهو منه — 'Everything for which you seek Allah's forgiveness is from you, and everything for which you praise Allah is from Him.'" When their letters reached al-Hajjaj and he examined them, he said: "They have taken it from a pure spring."

In al-Tara'if also: It is narrated that a man asked Ja‘far ibn Muhammad al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) about Decree and Destiny, and he said: ما استطعت أن تلوم العبد عليه فهو منه ، وما لم تستطع أن تلوم العبد عليه فهو من فعل الله — "Whatever you are able to blame the servant for is from him, and whatever you are unable to blame the servant for is from the act of Allah." Allah says to the servant: "Why did you disobey? Why did you transgress? Why did you drink wine? Why did you commit adultery?" This is the act of the servant. But He does not say to him: "Why did you fall ill? Why were you short? Why did you become white? Why did you become black?" because that is from the act of Allah, the Exalted.

In Nahj al-Balagha: He (peace be upon him) was asked about monotheism (التوحيد) and justice (العدل), and he said: التوحيد أن لا تتوهمه ، والعدل أن لا تتَّهمه — "Monotheism is that you do not imagine Him, and justice is that you do not accuse Him."

I say: The narrations regarding what has been discussed are very numerous; however, what we have cited encompasses the meanings of those we omitted. If you contemplate the preceding narrations, you will find them containing several specific methods of argumentation.

Among them is the argumentation based on the very existence of command (الأمر), prohibition (النهي), punishment (العقاب), and reward (الثواب), and their like, to prove the reality of choice (الاختيار) without compulsion (جبر) or delegation (تفويض). This is as seen in the narration transmitted from the Commander of the Faithful, ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام), in his response to the old man; its premise is close to what we derived from the speech of the Exalted.

Among them is the argumentation based on the occurrence of matters in the Qur’an that would not be true if compulsion or delegation were true, such as His saying, the Exalted: لِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ “To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth” (Qarai 5:120), and His saying: وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ “and your Lord is not tyrannical to [His] servants” (Qarai 41:46), and His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشَاءِ “Say, ‘Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecency’” (Qarai 7:28). It might be argued against this that an act is only an indecency (فاحشة) or injustice (ظلم) in relation to us, but when attributed to Him, the Exalted, it is called neither indecency nor injustice; thus, no indecency or injustice proceeds from Him. However, the beginning of the verse, with its specific implication, refutes this, for the Exalted says: وَإِذَا فَعَلُوا فَاحِشَةً قَالُوا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهَا آبَاءَنَا وَاللَّهُ أَمَرَنَا بِهَا قُلْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشَاءِ “When they commit an indecency, they say, ‘We found our fathers practicing it, and Allah has enjoined it upon us.’ Say, ‘Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecency’” (Qarai 7:28). The reference in His saying "to this" necessitates that the subsequent negation is directed toward that very act, whether it is called indecency or not.

Among them is the argumentation from the perspective of the attributes (الصفات). This is because Allah has named Himself with the Most Beautiful Names and described Himself with Sublime Attributes that would neither be true nor validly established under the assumption of compulsion or delegation. For He, the Exalted, is the Subduer (قهّار), the All-Powerful (قادر), the All-Generous (كريم), and the All-Merciful (رحيم). These are attributes whose meanings cannot be established unless the existence of everything is from Him, the Exalted, while the deficiency, corruption, and evil of anything do not trace back to His Holy Presence, as seen in the narrations we cited from al-Tawhid.

Among them is the argumentation using examples such as seeking forgiveness (الاستغفار) and the occurrence of blame (اللوم). For if the sin were not from the servant, there would be no meaning to his seeking forgiveness; and if the act were entirely from Allah, there would be no difference between one act and another regarding the occurrence of blame for some and its absence for others.

There are other narrations reported regarding the meanings of misguiding (الإضلال), sealing (الطبع), and seducing (الإغواء) attributed to Him, the Glorified.

In al-Uyun, narrated from Imam al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَتَرَكَهُمْ فِي ظُلُمَاتٍ لَّا يُبْصِرُونَ “and left them in a twofold darkness, sightless” (Qarai 2:17), he (عليه‌السلام) said: “Indeed, Allah is not described by ‘leaving’ (الترك) as His creation is described. Rather, when He knows they will not return from disbelief and misguidance, He withholds from them assistance and grace (اللطف) and leaves them to their own choice.”

Also in al-Uyun, from him (عليه‌السلام) regarding the saying of the Exalted: ختم الله على قلوبهم “Allah has set a seal on their hearts” (Qarai 2:7), he said: “The sealing (الختم) is the stamping (الطبع) upon the hearts of the disbelievers as a punishment for their disbelief, as Allah the Exalted said: بل طبع الله عليها بكفرهم فلا يؤمنون إلا قليلاً ‘rather Allah has set a seal on them for their faithlessness, so they do not believe except a few’ (Qarai 4:155).”

In al-Majma‘, from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) regarding the saying of the Exalted: إن الله لا يستحيي “Indeed Allah is not ashamed [to draw a similitude]” (Qarai 2:26), he said: “This statement from Allah is a refutation of those who claim that Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, misguides the servants and then punishes them for their misguidance...” the tradition continues. I say: the explanation of its meaning has already passed.

(A Philosophical Discussion)

There is no doubt that the entities we call species (أنواع) in the external world are those that perform specific actions (الأفاعيل النوعية), and they are their subjects (موضوعات). We only established the existence of these species and their distinct nature from others through their effects (الآثار) and actions. We observed through the senses various actions and differing effects without the senses perceiving anything beyond the accidental effects (الآثار العرضية). Then, through syllogism (القياس) and demonstration (البرهان), we established an efficient cause (علة فاعلة) for them and a subject that sustains them. We then judged these subjects—meaning the species—to be different due to the difference in the effects and actions witnessed by us. Thus, the witnessed difference in the effects of a human and other animal species, for example, is what necessitates the judgment that there are different species named such-and-such, having such-and-such effects and actions. Similarly, we only establish and judge the differences between accidents (الأعراض) and actions from the side of their subjects or their properties (خواص).

In any case, actions in relation to their subjects are primarily divided into two categories: First: The action proceeding from nature (الطبيعة) without knowledge (العلم) playing a role in its issuance, such as the actions of growth (النشوء), development (النمو), and nutrition (التغذي) in plants, and the movements of bodies. Of this type are health, sickness, and the like; for although they are known to us and subsist in us, the attachment of knowledge to them does not affect their existence or issuance at all; rather, they are entirely dependent on their natural agent. Second: The action proceeding from the agent insofar as it is known (معلوم) and knowledge has attached to it, as in the voluntary actions (الأفعال الارادية) of humans and other sentient animals. This type of action is only performed by its agent insofar as knowledge attaches to it, identifies it, and distinguishes it. Knowledge here only serves to specify and distinguish it from others. This distinction and specification only occur because of the applicability of a concept that constitutes a perfection (كمال) for the agent—an applicability mediated by knowledge. For any agent only performs that action which is a requirement of its perfection and the completeness of its existence. Thus, the action proceeding from knowledge only requires knowledge so that the agent may distinguish what is a perfection for it from what is not.

From here, we see that actions proceeding from ingrained habits (الملكات)—such as the issuance of organized letter sounds from a speaking human, or actions proceeding from them with some natural requirement and involvement, like breathing in a human, or actions proceeding from a human due to the dominance of grief, fear, or the like—none of these require deliberation (ترو) by the agent. This is because there is only one mental image (صورة علمية) applicable to the act and the agent, with no state of anticipation for the act; thus, he acts inevitably. As for actions that have multiple mental images, where some are instances of human perfection—either in reality or imagination—and others are not, such as bread in relation to a hungry Zayd: it is satiating and removes his hunger, but it could also be the property of another, or poisoned, or filthy such that nature recoils from it. A human only deliberates to give preponderance (ترجيح) to one of these titles in its application to the bread, for example. Once one title is specified and the others fall away, and it becomes an instance of the agent's perfection, the agent does not hesitate in his action at all. The first category we call an involuntary act (فعل إضطراري), like natural effects. The second category we call a voluntary act (فعل إرادي), like walking and speaking.

The voluntary act—proceeding from knowledge and will (ارادة)—is subdivided into two types: The preponderance of one of the two sides (acting or refraining) is either attributed to the agent himself without being influenced by another—like the hungry person who deliberates over eating bread he possesses until he decides to leave it and not eat it because it was the property of another without permission, thus choosing and selecting preservation; or he decides to eat and eats it by choice (إختياراً). Or, the preponderance and specification are attributed to the influence of another—like one whom a tyrant compels (يجبره) to an act by threatening him with death or the like, so he performs it by compulsion (إجباراً) without it being specified by his own selection and choice. The first type is called an optional act (فعل اختياري), and the second a compulsory act (فعل اجباري). However, you will find upon careful reflection that the compulsory act—even if we attribute it to the compulsion of the compeller and say he is the one who makes one of the two sides impossible through compulsion, leaving the agent with only one side—is also like the optional act in that it does not occur except after the compelled agent gives preponderance to the side of acting over refraining. Even if the one who compels is the cause of the act in a way, the act does not occur as long as it is not given preponderance in the view of the agent, even if his view is based in some way on the compulsion and threat of the compeller. Sound intuition (الوجدان) bears witness to this. From here, it becomes clear that the division of voluntary acts into optional and compulsory is not a real division (تقسيم حقيقي) that categorizes the subject into two species differing in essence (الذات) and effects (الآثار). For the voluntary act only requires a cognitive specification and preponderance that defines the course of action for the agent, and this is the same in both the optional and compulsory act. That the agent's preponderance in one is based on his own ease and in the other on another does not necessitate a specific difference (إختلاف نوعي) leading to a difference in effects. Do you not see that one seeking shade under a wall, if he sees the wall is about to collapse and goes out in fear, his act is considered optional? But if a tyrant threatens him that if he does not stand up, he will demolish the wall upon him, and he goes out in fear, his act is considered compulsory—without there being any difference between the two acts and the two preponderances at all, except that one of the preponderances is based on the will of the tyrant.

If you say: It is enough of a difference between the two acts that the optional act, in its issuance, accords with a benefit (مصلحة) for the agent, and it is an act upon which praise and blame are based, and which is followed by reward and punishment, among other effects; this is unlike the compulsory act, upon which none of that follows.

I say: The matter is as mentioned, but these effects are only according to the convention of the rational (اعتبار العقلاء) based on what accords with the ultimate social perfection. Thus, they are conventional effects (آثار اعتبارية), not ontological ones (حقيقية). Therefore, the discussion of compulsion and choice is not a philosophical discussion (بحث فلسفي), because philosophical discussion only deals with external existents and their concrete effects (آثارها العينية). As for matters that terminate in types of rational conventions (الإعتبارات العقلائية), philosophical discussion does not reach them, nor does demonstration (برهان) encompass them at all, even if they are valid in their own domain and produce their effects. Thus, it is necessary to redirect the aforementioned discussion through another path. We say: There is no doubt that every contingent (ممكن) originated thing (حادث) is in need of a cause (علة), and this judgment is established through demonstration. There is also no doubt that a thing does not exist until it becomes necessary (ما لم يجب لم يوجد); for as long as the side of its existence is not specified by a specifier, its relation to existence and non-existence is equal. If the thing existed while being so, it would not be in need of a cause, which is absurd. Thus, if the existence of a thing is assumed, it is described by necessity (الضرورة) as long as it exists, and this necessity is acquired from the side of the cause. If we take the entire realm of existence, it is like a chain composed of ordered, successive links, all of which are necessary in existence (واجبة الوجود). There is no place for a merely contingent matter in this chain.

Then we say: This relationship of necessity (النسبة الوجوبية) only arises from the relation of the effect (المعلول) to its complete cause (علتها التامة)—whether simple or composed of many things like the four causes, conditions (الشرائط), and preparatory factors (المعدات). But if the said effect is related to some parts of the cause, or to something else if assumed, the relation is necessarily one of contingency (الإمكان). This is because if it were by necessity, the complete cause would be unnecessary, which is absurd. Thus, in our natural world, there are two systems: the system of necessity (نظام الضرورة) and the system of contingency (نظام الامكان). The system of necessity is spread over complete causes and their effects, and no contingent matter exists between the parts of this system—neither an essence (ذات) nor the act of an essence. The system of contingency is spread over matter (المادة) and the forms (الصور) that matter has the potential to be clothed with, and the effects it can accept. If you assume one of the optional acts of a human and relate it to its complete cause—which is the human, knowledge, will, the existence of receptive matter, the realization of spatial and temporal conditions, and the removal of obstacles; in short, everything the act needs for its existence—the act is necessary and essential (واجباً ضرورياً). But if it is related to the human only—and it is known that he is but one part of the parts of the complete cause—the relation is one of contingency.

Then we say: The reason for the need and poverty (الفقر) toward a cause—as explained in its proper place—is that existence (which is the basis of ontological bestowal) is a contingent existence (وجوداً إمكانياً); that is, a connective existence (رابطاً) which is in reality not independent in itself. As long as the chain of connection does not terminate in that which is independent by essence (مستقل بالذات), the chain of poverty and need will not be broken.

From here, it is concluded firstly: that the effect, by virtue of its dependence on its cause, is not severed from its need for the Necessary Cause (العلة الواجبة) to which the chain of contingency terminates.

And secondly: that this need, since it is from the perspective of existence, is a need in existence while preserving all its existential specificities (خصوصياته الوجودية) and its connections to its causes and its temporal and spatial conditions, and so on.

Thus, two matters have become clear: First: Just as the human is dependent in existence upon the Divine Will (الإرادة الإلهية) like all other natural essences and their natural acts, so too are the acts of the human dependent in existence upon the Divine Will. Thus, what the Mu‘tazilites mentioned regarding human acts not being connected in existence to Allah, the Glorified, and their denial of predestination (القدر), is fundamentally baseless. Since this dependence is an existential dependence (إستناد وجودي), the existential specificities present in the effect are involved in it. Every effect is dependent on its cause with the existential limit (حده الوجودي) that it possesses. Just as an individual human is dependent on the First Cause with all his existential limits—of father, mother, time, place, shape, quantity, quality, and other material factors—so too the act of a human is dependent on the First Cause, taken with all its existential specificities. Thus, when this act is attributed to the First Cause and the Necessary Will, for example, that does not remove it from what it is, nor does it necessitate the nullification of the human will's influence, for example. For the Necessary Will only attached to the act as proceeding from the human through will and choice. If this act, at the time of its realization, were involuntary and without choice, it would necessitate the failure of His Will, the Exalted, to realize His intended object (مراده), which is impossible. Thus, what the proponents of compulsion (المجبّرة) among the Ash‘arites held—that the attachment of the Divine Will to voluntary acts necessitates the nullification of the influence of will and choice—is utterly false. The truth worthy of belief is that human acts have a relation to the agent and a relation to the Necessary Being (الواجب), and one of the two relations does not necessitate the nullification of the other, because they are longitudinal (طوليتين) and not horizontal (عرضيَّتين).

Second: Just as acts have a dependence on their complete causes (and you have learned that this relation is necessary and essential, like all other existents related to their complete causes by necessity), they also have a dependence on some parts of their complete causes, such as the human, for example. You have learned that this relation is one of contingency (الإمكان). Thus, the fact that an act is necessary in existence (ضروري الوجود) by considering its complete necessary cause does not necessitate that this act is not contingent (مكناً) from another perspective. For both relations are established and they are not contradictory, as previously mentioned. Thus, what a group of materialists among contemporary philosophers mentioned—regarding compulsion (الجبر) encompassing the system of nature and the denial of choice—is utterly false. Rather, the truth is that events, in relation to their complete causes, are necessary in existence, and in relation to their matter and parts of their causes, are contingent in existence. This is the criterion in the works and acts of man; thus, his conduct in all positions of action is based on hope (الرجاء), upbringing (التربية), and education (التعليم), and the like. There is no meaning to basing necessities and essentials upon upbringing and education, nor relying on hope regarding them, and this is obvious.

Ayahs

كَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَكُنتُمْ أَمْوَٰتًا فَأَحْيَـٰكُمْ ۖ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٢٨)
How can you be unfaithful to Allah, [seeing that] you were lifeless and He gave you life, then He will make you die and then bring you to life, and then you will be brought back to Him? (28)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ لَكُم مَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَسَوَّىٰهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَٰتٍ ۚ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٢٩)
It is He who created for you all that is in the earth, then He turned to the heaven and fashioned it into seven heavens, and He has knowledge of all things. (29)

(Commentary)

This is a second return to what was mentioned at the beginning of the discourse. After the Exalted explained what He explained at the start of the Surah, He clarified it by way of summary in His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اعْبُدُوا رَبَّكُمُ (O mankind! Worship your Lord...) [2:21] up to a few verses. Then He returned to it a second time, clarifying it by way of expansion and detail in His saying: كَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ (How can you ungratefully deny...) [2:28] up to twelve verses. This is achieved by explaining the reality of man (الإنسان), the treasures of perfection (ذخائر الكمال) that Allah the Exalted has deposited within him, the vast scope of his existential horizon (دائرة وجوده), and the stages this being traverses in the journey of his existence (مسير وجوده)—from the stations of death and life, then death, then life, and finally the return to Allah, the Glorified; indeed, "unto your Lord is the final end."

Included therein is a mention of the sum total of what Allah the Exalted has specifically granted to man in terms of the gifts of creation (التكوين) and legislation (التشريع): that he was dead and He gave him life, then He continues to cause him to die and gives him life until He returns him to Himself. He has created for him all that is on the earth, subjected the heavens to him, made him His vicegerent (خليفته) on earth, made His angels prostrate to him, settled his father in Paradise, opened for him the door of repentance (التوبة), and honored him with His worship (عبادته) and His guidance (هدايته). This is what suits the context of His saying: كَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَكُنتُمْ أَمْوَاتًا فَأَحْيَاكُمْ (How can you ungratefully deny Allah, then He gave you life...) [2:28], for the context is one of reproach and the reminding of favors (الامتنان).

His saying, the Exalted: كَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَكُنتُمْ أَمْوَاتًا (How can you ungratefully deny Allah, then He gave you life...). The context of this verse is similar to His saying, the Exalted: قَالُوا رَبَّنَا أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ فَاعْتَرَفْنَا بِذُنُوبِنَا فَهَلْ إِلَىٰ خُرُوجٍ مِّن سَبِيلٍ (They will say, ‘Our Lord! Twice did You cause us to die, and twice did You give us life, then we confessed our sins. Is there any way out [of this state]?’) [40:11]. This is among the verses used to prove the existence of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — between this world and the Hereafter. This is because it includes two instances of being caused to die (إماتتين). If one of them were the death that transfers one from this world, then in order to conceptualize the second instance of being caused to die, one must necessarily assume a life between the two deaths, which is the intermediate realm (البرزخ). This is a sound deduction that has also been noted in some narrations.

Some who deny the intermediate realm (البرزخ) have mentioned that the two verses—meaning His saying: كَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ [2:28] and His saying: قَالُوا رَبَّنَا [40:11]—share the same context and both include two deaths and two lives, so their meaning is identical. They argue that the first verse [2:28] clearly shows that the first "death" is the state of man before the soul is breathed into him in the life of this world. Thus, the first death and life are the death before the worldly life and the worldly life itself; the second death and life are the death from this world and the life on the Day of Resurrection. They claim the stages intended in the second verse [40:11] are the same as those in the first, so it does not indicate the intermediate realm (البرزخ).

However, this is an error. The two verses differ in context. In the first verse [2:28], what is mentioned is one death (موت واحد), one instance of being caused to die (إماتة واحدة), and two instances of being given life (إحيائان). In the second verse [40:11], there are two instances of being caused to die (إماتتان) and two instances of being given life (إحيائان). It is well known that "being caused to die" (الإماتة) cannot be realized without a preceding life, unlike "death" (الموت). Therefore, the first death in the first verse is not the same as the first instance of being caused to die in the second verse. Thus, it is clear in His saying, the Exalted: أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ (Twice did You cause us to die, and twice did You give us life) that the first instance of being caused to die is that which follows this world, and the first instance of being given life after it is for the intermediate realm (البرزخ), while the second instance of being caused to die and being given life are for the Hereafter on the Day of Resurrection.

As for His saying, the Exalted: وَكُنتُمْ أَمْوَاتًا فَأَحْيَاكُمْ (and you were dead, then He gave you life), He only intends the death before life, which is a state of death (موت) and not an act of being caused to die (إماتة), and the life is the life of this world. Furthermore, in His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (then to Him you shall be brought back), the fact that He separated the giving of life from the return with the word "then" (ثُمَّ) supports what we have mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكُنتُمْ أَمْوَاتًا (and you were dead) is an explanation of the reality of man (الإنسان) in terms of his existence. He is a transforming (متحوّل) and perfecting (متكامل) existence that travels along the path of his gradually changing and shifting existence, traversing it stage by stage. Before his emergence in the life of this world, man was dead, then he lived by the life-giving act of Allah, then he transforms through being caused to die and being given life, and so on. The Glorified has said: وَبَدَأَ خَلْقَ الْإِنسَانِ مِن طِينٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُ مِن سُلَالَةٍ مِّن مَّاءٍ مَّهِينٍ ثُمَّ سَوَّاهُ وَنَفَخَ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِهِ (He began the creation of man from clay. Then He made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid. Then He proportioned him and breathed into him of His spirit...) [32:7-9]. And the Exalted said: ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَاهُ خَلْقًا آخَرَ فَتَبَارَكَ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخَالِقِينَ (then We produced him as another creation. So blessed be Allah, the best of creators!) [23:14]. And the Exalted said: وَقَالُوا أَإِذَا ضَلَلْنَا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ بَلْ هُم بِلِقَاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كَافِرُونَ قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُم مَّلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ (They say, ‘When we have been lost in the dust, shall we indeed be created anew?’ Rather they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord. Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you...’) [32:10-11]. And the Exalted said: مِنْهَا خَلَقْنَاكُمْ وَفِيهَا نُعِيدُكُمْ وَمِنْهَا نُخْرِجُكُمْ تَارَةً أُخْرَىٰ (From it We created you, into it We will return you, and from it We will bring you forth another time) [20:55].

These verses, as you see (and we shall further clarify them in their respective places), indicate that man is a part of the earth, not separate or distinct from it. He was detached from it and then began to evolve through his various stages until he reached the stage where he was produced as "another creation" (خلقاً آخر). Thus, he is the one who transforms into another creation and reaches perfection through this new and recent perfection. Then the angel of death takes this man from the body—a type of taking where he is received in full (يستوفيه)—and then he returns to Allah, the Glorified. This is the path of human existence (صراط وجود الإنسان).

Furthermore, destiny (التقدير) has fashioned man in a way that links him to all other earthly and heavenly beings—from the simple elements (بسايط العناصر) and the powers emanating from them, to their compounds (مركَّباتها) such as animals, plants, minerals, and other things like water, air, and the like. Every natural being is similarly disposed (مفطور) toward connection with others to act and be acted upon, thereby maintaining the gift of its existence. However, the scope of man's action and the field of his endeavor are broader. How so? While this otherwise defenseless being interacts with other natural entities through proximity, distance, association, and separation for the sake of his simple life objectives, he is distinguished—by virtue of his endowment with perception (الإدراك) and thought (الفكر)—by modes of interaction that are beyond the capacity of all other beings, involving analysis (التفصيل), synthesis (التركيب), decomposition (الإفساد), and refinement (الإصلاح). There is no being except that it is subject to man's manipulation. At times, he mimics nature through industry (الصناعة) in things he cannot obtain from nature directly, and at times he resists nature by using nature itself. In short, he derives benefit for every purpose from everything. The passage of ages continues to support this wondrous species in multiplying his interactions and deepening his insights, so that Allah may "verify the truth by His words" and so that His saying may be proven true: سَخَّرَ لَكُم مَّا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا مِّنْهُ (He has disposed for you whatever there is in the heavens and whatever there is in the earth—all from Him) [45:13], and His saying: ثُمَّ اسْتَوَىٰ إِلَى السَّمَاءِ (Then He turned to the heaven...) [2:29]. Since this discourse occurs in the context of explaining blessings to complete the reminding of favors (الإمتنان), it implies that the turning toward the heaven was for the sake of man, and thus its proportioning into seven was also for his sake. Reflect deeply upon this.

So, that which we mentioned is the path of man in the journey of his existence, and this which we mentioned is the radiance of his action in his interactions within the world of creation (عالم الكون). This is what the Glorified mentions regarding the human world: where it begins and where it ends.

However, just as the Quran considers the beginning of his worldly life to be starting from and linked to (at times) cosmic nature, it also links it to the Lord, the Exalted and Sanctified. The Exalted said: وَقَدْ خَلَقْتُكَ مِن قَبْلُ وَلَمْ تَكُ شَيْئًا (when I created you before, and you were nothing) [19:9], and the Exalted said: إِنَّهُ هُوَ يُبْدِئُ وَيُعِيدُ (It is He who originates and brings back) [85:13]. Thus, man—while being a creature nurtured in the cradle of creation (التكوين), suckled from the breast of fashioning and origination, and evolving through the stages of existence—has his conduct linked to dead nature.
