Simultaneously, from the perspective of origination (الفطر) and innovation (الإبداع), he is linked and attached to the command of Allah (أمر الله) and His kingdom (ملكوته). The Exalted said: إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَن يَقُولَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ (All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be!’ and it is) [36:82], and the Exalted said: إِنَّمَا قَوْلُنَا لِشَيْءٍ إِذَا أَرَدْنَاهُ أَن نَّقُولَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ (All Our word to a thing, when We will it, is to say to it ‘Be!’ and it is) [16:40]. This is from the perspective of the beginning (البدء).

As for the return (العود) and coming back (الرجوع), the path of man is considered to branch into two ways: the path of felicity (السعادة) and the path of wretchedness (الشقاوة). As for the path of felicity, it is the nearest of paths, ending at the "Exalted Height" (الرفيع الأعلى); it continues to ascend and raise man until it brings him to his Lord. As for the path of wretchedness, it is a distant path that ends at the "lowest of the low" (اسفل السافلين) until it ends at the Lord of the worlds—and Allah encompasses them from behind. The explanation of this has already passed under His saying, the Exalted: اهدنا الصراط المستقيم (Guide us on the straight path) in Surah al-Fatihah.

This is a summary of the discourse regarding the path of man. As for the detailed discourse regarding his life before this world, within it, and after it, each will come in its proper place. However, His speech, the Exalted, only addresses these matters from the perspective of their connection to guidance (الهداية), misguidance (الضلال), felicity (السعادة), and wretchedness (الشقاء), and it bypasses research into anything else except to the extent that it touches upon the aforementioned purpose of the Quran.

And His saying, the Exalted: فسويهن سبع سموات (and He fashioned them into seven heavens). The discussion regarding the heaven (السماء) will come in Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah, Allah willing.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِنِّى جَاعِلٌ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ أَتَجْعَلُ فِيهَا مَن يُفْسِدُ فِيهَا وَيَسْفِكُ ٱلدِّمَآءَ وَنَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ ۖ قَالَ إِنِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٣٠)
When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth,’ they said, ‘Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood, while we celebrate Your praise and proclaim Your sanctity?’ He said, ‘Indeed I know what you do not know.’ (30)
وَعَلَّمَ ءَادَمَ ٱلْأَسْمَآءَ كُلَّهَا ثُمَّ عَرَضَهُمْ عَلَى ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ فَقَالَ أَنۢبِـُٔونِى بِأَسْمَآءِ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٣١)
And He taught Adam the Names, all of them; then presented them to the angels and said, ‘Tell me the names of these, if you are truthful.’ (31)
قَالُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ لَا عِلْمَ لَنَآ إِلَّا مَا عَلَّمْتَنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٣٢)
They said, ‘Immaculate are You! We have no knowledge except what You have taught us. Indeed You are the All-knowing, the All-wise.’ (32)
قَالَ يَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ أَنۢبِئْهُم بِأَسْمَآئِهِمْ ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَنۢبَأَهُم بِأَسْمَآئِهِمْ قَالَ أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكُمْ إِنِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ غَيْبَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَأَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ (٣٣)
He said, ‘O Adam, inform them of their names,’ and when he had informed them of their names, He said, ‘Did I not tell you that I know the Unseen of the heavens and the earth, and that I know whatever you disclose and whatever you conceal?’ (33)

(Commentary)

The verses inform us of the purpose behind sending man down to this world, the reality of establishing the vicegerency (الخلافة) — the status of being a representative or deputy of Allah — upon the earth, and what its effects and properties are. Unlike other prophetic stories, this account does not occur in the Quran except in this single location.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ (And when your Lord said...) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 2, Ayah 30]. Discussion regarding the meaning of "speech" (القول) from the Exalted, as well as speech from the angels and Satan, will follow, Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: قَالُوا أَتَجْعَلُ فِيهَا مَن يُفْسِدُ فِيهَا وَيَسْفِكُ الدِّمَاءَ (They said, ‘Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood...) up to His saying: (...and we sanctify You’) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 2, Ayah 30]. This suggests that they understood the occurrence of corruption and bloodshed from His saying, the Glorified: إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً (I am indeed going to set a viceroy on the earth) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 2, Ayah 30]. This is because an earthly being, insofar as it is material (مادي), is composed of the powers of anger (الغضبية) and desire (الشهوية). Furthermore, this realm is a realm of mutual interference (التزاحم), limited in its dimensions, and abundant in frictions. Its compositions are prone to dissolution, and its orders and rectifications are subject to corruption and the locus of nullification. Life cannot be sustained therein except through collective life (الحيوة النوعية), and survival cannot be perfected except through social gathering (الاجتماع) and cooperation (التعاون). Thus, it cannot be free from corruption and the shedding of blood.

From this, they understood that the intended vicegerency (الخلافة) would not occur on earth except through a multitude of individuals and a social system among them that ultimately leads to corruption and bloodshed. Vicegerency—which is the standing of one thing in the place of another—cannot be realized unless the vicegerent (الخليفة) reflects the one who appointed him (المستخلف) in all his existential affairs, effects, rulings, and management, insofar as he is the one being represented. Allah, the Glorified, in His existence, is named by the Most Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) and described by the Sublime Attributes (الصفات العليا), including attributes of Beauty (الجمال) and Majesty (الجلال). He is transcendent (منزه) in His essence from deficiency and sanctified (مقدس) in His action from evil and corruption—exalted is His greatness. An earthly vicegerent, being as he is, is not fit for this representation, nor does his existence—tainted by every deficiency and blemish—reflect the Divine Existence, which is sanctified and transcendent from all deficiencies and non-existences (الأعدام). Where is the dust compared to the Lord of lords?

This speech from the angels was in the context of seeking to know what they were ignorant of and clarifying what was problematic for them regarding the matter of this vicegerent; it was not an act of objection or contention at all. The proof for this is their saying, as narrated by Allah the Exalted: إِنَّكَ أَنتَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ (Indeed You are the All-knowing, the All-wise) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 2, Ayah 32], where they prefaced the sentence with the causal "indeed" (إنّ), indicating their submission to what follows it—so understand this.

In summary, their argument returns to the idea that the establishment of vicegerency is only so that the vicegerent may reflect his Appointer through his glorification (التسبيح) with His praise and his sanctification (التقديس) of Him through his existence. However, the earthly nature does not allow him to do so; rather, it drags him toward corruption and evil. The goal of this appointment—which is glorification and sanctification in the sense of reflection mentioned—is already achieved through our glorification with Your praise and our sanctification of You. Thus, we are Your vicegerents, or make us Your vicegerents. What, then, is the benefit of establishing this earthly vicegerency for You? Allah, the Glorified, refuted this by His saying: إِنِّي أَعْلَمُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (I know what you do not know) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 2, Ayah 30] and وَعَلَّمَ آدَمَ الْأَسْمَاءَ كُلَّهَا (And He taught Adam the Names, all of them) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 2, Ayah 31].

This context suggests:

First: That the mentioned vicegerency was specifically the vicegerency of Allah the Exalted. It was not the vicegerency of a species of earthly beings who were on the earth before man and became extinct, whom Allah then intended to replace with man, as some commentators have conjectured. This is because the answer Allah gave them—teaching Adam the Names—would not be appropriate for that. Accordingly, the vicegerency is not restricted to the person of Adam (peace be upon him); rather, his children share it with him without exclusivity. The meaning of teaching the Names would then be the depositing of this knowledge (العلم) within man such that its effects appear from him gradually and perpetually; if he were guided to the path, he could bring it from potentiality (القوة) into actuality (الفعل). The universality of the vicegerency is supported by His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ جَعَلَكُمْ خُلَفَاءَ مِن بَعْدِ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ (...when He made you successors after the people of Noah) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 7, Ayah 69], and His saying: ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَاكُمْ خَلَائِفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ (Then We made you successors on the earth) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 10, Ayah 14], and His saying: وَيَجْعَلُكُمْ خُلَفَاءَ الْأَرْضِ (...and makes you successors on the earth) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 27, Ayah 62].

Second: That He, the Glorified, did not deny corruption and bloodshed regarding the earthly vicegerent, nor did He belie the angels in their claim of glorification and sanctification; rather, He affirmed them in what they claimed. Instead, He manifested something else: that there is a matter which the angels are unable to carry and cannot endure, while this earthly vicegerent can endure it. For he reflects a matter from Allah the Exalted and carries a secret from Him that is not within the capacity of the angels. Inevitably, this matter compensates for the corruption and bloodshed.

Allah the Exalted later replaced His saying: إِنِّي أَعْلَمُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (I know what you do not know) with His saying: أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكُمْ إِنِّي أَعْلَمُ غَيْبَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (Did I not tell you that I know the Unseen of the heavens and the earth?) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 2, Ayah 33]. What is meant by this "Unseen" (الغيب) is the Names themselves, not Adam's knowledge of them. The angels did not know that there were Names they did not know; it was not that they knew of the existence of such Names but were ignorant that Adam knew them. Otherwise, there would have been no reason for Allah to ask them about the Names—which is obvious. Rather, the appropriate course would have been to limit the matter to His saying: يَا آدَمُ أَنبِئْهُم بِأَسْمَائِهِمْ (O Adam, inform them of their names) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 2, Ayah 33] so it would become clear to them that Adam knew them, rather than asking the angels about them. This context indicates that they claimed the vicegerency and were convinced of its absence in Adam, whereas it was necessary for the vicegerent to know the Names. So He asked them about the Names and they were ignorant of them, while Adam knew them. Thus, his fitness for it and their lack thereof was established. Allah concluded the question with His saying: إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ (...if you are truthful) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 2, Ayah 31], which suggests they had claimed something whose necessary prerequisite was knowledge of the Names.

His saying, the Exalted: وَعَلَّمَ آدَمَ الْأَسْمَاءَ كُلَّهَا ثُمَّ عَرَضَهُمْ (And He taught Adam the Names, all of them; then He presented them...) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 2, Ayah 31] suggests that these Names—or rather their named objects (المسميات)—were living, rational beings (موجودات أحياء عقلاء), veiled behind the curtain of the Unseen (الغيب). It also suggests that the knowledge of their names was not like the kind of knowledge we have of the names of things. Otherwise, by Adam informing them of the names, the angels would have become knowledgeable and become like Adam, equal to him. There would have been no honor (اكرام) or distinction (كرامة) for Adam in that Allah taught him names but did not teach them. Had He taught them, they would have been like Adam or more noble than him, and there would have been nothing in that to convince them or invalidate their argument.

What argument is established by Allah teaching a man the science of language (علم اللغة) and then boasting of him and establishing the proof against honored angels—who do not precede Him in speech and act by His command—by saying, "This is My vicegerent and capable of My honor, rather than you"? And by saying, "Inform Me of the languages that humans will later devise among themselves for communication if you are truthful in your claim or your question regarding My vicegerency"? Furthermore, the perfection of language lies in the knowledge of the intentions of the heart, and the angels have no need for speech in that regard; they receive intentions without mediation. Thus, they possess a perfection above the perfection of speech. In short, the knowledge the angels gained through Adam informing them of the names is different from the reality of the knowledge of the Names (حقيقة العلم بالأسماء) that Adam gained through Allah's teaching. One of the two matters was possible for the angels and within their capacity, while the other was not. Adam only deserved the Divine vicegerency through the knowledge of the Names, not the informing of them, for the angels said in their response: سُبْحَانَكَ لَا عِلْمَ لَنَا إِلَّا مَا عَلَّمْتَنَا (Immaculate are You! We have no knowledge except what You have taught us) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 2, Ayah 32], thereby negating knowledge.

It has become clear from what has passed that the knowledge of the names of these named objects must be such that it uncovers their realities (حقائق) and the essences of their existences (أعيان وجوداتهم), rather than merely what linguistic convention (الوضع اللغوي) provides in terms of giving a concept (المفهوم). These known named objects are external realities (حقائق خارجية) and concrete existences (وجودات عينية) which are, nonetheless, hidden under the veil of the Unseen—the Unseen of the heavens and the earth. Knowledge of them as they truly are was, firstly, possible for an earthly being but not a heavenly angel, and secondly, instrumental in the Divine vicegerency.

The "Names" (الأسماء) in His saying, the Exalted: وَعَلَّمَ آدَمَ الْأَسْمَاءَ كُلَّهَا (And He taught Adam the Names, all of them) is a plural defined by the definite article (ال), which signifies universality (العموم) as scholars have stated. Additionally, it is emphasized by His saying: كُلَّهَا (all of them). Thus, what is meant is every name that applies to a named object, without restriction or specific reference. Then, His saying: عَرَضَهُمْ (He presented them) indicates that every name—that is, its named object—possesses life and knowledge, and is yet under the veil of the Unseen, the Unseen of the heavens and the earth. While the annexation (إضافة) of "Unseen" to "the heavens and the earth" could in some contexts be an annexation of "from" (من), signifying partitive meaning, the context here—which is the manifestation of the perfection of His power and encompassment, and the incapacity and deficiency of the angels—requires that the annexation be that of "belonging to" (اللام). This signifies that the Names are matters hidden from the heavenly and earthly world, outside the perimeter of the cosmos (الكون).

When you reflect on these aspects—namely the universality of the Names, the fact that their named objects possess life and knowledge, and their being the Unseen of the heavens and the earth—you will necessarily conclude that they correspond to what is alluded to in His saying, the Exalted: وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ (There is not a thing but that its primary sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 15, Ayah 21]. There, the Glorified informs us that everything to which the name "thing" applies has with Him stored treasuries (خزائن) that are enduring with Him, neither exhausted nor measured by a limit, nor restricted by a boundary. Measure (القدر) and boundary (الحد) only occur at the level of sending down (الإنزال) and creation (الخلق). The multiplicity within these treasuries is not of the nature of numerical multiplicity (الكثرة العددية) that is inseparable from measurement and limitation, but rather a multiplicity of ranks (المراتب) and degrees (الدرجات). Some discussion on this will follow in Surah al-Hijr, Allah willing.

In conclusion, those whom Allah presented to the angels are exalted beings (موجودات عالية) preserved with Allah, veiled by the veils of the Unseen. Allah sent down every name in the world through their goodness and blessing, and everything in the heavens and the earth derived from their light and splendor. Despite their multiplicity and number, they do not multiply like the multiplication of individuals, nor do they differ like the difference of persons; rather, the matter there revolves around ranks and degrees. The descent of a name from them is of this type of descent.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ (...and I know what you reveal and what you used to conceal) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 2, Ayah 33]. These two categories were part of the relative Unseen (الغيب النسبي) which constitutes part of the heavens and the earth. For this reason, it was contrasted with His saying: "I know the Unseen of the heavens and the earth," so as to include both types of the Unseen: that which is outside the earthly and heavenly world and that which is not.

His saying, the Exalted: كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ (you used to conceal). The restriction of concealment with the past continuous "used to" (كنتم) suggests that there was something concealed specifically regarding Adam and the establishment of his vicegerency. This can be inferred from His saying in the following verse: فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰ وَاسْتَكْبَرَ وَكَانَ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ (...so they prostrated, all but Iblis: he unduly refused and acted arrogantly, and he was one of the faithless) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah 2, Ayah 34].

It appears that Iblis was a disbeliever (كافراً) before that time, and that his refusal to prostrate was connected to that; he had harbored this within himself. It also appears that the prostration of the angels and Iblis's refusal occurred between His saying: "I know what you do not know" and His saying: "I know what you reveal and what you used to conceal." This also reveals the secret behind replacing His saying: "I know what you do not know" the second time with His saying: "I know the Unseen of the heavens and the earth."

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "How did the angels know to say: أَتَجْعَلُ فِيهَا مَن يُفْسِدُ فِيهَا وَيَسْفِكُ الدِّمَاءَ (Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood), unless they had already seen someone who causes corruption and sheds blood?"

I say: This may refer to a cycle on earth prior to this cycle of the children of Adam, as mentioned in the reports. This does not contradict what was previously stated—that the angels understood this from His saying: إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً (I am indeed going to set a viceroy on the earth). In fact, the report is not complete without that; otherwise, this statement would have been a blameworthy analogy (قياس) by the angels, like the analogy of Iblis.

Also in Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from him (peace be upon him), Zurarah said: "I visited Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) and he asked: 'What do you have of the traditions of the Shi'a?' I said: 'I have many of them, and I have even considered lighting a fire to burn them.' He (peace be upon him) said: 'Hide them and you will forget what you find objectionable in them.' Then the thought of human beings occurred to me, and he said: 'How did the angels know when they said: "Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood"?'" He said: "Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) used to say when he narrated this tradition: 'This is a refutation against the Qadariyyah (القدرية) — those who deny predestination and attribute absolute creative power to human will.'" Then Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Adam (peace be upon him) had a close friend among the angels in heaven. When Adam was sent down from heaven to earth, the angel felt lonely and complained to Allah, asking Him for permission [to visit him]. He permitted him, so he descended to him and found him sitting in a desolate part of the earth. When Adam saw him, he placed his hand on his head and let out a loud cry." Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "They narrate that he made all of creation hear it." The angel said to him: "O Adam, I see only that you have disobeyed your Lord and burdened yourself with what you cannot bear. Do you know what Allah said to us regarding you and what we replied to Him?" He said: "No." He said: "He said: 'I am indeed going to set a viceroy on the earth.' We said: 'Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood?' Thus, He created you to be on the earth; is it fitting for you to be in heaven?" Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "By Allah, he consoled Adam with this three times."

I say: It is inferred from the narration that Adam's garden was in heaven, and other narrations regarding this will follow.

Also in Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu al-Abbas, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah: وَعَلَّمَ آدَمَ الْأَسْمَاءَ كُلَّهَا (And He taught Adam the Names, all of them). What did He teach him?" He said: "The earths, the mountains, the mountain passes, and the valleys." Then he looked at a carpet beneath him and said: "And this carpet is among what He taught him."

In the same Tafsir, from al-Fudayl ibn al-Abbas, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah: وَعَلَّمَ آدَمَ الْأَسْمَاءَ كُلَّهَا (And He taught Adam the Names, all of them). What are they?" He said: "The names of the valleys, the plants, the trees, and the mountains of the earth."

In the same Tafsir, from Dawud ibn Sarhan al-Attar, who said: "I was with Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) when he called for the dining table and we ate. Then he called for the basin (الطست) and the hand-towel (الدست سنان). I said: 'May I be your ransom! Is His saying: "And He taught Adam the Names, all of them"—are the basin and the hand-towel part of it?' He (peace be upon him) said: 'The mountain paths and the valleys,' and he gestured with his hand thus and so."

In al-Ma'ani, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, taught Adam the names of all His Proofs (حججه) — the divinely appointed guides who serve as the evidence of Allah's truth to creation. Then He presented them—while they were spirits (أرواح)—to the angels and said: 'Inform Me of the names of these if you are truthful in your claim that you are more entitled to the vicegerency on earth due to your glorification and sanctification than Adam.' They said: 'Immaculate are You! We have no knowledge except what You have taught us; indeed You are the All-knowing, the All-wise.' Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, said: 'O Adam, inform them of their names.' When he informed them of their names, they realized their magnificent status with Allah, exalted be His mention. They knew that they were more entitled to be the vicegerents of Allah on His earth and His Proofs over His creation. Then He veiled them from their sight and required their servitude through their guardianship (ولايتهم) and love for them. He said to them: 'Did I not tell you that I know the Unseen of the heavens and the earth and I know what you reveal and what you used to conceal?'"

I say: By referring back to the preceding commentary, you will understand the meaning of these narrations and that there is no contradiction between them and what preceded. It has already been stated that His saying: وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ (There is not a thing but that its primary sources are with Us) implies that there is nothing except that it has an existence in the treasuries of the Unseen, and that these things before us only came into existence by descending from there. Every name placed in correspondence to one of these named objects is a name for what is in the treasuries of the Unseen. Thus, whether it is said that Allah taught Adam the things that are in the treasuries of His Unseen—which are the Unseen of the heavens and the earth—or it is said that He taught Adam the names of everything—which are the Unseen of the heavens and the earth—the intended meaning and the result are one, and this is clear.

Appropriate to this context are several reports regarding the "clay" (الطينة) — the primordial substance from which beings are created, such as what is narrated in al-Bihar from Jabir ibn Abdillah, who said: "I said to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: 'What was the first thing Allah created?' He said: 'The light of your Prophet, O Jabir. Allah created it, then He created from it every good. Then He stationed it before Him in the station of proximity (مقام القرب) for as long as Allah willed. Then He divided it into parts: He created the Throne (العرش) from one part, the Pedestal (الكرسي) from one part, and the bearers of the Throne and the inhabitants of the Pedestal from one part. He stationed the fourth part in the station of love (مقام الحب) for as long as Allah willed. Then He divided it into parts: He created the Pen (القلم) from one part, the Tablet (اللوح) from one part, and Paradise (الجنة) from one part. He stationed the fourth part in the station of fear (مقام الخوف) for as long as Allah willed. Then He divided it into portions: He created the angels from one portion, the sun from one portion, and the moon from one portion. He stationed the fourth part in the station of hope (مقام الرجاء) for as long as Allah willed. Then He divided it into portions: He created the intellect (العقل) from one portion, knowledge (العلم) and forbearance (الحلم) from one portion, and protection from sin (العصمة) and success (التوفيق) from one portion. He stationed the fourth part in the station of modesty (مقام الحياء) for as long as Allah willed. Then He looked upon it with the eye of awe, and that light perspired, and one hundred and twenty-four thousand drops dripped from it. From every drop, Allah created the spirit of a prophet and a messenger. Then the spirits of the prophets exhaled, and from their breaths, Allah created the spirits of the friends of Allah (الأولياء), the martyrs (الشهداء), and the righteous (الصالحين).'"

I say: The reports regarding these meanings are numerous and mutually supporting (متظافرة). If you cast a glance of reflection and scrutiny upon them, you will find them to be witnesses to what we have presented. A portion of the discussion regarding some of them will follow. Beware of dismissing such noble traditions—transmitted from the mines of knowledge and the springs of wisdom—as being among the fabrications of the Sufis or their delusions. For creation has secrets. Here are the scientists from the various classes of mankind, sparing no effort in researching the secrets of nature since humanity began to spread; yet whenever a single known fact appears to them, many unknowns become manifest to them. This is the world of nature, the narrowest and lowest of worlds; so what do you think of what lies beyond it, which are the worlds of light and vastness?

Ayah

وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰ وَٱسْتَكْبَرَ وَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٣٤)
And when We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis: he refused and acted arrogantly, and he was one of the faithless. (34)

(Commentary)

You have already realized that His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ (and what you used to conceal), indicates the occurrence of a concealed matter that became manifest after having been hidden. This is not without a connection to His saying: أَبَىٰ وَاسْتَكْبَرَ وَكَانَ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ (he refused and acted arrogantly, and he was one of the disbelievers), where He did not simply say "he refused, acted arrogantly, and disbelieved." You also realized that the story of the prostration (السجدة) is situated like an event—or is indeed an event—occurring between His saying, the Exalted: إِنِّي أَعْلَمُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (Indeed I know what you do not know), and His saying: وَأَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ (and I know what you reveal and what you used to conceal). Thus, His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ (And when We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam’), is like a sentence extracted from between other sentences to transition through it to the story of the Garden (الجنة). For these verses, as you have learned, were only brought forth to explain the nature of human vicegerency (خلافة الإنسان), its position, the manner of his descent to the world, and what his affair leads to in terms of felicity (سعادة) and wretchedness (شقاء). Therefore, what matters regarding the story of the prostration here is only its summary that leads to the story of the Garden and the descent (هبوط) of Adam. This is the reason for shifting from elaboration (الإطناب) to conciseness (الإيجاز). Perhaps this is also the secret behind the transition (الإلتفات) from the third person (الغيبة) to the first person (التكلم) in His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا (And when We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate’), after His saying: وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ (And when your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set...’). Based on what has passed, the attribution of concealment (الكتمان) to the angels—while it was the act of Iblis—is based on the linguistic convention of attributing the act of an individual to the group if he is mixed among them and not distinguished from them. Another possibility exists: that the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً (Indeed I am going to set a vicegerent on the earth), implies the absolute nature of vicegerency (إطلاق الخلافة) even over the angels, as supported by their being commanded a second time to prostrate. This would cause a thought to occur in the hearts of the angels, as they did not think an earthly being could prevail over everything, even over them. Some narrations point to this meaning, as will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ (Prostrate before Adam), implies the permissibility of prostrating to other than Allah in general, provided it is a greeting (تحية) and an honoring (تكرمة) of the other, and it contains submission to Allah, the Exalted, by complying with His command. Its parallel is His saying, the Exalted, in the story of Yusuf (peace be upon him): وَرَفَعَ أَبَوَيْهِ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ وَخَرُّوا لَهُ سُجَّدًا ۖ وَقَالَ يَا أَبَتِ هَٰذَا تَأْوِيلُ رُؤْيَايَ مِن قَبْلُ قَدْ جَعَلَهَا رَبِّي حَقًّا — "And he seated his parents high upon the throne, and they fell down prostrate before him. He said, ‘Father, this is the fulfillment of my dream of long ago, which my Lord has made come true.’" (Yusuf: 100). The summary of the matter is that you have already learned in Surat al-Fatihah that worship (العبادة) — the act of the servant placing himself in the station of servitude and performing that which establishes and confirms it — means that the act of worship must possess the capacity to manifest the Lordship (المولوية) of the Master or the servitude (العبدية) of the servant, such as prostration (السجدة), bowing (الركوع), standing (القيام) before him while he sits, and walking behind him while he walks, and so on. Whenever this aforementioned capacity increases, the act of worship becomes more defined as servitude. The clearest of acts in indicating the might of Lordship and the lowliness of servitude is prostration (السجدة), due to the falling upon the earth and placing the forehead upon it. As for what some have imagined—that prostration is an essential worship (عبادة ذاتية)—it is of no consequence, for that which is essential (الذاتي) does not vary or fail to occur. This very act can be performed by its doer with a motive other than veneration and worship, such as mockery and derision, in which case it would not be worship despite containing everything that the act of worship contains. Yes, the meaning of worship is clearer in prostration than in other acts. Since it is not an essential worship, it is not by its very essence (لذاته) exclusive to Allah, the Glorified, based on the premise that the One worshipped (المعبود) is restricted to Him, the Exalted. If there were a prohibition, it would be from the perspective of a religious (شرعي) or rational (عقلي) prohibition. That which is prohibited religiously or rationally is nothing other than attributing Lordship (الربوبية) to other than Him, the Exalted. As for greeting another or honoring them without attributing Lordship, but merely for the sake of social custom and greeting, there is no evidence for its prohibition. However, the religious taste (الذوق الديني) derived from intimacy with the outward aspects of religion dictates the exclusivity of this act to Him, the Exalted, and the prohibition of using it in other than His case, even if nothing but greeting and honoring is intended. As for the prohibition of everything that manifests sincerity to Allah by expressing love for His righteous servants or for the graves of His friends (أولياء) or their relics, it is something for which no rational or textual evidence exists at all. We shall return to the discussion of this subject in a suitable place, Allah, the Exalted, willing.

(Discussion of Narrations)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "When Allah created Adam, He commanded the angels to prostrate to him. The angels said within themselves: 'We did not think that Allah created a creation more noble to Him than us, for we are His neighbors and we are the closest of creation to Him.' So Allah said: 'Did I not say to you that I know what you reveal and what you used to conceal?' regarding what they revealed of the matter of the children of the Jinn (بني الجان) and what they concealed within themselves. So the angels who said what they said sought refuge (لاذت) with the Throne (العرش)."

In the same Tafsir, narrated from Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) regarding its meaning, it says: "When the angels realized they had fallen into a mistake, they sought refuge with the Throne; and they were a group of angels, those who were around the Throne, not all the angels..." until he said: "So they seek refuge around the Throne until the Day of Resurrection."

I say: The content of these two narrations can be inferred from His saying, recounting the words of the angels: وَنَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ (while we celebrate Your praise and proclaim Your sanctity) until His saying: سُبْحَانَكَ لَا عِلْمَ لَنَا إِلَّا مَا عَلَّمْتَنَا ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ (Immaculate are You! We have no knowledge except what You have taught us. Indeed You are the All-knowing, the All-wise).

It will be mentioned later that the Throne (العرش) is knowledge (العلم), and narrations to this effect have been transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), so understand that. Accordingly, the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: وَكَانَ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ (and he was one of the disbelievers), refers to the people of Iblis from among the Jinn (الجان) who were created before man. The Exalted said: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ مِن صَلْصَالٍ مِّنْ حَمَإٍ مَّسْنُونٍ وَالْجَانَّ خَلَقْنَاهُ مِن قَبْلُ مِن نَّارِ السَّمُومِ — "Certainly We created man out of a dry clay [drawn] from an altered mud. And the jinn We created before from a smokeless fire." (al-Hijr: 26-27). According to this narration, the attribution of concealment (الكتمان) to all the angels does not require any special interpretation; rather, it is literal, for the concealed thought occurred in the hearts of all the angels. There is no contradiction between this narration and those indicating that the concealed matter was what Iblis was hiding of his refusal to submit to Adam and his arrogance if called to prostrate, for it is possible to derive both meanings, as is indeed the case.

In Qisas al-Anbiya, narrated from Abu Basir, who said: "I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'Did the angels prostrate and place their foreheads on the ground?' He said: 'Yes, as an honoring (تكرمة) from Allah, the Exalted.'"

In Tuhaf al-Uqul, it is said: "The prostration of the angels to Adam was only an act of obedience to Allah and out of love from them for Adam."

In al-Ihtijaj, narrated from Musa ibn Ja'far, from his forefathers: "A Jewish man asked the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) about the miracles of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in comparison to the miracles of the prophets. He said: 'This is Adam, for whom Allah made His angels prostrate; did He do anything like this for Muhammad?' Ali said: 'That was indeed so, but Allah made His angels prostrate to Adam, yet their prostration was not a prostration of obedience such that they worshipped Adam besides Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, but rather an acknowledgment of Adam's excellence and a mercy from Allah upon him. And Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was given what is superior to this: for Allah, the Majestic and Sublime, blessed him in His dominion (جبروت), as did all the angels, and the believers were commanded to seek blessings (الصلوة) upon him; so this is an increase for him, O Jew.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi: "Allah created Adam and he remained as a form for forty years. The accursed Iblis would pass by him and say: 'For what matter were you created?' He said: 'The Knower (العالم).' Iblis said: 'If Allah commands me to prostrate to this, I shall surely disobey Him...'" until he said: "Then Allah, the Exalted, said to the angels: 'Prostrate to Adam,' and they prostrated. Then Iblis brought out what was in his heart of envy (الحسد) and refused to prostrate."

In al-Bihar, from Qisas al-Anbiya, narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "Iblis was commanded to prostrate to Adam, and he said: 'O Lord, by Your Might, if You excuse me from prostrating to Adam, I shall surely worship You with a worship such as no one has ever worshipped You with the like of it.' Allah, Majestic is His Majesty, said: 'I love to be obeyed from where I intend.'" And he said: "Indeed, Iblis wailed four times: the first on the day he was cursed, the day he was cast down to the earth, the day Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was sent after a hiatus (فترة) of messengers, and when the Mother of the Book (أم الكتاب) was revealed. And he snorted twice: when Adam ate from the tree, and when he was cast down from the Garden." And he said regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَبَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْآتُهُمَا (their nakedness became exposed to them): "Their nakedness was not seen, then it became visible and prominent." And he said: "The tree that Adam was forbidden from was the ear of grain (السنبلة)."

I say: In the narrations—which are numerous—there is support for what we have mentioned regarding the prostration (السجدة).

Ayahs

وَقُلْنَا يَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ ٱسْكُنْ أَنتَ وَزَوْجُكَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَكُلَا مِنْهَا رَغَدًا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلشَّجَرَةَ فَتَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٣٥)
We said, ‘O Adam, dwell with your mate in paradise and eat thereof freely whencesoever you wish, but do not approach this tree, lest you should be among the wrongdoers.’ (35)
فَأَزَلَّهُمَا ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ عَنْهَا فَأَخْرَجَهُمَا مِمَّا كَانَا فِيهِ ۖ وَقُلْنَا ٱهْبِطُوا۟ بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ ۖ وَلَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَـٰعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (٣٦)
Then Satan caused them to stumble from it, and he dislodged them from what [state] they were in; and We said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode and sustenance for a time.’ (36)
فَتَلَقَّىٰٓ ءَادَمُ مِن رَّبِّهِۦ كَلِمَـٰتٍ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٣٧)
Then Adam received certain words from his Lord, and He turned to him clemently. Indeed He is the All-clement, the All-merciful. (37)
قُلْنَا ٱهْبِطُوا۟ مِنْهَا جَمِيعًا ۖ فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّى هُدًى فَمَن تَبِعَ هُدَاىَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٣٨)
We said, ‘Get down from it, all together! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve. (38)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٣٩)
But those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]. (39)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: "We said, 'O Adam, dwell...'" (وقلنا يا آدم اسكن): While the story of the angels prostrating to Adam is repeated in several places in the Holy Quran, the story of the Garden (الجنة) occurs in only three places:

First: Here, in Surah al-Baqarah.

Second: In Surah al-A'raf. Allah, the Exalted, says: وَيَا آدَمُ اسْكُنْ أَنتَ وَزَوْجُكَ الْجَنَّةَ فَكُلَا مِنْ حَيْثُ شِئْتُمَا وَلَا تَقْرَبَا هَٰذِهِ الشَّجَرَةَ فَتَكُونَا مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ . فَوَسْوَسَ لَهُمَا الشَّيْطَانُ لِيُبْدِيَ لَهُمَا مَا وُورِيَ عَنْهُمَا مِن سَوْآتِهِمَا وَقَالَ : مَا نَهَاكُمَا رَبُّكُمَا عَنْ هَٰذِهِ الشَّجَرَةِ إِلَّا أَن تَكُونَا مَلَكَيْنِ أَوْ تَكُونَا مِنَ الْخَالِدِينَ . وَقَاسَمَهُمَا إِنِّي لَكُمَا لَمِنَ النَّاصِحِينَ . فَدَلَّاهُمَا بِغُرُورٍ فَلَمَّا ذَاقَا الشَّجَرَةَ بَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْآتُهُمَا وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ الْجَنَّةِ وَنَادَاهُمَا رَبُّهُمَا أَلَمْ أَنْهَكُمَا عَن تِلْكُمَا الشَّجَرَةِ وَأَقُل لَّكُمَا إِنَّ الشَّيْطَانَ لَكُمَا عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ . قَالَا : رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَا أَنفُسَنَا وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ . قَالَ : اهْبِطُوا بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَاعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ . قَالَ : فِيهَا تَحْيَوْنَ وَفِيهَا تَمُوتُونَ وَمِنْهَا تُخْرَجُونَ — "‘O Adam, dwell with your mate in paradise, and eat thereof whence you wish; but do not approach this tree, lest you should be among the wrongdoers.’ Then Satan tempted them, to expose to them their nakedness that had been hidden from them, and he said, ‘Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree lest you should become angels, or lest you should become immortal.’ And he swore to them, ‘I am indeed a well-wisher to you.’ So he brought about their fall by delusion. So when they tasted of the tree, their nakedness became exposed to them, and they began to stitch over themselves with the leaves of paradise. Their Lord called out to them, ‘Did I not forbid you from that tree, and tell you, “Satan is indeed your manifest enemy”?’ They said, ‘Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers.’ He said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode and sustenance for a time.’ He said, ‘In it you shall live, and in it you shall die, and from it you shall be brought forth.’" (7:19-25).

Third: In Surah Ta-Ha. Allah, the Exalted, says: وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنَا إِلَىٰ آدَمَ مِن قَبْلُ فَنَسِيَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُ عَزْمًا . وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰ . فَقُلْنَا يَا آدَمُ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا عَدُوٌّ لَّكَ وَلِزَوْجِكَ فَلَا يُخْرِجَنَّكُمَا مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ فَتَشْقَىٰ . إِنَّ لَكَ أَلَّا تَجُوعَ فِيهَا وَلَا تَعْرَىٰ ، وَأَنَّكَ لَا تَظْمَأُ فِيهَا وَلَا تَضْحَ . فَوَسْوَسَ إِلَيْهِ الشَّيْطَانُ قَالَ : يَا آدَمُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكَ عَلَىٰ شَجَرَةِ الْخُلْدِ وَمُلْكٍ لَّا يَبْلَىٰ . فَأَكَلَا مِنْهَا فَبَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْآتُهُمَا وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ الْجَنَّةِ وَعَصَىٰ آدَمُ رَبَّهُ فَغَوَىٰ . ثُمَّ اجْتَبَاهُ رَبُّهُ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ . قَالَ : اهْبِطَا مِنْهَا جَمِيعًا بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ . وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ أَعْمَىٰ . قَالَ : رَبِّ لِمَ حَشَرْتَنِي أَعْمَىٰ وَقَدْ كُنتُ بَصِيرًا . قَالَ : كَذَٰلِكَ أَتَتْكَ آيَاتُنَا فَنَسِيتَهَا وَكَذَٰلِكَ الْيَوْمَ تُنسَ — "Certainly We had enjoined Adam before; but he forgot, and We did not find any primacy in him. When We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis; he refused. So We said, ‘O Adam! This is indeed an enemy to you and your mate. So do not let him expel you from paradise, or you will be miserable. Indeed you will neither go hungry in it nor go naked. Indeed you will neither thirst in it nor suffer from the sun.’ Then Satan tempted him. He said, ‘O Adam! Shall I guide you to the tree of eternity and an undying kingdom?’ So they ate of it, and their nakedness became exposed to them, and they began to stitch over themselves with the leaves of paradise. Adam disobeyed his Lord, and went astray. Then his Lord chose him, and He turned to him clemently, and guided him. He said, ‘Get down both of you from it, all together, being enemies of one another! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall not go astray, nor be miserable. But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall raise him blind.’ He will say, ‘My Lord! Why have You raised me blind, though I used to see?’ He will say, ‘So it is. Our signs came to you, but you forgot them, and thus you will be forgotten today.’" (20:115-126).

The context of the verses, especially the saying of the Exalted at the beginning of the story: "Indeed, I am going to set a vicegerent on the earth" (إني جاعل في الأرض خليفة), indicates that Adam (peace be upon him) was created specifically to live on the earth and die therein. Allah only settled them in the Garden to test them and so that their nakedness (سوآتهما) would be exposed to them, leading to their descent to the earth. Similarly, the context of His saying in Surah Ta-Ha: "So We said, 'O Adam...'" (فقلنا يا آدم), and in Surah al-A'raf: "And O Adam, dwell..." (ويا آدم اسكن), weaves the story of the Garden with the story of the angels' prostration as a single, continuous narrative.

In short, he (peace be upon him) was created to inhabit the earth, and the path to settling on the earth was this very course: his being preferred over the angels to establish his vicegerency (الخلافة), then the command to them to prostrate, then the dwelling in the Garden, and the prohibition from approaching the forbidden tree until they ate from it and their nakedness was exposed, causing them to descend to the earth. Thus, the final factor for settling on the earth and choosing the worldly life (الحيوة الدنيا) was the appearance of the nakedness (السوأة)—which refers to the private parts (العورة), as evidenced by His saying: "and they began to stitch over themselves with the leaves of paradise." This represents animalistic inclination (التمايل الحيواني), which also necessitates nutrition and growth. Therefore, Iblis had no concern other than exposing their nakedness.

Although Allah had fashioned Adam and his wife with an earthly, human fashioning (تسوية أرضية بشرية) before entering them into the Garden, they did not remain long after this fashioning, nor were they given much respite to complete their perception of their nakedness or other necessities and requirements of worldly life in this world before Allah entered them into the Garden. He entered them into the Garden at a time when they had not yet separated or disconnected their perception from the realm of the spirit (عالم الروح) and the angels (الملائكة). The evidence for this is His saying: "to expose to them what had been hidden (ووري) from them," and He did not say "what was hidden (كان ووري) from them." This suggests that the concealment of the nakedness was not possible in the worldly life as a permanent state; rather, it was maintained for a brief moment, followed immediately by the dwelling in the Garden.

Thus, the appearance of the nakedness was a decreed and inevitable matter (مقضياً محتوماً) in the earthly life and upon eating from the tree. For this reason, the Exalted said: "So do not let him expel you from paradise, or you will be miserable (فتشقى)," and He said: "and He expelled them from what they were in." Furthermore, the Exalted forgave their mistake after they repented, yet He did not return them to the Garden; instead, He sent them down to the world to live therein. Had the earthly life, along with the eating from the tree and the appearance of the nakedness, not been an inevitable decree, and had returning to the Garden thereafter not been impossible, they would have returned to it after the removal of the mistake. Thus, the factor in their exit from the Garden and their descent was the eating from the tree and the appearance of the nakedness, which occurred through the whispering (وسوسة) of the accursed Satan.

The Exalted said in Surah Ta-Ha at the beginning of the story: وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنَا إِلَىٰ آدَمَ مِن قَبْلُ فَنَسِيَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُ عَزْمًا — "Certainly We had enjoined Adam before; but he forgot, and We did not find any primacy in him." Then He narrated the story. Is this covenant (العهد) His saying: "do not approach this tree, lest you should be among the wrongdoers"? Or is it His saying: "This is indeed an enemy to you and your mate"? Or is it the covenant in the sense of the universal covenant (الميثاق العمومي) taken from all human beings, and from the prophets specifically in a more emphasized and solemn manner?

The first possibility is incorrect because of His saying: "Then Satan tempted them... and he said, ‘Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree lest you should become angels...’ and he swore to them..." These verses show that at the time of committing the mistake and approaching the tree, they were mindful of the prohibition. Yet the Exalted said: "but he forgot, and We did not find any primacy in him." Therefore, the mentioned covenant is not the prohibition against approaching the tree. As for the second possibility—that the mentioned covenant is the warning against following Iblis—while it is not entirely far-fetched, the apparent meaning of the verses does not support it, as the covenant is specified to Adam (peace be upon him) alone, as is evident from the verse.

Furthermore, the warning against Iblis was for both of them together. Also, the conclusion of the verses in Surah Ta-Ha, which corresponds to their beginning, suits the covenant in the sense of the universal covenant (الميثاق الكلي), not the warning against Iblis. The Exalted says: فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ أَعْمَىٰ — "Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall not go astray, nor be miserable. But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall raise him blind."

By way of application, His saying: "But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life" corresponds to the forgetting of the covenant. As you can see, this is more fitting for the covenant in the sense of the covenant of Lordship (الميثاق على الربوبية) and servitude (العبودية) than for the warning against Iblis. Conceptually, there is little correlation between "disregarding the remembrance" and "following Iblis," whereas it is most fitting for the covenant of Lordship. The covenant of Lordship means that man should never forget that the Exalted is his Lord—meaning his Owner (مالكاً) and Governor (مدبراً)—and that man should never, in any state, forget that he is an absolute possession (مملوك طلق) who owns nothing for himself: neither benefit nor harm, neither death nor life nor resurrection; neither in essence (ذاتاً), nor attribute (وصفاً), nor action (فعلاً).

The mistake (الخطيئة) that stands in opposition to this is man's disregard for the remembrance of his Lord's station and his heedlessness of Him by turning toward himself or toward that which pertains to himself of the fleeting and decaying ornaments of the worldly life.

However, if you look closely at the worldly life in its various aspects, its scattered parts, and its commonality between the believer and the disbeliever, you will find it, in reality and in its inner depth (الباطن), to be different in the two cases according to the taste of knowledge of Allah (العلم بالله) and ignorance of Him. The one who knows the station of his Lord (العارف بمقام ربه), when he looks at himself and at the worldly life—which encompasses all types of impurities, varieties of pain, and sorts of hardships such as death and life, health and sickness, abundance and poverty, ease and toil, gain and loss—sees that all of it (whether within the human soul or outside it) is owned by his Lord. Nothing has independence in or of itself; rather, everything belongs to Him who possesses only beauty (الحسن), splendor (البهاء), and goodness (الخير) as befits His might and majesty. Nothing emanates from His presence except the beautiful and the good.

When he looks at things in this way, he sees no hardship that he dislikes, no fear that he dreads, no awe that he shudders at, and no danger that he guards against. Rather, he sees everything he beholds as beautiful and beloved, except what his Lord commands him to dislike or hate; even then, he hates it because of His command, and he loves what He loves and finds pleasure and joy in His command. He has no concern other than his Lord. All this is because he sees everything as the absolute property of his Lord, with no other having any share or portion in it. What business has he with the Owner of the affair and how He disposes of His property—whether by giving life or death, benefit or harm, and so on? This is the "wholesome life" (الحيوة الطيبة) in which there is no misery whatsoever; it is a light with no darkness, a joy with no grief, a finding with no loss, and a wealth with no poverty—all through Allah, the Glorified.

In contrast to this life is the life of the one ignorant of his Lord's station. This poor soul, due to his disconnection from his Lord, does not cast his gaze upon any existent—himself or otherwise—without seeing it as independent in itself, harmful or beneficial, good or evil. Thus, he fluctuates in his life between fear of what he dreads losing, caution against what he fears happening, grief over what escapes him, and regret over what he loses of status, wealth, children, supporters, or anything else he loves, relies upon, and prefers.

Whenever his skin is toughened by habituating to hardship and settling into bitterness, it is replaced by another skin so that he may taste the punishment with a heart that is agitated and anxious, an inner being that is melting and burning, and a chest that is narrow and constrained as if he were climbing into the sky. Thus does Allah place abomination upon those who do not believe.

Once you understand this, you will know that the return of both matters—namely, the forgetting of the covenant and the misery of the worldly life—is one, and that worldly misery is a branch of forgetting the covenant.

This is what His speech, the Glorified, suggests when He presented the comprehensive obligation for the people of the world in Surah Ta-Ha, saying: فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ . وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ أَعْمَىٰ — "Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall not go astray, nor be miserable. But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall raise him blind."

And He replaced this in this Surah with His saying: فَمَن تَبِعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ — "then whoever follows My guidance shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve."

From here, you may surmise, if you are discerning, that the tree was a tree whose approach entailed the toil and misery of the worldly life—which is for man to live in the world forgetting his Lord and heedless of His station. It is as if Adam (peace be upon him) wanted to combine the tree with the covenant taken from him, but he was unable to do so; thus, he forgot the covenant and fell into the toil of the worldly life, which was then remedied for him through repentance (التوبة).

His saying, the Exalted: "and eat thereof in abundance (رغداً)" (وكلا منها رغداً): Al-Raghad (الرغد) means pleasantness and goodness of life. It is said, "the people made their livestock arghada" when they left them to graze as they pleased. "A raghad people" and " raghad women" mean those possessing a prosperous life.

And His saying, the Exalted: "but do not approach this tree" (ولا تقربا هذه الشجرة): It seems the prohibition was actually against eating the fruit, but it was attached to "approaching" the tree to signal the severity of the prohibition and as an exaggeration in emphasis. This is evidenced by His saying: "So when they tasted of the tree, their nakedness became exposed to them" (al-A'raf: 22), and His saying: "So they ate of it, and their nakedness became exposed to them" (Ta-Ha: 121). Thus, the violation was by eating, which is what was forbidden by the words "do not approach."

His saying, the Exalted: "lest you should be among the wrongdoers (الظالمين)" (فتكونا من الظالمين): This is from dhulm (ظلم) — wrongdoing or injustice, not from dhulmah (ظلمة) — darkness, as some have supposed. They both admitted to their wrongdoing when they said, as Allah narrated from them: "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves (ظلمنا أنفسنا)! If You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us..."

However, in Surah Ta-Ha, Allah replaced this phrase—namely, "lest you should be among the wrongdoers"—with His saying: "or you will be miserable (فتشقى)." Misery (الشقاء) here means toil (التعب). Then He explained and detailed this toil, saying: إِنَّ لَكَ أَلَّا تَجُوعَ فِيهَا وَلَا تَعْرَىٰ وَأَنَّكَ لَا تَظْمَأُ فِيهَا وَلَا تَضْحَىٰ — "Indeed you will neither go hungry in it nor go naked. Indeed you will neither thirst in it nor suffer from the sun."

From this, it becomes clear that the consequence of this wrongdoing was falling into the toil of the life of this world—hunger, thirst, nakedness, and hardship. Accordingly, the wrongdoing on their part was only wrongdoing against themselves, not in the sense of technical sin (المعصية المصطلحة) or wrongdoing against Allah, the Glorified. From here, it also appears that this prohibition—namely, "do not approach"—was only a cautionary and advisory prohibition (نهياً تنزيهياً إرشادياً) by which He guided them to what contained the well-being and benefit of the subject in the station of counsel, not a legislative command (نهياً مولوياً).

They only wronged themselves by leaving the Garden. Furthermore, the penalty for violating a legislative command (النهي المولوي) is removed by repentance when it is accepted, but it was not removed in their case. They repented and their repentance was accepted, yet they did not return to the Garden where they had been. Had the obligation not been advisory (إرشادي)—having only ontological consequences (تبعة تكوينية) rather than legislative ones (تشريعية)—the acceptance of repentance would have necessitated their return to the station of proximity (مقام القرب) they were in. More will be said on this later, God willing.

His saying, the Glorified: "Then Satan caused them to slip (فأزلهما الشيطان)": The apparent meaning of this sentence, like its counterparts, does not necessarily imply more than Satan's whispering (وسوسة) to them, just as he whispers to us (the children of Adam) by casting suggestions into the heart without the person being seen.

However, the apparent meaning of verses such as His saying in Surah Ta-Ha: "So We said, 'O Adam! This is indeed an enemy to you and your mate,'" indicates that the Exalted showed them Satan and made him known to them by person and eye, not just by description. Likewise, His saying narrating from Satan: "O Adam! Shall I guide you to the tree of eternity..." where the speech is presented in the form of a narrated address, indicates a perceived speaker.

Similarly, His saying in Surah al-A'raf: "And he swore to them, ‘I am indeed a well-wisher to you’" (وقاسمهما إني لكما لمن الناصحين); an oath only comes from a perceived swearer.

Also, His saying: "Their Lord called out to them, ‘Did I not forbid you from that tree, and tell you, “Satan is indeed your manifest enemy”?’" All of this indicates that he was appearing to them and they were witnessing him. If their state (peace be upon them) were like ours—not witnessing him during the whispering—it would have been permissible for them to say: "Our Lord, we did not perceive him; we thought these whispers were our own thoughts without sensing his presence or intending to violate Your instruction to beware of his whispering."

In short, they were witnessing him and knew him. The prophets, who are protected by the protection (عصمة) of Allah, likewise know him and witness him when he confronts them, as mentioned in narrations regarding Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, John, Job, Ishmael, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وعليهم).

Furthermore, the apparent meaning of these verses, like His saying: "Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree," suggests that they were with him—may he be cursed—near the tree in the Garden. He had entered the Garden, accompanied them, and deceived them with his whispering. There is no problem in this, as the Garden was not the Garden of Eternity (جنة الخلد) such that Satan could not enter it. The proof for this is the exit of all of them from this Garden.

As for His saying addressing Iblis: "‘Get down from it, for it is not for you to be arrogant therein. So get out!’" (al-A'raf: 13), it is possible that the intended meaning is the exit from the company of the angels, or the exit from the heaven (السماء) insofar as it is a station of proximity and honor.

His saying, the Exalted: "He said, 'Get down, being enemies of one another...'" (قال اهبطوا بعضكم لبعض عدو): The apparent context is that it is an address to Adam, his wife, and Iblis. Iblis alone was specified with the address in Surah al-A'raf where He said: "He said, ‘Get down from it, for it is not for you to be arrogant therein...’" Thus, His saying "Get down (plural)" (اهبطوا) is like a combination of the two addresses and a narration of a decree by which Allah ordained enmity between the accursed Iblis and Adam, his wife, and their progeny. Likewise, He decreed their life on the earth, their death therein, and their resurrection from it.

The progeny of Adam are included with Adam in this ruling, as may be sensed from the apparent meaning of His saying: "In it you shall live, and in it you shall die, and from it you shall be brought forth," and as will come in His saying: "Certainly We created you, then We fashioned you, then We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam’" (al-A'raf: 11).

The angels' prostration to Adam (peace be upon him) was because he was an earthly vicegerent (خليفة أرضي). Thus, the one prostrated to was Adam (peace be upon him), but the ruling of the prostration applied to all of humanity. Adam's standing in the position of the one prostrated to was in the capacity of a prototype (الأنموذج) and a representative (النائب).

In short, this story narrated by Allah—of Adam and his wife dwelling in the Garden, then their descent due to eating from the tree—resembles a parable (المثل) illustrating the state of happiness and dignity man was in before his descent to the world: dwelling in the enclosure of holiness (حظيرة القدس), the abode of loftiness and proximity, the house of blessing, joy, intimacy, and light, with pure companions, spiritual friends, and the neighborhood of the Lord of the worlds.

Then, he chooses in its place every toil, hardship, dislike, and pain by inclining toward a fleeting life and a lowly, putrid carcass. Then, if he returns to his Lord after that, He would return him to the abode of His dignity and happiness. But if he does not return to Him, clings to the earth, and follows his desires, then he has exchanged the blessing of Allah for disbelief and has brought himself to the abode of ruin—Hell, which he shall enter, and an evil settlement it is.

His saying, the Exalted: "Then Adam received (فتلقى) certain words from his Lord, and He turned to him clemently" (فتلقى آدم من ربه كلمات فتاب عليه): Al-talaqqi (التلقي) is al-talaqqun (التلقن) — the receiving of speech with understanding and comprehension. This receiving was the path that facilitated Adam's (peace be upon him) repentance.

From this, it appears that repentance (التوبة) is of two types: repentance from Allah, which is His returning to the servant with mercy; and repentance from the servant, which is his returning to Allah with seeking forgiveness (الاستغفار) and uprooting the sin.

The servant's repentance is flanked by two repentances from Allah. For the servant is never independent of his Lord in any state; his turning away from sin toward Him requires His success-granting (توفيق), assistance, and mercy so that the repentance may be realized. Then, there is a need for His acceptance, care, and mercy. Thus, when the servant's repentance is accepted, it lies between two repentances from Allah, as indicated by His saying: "Then He turned to them clemently (تاب عليهم) so that they might repent (ليتوبوا)" (al-Tawbah: 118).

The reading with Adam in the accusative case (نصب) and words in the nominative case (رفع) suits this point, although the other reading (with Adam in the nominative and words in the accusative) does not contradict it either.

As for what these words were, it is possible they are what Allah narrates from them in Surah al-A'raf: "They said, ‘Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers’" (al-A'raf: 23). However, the occurrence of these words—namely, "They said, 'Our Lord, we have wronged...'"—before His saying "He said, 'Get down'" in Surah al-A'raf, and the occurrence of His saying "Then Adam received..." after His saying "We said, 'Get down'" in this Surah, does not support this.

But there is something here: You have seen at the beginning of the story that when Allah said: "Indeed, I am going to set a vicegerent on the earth," the angels said: "Will You set in it one who will cause corruption in it and shed blood, while we celebrate Your praise and proclaim Your holiness?" The Exalted did not refute their claim regarding the earthly vicegerent or the accusations they cast; He only answered by teaching Adam all the names (الأسماء).

Had there not been something in the teaching of the names that closed the door to their objection, their speech would not have been cut off, nor would the proof have been established against them. Thus, among the names Allah taught Adam was something that benefits the sinner when he sins and the wrongdoer when he errs. Perhaps his receiving from his Lord pertained to some of those names; so understand this.

Know that although Adam (peace be upon him) wronged himself by casting it onto the brink of destruction and the fork between the paths of happiness and misery—namely, the world—had he remained at his point of descent, he would have perished. And had he returned to his initial happiness, he would have toiled and wronged himself. Thus, he (peace be upon him) was a wrongdoer to himself in every estimation. However, through his descent, he prepared for himself a degree of happiness and a station of perfection that he would not have attained had he not descended, nor would he have attained it had he descended without the mistake.

When could he have witnessed his own poverty, humiliation, destitution, need, and deficiency while he possessed, in every toil and hardship that befell him, a spirit and ease in the enclosure of holiness and the neighborhood of the Lord of the worlds? For Allah has attributes of pardon, forgiveness, repentance, concealment, grace, kindness, and mercy that are only attained by sinners; and He has, in the days of time, fragrances that are only enjoyed by those who expose themselves to them.

This repentance is what necessitated the legislation of the path expected to be traversed and the cleansing of the dwelling hoped to be inhabited; behind it lies the legislation of the religion (الدين) and the establishment of the creed (الملة).

This is evidenced by what you see in Allah's speech, where He repeatedly places repentance before faith (الإيمان). The Exalted said: "So be steadfast as you have been commanded, and those who have repented with you" (Hud: 112), and He said: "Indeed I am All-forgiving toward him who repents and believes" (Ta-Ha: 82), among other verses.

His saying, the Exalted: "We said, 'Get down from it, all together! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me...'" (قلنا اهبطوا منها جميعاً فإما يأتينكم مني هدى). This is the first legislation of religion for Adam (peace be upon him) and his progeny. He summarized the entire religion in two sentences to which nothing shall be added until the Day of Resurrection.

If you reflect on this story (the story of the Garden), especially what occurred in Surah Ta-Ha, you will find that the course of the story necessitated two decrees (قضائين) from the Exalted regarding Adam and his progeny. The eating from the tree necessitated His ruling and decree of descent, settlement on the earth, and living therein—that miserable life they were warned of when forbidden from approaching the tree.

Secondly, the repentance was followed by a second decree and ruling from the Exalted: honoring Adam and his progeny with guidance (الهداية) toward servitude (العبودية). Thus, what was decreed first was the earthly life itself; then, through repentance, Allah made that life wholesome by mounting upon it the guidance toward servitude. Thus, life was composed of an earthly life and a heavenly life.

This is what is understood from the repetition of the command to descend in this Surah, where the Exalted said: "And We said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode and sustenance for a time’" and then said: "We said, ‘Get down from it, all together! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me...’"

The placement of repentance between the two commands to descend suggests that the repentance occurred while they had not yet separated from the Garden, even if they were no longer in it as they had been settled before.

This is also suggested by His saying: "Their Lord called out to them, ‘Did I not forbid you from that (تلكما) tree...’" after He had said to them: "do not approach this (هذه) tree." He used the word "that" (تلكما), which is a demonstrative for the distant, after having used "this" (هذه), which is for the near. And He used the word "called out" (نادى), which is for the distant, after having used "said" (قال), which is for the near; so understand.

Know that the apparent meaning of His saying: "And We said, ‘Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode and sustenance for a time’" and His saying: "He said, ‘In it you shall live, and in it you shall die, and from it you shall be brought forth’" is that the mode of this life after the descent differs from its mode in the Garden before the descent. This is a life whose reality is intertwined with the reality of the earth, characterized by toil and misery, necessitating that man be formed in the earth, then returned to it by death, then brought out from it by resurrection.

Thus, the earthly life differs from the life of the Garden; the life of the Garden was a heavenly life, not an earthly one.

From here, one can conclude with certainty that Adam's Garden was in the heaven (السماء), even if it was not the Garden of the Hereafter—the Garden of Eternity from which no one who enters ever exits.

Yes, the discussion remains regarding the meaning of "the heaven" (السماء), and perhaps we shall be granted the success to exhaustively research it, God willing.

One thing remains here, which is the discussion regarding Adam's mistake (خطيئة). We say: although the apparent meaning of the verses at first glance suggests the occurrence of disobedience (المعصية) and mistake from him (peace be upon him)—as the Exalted said: "lest you should be among the wrongdoers," and "Adam disobeyed his Lord, and went astray," and as he admitted in what Allah narrated from them: "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers"—

However, reflection on the verses of the story and precision regarding the prohibition issued against eating from the tree necessitates the certainty that the mentioned prohibition was not a legislative prohibition (نهياً مولوياً). Rather, it was an advisory prohibition (نهي إرشادي) intended to guide and direct toward the benefit and good inherent in the subject of the obligation, not a legislative command or will.

This is evidenced by: First: The Exalted made "wrongdoing" (الظلم) a consequence of the prohibition in this Surah and in Surah al-A'raf, saying: "do not approach this tree, lest you should be among the wrongdoers." Then He replaced it in Surah Ta-Ha with His saying: "or you will be miserable (فتشقى)," making it a consequence of leaving the Garden. Misery (الشقاء) means toil (التعب). Then He mentioned after it, as if in explanation: "Indeed you will neither go hungry in it nor go naked. Indeed you will neither thirst in it nor suffer from the sun."

Thus, He clarified that the intended misery is worldly toil, which this earthly life entails—hunger, thirst, nakedness, and so on.

Guarding against these matters is what necessitated such a prohibition, not any other legislative aspect. Thus, the prohibition was advisory, and violating an advisory prohibition does not constitute a legislative disobedience or a transgression of the bounds of servitude. Accordingly, the intended "wrongdoing" (الظلم) in the verses is their wrongdoing against themselves by casting themselves into toil and destruction, not the blameworthy wrongdoing in the context of Lordship and servitude; this is clear.

Second: Repentance (التوبة)—which is the return from the servant—when followed by acceptance from the side of the Master, necessitates that the sin becomes as if it were no sin, and the disobedience as if it never occurred. The repentant sinner is treated as an obedient and submissive one, and his action is treated as compliance and submission.

Had the prohibition against eating from the tree been legislative, and had the repentance been for a servant's sin and a return from violating a legislative prohibition, it would have been necessary for them to return to the Garden; yet they did not return.

From here, it is known that the consequence of the forbidden eating—namely, the exit from the Garden—was a necessary, ontological consequence (إستتباعاً ضرورياً تكوينياً), similar to the consequence of poison leading to death or fire leading to burning, as is the case in advisory obligations. It was not a consequence of the nature of legislative punishment (المجازاة المولوية) in legislative obligations—such as entering the Fire for one who abandons prayer, or deserving blame and necessitating distance in general, social, legislative violations.

Third: His saying, the Exalted: "We said, ‘Get down from it, all together! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve. But those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it forever.’"

This is a comprehensive statement for all detailed legislations that Allah sent down in this world through His angels, books, and messengers. It narrates the first legislation enacted for man in this world—which is the world of Adam and his progeny. As Allah narrates, it occurred after the second command to descend. It is clear that the command to descend is an ontological command (أمر تكويني) subsequent to being in the Garden and committing the mistake. Thus, at the time of violating the prohibition and approaching the tree, there was neither a legislated religion nor a legislative obligation. Therefore, no servant's sin or legislative disobedience was realized at that time.

This does not contradict the fact that the address to the angels and Iblis to prostrate—which was before the address "do not approach"—was a legislative address, because the subject of the obligation (المكلف) was different.

If you say: "If the prohibition was advisory and not legislative, what is the meaning of Allah counting their action as wrongdoing (ظلم), disobedience (عصيان), and straying (غواية)?"

I say: As for wrongdoing, it has already been mentioned that the intended meaning is their wrongdoing against themselves in the presence of Allah. As for disobedience (العصيان), linguistically it means the lack of being affected (عدم الإنفعال) or being affected with difficulty, as it is said: "I broke it and it broke (إنكسر), and I broke it and it disobeyed (عصى)." Disobedience—which is the lack of being affected by a command or prohibition—occurs in the context of advisory addresses just as it occurs in legislative obligations.

As for the meaning of disobedience (المعصية) becoming restricted in these times among us Muslims to the violation of commands like "pray," "fast," "perform Hajj," or "do not drink wine," "do not commit adultery," and the like—this is a restriction by way of technical religious terminology (الحقيقة الشرعية أو المتشرعة) which does not harm the generality of the meaning according to language and general usage.

As for straying (الغواية), it is man's inability, for example, to maintain the objective and manage himself in his livelihood in a way that suits and accords with the objective.

Clearly, this varies according to the context, whether advisory or legislative.

If you say: "Then what is the meaning of repentance (التوبة) and their saying: ‘If You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers’?"

I say: Repentance, as mentioned, is "returning," and returning varies according to its contexts.

Just as it is permissible for a servant who rebels against his master's command and will to repent to him, so that his lost station of proximity to him may be restored; it is likewise permissible for a sick person—whom a doctor has forbidden, through an advisory prohibition, from eating a specific fruit or food solely for the sake of his health and well-being—to not desist from the prohibition, commit it, suffer harm, and be on the brink of destruction.
