Thus, they raised objections based on several points and concluded the invalidity of this Quranic reality without reflecting on what His speech, the Exalted, provides. Here are some of them:

The First Objection: That removing punishment from a criminal on the Day of Resurrection, after Allah has established it through threat (الوعيد), must be either justice (عدلاً) or injustice (ظلماً). If it is justice, then the original ruling that entailed punishment was injustice, which is unbecoming of His sanctity and holiness. If it is injustice, then the intercession of the prophets, for example, would be a request for Him to commit injustice, which is an ignorance that cannot be attributed to them (صلوات الله عليهم).

The answer to this is, firstly: by way of "rebuttal" (النقض). It is rebutted by the "test commands" (الأوامر الامتحانية). Removing a test ruling later and establishing it initially are both acts of justice. The wisdom in them is to test the inner secret of the person charged, to manifest his hidden state, or to bring what is in his potential into actuality. Thus, in the case of intercession, it can also be said that salvation might be written for all believers. Then, rulings and various types of punishments for their violation are set so that the disbelievers perish due to their disbelief. As for the believers, the ranks of the virtuous among them are raised through obedience, while the sinners remain and attain the salvation written for them through intercession—even if only regarding some types or instances of punishment, while enduring others, such as the states of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — and the terrors of the Day of Resurrection. Thus, the original establishment of the ruling and its punishment would be justice, and the subsequent removal of the punishment would also be justice.

Secondly: by way of "resolution" (الحل). The removal of punishment through intercession only differs from the first ruling regarding the mentioned justice and injustice if the removal of punishment through intercession were a "nullification" (نقضاً) of the first ruling or a nullification of the ruling's entailment of punishment. You have learned that it is not so. Rather, the effect of intercession is through "prevailing" (الحكومة), not through "opposition" (المضادة). It involves removing the criminal from being an instance of the encompassing punishment by making him an instance of the encompassing mercy from His other attributes, such as mercy, pardon, and forgiveness, including His honoring and magnifying the intercessor.

The Second Objection: That the custom (سنة) of Allah, the Exalted, has proceeded upon safeguarding His acts from inconsistency and variation. What He has decreed and judged, He implements in a uniform manner without exception. Upon this, the custom of causes has proceeded. The Exalted said: هَٰذَا صِرَاطٌ عَلَيَّ مُسْتَقِيمٌ إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمَوْعِدُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — "‘This is a straight path [the maintenance of] which rests on Me. Indeed, as for My servants, you do not have any authority over them, except the perverse who follow you, and indeed hell is the tryst of them all’" (15:41-43). And the Exalted said: وَأَنَّ هَٰذَا صِرَاطِي مُسْتَقِيمًا فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ — "‘This is indeed My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you from His way’" (6:153). And the Exalted said: فَلَن تَجِدَ لِسُنَّتِ اللَّهِ تَبْدِيلًا وَلَن تَجِدَ لِسُنَّتِ اللَّهِ تَحْوِيلًا — "you will never find any change in Allah’s custom, and you will never find any alteration in Allah’s custom" (35:43). The realization of intercession would cause variation in action. For removing punishment through intercession from all criminals for all their crimes would lead to the nullification of the impossible premise and would be a form of "play" (لعب) that certainly contradicts wisdom. Removing it from some criminals or for some of their crimes and sins would be a variation in His act and a change and alteration in His prevailing custom and permanent way. For there is no difference between criminals in that each is a criminal, nor between sins in that each is a sin and a departure from the state of servitude. Thus, specifying some of them or some of their deeds for pardon and overlooking through intercession, while excluding others, is impossible. Intercession and its like only occur in the custom of this life, where deeds and acts are built upon whims and illusions that might judge truth and falsehood equally, and proceed from wisdom and ignorance in the same manner.

The answer is that there is no doubt that His path, the Exalted, is straight and His custom is one. However, this single, non-varying custom is not based on a single attribute among His attributes, such as the attribute of legislation and judgment, such that a ruling never fails its subject nor a penalty its place. Rather, it is based on what is necessitated by all His attributes related to the act.

To clarify: Allah, the Glorified, is the Bestower and Giver of everything in existence—life, death, provision, blessing, and so on. These are diverse matters that do not relate to Him, the Glorified, equally or through a single link, for that would mean the nullification of connection and causality. He, the Exalted, does not heal a sick person without a necessitating cause and a justifying interest. He does not heal him because He is Allah the Bringer of Death, the Avenger, the Severe in Punishment, but because He is Allah the Compassionate, the Merciful, the Benefactor, the Healer, the Granter of Health, for example. Nor does He destroy an arrogant tyrant without a cause, because He is Compassionate and Merciful to him, but because He is Allah the Avenger, the Severe in Punishment, the Subduer, for example. The Quran speaks of this. Every event among events, with the existential aspects it contains, is attributed to Him through one or more of His supreme attributes that are caused by the harmony and concord occurring between them and the necessity resulting from that. If you wish, you could say: every matter among matters relates to Him, the Exalted, from the perspective of the interests and goods it contains. Once you know this, you will know that the straightness of His path, the non-alteration of His custom, and the non-variation of His act are only in relation to what He does through all the relevant attributes, not in relation to the requirement of a single, limited attribute. If you wish, you could say: in relation to what results from the action, reaction, and the "breaking and counter-breaking" (الكسر والانكسار) occurring between the ruling and the interests related to the case, not in relation to the requirement of a single interest. If there were only the cause of the established ruling, it would not change or vary for the righteous or the wicked, the believer or the disbeliever. But causes are many; sometimes the agreement of several of them necessitates something other than what some of them require. So understand this.

Thus, the occurrence of intercession and the removal of punishment—which is the effect of several causes such as mercy, forgiveness, judgment, decree, giving every rightful person his due, and decisiveness in judgment—does not necessitate a variation in the prevailing custom or a deviation from the straight path.

The Third Objection: That intercession as known among people is when the intercessor induces the one from whom intercession is sought to perform or abandon an act that he had previously intended or judged. Thus, intercession is only realized by abandoning the will and abrogating it for the sake of the intercessor. As for a just ruler, he does not accept intercession unless his knowledge of what he intended or judged changes—as if he had erred and then recognized the correct course, seeing that the interest or the deed lies in opposition to what he had intended or judged. As for a despotic, unjust ruler, he accepts the intercession of those near to him while knowing it is an injustice and that justice lies elsewhere, but he prefers the interest of his connection to the near intercessor over justice. Both types are impossible for Allah, the Exalted, because His will is according to His knowledge, and His knowledge is eternal and does not change.

The answer is that this, in His case, is not a change of will or knowledge at all. Rather, the change is in the "object of will" (المراد) and the "object of knowledge" (المعلوم). He, the Glorified, knows that a certain person will undergo changes in states, being in such a state at such a time due to the conjunction of specific causes and conditions, so He wills for him with one will. Then, at another time, he will be in another new state that contradicts the first due to the conjunction of other causes and conditions, so He wills for him with another will. "Every day He is engaged in some affair." He, the Exalted, has said: يَمْحُو اللَّهُ مَا يَشَاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِندَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتَابِ — "Allah effaces and confirms whatever He wishes, and with Him is the Mother of the Book" (13:39). And He said: بَلْ يَدَاهُ مَبْسُوطَتَانِ يُنفِقُ كَيْفَ يَشَاءُ — "Rather His hands are wide open: He spends as He wishes" (5:64). An example of this: we know that the air will be covered by darkness, so our vision will not work while the need for it remains. Then the darkness will clear with the light of the sun. Our will, at the onset of night, relates to seeking light from a lamp, and at its end, to extinguishing it. The knowledge and the will are not changing; rather, the object of knowledge and the object of will have changed, so they ceased to be applicable to that knowledge and will. Not every knowledge applies to every object of knowledge, nor does every will relate to every object of will. Yes, the change of knowledge and will that is impossible for Him, the Exalted, is the failure of the application of knowledge to the object of knowledge and will to the object of will while the object of knowledge and will remain as they were—which is error and revocation. For example, seeing a silhouette and judging it to be a human, then it becomes clear it is a horse, so the knowledge changes. Or intending a matter for some interest, then it appears to you that the interest lies elsewhere, so your will is revoked. These two are not permissible in His case, the Exalted. Intercession and the removal of punishment through it are not of this type, as you have learned.

The Fourth Objection: That the promise of intercession from Him, the Exalted, or its conveyance by the prophets (عليه السلام), necessitates emboldening people to commit sins and inciting them to violate the sanctities of Allah, the Exalted. This contradicts the sole purpose of religion, which is to lead people toward servitude and obedience. Therefore, what the Book and Sunnah indicate must be interpreted in a way that does not conflict with this self-evident principle.

The answer to this is, firstly: by way of "rebuttal" (النقض) through the verses indicating the encompassment of forgiveness and the vastness of mercy, such as His saying: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَاءُ — "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes" (4:48). This verse—as mentioned—is not about the context of repentance, as evidenced by His exception of polytheism, which is forgiven through repentance.

Secondly: by way of "resolution" (الحل). The promise of intercession or its conveyance only necessitates emboldening people to sin and inciting them to rebellion and opposition under two conditions:

One: Specifying the criminal himself and his description, or specifying the sin in which intercession occurs, with a specification that leaves no ambiguity, in a definitive manner without making it contingent upon a permissible condition.

Two: That intercession influences all types of punishment and all its times, such that it uproots it from its foundation entirely.

If it were said: "Such a group of people, or all people, will not be punished for what they have committed, nor will they be held accountable for their sins ever," or if it were said, "Such a sin has no punishment at all," that would be a false statement and a "play" with the rulings and duties directed at those charged. However, if the matter is left ambiguous regarding the two conditions—not specifying which sins intercession covers, nor for which sinners, nor whether the removed punishment is all punishments in all times and states—then no soul knows whether it will attain the promised intercession or not. Thus, it does not become emboldened to violate the sanctities of Allah, the Exalted. Rather, this awakens the faculty of hope (الرجاء), so that witnessing its sins and offenses does not lead to despair of Allah's mercy or hopelessness of Allah's spirit. Additionally, His saying: إِن تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبَائِرَ مَا تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّئَاتِكُمْ — "If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds" (4:31). This verse indicates the removal of punishment for minor misdeeds and sins on the condition of avoiding major sins. If it is permissible for Allah, the Glorified, to say: "If you guard against the major sins, We will forgive your minor ones," then it is permissible to say: "If you preserve your faith until you come to Me on the Day of Meeting with a sound faith, I will accept the intercession of the intercessors for you." For the entire matter lies in preserving faith, and sins weaken faith, harden the heart, and bring about polytheism. The Exalted has said: فَلَا يَأْمَنُ مَكْرَ اللَّهِ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْخَاسِرُونَ — "No one feels safe from Allah’s stratagem except the people who are losers" (7:99). And He said: كَلَّا بَلْ رَانَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِم مَّا كَانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ — "No indeed! Rather their hearts have been sullied by what they have been earning" (83:14). And He said: ثُمَّ كَانَ عَاقِبَةَ الَّذِينَ أَسَاءُوا السُّوأَىٰ أَن كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ — "Then the fate of those who committed misdeeds was that they denied the signs of Allah" (30:10). This might even lead to one's abandonment of sins, embarking upon the path of God-consciousness (التقوى), becoming one of the virtuous, and becoming independent of intercession in this sense. This is among the greatest benefits. Likewise, if the criminal for whom intercession is made or the crime for which intercession is made is specified, but it is explicitly stated that it covers only some aspects of punishment or some of its times, it certainly does not necessitate the emboldening of criminals.

The Quran has not spoken with specification regarding particular criminals or particular sins, nor has it spoken of removing punishment except in part, as will follow. Thus, there is no objection at all.

The Fifth Objection: That reason, if it indicates anything, only indicates the possibility of the occurrence of intercession, not...

The actual occurrence of intercession (الشفاعة) is denied based on the claim that the original indication of its possibility is rejected. As for the transmitted evidence (النقل), what the Quran contains does not indicate its occurrence. Indeed, there are verses indicating the absolute negation of intercession, such as His saying: لَّا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ وَلَا خُلَّةٌ وَلَا شَفَاعَةٌ... — "in which there will be no bargaining, neither friendship, nor intercession" [2:254]. Others speak of the negation of any benefit from intercession, such as His saying, the Exalted: فَمَا تَنفَعُهُمْ شَفَاعَةُ الشَّافِعِينَ... — "So the intercession of the intercessors will not avail them" [74:48]. Still others imply negation through expressions like His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ... — "except by His leave" [2:255], and His saying: إِلَّا مِن بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِ... — "except after His leave" [10:3], and His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ... — "except for him with whom He is well-pleased" [21:28]. Such exceptions—meaning exceptions by leave (الإذن) and will (المشيئة)—are common in the Quranic style in the context of categorical negation to intimate that the matter remains subject to His leave and will, the Glorified, such as His saying, the Exalted: سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلَا تنسَىٰ إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ اللَّهُ... — "We shall make you recite, and you will not forget, except what Allah wills" [87:6-7], and His saying, the Exalted: خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ السَّمَاوَاتُ وَالْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ رَبُّكَ... — "they shall remain in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord may wish" [11:107]. Thus, there is no definitive text (نص قطعي) in the Quran regarding the occurrence of intercession. As for the Sunnah (السنة), the narrations (الروايات) concerning specific details cannot be relied upon, and the certain portion among them does not exceed the indication found in the Book.

The response: As for the verses negating intercession, you have already learned that they do not negate intercession absolutely, but rather intercession without the leave and pleasure of Allah. As for the verses negating the benefit of intercession—according to the claim of the objector—they actually affirm intercession rather than negate it. These verses occur in Surah al-Muddaththir and only negate the benefit for a specific group of criminals (المجرمين), not for all of them. Furthermore, intercession (الشفاعة) here is mentioned in a genitive construction (مضافة), not as a bare noun detached from addition. There is a difference between saying, "Intercession shall not benefit them," and saying, "The intercession of the intercessors (شفاعة الشافعين) shall not benefit them." For the genitive infinitive (المصدر المضاف) suggests the actual occurrence of the act in reality, unlike that which is detached from addition; this was explicitly stated by Shaykh Abd al-Qahir in Dala'il al-I'jaz. Thus, His saying: "the intercession of the intercessors" indicates that some intercession will indeed occur, though these specific people will not benefit from it. Moreover, the use of the plural form in "intercessors" (الشافعين) also indicates this, similar to His sayings: كَانَتْ مِنَ الْغَابِرِينَ... — "she was one of those who remained behind" [7:83], وَكَانَ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ... — "and he was one of the faithless" [2:34], فَكَانَ مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ... — "and he became one of the perverse" [7:175], and لَا يَنَالُ عَهْدِي الظَّالِمِينَ... — "My pledge does not extend to the wrongdoers" [2:124], and the like. Were it not so, the use of the plural form—which carries a meaning beyond that of the singular—would be a redundant futility in speech. Therefore, His saying: "So the intercession of the intercessors will not avail them" is among the verses that affirm intercession, not those that negate it.

As for the verses containing the exception of leave (الإذن) and pleasure (الإرتضاء), the indication of His saying: إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ... — "except by His leave" [2:255] and His saying: إِلَّا مِن بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِ... — "except after His leave" [10:3] toward the actual occurrence—given that it is a genitive infinitive—is something that no one acquainted with the styles of speech should deny. Likewise, the claim that His saying: إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ... and His saying: إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ... — "except for him with whom He is well-pleased" [21:28] carry the same meaning, namely "will" (المشية), is an opinion that should not be entertained. Furthermore, the exception occurs in the context of intercession in various ways, such as: "except by His leave," "except after His leave," "except for him with whom He is well-pleased," and His saying: إِلَّا مَن شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ... — "excepting such as testify to the truth and they have knowledge" [43:86], among others. Suppose that leave and pleasure were identical to "will"; could such a thing be uttered regarding His saying: "excepting such as testify to the truth and they have knowledge"? Is the intent of this exception also the exception of "will"? This and similar instances of laxity in explanation are not appropriate to attribute even to common street speech, let alone to eloquent speech (الكلام البليغ)! And how much less so to the most eloquent of speech! As for the Sunnah, the discussion regarding its indication of what corresponds to the indication of the Book will follow.

The Sixth Objection: The verses are not explicit in the removal of the punishment established for the criminals (المجرمين) on the Day of Resurrection after the crime has been proven and the punishment necessitated. Rather, what is meant by them is the intercession of the Prophets in the sense of their mediation, qua Prophets, between the people and their Lord by receiving the laws through revelation (الوحي), conveying them to the people, and guiding them. This amount is like a seed that grows and from which future destinies, attributes, and states arise. Thus, they (peace be upon them) are the intercessors for the believers in this world and their intercessors in the Hereafter.

The response: There is no dispute that this is among the instances of intercession, but intercession is not restricted to it, as previously explained. Among the proofs for this is His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَاءُ... — "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes" [4:48]. It has already been explained that this verse concerns cases other than faith (الإيمان) and repentance (التوبة), whereas the intercession established by the objector for the Prophets is only by way of the call to faith and repentance.

The Seventh Objection: The path of reason (العقل) does not lead to the realization of intercession, and what the Quran speaks of are metaphorical verses (آيات متشابهة) that negate it at times and affirm it at others, sometimes restricting it and sometimes leaving it absolute. The religious etiquette (الأدب الديني) is to believe in it and to refer the knowledge of it back to Allah the Exalted.

The response: The metaphorical verses (المتشابهة) become clear (محكمات) like the others by referring them back to the clear verses (المحكمات). This is a matter feasible for us, and no veil has been drawn before it, as will be explained in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: مِنْهُ آيَاتٌ مُّحْكَمَاتٌ هُنَّ أُمُّ الْكِتَابِ وَأُخَرُ مُتَشَابِهَاتٌ... — "In it are verses that are decisive—they are the Mother of the Book—and others are metaphorical" [3:7].

3 - For whom does intercession take place?

You have learned that specifying those for whom intercession is made on the Day of Resurrection does not entirely suit religious training (التربية الدينية), unless they are identified in a way that is not devoid of a tinge of ambiguity (إبهام). The Quranic exposition followed this course. The Exalted said: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ... — "Every soul is hostage to what it has earned, except the People of the Right Hand. [They will be] in gardens, questioning concerning the guilty: 'What drew you into Hell?' They will say, 'We were not among those who prayed, nor did we feed the needy, and we used to gossip with the gossipers, and we used to deny the Day of Retribution, until death came to us.' So the intercession of the intercessors will not avail them" [74:38-48]. In these verses, the Glorified explained that every soul on the Day of Resurrection is held hostage (رهينة) by the sins it has earned and seized by the errors it previously committed, except for the People of the Right Hand (أصحاب اليمين), for they have been released from the pledge, set free, and settled in the gardens. Then He mentioned that they are not veiled from the criminals (المجرمين) who are held hostage by their deeds and seized in Hell (سقر); they question them about their entry into the Fire, and the criminals respond by pointing to several attributes that led them to the Fire. It was then concluded based on these attributes that the intercession of the intercessors did not benefit them.

The implication of this exposition is that the People of the Right Hand are not characterized by these attributes, which the discourse indicates are the barriers to being included in intercession. Since they are not characterized by these attributes that prevent the inclusion of intercession, and since Allah the Exalted has released their souls from the hostage-state of sins and offenses—unlike the criminals who are deprived of intercession and driven into Hell—this release and extraction is only through intercession. Thus, the People of the Right Hand are those who receive intercession. In the verses, there is a definition of the People of the Right Hand through the absence of the aforementioned attributes from them. To explain: these verses occur in Surah al-Muddaththir, which is among the surahs revealed in Mecca at the beginning of the Mission, as indicated by the contents of its verses. At that time, prayer (الصلوة) and zakat (الزكوة) had not yet been legislated in the form they exist today. Thus, what is meant by prayer in the saying "We were not among those who prayed" is turning toward Allah with servile humility (الخضوع العبودي), and by "feeding the needy" is absolute spending on the destitute for the sake of Allah, rather than the specific prayer and zakat known in the Islamic Sharia. "Gossip" (الخوض) is plunging into the diversions of life and the vanities of the world that distract a person from facing the Hereafter and remembering the reckoning on the Day of Retribution, or deeply engaging in defaming the signs of Allah that remind one of the Day of Reckoning, bringing glad tidings and warnings. By being stained with these four attributes—neglecting prayer to Allah, neglecting spending in the way of Allah, gossiping, and denying the Day of Retribution—the pillars of religion are demolished. Conversely, by being characterized by their opposites, its foundation stands firm. For religion (الدين) is the following of the pure guides by turning away from clinging to the earth and turning toward the day of meeting Allah; these two are the abandonment of gossip and the affirmation of the Day of Retribution. The practical necessity of these two is turning toward Allah in worship (العبادة) and striving to fulfill the needs of the living community; these two are prayer and spending in the way of Allah. Thus, religion is sustained in terms of both knowledge (العلم) and action (العمل) by these four traits, and they necessitate the remaining pillars like monotheism (التوحيد) and prophethood (النبوة) with a necessary entailment. Therefore, the People of the Right Hand are those who succeed through intercession, and they are those whose religion and belief are pleasing (مرضيون), whether their deeds were pleasing and thus not in need of intercession on the Day of Resurrection, or not. They are the ones intended by intercession; thus, intercession is for the sinners among the People of the Right Hand. The Exalted has said: إِن تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبَائِرَ مَا تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنكُمْ سَيِّئَاتِكُمْ... — "If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds" [4:31]. Thus, whoever has a sin remaining until the Day of Resurrection is inevitably among the people of major sins (أهل الكبائر), for if the sin were only among the minor ones, it would have been absolved. It has thus become clear that intercession is for the people of major sins among the People of the Right Hand. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said: إنما شفاعتي لأهل الكبائر من امتي... — "My intercession is only for the people of major sins from my community; as for the virtuous, there is no way against them."

From another perspective, these people were named "People of the Right Hand" (أصحاب اليمين) in contrast to the "People of the Left Hand" (أصحاب الشمال), and they are sometimes called "People of the Right" (أصحاب الميمنة) in contrast to the "People of the Ill-Omen" (أصحاب المشئمة). This is among the terms coined by the Quran, derived from a person being given his book on the Day of Resurrection in his right hand or his left hand. The Exalted said: يَوْمَ نَدْعُو كُلَّ أُنَاسٍ بِإِمَامِهِمْ... — "The day We shall summon every group of people with their imam, then whoever is given his book in his right hand—they will read it, and they will not be wronged so much as the husk of a date-stone. But whoever has been blind in this [world], will be blind in the Hereafter, and [even] more astray from the [right] path" [17:71-72]. We shall explain regarding this verse, Allah willing, that being given the book in the right hand means following the Imam of Truth, and being given it in the left hand means following the imam of misguidance, as He said regarding Pharaoh: يَقْدُمُ قَوْمَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ النَّارَ... — "He will lead his people on the Day of Resurrection and conduct them into the Fire" [11:98]. In short, the basis for the naming as "People of the Right Hand" also refers back to the pleasure with their religion (إرتضاء الدين), just as the characterization with the four aforementioned attributes refers back to it.

Furthermore, He, the Exalted, said in another place in His speech: وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ... — "and they do not intercede except for him with whom He is well-pleased" [21:28]. Thus, He affirmed intercession for the one with whom He is pleased (من إرتضی). He left the "pleasure" (الإرتضاء) absolute without restricting it to deeds or the like, as He did in His saying: إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمَٰنُ وَرَضِيَ لَهُ قَوْلًا... — "except him whom the All-beneficent has permitted and whose word He is pleased with" [20:109]. Thus, we understood that what is meant by it is the pleasure with themselves, meaning the pleasure with their religion, not the pleasure with their deeds. This verse also returns, in terms of its benefit, to what the previous verses returned to. Then He, the Exalted, said: يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ الْمُتَّقِينَ إِلَى الرَّحْمَـٰنِ وَفْدًا... — "The day We shall gather the God-conscious toward the All-beneficent as a diplomatic corps, and drive the guilty into hell like a thirsty herd. They will have no power of intercession, except him who has taken a covenant with the All-beneficent" [19:85-87]. Thus, he possesses intercession (meaning the passive infinitive, i.e., being interceded for). Not every criminal (مجرم) is a disbeliever (كافر) destined for the Fire, as evidenced by His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُ مَن يَأْتِ رَبَّهُ مُجْرِمًا فَإِنَّ لَهُ جَهَنَّمَ... — "Whoever comes to his Lord as a criminal, there is indeed hell for him, wherein he shall neither die nor live. But whoever comes to Him as a believer, having done righteous deeds—for such there will be the highest stations" [20:74-75]. Thus, whoever is not a believer who has done righteous deeds is a criminal, whether he did not believe at all, or believed but did not do righteous deeds. Among the criminals are those who were upon the religion of truth but did not do righteous deeds; it is he who has taken a covenant (عهداً) with Allah, according to His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يَا بَنِي آدَمَ أَن لَّا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطَانَ... — "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, 'Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. Worship Me. That is a straight path'?" [36:60-61]. His saying, the Exalted: "Worship Me" is a covenant in the sense of a command, and His saying: "That is a straight path" is a covenant in the sense of an obligation, because the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — includes guidance to felicity and salvation. These are a group of the people of faith who enter the Fire due to their evil deeds, then are saved from it through intercession. To this meaning, His saying, the Exalted, alludes: قَالُوا لَن تَمَسَّنَا النَّارُ إِلَّا أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَةً... — "They say, 'The Fire shall not touch us except for a number of days.' Say, 'Have you taken a pledge from Allah...?'" [2:80]. These verses also return to what the previous verses returned to, and all of them indicate that the subject of intercession—meaning those for whom intercession is made on the Day of Resurrection—are those who profess the religion of truth among the people of major sins, and they are those whose religion Allah has pleased.

4 - From whom does intercession occur?

You have learned that intercession is of two types: ontological (تكوينية) and legislative (تشريعية). As for ontological intercession, all cosmic causes (الأسباب الكونية) are intercessors before Allah insofar as they are intermediaries between Him and things. As for legislative intercession—which occurs in the realm of obligation (التكليف) and recompense—it includes that which seeks forgiveness from Allah the Glorified or proximity and nearness in this world; thus, it is an interceding intermediary between Him and His servant. Among these is repentance (التوبة), as the Exalted said: قُلْ يَا عِبَادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلَىٰ أَنفُسِهِمْ... — "Say, [that Allah declares,] 'O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah forgives all sins. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. Turn penitently to your Lord...'" [39:53-54]. Its scope encompasses all sins, even polytheism (الشرك). Among these is faith (الإيمان); the Exalted said: آمِنُوا بِرَسُولِهِ... — "believe in His Apostle... and He will forgive you" [57:28]. Among these is every righteous deed (عمل صالح); the Exalted said: وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ... — "Allah has promised those who have faith and do righteous deeds that for them there shall be forgiveness and a great reward" [5:9]. The Exalted also said: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَابْتَغُوا إِلَيْهِ الْوَسِيلَةَ... — "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and seek the means of recourse to Him" [5:35], and the verses regarding this are many. Among these is the Quran, due to His saying, the Exalted: يَهْدِي بِهِ اللَّهُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ رِضْوَانَهُ... — "With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure to the ways of peace, and brings them out from darkness into light by His will, and guides them to a straight path" [5:16].

Among these is everything that has a connection to a righteous deed, such as mosques, blessed places, and noble days. Among these are the Prophets and Messengers through their seeking forgiveness for their nations. The Exalted said: وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ إِذ ظَّلَمُوا أَنفُسَهُمْ جَاءُوكَ... — "Had they, when they wronged themselves, come to you and pleaded to Allah for forgiveness, and the Apostle had pleaded for forgiveness for them, they would have surely found Allah all-clement, all-merciful" [4:64]. Among these are the angels in their seeking forgiveness for the believers; the Exalted said: الَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ الْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُ... — "Those who bear the Throne, and those who are around it, celebrate the praise of their Lord and have faith in Him, and they plead for forgiveness for the faithful" [40:7]. The Exalted also said: وَالْمَلَائِكَةُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ... — "and the angels celebrate the praise of their Lord and plead for forgiveness for those who are on the earth. Behold! Allah is indeed the All-forgiving, the All-merciful" [42:5]. Among these are the believers through their seeking forgiveness for themselves and for their fellow believers; the Exalted said, narrating from them: وَاعْفُ عَنَّا وَاغْفِرْ لَنَا وَارْحَمْنَا... — "and excuse us, and forgive us, and have mercy on us. You are our Master" [2:286].

Among these is the intercessor on the Day of Resurrection in the sense you have learned. Among them are the Prophets; the Exalted said: وَقَالُوا اتَّخَذَ الرَّحْمَٰنُ وَلَدًا... — "They say, 'The All-beneficent has taken a son.' Immaculate is He! Rather they are [His] honored servants... and they do not intercede except for him with whom He is well-pleased" [21:26-28]. For among them is Jesus son of Mary, and he is a Prophet. The Exalted also said: وَلَا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ الشَّفَاعَةَ... — "Those whom they invoke besides Him have no power of intercession, excepting such as testify to the truth and they have knowledge" [43:86]. These two verses also indicate the permissibility of intercession from the angels, because they [the polytheists] said they were the daughters of Allah the Glorified. Among them are the angels; the Exalted said: وَكَم مِّن مَّلَكٍ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ لَا تُغْنِي شَفَاعَتُهُمْ... — "How many an angel there is in the heavens whose intercession is of no avail in any way except after Allah grants leave to whomever He wishes and approves" [53:26]. The Exalted also said: يَوْمئِذٍ لَّا تَنفَعُ الشَّفَاعَةُ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمَٰنُ... — "On that day intercession will not avail, except him whom the All-beneficent has permitted and whose word He is pleased with. He knows what is before them and what is behind them" [20:109-110]. Among them are the witnesses (الشهداء) — those who bear witness to the deeds of people — due to the indication of His saying, the Exalted: "excepting such as testify to the truth and they have knowledge" [43:86], regarding their possession of intercession because of their testimony to the truth. Thus, every witness is an intercessor who possesses the right to intercede. However, this testimony (الشهادة), as previously mentioned in Surah al-Fatihah and as will come in His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَاكُمْ أُمَّةً وَسَطًا... — "Thus We have made you a middle nation that you may be witnesses to the people" [2:143], is the testimony of deeds (شهادة الاعمال), not testimony in the sense of being killed in the field of battle. From here it becomes clear that the believers are also among the intercessors, for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, informed of their joining the witnesses on the Day of Resurrection; the Exalted said: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِ أُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الصِّدِّيقُونَ وَالشُّهَدَاءُ... — "Those who have faith in Allah and His apostles—it is they who are the truthful and the witnesses with their Lord" [57:19], as will be explained.

5 - To what does intercession relate?

You have learned that intercession is of two types: ontological (تكوينية), which relates to every ontological cause in the world of causes; and legislative (تشريعية), which relates to reward and punishment. Among the latter is that which relates to the punishment of every sin, from polytheism (الشرك) to what is below it, such as the intercession of repentance and faith before the Day of Resurrection. Among them is that which relates to the consequences of some sins, such as some righteous deeds. As for the disputed intercession—namely the intercession of the Prophets and others on the Day of Resurrection to remove the punishment from one who deserved it according to the reckoning—you have learned in the third point that its subject is the people of major sins (أهل المعاصي الكبيرة) among those who profess the religion of truth and whose religion Allah has pleased.

6 - When does intercession benefit?

We mean by this also the intercession that removes punishment. That which indicates this is the saying of the Glorified: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ... — "Every soul is hostage to what it has earned, except the People of the Right Hand. [They will be] in gardens, questioning concerning the guilty: 'What drew you into Hell?'" [74:38-42]. These verses, as mentioned, indicate the characterization of those who receive intercession and those who are deprived of it. However, they also indicate that intercession only benefits in the release from this hostage-state (الرهانة) and the residence and eternity in the prison of the Fire. As for the terrors and calamities of the Day of Resurrection that precede it, there is no evidence for the occurrence of intercession therein, unless the verse indicates the restriction of intercession to the salvation from the hostage-state of the Fire.

Know that it is possible to infer from these verses that this questioning occurs after the people of Paradise have settled in Paradise and the people of the Fire in the Fire, and that intercession relates to a group of criminals by extracting them from the Fire. This is because of His saying: "in gardens," which indicates settlement, and His saying: "What drew you (سلككم)," for "drawing in" (السلوك) is entry, but not just any entry; rather, it is entry by way of stacking, gathering, and ordering, which carries the meaning of settlement. Likewise, His saying: "will not avail them (فما تنفعهم)," for "ma" (ما) is for the negation of the present state (نفي الحال); so understand that.

As for the realm of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — and what indicates the presence of the Prophet (peace be upon him) and the Imams (peace be upon them) at the time of death and during the questioning in the grave, and their assisting him in hardships—as will come in His saying, the Exalted: وَإِن مِّنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِ... — "There is none among the People of the Book but will surely believe in him before his death" [4:159]—this is not part of intercession before Allah at all. Rather, it is part of the path of disposals (التصرفات) and authority (الحكومة) granted to them by the leave of Allah the Glorified. The Exalted said: وَعَلَى الْأَعْرَافِ رِجَالٌ يَعْرِفُونَ كُلًّا بِسِيمَاهُمْ... — "And on the Elevations will be certain men who recognize each of them by their marks. They will call out to the inhabitants of paradise, 'Peace be to you!' They will not have entered it, though they are eager... And the occupants of the Elevations will call out to certain men whom they recognize by their marks, saying, 'Your gathering did not avail you, nor what you used to wax proud about. Are these the ones of whom you swore that Allah would not extend any mercy to them? Enter paradise! You shall have no fear, nor shall you grieve'" [7:46-49]. Of this kind, from one perspective, is His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ نَدْعُو كُلَّ أُنَاسٍ بِإِمَامِهِمْ... — "The day We shall summon every group of people with their imam, then whoever is given his book in his right hand..." [17:71]. Thus, the mediation of the Imam in the summoning and the giving of the book is of the kind of granted authority; so understand.

In conclusion, what is gathered regarding the matter of intercession is its occurrence at the final station among the stations of the Day of Resurrection, by seeking forgiveness through preventing entry into the Fire, or extracting some who had entered it, through the expansion of mercy or the manifestation of dignity (الكرامة).

(Narrative Discussion)

In Amali al-Saduq: Narrated from al-Husayn ibn Khalid, from al-Rida, from his forefathers, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), who said: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: من لم يؤمن بحوضي فلا أورده الله حوضي... — "Whoever does not believe in my Basin (الحوض), may Allah not bring him to my Basin; and whoever does not believe in my intercession, may Allah not grant him my intercession." Then he (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: إنما شفاعتي لأهل الكبائر من أُمتي... — "My intercession is only for the people of major sins from my community; as for the virtuous among them, there is no way against them." Al-Husayn ibn Khalid said: I said to al-Rida (peace be upon him): "O son of the Messenger of Allah, what then is the meaning of the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ... — 'and they do not intercede except for him with whom He is well-pleased' [21:28]?" He (peace be upon him) replied: لا يشفعون إلا لمن إرتضى الله دينه... — "They do not intercede except for him whose religion Allah has pleased."

I say: His saying (peace be upon him and his progeny): "My intercession is only..."—this meaning has been narrated by both parties [Sunni and Shi'a] through multiple paths from him (peace be upon him and his progeny), and the derivation of its meaning from the verses has already been mentioned.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi: Narrated from Sama'ah ibn Mihran, from Abu Ibrahim (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: عسى أن يبعثك ربك مقاماً محموداً... — "maybe your Lord will raise you to a praiseworthy station" [17:79]. He said: يقوم الناس يوم القيامة مقدار أربعين عاماً... — "The people will stand on the Day of Resurrection for a period of forty years, and the sun will be commanded to ride over the heads of the servants, and sweat will bridle them. The earth will be commanded not to accept anything of their sweat. They will go to Adam and seek intercession from him, but he will direct them to Noah; Noah will direct them to Abraham; Abraham will direct them to Moses; Moses will direct them to Jesus; and Jesus will direct them, saying: 'You must go to Muhammad, the Seal of Humanity.' Then Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny) will say: 'I am for it.' He will proceed until he reaches the gate of Paradise and knocks. It will be said to him: 'Who is this?'—and Allah knows best—and he will say: 'Muhammad.' It will be said: 'Open for him.' When the gate is opened, he will face his Lord and fall down prostrate. He will not raise his head until it is said to him: 'Speak and ask, you shall be given; and intercede, your intercession shall be accepted.' He will raise his head and face his Lord, then fall down prostrate again, and the like will be said to him. He will raise his head until he intercedes even for those who have been burned by the Fire. There will be no one among the people on the Day of Resurrection in all the nations more distinguished (أوجه) than Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny), and that is the saying of Allah the Exalted: 'maybe your Lord will raise you to a praiseworthy station.'"

I say: This meaning is widely transmitted (مستفيض), narrated in brief and in detail through multiple paths from both the general [Sunni] and the specific [Shi'a] sources. In them is an indication that the "praiseworthy station" (المقام المحمود) in the verse is the station of intercession. This does not contradict the fact that others among the Prophets and others are permitted to intercede, for it is possible that their intercession is a branch of his intercession, such that its opening is at his hand (peace be upon him and his progeny).

Also in Tafsir al-Ayyashi: Narrated from one of the two [Imams al-Baqir or al-Sadiq] (peace be upon them) regarding His saying, the Exalted: "maybe your Lord will raise you to a praiseworthy station." He said: هي الشفاعة... — "It is intercession."

Also in Tafsir al-Ayyashi: Narrated from Ubayd ibn Zurarah, who said: Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) was asked about the believer: "Does he have intercession?" He replied: "Yes." A man from the group said to him: "Will the believer need the intercession of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny) on that day?" He replied: نعم إن للمؤمنين خطايا وذنوباً... — "Yes, for the believers have errors and sins, and there is no one but that he will need the intercession of Muhammad on that day." He said: And a man asked him about the saying of the Messenger of Allah: "I am the master of the children of Adam, and no boast." He said: "Yes." He said: يأخذ حلقة باب الجنة فيفتحها... — "He will take the ring of the gate of Paradise and open it, then fall down prostrate. Allah will say: 'Raise your head; intercede, your intercession shall be accepted; ask, you shall be given.' He will raise his head, then fall down prostrate again. Allah will say: 'Raise your head; intercede, your intercession shall be accepted; and ask, you shall be given.' Then he will raise his head and intercede, and his intercession will be accepted, and he will ask and be given."

In Tafsir al-Furat: Narrated from Muhammad ibn al-Qasim ibn Ubayd, through his chain of narrators from Bishr ibn Shurayh al-Basri, who said: I said to Muhammad ibn Ali (peace be upon him): "Which verse in the Book of Allah is the most hopeful (أرجى)?" He said: "What do your people say about it?" I said: "They say: يَا عِبَادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلَىٰ أَنفُسِهِمْ... — 'O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah' [39:53]." He said: "But we, the People of the House (أهل البيت), do not say that." I said: "Then what do you say regarding it?" He said: "We say: وَلَسَوْفَ يُعْطِيكَ رَبُّكَ فَتَرْضَىٰ... — 'And soon your Lord will give you [that with which] you will be pleased' [93:5]; it is intercession, by Allah, intercession, by Allah, intercession."

I say: As for His saying, the Exalted: "maybe your Lord will raise you to a praiseworthy station," the wording of the verse may also support this, in addition to what has been widely transmitted from him (peace be upon him and his progeny) that it is the station of intercession. For His saying, the Exalted: "will raise you" indicates that it is a station he will attain on the Day of Resurrection. The word "praiseworthy" (محموداً) is absolute; it is a praise not restricted by anything, which indicates its occurrence from all people, from the first to the last. Praise (الحمد) is the commendation for a voluntary beautiful act; thus, it contains an indication of the occurrence of an act from him (peace be upon him and his progeny) from which everyone benefits and profits, and thus they praise him for it. For this reason, he (peace be upon him) said in the previous narration of Ubayd ibn Zurarah: "and there is no one but that he will need the intercession of Muhammad on that day." This meaning will be further explained in another plausible way.

As for His saying, the Exalted: "And soon your Lord will give you [that with which] you will be pleased" being the most hopeful verse in the Book of Allah, rather than His saying, the Exalted: "O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair...", although the prohibition of despair is repeatedly mentioned in the Noble Quran, the saying of him (peace be upon him and his progeny) narrating from Abraham (peace be upon him): وَمَن يَقْنَطُ مِن رَّحْمَةِ رَبِّهِ... — "And who despairs of his Lord’s mercy except the astray?" [15:56], and His saying, the Exalted, narrating from Jacob (peace be upon him): إِنَّهُ لَا يَيْأَسُ مِن رَّوْحِ اللَّهِ... — "Indeed no one despairs of Allah’s comfort except the faithless lot" [12:87], both refer to despair and hopelessness regarding ontological mercy (الرحمه التكوينية), as evidenced by the context.

As for His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يَا عِبَادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلَىٰ أَنفُسِهِمْ... — "Say, [that Allah declares,] 'O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah forgives all sins. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. Turn penitently to your Lord...'" [39:53-54] until the end of the verses, although it is a prohibition of despair regarding legislative mercy (الرحمة التشريعية)—by the context of His saying "committed excesses against their own souls," which suggests that the despair in the verse is despair due to sin—and although the Glorified has generalized forgiveness for all sins without exception, He concluded it with the command to repent, submit, and act by following. Thus, the verse indicates that the servant who has committed excesses against himself should not despair of the comfort of Allah as long as he can choose repentance, submission, and righteous action.

In short, this is a restricted mercy that Allah has commanded His servants to cling to, and the hope for a restricted mercy is not like the hope for a general mercy and an absolute giving and pleasing that Allah promised to His Messenger, whom He made a mercy to all the worlds. That promise gladdens the soul of the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) with His saying, the Exalted: "And soon your Lord will give you [that with which] you will be pleased."

To clarify: the verse is in the context of bestowing favor (الامتنان), and it contains a promise specific to the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny), the like of which Allah the Glorified never promised to any of His creation. He did not restrict the giving to anything; it is an absolute giving. Allah has promised something similar to a group of His servants in Paradise, saying: لَهُم مَّا يَشَاءُونَ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ... — "They will have whatever they wish with their Lord" [42:22], and: لَهُم مَّا يَشَاءُونَ فِيهَا وَلَدَيْنَا مَزِيدٌ... — "There they will have whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more" [50:35]. Thus, He informed that they have there what is beyond their wish. Wishing (المشيَّة) relates to everything that occurs to the human mind of felicity and good; thus, there is that which has not occurred to the heart of any mortal, as He said: فَلَا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ مَّا أُخْفِيَ لَهُم... — "No one knows what has been kept in store for them out of the delight of the eyes" [32:17]. If this is the extent of what Allah has given to His servants who believed and did righteous deeds—which is a matter beyond measure, as you have known—then what He gives to His Messenger (peace be upon him and his progeny) in the context of bestowing favor is wider and greater; so understand.

This is the status of His giving, the Exalted. As for the status of the pleasure (رضى) of the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny), it is well known that this pleasure is not the pleasure with what Allah has apportioned, which is a companion to the command of Allah. For Allah is the Absolute Owner and Self-Sufficient, and the servant has nothing but poverty and need; thus, he ought to be pleased with the little and the much that his Lord gives him, and he ought to be pleased with what Allah has decreed for him, whether it pleases or grieves him. If this is so, then the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) is the most knowledgeable and the most active in this; he desires nothing but what Allah desires for him. However, this pleasure, since it is placed in contrast to giving, conveys another meaning, similar to enriching the poor with what he laments the loss of, and pleasing the hungry by satiating him. It is the pleasing through giving without limit. This also is something Allah promised the like of to a group of His servants. He, the most Excellent of speakers, said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ... — "Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds—it is they who are the best of creatures. Their reward, near their Lord, is the Gardens of Eden, with streams running in them, to remain in them forever. Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him. That is for him who fears his Lord" [98:7-8]. This also, due to its position of bestowing favor and specificity, must be something beyond what the believers have and wider than that. The Exalted has said regarding His Messenger: بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ... — "most kind and merciful to the faithful" [9:128]. Thus, He confirmed his kindness. How could the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) be pleased and his soul be at peace while he enjoys the delights of Paradise and strolls in its gardens, while a group of the believers are entangled in the depths of the Blaze, imprisoned under the layers of the Fire, while they acknowledge the Lordship of Allah, the Messengership of His Messenger, and the truth of what he brought? It was only that ignorance overcame them and Satan played with them, so they committed sins without defiance or arrogance. If one of us reviews what has passed of his life and looks at how he fell short in perfection and advancement, he blames himself for his negligence in his striving and seeking; then he considers the ignorance of youth and the lack of experience, and perhaps the fire of his anger is extinguished and the surge of his blame is broken due to a partial mercy that Allah deposited in his primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being. What then do you think of the mercy of the Lord of all the worlds in a station where there is nothing but the ignorance of a weak human, the dignity of the kind and merciful Prophet, and the mercy of the Most Merciful of the merciful? And he has seen what he has seen of the consequence of his affair from the moment the claws of death seized him until the final stations of the Day of Resurrection.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَنفَعُ الشَّفَاعَةُ عِندَهُ إِلَّا لِمَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ... — "Intercession is of no avail with Him, except for those whom He permits" [34:23]. Narrated from Abu al-Abbas al-Mukabbir, who said: A client (مولى) of a woman of Ali ibn al-Husayn, called Abu Ayman, entered and said: "O Abu Ja'far, you deceive the people and say: 'The intercession of Muhammad, the intercession of Muhammad'!" Abu Ja'far [Imam al-Baqir] became angry until his face changed color, then he said: ويحك يا أبا أيمن أغرّك أن عفّ بطنك وفرجك... — "Woe to you, O Abu Ayman! Has it deceived you that your belly and your private parts are chaste? If you were to see the terrors of the Resurrection, you would surely find yourself in need of the intercession of Muhammad. Woe to you! Does he intercede except for those for whom the Fire has become mandatory?" He said: ما من أحد من الأولين والآخرين إلا وهو محتاج إلى شفاعة محمد... — "There is no one from the first to the last but that he is in need of the intercession of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny) on the Day of Resurrection." Then Abu Ja'far said: إن لرسول الله الشفاعة في امته... — "Indeed, the Messenger of Allah has intercession for his community, and we have intercession for our Shi'a, and our Shi'a have intercession for their families." Then he said: وإن المؤمن ليشفع في مثل ربيعة ومضر... — "And indeed, the believer shall intercede for the likes of [the tribes of] Rabi'ah and Mudar; and indeed, the believer shall intercede for his servant and say: 'O Lord, the right of my service! He used to protect me from the heat and the cold.'"

I say: His saying (peace be upon him): "There is no one from the first to the last but that he is in need of the intercession of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny)"—its apparent meaning is that this general intercession is other than the one he mentioned by saying: "Woe to you! Does he intercede except for those for whom the Fire has become mandatory?" A similar meaning has already been mentioned in the narration of al-Ayyashi from Ubayd ibn Zurarah from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him). Regarding this meaning, there are other narrations narrated by both the general and the specific sources. It is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ الشَّفَاعَةَ... — "Those whom they invoke besides Him have no power of intercession, excepting such as testify to the truth and they have knowledge" [43:86], as it implies that the criterion for intercession is testimony (الشهادة). Thus, the witnesses (الشهداء) are the intercessors who possess intercession. It will come, Allah willing, in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَاكُمْ أُمَّةً وَسَطًا... — "Thus We have made you a middle nation that you may be witnesses to the people, and that the Apostle may be a witness to you" [2:143], that the Prophets are witnesses and that Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny) is a witness over them. Thus, he (peace be upon him and his progeny) is the witness of the witnesses; therefore, he is the intercessor of the intercessors. Were it not for the testimony of the witnesses, the foundation of the Resurrection would not stand.

Also in Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Intercession is of no avail with Him, except for those whom He permits." He (peace be upon him) said: لا يشفع أحد من أنبياء الله ورسله... — "None of the Prophets of Allah or His Messengers shall intercede until Allah permits him, except for the Messenger of Allah, for Allah permitted him intercession before the Day of Resurrection. Intercession is for him and for the Imams from his progeny, then after that for the Prophets."

In al-Khisal, narrated from Imam ‘Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Three groups shall intercede with Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and their intercession will be accepted: the Prophets, then the scholars, then the martyrs.'"

I say: It appears that the intended meaning of "the martyrs" (الشهداء) here refers to those slain on the battlefield of combat, as is commonly understood in the parlance of the Imams in the narrations, rather than the "witnesses of deeds" (شهداء الأعمال) as per the Quranic terminology.

In al-Khisal, in the "Hadith of the Four Hundred," he (peace be upon him) said: "We possess the power of intercession, and those who love us possess the power of intercession."

I say: There are numerous narrations regarding the intercession of the Mistress of Women, Fatimah (peace be upon her), the intercession of her progeny other than the Imams, and the intercession of the believers—even the miscarried fetus among them. In the well-known hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): تناكحوا تناسلوا فإني أُباهي بكم الامم يوم القيامة ولو بالسقط يقوم محبنطئاً على باب الجنة فيقال له : أُدخل فيقول : لا حتى يدخل أبواي — "Marry and procreate, for I shall boast of your numbers before the nations on the Day of Resurrection, even by the miscarried fetus who will stand aggrieved at the gate of Paradise. It will be said to him: 'Enter,' and he will reply: 'No, not until my parents enter,'" to the end of the hadith.

In al-Khisal, from Abu ‘Abdillah, from his father, from his grandfather, from ‘Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Paradise has eight gates: a gate through which the Prophets and the truthful (الصديقون) enter; a gate through which the martyrs and the righteous (الصالحون) enter; and five gates through which our Shi‘a and those who love us enter. I shall remain standing upon the Path (الصراط), supplicating and saying: 'My Lord, keep safe my Shi‘a, those who love me, my helpers, and those who took me as a guardian in the worldly abode.' Then a call will come from the depths of the Throne: 'Your supplication has been answered, and your intercession for your Shi‘a has been accepted.' Every man from among my Shi‘a, those who took me as a guardian, helped me, and fought those who showed enmity toward me by deed or word, shall intercede for seventy thousand of his neighbors and relatives. And there is a gate through which the rest of the Muslims enter—those who bear witness that there is no god but Allah and in whose hearts there was not an atom’s weight of hatred toward us, the People of the House (أهل البيت)."

In al-Kafi, from Hafs the Mu’adhin, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) in his letter to his companions, he (peace be upon him) said: "Know that none of Allah’s creation can avail you against Him—neither a proximate angel (ملك مقرب) nor a sent messenger (نبي مرسل), nor anyone besides them. Whoever desires that the intercession of the intercessors (شفاعة الشافعين) should benefit him before Allah, let him beseech Allah to be pleased with him."

In the Tafsir of al-Furat, with its chain of transmission from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "Jabir said to Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): 'May I be your ransom, O son of the Messenger of Allah! Relate to me a hadith concerning your grandmother Fatimah.'" He then cited the hadith mentioning the intercession of Fatimah on the Day of Resurrection until he said: "Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: 'By Allah, none shall remain among the people [at that stage] except the doubter, the disbeliever, or the hypocrite. When they are placed between the ranks, they will cry out as Allah the Exalted said: فَمَا لَنَا مِن شَافِعِينَ (100) وَلَا صَدِيقٍ حَمِيمٍ (101) فَلَوْ أَنَّ لَنَا كَرَّةً فَنَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (102) (100. Now we have no intercessors, 101. nor any sympathetic friend. 102. If only there were another turn for us, we would be among the faithful.)' Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: 'Far from it, far from it! They are denied what they seek; وَلَوْ رُدُّوا لَعَادُوا لِمَا نُهُوا عَنْهُ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكَاذِبُونَ (Were they sent back, they would surely return to what they were forbidden, and they are indeed liars.)'"

I say: His (peace be upon him) reliance upon the saying of the Exalted: فَمَا لَنَا مِن شَافِعِينَ (Now we have no intercessors) indicates an intuitive understanding that these verses signify the actual occurrence of intercession. Those who deny intercession have also relied on this verse to negate it. However, it has become clear from what we previously presented regarding the saying of the Exalted: فَمَا تَنفَعُهُمْ شَفَاعَةُ الشَّافِعِينَ (So the intercession of the intercessors will not avail them) how it indicates the occurrence of intercession in general. For if the intent were mere negation, the proper phrasing would have been: "Now we have no intercessor (شفيع) nor any sympathetic friend." Thus, using the plural form (شافعين) within the context of negation indicates that intercession does occur from a group, but it does not benefit them. Furthermore, the saying of the Exalted: فَلَوْ أَنَّ لَنَا كَرَّةً فَنَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (If only there were another turn for us, we would be among the faithful), coming after the saying: فَمَا لَنَا مِن شَافِعِينَ وَلَا صَدِيقٍ حَمِيمٍ, is framed as a lamentation (التحسر) and a wish (تمنّ) occurring within that lament. It is well known that a wish in the context of lamentation only concerns that which one has lost and that which one grieves over. Thus, the meaning of their saying: "If only there were another turn for us," is: "O, would that we were returned so that we might be among the believers and thereby attain the intercession of the intercessors just as the believers attained it." Therefore, this verse is among those that prove the occurrence of intercession.

In al-Tawhid, from al-Kazim, from his father, from his forefathers, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who said: "My intercession is only for those of my nation who have committed major sins (أهل الكبائر); as for the virtuous (المحسنون), there is no way against them." It was asked: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, how can there be intercession for those who commit major sins when Allah the Exalted says: وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ (and they do not intercede except for him with whom He is pleased), and one who commits a major sin is not one with whom He is pleased?" He (peace be upon him) replied: "There is no believer who commits a sin except that it distresses him and he regrets it. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Regret suffices as repentance (توبة).' And he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Whoever is gladdened by his good deed and distressed by his evil deed is a believer.' Thus, whoever does not regret a sin he commits is not a believer, and intercession is not mandatory for him; he is a wrongdoer (ظالم), and Allah, exalted be His mention, says: مَا لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَلَا شَفِيعٍ يُطَاعُ (The wrongdoers will have no sympathetic friend, nor any intercessor who would be heard.)" It was said to him: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, how can one who does not regret a sin he commits not be a believer?" He replied: "No one commits a major sin among acts of disobedience while knowing he will be punished for it except that he regrets what he committed. Whenever he regrets, he is a repentant one (تائب) deserving of intercession. But whenever he does not regret it, he is persistent (مصر), and the persistent one is not forgiven because he does not believe in the punishment for what he committed. Had he believed in the punishment, he would have regretted it. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'No major sin remains with seeking forgiveness (استغفار), and no minor sin remains with persistence (إصرار).' As for the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ, they do not intercede except for him whose religion (دين) Allah is pleased with. Religion is the affirmation of recompense for good and evil deeds. Thus, whoever’s religion is pleasing will regret the sins he committed due to his knowledge of their consequence on the Day of Resurrection."

I say: His (peace be upon him) saying: "and he was a wrongdoer (ظالماً)," contains a definition of the "wrongdoer" on the Day of Resurrection and an allusion to how the Quran defines him where it says: فَأَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ بَيْنَهُمْ أَن لَّعْنَةُ اللَّهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ (44) الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوجًا وَهُم بِالْآخِرَةِ كَافِرُونَ (45) (44. ...Thereupon a herald will announce among them: ‘May Allah’s curse be on the wrongdoers! 45. Those who bar [others] from the way of Allah and seek to make it crooked, and who disbelieve in the Hereafter.’) This refers to one who does not believe in the Day of Recompense; thus, he does not grieve over missing Allah’s commands, nor is he distressed by violating His prohibitions—either by denying all true knowledge and religious teachings or by belittling them and disregarding the recompense and the religion on the Day of Recompense. In such a case, his verbal profession of it would be a mockery of its command and a denial of it. His (peace be upon him) saying: "then you are a repentant one deserving of intercession," means one who has returned to Allah, possessing a pleasing religion (دين مرضي) and thus deserving of intercession. As for "repentance" (التوبة) in its technical sense, it is itself an intercessor and a means of salvation. His (peace be upon him) saying: "The Prophet said: 'No major sin remains with seeking forgiveness...'"—his reliance on this is because persistence (الإصرار), which is the lack of aversion to the sin and the absence of regret for it, removes the sin from its normal status to another status, which is the denial of the Return (المعاد) and wrongdoing (الظلم) against the signs of Allah. Thus, it is not forgiven, for a sin is only forgiven either through repentance or through intercession, both of which are contingent upon a pleasing religion; and in that case, there is neither repentance nor a pleasing religion.

A similar meaning occurs in a narration in al-‘Ilal from Abu Ishaq al-Laythi, who said: "I said to Abu Ja‘far Muhammad ibn ‘Ali al-Baqir (peace be upon him): 'O son of the Messenger of Allah, inform me about the insightful believer (المؤمن المستبصر): when he reaches the peak of knowledge and becomes perfect, does he commit adultery?' He said: 'By Allah, no.' I said: 'Does he commit sodomy?' He said: 'By Allah, no.' I said: 'Does he steal?' He said: 'No.' I said: 'Does he drink wine?' He said: 'No.' I said: 'Does he commit any of these major sins or any of these indecencies?' He said: 'No.' I said: 'Does he commit any sin at all?' He said: 'Yes, and he is a sinning believer who is a Muslim.' I said: 'What is the meaning of Muslim?' He said: 'A Muslim is one who does not let the sin cling to him nor persists in it,'" to the end of the hadith.

In al-Khisal, with chains of transmission from al-Rida, from his forefathers (peace be upon them), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'When the Day of Resurrection comes, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, will manifest Himself to His believing servant and confront him with his sins, one by one. Then Allah will forgive him; Allah will not let a proximate angel or a sent messenger see them, and He will conceal them so that no one discovers them. Then He will say to his evil deeds: "Become good deeds."'"

From Sahih Muslim, attributed to Abu Dharr, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'A man will be brought on the Day of Resurrection and it will be said: "Present his minor sins to him and set aside his major ones." It will be said: "You did such and such on such and such a day," and he will confess, not denying it, while he is terrified of the major sins. Then it will be said: "Give him a good deed in place of every evil deed." He will then say: "Indeed, I have sins that I do not see here."' He [Abu Dharr] said: 'I saw the Messenger of Allah laugh until his premolars were visible.'"

In al-Amali, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "When the Day of Resurrection comes, Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, will spread His mercy such that even Iblis will crave His mercy."

I say: The last three narrations are among the general reports (المطلقات). The reports indicating the occurrence of the intercession of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) on the Day of Resurrection—from the paths of the Imams of the People of the House as well as from the paths of the Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama‘ah—reach the level of mass-transmission (التواتر). Taken as a whole, they indicate a single meaning: intercession for the sinners among the people of faith, either by preventing them from entering the Fire or by taking them out of it after they have entered. What is certain from them is that the sinners among the people of faith will not abide eternally in the Fire. You have already seen that the Quran also does not indicate more than that.

(Philosophical Discussion)

Although rational proofs (البراهين العقلية) fall short of providing the specific details mentioned in the Book and the Sunnah regarding the Return (المعاد)—due to their inability to grasp the intermediate premises (المقدمات المتوسطة) required for such deduction, as noted by Shaykh Ibn Sina—they do grasp the intellectual (العقلية) and imaginal (المثالية) perfections that man will face on the paths of felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة) after the soul separates from the body, based on the rational and imaginal immateriality (التجرد) for which proof has been established.

In his initial state, every action a human performs produces a psychic disposition (هيئة نفسانية) and a state (حال) of felicity or wretchedness. By felicity, we mean what is good for him insofar as he is human, and by wretchedness, its opposite. Through repetition, these states become a deeply rooted habit (ملكة), from which an "image" (صورة)—either felicitous or wretched—is formed for the soul, serving as the principle for further psychic dispositions and images. If it is felicitous, its effects are existential (وجودية) and compatible with the new image and with the soul, which acts as the receptive matter (المادة القابلة) for it. If it is wretched, its effects are privative (عدمية) matters that, upon analysis, trace back to loss and evil. The felicitous soul finds pleasure in its effects insofar as it is human, and insofar as it is an actually felicitous human. As for the wretched soul, although its effects are familiar and compatible with it insofar as it is their principle, it suffers pain through them insofar as it is human. This applies to souls that are complete in their felicity or wretchedness—meaning the human who is felicitous in essence (ذاتاً) and righteous in action, and the human who is wretched in essence and wicked in action.

As for those deficient in their felicity or wretchedness—meaning the human who is felicitous in essence but wretched in action—this refers to one whose essence possesses a felicitous image through true and firm belief, yet his soul contains wretched and vile dispositions from sins and offenses acquired during its attachment to the worldly body and its nursing from the breast of choice. These are forced (قسرية) matters, incompatible with his essence. Proof has been established that what is forced does not endure (القسر لا يدوم). Thus, this soul will be granted purification from them in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — or on the Day of Resurrection, depending on the strength of their rooting in the soul. The same applies to the accidental felicitous dispositions of a wretched soul; they will be stripped away and vanish, whether quickly or slowly. As for the soul whose actuality (فعلية) of felicity or wretchedness was not completed in the worldly life such that it departed the body in a state of weakness and deficiency (مستضعفة ناقصة), it is among those deferred to the command of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. This is what rational proofs necessitate regarding recompense through reward and punishment, necessitating that they be among the concomitants and results of actions; for the conventional and relative (الوضعية الإعتبارية) links must ultimately trace back in the Hereafter to real, existential (حقيقية وجودية) links.

Furthermore, proofs are established that existential perfection (الكمال الوجودي) varies according to the levels of perfection, deficiency, intensity, and weakness—this is systematic ambiguity (التشكيك), particularly in immaterial light. Thus, these souls have different ranks in proximity to and distance from the Principle of perfection and its ultimate end in their upward journey and return to that from which they began; they are ranked one above the other. This is the nature of efficient causes (العلل الفاعلية) — in the sense of "that by which" (ما به) — and the intermediaries of grace (وسائط الفيض). Some souls—namely the complete and perfect souls like those of the Prophets (peace be upon them), especially those at the highest degrees of perfection and actuality—act as intermediaries in the removal of forced, wretched, and vile dispositions from the souls of the weak and those below them among the felicitous when such dispositions have forcibly clung to them. This is the intercession (الشفاعة) specific to those who have committed sins.

(Social Discussion)

The principles of sociology dictate that human society cannot preserve its life or sustain its existence except through established and recognized laws (قوانين موضوعة معتبرة) that oversee its condition and govern the actions and affairs of individuals. These laws arise from the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — of society and the instinct of the individuals within it, according to existing conditions. Guided by these laws, all social classes move forward, each according to what suits its status and fits its position. Through this, society moves at a swift pace, and from the harmony of its parts and the interaction of its diverse elements, social justice (العدل الاجتماعي) is born. These laws are established for material interests and benefits required for the advancement of material society, as well as for spiritual perfections like noble and virtuous ethics—such as truthfulness in speech, fulfillment of covenants, sincerity, and so on—to which social well-being calls.

Since laws and rulings are conventional (وضعية) rather than ontological (حقيقية), they require the completion of their effect through the establishment of other prescribed rulings regarding recompense (المجازاة), to serve as a safeguard for their sanctity against the transgression of impulsive individuals and the negligence of others.
