Therefore, whenever a government—any government—is strong in enforcing the regulations of recompense, society does not falter in its progress, nor does the traveler stray from his path and goal. But whenever it weakens, chaos and disorder (الهرج والمرج) intensify within it, and it deviates from its course. Among the necessary teachings to be solidified in society is the indoctrination of the matter of recompense and the creation of belief in it within the souls of individuals. It is mandatory to guard against the entry of hope into their souls for escaping the judgment of recompense and the consequences of opposition and disobedience through intercession, bribery, or any of the destructive ruses and intrigues.

For this reason, some have criticized the Christian religion for what occurred within it—that Christ ransomed the people for their sins through his crucifixion. Thus, people rely on him to save them from the hand of judgment on the Day of Resurrection. In such a case, religion would be a destroyer of humanity, a delayer of civilization, and a force that drives man backward, as has been said. Statistics are cited to show that religious people are more prone to lying and further from justice than others, and this is only because they rely on the truth of their religion and the storage of intercession for their benefit on the Day of Resurrection; thus, they do not care what they do. This is in contrast to others, who were left to their instincts and primordial natures, the judgment of which was not nullified by what nullified it in the religious. Thus, their nature judged the ugliness of deviating from what contradicts the judgment of humanity and virtuous civilization.

Based on this, a group of researchers has resorted to interpreting what has been narrated specifically regarding intercession in Islam, which the Book has spoken of and the Sunnah has mass-transmitted.

By my life, neither does Islam establish intercession in the sense they have interpreted, nor does the intercession it establishes produce the effect they have imagined for it. It is mandatory for the researcher in religious knowledge—and in applying what Islam has legislated to the structure of a sound society and virtuous civilization—to grasp the entirety of what Islam intended of principles and laws applicable to society, and the manner of that application. Then he should grasp what the promised intercession is and what its place and position are among the teachings it brought.

He will know, firstly: that the intercession the Quran establishes is that the believers will not abide eternally in the Fire on the Day of Resurrection, provided they meet their Lord with a pleasing faith (الإيمان المرضي) and the true religion (الدين الحق). Thus, it is a promise the Quran has made conditionally. Then it stated that faith, in terms of its persistence, is in grave danger due to sins, especially major ones, and particularly addiction to them and persistence in them; for that is the brink of a precipice of permanent destruction. Through this, the hope of salvation and the fear of destruction are attained, and the soul of the believer moves between fear and hope (الخوف والرجاء). Thus, he worships his Lord out of desire and awe, and moves through his life in a balanced course, deviating neither toward the lethargy of despair nor the laziness of overconfidence.

Secondly: Islam has established social laws—both material and spiritual—that encompass all individual and social movements and stillness. Then, for every article of its laws, it considered the appropriate consequence and recompense, ranging from blood money (دية), prescribed punishments (حد), and discretionary punishments (تعزير), until it reaches the deprivation of social privileges, blame, censure, and condemnation. Then it safeguarded this after empowering the government of the guardians of the affair (أولياء الأمر) by giving everyone authority over everyone through "enjoining the good and forbidding the evil" (الأمر بالمعروف والنهي عن المنكر). Then it brought this to life by breathing into it the spirit of religious call, which includes warning and glad tidings of punishment and reward in the Hereafter. It built the foundation of its education by indoctrinating the knowledge of the Beginning and the Return (المبدء والمعاد) upon this order.

This is what Islam aims for in its teaching; the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) brought it, and the experiments that occurred in his era and the era of those who followed him confirmed it, until the hands of the rulers in the Umayyad dynasty and those who followed them toyed with it in their despotism and their playing with the rulings of religion and their nullification of religious limits and policies. This continued until matters reached what they have reached today: the banners of "freedom" were raised, Western civilization appeared, and nothing remained of religion among Muslims except like the dregs in a vessel. This manifest weakness in religious policy and the regression of Muslims is the cause of their decline in virtues and excellences, their degradation in noble ethics and manners, their immersion in amusements and desires, and their plunging into indecencies and abominations. It is this that drove them to violate every sanctity and commit everything that even those not affiliated with religion find repulsive—not what the objector imagines of attributing corruption to certain religious teachings that have no goal or content except the felicity of man in his future and his present. And Allah is the Helper. The statistics they mentioned were only conducted on a collective of "religious" people who have no guardian or strong protector, and on a collective of those not affiliated with religion whose social education and training act as guardians and protectors of their social well-being. Thus, such statistics do not prove anything regarding what he intended.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ نَجَّيْنَـٰكُم مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ يُذَبِّحُونَ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ وَيَسْتَحْيُونَ نِسَآءَكُمْ ۚ وَفِى ذَٰلِكُم بَلَآءٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ (٤٩)
[Remember] when We delivered you from Pharaoh’s clan who inflicted a terrible torment on you, and slaughtered your sons and spared your women, and in that there was a great test from your Lord. (49)
وَإِذْ فَرَقْنَا بِكُمُ ٱلْبَحْرَ فَأَنجَيْنَـٰكُمْ وَأَغْرَقْنَآ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَأَنتُمْ تَنظُرُونَ (٥٠)
And when We parted the sea with you, and We delivered you and drowned Pharaoh’s clan as you looked on. (50)
وَإِذْ وَٰعَدْنَا مُوسَىٰٓ أَرْبَعِينَ لَيْلَةً ثُمَّ ٱتَّخَذْتُمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ وَأَنتُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ (٥١)
And when We made an appointment with Moses for forty nights, you took up the Calf [for worship] in his absence, and you were wrongdoers. (51)
ثُمَّ عَفَوْنَا عَنكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٥٢)
Then We excused you after that so that you might give thanks. (52)
وَإِذْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْفُرْقَانَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ (٥٣)
And when We gave Moses the Book and the Criterion so that you might be guided. (53)
وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنَّكُمْ ظَلَمْتُمْ أَنفُسَكُم بِٱتِّخَاذِكُمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ فَتُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ بَارِئِكُمْ فَٱقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ عِندَ بَارِئِكُمْ فَتَابَ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٥٤)
And [recall] when Moses said to his people, ‘O my people! You have indeed wronged yourselves by taking up the Calf [for worship]. Now turn penitently to your Maker, and slay [the guilty among] your folks. That will be better for you with your Maker.’ Then He turned to you clemently. Indeed, He is the All-clement, the All-merciful. (54)
وَإِذْ قُلْتُمْ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ لَن نُّؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّىٰ نَرَى ٱللَّهَ جَهْرَةً فَأَخَذَتْكُمُ ٱلصَّـٰعِقَةُ وَأَنتُمْ تَنظُرُونَ (٥٥)
And when you said, ‘O Moses, we will not believe you until we see Allah visibly.’ Thereupon a thunderbolt seized you as you looked on. (55)
ثُمَّ بَعَثْنَـٰكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَوْتِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٥٦)
Then We raised you up after your death so that you might give thanks. (56)
وَظَلَّلْنَا عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْغَمَامَ وَأَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَنَّ وَٱلسَّلْوَىٰ ۖ كُلُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ ۖ وَمَا ظَلَمُونَا وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (٥٧)
We shaded you with clouds, and sent down to you manna and quails [saying]: ‘Eat of the good things We have provided for you.’ And they did not wrong Us, but they used to wrong [only] themselves. (57)
وَإِذْ قُلْنَا ٱدْخُلُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةَ فَكُلُوا۟ مِنْهَا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمْ رَغَدًا وَٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْبَابَ سُجَّدًا وَقُولُوا۟ حِطَّةٌ نَّغْفِرْ لَكُمْ خَطَـٰيَـٰكُمْ ۚ وَسَنَزِيدُ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٥٨)
And when We said, ‘Enter this town, and eat thereof freely whencesoever you wish, and enter while prostrating at the gate, and say, ‘‘Relieve [us of the burden of our sins],’’ so that We may forgive your iniquities and We will soon enhance the virtuous.’ (58)
فَبَدَّلَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ قَوْلًا غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى قِيلَ لَهُمْ فَأَنزَلْنَا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ رِجْزًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ (٥٩)
But the wrongdoers changed the saying with other than what they were told. So We sent down on those who were wrongdoers a plague from the sky because of the transgressions they used to commit. (59)
۞ وَإِذِ ٱسْتَسْقَىٰ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦ فَقُلْنَا ٱضْرِب بِّعَصَاكَ ٱلْحَجَرَ ۖ فَٱنفَجَرَتْ مِنْهُ ٱثْنَتَا عَشْرَةَ عَيْنًا ۖ قَدْ عَلِمَ كُلُّ أُنَاسٍ مَّشْرَبَهُمْ ۖ كُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ مِن رِّزْقِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ (٦٠)
And when Moses prayed for water for his people, We said, ‘Strike the rock with your staff.’ Thereat twelve fountains gushed forth from it; every tribe came to know its drinking-place. ‘Eat and drink of Allah’s provision, and do not act wickedly on the earth, causing corruption.’ (60)
وَإِذْ قُلْتُمْ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ لَن نَّصْبِرَ عَلَىٰ طَعَامٍ وَٰحِدٍ فَٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُخْرِجْ لَنَا مِمَّا تُنۢبِتُ ٱلْأَرْضُ مِنۢ بَقْلِهَا وَقِثَّآئِهَا وَفُومِهَا وَعَدَسِهَا وَبَصَلِهَا ۖ قَالَ أَتَسْتَبْدِلُونَ ٱلَّذِى هُوَ أَدْنَىٰ بِٱلَّذِى هُوَ خَيْرٌ ۚ ٱهْبِطُوا۟ مِصْرًا فَإِنَّ لَكُم مَّا سَأَلْتُمْ ۗ وَضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلذِّلَّةُ وَٱلْمَسْكَنَةُ وَبَآءُو بِغَضَبٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا عَصَوا۟ وَّكَانُوا۟ يَعْتَدُونَ (٦١)
And when you said, ‘O Moses, ‘We will not put up with one kind of food. So invoke your Lord for us, so that He may bring forth for us of that which the earth grows—its greens and cucumbers, its garlic, lentils, and onions.’ He said, ‘Do you seek to replace what is superior with that which is inferior? Go down to any town and you will indeed get what you ask for!’ So they were struck with abasement and poverty, and they earned Allah’s wrath. That, because they would deny the signs of Allah and kill the prophets unjustly. That, because they would disobey and commit transgressions. (61)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: and let your women live (ويستحيون نساءكم), meaning they left them alive for service rather than killing them as they did the sons. Thus, istihya’ (الإستحياء) is the seeking of life (الحيوة). It is also possible that the meaning is that they committed acts of indecency that caused the loss of their modesty (حياء). The meaning of yasumunakum (يسومونكم) is that they inflicted or imposed upon you.

His saying, the Exalted: And when We parted the sea for you (إذ فرقنا بكم البحر). Parting (الفرق) is the opposite of joining, similar to separation (الفصل) and connection (الوصل). Parting in the sea refers to splitting it. The letter ba (ب) denotes causality (السببية) or accompaniment (الملابسة); that is, "We parted the sea because of your deliverance" or "due to your accompaniment in entering the sea."

His saying, the Exalted: And when We appointed for Moses forty nights (واعدنا موسى اربعين ليلة). The Exalted narrated this story in Surah al-A‘raf by His saying: وَوَاعَدْنَا مُوسَىٰ ثَلَاثِينَ لَيْلَةً وَأَتْمَمْنَاهَا بِعَشْرٍ فَتَمَّ مِيقَاتُ رَبِّهِ أَرْبَعِينَ لَيْلَةً — We appointed for Moses thirty nights, and completed them with ten [more]; so the appointment of his Lord was completed in forty nights. (7:142). The appointment was mentioned as forty nights here either by way of predominance (التغليب) or because the final ten nights were a separate appointment. Thus, the forty is the sum of the two appointments, as mentioned in the narrations.

His saying, the Exalted: so turn in repentance to your Creator (فتوبوا إلى بارئكم). The Creator (الباريء) is one of the Most Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى), as the Exalted said: هُوَ اللَّهُ الْخَالِقُ الْبَارِئُ الْمُصَوِّرُ لَهُ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ — He is Allah, the Creator, the Maker, the Formed; to Him belong the Best Names. (59:24). It occurs in three places in His speech: two of them are in this verse. Perhaps this Name was specifically chosen here among other suitable names because its meaning is close to "the Creator" and "the Originator," derived from bara’a (برء), which means to separate (فصل), because He separates creation from non-existence or man from the earth. It is as if the Exalted is saying: "This repentance and your killing of yourselves—though it be the most difficult of commands—is commanded by Allah, who brought you into being (برئكم). He who loved your existence, which is good for you, now loves for death to befall you [as a purification], so it is good for you. How could He not love what is good for you when He is the one who created you (برئكم)?" Thus, the choice of the term "the Creator" (الباريء) and its annexation to them in His saying "to your Creator" (إلى بارئكم) and "with your Creator" (عند بارئكم) is to signal a special relationship (الاختصاص) to stir feelings of love.

His saying, the Exalted: That is better for you with your Creator (ذلكم خير لكم عند بارئكم). The apparent sense of the verse and what preceded it is that these addresses—and the enumeration of their various transgressions and sins—are attributed to the whole group despite being committed by only some of them. This is because they were a unified community with a single national identity (قومية), where some were pleased with the actions of others. Thus, the actions of some are attributed to the rest due to the unity (الوحدة) existing among them. Not all of the Children of Israel worshipped the calf, nor did all of them kill the prophets, among other sins. Based on this, His saying, the Exalted, and slay yourselves (واقتلوا أنفسكم) means the killing of some of them—namely those who worshipped the calf—as indicated by His saying: Indeed you have wronged yourselves by taking up the calf (إنكم ظلمتم أنفسكم باتخاذكم العجل). His saying, That is better for you with your Creator (ذلكم خير لكم عند بارئكم) is the completion of the account of Moses' speech, as is apparent. His saying, so He turned to you clemently (فتاب عليكم) indicates the descent of repentance and its acceptance. Narrations state that repentance was granted before all the criminals among them were killed.

From this, it becomes clear that the command was a trial (إمتحاني), similar to what occurred in the story of Abraham's (عليه السلام) vision and the sacrifice of Ishmael: يَا إِبْرَاهِيمُ قَدْ صَدَّقْتَ الرُّؤْيَا — ‘O Abraham! You have indeed fulfilled the vision!’ (37:105). Moses (عليه السلام) mentioned: so turn in repentance to your Creator and slay yourselves; that is better for you with your Creator, and Allah, the Glorified, ratified his (عليه السلام) statement, considering the killing of some as the killing of all, and sent down repentance by His saying: so He turned to you clemently (فتاب عليكم).

His saying, the Exalted: a plague from the sky (رجزاً من السماء). Rijz (الرجز) is punishment (العذاب).

His saying, the Exalted: and do not act wickedly (ولا تعثوا). ‘Ayth (العيث) and ‘atha (العثى) refer to the most severe form of corruption (الفساد).

His saying, the Exalted: its cucumbers, its garlic (وقثائها وفومها). Qiththa’ (القثاء) is cucumber (الخيار) and fum (الفوم) is garlic (الثوم) or wheat (الحنطة).

His saying, the Exalted: and they became deserving of the wrath (وبائوا بغضب), meaning they returned (رجعوا).

His saying, the Exalted: That was because they used to deny (ذلك بأنهم كانوا يكفرون) is the causal explanation (تعليل) for what preceded it.

His saying, the Exalted: That was because they were rebellious (ذلك بما عصوا) is a causal explanation for the previous explanation. Thus, their rebellion and their persistence in transgression (الاعتداء) were the causes of their disbelief in the signs and their killing of the prophets, as the Exalted said: ثُمَّ كَانَ عَاقِبَةَ الَّذِينَ أَسَاءُوا السُّوأَىٰ أَن كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ وَكَانُوا بِهَا يَسْتَهْزِئُونَ — Then the fate of those who committed misdeeds was the worst [fate], for they denied the signs of Allah and used to deride them. (30:10). Regarding the causal explanation through rebellion (المعصية), there is a perspective that will be discussed in the upcoming section.

(Narrative Discourse)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "And when We appointed for Moses forty nights," it is narrated from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) that he said: "It was in the [divine] knowledge and decree thirty nights, then a change in the creative decree (بدا) occurred and He added ten; thus the first and the last appointment of his Lord was completed as forty nights."

I say: This narration supports what was mentioned earlier—that the forty is the sum of two appointments.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, narrated from ‘Ali (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: And when Moses said to his people, ‘O my people! You have indeed wronged yourselves...’ the verse. He said: "They said to Moses: 'What is our repentance?' He said: 'Some of you shall kill others.' So they took knives, and a man would kill his brother, his father, and his son, and by Allah, he did not care whom he killed, until seventy thousand of them were killed. Then Allah revealed to Moses: 'Command them to stay their hands; He has forgiven those who were killed and accepted the repentance of those who remain.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi: He (عليه السلام) said: "When Moses went out to the appointed time and returned to his people while they had worshipped the calf, Moses said to them: 'O my people! You have indeed wronged yourselves by taking up the calf, so turn in repentance to your Creator and slay yourselves; that is better for you with your Creator.' They said to him: 'How shall we kill ourselves?' Moses said to them: 'Go each one of you to the Holy House (بيت المقدس) with a knife, a piece of iron, or a sword. When I ascend the pulpit of the Children of Israel, you must be masked so that no one recognizes his companion, and kill one another.' So seventy thousand men from those who had worshipped the calf gathered at the Holy House. When Moses had prayed with them and ascended the pulpit, they began killing one another until Gabriel descended and said: 'Tell them, O Moses, to stop the killing, for Allah has accepted their repentance.' Ten thousand of them had been killed, and Allah sent down: that is better for you with your Creator, so He turned to you clemently; indeed He is the All-clement, the All-merciful."

I say: This narration, as you see, indicates that His saying, the Exalted, That is better for you with your Creator was spoken by Moses and also spoken by Him, the Glorified. Thus, it was a ratification of a word spoken by Moses and a disclosure that it was complete, contrary to what might appear from the literal sense that it was incomplete. For the literal sense suggests that Moses made the killing of everyone a "good" for them with their Creator, yet only some were killed rather than all. Thus, the Glorified made the killing that actually occurred the "good" that Moses (عليه السلام) had mentioned, as previously explained.

Also in Tafsir al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: And We shaded you with clouds... the verse. It is narrated that when Moses crossed the sea with the Children of Israel, they descended into a wilderness (مفازة). They said: "O Moses, you have destroyed us, killed us, and brought us out from inhabited land to a wilderness where there is no shade, no trees, and no water." So a cloud would come during the day to shade them from the sun, and manna (المنّ) would descend upon them at night, falling on the plants, trees, and stones for them to eat. In the evening, a roasted bird would come to them and land on their tables; when they had eaten and drunk, it would fly away. Moses had a stone with him which he would place in the middle of the camp and strike with his staff, and twelve springs would gush forth from it, as Allah narrated, going to each tribe in its quarters—and they were twelve tribes.

In al-Kafi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: They did not wrong Us, but they used to wrong [only] themselves, narrated from Abu al-Hasan al-Madi (عليه السلام) who said: "Indeed, Allah is too Mighty and too Protected to be wronged or to attribute wrong to Himself. However, He has joined us with Himself (خلطنا بنفسه), making the wrong done to us as wrong done to Him, and our guardianship (ولايتنا) as His guardianship. Then Allah sent down the Quran regarding this to His Prophet, saying: They did not wrong Us, but they used to wrong [only] themselves." The narrator said: "I asked: 'Is this a revelation (تنزيل)?'" He replied: "Yes."

I say: A similar narration is also reported from al-Baqir (عليه السلام). His (عليه السلام) saying "too Protected to be wronged" (أمنع من أن يظلم)—in the passive voice—is an explanation of His saying, the Exalted: They did not wrong Us. His saying "or to attribute wrong to Himself" (أو ينسب نفسه إلى الظلم)—in the active voice—and his saying "but He joined us with Himself" means He joined us—the assembly of Prophets, Successors, and Imams—with Himself. His saying "I asked: 'Is this a revelation?' He replied: 'Yes'" is explained by the fact that negation in such instances is only valid where affirmation is possible or where its possibility might be imagined. One does not say of a wall that it "does not see" or "does not wrong" except for a specific rhetorical point (نكتة). He, the Glorified, is too exalted for His speech to allow the delusion that He could be wronged or that it could occur to Him. Thus, the rhetorical point in this negation is the aforementioned "joining," because great figures often speak on behalf of their servants and assistants.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: That was because they used to deny the signs of Allah... the verse. Narrated from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) that he read this verse: That was because they used to deny the signs of Allah and kill the prophets unjustly; that was because they were rebellious and used to transgress. He said: "By Allah, they did not strike them with their hands nor kill them with their swords, but they heard their secrets and broadcasted them (أذاعوها), so they were seized because of that and killed. Thus, it was killing, transgression, and a calamity."

I say: A similar narration is in al-Kafi from him (عليه السلام). It is as if he (عليه السلام) derived this from His saying, the Exalted: that was because they were rebellious and used to transgress, for killing—especially the killing of prophets—and disbelief in the signs of Allah are not typically explained by mere "rebellion" (عصيان); rather, the matter is the reverse due to the severity and gravity of those acts. However, when rebellion is understood as the failure to maintain secrecy and caution, such a causal explanation becomes valid.

Ayah

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ وَٱلصَّـٰبِـِٔينَ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٦٢)
Indeed the faithful, the Jews, the Christians and the Sabaeans—those of them who have faith in Allah and the Last Day and act righteously—they shall have their reward from their Lord, and they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (62)

(Commentary)

The repetition of belief (الإيمان) a second time—which refers to being characterized by its true reality, as suggested by the context—indicates that the intended meaning of "those who believe" (الذين آمنوا) at the beginning of the verse refers to those who are characterized by belief outwardly (ظاهراً) and are identified by this name. Thus, the sum of the meaning is that mere names and being titled with them—such as "the believers" (المؤمنون), "the Jews" (اليهود), "the Christians" (النصارى), and "the Sabaeans" (الصابئون)—do not necessitate a reward from Allah the Exalted, nor do they grant security from punishment. This is contrary to their claim: "None shall enter Paradise unless he be a Jew or a Christian." Rather, the crux of the matter and the cause of honor and felicity is the reality of belief (حقيقة الإيمان) in Allah and the Last Day, and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح).

For this reason, He did not say "whoever of them believes" by using a pronoun referring back to the relative pronoun (الموصول) as is required in a relative clause (الصلة), lest it be an affirmation of some benefit in the mere naming, as is evident from the literary structure (النظم). This is among the themes repeated in the verses of the Quran: that felicity and honor revolve around servitude (العبودية) — the state of absolute submission and devotion to Allah. No name among these names benefits its bearer in the least, nor does any attribute of perfection remain with its possessor or save him, except through the adherence to servitude. The Prophets and those below them are equal in this regard. Indeed, the Exalted said regarding His Prophets, after describing them with every beautiful attribute: وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ "Had they ascribed partners [to Allah], what they used to do would have come to naught." (Al-An'am: 88).

The Exalted also said regarding the companions of His Prophet and those who believed with him, despite mentioning their great status and high rank: وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ مِنْهُم مَّغْفِرَةً وَأَجْرًا عَظِيمًا "Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward." (Al-Fath: 29). Thus, He included the word "of them" (منهم). Regarding others who were given the signs of Allah the Exalted, He said: وَلَوْ شِئْنَا لَرَفَعْنَاهُ بِهَا وَلَٰكِنَّهُ أَخْلَدَ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ وَاتَّبَعَ هَوَاهُ "Had We wished, We would have surely raised him by their means, but he clung to the earth and followed his [low] desires." (Al-A'raf: 176). There are many other verses explicitly stating that honor lies in the reality (الحقيقة) and not the outward appearance (الظاهر).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Narrated from Salman al-Farsi, who said: "I asked the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم about the people of a religion I was with; I mentioned their prayer and worship, and then it was revealed: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هَادُوا 'Indeed the faithful, the Jews...'" (the verse).

I say: The revelation of this verse concerning the companions of Salman has also been narrated through several other chains.

In al-Ma'ani: From Ibn Faddal, who said: "I said to al-Rida عليه‌السلام: 'Why were the Christians (النصارى) named Nasara?' He replied: 'Because they were from a village called Nazareth (ناصره) in the land of Syria, where Mary and Jesus settled after their return from Egypt.'"

I say: There is a discussion regarding this narration that we shall address in the stories of Jesus عليه‌السلام in Surah Al 'Imran, Allah willing.

It is also narrated that the Jews (اليهود) were named Yahud because they are from the descendants of Judah (يهودا), the son of Jacob.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: He said: He عليه‌السلام said: "The Sabaeans (الصابئون) are a people who are neither Magians, nor Jews, nor Christians, nor Muslims; they worship the stars and the planets."

I say: This is a form of paganism (الوثنية), except that idol worship is not limited to them; rather, what distinguishes them is the worship of idols representing the planets.

(Historical Discourse)

Abu Rayhan al-Biruni mentioned in al-Athar al-Baqiyah the following text: "The first of those mentioned among them—meaning the false prophets—is Budhasaf (يوذاسف). He appeared after one year of the reign of Tahmuras in the land of India and brought the Persian script. He called to the creed of the Sabaeans, and a great multitude followed him. The Pishdadian kings and some of the Kayanids who inhabited Balkh used to venerate the two luminaries (the sun and moon), the planets, and the elemental wholes (كليات العناصر), sanctifying them until the appearance of Zoroaster after thirty years of the reign of Vishtaspa. The remnants of those Sabaeans are in Harran, attributed to their location, so they are called the Harranians. It has been said that the name is attributed to Hadan bin Tarakh, the brother of Abraham عليه‌السلام, and that he was among their leaders, the most deeply involved in the religion and the most steadfast in adhering to it.

Ibn Sankala the Christian recounted from them in his book—in which he intended to refute their sect and thus filled it with lies and falsehoods—that they say Abraham عليه‌السلام only left their community because he developed leprosy on his foreskin, and whoever had that was considered impure and they would not associate with him. So he cut off his foreskin for that reason—meaning he was circumcised—and entered one of the houses of the idols. He heard a voice from an idol saying to him: 'O Abraham, you left us with one defect and came back to us with two; leave and do not return to us.' Rage drove him to break them into pieces, and he left their community. Then he regretted what he had done and intended to sacrifice his son to the planet Jupiter, according to their custom of sacrificing their children—so he claimed. When the planet Jupiter recognized the sincerity of his repentance, it ransomed him with a ram.

Abd al-Masih ibn Ishaq al-Kindi recounted about them in his response to the letter of Abd Allah ibn Isma'il al-Hashimi, that they are known for sacrificing humans, but that is not possible for them today openly. We only know of them that they are a people who profess the oneness of Allah (يوحدون الله) and declare Him transcendent (ينزهونه) above all foul things. They describe Him through negation (السلب) rather than affirmation (الايجاب), such as saying: 'He is not limited, He is not seen, He does not wrong, and He does not act unjustly.' They call Him by the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) metaphorically, as they believe He has no real attributes (صفة بالحقيقة). They attribute the governance (التدبير) of the world to the celestial sphere and its bodies, and they believe in their life, speech, hearing, and sight. They venerate the lights. Among their remnants is the dome above the mihrab near the maqsura of the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus; it was their place of prayer. The Greeks and Romans were upon their religion, then it fell into the hands of the Jews, who made it their synagogue, then the Christians overcame them and turned it into a church until Islam and its people came and took it as a mosque. They had temples and idols named after the sun with known shapes, as mentioned by Abu Ma'shar al-Balkhi in his book on houses of worship, such as the temple of Baalbek which belonged to the idol of the sun, and Qarran, which is attributed to the moon, its construction being in the shape of a taylasan (a hooded cloak). Near it is a village called Salamsin, its ancient name being Sanam Sin, meaning the idol of the moon, and another village called Tar' 'Uz, meaning the gate of Venus. They mention that the Kaaba and its idols belonged to them, and its worshippers were among them; that al-Lat was in the name of Saturn, and al-'Uzza in the name of Venus. They have many prophets, most of whom were Greek philosophers like Hermes the Egyptian, Agathodaimon, Walis, Pythagoras, and Babaswar, the maternal grandfather of Plato, and their likes. Some of them prohibited fish for fear of it being foam, and chicks because they are always feverish, and garlic because it causes headaches and burns the blood or the semen which is the foundation of the world, and fava beans because they dull and corrupt the intellect and because they first grew in a human skull. They have three prescribed prayers.

The first: At sunrise, eight rak'ahs.

The second: At the decline of the sun from the middle of the sky, five rak'ahs. In every rak'ah of their prayer, there are three prostrations. They perform supererogatory prayers (يتنفلون) in the second hour of the day and another in the ninth hour of the day.

The third: In the third hour of the night. They pray in a state of purity and ablution (وضوء), and they perform the ritual bath (يغتسلون) after sexual impurity (الجنابة). They do not circumcise, claiming they were not commanded to do so. Most of their laws regarding marriage and legal punishments (الحدود) are like the laws of the Muslims, and regarding impurity upon touching the dead and such matters, they are similar to the Torah. They have offerings (قرابين) related to the planets, their idols, and their temples, and sacrifices performed by their priests and diviners, through which they extract knowledge of what might happen to the one making the offering and answers to what is asked. Hermes is sometimes called Idris, who is mentioned in the Torah as Enoch, and some of them claimed that Budhasaf is Hermes.

It has been said that these Harranians are not the true Sabaeans; rather, they are those named in books as the Hunafa and the pagans (الوثنية). For the Sabaeans are those who remained in Babylon among the tribes that rose in the days of Cyrus and Artaxerxes to Jerusalem; they inclined toward the laws of the Magians and leaned toward the religion of Nebuchadnezzar. Thus, they adopted a path that was a mixture of Magianism and Judaism, like the Samaritans in Syria. Most of them are found in Wasit and the rural lands of Iraq in the region of Ja'far, al-Jamida, and Nahray al-Silah, attributing themselves to Enos the son of Seth. They oppose the Harranians and criticize their doctrines, agreeing with them only in a few things, to the extent that they face the North Pole in prayer while the Harranians face the South. Some of the People of the Book claimed that Methuselah had a son other than Lemech named Sabi, and that the Sabaeans were named after him. Before the appearance of religious laws and the emergence of Budhasaf, people were Shaminiyyun, inhabitants of the eastern side of the earth, and they were idol worshippers. Their remnants are now in India, China, and the Tughuzghuz, and the people of Khorasan call them Shamnan. Their traces, temples (بهاراتهم), idols, and shrines (فرخاراتهم) are visible in the frontiers of Khorasan adjacent to India. They believe in the eternity of time (قدم الدهر), the transmigration of souls (تناسخ الأرواح), and the falling of the celestial sphere in an infinite void, which is why it moves in a circle, for a circular thing, when displaced, descends with rotation—so they claimed. Some of them acknowledged the temporal creation of the world (حدوث العالم) and claimed its duration is one million years." (End of the relevant excerpt).

I say: What he attributed to some—interpreting the Sabaeans as a sect mixed from Magianism and Judaism along with elements from the Harranians—is most consistent with what is in the verse, for the apparent context suggests that the enumeration is of the people of established creeds (أهل الملة).

Ayahs

وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَكُمْ وَرَفَعْنَا فَوْقَكُمُ ٱلطُّورَ خُذُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكُم بِقُوَّةٍ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ مَا فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (٦٣)
And when We took a pledge from you, and raised the Mount above you, [declaring], ‘Hold on with power to what We have given you and remember that which is in it so that you may be Godwary.’ (63)
ثُمَّ تَوَلَّيْتُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ فَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُۥ لَكُنتُم مِّنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٦٤)
Again you turned away after that; and were it not for Allah’s grace on you and His mercy, you would have surely been among the losers. (64)
وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱعْتَدَوْا۟ مِنكُمْ فِى ٱلسَّبْتِ فَقُلْنَا لَهُمْ كُونُوا۟ قِرَدَةً خَـٰسِـِٔينَ (٦٥)
And certainly you know those of you who violated the Sabbath, whereupon We said to them, ‘Be you spurned apes.’ (65)
فَجَعَلْنَـٰهَا نَكَـٰلًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهَا وَمَا خَلْفَهَا وَمَوْعِظَةً لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (٦٦)
So We made it an exemplary punishment for the present and the succeeding [generations], and an advice to the Godwary. (66)
وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَن تَذْبَحُوا۟ بَقَرَةً ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ أَتَتَّخِذُنَا هُزُوًا ۖ قَالَ أَعُوذُ بِٱللَّهِ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (٦٧)
And when Moses said to his people, ‘Allah commands you to slaughter a cow,’ they said, ‘Are you mocking us?’ He said, ‘I seek Allah’s protection lest I should be one of the ignorant!’ (67)
قَالُوا۟ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّن لَّنَا مَا هِىَ ۚ قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ يَقُولُ إِنَّهَا بَقَرَةٌ لَّا فَارِضٌ وَلَا بِكْرٌ عَوَانٌۢ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ فَٱفْعَلُوا۟ مَا تُؤْمَرُونَ (٦٨)
They said, ‘Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what she may be.’ He said, ‘He says, she is a cow, neither old nor young, of a middle age. Now do what you are commanded.’ (68)
قَالُوا۟ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّن لَّنَا مَا لَوْنُهَا ۚ قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ يَقُولُ إِنَّهَا بَقَرَةٌ صَفْرَآءُ فَاقِعٌ لَّوْنُهَا تَسُرُّ ٱلنَّـٰظِرِينَ (٦٩)
They said, ‘Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what her colour may be.’ He said, ‘He says, she is a cow that is yellow, of a bright hue, pleasing to the onlookers.’ (69)
قَالُوا۟ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّن لَّنَا مَا هِىَ إِنَّ ٱلْبَقَرَ تَشَـٰبَهَ عَلَيْنَا وَإِنَّآ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَمُهْتَدُونَ (٧٠)
They said, ‘Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what she may be. Indeed all cows are much alike to us, and if Allah wishes we will surely be guided.’ (70)
قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ يَقُولُ إِنَّهَا بَقَرَةٌ لَّا ذَلُولٌ تُثِيرُ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَلَا تَسْقِى ٱلْحَرْثَ مُسَلَّمَةٌ لَّا شِيَةَ فِيهَا ۚ قَالُوا۟ ٱلْـَٔـٰنَ جِئْتَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ فَذَبَحُوهَا وَمَا كَادُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ (٧١)
He said, ‘He says, She is a cow not broken to till the earth or water the tillage, sound and without blemish.’ They said, ‘Now have you come up with the truth!’ And they slaughtered it, though they were about not to do it. (71)
وَإِذْ قَتَلْتُمْ نَفْسًا فَٱدَّٰرَْٰٔتُمْ فِيهَا ۖ وَٱللَّهُ مُخْرِجٌ مَّا كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ (٧٢)
And when you killed a soul, and accused one another about it—and Allah was to expose what you were concealing— (72)
فَقُلْنَا ٱضْرِبُوهُ بِبَعْضِهَا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُحْىِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَيُرِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٧٣)
We said, ‘Strike him with a piece of it:’ thus does Allah revive the dead, and He shows you His signs so that you may exercise your reason. (73)
ثُمَّ قَسَتْ قُلُوبُكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ فَهِىَ كَٱلْحِجَارَةِ أَوْ أَشَدُّ قَسْوَةً ۚ وَإِنَّ مِنَ ٱلْحِجَارَةِ لَمَا يَتَفَجَّرُ مِنْهُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۚ وَإِنَّ مِنْهَا لَمَا يَشَّقَّقُ فَيَخْرُجُ مِنْهُ ٱلْمَآءُ ۚ وَإِنَّ مِنْهَا لَمَا يَهْبِطُ مِنْ خَشْيَةِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٧٤)
Then your hearts hardened after that; so they are like stones, or even harder. For there are some stones from which streams gush forth, and there are some of them that split, and water issues from them, and there are some that fall for the fear of Allah. And Allah is not oblivious of what you do. (74)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: And We raised the mountain above you (رفعنا فوقكم الطور). The mountain (الطور) is the hill, as He substituted it in His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ نَتَقْنَا الْجَبَلَ فَوْقَهُمْ كَأَنَّهُ ظُلَّةٌ — "And when We plucked the mountain [and held it] above them as if it were a canopy" (7:171). Plucking (النتق) means pulling and uprooting. The context of the verse—mentioning the taking of the covenant first, then the command to hold fast to what they were given, and finally mentioning what is contained therein, while placing the raising of the mountain above them between the two commands and remaining silent about the cause and purpose of the raising—indicates that it was to strike them with awe through the greatness of Divine Power, without it being for the purpose of forcing or coercing them to act upon what they were given. Otherwise, there would be no point in taking a covenant. Thus, what is sometimes said—that if the raising of the mountain above them were taken literally, it would be a miraculous sign (آية معجزة) necessitating their compulsion and coercion to act, whereas He, the Glorified, has said: لَا إِكْرَاهَ فِي الدِّينِ — "There is no compulsion in religion" (2:256), and the Exalted said: أَفَأَنتَ تُكْرِهُ النَّاسَ حَتَّىٰ يَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ — "Will you then force people until they become believers?" (10:99)—is not well-founded. For the verse, as previously mentioned, does not indicate more than intimidation and striking awe. If the mere raising of the mountain above the Children of Israel were a coercion for them to believe or act, then most of the miracles (معجزات) of Moses would have necessitated coercion. Indeed, this interpretation—diverting the verse from its literal meaning (ظاهرها) and claiming that the Children of Israel were at the base of the mountain, which then shook and trembled until its peak loomed over them, leading them to think it was falling upon them, so it was expressed as raising it or plucking it above them—is based on the principle of denying miracles and supernatural events (خوارق العادات), which has already been discussed. If such interpretations were permissible, no literal meaning (ظهور) would remain for speech, nor would the eloquence (بلاغة) and clarity (فصاحة) of speech have any foundation to rely upon or stand by.

His saying, the Exalted: so that you may be God-conscious (لعلكم تتقون). The word la'alla (لعل) is a particle of hope (ترجٍّ). What is required for hope to be valid in speech is its correctness, whether it resides in the speaker, the addressee, or the context (المقام)—as if the situation itself is one of hope even if the speaker and addressee do not feel it. This is not free from a tinge of ignorance regarding the outcome of the matter. Thus, hope in the speech of the Exalted is either with respect to the addressee or with respect to the context. As for Him, the Exalted, it is impossible to attribute hope to Him due to His knowledge of the outcomes of all matters, as al-Raghib pointed out in his Mufradat.

His saying, the Exalted: Be as lowly apes (كونوا قردة خاسئين), meaning abject and humiliated (صاغرين).

His saying, the Exalted: So We made it an exemplary punishment (فجعلناها نكالاً), meaning a lesson (عبرة) to be heeded. Exemplary punishment (النكال) is the humiliation and degradation inflicted upon one individual so that others may take heed.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَن تَذْبَحُوا بَقَرَةً — "And when Moses said to his people, ‘Indeed Allah commands you to slaughter a cow...’" (2:67). This is the story of the cow of the Children of Israel, after which the Surah was named Surat al-Baqarah. The manner in which the Quran presents this story is remarkable. The story is divided into parts; He, the Exalted, said: "And when Moses said to his people..." until the end, then He said: وَإِذْ قَتَلْتُمْ نَفْسًا فَادَّارَأْتُمْ فِيهَا — "And when you killed a soul and disputed concerning it" (2:72). He took a section from the middle and placed it first, then placed the beginning and the end of the story second. Furthermore, the discourse with the Children of Israel in the preceding verses was in the form of direct address (الخطاب), but then shifted through a transition (الإلتفات) to the third person (الغيبة) where He said: "And when Moses said to his people," then shifted back to direct address a second time with His saying: "And when you killed a soul and disputed concerning it."

As for the transition in His saying, the Exalted: "And when Moses said to his people: ‘Indeed Allah commands you to slaughter a cow,’" it involves turning the address away from the Children of Israel and directing it toward the Prophet in a portion of the story—which is the command to slaughter the cow and its description—so that it serves as an explanatory introduction (مقدمة موضحة) for the address that will be directed to the Children of Israel in His saying: "And when you killed a soul and disputed concerning it, and Allah was to bring forth what you were hiding. So We said, ‘Strike him with a piece of it.’ Thus does Allah give life to the dead and He shows you His signs so that you may exercise your reason." These two verses follow the sequence of the previous addresses. Thus, these five verses, from His saying: "And when Moses said..." to His saying: "...and they almost did not do it," are like a parenthetical statement (المعترضة) in the discourse, explaining the meaning of the subsequent address. This is in addition to what they contain of evidence regarding their ill-manners and their harassment of their Messenger by accusing him of superfluous and vain talk (لغو الكلام), along with their obstinacy (تعنت), severity, and insistence on clarification and questioning. This necessitated attributing ambiguity (الإبهام) to Divine commands and the explanations of Prophets, along with a tinge of insult and apparent belittlement of the station of Lordship (مقام الربوبية) in their speech. Consider the saying of Moses (peace be upon him) to them: "Indeed Allah commands you to slaughter a cow," and their reply: "Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what she is," and their second reply: "Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what her color is," and their third reply: "Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what she is; indeed, cows are much alike to us." In all instances, they used the term "your Lord" (ربك) without saying "our Lord." Then they repeated their question "what she is" and said "indeed, cows are much alike to us," claiming similarity after the clarification. They did not say "the cow (البقرة) has become similar to us," but rather "cows (البقر) are much alike to us," as if they were claiming that the genus of cows is similar and that this effect is only produced by certain individuals of this species, and that this amount of clarification is insufficient to specify and identify the required individual. This is despite the fact that the effect belongs to Allah, exalted is His Name (عز إسمه), not to the cow. He had commanded them to slaughter "a cow," using an absolute (إطلاق) term without restricting it with any condition, and they could have taken it in its absolute sense. Furthermore, consider their saying to their Prophet: "Do you take us in mockery?" which implies accusing him (peace be upon him) of ignorance (الجهالة) and vanity, until he negated it from himself by saying: "I seek refuge in Allah from being among the ignorant." Finally, consider their saying after the completion of the Divine clarification: "Now you have come with the truth," which implies the negation of truth from the previous clarifications, necessitating the attribution of falsehood to the style of Divine clarification and Prophetic delivery.

In short, the presentation of this portion of the story was to clarify the matter in the subsequent address as mentioned, in addition to another subtle point: the story of the cow is not mentioned in the Torah present with the Jews today. Thus, it was appropriate that they not be addressed with this story at all, or be addressed with it after clarifying how their hands had played with distortion (التحريف). So, He initially turned away from addressing them by directing the speech to the Prophet, and then, after establishing the foundation, He returned to the continuous sequence of addressing them. Indeed, in this section of the Torah, there is a ruling that is not devoid of some indication that the story occurred. Here is the text of the Torah:

It says in the twenty-first chapter of the Book of Deuteronomy: "If one be found slain in the land which the Lord thy God giveth thee to possess it, lying in the field, and it be not known who hath slain him: Then thy elders and thy judges shall come forth, and they shall measure unto the cities which are round about him that is slain: And it shall be, that the city which is next unto the slain man, even the elders of that city shall take an heifer, which hath not been wrought with, and which hath not drawn in the yoke; And the elders of that city shall bring down the heifer unto a rough valley, which is neither eared nor sown, and shall strike off the heifer's neck there in the valley: And the priests the sons of Levi shall come near; for them the Lord thy God hath chosen to minister unto him, and to bless in the name of the Lord; and by their word shall every controversy and every stroke be tried: And all the elders of that city, that are next unto the slain man, shall wash their hands over the heifer that is beheaded in the valley: And they shall answer and say, Our hands have not shed this blood, neither have our eyes seen it. Be merciful, O Lord, unto thy people Israel, whom thou hast redeemed, and lay not innocent blood unto thy people of Israel's charge. And the blood shall be forgiven them." [End of quote].

Once you recognize this in its length, you will realize that the presentation of this story in this manner is not a case of splitting a story. Rather, the story is explained in a summary (إجمال) fashion in the address found in His saying: "And when you killed a soul..." etc., and a portion of the story is brought forth with a detailed explanation in the form of another story for a reason that necessitated it.

So His saying, the Exalted: "And when Moses said to his people" is an address to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), and it is a discourse in the form of a story, but it is merely an explanatory introduction for the subsequent address. The cause that prompted this command and the intended goal were not mentioned with it; rather, it was left absolute (إطلاقاً) so that the listener's soul would be alerted, stand in a state of inquiry, and become active upon hearing the core of the story and grasping the connection between the two discourses. Therefore, when the Children of Israel heard His saying: "Indeed Allah commands you to slaughter a cow," they were astonished and took it only to mean that the Prophet of Allah, Moses, was mocking them, because they found no connection between slaughtering a cow and what they were asking regarding the resolution of the dispute and identifying the killer. They said: "Do you take us in mockery and ridicule?"

They only said this because they lacked the spirit of obedience and listening, and because the trait of arrogance (الإستكبار) and rebellion (العتو) had become firmly established in them. Their claim was: "We do not hover around blameworthy imitation (التقليد المذموم); we only believe in what we witness and see," just as they said to Moses: "We will not believe you until we see Allah openly." They fell into what they fell into because of their independent judgment and decree in matters where they had the right and where they did not. They judged the intelligible (المعقول) by the sensible (المحسوس), demanding to witness the Lord with the physical eye, saying: يَا مُوسَى اجْعَل لَّنَا إِلَٰهًا كَمَا لَهُمْ آلِهَةٌ قَالَ إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ — "O Moses, make for us a god like the gods they have. He said, ‘Indeed you are an ignorant people’" (7:138). They imagined that their Prophet Moses was like them, following whims as they did and playing as they played, so they accused him of mockery, folly, and ignorance until he rebuffed them, saying: "I seek refuge in Allah from being among the ignorant." He sought refuge in Allah and did not merely inform them that he was not ignorant because this was an adherence to Divine protection (العصمة الإلهية) which never fails, rather than moral wisdom (الحكمة الخلقية) which might fail.

They imagined that a person should not accept a statement except with evidence, and this is true. However, they erred in their belief that for every ruling, one must discover its evidence in detail (تفصيلا), and that a summary (الإجمال) is not sufficient. For this reason, they demanded the detailed descriptions of the cow, based on their judgment that the genus of cows does not possess the property of giving life; if it must be so, then it must be in a specific individual that must be identified with perfectly clear descriptions. Therefore, they said: "Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what she is." This was a self-imposed hardship (تشديد) on their part without cause, so Allah made it hard for them. Moses said: "He says she is a cow neither old (فارض)—meaning an aged cow that has stopped calving—nor a virgin (بكر)—meaning one that has not calved—middle-aged (عوان) between that." 'Awan (العوان) among women and animals refers to those in middle age, i.e., situated in age between the aforementioned old and virgin. Then their Lord showed them mercy and exhorted them not to persist in questioning nor to make things hard for themselves, and to be content with what had been clarified for them, saying: "So do what you are commanded." But they were not deterred by that; rather, they said: "Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what her color is." He said: "He says she is a yellow cow, bright (فاقع)—intensely yellow in the purity of its color—pleasing to the onlookers." With that, the description of the cow was completed in terms of its nature and color. Yet, they were still not satisfied and repeated their first statement without any hesitation or restraint, saying: "Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what she is; indeed, cows are much alike to us, and indeed, if Allah wills, we will be guided." So He answered them a second time with a clarification of her essence and color, saying: "He says she is a cow not broken (لا ذلول)—meaning not tamed for plowing or watering—she does not plow the earth nor water the tilth, sound, without blemish." When the clarification was complete and they found nothing more to ask, they said: "Now you have come with the truth"—the words of one who admits the reality due to being cornered by proof and argument, without finding a way to reject it. Thus, they admitted the truth out of necessity, making excuses for their initial rush to denial by claiming the statement was not previously clear or perfectly manifest. The evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted: "So they slaughtered her, and they almost did not do it."

His saying, the Exalted: And when you killed a soul and disputed concerning it (إذ قتلتم نفساً فادارأتم فيها). This is the beginning of the core story. Disputing (التدارء) is mutual pushing, from dar' (الدرء) meaning to push. They had killed a soul—and every group among them was pushing the blame for the blood away from themselves onto others—and Allah, the Glorified, willed to bring forth what they were hiding.

His saying, the Exalted: So We said, ‘Strike him with a piece of it’ (فقلنا إضربوه ببعضها). The first of the two pronouns refers to the "soul" (النفس) in the sense of the slain person, and the second refers to the "cow" (البقرة). It has been said that the purpose of the story is to explain the origin of the legal ruling so that it aligns with the ruling mentioned in the Torah which we quoted, and that "giving life to the dead" means discovering the blood of the slain through the means of legislating this ruling, similar to what He, the Exalted, mentioned in His saying: وَلَكُمْ فِي الْقِصَاصِ حَيَاةٌ — "There is life for you in retribution" (2:179), without there being an actual resurrection in a miraculous manner. However, as you know, the context of the speech—especially His saying, the Exalted: "So We said, ‘Strike him with a piece of it.’ Thus does Allah give life to the dead"—refutes this.

His saying, the Exalted: Then your hearts hardened, so they are like stones or even harder in hardness (ثم قست قلوبكم فهي كالحجارة أو اشد قسوة). Hardness (القسوة) in the heart is like solidity in stone. The word "or" (أو) here means "rather" (بل), and the meaning of it being "rather" is the application of its sense to its context. He explained the intensity of their hearts' hardness by His saying: "And indeed, of the stones there are those from which rivers gush forth." A comparison was made between stones and water because stones are used as a proverb for solidity, just as water is used as a proverb for softness. These stones, despite their perfect solidity, have rivers gushing from them despite the softness of their water, and they split so that water comes out of them despite its softness and their solidity. Yet, no state compatible with the truth issues from their hearts, nor any word of truth compatible with actual perfection.

His saying, the Exalted: And indeed, of them are those that fall from the fear of Allah (وإن منها لما يهبط من خشية الله). The falling (هبوط) of stones is what we witness of the splitting of rocks on mountain peaks, and the falling of pieces of them due to earthquakes, or the turning of ice that permeates them in winter into water in spring, and so on. This falling, which is attributed to its natural causes, is considered a falling from the fear of Allah (خشية الله) because all causes terminate with Allah, the Glorified. The reaction of the stones in their falling to their specific cause is a reaction to Allah's command to them to fall. They are aware of their Lord's command with a primordial, existential awareness (شعوراً تكوينياً), as He, the Exalted, said: وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ وَلَٰكِن لَّا تَفْقَهُونَ تَسْبِيحَهُمْ — "There is not a thing but celebrates His praise, but you do not understand their glorification" (17:44), and He, the Exalted, said: كُلٌّ لَّهُ قَانِتُونَ — "All are obedient to Him" (2:116). Conscious reaction is fear (الخشية), so they fall from the fear of Allah. The verse follows the same course as His saying, the Exalted: وَيُسَبِّحُ الرَّعْدُ بِحَمْدِهِ وَالْمَلَائِكَةُ مِنْ خِيفَتِهِ — "The thunder celebrates His praise, and the angels [too], in awe of Him" (13:13), and His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ مَن فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ طَوْعًا وَكَرْهًا وَظِلَالُهُم بِالْغُدُوِّ وَالْآصَالِ — "To Allah prostrates whoever is in the heavens and the earth, willingly or unwillingly, and their shadows at morning and evening" (13:15), where the sound of thunder is considered a glorification with praise, and shadows are considered prostrating to Allah, the Glorified, among other verses where the discourse follows the path of analytical interpretation, as is not hidden.

In short, His saying: "And indeed, of them are those that fall," is a second explanation of their hearts being harder than stones; for stones fear Allah and fall from His fear, while their hearts do not fear Allah nor stand in awe of Him.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Mahasin, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: Hold fast to what We have given you with power (خذوا ما آتيناكم بقوة): "Is it the power of the bodies or the power of the heart?" He (peace be upon him) said: "In both of them together."

I say: It was also narrated by al-Ayyashi in his Tafsir.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from al-Halabi regarding His saying, the Exalted: And remember what is in it (وأذكروا ما فيه), he said: "He said: Remember what is in it, and remember the punishment for abandoning it."

I say: This is inferred from the context of His saying, the Exalted: "And We raised the mountain above you: Hold fast..."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, from Abu Hurayrah, he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'Had the Children of Israel not said: "And indeed, if Allah wills, we will be guided," they would never have been given [the cow]. And had they taken any cow from among the cows and slaughtered it, it would have sufficed them. But they made it hard [on themselves], so Allah made it hard for them.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from Ibn Faddal, he said: "I heard Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him) saying: 'Indeed, Allah commanded the Children of Israel to slaughter a cow, and they only needed its tail, but Allah made it hard for them.'"

In al-Ma'ani and Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from al-Bazanti, he said: "I heard al-Rida (peace be upon him) saying: 'A man from the Children of Israel killed a relative of his, then took him and threw him on the path of the best tribe among the tribes of the Children of Israel. Then he came seeking his blood. They said to Moses: "The tribe of the family of so-and-so killed so-and-so, so inform us who killed him." He said: "Bring me a cow." They said: "Do you take us in mockery?" He said: "I seek refuge in Allah from being among the ignorant." Had they intended any cow, it would have sufficed them, but they made it hard, so Allah made it hard for them. They said: "Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what she is." He said: "He says she is a cow neither old nor a virgin"—meaning neither small nor large—"middle-aged between that." Had they intended any cow, it would have sufficed them, but they made it hard, so Allah made it hard for them. They said: "Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what her color is." He said: "He says she is a yellow cow, bright in color, pleasing to the onlookers." Had they intended any cow, it would have sufficed them, but they made it hard, so Allah made it hard for them. They said: "Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what she is; indeed, cows are much alike to us, and indeed, if Allah wills, we will be guided." He said: "He says she is a cow not broken to plow the earth nor water the tilth, sound, without blemish." They said: "Now you have come with the truth." So they sought her and found her with a youth from the Children of Israel. He said: "I will not sell her except for her skin's fill of gold." They came to Moses and told him that. He said: "Buy her." So they bought her and brought her. He commanded her slaughter, then commanded that they strike the dead man with her tail. When they did that, the slain man came to life and said: "O Messenger of Allah, indeed my cousin killed me, not the one he accused of my murder." Thus they knew his killer. Some of the companions of the Messenger of Allah, Moses, said: "Indeed, this cow has a story." He said: "And what is it?" He said: "A youth from the Children of Israel was dutiful to his father. He had bought some merchandise and came to his father while the keys were under his head. He disliked waking him, so he left that deal. His father woke up and he informed him. He said: 'You have done well; this cow is yours as a compensation for what you missed.' So the Messenger of Allah, Moses, said: 'Look at what dutifulness (البر) has brought to its people.'"'"

I say: The narrations, as you see, are consistent with the summary of what we have inferred from the noble verses.

(Philosophical Discussion)

The Surah, as you see, contains several miraculous signs (الآيات المعجزة) in the stories of the Children of Israel and others, such as the parting of the sea and the drowning of the House of Pharaoh in His saying, the Exalted: "And when We parted the sea for you and drowned the House of Pharaoh..." the verse; the seizing of the Children of Israel by the thunderbolt and their revival after death in His saying, the Exalted: "And when you said, ‘O Moses, we will not believe you...’" the verse; the shading by the clouds and the sending down of manna and quails upon them in His saying, the Exalted: "And We shaded you with clouds..." the verse; the gushing of springs from the stone in His saying, the Exalted: "And when Moses prayed for water for his people..." the verse; the raising of the mountain above them in His saying, the Exalted: "And We raised the mountain above you..." the verse; the metamorphosis (مسخ) of a group of them in His saying, the Exalted: "So We said to them, ‘Be as lowly apes’" the verse; the revival of the slain man with a piece of the slaughtered cow in His saying: "So We said, ‘Strike him with a piece of it’" the verse; as well as the revival of another group in His saying: "Have you not regarded those who left their homes..." the verse; the revival of the one who passed by a ruined village in His saying: "Or him who passed by a town which had fallen into ruin..." the verse; and the revival of the birds at the hand of Abraham in His saying, the Exalted: "And when Abraham said, ‘My Lord! Show me how You give life to the dead’" the verse. These are twelve miraculous signs that break the natural order (خارقة للعادة), most of which occurred among the Children of Israel—mentioned by the Quran. We have already explained the possibility of the occurrence of miracles and that supernatural events are permissible in existence, and yet they do not violate the universal law of cause and effect (العلية والمعلولية). It has been clarified thereby that there is no evidence for interpreting the verses that are literal (الظاهرة) in the occurrence of miracles or diverting them from their literal meanings as long as the event is possible, unlike impossibilities (المحالات)—such as the division of three into two equal parts or the birth of a child who is his own father—for there is no way for these to be permissible.

Yes, some miracles, such as the revival of the dead and metamorphosis (المسخ), are subject to another discussion. It has been said: It has been established in its proper place that an existent which possesses the potentiality (قوة) for perfection and actuality (الفعلية), once it emerges from potentiality to actuality, it is impossible for it to return to potentiality again. Likewise, everything that is more perfect in existence does not return in its perfective journey (سيره الإستكمالي) to that which is less perfect in existence than it, in that respect. Man, through death, becomes disembodied (يتجرد) in his soul from matter, returning as an imaginal (مثالياً) or intellectual (عقلياً) disembodied existent. These two ranks are above the rank of matter, and existence in them is stronger than material existence. Thus, it is impossible for the soul, after its death, to attach to matter again; otherwise, it would necessitate the return of a thing to potentiality after its emergence into actuality, which is impossible. Also, man is stronger in existence than other types of animals, so it is impossible for man to return to being something from other types of animals through metamorphosis.

I say: What he mentioned regarding the impossibility of the return of that which is in potentiality to potentiality again after its emergence into actuality is a truth about which there is no doubt. However, the return of the dead to their worldly life again in some cases, as well as metamorphosis, are not instances of this. The explanation for this is: What is gathered from sense and proof is that the material vegetal substance (الجوهر النباتي المادي), if it falls into the path of animal perfection, moves toward animality, becoming formed by the animal form (الصورة الحيوانية), which is a disembodied form in the sense of imaginal disembodiment (التجرد البرزخي). Its reality is the thing's perception of itself through a particular, imaginal perception. This form is a complete existence for the vegetal substance and an actuality for this potentiality which it donned through substantial motion (الحركة الجوهرية). It is impossible for it to return one day to the material substance and become it, except that it separates from its matter, so the matter remains with a material form, like an animal that dies and becomes a motionless corpse. Then, the animal form is a principle for perceptive acts that issue from it, and cognitive states (أحوال علمية) that follow from it. The soul is imprinted with each of these states by their issuance from it, and one imprint follows another. When these imprints, which are similar and homogeneous, accumulate, a single imprint is formed and becomes a stable form incapable of vanishing—a firmly rooted habit (ملكة راسخة). This is a new psychic form (صورة نفسانية) by which an animal soul can be diversified, becoming a specific animal with a specific diversifying form, such as the form of cunning, malice, lust, loyalty, predation, and so on. If a habit is not formed, the soul remains at its previous simple (ساذجة) level, like a plant that, if it stops its substantial motion, remains a plant and does not emerge into animal actuality. If the imaginal soul were to reach perfection in its states and acts by obtaining the form instantaneously, its connection with the body would be severed at the first moment of its existence. However, it reaches perfection through its perceptive acts related to matter little by little until it becomes a specific animal, if it lives a natural lifespan or a significant portion of it. If a barrier like premature death (الموت الإخترامي) intervenes before the completion of the natural lifespan or a significant portion, it remains in its simple state of animality. Then, if the animal falls into the path of humanity—which is the existence that intellects its own essence with a universal intellection (تعقلا كلياً) disembodied from matter and its concomitants like dimensions, colors, and others—it emerges through substantial motion from the actuality of the imaginal (المثال), which is the potentiality of the intellect, to the actuality of intellectual disembodiment (التجرد العقلي). Thus, the form of man in actuality is realized for it. It is impossible for this actuality to return to its potentiality, which is imaginal disembodiment, just as was mentioned regarding the animal.

Then, for this form also, there are acts and states whose gradual accumulation results in a new specific form that necessitates the diversification of the human species, just as was mentioned regarding the animal species.

Once you understand what we have mentioned, it becomes clear to you that if we assume a person returns to the world after death and the connection to matter is renewed for his soul—especially the matter that was connected to his soul before—this does not invalidate the principle of his soul's disembodiment. For it was disembodied before the severance of the connection, and it remains so with it; even with the second connection, it preserves its disembodiment. What happened to it through death was that the tool (الأداة) that linked its action to matter was lost to it, so it became unable to perform a material act, like a craftsman who loses his tools and the necessary instruments. Thus, when the soul returns to its actual connection with matter, it begins to use its bodily powers and tools, and it places what it acquired of states and habits through actions on top of what was already present and obtained by it before, reaching a new perfection through them without this being a backward return or a downward journey from perfection to deficiency, or from actuality to potentiality.

If you say: This necessitates the claim of permanent coercion (القسر الدائم), despite the necessity of its invalidity. For if the disembodied soul, separated from the body, retained in its nature the possibility of subsequent perfection through material acts by connecting to matter again, its remaining in deprivation from perfection forever would be a deprivation from what its nature demands. Not every soul returns to the world through a miracle or a supernatural event, and continuous deprivation is permanent coercion.

I say: These souls that emerged from potentiality to actuality in the world, reached a certain limit, and died there, do not remain in the possibility of subsequent perfection forever. Rather, they settle upon their present actuality after a while or emerge into the intellectual form (الصورة العقلية) suitable for that and remain upon it, and the aforementioned possibility vanishes thereafter. A person who died with a simple soul, but performed actions and mixed a righteous deed with another that was evil—if he had lived for a while, he might have acquired a happy or wretched form upon his simple soul. Likewise, if he returned to the world after death and lived, he might acquire a new specific form upon his previous form. If he does not return, he is in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — rewarded or punished for what he earned of actions until he is formed by an intellectual form suitable for his previous imaginal form. At that point, the aforementioned possibility is invalidated, and the possibilities of intellectual perfections remain. If he returns to the world—like the Prophets and the Friends of Allah (الأولياء) were they to return to the world after their death—it is possible for another intellectual form to be obtained through matter and the acts related to it. If he does not return, he has nothing but the perfection he earned, ascending in its ranks and traveling on its path.

It is well known that this is not permanent coercion. If the mere deprivation of an existent from its possible perfection through the work of factors and the influence of effective causes were permanent coercion, then most events of this world—which is the abode of mutual interference (التزاحم) and the home of opposition (التضاد)—or all of them, would be permanent coercion. For all parts of this natural world influence all others. Permanent coercion is only when there is placed in the instinct (غريزة) of a species the demand for a certain perfection or a readiness (استعداد), and then the effect of that never appears, either due to something within its essence or something outside its essence directed toward its invalidation according to the instinct. Thus, the instinctualizing of the species that demands or is ready for perfection would be a futile instinctualizing and a vain, nonsensical creation. Understand this. Likewise, if we assume a person whose form changed into the form of another type of animal, such as an ape or a pig, it is merely a form upon a form; he is a pig-man or an ape-man, not a man whose humanity was invalidated and replaced by the porcine or simian form. When a man earns a form from the forms of habits (الملكات), his soul is formed by it, and there is no evidence for the impossibility of its emergence in this world from latency (الكمون) to manifestation (البروز), just as it will appear in the Hereafter after death. It has already been mentioned that the human soul, at the beginning of its origination, is in a state of simplicity (السذاجة) and can be diversified by a specific form that specifies it after ambiguity and restricts it after absolute potentiality and acceptance. Thus, the metamorphosed (الممسوخ) from among humans is a metamorphosed human, not a metamorphosed being devoid of humanity. Furthermore, we read in daily publications of news from scientific societies in Europe and America what suggests the possibility of life after death and the transformation of the human form into a metamorphosed form. Although we do not rely on such news in these discussions, it is incumbent upon researchers among the educated not to forget today what they were reciting yesterday.

If you say: Based on this, there is no barrier to believing in reincarnation (التناسخ).

I say: Not at all. For reincarnation—which is the attachment of the soul that has reached its type of perfection after separating from the body to another body—is impossible. For if this body possesses a soul, reincarnation would necessitate the attachment of two souls to one body, which is the unity of the many and the multiplicity of the one. If it does not possess a soul, it would necessitate the return of that which is in actuality to potentiality, like the return of an old man to childhood. Likewise, it is impossible for a perfected, separated human soul to attach to a vegetal or animal body, based on the explanation already provided.

(Scientific and Ethical Discussion)

The nation of the past with the most stories in the Quran is the nation of the Children of Israel, and the Prophet mentioned most in it is Moses son of 'Imran (peace be upon him). His name is mentioned in the Quran in one hundred and thirty-six places—double what is mentioned of Abraham (peace be upon him), who is the most mentioned Prophet after Moses, having been mentioned in sixty-nine places, according to what has been said about both. The apparent reason for this is that Islam is the upright religion (الدين الحنيف) based on monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — whose foundation was laid by Abraham (peace be upon him) and which Allah, the Glorified, completed and perfected for His Prophet Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). The Exalted said: مِّلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْرَاهِيمَ هُوَ سَمَّاكُمُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ مِن قَبْلُ — "The faith of your father, Abraham. He named you ‘Muslims’ before" (22:78). The Children of Israel were the most obstinate and contentious of nations, and the furthest from submission to the truth, just as the disbelievers of the Arabs with whom the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) was tested were of this character. The matter reached the point where it was revealed concerning them: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَوَاءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ — "As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith" (2:6).

You do not see a vice among the vices of the Children of Israel in their hardness and coarseness mentioned by the Quran except that it is found in them. In any case, if you reflect upon the stories of the Children of Israel mentioned in the Quran and contemplate them and the secrets of their character contained therein, you will find that they were a people deeply immersed in matter, devoted to the sensory pleasures of formal life. This nation did not believe in what lies beyond the senses and did not submit except to material pleasure and perfection. They are the same today. This condition is what placed their reason and will under the submission of the senses and matter; they do not intellect except what these two permit, and they do not will except what these two allow. Submission to the senses necessitated that they not accept any statement unless the senses pointed to it, even if it were true. Submission to matter necessitated that they accept everything their leaders—who were given material beauty and the ornaments of life—wanted or deemed good for them, even if it were not true. This produced in them a contradiction in word and deed. They condemn all following under the name of "imitation" (تقليد), even if it is appropriate, if it is far from their senses; and they praise all following under the name of "the fortune of life," even if it is inappropriate, if it is compatible with their material whims. They were aided in this by their long stay and extended residence in Egypt under the humiliation, enslavement, and torture of the Egyptians, who inflicted upon them a grievous punishment, slaughtering their sons and sparing their women; and in that was a great trial from their Lord.
