In short, they were therefore difficult to lead in what their Prophets and the godly among their scholars commanded them for the benefit of their worldly and otherworldly life (recall their stances with Moses and others), and quick to join what the biased and arrogant among them called them to.

Truth and reality are afflicted today with a similar trial by the material civilization presented to them by the Western world. It is built upon the foundation of the senses and matter; no proof is accepted regarding what is beyond the senses, and no proof is asked for regarding what contains a material, sensory pleasure. This has necessitated the invalidation of the human instinct (الغريزة الإنسانية) in its judgments, and the departure of high knowledge and virtuous character from among us. Thus, it has begun to threaten humanity with collapse and the human society with the most severe corruption; and its news shall surely be known after a while.

A full investigation into ethics produces the opposite of that: not every proof is required, and not every imitation is blameworthy. The explanation for this is: The human species, insofar as it is human, only moves toward its vital perfection through its volitional acts (أفعاله الارادية) which depend on thought and will. Will is impossible to realize except through thought. Thus, thought is the sole foundation upon which necessary existential perfection is built. Therefore, man must have practical or theoretical affirmations (تصديقات) to which his existential perfection is linked, either directly or indirectly. these are the propositions by which we justify our individual or social acts, or which we bring to our minds and then realize in the external world through our acts.

Furthermore, it is in the instinct of man to search for the causes (علل) of the events he finds or the information that strikes his mind. No act issues from him by which he intends to bring what is present in his mind into the external world unless its necessitating cause is present in his mind. Nor does he accept a theoretical affirmation unless it relies on the affirmation of its cause in some way. This is the nature of man which he never oversteps. If we encounter cases where the opposite seems to appear, then through reflection and contemplation, the doubt is resolved. Searching for the cause and relying upon and finding tranquility in it is primordial (فطري), and the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — does not vary, nor does its action fail. This leads man to what is beyond his capacity of intellectual work and the acts branching from it, due to the breadth of natural need, such that a single man cannot fulfill it by relying on himself and leaning on the power of his personal nature. So, the primordial nature devised a way to send him toward society, which is civilization and culture. It distributed the doors of vital need among the individuals of society, and entrusted each door to a group, like the organs of an animal in their different tasks whose benefit and return are gathered in itself. Human needs continue to increase in quantity and breadth, and arts, industries, and sciences branch out. At that point, specialists among scholars and craftsmen are trained. Many sciences and industries were once a single science or industry managed by one person, but today we see every door of them as a science or sciences, or an industry or industries—like medicine, which was anciently counted as one branch of the natural sciences, but today consists of many branches such that a single specialist scholar cannot manage more than one of them.

This calls man, through primordial inspiration (الإلهام الفطري), to be independent in what concerns his specific human work in searching for its cause, and to follow, in other matters, those upon whose expertise and skill he relies.

Thus, the practice of the rational (بناء العقلاء) among the members of society is to return to the people of expertise (أهل الخبرة). The reality of this following is the technical term "imitation" (التقليد) and relying on the summary proof (الدليل الاجمالي) in what is not within the capacity of man.

to attain the evidence for its details, just as he is naturally disposed toward independence (الاستقلال) in searching for detailed evidence in matters where it is within his capacity to grasp the details of their cause and proof. The crux of the entire matter is that man does not rely on anything other than knowledge (العلم). Therefore, according to the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — it is obligatory to practice ijtihad (الاجتهاد), which is independence in investigating the cause (العلة) whenever that is possible, and taqlid (التقليد), which is following and the recourse of the ignorant to the learned in matters where such investigation is not possible. Since it is impossible for any individual of the human species to be entirely self-sufficient, managing all the affairs of the foundation upon which life rests, it is likewise impossible for any human individual to exist without following and taqlid (التقليد). Whoever claims otherwise or imagines himself to be free of taqlid (التقليد) in his life has surely fooled himself.

Yes, taqlid (التقليد) in matters where it is possible for man to grasp their cause and reason—just like ijtihad (الاجتهاد) in matters he has no way of accessing or attaining—is among the vices (الرذائل) that destroy society and annihilate virtuous civilization. Absolute following (الاتباع المحض) is not permissible except toward Allah, the Glorified, because He is the Cause (السبب) to Whom all causes ultimately return.

Ayahs

۞ أَفَتَطْمَعُونَ أَن يُؤْمِنُوا۟ لَكُمْ وَقَدْ كَانَ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمْ يَسْمَعُونَ كَلَـٰمَ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ يُحَرِّفُونَهُۥ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا عَقَلُوهُ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (٧٥)
Are you then eager that they should believe you, though a part of them would hear the word of Allah and then they would distort it after they had understood it, and they knew [what they were doing]? (75)
وَإِذَا لَقُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَإِذَا خَلَا بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ قَالُوٓا۟ أَتُحَدِّثُونَهُم بِمَا فَتَحَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ لِيُحَآجُّوكُم بِهِۦ عِندَ رَبِّكُمْ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٧٦)
When they meet the faithful, they say, ‘We believe,’ and when they are alone with one another, they say, ‘Do you recount to them what Allah has revealed to you, so that they may argue with you therewith before your Lord? Do you not exercise your reason?’ (76)
أَوَلَا يَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ (٧٧)
Do they not know that Allah knows whatever they hide and whatever they disclose? (77)
وَمِنْهُمْ أُمِّيُّونَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ إِلَّآ أَمَانِىَّ وَإِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَظُنُّونَ (٧٨)
And among them are the illiterate who know nothing of the Book except hearsay, and they only make conjectures. (78)
فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يَكْتُبُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ ثُمَّ يَقُولُونَ هَـٰذَا مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ لِيَشْتَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۖ فَوَيْلٌ لَّهُم مِّمَّا كَتَبَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَوَيْلٌ لَّهُم مِّمَّا يَكْسِبُونَ (٧٩)
So woe to them who write the Book with their hands and then say, ‘This is from Allah,’ that they may sell it for a paltry gain. So woe to them for what their hands have written, and woe to them for what they earn! (79)
وَقَالُوا۟ لَن تَمَسَّنَا ٱلنَّارُ إِلَّآ أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَةً ۚ قُلْ أَتَّخَذْتُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ عَهْدًا فَلَن يُخْلِفَ ٱللَّهُ عَهْدَهُۥٓ ۖ أَمْ تَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٨٠)
And they say, ‘The Fire shall not touch us except for a number of days.’ Say, ‘Have you taken a promise from Allah? If so, Allah will never break His promise. Do you ascribe to Allah what you do not know?’ (80)
بَلَىٰ مَن كَسَبَ سَيِّئَةً وَأَحَـٰطَتْ بِهِۦ خَطِيٓـَٔتُهُۥ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٨١)
Certainly whoever commits misdeeds and is besieged by his iniquity—such shall be the inmates of the Fire, and they will remain in it [forever]. (81)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٨٢)
And those who have faith and do righteous deeds—they shall be the inhabitants of paradise; they will remain in it [forever]. (82)

(Commentary)

The context, especially what appears at the conclusion of the verses, indicates that the disbelievers (الكفار)—particularly those of Medina due to their proximity—regarded the Jews before the Prophetic mission as those who possessed knowledge of the religion (الدين) and the Book (الكتاب), and as potential supporters of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم. Consequently, there was a greater hope for their faith (الإيمان) than for others; it was expected that they would believe in him in crowds, thereby strengthening him, manifesting his light, and spreading his call. However, when the Prophet migrated to Medina and their conduct unfolded as it did, hope turned to despair and expectation to hopelessness. Thus, the Exalted says: أَفَتَطْمَعُونَ أَن يُؤْمِنُوا لَكُمْ "Do you then hope that they will believe you..." (2:75), meaning that concealing truths and distorting speech are among their inherent traits (شيمهم). Therefore, one should not find it improbable that they would recant what they said or breach what they had firmly concluded.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَتَطْمَعُونَ أَن يُؤْمِنُوا لَكُمْ "Do you then hope that they will believe you" (2:75). In this, there is a transition (التفات) from addressing the Children of Israel to addressing the Prophet and those who believe, placing the former in the third person (الغيبة). The reason for this is that when He narrated the story of the Cow and shifted therein from addressing the Children of Israel to speaking of them in the third person—due to the distortion (التحريف) that occurred by its omission from the Torah, as previously mentioned—He intended to complete the explanation in the third-person mode by pointing to their distortion of the Book of Allah (كتاب الله), and thus directed the discourse accordingly.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا لَقُوا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا قَالُوا آمَنَّا وَإِذَا خَلَا "When they meet those who have faith, they say, ‘We believe,’ and when they are alone..." (2:76). There is no contrast between the two conditions introduced by "when" (إذَا) in both instances, as there is in His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا لَقُوا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا قَالُوا آمَنَّا وَإِذَا خَلَوْا إِلَىٰ شَيَاطِينِهِمْ قَالُوا إِنَّا مَعَكُمْ إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ مُسْتَهْزِئُونَ "When they meet those who have faith, they say, ‘We believe,’ and when they are alone with their devils, they say, ‘We are with you; we were only mocking’" (2:14). Rather, the intent is to describe two other instances of their crimes and ignorance (جهالتهم).

The first: That they practice hypocrisy (ينافقون), feigning faith (الإيمان) to protect themselves from harm, criticism, and death.

The second: That they seek to obscure and hide the matter from Allah, the Glorified, who knows their secrets and their public affairs. This is because the commoners among them, who possessed a simplicity of soul (بساطة النفس), would sometimes open up to the believers and relate to them some of what was in their books regarding the glad tidings of the Prophet or that which would benefit the believers in confirming his prophethood, as is suggested by the tone of the discourse. Their leaders would then forbid them, justifying this by saying that such knowledge was what Allah had "opened" (فتح) to them, and it should not be disclosed to the believers lest they use it to contend with them before their Lord. It is as if they believed that if the believers did not contend with them before their Lord, Allah would not be aware of it and would not hold them accountable. This implies that Allah, the Exalted, only knows the public aspect of a matter, not its secret or inner reality (باطنه). This is an extreme form of ignorance (الجهل). Thus, Allah, the Glorified, refuted them by His saying: أَوَلَا يَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ "Do they not know that Allah knows whatever they hide and whatever they disclose?" (2:77). For this type of knowledge—which relates to the outward appearance of a matter rather than its inward reality—is only the knowledge derived from the senses (الحس), which requires a material body (بدن مادي) equipped with physical instruments, restricted by the constraints of time and space, and produced by other material causes. That which is such is a product of the world (مصنوع من العالم), not the Creator of the world (صانع العالم).

This is also among the evidences for what we previously established: that the Children of Israel, due to their conviction in the primacy of matter (أصالة المادة), would apply to Allah, the Glorified, the same laws that apply to matter. They thought of Him as an existent (موجود) acting within matter, superior and dominant over it, but in the same way a material cause acts upon and dominates a material effect. This is not exclusive to the Jews; rather, it is the condition of everyone who adheres to the primacy of matter among the religious and others. They do not judge the Presence of His Sanctity (ساحة قدسه), the Glorified, except by what they conceive of the attributes of material things—such as life, knowledge, power, choice, will, decree, judgment, the management of affairs, and the finalization of destiny. This is a disease for which no medicine is effective; "and signs and warners do not avail a people who do not understand." It reached the point where those who have no firm hold on their true religion nor any standing in their true gnosis mocked them, saying that the Muslims narrate from their Prophet that Allah created Adam in His image, while the members of his community create Allah in the image of Adam. These people fluctuate between affirming for their Lord all the laws of matter—as done by the anthropomorphists (المشبهة) among the Muslims or those who follow their path even if not known for anthropomorphism—or not understanding anything of His attributes of beauty (أوصاف جماله), thus negating them all by reducing them to negations (السلوب), claiming that the words describing the attributes of the Exalted are applied to Him only through verbal homonymy (الاشتراك اللفظي).

Thus, when we say: "He is existent (موجود), enduring (ثابت), knowing (عالم), powerful (قادر), and living (حي)," they claim these are meanings we do not understand or conceive; therefore, it is necessary to refer their meanings to negation. For example, the meaning would be that He is "not non-existent," "not ceasing," "not ignorant," "not powerless," and "not dead." Take heed, O you who possess insight! This implies their claim that they believe in what they do not perceive, worship what they do not understand, and call toward what they cannot conceive, nor can any human conceive it. The religious call (الدعوة الدينية) has spared them the burden of these falsehoods through the truth. It has commanded the commoners to preserve the reality of the word and the core of the truth between anthropomorphism (التشبيه) and transcendence (التنزيه), saying: "Indeed, Allah, the Glorified, is a thing unlike other things; He possesses knowledge unlike our knowledge, power unlike our power, and life unlike our life; He is a Willer without hesitation, and a Speaker without the parting of lips." As for the elite (الخاصة), they must contemplate His signs and gain deep understanding (يتفقهوا) in His religion, for Allah, the Glorified, has said: هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُونَ وَالَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ إِنَّمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ "Are those who know equal to those who do not know? Only those who possess intellect take admonition" (39:9). Just as the elite are not equal to the commoners in the degrees of gnosis (المعرفة), they are likewise not equal to them in the obligations (التكاليف) directed toward them. This is the religious instruction revealed for them, if only they would accept it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْهُمْ أُمِّيُّونَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ الْكِتَابَ إِلَّا أَمَانِيَّ "And among them are unlettered ones who know nothing of the Book except fancies" (2:78). The unlettered (الأمي) is one who neither reads nor writes, attributed to the "mother" (الأم) because a mother's affection and compassion would prevent her from sending her child to a teacher and surrendering him to his training; thus, he would suffice with the mother's upbringing. "Fancies" (الأماني) is the plural of umniyyah, which refers to lies (الأكاذيب). The sum of the meaning is that they are divided between those who read and write the Book and thus distort it, and those who neither read nor write and know nothing of the Book except the lies of the distorters.

His saying, the Exalted: فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يَكْتُبُونَ "So woe to them who write..." (2:79). Woe (الويل) is perdition, severe punishment, grief, disgrace, humiliation, and everything that a human fears most intensely. "Purchasing" (الإشتراء) is buying (الابتياع).

His saying, the Exalted: فَوَيْلٌ لَّهُم مِّمَّا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَوَيْلٌ لَّهُمْ "So woe to them for what their hands have written, and woe to them..." (2:79). The pronouns refer either to the Children of Israel or specifically to the distorters among them; there is a basis for both. According to the first view, "woe" is established for the unlettered among them as well.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلَىٰ مَن كَسَبَ سَيِّئَةً وَأَحَاطَتْ بِهِ خَطِيئَتُهُ "Yes, whoever earns a misdeed and is besieged by his sin..." (2:81). Sin (الخطِيئة) is the state that occurs to the soul (النفس) from earning a misdeed (السيئة). For this reason, He mentioned the "besieging of the sin" after mentioning the "earning of the misdeed." The besieging of the sin (إحاطة الخطيئة) necessitates that the besieged person's path to salvation is cut off, as if guidance (الهداية)—due to the sin surrounding him—cannot find a way to him. Thus, he is among the inmates of the Fire, abiding therein forever. If there were any actual faith (الإيمان) in his heart, or if he possessed some traits and dispositions (الملكات) that do not repel the truth—such as fairness and submission to the truth, or the like—then guidance and felicity (السعادة) would have a possible entry point to him. Therefore, the besieging of the sin is not realized except through polytheism (الشرك), regarding which the Exalted said: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَاءُ "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes" (4:48); and on the other hand, through disbelief (الكفر) and denying the signs, as the Glorified said: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا أُولَٰئِكَ أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيهَا خَالِدُونَ "And those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]" (2:39). Thus, "earning a misdeed" and "the besieging of the sin" serve as a comprehensive expression for that which necessitates eternal residence in the Fire.

Know that these two verses are close in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هَادُوا وَالنَّصَارَىٰ وَالصَّابِئِينَ "Indeed the faithful, the Jews, the Christians and the Sabaeans..." (2:62). The difference is that these two verses—meaning His saying: "Yes, whoever earns a misdeed"—are in the context of explaining that the criterion for felicity is the reality of faith and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح), rather than mere claims. Whereas the two preceding verses—meaning His saying: "Indeed the faithful..."—are in the context of explaining that the criterion is the reality of faith and righteous deeds rather than being called by certain names.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma': Regarding His saying: وَإِذَا لَقُوا الَّذِينَ "When they meet those..." (2:76), narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), who said: "There was a group of Jews who were not among the obstinate conspirators; when they met the Muslims, they would speak to them of the description of Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم found in the Torah. Their leaders forbade them from this, saying: 'Do not inform them of the description of Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم in the Torah, lest they use it to contend with you before your Lord.' Then this verse was revealed."

In al-Kafi, narrated from one of the two Imams (peace be upon them) regarding His saying, the Exalted: بَلَىٰ مَن كَسَبَ سَيِّئَةً "Yes, whoever earns a misdeed" (2:81), he said: "When they deny the mastership (ولاية) of the Commander of the Faithful, then those are the inmates of the Fire, abiding therein forever."

I say: A meaning close to this was narrated by al-Shaykh in his Amali from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم. Both narrations are instances of applying a general principle to a specific instance (الجرى والتطبيق). The Glorified has counted mastership (الولاية) as a "good deed" (حسنة) in His saying: قُل لَّا أَسْأَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبَىٰ وَمَن يَقْتَرِفْ حَسَنَةً نَّزِدْ لَهُ فِيهَا حُسْنًا "Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it except love of [my] relatives.’ Whoever performs a good deed, We shall enhance its goodness for him" (42:23). It is possible that this is an interpretation (تفسير) of what will come in Surah al-Ma'idah: that it is the action according to the requirements of monotheism (التوحيد). It was attributed to Ali (peace be upon him) because he was the first from this community to open this door; so wait for it.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ لَا تَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَانًا وَذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَقُولُوا۟ لِلنَّاسِ حُسْنًا وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ ثُمَّ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّنكُمْ وَأَنتُم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٨٣)
When We took a pledge from the Children of Israel, [saying]: ‘Worship no one but Allah, do good to your parents, relatives, orphans, and the needy, speak kindly to people, maintain the prayer, and give the zakat,’ you turned away, except a few of you, and you were disregardful. (83)
وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَكُمْ لَا تَسْفِكُونَ دِمَآءَكُمْ وَلَا تُخْرِجُونَ أَنفُسَكُم مِّن دِيَـٰرِكُمْ ثُمَّ أَقْرَرْتُمْ وَأَنتُمْ تَشْهَدُونَ (٨٤)
And when We took a pledge from you, [saying]: ‘You shall not shed your [own people’s] blood, and you shall not expel your folks from your homes,’ you pledged, and you testify [to this pledge of your ancestors]. (84)
ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ تَقْتُلُونَ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَتُخْرِجُونَ فَرِيقًا مِّنكُم مِّن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ تَظَـٰهَرُونَ عَلَيْهِم بِٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ وَإِن يَأْتُوكُمْ أُسَـٰرَىٰ تُفَـٰدُوهُمْ وَهُوَ مُحَرَّمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ إِخْرَاجُهُمْ ۚ أَفَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِبَعْضِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَتَكْفُرُونَ بِبَعْضٍ ۚ فَمَا جَزَآءُ مَن يَفْعَلُ ذَٰلِكَ مِنكُمْ إِلَّا خِزْىٌ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰٓ أَشَدِّ ٱلْعَذَابِ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٨٥)
Then there you were, killing your folks and expelling a part of your folks from their homes, backing one another against them in sin and aggression! And if they came to you as captives, you would ransom them, though their expulsion itself was forbidden you. What! Do you believe in part of the Book and deny another part? So what is the requital of those of you who do that except disgrace in the life of this world? And on the Day of Resurrection, they shall be consigned to the severest punishment. And Allah is not oblivious of what you do. (85)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ فَلَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (٨٦)
They are the ones who bought the life of this world for the Hereafter; so their punishment shall not be lightened, nor will they be helped. (86)
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَقَفَّيْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ بِٱلرُّسُلِ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَا عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَأَيَّدْنَـٰهُ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ ۗ أَفَكُلَّمَا جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌۢ بِمَا لَا تَهْوَىٰٓ أَنفُسُكُمُ ٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ فَفَرِيقًا كَذَّبْتُمْ وَفَرِيقًا تَقْتُلُونَ (٨٧)
Certainly, We gave Moses the Book and followed him with the apostles, and We gave Jesus, the son of Mary, manifest proofs and confirmed him with the Holy Spirit. Is it not that whenever an apostle brought you that which was not to your liking, you would act arrogantly; so you would impugn a group [of them], and slay a[nother] group? (87)
وَقَالُوا۟ قُلُوبُنَا غُلْفٌۢ ۚ بَل لَّعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَقَلِيلًا مَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٨٨)
And they say, ‘Our hearts are uncircumcised.’ Rather, Allah has cursed them for their unfaith, so few of them have faith. (88)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَاقَ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ (And when We took a pledge from the Children of Israel): In its exquisite composition, the verse begins in the third person (الغيبة) and concludes in the second person (الخطاب) where it says: ثُمَّ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّنكُمْ وَأَنتُم مُّعْرِضُونَ (Then you turned away, except a few of you, and you were disregardful). Furthermore, it first mentions the pledge (الميثاق) — the taking of a solemn covenant — which can only occur through speech. It then relates the content of what the pledge was taken upon, beginning with a declarative statement (الخبر) where it says: لَا تَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا اللَّهَ (Do not worship [anyone] but Allah), and concluding with an imperative construction (الإنشاء) where it says: وَقُولُوا لِلنَّاسِ حُسْنًا (and speak kindly to people), etc.

Perhaps the reason for all of this is that the verses addressing the state of the Children of Israel began in the second person because they contained rebuke and censure. As the discourse proceeded accordingly, the context remained in the second person. Then, when the address shifted to the third person after the story of the Cow due to a specific subtle point (نكتة) necessitating it—as previously mentioned—until it reached this verse, it likewise began in the third person. However, since the pledge (الميثاق) was made through speech and its narration was intended, it was recounted in the second person, thus it was said: لَا تَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا اللَّهَ (Do not worship [anyone] but Allah), which is a prohibition (نهي) in the form of a declarative statement (الخبر). This was done to indicate the extreme importance of the matter, as if the One prohibiting does not doubt that the prohibited act will remain unactualized in external reality, nor does He suspect that the person bound by the pledge will fail to desist from what was forbidden; thus, the act will certainly not occur. Similarly, His saying: وَبِالْوَالِدَيْنِ إِحْسَانًا وَذِي الْقُرْبَىٰ وَالْيَتَامَىٰ وَالْمَسَاكِينِ (and [be good] to parents, the relatives, orphans, and the needy) is an entire command (أمر) in the form of a declarative statement.

Furthermore, the transition to the second person for the sake of the narration provided an opportunity to transition back to the original mode of address—which is the direct address to the Children of Israel—due to the continuity in His saying: وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكَاةَ ثُمَّ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ (and maintain the prayer and give the zakat. Then you turned away...), and thus the context became harmonized.

His saying, the Exalted: وَبِالْوَالِدَيْنِ إِحْسَانًا (and [be good] to parents): This is either a command or a declaration with the meaning of a command. The implied meaning is: "and show kindness (إحسانا) to parents," and to the relatives, orphans, and the needy. Or the implied meaning is: "and you shall show kindness to parents," etc. The objects of kindness have been arranged starting from the most important and closest to the important and more distant. A person's relatives are closer to him than others, and the parents—who are the root (الأصل) upon which the tree of his existence leans and stands—are closer than any other kin. Among those who are not relatives, the orphans (اليتامى) are more deserving of kindness due to their young age and their loss of those who would manage their affairs, more so than the needy (المساكين).

Regarding this, His saying: وَالْيَتَامَىٰ (and the orphans): An orphan (اليتيم) is one whose father has died; one whose mother has died is not called an orphan. It is said that "orphanhood" among humans is from the father's side, while in other animals, it is from the mother's side. His saying, the Exalted: وَالْمَسَاكِينِ (and the needy): This is the plural of miskin (مسكين), which refers to the poor person (الفقير) who is destitute and humbled. His saying, the Exalted: حُسْنًا (kindly/goodness): This is a verbal noun (مصدر) used with the meaning of an adjective, employed here for emphasis (المبالغة). In some recitations, it is read as hasanan (حَسَناً), with a fatha on the ha and sin, as an assimilated adjective (صفة مشبهة). The meaning is: "Speak to people a good word," which is an allusion (كناية) to good social conduct (حسن المعاشرة) with people, whether they be disbelievers or believers. This does not contradict the ruling of combat (القتال) such that the verse of combat would be considered an abrogation (ناسخة) of it; for the context of combat is distinct from the context of social conduct. Thus, the command for good conduct is not contradicted, just as harsh speech in the context of discipline (التأديب) does not contradict good conduct.

His saying, the Exalted: لَا تَسْفِكُونَ دِمَاءَكُمْ (You shall not shed your [own] blood): This is a declaration (خبر) with the meaning of an imperative (الإنشاء), similar to what was mentioned regarding His saying: لَا تَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا اللَّهَ (Do not worship [anyone] but Allah). Shedding (السفك) means pouring out.

His saying, the Exalted: تَظَاهَرُونَ عَلَيْهِم (backing each other up against them): Tazahur (التظاهر) is mutual assistance (التعاون). A zahir (الظهير) is an assistant (العون), derived from the word "back" (الظهر) because the assistant stands behind a person's back.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ مُحَرَّمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ إِخْرَاجُهُمْ (while their expulsion was forbidden to you): The pronoun (الضمير) refers to the matter or the story (للشأن والقصة), similar to His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هُوَ اللَّهُ أَحَدٌ (Say, ‘He is Allah, the One’).

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِبَعْضِ الْكِتَابِ (Do you believe in a part of the Book): Meaning, what is the difference between the expulsion and the ransom, such that you accepted the ruling of ransom but abandoned the ruling of expulsion, while both are in the Book? Do you believe in a part of the Book and disbelieve in another?

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَفَّيْنَا (and We followed him up): Taqfiyah (التقفية) is to make one follow another, as if one follows at the heels (قفأ) of another.

His saying, the Exalted: وَآتَيْنَا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ الْبَيِّنَاتِ (and We gave Jesus, son of Mary, manifest proofs): The discussion regarding this will come in Surah Al-Imran.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالُوا قُلُوبُنَا غُلْفٌ (And they say, ‘Our hearts are uncircumcised’): Ghulf (غلف) is the plural of aghlaf (أغلف), derived from ghilaf (الغلاف) — a cover or casing. It means: "Our hearts are preserved under wrappings, veils, and barriers." It is thus similar to His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالُوا قُلُوبُنَا فِي أَكِنَّةٍ مِّمَّا تَدْعُونَا إِلَيْهِ (And they say, ‘Our hearts are in veils from what you invite us to’) [Fussilat: 5]. This is an allusion (كناية) to the impossibility of them hearing what they are being invited toward.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَقُولُوا لِلنَّاسِ حُسْنًا (and speak kindly to people): قولوا للناس أحسن ما تحبون أن يقال فيكم — "Say to people the best of what you would love to be said about you."

Also in al-Kafi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: قولوا للناس ولا تقولوا الا خيراً حتى تعلموا ما هو — "Speak to people and say nothing but good until you know what it is."

In al-Ma'ani, narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), who said: قولوا للناس أحسن ما تحبون أن يقال لكم ، فإن الله عز وجل يبغض السباب اللعان الطعان على المؤمنين الفاحش المفحش السائل ويحب الحيي الحليم العفيف المتعفف — "Say to people the best of what you would love to be said to you; for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, hates the curser, the reviler, the one who slanders believers, the foul-mouthed, the indecent, and the persistent beggar, and He loves the modest, the clement, the chaste, and the one who restrains himself."

I say: A similar tradition is narrated in al-Kafi through another chain from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), and likewise by al-Ayyashi from him (peace be upon him). A tradition similar to the second is in al-Kafi from him. A tradition similar to the third is narrated by al-Ayyashi from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him). It seems these meanings were derived from the absolute nature of "goodness" (الحسن) according to the speaker and its absolute nature in terms of its application.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: ان الله بعث محمداً صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم بخمسة أسياف فسيف على أهل الذمة . قال الله : وقولوا للناس حسناً ، نزلت في أهل الذمة ثم نسختها أخرى قوله : قَاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ — "Indeed, Allah sent Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny) with five swords... and a sword against the People of the Dhimmah (أهل الذمة). Allah said: 'And speak kindly to people'; this was revealed regarding the People of the Dhimmah, then it was abrogated by another verse: 'Fight those who do not believe...'" (the tradition continues).

I say: This is an instance of him (peace be upon him) taking another absolute sense of "speech" (القول), which is its inclusion of both verbal speech and general treatment (التعرض). It is said: "Do not say to him anything but what is good and kind," meaning: "Do not treat him with anything but goodness and kindness, and do not touch him except with goodness and kindness." If the abrogation (النسخ) in his saying (peace be upon him) is abrogation in the specific sense (النسخ بالمعنى الأخص) — the technical term for the replacement of one legal ruling by another — then it is as stated. However, it is possible that what is meant is abrogation in the broader sense (النسخ بالمعنى الأعم), as will be discussed regarding His saying, the Exalted: مَا نَنسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنسِهَا (Any verse that We abrogate or cause to be forgotten) [Al-Baqarah: 106]. This usage is frequent in the speech of the Imams (peace be upon them), such that this verse and the verse of combat would not be identical in their subject of application.

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ كِتَـٰبٌ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَهُمْ وَكَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم مَّا عَرَفُوا۟ كَفَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ۚ فَلَعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٨٩)
And when there came to them a Book from Allah, confirming that which is with them—and earlier they would pray for victory over the pagans—so when there came to them what they recognized, they denied it. So may the curse of Allah be on the faithless! (89)
بِئْسَمَا ٱشْتَرَوْا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَنفُسَهُمْ أَن يَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ بَغْيًا أَن يُنَزِّلَ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ فَبَآءُو بِغَضَبٍ عَلَىٰ غَضَبٍ ۚ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (٩٠)
Evil is that for which they have sold their souls, by defying what Allah has sent down, out of envy that Allah should bestow His grace on any of His servants that He wishes. Thus, they earned wrath upon wrath, and there is a humiliating punishment for the faithless. (90)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ قَالُوا۟ نُؤْمِنُ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا وَيَكْفُرُونَ بِمَا وَرَآءَهُۥ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا مَعَهُمْ ۗ قُلْ فَلِمَ تَقْتُلُونَ أَنۢبِيَآءَ ٱللَّهِ مِن قَبْلُ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٩١)
And when they are told, ‘Believe in what Allah has sent down,’ they say, ‘We believe in what was sent down to us,’ and they disbelieve what is besides it, though it is the truth confirming what is with them. Say, ‘Then why would you kill the prophets of Allah formerly, should you be faithful?’ (91)
۞ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَكُم مُّوسَىٰ بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱتَّخَذْتُمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ وَأَنتُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ (٩٢)
Certainly Moses brought you manifest proofs, but then you took up the Calf in his absence and you were wrongdoers. (92)
وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَكُمْ وَرَفَعْنَا فَوْقَكُمُ ٱلطُّورَ خُذُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكُم بِقُوَّةٍ وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ ۖ قَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَعَصَيْنَا وَأُشْرِبُوا۟ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ بِكُفْرِهِمْ ۚ قُلْ بِئْسَمَا يَأْمُرُكُم بِهِۦٓ إِيمَـٰنُكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٩٣)
And when We took covenant with you and raised the Mount above you, [declaring], ‘Hold on with power to what We have given you, and do listen!’ They said, ‘We hear, and disobey,’ and their hearts had been imbued with [the love of] the Calf, due to their unfaith. Say, ‘Evil is that to which your faith prompts you, should you be faithful!’ (93)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: ولما جائهم (And when there came to them...) etc. The context indicates that "this Book" refers to the Quran.

And His saying: وكانوا من قبل يستفتحون على الذين كفروا (And before that they would pray for victory over the faithless). This refers to the occurrence of harassment against them by the Arab disbelievers, and that they used to seek victory (يستفتحون)—to pray for triumph over their enemies—through the advent of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and his migration. This seeking of victory had continued from them before the migration (الهجرة) to such an extent that the Arab disbelievers also knew of it from them, as indicated by the use of the past continuous "they used to" (كانوا). And His saying: فلما جائهم ما عرفوا (So when there came to them what they recognized). That is, they recognized that he was the one, due to the correspondence of the descriptions they possessed with him, yet they disbelieved.

His saying, the Exalted: بئسما إشتروا (Evil is that for which they have sold...) is an explanation of the reason for their disbelief after having knowledge, and that the sole reason for this was rebellion (البغي) and envy (الحسد). Thus, His saying بغياً (out of envy) is a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق نوعي) describing the motive. His saying أن ينزل الله (that Allah should send down) is connected to it. And His saying, the Exalted: فبائوا بغضب على غضب (So they drew [Allah’s] wrath upon wrath). That is, they returned accompanied by, or enveloped in, wrath due to their disbelief in the Quran, added upon the wrath due to their prior disbelief in the Torah (التوراة). The meaning is that before the advent and migration, they were supporters of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and seekers of victory through him and the Book revealed to him. Then, when the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) came to them and the Quran was revealed to him, and they recognized that he was the one through whom they used to seek victory and whose arrival they awaited, envy was stirred within them and arrogance (الإستكبار) seized them. Consequently, they disbelieved and denied what they used to mention, just as they used to disbelieve in the Torah before; thus, this act of theirs was disbelief upon disbelief.

His saying, the Exalted: ويكفرون بما ورائه (And they disbelieve in what is beyond it). That is, they manifest disbelief in what is beyond it; otherwise, they are also disbelievers in that which was revealed to them, namely the Torah.

His saying, the Exalted: قل فلم تقتلون (Say, ‘Why then did you kill...’). The letter fa (ف) denotes a logical derivation (التفريع). The question is derived from their claim: "We believe in what was revealed to us." That is, if your saying "We believe in what was revealed to us" were true and sincere, then why did you kill the prophets of Allah? And why did you disbelieve in Moses by taking the calf? And why did you say, upon the taking of the covenant and the raising of the Mount: "We hear and we disobey"?

His saying, the Exalted: واشربوا في قلوبهم العجل (And their hearts were made to drink [the love of] the calf). "Making to drink" (الإشراب) is to give water. What is meant by "the calf" is the love of the calf; it was placed in its stead for emphasis (المبالغة), as if they had been made to drink the calf itself. His saying "in their hearts" (في قلوبهم) relates to this. Thus, the speech contains two metaphors, or a metaphor and a metonymy.

His saying, the Exalted: قل بئسما يأمركم به إيمانكم (Say, ‘Evil is that which your faith commands you...’). This is in the position of drawing a conclusion from the arguments presented against them regarding the killing of prophets, disbelief in Moses, and arrogance in announcing disobedience. It carries a sense of mockery (الاستهزاء) toward them.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَلَمَّا جَاءَهُمْ كِتَابٌ مِّنْ عِندِ اللَّهِ مُصَدِّقٌ (And when there came to them a Book from Allah, confirming...) the verse. He (peace be upon him) said: "The Jews used to find in their books that the place of migration of Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), was between 'Ayr and Uhud. So they went out seeking that location. They passed by a mountain called Haddad and said: 'Haddad and Uhud are the same.' So they dispersed around it; some settled in Tayma, some in Fadak, and some in Khaybar. Those in Tayma yearned for some of their brothers, and a Bedouin from the tribe of Qays passed by them. They hired him, and he said to them: 'I will take you through the area between 'Ayr and Uhud.' They said to him: 'When you pass by them, inform us.' When he reached the middle of the land of Medina with them, he said: 'That is 'Ayr and this is Uhud.' They dismounted from his camels and said to him: 'We have found our objective, so we have no need for your camels; go wherever you wish.' They wrote to their brothers in Fadak and Khaybar: 'We have found the place, so come to us.' They wrote back: 'We have settled in our homes and acquired wealth, and we are close to you; so when that [event] occurs, we will hasten to you.' They acquired wealth in the land of Medina. When their wealth increased, news of this reached Tubba', so he raided them. They fortified themselves against him, and he besieged them, then he granted them amnesty. They came down to him, and he said to them: 'I have found your land pleasant, and I see myself staying among you.' They said: 'That is not for you; it is the place of migration of a Prophet, and it belongs to no one until that happens.' He said to them: 'Then I shall leave among you those of my family who, when that happens, will assist and support him.' So he left the two tribes you see: the Aws and the Khazraj. When they became numerous there, they would take the wealth of the Jews. The Jews would say to them: 'Indeed, when Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) is sent, we shall surely expel you from our homes and our wealth.' But when Allah sent Muhammad, the Ansar believed in him while the Jews disbelieved in him. This is [the meaning of] His saying, the Exalted: 'And before that they would pray for victory over the faithless...' to the end of the verse."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Abu Nu'aym (in al-Dala'il) narrated from Ibn Abbas that the Jews used to seek victory over the Aws and the Khazraj by the Messenger of Allah before his mission. When Allah sent him from among the Arabs, they disbelieved in him and denied what they used to say about him. Mu'adh ibn Jabal, Bishr ibn al-Bara, and Dawud ibn Salama said to them: "O assembly of Jews! Be God-conscious (اتقوا الله) and submit (أسلموا), for you used to seek victory over us through Muhammad when we were people of polytheism (شرك), and you would tell us that he would be sent and describe him by his attributes." Salam ibn Mishkam, one of the Banu al-Nadir, said: "He has not brought us anything we recognize, and he is not the one we used to mention to you." So Allah revealed: وَلَمَّا جَاءَهُمْ كِتَابٌ مِّنْ عِندِ اللَّهِ (And when there came to them a Book from Allah...) the verse.

Also in al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Nu'aym narrated in al-Dala'il via 'Ata and al-Dahhak from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Jews of Banu Qurayza and al-Nadir, before Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) was sent, used to seek victory from Allah, praying against those who disbelieved, saying: 'O Allah, we ask Your help by the right of the Unlettered Prophet (النبي الأمي) that You grant us victory over them,' and they would be granted victory. But when there came to them what they recognized—meaning Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم)—and they had no doubt about him, they disbelieved in him."

I say: Narrations close to these two meanings have been reported through other chains as well. Some commentators, after referring to the last narration and its likes, said: "Despite the weakness of its narrators and its contradiction to other reported narrations, it is anomalous (شاذة) in meaning by making the 'seeking of victory' (الاستفتاح) a supplication by the person of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), and in some [versions] 'by his right' (بحقه). This is not legislated, and no one has a right (حق) over Allah such that He should be supplicated by it." End of quote.

This [objection] arises from a lack of reflection on the meaning of "right" (الحق) and the meaning of an "oath" (القسم). To explain: An oath is the binding of a statement or an origination (الإنشاء) to something possessing nobility (شرافة) and dignity (كرامة) by virtue of its being noble or dignified, such that its nobility or dignity would be nullified by the nullification of the linguistic relation. If it is a statement (خبر), it is by the nullification of its truthfulness; if it is an origination—a command or prohibition—it is by the failure to comply with the obligation. If you say: "By my life (لعمري), Zayd is standing," you have bound the truth of your speech to the nobility of your life and existence, making it contingent upon it, such that if your statement were false, your life would be devoid of nobility. Similarly, if you say: "Do such-and-half, by my life," or "I adjure you by my life to do such-and-such," you have bound your command to the honor of your life, such that if your addressee does not comply, the honor of your life and the value of your existence would vanish.

From this, it becomes clear, firstly: that the oath is the highest level of emphasis (التأكيد) in speech, as mentioned by the people of literature.

Secondly: The object of the oath (المقسم به) must be more noble than that which it qualifies; there is no sense in emphasizing speech with something lower than it in honor and dignity. Allah, the Exalted, has sworn in His Book by His own Name and Attribute, such as His saying: وَاللَّهِ رَبِّنَا (By Allah, our Lord) and His saying: فَوَرَبِّكَ لَنَسْأَلَنَّهُمْ (By your Lord, We will surely question them) and His saying: فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ (By Your might, I will surely lead them all astray). He also swore by His Prophet, His angels, and His Books, and He swore by His creations such as the heaven, the earth, the sun, the moon, the stars, the night, the day, the mountains, the seas, the lands, man, trees, the fig, and the olive. This is only because they possess a true nobility (شرافة حقة) by Allah’s ennobling them, and a dignity (كرامة) before Allah, insofar as each of them is either a possessor of one of His holy and dignified attributes by the dignity of His transcendent Essence, or an act attributed to the Source of Splendor and Sanctity—and everything is noble by the nobility of His noble Essence. So what prevents a supplicant among us, when asking Allah for something, from asking Him by one of these things, insofar as Allah, the Glorified, has ennobled it and sworn by it? And what has made the matter so trivial specifically regarding the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) such that he is excluded from this universal principle and excepted from this general rule?

By my life (ولعمري), the Messenger of Allah, Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), is not more trivial before Allah than an Iraqi fig or a Syrian olive, and Allah has sworn by his noble person, saying: لَعَمْرُكَ إِنَّهُمْ لَفِي سَكْرَتِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (By your life, they were surely bewildered in their intoxication) [Al-Hijr: 72].

Furthermore, "the right" (الحق)—the opposite of which is "the false" (الباطل)—is that which is established and real (الثابت الواقع) in external reality insofar as it is so, such as the earth, man, and every matter established in itself. This includes financial rights and other social rights, as they are established from the perspective of society. The Quran has nullified everything claimed as a right except what Allah has realized and established, whether in creation (الإيجاد) or in legislation (التشريع). Thus, the right in the world of legislation and the domain of religious society is what Allah has made a right, such as financial rights, the rights of brothers, and the rights of parents over the child. He, the Glorified, is not subject to the judgment of anyone such that they could impose upon Him what He must fulfill, as might appear from some Mu'tazilite arguments. However, it is possible for Him to impose a right upon Himself—an imposition according to the language of legislation—such that it becomes a right for another upon Him, the Exalted, as He said: كَذَٰلِكَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنَا نُنجِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (Even so, it is a duty incumbent upon Us to deliver the faithful) [Yunus: 103], and He said: وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنَا لِعِبَادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ . إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنصُورُونَ . وَإِنَّ جُندَنَا لَهُمُ الْغَالِبُونَ (Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles: that they will indeed be the ones to be helped, and that Our host will indeed be the victors) [As-Saffat: 171-173].

Victory (النصر), as you see, is absolute, not restricted by anything. Thus, deliverance is a right for the believers upon Allah, and victory is a right for the Messenger upon Allah, the Exalted. Allah has ennobled this right by making it His own; thus, it was an act from Him, attributed to Him, and ennobled by Him. Therefore, there is no barrier to swearing by it to Allah, the Exalted, for He is the One who establishes and ennobles the right and swears by every noble matter.

Once you understand what we have mentioned, you will know that there is no barrier to Allah swearing by His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) or by the right of His Prophet, and likewise His swearing by His pure friends (أوليائه) or by their right, for He has established for them a right upon Himself to grant them victory on the path of felicity (صراط السعادة) with every victory related to it, as you have learned.

As for the saying of the objector: "No one has a right upon Allah," it is a weak statement.

Yes, there is no right upon Allah that another establishes against Him, such that He would be subject to the judgment of another or overcome by the power of someone else. No one disputes this. Nor does the supplicant call upon Him by a right that another has imposed upon Him, but rather by what He, the Exalted, has established for Himself through His promise that is never broken. This is the matter.

Ayahs

قُلْ إِن كَانَتْ لَكُمُ ٱلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَالِصَةً مِّن دُونِ ٱلنَّاسِ فَتَمَنَّوُا۟ ٱلْمَوْتَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٩٤)
Say, ‘If the abode of the Hereafter were exclusively for you with Allah, and not for other people, then long for death, should you be truthful.’ (94)
وَلَن يَتَمَنَّوْهُ أَبَدًۢا بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٩٥)
But they will not long for it ever because of what their hands have sent ahead, and Allah knows best the wrongdoers. (95)
وَلَتَجِدَنَّهُمْ أَحْرَصَ ٱلنَّاسِ عَلَىٰ حَيَوٰةٍ وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ ۚ يَوَدُّ أَحَدُهُمْ لَوْ يُعَمَّرُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ وَمَا هُوَ بِمُزَحْزِحِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ أَن يُعَمَّرَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ (٩٦)
Surely, you will find them the greediest for life, of all people even the idolaters. Each of them is eager to live a thousand years, though it would not deliver him from the punishment, were he to live [that long]. And Allah watches what they do. (96)
قُلْ مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِّجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُۥ نَزَّلَهُۥ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَهُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٩٧)
Say, ‘Whoever is an enemy of Gabriel [should know that] it is he who has brought it down on your heart with the will of Allah, confirming what has been [revealed] before it and as a guidance and good news for the faithful.’ (97)
مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِّلَّهِ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتِهِۦ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَجِبْرِيلَ وَمِيكَىٰلَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَدُوٌّ لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٩٨)
[Say,] ‘Whoever is an enemy of Allah, His angels and His apostles and Gabriel and Michael, [let him know that] Allah is the enemy of the faithless.’ (98)
وَلَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۖ وَمَا يَكْفُرُ بِهَآ إِلَّا ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (٩٩)
We have certainly sent down manifest signs to you, and no one denies them except transgressors. (99)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: "Say, 'If the abode of the Hereafter...'" etc. Since their saying, "The Fire shall not touch us except for a few numbered days," and their saying, "We believe in what was sent down to us," in response to what was said to them, "Believe in what Allah has sent down," both imply their claim that they alone are saved in the Hereafter to the exclusion of others, and that their salvation and felicity therein are untainted by destruction or misery—because they believe they will not be punished except for a few numbered days, namely the days they worshipped the calf—Allah the Exalted confronted them with a discourse that exposes the falsehood of their claims and shows that they know this without hesitation or doubt. Thus, the Exalted said to His Prophet: قُلْ إِن كَانَتْ لَكُمُ الدَّارُ الْآخِرَةُ "Say, 'If the abode of the Hereafter'"—meaning the felicity of that abode, for whoever owns an abode disposes of it as he deems good and pleasing, and resides in its most beautiful and felicitous parts. His saying, the Exalted: عِندَ اللَّهِ "with Allah"—meaning established with Him, the Exalted, by His judgment and permission; it is like His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِندَ اللَّهِ الْإِسْلَامُ "Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam" (3:19). His saying, the Exalted: خَالِصَةً "exclusively"—meaning untainted by what you dislike of punishment or humiliation, based on your claim that you will not be punished therein except for a few numbered days. His saying, the Exalted: مِّن دُونِ النَّاسِ "to the exclusion of [other] people"—this is due to your claim of the invalidity of every religion except yours. His saying, the Exalted: فَتَمَنَّوُا الْمَوْتَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ "then long for death should you be truthful." This is like His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ هَادُوا إِن زَعَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ أَوْلِيَاءُ لِلَّهِ مِن دُونِ النَّاسِ فَتَمَنَّوُا الْمَوْتَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ "Say, 'O Jews! If you claim that you are Allah’s favorites, to the exclusion of other people, then long for death, should you be truthful'" (62:6).

This is a holding to account based on an innate necessity (اللازم الفطري) whose effect is manifest in the external world such that not the slightest doubt can occur regarding it. It is that a human being—indeed, every conscious being—when given a choice between comfort and fatigue, chooses comfort without hesitation or vacillation. When given a choice between a life and existence that is murky and tainted and another that is exclusive and pure, he certainly chooses the pure and pleasant one. Even if he were supposedly afflicted with what he inclines away from—such as a miserable and wretched life or a distressed existence—he would never cease longing for the other good and pleasant one; his heart would never be free from yearning for it, his tongue from mentioning it, and his actions from striving toward it.

Therefore, if they were truthful in their claim that the exclusive felicity of the Hereafter belongs to them to the exclusion of other people, it would be mandatory for them to long for it in their hearts (جناناً), with their tongues (لساناً), and with their limbs (أركاناً). But they will never long for it, ever, because of what their hands have sent ahead—such as the killing of prophets, disbelieving in Moses, and breaking covenants. And Allah is All-Knowing of the wrongdoers (الظالمين).

His saying, the Exalted: بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ "because of what their hands have sent ahead" is a metonymy (كناية) for action (العمل), for most actions, as perceived by the senses, occur by means of the hand, and are then presented to the one who benefits from them or seeks them. There are two subtle points (عنايتان) here: attributing the "sending ahead" to the hands rather than the owners of the hands, and counting every act as a work of the hands.

In short, the actions of a human being—especially those that proceed from him continuously—are the best evidence of what is concealed in his conscience (ضميره) and rooted in his inner self (باطنه). Wicked deeds and foul actions only reveal a foul inner nature (طوية) that refuses to incline toward the meeting with Allah and residing in the abode of His friends.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَتَجِدَنَّهُمْ أَحْرَصَ النَّاسِ عَلَىٰ حَيَاةٍ "And you will surely find them the greediest of people for life" is like an explanatory proof for His saying, the Exalted: "And they will never long for it, ever." That is, what bears witness to the fact that they will never long for death is that they are the greediest of people for this worldly life; nothing veils or prevents the longing for the abode of the Hereafter except greed for this world and clinging to it. The use of the indefinite form (التنكير) in His saying: عَلَىٰ حَيَاةٍ "for a life" is for disparagement (التحقير), as He, the Exalted, said: وَمَا هَٰذِهِ الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوَانُ لَوْ كَانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ "The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life (الحيوان), had they known" (29:64).

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا "and [greedier] than the polytheists." It appears that this is coordinated (عطف) with "the people" (الناس), and the meaning is: "And you will surely find them greedier than the polytheists."

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا هُوَ بِمُزَحْزِحِهِ مِنَ الْعَذَابِ أَن يُعَمَّرَ "but it will not distance him from the punishment that he should be granted a [long] life." It appears that مَا is negating (نافية). The pronoun هُوَ (it) either refers to the matter and the story (للشأن والقصة), in which case أَن يُعَمَّرَ (that he be granted life) is the subject (مبتدأ) and its predicate (خبر) is بِمُزَحْزِحِهِ (distancing him); or it refers back to what is indicated by His saying: "one of them desires," meaning: "and that which he desires will not distance him from the punishment," and His saying أَن يُعَمَّرَ is an explanation (بيان) for it. The meaning of the verse is: They will never long for death, and I swear you will surely find them the greediest of people for this lowly, wretched life that diverts from that felicitous, good life. Rather, you find them greedier for life than the polytheists (الذين أشركوا) who do not believe in a resurrection (بعثاً) or a rising (نشوراً). One of them desires to be granted the longest of lives, yet the longest of lives will not distance him from the punishment, because life—when compared to the Hereafter—is limited and reaches a term and an appointed time (أجل).

His saying, the Exalted: يَوَدُّ أَحَدُهُمْ لَوْ يُعَمَّرُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ "Each of them is eager to live a thousand years"—meaning the longest and most extensive life. Thus, "a thousand" is a metonymy for a great number; it is the final rank of numbers in terms of individual word formation (الوضع الافرادي) among the Arabs, and anything beyond it is expressed through repetition and composition, such as ten thousand, a hundred thousand, and a thousand thousand.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ "and Allah sees best what they do." The All-Seeing (البصير) is one of His Beautiful Names, and its meaning is knowledge of things seen (المبصرات); thus, it is a branch of the name the All-Knowing (العليم).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِّجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ "Say, 'Whoever is an enemy to Gabriel, then it is he who has brought it down on your heart...'" etc. The context indicates that the verse was revealed as a response to what the Jews said, and that they refused and disdained to believe in what was sent down to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, justifying this by saying they were enemies of Gabriel, who brought the revelation (الوحي) to him. The evidence for this is that Allah, the Glorified, answers them in the Quran regarding both the Quran and Gabriel in the two verses, and the reports concerning the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) support this. He answered their saying, "We do not believe in the Quran because of our enmity toward Gabriel who brought it down," with several points: First: Gabriel only brought it down upon your heart by Allah's permission, not from himself; thus, their enmity toward Gabriel should not necessitate their turning away from a speech sent down by Allah's permission. Second: The Quran confirms (مصدق) what is in their hands of the true Book, and it makes no sense to believe in one thing while disbelieving in that which confirms it. Third: The Quran is a guidance (هدى) for the believers. Fourth: It is a glad tiding (بشرى), and how can it be right for a rational person to deviate from guidance and ignore glad tidings, even if the one who brought them were his enemy?

He answered their saying, "We are enemies of Gabriel," by stating that Gabriel is an angel among the angels who has no role except to obey what Allah, the Glorified, commands him, just like Michael and the rest of the angels. They are honored servants who do not disobey Allah in what He commands them and they do what they are commanded. Likewise, the messengers of Allah have no role except through Allah and from Allah, the Glorified. Thus, hating them and treating them as enemies is hating and treating Allah as an enemy. Whoever is an enemy to Allah, His angels, His messengers, Gabriel, and Michael—then indeed Allah is an enemy to them. The two verses point to these two answers.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ "then it is he who has brought it down on your heart." There is a shift (إلتفات) here from the first person to the second person address. It would have been expected to say "on my heart," but it was changed to the second person address to indicate that just as Gabriel has no independent role in sending it down—being merely a commanded and obedient servant—likewise the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم has no independent role in receiving and conveying it, except that his heart is a vessel for the revelation (الوحي) over which he has no personal control, and he is commanded to convey it.

Know that these verses contain, in their concluding parts, various types of shifts (إلتفات), even though the basis in them is the address to the Children of Israel. However, when the address is one of blame and reproach and the speech becomes lengthy, the situation becomes one of weariness (إستملال) in speaking with the addressee and a belittling of his status. It is then appropriate for the eloquent speaker to turn away from the direct address time after time through shift after shift, to indicate that He is not pleased with addressing them due to their poor listening and the baseness of their souls, yet He is not pleased with leaving the address entirely, so as to manifest the right of judgment against them.

His saying, the Exalted: عَدُوٌّ لِّلْكَافِرِينَ "an enemy to the faithless." Here, a manifest noun (الظاهر) is placed in the position of a pronoun (المضمر). The subtle point (النكتة) in this is to indicate the cause of the judgment (علة الحكم), as if it were said: "For Allah is an enemy to them because they are disbelievers, and Allah is an enemy to the disbelievers."

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا يَكْفُرُ بِهَا إِلَّا الْفَاسِقُونَ "and none defy them except the transgressors." This indicates the cause of disbelief (الكفر), which is transgression (الفسق); thus, they are transgressors because of their disbelief. It is not unlikely that the definite article (اللام) in His saying: الْفَاسِقُونَ "the transgressors" refers to a previous mention (للعهد الذكري), pointing to what preceded in the beginning of the Surah in His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفَاسِقِينَ الَّذِينَ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَ اللَّهِ مِن بَعْدِ مِيثَاقِهِ "and He misleads by it none but the transgressors—those who break the covenant of Allah after its pledge" (2:26-27).

As for the discussion regarding Gabriel and the manner of his bringing down the Quran upon the heart of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, as well as the discussion regarding Michael and the angels, it will come in its appropriate place, Allah willing.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Say, 'Whoever is an enemy to Gabriel...'" the two verses, Ibn Abbas said: The reason for the revelation of the verse was what is narrated: When the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم arrived in Medina, Ibn Suriya and a group of Jews from the people of Fadak came and asked him, saying: "O Muhammad, how is your sleep? For we have been informed about the sleep of the Prophet who comes at the end of time."

He said: تنام عيناي وقلبي يقظان — "My eyes sleep, but my heart is awake." They said: "You have spoken the truth, O Muhammad. Now tell us about the child: is it from the man or the woman?" He said: "As for the bones, nerves, and veins, they are from the man; as for the flesh, blood, nails, and hair, they are from the woman." They said: "You have spoken the truth, O Muhammad. Why is it that a child resembles his paternal uncles and has nothing of the resemblance of his maternal uncles? Or resembles his maternal uncles and has nothing of the resemblance of his paternal uncles?" He said: "Whichever of the two fluids prevails, the resemblance belongs to it." They said: "You have spoken the truth, O Muhammad. Now tell us about your Lord, what is He?" So Allah, the Glorified, sent down: قُلْ هُوَ اللَّهُ أَحَدٌ "Say, 'He is Allah, the One'" to the end of the Surah. Then Ibn Suriya said to him: "One trait remains; if you tell it, I will believe in you and follow you. Which angel comes to you with what Allah sends down to you?" He said: "Gabriel." He said: "That is our enemy; he brings fighting, hardship, and war. Michael brings ease and prosperity. If it were Michael who came to you, we would have believed in you."

I say: His saying: تنام عيناي وقلبي يقظان — "My eyes sleep, but my heart is awake" has been narrated extensively (استفاض) by both the general (Sunni) and the specific (Shi'i) sources—that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم would have his eyes sleep while his heart did not sleep. Its meaning is that he was not heedless of himself during sleep; while in sleep, he knew that he was sleeping and that what he was seeing was a vision (رؤيا) he was seeing, not wakefulness. This is something that occasionally happens to the righteous when their souls are pure and occupied with the remembrance of their Lord's station. This is because the soul's oversight of its Lord's station does not leave it heedless of its mode of worldly life and the nature of its attachment to its Lord. This is a type of witnessing (مشاهدة) that clarifies to a human being that he is in the world of this life in a state identical to sleep—both the sleep that people see as only sleep, and the wakefulness that people see as wakefulness—and that people, while they are secluded at the door of the senses and clinging to the earth of nature, are asleep even if they consider themselves awake. It is narrated from Ali عليه‌السلام: الناس نيام فإذا ماتوا إنتبهوا — "People are asleep; when they die, they wake up." A more complete treatment of this discussion, as well as the discussion of the other segments of this hadith, will come in appropriate places in this book, Allah willing.

Ayahs

أَوَكُلَّمَا عَـٰهَدُوا۟ عَهْدًا نَّبَذَهُۥ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُم ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٠٠)
Is it not that whenever they made a covenant, a part of them would cast it away? Rather, the majority of them do not have faith. (100)
وَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَهُمْ نَبَذَ فَرِيقٌ مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ كِتَـٰبَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَآءَ ظُهُورِهِمْ كَأَنَّهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٠١)
And when there came to them an apostle from Allah, confirming that which is with them, a part of those who were given the Book cast the Book of Allah behind their back, as if they did not know [that it is Allah’s Book]. (101)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: "flung it aside" (نَبَذَهُ). To fling aside (النبذ) means to cast away or discard.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا جَاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ "And when there came to them an apostle" — the intended meaning here is the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), not every messenger who came to them confirming what was with them. This is because the phrasing "And when there came to them" (ولما جائهم) does not denote continuity or repetition, but rather indicates a single, specific occurrence. The verse refers to their opposition to the truth (الحق) manifested in their concealment of the glad tidings found in the Torah (التوراة) and their refusal to believe in the one who confirms what is already with them.

Ayahs

وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ مَا تَتْلُوا۟ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينُ عَلَىٰ مُلْكِ سُلَيْمَـٰنَ ۖ وَمَا كَفَرَ سُلَيْمَـٰنُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُعَلِّمُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ ٱلسِّحْرَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَى ٱلْمَلَكَيْنِ بِبَابِلَ هَـٰرُوتَ وَمَـٰرُوتَ ۚ وَمَا يُعَلِّمَانِ مِنْ أَحَدٍ حَتَّىٰ يَقُولَآ إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ فِتْنَةٌ فَلَا تَكْفُرْ ۖ فَيَتَعَلَّمُونَ مِنْهُمَا مَا يُفَرِّقُونَ بِهِۦ بَيْنَ ٱلْمَرْءِ وَزَوْجِهِۦ ۚ وَمَا هُم بِضَآرِّينَ بِهِۦ مِنْ أَحَدٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَيَتَعَلَّمُونَ مَا يَضُرُّهُمْ وَلَا يَنفَعُهُمْ ۚ وَلَقَدْ عَلِمُوا۟ لَمَنِ ٱشْتَرَىٰهُ مَا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنْ خَلَـٰقٍ ۚ وَلَبِئْسَ مَا شَرَوْا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ (١٠٢)
And they followed what the devils pursued during Solomon’s reign —and Solomon was not faithless but it was the devils who were faithless—teaching the people magic and what was sent down to the two angels at Babylon, Harut and Marut, who would not teach anyone without telling [him], ‘We are only a test, so do not be faithless.’ But they would learn from those two that with which they would cause a split between man and his wife—though they could not harm anyone with it except with Allah’s leave. They would learn that which would harm them and bring them no benefit; though they certainly knew that anyone who buys it has no share in the Hereafter. Surely, evil is that for which they sold their souls, had they known! (102)
وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟ لَمَثُوبَةٌ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ ۖ لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ (١٠٣)
Had they been faithful and Godwary, the reward from Allah would have been better, had they known! (103)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: "And they followed what the devils pursued during the reign of Solomon..." to the end of the verse. The commentators have differed in the interpretation of this verse with a strange divergence, the like of which is hardly found in any other verse of the Majestic Quran. They differed regarding the referent of the pronoun in His saying: "And they followed" (واتبعوا) — the act of adopting and adhering to a path or practice. Are they the Jews who lived during the time of Solomon, or those during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), or all of them? They differed regarding His saying: "pursued" (تتلوا) — to follow, recite, or fabricate. Is it in the sense that the devils followed it and acted upon it, or in the sense of reciting, or in the sense of lying? They differed regarding His saying: "the devils" (الشياطين). It was said they are the devils of the jinn (الجن), and it was said they are the devils of men (الإنس), and it was said they are both together. They differed regarding His saying: "during the reign of Solomon" (على ملك سليمان). It was said it means "in" the reign of Solomon, and it was said it means "during the era" of Solomon's reign, and it was said it means "against" the reign of Solomon—preserving the literal sense of superiority in the meaning of "upon" (على) — and it was said it means "upon the era" of Solomon's reign. They differed regarding His saying: "but the devils disbelieved" (ولكن الشياطين كفروا). It was said they disbelieved by what they extracted of sorcery (السحر) — the use of hidden forces to produce supernatural effects — to the people; it was said they disbelieved by what they attributed to Solomon of sorcery; and it was said they practiced sorcery, so sorcery was expressed as disbelief (الكفر). They differed regarding His saying: "teaching the people sorcery" (يعلمون الناس السحر). It was said they cast sorcery to them and they learned it; it was said they directed the people to the extraction of sorcery which was buried under Solomon's throne, so they extracted it and learned it. They differed regarding His saying: "and what was sent down to the two angels" (وما أنزل على الملكين). It was said that "what" (ما) is a relative pronoun and the conjunction is to His saying: "what they pursued" (ما تتلوه); it was said "what" is a relative pronoun and the conjunction is to His saying: "sorcery" (السحر), meaning they teach them what was sent down to the two angels; and it was said "what" is a negating particle and the "and" (الواو) is for commencement, meaning: "and sorcery was not sent down to the two angels as the Jews claim." They differed regarding the meaning of "sending down" (الإنزال). It was said it was a sending down from the heavens, and it was said rather from the highlands and elevations of the earth. They differed regarding His saying: "the two angels" (الملكين). It was said they were two of the angels of heaven; it was said rather they were two human kings (مَلِكَيْن) with a kasrah on the lam if we read it as such, as it was read in the irregular recitations; or two angels (مَلَكَيْن) with a fathah on the lam, meaning two righteous men, or two who feigned righteousness, if we read it according to the well-known recitation. They differed regarding His saying: "at Babylon" (ببابل). It was said it is Babylon of Iraq; it was said Babylon of Damavand; and it was said from Nasibin to Ra's al-Ayn. They differed regarding His saying: "and they did not teach" (وما يعلمان). It was said "taught" (علّم) in its literal sense, and it was said "taught" in the sense of "informed." They differed regarding His saying: "so do not disbelieve" (فلا تكفر). It was said: "do not disbelieve by practicing sorcery"; it was said: "do not disbelieve by learning it"; and it was said by both together.

They differed regarding His saying: "so they learn from them" (فيتعلمون منهما). It was said: meaning from Harut and Marut; it was said: meaning from sorcery and disbelief; and it was said: as a substitute for what the two angels taught by way of prohibition against doing it. They differed regarding His saying: "that by which they cause a separation between a man and his wife" (ما يفرقون به بين المرء وزوجه). It was said: meaning they create love and hatred between them; it was said they entice one of the spouses and incite them toward disbelief and polytheism (الشرك), so the difference in creed and religion separates them; and it was said they strive between them with slander and malicious gossip, which leads to separation. This is a portion of the differences in the interpretation of the words and sentences of the verse containing the story. There are other differences outside the story in the conclusion of the verse and in the story itself: is it a factual story or an explanation by way of parable? Or something else? If you multiply some of the numbers of possibilities we mentioned by others, the possibilities rise to an astonishing amount, nearly one million two hundred and sixty thousand possibilities (4 x 39 x 24)!

By the life of Allah, this is among the wonders of the Quran's composition (نظم) — the precise arrangement and structural harmony of the text. The verse fluctuates between doctrines and possibilities that baffle the intellects and bewilder the minds, yet the speech remains reclining upon the throne of its beauty, adorned in its most exquisite splendor, embellished with the ornaments of its eloquence (البلاغة) and lucidity (الفصاحة). A similar instance to this verse will come before you in His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمَن كَانَ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِ وَيَتْلُوهُ شَاهِدٌ مِّنْهُ وَمِن قَبْلِهِ كِتَابُ مُوسَىٰ إِمَامًا وَرَحْمَةً "Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord, and whom a witness from Him follows, and before him was the Book of Moses, a guide and a mercy..." (Hud: 17).

What ought to be said is that the verse, by its context, addresses another affair among the affairs of the Jews, which is the circulation of sorcery among them, and that they used to base its origin on a well-known story or two stories known to them, in which there is mention of the matter of Solomon the Prophet and the two angels at Babylon, Harut and Marut. Thus, the speech is conjoined to the story they possess and claim. However, the Jews—as the Quran mentions of them—are a people of distortion (تحريف) and alteration (تغيير) regarding knowledge and truths. One cannot trust, nor is it safe from their conduct, that they would present historical stories without distorting and altering them, as is their habit with knowledge; they incline at every moment toward what suits their interests in word and deed. What appears from the folds of the verse's sentences is sufficient. In any case, it appears from the verse that the Jews were practicing sorcery among themselves, attributing it to Solomon, claiming that Solomon (عليه‌السلام) only possessed the kingdom and subjugated the jinn, humans, wild beasts, and birds, and performed wondrous and miraculous feats through sorcery, which is part of what was in their hands. They attributed another part of it to the two angels at Babylon, Harut and Marut. So the Quran refuted them, stating that Solomon (عليه‌السلام) did not practice sorcery. How could he, when sorcery is disbelief in Allah and an interference in the universe contrary to the habit Allah established, manifesting it upon the imagination and senses of living beings? Solomon (عليه‌السلام) did not disbelieve, for he was an infallible prophet (نبي معصوم).
