This is His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا كَفَرَ سُلَيْمَانُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ الشَّيَاطِينَ كَفَرُوا يُعَلِّمُونَ النَّاسَ السِّحْرَ "Solomon did not faithlessly contend, but it was the devils who were faithless, teaching the people magic..." and His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ عَلِمُوا لَمَنِ اشْتَرَاهُ مَا لَهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنْ خَلَاقٍ "...And they certainly knew that whoever buys it has no share in the Hereafter." Solomon (عليه‌السلام) is of a higher standing and more sacred presence than to have sorcery and disbelief attributed to him. Allah has magnified his status in several places of His speech in various Meccan surahs revealed before this surah, such as Surah al-An'am, al-Anbiya, al-Naml, and Surah Sad. In these, it is mentioned that he was a righteous servant and a sent prophet to whom Allah gave knowledge and wisdom, and bestowed upon him a kingdom that would not belong to anyone after him. Thus, he was not a sorcerer; rather, these are superstitious stories and myths (الأساطير) which the devils fabricated and "pursued" (تلوها) — recited — to their allies among men, and they disbelieved by misleading the people through the teaching of sorcery. The Quran also refuted them regarding the two angels at Babylon, Harut and Marut, stating that even if that was sent down to them, there is no harm in that, for it was a trial (فتنة) and a divine test (امتحان), just as He inspired the hearts of the children of Adam with the ways of evil and corruption as a trial and test, and it is part of the Decree (القدر). So, although sorcery was sent down to them, they would not teach anyone without saying to him: "We are only a trial, so do not disbelieve" by using the sorcery you learn in an improper place, such as for nullifying sorcery or exposing the transgression of its practitioners. Yet, they would learn from them that by which they corrupt the most righteous thing Allah placed in nature and habit, separating thereby between a man and his wife, seeking evil and corruption, and learning what harms them and does not benefit them.

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: "And they followed" (واتبعوا) means the Jews who came after the era of Solomon followed, through inheritance from predecessors to successors, "what they pursued" (ما تتلوا), meaning what the devils among the jinn fabricated and lied about "during the reign of Solomon." The evidence that "pursued" (تتلوا) is in the sense of "lied" is its being made transitive with the preposition "against" (على). The evidence that the devils are the jinn is that these were under the subjugation of Solomon and punished by his punishment; by that, he (عليه‌السلام) would restrain them from corruption. The Exalted said: وَمِنَ الشَّيَاطِينِ مَن يَغُوصُونَ لَهُ وَيَعْمَلُونَ عَمَلًا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ وَكُنَّا لَهُمْ حَافِظِينَ "And of the devils were some who dived for him and performed other tasks besides that, and We were watchful over them" (al-Anbiya: 82). And the Exalted said: فَلَمَّا خَرَّ تَبَيَّنَتِ الْجِنُّ أَن لَّوْ كَانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ الْغَيْبَ مَا لَبِثُوا فِي الْعَذَابِ الْمُهِينِ "...when he fell down, the jinn realized that had they known the Unseen, they would not have remained in a humiliating punishment" (Saba: 14).

His saying, the Exalted: "Solomon did not disbelieve," meaning while the situation was that Solomon did not practice sorcery such that he would disbelieve, "but the devils disbelieved," while the situation was that they were misleading the people and teaching them sorcery.

His saying, the Exalted: "And what was sent down," meaning the Jews followed what was sent down by way of notification and inspiration (الإلهام) — the divine casting of an idea into the heart — to the two angels at Babylon, Harut and Marut, while the situation was that they would not teach sorcery to anyone until they warned him against practicing it, saying: "We are only a trial for you and a test by which you are tested through what we teach you, so do not disbelieve by using it."

His saying, the Exalted: "So they learn from them," meaning from the two angels, Harut and Marut, "that by which they cause a separation," meaning a sorcery by whose practice and effect they separate between a man and his wife.

His saying, the Exalted: "But they do not harm anyone with it except by Allah's permission." This is a rebuttal to what might occur to the imagination (الوهم) — the faculty that perceives particular meanings — that they, by that, corrupt the order of creation and existence (التكوين), outstrip the decree of Allah, and nullify His command. He refuted this by stating that sorcery itself is part of the Decree (القدر); it does not affect except by Allah's permission, so they cannot frustrate Him. He placed this sentence before His saying: "And they learn what harms them and does not benefit them," because this sentence—meaning: "So they learn from them that by which they cause a separation between a man and his wife"—alone contains the mention of the effect, so it was followed by the statement that this effect is by Allah's permission.

His saying, the Exalted: "And they certainly knew that whoever buys it has no share in the Hereafter." They knew that by their intellects (العقول), for the intellect does not doubt that sorcery is the most ominous source of corruption in human society. They also knew that from the words of Moses, for he is the one who said: وَلَا يُفْلِحُ السَّاحِرُ حَيْثُ أَتَىٰ "...and the magician does not succeed wherever he may come" (Taha: 69).

His saying, the Exalted: "Surely evil is what they sold themselves for, had they known," meaning that despite their knowing it to be an evil for them and a corrupter of their Hereafter, they were as if they did not know it, since they did not act upon what they knew. For knowledge (العلم), if it does not guide its possessor to the straight path (الصراط المستقيم), is misguidance and ignorance, not knowledge. The Exalted said: أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلَٰهَهُ هَوَاهُ وَأَضَلَّهُ اللَّهُ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ "Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god and whom Allah has led astray knowingly..." (al-Jathiyah: 23). Thus, these people, despite their knowledge of the matter, are such that one should wish for them knowledge and guidance.

His saying, the Exalted: "And had they believed and been God-conscious," etc., meaning had they followed faith (الإيمان) and godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah's presence and avoiding His displeasure — instead of following the myths of the devils and the disbelief of sorcery. In this is evidence that disbelief through sorcery is disbelief at the level of action (العمل), like the abandonment of zakat (الزكاة), not disbelief at the level of belief (الاعتقاد). Had sorcery been disbelief in belief, the Exalted would have said: "And had they believed, a reward..." etc., and restricted it to faith without mentioning godwariness. The Jews believed, but since they were not godwary and did not observe the prohibitions of Allah, He did not give weight to their faith, and they were as disbelievers.

His saying, the Exalted: "a reward from Allah would have been better, had they known," meaning better than the rewards and benefits they seek through sorcery and acquire through disbelief.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi and al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "And they followed what the devils pursued during the reign of Solomon," narrated from Imam al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام) in a tradition: فلما هلك سليمان وضع إبليس السحر وكتبه في كتاب ثم طواه وكتب على ظهره ، هذا ما وضع آصف بن برخيا للملك سليمان بن داود من ذخائر كنوز العلم من أراد كذا وكذا فليعمل كذا وكذا ثم دفنه تحت سريره ثم استتاره لهم فقرأه فقال الكافرون : ما كان يغلبنا سليمان إلا بهذا ، وقال المؤمنون : بل هو عبد الله ونبيه ، فقال الله جل ذكره : وإتبعوا ما تتلوا الشياطين على ملك سليمان — "When Solomon died, Iblis fabricated sorcery and wrote it in a book, then rolled it up and wrote on its back: 'This is what Asaf ibn Barkhiya placed for King Solomon son of David of the treasures of the hoards of knowledge; whoever wants such-and-such, let him do such-and-such.' Then he buried it under his throne, then uncovered it for them and read it. The disbelievers said: 'Solomon only used to overcome us with this.' The believers said: 'Rather, he is the servant of Allah and His prophet.' Then Allah, Majestic is His mention, said: 'And they followed what the devils pursued during the reign of Solomon.'"

I say: Attributing the fabrication, writing, and reading to Iblis does not contradict its attribution to the rest of the devils among the jinn and men, for all evil terminates in him and spreads from him—may Allah curse him—to his allies through inspiration (الوحي) and whispering (الوسوسة). This is common in the language of the narrations (الأخبار). The literal sense of the tradition is that the word "pursued" (تتلوه) is from "recitation" (التلاوة) in the sense of reading. This does not contradict what we deemed apparent in the previous commentary: that "pursued" (تتلو) is in the sense of "lied," because the implication of the meaning of lying is through inclusion (تضمين) or something similar. The estimation of His saying: "the devils pursued during the reign of Solomon" is: "they read it, lying against the reign of Solomon." The root of the meaning of "pursued" (تلا يتلو) returns to the meaning of "followed" (ولى يلى) and "guardianship" (الولاية), which is to possess a thing in terms of order and the occurrence of one part of it after another. The discussion on this will come in Surah al-Ma'idah in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا وَلِيُّكُمُ اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ "Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle..." (al-Ma'idah: 55).

In al-Uyun, in a tradition of Imam al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) with al-Ma'mun: وأما هاروت وماروت فكانا ملكين علما الناس السحر ليتحرزوا به عن سحر السحرة ويبطلوا كيدهم وما علما أحداً من ذلك شيئاً إلا قالا له إنما نحن فتنة فلا تكفر فكفر قوم باستعمالهم لما امروا بالاحتراز عنه وجعلوا يفرقون بما يعملونه بين المرء وزوجه ، قال الله تعالى : وما هم بضارين به من أحد إلا بإذن الله — "As for Harut and Marut, they were two angels who taught the people sorcery so they might guard themselves thereby against the sorcery of the sorcerers and nullify their plot. They did not teach anyone anything of that except that they said to him: 'We are only a trial, so do not disbelieve.' But a people disbelieved by their use of what they were commanded to guard against, and they began to separate, by what they practiced, between a man and his wife. Allah the Exalted said: 'But they do not harm anyone with it except by Allah's permission.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: كان سليمان إذا أراد أن يدخل الخلاء أو يأتي شيئاً من شأنه أعطى الجرادة وهي إمرأته خاتمه فلما أراد الله أن يبتلى سليمان بالذي ابتلاه به أعطى الجرادة ذلك اليوم خاتمه فجاء الشيطان في صورة سليمان فقال لها : هاتي خاتمي فأخذه ولبسه فلما لبسه دانت له شياطين الجن والإنس فجائها سليمان فقال : هاتي خاتمي فقالت كذبت لست سليمان فعرف أنه بلاء ابتلى به فانطلقت الشياطين فكتبت في تلك الأيام كتباً فيها سحر وكفر ثم دفنوها تحت كرسي سليمان ثم أخرجوها فقرئوها على الناس فقالوا إنما كان سليمان يغلب الناس بهذه الكتب فبرء الناس من سليمان واكفروه حتى بعث الله محمداً وأنزل عليه : وما كفر سليمان ولكن الشياطين كفروا — "Whenever Solomon wanted to enter the privy or attend to some of his affairs, he would give al-Jaradah—who was his wife—his ring. When Allah willed to test Solomon with that which He tested him, he gave al-Jaradah his ring that day. Then the devil came in the form of Solomon and said to her: 'Give me my ring.' He took it and wore it. When he wore it, the devils of the jinn and men became subservient to him. Then Solomon came to her and said: 'Give me my ring.' She said: 'You have lied, you are not Solomon.' So he knew it was a trial he was tested with. The devils then went and wrote, during those days, books containing sorcery and disbelief, then buried them under Solomon's throne, then extracted them and read them to the people. They said: 'Solomon only used to overcome the people with these books.' So the people washed their hands of Solomon and deemed him a disbeliever until Allah sent Muhammad and revealed to him: 'Solomon did not disbelieve, but it was the devils who disbelieved.'"

I say: The story is narrated in other reports; it is a long story among the stories mentioned regarding the "slips of the prophets."

In al-Durr al-Manthur as well, Sa'id ibn Jarir and al-Khatib in his Tarikh narrated from Nafi', who said: سافرت مع ابن عمر فلما كان في آخر الليل ، قال يا نافع : انظر هل طلعت الحمراء ؟ قلت : لا ، مرتين أو ثلثاً ثم قلت : قد طلعت . قال : لا مرحباً بها ولا أهلاً . قلت : سبحان الله نجم مسخر سامع مطيع . قال ما قلت لك إلا ما سمعت من رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم . قال : إن الملائكة قالت : يا رب كيف صبرك على بني آدم في الخطايا والذنوب ؟ قال : إني أبليتهم وعافيتهم . قالوا لو كنا مكانهم ما عصيناك ، قال : فاختاروا ملكين منكم ، فلم يألوا جهداً أن يختاروا فاختاروا هاروت وماروت فنزلا ، فألقى الله عليهما الشبق . قلت : وما الشبق ؟ قال : الشهوة فجائت امرأة يقال لها الزهرة فوقعت في قلوبهما فجعل كل واحد منهما يخفى عن صاحبه ما في نفسه ثم قال أحدهما للآخر هل وقع في نفسك ما وقع في قلبي ؟ قال : نعم ، فطالباها لأنفسهما فقالت لا امكنكما حتى تعلماني الاسم الذي تعرجان به إلى السماء وتهبطان فأبيا ثم سئلاها أيضاً فأبت . ففعلا فلما استطيرت طمسها الله كوكباً وقطع أجنحتهما ثم سألا التوبة من ربهما فخيرهما فقال ان شئتما رددتكما إلى ما كنتما عليه ، فإذا كان يوم القيامة عذبتكما ، وان شئتما عذبتكما في الدنيا فإذا كان يوم القيامة رددتكما إلى ما كنتما عليه ، فقال أحدهما لصاحبه إن عذاب الدنيا ينقطع ويزول فاختارا عذاب الدنيا على عذاب الآخرة فأوحى الله اليهما أن ائتيا بابل فانطلقا إلى بابل فخسف بهما فهما منكوسان بين السماء والأرض معذبان إلى يوم القيامة — "I traveled with Ibn Umar, and when it was the end of the night, he said: 'O Nafi', look, has the Red One (Venus) risen?' I said: 'No,' twice or thrice. Then I said: 'It has risen.' He said: 'No welcome to it nor greeting.' I said: 'Glory be to Allah! A subservient star, hearing and obeying.' He said: 'I have only told you what I heard from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم).' He said: 'The angels said: "O Lord, how is Your patience with the children of Adam in their errors and sins?" He said: "I have tested them and granted them well-being." They said: "Had we been in their place, we would not have disobeyed You." He said: "Then choose two angels from among you." They spared no effort in choosing, and they chose Harut and Marut. They descended, and Allah cast upon them al-shabaq.' I asked: 'What is al-shabaq?' He said: 'Lust. Then a woman called al-Zuhara (Venus) came and fell into their hearts. Each of them began to hide from his companion what was in his soul. Then one said to the other: "Has there fallen into your soul what has fallen into my heart?" He said: "Yes." So they sought her for themselves. She said: "I will not enable you until you teach me the Name by which you ascend to heaven and descend." They refused. Then they asked her again and she refused. So they did. When she was flown up, Allah transformed her into a star and cut off their wings. Then they asked for repentance from their Lord, and He gave them a choice. He said: "If you wish, I will return you to what you were, and on the Day of Resurrection I will punish you; or if you wish, I will punish you in this world, and on the Day of Resurrection I will return you to what you were." One said to his companion: "The punishment of this world is interrupted and ceases." So they chose the punishment of this world over the punishment of the Hereafter. Then Allah revealed to them to go to Babylon. They went to Babylon and were swallowed by the earth; they are suspended upside down between heaven and earth, punished until the Day of Resurrection.'"

I say: Something close to this has been narrated in some Shi'a books as a marfu' (مرفوع) — a tradition attributed to the Prophet or an Imam without a complete chain — narration from Imam al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام). Al-Suyuti narrated more than twenty traditions regarding this meaning in the matter of Harut, Marut, and al-Zuhara; they declared the chains of some of them to be authentic. At the end of their chains are several companions such as Ibn Abbas, Ibn Mas'ud, Ali, Abu al-Darda, Umar, Aisha, and Ibn Umar. This is a mythical story attributed to the honored angels, whose sanctity and purity of existence from polytheism and disobedience—the gravest polytheism and the foulest disobedience: idol worship, murder, adultery, and wine drinking—the Quran has explicitly stated. It also attributes to the planet Venus (الزهرة) that it was an adulterous woman who was transformed—which is a mockery—while it is a pure celestial planet in its vanguard and creation, by which Allah the Exalted swore in His saying: الْجَوَارِ الْكُنَّسِ "the planets that run their course and set" (al-Takwir: 16). Moreover, astronomy has today revealed its identity and uncovered its element, quantity, quality, and other affairs.

Thus, this story, like the one before it mentioned in the previous narration, corresponds to what the Jews possess—as it is said—of the story of Harut and Marut, that mythical story which resembles the myths of the Greeks regarding the planets and stars.

From here, it becomes clear to the contemplative researcher that these traditions—like others narrated regarding the disparagement of the prophets and their slips—are not free from fabrications (الدس) that the Jews inserted into them. They reveal their precise infiltration and deep influence among the scholars of tradition (أصحاب الحديث) in the early period; they played with their narrations through all the fabrication and mixing they desired, and they were aided in that by another group of people.

However, Allah, exalted is His Name, has placed His Book in a divine safeguard from the whims of the whimsical among His enemies. Whenever a devil from among their devils attempts to steal a hearing, he is followed by a manifest flame. The Mightiest of speakers said: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحَافِظُونَ "Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it" (al-Hijr: 9). And He said: وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتَابٌ عَزِيزٌ لَّا يَأْتِيهِ الْبَاطِلُ مِن بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلَا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ تَنزِيلٌ مِّنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ "and indeed it is an august Book: falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it, [it is] a gradual revelation from One All-wise, All-laudable" (Fussilat: 41-42). And He said: وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ مَا هُوَ شِفَاءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلَا يَزِيدُ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا خَسَارًا "We send down in the Quran that which is a cure and mercy for the believers, and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss" (al-Isra: 82).

He spoke generally and did not restrict it. Thus, there is no mixing or fabrication except that the Quran repels it and manifests the loss of its author by exposing his condition and reading the page of his history. The Messenger of Allah said in what both sects have narrated: "Whatever agrees with the Book of Allah, take it; and whatever contradicts it, leave it." Thus, he gave a universal criterion (ميزان) by which the transmitted knowledge from him and his successors is weighed. In short, through the Quran, falsehood is repelled from the presence of truth, then its vanity soon appears and it is put to death in living hearts just as it was put to death in external realities. The Exalted said: بَلْ نَقْذِفُ بِالْحَقِّ عَلَى الْبَاطِلِ فَيَدْمَغُهُ "Rather We hurl the truth against falsehood, and it knocks out its brain..." (al-Anbiya: 18). And the Exalted said: وَيُرِيدُ اللَّهُ أَن يُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِمَاتِهِ "...and Allah desires to confirm the truth by His words..." (al-Anfal: 7). And the Exalted said: لِيُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ وَيُبْطِلَ الْبَاطِلَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُجْرِمُونَ "that He might confirm the truth and bring falsehood to naught, though the guilty should be averse" (al-Anfal: 8). There is no meaning to confirming the truth nor to bringing falsehood to naught except the manifestation of their respective attributes.

Some people, especially those of our era who are deeply immersed in materialist research and intimidated by modern Western civilization, have derived a bad conclusion from this aforementioned reality. They have taken to discarding all that the Sunnah (السنة) — the prophetic tradition — of the Messenger of Allah contains and what the collections of narrations comprise. They have followed the path of negligence (التفريط) in this, in contrast to the path of excess (الإفراط) followed by some traditionists (الأخباريين), scholars of tradition, the Haruriyyah, and others, who accept every narrated report regardless of its nature. Just as absolute acceptance is a denial of the criteria established in the religion to distinguish truth from falsehood and an attribution of vanity and idle talk to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), so too is total rejection a denial of them, and an annulment and nullification of the Majestic Book, which falsehood cannot approach from before it nor from behind it. He, Majestic is His praise, says: مَا آتَاكُمُ الرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَمَا نَهَاكُمْ عَنْهُ فَانتَهُوا "...Take whatever the Apostle gives you, and relinquish whatever he forbids you..." (al-Hashr: 7). And the Exalted said: وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطَاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ "We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave..." (al-Nisa: 64). For if the word of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) had no authority (حجية), or if what is transmitted of his word (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) to us—the community of those absent during his era or existing after his departure from the world—had no authority, not a single stone of the religion would remain standing. Relying on transmission and tradition is something humans encounter and accept in their social life with a necessity they are compelled to by self-evidence; their human primordial nature (الفطرة) guides them to it, and they cannot do without it. As for the occurrence of fabrication and mixing in transmitted religious knowledge, it is not an innovation unique to religion. How could it be, when the mill of society in all its directions and pillars turns upon the daily general and specific circulating reports, and the presence of lying, fabrication, and mixing in them is even greater, and the hands of major and minor politics play with them more? We, according to our human primordial nature, do not act upon the mere hearing of reports transmitted to us in the social assembly; rather, we present each one of them to the criterion we possess by which it can be weighed. If it agrees with it and confirms it, we accept it; if it contradicts it and denies it, we discard it; and if nothing of its matter becomes clear and its truth is not distinguished from its falsehood, nor its honesty from its lying, we suspend judgment (توقفنا) regarding it without acceptance or rejection, based on the caution (الإحتياط) we are naturally disposed toward regarding evils and harms.

All of this is on the condition of expertise (الخبرة) in the type of report transmitted to us. As for that in which a person has no expertise regarding the reports and their contents, the way of the rational people (العقلاء) in society is to refer to the people of expertise and to take what they see and judge therein.

This is what our primordial construction in human society is based upon. The religious criterion established to distinguish truth from falsehood, as well as honesty from lying, does not differ from that; rather, it is the very same thing. It is the presentation to the Book of Allah: if something becomes clear from it, it is taken; if it does not become clear due to an ambiguity (شبهة), then one halts at the ambiguity. Regarding this, there are mutawatir (متواترة) — successive and widely transmitted — reports from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and the Imams from his household. All of this applies to matters other than jurisprudential issues (المسائل الفقهية); as for those, the reference in researching them is the science of the principles of jurisprudence (أصول الفقه).

(Philosophical Discussion)

It is well known that supernatural acts (أفعال خارقة للعادة) occur through observation and transmission. Rarely is one of us found who has not witnessed something of supernatural acts or had something of that transmitted to him—little or much. However, precise research into many of them reveals their return to ordinary natural causes. Many of these acts are those by which their practitioners strengthen themselves through habituation and practice, such as eating poisons, carrying heavy weights, and walking on a rope stretched in the air, among others. Many of them rely on natural causes hidden from the people and unknown to them, such as one who enters fire and is not burned by it due to coating his body with talc, or one who writes a book with no visible script that only its owner can read—having written with a fluid that only appears when the book is exposed to fire—and so on. Many of them occur through rapid movements hidden from the senses due to their speed, so the sense only perceives that it occurred without a natural cause, like the supernatural feats performed by practitioners of sleight of hand (الشعبذة). All of these are based on ordinary causes hidden from our senses or beyond our power. However, some of these supernatural acts cannot be analyzed into ordinary natural causes, such as informing about some of the unseen (المغيبات), especially what occurs in the future, and acts of love, hatred, binding, loosening, hypnotism, causing illness, sleep-binding, summoning, and movements by will (الإرادة) — the faculty of choice and volition — that occur from practitioners of spiritual disciplines (الرياضات). These are matters that cannot be denied; we have witnessed some of them and others have been transmitted to us through reports that cannot be impugned. Indeed, there exists today among their practitioners in India, Iran, and the West a group from whom types of these supernatural acts are witnessed. Complete reflection on the methods of spiritual disciplines that grant these supernatural acts, and practical experience in their actions and will, necessitates the statement that they are based on the power of the will and the belief in its effect, despite the diversity of their types. For the will follows knowledge and the conviction (الاذعان) preceding it. Sometimes it exists in its absolute form, and sometimes it exists upon the presence of specific conditions, such as writing something specific with a specific ink in a specific place in some acts of love and hatred, or placing a mirror facing the face of a specific child during the summoning of a spirit, or reciting a specific incantation (عوذة), and so on. All of these are conditions for the attainment of the acting will. Thus, when knowledge becomes complete and certain knowledge (علماً قاطعاً), it grants the senses the observation of what was certain. You can test the truth of that by suggesting to yourself that such-and-such a thing or person is present with you, whom you witness with your senses, then imagining it such that you do not doubt it and do not pay attention to its absence nor to anything else; you will find it before you as you desire. Sometimes there is found in the records the treatment by some physicians of fatal diseases by suggesting health to the patient.

If the matter is as such, then if the will becomes strong, it can affect someone other than the willing person, similar to what it creates in the soul of the willing person himself, either without condition or restriction, or with some conditions.

From what has passed, several matters become clear: First: that the criterion in this effect is the realization of certain knowledge (العلم الجازم) on the part of the practitioner of the supernatural act. As for the correspondence of this knowledge to external reality, it is not necessary, as was believed by the practitioners of the subjugation of planets (تسخير الكواكب) regarding the spirits (الأرواح) attached to the celestial bodies. It is possible that the angels and devils, whose names the practitioners of invocations and incantations extract and call upon through specific methods they possess, are of this category. Likewise, what the practitioners of spirit summoning (إحضار الارواح) believe regarding the presence of the spirit: they have no evidence for more than its presence in their imagination or senses rather than in the external world; otherwise, everyone present with them would see it, as everyone possesses a natural sense. By this, another ambiguity is resolved regarding the summoning of the spirit of one who is alive and awake, occupied with his affairs without being aware of it—for a single human has only one spirit. By this, another ambiguity is also resolved: that the spirit is a non-material substance (جوهر مجرد) that has no relation to a specific time and place over another. By this, a third ambiguity is also resolved: that a single spirit may appear to one person in a form different from the form in which it appears to another. By this, a fourth ambiguity is also resolved: that spirits may lie during summoning in their reports, and some may contradict others. The answer to all of this is that the spirit only appears in the perceptions of the person summoning it, not outside of them in the way we perceive natural material things.

Second: that the possessor of this effective will may rely in his will upon the power of his own soul and the stability of his "I-ness" (إنيته), as is the case with most practitioners of spiritual disciplines in their will; thus, it will inevitably be limited in power and restricted in effect for the willer and in the external world. Or he may rely therein upon his Lord, like the prophets and the friends of Allah (الأولياء) among the practitioners of servitude (العبودية) to Allah and the masters of certainty (اليقين) in Allah. They do not will anything except for their Lord and by their Lord. This is a pure will in which the soul from which this will emerges has no independence whatsoever, nor is it colored by any of the colors of psychological inclinations, nor does it have any reliance except upon the Truth (الحق). Thus, it is a divine will (ارادة ربانية), neither limited nor restricted.

The second category: if it acts in the context of challenge (التحدي), as is the case with most of what is narrated from the prophets, it is called a miracle (آية معجزة) — an evidentiary sign that incapacitates others from producing its like. If it is realized outside the context of challenge, it is called a charism (كرامة) — a divine favor granted to a saint — or the answering of a prayer (استجابة دعوة) if it was with a supplication. The first category: if it is through seeking information and aid from a jinn or spirit or the like, it is called soothsaying (كهانة); and if it is through an invocation, incantation, charm, or the like, it is called sorcery (سحراً).

Third: that since the matter revolves around the will in its strength, and it is of various levels of strength and weakness, it is possible for some to nullify the effect of others, such as the confrontation between sorcery and a miracle. Or some souls may not affect others if they differ in levels of strength, which is witnessed in the acts of hypnotism and summoning. A portion of the discussion on this will follow.

(Scientific Discussion)

The sciences researching wondrous effects are many, and a universal statement on their classification and regulation is very difficult. The most well-known among their practitioners are what we mention: Among them is Simiya (السيمياء): which is the science researching the mixing of volitional powers with specific material powers to obtain wondrous disposal in natural affairs. Part of it is the disposal in the imagination called "eye-sorcery" (سحر العيون), and this art is among the truest instances of sorcery. Among them is Limiya (الليمياء): which is the science researching the manner of volitional effects through their connection with the strong, high spirits, such as the spirits entrusted with the planets and events and others, by subjugating them, or by connection and seeking aid from the jinn by subjugating them; this is the art of subjugations (التسخيرات). Among them is Himiya (الهيمياء): which is the science researching the combination of the powers of the celestial world with the terrestrial elements to obtain wondrous effects; this is talismans (الطلسمات). For the celestial planets and heavenly positions have connections with material events, just as the elements, compounds, and their natural qualities do. So if the heavenly forms suitable for an event—such as the death of so-and-one, the life of so-and-one, or the survival of so-and-one, for example—are combined with the suitable material form, that results in obtaining the objective; this is the meaning of a talisman. Among them is Rimiya (الريمياء): which is the science researching the employment of material powers to obtain their effects such that it appears to the senses that they are supernatural effects in some way; this is sleight of hand (الشعبذة). These four arts, along with a fifth art that follows them, which is Kimiya (الكيميا) — alchemy — researching the manner of changing the forms of elements into one another, were called among them "the five hidden sciences." Our teacher al-Baha'i said: "The best of the composed books in these arts is a book I saw in the city of Herat called Kulla Sirr (كله سر); its name was composed from the initials of the names of these sciences: al-Kimiya, al-Limiya, al-Himiya, al-Simiya, and al-Rimiya." End of the summary of his words.

Among the authoritative books therein are the summary of the books of Balinas, the epistles of al-Khusrawshahi, al-Dhakhirah al-Iskandariyyah, al-Sirr al-Maktum by al-Razi, al-Taskhirat by al-Sakaki, and A'mal al-Kawakib al-Sab'ah by the sage Tamtam al-Hindi.

Among the sciences attached to what has passed is the science of numbers and magic squares (علم الاعداد والأوفاق): which researches the connections of numbers and letters for objectives, and the placement of the number or letters suitable for the objective in triangular, square, or other tables according to a specific order. Among them is al-Khafiyah (الخافية): which is the breaking down of the letters of the objective or what suits the objective of the names, and the extraction of the names of the angels or devils entrusted with the objective, and the invocation with the incantations composed of them to attain the objective. Among the authoritative books therein according to them are the books of Sheikh Abu al-Abbas al-Tuni, Sayyid Husayn al-Akhlati, and others.

Among the arts attached to them circulating today are hypnotism (التنويم المغناطيسي) and spirit summoning (احضار الارواح). They are, as passed, from the effect of the will and the disposal in the imagination. Many books and epistles have been authored regarding them, and the fame of their matter suffices from referring to them here. The purpose of what we have mentioned at length is to clarify the application of what applies of them to sorcery or soothsaying.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَقُولُوا۟ رَٰعِنَا وَقُولُوا۟ ٱنظُرْنَا وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ ۗ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٠٤)
O you who have faith! Do not say Ra‘ina, but say Unzurna, and listen! And there is a painful punishment for the faithless. (104)
مَّا يَوَدُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَلَا ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ أَن يُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَخْتَصُّ بِرَحْمَتِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (١٠٥)
Neither the faithless from among the People of the Book nor the idolaters like that any good be showered on you from your Lord; but Allah singles out for His mercy whomever He wishes, and Allah is dispenser of a mighty grace. (105)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: يا أيها الذين آمنوا (O you who have faith): This is the first instance in the Quran where the believers are addressed with the expression "O you who have faith" (يا أيها الذين آمنوا). This form of address occurs in the Quran in approximately eighty-five places. Referring to the believers with the phrase "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا)—whether in the form of a direct address or otherwise—is something exclusive to this nation (الأمة). As for previous nations, they were referred to by the term "people" (القوم), such as His saying: قَوْمَ نُوحٍ أَوْ قَوْمَ هُودٍ — "the people of Noah or the people of Hud" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-A'raf, Ayah 59 & 65], and His saying: قَالَ يَا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ — "He said, ‘O my people! Tell me, should I stand on a manifest proof...’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Hud, Ayah 63], and His saying: اصحاب مدين وأصحاب الرس — "the inhabitants of Midian and the inhabitants of Rass" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Hajj, Ayah 44 & Surah Al-Furqan, Ayah 38], and "the Children of Israel" (بني إسرائيل) and "O Children of Israel" (يا بني إسرائيل). Thus, the expression "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) is an honorific title (التشرف) exclusive to this nation.

However, contemplation (التدبر) on His speech, the Exalted, reveals that the expression "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) in His discourse is intended to mean something different from the term "the believers" (المؤمنون)—such as His saying, the Exalted: وَتُوبُوا إِلَى اللَّهِ جَمِيعًا أَيُّهَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ — "and rally to Allah in repentance, O faithful" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah An-Nur, Ayah 31]—in terms of its referent (المصداق). The Exalted says: الَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ الْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا رَبَّنَا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ رَّحْمَةً وَعِلْمًا فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تَابُوا وَاتَّبَعُوا سَبِيلَكَ وَقِهِمْ عَذَابَ الْجَحِيمِ ، رَبَّنَا وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ الَّتِي وَعَدتَّهُمْ وَمَن صَلَحَ مِنْ آبَائِهِمْ وَأَزْوَاجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ — "Those who bear the Throne, and those who are around it, celebrate the praise of their Lord and have faith in Him, and they plead for forgiveness for the faithful: ‘Our Lord, You embrace all things in mercy and knowledge! So forgive those who repent and follow Your way, and save them from the punishment of hell. Our Lord, admit them into the Gardens of Eden, which You have promised them, along with whoever is righteous among their forebears, their spouses and their descendants. Indeed You are the All-mighty, the All-wise.’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Ghafir, Ayah 7-8].

He made the seeking of forgiveness by the angels and the bearers of the Throne first for "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا), then He substituted it a second time with His saying: "for those who repent and follow" (للذين تابوا واتبعوا), and repentance (التوبة) is the act of returning (الرجوع). Then He linked their supplication to "those who have faith" and joined to them their fathers and descendants. If those described as "those who have faith" were simply all the people of faith in the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, regardless of their status, then "those who have faith" would have encompassed everyone—fathers, children, and spouses—and there would have been no place for the conjunction (العطف) or the distinction; everyone would have been on the same level and placed in a single rank.

This meaning is also derived from His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُم بِإِيمَانٍ أَلْحَقْنَا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَمَا أَلَتْنَاهُم مِّن/ عَمَلِهِم مِّن شَيْءٍ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِمَا كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ — "As for those who have faith and whose descendants followed them in faith, We will unite their descendants with them, and We will not stint them anything of their deeds. Every person is a hostage to what he has earned." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah At-Tur, Ayah 21]. If their descendants who followed them in faith were themselves the referents (مصداقاً) of "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) in His speech, there would be no reason for "uniting" (الإلحاق) them. Furthermore, if His saying "and whose descendants followed them" (وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُم) were a contextual clue (قرينة) indicating specific individuals from "those who have faith"—namely every group of believers in relation to their believing descendants—there would still be no reason for the "uniting," nor would there be a correct justification for His saying "and We will not stint them anything of their deeds," except in the case of the final generation who have no descendants to follow them in faith and are thus united with their fathers. While this is a conceivable meaning, the context (سياق) of the verse—which is a context of bestowing honor (التشريف)—rejects this. Under that interpretation, the meaning would revert to something like our saying: "The believers are from one another" or "some are united with others," where they are all in one rank without any superiority (شرافة) of some over others, nor of the predecessor over the successor, since the criterion is faith (الإيمان), which is the same in all of them. This contradicts the context of the verse, which indicates a kind of nobility (كرامة) and honor for the predecessor by uniting his descendants with him.

Thus, His saying "and whose descendants followed them in faith" is a contextual clue indicating specific individuals by His saying "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا); they are the foremost pioneers (السابقون الأولون) in faith in the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم from the Emigrants (المهاجرين) and the Helpers (الأنصار) during the days of hardship. Therefore, the phrase "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) is a word of honor (كلمة تشريف) intended for these individuals. This is also suggested by His saying, the Exalted: لِلْفُقَرَاءِ الْمُهَاجِرِينَ — "[They are also] for the poor Emigrants..." until He says: وَالَّذِينَ تَبَوَّءُوا الدَّارَ وَالْإِيمَانَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ — "And [also for] those who were settled in the land and [abided] in faith before them..." until He says: وَالَّذِينَ جَاءُوا مِن بَعْدِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لَنَا وَلِإِخْوَانِنَا الَّذِينَ سَبَقُونَا بِالْإِيمَانِ وَلَا تَجْعَلْ فِي قُلُوبِنَا غِلًّا لِّلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا رَبَّنَا إِنَّكَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ — "And [also for] those who came after them, who say, ‘Our Lord, forgive us and our brethren who preceded us in faith, and do not put any rancour in our hearts toward the faithful. Our Lord, You are indeed most kind and merciful.’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Hashr, Ayah 8-10]. If the referent (مصداق) of His saying "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) were identical to the referent of His saying "those who preceded us in faith" (الذين سبقونا بالإيمان), it would be a case of placing a manifest noun (الظاهر) in the place of a pronoun (المضمر) without an apparent reason.

What has been mentioned is also suggested by His saying, the Exalted: مُّحَمَّدٌ رَّسُولُ اللَّهِ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ رُحَمَاءُ بَيْنَهُمْ تَرَاهُمْ رُكَّعًا سُجَّدًا يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَرِضْوَانًا — "Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves. You see them bowing and prostrating [in worship], seeking Allah’s grace and [His] pleasure..." until He says: وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ مِنْهُم مَّغْفِرَةً وَأَجْرًا عَظِيمًا — "Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Fath, Ayah 29].

In summary, the phrase is a word of honor (كلمة تشريف) exclusive to the foremost pioneers among the believers. It is not unlikely that a similar reasoning applies to the phrase "those who disbelieve" (الذين كفروا), referring to the foremost in disbelief in the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم from the polytheists of Mecca and their peers, as suggested by verses such as His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَوَاءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ — "As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 6].

If you say: Based on what has passed, the address to "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) would be restricted to a specific number of those present during the time of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, whereas the scholars have mentioned that these are general addresses for the time of presence and otherwise, for those present in the era of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم and others, especially based on the approach of treating the address as a real proposition (قضية حقيقية).

I say: Yes, it is an honorific address (خطاب تشريفي) that is specific to some, but that does not necessitate that the legal obligations (التكاليف) contained within the address are restricted to them. The breadth or narrowness of a legal obligation has causes other than those that necessitate the breadth or narrowness of an address. Just as obligations devoid of a direct address are general and broad without an address, the introduction of some obligations with the address "O you who have faith" (يا ايها الذين آمنوا) is like the introduction of other addresses with the phrase "O Prophet" (يا أيها النبي) or "O Messenger" (يا أيها الرسول), based on honor (التشريف), while the obligation is general and the intent is broad. Despite all this, the honorific exclusivity we mentioned does not prevent the term "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) from being applied to others besides those specifically honored, provided there is a contextual clue (قرينة) indicating that, such as His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا ثُمَّ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا ثُمَّ ازْدَادُوا كُفْرًا لَّمْ يَكُنِ اللَّهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ — "As for those who believe and then disbelieve, then believe [again] and then disbelieve and then increase in their disbelief, Allah shall never forgive them..." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah An-Nisa, Ayah 137], and His saying, the Exalted, narrating from Noah: وَمَا أَنَا بِطَارِدِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّهُم مُّلَاقُو رَبِّهِمْ — "and I will not drive away those who have faith; they will indeed encounter their Lord..." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Hud, Ayah 29].

His saying, the Exalted: لا تقولوا راعنا وقولوا أنظرنا (Do not say ‘Ra‘ina,’ but say ‘Unzurna’): That is, replace the word ra‘ina (راعنا) with the word unzurna (انظرنا); and if you do not do so, it will be an act of disbelief (كفر) from you, and for the disbelievers is a painful punishment. Thus, it contains a severe prohibition against saying ra‘ina. This is a word mentioned in another verse which explained its meaning in general, namely His saying, the Exalted: مِّنَ الَّذِينَ هَادُوا يُحَرِّفُونَ الْكَلِمَ عَن مَّوَاضِعِهِ وَيَقُولُونَ سَمِعْنَا وَعَصَيْنَا وَاسْمَعْ غَيْرَ مُسْمَعٍ وَرَاعِنَا لَيًّا بِأَلْسِنَتِهِمْ وَطَعْنًا فِي الدِّينِ — "Among the Jews are those who pervert words from their meanings, and say, ‘We hear and disobey’ and ‘Listen without being listened to!’ and ‘Ra‘ina,’ twisting their tongues and reviling the religion." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah An-Nisa, Ayah 46]. From this, it is known that the Jews intended by their saying to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم ra‘ina something similar to the meaning of His saying: "Listen without being listened to" (اسمع غير مسمع). For this reason, the prohibition was issued against addressing the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم with that.

At that point, it aligns with what has been narrated: that the Muslims used to address the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم with that when he delivered a speech to them, saying, "Observe us (ra‘ina), O Messenger of Allah"—intending "give us time and look at us (unthurna) so that we may understand what you say." However, the word in the language of the Jews carried the meaning of an insult. The Jews seized this opportunity and began addressing the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم with it, appearing to be polite with him while intending the insult; its meaning to them was "Listen, may you not hear." Thus, it was revealed: "Among the Jews are those who pervert words from their meanings, and say, ‘We hear and disobey’ and ‘Listen without being listened to!’ and ‘Ra‘ina’..." the verse. Allah prohibited the believers from this word and commanded them to say what carries its intended meaning, which is "look at us" (unzurna), so He said: لا تقولوا راعنا وتقولوا أنظرنا — "Do not say ‘Ra‘ina,’ but say ‘Unzurna’."

His saying, the Exalted: وللكافرين عذاب أليم (and for the faithless there is a painful punishment): He intends those who rebel against this prohibition. This is one of the instances where disbelief (الكفر) is applied to the abandonment of secondary legal obligations (التكاليف الفرعية).

His saying, the Exalted: ما يود الذين كفروا من أهل الكتاب (Neither the faithless from among the People of the Book...): If the intent by "People of the Book" (أهل الكتاب) is the Jews specifically—as is apparent since the preceding addresses were directed at them—then describing them as "People of the Book" serves to point to the cause (العلة). That is, because they are People of a Book, they do not wish for the Book to be revealed to the believers, as that would necessitate the end of their exclusive status as the people of the Book. This is despite the fact that such an attitude is a form of stinginess (ضنّة) regarding something they do not own, and an opposition to Allah, the Glorified, in the vastness of His mercy and the greatness of His grace. If the intent is the People of the Book in general—both Jews and Christians—then it is a generalization after specification, due to the two groups sharing certain traits and their shared resentment toward Islam. This view may be supported by some subsequent verses, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالُوا لَن يَدْخُلَ الْجَنَّةَ إِلَّا مَن كَانَ هُودًا أَوْ نَصَارَىٰ — "And they say, ‘No one will enter paradise except one who is a Jew or a Christian.’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 111], and His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالَتِ الْيَهُودُ لَيْسَتِ النَّصَارَىٰ عَلَىٰ شَيْءٍ وَقَالَتِ النَّصَارَىٰ لَيْسَتِ الْيَهُودُ عَلَىٰ شَيْءٍ وَهُمْ يَتْلُونَ الْكِتَابَ — "The Jews say, ‘The Christians stand on nothing,’ and the Christians say, ‘The Jews stand on nothing,’ though they follow the [same] Book." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 113].

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Nu'aym narrated in al-Hilyah from Ibn Abbas, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said: "Allah did not reveal any verse containing 'O you who have faith' (يا ايها الذين آمنوا) except that Ali is its head and its leader."

I say: The narration supports what we will mention of the narrations regarding several verses stating that they are about Ali or the People of the House (أهل البيت), similar to what is in His saying, the Exalted: كُنتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ — "You are the best nation [ever] brought forth for mankind..." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Imran, Ayah 110], and His saying, the Exalted: لِّتَكُونُوا شُهَدَاءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ — "so that you may be witnesses to mankind..." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 143], and His saying, the Exalted: وَكُونُوا مَعَ الصَّادِقِينَ — "and be with the truthful" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah At-Tawbah, Ayah 119].

Ayahs

۞ مَا نَنسَخْ مِنْ ءَايَةٍ أَوْ نُنسِهَا نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِّنْهَآ أَوْ مِثْلِهَآ ۗ أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٠٦)
For any verse that We abrogate or cause to be forgotten, We bring another which is better than it, or similar to it. Do you not know that Allah has power over all things? (106)
أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (١٠٧)
Do you not know that to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth? And besides Allah, you do not have any guardian or helper. (107)

(Commentary)

The two verses concern abrogation (النسخ). It is well known that abrogation in the sense recognized by the jurists (الفقهاء)—which is the disclosure of the end of a ruling’s duration and the expiration of its term—is a technical term derived from the verse and based upon it. It is among the instances of what is derived from the verse regarding the meaning of abrogation, according to the apparent generality (إطلاق) of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: مَا نَنسَخْ (Whatever ayah We abrogate). Abrogation (النسخ) is removal (الإزالة). It is said: "The sun abrogated (نسخت) the shadow" when it removes it and takes it away. The Exalted says: وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رَّسُولٍ وَلَا نَبِيٍّ إِلَّا إِذَا تَمَنَّىٰ أَلْقَى الشَّيْطَانُ فِي أُمْنِيَّتِهِ فَيَنسَخُ اللَّهُ مَا يُلْقِي الشَّيْطَانُ We did not send before you any apostle or prophet but that when he recited [the [divine] messages] Satan cast [doubts] into his recitation. Thereupon Allah abrogates what Satan casts (22:52). Also from this is their saying: "I abrogated (نسخت) the book" when it is transcribed from one copy to another; it is as if the book has been taken away and replaced in its stead. For this reason, the word "abrogation" (النسخ) was substituted for "replacement" (التبديل) in His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا بَدَّلْنَا آيَةً مَّكَانَ آيَةٍ وَاللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يُنَزِّلُ قَالُوا إِنَّمَا أَنتَ مُفْتَرٍ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ When We replace a verse with another—and Allah knows best what He sends down—they say, ‘You are indeed a fabricator.’ Rather, most of them do not know (16:101). In any case, abrogation does not necessitate the removal of the verse itself from existence or the nullification of its occurrence. Rather, since the ruling was attached to the description—which is the "verse" (الآية) or "sign" (العلامة)—along with the reasoning (التعليل) that follows it in the verse in His saying, the Exalted: "Do you not know...", etc., this indicates that what is meant by abrogation is the removal of the effect of the verse insofar as it is a verse. I mean the removal of a thing's status as a verse (آية) and a sign (علامة) while preserving its essence (أصل). Thus, through abrogation, its effect—whether a legal obligation (تكليف) or otherwise—is removed while its essence remains. This is what is understood from the pairing of His saying: نُنسِهَا (We cause it to be forgotten) with His saying: مَا نَنسَخْ (Whatever We abrogate). Causing to forget (الإنساء) is the causative form of forgetting (النسيان), which is removal from knowledge (العلم), just as abrogation (النسخ) is removal from external reality (العين). Thus, the meaning is: whatever verse We remove from external reality or from knowledge, We bring one better than it or like it.

Furthermore, a thing's status as a verse (آية) varies according to different things, aspects, and perspectives. Some parts of the Quran are a verse of Allah, the Glorified, by virtue of the inability of humans to produce the like of it. Divine rulings and obligations (التكاليف) are verses of Him, the Exalted, by virtue of the attainment of God-consciousness (التقوى) and proximity (القرب) to Him through them. External beings (الموجودات العينية) are verses of Him, the Exalted, by virtue of their existence disclosing the existence of their Maker, and by the particularities of their existence disclosing the particularities of His attributes and names, the Glorified. The prophets of Allah and His friends (الأولياء) are verses of Him, the Exalted, by virtue of their calling to Him through word and deed, and so on. For this reason, a verse accepts intensity and weakness; Allah, the Exalted, said: لَقَدْ رَأَىٰ مِنْ آيَاتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرَىٰ He certainly saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord (53:18).

From another perspective, a verse might be a verse from a single aspect, or it might possess many aspects. Its abrogation and removal can be conceived in its single aspect, such as its destruction (إهلاك), just as it can be conceived in some of its aspects to the exclusion of others if it possesses many aspects—such as a verse of the Quran being abrogated in terms of its legal ruling (حكم شرعي) while remaining in terms of its eloquence (بلاغة) and inimitability (إعجاز), and the like.

This meaning that we have inferred from the generality of the meaning of abrogation is what is supported by the general reasoning (التعليل) understood from His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ * أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ Do you not know that Allah has power over all things? Do you not know that to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth? (2:106-107). This is because the imagined objection in this context, or the actual objection from the Jews as reported regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) of the verse concerning the meaning of abrogation, relates to it from two perspectives:

First: From the perspective that if the verse is from Allah, the Exalted, it must preserve a real interest (مصلحة حقيقية) that nothing else can preserve. If the verse were removed, that interest would be lost, and nothing could take its place to preserve that interest or compensate for the lost benefit of creation and the interest of the servants. His state, the Exalted, is not like the state of His servants, nor is His knowledge like their knowledge, which changes with the change of external factors—such that His knowledge relates to an interest one day and He decrees a ruling, then His knowledge changes tomorrow and relates to another interest that was missed yesterday, so the ruling changes, and He decrees the nullification of what He previously ruled and the arrival of another later. Thus, a new ruling would emerge every day, and one color would appear after another, as is the case with servants who do not encompass the aspects of benefit in things; their rulings and conditions change with the change of knowledge regarding interests and harms, in terms of increase, decrease, origination, and persistence. This perspective traces back to the denial of the generality and absolute nature of Power (القدرة).

Second: That even if Power is absolute, the realization of creation (الإيجاد) and the actuality of existence (فعلية الوجود) make change impossible. For a thing does not change from what it has necessarily become. This is like a human being in his voluntary act (الفعل الاختياري); the act is voluntary for the human as long as it has not issued from him, but once it has issued, its establishment becomes necessary (ضروري) and is no longer voluntary for him. This perspective traces back to the denial of the absolute nature of Ownership (الملكية) and the impermissibility of certain disposals after the reins have been released by others, as the Jews said: "The hand of Allah is tied." Thus, the Glorified pointed to the answer to the first by His saying: أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (Do you not know that Allah has power over all things?), meaning He is not unable to establish what is better than what was lost or to establish what is like what was lost in its place. He pointed to the answer to the second by His saying: أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ مِن وَلِيٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (Do you not know that to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth? And besides Allah you do not have any guardian or helper), meaning that the kingdom of the heavens and the earth belongs to Allah, the Glorified, so He may dispose of His kingdom however He wills. No one else possesses any part of the kingdom such that it would necessitate the closing of one of the doors of His disposal, the Glorified, or be a barrier against any of His disposals. Nothing owns anything, neither originally nor by His granting of ownership (تمليك). For the granting of ownership that He grants to others is not like our granting of ownership to one another in a way that nullifies the ownership of the first and establishes the ownership of the second; rather, He is the Owner (مالك) of what He grants to others at the very moment they own it. If we look at the reality of the matter, absolute ownership and absolute disposal belong to Him alone. If we look at what He has made us own through His ownership without independence (استقلال), He is the Guardian (الولي) for us. And if we look at the apparent independence He has bestowed upon us—which is in reality poverty in the form of wealth, and dependency in the form of independence—it is also not possible for us to manage our affairs without His assistance and help; thus, He is the Helper (النصير) for us.

This which we have mentioned is what is necessitated by the apparent restriction (الحصر) in His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (Indeed, to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth). Thus, His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ * أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ is arranged according to the order of the two imagined objections. A proof that they are two distinct objections is the separation between the two sentences without a conjunction. His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ مِن وَلِيٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (And besides Allah you do not have any guardian or helper) contains two matters that are like completions of the answer. That is: even if you do not look at His absolute ownership, but rather look at the bestowed ownership you possess, since it is a bestowed ownership without separation or independence, He alone is your Guardian (ولي); thus, He may dispose of you and what you possess however He wills. And if you do not look at your lack of independence in ownership, but rather look at the appearance of the ownership and independence you possess and freeze upon that alone, you will see that the power, ownership, and independence you have do not suffice on their own, nor do they make your goals obedient to you or subject to your intentions and will alone; rather, there must be Allah's assistance and help with them. Thus, He is the Helper (نصير) for you, and He has the right to dispose through this path. Therefore, the Glorified has the right to dispose of your affairs from whichever path you take. In His saying: وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ (And besides Allah you do not have...), the explicit noun was used in place of the pronoun in view of the sentence being like an independent piece of speech, as the answer is complete without it.

From what has passed, it has become clear: First, that abrogation is not restricted to legal rulings but also encompasses existential matters (التكوينيات).

Second: That abrogation does not occur without two sides: the abrogator (ناسخ) and the abrogated (منسوخ).

Third: That the abrogator contains the perfection (كمال) or interest (مصلحة) that was in the abrogated.

Fourth: That the abrogator contradicts the abrogated in its form, and the contradiction between them is only lifted by virtue of both encompassing a shared interest. Thus, when a prophet dies and another prophet is sent in his place—and both are verses among the verses of Allah, the Exalted, one abrogating the other—this is a progression according to what is necessitated by the law of nature (ناموس الطبيعة) regarding life, death, provision, and lifespan, and what is necessitated by the differing interests of the servants according to the difference of eras and the perfection of human individuals. When a religious ruling is abrogated by another religious ruling, all of it encompasses the interest of the religion, and each of the two rulings is more fitting for the interest of the time and more suitable for the state of the believers—such as the ruling of forgiveness at the beginning of the call when the Muslims had neither numbers nor equipment, and the ruling of Jihad after that when Islam became strong, and they prepared what power they could and struck terror into the hearts of the disbelievers and polytheists. Despite this, the abrogated verses are not devoid of hints and allusions toward abrogation, as in His saying, the Exalted: فَاعْفُوا وَاصْفَحُوا حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَ اللَّهُ بِأَمْرِهِ So excuse and forbear until Allah issues His edict (2:109), which was abrogated by the verse of fighting; and His saying, the Exalted: فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ فِي الْبُيُوتِ حَتَّىٰ يَتَوَفَّاهُنَّ الْمَوْتُ أَوْ يَجْعَلَ اللَّهُ لَهُنَّ سَبِيلًا then confine them to their houses until death takes them away or Allah opens a way for them (4:15), which was abrogated by the verse of flogging. Thus, His saying: "until Allah issues His edict" and His saying: "or Allah opens a way for them" are not devoid of an indication that the ruling is temporary and deferred, and that abrogation will follow it.

Fifth: That the relationship between the abrogator (الناسخ) and the abrogated (المنسوخ) is not the same as the relationship between the general (العام) and the specific (الخاص), or between the absolute (المطلق) and the qualified (المقيد), or between the ambiguous (المجمل) and the clarified (المبين). For that which lifts the contradiction between the abrogator and the abrogated after it has been established between them according to the linguistic appearance (الظهور اللفظي) is the wisdom (الحكمة) and interest (المصلحة) present between them. This is in contrast to what lifts the contradiction between the general and the specific, the absolute and the qualified, and the ambiguous and the clarified; for that is the strength of the linguistic appearance present in the specific, the qualified, and the clarified, which interprets the general through specification (تخصيص), the absolute through qualification (تقييد), and the ambiguous through clarification (تبيين), as explained in the science of the principles of jurisprudence (أصول الفقه). The same applies to the clear (المحكم) and the metaphorical (المتشابه), as will come regarding His saying: مِنْهُ آيَاتٌ مُّحْكَمَاتٌ هُنَّ أُمُّ الْكِتَابِ وَأُخَرُ مُتَشَابِهَاتٌ In it are verses that are decisive—they are the Mother of the Book—and others are metaphorical (3:7).

His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ نُنسِهَا (or cause it to be forgotten). It is recited with a damma on the nun and a kasra on the sin, from "causing to forget" (الإنساء) in the sense of removal from knowledge and memory, and its explanation has already passed. This is an absolute or general statement not restricted to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); rather, it does not include him at all due to His saying, the Exalted: سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلَا تَنسَىٰ * إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ اللَّهُ We shall have you recite [the Quran] and then you will not forget [any of it], except what Allah wills (87:6-7). This is a Meccan verse, while the verse of abrogation is Medinan; thus, forgetting is not permissible for him after His saying, the Exalted: "then you will not forget." As for its inclusion of the exception in His saying: "except what Allah wills," it is of the same nature as the exception occurring in His saying, the Exalted: خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ السَّمَاوَاتُ وَالْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ رَبُّكَ عَطَاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ to remain in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord may wish—an endless bounty (11:108). It was brought to affirm the persistence of Power (القدرة) alongside the act to change the matter. If the exception were intended to describe what actually occurs in external reality, there would be no meaning to the favor (الامتنان) in His saying: "then you will not forget," since every human and animal possessing memory and retention likewise remembers and forgets, and both their remembering and forgetting are from Him, the Exalted, and by His will. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was already like that before this promised recitation of favor in His saying: "We shall have you recite"—he would remember by the will of Allah and forget by the will of Allah, the Exalted. Thus, the meaning of the exception is nothing but the affirmation of the absolute nature of Power; meaning: We shall have you recite so you shall never forget, yet Allah is still powerful over causing you to forget this. It is also recited as نَنسَأَهَا with a fatha on the nun and the hamza, from "postponement" (نسيء) when something is delayed. The meaning according to this would be: whatever verse We abrogate by removing it or We postpone by delaying its manifestation, We bring one better than it or like it. Divine disposal through advancement and postponement in His verses does not necessitate the loss of perfection or interest. The proof that the intent is to explain that divine disposal is always based on perfection and interest is His saying: "better than it or like it," for "betterness" (الخيرية) only exists in the perfection of an existing thing or the interest of a legislated ruling. In that case, an existent would be similar to another in betterness or exceed it in that. So understand.

(Narrative Discussion)

Narrations from both sides (the Sunnis and the Shi'a) have multiplied from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), the Companions, and from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) that in the Quran there is the abrogator (ناسخ) and the abrogated (منسوخ).

In the Tafsir of al-Nu'mani, from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام), after mentioning several verses of the abrogator and the abrogated, he (عليه السلام) said: "And His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ (I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me) was abrogated by His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: وَلَا يَزَالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ * إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ رَبُّكَ وَلِذَٰلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ (and they will not cease to differ, except those on whom your Lord has mercy—and for that He created them), meaning for mercy He created them."

I say: In this is an indication of his (عليه السلام) taking abrogation in the verse in a sense broader than the abrogation occurring in legislation (التشريع). The second verse establishes a reality that necessitates the delimitation of the reality established by the first verse. To put it clearly: the first verse establishes a goal (غاية) for creation, which is worship (العبادة), and Allah, the Glorified, is never overcome in the goal He intends in any of His acts. However, the Glorified created them with the possibility of differing, so they will not cease to differ in guidance and misguidance—they will not cease to differ except for those whom divine care has taken hold of and whom the mercy of guidance has encompassed. "And for that He created them," meaning for this mercy He created them. Thus, the second verse establishes a goal for creation, which is the mercy coupled with worship and guidance, and this only exists in some to the exclusion of all. The first verse was establishing worship as a goal for everyone; thus, this worship was made the goal of everyone from the perspective that some are created for the sake of others, and those others for yet others, until it ends with the people of worship—who are the worshippers created for worship. Thus, it is correct that worship is the goal for all, similar to building a garden and planting a tree for its fruit or for its financial benefits. So the second verse abrogates the absolute nature (إطلاق) of the first verse. Also in the Tafsir of al-Nu'mani from him (عليه السلام), he said: "And His saying, the Exalted: وَإِن مِّنكُمْ إِلَّا وَارِدُهَا كَانَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ حَتْمًا مَّقْضِيًّا (There is none of you but will come to it: a matter that is a determinate decree of your Lord) was abrogated by His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُم مِّنَّا الْحُسْنَىٰ أُولَـٰئِكَ عَنْهَا مُبْعَدُونَ * لَا يَسْمَعُونَ حَسِيسَهَا وَهُمْ فِي مَا اشْتَهَتْ أَنفُسُهُمْ خَالِدُونَ * لَا يَحْزُنُهُمُ الْفَزَعُ الْأَكْبَرُ (Indeed those to whom the best [reward] has been foreordained by Us will be kept far from it. They will not hear its sound, and they will remain forever in what their souls desire. The Great Terror will not grieve them)."

I say: The two verses are not of the category of the general (العام) and the specific (الخاص) because of His saying, the Exalted: "a matter that is a determinate decree of your Lord," and a determinate decree (القضاء الحتم) is not subject to being lifted nor is its nullification possible. The meaning of this abrogation will appear from what will come, if Allah wills, regarding His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُم مِّنَّا الْحُسْنَىٰ أُولَٰئِكَ عَنْهَا مُبْعَدُونَ (21:101).

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام): "Among abrogation is al-bada' (البداء) — the change in the apparent divine decree due to the manifestation of a new reality known to Allah from eternity — which is contained in His saying, the Exalted: يَمْحُوا اللَّهُ مَا يَشَاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِندَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتَابِ (Allah effaces and confirms whatever He wishes, and with Him is the Mother of the Book), and the salvation of the people of Jonah."

I say: The reason for this is clear.

In some reports from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt, he (عليه السلام) counted the death of an Imam and the standing of another Imam in his place as being from abrogation.

I say: Its explanation has already passed, and the reports regarding these meanings are numerous and widely transmitted (مستفيضة).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd ibn Humayd, Abu Dawud in his al-Nasikh, and Ibn Jarir extracted from Qatadah, who said: "A verse used to abrogate a verse, and the Prophet of Allah would recite the verse and the Surah and whatever Allah willed of the Surah, then it would be raised and Allah would cause His Prophet to forget it. So Allah said, narrating to His Prophet: مَا نَنسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنسِهَا نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِّنْهَا (Whatever ayah We abrogate or cause to be forgotten, We bring one better than it), he says: in it is lightening, in it is concession, in it is a command, in it is a prohibition."

I say: Numerous narrations were also reported therein regarding the meaning of "causing to forget" (الإنساء), and all of them are rejected due to their contradiction with the Book, as passed in the explanation of His saying: أَوْ نُنسِهَا.

Ayahs

أَمْ تُرِيدُونَ أَن تَسْـَٔلُوا۟ رَسُولَكُمْ كَمَا سُئِلَ مُوسَىٰ مِن قَبْلُ ۗ وَمَن يَتَبَدَّلِ ٱلْكُفْرَ بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (١٠٨)
Would you question your Apostle as Moses was questioned formerly? Whoever changes faith for unfaith certainly strays from the right way. (108)
وَدَّ كَثِيرٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَوْ يَرُدُّونَكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ كُفَّارًا حَسَدًا مِّنْ عِندِ أَنفُسِهِم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۖ فَٱعْفُوا۟ وَٱصْفَحُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِىَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٠٩)
Many of the People of the Book are eager to turn you into unbelievers after your faith, out of their inner envy, [and] after the truth had become manifest to them. Yet excuse [them] and forbear until Allah issues His edict. Indeed Allah has power over all things. (109)
وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ ۚ وَمَا تُقَدِّمُوا۟ لِأَنفُسِكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ تَجِدُوهُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (١١٠)
And maintain the prayer and give the zakat. Any good that you send ahead for your own souls, you shall find it with Allah. Indeed Allah watches what you do. (110)
وَقَالُوا۟ لَن يَدْخُلَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ إِلَّا مَن كَانَ هُودًا أَوْ نَصَـٰرَىٰ ۗ تِلْكَ أَمَانِيُّهُمْ ۗ قُلْ هَاتُوا۟ بُرْهَـٰنَكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (١١١)
And they say, ‘No one will enter paradise except one who is a Jew or Christian.’ Those are their [false] hopes! Say, ‘Produce your evidence, should you be truthful.’ (111)
بَلَىٰ مَنْ أَسْلَمَ وَجْهَهُۥ لِلَّهِ وَهُوَ مُحْسِنٌ فَلَهُۥٓ أَجْرُهُۥ عِندَ رَبِّهِۦ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (١١٢)
Certainly, whoever submits his will to Allah and is virtuous, he shall have his reward from his Lord, and they will have no fear, nor shall they grieve. (112)
وَقَالَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ لَيْسَتِ ٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ وَقَالَتِ ٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ لَيْسَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ وَهُمْ يَتْلُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ مِثْلَ قَوْلِهِمْ ۚ فَٱللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (١١٣)
The Jews say, ‘The Christians stand on nothing,’ and the Christians say, ‘The Jews stand on nothing,’ though they follow the [same] Book. So said those who had no knowledge, [words] similar to what they say. Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ. (113)
وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن مَّنَعَ مَسَـٰجِدَ ٱللَّهِ أَن يُذْكَرَ فِيهَا ٱسْمُهُۥ وَسَعَىٰ فِى خَرَابِهَآ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَا كَانَ لَهُمْ أَن يَدْخُلُوهَآ إِلَّا خَآئِفِينَ ۚ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا خِزْىٌ وَلَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (١١٤)
Who is a greater wrongdoer than those who deny access to the mosques of Allah lest His Name be celebrated therein, and try to ruin them? Such ones may not enter them, except in fear. There is disgrace for them in this world and a great punishment in the Hereafter. (114)
وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْمَشْرِقُ وَٱلْمَغْرِبُ ۚ فَأَيْنَمَا تُوَلُّوا۟ فَثَمَّ وَجْهُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (١١٥)
To Allah belong the east and the west: so whichever way you turn, there is the face of Allah! Allah is indeed all-bounteous, all-knowing. (115)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: Or do you seek to petition your Apostle... The context of the verse indicates that some of the Muslims—from among those who believed in the Prophet—asked the Prophet for things in the same manner that the Jews petitioned their prophet, Moses (peace be upon him). Allah, the Glorified, rebuked them for this within the context of His rebuke of the Jews for what they did to Moses and the prophets after him, and the transmitted reports (النقل) support this.

His saying, the Exalted: the right way (سواء السبيل)—meaning the levelness or evenness of the path.

His saying, the Exalted: Many of the People of the Book desire... It is narrated that this refers to Huyayy ibn Akhtab and some of those with him from the fanatical Jews.

His saying, the Exalted: so excuse and forbear—they said: This is a verse abrogated (منسوخة) by the verse of fighting (آية القتال).

His saying, the Exalted: until Allah issues His edict (بأمره)—there is in this, as previously mentioned, an allusion to a ruling that Allah the Exalted would legislate regarding them. Its parallel is His saying, the Exalted, in the following verse: أُولَـٰئِكَ مَا كَانَ لَهُمْ أَن يَدْخُلُوهَا إِلَّا خَائِفِينَ "It is not for such as these to enter them except in fear" (2:114), along with His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا الْمُشْرِكُونَ نَجَسٌ فَلَا يَقْرَبُوا الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرَامَ بَعْدَ عَامِهِمْ هَٰذَا "Indeed the polytheists are impure; so let them not approach the Holy Mosque after this year of theirs" (9:28). The discussion regarding the meaning of "the command/edict" (الأمر) will follow in His saying, the Exalted: يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي "They question you concerning the Spirit. Say, ‘The Spirit is of my Lord’s command’" (17:85).
