His saying, the Exalted: And they say, ‘No one will enter paradise...’—this is the beginning of explicitly associating the Christians with the Jews and directing the discourse toward explaining their collective crimes.

His saying, the Exalted: Certainly, whoever surrenders his self to Allah...—this is a third assault against them to explain that felicity (السعادة) does not revolve around a name, and no one possesses any special dignity before Allah except through the reality of faith (الإيمان) and servitude (العبودية). The first of these is His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هَادُوا "Indeed the faithful, and those who are Jews..." (2:62). The second is His saying, the Exalted: بَلَىٰ مَن كَسَبَ سَيِّئَةً وَأَحَاطَتْ بِهِ خَطِيئَتُهُ "Certainly, whoever earns a misdeed and is besieged by his sin..." (2:81). The third is this verse. From the application of these verses, one can derive the interpretation of faith (الإيمان) as the surrender of the self (إسلام الوجه) to Allah, and the interpretation of virtue (الإحسان) as righteous deeds (العمل الصالح).

His saying, the Exalted: while they recite the Book—meaning they act according to what they were given of the Book of Allah; it is not fitting for them to say such things while the Book clarifies the truth to them. The evidence for this is His saying: كَذَٰلِكَ قَالَ الَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ مِثْلَ قَوْلِهِمْ "Likewise those who do not know say the like of their saying" (2:113). Thus, "those who do not know" (الذين لا يعلمون) refers to those other than the People of the Book, such as the disbelievers and the polytheists of the Arabs, who said: "The Muslims are based on nothing," or "The People of the Book are based on nothing."

His saying, the Exalted: Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who denies...—the apparent context suggests that these were the disbelievers of Mecca before the Migration (الهجرة), as these verses were revealed in the early period of the Messenger of Allah's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) arrival in Medina.

His saying, the Exalted: It is not for such as these to enter them except in fear—this indicates that the event has passed and concluded due to the use of the past tense "was/were" (كان). Thus, it applies to the disbelievers of the Quraysh and their actions in Mecca, as supported by transmitted reports (النقل) that those who prevented others were the disbelievers of Mecca; they used to prevent the Muslims from praying in the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام) and the mosques they had established in the courtyard of the Kaaba.

His saying, the Exalted: To Allah belong the east and the west: so whichever way you turn, there is the face of Allah. The east, the west, and every direction, wherever it may be, belongs to Allah by virtue of true ownership (الملك) which accepts neither change nor transfer—unlike the ownership that exists among us, the members of society. Since His ownership, the Exalted, is established over the essence (ذات) of the thing, encompassing its very self and its effects—unlike our ownership, which is established over the effects and benefits of things, not their essences—and since ownership (الملك) as such cannot subsist except through its owner, Allah the Glorified is the Sustainer (قائم) of these directions, encompassing them and being with them. Therefore, whoever turns toward any direction is turning toward Him, the Exalted.

Since the east and the west are two relative directions that approximately encompass all other directions—as nothing remains outside them except the true North and South poles—the absolute nature of His saying "whichever way" (فأينما) was not restricted by them (i.e., by saying "whichever of these two ways you turn"). It is as if wherever a person turns his face, there is either an east or a west. Thus, His saying "To Allah belong the east and the west" is equivalent to our saying: "To Allah belong all directions." These two were specifically mentioned because the directions a person intends to face are only determined by the rising (شروق) and setting (غروب) of the sun and other luminous celestial bodies.

His saying, the Exalted: there is the face of Allah—in this, the cause of the ruling in the recompense (الجزاء) is placed in the position of the recompense itself. The underlying meaning—and Allah knows best—is: "Whichever way you turn, that is permissible for you, for the face of Allah is there." This interpretation is supported by the justification of the ruling in His saying, the Exalted: Indeed Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing. That is, Allah is all-bounteous (واسع) in ownership and encompassment, all-knowing (عليم) of your intentions wherever you turn. He is not like a single human being or other corporeal creatures (خلق جسماني) who cannot be turned toward unless they are in a specific direction, nor does He only know the intention of the one turning toward Him from a specific side, such as his front only. Thus, turning toward any direction is a turning toward Allah, known to Him, the Glorified.

Know that this is an expansion (توسعة) regarding the direction of prayer (القبلة) in terms of orientation (الجهة), not in terms of location (المكان). The evidence for this is His saying: To Allah belong the east and the west.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Tahdhib, narrated from Muhammad ibn al-Husayn, who said: I wrote to the Righteous Servant (عبد صالح) [Imam al-Kazim] (peace be upon him) regarding a man who prays on a cloudy day in a desert land and does not know the direction of prayer (القبلة). He prays, and when he finishes his prayer, the sun appears and he finds he has prayed toward other than the direction of prayer. Should his prayer be counted, or must he repeat it? He wrote: يعيد ما لم يفت الوقت ، أو لم يعلم أن الله يقول : ـ وقوله الحق ـ فأينما تولوا فثم وجه الله — "He repeats it as long as the time has not passed. Or does he not know that Allah says—and His saying is the truth—'so whichever way you turn, there is the face of Allah'?"

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: To Allah belong the east and the west... etc., he (peace be upon him) said: أنزل الله هذه الآية في التطوع خاصة فأينما تولوا فثم وجه الله إن الله واسع عليم ، وصلى رسول الله إيمائاً على راحلته أينما توجهت به حين خرج الى خيبر ، وحين رجع من مكة ، وجعل الكعبة خلف ظهره — "Allah revealed this verse specifically regarding supererogatory prayers (التطوع): 'so whichever way you turn, there is the face of Allah; indeed Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing.' The Messenger of Allah prayed by gesturing (إيمائاً) upon his mount wherever it turned with him when he went out to Khaybar and when he returned from Mecca, and he placed the Kaaba behind his back."

I say: Al-Ayyashi also narrated something similar to this from Zurarah from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), as did al-Qummi and al-Shaykh [al-Tusi] from Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him), and al-Saduq from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

Know that if you examine the reports of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) with due scrutiny regarding the instances of the general (العام) and the specific (الخاص), and the absolute (المطلق) and the restricted (المقيد) in the Quran, you will find that they often derive one ruling from the general, and another ruling from the specific—meaning the general along with the specifier (المخصص). From the general, for instance, they may derive recommendation (الاستحباب), as is most common, and from the specific, obligation (الوجوب). The same applies to detestability (الكراهة) and prohibition (الحرمة), and so on. This is one of the fundamental keys to interpretation (مفاتيح التفسير) in the reports narrated from them, and a vast number of their traditions revolve around it. From this, you can extract two principles regarding Quranic knowledge:

First: That every sentence on its own, and also with each of its qualifiers, speaks of a fixed reality (حقيقة ثابتة) among the realities or a fixed ruling (حكم ثابت) among the rulings. For example, His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ اللَّهُ ثُمَّ ذَرْهُمْ فِي خَوْضِهِمْ يَلْعَبُونَ "Say, ‘Allah.’ Then leave them to play around in their impious gossip" (6:91). In this, there are four meanings: First: "Say, ‘Allah’"; second: "Say, ‘Allah,’ then leave them"; third: "Say, ‘Allah,’ then leave them in their impious gossip"; and fourth: "Say, ‘Allah,’ then leave them to play around in their impious gossip." Consider the like of this in everything possible.

Second: That when two stories or two meanings share a sentence or something similar, they both return to a single source (مرجع واحد). These are two secrets beneath which lie many secrets, and Allah is the Guide.

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَدًا ۗ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ ۖ بَل لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ كُلٌّ لَّهُۥ قَـٰنِتُونَ (١١٦)
And they say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He! Rather, to Him belongs whatever there is in the heavens and the earth. All are obedient to Him, (116)
بَدِيعُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَإِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (١١٧)
the Originator of the heavens and the earth. When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is. (117)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَدًا They say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ [Qarai: 2:116] The context indicates that those who made this claim were the Jews and the Christians; for the Jews said, "Ezra is the son of Allah," and the Christians said, "The Messiah is the son of Allah." Indeed, the discourse is directed toward the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب). The People of the Book initially uttered this statement—meaning their saying, "Allah has taken a son"—as a way of honoring their prophets, just as they said, "We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones." Subsequently, it took on the guise of literal truth and reality. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, refuted them in these two verses, turning away from their claim by His saying: بَل لَّهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ No, to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens... [Qarai: 2:116].

This refutation comprises two proofs, each of which negates the possibility of birth or the existence of an offspring from Him, the Glorified. For taking a son (اتخاذ الولد) entails that a natural being (موجود طبيعي) separates some parts of its own existence and detaches them from itself, then, through gradual nurturing (تربية تدريجية), transforms that part into an individual of its own species, similar to itself. However, He, the Glorified, is transcendent above having a likeness (المثل); rather, everything in the heavens and the earth is His property (مملوك له), subsisting in its essence through Him (قائم الذات به), and is devoutly obedient (قانت)—abased in an existential humility (ذلة وجودية) before Him. How then could any thing be a son to Him, being a specific likeness (مماثلاً نوعياً) in relation to Him?

Furthermore, He, the Glorified, is the Originator (بديع) of the heavens and the earth; He only creates what He creates without a prior model (مثال سابق). Thus, nothing of His creation resembles a previous creation, nor does His act resemble the act of another in terms of imitation, likeness, or gradual progression (التدريج). He does not achieve results through intermediary causes (التوصل بالأسباب); rather, when He decrees a matter, He only says to it, "Be," and it is (كن فيكون), without a prior model or gradual process. How then can the "taking of a son" be attributed to Him, when its realization requires nurturing and gradual development?

Thus, His saying: لَّهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۖ كُلٌّ لَّهُ قَانِتُونَ To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth; all are obedient to Him [Qarai: 2:116] is a complete proof. And His saying: بَدِيعُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۖ وَإِذَا قَضَىٰ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ Originator of the heavens and the earth! When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is [Qarai: 2:116] is another complete proof. From these two verses, the following is derived:

First: The law of worship (العبادة) encompasses all created beings among those in the heavens and the earth.

Second: His act, the Exalted, is non-gradual (غير تدريجي). It is inferred from here that every gradual being (موجود تدريجي) possesses a non-gradual aspect through which it emanates from Him, the Exalted, as He said: إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَن يَقُولَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is [Qarai: 36:82], and He said: وَمَا أَمْرُنَا إِلَّا وَاحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ Our command is but a single word, like the twinkling of an eye [Qarai: 54:50]. The detailed discussion of this Quranic reality will come, Allah willing, in the commentary on His saying: إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا All His command, when He wills something... [Qarai: 36:82], so await it.

His saying, the Exalted: سُبْحَانَهُ Immaculate is He! [Qarai: 2:116] is a verbal noun (مصدر) meaning "glorification" (التسبيح). It is only used in the possessive construction (مضافاً) and functions as an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) for an omitted verb—that is, "I glorify Him with a glorification" (سبحته تسبيحاً). The verb was omitted, and the verbal noun was annexed to the object pronoun and placed in its stead. This word conveys a divine discipline (تأديب إلهي) of declaring transcendence (التنزيه) whenever something is mentioned that is unbecoming of His holy presence, the Exalted and Sanctified.

His saying, the Exalted: كُلٌّ لَّهُ قَانِتُونَ all are obedient to Him [Qarai: 2:116]. Devout obedience (القنوت) is worship (العبادة) and self-abasement (التذلل).

His saying, the Exalted: بَدِيعُ السَّمَاوَاتِ Originator of the heavens [Qarai: 2:116]. The originality (بداعة) of a thing is its lack of resemblance to anything else that is known or familiar.

His saying, the Exalted: فَيَكُونُ and it is [Qarai: 2:116] is a resultative (تفريع) following the command "Be" (كن), and it is not in a conditional context that would require the jussive mood.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi and al-Basa'ir, narrated from Sudayr al-Sayrafi, who said: I heard 'Imran ibn A'yan asking Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: بَدِيعُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (Originator of the heavens and the earth). Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: إن الله عز وجل ابتدع الأشياء كلها بعلمه على غير مثال كان قبله ، فابتدع السموات والأرضين ولم يكن قبلهن سموات ولا أرضون أما تسمع لقوله : وكان عرشه على الماء ؟ — "Indeed, Allah, Mighty and Majestic, originated all things by His knowledge without any prior model. He originated the heavens and the earths when there were no heavens or earths before them. Have you not heard His saying: 'And His Throne was upon the water'?"

I say: In this narration, there is another subtle insight: that the "water" intended in His saying, the Exalted: وَكَانَ عَرْشُهُ عَلَى الْمَاءِ (And His Throne was upon the water) is something other than the physical instance (المصداق) of water that we have. This is evidenced by the fact that creation is established upon originality (البداعة), and the Divine Sovereignty (السلطنة الإلهية) was established and poised upon the "water" before the creation of these heavens and earths; thus, it is something other than [this] water. The completion of this discussion will come under His saying, the Exalted: وَكَانَ عَرْشُهُ عَلَى الْمَاءِ and His Throne was upon the water [Qarai: 11:7].

(Scientific and Philosophical Discussion)

Experiments have demonstrated that any two existing things differ in their individual identities (الشخصيات), even if they are united in their universal concepts (الكليات). Even for two beings where the senses cannot distinguish the point of difference between them, the "armed sense" (microscopic or enhanced perception) perceives it. Philosophical proof (البرهان الفلسفي) also necessitates this; for if two hypothesized beings were not distinguished from one another by something external to their essence (الذات), the cause of the hypothesized multiplicity would not be external to their essences. In that case, the essence would be pure (صرفة) and unmixed, and a pure thing (صرف الشيء) does not double or repeat. Thus, what was hypothesized as multiple would be one and not multiple. Therefore, every existing thing is a being whose essence is distinct from any other being. Every existent is original in its existence (بديع الوجود), without a prior or familiar model. And Allah, the Glorified, is the Originator (المبتدع), the Originator of the heavens and the earth.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ لَوْلَا يُكَلِّمُنَا ٱللَّهُ أَوْ تَأْتِينَآ ءَايَةٌ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّثْلَ قَوْلِهِمْ ۘ تَشَـٰبَهَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ ۗ قَدْ بَيَّنَّا ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ (١١٨)
Those who have no knowledge say, ‘Why does not Allah speak to us, or come to us a sign?’ So said those who were before them, [words] similar to what they say. Alike are their hearts. We have certainly made the signs clear for a people who have certainty. (118)
إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا ۖ وَلَا تُسْـَٔلُ عَنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (١١٩)
Indeed We have sent you with the truth, as a bearer of good news and as a warner, and you will not be questioned concerning the inmates of hell. (119)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: وقال الذين لا يعلمون — And those who do not know say... (2:118). These are the polytheists (المشركون) other than the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب). This is indicated by the preceding contrast in His saying, the Exalted: وقالت اليهود ليست النصارى على شيء وقالت النصارى ليست اليهود على شيء وهم يتلون الكتاب كذلك قال الذين لا يعلمون مثل قولهم — The Jews say, ‘The Christians stand on nothing,’ and the Christians say, ‘The Jews stand on nothing,’ though they recite the Book. Even so do those who do not know say the like of their saying (2:113). In that verse, the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) were joined in their speech to the polytheists and the faithless (الكفار) among the Arabs; whereas in this verse, the polytheists and the faithless are joined to them. Thus He said: وقال الذين لا يعلمون لولا يكلمنا الله أو تأتينا آية — And those who do not know say, ‘Why does not Allah speak to us, or a sign come to us?’ (2:118). كذلك قال الذين من قبلهم — Even so said those who were before them (2:118)—and these are the People of the Book, specifically the Jews among them—when they proposed similar sayings to the Prophet of Allah, Moses (عليه‌السلام). Thus, they and the faithless are alike in their thoughts and opinions; these say what those said, and vice versa: تشابهت قلوبهم — Their hearts are alike (2:118).

His saying, the Exalted: قد بينا الآيات لقوم يوقنون — We have certainly made the signs clear for a people who have certainty (2:118). This is a response to the saying of "those who do not know," etc. The meaning is that the signs (الآيات) they demand have already been brought and made clear, but they do not benefit anyone except a people who possess certainty (يوقنون) regarding the signs of Allah. As for these who "do not know," their hearts are veiled by the shroud of ignorance (الجهل) and afflicted by the maladies of prejudice (العصبية) and stubbornness (العناد); and signs avail nothing for a people who do not know. From this, the reason for describing them as lacking knowledge (عدم العلم) becomes apparent. He then reinforced this by directing the discourse to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), indicating that he is sent from Allah with the truth as a bearer of good news (بشيراً) and a warner (نذيراً). Therefore, let his soul be at peace, and let him know that these are the inmates of hell (أصحاب الجحيم), for whom this fate is decreed; there is no hope for their guidance or salvation.

His saying, the Exalted: ولا تسأل عن أصحاب الجحيم — and you will not be questioned about the inmates of hell (2:119). This follows the same course as His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَوَاءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ — As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith (2:6).

Ayahs

وَلَن تَرْضَىٰ عَنكَ ٱلْيَهُودُ وَلَا ٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ حَتَّىٰ تَتَّبِعَ مِلَّتَهُمْ ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ هُوَ ٱلْهُدَىٰ ۗ وَلَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَآءَهُم بَعْدَ ٱلَّذِى جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ ۙ مَا لَكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (١٢٠)
Never will the Jews be pleased with you, nor the Christians, unless you followed their creed. Say, ‘Indeed it is the guidance of Allah which is [true] guidance.’ And should you follow their desires after the knowledge that has come to you, you will not have against Allah any guardian or helper. (120)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَتْلُونَهُۥ حَقَّ تِلَاوَتِهِۦٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ ۗ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (١٢١)
Those to whom We have given the Book follow it as it ought to be followed: they have faith in it. As for those who deny it—it is they who are the losers. (121)
يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتِىَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَأَنِّى فَضَّلْتُكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٢٢)
O Children of Israel, remember My blessing which I bestowed upon you, and that I gave you an advantage over all the nations. (122)
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ يَوْمًا لَّا تَجْزِى نَفْسٌ عَن نَّفْسٍ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يُقْبَلُ مِنْهَا عَدْلٌ وَلَا تَنفَعُهَا شَفَـٰعَةٌ وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (١٢٣)
Beware of the Day when no soul will compensate for another, neither will any ransom be accepted from it, nor will any intercession benefit it, nor will they be helped. (123)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَن تَرْضَىٰ عَنكَ الْيَهُودُ وَلَا النَّصَارَىٰ "Never will the Jews be pleased with you, nor the Christians" [2:120]. This is a return to addressing the two groups after a transition to others. It serves to gather the various strands of the discourse that had become dispersed. It is as if, after these addresses and rebukes directed at them, He returns to His Messenger and says to him: "These people will never be pleased with you until you follow their creed (الملة) — the path of religion and social practice — which they have innovated through their whims (الأهواء) and organized according to their own opinions." He then commanded him to respond to them by saying: قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى اللَّهِ هُوَ الْهُدَىٰ "Say, ‘Indeed it is the guidance of Allah which is [true] guidance’" [2:120]. This means that following is only for the purpose of guidance (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — and there is no guidance except the guidance of Allah and the truth that must be followed. Anything else—namely, their creed—is not guidance; rather, it is their whims (الأهواء) which they have dressed in the garb of religion and named a "creed."

Thus, in His saying, "Say, ‘Indeed it is the guidance of Allah...’", He made "guidance" an allusion to the revealed Quran. Then, by attributing it to Allah, He established the validity of the restriction (الحصر) in His saying, "Indeed it is the guidance of Allah which is [true] guidance," by way of "reversal restriction" (قصر القلب) — a rhetorical device used to correct a misconception by asserting the opposite of what the listener believes. This implies that their creed is devoid of guidance and establishes that it consists merely of their whims. Consequently, this necessitates that what the Prophet possesses is knowledge (العلم), while what they possess is ignorance. This provided the context to conclude the speech with His saying: وَلَئِنِ اتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَاءَهُم بَعْدَ الَّذِي جَاءَكَ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ ۙ مَا لَكَ مِنَ اللَّهِ مِن وَلِيٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ "And if you follow their desires after the knowledge that has come to you, you will have no guardian or helper from Allah" [2:120]. Observe the deep-rooted principles of demonstration (البرهان) and the facets of eloquence (البلاغة) contained in this speech, despite its conciseness, and the fluidity and clarity of its exposition.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ آتَيْنَاهُمُ الْكِتَابَ "Those to whom We have given the Book" [2:121]. Based on the context of the restriction understood from His saying, أُولَٰئِكَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ "it is they who believe in it" [2:121], this sentence could be an answer to a hypothetical question prompted by the previous verse: "If there is no hope for their faith, then who among them will believe? And is directing the call to them in vain?" The answer is that those to whom We have given the Book, while they recite it with a true recitation (حق تلاوته), it is they who believe in their Book and thus believe in you. Or it means they believe in the Book—the revealed Book of Allah, whatever it may be—or they believe in the Book which is the Quran. Accordingly, the restriction in His saying, "it is they who believe in it," is a "restriction of isolation" (قصر إفراد) — asserting a quality for only one of two entities, and the pronoun in "in it" (به), according to some interpretations, involves a "trope of reference" (استخدام) — where a word is used in one sense and its pronoun refers back to it in another.

The "those to whom the Book was given" refers to a group of Jews and Christians who were not followers of whims but were among the people of truth. The "Book" would then refer to the Torah and the Gospel. However, if the intended meaning is the believers in the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم and the "Book" is the Quran, then the meaning is: those to whom We have given the Quran and who recite it with a true recitation, it is they who believe in the Quran, not those who follow their whims. In this case, the restriction is a "reversal restriction" (قصر قلب).

His saying, the Exalted: يَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ اذْكُرُوا "O Children of Israel, remember..." [2:122], until the end of the two verses. This is a return of the conclusion of the discourse to its beginning, and its end to its start. With this, a major section of the addresses to the Children of Israel is concluded.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Irshad of al-Daylami, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام regarding His saying: الَّذِينَ آتَيْنَاهُمُ الْكِتَابَ يَتْلُونَهُ حَقَّ تِلَاوَتِهِ "Those to whom We have given the Book recite it with a true recitation," he said: يرتلون آياته ويتفقهون به ويعملون بأحكامه ، ويرجون وعده ، ويخافون وعيده ، ويعتبرون بقصصه ، ويأتمرون بأوامره ، وينتهون بنواهيه ، ما هو والله حفظ آياته ، ودرس حروفه ، وتلاوة سورة ، ودرس أعشاره وأخماسه ، حفظوا حروفه وأضاعوا حدوده ، وإنما هو تدبر آياته والعمل بأحكامه ، قال الله تعالى : كتاب أنزلناه إليك مبارك ليدبّروا آياته — "They recite its verses slowly and distinctly, seek deep understanding (يتفقهون) through it, act according to its rulings, hope for its promise, fear its threat, take lessons from its stories, carry out its commands, and desist at its prohibitions. By Allah, it is not merely memorizing its verses, studying its letters, reciting its chapters, or studying its tenths and fifths. They memorized its letters but neglected its boundaries. Rather, it is contemplating (تدبر) its verses and acting upon its rulings. Allah the Exalted said: '[It is] a blessed Book that We have sent down to you, so that they may contemplate its signs' [38:29]."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: يَتْلُونَهُ حَقَّ تِلَاوَتِهِ "they recite it with a true recitation," he عليه‌السلام said: الوقوف عند الجنة والنار — "Pausing at [the mention of] Paradise and the Fire."

I say: What is meant by this is contemplation (التدبر).

In al-Kafi, narrated from him عليه‌السلام regarding the verse, he عليه‌السلام said: هم الأئمة — "They are the Imams."

I say: This is from the perspective of "conceptual flow and application" (الجري والانطباق) — the principle that Quranic verses apply to their most perfect archetypes and continue to be relevant across time — to the most perfect instance (المصداق).

Ayah

۞ وَإِذِ ٱبْتَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ رَبُّهُۥ بِكَلِمَـٰتٍ فَأَتَمَّهُنَّ ۖ قَالَ إِنِّى جَاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِمَامًا ۖ قَالَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِى ۖ قَالَ لَا يَنَالُ عَهْدِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٢٤)
When his Lord tested Abraham with certain words and he fulfilled them, He said, ‘I am making you the Imam of mankind.’ Said he, ‘And from among my descendants?’ He said, ‘My pledge does not extend to the unjust.’ (124)

(Commentary)

This section begins with segments of the stories of Ibrahim (peace be upon him), serving as a preamble and introduction to the verses regarding the changing of the Qiblah (القبلة) — the direction of prayer — and the verses on the rulings of Hajj (الحج) — the pilgrimage to Makkah. Along with these is an exposition of the reality of the primordial religion (الدين الحنيف) — the pure, monotheistic faith — of Islam in its various degrees: from the principles of divine knowledge (أصول المعارف), to ethics (الأخلاق), and the general branches of jurisprudential rulings (الأحكام الفرعية الفقهية). The verses encompass the story of Allah, the Exalted, singling him out for the Imamate (الإمامة) — the divine office of leadership and guidance — his building of the Kaaba, and his prayer for the sending of the Messenger.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذِ ابْتَلَىٰ إِبْرَاهِيمَ رَبُّهُ بِكَلِمَاتٍ فَأَتَمَّهُنَّ ۖ قَالَ إِنِّي جَاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِمَامًا ۖ قَالَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِي ۖ قَالَ لَا يَنَالُ عَهْدِي الظَّالِمِينَ — "And when his Lord tested Abraham with certain words, and he fulfilled them, He said, ‘I am making you the Imam of mankind.’ Said he, ‘And from among my descendants?’ He said, ‘My pledge does not extend to the wrongdoers.’" (2:124). This is an allusion to the story of his being granted the Imamate and being honored with it. This event occurred toward the end of Ibrahim’s (peace be upon him) life, after he had reached old age, after the birth of Ismail and Ishaq, and after he had settled Ismail and his mother in Makkah, as some commentators have also noted. The evidence for this is his saying (peace be upon him), as recounted by Allah, the Glorified, after Allah said to him: "I am making you the Imam of mankind." He said: "And from among my descendants?" Before the angels came with the glad tidings of Ismail and Ishaq, he (peace be upon him) did not know, nor did he expect, that he would have any descendants after him. Indeed, even after the angels gave him the glad tidings of children, he addressed them in a manner that outwardly suggested despair and hopelessness, as the Exalted says: وَنَبِّئْهُمْ عَن ضَيْفِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ إِذْ دَخَلُوا عَلَيْهِ فَقَالُوا سَلَامًا قَالَ إِنَّا مِنكُمْ وَجِلُونَ قَالُوا لَا تَوْجَلْ إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلَامٍ عَلِيمٍ قَالَ أَبَشَّرْتُمُونِي عَلَىٰ أَن مَّسَّنِيَ الْكِبَرُ فَبِمَ تُبَشِّرُونَ قَالُوا بَشَّرْنَاكَ بِالْحَقِّ فَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ الْقَانِطِينَ — "And inform them about Abraham’s guests, when they entered upon him and said, ‘Peace!’ He said, ‘We are indeed afraid of you.’ They said, ‘Do not be afraid. Indeed we give you the good news of a wise boy.’ He said, ‘Do you give me good news though old age has visited me? So what is the good news that you give?’ They said, ‘We have given you good news in truth; so do not be among the despondent.’" (15:51-55). Similarly, his wife reacted as Allah, the Exalted, recounts in the story of the glad tidings: وَامْرَأَتُهُ قَائِمَةٌ فَضَحِكَتْ فَبَشَّرْنَاهَا بِإِسْحَاقَ وَمِن وَرَاءِ إِسْحَاقَ يَعْقُوبَ قَالَتْ يَا وَيْلَتَىٰ أَأَلِدُ وَأَنَا عَجُوزٌ وَهَٰذَا بَعْلِي شَيْخًا ۖ إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَشَيْءٌ عَجِيبٌ قَالُوا أَتَعْجَبِينَ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللَّهِ ۖ رَحْمَتُ اللَّهِ وَبَرَكَاتُهُ عَلَيْكُمْ أَهْلَ الْبَيْتِ ۚ إِنَّهُ حَمِيدٌ مَّجِيدٌ — "And his wife was standing by and she laughed. So We gave her the good news of Isaac, and of Jacob after Isaac. She said, ‘Oh, woe to me! Shall I give birth when I am an old woman, and this husband of mine is an old man? This is indeed an odd thing!’ They said, ‘Are you amazed at Allah’s dispensation? The mercy of Allah and His blessings be upon you, O people of the household! Indeed He is All-laudable, All-glorious.’" (11:71-73). As you can see, the speech of both of them shows traces of despair and hopelessness; therefore, the angels responded with a type of speech intended to comfort them and put their minds at ease. Thus, neither he nor his family knew that he would be granted offspring. His saying (peace be upon him), "And from among my descendants?" after the saying of the Exalted, "I am making you the Imam of mankind," is the speech of one who believes he has descendants. How could someone with even the slightest proficiency in the etiquette of speech — especially one like Ibrahim, the Friend (الخليل), in a discourse with his Majestic Lord — utter something about which he has no knowledge? Had that been the case, it would have been necessary for him to say, "And from among my descendants, if You grant me descendants," or something to that effect. Therefore, the event occurred, as we mentioned, toward the end of Ibrahim's life after the glad tidings.

Furthermore, the saying of the Exalted: "And when his Lord tested Ibrahim with certain words and he fulfilled them, He said: 'I am making you the Imam of mankind,'" indicates that this bestowed Imamate only came after he was tested with the trials (الامتحانات) Allah subjected him to. These are none other than the various types of tribulations (البلاء) he (peace be upon him) was tested with during his life. The Quran explicitly states that one of the clearest of these was the trial of the sacrifice of Ismail. The Exalted says: قَالَ يَا بُنَيَّ إِنِّي أَرَىٰ فِي الْمَنَامِ أَنِّي أَذْبَحُكَ — "He said, ‘O my son! I have seen in a dream that I am sacrificing you...’" until He says: إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَهُوَ الْبَلَاءُ الْمُبِينُ — "This was indeed a manifest test." (37:102-106).

This event occurred in Ibrahim's old age, as Allah, the Exalted, recounts from his saying: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي وَهَبَ لِي عَلَى الْكِبَرِ إِسْمَاعِيلَ وَإِسْحَاقَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّي لَسَمِيعُ الدُّعَاءِ — "All praise belongs to Allah, who, despite [my] old age, gave me Ishmael and Isaac. Indeed my Lord hears all supplications." (14:39).

Let us return to the words of the verse. His saying: "And when his Lord tested (ابتلى) Ibrahim." Testing (الابتلاء) and trial (البلاء) are synonymous. You say: "I tested him (ابتليته)" or "I tried him (بلوته)" with such-and-forth, meaning I examined and tested him. This occurs when you present a matter to someone or place them in an event to test them thereby, bringing to light the latent psychological traits (الصفات النفسانية) they possess, such as obedience, courage, generosity, chastity, knowledge, and loyalty, or their opposites. Therefore, testing (الابتلاء) only occurs through action (العمل), for it is action that reveals the latent qualities of a human being, unlike speech, which is subject to truth or falsehood. The Exalted says: إِنَّا بَلَوْنَاهُمْ كَمَا بَلَوْنَا أَصْحَابَ الْجَنَّةِ — "Indeed We have tested them just as We tested the owners of the garden" (68:17), and the Exalted says: إِنَّ اللَّهَ مُبْتَلِيكُم بِنَهَرٍ — "Allah will test you with a river" (2:249).

Thus, the connection of the "testing" in the verse to the "words" (الكلمات) — if what is meant by them is verbal statements — is only by virtue of their connection to action and their recounting of covenants (العهود) and commands (الأوامر) related to action. This is like the saying of the Exalted: وَقُولُوا لِلنَّاسِ حُسْنًا — "and speak kindly to people" (2:83), meaning: interact with them in a beautiful manner.

Regarding His saying: "with certain words (بكلمات) and he fulfilled them (فأتمهن)." The term "words" (الكلمات) is the plural of "word" (كلمة). Although it is sometimes applied in the Quran to an external entity (العين الخارجي) rather than a verbal utterance — as in the saying of the Exalted: بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنْهُ اسْمُهُ الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ — "with a word from Him whose name is the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary" (3:45) — this is done by way of considering it a "saying" (القول), as the Exalted says: إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسَىٰ عِندَ اللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ ۖ خَلَقَهُ مِن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ قَالَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ — "Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was." (3:59).

Everything attributed to the Exalted as a "word" (الكلمة) in the Quran is intended as a "saying" (القول), such as the saying of the Exalted: وَلَا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِمَاتِ اللَّهِ — "and none can change the words of Allah" (6:34); and His saying: لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِكَلِمَاتِ اللَّهِ — "there is no changing the words of Allah" (10:64); and His saying: وَيُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِمَاتِهِ — "and confirm the truth by His words" (8:7); and His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِمْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ — "Indeed those against whom the word of your Lord has become due will not believe" (10:96); and His saying: وَلَٰكِنْ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَةُ الْعَذَابِ — "but the word of punishment has become due" (39:71); and His saying: وَكَذَٰلِكَ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّهُمْ أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ — "Thus the word of your Lord became due against the faithless that they are the inmates of the Fire" (40:6); and His saying: وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ إِلَىٰ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى لَّقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ — "and had it not been for a prior word from your Lord [postponing their punishment] until a specified time, the matter would have been decided between them" (42:14); and His saying: وَكَلِمَةُ اللَّهِ هِيَ الْعُلْيَا — "and the word of Allah is the highest" (9:40); and His saying: قَالَ فَالْحَقُّ وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ — "He said, ‘The truth is—and the truth I say—’" (38:84); and His saying: إِنَّمَا قَوْلُنَا لِشَيْءٍ إِذَا أَرَدْنَاهُ أَن نَّقُولَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ — "All that We say to a thing, when We will it, is to say to it ‘Be!’ and it is." (16:40). In these and similar instances, "saying" (القول) is intended in the sense that speech is the directing of what the speaker wishes to inform the listener of, as in a report (الأخبار), or for the purpose of imposing it upon him, as in a creative command (الانشاء). For this reason, it is sometimes described in the speech of the Exalted as being "complete" (التمام), as in the saying of the Exalted: وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ صِدْقًا وَعَدْلًا ۚ لَّا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِمَاتِهِ — "The word of your Lord has been completed in truth and justice. Nothing can change His words" (6:115); and the saying of the Exalted: وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ الْحُسْنَىٰ عَلَىٰ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ — "And the best word of your Lord was fulfilled for the Children of Israel" (7:137). It is as if the word, when issued by its speaker, is still incomplete (ناقصة) until it is clothed in the garment of action and becomes a realized truth (صدقاً).

This does not contradict the fact that the saying (قول) of the Exalted is His act (فعل). Objective realities (الحقائق الواقعية) have their own governing principles, while linguistic and verbal considerations (العنايات الكلامية اللفظية) have others. Whatever Allah, the Glorified, wishes to manifest to one of His prophets or others after it was hidden, or wishes to impose upon someone, is a "saying" and "speech" for Him, because it encompasses the purpose of saying and speech and contains the goal of report, news, command, and prohibition. Applying the terms "saying" (القول) and "word" (الكلمة) to such things is common in usage when they contain what a saying or word conveys. You might say: "I shall certainly do such-and-such because of a saying I said and a word I previously gave," even if you did not literally utter a saying or provide a word; rather, you made a firm resolve (عزيمة) that no intercession of an intercessor or weakness of will could break. From this is the verse of Antarah:

And my saying to it whenever it gasped and heaved: "Stay in your place; you shall be praised or find rest."

By "saying" (القول), he means the psychological preparation of himself for steadfastness and the resolve to keep his soul in its place, so that it may win praise if killed, or find rest if it triumphs.

Once you understand this, it becomes clear that what is meant by the saying of the Exalted, "with certain words" (بكلمات), are the decrees (قضايا) he was tested with and the divine covenants (عهود إلهية) required of him, such as his trial with the stars, the idols, the fire, the migration, the sacrifice of his son, and so on. The text does not specify what these words were because the primary purpose does not depend on that. However, His saying: "He said: 'I am making you the Imam of mankind,'" by virtue of its following the "words," indicates that they were matters through which his worthiness (لياقته) for the station of Imamate was established.

These are the "words." As for his "fulfilling them" (إتمامهن), if the pronoun in the saying of the Exalted "fulfilled them" refers to Ibrahim, then the meaning of his fulfilling them is his performing what was required of him and his compliance with what he was commanded. If the pronoun refers to the Exalted — as is outwardly apparent — then the meaning is His granting him the success (توفيقه) to achieve what was required and assisting him in that. As for what some have mentioned — that the "words" refer to the saying of the Exalted: "He said: 'I am making you the Imam of mankind'" until the end of the verses — this is a meaning that should not be relied upon, as it is not customary in the Quran to apply the term "words" (الكلمات) to complete sentences of speech.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنِّي جَاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِمَامًا — "I am making you the Imam of mankind," meaning an exemplar (مقتدى) whom people emulate, following you in your words and actions. Thus, the Imam (الإمام) is the one whom people emulate and follow. For this reason, several commentators mentioned that what is meant here is Prophethood (النبوة), because a prophet is emulated by his community in their religion. The Exalted says: وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطَاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ — "We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave" (4:64). However, this interpretation is utterly untenable.

Firstly: Because His saying "Imam" (إماماً) is the second object (مفعول ثان) of its governing agent, which is His saying "making you" (جاعلك). The active participle (اسم الفاعل) does not govern if it is in the past tense; it only governs if it is in the present or future tense. Thus, His saying, "I am making you the Imam of mankind," is a promise to him of the Imamate in the future. Yet, this was a revelation (وحي), and revelation only occurs with Prophethood. Thus, he (peace be upon him) was already a prophet before assuming the Imamate. Therefore, the Imamate in the verse is not synonymous with Prophethood (as noted by some commentators).

Secondly: Because we explained at the beginning of the discourse that the story of the Imamate occurred toward the end of Ibrahim’s (peace be upon him) life, after the glad tidings of Ishaq and Ismail. The angels only brought the glad tidings while on their way to the people of Lut to destroy them. Ibrahim was already a commissioned prophet at that time. Since he was a prophet before becoming an Imam, his Imamate is distinct from his Prophethood.

The origin of this interpretation and its like is the trivialization (الابتذال) that has affected the meanings of terms in the Noble Quran in the eyes of people due to repeated usage over time. Among these terms is the word "Imamate" (الإمامة). Some interpreted it as Prophethood, precedence, or being one who is obeyed in general. Others interpreted it as the Caliphate (الخلافة), the Successorship (الوصاية), or leadership (الرئاسة) in matters of religion and the world. None of these are correct. Prophethood (النبوة) means bearing news (النبأ) from Allah's side. Messengership (الرسالة) means bearing the delivery of the message (التبليغ). Being obeyed (المطاعية) and obedience (الاطاعة) is the human acceptance of what another sees or commands, which is a necessary consequence of Prophethood and Messengership. The Caliphate (الخلافة) is a type of deputyship (النيابة), as is the Successorship (الوصاية). Leadership (الرئاسة) is a type of being obeyed, where one is the source of authority in society. All these meanings are distinct from the meaning of Imamate (الإمامة), which is for a person to be such that others emulate him, aligning their actions and words with his actions and words by way of following (التبعية). It makes no sense to say to a prophet whose obedience is already mandatory: "I am making you a prophet for mankind," or "one who is obeyed in what you deliver through your prophethood," or "a leader who commands and prohibits in religion," or "a successor," or "a caliph on earth who judges between people in their disputes by the law of Allah."

The Imamate does not differ from the preceding "words" and is not specific to its context merely by linguistic consideration. It is not correct to say to a prophet — for whom being obeyed is a necessary consequence of his prophethood — "I am making you one who is obeyed by mankind" after having already made him so. Nor is it correct to say to him something that amounts to the same meaning, even if it differs by mere linguistic consideration, for the same objection remains. These divine gifts are not limited to mere verbal concepts; rather, beneath them lie realities of true knowledge (المعارف الحقيقية). Thus, the meaning of Imamate has a reality beyond these concepts.

What we find in the speech of the Exalted is that whenever He addresses the meaning of Imamate, He accompanies it with guidance (الهداية) by way of explanation. The Exalted says in the stories of Ibrahim (peace be upon him): وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ نَافِلَةً ۖ وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنَا صَالِحِينَ وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا — "And We gave him Isaac, and Jacob as well, and each of them We made righteous. We made them Imams, guiding by Our command" (21:72-73). And the Glorified says: وَجَعَلْنَا مِنْهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا لَمَّا صَبَرُوا ۖ وَكَانُوا بِآيَاتِنَا يُوقِنُونَ — "And We appointed from among them Imams, guiding by Our command, when they had been patient and had conviction in Our signs." (32:24). Thus, He described it with "guidance" as a defining attribute, then restricted it by "the command" (الأمر). He thereby clarified that the Imamate is not absolute guidance, but rather the guidance that occurs by the command of Allah. This "command" (الأمر) is that whose reality He explained in His saying: إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَن يَقُولَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ فَسُبْحَانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is. So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things" (36:82-83); and His saying: وَمَا أَمْرُنَا إِلَّا وَاحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ — "Our command is but a single [word], like the twinkling of an eye." (54:50). We will explain regarding these two verses that the Divine Command — which the aforementioned verse calls the Dominion (الملكوت) — is another aspect of creation (الخلق) through which they face Allah, the Glorified. It is pure and sanctified from the constraints of time and space, free from change and alteration. It is what is meant by the word "Be" (كن), which is nothing other than the objective existence (الوجود العيني) of the thing. This is in contrast to "creation" (الخلق), which is the other of the two aspects of things, characterized by change, gradualness, and conformity to the laws of motion and time. Keep this in mind in its summary form until the detailed explanation comes to you, if Allah the Mighty wills.

In short, the Imam is a guide who guides by a celestial command (أمر ملكوتي) that accompanies him. Thus, the Imamate, according to the inner dimension (الباطن), is a type of guardianship (ولاية) over people in their actions. Its guidance is the "conveying" of them to the goal by the command of Allah, rather than the mere "showing of the path," which is the function of the Prophet, the Messenger, and every believer who guides toward Allah, the Glorified, through sincere advice and goodly exhortation. The Exalted says: وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسَانِ قَوْمِهِ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ ۖ فَيُضِلُّ اللَّهُ مَن يَشَاءُ وَيَهْدِي مَن يَشَاءُ — "We did not send any apostle except with the language of his people, so that he might make [Our messages] clear to them. Then Allah leads astray whomever He wishes, and guides whomever He wishes" (14:4). And the Exalted says regarding the believer from the family of Pharaoh: وَقَالَ الَّذِي آمَنَ يَا قَوْمِ اتَّبِعُونِ أَهْدِكُمْ سَبِيلَ الرَّشَادِ — "The one who had faith said, ‘O my people! Follow me, I will guide you to the way of rectitude.’" (40:38). And the Exalted says: فَلَوْلَا نَفَرَ مِن كُلِّ فِرْقَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ طَائِفَةٌ لِّيَتَفَقَّهُوا فِي الدِّينِ وَلِيُنذِرُوا قَوْمَهُمْ إِذَا رَجَعُوا إِلَيْهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَحْذَرُونَ — "But why should not there go forth a group from each of their sections to become learned in religion, and to warn their people when they return to them, so that they may beware?" (9:122). This meaning will become even clearer to you.

Furthermore, He explained the reason for the bestowal of the Imamate by His saying: "when they had been patient and had conviction in Our signs." He clarified that the criterion for this is their patience (صبرهم) for the sake of Allah — and He mentioned patience in an absolute sense, so it applies to everything a servant is tested and tried with in his servitude — and their being people of conviction (موقنين) prior to that. Among the stories of Ibrahim (peace be upon him), He mentioned His saying: وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُرِي إِبْرَاهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ — "Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who have conviction." (6:75). As you can see, the verse outwardly indicates that the showing of the Dominion (الملكوت) to Ibrahim was a preamble to the effusion of conviction (اليقين) upon him. It becomes clear from this that conviction is inseparable from the witnessing of the Dominion, as is apparent from the saying of the Exalted: كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ — "No indeed! Were you to know with certain knowledge, you would surely see hell." (102:6-7). And the saying of the Exalted: كَلَّا ۖ بَلْ ۜ رَانَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِم مَّا كَانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ كَلَّا إِنَّهُمْ عَن رَّبِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَّمَحْجُوبُونَ — "No indeed! Rather their hearts have been sullied by what they have been earning. No indeed! They will surely be barred from their Lord on that day" — until He says — كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْأَبْرَارِ لَفِي عِلِّيِّينَ وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا عِلِّيُّونَ كِتَابٌ مَّرْقُومٌ يَشْهَدُهُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ — "No indeed! The record of the pious is indeed in ‘Illiyyun. And what will show you what is ‘Illiyyun? It is a written record, witnessed by those brought near [to Allah]." (83:14-21). These verses indicate that those brought near (المقربون) are those who are not barred from their Lord by any veil of the heart — such as sin, ignorance, doubt, or hesitation. They are the people of conviction (اليقين) in Allah, and they witness 'Illiyyun just as they witness Hell.

In short, the Imam must be a person of conviction (يقين) for whom the world of the Dominion (عالم الملكوت) is unveiled — one who is realized through the words (كلمات) of Allah, the Glorified. It has already been mentioned that the Dominion is the "Command" (الأمر), which is the inner aspect (الوجه الباطن) of the two aspects of this world. Thus, His saying, "guiding by Our command," clearly indicates that whatever the matter of guidance relates to — namely, hearts and actions — the Imam possesses its inner reality and its "command-based" aspect (وجهه الأمريّ), which is present before him and not hidden from him. It is well known that hearts and actions, like all other things, possess two aspects. Thus, the actions of the servants, both good and evil, are present before the Imam and reach him. He is the overseer (المهيمن) of both paths: the path of felicity and the path of wretchedness. The Exalted also said: يَوْمَ نَدْعُو كُلَّ أُنَاسٍ بِإِمَامِهِمْ — "The day We shall summon every group of people with their Imam" (17:71). The interpretation of this as the True Imam, rather than the book of deeds as might be thought from its outward appearance, will follow. Thus, the Imam is the one who leads people to Allah, the Glorified, on the day when secrets are tested, just as he leads them to Him in the outward and inward of this worldly life. Furthermore, the verse implies that no time or era is devoid of an Imam, due to the saying of the Exalted, "every group of people," as will be explained in the commentary on that verse.

Moreover, this meaning — namely, the Imamate — despite its nobility and greatness, can only be sustained by one who is essentially felicitous (سعيد الذات) in himself. For one whose essence might be clothed in wrongdoing (الظلم) and wretchedness (الشقاء), his felicity only comes through guidance from another. Allah, the Exalted, has said: أَفَمَن يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ أَحَقُّ أَن يُتَّبَعَ أَمَّن لَّا يَهِدِّي إِلَّا أَن يُهْدَىٰ — "Is He who guides to the truth worthier to be followed, or he who guides not unless he [himself] is guided?" (10:35). In the verse, a contrast is made between the one who guides to the truth and the one who is not guided except by another — meaning the one who is guided by another. This contrast necessitates that the one who guides to the truth must be guided by himself (مهتدياً بنفسه), and that the one who is guided by another can never be a guide to the truth.

From this, two points are concluded: First: That the Imam must be infallible (معصوماً) from error and sin; otherwise, he would not be guided by himself, as mentioned. This is also indicated by the saying of the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا وَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْرَاتِ وَإِقَامَ الصَّلَاةِ وَإِيتَاءَ الزَّكَاةِ ۖ وَكَانُوا لَنَا عَابِدِينَ — "We made them Imams, guiding by Our command, and We revealed to them the performance of good deeds, the maintenance of prayer, and the giving of zakat, and they used to worship Us." (21:73). Thus, the actions of the Imam are "good deeds" (خيرات) to which he is guided not by the guidance of another, but by being guided in himself through divine support and lordly direction. The evidence for this is His saying: "the performance of good deeds" (فِعْلَ الْخَيْرَاتِ), based on the principle that an annexed infinitive (المصدر المضاف) indicates occurrence. There is a difference between saying, "We revealed to them to perform good deeds," which does not necessarily indicate realization and occurrence, and His saying, "We revealed to them the performance of good deeds," which indicates that the good deeds they performed were by way of an internal revelation and celestial support. Second: The converse of the first point, which is that whoever is not infallible can never be an Imam who guides to the truth.

Through this exposition, it becomes clear that what is meant by "the wrongdoers" (الظالمين) in the saying of the Exalted, "He said: 'My pledge does not extend to the wrongdoers,'" is anyone from whom any wrongdoing (ظلم) has issued, whether polytheism (شرك) or sin (معصية), even if it was for a period of his life and he subsequently repented and became righteous.

One of our teachers (may Allah have mercy on him) was asked about the manner in which the verse proves the infallibility (عصمة) of the Imam.

He replied: People, according to rational division, fall into four categories: one who is a wrongdoer throughout his entire life; one who is never a wrongdoer throughout his entire life; one who is a wrongdoer in the first part of his life but not the last; and one who is the opposite of that. Ibrahim (peace be upon him) is of too great a status to ask for the Imamate for the first and fourth categories of his descendants. Thus, two categories remain. Allah negated one of them, which is the one who is a wrongdoer in the first part of his life but not the last. Therefore, the other remains, which is the one who is never a wrongdoer throughout his entire life. (End of quote). From the preceding exposition, several matters have become clear:

First: That the Imamate is a divine appointment (مجعولة). Second: That the Imam must be infallible (معصوماً) with a divine infallibility. Third: That the earth, as long as there are people on it, is never devoid of a True Imam. Fourth: That the Imam must be supported (مؤيداً) by Allah, the Exalted. Fifth: That the actions of the servants are not hidden from the knowledge of the Imam. Sixth: That he must be knowledgeable of everything people need in the affairs of their worldly life and their hereafter. Seventh: That it is impossible for there to exist among them anyone who surpasses him in the virtues of the soul (فضائل النفس).

These are seven issues that are the fundamental matters of the Imamate, provided by this noble verse when joined with other verses. And Allah is the Guide.

If you say: If the Imamate is guidance by the command of Allah, and it is guidance to the truth which is inseparable from being guided by essence — as inferred from the saying of the Exalted: "Is He who guides to the truth worthier to be followed..." — then all prophets would certainly be Imams. This is because it is clear that the prophethood of a prophet is not complete except by guidance from Allah's side through revelation, without it being acquired from another through teaching or instruction. In that case, the gift of Prophethood would necessitate the gift of Imamate, and the problem would return to you.

I say: What is obtained from the previous exposition inferred from the verse is that guidance by the truth (الهداية بالحق), which is the Imamate, necessitates being guided by the truth (الاهتداء بالحق). As for the converse — that everyone who is guided by the truth is a guide for others by the truth, such that every prophet, by virtue of being guided by essence, is an Imam — this has not yet been established. Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned this "being guided by the truth" without pairing it with "guiding others by the truth" in His saying: وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ ۚ كُلًّا هَدَيْنَا ۚ وَنُوحًا هَدَيْنَا مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِهِ دَاوُودَ وَسُلَيْمَانَ وَأَيُّوبَ وَيُوسُفَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَهَارُونَ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ وَزَكَرِيَّا وَيَحْيَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَإِلْيَاسَ ۖ كُلٌّ مِّنَ الصَّالِحِينَ وَإِسْمَاعِيلَ وَالْيَسَعَ وَيُونُسَ وَلُوطًا ۚ وَكُلًّا فَضَّلْنَا عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ وَمِنْ آبَائِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ وَإِخْوَانِهِمْ ۖ وَاجْتَبَيْنَاهُمْ وَهَدَيْنَاهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ ذَٰلِكَ هُدَى اللَّهِ يَهْدِي بِهِ مَن يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ ۚ وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ آتَيْنَاهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ ۚ فَإِن يَكْفُرْ بِهَا هَٰؤُلَاءِ فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنَا بِهَا قَوْمًا لَّيْسُوا بِهَا بِكَافِرِينَ أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَدَى اللَّهُ ۖ فَبِهُدَاهُمُ اقْتَدِهْ — "And We gave him Isaac and Jacob and guided each of them. And We had guided Noah before, and from his offspring, David and Solomon, Job, Joseph, Moses and Aaron—thus do We reward the virtuous—and Zechariah, John, Jesus and Ilyas—each of them among the righteous—and Ishmael, Elisha, Jonah and Lot—each We exalted above all the worlds. And from among their fathers, their offspring and their brothers—We chose them and guided them to a straight path. That is Allah’s guidance: with it He guides whomever He wishes of His servants. And were they to ascribe any partners [to Him], what they used to do would surely fail them. They are the ones whom We gave the Book, the judgement and prophethood. So if these disbelieve in them, We have certainly entrusted them to a people who will never disbelieve in them. They are the ones whom Allah has guided. So follow their guidance." (6:84-90). The context of the verses, as you see, indicates that this guidance is a matter that is not subject to change or failure, and that this guidance will not be removed from the community after the Messenger of Allah, but rather from the descendants of Ibrahim among them specifically. This is indicated by the saying of the Exalted: وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِ إِنَّنِي بَرَاءٌ مِّمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا الَّذِي فَطَرَنِي فَإِنَّهُ سَيَهْدِينِ وَجَعَلَهَا كَلِمَةً بَاقِيَةً فِي عَقِبِهِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ — "When Abraham said to his father and his people, ‘I am indeed quit of what you worship, except Him who created me, for He will surely guide me.’ He made it a lasting word among his posterity so that they may come back [to the right path]." (43:26-28). He informed his people of his disavowal in the present and informed them of his guidance in the future. This is the true guidance by the command of Allah, not the guidance provided by rational reflection and consideration, for that was already present and indicated by his saying: "I am indeed quit of what you worship, except Him who created me." Then Allah informed that He made this guidance a "lasting word" (كلمة باقية) among Ibrahim's posterity. This is one of the instances where the Quran applies the term "word" (الكلمة) to an external matter rather than a verbal utterance, as in the saying of the Exalted: وَأَلْزَمَهُمْ كَلِمَةَ التَّقْوَىٰ وَكانُوا أَحَقَّ بِهَا — "and He made them abide by the word of God-wariness, and they were worthier of it" (48:26).

It has become clear from what has been mentioned that the Imamate remains in the children of Ibrahim after him. The saying of the Exalted: "He said: 'And from among my descendants?' He said: 'My pledge does not extend to the wrongdoers,'" alludes to this. Ibrahim (peace be upon him) only asked for the Imamate for some of his descendants, not all of them. Thus, he was answered by its negation for the wrongdoers among his children. Not all of his children are necessarily wrongdoers, such that negating it for the wrongdoers would be a negation for all of them. Thus, there is an answer to what he asked, along with the clarification that it is a "pledge" (عهد), and His pledge, the Exalted, does not extend to the wrongdoers.

His saying, the Exalted: لَا يَنَالُ عَهْدِي الظَّالِمِينَ — "My pledge does not extend to the wrongdoers." In this expression is an allusion to the extreme distance of the wrongdoers from the presence of the Divine Pledge. This is a type of metaphorical allusion (استعارة بالكناية).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): إن الله عز وجل اتخذ إبراهيم عبداً قبل أن يتخذه نبياً ، وإن الله اتخذه نبياً قبل أن يتخذه رسولاً ، وإن الله اتخذه رسولاً قبل أن يتخذه خليلاً ، وأن الله اتخذه خليلاً قبل أن يتخذه إماماً ، فلما جمع له الأشياء قال : « إِنِّي جَاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِمَامًا » قال عليه‌السلام : فمن عظمها في عين إبراهيم قال : ومن ذريتي قال لا ينال عهدي الظالمين قال : لا يكون السفيه إمام التقيّ — "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, took Ibrahim as a servant (عبداً) before He took him as a prophet (نبياً). And Allah took him as a prophet before He took him as a messenger (رسولاً). And Allah took him as a messenger before He took him as a friend (خليلاً). And Allah took him as a friend before He took him as an Imam (إماماً). When He had gathered all these things for him, He said: 'I am making you the Imam of mankind.'" He (peace be upon him) said: "Because of its greatness in Ibrahim's eyes, he said: 'And from among my descendants?' He said: 'My pledge does not extend to the wrongdoers.'" He said: "The foolish one (السفيه) cannot be the Imam of the God-conscious (التقيّ)."

I say: This meaning has also been narrated from him through another chain, and from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) through another chain, and al-Mufid narrated it from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

His saying: "Indeed, Allah took Ibrahim as a servant before He took him as a prophet," is inferred from the saying of the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ رُشْدَهُ مِن قَبْلُ وَكُنَّا بِهِ عَالِمِينَ — "Certainly We had given Abraham his rectitude before, and We knew him well" (21:51) until His saying: مِّنَ الشَّاهِدِينَ — "among the witnesses" (21:56). This is the taking of him into servitude at the beginning of Ibrahim's affair.

Know that Allah's taking someone from among mankind as a servant (عبداً) is different from that person being a servant in himself. Servitude (العبدية) is a necessary consequence of origination and creation; it is inseparable from any creature possessing understanding and consciousness. It does not accept appointment or "taking," for it is the fact that a human being, for example, is owned in his existence by his Lord, being created and fashioned by Him, whether he conducts his life according to what his essential owned-ness requires and submits to the Lordship of his Mighty Lord, or does not. The Exalted says: إِن كُلُّ مَن فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِلَّا آتِي الرَّحْمَٰنِ عَبْدًا — "There is none in the heavens and the earth but he comes to the All-beneficent as a servant." (19:93). However, if one does not conduct oneself according to the norms of servitude and the laws of bondage, out of arrogance on earth and rebellion, it is appropriate that he not be called a "servant" in view of the ultimate goals. For the servant is the one who has submitted his face to his Lord and surrendered the management of himself to Him. Thus, it is fitting that the name "servant" only be applied to one who is a servant in himself and a servant in his actions. He is the servant in reality. The Exalted says: وَعِبَادُ الرَّحْمَٰنِ الَّذِينَ يَمْشُونَ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ هَوْنًا — "The servants of the All-beneficent are those who walk humbly on the earth" (25:63). Accordingly, Allah's "taking" a human as a servant — which is the acceptance of his being a servant and turning toward him with Lordship — is the Guardianship (الولاية). It is His taking charge of his affairs just as a master takes charge of the affairs of his servant. Servitude is the key to Guardianship, as indicated by the saying of the Exalted: إِنَّ وَلِيِّيَ اللَّهُ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْكِتَابَ ۖ وَهُوَ يَتَوَلَّى الصَّالِحِينَ — "Indeed my guardian is Allah, who sent down the Book, and He takes charge of the righteous" (7:196), meaning those worthy of Guardianship. Indeed, the Exalted has named the Prophet in several verses of His Book as "the servant." The Exalted says: الَّذِي أَنزَلَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِ الْكِتَابَ — "who sent down the Book to His servant" (18:1); and the Exalted says: يُنَزِّلُ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِ آيَاتٍ بَيِّنَاتٍ — "He sends down manifest signs to His servant" (57:9); and the Exalted says: قَامَ عَبْدُ اللَّهِ يَدْعُوهُ — "the servant of Allah stood up to supplicate Him" (72:19). Thus, it has become clear that the "taking" for servitude is the Guardianship.

His saying (peace be upon him): "And Allah took him as a prophet before He took him as a messenger." The difference between the Prophet (النبي) and the Messenger (الرسول), as appears from the narrations reported from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt, is that the Prophet is the one who sees in a dream what is revealed to him, while the Messenger is the one who witnesses the angel and the angel speaks to him. What appears from the stories of Ibrahim is this sequence. The Exalted says: وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتَابِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ ۚ إِنَّهُ كَانَ صِدِّيقًا نَّبِيًّا إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ يَا أَبَتِ لِمَ تَعْبُدُ مَا لَا يَسْمَعُ وَلَا يُبْصِرُ وَلَا يُغْنِي عَنكَ شَيْئًا — "And mention in the Book Abraham. Indeed he was a truthful one, a prophet, when he said to his father, ‘O father! Why do you worship what neither hears nor sees, and is of no avail to you in any way?’" (19:41-42). The outward meaning of the verse is that he (peace be upon him) was a truthful prophet when he addressed his father with that. This would be a confirmation of what Ibrahim (peace be upon him) informed when he first came to his people: إِنَّنِي بَرَاءٌ مِّمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا الَّذِي فَطَرَنِي فَإِنَّهُ سَيَهْدِينِ — "‘I am indeed quit of what you worship, except Him who created me, for He will surely guide me.’" (43:26-27). And the Exalted says: وَلَقَدْ جَاءَتْ رُسُلُنَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ بِالْبُشْرَىٰ قَالُوا سَلَامًا ۖ قَالَ سَلَامٌ — "Certainly Our messengers came to Abraham with the good news. They said, ‘Peace!’ ‘Peace!’ he said." (11:69). This story — which involves witnessing the angels and their speaking to him — occurred during Ibrahim's (peace be upon him) old age, after he had left his father and his people.

His saying (peace be upon him): "Indeed, Allah took him as a messenger before He took him as a friend." This is inferred from the saying of the Exalted: وَاتَّبَعَ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ حَنِيفًا ۗ وَاتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ إِبْرَاهِيمَ خَلِيلًا — "and followed the creed of Abraham, a Hanif? And Allah took Abraham for a dedicated friend." (4:125). Its outward meaning is that He only took him as a friend because of this primordial creed (الملة الحنيفية) which he legislated by the command of his Lord, as the context is one of explaining the nobility of the creed of Ibrahim the Hanif, through which Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was honored with friendship (الخلة). The "friend" (الخليل) is more specific than the "truthful friend" (الصديق), for one of two who love each other is called a siddiq if he is truthful in his interaction and companionship, then he becomes a khalil when he restricts his needs to his friend. Al-Khallah (الخلة) means poverty and need.

His saying (peace be upon him): "And Allah took him as a friend before He took him as an Imam," etc. Its meaning becomes clear from the preceding exposition.

His saying: "The foolish one cannot be the Imam of the God-conscious," is an allusion to the saying of the Exalted: وَمَن يَرْغَبُ عَن مِّلَّةِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ إِلَّا مَن سَفِهَ نَفْسَهُ ۚ وَلَقَدِ اصْطَفَيْنَاهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا ۖ وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ إِذْ قَالَ لَهُ رَبُّهُ أَسْلِمْ ۖ قَالَ أَسْلَمْتُ لِرَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ — "And who is it that turns away from the creed of Abraham except one who befools himself? We certainly chose him in the world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the righteous. When his Lord said to him, ‘Submit,’ he said, ‘I submit to the Lord of all the worlds.’" (2:130-131). Allah, the Glorified, named the turning away from the creed of Ibrahim — which is wrongdoing (الظلم) — as "foolishness" (سفهاً), and contrasted it with "choosing" (الاصطفاء). He explained "choosing" as "submission" (الإسلام), as appears by reflecting on His saying: "When his Lord said to him, 'Submit.'" Then He made submission and God-consciousness (التقوى) one and the same, or following the same course, in His saying: اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ حَقَّ تُقَاتِهِ وَلَا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ — "be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him, and do not die except as Muslims." (3:102). Understand this well.

From al-Mufid, from Durust and Hisham, from them (peace be upon them), who said: قد كان ابراهيم نبياً وليس بإمام ، حتى قال الله تبارك وتعالى : « إِنِّي جَاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِمَامًا قَالَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِي » فقال الله تبارك وتعالى : لا ينال عهدي الظالمين ، من عبد صنماً أو وثناً أو مثالاً ، لا يكون اماماً — "Ibrahim was a prophet and not an Imam until Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, said: 'I am making you the Imam of mankind.' He said: 'And from among my descendants?' Then Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, said: 'My pledge does not extend to the wrongdoers.' Whoever worshipped an idol, an image, or a statue cannot be an Imam."

I say: Its meaning has become clear from what has passed.

In the Amali of the Shaykh, with its chain, and from the Manaqib of Ibn al-Maghazili, in a marfu' narration from Ibn Mas'ud, from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) regarding the verse, concerning Allah's saying to Ibrahim: من سجد لصنم دوني لا أجعله اماماً . قال ( ع ) وانتهت الدعوة الى والى أخي عليّ ، لم يسجد أحدنا لصنم قط — "Whoever prostrated to an idol besides Me, I shall not make him an Imam." He (peace be upon him) said: "And the prayer reached me and my brother Ali; neither of us ever prostrated to an idol."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Waki' and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) from the Prophet regarding His saying: "My pledge does not extend to the wrongdoers." He said: لا طاعة الا في المعروف — "There is no obedience except in what is right."

Also in al-Durr al-Manthur: Abd ibn Humayd narrated from Imran ibn Husayn: I heard the Prophet saying: لا طاعة لمخلوق في معصية الله — "There is no obedience to a creature in disobedience to Allah."

I say: Their meanings are clear from what has passed.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, with chains from Safwan al-Jammal, who said: We were in Makkah and the conversation turned to the saying of Allah: "And when his Lord tested Ibrahim with certain words and he fulfilled them." He said: فأتمهن بمحمد وعلي والأئمة من ولد علي في قول الله : « ذُرِّيَّةً بَعْضُهَا مِن بَعْضٍ وَاللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ » — "He fulfilled them with Muhammad, Ali, and the Imams from the descendants of Ali, in the saying of Allah: 'descendants, some of them from others, and Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing.'"

I say: The narration is based on the meaning of "word" (الكلمة) being the Imamate, as it was interpreted in the saying of the Exalted: "for He will surely guide me. And He made it a lasting word among his posterity..." Thus, the meaning of the verse would be: And when his Lord tested Ibrahim with certain words — which are his Imamate and the Imamate of Ishaq and his descendants — and He fulfilled them with the Imamate of Muhammad and the Imams from his household from the descendants of Ismail. Then He clarified the matter with His saying: "He said: 'I am making you the Imam of mankind'" until the end of the verse.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ جَعَلْنَا ٱلْبَيْتَ مَثَابَةً لِّلنَّاسِ وَأَمْنًا وَٱتَّخِذُوا۟ مِن مَّقَامِ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ مُصَلًّى ۖ وَعَهِدْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ أَن طَهِّرَا بَيْتِىَ لِلطَّآئِفِينَ وَٱلْعَـٰكِفِينَ وَٱلرُّكَّعِ ٱلسُّجُودِ (١٢٥)
And [remember] when We made the House a place of reward for mankind and a sanctuary, [declaring], ‘Take the venue of prayer from Abraham’s Station.’ We charged Abraham and Ishmael [with its upkeep, saying], ‘Purify My House for those who go around it, [for] those who make it a retreat and [for] those who bow and prostrate.’ (125)
وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ رَبِّ ٱجْعَلْ هَـٰذَا بَلَدًا ءَامِنًا وَٱرْزُقْ أَهْلَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ مِنْهُم بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۖ قَالَ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَأُمَتِّعُهُۥ قَلِيلًا ثُمَّ أَضْطَرُّهُۥٓ إِلَىٰ عَذَابِ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٢٦)
And when Abraham said, ‘My Lord, make this a secure town, and provide its people with fruits—such of them as have faith in Allah and the Last Day,’ He said, ‘As for him who is faithless, I will provide for him [too] for a short time, then I will shove him toward the punishment of the Fire and it is an evil destination.’ (126)
وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ ٱلْقَوَاعِدَ مِنَ ٱلْبَيْتِ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلُ رَبَّنَا تَقَبَّلْ مِنَّآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (١٢٧)
As Abraham raised the foundations of the House with Ishmael, [they prayed]: ‘Our Lord, accept it from us! Indeed You are the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (127)
رَبَّنَا وَٱجْعَلْنَا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِنَآ أُمَّةً مُّسْلِمَةً لَّكَ وَأَرِنَا مَنَاسِكَنَا وَتُبْ عَلَيْنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٢٨)
‘Our Lord, make us submissive to You, and [raise] from our progeny a nation submissive to You, and show us our rites [of worship], and turn to us clemently. Indeed You are the All-clement, the All-merciful.’ (128)
رَبَّنَا وَٱبْعَثْ فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِكَ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١٢٩)
‘Our Lord, raise amongst them an apostle from among them, who will recite to them Your signs and teach them the Book and wisdom and purify them. Indeed You are the All-mighty, the All-wise.’ (129)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: وإذ جعلنا البيت مثابة للناس وأمناً (And when We made the House a place of return for the people and a sanctuary) is an allusion to the legislation of the Pilgrimage (الحج) and the sanctuary status (الأمن) of the House. The "place of return" (المثابة) is the resort or destination, derived from thāba yathūbu (ثاب يثوب) when one returns.

His saying, the Exalted: واتخذوا من مقام ابراهيم مصلى (Take the venue of Abraham’s station as a place of prayer) is as if it is conjoined to His saying: "We made the House a place of return," in terms of meaning. Since His saying: "We made the House a place of return" was an allusion to legislation, the meaning becomes: "And when We said to the people: Return to the House and perform pilgrimage to it, and take the station of Abraham as a place of prayer." It is also said that the speech assumes a suppressed command of "saying," the estimation being: "And We said: Take the station of Abraham as a place of prayer." The "place of prayer" (المصلى) is a noun of place derived from al-ṣalāh (الصلاة) in the sense of supplication (الدعاء); meaning: take from his station (عليه السلام) a place for supplication. It appears that His saying: "We made the House a place of return..." serves as a preamble (التوطئة) by which the basis for the legislation of prayer is indicated. For this reason, He did not say: "And pray (وصلوا) in the station of Abraham," but rather said: "Take the venue of Abraham’s station as a place of prayer." Thus, the command was not attached to the act of prayer in the station itself, but rather to the "taking" of a place of prayer from it.

His saying, the Exalted: وعهدنا إلى إبراهيم وإسمعيل أن طهرا (and We entrusted Abraham and Ishmael [with the task]: ‘Purify...) The "entrusting" (العهد) is the command (الأمر). "Purification" (التطهير) is either the clearing of the House for the worship of those who circumambulate (الطائفين), those who stay for devotion (العاكفين), and those who pray (المصلين), and for their rites; in which case it would be a metaphorical metonymy (الاستعارة بالكناية), the original meaning being: "Clear My House for the worship of the worshippers," and that is its purification. Or, it means cleaning it from filth and impurities arising from the indifference of people. "Those who bow and prostrate" (الركع السجود) are the plurals of "one who bows" (راكع) and "one who prostrates" (ساجد), and the intended meaning is those who pray.

His saying, the Exalted: وإذ قال ابراهيم رب اجعل (And when Abraham said, ‘My Lord, make...) This is a supplication (دعاء) offered by Abraham, asking for security for the people of Mecca and for sustenance. His supplication was answered; and far be it from Allah, the Glorified, to recount in His speech a supplication that He neither answers nor rejects in His truthful speech, for then His speech would contain vain satire (هجاء لغو) uttered by an ignorant trifler. Indeed, the Exalted has said: وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ — "and I speak the truth" (38:84), and the Exalted said: إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلٌ فَصْلٌ وَمَا هُوَ بِالْهَزْلِ — "It is indeed a decisive word, and it is not a jest" (86:13-14).
