The Great Quran has narrated many supplications from this noble Prophet that he offered and asked of his Lord, such as his supplication for himself at the beginning of his affair, his supplication upon his migration to Syria, his supplication and request for the preservation of a good mention (بقاء الذكر الخير), his supplication for himself, his progeny, his parents, and the believing men and women, his supplication for the people of Mecca after building the House, and his supplication and request for the sending of the Prophet from his progeny. These supplications and requests embody his hopes and characterize his struggles and efforts for the sake of Allah and the virtues of his sacred soul. In short, through these and the rest of his stories and the praise his Lord bestowed upon him, the account of his noble life can be derived. We shall address what is possible of that in Surah al-An'am.

His saying, the Exalted: من آمن منهم (such of them as have faith) When he (peace be upon him) asked for security for the city of Mecca, and then asked for its people to be provided with fruits, he sensed that among the people there would be believers and disbelievers. His supplication for the people's sustenance would encompass both the disbeliever and the believer, yet he had already disassociated himself from the disbelievers and what they worship. The Exalted said: فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ أَنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ لِّلَّهِ تَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُ — "but when it became clear to him that he was an enemy of Allah, he disowned him" (9:114). Thus, the Exalted bore witness to his disavowal (البراءة) and disassociation (التبري) from every enemy of Allah, even his father. Therefore, when he sensed the generality of his supplication, he restricted it by saying: "such of them as have faith"—even though he knew that their provision of fruits could not be completed without the participation of the disbelievers, according to the laws of social worldly life. However, he specified his request—and Allah knows best—to what He decrees for the rest of His servants and intends for them. Thus, he was answered (peace be upon him) with that which encompasses both the believer and the disbeliever. In this is an explanation that what is answered of his supplication is that which follows the course of habit and the law of nature without breaking the norm or nullifying the apparent law of nature. He did not say: "And provide such of its people as have faith with fruits," because the request was to grant dignity to the city for the sake of the dignity of the Sacred House, and no fruit is produced in a valley without cultivation (غير ذي زرع) where the House was placed. Were it not for that, the city would not have been inhabited, nor would there have been people to dwell in it.

His saying, the Exalted: ومن كفر فامتعه قليلاً (And whoever disbelieves, I will let him enjoy for a little) It is recited as fa-umatti'uhu (فامتعه) from the forms if'āl (إفعال) and taf'īl (تفعيل); imtā' (الامتاع) and tamtī' (التمتيع) carry the same meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: ثم اضطره الى عذاب النار (then I will compel him to the punishment of the Fire) etc. In this is an allusion to the increased honor of the House and a consolation for the soul of Abraham (peace be upon him). It is as if it were said: "What you asked regarding the honoring of the House by providing for the believers among the people of this city, I have answered and more. Let the disbeliever not be deceived by this into thinking he has dignity before Allah; rather, this is an honoring of this city and an answer to your supplication with more than what you asked. He shall then be compelled to the punishment of the Fire, and an evil destination it is."

His saying, the Exalted: واذ يرفع ابراهيم القواعد من البيت واسمعيل (As Abraham raised the foundations of the House with Ishmael) "Foundations" (القواعد) is the plural of "foundation" (قاعدة), which is what is settled of the building upon the earth and upon which the rest rests. "Raising the foundations" is a metaphor (مجاز) by considering what is placed upon them as part of them, and attributing the "raising" related to the whole to the foundations alone. In His saying, the Exalted: "of the House" (من البيت), there is a hint toward this metaphorical consideration.

His saying, the Exalted: ربنا تقبل منا انك أنت السميع العليم (Our Lord, accept it from us; indeed You are the All-hearing, the All-knowing) is a supplication by Abraham and Ishmael. It is not based on a suppressed "saying" or its like, meaning: "They say: Our Lord, accept from us..." rather, it is in reality a narration of the speech itself. For His saying: "Abraham raised the foundations of the House with Ishmael" is a narration of a past state (حكاية الحال الماضية); they are depicted in such a way as if they are being witnessed while engaged in the raising. The listener sees them in that state and then hears their supplication in their own words without the mediation of the speaker pointing to their position and action. This is frequent in the Quran and is among the most beautiful of Quranic styles—and all of them are beautiful. It contains a depiction of the story and a proximity to the senses that is not found, nor is there anything of its kind of brilliance, in accepting it through a suppressed "saying" and the like.

In the omission of the object of acceptance—which is the building of the House—there is humility in the station of servitude (العبودية) and a belittling of what they performed. The meaning is: "Our Lord, accept from us this small deed; indeed You are the All-hearing of our supplication, the All-knowing of what we intended in our hearts."

His saying, the Exalted: ربنا واجعلنا مسلمين لك ، ومن ذريتنا أمة مسلمة لك (Our Lord, make us submissive to You, and [raise] from our progeny a nation submissive to You) It is self-evident that "submission" (الاسلام), according to the term current among us and the meaning that first comes to our minds, is the first level of servitude. By it, the adherent is distinguished from others, and it is the adoption of the outward religious beliefs and acts, encompassing both faith (الإيمان) and hypocrisy (النفاق). Abraham (peace be upon him)—who is the Prophet, the Messenger, one of the five "Possessors of Resolve" (أولي العزم), and the founder of the primordial monotheistic creed (الملة الحنيفية)—is too sublime for it to be conceived that he had not attained it until this time. Likewise, his son Ishmael, the Messenger of Allah and his "sacrificed one" (ذبيحه). Nor is it that they had attained it but did not know it, or knew it and desired to remain upon it, while they were in such proximity and station before Allah. The context is the station of supplication during the building of the Sacred House, and they are most knowledgeable of Whom they are asking, who He is, and what His status is. Furthermore, this "submission" is among the volitional matters (الامور الاختيارية) to which command and prohibition relate, as the Exalted said: إِذْ قَالَ لَهُ رَبُّهُ أَسْلِمْ قَالَ أَسْلَمْتُ لِرَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ — "When his Lord said to him, ‘Submit,’ he said, ‘I submit to the Lord of all the worlds’" (2:131). There is no meaning in attributing what is such to Allah, the Glorified, or asking for what is a volitional act of man as such, without a specific consideration that would make such a request valid.

Therefore, this requested "submission" is other than what is current and comes to mind among us. For submission has levels (مراتب), and the proof that it possesses levels is His saying, the Exalted: "When his Lord said to him, ‘Submit,’ he said, ‘I submit...’" (the verse), where He commands Abraham with submission while he was already a Muslim. Thus, the intended meaning of this requested submission is other than the existing submission he possessed. There are parallels to this in the Quran.

This submission is what we shall explain in its meaning: it is the perfection of servitude (تمام العبودية) and the servant's surrender of all he possesses to his Lord. Although it is a volitional meaning for man through its preliminaries, when attributed to the ordinary man and his conventional heart-state, it is non-volitional in the sense that it is impossible for him to attain—given his state—just like the other stations of guardianship (الولاية) and its high stages, and like the other ascents of perfection far removed from the state of the average, conventional man due to their arduous preliminaries. For this reason, it can be considered a divine matter outside the volition of man, and it is asked of Allah, the Glorified, to bestow it and to make man characterized by it.

Furthermore, there is a subtler view here: that which is attributed to man and considered volitional for him are the acts (الأفعال). As for the attributes (الصفات) and ingrained dispositions (الملكات) resulting from their repeated performance, they are not volitional in reality. Thus, it is permissible or even necessary to attribute them to Him, the Exalted, especially if they are among the good deeds and virtues whose attribution to Him is more appropriate than their attribution to man. Upon this the custom of the Quran has proceeded, as in His saying, the Exalted: رَبِّ اجْعَلْنِي مُقِيمَ الصَّلَاةِ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِي — "My Lord! Make me a maintainer of the prayer, and [also] of my descendants" (14:40), and His saying: وَأَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ — "and join me with the righteous" (26:83), and His saying: رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِي أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ الَّتِي أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ ، وَعَلَىٰ وَالِدَيَّ وَأَنْ أَعْمَلَ صَالِحًا تَرْضَاهُ — "My Lord! Inspire me to give thanks for Your blessing with which You have blessed me and my parents, and that I may do righteous deeds that please You" (27:19), and His saying: "Our Lord, make us submissive to You" (the verse). Thus, it has become clear that the intended meaning of submission is other than the meaning alluded to in His saying, the Exalted: قَالَتِ الْأَعْرَابُ آمَنَّا قُل لَّمْ تُؤْمِنُوا وَلَٰكِن قُولُوا أَسْلَمْنَا وَلَمَّا يَدْخُلِ الْإِيمَانُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ — "The Bedouins say, ‘We have faith.’ Say, ‘You do not have faith yet; rather say, "We have embraced Islam," for faith has not yet entered into your hearts’" (49:14). Rather, it is a more sublime and higher meaning, the explanation of which will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: وأرنا مناسكنا وتب علينا . انك أنت التواب الرحيم (and show us our holy rites, and turn to us clemently. Indeed You are the All-clement, the All-merciful) also points to the aforementioned meaning of submission. "Rites" (المناسك) is the plural of mansak (منسك) in the sense of worship (العبادة), as in His saying: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ جَعَلْنَا مَنسَكًا — "For every nation We have appointed a rite" (22:34), or in the sense of the act of worship (المتعبد), meaning the act performed as worship. The annexation of the verbal noun (إضافة المصدر) implies realization (التحقق). Thus, the intended meaning of "our rites" is the acts of worship proceeding from them and the deeds they perform, rather than the acts and deeds intended to proceed from them. Therefore, His saying: "show us" (أرنا) does not mean "teach us" or "grant us success," but rather the guidance by showing the reality of the act proceeding from them, as we alluded to in His saying, the Exalted: وَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْرَاتِ ، وَإِقَامَ الصَّلَاةِ وَإِيتَاءَ الزَّكَاةِ — "and We revealed to them the performance of good deeds, the maintenance of prayers, and the giving of zakat" (21:73). We shall explain in its place that this revelation (الوحي) is a guidance in the act itself, not a teaching of the required obligation. It is as if this is alluded to in His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرْ عِبَادَنَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ ، وَإِسْحَاقَ ، وَيَعْقُوبَ ، أُولِي الْأَيْدِي وَالْأَبْصَارِ . إِنَّا أَخْلَصْنَاهُم بِخَالِصَةٍ ذِكْرَى الدَّارِ — "And remember Our servants, Abraham, Isaac and Jacob, men of strength and insight. Indeed We purified them with an exclusive quality: the remembrance of the [ultimate] abode" (38:45-46).

It has thus become clear that the intended meaning of submission and insight in worship is other than the common, conventional meaning. Likewise, the intended meaning of His saying: "and turn to us clemently" (تب علينا), because Abraham and Ishmael were two Prophets protected by the protection (عصمة) of Allah, the Exalted; no sin proceeds from them such that their repentance (توبة) from it would be valid, like our repentance from the sins proceeding from us.

If you say: All that has been mentioned regarding the meaning of submission, the showing of rites, and repentance is what befits the status of Abraham and Ishmael (peace be upon them); it does not necessarily follow that this was his intention regarding his progeny. For he did not include his progeny with himself and his son Ishmael except in the call to submission, and he asked for submission for them with a different phrasing in another sentence, saying: "and [raise] from our progeny a nation submissive to You," and he did not say: "and make us and our progeny submissive," or what would lead to that meaning. So what prevents his intention regarding submission from being that which encompasses all its levels, even the outward submission? For outward submission also has beautiful effects and precious goals in human society, which it is valid for Abraham (peace be upon him) to desire and ask of his Lord, just as it was so for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who was satisfied with the outward profession of the two testimonies (الشهادتين) as submission, by which blood is spared, marriage is permitted, and inheritance is owned. Accordingly, the intended meaning of submission in His saying: "Our Lord, make us submissive to You" is what befits the status of Abraham and Ishmael, and in His saying: "and from our progeny a nation submissive to You" is what is appropriate for the status of the nation in which there is the hypocrite, the weak in faith, and the strong in faith, and all are Muslims.

I say: The station of legislation (التشريع) and the station of asking from Allah are two different stations; they have two distinct rulings, and one should not be measured against the other. That which the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was satisfied with from his nation—the outward profession of the two testimonies as submission—was only for the wisdom of expanding power and preserving the outward righteous order, so that it might be like the shell protecting the kernel, which is the reality of Islam, and safeguarding it from the collision of incidental afflictions.

As for the station of supplication and asking from Allah, the authority therein belongs to the realities (الحقائق), and the purpose there is related to the truth of the matter and pure proximity. The Prophets have no desire for the outward for the sake of its being outward, nor did Abraham (peace be upon him) have such a desire for his progeny. Had he such a desire, he would have begun with it for his father before his progeny, yet he disassociated from him when it became clear that he was an enemy of Allah. He did not say in what Allah narrated of his supplication: وَلَا تُخْزِنِي يَوْمَ يُبْعَثُونَ ، يَوْمَ لَا يَنفَعُ مَالٌ وَلَا بَنُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ — "And do not disgrace me on the day they will be resurrected, the day when neither wealth nor children will avail, except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart" (26:87-89). Nor did he say: وَاجْعَل لِّي لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ فِي الْآخِرِينَ — "and appoint for me a truthful mention among the posterity" (26:84), but rather he was satisfied with a "mention" among the posterity, and so on.

Thus, the submission he asked for his progeny is nothing but the reality of Islam, and in His saying: "a nation submissive to You" (امة مسلمة لك), there is an allusion to that. If the intended meaning were merely the applicability of the name of Islam to the progeny, it would have been said: "a submissive nation" (امة مسلمة), and the phrase "to You" (لك) would have been omitted.

His saying, the Exalted: ربنا وابعث فيهم رسولاً منهم (Our Lord, raise amongst them a messenger from among them) etc. is a supplication for the Prophet (peace be upon him), and he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to say: "I am the supplication of Abraham."

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, narrated from al-Kattani: He said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about a man who forgot to pray the two cycles (ركعتين) at the station of Abraham during the circumambulation (طواف) of the Pilgrimage or the Visitation (عمرة). He (peace be upon him) said: "If he is still in the city, he should pray the two cycles at the station of Abraham, for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: 'Take the venue of Abraham’s station as a place of prayer.' But if he has already departed, I do not command him to return."

I say: A similar narration was reported by al-Shaykh in al-Tahdhib and al-Ayyashi in his Tafsir through several chains. The specifics of the ruling—which is prayer at the station or behind it, as in some narrations: "No one is to pray the two cycles of circumambulation except behind the station," the tradition—are derived from the word "from" (من) and "place of prayer" (مصلى) in His saying: "Take the venue of Abraham’s station as a place of prayer."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: "Purify My House for those who circumambulate..." (the verse), he said: it means "Keep the polytheists away from it."

In al-Kafi, narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says in His Book: 'Purify My House for those who circumambulate, those who stay for devotion, and those who bow and prostrate.' Thus, it is appropriate for the servant not to enter Mecca except that he is pure, having washed away his sweat and grime, and purified himself."

I say: This meaning is narrated in other traditions. The derivation of the purity of the one entering from the purity of the place entered might be completed through other verses, such as His saying: الطَّيِّبَاتُ لِلطَّيِّبِينَ ، وَالطَّيِّبُونَ لِلطَّيِّبَاتِ — "and good women are for good men, and good men are for good women" (24:26) and its like.

In al-Majma', narrated from Ibn Abbas, he said: When Abraham brought Ishmael and Hagar and placed them in Mecca, a period of time passed. The Jurhumites settled there, and Ishmael married a woman from among them. Hagar died. Abraham sought permission from Sarah [to visit], and she permitted him on the condition that he would not dismount. Abraham arrived after Hagar had died, so he went to Ishmael's house and said to his wife: "Where is your companion?" She said to him: "He is not here; he went hunting." Ishmael used to go out of the Sanctuary (الحرم) to hunt and return. Abraham said to her: "Do you have any hospitality?" She said: "I have nothing, and I have no one." Abraham said to her: "When your husband comes, convey my greetings to him and tell him: He should change the threshold of his door." Abraham left. Ishmael came and found the scent of his father. He said to his wife: "Did anyone come to you?" She said: "An old man of such and such description came to me," as if belittling his status. He said: "What did he say to you?" She said: "He said to me: Convey greetings to your husband and tell him: He should change the threshold of his door." So he divorced her and married another. Abraham remained as long as Allah willed him to remain, then he sought permission from Sarah to visit Ishmael. She permitted him and conditioned that he would not dismount. Abraham came until he reached Ishmael's door and said to his wife: "Where is your companion?" She said: "He went hunting and will come now, if Allah wills. Dismount, may Allah have mercy on you." He said to her: "Do you have any hospitality?" She said: "Yes." She brought milk and meat, and he prayed for blessing for her. Had she brought bread, wheat, barley, or dates that day, it would have become the land of Allah most abundant in wheat, barley, and dates. She said to him: "Dismount so I may wash your head." He did not dismount, so she brought the Station (المقام) and placed it at his side. He placed his foot upon it, and the mark of his foot remained on it. She washed the right side of his head, then moved the Station to his left side and washed the left side of his head, and the mark of his foot remained on it. He said to her: "When your husband comes, convey my greetings to him and tell him: The threshold of your door has become upright." When Ishmael (peace be upon him) came, he found the scent of his father and said to his wife: "Did anyone come to you?" She said: "Yes, an old man with the most beautiful face and the most pleasant scent. He said to me such and such, and I said to him such and such, and I washed his head, and this is the place of his feet on the Station." Ishmael said to her: "That was Abraham."

I say: Al-Qummi narrated in his Tafsir something close to this.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: Abraham was residing in the desert of Syria. When Ishmael was born to him from Hagar, Sarah was deeply grieved by that because she had no child. She used to harm Abraham regarding Hagar and grieve him. Abraham complained of that to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and Allah revealed to him: "The likeness of a woman is like a crooked rib; if you leave it, you enjoy it, and if you straighten it, you break it." Then He commanded him to take Ishmael and his mother out. He said: "O Lord, to which place?" He said: "To My Sanctuary and My security, and the first spot I created of the earth, which is Mecca." So Allah sent Gabriel to him with the Buraq, and he carried Hagar, Ishmael, and Abraham. Abraham would not pass by a beautiful place with trees, crops, and palms except that he would say: "O Gabriel, to here, to here!" and Gabriel would say: "No, proceed, proceed," until he reached Mecca and placed him at the site of the House. Abraham had covenanted with Sarah not to dismount until he returned to her. When they descended in that place, there were trees, so Hagar threw a cloak she had over those trees, and they shaded themselves under it. When Abraham released them and placed them, he intended to return from them to Sarah. Hagar said to him: "O Abraham, do you leave us in a place where there is no companion, no water, and no crops?" Abraham said: "Allah, Who commanded me to place you in this spot, is sufficient for you." Then he departed from them. When he reached Kada' (a mountain at Dhu Tuwa), Abraham turned and said: "O Lord, I have settled some of my progeny in a valley without cultivation, near Your Sacred House, our Lord, that they may maintain the prayer. So make the hearts of people incline toward them, and provide them with fruits, that they may be grateful." Then he went on, and Hagar remained. When the day rose, Ishmael became thirsty. Hagar stood at the place of the "striving" (السعي) and ascended Safa, and the mirage shimmered for her in the valley, so she thought it was water. She descended into the belly of the valley and strove. When she reached Marwah, Ishmael was hidden from her. She returned until she reached Safa. She looked until she had done that seven times. On the seventh turn, while she was on Marwah, she looked at Ishmael and water had appeared from beneath his feet. She returned until she gathered sand around it, for it was flowing, and she "contained" it (زمَّته) with what she placed around it; for that reason, it was named Zamzam. Jurhum was settled at Dhu al-Majaz and Arafat. When the water appeared in Mecca, the birds and wild animals flocked to the water. Jurhum saw the flocking of birds and animals to that place and followed them until they saw a woman and a boy settled in that spot, having shaded themselves with a tree, and water had appeared for them. They said to Hagar: "Who are you, and what is your story and the story of this boy?" She said: "I am the mother of the son of Abraham, the Friend of the All-Beneficent, and this is his son. Allah commanded him to settle us here." They said to her: "Will you permit us to be near you?" She said to them: "Until Abraham comes." When Abraham visited them on the third day, Hagar said: "O Friend of Allah, here are people from Jurhum asking you to permit them to be near us. Will you permit them in that?" Abraham said: "Yes." So Hagar permitted them, and they settled near them and pitched their tents. Hagar and Ishmael found company in them. When Abraham visited them a second time, he saw the multitude of people around them and was greatly delighted by that. When Ishmael grew up—and each of the Jurhumites had gifted Ishmael a sheep or two, so Hagar and Ishmael lived by them—and when Ishmael reached manhood, Allah commanded Abraham to build the House... until he said: When Allah commanded Abraham to build the House, he did not know in which place to build it. So Allah sent Gabriel, and he marked for him the site of the House... until he said: So Abraham built the House, and Ishmael moved [stones] from Dhu Tuwa, and he raised it into the sky nine cubits. Then he guided him to the location of the Stone (الحجر), so Abraham extracted it and placed it in its position where it is now. When he built it, he made for it two doors: a door to the east and a door to the west. The door to the west is called al-Mustajar. Then he threw over it trees and lemon grass (al-idhkhir), and Hagar threw over her door a cloak she had, and they used to be under it. When he built and finished it, Abraham and Ishmael performed the Pilgrimage. Gabriel descended upon them on the Day of Tarwiyah, the eighth of Dhu al-Hijjah, and said: "O Abraham, stand and quench yourself with water (ارتو من الماء)," because there was no water at Mina and Arafat; thus it was named Tarwiyah for that reason. Then he took him out to Mina and he spent the night there, and he did with him what he had done with Adam. When Abraham finished building the House, he said: رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هَـٰذَا بَلَدًا آمِنًا ، وَارْزُقْ أَهْلَهُ مِنَ الثَّمَرَاتِ ، مَنْ آمَنَ مِنْهُم (the verse). He (peace be upon him) said: "From the fruits of the hearts," meaning: make them beloved to people so they may find company in them and return to them.

I say: This which we have summarized from the reports of the story is what is contained in the narrations regarding the summary of the story, and several of them have included it. It has been mentioned in other reports that the history of building the House includes matters that break the norm (خارقة للعادة). In some reports, the House, when first placed, was a dome of light (قبة من نور) that descended upon Adam and settled in the spot where Abraham built the House. It remained until the Flood of Noah occurred; when the world was submerged, Allah, the Exalted, raised it, and the spot was not submerged; for that reason, the House was named al-Atiq (the Ancient).

In some reports: Allah sent down the foundations of the House from Paradise.

In some of them: The Black Stone (الحجر الأسود) descended from Paradise—and it was whiter than snow—but it turned black when the hands of the disbelievers touched it.

In al-Kafi also, from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them), he said: "Allah commanded Abraham to build the Kaaba, and to raise its foundations, and to show the people their rites. So Abraham and Ishmael built the House, one course every day, until they reached the position of the Black Stone." Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: "Then [the mountain of] Abu Qubays called out: 'I have a trust for you,' and it gave him the Stone, and he placed it in its position."

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from al-Thawri from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), he said: I asked him about the Stone. He said: "Three stones descended from Paradise: the Black Stone which Abraham was entrusted with, the Station of Abraham, and the Stone of the Children of Israel."

In some reports: The Black Stone was an angel from among the angels.

I say: Parallels to these meanings are many, narrated in the reports of both the general and the specific [schools]. Although they are solitary reports (آحاد) not reaching the level of mass-transmission (تواتر) in wording or meaning, they are not without parallel in the chapters of religious knowledge, and there is no reason to discard them entirely.

As for what has been narrated regarding the descent of the dome upon Adam, and likewise Abraham's journey to Mecca on the Buraq, and the like, which are miracles breaking the natural norm (كرامة خارقة لعادة الطبيعة), these are matters for which there is no proof of impossibility. Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, has distinguished His Prophets with many of these miraculous signs (الآيات المعجزة) and extraordinary honors, and the Quran affirms many instances of them.

As for what has been narrated regarding the descent of the foundations of the House from Paradise, the descent of the Black Stone from Paradise, the descent of the stone of the Station—and it is said that it is buried under the structure known today as the Station of Abraham—from Paradise, and what resembles that, this is as we mentioned: it has many parallels. It has been narrated regarding several plants, fruits, and other things that they are from Paradise. Likewise, what has been narrated that they are from Hell (جهنم), and from the eruption of the Blaze (فورة الجحيم). From this category are the reports of the "clay" (الطينة) stating that the clay of the felicitous is from Paradise and the clay of the wretched is from the Fire, or that they are from Illiyyin and Sijjin. From this category also is what has been narrated that the Paradise of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) is in some earthly places and the Fire of the intermediate realm is in others, and that the grave is either a garden from the gardens of Paradise or a pit from the pits of the Fire, among other things that the insightful researcher encounters in the folds of the reports. These, as we mentioned, reach such a level of frequency that their totality, as a whole, is not something to be discarded or disputed in its issuance or the correctness of its attribution. Rather, it is from the divine sciences (الهيات المعارف) that the Noble Quran has allowed, and toward whose path the reports have inclined. This is necessitated by the speech of the Exalted: that the things which are in this witnessed natural plane (النشأه الطبيعية) have all descended to it from Allah, the Glorified. Whatever of them is a beautiful good, or a means to good, or a vessel for good, is from Paradise and to it it returns. Whatever of them is evil, or a means to evil, or a vessel for evil, is from the Fire and to it it returns. The Exalted said: وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ ، وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ — "There is not a thing but that its sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (15:21). This informs that everything exists with Him, the Exalted, in an existence not limited by a limit nor measured by a measure, and upon "sending down" (التنزيل)—which is the gradual descent—it becomes measured by its measure and limited by its limit. This is on a general level. It has also been mentioned specifically, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنزَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ الْأَنْعَامِ ثَمَانِيَةَ أَزْوَاجٍ — "and He sent down for you eight mates of the livestock" (39:6), and His saying: وَأَنزَلْنَا الْحَدِيدَ — "and We sent down iron" (57:25), and His saying: وَفِي السَّمَاءِ رِزْقُكُمْ وَمَا تُوعَدُونَ — "And in the sky is your provision and what you are promised" (51:22), according to what will follow in the clarification of their meaning, if Allah the Mighty wills. Thus, everything is descending to the world from Allah, the Glorified, and He has informed in His speech that everything returns to Him, the Glorified, saying: وَأَنَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ الْمُنتَهَىٰ — "and that the terminus is toward your Lord" (53:42), and the Exalted said: إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ الرُّجْعَىٰ — "Indeed the return is to your Lord" (96:8), and He said: وَإِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ — "and toward Him is the destination" (64:3), and the Exalted said: أَلَا إِلَى اللَّهِ تَصِيرُ الْأُمُورُ — "Look! To Allah do all matters come" (42:53), among many other verses.

He also informed that things—while between their beginning and their return—proceed according to what their beginning requires and what their portion of felicity and wretchedness, and good and evil, decrees. The Exalted said: كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلَىٰ شَاكِلَتِهِ — "Everyone acts according to his character" (17:84), and He said: وَلِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيهَا — "Everyone has a direction to which he turns" (2:148). The clarification of their indication will follow. The purpose here is merely to point to what completes the discussion: that these reports narrating that these natural things are from Paradise or from the Fire, when they are inseparable from the aspect of felicity or wretchedness, are not devoid of an aspect of correctness due to their correspondence with established Quranic principles in general, even if that does not necessitate that every single one is correct and can be relied upon. So understand the intent.

A speaker might say: His saying, the Exalted: "As Abraham raised the foundations of the House with Ishmael..." (the verse) is apparent in that they were the ones who built this House for the worship of Allah in those pagan lands. However, the storytellers and the commentators who followed them have brought us something other than what Allah narrated to us. They have innovated in their narrations regarding the antiquity of the House, the pilgrimage of Adam, its rising to the sky at the time of the Flood, and the Black Stone being from the stones of Paradise. These storytellers wanted to adorn and embellish the religion with these narrations of theirs. These adornments with flowery speech, though they have their effect on the hearts of the common folk, the possessors of intellect and insight among the people of knowledge know that the spiritual honor (الشرف المعنوي) which Allah, the Glorified, bestowed by honoring some things over others—the honor of the House is only by its being a House of Allah, attributed to Him, and the honor of the Black Stone is by its being a place for "touching" (الاستلام) as if it were the hand of Allah, the Glorified. As for the stone being originally a ruby, a pearl, or anything else, it does not necessitate a merit in it or a real honor for it. What is the difference between a black stone and a white stone before Allah in the market of realities? The honor of this House is by Allah's naming it His House and making it a place for various types of His worship that are not in others—as mentioned before—not by its stones surpassing other stones, nor by its location surpassing other locations, nor by its being from the sky and the world of light. Likewise, the honor of the Prophets over other humans is not due to a merit in their bodies or their clothing, but rather due to Allah's choosing them and distinguishing them with Prophethood, which is a spiritual matter, while the people of the world were better in adornment and more abundant in blessing than they.

He said: These narrations are corrupt in their contradiction and conflict within themselves, corrupt in the lack of authenticity of their chains, and corrupt in their opposition to the outward meaning of the Book.

He said: These narrations are Israelite myths (خرافات إسرائيلية) spread by Jewish heretics among the Muslims to distort their religion for them and to repel the People of the Book from it.

I say: What he mentioned is not devoid of an aspect [of truth] in general, but he has exceeded in his critique, and his speech has been afflicted by a confusion that is worse and more hideous.

As for his saying that these narrations are corrupt, firstly due to contradiction and conflict, and secondly due to opposition to the Book: the contradiction or conflict only harms if every single one of them is taken individually. As for taking them as a whole (in the sense of not discarding all of them for not containing what is rationally impossible or narratively prohibited), the existing conflict within them does not harm it. We mean by this the narrations linked to the sources of protection (العصمة), such as the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the pure ones of his household. As for others among the commentators of the Companions and the Successors, their status is like that of other people; the status of what is narrated of their speech that is free from contradiction is like their speech that contains contradiction. In short, there is no reason to discard a narration or narrations unless they oppose the Book or the definitive Sunnah (السنّة القطعية), or if signs of lying and fabrication appear from them. Just as there is no authority (حجية) except for the Book and the definitive Sunnah in the principles of divine religious knowledge.

Thus, there is that which must be accepted, which is the Book and the definitive Sunnah; and there is that which must be discarded, which is what opposes them of the reports; and there is that for which there is no proof for its rejection nor for its acceptance, which is what has no proof from the side of reason for its impossibility, nor from the side of narration—meaning the Book and the definitive Sunnah—for its prohibition.

By this, the corruption of his objection regarding the lack of authenticity of their chains becomes clear, for that does not necessitate discarding as long as it does not oppose reason or sound narration.

As for their opposition to the outward meaning of His saying: "As Abraham raised the foundations..." (the verse), I wonder: how does the noble verse indicate the negation of the Black Stone being from Paradise? Or how does it indicate the negation of the descent of a dome upon the spot in the time of Adam, then its rising in the time of Noah? Does the verse indicate more than that this House built of stone and clay is the building of Abraham? What connection does it have, affirmatively or negatively, with what is contained in the narrations we alluded to? Indeed, the temperament of this speaker does not find it pleasant, nor does his opinion approve of it due to a sectarian bias (عصبية مذهبية) that necessitates the negation of the spiritual aspects of realities from the Prophets, and the reliance of religious outward forms upon spiritual principles and roots; or due to an involuntary following of the natural sciences advanced today, which decree that every event among natural events, or what is connected to them in any way of spiritual matters, must be explained by a material explanation or what ends in matter, which governs all affairs of events like social instructions.

It was necessary to reflect that the natural sciences' function is to research the properties of matter, its compositions, and the connection of natural effects to their subjects—that natural connection. Likewise, the social sciences only research the social links between social events only.

As for the realities that lie outside the domain of matter and its field of operation—those which encompass nature and its properties, and maintain non-material, spiritual connections (ارتباطاتها المعنوية) with cosmic events and all that our sensory world contains—these are matters beyond the scope of the natural and social sciences. These sciences are in no position to speak of them, nor to attempt to prove them, nor to pass judgment by denying them. Natural sciences can only judge that a house, in the natural world, requires components of clay and stone, and a builder who constructs it and gives it the form of a house through his movements and actions; or how a room is formed from black stones. Similarly, social research identifies the social events that led to Ibrahim’s building of the House, which are chapters from the history of his life, the lives of Hajar and Isma’il, the history of Tihamah, the arrival of the tribe of Jurhum, and so forth.

However, as for the relationship of this stone, for example, to the promised Paradise or Fire, it is not the function of these sciences to investigate it, nor to deny what has been said or may be said regarding it. You have already learned that the Noble Quran is the speaker (الناطق) declaring that these natural, material existences have descended to their place of settlement and stability from Allah, the Glorified, and are subsequently returning to Him and moving toward Him—"either to Paradise or to the Fire." It is also the speaker declaring that deeds ascend to Allah, are raised toward Him, and reach Him, despite being natural movements and positions that have been composed through a social, conventional composition (تألفاً اعتبارياً اجتماعياً) without a creative reality (حقيقة تكوينية). The Exalted says: لَن يَنَالَ اللَّهَ لُحُومُهَا وَلَا دِمَاؤُهَا وَلَٰكِن يَنَالُهُ التَّقْوَىٰ مِنكُمْ "It is not their flesh or their blood that reaches Allah. Rather, it is your God-consciousness (التقوى) — the innate disposition of godwariness and piety — that reaches Him" (Al-Hajj: 37). God-consciousness is an act, or a quality resulting from an act. The Exalted also says: إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ "To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) — actions performed with sincerity and in accordance with divine command" (Fatir: 10). Thus, it is incumbent upon the religious researcher to contemplate these verses and realize that religious knowledge (المعارف الدينية) has no direct conflict with the natural or social sciences from a purely natural or social perspective; rather, its reliance and foundation rest upon realities and meanings beyond that.

As for his claim that the nobility of the Prophets, the sanctuaries, and the matters attributed to them—such as the House and the Black Stone—is not an outward nobility but a spiritual nobility (شرف معنوي) arising from divine preference, this is a true statement. However, it must be understood according to the true meaning it contains. What is this spiritual matter that entails nobility? If it is among the meanings that social needs bestow upon their subjects and materials—similar to the ranks and positions circulated by states and nations, such as leadership and command among humans, the high value of gold and silver, the honor of parents, and the sanctity of laws and statutes—then these are merely meanings that societies conventionally posit (يعتبرها) due to the necessity of worldly needs. They have no effect outside of the imagination and social convention (الاعتبار الاجتماعي). It is well known that such a society does not transcend the social world created by vital necessity, and Allah, Exalted is His Authority, is far too holy for such needs—which strike at human life—to reach His presence.

Furthermore, if it were permissible for a Prophet to be honored with this non-real nobility, then it should be permissible for a house or a stone to be honored with the like. However, if this nobility is real and objective (حقيقياً واقعياً)—akin to the relationship between light and darkness, knowledge and ignorance, or intellect and folly—such that the reality of a Prophet’s existence is different from the reality of others' existence (even if the outward senses do not perceive it), then this is what befits His holy presence in terms of action and judgment. As Allah the Exalted says: وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَاعِبِينَ مَا خَلَقْنَاهُمَا إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. We did not create them except with the Truth (الحق) — the purposeful and wise reality underlying all creation — but most of them do not know" (Ad-Dukhan: 38-39). The explanation of this will follow. This nobility would then return to a real, spiritual, non-material relationship with the metaphysical realm (ما وراء الطبيعة). If it is permissible for this to be realized in the Prophets in a certain way, then it should be permissible for it to be realized in things other than the Prophets, such as the House and the Stone, even if these real spiritual relationships are expressed in terms whose outward appearance suggests the common meanings known to the public and conventionalized by social groups.

I wonder: what will these people do with the verses that speak of the adornment of Paradise and the honoring of its inhabitants with gold and silver? These are two metals that possess no nobility except for their high price based on their scarcity of existence. What is intended by honoring the people of Paradise with them? What effect does the meaning of wealth have in Paradise, when there is no meaning for financial convention (الاعتبار المالي) outside the vessel of social life? Do these divine statements and religious appearances have any explanation other than being veils of speech and curtains behind which lie secrets? If such statements are permissible regarding the matters of the next life (نشأة الآخرة), then their counterparts should be permissible in certain matters of this worldly life (نشأة الدنيا).

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, narrated from al-Zubayri from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: I said to him: "Inform me about the Ummah (أمة) — the community or nation — of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who are they?" He said: "The Ummah of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) are the Banu Hashim specifically." I said: "What is the proof that the Ummah of Muhammad are his Household (أهل بيته) whom you mentioned, to the exclusion of others?" He said: "The saying of Allah: وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْرَاهِيمُ الْقَوَاعِدَ مِنَ الْبَيْتِ وَإِسْمَاعِيلُ رَبَّنَا تَقَبَّلْ مِنَّا إِنَّكَ أَنتَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ رَبَّنَا وَاجْعَلْنَا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِنَا أُمَّةً مُّسْلِمَةً لَّكَ وَأَرِنَا مَنَاسِكَنَا وَتُبْ عَلَيْنَا إِنَّكَ أَنتَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ 'As Ibrahim stayed the foundations of the House, with Isma‘il, [they prayed]: ‘Our Lord, accept it from us! Indeed You are the All-hearing, the All-knowing. Our Lord, make us submissive to You, and [raise] from our progeny a nation submissive to You, and show us our holy rites of Hajj (المناسك) — the prescribed acts of worship and pilgrimage — and turn to us clemently. Indeed You are the All-clement, the All-merciful' (Al-Baqarah: 127-128). When Allah answered Ibrahim and Isma’il and placed from their progeny a submissive nation (أمة مسلمة) and sent among them a Messenger from them—meaning from that nation—who recites His signs to them, purifies them, and teaches them the Book and Wisdom, he followed his first supplication with another. He asked for them purification from polytheism (الشرك) and from the worship of idols, so that his matter regarding them would be sound and they would not follow others. He said: وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَن نَّعْبُدَ الْأَصْنَامَ رَبِّ إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ النَّاسِ فَمَن تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي وَمَنْ عَصَانِي فَإِنَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ 'and keep me and my children away from worshipping idols. My Lord! They have indeed misled many of the people. So whoever follows me indeed belongs to me, and as for him who disobeys me, well, You are indeed All-forgiving, All-merciful' (Ibrahim: 35-36). In this is a proof that the Imams and the submissive nation (الأمة المسلمة) among whom Muhammad was sent can only be from the progeny of Ibrahim, because of his saying: 'and keep me and my children away from worshipping idols.'"

I say: His (peace be upon him) deduction is extremely clear. Ibrahim (peace be upon him) only asked for a submissive nation from his progeny specifically. It is known from the conclusion of his supplication—"Our Lord, and raise up among them a messenger from them..."—that this submissive nation is the Ummah of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). However, it is not the Ummah of Muhammad in the sense of those to whom he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was sent, nor the Ummah of Muhammad in the sense of those who believed in his prophethood, for that Ummah is more general than the progeny of Ibrahim and Isma’il. Rather, it is a submissive nation from the progeny of Ibrahim (peace be upon him). Then he asked his Lord to keep his progeny and children away from polytheism and misguidance, which is inerrancy (العصمة) — the divine protection from sin and error. It is well known that among the progeny of Ibrahim and Isma’il—who are the Arabs of Mudar or Quraysh specifically—there are those who are misguided and polytheists. Thus, his intent by "my children" (بني) in his saying: "and my children," refers to the people of inerrancy (أهل العصمة) from his progeny specifically, who are the Prophet and his pure progeny (عترته الطاهرة). These are the Ummah of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the supplication of Ibrahim (peace be upon him). Perhaps this subtle point is what necessitated the shift from the word "progeny" (الذرية) to the word "children" (البنين). This is supported by his (peace be upon him) saying: "So whoever follows me indeed belongs to me, and as for him who disobeys me, well, You are indeed All-forgiving, All-merciful," where he used the "fa" of derivation (فاء التفريع) and established that whoever follows him is a part of himself, while remaining silent about others as if he disowns or does not recognize them.

As for his (peace be upon him) saying: "He asked for them purification from polytheism and from the worship of idols," Ibrahim (peace be upon him) only asked for purification from idol worship, but he (peace be upon him) justified it by "misguidance" (الضلال). This resulted in the request for purification from all misguidance—from idol worship and from any polytheism, even sins. For every sin is a form of polytheism, as previously explained regarding the saying of the Exalted: صِرَاطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ "The path of those whom You have blessed" (Al-Fatihah: 7).

And his (peace be upon him) saying: "In this is a proof that the Imams and the submissive nation... etc.," means that they are one and the same, and they are from the progeny of Ibrahim, as previously explained.

If you say: "If the word Ummah in these verses and their likes—such as the saying of the Exalted: كُنتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ 'You are the best nation [ever] brought forth for mankind' (Al-Imran: 110)—referred only to a specific number from the Ummah to the exclusion of the rest, it would necessitate a metaphorical use (المجاز) in speech without a justifying cause. There would be no justification for attributing this to the speech of the Exalted. Furthermore, the fact that the addresses of the Quran are directed to the entire Ummah of those who believed in the Prophet is a self-evident matter that requires no proof (حجة)."

I say: The use of the term "Ummah of Muhammad" to mean all those who believed in his call is a terminology that emerged after the revelation of the Quran and the spread of the Islamic call. Otherwise, Ummah means a "people" (القوم), as the Exalted said: عَلَىٰ أُمَمٍ مِّمَّن مَّعَكَ وَأُمَمٌ سَنُمَتِّعُهُمْ "upon nations [to descend] from those who are with you, and [other] nations whom We shall provide for" (Hud: 48). Sometimes it is applied to a single individual, as in the saying of the Exalted: إِنَّ إِبْرَاهِيمَ كَانَ أُمَّةً قَانِتًا لِّلَّهِ "Indeed Ibrahim was a nation (أمة), obedient to Allah" (An-Nahl: 120). Accordingly, its meaning in terms of breadth or narrowness follows the context in which the word is used or the meaning intended.

Thus, the saying of the Exalted: "Our Lord, make us submissive to You, and [raise] from our progeny a nation submissive to You"—given that the context is one of supplication as previously explained—refers only to a specific number of those who believed in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Similarly, His saying: "You are the best nation brought forth for mankind," which is in a context of bestowing favor, magnifying status, and elevating rank, does not include the entire Ummah. How could it include the Pharaohs and deceivers (دجاجلتها) of this Ummah, who found no trace of the religion except to erase and obliterate it, and no bone of its saints except to break it? The full explanation will come regarding this verse, Allah willing. It is similar to the saying of the Exalted to the Children of Israel: وَأَنِّي فَضَّلْتُكُمْ عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ "and that I gave you an advantage over all the nations" (Al-Baqarah: 47), for among them was Qarun, and the verse certainly does not include him. Likewise, the saying of the Exalted: وَقَالَ الرَّسُولُ يَا رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِي اتَّخَذُوا هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنَ مَهْجُورًا "And the Apostle will say, ‘O my Lord! Indeed my people consigned this Quran to oblivion’" (Al-Furqan: 30), does not encompass this entire Ummah, for among them are the friends of the Quran and men whom neither trade nor sale distracts from the remembrance of Allah.

As for the saying of the Exalted: تِلْكَ أُمَّةٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَلَكُم مَّا كَسَبْتُمْ وَلَا تُسْأَلُونَ عَمَّا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ "That was a nation that has passed: for it there will be what it has earned, and for you there will be what you have earned, and you will not be questioned about what they used to do" (Al-Baqarah: 134), the address here is directed to the entire Ummah of those who believed in the Prophet or those to whom he was sent.

(Scientific Discussion)

If we return to the story of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) and his journey with his son and his wife to the land of Makkah, their settlement there, and what transpired for them until the matter culminated in the sacrifice of Isma’il and his ransom by Allah, and their building of the House, we find the story to be a complete cycle of the journey of servitude (السير العبودي). It is a journey by which the servant travels from the station of the self to the proximity of his Lord, and from the land of distance to the enclosure of proximity, by turning away from the ornaments of the world, its pleasures, and its aspirations of status, wealth, women, and children. It is a process of uprooting and freeing oneself from the whispers of the devils and their clouding of the purity of sincerity (الإخلاص), and turning toward the station of the Lord and the Abode of Grandeur.

These are scattered, ordered events that have been sequenced and composed into a historical narrative that recounts a journey of servitude from the servant to Allah, the Glorified. It encompasses the etiquette of journeying (أدب السير), seeking, and presence, and the forms of love, infatuation, and sincerity, such that the more you increase in contemplation and scrutiny, the more it increases you in enlightenment and brilliance.

Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, commanded His friend Ibrahim to legislate the act of Hajj for the people, as He said: وَأَذِّن فِي النَّاسِ بِالْحَجِّ يَأْتُوكَ رِجَالًا وَعَلَىٰ كُلِّ ضَامِرٍ يَأْتِينَ مِن كُلِّ فَجٍّ عَمِيقٍ "And proclaim the Hajj to the people: they shall come to you on foot and on every lean camel coming from every distant pathway" (Al-Hajj: 27). Although what he (peace be upon him) legislated is not known to us in all its details, it was a religious rite among the Arabs in the Age of Ignorance until Allah sent the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who legislated what he legislated. He did not contradict what Ibrahim had legislated except by way of completion, as indicated by the saying of the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّنِي هَدَانِي رَبِّي إِلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ دِينًا قِيَمًا مِّلَّةَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ حَنِيفًا "Say, ‘Indeed my Lord has guided me to a straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — a right religion, the creed of Ibrahim, a Hanif’" (Al-An'am: 161), and His saying: شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ الدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِ نُوحًا وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Ibrahim, Moses and Jesus" (Ash-Shura: 13).

In any case, the rites of Hajj (نسك الحج) legislated by the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—comprising the Ihram, the standing at Arafat, the overnight stay at the Mash'ar, the sacrifice, the stoning of the pillars, the Sa'y between Safa and Marwah, the Tawaf, and the prayer at the Station—recount the story of Ibrahim. They represent his stations and the stations of his family and their scenes. And what pure, divine stations they are, to which the attraction of Lordship (جذبة الربوبية) leads and toward which the humility of servitude (ذلة العبودية) drives.

The legislated acts of worship—upon their legislators be the best of peace—are images of the stations of the perfected ones among the Prophets before their Lord. They are representations that recount their points of entry and exit in their journey to the station of proximity and nearness, as the Exalted said: لَّقَدْ كَانَ لَكُمْ فِي رَسُولِ اللَّهِ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ "In the Apostle of Allah there is certainly for you a good exemplar" (Al-Ahzab: 21). This is a fundamental principle. In the narrations explaining the wisdom of acts of worship and the secrets of their establishment and legislation, there are many witnesses to this meaning, which the insightful researcher will encounter.

Ayahs

وَمَن يَرْغَبُ عَن مِّلَّةِ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ إِلَّا مَن سَفِهَ نَفْسَهُۥ ۚ وَلَقَدِ ٱصْطَفَيْنَـٰهُ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١٣٠)
And who will [ever] forsake Abraham’s creed except one who debases himself? We certainly chose him in the [present] world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous. (130)
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُۥ رَبُّهُۥٓ أَسْلِمْ ۖ قَالَ أَسْلَمْتُ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٣١)
When his Lord said to him, ‘Submit,’ he said, ‘I submit to the Lord of all the worlds.’ (131)
وَوَصَّىٰ بِهَآ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ بَنِيهِ وَيَعْقُوبُ يَـٰبَنِىَّ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰ لَكُمُ ٱلدِّينَ فَلَا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (١٣٢)
Abraham enjoined this [creed] upon his children, and [so did] Jacob, [saying], ‘My children! Allah has indeed chosen this religion for you; so do not die except as those who have surrendered themselves [to Allah]. (132)
أَمْ كُنتُمْ شُهَدَآءَ إِذْ حَضَرَ يَعْقُوبَ ٱلْمَوْتُ إِذْ قَالَ لِبَنِيهِ مَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِنۢ بَعْدِى قَالُوا۟ نَعْبُدُ إِلَـٰهَكَ وَإِلَـٰهَ ءَابَآئِكَ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ إِلَـٰهًا وَٰحِدًا وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ (١٣٣)
Were you witnesses when death approached Jacob, when he said to his children, ‘What will you worship after me?’ They said, ‘We will worship your God and the God of your fathers, Abraham, Ishmael, and Isaac, the One God, and to Him do we submit.’ (133)
تِلْكَ أُمَّةٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ ۖ لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَلَكُم مَّا كَسَبْتُمْ ۖ وَلَا تُسْـَٔلُونَ عَمَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٣٤)
That was a nation that has passed: for it there will be what it has earned, and for you there will be what you have earned, and you will not be questioned about what they used to do. (134)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: And who will turn away from the creed of Abraham except one who befools himself? (ومن يرغب عن ملّة إبراهيم إلا من سفه نفسه). The verb "to desire" (الرغبة), when followed by the preposition "from" (عن), conveys the meaning of turning away and aversion. When followed by "in" (في), it conveys the meaning of longing and inclination. The verb "to befool" (سفه) can be used both transitively and intransitively. For this reason, some have mentioned that "himself" (نفسه) is the direct object (مفعول) of "befools" (سفه), while others have stated it is a specification (تمييز) rather than an object. In any case, the meaning is that turning away from the creed of Abraham (ملة إبراهيم) — the primordial path of monotheism established by the Prophet Abraham — stems from the foolishness of the soul and its failure to distinguish what benefits it from what harms it. From this verse, one can derive the meaning of what has been narrated in the tradition: "Intellect (العقل) is that by which the All-Beneficent is worshipped."

His saying, the Exalted: Certainly We chose him in the present world (ولقد اصطفيناه في الدنيا). Selection (الاصطفاء) is to take the best (صفوة) of a thing and distinguish it from others when they are mixed. This meaning, in view of the stations of guardianship (الولاية) — the state of proximity and divine authority granted by Allah to His chosen servants — applies to the sincerity of servitude (خلوص العبودية). This is when the servant conducts all his affairs according to what is necessitated by his being owned and his servitude, characterized by pure submission (التسليم) to his Lord. It is the realization of religion (الدين) in all affairs, for religion consists only of the elements of servitude in the matters of this world and the Hereafter, and the submission of whatever Allah is pleased with for His servant in all his affairs, as Allah the Exalted said: إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِندَ اللَّهِ الْإِسْلَامُ — "Indeed, with Allah religion is submission" (3:19). Thus, it becomes clear that the station of selection (الاصطفاء) is the very station of submission (الإسلام). This is evidenced by His saying, the Exalted: When his Lord said to him, ‘Submit,’ he said, ‘I submit to the Lord of all the worlds’ (إِذْ قَالَ لَهُ رَبُّهُ أَسْلَمْ ، قَالَ أَسْلَمْتُ لِرَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ). It appears that the adverbial clause "when" (إذ) is connected to "We chose him" (اصطفيناه). Thus, the meaning would be that his selection occurred when his Lord said to him, "Submit," and he submitted to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. Therefore, His saying, the Exalted, "When his Lord said to him, 'Submit,' he said, 'I submit to the Lord of all the worlds,'" serves as an explanation for His saying, "We chose him."

In this discourse, there is a shift (التفات) from the first person to the third person in His saying, "When his Lord said to him, 'Submit,'" rather than saying, "When We said to him, 'Submit.'" There is another shift from the second person to the third person in the reported speech of Abraham: "He said, 'I submit to the Lord of all the worlds,'" rather than saying, "He said, 'I submit to You.'" As for the first shift, the subtle point (النكتة) therein is to indicate that it was a secret kept by his Lord, as He confided it to him in their private intimacy. For the listener addressed has a connection with the speaker; however, when the speaker departs from the attribute of presence, the addressed is cut off from his station, and a veil is drawn between him and the affair or story of the speaker. Thus, it conveys that the story belongs to the intimate conversations of fellowship and the specificities of seclusion.

As for the second: It is because His saying, the Exalted, "When his Lord said to him," conveys the meaning of being singled out for grace and ease in intimate discourse. However, the etiquette of presence (أدب الحضور) required of Abraham — who is a servant bearing the mark of lowliness and humility — that he should not be overly familiar, nor regard himself as uniquely chosen for the honor of proximity or distinguished by the enclosure of intimacy. Rather, he sees himself as one of the lowly, nurtured servants (العبيد الأذلاء المربوبين). Thus, he submits to a Lord before whom all the worlds are humbled, saying: "I submit to the Lord of all the worlds."

Submission (الإسلام), surrender (التسليم), and resignation (الاستسلام) share the same meaning, derived from peace (السلم). When one of two things is in such a state relative to the other that it does not disobey or resist it, it has submitted, surrendered, and resigned to it. The Exalted said: بَلَىٰ مَنْ أَسْلَمْ وَجْهَهُ لِلَّهِ — "Certainly, whoever submits his will to Allah" (2:112), and the Exalted said: وَجَّهْتُ وَجْهِيَ لِلَّذِي فَطَرَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ حَنِيفًا — "Indeed I have turned my face toward Him who originated the heavens and the earth, as a hanif" (6:79). The "face" (وجه) of a thing is that by which it confronts you, and in relation to the Exalted, it represents the entirety of the thing's existence. Thus, the submission (إسلام) of a human being to the Exalted is the description of obedience and acceptance of whatever comes to him from Allah, the Glorified, whether it be a creative decree (حكم تكويني) — the ontological laws and destiny ordained by Allah — such as measure (قدر) and decree (قضاء), or a legislative decree (تشريعي) such as a command, a prohibition, or otherwise. From here, it possesses levels (مراتب) according to the hierarchy of what is received.

The First: Among the levels of submission (الإسلام) is the acceptance of the outward commands and prohibitions by uttering the two testimonies (الشهادتين) with the tongue, whether the heart agrees or disagrees. The Exalted said: قَالَتِ الْأَعْرَابُ آمَنَّا قُل لَّمْ تُؤْمِنُوا وَلَٰكِن قُولُوا أَسْلَمْنَا وَلَمَّا يَدْخُلِ الْإِيمَانُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ — "The Bedouins say, ‘We have faith.’ Say, ‘You do not have faith yet; rather say, “We have embraced Islam,” for faith has not yet entered into your hearts’" (49:14). Following submission in this sense is the first level of faith (الإيمان) — the inner conviction and trust in Allah and His message — which is the heart's general assent to the content of the two testimonies, necessitating action in most secondary branches of law.

The Second: That which follows faith in the first level. It is the heart's submission and obedience to the majority of detailed true beliefs (الاعتقادات الحقة التفصيلية) and the righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) that follow them, even if it remains possible to slip in some instances. Allah the Exalted said in describing the God-conscious (المتقين): الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِآيَاتِنَا وَكَانُوا مُسْلِمِينَ — "[To] those who believed in Our signs and had submitted" (43:69). He also said: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ادْخُلُوا فِي السِّلْمِ كَافَّةً — "O you who have faith! Enter into submission, all together" (2:208). Thus, there is a type of submission (إسلام) that follows faith as a realization; it is different from the first level of submission. Following this submission is the second level of faith, which is the detailed belief in religious truths. The Exalted said: إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَرْتَابُوا وَجَاهَدُوا بِأَمْوَالِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الصَّادِقُونَ — "The faithful are only those who have attained to faith in Allah and His Apostle and then have never doubted, and who wage jihad with their possessions and their persons in the way of Allah. It is they who are the truthful" (49:15). He also said: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ تِجَارَةٍ تُنجِيكُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ ، تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ ، وَتُجَاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ بِأَمْوَالِكُمْ وَأَنفُسِكُمْ — "O you who have faith! Shall I guide you to a commerce that will deliver you from a painful punishment? You have faith in Allah and His Apostle, and wage jihad in the way of Allah with your possessions and your persons" (61:10-11). In this, there is a guidance for the believers toward faith; thus, faith is distinct from faith.

The Third: That which follows faith in the second level. When the soul becomes habituated to the aforementioned faith and adopts its character, it becomes firmly established within it. Consequently, all the animalistic (البهيمية) and predatory (السبعية) powers — in short, the powers inclined toward worldly whims and its vanishing, perishing ornaments — become submissive to it. The human being then begins to worship Allah as if he sees Him; for if he does not see Him, surely Allah sees him. He finds nothing in his inner self or secret heart that does not submit to His command and prohibition, or that is resentful of His decree and measure. Allah the Glorified said: فَلَا وَرَبِّكَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّىٰ يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيمَا شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لَا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنفُسِهِمْ حَرَجًا مِّمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَيُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيمًا — "But no, by your Lord! They will not believe until they make you a judge in their disputes, then do not find within their hearts any dissent to your verdict and submit in full submission" (4:65). Following this level of submission is the third level of faith. Allah the Exalted said: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ — "Certain is the deliverance of the faithful" (23:1), until He said: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنِ اللَّغْوِ مُعْرِضُونَ — "and who avoid vain talk" (23:3). Of this level is His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ قَالَ لَهُ رَبُّهُ أَسْلَمْ ، قَالَ أَسْلَمْتُ لِرَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ — "When his Lord said to him, ‘Submit,’ he said, ‘I submit to the Lord of all the worlds’" (2:131), among others. Sometimes the second and third levels are counted as a single level.

Virtuous traits such as contentment (الرضاء), submission (التسليم), seeking reward from Allah (الحسبة), patience (الصبر) for the sake of Allah, complete asceticism (الزهد), piety (الورع), and loving and hating for the sake of Allah are among the necessities of this level.

The Fourth: That which follows the third level of faith. The state of the human being in the previous level with his Lord is like the state of a owned servant with his master, as he was fulfilling the duty of his servitude in the truest sense, which is pure submission to what the Master wants, loves, or is pleased with. However, the matter regarding the ownership of the Lord of all the worlds over His creation is far greater than that. It is the reality of ownership (حقيقة الملك) — the absolute sovereignty of Allah where nothing possesses independent existence or agency — wherein nothing has independence apart from Him, neither in essence (ذاتاً), nor in attribute (صفة), nor in action (فعلاً), in a manner befitting His majesty, exalted is His majesty.

Thus, the human being — while in the previous level of submission — may be overtaken by Divine care (العناية الربانية), which makes him witness that sovereignty belongs to Allah alone; nothing other than Him owns anything for itself except through Him; there is no Lord but Him. This is a bestowed (وهبيّ) meaning and a Divine effusion (إفاضة إلهية) in which the human will has no effect. Perhaps His saying, the Exalted: Our Lord, make us submissive to You, and [raise] from our progeny a nation submissive to You, and show us our holy rites (2:128), is an allusion to this level of submission. For His saying, the Exalted: "When his Lord said to him, 'Submit,' he said, 'I submit to the Lord of all the worlds,'" apparently refers to a legislative command (أمر تشريعي), not a creative one (تكويني). Thus, Abraham was a submitter (مسلماً) by his own choice, responding to the call of his Lord and obeying His command. This was among the commands directed to him (peace be upon him) in the early stages of his state. Therefore, his request at the end of his life, along with his son Ishmael, for submission (الإسلام) and to be shown the rites, is a request for a matter whose reins are not in his hand, or a request for steadfastness in a matter that is not in his hand. Thus, the submission requested in the verse is this level of submission. Following submission in this sense is the fourth level of faith, which is the encompassing of this state over all states and actions. The Exalted said: أَلَا إِنَّ أَوْلِيَاءَ اللَّهِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ ، الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكَانُوا يَتَّقُونَ — "Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve—those who have faith and are God-conscious" (10:62-63). For these believers mentioned in the verse must be certain that nothing has independence apart from Allah, and no cause has effect except by the permission of Allah, so that they do not grieve over a misfortune that has occurred, nor fear a potential danger. Otherwise, there would be no meaning to them being such that nothing frightens them and no matter grieves them. So this type of faith comes after the aforementioned submission; so understand.

His saying, the Exalted: and in the Hereafter he will surely be among the righteous (وإنه في الآخرة لمن الصالحين). Righteousness (الصلاح) — the state of being fit or virtuous in the sight of Allah — which is suitability in a certain respect, is sometimes attributed in His speech to the action of a human being and sometimes to his soul and essence. The Exalted said: فَلْيَعْمَلْ عَمَلًا صَالِحًا — "let him act with righteous conduct" (18:110), and the Exalted said: وَأَنكِحُوا الْأَيَامَىٰ مِنكُمْ وَالصَّالِحِينَ مِنْ عِبَادِكُمْ وَإِمَائِكُمْ — "Marry off those who are single among you, and the upright among your male and female slaves" (24:32).

Although the righteousness of action (صلاح العمل) has not been explicitly defined in His speech, He has attributed to it certain effects through which its meaning becomes clear.

Among them: that it is suitable for the sake of Allah. The Exalted said: صَبَرُوا ابْتِغَاءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِمْ — "who are patient seeking the face of their Lord" (13:22), and the Exalted said: وَمَا تُنفِقُونَ إِلَّا ابْتِغَاءَ وَجْهِ اللَّهِ — "and you do not spend except to seek Allah’s pleasure" (2:272).

Among them: that it is suitable to be rewarded. The Exalted said: ثَوَابُ اللَّهِ خَيْرٌ لِّمَنْ آمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَالِحًا — "Allah’s reward is better for him who has faith and acts righteously" (28:80).

Among them: that it raises the "good word" ascending to Allah, the Glorified. The Exalted said: إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ — "To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous conduct" (35:10). It is derived from these effects attributed to it that the righteousness of action means its preparedness and suitability to be clothed in the garment of honor and to be an aid and support for the ascent of the "good word" to Him, the Exalted. The Exalted said: وَلَٰكِن يَنَالُهُ التَّقْوَىٰ مِنكُمْ — "but it is your God-consciousness that reaches Him" (22:37), and the Exalted said: كُلًّا نُّمِدُّ هَٰؤُلَاءِ ، وَهَٰؤُلَاءِ مِنْ عَطَاءِ رَبِّكَ ، وَمَا كَانَ عَطَاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُورًا — "To these and to those—to all We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not barred" (17:20). Thus, His gift, the Exalted, is like the form (الصورة), and the righteousness of action is like the matter (المادة).

As for the righteousness of the soul and essence (صلاح النفس والذات), the Exalted has said: وَمَن يُطِعِ اللَّهَ وَالرَّسُولَ فَأُولَٰئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ النَّبِيِّينَ ، وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ ، وَالشُّهَدَاءِ وَالصَّالِحِينَ ، وَحَسُنَ أُولَٰئِكَ رَفِيقًا — "Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle—they are with those whom Allah has blessed, including the prophets and the truthful, the martyrs and the righteous, and excellent companions are they!" (4:69). The Exalted said: وَأَدْخَلْنَاهُمْ فِي رَحْمَتِنَا إِنَّهُم مِّنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "and We admitted them into Our mercy. Indeed they were among the righteous" (21:86). The Exalted said, narrating from Solomon: وَأَدْخِلْنِي بِرَحْمَتِكَ فِي عِبَادِكَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "and admit me, by Your mercy, among Your righteous servants" (27:19). The Exalted said: وَلُوطًا آتَيْنَاهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا — "And Lot, We gave him judgement and knowledge" (21:74), until His saying: وَأَدْخَلْنَاهُ فِي رَحْمَتِنَا إِنَّهُ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "and We admitted him into Our mercy. Indeed he was one of the righteous" (21:75). The meaning is not righteousness for the sake of absolute, general Divine mercy that encompasses everything, nor the specific mercy for the believers as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَسَأَكْتُبُهَا لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ — "My mercy embraces all things, and I shall appoint it for those who are God-conscious" (7:156). This is because these people, namely the righteous (الصالحون), are a specific group among the God-conscious believers, and some mercy is specific to some and not others. The Exalted said: يَخْتَصُّ بِرَحْمَتِهِ مَن يَشَاءُ — "He singles out for His mercy whomever He wishes" (2:105). Nor is the meaning the absolute honor of guardianship (الولاية) — the state where Allah takes charge of His servant's affairs — for although the righteous are honored by that and were among the honored guardians (الأولياء) as we explained previously regarding His saying, the Exalted: اهْدِنَا الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ — "Guide us on the straight path" (1:6), and as will come in the commentary of the verse, this guardianship is a shared attribute between them and the prophets, the truthful, and the martyrs. Therefore, it would not be correct to count them as a specific group in contrast to them.

Yes, the effect specific to righteousness (الصلاح) is the admission into mercy, which is the general security from punishment, as both meanings have been mentioned together regarding Paradise. The Exalted said: فَيُدْخِلُهُمْ رَبُّهمْ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ — "their Lord will admit them into His mercy" (45:30), meaning into Paradise. The Exalted said: يَدْعُونَ فِيهَا بِكُلِّ فَاكِهَةٍ آمِنِينَ — "They will call therein for every kind of fruit, secure" (44:55), meaning in Paradise.

If you contemplate His saying, the Exalted: "and We admitted him into Our mercy" (21:75), and His saying: "and We made them all righteous" (21:72) — where He attributed the action to Himself, the Exalted, not to the servant — and then consider that He restricted reward and gratitude to what corresponds to action and effort, you would conclude that essential righteousness (الصلاح الذاتي) is an honor that does not correspond to action and will. This may clarify the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: لَهُم مَّا يَشَاءُونَ فِيهَا — "They will have whatever they wish therein" (50:35) — which is what is attained through action — and His saying: وَلَدَيْنَا مَزِيدٌ — "and with Us there is yet more" — which is a matter other than what is attained through action, as will be explained, Allah willing, in the commentary of His saying, the Exalted: "They will have whatever they wish therein" (50:35).

Furthermore, if you contemplate the state of Abraham and his status — that he was a commissioned prophet, one of the "Arch-Prophets" (اولى العزم) — the five major prophets who brought new divine laws — that he was an Imam, and that he was an exemplar for a number of prophets and messengers after him; and that he was among the righteous by the text of His saying, the Exalted: "and We made them all righteous" (21:72), which apparently refers to an immediate righteousness; while those below him in merit among the prophets were honored with this immediate righteousness — yet he (peace be upon him), despite all that, asks to be joined with the righteous. This apparently indicates that there are a people among the righteous who preceded him, and he asks to be joined with them in what they preceded him in. He was answered regarding this in the Hereafter, as Allah narrates in three places of His speech, where the Exalted said: وَلَقَدِ اصْطَفَيْنَاهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "Certainly We chose him in the present world, and in the Hereafter he will surely be among the righteous" (2:130). The Exalted said: وَآتَيْنَاهُ أَجْرَهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "We gave him his reward in the world, and in the Hereafter he will surely be among the righteous" (29:27).
