Some commentators mentioned that the inclusion in this verse—the verse of the change of the direction of prayer—of the phrase "and that I may complete My blessing upon you and so that you may be guided," along with the inclusion of these two phrases in the saying of the Exalted in Surah al-Fath regarding the conquest of Mecca, where the Exalted said: إِنَّا فَتَحْنَا لَكَ فَتْحًا مُّبِينًا لِّيَغْفِرَ لَكَ اللَّهُ مَا تَقَدَّمَ مِن ذَنبِكَ وَمَا تَأَخَّرَ وَيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكَ وَيَهْدِيَكَ صِرَاطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا — "Indeed We have inaugurated for you a clear victory, that Allah may forgive you what is past of your sin and what is to come, and that He may complete His blessing upon you and guide you on a straight path" (48:1-2), indicates that it contains the glad tidings of the conquest of Mecca.

The explanation of this is that the Kaaba, at the beginning of Islam, was occupied by the idols and images of the polytheists, and the authority was with them. Islam had not yet become strong enough to manifest its dominance and power. So Allah guided His Messenger to face the Holy House, as it was the direction of prayer for the Jews, who were closer in their religion to Islam than the polytheists. Then, when the matter of Islam became manifest with the migration of the Messenger of Allah to Medina, and the time of the conquest drew near, and the purification of the House from the filth of idols was expected, the command came to change the direction of prayer. This was the great blessing (النعمة) that was specific to the Muslims. In the verse of the change, the completion of the blessing and guidance was promised, which is the clearing of the Kaaba from the impurities of idols and its designation as the direction of prayer toward which Allah is worshipped, with the Muslims being the ones specific to it and it being specific to them. Thus, it is a glad tiding of the conquest of Mecca. Then, when the conquest of Mecca was mentioned at the time it occurred, He pointed to what He had promised them of the completion of the blessing and the glad tiding by His saying: "and that He may complete His blessing upon you and guide you on a straight path."

This discourse, although seemingly plausible, is devoid of deep reflection. The apparent meaning of the verses does not support it. For that which indicates the promise of the completion of the blessing in this verse: "and that I may complete My blessing upon you and so that you may be guided," is the "lam" of purpose (لام الغاية). The verse of Surah al-Fath, which he took as the fulfillment of this promise and the realization of this glad tiding—namely His saying, the Exalted: "that Allah may forgive you... and that He may complete His blessing upon you and guide you on a straight path"—contains this very same "lam." Thus, both verses contain the beautiful promise of the completion of the blessing. Furthermore, the verse of the Hajj [or rather, the qiblah] contains the promise of the completion of the blessing for all Muslims, while the verse of al-Fath contains it for the Messenger of Allah specifically. Thus, the context in the two verses is different.

If there were a verse recounting the fulfillment of the promise contained in the two verses, it would be the saying of the Exalted: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْإِسْلَامَ دِينًا — "Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed My blessing upon you, and I am pleased with Islam as your religion" (5:3). The discussion on the meaning of blessing (النعمة) and the identification of this blessing with which Allah, the Glorified, favors [the believers] in the verse will follow.

Similar to these two verses in containing the promise of the completion of the blessing is the saying of the Exalted: وَلَٰكِن يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ وَلِيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ — "but He desires to purify you and to complete His blessing upon you so that you may be grateful" (5:6), and the saying of the Exalted: كَذَٰلِكَ يُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْلِمُونَ — "Thus does He complete His blessing upon you so that you may submit" (16:81). Some discussion appropriate to this context will follow, God willing, in the commentary on these verses.

His saying, the Exalted: كما أرسلنا فيكم رسولاً منكم — As We sent to you an apostle from among yourselves. The apparent meaning of the verse is that the "kaf" is for comparison and the "ma" is infinitival (مصدرية). So the meaning is: We have blessed you by making the House built by Abraham—for which he prayed for blessings and goodness—your direction of prayer, just as We sent among you a Messenger from among yourselves who recites Our signs to you, teaches you the Book and wisdom, and purifies you, in response to the prayer of Abraham, when he and his son Ishmael said: "Our Lord, raise amongst them an apostle from among them, who should recite to them Your signs, and teach them the Book and wisdom, and purify them." In this, there is a favor (امتنان) upon them for the sending of the Messenger, just as there is a favor for making the Kaaba the direction of prayer. From here, it appears that the one addressed by His saying, "among you a Messenger from among yourselves," is the Muslim nation—specifically the guardians of the religion among the nation in reality, and all Muslims from the progeny of Ishmael (the Mudar Arabs) in appearance, and all Arabs, indeed all Muslims, according to the ruling.

His saying, the Exalted: يتلوا عليكم آياتنا (who recites to you Our signs): It is evident that these are the signs (آيات) of the Quran because of His saying "recites" (يتلو), for the primary concern in recitation (التلاوة) is directed toward the wording rather than the meaning. Purification (التزكية) is cleansing (التطهير), which is the removal of impurities and filth (الأدناس والقذارات); thus, it encompasses the removal of corrupt beliefs such as polytheism (الشرك) and disbelief (الكفر), the removal of base traits (الملكات الرذيلة) of character such as arrogance and stinginess, and the removal of heinous acts and deeds such as murder, adultery, and drinking wine. The teaching of the Book (الكتاب) and Wisdom (الحكمة), and the teaching of what they did not know, encompasses all fundamental (الأصلية) and derivative (الفرعية) branches of knowledge.

Know that these noble verses contain instances of grammatical shift (الالتفات) — the transition between different grammatical persons to highlight specific meanings — where He, the Exalted, is referred to in the third person, the first person singular, and the first person plural. Similarly, others besides Him, the Exalted, are referred to in the third person, the second person, and the first person. The subtle point (النكتة) behind this is not hidden from the insightful contemplator.

(Narrative Discourse)

In al-Majma‘, citing al-Qummi in his Tafsir regarding His saying, the Exalted: سَيَقُولُ السُّفَهَاءُ (The foolish among the people will say...), narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "The direction of prayer (القبلة) was changed to the Kaaba (الكعبة) after the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had prayed toward Jerusalem (بيت المقدس) for thirteen years in Mecca. After his migration to Medina, he prayed toward Jerusalem for seven months." He said: "Then Allah directed him toward Mecca. This was because the Jews used to taunt the Messenger of Allah, saying: 'You are a follower of ours, praying toward our qiblah.' The Messenger of Allah became deeply distressed by this. He went out in the middle of the night, looking toward the horizons of the sky, awaiting a command from Allah regarding this matter. When morning came and the time for the noon prayer (الظهر) arrived, he was in the Mosque of Bani Salim. He had already prayed two units (ركعتين) of the noon prayer when Gabriel descended, took him by his arms, and turned him toward the Kaaba, and He revealed to him: قَدْ نَرَىٰ تَقَلُّبَ وَجْهِكَ فِي السَّمَاءِ فَلَنُوَلِّيَنَّكَ قِبْلَةً تَرْضَاهَا فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ (We certainly see you turning your face about in the sky. We will surely turn you to a qiblah of your liking: so turn your face towards the Holy Mosque [2:144]). Thus, he had prayed two units toward Jerusalem and two units toward the Kaaba. Then the Jews and the foolish ones said: 'What has turned them from the qiblah they used to face?'"

I say: The narrations transmitted through both Sunni and Shi'a chains are numerous, preserved in the compendiums of hadith, and are similar in content. There is disagreement regarding the date of this event; most reports—and this is the most correct view—state it occurred in Rajab of the second year of the Hijra, the seventeenth month thereof. Some matters related to this topic will be discussed in a separate section, God willing.

It is narrated through the chains of the Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama‘ah regarding the testimony of this nation (الأمة) over the people, and the Prophet's testimony over them, that on the Day of Resurrection, the nations will deny that the prophets conveyed the message. Allah will then demand proof from the prophets that they had conveyed it—though He knows best—and the nation of Muhammad will be brought forward to testify. The other nations will ask: "How do you know?" They will reply: "We knew that through the report of Allah, the Exalted, in His speaking Book, upon the tongue of His truthful Prophet." Then Muhammad will be brought and asked about the state of his nation; he will attest to their purity (يزكيهم) and testify to their justice (عدالتهم). This is the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: فَكَيْفَ إِذَا جِئْنَا مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ بِشَهِيدٍ (So how shall it be, when We bring from every nation a witness... [4:41]).

I say: What this report contains—which is supported by other reports transmitted by al-Suyuti in al-Durr al-Manthur and others—regarding the Messenger of Allah's attestation to the purity of his nation and his declaration of their justice (تعديله), perhaps refers to his declaration of justice for some of them rather than all of them. Otherwise, it is refuted by the established necessity (الضرورة) of the Book and the Sunnah. How could one validate or justify these atrocities, the likes of which can hardly be found, nor even a prototype of them, in any of the past nations? And how can the Pharaohs and tyrants (طواغيتها) of this nation be purified and declared just? Is this anything but an assault on the upright religion (الدين الحنيف) and a mockery of the realities of this luminous faith (الملة البيضاء)? Furthermore, the hadith involves the endorsement of theoretical testimony (الشهادة النظرية) rather than the testimony of direct witnessing (شهادة التحمل).

In al-Manaqib, regarding this meaning, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "None shall be witnesses (شهداء) over the people except the Imams and the Messengers. As for the nation (الأمة), it is not permissible for Allah to call them as witnesses when among them are those whose testimony would not be accepted for a bundle of herbs."

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: لِّتَكُونُوا شُهَدَاءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ وَيَكُونَ الرَّسُولُ عَلَيْكُمْ شَهِيدًا (...that you may be witnesses to mankind, and that the Apostle may be a witness to you [2:143]): "If you think that Allah meant by this verse all the people of the qiblah from among the monotheists (الموحدين), do you then see that one whose testimony is not accepted in this world for a measure of dates will have his testimony sought by Allah on the Day of Resurrection and accepted from him in the presence of all the past nations? Never! Allah did not mean such as these from His creation. He meant the nation for whom the prayer of Abraham was answered: كُنتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ (You are the best nation [ever] brought forth for mankind [3:110]); they are the middle nation (الأمة الوسطى) and they are the best nation brought forth for mankind."

I say: The explanation of this has already passed in the commentary on the verse, derived from the Book.

In Qurb al-Isnad, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), from his father, from the Prophet, who said: "Among what Allah gave my nation and favored them with over other nations are three characteristics He did not give except to a prophet..."—until he said—"And when He sent a prophet, He made him a witness over his people, and indeed Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, made my nation a witness over creation, where He says: لِيَكُونَ الرَّسُولُ شَهِيدًا عَلَيْكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا شُهَدَاءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ (...that the Apostle may be a witness to you and that you may be witnesses to mankind [22:78])."

I say: This hadith does not contradict what has passed, for the "nation" intended is the submitted nation (الأمة المسلمة) for whom the prayer of Abraham was answered.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) in a hadith describing the Day of Resurrection, he (peace be upon him) said: "They will gather in a station where all of creation will be made to speak, and no one shall speak except him whom the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) permits and who speaks aright. Then the Messenger will be stood up and questioned; that is His saying to Muhammad: فَكَيْفَ إِذَا جِئْنَا مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ بِشَهِيدٍ وَجِئْنَا بِكَ عَلَىٰ هَٰؤُلَاءِ شَهِيدًا (So how shall it be, when We bring from every nation a witness and We bring you as a witness against them? [4:41]). He is the witness over the witnesses, and the witnesses are the Messengers."

In al-Tahdhib, narrated from Abu Basir from one of the two Imams (peace be upon them), he said: "I said to him: 'Did He command him to pray toward Jerusalem?' He said: 'Yes. Do you not see that Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, says: وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الْقِبْلَةَ الَّتِي كُنتَ عَلَيْهَا إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ مَن يَتَّبِعُ الرَّسُولَ مِمَّن يَنقَلِبُ عَلَىٰ عَقِبَيْهِ (We did not appoint the qiblah you were following except that We may distinguish those who follow the Apostle from those who turn back on their heels [2:143])?'"

I say: The implication of the hadith is that His saying, the Exalted, "which you were following" (التي كنت عليها) is a description of the qiblah, meaning Jerusalem, and that it was the qiblah the Messenger of Allah was following. This is supported by the context of the verses as previously mentioned.

From here, support is found for what is in some reports from Imam al-Askari (peace be upon him): "The desire of the people of Mecca was for the Kaaba, so Allah wanted to distinguish the follower of Muhammad from his opponents by following the qiblah they disliked while Muhammad commanded it. And since the desire of the people of Medina was for Jerusalem, He commanded them to oppose it and turn toward the Kaaba to distinguish who follows Muhammad in what he dislikes, thus being his confirmer and in agreement with him." This also clarifies the invalidity of what has been said: that His saying, the Exalted, "which you were following" (التي كنت عليها) is the second object of "We appointed" (جعلنا), meaning: "We did not appoint the qiblah—meaning the Kaaba—which you were following before Jerusalem..." They argued for this using His saying: "except that We may distinguish those who follow the Apostle." This is invalid, and its invalidity is clear from what has preceded.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, narrated from al-Zubayri from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "I said to him: 'Will you not inform me about faith (الإيمان)? Is it action and speech, or speech without action?' He said: 'Faith is entirely action, and speech is part of that action, an obligation from Allah, clarified in His Book, its light is clear and its proof is firm; the Book testifies to it and calls toward it. When Allah turned His Prophet toward the Kaaba away from Jerusalem, the Muslims said to the Prophet: "What do you see regarding our prayers that we used to pray toward Jerusalem? What is our state regarding them, and what is the state of those of our dead who passed away while they were praying toward Jerusalem?" So Allah revealed: وَمَا كَانَ اللَّهُ لِيُضِيعَ إِيمَانَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ بِالنَّاسِ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (...and Allah would not let your faith go to waste. Indeed Allah is most kind and merciful to mankind [2:143]). Thus, He named the prayer "faith." So whoever is wary of Allah, guarding his limbs and fulfilling for every one of his limbs what Allah has obligated upon it, will meet Allah with his faith complete, being among the people of Paradise. And whoever betrays any part of it or transgresses what Allah commanded regarding it, will meet Allah with deficient faith.'"

I say: Al-Kulayni also narrated it. Its inclusion of the revelation of His saying: "and Allah would not let your faith go to waste" after the change of the qiblah does not contradict the previous explanation.

In al-Faqih, it is mentioned that the Prophet prayed toward Jerusalem for thirteen years in Mecca and nineteen months in Medina. Then the Jews taunted him, saying: "You are a follower of our qiblah." He became deeply distressed by this. During part of the night, he went out turning his face toward the horizons of the sky. When morning came, he prayed the dawn prayer (الغداة). When he had prayed two units of the noon prayer, Gabriel came and said to him: قَدْ نَرَىٰ تَقَلُّبَ وَجْهِكَ فِي السَّمَاءِ فَلَنُوَلِّيَنَّكَ قِبْلَةً تَرْضَاهَا فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ (We certainly see you turning your face about in the sky. We will surely turn you to a qiblah of your liking: so turn your face towards the Holy Mosque [2:144]). Then he took the Prophet's hand and turned his face toward the Kaaba, and those behind him turned their faces until the men stood in the place of the women and the women in the place of the men. Thus, the first part of his prayer was toward Jerusalem and the last part toward the Kaaba. The news reached a mosque in Medina while its people had prayed two units of the afternoon prayer (العصر), so they turned toward the qiblah. The first part of their prayer was toward Jerusalem and the last part toward the Kaaba, so that mosque was named the Mosque of the Two Qiblahs (مسجد القبلتين).

I say: Al-Qummi narrated something similar, and that the Prophet was in the Mosque of Bani Salim.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ (...so turn your face towards the Holy Mosque [2:144]), he said: "Face the qiblah, and do not turn your face away from the qiblah, for it would invalidate your prayer. Indeed, Allah says to His Prophet regarding the obligatory prayer (الفريضة): 'so turn your face towards the Holy Mosque, and wherever you may be, turn your faces towards it.'"

I say: The reports stating that the verse was revealed regarding the obligatory prayer and is specific to it are numerous and widely transmitted (مستفيضة).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ آتَيْنَاهُمُ الْكِتَابَ يَعْرِفُونَهُ (Those whom We have given the Book recognize him... [2:146]), he said: "This verse was revealed regarding the Jews and the Christians. Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, says: 'And those whom We have given the Book recognize him,' meaning they recognize the Messenger of Allah as they recognize their own sons, because Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, had revealed to them in the Torah, the Gospel, and the Psalms the description of Muhammad, the description of his companions, and his migration. This is His saying, the Exalted: مُّحَمَّدٌ رَّسُولُ اللَّهِ ۚ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ رُحَمَاءُ بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ تَرَاهُمْ رُكَّعًا سُجَّدًا يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَرِضْوَانًا ۖ سِيمَاهُمْ فِي وُجُوهِهِم مِّنْ أَثَرِ السُّجُودِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مَثَلُهُمْ فِي التَّوْرَاةِ ۚ وَمَثَلُهُمْ فِي الْإِنجِيلِ (Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves. You see them bowing and prostrating [in worship], seeking Allah’s grace and [His] pleasure. Their mark is on their faces from the effect of prostration. That is their description in the Torah, and their description in the Evangel... [48:29]). This is the description of the Messenger of Allah in the Torah and the description of his companions. When Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, sent him, the People of the Book recognized him, just as He, Majestic is His Majesty, said: فَلَمَّا جَاءَهُم مَّا عَرَفُوا كَفَرُوا بِهِ (...so when there came to them that which they recognized, they denied it [2:89])."

I say: Something similar was narrated in al-Kafi from Ali (peace be upon him).

In numerous reports from Shi'a chains, His saying, the Exalted: أَيْنَ مَا تَكُونُوا يَأْتِ بِكُمُ اللَّهُ جَمِيعًا (...Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you all together [2:148]) refers to the companions of the Upholder (القائم) — the promised savior from the progeny of the Prophet. In some of them, it is stated that this is by way of application (التطبيق) and flow (الجري) — the principle that Quranic verses apply to new instances over time.

In a hadith from Sunni chains regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلِأُتِمَّ نِعْمَتِي عَلَيْكُمْ (...and that I may complete My blessing upon you [2:150]), narrated from Ali: "The completion of the blessing is dying upon Islam."

In another hadith from their chains: "The completion of the blessing is entering Paradise."

(Scientific Discourse)

The legislation of the qiblah in Islam and the requirement of facing it in prayer—which is a universal worship among Muslims—as well as in slaughtering animals and other matters that concern the general public, made people most in need of researching the direction of the qiblah and determining it. Initially, this was done through conjecture, estimation, and a type of guesswork. Later, the general need prompted mathematicians among their scholars to bring it closer to precision. They utilized the tables set in astronomical handbooks (الزيجات) to determine the latitude (عرض) and longitude (طول) of cities. They derived the deviation (انحراف) of Mecca from the southern point in a city—that is, the deviation of the line connecting the city and Mecca from the line connecting the city and the southern point (the meridian line/خط نصف النهار)—using sines (الجيوب) and trigonometry (المثلثات). Then they determined this for every Muslim city using the well-known Indian Circle (الدائرة الهندية) which determines the meridian line, followed by the degrees of deviation and the line of the qiblah.

Later, to speed up the process and for ease of use, they employed the magnetic instrument known as the compass (الحك). With its needle, it determines the directions of North and South, thus substituting for the Indian Circle in determining the southern point. By knowing the degree of deviation of the city, the user can identify the direction of the qiblah.

However, this effort of theirs—may Allah reward their efforts—was not free from deficiency and error from two perspectives. As for the first: later mathematicians discovered that the predecessors had erred in determining longitude, which affected the calculation of deviation and thus the identification of the direction of the Kaaba. This was because their method for determining latitude—which is recording the elevation of the North Pole—was closer to precision, unlike the method for determining longitude, which was recording the distance between two points sharing a common celestial event like an eclipse by the amount of the sun's apparent movement, which is estimation by the hour. This was difficult and lacked precision with ancient tools. However, the availability of modern tools and the closeness of communications today has made the matter entirely easy. Yet the need remained until the eminent and famous scholar, al-Sardar al-Kabuli—may Allah have mercy on him—undertook this task in recent times. He derived the qiblah deviation using modern principles and wrote his well-known treatise Tuhfat al-Ajillah fi Ma‘rifat al-Qiblah. It is an elegant treatise in which he explained the method of deriving the qiblah through mathematical demonstration and provided tables for determining the qiblah of various cities.

Among the most beautiful things he was granted success in—may Allah reward his effort—is what he revealed of a brilliant miracle (كرامة) for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in his preserved prayer niche (المحراب) in the Prophet's Mosque in Medina. This is because Medina, according to the calculations of the ancients, was at latitude 25 degrees and longitude 75 degrees 20 minutes. The qiblah of the Prophet's prayer niche in his mosque did not align with this. Therefore, scholars were constantly researching the matter of the prayer niche's qiblah, sometimes mentioning reasons for its deviation that did not correspond to the reality of the matter. However, he—may Allah have mercy on him—clarified that Medina is at latitude 24 degrees 57 minutes and longitude 39 degrees 59 minutes, with a deviation of approximately 0 degrees 45 minutes. The qiblah of the prayer niche aligned with this perfectly. Thus appeared a brilliant miracle for the Prophet in his qiblah toward which he turned his face while in prayer, and it was mentioned that Gabriel took his hand and turned his face toward the Kaaba. Allah and His Messenger have spoken the truth.

After him, the eminent engineer and leader Abdul Razzaq al-Bagha'iri—may Allah have mercy on him—derived the qiblah for most regions of the earth and published a treatise on knowing the qiblah, which are tables mentioning one thousand five hundred locations on earth. With this, the blessing in identifying the qiblah was completed.

As for the second perspective, which is the magnetic perspective: they found that the magnetic poles of the Earth do not coincide with its geographical poles. The magnetic North Pole, for instance—noting that it changes over time—is approximately one thousand miles away from the geographical North Pole. Based on this, the compass does not identify the geographical South Pole itself; rather, the discrepancy might reach an amount that cannot be overlooked. This prompted the eminent mathematical engineer and leader Husayn Ali Razm Ara in these days—the year 1332 Solar Hijri—to solve this dilemma. He derived the amount of discrepancy between the geographical and magnetic poles according to different points and identified the qiblah deviation from the magnetic pole for approximately one thousand locations on earth. He invented a compass that provides an approximation very close to precision in identifying the qiblah, and it is in use today—may Allah reward his effort.

(Social Discourse)

One who contemplates the affairs of human society and looks at the properties and effects that follow this matter called "society" (الاجتماع)—from the perspective that it is a society—will not doubt that this society was formed, then branched out and expanded into its various divisions and facets, by human nature (الطبيعة الانسانية). This occurred when it sensed, through inspiration from Allah, the Glorified, its needs for survival and perfection through social acts. Thus, it resorts to society and adheres to it to succeed in its actions, movements, and stillness within the cradle of social upbringing and with its assistance. Then it sensed and was inspired with knowledge (mental images/صور ذهنية) and perceptions that it applies to matter, to its needs therein, and to its actions and the directions of its actions. These serve as the link and connection between it and its actions and needs, such as the belief in beauty and ugliness (الحسن والقبح), what is obligatory, what is appropriate, and other social principles like leadership and followership, ownership and exclusivity, common and specific transactions, and other general rules, laws, customs, and national traditions that are not free from change and variation according to different peoples, regions, and eras. All these meanings and the rules established upon them are the product of human nature through inspiration from Allah, the Glorified. Human nature refined them to represent the meanings it believes in and desires in the external world, then moves toward them through action, deed, omission, and perfection.

The devotional turning (التوجه العبادي) toward Allah, the Glorified—Who is transcendent above the affairs of matter and sanctified beyond the attachment of material sense—if it is intended to transcend the limits of the heart and conscience and descend into the realm of actions (which only revolve around material things), there is no alternative or escape but for it to be by way of representation (التمثيل). This is done by observing the heart's turnings in their various specificities, then representing them in action through suitable forms and shapes of deeds. For example, prostration (السجدة) is intended for self-abasement, bowing (الركوع) for glorification, circumambulation (الطواف) for the sacrifice of the self, standing (القيام) for magnifying, and ablution (الوضوء) and bathing (الغسل) for purity for the Presence, and so on. There is no doubt that turning toward the Worshipped and facing Him by the servant in his servitude is the spirit of his worship, without which it would have no life or existence. To represent this, worship needs it for its perfection, stability, and the establishment of its reality.

The polytheists, the star-worshippers, and the worshippers of other bodies—human or otherwise—used to face their idols and gods, turning toward them with their bodies in close proximity.

However, the religion of the prophets—and we specifically mention among them the religion of Islam, which confirms them all—established the Kaaba as the qiblah and commanded facing it in prayer, for which no Muslim is excused, wherever he may be in the regions and horizons of the earth. It forbade facing it or turning one's back to it in certain states and recommended it in others. Thus, it preserved the turning of the human heart toward the House of Allah, ensuring that he does not forget his Lord in his solitude or public life, his standing or sitting, his sleep or wakefulness, his ritual or worship, even in his lowliest and most mundane states. This is from the perspective of the individual.

As for the perspective of society, the matter is more wondrous and the effect more manifest and impactful. It has gathered people, despite their different times and places, to turn toward a single point, representing thereby their intellectual unity, the connection of their community, and the harmony of their hearts. This is the most subtle spirit that can permeate all the affairs of individuals in their material and spiritual vitality. It provides the highest form of society and the most complete and powerful unity. Allah, the Exalted, has favored His Muslim servants with this and preserved through it the unity of their religion and the might of their gathering, even after they split into factions and divided into various sects and divergent paths where no two of them agree on an opinion. We thank Allah, the Exalted, for His favors.

Ayah

فَٱذْكُرُونِىٓ أَذْكُرْكُمْ وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لِى وَلَا تَكْفُرُونِ (١٥٢)
Remember Me and I will remember you, and thank Me, and do not be ungrateful to Me. (152)

(Commentary)

When Allah, the Exalted, bestowed His favor upon the Prophet and the Muslims by sending the Noble Prophet from among them to them—a blessing (النعمة) that cannot be measured—and granted them gift upon gift, which is His remembrance (الذكر) of them, as He did not forget them in guiding them to the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — and leading them toward the ultimate perfection; and in addition to that, His establishing of the qiblah, which contains the perfection of their religion, the unification of their worship, and the rectification of their religious and social virtue; He branched out from this an invitation to remember Him and thank Him. This was so that He might remember them with His blessing in return for their remembrance of Him through servitude and obedience, and that He might increase them for their gratitude for His blessing and their avoidance of ingratitude. Indeed, the Exalted has said: وَاذْكُر رَّبَّكَ إِذَا نَسِيتَ وَقُلْ عَسَىٰ أَن يَهْدِيَنِ رَبِّي لِأَقْرَبَ مِنْ هَٰذَا رَشَدًا "And remember your Lord when you forget, and say, ‘Maybe my Lord will guide me to [something] closer to rectitude than this.’" (18:24). And the Exalted said: لَئِن شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ "If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing]" (14:7). Both of these verses were revealed before the verses of the qiblah in Surat al-Baqarah.

Furthermore, remembrance (الذكر) sometimes stands in contrast to heedlessness (الغفلة), as in His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تُطِعْ مَنْ أَغْفَلْنَا قَلْبَهُ عَن ذِكْرِنَا "and do not obey him whose heart We have made oblivious to Our remembrance" (18:28). Heedlessness is the absence of the knowledge of knowledge (العلم بالعلم), despite the existence of the primary knowledge itself; thus, remembrance is its opposite, which is the knowledge of knowledge. At other times, it stands in contrast to forgetfulness (النسيان), which is the fading of the image of knowledge from the treasury of the mind; thus, remembrance is its opposite. From this category is His saying, the Exalted: "And remember your Lord when you forget..." In this sense, like forgetfulness, it is a concept (معنى) possessing effects and properties that branch out from it. For this reason, remembrance—like forgetfulness—is sometimes applied to instances where their effects are realized even if their essences are not. For if you do not assist your friend while knowing his need for your help, you have "forgotten" him, even though you actually remember him; the same applies to remembrance.

It appears that applying the term "remembrance" to verbal remembrance (الذكر اللفظي) is of this nature, for speaking about a thing is one of the effects of remembering it in the heart. The Exalted said: قُلْ سَأَتْلُو عَلَيْكُم مِّنْهُ ذِكْرًا "Say, ‘I will relate to you a narrative of him.’" (18:83). There are many similar examples. Even if verbal remembrance were also remembrance in a literal sense, it would be among the levels of remembrance, as it is limited to and confined within it. In short, remembrance has levels, as the Exalted said: أَلَا بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ "Look! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance!" (13:28). And He said: وَاذْكُر رَّبَّكَ فِي نَفْسِكَ تَضَرُّعًا وَخِيفَةً وَدُونَ الْجَهْرِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ "And remember your Lord within your self, in humility and fear, and without raising your voice" (7:205). And the Exalted said: فَاذْكُرُوا اللَّهَ كَذِكْرِكُمْ آبَاءَكُمْ أَوْ أَشَدَّ ذِكْرًا "celebrate Allah’s remembrance as you remember your fathers, or with a more frequent remembrance" (2:200). Intensity (الشدة) is an attribute of the meaning (المعنى), not the word. And the Exalted said: وَاذْكُر رَّبَّكَ إِذَا نَسِيتَ وَقُلْ عَسَىٰ أَن يَهْدِيَنِ رَبِّي لِأَقْرَبَ مِنْ هَٰذَا رَشَدًا "And remember your Lord when you forget, and say, ‘Maybe my Lord will guide me to [something] closer to rectitude than this.’" (18:24). The conclusion of this verse indicates a command to hope for that which is of a higher station than what one is currently in. Thus, the meaning resolves to: if you descend from a level of His remembrance to a level below it—which is forgetfulness—then remember your Lord and hope thereby for that which is a closer path and a higher station. The result is that heart-centered remembrance (الذكر القلبي) possesses levels within itself. Through this, the validity of the statement that "remembrance is the presence (حضور) of the meaning before the soul" becomes clear, for presence is something that possesses levels.

If His saying, the Exalted, "So remember Me" (فاذكروني)—which is a verb literally relating to the first-person pronoun—were taken without metaphor, it would imply that man possesses another mode (سنخ) of knowledge different from this conventional knowledge we possess, which is the acquisition of the image and concept of the known by the knower. For whatever is conceived of this nature constitutes a limitation and a description (توصيف) of the known by the knower, yet His sanctuary, the Glorified, is transcendent beyond the descriptions of the describers. The Exalted said: سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ إِلَّا عِبَادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ "Clear is Allah of what they allege, [all] except Allah’s chosen servants." (37:159-160). And He said: وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِهِ عِلْمًا "while they do not comprehend Him in [their] knowledge." (20:110). Some matters related to this station will be discussed in the commentary on these two verses, Allah willing.

(Narrative Discussion)

Narrations regarding the merit of remembrance (الذكر) are numerous through both Sunni and Shi'i chains. It has been narrated through various paths that the remembrance of Allah is good in every state.

In Iddat al-Da'i, it is narrated that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم came out to his companions and said: ارتعوا في رياض الجنة — "Graze in the gardens of Paradise." They asked: "O Messenger of Allah, what are the gardens of Paradise?" He replied: مجالس الذكر اغدوا وروحوا واذكروا ، ومن كان يحب أن يعلم منزلته عند الله فلينظر كيف منزلة الله عنده ، فإن الله تعالى ينزل العبد حيث أنزل العبد الله من نفسه ، واعلموا : أن خير أعمالكم عند مليككم وأزكاها وأرفعها في درجاتكم ، وخير ما طلعت عليه الشمس ذكر الله تعالى ، فانه تعالى أخبر عن نفسه فقال : أنا جليس من ذكرني ، وقال تعالى : فاذكروني أذكركم بنعمتي ، اذكروني بالطاعة والعبادة أذكركم بالنِعَم والإحسان والراحة والرضوان — "The circles of remembrance. Go forth in the morning and evening and remember [Allah]. Whoever wishes to know his status with Allah, let him look at Allah's status with him; for Allah, the Exalted, places the servant where the servant has placed Allah within himself. Know that the best of your deeds in the sight of your Sovereign, the purest of them, and the highest in your ranks—and the best thing upon which the sun has risen—is the remembrance of Allah, the Exalted. For He, the Exalted, has informed about Himself, saying: 'I am the companion of him who remembers Me.' And the Exalted said: 'So remember Me, I will remember you with My blessing; remember Me with obedience and worship, I will remember you with favors, excellence, comfort, and pleasure.'"

In al-Mahasin and the Da'awat of al-Rawandi, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: ان الله تبارك وتعالى يقول : من شغل بذكري عن مسئلتي ، اعطيه افضل ما اعطي من سئلني — "Indeed, Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, says: 'Whoever is so occupied with My remembrance that he forgets to ask of Me, I shall give him the best of what I give to those who ask of Me.'"

In al-Ma'ani, from Husayn al-Bazzaz, he said: "Abu Abdillah عليه السلام said to me: 'Shall I not inform you of the most difficult things Allah has obligated upon His creation?' I said: 'Yes.' He said: إنصاف الناس من نفسك ، ومواساتك لأخيك ، وذكر الله في كل موطن ، أما إني لا اقول : سبحان الله والحمد لله ولا إله إلا الله والله اكبر ، وان كان هذا من ذاك ، ولكن ذكر الله في كل موطن ، إذا هجمت على طاعته أو معصيته — 'Treating people with justice from yourself, assisting your brother, and the remembrance of Allah in every situation. Indeed, I do not mean [only] saying: "Glory be to Allah, praise be to Allah, there is no god but Allah, and Allah is Great," although that is part of it; rather, it is the remembrance of Allah in every situation, whether you are confronted with an act of obedience to Him or a disobedience to Him.'"

I say: This meaning is narrated through many paths from the Prophet and the People of his House (أهل بيته) عليهم السلام. In some of them, it is linked to the saying of Allah: الذين اذا مسهم طائف من الشيطان تذكروا فاذا هم مبصرون — "When those who are God-conscious are touched by a visitation from Satan, they remember [Allah] and then they see clearly." (7:201).

In Iddat al-Da'i, from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, he said: "The Glorified said: إذا علمت أن الغالب على عبدي الاشتغال بي ، نقلت شهوته في مسئلتي ومناجاتي ، فإذا كان عبدي كذلك وأراد ان يسهو حلت بينه وبين أن يسهو ، أولئك أوليائي حقاً ، أولئال أبطال حقاً ، أولئك الذين إذا اردت أن أهلك أهل الأرض عقوبة زويتها عنهم من أجل أولئك الابطال — 'When I know that the dominant state of My servant is preoccupation with Me, I turn his desire toward asking of Me and conversing with Me. When My servant is thus, and he intends to be heedless, I intervene between him and his heedlessness. Those are My true friends; those are the true heroes. Those are the ones for whose sake I avert punishment from the people of the earth when I intend to destroy them.'"

In al-Mahasin, from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, he said: "Allah, the Exalted, said: ابن آدم اذكرني في نفسك اذكرك في نفسي ، ابن آدم اذكرني في خلاء أذكرك في خلاء ، اذكرني في ملاء أذكرك في ملاء خير من ملاءك — 'O son of Adam, remember Me within yourself, I will remember you within Myself. O son of Adam, remember Me in private, I will remember you in private. Remember Me in a gathering, I will remember you in a gathering better than yours.'" And he said: ما من عبد يذكر الله في ملاء من الناس الا ذكره الله في ملاء من الملائكة — "There is no servant who remembers Allah in a gathering of people except that Allah remembers him in a gathering of angels."

I say: This meaning has been narrated through many paths in the books of both groups (Sunni and Shi'i).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Tabarani, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu'ab al-Iman narrated from Ibn Mas'ud that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم said: من أعطى أربعاً أعطي أربعاً ، وتفسير ذلك في في كتاب الله من أُعطي الذكر ذكره الله ، لأن الله يقول : اذكروني اذكركم ، ومن اعطى الدعاء اعطي الإجابة ، لان الله يقول : ادعوني استجب لكم ، ومن اعطي الشكر أُعطي الزيادة ، لان الله يقول : لئن شكرتم لأزيدنكم ، ومن اعطي الاستغفار اعطي المغفرة لان الله يقول : استغفروا ربكم إنه كان غفَّارا — "Whoever is given four things is given four [others], and the explanation of that is in the Book of Allah: whoever is given remembrance, Allah remembers him, because Allah says: 'Remember Me, I will remember you'; whoever is given supplication is given an answer, because Allah says: 'Call upon Me, I will answer you'; whoever is given gratitude is given increase, because Allah says: 'If you are grateful, I will surely increase you'; and whoever is given seeking forgiveness is given forgiveness, because Allah says: 'Plead with your Lord for forgiveness; indeed He is All-forgiving.'"

Also in al-Durr al-Manthur, Sa'id ibn Mansur, Ibn al-Mundhir, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu'ab al-Iman narrated from Khalid ibn Abi Imran that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم said: من أطاع الله فقد ذكر الله ، وان قلت صلوته وصيامه وتلاوته للقرآن ، ومن عصى الله فقد نسي الله ، وإن كثرت صلوته وصيامه وتلاوته للقرآن — "Whoever obeys Allah has remembered Allah, even if his prayers, fasting, and recitation of the Quran are few. And whoever disobeys Allah has forgotten Allah, even if his prayers, fasting, and recitation of the Quran are many."

I say: In the hadith is an indication that disobedience (المعصية) is not realized by the servant except through heedlessness (الغفلة) and forgetfulness (النسيان). For if a human being remembered the reality of his disobedience and its consequences, he would not commit it. Even the one who disobeys Allah and is indifferent—when he is reminded of Allah at that moment and yet pays no heed to the station of his Lord—is a rebel who is ignorant of the station of his Lord, the loftiness of His majesty, and the nature of His encompassment. Another narration points to this as well, narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Abi Hind al-Dari, from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم: "Allah said: اذكرونی بطاعتي أذكركم بمغفرتي ومن ذكرني ـ وهو مطيع ـ فحق علي أن اذكره بمغفرتي ، ومن ذكرني ـ وهو عاص ـ فحق علي أن أذكره بمقت — 'Remember Me through My obedience, I will remember you with My forgiveness. Whoever remembers Me while being obedient, it is incumbent upon Me to remember him with My forgiveness. And whoever remembers Me while being disobedient, it is incumbent upon Me to remember him with My wrath.'" The "remembrance" mentioned in this hadith during an act of disobedience is what the verse and other narrations call "forgetfulness," because the effects of remembrance do not follow from it. There are remaining points to this discussion, a portion of which will follow.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱسْتَعِينُوا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ وَٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (١٥٣)
O you who have faith! Take recourse in patience and prayer; indeed Allah is with the patient. (153)
وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ لِمَن يُقْتَلُ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَمْوَٰتٌۢ ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَآءٌ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تَشْعُرُونَ (١٥٤)
Do not call those who were slain in Allah’s way ‘dead.’ No, they are living, but you are not aware. (154)
وَلَنَبْلُوَنَّكُم بِشَىْءٍ مِّنَ ٱلْخَوْفِ وَٱلْجُوعِ وَنَقْصٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْوَٰلِ وَٱلْأَنفُسِ وَٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (١٥٥)
We will surely test you with a measure of fear and hunger and a loss of wealth, lives, and fruits; and give good news to the patient (155)
ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَآ أَصَـٰبَتْهُم مُّصِيبَةٌ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَإِنَّآ إِلَيْهِ رَٰجِعُونَ (١٥٦)
—those who, when an affliction visits them, say, ‘Indeed we belong to Allah and to Him do we indeed return.’ (156)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ صَلَوَٰتٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَرَحْمَةٌ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُهْتَدُونَ (١٥٧)
It is they who receive the blessings of their Lord and [His] mercy, and it is they who are the [rightly] guided. (157)

(Commentary)

These are five verses united in context (السياق), harmonious in their phrasing, and cohesive in their meanings. The first leads to the last, and the last refers back to the first. This reveals that they were revealed all at once, not separately. Their context proclaims that they were revealed shortly before the command to fight and the legislation of the ruling of struggle (الجهاد). They contain a mention of a trial (بلاء) that would soon face the believers and a calamity (مصيبة) that would strike them—and not just any trial or calamity, but a general trial that is not of ordinary occurrence but rather continuous in its happening. Indeed, the human species, like all other species existing in this natural plane of existence (النشأة الطبيعية), is not free in its individuals from particular events (الحادثة الجزئية) that disrupt the order of the individual in his personal life: such as death, illness, fear, hunger, grief, and deprivation. This is the sunnah (السنة) — the established way and law — of Allah that has proceeded among His servants and His creation. For this abode is the abode of mutual interference (التزاحم), and this plane of existence is the plane of change and transformation; and you will never find any change in the sunnah of Allah, nor will you ever find any alteration in the sunnah of Allah.

An individual trial, though difficult for the person afflicted by it and disliked, is not as terrifying or awe-inspiring as those general trials and tribulations. An individual draws strength for his intellect, resolve, and the steadfastness of his soul from the strengths of other individuals. As for general and all-encompassing trials, they strip away the collective consciousness (الشعور العمومي) and the totality of opinion, prudence, and management from the social body. The system of life is disrupted for them; fear is multiplied, alienation (الوحشة) accumulates, and the intellect and consciousness become agitated, while resolve and steadfastness fail. Thus, the general trial and the all-encompassing tribulation are more difficult and bitter, and it is this which the verses allude to.

Nor is it just any general trial like a plague or famine, but rather a general trial to which they brought themselves near. For they had embraced the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and answered the call of truth (دعوة الحق), while the world, and specifically their own people, opposed them in it. These opponents had no aim but to extinguish the light of Allah, uproot the word of justice, and nullify the call of truth. There was no means to sever the root of the dispute and end the disagreement other than fighting (القتال). All other means, such as establishing proof, spreading discord, or casting whispers and doubt, had become sterile and unproductive. The proof was with the Prophet, and the whispering, discord, and intrigue did not produce an effect that would satisfy the enemies of the religion. Thus, they had no means left but fighting and using it to block the path of truth and extinguish the brilliant, shining light of the religion. This was from the side of disbelief (الكفر); and the matter from the side of the religion was even clearer: there was no way to spread the word of monotheism, disseminate the true religion and the rule of justice, and uproot falsehood (الباطل) except through fighting. Indeed, the experiences extending from the time man descended into this abode demonstrate that truth only takes effect when falsehood is removed, and it will never be removed except by some exercise of power and force.

In short, there is an allusion in the verses to the approach of this tribulation by mentioning killing in the way of Allah, describing it with a description that leaves no disliked aspect or evil quality within it. It is that it is not death, but life—and what a life!

Thus, the verses rouse the believers to fight and inform them that before them is a trial and tribulation; they shall not attain the stations of excellence, the blessings of their Lord and His mercy, and guidance through His leading (الاهتداء بهدايته) except by being patient (الصبر) through it and enduring its hardships. He teaches them what to seek help from in facing it, which is patience and prayer (الصلوة). As for patience: it alone is the protection against anxiety (الجزع) and the disruption of management. As for prayer: it is a turning toward the Lord and a total devotion (الانقطاع) to Him in Whose hand is the command, and the realization that all power belongs to Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ وَالصَّلَاةِ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ مَعَ الصَّابِرِينَ — "O you who have faith! Take recourse in patience and prayer; indeed Allah is with the patient" (2:153). A summary of the discussion on patience and prayer has already preceded in the commentary (تفسير) on His saying: وَاسْتَعِينُوا بِالصَّبْرِ وَالصَّلَاةِ ۚ وَإِنَّهَا لَكَبِيرَةٌ إِلَّا عَلَى الْخَاشِعِينَ — "And take recourse in patience and prayer, and it is indeed hard except for the humble" (2:45). Patience (الصبر) is among the greatest of traits (الملكات) and states that the Quran praises, and it repeats the command for it until it reaches nearly seventy places in the Quran, such that it is said regarding it: إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ — "that is indeed the steadiest of courses" (31:17). And it is said: وَمَا يُلَقَّاهَا إِلَّا الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا وَمَا يُلَقَّاهَا إِلَّا ذُو حَظٍّ عَظِيمٍ — "But none is granted it except those who are patient, and none is granted it except the greatly endowed" (41:35). And it is said: إِنَّمَا يُوَفَّى الصَّابِرُونَ أَجْرَهُم بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ — "Indeed the patient will be paid their reward in full without any reckoning" (39:10).

Prayer (الصلوة) is among the greatest acts of worship (العبادات) urged in the Quran, such that it is said regarding it: إِنَّ الصَّلَاةَ تَنْهَىٰ عَنِ الْفَحْشَاءِ وَالْمُنكَرِ — "Indeed prayer prevents indecencies and wrongs" (29:45). Allah has not given any exhortations in His Book except that prayer was their head and their beginning.

Then He, the Glorified, described patience by saying that Allah is with the patient (الصابرين) who are characterized by patience. He did not describe prayer in this way, as He did in His saying: "And take recourse in patience and prayer, and it is indeed hard..." because the context in these verses is the context of facing terrors and clashing with champions. Thus, the emphasis on the matter of patience is more appropriate, unlike the previous verse. Therefore, it was said: "Indeed Allah is with the patient." This "withness" (المعية) is different from the withness indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ مَعَكُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ — "and He is with you wherever you may be" (57:4). For that is the withness of all-encompassing presence (الإحاطة) and sustaining guardianship (القيمومة), unlike the withness with the patient, which is a withness of assistance (إعانة); thus, patience is the key to relief.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَقُولُوا لِمَن يُقْتَلُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَمْوَاتٌ ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَاءٌ وَلَٰكِن لَّا تَشْعُرُونَ — "Do not call those who were slain in Allah’s way ‘dead.’ No, they are living, but you are not aware" (2:154). It might be said: The address is to the believers who have believed in Allah, His Messenger, and the Last Day, and have submitted to the life of the hereafter (الحيوة الاخرة). It is inconceivable for them to say that a human being is nullified by death after they have answered the call of truth and heard many verses speaking of the Resurrection (المعاد). Furthermore, the verse only establishes life after death for a specific group, namely the martyrs (الشهداء) killed in the way of Allah, in contrast to other believers and all the disbelievers, even though the ruling of life after death is general and encompasses everyone. Therefore, what is meant by "life" is the survival of the name and beautiful remembrance throughout the ages. A group of commentators (المفسرين) has interpreted it in this way.

This is refuted firstly: by the fact that this being "life" exists only in the imagination (الوهم) and not in external reality (الخارج). It would be an imaginary life that has nothing in reality but the name. Such an illusory subject is not worthy of His speech, for He, the Exalted, calls to the truth and says: فَمَاذَا بَعْدَ الْحَقِّ إِلَّا الضَّلَالُ — "So what is there after the truth except error?" (10:32). As for what Ibrahim asked for in his saying: وَاجْعَل لِّي لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ فِي الْآخِرِينَ — "and confer on me a worthy repute among the posterity" (26:84), he only intended by it the survival of his true call and his truthful tongue after him, not merely good praise and beautiful remembrance.

Indeed, this false saying and deceptive illusion are only suitable for the condition of materialists (الماديين) and naturalists. They believed in the materiality of souls and their nullification upon death, and they denied the life of the hereafter. Then they sensed the human being's innate need, by primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — to speak of the survival of souls and their being affected by happiness and misery after death in noble matters, which cannot be attained without sacrifice and self-offering. This is especially true in great resolves where some people die and are killed so that others may live. If everyone who died was simply gone (فات), there would be no motive for a human being (especially if he believes in death as total loss) to nullify his own self so that the selves of others might remain. Nor would there be any incentive for him to deprive himself of the pleasure of enjoying all he is capable of through injustice so that others may enjoy justice. A rational person does not give something unless he takes a substitute for it. As for giving without a substitute, and leaving without taking—such as dying for the sake of the life of another, or deprivation for the sake of another's enjoyment—the human primordial nature (الفطرة الإنسانية) rejects it. When they sensed this, the need to repair this deficiency led them to invent these false illusions, which have no place except in the arena of the imagination and the enclosure of fancy. They said: A human being free from the bondage of illusions and superstitions must sacrifice his life for his homeland, or for whatever constitutes his honor, to attain eternal life through good mention and beautiful praise; and he must deprive himself of some of his enjoyments in society so that others may attain them, so that the social order and civilization may be established and social justice completed, thereby attaining a life of honor and loftiness.

I wonder, if there is no human being, and this material composition is nullified, and thereby all its properties—including life and consciousness—are nullified, then who is it that attains this life and this honor? And who is it that perceives it and takes pleasure in it? Is this anything but a superstition?

Secondly: The end of the verse—which is His saying, the Exalted: "but you are not aware"—does not suit this meaning. Rather, it would have been appropriate to say: "No, they are living through the survival of their beautiful remembrance and the praise of people for them after them," because that would be suitable for the station of consolation and heart-soothing.

Thirdly: The counterpart to this verse—which explains it—describes their life after being killed in a way that contradicts this meaning. The Exalted said: وَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَمْوَاتًا ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَاءٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ — "Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah to be dead; no, they are living and provided for near their Lord" (3:169), until the end of the verses. It is well known that this life is an external, real life, not a figurative one.

Fourthly: Ignorance of this life after death was not entirely far-fetched for some Muslims in the middle of the era of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). That which is a definitive text (نص) incapable of interpretation (التأويل) is only the Resurrection for the Day of Judgment. As for what is between death and the Gathering—which is the intermediate life (الحيوة البرزخية) — the state between death and resurrection — although it is among the true gnoses (المعارف) explained by the Quran, it is not among the necessities (ضروريات) of the Quran. The Muslims are not in consensus (مجمعين) upon it; rather, some deny it even today among those who believe that the soul (النفس) is not immaterial (مجردة) from matter and that the human being's existence is nullified by death and the dissolution of the composition, and then Allah resurrects him for judgment on the Day of Resurrection. Thus, it is possible that what is intended is the explanation of the life of the martyrs in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) due to the ignorance of some believers regarding that, even if others knew it.

In short: What is meant by "life" in the verse is real life, not figurative life. Allah, the Glorified, has counted the life of the disbeliever after his death as destruction and ruin in several places in His speech, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَأَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دَارَ الْبَوارِ — "and settled their people in the abode of ruin" (14:28), among other verses. Thus, life is the life of happiness, and those living with this life are the believers specifically, as He said: وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوَانُ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ — "and the abode of the Hereafter is indeed the [true] Life, had they known" (29:64). They did not know because their senses are restricted to perceiving the properties of life in worldly matter. As for what is beyond it, if they do not perceive it, they do not distinguish between it and non-existence, so they imagine it to be non-existence. What the imagination (الوهم) conceives is common to both the believer and the disbeliever in this world. Therefore, He said in this verse: "No, they are living, but you are not aware"—meaning, with your senses—just as He said in the other verse: "is indeed the [true] Life, had they known"—meaning, with certainty (اليقين), as the Exalted said: كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ — "No indeed! Were you to know with certain knowledge, you would surely see hell" (102:5-6).

The meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: Do not say of those who are killed in the way of Allah that they are dead, and do not believe in their non-existence and nullification as the word "death" implies to you and its opposition to life, and as your senses suggest to you. They are not dead in the sense of nullification; rather, they are living, but your senses do not attain that nor perceive it. Addressing the believers with this—even though all or most of them knew of the survival of the human life after death and the non-nullification of his essence (الذات)—is only to awaken them and alert them to what is known to them, so that by paying attention to it, the distress may be lifted from their chests and the agitation and anxiety from their hearts when the calamity of being killed strikes them. For with this realization, nothing remains of the effects of being killed for the guardians of the slain except a separation for a few days in this world, which is trivial in comparison to the pleasure of Allah, the Glorified, and what the slain has attained of the good life, the permanent blessing, and "pleasure from Allah which is greater." This is similar to addressing the Prophet with such as His saying, the Exalted: الْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُمْتَرِينَ — "The truth is from your Lord; so do not be among the doubters" (2:147), even though he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was the first of those certain of the signs of his Lord. However, it is a speech used as an allusion (كنِّي به) to the clarity of the matter and its manifestation such that it does not accept any mental passing thought to the contrary.

The Plane of the Intermediate Realm (نشأة البرزخ)

The verse indicates with clear indication the intermediate life (الحيوة البرزخية) of the human being, as does its counterpart verse: وَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَمْوَاتًا ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَاءٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ — "Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah to be dead; no, they are living and provided for near their Lord" (3:169). The verses regarding this are many.

Among the most strange things is what some people have mentioned regarding the verse: that it was revealed concerning the martyrs of Badr, so it is specific to them only and does not extend to others. Indeed, one of the investigative commentators (المحققين) did well in the commentary on His saying: "And take recourse in patience and prayer," when he asked Allah for patience to endure such statements.

I wonder what these people intend by this statement of theirs? In what manner do they conceive the life of the martyrs of Badr after their being killed, given their claim of the non-existence of the human being after death and killing, and the dissolution and nullification of his composition? Is it by way of a miracle (الإعجاز): by Allah singling them out with a nobility (كرامة) with which He did not honor the Noble Prophet and the rest of the prophets, messengers, and near-stationed saints (الاولياء المقربين), in that Allah specifically maintained their existence after non-existence? This would not be a miracle but the bringing into existence of an impossibility (محال) that is necessarily impossible, and there is no miracle in the impossible. If the intellect allowed the nullification of this ruling despite its self-evidence, no necessary ruling would remain firm, let alone anything below it. Or is it by way of an exception in the ruling of the senses (الحس), such that the senses are mistaken regarding the matter of these martyrs? Thus, they are living, provided for with food, drink, and other enjoyments—while being hidden from the senses—and what the senses attained of their matter through killing, severing of limbs, loss of sensation, and dissolution of composition was entirely mistaken. If such errors were permissible for the senses—being correct in one thing and mistaken in another without a specificator (مخصص)—trust in them would be absolutely nullified. If the specificator were the Divine Will, its attachment would require another specificator. The problem—which is the lack of trust in perception—remains as it is; for it would be possible to find what is not real as real, and the real as not real. How could a rational person be satisfied to utter such a thing? Is it anything but sophistry (سفسطة)?

In this statement of theirs, they have followed the path of the common traditionists (المحدثين), who hold that things hidden from our senses which are indicated by the religious appearances (الظواهر) of the Book and the Sunnah—such as angels, the souls of the believers, and other such things—are material, natural existents and subtle bodies (أجسام لطيفة) that can dwell in and penetrate dense bodies, in the form of a human and the like, performing all human actions, for example, and having powers like ours, except that they are not governed by the laws of nature: such as change, transformation, composition, and its dissolution, and natural life and death. If Allah wills their appearance, they appear to our senses, and if He does not will it, or wills that they do not appear, they do not appear—a pure will without a specificator in the side of the senses or those things.

This statement of theirs is based on the denial of causality (العليَّة والمعلولية) between things. If this false wish were true, all intellectual truths and scientific rulings would be nullified, let alone religious gnoses. The turn would not even reach their subtle, honored bodies which the hand of material, natural influence and being influenced does not reach—and this is obvious.

It has become clear from what has passed: that the verse indicates the intermediate life (الحيوة البرزخيَّة), which is called the world of the grave (عالم القبر), an intermediate world between death and the Resurrection, in which the deceased is blessed or punished until the Resurrection occurs.

Among the verses indicating it—which is a counterpart to this noble verse—is His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَمْوَاتًا ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَاءٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ . فَرِحِينَ بِمَا آتَاهُمُ اللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِ وَيَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِالَّذِينَ لَمْ يَلْحَقُوا بِهِم مِّنْ خَلْفِهِمْ أَلَّا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ . يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِنِعْمَةٍ مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَفَضْلٍ وَأَنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "Do not suppose those who were slain in the way of Allah to be dead; no, they are living and provided for near their Lord, exulting in what Allah has given them out of His grace, and rejoicing for those who have not yet joined them from [those left] behind them, that they shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve. They rejoice in Allah’s blessing and grace, and that Allah does not waste the reward of the faithful" (3:169-171). The demonstration of the verse's indication of the objective has already passed. If the one who claims these verses are specific to the martyrs of Badr were to contemplate the text of the verses, he would find that their context implies the participation of other believers with them in life and blessing after death.

Among the verses is His saying, the Exalted: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا جَاءَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قَالَ رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ . لَعَلِّي أَعْمَلُ صَالِحًا فِيمَا تَرَكْتُ ۚ كَلَّا ۚ إِنَّهَا كَلِمَةٌ هُوَ قَائِلُهَا ۖ وَمِن وَرَائِهِم بَرْزَخٌ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ — "When death comes to one of them, he says, ‘My Lord! Take me back, that I may act righteously in what I have left behind.’ ‘By no means! These are mere words that he says.’ And before them is a barrier until the day they will be resurrected" (23:99-100). The verse is clear in its indication that there is an intermediate life between their worldly life and their life after the Resurrection. The full discussion on the verse will come, Allah willing.

Among the verses is His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَاءَنَا لَوْلَا أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْمَلَائِكَةُ أَوْ نَرَىٰ رَبَّنَا ۗ لَقَدِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فِي أَنفُسِهِمْ وَعَتَوْا عُتُوًّا كَبِيرًا . يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ الْمَلَائِكَةَ (and it is known that what is meant is the first time they see them, which is the day of death, as other verses indicate) لَا بُشْرَىٰ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُجْرِمِينَ وَيَقُولُونَ حِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا . وَقَدِمْنَا إِلَىٰ مَا عَمِلُوا مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْنَاهُ هَبَاءً مَّنثُورًا . أَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَيْرٌ مُّسْتَقَرًّا وَأَحْسَنُ مَقِيلًا . وَيَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ السَّمَاءُ بِالْغَمَامِ (and that is the Day of Resurrection) وَنُزِّلَ الْمَلَائِكَةُ تَنزِيلًا . الْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُّ لِلرَّحْمَٰنِ ۚ وَكَانَ يَوْمًا عَلَى الْكَافِرِينَ عَسِيرًا — "Those who do not expect to encounter Us say, ‘Why are the angels not sent down to us, or why do we not see our Lord?’ Certainly they are too proud of themselves and have been greatly rebellious. The day they see the angels, there will be no good news for the guilty on that day, and they will say, ‘Keep off!’ We shall set out for the deeds they have done and make them like scattered dust. On that day the inhabitants of paradise will have a better abode and a better resting place. The day when the sky is rent asunder with clouds and the angels are sent down in a grand descent. On that day the true sovereignty will belong to the All-beneficent, and it will be a hard day for the faithless" (25:21-26). Its indication is clear, and the detailed discussion on it will come in its place, Allah willing.

Among the verses is His saying, the Exalted: قَالُوا رَبَّنَا أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ فَاعْتَرَفْنَا بِذُنُوبِنَا فَهَلْ إِلَىٰ خُرُوجٍ مِّن سَبِيلٍ — "They will say, ‘Our Lord! Twice didst Thou cause us to die, and twice didst Thou give us life, then we confessed our sins. Is there any way out?’" (40:11). Here, until the Day of Resurrection—which is the day of this statement of theirs—there are two causings of death and two givings of life. The meaning will not be sound except by establishing the intermediate realm (البرزخ), such that there is a causing of death and a giving of life in the intermediate realm, and a giving of life on the Day of Resurrection. If one of the two givings of life were in this world and the other in the hereafter, there would be only one causing of death without a second. Discussion related to this station has already passed regarding His saying, the Exalted: كَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَكُنتُمْ أَمْوَاتًا فَأَحْيَاكُمْ — "How can you deny Allah? You were lifeless and He gave you life" (2:28), so refer back to it.

Among the verses is His saying, the Exalted: وَحَاقَ بِآلِ فِرْعَوْنَ سُوءُ الْعَذَابِ . النَّارُ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْهَا غُدُوًّا وَعَشِيًّا ۖ وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ أَدْخِلُوا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَشَدَّ الْعَذَابِ — "and a terrible punishment besieged Pharaoh’s clan: the Fire, to which they are exposed morning and evening; and on the day when the Hour sets in [it will be said], ‘Admit Pharaoh’s clan into the severest punishment!’" (40:45-46). For it is known that on the Day of Resurrection there is no morning (بكرة) nor evening (عشى); it is a day unlike the [worldly] day.

The verses from which this Quranic reality is benefited, or to which they point, are many, such as His saying, the Exalted: تَاللَّهِ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا إِلَىٰ أُمَمٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ أَعْمَالَهُمْ فَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُمُ الْيَوْمَ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ — "By Allah, We certainly sent [apostles] to nations before you, but Satan made their deeds seem goodly to them. So he is their patron today, and there is a painful punishment for them" (16:63), among others.

(The Immateriality of the Soul)

Through contemplation (التدبر) on the verse and the other verses we have mentioned, another broader reality becomes clear: the immateriality of the soul (تجرد النفس) — its being a reality beyond the body, whose ruling is different from the ruling of the body and other physical compositions. It has a mode of union with the body, managing it through consciousness, will, and other perceptive attributes. Contemplation on the previously mentioned verses clarifies this meaning, for they imply that the human being in his person (بشخصه) is not the body; he does not die with the death of the body, nor does he perish with its perishing, the dissolution of its composition, and the scattering of its parts. He remains after the perishing of the body in a pleasant, eternal life and permanent blessing, or in necessary misery and painful punishment. His happiness in this life and his misery in it are linked to the nature of his traits (ملكاته) and his deeds, not to physical aspects and social rulings.

These are meanings provided by these noble verses, and it is clear that these are rulings that differ from physical rulings and are incompatible with material, worldly properties from all aspects. Thus, the human soul is other than the body.

Among the verses indicating this is His saying, the Exalted: اللَّهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِهَا وَالَّتِي لَمْ تَمُتْ فِي مَنَامِهَا ۖ فَيُمْسِكُ الَّتِي قَضَىٰ عَلَيْهَا الْمَوْتَ وَيُرْسِلُ الْأُخْرَىٰ — "Allah takes the souls at the time of their death, and those who have not died, in their sleep. Then He retains those for whom He has ordained death and releases the others" (39:42). Taking (التوفي) and full retrieval (الإستيفاء) is the taking of a right in its entirety and completeness. What the verse contains of taking, retaining, and releasing is manifest in the distinction between the soul and the body.

Among the verses is His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالُوا أَإِذَا ضَلَلْنَا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ ۚ بَلْ هُم بِلِقَاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كَافِرُونَ . قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُم مَّلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ — "They say, ‘When we have been lost in the dust, shall we indeed be created anew?’ Indeed, they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord. Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you fully, then you will be brought back to your Lord’" (32:10-11). The Glorified mentioned a doubt from the doubts of the disbelievers who deny the Resurrection, which is: after death and the dissolution of the composition of our bodies, our limbs are separated, our parts scattered, and our forms changed, so we are "lost in the earth" and the senses of perceivers lose us; how then is it possible for us to occur a second time in a new creation? This is mere deeming it far-fetched. Allah taught His Messenger the answer to it by His saying: "Say: The angel of death who has been given charge of you will take you fully..." The summary of the answer is that there is an angel in charge of you who takes you fully and receives you; he does not let you be lost, and you are in his grasp and protection. What is lost in the earth is only your bodies, not your souls, which are indicated by the pronoun "you" (كم), for he takes you fully.

Among the verses is His saying, the Exalted: وَنَفَخَ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِهِ — "and breathed into him of His spirit" (32:9). He mentioned this regarding the creation of the human being, then the Exalted said: يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ ۖ قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي — "They question you concerning the Spirit. Say, ‘The Spirit is of my Lord’s command’" (17:85). Thus, He informed that the spirit (الروح) is of the nature of His command (أمر). Then He defined the command in His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَن يَقُولَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ . فَسُبْحَانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is. So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things" (36:82-83). Thus, He informed that the spirit is from the dominion (الملكوت) and that it is the word "Be" (كن). Then He defined the command by describing it with another description in His saying: وَمَا أَمْرُنَا إِلَّا وَاحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ — "Our command is but a single [word], like the twinkling of an eye" (54:50). The expression "like the twinkling of an eye" indicates that the command, which is the word "Be," is of instantaneous existence (دفعي الوجود), not gradual. It exists without its existence being conditioned or restricted by time or place. From here it becomes clear that the command—and from it the spirit—is something non-physical and non-material. For material, physical existents have as one of their general laws that they are gradual in existence, restricted by time and place. Thus, the spirit that belongs to the human being is not material or physical, even if it has a connection to it.

There are verses that reveal the nature of this connection. The Exalted said: مِنْهَا خَلَقْنَاكُمْ — "From it We created you" (20:55). And the Exalted said: خَلَقَ الْإِنسَانَ مِن صَلْصَالٍ كَالْفَخَّارِ — "He created man from dry clay like in pottery" (55:14). And the Exalted said: وَبَدَأَ خَلْقَ الْإِنسَانِ مِن طِينٍ . ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُ مِن سُلَالَةٍ مِّن مَّاءٍ مَّهِينٍ — "and He began the creation of man from clay. Then He made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid" (32:7-8). Then He, the Glorified and Exalted, said: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ مِن سُلَالَةٍ مِّن طِينٍ . ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَاهُ نُطْفَةً فِي قَرَارٍ مَّكِينٍ . ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا النُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْمُضْغَةَ عِظَامًا فَكَسَوْنَا الْعِظَامَ لَحْمًا ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَاهُ خَلْقًا آخَرَ ۚ فَتَبَارَكَ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخَالِقِينَ — "Certainly We created man from an extract of clay. Then We made him a drop of [seminal] fluid [lodged] in a secure abode. Then We created the drop of fluid as a clinging mass. Then We created the clinging mass as a fleshy tissue. Then We created the fleshy tissue as bones. Then We clothed the bones with flesh. Then We produced him as yet another creature. So blessed is Allah, the best of creators!" (22:12-14). Thus, He informed that the human being was nothing but a natural body upon which different, changing forms occurred. Then Allah produced this, which was a rigid, lifeless body, as "another creature" possessing consciousness and will, performing actions of consciousness, will, thought, disposal in the realms of existence, and management in the affairs of the world through moving, changing, and transforming—among other things that do not proceed from bodies and physical things. Thus, neither is it physical, nor is its subject that performs it physical.

The soul, in relation to the body whose matter ends in its production—which is the body from which the soul originates—is like the fruit from the tree or the light from the oil, in a remote sense. By this, the nature of its connection to the body at its inception becomes clear; then, through death, the connection is severed and the hold is nullified. In its first existence, it is the very same as the body, then it becomes distinct through its production from it, then it becomes entirely independent of it. This is what the mentioned noble verses provide through their apparent meaning (بظهورها). There are many verses that provide this reality through allusion and pointing, which the insightful contemplator will encounter. And Allah is the Guide.
