If the matter regarding the principles is of this sort, then the branches that lead back to them upon analysis are also subject to this acceptance, even if some variation occurs in their instances (مصداق) in terms of application, as we shall point out.

Once you understand what we have mentioned, the reason for the collapse of what we quoted from their claims regarding ethics will become apparent to you. Here is the explanation:

As for their saying: "Absolute beauty and ugliness do not exist; rather, what exists is relative beauty and ugliness, which change and vary according to regions, times, and societies," this is a fallacy arising from the confusion between conceptual absoluteness (الإطلاق المفهومي) in the sense of universality (الكلية) and existential absoluteness (الإطلاق الوجودي) in the sense of continuity of existence. Absolute, universal beauty and ugliness do not exist externally with the attribute of universality and absoluteness, but they are not what necessitate the result we intend. As for absolute, continuous beauty and ugliness in the sense of their continuity as rulings of society as long as society continues with the continuity of nature, they do exist. For the goal of society is the happiness of the species, and it is impossible for all possible and hypothetical actions to conform to society however it is conceived. Thus, there are always actions that conform and actions that conflict; therefore, there is always beauty and ugliness.

Accordingly, how can any society be conceived, however it may be, without its members believing that it is obligatory to give every possessor of a right his right, or that seeking benefits to the appropriate extent is obligatory, or that defending the interests of society to the appropriate extent is necessary, or that the knowledge by which human benefits are distinguished from others is a beautiful virtue? These are justice, chastity, courage, and wisdom, which we mentioned that human society, however conceived, cannot but judge as beautiful and as human virtues. Similarly, how is it possible for a society not to judge the necessity of restraint and being affected by the manifestation of the foul and hideous? This is modesty (الحياء), which is a branch of chastity. Or not to judge the necessity of anger and the change of soul at the violation of sanctities and the erosion of rights? This is zeal (الغيرة), which is a branch of courage. Or not to judge the necessity of restricting oneself to the social rights one possesses? This is contentment (القناعة). Or not to judge the necessity of preserving the self in its social position without trampling on people or belittling them through arrogance and wrongful transgression? This is humility (التواضع). And so it is for every single one of the branches of the virtues.

As for what they claim regarding the variation of views in different societies concerning specific virtues, and a single trait becoming a virtue for one people and a vice for another in particular examples, this is not due to a variation in the view regarding the social ruling—such that one people believes in the obligation of following a beautiful virtue and others do not—but rather due to the variation in the application of the ruling to the instance (المصداق).

For example, the societies that were managed by autocratic governments used to see for the throne of the king complete choice to do as he pleased and rule as he wished. This was not due to their poor opinion of justice, but because of their belief that this was among the rights of sovereignty and kingship. Thus, it was not oppression from the position of sovereignty, but rather the fulfillment of his rightful rights according to their claim.

Another example: knowledge was once considered a disgrace for kings in some societies, as is recounted about the French nation in the Middle Ages. This was not because they belittled the virtue of knowledge, but because of their claim that knowledge of politics and the arts of governing conflicted with sovereign preoccupations.

Another example: the chastity of women in the sense of guarding the private parts from other than the husband, as well as modesty in women, and zeal in their men, as well as a number of virtues like contentment and humility, are traits whose virtue is not acknowledged in some societies. However, this is because their specific society does not consider them instances of chastity, modesty, zeal, contentment, and humility—not because these virtues are not virtues to them. The proof for this is the existence of their principle among them; for they praise the chastity of a ruler in his ruling and a judge in his judgment, they praise modesty in not violating the laws, they praise zeal in defending independence, civilization, and all their sanctities, they praise contentment with the rights the law has assigned to them, and they praise humility toward their leaders and guides in society.

As for their saying that ethics in their beauty revolve around their conformity to the goal of the social objective (المرام الاجتماعي), and their concluding this from the fact that their beauty revolves around conformity to the goal of society, there is a clear fallacy in this. For what is meant by "society" (الاجتماع) is the form resulting from the operation of the sum of laws that nature has established among socialized individuals, which must lead to their happiness were it not for the disruption of its order and flow; it necessarily has rulings of beauty, ugliness, virtue, and vice. What is meant by "objective" (المرام) is the sum of hypotheses set forth to create a society in a new form by imposing them upon socialized individuals. That is, society and the social objective are distinct in terms of actuality and potentiality, and realization and the hypothesis of realization. How then can the ruling of one become identical to the ruling of the other? And how can beauty, ugliness, virtue, and vice—which the general society has determined by the necessity of human nature—be transformed into what is judged by an objective that is nothing but a hypothesis from a hypothesizer?

If it were said: general natural society has no ruling of its own, but the ruling belongs to the objective—especially if it is a hypothesis suited to the happiness of individuals—the previous discussion regarding beauty, ugliness, virtue, and vice would return, and that they ultimately trace back to a continuous necessity from nature.

Moreover, there is another problem here: if beauty, ugliness, and other social rulings—which are what social arguments rely upon and from which deductions are formed—were dependent on the objective, and it is possible, indeed a reality, that different, contradictory, and distinct objectives are realized, this would lead to the disappearance of the common argument accepted by all societies. Progress and success would then belong only to power and coercion. How can it be said that human nature led its individuals to a social life where there is no understanding between its parts and no ruling that unites them except a ruling that nullifies society itself? Is this not a hideous contradiction in the ruling of nature and its existential necessity?

(Another Narrative Discussion)

On Miscellaneous Matters Related to What Preceded

From al-Baqir (peace be upon him), who said: "A man came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'I am desirous and energetic for Jihad.' He said: 'Then perform Jihad in the way of Allah, for if you are killed, you will be alive with Allah, provided for; and if you die, your reward has already fallen upon Allah...'" (the hadith).

His saying (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "And if you die..." etc., is an allusion to the saying of the Exalted: وَمَن يَخْرُجْ مِن بَيْتِهِ مُهَاجِرًا إِلَى اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ ثُمَّ يُدْرِكْهُ الْمَوْتُ فَقَدْ وَقَعَ أَجْرُهُ عَلَى اللَّهِ — "And whoever leaves his home migrating toward Allah and His Apostle, and then death overtakes him, his reward has already fallen on Allah" (An-Nisa: 100). In this, there is an indication that going out for Jihad is a migration (مهاجرة) toward Allah and His Messenger.

In al-Kafi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the Prophet Ishmael, whom Allah, the Glorified, named "Truthful to the Promise" (صادق الوعد): He (peace be upon him) said: "He was only named Truthful to the Promise because he promised a man at a place and waited for him at that place for a year; so Allah (عز وجل) named him Truthful to the Promise. Then the man came to him after that time, and Ishmael said to him: 'I have not ceased waiting for you...'" (the hadith).

I say: This is a matter that the ordinary intellect (العقل العادي) might judge as deviating from the path of moderation, yet Allah, the Glorified, made it a merit for him (peace be upon him), such that He magnified his status and raised his mention by His saying: وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتَابِ إِسْمَاعِيلَ إِنَّهُ كَانَ صَادِقَ الْوَعْدِ وَكَانَ رَسُولًا نَّبِيًّا وَكَانَ يَأْمُرُ أَهْلَهُ بِالصَّلَاةِ وَالزَّكَاةِ وَكَانَ عِندَ رَبِّهِ مَرْضِيًّا — "And mention in the Book Ishmael. Indeed he was true to his promise, and he was an apostle, a prophet. He used to bid his family to prayer and to maintain the alms-tax, and he was pleasing to his Lord" (Maryam: 54-55). This is only because the scale (الميزان) by which this action was weighed is different from the scale in the hand of the ordinary intellect. For the ordinary intellect has a training through its own management, while Allah, the Glorified, has a training for His friends (أولياء) through His support, and the word of Allah is the highest. Instances of this case are many, narrated and transmitted from the Prophet, the Imams, and the friends of Allah.

If you say: How can the Law (الشرع) differ from the intellect (العقل) in matters where the intellect has a path?

I say: As for the ruling of the intellect in what it has a path toward, it is in its proper place. However, it requires a subject (موضوع) upon which its ruling falls. You have learned in what preceded that such sciences in the third path we mentioned do not leave for the intellect a subject to judge in or upon. This is the path of divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية). It appears that the Prophet Ishmael (peace be upon him) had made an absolute statement in his promise by saying: "I will wait for you here until you return to me," then he committed himself to the absoluteness of his word to protect his soul from breaking the covenant and lying in the promise, and to preserve what Allah had cast into his heart and made flow upon his tongue. A similar thing has been narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he was at the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام) and one of his companions promised to return to him, and the Prophet promised to wait for him until he returned. The man went about his business and did not return, so the Prophet waited for him for three days in the place he had promised until the man passed by him after the three days while he was sitting waiting, the man having forgotten the promise (the hadith).

In al-Khasa'is by al-Sayyid al-Radi, from Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), who said—having heard a man saying: "Indeed we belong to Allah and to Him we shall return"—: "O you! Our saying 'Indeed we belong to Allah' is an acknowledgment from us of His ownership (الملك), and 'Indeed to Him we shall return' is an acknowledgment from us of our perishing (الهلاك)."

I say: Its meaning has become clear through what preceded, and it was narrated in al-Kafi in detail.

In al-Kafi: from Ishaq ibn ‘Ammar and ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Allah (عز وجل) said: I have made the world a loan (قرضاً) among My servants; so whoever gives Me a loan in it, I give him for every one ten to seven hundred fold. And whoever does not give Me a loan, and I take something from him by force (قسراً), I give him three traits which, if I gave one of them to My angels, they would be pleased with Me for it.'" Then Abu ‘Abdullah said: "The saying of Allah: الَّذِينَ إِذَا أَصَابَتْهُم مُّصِيبَةٌ قَالُوا إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَإِنَّا إِلَيْهِ رَاجِعُونَ ، أُولَئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ صَلَوَاتٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ — 'those who, when an affliction visits them, say, "Indeed we belong to Allah and to Him do we indeed return." It is they who receive the blessings from their Lord' (Al-Baqarah: 156-157). This is one of the three traits. وَرَحْمَةٌ — 'and [His] mercy' is the second. وَأُولَئِكَ هُمُ الْمُهْتَدُونَ — 'and it is they who are the [rightly] guided' is the third." Then Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him) said: "This is for the one from whom Allah takes something by force."

I say: The narration is reported through other similar chains.

In al-Ma'ani, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Prayer (الصلوة) from Allah is mercy (رحمة), from the angels it is purification (التزكية), and from people it is supplication (دعاء)."

I say: There are several other narrations with this meaning. There is an apparent conflict between this narration and the one before it, as the previous narration counts "blessings" (الصلوات) as distinct from "mercy" (الرحمة), which is supported by the literal wording of His saying: عَلَيْهِمْ صَلَوَاتٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَرَحْمَةٌ — "receive the blessings from their Lord and [His] mercy." This narration, however, counts it as mercy. The conflict is resolved by referring to the explanation provided previously.

Ayah

۞ إِنَّ ٱلصَّفَا وَٱلْمَرْوَةَ مِن شَعَآئِرِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَمَنْ حَجَّ ٱلْبَيْتَ أَوِ ٱعْتَمَرَ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِ أَن يَطَّوَّفَ بِهِمَا ۚ وَمَن تَطَوَّعَ خَيْرًا فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَاكِرٌ عَلِيمٌ (١٥٨)
Indeed Safa and Marwah are among Allah’s sacraments. So whoever makes hajj to the House, or performs the ‘umrah, there is no sin upon him to circuit between them. Should anyone do good of his own accord, then Allah is indeed appreciative, all-knowing. (158)

(Commentary)

Al-Safa (الصفا) and al-Marwah (المروة) are two locations in Mecca where pilgrims perform the act of al-sa‘y (السعي) — the ritual walking or running between the two hills. They are two hills separated by a distance of seven hundred and sixty and a half cubits, according to what has been said. The linguistic root of al-Safa refers to a hard, smooth rock, while the root of al-Marwah refers to a hard stone. Al-Sha‘a’ir (الشعائر) is the plural of sha‘irah (شعيرة), which means a sign (العلامة). From this root comes al-mash‘ar (المشعر) — a place of ritual or a landmark of worship — and our expression: "He marked the sacrificial animal" (أشعر الهدى), meaning he made it known as such. Al-Hajj (الحج) is "intent after intent," meaning repeated intention; in legal (شرعي) terminology, it refers to the well-known ritual performed among Muslims. Al-I‘timar (الاعتمار) means visiting; its root is al-‘imarah (العمارة) — habitation or cultivation — because lands are inhabited through visitation. In legal terminology, it refers to visiting the House (the Kaaba) in the prescribed manner. Al-Junah (الجناح) is a deviation from truth and justice, and it is used to mean sin (الإثم); thus, the negation of al-junah amounts to permissibility. Al-Tatawwuf (التطوف) is derived from al-tawaf (الطواف), which is to go around something, a movement where the end meets the beginning. From this, it is understood that it is not strictly necessary for it to be around something; rather, that is merely one of its prominent instances. It is based on this meaning that al-tatawwuf is used in the verse, for what is intended is al-sa‘y, which is traversing the distance between al-Safa and al-Marwah seven consecutive times. Al-Tatawwu‘ (التطوع) is from al-taw‘ (الطوع), meaning obedience (الطاعة). It has been said that al-tatawwu‘ differs from al-ita‘ah (الإطاعة) in that it is used specifically for recommended (المندوب) acts, unlike al-ita‘ah. Perhaps this—if the view is correct—is based on the consideration that an obligatory act, being compulsory, is as if it is not performed purely by choice (طوعاً), whereas a recommended act is performed purely by choice without any trace of compulsion. This is a subtle conceptual nuance; otherwise, the root of al-taw‘ is simply the opposite of compulsion (الكره) and does not contradict an obligatory command. The Exalted says: فَقَالَ لَهَا وَلِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيَا طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا "Then He said to it and to the earth, ‘Come! willingly or unwillingly’" (41:11). The linguistic root of the form tafa‘‘ul (التفعل) implies taking something for oneself, as when we say: "He distinguished himself" (تميز), meaning he took to distinguishing; or "He learned the thing" (تعلم الشيء), meaning he took to knowing it. Thus, "He volunteered good" (تطوع خيراً) means he took to performing good by his own will. Therefore, there is no linguistic evidence to restrict al-tatawwu‘ to the fulfillment of recommended acts, unless necessitated by the aforementioned conventional (عرفية) nuance.

His saying, the Exalted: إن الصفا والمروة من شعائر الله (Indeed Safa and Marwah are among the sacraments of Allah) up to His saying: يطوف بهما (to go between them) indicates that these two places are marked with the sign of Allah, the Glorified; they point toward Him and serve as a reminder of Him. Their specific designation as being among the sacraments (الشعائر), to the exclusion of all other things, indicates that what is meant by sacraments here are not the ontological signs (الشعائر التكوينية) but rather that they are two sacraments by virtue of Allah making them places of worship (معبدين) wherein He is worshipped. Thus, they remind one of Allah, the Glorified. Their being two sacraments indicates that He, the Exalted, has legislated a form of worship connected to them. The branching of His saying: فَمَنْ حَجَّ الْبَيْتَ أَوِ اعْتَمَرَ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِ أَن يَطَّوَّفَ بِهِمَا "So whoever makes a hajj to the House, or performs the ‘umrah, there is no sin upon him to circuit between them" (2:158) is only to announce the original legislation of al-sa‘y between al-Safa and al-Marwah, not to denote that it is merely recommended. Had the intent been to denote recommendation, it would have been more appropriate to the context of the speech to praise the act of al-tatawwuf rather than to negate blame for it. The gist of the meaning is that since al-Safa and al-Marwah are two places of worship and two ritual sites among the sanctuaries of Allah, it does not harm you to worship Him in them. This is the language of legislation (التشريع). If the intent were to denote recommendation, it would have been more fitting to state that since al-Safa and al-Marwah are among the sacraments of Allah, then Allah loves the al-sa‘y between them—and this is obvious. Using such expressions, which on their own do not denote obligation, is common in the Quran in the context of legislation, such as His saying regarding jihad: ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ "That is better for you" (61:11); and regarding fasting: وَأَن تَصُومُوا خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ "and that you fast is better for you" (2:184); and regarding shortening the prayer: فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَقْصُرُوا مِنَ الصَّلَاةِ "there is no sin upon you in shortening the prayer" (4:101).

His saying, the Exalted: ومن تطوّع خيراً فإن الله شاكر عليم (And whoever volunteers a good deed, then Allah is indeed Appreciative, All-knowing). If this is coordinated (معطوفاً) with the clause following the "fa" of branching in His saying: فمن حجّ البيت أو اعتمر, then it would serve as a rationale for the legislation of al-tatawwuf in a broader sense than the specific reason explained by His saying: إنَّ الصّفا والمروة. In this case, al-tatawwu‘ would mean absolute obedience (الإطاعة), not specifically recommended obedience. However, if it is a new sentence (استينافاً) coordinated with the beginning of the verse, it would be intended to convey the belovedness of al-tatawwuf in itself—if "volunteering good" refers to al-tatawwuf—or to convey the belovedness of Hajj and Umrah if they are what is meant by "volunteering good."

The Appreciative (الشَّاكر) and the All-Knowing (العليم) are two of the Beautiful Names of Allah. Gratitude (الشكر) is the response of one who has been treated kindly to the kindness of the benefactor by manifesting it through speech or action—such as one who is granted wealth by a benefactor and repays him with beautiful praise indicating his favor, or by using the wealth in a way that pleases him and reveals his bounty. Although Allah, the Glorified, is the Benefactor whose kindness is eternal and from Whom all kindness originates—such that no one has any favor over Him to necessitate gratitude—He, exalted is His praise, has counted righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة), which are in reality His kindness toward His servants, as kindness from the servant toward Him. Thus, He rewards them with gratitude and kindness, which is kindness upon kindness. He, the Exalted, says: هَلْ جَزَاءُ الْإِحْسَانِ إِلَّا الْإِحْسَانُ "Is the recompense of goodness anything but goodness?" (55:60), and He says: إِنَّ هَٰذَا كَانَ لَكُمْ جَزَاءً وَكَانَ سَعْيُكُم مَّشْكُورًا "This is indeed your reward, and your endeavor has been appreciated" (76:22). Therefore, the application of "the Appreciative" (الشّاكر) to Him, the Exalted, is according to the literal meaning of the word, without metaphor.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi: From some of our companions, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "I asked him about the al-sa‘y between al-Safa and al-Marwah: Is it an obligation (فريضة) or a sunnah?" He said: "An obligation." I said: "Does Allah not say: فلا جناح عليه أن يطوَّف بهما (there is no sin upon him to circuit between them)?" He said: "That was during the Compensatory Umrah (‘Umrat al-Qada’). This was because the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had stipulated that they [the polytheists] remove the idols, but one of his companions was preoccupied until the idols were returned." He said: "So Allah revealed: إن الصّفا والمروة من شعائر الله فمن حجَّ البيت أو اعتمر فلا جناح عليه أن يطوَّف بهما, meaning even while the idols were upon them."

I say: A similar report is narrated in al-Kafi.

Also in al-Kafi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in a narration regarding the Hajj of the Prophet (peace be upon him): After he had circumambulated the House and prayed his two units of prayer, he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "إنَّ الصّفا والمروة من شعائر الله فابدء بما بدء الله عز وجل" — "Indeed, Safa and Marwah are among the sacraments of Allah, so begin with what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, began with." And the Muslims used to think that al-sa‘y between al-Safa and al-Marwah was something the polytheists had invented, so Allah revealed: إن الصفا والمروة من شعائر الله فمن حج البيت أو اعتمر فلا جناح عليه أن يطوف بهما.

I say: There is no contradiction between the two narrations regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول), as is apparent. His saying (peace be upon him) in the narration, "So begin with what Allah began with," is the criterion for the legislation. It has already been mentioned in the account of Hagar and her al-sa‘y seven times between al-Safa and al-Marwah that the sunnah was established by that.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: From ‘Amir al-Sha‘bi, who said: "There was an idol on al-Safa called Isaf, and an idol on al-Marwah called Na’ilah. The people of the Age of Ignorance (al-Jahiliyyah), when they circumambulated the House, would perform al-sa‘y between them and touch the two idols. When the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) arrived, they said: 'O Messenger of Allah, al-Safa and al-Marwah were only circumambulated because of the two idols, and circumambulating them is not among the sacraments.' So Allah revealed: إن الصفا والمروة (the verse). He mentioned al-Safa because of the idol that was upon it, and He affirmed al-Marwah because of the idol that was stationed upon it."

I say: Both schools [Sunni and Shi'a] have narrated many reports conveying these meanings.

The implication of all these narrations is that the verse was revealed regarding the legislation of al-sa‘y in a year in which the Muslims performed Hajj. Surat al-Baqarah was the first surah revealed in Medina. From this, it can be concluded that the verse does not share the same context (سياق) as the preceding verses regarding the qiblah, for those were revealed in the second year of the Hijrah as previously mentioned, nor with the verses at the opening of the surah, for those were revealed in the first year of the Hijrah. Thus, the verses have many diverse contexts, not a single one.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْتُمُونَ مَآ أَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَٱلْهُدَىٰ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا بَيَّنَّـٰهُ لِلنَّاسِ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ۙ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَلْعَنُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَيَلْعَنُهُمُ ٱللَّـٰعِنُونَ (١٥٩)
Indeed those who conceal what We have sent down of manifest proofs and guidance, after We have clarified it in the Book for mankind—they shall be cursed by Allah and cursed by the cursers, (159)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ وَأَصْلَحُوا۟ وَبَيَّنُوا۟ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَأَنَا ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٦٠)
except such as repent, make amends, and clarify—those I shall pardon, and I am the All-clement, the All-merciful. (160)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ وَٱلنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ (١٦١)
Indeed those who turn faithless and die while they are faithless—it is they on whom shall be the curse of Allah, the angels and all mankind. (161)
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ لَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ (١٦٢)
They will remain in it [forever] and their punishment shall not be lightened, nor will they be granted any respite. (162)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْتُمُونَ مَا أَنزَلْنَا مِنَ الْبَيِّنَاتِ وَالْهُدَىٰ (Indeed those who conceal what We have sent down of manifest proofs and guidance): What is apparent—and Allah knows best—is that "guidance" (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — refers to the knowledge and laws contained within the divine religion that guide its followers toward felicity (السعادة). "Manifest proofs" (البينات) — the clear signs and evidences — refers to the verses and arguments that serve as clear indicators, proofs, and witnesses to the truth, which is the guidance itself. Thus, "manifest proofs" in the speech of the Exalted is a specific description of the revealed verses. Accordingly, "concealment" (الكتمان) — the act of hiding or withholding — is broader than merely hiding the original verse and not showing it to the people; it also includes concealing its significance through interpretation (التأويل) or diverting its meaning through explanation (التوجيه), just as the Jews used to do with the glad tidings of the Prophethood. What the people were ignorant of, they would not disclose to them, and what the people already knew, they would interpret away by diverting it from him, صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم.

His saying, the Exalted: مِن بَعْدِ مَا بَيَّنَّاهُ لِلنَّاسِ (after We have clarified it for mankind): This indicates that their concealment only occurred after the clarification (البيان) and the matter becoming manifest (التبين) to the people, not just to themselves. This is because the manifestation of truth to every single individual among the people is a matter that the existing, familiar system of this world cannot sustain—not only in the case of revelation (الوحي) but in every public announcement and absolute clarification. Rather, it occurs through the news reaching some people without an intermediary and reaching others through them, by the present informing the absent and the learned informing the ignorant. Thus, the scholar (العالم) is considered one of the intermediaries and instruments of transmission, like the tongue and speech. When the news is clarified to the scholar—from whom a covenant (الميثاق) has been taken regarding his knowledge—along with others who were directly addressed, it has indeed been clarified to the people. Therefore, the scholar's concealment of this knowledge is a concealment of knowledge from the people after it was clarified to them. This is the sole reason that Allah, the Glorified, has cited as the cause for people's disagreement in religion and their scattering into the paths of guidance and misguidance. Otherwise, religion is primordial (فطري) — rooted in the innate disposition toward truth — accepted by the primordial nature (الفطرة) and submitted to by the faculty of discernment (القوة المميزة) once it has been clarified to it. The Exalted said: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللَّهِ ذَٰلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion, but most people do not know. 30:30). Thus, religion is primordial according to creation (الخلقة); the primordial nature never rejects it if it appears to it through the clarity of the heart—as in the case of the Prophets—or through verbal clarification. Inevitably, this second way leads back to the first; so understand this.

For this reason, the verse combined the fact that religion is primordial according to creation with the lack of knowledge of it, saying: "the nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind," and then saying: "but most people do not know." The Exalted also said: وَأَنزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتَابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ مِن بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّنَاتُ بَغْيًا بَيْنَهُمْ (and He sent down with them the Book with the truth to judge between the people concerning that about which they differed. And none differed in it except those who had been given it, after the manifest proofs had come to them, out of envy among themselves. 2:213). This informs us that the disagreement regarding what the Book contains arises only from the rebellion (البغي) — the transgression and envy — of the scholars who carry it. Thus, religious disagreements and deviations from the straight path are the effects of the rebellion of scholars through concealment, misinterpretation, and distortion (التحريف), and their injustice (الظلم). Allah even defined injustice by this on the Day of Resurrection, as He said: فَأَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ بَيْنَهُمْ أَن لَّعْنَةُ اللَّهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا (Whereupon a herald will announce between them: ‘May the curse of Allah be on the wrongdoers, who bar [others] from the way of Allah and seek to make it crooked.’ 7:44-45). The verses in this sense are numerous.

It has thus become clear that this verse is built upon the other verse; that is, His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed those who conceal what We have sent down of manifest proofs and guidance after We have clarified it for mankind in the Book..." is built upon His saying, the Exalted: "Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book... and none differed in it except those who had been given it, out of envy among themselves..." and it points to the recompense for this rebellion in its conclusion, which is His saying: "those are the ones whom Allah curses," etc.

His saying, the Exalted: أُولَٰئِكَ يَلْعَنُهُمُ اللَّهُ وَيَلْعَنُهُمُ اللَّاعِنُونَ (it is they whom Allah shall curse, and they shall [also] be cursed by the cursers): This is an explanation of the recompense for the rebellion of those who conceal the manifest proofs and guidance that Allah sent down. It consists of the curse (اللعن) — the removal from divine mercy — from Allah and the curse from every curser. The word "curse" is repeated because the nature of the curse differs: from Allah, it is the distancing from mercy and felicity, while from the cursers, it is their asking this of Allah. The curse from Him and from the cursers is mentioned in absolute terms (أطلق), as are the "cursers" themselves. This indicates the direction of every curse from every curser toward them. Reason (الاعتبار) supports this, for what a curser intends by his curse is the distancing from felicity, and in reality, there is no felicity except the true religious felicity. Since this felicity has been clarified by Allah and is accepted by the primordial nature, no one is deprived of it except through rejection and denial (الجحود). All such deprivation is only for the one who knew of it and denied it out of knowledge, not for the one who did not know of it and to whom it was not clarified. The covenant was taken from the scholars to spread their knowledge and disseminate the manifest proofs and guidance they possess; when they conceal it and refrain from spreading it, they have indeed denied it. Therefore, "it is they whom Allah shall curse, and they shall [also] be cursed by the cursers." The following verse bears witness to what we have mentioned: "Indeed those who disbelieve and die while they are faithless..." until His saying, "all together." For it is apparent that the word "Indeed" (إن) is for providing the reason (التعليل) or for emphasizing the content of this verse by repeating its meaning, which is: "those who disbelieve and die while they are faithless."

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تَابُوا وَأَصْلَحُوا وَبَيَّنُوا (except such as repent, make amends, and clarify): This is an exception from the previous verse. The meaning of conditioning their repentance (التوبة) upon "clarification" (التبين) is that their state must become clear and they must manifest their repentance. This necessitates that they clarify to the people what they had concealed and that they were indeed concealers. Otherwise, they have not yet repented, because they would still be concealers by hiding the fact that they were concealers.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَمَاتُوا وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ (Indeed those who disbelieve and die while they are faithless): This is an allusion (كناية) to their persistence in their disbelief (الكفر), their stubbornness, and their obstinacy in accepting the truth. For one who does not follow the true religion, not out of stubbornness or arrogance but because it was not clarified to him, is not a disbeliever (كافر) in reality, but rather one who is "deemed weak" (مستضعف), whose matter is left to Allah. This is evidenced by the fact that the disbelief of the disbelievers is qualified in most verses with "denial" (التكذيب), especially in the verses regarding the descent of Adam, which contain the first legislation ordained for the human species. The Exalted said: قُلْنَا اهْبِطُوا مِنْهَا جَمِيعًا فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى (We said, ‘Get down from it, all of you! Yet if there comes to you a guidance from Me...) until His saying: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا أُولَٰئِكَ أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيهَا خَالِدُونَ (As for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]. 2:39). Thus, the "those who disbelieve" intended in the verse are the stubborn deniers—and they are the ones who conceal what Allah sent down. Allah recompensed them by saying: أُولَٰئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَعْنَةُ اللَّهِ وَالْمَلَائِكَةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ (it is they on whom shall be the curse of Allah, the angels and all mankind). This is a decree from Allah, the Glorified, that every curse uttered by an angel or any of all mankind, without exception, shall be attached to them. These people follow the path of Satan, for Allah, the Glorified, said regarding him: وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ اللَّعْنَةَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ (And indeed the curse shall be upon you until the Day of Retribution. 15:35), placing the entirety of the curse upon him. Thus, these people—the scholars who conceal their knowledge—are partners with Satan in the general, absolute curse and its likes. How severe is the tone of this verse and how momentous is its matter! There will follow in the discussion of His saying, the Exalted: لِيَمِيزَ اللَّهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ وَيَجْعَلَ الْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فَيَرْكُمَهُ جَمِيعًا فَيَجْعَلَهُ فِي جَهَنَّمَ (so that Allah may separate the bad from the good, and pile up the bad on one another, and heap them all together, and cast them into hell. 8:37) that which pertains to this station, if Allah the Almighty wills.

His saying, the Exalted: خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا (remaining in it [forever]): That is, in the curse. His saying: لَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ الْعَذَابُ وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ (their punishment shall not be lightened, nor shall they be granted any respite): The shift in the context (تبديل السياق) by placing "punishment" (العذاب) in the place of "curse" (اللعنة) indicates that the curse will be transformed into punishment for them.

Know that in these verses there are instances of grammatical shift (الالتفات). In the first verse, there is a shift from the first-person plural (التكلم مع الغير) to the third-person (الغيبة) in His saying: "it is they whom Allah shall curse," because the context is one of intensifying wrath (السخط). Wrath intensifies when the name or attribute of the one to whom it is attributed is magnified—and none is greater than Allah, the Glorified. Thus, the curse was attributed to Him so that it might reach the utmost degree of severity. Then, in the second verse, there is a shift from the third-person to the first-person singular (التكلم وحده) in His saying: فَأُولَٰئِكَ أَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَنَا التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ (it is they to whom I shall turn [mercifully], and I am the All-clement, the All-merciful), to indicate the perfection of mercy and compassion by setting aside every title and attribute and the Exalted Himself undertaking the matter. The compassion and tenderness derived from this sentence are not like what would be derived from saying, for example: "it is they to whom Allah shall turn" or "their Lord shall turn." Then, in the third verse, there is a shift from the first-person singular back to the third-person in His saying: "it is they on whom shall be the curse of Allah," and the reason for this is similar to what we mentioned regarding the shift in the first verse.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from some of our companions from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: I said to him: "Inform me about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: 'Indeed those who conceal...'" He (peace be upon him) said: "We are intended by it—and Allah's help is sought—meaning that when the matter reaches one of us, he has no choice or capacity but to clarify to the people who the one after him shall be."

And from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: "It means us, and Allah's help is sought."

And from Muhammad ibn Muslim, he (peace be upon him) said: "They are the People of the Book."

I say: All of this is of the nature of "conceptual flow" (الجري) and "application" (الانطباق) — the principle that a verse's meaning applies to subsequent instances beyond its original context — otherwise, the verse is general (مطلقة).

In some narrations from Imam Ali (peace be upon him), its interpretation is "the scholars when they become corrupt."

In al-Majma', from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) regarding the verse, he said: "Whoever is asked about knowledge that he knows and conceals it, he shall be bridled on the Day of Resurrection with a bridle of fire," and that is His saying: "it is they whom Allah shall curse, and they shall [also] be cursed by the cursers."

I say: These two reports support what we have previously stated.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: "and they shall [also] be cursed by the cursers," he said: "We are they," and they have said: "It is the insects of the earth."

I say: This is an allusion to what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَيَقُولُ الْأَشْهَادُ هَٰؤُلَاءِ الَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ أَلَا لَعْنَةُ اللَّهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ (and the witnesses will say, ‘These are they who lied against their Lord.’ Behold! The curse of Allah is upon the wrongdoers. 11:18). For they are the witnesses (الأشهاد) who are permitted to speak on the Day of Resurrection and who speak what is right. As for the saying, "It is the insects of the earth," it is narrated from the commentators such as Mujahid, Ikrimah, and others, and it is sometimes attributed in some narrations to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Indeed those who conceal what We have sent down of manifest proofs and guidance," [was revealed] regarding Ali.

I say: This is of the nature of conceptual flow and application.

Ayahs

وَإِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٦٣)
Your god is the One God; there is no god except Him, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful. (163)
إِنَّ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلَّتِى تَجْرِى فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ بِمَا يَنفَعُ ٱلنَّاسَ وَمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مِن مَّآءٍ فَأَحْيَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا وَبَثَّ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ دَآبَّةٍ وَتَصْرِيفِ ٱلرِّيَـٰحِ وَٱلسَّحَابِ ٱلْمُسَخَّرِ بَيْنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (١٦٤)
Indeed in the creation of the heavens and the earth, and the alternation of night and day, and the ships that sail at sea with profit to men, and the water that Allah sends down from the sky—with which He revives the earth after its death, and scatters therein every kind of animal—and the changing of the winds, and the clouds disposed between the sky and the earth, there are surely signs for a people who exercise their reason. (164)
وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَتَّخِذُ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَندَادًا يُحِبُّونَهُمْ كَحُبِّ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَشَدُّ حُبًّا لِّلَّهِ ۗ وَلَوْ يَرَى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ إِذْ يَرَوْنَ ٱلْعَذَابَ أَنَّ ٱلْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعَذَابِ (١٦٥)
Among the people are those who set up compeers besides Allah, loving them as if loving Allah—but the faithful have a more ardent love for Allah—though the wrongdoers will see, when they sight the punishment, that power, altogether, belongs to Allah, and that Allah is severe in punishment. (165)
إِذْ تَبَرَّأَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتُّبِعُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ وَرَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ وَتَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَسْبَابُ (١٦٦)
When those who were followed will disown the followers, and they will sight the punishment while all their means of recourse will be cut off, (166)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ لَوْ أَنَّ لَنَا كَرَّةً فَنَتَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُمْ كَمَا تَبَرَّءُوا۟ مِنَّا ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُرِيهِمُ ٱللَّهُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ حَسَرَٰتٍ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ وَمَا هُم بِخَـٰرِجِينَ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ (١٦٧)
and when the followers will say, ‘Had there been another turn for us, we would disown them as they disown us [now]!’ Thus shall Allah show them their deeds as regrets for themselves, and they shall not leave the Fire. (167)

(Commentary)

The verses are unified and consistent, possessing a single order—they mention monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — establishing the proof for it, and mentioning polytheism (الشرك) and what its end entails.

His saying, the Exalted: وإلهكم إله واحد (And your God is one God). The meaning of "God" (الإله) has already been discussed in the commentary on the Basmalah (البسملة) of Surat al-Hamd at the beginning of the book. As for "oneness" (الوحدة), its concept is among the self-evident (البديهية) concepts that we do not need a definer to guide us toward its conceptualization. A thing may be characterized by oneness in terms of one of its attributes, such as "one man," "one scholar," or "one poet"; this indicates that the attribute within it does not accept partnership (الشركة) nor is it subject to multiplicity (الكثرة). For instance, the "manhood" within Zayd—who is one man—is not divided between him and another, unlike what is in Zayd and ‘Amr—who are two men—for it is divided between two and multiplied by them. Thus, Zayd, from the perspective of this attribute—manhood—is one and does not accept multiplicity, even if from the perspective of this attribute and others, such as his knowledge, power, life, and the like, he is not one but truly multiple. Allah, the Glorified, is One from the perspective that the attribute in which no other shares with Him, such as Divinity (الألوهية), is unique to Him; He is One in Divinity, and no other shares it with Him, the Exalted. Similarly, regarding knowledge, power, and life: He possesses knowledge not like other knowledges, and power and life not like the power and life of others. He is also One from the perspective that the attributes He possesses do not multiply or become numerous except conceptually (مفهوماً) only; His knowledge, power, and life are all one thing, which is His Essence (ذاته). None of them is other than the other; rather, He, the Exalted, knows by His power, is powerful by His life, and is living by His knowledge—unlike others in the multiplicity of attributes both in reality (عيناً) and conceptually.

Sometimes a thing is characterized by oneness from the perspective of its essence (ذاته), which is the absence of multiplicity and composition (التجزي) in the essence by itself; thus, it does not divide into part and part, or into essence and name, and so on. This oneness is what is called the "Unicity of the Essence" (أحدية الذات). This meaning is indicated by the term "One" (أحد), which does not occur in speech without being restricted by an annexation (الإضافة) except when it occurs in the context of negation (النفي), prohibition (النهي), or what carries their meaning, such as our saying: "No one (أحد) came to me." In this case, the very core of the essence is negated, whether it be one or many, because oneness is taken as part of the core of the essence, not as one of its attributes. This is in contrast to our saying: "One (واحد) did not come to me," for this statement is not falsified by the coming of two or more, because oneness here is taken as an attribute of the comer—which is manhood in "one man," for example. Retain this summary until we explain it fully in His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هُوَ اللَّهُ أَحَدٌ (Say, ‘He is Allah, the One.’) [112:1], Allah willing.

In short, His saying: وإلهكم إله واحد (And your God is one God) signifies in its entirety the exclusivity of Divinity to Allah, exalted is His Name, and His oneness therein is a oneness befitting the court of His holiness, blessed and exalted is He. This is because the term "one" (الواحد), according to the understanding of these addressees, does not indicate more than the general concept of oneness that can be applied to various types, only some of which are befitting of Allah, the Glorified. There is numerical oneness (وحدة عددية), specific oneness (وحدة نوعية), generic oneness (وحدة جنسية), and so on. Thus, the imagination of each addressee might go toward what he believes and perceives of the meaning. Had it been said: "And Allah is one God," it would not have constituted monotheism (توحيد), because the proponents of polytheism believe that He, the Exalted, is one God, just as each of their deities is one god. Had it been said: "And your God is one," it would not have been an explicit text for monotheism, for it would be possible for the imagination to think He is one in species (النوع)—which is Divinity—similar to what is said in enumerating types of animals: "The horse is one, and the mule is one," despite each being multiple in number. However, when it was said: وإلهكم إله واحد (And your God is one God), establishing the meaning of "one God"—which is in contrast to two gods or many gods—upon His saying: "your God," it became an explicit text for monotheism by restricting the core of Divinity to one of the deities they believed in.

His saying, the Exalted: لا إله إلا هو (There is no god except Him). This was brought to emphasize the explicitness of the previous sentence in monotheism and to negate every delusion or interpretation that could be attached to it. The negation here is a "negation of the genus" (نفي الجنس), and what is meant by "God" (الإله) is that which truly and realistically fits the description of God. In that case, it is correct that the omitted predicate (الخبر المحذوف) is "existent" (موجود) or "being" (كائن) or the like. The estimation would be: "No god in reality and truth is existent." Since the word of majesty (Allah) is in the nominative case (مرفوعاً) and not the accusative (منصوباً), the word "except" (إلا) is not for exception (للاستثناء), but rather an adjective (وصف) meaning "other than" (غير). The meaning is: "No god other than Allah is existent."

Thus, it has become clear that the sentence—meaning His saying: لا إله إلا هو (There is no god except Him)—is intended to negate deities other than Allah that are imagined and fantasized, not to negate everything other than Allah and establish the existence of Allah, the Glorified, as many have imagined. This is evidenced by the fact that the context only requires negation to establish His oneness in Divinity, not both affirmation and negation together. Furthermore, the Noble Quran considers the core of His existence, blessed and exalted is He, to be self-evident (بديهياً), not requiring intellectual assent (التصديق العقلي); rather, its concern is with establishing attributes such as oneness, being the Originator (الفاطرية), knowledge, power, and so on.

Perhaps an objection might be raised regarding the estimation of the predicate as the word "existent" (الموجود) or its equivalent, in that it establishes the negation of the existence of a god other than Allah, not the negation of its possibility (إمكانه). The response is that there is no meaning to the assumption of a "possible existent" (موجود ممكن)—equal in existence and non-existence—to whom the existence of all existents and all their affairs actually return. Sometimes it is answered by estimating "rightful" (حق), meaning: "There is no rightful deity (معبود حق) except Him."

His saying, the Exalted: الرحمن الرحيم (The All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful). The discussion regarding their meanings has already passed in the commentary on the Basmalah of Surat al-Fatihah. By mentioning these two names, the meaning of Lordship (الربوبية) is completed; for to Him, the Exalted, every general gift returns by virtue of His All-Beneficence (الرحمانية), and every specific gift occurring on the path of guidance and otherworldly happiness returns by virtue of His Mercy (الرحيمية).

His saying, the Exalted: إن في خلق السموات والأرض (Indeed in the creation of the heavens and the earth...) to the end of the verse. The context, as mentioned at the beginning of the commentary, indicates that the verse is intended to provide evidence and proof for what the previous verse contained—namely, His saying, the Exalted: وإلهكم إله واحد لا إله إلا هو الرحمن الرحيم (And your God is one God; there is no god except Him, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful). The verse, according to its meaning, decomposes into the fact that each of these things has a God, that the God of all is one, that this one God is your God, that He is All-Beneficent, bestowing general mercy, and that He is Most Merciful, leading to the ultimate happiness—which is the happiness of the Hereafter. These are true realities, and in the creation of the heavens and the earth, the alternation of night and day, until the end of what is mentioned in the verse, are signs (آيات) indicating them for a people who exercise intellect (يعقلون).

If the intent were to establish proof for the existence of the God of man, or that the God of man is one, then all of these would be but a single sign indicating that through the continuity of governance (اتصال التدبير). In that case, it would have been appropriate for the previous verse to say: "And your God is one, there is no god except Him." Thus, the verse is intended to provide evidence for the existence of God and His oneness, in the sense that the God of everything other than man in the great system is one, and that He is precisely the God of man.

The summary of the evidence is that these heavens that have transcended us and shaded us with their wondrous creation, and the earth that has carried us and borne us with its amazing affairs and all the strange transformations and fluctuations within it—such as the alternation of night and day, the sailing ships, the descending rains, the shifting winds, and the subjected clouds—are matters inherently in need of a Maker and Originator. Thus, each of them has an originating God (and this is the first proof).

Furthermore, these celestial bodies, differing in smallness and greatness, and in distance and proximity (the smallest found by scientific investigation is equivalent to 0.0000000000000000000000033 of a cubic centimeter, and the largest is equivalent to millions of times the volume of the earth, being a sphere whose diameter is approximately 9,000 miles; and the distance discovered between two celestial bodies is nearly three million light-years, with a light-year being a distance equal to approximately 365 × 24 × 60 × 60 × 300,000 kilometers)—look at these figures that dazzle the intellect and stun the mind, and judge as you will regarding the strangeness and wonder of the matter—some of them act upon others, and some are acted upon by others, wherever they are and however they are, through universal gravitation and the bestowal of light and heat. Through this, the law of universal motion and universal time is animated. This is a general, permanent system under a fixed law, such that even general relativity, which dictates change in the laws of motion in the physical world, does not shy away from admitting that universal change is also governed by another fixed law of change and transformation. Furthermore, this universal motion and transformation manifest in every part of the world in a specific form, as between the sun of our world and its system, then narrowing in circle as in our earth with its specific events and bodies, such as the moon, night and day, winds, clouds, and rain. Then the circle narrows further, as in terrestrial formations: minerals, plants, animals, and other compositions; then in each of their species; then the circle narrows until it reaches the elements, then the atoms, then the parts of atoms, until it reaches the end of what scientific investigation accessible to man has reached today, which is the electron and the proton. There, one finds something similar to solar systems: a central body and things revolving around it, like the rotation of planets in their orbits around their sun and their swimming in their spheres.

In whichever of these positions a person stands, he witnesses a wondrous system with transformations and changes by which the core of his world is preserved, and a divine law (سنة إلهية) is animated whose wonders never cease and whose marvels never end. There is no exception in its course even if it were one, and no coincidence in its folding even if it were rare or stray. Its shore cannot be reached, and its stages cannot be crossed. Whenever you assemble several of them, moving from the minute to the grand, you find them to be nothing more than a single world with a single system and a continuous governance, until the matter reaches the extent of scientific expansion today through aided senses and precise observations. Whenever you analyze and divide them, returning from the whole to the part until you reach something like the molecule, you find that nothing of the single world with its single system and continuous governance is lost—despite the fact that every two of these existents are distinct in essence (ذاتاً), law (حكماً), and individual identity (شخصاً).

Thus, the world is one thing and the governance is continuous; all parts are subjected under one system, however numerous and varied their laws may be. "And all faces shall be humbled before the Living, the Sustainer" (وعنت الوجوه للحي القيوم). Therefore, the God of the world, its Originator and the Governor of its affairs, is One (and this is the second proof).

Furthermore, man, who is a terrestrial being, living on the earth, residing on the earth, then dying and returning to the earth, does not lack in any part of his existence or survival anything more than this universal system, whose governance is continuous and whose system is one. These celestial bodies in their illumination and heating, and this earth in the alternation of its night and day, its winds, clouds, rains, and the benefits that flow from region to region in terms of sustenance and goods, are what man needs for his material necessity and the governance of his existence and survival—"And Allah encompasses them from behind" (والله من ورائهم محيط). Thus, their God, who originated them and governs their affairs, is the God of man, who originated him and governs his affairs (and this is the third proof).

Furthermore, this God is the One who gives to each what it needs for its existential happiness and what it needs for its happiness in its ultimate end and Hereafter, if it has an ultimate otherworldly happiness; for the Hereafter is the consequence of this abode. How can the consequence of the matter be governed by other than the One who governs the matter itself? (And this is the proof for the two names: the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful).

At this point, the justification of the first verse by the second is completed. The beginning of the verse with the word "Indeed" (إن), which indicates causation (التعليل), is an allusion to this—and Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: إن في خلق السموات والأرض (Indeed in the creation of the heavens and the earth) is an allusion to the essences of the celestial bodies and the earth, including the wonders of creation and the marvels of craftsmanship contained in their compositions—from forms by which their names are established, and materials from which their essences are composed, and the transformation of some into others, and the decrease or increase that occurs to them, and the composition or decomposition that happens to them, as He said: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا نَأْتِي الْأَرْضَ نَنقُصُهَا مِنْ أَطْرَافِهَا (Have they not seen how We visit the land, reducing it from its edges?) [13:41], and He said: أَوَلَمْ يَرَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ كَانَتَا رَتْقًا فَفَتَقْنَاهُمَا وَجَعَلْنَا مِنَ الْمَاءِ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ حَيٍّ (Have the faithless not regarded that the heavens and the earth were a solid mass, then We split them asunder, and We made every living thing out of water?) [21:30].

His saying, the Exalted: واختلاف الليل والنهار (and the alternation of night and day), which is the decrease and increase, and the length and shortness occurring to them due to the meeting of two natural factors. One is the daily motion of the earth on its axis, which draws night and day by facing half of the sphere—and slightly more—always toward the sun, thereby acquiring light and absorbing heat, which is called "day"; and the hiding of the sun from the other half—and slightly less—entering under the conical shadow and remaining dark, which is called "night." They continue to rotate around the earth. The other factor is the inclination of the surface of the equatorial circle or the celestial equator from the surface of the earth's orbit in its translational motion toward the north and south; this is what causes the sun's inclination from the equator to the north or south, drawing the seasons. This causes the equality of night and day in the equatorial region and at the poles. As for the poles, they have in each complete solar year one day and one night, each equal to half a year; night at the North Pole is day at the South Pole, and vice versa. As for the equatorial point, it has in each solar year approximately three hundred and sixty-five nights and days, and the day and night there are equal. As for the rest of the regions, the night and day differ in number and in length and shortness according to the proximity to the equatorial point and the poles. All of this is explained and clarified in the sciences related to it.

This difference is what causes the variation in the arrival of light and heat, and it is what causes the variation in the factors leading to the variation in the occurrence of terrestrial compositions and the transformations in their being, from which man derives various benefits.

His saying, the Exalted: والفلك التي تجري في البحر بما ينفع الناس (and the ships that sail at sea with what profits mankind). "The ship" (الفلك) is used for both singular and plural. Al-Fulk (الفلك) and al-Fulkah (الفلكة) are like at-Tamr (التمر) and at-Tamrah (التمرة). What is meant by "what profits mankind" are goods and sustenance transported from coast to coast and from one region of the earth to another.

Including the ships among the natural existents and events in which human choice has no part—like the heaven, the earth, and the alternation of night and day—is an indication that they also return, like them, to the craftsmanship of Allah, the Glorified, in nature. For the attribution of the act to man, according to precision, is no more than the attribution of the act to any of the natural causes. The choice (الاختيار) that man boasts of does not make him a complete, independent cause (سبباً تاماً مستقلاً) that is not in need of the will of Allah, the Glorified, nor does it make him any less in need of Him, the Exalted, relative to other natural causes. There is no difference, in terms of the need for the will of Allah, the Glorified, between a natural force acting upon matter—resulting through action, passion, movement, and composition/decomposition in a form such as the form of a stone, for example—and man acting through movement, bringing near, and distancing in matter to produce a form such as the form of a ship, for example. All of them return to the craftsmanship and origination of Allah; nothing is independent or self-sufficient from Him, the Exalted, in its essence or its act.

Thus, the ship, like all other natural existents, is in need of God for its existence and in need of God for the governance of its affairs without difference. Allah has alluded to this reality by His saying: وَاللَّهُ خَلَقَكُمْ وَمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (And Allah has created you and whatever you make) [37:96], as He narrated from Abraham in what he said to his people specifically regarding the idols they took as gods; for it is known that an idol is nothing but an industrial existent like the ship that sails in the sea. Allah, the Exalted, said: وَلَهُ الْجَوارِ الْمُنْشَآتُ فِي الْبَحْرِ كَالْأَعْلامِ (His are the sailing ships, reared up in the sea like landmarks) [55:24], thus counting them as His own property. And He, the Exalted, said: وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْفُلْكَ لِتَجْرِيَ فِي الْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِ (and He has disposed the ships for you, to sail on the sea by His command) [14:32], thus attributing the governance of their affairs to Himself.

(A Discussion on the Attribution of Human Manufactures to Allah, the Glorified)

How heedless are those who consider industrial things—which man makes—to be manufactured and created by man, severed in their attribution from the God of the world, exalted is His Name, relying on the fact that they are created by human will and choice.

A group of them—the materialists among those who deny the existence of the Maker—claimed that the argument of the religious people in proving the Maker is that they found in nature events and existents whose material causes they were ignorant of. Due to their adherence to the universality of the law of cause and effect (العلية والمعلولية) in things and events, they were compelled to judge that these events—whose causes were still unknown to them—must have causes. This resulted in the claim that these events of unknown cause have a cause of unknown essence (مجهولة الكنه) beyond the world of nature, which is Allah, the Glorified. Thus, the claim that the Maker exists is a hypothesis necessitated by what early man found of material events with unknown causes, such as celestial events and many terrestrial events, and what he found of spiritual events and properties whose material causes science has not uncovered to this day.

They said: Modern science has succeeded in its progress in solving the problem of material events and has uncovered their causes, thereby nullifying one of the two pillars of this hypothesis—namely, the need of material events with unknown causes for causes beyond them. The other pillar remains: the need of spiritual events for their causes and their return to an immaterial cause. Recent progress in organic chemistry promises us that man will soon discover the causes of the spirit and will be able to manufacture vital germs, compose any spiritual existent, and originate any spiritual property. At that point, the foundation of the aforementioned hypothesis will collapse, and man will create in nature whatever spiritual beings he wishes, just as he creates today whatever natural things he wishes. Previously, he was unwilling to attribute creation except to a hypothesized cause beyond nature, driven to this hypothesis by ignorance of the causes of events—this is what they mentioned.

If these poor souls would wake up a little from the intoxication of heedlessness and pride, they would see that the theists, from the very first moment they acknowledged a God for the world—and no beginning for Him will be found—established this originating cause for the entire world. Among its parts are events with known causes—and among them are events with unknown causes—and the whole, insofar as it is a whole, is in need, according to them, of an external cause. Thus, what those people establish is different from what these people negate.

The affirmers—and research and history have not been able to determine a starting point for their appearance in the history of human life—established for the entire world one or many Makers (though the Quran establishes the precedence of the religion of monotheism over idolatry, as explained by the German Orientalist Dr. Max Müller, a leader in deciphering Sanskrit). Even the earliest humans among them witnessed causes in some material events. Their establishment of a creating God for the entire world, based on the general law of causality, was not so they could rest in the case of events with unknown causes—which would result in the claim that some of the world needs God while the other part is independent of Him. Rather, it was due to their conviction that this world, composed of a series of natural causes and effects in its totality and unity, is not independent of the need for a cause above all causes, upon which all the interactions and influences occurring between its parts rely. Establishing this high cause does not nullify the general law of causality running between the parts of the world themselves, nor does the existence of material causes in cases of material effects suffice to dispense with their collective attribution to a high cause outside their series. "Being outside" does not mean the cause stands at the head of the series, but rather its encompassment of it from every assumed direction.

One of the strange contradictions in their speech is that they advocate absolute determinism (الجبر المطلق) regarding events—including human acts. No act or any other event exists except that it is a deterministic effect of causes according to them. Yet, they imagine that if man were to create another man, it would not return to the cause of the world, if a cause were assumed for it.

This meaning that we have stated—despite its subtlety and precision, even if simple common understanding cannot articulate it—is present summarily in their minds when they speak of the attribution of the entire world to the creating God, including its causes and effects—this is firstly.

Furthermore, the intellectual proofs established by the theists among the researching philosophers were established after proving the universality of causality. They built them upon the necessity of the return of possible causes (العلل الممكنة) to a Necessary Existent (واجب الوجود). They have continued on this path of inquiry for thousands of years, from the earliest eras of philosophy to our day, and they have never doubted the attribution of effects—which have their possible natural causes—to a Necessary Cause. Thus, their attribution to the Necessary Cause is not due to ignorance of the natural cause in effects with unknown causes, as these people imagine—this is secondly.

Furthermore, the Quran, which establishes the monotheism of God, only establishes it while affirming the operation of the general law of causality between the parts of the world, accepting the attribution of every event to a cause specific to it, and confirming what the sound intellect judges in that regard. It attributes natural acts to their natural subjects and agents, and attributes to man his voluntary acts (أفعاله الاختيارية) in many verses that need no citation. Then it attributes all of them to Allah, the Glorified, without exception. He, the Exalted, said: اللَّهُ خَالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ (Allah is the creator of all things) [39:62], and He, the Exalted, said: ذَٰلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ خَالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ لَّا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ (That is Allah, your Lord, the creator of all things, there is no god except Him) [40:62], and He, the Exalted, said: أَلَا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ (Look! All creation and command belong to Him) [7:54], and He, the Exalted, said: لَهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ (To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth) [20:6]. Thus, everything to which the name "thing" applies is created by Allah and attributed to Him in a manner befitting the court of His holiness and perfection. In other verses, He has combined both affirmations, attributing the act to its agent and to Allah, the Glorified, together, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ خَلَقَكُمْ وَمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (And Allah has created you and whatever you make) [37:96]; He attributed the deeds of people to them and attributed the creation of themselves and their deeds to Him, the Exalted. And He, the Exalted, said: وَمَا رَمَيْتَ إِذْ رَمَيْتَ وَلَٰكِنَّ اللَّهَ رَمَىٰ (And you did not throw when you threw, rather it was Allah who threw) [8:17]; He attributed the throwing to the Messenger of Allah, then negated it from him and attributed it to Allah, the Exalted, and so on.

From this category are other verses that combine the two affirmations in a general way, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيرًا (and created everything and ordained it in a precise measure) [25:2], and He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّا كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْنَاهُ بِقَدَرٍ (Indeed We have created everything in a measure) [54:49]—until He said—وَكُلُّ صَغِيرٍ وَكَبِيرٍ مُّسْتَطَرٌ (and every small and great thing is recorded) [54:53], and He, the Exalted, said: قَدْ جَعَلَ اللَّهُ لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدْرًا (Allah has already set a measure for everything) [65:3], and He, the Exalted, said: وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ (There is not a thing but that its sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure) [15:21]. For the "ordaining" (تقدير) of everything is making it limited by the limits of material causes and temporal and spatial conditions.

In short, the fact that the establishment of the existence of the one God in the Quran is based on the establishment of cause and effect between all parts of the world, and then the attribution of all to the Originating God, the Maker of all, is something that admits no doubt or suspicion—not as these people claim, attributing some to Allah and others to their known material causes—this is thirdly.

Indeed, what led them to this claim was what they received from a group of sectarian leaders who discussed this issue and its like in a "commoner's philosophy" that the Church used to spread in the Middle Ages, or which weak theologians of other religions relied upon. It was composed of distorted issues that were not truly issues, and weak arguments and deductions lacking soundness of vision. When these people wanted to clarify their truthful claim (which their intellects judged to be correct in summary) and move it from summary to detail, the weakness of their reasoning and thought pushed them off the path. They generalized the claim and expanded the evidence, judging that every effect with an unknown cause is attributed to Allah, the Glorified, without intermediary. They negated the need of voluntary acts for a necessitating cause, or the need of man in the issuance of his voluntary act for Allah, the Exalted, claiming his independence in his act. The discussion regarding their view has already passed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفَاسِقِينَ (and He does not mislead by it any except the transgressors) [2:26], and we will mention some of the discussion here.

A group of them—some traditionists and theologians among the literalist Muslims and a group of others—were unable to conceive a correct meaning for the attribution of man's voluntary acts to Allah, the Glorified, in a manner befitting the Divine station. Thus, they negated the attribution of man's manufactures to Him, the Glorified, especially in what he made specifically for sin, such as wine and instruments of idle play and gambling, and so on. Allah, the Exalted, said: إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَالْمَيْسِرُ وَالْأَنصَابُ وَالْأَزْلَامُ رِجْسٌ مِّنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطَانِ فَاجْتَنِبُوهُ (Indeed wine, gambling, idols, and divining arrows are abominations of Satan’s doing, so avoid them) [5:90]. It is known that what Allah, the Glorified, counted as the work of Satan cannot be attributed to Him.

What has passed previously clarifies the falsehood of this delusion both textually and intellectually. Voluntary acts, just as they have an attribution to Allah, the Glorified, in a manner befitting Him, so too do their results—which are the industrial matters that man makes for the motive of fulfilling vital needs.

Furthermore, the "idols" (الأنصاب) mentioned in the previous verse are the set-up statues and images that are worshipped, which Allah, the Glorified, mentioned are created by Him in His saying: وَاللَّهُ خَلَقَكُمْ وَمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (And Allah has created you and whatever you make). From here it becomes clear that they have different aspects of attribution: from some aspects, they are attributed to Allah, the Glorified—which is the nature of their existence regardless of the description of sin attached to them. For an idol, in reality, is nothing but a stone or metal with a specific shape, and there is nothing in it that necessitates negating its attribution to the Originator of everything. As for it being a worshipped idol besides Allah, the Glorified, this is the aspect that must be negated from Him, the Exalted, and attributed to the work of another, whether Satan or man. The same applies to other things in terms of their attribution to Him, the Exalted, and to others.

Thus, it has become clear from all that has passed that industrial matters are attributed to creation just as natural matters are attributed, without difference. Indeed, the matter of attribution to creation revolves around the share of the thing in existence—so understand that.

His saying, the Exalted: وما أنزل الله من السماء من ماء فأحيى به الأرض بعد موتها وبث فيها من كل دابة (and the water that Allah sends down from the sky, with which He revives the earth after its death, and scatters in it every kind of moving creature). Its reality is different elements carried by the water of the seas and elsewhere, which then condense into rising vapor carrying heat until it reaches the extreme cold of the air, where it turns into water dripping in the form of rain, or freezes again to become snow or hail, then descends due to its weight to the earth. The earth drinks it and is revived by it, or stores it so it emerges in the form of springs in the earth, by which is the life of everything. The water descending from the sky is an existential event following a system perfected to the utmost degree, without failure or exception, and to it is attributed the growth of plants and the formation of animals of every type.

From the perspective of its being limited by the events of the world that surround it vertically and horizontally—with which it all becomes one thing—it is not independent of an Originator who originates it and a cause that manifests it; thus, it has one God. From the perspective that it is something upon which the existence of man—in its inception and survival—relies, it indicates that its God is the God of man.

His saying, the Exalted: وتصريف الرياح (and the shifting of the winds), which is directing them from side to side by various natural factors. Most often, the light rays falling on the air from the sun turn into heat within it, causing it to become subtle and light, because heat is one of its factors. Thus, it cannot carry the cold, heavy air above it or adjacent to it, which then descends upon it and pushes it forcefully; the subtle air then flows in the opposite direction of the push, which is the wind. Among its benefits are the pollination of plants, the repelling of vaporous densities and rising foul odors, and the driving of rain clouds and others. Thus, in it is the life of plants, animals, and man.

In its existence, it indicates God, and in its harmony with other existents and its unity with them—as passed—it indicates one God for the world. In its occurrence as a path to the existence and survival of man, it indicates that the God of man and others is one.

His saying, the Exalted: والسحاب المسخر بين السماء والأرض (and the clouds disposed between the sky and the earth). "The cloud" (السحاب) is the condensed vapor from which rain comes. It is called dabab (ضباب) as long as it is not separated from the earth; when it separates and rises, it is called sahab (سحاب), ghaym (غيم), ghamām (غمام), and so on. "Disposing" (التسخير) is the subduing of a thing and humbling it in its work. The cloud is disposed and subdued in its movement and raining by the wind, cold, and other factors set over it by the permission of Allah. The discussion regarding the cloud being a sign is similar to the discussion regarding other things mentioned with it.

Know that the alternation of night and day, the water descending from the sky, the shifting winds, and the disposed clouds are the collective general events from which the system of creation in terrestrial matters—plant and animal compositions and others—is formed. This verse is like a detail, in a way, for the summary of His saying, the Exalted: وَبَارَكَ فِيهَا وَقَدَّرَ فِيهَا أَقْوَاتَهَا فِي أَرْبَعَةِ أَيَّامٍ سَوَاءً لِّلسَّائِلِينَ (and He blessed it, and ordained its foods therein in four days, alike for all solicitors) [41:10].

His saying, the Exalted: لآيات لقوم يعقلون (are signs for a people who exercise intellect). "Intellect" (العقل)—the verbal noun of ‘aqala ya‘qilu—is the perception of a thing and its complete understanding.
