From it, "the intellect" is the name for that by which man distinguishes between benefit and corruption, between truth and falsehood, and between sincerity and lying. It is the perceiving soul (النفس) of man itself, not one of its powers which are like branches of the soul, such as the retentive power, the visual power, and others.

His saying, the Exalted: ومن الناس من يتخذ من دون الله أنداداً (Among the people are those who set up equals besides Allah). "The equal" (الند) is like "the peer" (المثل) in weight and meaning. He did not say "those who set up equals for Allah" (لله أنداداً) as He expressed in other places, such as His saying, the Exalted: فَلَا تَجْعَلُوا لِلَّهِ أَندَادًا (so do not set up equals for Allah) [2:22], and His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ أَندَادًا (and they set up equals for Allah) [14:30], and others. This is because the context was preceded by the restriction in His saying: وإلهكم إله واحد لا إله إلا هو (And your God is one God; there is no god except Him...). Thus, whoever set up equals for Allah had violated the restriction without justification and took as a god one whom he knows is not a god, following whim and belittling the judgment of his intellect. Therefore, He used the indefinite form (أنداداً) to belittle their status, saying: "Among the people are those who set up equals besides Allah."

His saying, the Exalted: يحبونهم كحب الله والذين آمنوا أشد حباً لله (loving them as if loving Allah—but the faithful are more ardent in their love of Allah). In the expression "loving them" (يحبونهم), there is an indication that what is meant by "equals" (الأنداد) is not only idols, but includes angels and individuals among humans whom they took as lords besides Allah, the Exalted. Rather, it encompasses every obeyed one besides Allah without Allah permitting his obedience, as evidenced by what is at the end of the verses: إِذْ تَبَرَّأَ الَّذِينَ اتُّبِعُوا مِنَ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوا (when those who were followed will disown those who followed [them]) [2:166], and as He, the Exalted, said: وَلَا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنَا بَعْضًا أَرْبَابًا مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ (and that none of us take others as lords besides Allah) [3:64], and He, the Exalted, said: اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبَارَهُمْ وَرُهْبَانَهُمْ أَرْبَابًا مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ (They have taken their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah) [9:31]. In the verse is evidence that love (الحب) truly relates to Allah, the Exalted, contrary to those who said that love—being a lustful description—relates to bodies and physical things, and does not truly relate to Him, the Glorified; and that the meaning of what has been narrated regarding love for Him is obedience by complying with the command and refraining from the prohibition, metaphorically, as in His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِن كُنتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللَّهَ فَاتَّبِعُونِي يُحْبِبْكُمُ اللَّهُ (Say, ‘If you love Allah, then follow me; Allah will love you’) [3:31].

The verse is an argument against them, for His saying, the Exalted: أشد حباً لله (more ardent in their love of Allah) indicates that His love, the Exalted, accepts intensification (الاشتداد), and it is stronger in the believers than it is in those who set up equals for Allah. If what was meant by love were obedience metaphorically, the meaning would be: "and the faithful are more obedient to Allah," and the meaning of preference (التفضيل) would not be sound, because the obedience of others is not considered obedience before Allah, the Glorified. Thus, what is meant by love is its literal meaning.

This is also indicated by His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِن كَانَ آبَاؤُكُمْ وَأَبْنَاؤُكُمْ—until His saying—أَحَبَّ إِلَيْكُم مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ (Say, ‘If your fathers and your sons... are dearer to you than Allah and His Apostle’) [9:24]. It is apparent that the love relating to Allah, the love relating to His Messenger, and the love relating to fathers, sons, wealth, and others are all of the same kind because of His saying "dearer to you" (أحب إليكم); the elative (أفعل التفضيل) requires the participation of the preferred and the preferred-over in the core meaning, while differing in terms of increase and decrease.

Furthermore, the verse censured those who set up equals by saying: "loving them as if loving Allah," then praised the believers for being "more ardent in their love of Allah," the Glorified. The contrast between the two groups indicates that their censure is only for their distribution of divine love between Allah and the equals they set up. Although this, on its surface, might suggest that if they had given Him, the Glorified, a larger share, they would not have been censured for that, the end of the verse negates this. For His saying: "when they see the punishment, that power belongs entirely to Allah," and His saying: "when those who were followed will disown those who followed [them] and they see the punishment and all their means are cut off," and His saying: "Thus will Allah show them their deeds as regrets for them"—all witness that the censure was not directed at love insofar as it is love, but rather from the perspective of its necessary consequence, which is following (الاتباع). This following of theirs was due to their claim that those equals possessed power by which they could strengthen themselves to gain a beloved or repel a disliked thing from themselves. Thus, they abandoned following the Truth entirely or in some matters. One who follows Allah in some of His commands but not others is not a follower of Him. At that point, the aforementioned suggestion is repelled, and it becomes clear that this love must not have any partner for Allah; otherwise, it is polytheism (الشرك). The intensification of this love is inseparable from the exclusivity of following Allah's command; therefore, He praised the believers for that in His saying: "but the faithful are more ardent in their love of Allah."

Since this praise and censure are related to love from the perspective of its effect—which is following—if the love for another were followed by obedience to Allah, the Exalted, in His command and prohibition, because the other calls to His obedience—having no status other than that—no censure would be directed toward it at all. As He, the Exalted, said: قُلْ إِن كَانَ آبَاؤُكُمْ وَأَبْنَاؤُكُمْ—until His saying—أَحَبَّ إِلَيْكُم مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ (Say, ‘If your fathers and your sons... are dearer to you than Allah and His Apostle’) [9:24]; He established a love for His Messenger just as He established it for Himself, because his love (peace be upon him) is the love of Allah, the Exalted, for its effect—which is following—is the very following of Allah, the Exalted. For Allah, the Glorified, is the One who calls to the obedience of His Messenger and commands following him. He, the Exalted, said: وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطَاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ (We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave) [4:64], and He, the Exalted, said: قُلْ إِن كُنتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللَّهَ فَاتَّبِعُونِي يُحْبِبْكُمُ اللَّهُ (Say, ‘If you love Allah, then follow me; Allah will love you’). Likewise is the following of everyone who guides to Allah by his following, such as a scholar who guides by his knowledge, or a sign that aids by its indication, or a Quran that brings near by its recitation, and the like; for all of them are loved with the love of Allah, and following them is an obedience counted as a means of proximity to Him.

It has become clear by this explanation that whoever loves something besides Allah seeking power in it, and thus follows it in its causation toward a need he attains from it, or follows it by obeying it in something Allah did not command, has taken equals besides Allah, and Allah will show them their deeds as regrets for them. And the believers are those who love none but Allah, seek no power except from Allah, and follow nothing except what is of Allah's command and prohibition; those are the ones sincere to Allah in religion.

It has also become clear that the love of one whose love is part of the love of Allah, and whose following is the following of Allah—such as the Prophet and his Family, the scholars of Allah, the Book of Allah, the Sunnah of His Prophet, and everything that reminds of Allah in a way—is sincerity to Allah and is not part of censured polytheism in any way. Seeking proximity through their love and following is seeking proximity to Allah, and honoring them in what is considered honoring is part of the godwariness (تقوى) of Allah. He, the Exalted, said: وَمَن يُعَظِّمْ شَعَائِرَ اللَّهِ فَإِنَّهَا مِن تَقْوَى الْقُلُوبِ (and whoever venerates the sacraments of Allah—indeed that comes from the godwariness of hearts) [22:32]. The "sacraments" (الشعائر) are the indicating signs; it was not restricted to something like Safa and Marwah and others. Thus, everything that is among the sacraments of Allah, His signs, and His marks that remind of Him—its veneration is part of the godwariness of Allah and is encompassed by all the verses commanding godwariness.

Indeed, it is not hidden from anyone with a modicum of sense that giving independence to these sacraments and signs in opposition to Allah, and believing that they possess for themselves or others benefit, harm, death, life, or resurrection, removes them from being sacraments and signs and enters them into the fold of Divinity and polytheism with Allah the Great—we seek refuge in Allah, the Exalted.

His saying, the Exalted: ولو يرى الذين ظلموا إذ يرون العذاب أن القوة لله جميعاً وأن الله شديد العقاب (That the wrongdoers may see, when they sight the punishment, that power belongs entirely to Allah, and that Allah is severe in punishment). The apparent context is that His saying "when" (إذ) is the object of "see" (يرى), and that His saying "that power belongs to Allah" to the end of the verse is an explanation of the punishment, and "if" (لو) is for wishing (التمني). The meaning is: "If only they could see in the world a day in which they witness the punishment, witnessing that power belongs entirely to Allah—having erred in giving some of it to their equals—and that Allah is severe in His punishment and in making them taste the consequence of this error." What is meant by "punishment" in the verse—according to what the following explains—is their witnessing the error in their taking equals in whom power was imagined, and witnessing the consequence of this error. This is supported by the two following verses: "when those who were followed will disown those who followed [them]"—so no benefit they expected reached the followers from the followed—"and they see the punishment and all their means are cut off"—so no influence remained for anything besides Allah. And those who followed said: "If only we could have another turn"—which is a wish to return to the world—"then we would disown them"—meaning the followed equals in the world—"as they have disowned us" in the Hereafter. "Thus will Allah show them"—meaning those who wronged by taking equals—"their deeds"—which are their love and following of them in the world—"as regrets for them, and they shall not leave the Fire."

His saying, the Exalted: وما هم بخارجين من النار (and they shall not leave the Fire). In it is an argument against those who claim the cessation of punishment, based on the apparent meanings.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Khisal, al-Tawhid, and al-Ma‘ani, narrated from Shurayh ibn Hani, who said: "A Bedouin stood up on the Day of Jamal before the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) and said: 'O Commander of the Faithful, do you say that Allah is one?' The people attacked him and said: 'O Bedouin, do you not see the preoccupation of the heart of the Commander of the Faithful?' The Commander of the Faithful said: 'Leave him, for what the Bedouin wants is what we want from the people.' Then he (peace be upon him) said: 'O Bedouin, the statement that Allah is one is of four types: two of them are not permissible for Allah, the Exalted, and two are established for Him. As for the two that are not permissible for Him: the statement of one who says "one" intending the category of numbers—this is not permissible, for that which has no second does not enter the category of numbers; do you not see that he who said "He is the third of three" disbelieved? And the statement of one who says "He is one of the people," intending the species from the genus—this is not permissible, for it is anthropomorphism (تشبيه), and our Lord is far exalted and transcendent above that. As for the two types that are established for Him: the statement of one who says "He is one, having no likeness among things"—such is our Lord; and the statement of one who says that He, the Mighty and Majestic, is "unitary in meaning" (أحدي المعنى), meaning that He does not divide in existence, nor in intellect, nor in imagination—such is our Lord.'"

I say: The two types he (peace be upon him) established are, as you see, applicable to what we mentioned in the explanation of His saying, the Exalted: وإلهكم إله واحد (And your God is one God...).

It has been repeated in the sermons narrated from Ali (peace be upon him), al-Rida (peace be upon him), and others from the Imams of the People of the House: their saying: "He is one, but not by number" (إنه واحد لا بالعدد). This is what has passed regarding the meaning of the pureness of His Essence which refuses number. In the supplication of al-Sahifah al-Kamilah: "To You belongs the oneness of number" (لك وحدانية العدد). This is interpreted as ownership (الملكية), meaning: "You own the oneness of number without being characterized by it," for intellect and transmission both stand on the fact that His existence, the Glorified, is pure and does not double or repeat in its essence and reality.

In al-Kafi, al-Ikhtisas, and the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَن يَتَّخِذُ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ أَندَادًا (Among the people are those who set up equals besides Allah...)—in a tradition—he said: "By Allah, O Jabir, they are the leaders of the oppressors and their followers." In the narration of al-‘Ayyashi: "By Allah, O Jabir, they are the leaders of oppression and their followers."

I say: Its meaning has become clear through the preceding explanation. His (peace be upon him) expression "leaders of oppression" is due to His saying, the Exalted: "That the wrongdoers (ظلموا) may see"; thus He counted the followers who take equals as wrongdoers, so those followed would be the leaders of the wrongdoers and the leaders of oppression.

In al-Kafi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: كذلك يريهم الله أعمالهم حسرات عليهم (Thus will Allah show them their deeds as regrets for them...), he said: "It is the man who leaves his wealth, not spending it in obedience to Allah out of stinginess, then he dies and leaves it to one who acts with it in obedience to Allah or in disobedience to Allah. If he acts with it in obedience to Allah, he [the deceased] sees it in the scale of another, so he sees it as a regret—while the wealth was his. And if he acts with it in disobedience to Allah, he [the deceased] strengthened him with that wealth until he acted with it in disobedience to Allah."

I say: This meaning was narrated by al-‘Ayyashi, al-Saduq, al-Mufid, and al-Tabarsi from al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (peace be upon them), and it looks toward the expansion in the meaning of "equals" (الأنداد), and it is indeed so, as previously mentioned.

(Philosophical Discussion)

Among the intuitive meanings (المعاني الوجدانية) we possess is a meaning we call "love" (الحب), as in the cases of love for food, love for women, love for wealth, love for status, and love for knowledge. These are five instances whose existence within us we do not doubt, nor do we doubt that we use the term "love" in them with a single meaning by way of semantic sharing (الاشتراك المعنوي) rather than mere homonymy (اللفظي). There is no doubt that the instances are different; so is it a specific difference (اختلاف نوعي) or otherwise?

If we examine closely the love for that which constitutes nourishment, such as fruit for instance, we find it beloved to us due to its connection to the function of the nutritive faculty (القوة الغاذية). Were it not for the action of this faculty and the physical perfection (الاستكمال البدني) man achieves through it, the food would not be beloved, nor would love be realized. Thus, in reality, love exists between the nutritive faculty and its action, and the pleasure (اللذة) one finds during that action. By pleasure, we do not mean the pleasure of the sense of taste, for that is merely a servant to the nutritive faculty and not the faculty itself; rather, we mean the specific satisfaction (الرضى الخاص) that the faculty finds in its own action.

Furthermore, if we test the state of the love for women, we find that love in this context relates in reality to coitus (الوقاع), and its relation to the women themselves is secondary and derivative (بالتبع), just as the love for nourishment was related to the food itself secondarily and derivatively. Coitus is the effect of a faculty deposited within the animal, just as nutrition was likewise an effect of a faculty within it. From this, it is known that these two types of love trace back to a single source: an existential connection (تعلق وجودي) between these two faculties and their action—that is, their actual perfection (كمالهما الفعلي).

It is possible, then, to suppose that love is a connection exclusive to these two instances and does not exist elsewhere; however, testing by effects (الآثار) refutes this. This connection called "love" has an effect upon the subject (المتعلق) —namely, the movement of the faculty and its attraction toward the action when it lacks it, and its distress at leaving it when it finds it. We find these two characteristics—or this single characteristic—present in the case of all our perceptive faculties (القوة الادراكية) and their actions. Our faculties of sight, hearing, memory, imagination, and all other external and internal senses—whether active or passive—possess this quality. All of them love their actions and are attracted to them, and this is only because their actions are perfections (كمالات) for them, through which their deficiency and natural need are fulfilled. At this point, the matter becomes clear regarding the love of wealth, the love of status, and the love of knowledge, for man achieves a certain type of perfection (استكمال) through wealth, status, and knowledge.

From here, it is concluded that love is a specific connection (تعلق خاص) and a particular conscious attraction (انجذاب مخصوص شعوري) between man and his perfection. Precise experimentation through effects and properties has shown that this exists in animals other than humans. It has become clear that this is because the lover is either an agent of, or affected by, the action and effect he loves, and is consequently attached to everything related to it, as mentioned in the discussion on eating and fruit. This also applies to beings other than animals, provided there is a process of perfecting (استكمال) or an emanation of perfection (إفاضة لكمال) accompanied by consciousness (الشعور).

From another perspective, since love is an existential connection (تعلق وجودي) between the lover and the beloved, it constitutes a standing bond (رابطة قائمة) between them. Thus, if the effect (المعلول) that is connected to the love of its cause (العلة) were a conscious being, it would find the love of its cause within itself, provided it possessed a soul and substantial independence (استقلال جوهري).

From all that has preceded, it is concluded:

First: That love is an existential connection (تعلق وجودي) and a specific attraction between the perfecting cause (العلة المكملة), or what resembles it, and the perfected effect (المعلول المستكمل), or what resembles it. Hence, we love our actions because we achieve perfection through them, and we love that which our actions relate to, such as the food we consume, the spouse we enjoy, the wealth we manage, the status we benefit from, the benefactor who bestows favors upon us, the teacher who instructs us, the guide who directs us, the helper who assists us, the student who learns from us, the servant who serves us, or any obedient person who follows and submits to us. These are categories of love; some are natural (طبيعي), some are imaginary (خيالي), and some are intellectual (عقلي).

Second: That love possesses different degrees of intensity and weakness, for it is an existential bond—and existence (الوجود) is graded in its levels (مشكك في مراتبه). It is well known that the existential connection between a complete cause (العلة التامة) and its effect is not like the connection between incomplete causes (العلل الناقصة) and their effects. Furthermore, the perfection through which love is connected differs in terms of being necessary or non-necessary, and in terms of being material, such as nutrition, or non-material, such as knowledge. Through this, the falsity of the view that limits love to material things becomes apparent—to the extent that some have claimed its origin is the love of food and everything else reduces to it, while others claimed the origin in this chapter is the love of coitus and everything else returns to it.

Third: That Allah, the Glorified, is worthy of love from every conceivable aspect. In His essence, He is a Being possessed of infinite perfection (كمال غير متناه), and any other perfection assumed is finite. That which is finite is existentially dependent upon the Infinite; this is an essential love (حب ذاتي) that is impossible to remove. Moreover, He, the Exalted, is our Creator and Benefactor, bestowing upon us blessings infinite in number and duration; thus, we love Him as we love every benefactor for his beneficence.

Fourth: Since love is an existential bond—and existential bonds are not external to the existence of their subject but are among its descents (تنزلاته)—it follows that everything loves its own essence (الذات). It has already been mentioned that one loves what is related to what one loves; thus, one loves the effects of one's existence. From here, it appears that Allah, the Glorified, loves His creation out of love for His Essence, and He loves His creation for their acceptance of His favor upon them, and He loves His creation for their acceptance of His guidance.

Fifth: The necessity of consciousness (الشعور) and knowledge (العلم) in the context of love is only according to the particular instance (المصداق); otherwise, the existential connection, which is the reality of love, does not depend on consciousness qua connection. From here, it appears that non-conscious natural faculties and principles possess a love for their effects and actions.

Sixth: It is concluded from the above that love is a reality pervading all existing things (حقيقة سارية في الموجودات).

( Another Philosophical Discussion )

The question of the cessation of punishment versus its eternity (الخلود) is a matter where the views of researchers have differed, both from the perspective of rational inquiry and from the perspective of literal textual appearances (الظواهر اللفظية).

What can be said is: As for the textual appearances, the Book is explicit regarding eternity. The Exalted says: وَمَا هُم بِخَارِجِينَ مِنَ النَّارِ "and they shall not leave the Fire" (2:167). The Sunnah (السنة) — the recorded practice and sayings of the Prophet and the Imams — from the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) is widely transmitted (مستفيضة) regarding this. Narrations have been reported through other paths suggesting cessation and the denial of eternity, but these are discarded due to their contradiction with the Book.

As for the rational perspective, we have previously mentioned in the discussion under the saying of the Exalted: وَاتَّقُوا يَوْمًا لَّا تجْزِي نَفْسٌ عَن نَّفْسٍ شَيْئًا "And beware of the day when no soul shall compensate for another" (2:48), that demonstrating the specifics of what the Sharia has brought regarding the Resurrection (المعاد) through universal rational premises (المقدمات الكلية العقلية) is beyond our capacity, because the intellect (العقل) does not perceive particulars (الجزئيات). The way forward in this is to believe what the Truthful Prophet has brought through revelation (الوحي), based on the proof of his truthfulness.

As for the intellectual bliss and punishment that befall the soul due to its immateriality (تجردها) and its acquisition of virtuous or base characters and dispositions (ملكات), or its being clothed in beautiful or ugly states, you have learned that these states and dispositions appear to the soul in their form of ugliness or beauty. Thus, it finds bliss in those that are beautiful if its essence is felicitous (سعيدة), and it is punished by those that are ugly and distorted, whether its essence is felicitous or wretched (شقية).

Furthermore, those forms that are not firmly rooted (غير راسخة) in the soul and are incompatible with its essence will eventually vanish, because compulsion (القسر) cannot be permanent or predominant. Such a soul is one that is felicitous in its essence but possesses wretched, base forms that are capable of being removed, such as the soul of a sinful believer. All of this is clear.

However, as for the base forms that have become firmly rooted in the soul until they become actual forms (صور), or like new forms that grant the thing a new species-identity (نوعية)—such as a miserly man for whom miserliness has become the form of his humanity, just as rationality (النطق) is the form of his animality through which he becomes a new species under the genus of animal—the miserly man is also a new species under the genus of human. It is well known that this species is an immaterial species in itself, eternal in existence. All that used to proceed from him by compulsion (بالقسر) while the trait was not yet rooted—whereby he was punished and tasted the consequence of his affair—now proceeds from this species by the permission of Allah without compulsion. However, since these actions proceed from his species-identity without compulsion, they are permanent without cessation, unlike when they were occurring by compulsion.

Such a person, punished by the necessary implications of his dispositions, is in one respect like someone afflicted with melancholia (الماليخوليا) or a persistent nightmare (الكابوس). Terrifying or distorted images continue to proceed from his imaginative faculty, by which he is punished, yet he himself is the one who produces them without an external compeller. If they were not compatible with his diseased nature, he would not have produced them. Thus, although he does not suffer in terms of the actions originating from his own self, he is punished by them in the sense that punishment is that which a person flees from if not yet afflicted, and desires to escape if afflicted. This definition applies to the distorted matters and unbeautiful forms that confront the wretched person in his afterlife. Thus, it has become clear that the punishment is eternal and non-ceasing for the wretched person whose essence possesses an inseparable wretchedness (شقوة لازمة).

Objections have been raised here that are clearly fallacious and patently false: such as the claim that Allah, the Glorified, possesses vast, infinite mercy, so how can His mercy allow Him to create someone whose destination is an eternal punishment that nothing can withstand?

And the claim that punishment is only punishment if it is incompatible with one's nature, thus being a compulsion (قسر), and there is no meaning to permanent compulsion; so how can the existence of permanent punishment be valid?

And the claim that the servant only committed a sin that was finite in duration, so how can he be recompensed with an eternal punishment?

And the claim that the people of wretchedness (أهل الشقاء) do not fall short in their service to the system of creation compared to the service of the people of felicity (أهل السعادة), and were it not for them, no felicity would be realized for the felicitous; so what is the reason for their falling into eternal punishment?

And the claim that punishment for those who disobey Allah's commands and prohibitions is "vengeance" (انتقام), and vengeance only exists to rectify a deficiency that the oppressive sinner caused to the powerful avenger. This is impossible for Allah, for He is the Absolute Self-Sufficient (الغني المطلق); so how can punishment proceed from Him, especially eternal punishment?

These and similar points are the objections they have raised against the eternity of punishment and its lack of cessation. By grasping what we have explained regarding the meaning of eternal punishment, you realize that they are fundamentally baseless. Eternal punishment is an effect and a property of the "form of wretchedness" (صورة الشقاء) that has become inseparable from the wretched person. His essence becomes shaped by it after the completion of a profound potentiality (الاستعداد الشديد) that occurred in his essence, which was receptive to it through the states that befell it, ultimately tracing back to his own choice (اختياره). The intensification of a complete potentiality is what necessitates, in all events, the emanation of the form appropriate to the nature of that potentiality. Just as it is not permissible to ask about the cause of the realization of human actions after the human form has entered into matter—because the cause, which is the human form, exists—likewise, there is no meaning to asking about the "why" (لمية) of the sequence of the necessary effects of wretchedness, including eternal punishment, after the realization of the inseparable form of wretchedness that traces back to choice. These are its effects and properties, and thus all the questions are nullified. This is the summary answer to them.

As for the detailed answer:

The answer to the first: Mercy (الرحمة) in His case, the Exalted, does not mean tenderness of heart, pity, or internal affection, for these necessitate materiality—Exalted is He far above that. Rather, its meaning is bestowal (العطية) and emanation (الإفاضة) of what is appropriate to the complete potentiality (الاستعداد التام) attained in the recipient. For one who is prepared with a profound, complete potentiality loves what he is prepared for, seeks it, and asks for it with the tongue of his potentiality; thus, what he seeks and asks for is emanated upon him. Mercy is of two kinds: General Mercy (رحمة عامة), which is the giving of what a thing is prepared for and yearns for in the path of existence and being; and Specific Mercy (رحمة خاصة), which is the giving of what a thing is prepared for in the path of guidance toward monotheism (التوحيد) and the felicity of proximity. Granting the inseparable form of wretchedness, whose effect is eternal punishment, to the person prepared for it by his profound potentiality does not contradict General Mercy; rather, it is part of it. As for Specific Mercy, there is no meaning for it to encompass one who is outside of its path. Thus, if the objector says that eternal punishment contradicts mercy, if he means General Mercy, it is not so, for it is part of General Mercy. If he means Specific Mercy, it is not so because such a person is not a subject for it. Furthermore, if the objection were valid, it would also apply to temporary punishment and even types of worldly punishment, which is obvious.

The answer to the second: One must clarify the meaning of "incompatibility with nature." Sometimes it means a lack of affinity (السنخية) between the subject and the effect present with it; this is the "compulsory action" (الفعل القسري) that proceeds from an external compeller. Its opposite is the "compatible effect" (الأثر الملائم) which proceeds from the nature of a thing when ailments are joined to it and then become firmly rooted in it, becoming a form (صورة) within the thing. The thing then begins to seek it by virtue of this existence, while simultaneously not "loving" it in the sense we illustrated with the example of the melancholic. These effects are compatible with his essence in terms of their proceeding from his wretched, base nature—and effects proceeding from natures are compatible—yet they are themselves punishment because the definition of punishment applies to them, as the thing does not find them pleasing. They are unpleasing from the perspective of taste and experience (الوجدان) while being pleasing from the perspective of their origination (الصدور).

The answer to the third: Punishment is, in reality, the sequence of an unpleasing effect upon its truly established subject, which is the form of wretchedness. This effect is the result (معلول) of the form attained after the realization of preparatory causes (علل معدة), which are the limited acts of disobedience. It is not the result of those limited preparatory causes themselves, such that it would necessitate a finite cause producing an infinite effect, which is impossible. An analogy to this is that limited, numbered preparatory causes and proximities necessitated that matter be shaped by the human form, thus becoming a human from whom human effects proceed as results of the aforementioned form. It is meaningless to ask: "Human effects proceed from the human after death permanently and eternally due to the occurrence of limited, finite preparators for the matter; so how did a collection of causes with a finite end become a reason for the origination of the mentioned effects and their remaining with the human forever?" This is because their efficient cause (العلة الفاعلة)—which is the human form—is assumed to be present with them always. Just as this question is meaningless, so too is the other.

The answer to the fourth: Service (الخدمة) and servitude (العبودية) are also, like mercy, of two kinds: General Servitude (عبودية عامة), which is existential submission and being affected by the Source of Existence; and Specific Servitude (عبودية خاصة), which is submission and compliance in the path of guidance toward monotheism. Each of the two kinds has a corresponding reward and an effect that follows it and pertains to it from mercy. The reward for General Servitude in the system of creation is General Mercy; both permanent bliss and permanent punishment are from General Mercy. The reward for Specific Servitude is Specific Mercy, which is bliss and Paradise, and this is clear. Furthermore, if this objection were valid, it would apply to temporary punishment in the Hereafter and even in this world.

The answer to the fifth: Eternal punishment is attributed to the form of wretchedness within the human, as you have learned, and to Allah, the Glorified, in the sense that is said of every existing thing: that it is attributed to Him, the Exalted. It is not in the sense of vengeance (الانتقام) and seeking personal satisfaction (تشفّي الصدر), which are impossible for Him, the Exalted. Yes, vengeance in the sense of a difficult recompense (الجزاء الشاق) and an evil effect with which the Master recompenses His servant in response to his overstepping the bounds of servitude and his exiting the arena of compliance into the field of rebellion and opposition—this is applicable to Him, the Exalted. However, the fact that punishment is vengeance in this sense does not necessitate any objection whatsoever. Moreover, if this objection were valid, it would apply to temporary, non-eternal punishment in the Hereafter, and even in the world.

( A Quranic and Hadith Discussion Completing the Previous Discussion )

Know that this method of argumentation to refute the aforementioned doubt is also employed in the Book and the Sunnah. The Exalted says: مَّن كَانَ يُرِيدُ الْعَاجِلَةَ عَجَّلْنَا لَهُ فِيهَا مَا نَشَاءُ لِمَن نُّرِيدُ ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا لَهُ جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَاهَا مَذْمُومًا مَّدْحُورًا * وَمَنْ أَرَادَ الْآخِرَةَ وَسَعَىٰ لَهَا سَعْيَهَا وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولَٰئِكَ كَانَ سَعْيُهُم مَّشْكُورًا * كُلًّا نُّمِدُّ هَٰؤُلَاءِ وَهَٰؤُلَاءِ مِنْ عَطَاءِ رَبِّكَ وَمَا كَانَ عَطَاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُورًا "Whoever desires the transitory life, We expedite for him therein whatever We wish, for whomever We desire. Then We appoint hell for him, to enter it, blamed and spurned. Whoever desires the Hereafter and strives for it with an effort worthy of it, should he be faithful—the endeavour of such shall be appreciated. To these and to those—to all We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not confined." (17:18-20).

As you see, the verse makes both punishment and appreciation (الشكر) part of the "bounty" (العطاء) and mercy, and it makes the realization of each of them linked to the servant's will and striving. This is exactly the path we have taken in the core of the issue and in refuting the objections against it. There are other verses with this meaning that we shall discuss in their appropriate places, Allah the Exalted willing.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ كُلُوا۟ مِمَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ حَلَـٰلًا طَيِّبًا وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (١٦٨)
O mankind! Eat of what is lawful and pure in the earth, and do not follow in Satan’s steps. Indeed, he is your manifest enemy. (168)
إِنَّمَا يَأْمُرُكُم بِٱلسُّوٓءِ وَٱلْفَحْشَآءِ وَأَن تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (١٦٩)
He only prompts you to [commit] evil and indecent acts, and that you attribute to Allah what you do not know. (169)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ قَالُوا۟ بَلْ نَتَّبِعُ مَآ أَلْفَيْنَا عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَنَآ ۗ أَوَلَوْ كَانَ ءَابَآؤُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ (١٧٠)
When they are told, ‘Follow what Allah has sent down,’ they say, ‘No, We will follow what we have found our fathers following.’ What, even if their fathers neither exercised their reason nor were guided?! (170)
وَمَثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ كَمَثَلِ ٱلَّذِى يَنْعِقُ بِمَا لَا يَسْمَعُ إِلَّا دُعَآءً وَنِدَآءً ۚ صُمٌّۢ بُكْمٌ عُمْىٌ فَهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (١٧١)
The parable of the faithless is that of someone who shouts after that which does not hear [anything] except a call and cry: deaf, dumb, and blind, they do not exercise their reason. (171)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ كُلُوا مِمَّا فِي الْأَرْضِ حَلَالًا طَيِّبًا (O mankind! Eat of what is lawful and pure in the earth...) [QARAI TRANSLATION NEEDED: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 168] until the end of the two verses. Lawful (الحلال) is the opposite of the forbidden (الحرام) which is prohibited to enter upon. Al-Hill (الحل) is the opposite of al-Hurmah (الحرمة) — prohibition; it is also the opposite of al-Haram (الحرم) — the sacred precinct; and it is the opposite of al-Aqd (العقد) — the knot or obligation. In all its usages, it conveys the meaning of a thing's freedom (حرية) in its action and effect. Pure (الطيب) — the opposite of the loathsome (الخبيث) — is that which is agreeable to the soul and the thing itself, such as "pure speech" (الطيب من القول) due to its agreeableness to the sense of hearing, "pure fragrance" (الطيب من العطر) which agrees with the sense of smell, and a "pure place" (الطيب من المكان) which agrees with the state of the one residing in it. Steps (الخطوات), with two dammahs, is the plural of khutwah (خطوة), which is the distance between the two feet of a walker. It is also recited as khatawat (خطوات) with two fathahs, being the plural of khatawah (خطوة), meaning a single instance or pace. The "steps of Satan" (خطوات الشيطان) are those matters whose relation to the purpose of Satan — which is seduction (الإغواء) through polytheism (الشرك) — is like the relation of a walker's steps to his destination and goal. Thus, they are the matters that serve as preliminaries to polytheism and distance from Allah, the Glorified. Command (الأمر) is the commander's imposition of his own will upon the commanded so that he performs what the commander desires. The command from Satan is his whispering (وسوسته) and his imposition of what he desires upon the human being by casting it into his heart and beautifying it in his sight. Evil (السوء) is that which a human being finds repulsive and considers ugly from a social perspective; when it exceeds its limit and passes its proper bounds, it becomes indecency (فحشاء). For this reason, adultery is called indecency (الفحشاء), and it is a verbal noun like al-sarra (السراء) — prosperity — and al-darra (الضراء) — adversity.

The Exalted has generalized the address to all people because the ruling He strikes their ears with and clarifies for them is something that afflicts everyone. As for the polytheists (المشركون), they had matters they had forbidden for themselves as a fabrication against Allah, as it is narrated that the tribes of Thaqif, Khuza'ah, Banu Amir ibn Sa'sa'ah, and Banu Mudlij had forbidden for themselves certain things from crops, livestock, the bahirah, the sa'ibah, and the wasilah. This was among the Arabs, and among others, there are also many things of this sort. As for the believers (المؤمنون), there might have remained among them after Islam superstitious matters (امور خرافية) according to the law of inherited national character, customs, and traditions that were abrogated by non-gradual abrogators (نواسخ غير تدريجية) such as religions, laws, and the like. For every new method, whether religious or worldly, when it descends upon a people, it first directs itself toward the foundations and roots of the old method and cuts them. If it continues in its vitality and strength — through proper cultivation and sound acceptance — it kills the branches and cuts the tails; otherwise, remnants of the old mix with the new, coalesce with it, and become like a vegetative compound (مركب نباتي) that is neither this nor that.

Thus, the Exalted commanded people to eat of what is in the earth. Eating (الأكل) is swallowing after chewing, and sometimes "eating" is used as a metaphor for any form of disposal or use of wealth (مطلق التصرف في الأموال), since eating is the primary human act and the pillar of his life, as the Exalted said: لَا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوَالَكُم بَيْنَكُم بِالْبَاطِلِ إِلَّا أَن تَكُونَ تِجَارَةً عَن تَرَاضٍ (do not eat each other’s wealth unjustly, but let there be trade by mutual consent) [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah An-Nisa, Ayah 29]. The verse does not resist being interpreted in this broad sense due to its absolute nature (إطلاقها). The meaning is: eat, dispose of, and enjoy the divine blessings (النعم الإلهية) in the earth which the nature of the earth has prepared for you by the permission and subjugation of Allah, as a lawful and pure eating. That is, let no barrier from your own natures or the nature of the earth prevent you from eating it or disposing of it — such as that which by its nature is not edible, or that which the human nature does not accept eating — nor should your natures recoil from eating that which the nature accepts but the innate disposition (السليقة) finds repulsive and rejects, such as food obtained through an impermissible means.

His saying, the Exalted: كُلُوا مِمَّا فِي الْأَرْضِ حَلَالًا طَيِّبًا (Eat of what is lawful and pure in the earth), implies a general permissibility (الإباحة العامة) without restriction or condition, except that His saying: وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا خُطُوَاتِ الشَّيْطَانِ (and do not follow in Satan’s steps) [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 168] etc., implies that there are matters here called "the steps of Satan" related to this lawful and pure eating — either abstaining from eating in following Satan, or proceeding with it in following Satan. Then He mentioned the criterion for what constitutes following Satan: that it is evil (سوء), indecency (فحشاء), and saying about Allah, the Glorified, what one does not know. If abstaining is not permissible except by the pleasure of Allah, the Exalted, then the act is likewise. Thus, eating of what is in the earth is not lawful and pure except that Allah, the Exalted, permits it and legislates it; and He has legislated it through this verse and its likes, and He does not prevent it through prohibition or deterrence, as will come in His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَيْتَةَ وَالدَّمَ (He has only forbidden you carrion, blood...) [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 173]. So the meaning of the verse — and Allah knows best — returns to something like our saying: Eat of the blessings of Allah in the earth created for you, for Allah has made them lawful and pure for you, and do not leave some of them by way of abstention and refusal, for that would be evil, indecency, and saying without knowledge — meaning a legislation (تشريع) that is not yours to make — and that is following the steps of Satan.

The verse indicates firstly: the general lawfulness (عموم الحلية) of all disposals and uses except what the evidence excludes, for Allah, the Glorified, has the right to prohibit that which He has given permission for.

Secondly: that abstaining from what Allah has made lawful without scientific evidence (دليل علمي) is a forbidden legislation (تشريع محرم).

Thirdly: that what is meant by "following the steps of Satan" is to worship Allah through that which He has not permitted to be used in worship. For He did not forbid walking and proceeding, but rather the walking in which a person places his foot in the place of Satan's foot, such that his gait coincides with Satan's gait, thereby becoming a follower of his steps. From here it is known that the generality of the rationale (عموم التعليل), which is His saying: إِنَّمَا يَأْمُرُكُم (He only commands you...) [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 169] etc., although it necessitates the prohibition of rushing into an act without knowledge just as it necessitates the prohibition of abstaining without knowledge, it is not the primary intent in the address; for that is not from "following the steps of Satan," even if it is a following of Satan.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا يَأْمُرُكُم بِالسُّوءِ وَالْفَحْشَاءِ وَأَن تَقُولُوا عَلَى اللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (He only commands you to [do] evil and indecency, and that you should attribute to Allah what you do not know) [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 169]. Evil and indecency occur in action, in contrast to speech. By this it becomes clear that what Satan commands is restricted to the action which is evil and indecency, and the speech which is saying without knowledge.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ اتَّبِعُوا مَا أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ قَالُوا بَلْ نَتَّبِعُ مَا أَلْفَيْنَا (And when they are told, ‘Follow what Allah has sent down,’ they say, ‘No, we will follow what we found...) [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 170]. Al-Ilfa' (الإلفاء) is finding (الوجدان), i.e., what we found our fathers upon. The verse bears witness to what we derived from the previous verse regarding the meaning of the steps of Satan.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَوْ كَانَ آبَاؤُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ شَيْئًا وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ (What! Even if their fathers did not understand anything and were not guided?!) [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 170]. This is a response to their statement, and its clarification is that it is a statement without knowledge or clarity, and it contradicts explicit reason (صريح العقل). For their saying: "No, we will follow what we found our fathers upon," is an absolute statement — meaning we follow our fathers in whatever state and with whatever description they had, even if they did not understand anything and were not guided, and we say what they did was the truth. This is the "saying without knowledge" (القول بغير علم), and it leads to saying what no rational person would say if he were alert to it. Had they followed their fathers in what they knew and were guided in, while they themselves knew that their fathers knew and were guided, it would not have been of the nature of being guided without knowledge.

From here it is known that His saying, the Exalted: لَا يَعْقِلُونَ شَيْئًا وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ (did not understand anything and were not guided), is not mentioned by way of hyperbole (المبالغة), considering that the negation of absolute knowledge from their fathers — while they knew many things in their lives — could only be understood as hyperbole.

This is because the speech is framed as a hypothetical (سوق الفرض) by presenting a premise (تقدير) in which no one would say following is permissible, in order to invalidate the absolute nature of their claim: "we follow what we found our fathers upon," and this is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَثَلُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا كَمَثَلِ الَّذِي يَنْعِقُ بِمَا لَا يَسْمَعُ إِلَّا دُعَاءً وَنِدَاءً (The parable of the faithless is that of one who shouts at that which hears nothing but a call and a cry...) [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 171].

The parable (المثل) is the current proverb, and al-mathal is also the description (الوصف), as in His saying, the Exalted: انظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا لَكَ الْأَمْثَالَ فَضَلُّوا فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلًا (Look how they coin similitudes for you! So they have gone astray, and cannot find a way) [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Furqan, Ayah 9]. Al-Naiq (النعيق) is the sound of the shepherd to his sheep to drive them; it is said: "the shepherd shouted (نعق) at the sheep" when he cries out to drive them. Al-Nida' (النداء) is the verbal noun of nada (نادى) — to call out; it is more specific than al-du'a' (الدعاء) — calling/supplication — as it contains the meaning of raising the voice and the like, unlike al-du'a'. The meaning — and Allah knows best — is: your parable in calling those who disbelieve is like the parable of one who shouts at beasts that hear nothing from his shouting except a mere call and a cry; so they are driven away by the mere striking of the sound upon their hearing without understanding anything. Thus, they are deaf (صمّ), not hearing speech that benefits them; dumb (بكم), not speaking what conveys meaning; and blind (عمي), not seeing anything; so they do not understand anything because the paths leading to intellection (التعقل) are blocked for them.

From this, it appears that in the speech there is an inversion (قلب) or another literary consideration (عناية) that returns to it. For the parable of "one who shouts at that which hears nothing but a call and a cry" is the parable of the one who calls them to guidance, not the parable of the disbelievers who are called to guidance. However, since the three descriptions derived from the parable and mentioned after it — namely His saying: صُمٌّ بُكْمٌ عُمْيٌ فَهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (Deaf, dumb, and blind, they do not understand) — were descriptions of those who disbelieve, not of the one who calls them to the truth, this necessitated attributing the parable to those who disbelieve rather than to the Messenger of Allah, the Exalted; thus it produced what resembles an inversion.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Tahdhib, narrated from Abd al-Rahman, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about a man who swore to sacrifice his son. He said: 'That is from the steps of Satan (خطوات الشيطان).'"

And from Mansur ibn Hazim, who also said: "Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said to me: 'Have you not heard of Tariq? Tariq was a slave-dealer in Medina, and he came to Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) and said: "O Abu Ja'far, I have sworn by divorce (الطلاق), manumission (العتاق), and vows (النذر)?" He said to him: "O Tariq, this is from the steps of Satan."'"

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Every oath (يمين) by other than Allah is from the steps of Satan."

In al-Kafi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "If a man swears an oath regarding something — and that which he swore upon is better to perform than to leave — let him perform that which is better, and there is no expiation (كفارة) for him; that is only from the steps of Satan."

I say: The traditions, as you see, are based on the fact that what is meant by "the steps of Satan" are acts by which one seeks proximity (يتقرب بها) but which do not actually grant proximity because they are not recognized in the Sharia (العبرة بها شرعاً), as we mentioned in the previous commentary. Yes, regarding divorce and the like, there is another reason for invalidity, which is the conditionality (التعليق) that contradicts the creative intent (الانشاء), and the matter is jurisprudential (فقهية). What is meant by "an oath by other than Allah" is the oath upon which the effect of a legal oath (اليمين الشرعي) is placed, or swearing by that which Allah has not sworn by and for which no dignity (كرامة) has been established.

In al-Majma', from al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمَثَلُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا كَمَثَلِ الَّذِي يَنْعِقُ (The parable of the faithless is that of one who shouts...), he said: "That is, their parable in your calling them to faith is like the parable of the shouter (الناعق) in his calling the beasts shouted at (المنعوق به) which do not understand but only hear the sound."

(Ethical and Social Discussion)

The opinions and beliefs that a human being adopts are either theoretical (نظرية), having no direct connection to action — such as matters related to mathematics, natural sciences, and metaphysics — or practical (عملية), directly related to action — such as matters related to what ought to be done and what ought not. The path in the first category is to follow knowledge (العلم) and certainty (اليقين) that terminates in demonstration (برهان) or sense perception (حس). In the second category, it is to follow what leads to the good (الخير) in which lies the happiness (سعادة) of the human being or what is beneficial for it, and to avoid what leads to his wretchedness (شقائه) or harms his happiness. As for believing in what one has no knowledge of being true in the first category, and believing in what one does not know to be good or evil, it is a superstitious belief (اعتقاد خرافي).

Since the human being's opinions terminate in the requirement of the primordial nature (الفطرة) which searches for the causes of things, and the nature (الطبيعة) which drives him toward perfection (الاستكمال) through that which is truly his perfection, his soul does not submit to a superstitious opinion taken blindly and out of ignorance. However, the psychological emotions (العواطف النفسانية) and internal feelings (الإحساسات الباطنية) stirred by the imagination (الخيال) — the primary ones being fear and hope — sometimes necessitate for him the adoption of superstition. This is because the imagination depicts for him images accompanied by fear or hope, and the feeling of fear or hope preserves them, not letting them vanish from the fearful or hopeful soul. Just as when a human being enters a valley — alone without a companion, the night being pitch black and sight failing to perceive — and there is no securer to secure him by distinguishing dangers from others through light or the like, you see that his imagination depicts for him every shadow that appears to him as a terrifying ghoul intending his destruction or as one of the spirits. It might depict for him movement, coming and going, ascending to the sky and descending to the earth, and various shapes and forms. Then the imagination continues to repeat this fabricated likeness to him whenever he remembers it, while his state remains one of fear. Then he might transmit it to others, creating in them a state similar to his own, and it continues to spread — while it is a superstitious subject that does not terminate in reality.

Sometimes the imagination incites the instinct of defense in the human being to establish actions to ward off the evil of this imagined being, and he urges others to perform them for safety from its evil; thus a superstitious custom (سنّة خرافية) is established.

The human being, since the earliest ages of his life, has been afflicted with superstitious opinions until today. It is not as some think, that these are characteristics of Easterners; they exist among Westerners just as much, if they are not even more eager for them.

The elite among humans — namely the scholars — have never ceased to use subtle means to erase the traces of these superstitions entrenched in the souls of the common people, means that would cause the commoners to become alert and awakened regarding them. Yet the disease has exhausted the physician; for the human being is not free from imitation (التقليد) and following in theoretical opinions and factual information on one hand, and from psychological feelings and emotions on the other. It is enough for you to know that the treatment has not succeeded to this day.

Most amazing of all is what the people of civilization and natural scientists see today! They have mentioned that science today builds its foundation upon sense perception (الحس) and experiment (التجربة) and rejects what is besides that, and that civility and civilization build their foundation upon the perfection of society (استكمال الاجتماع) in every possible perfection as much as possible, and they have built education upon this.

Yet this itself — and it is amazing — is from following superstition. For the natural sciences only search for the properties of nature and establish them for their subjects; in other words, these material sciences only ever uncover the hidden properties of matter. As for what is beyond that, they have no way to negate or invalidate it. Thus, the belief in the non-existence of what sense perception and experiment do not reach, without evidence, is among the most manifest of superstitions.

Likewise, building civilization upon the aforementioned perfection of society; for this perfection and the attainment of social happiness might necessitate depriving some individuals of their individual vital happiness, such as enduring death and sacrifice in defense of the homeland, the law, or the goal. Deprivation of personal happiness for the sake of protecting the sanctuary of society — in these deprivations, a human being only proceeds out of a belief in perfection (عقيدة الاستكمال), seeing them as perfections — yet they are not perfections for his own soul; rather, they are non-existence and deprivation for it. They are only perfections — if they are perfections — for society qua society. The human being only desires society for the sake of himself, not himself for the sake of society. For all these reasons, these societies have schemed for their individuals, so they indoctrinated them that the human being acquires through sacrifice a beautiful mention (ذكراً جميلاً) and a name that remains in pride forever, which is the "eternal life." This is a superstition; what life is there after nullification and annihilation, except that we call it life — a naming behind which there is nothing?

Similar to it is the saying: "The human being must endure the bitterness of the law and be patient with deprivation in some of what his soul desires so that society may be preserved, thereby attaining his perfection in the remainder." Thus he believes that the perfection of society is his own perfection. This is a superstition; for the perfection of society is only his perfection where the two perfections coincide, but not otherwise. So what obligation is there upon an individual regarding his perfection, or a society of a people regarding the society of the world, if he is able to attain what he seeks of his hopes even through injustice (الجور) and by surpassing in power and capability without any resisting opponent, to believe that the perfection of society is his perfection and beautiful mention is his pride? Just as the powerful nations never cease to benefit from the lives of weak nations; they do not find a place to tread but they tread it, nor a gain but they gain it, nor a soul but they enslave and subjugate it. Is that anything but treating a chronic disease with annihilation?

As for the path the Quran has taken in this regard, it is its command to follow what Allah has sent down and the prohibition of saying without knowledge; this is in theory (النظر). As for action (العمل), it is its command to seek what is with Allah in it. If it coincides with what the soul desires, it contains the happiness of this world and the Hereafter; and if it contains its deprivation, then with Allah is a great reward, and what is with Allah is better and more lasting.

That which the proponents of sense perception say — that following religion is an imitation (تقليد) which science prohibits, and that it is from the superstitions of the second era of the four eras that have passed over the human species (which are the era of myths, the era of religion, the era of philosophy, and the era of science, which is what humanity is upon today in following science and rejecting superstitions) — is a saying without knowledge and a superstitious opinion.

As for their claim that following religion is imitation, it is invalidated by the fact that religion is a composite whole (مجموع مركب) of the knowledge of the Origin (المبدء) and the Return (المعاد), and of social laws consisting of acts of worship (العبادات) and transactions (المعاملات) taken through the path of revelation (الوحي) and prophethood (النبوة) whose truthfulness is established by demonstration (البرهان), and a collection of reports reported by the truthful one as true. Following them is a following of knowledge because the premise is the knowledge of the truthfulness of their reporter through demonstration. There has already passed, in the discussion following His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَن تَذْبَحُوا بَقَرَةً (And when Moses said to his people, ‘Allah commands you to slaughter a cow...’) [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 67], a discourse on imitation (التقليد), so refer to it.

It is amazing that this saying is the saying of one who has nothing in the foundations of life and the customs of society — in his food, drink, clothing, marriage, dwelling, and other things — except blind imitation and following of whim (اتباع الهوى) without verification or clarity. Yes, they invented another name for imitation, which is "following the custom" (اتباع السنّة) that the advanced world approves of. Thus, imitation became erased in name but established in form, forsaken in word but familiar in meaning. "Cast your bucket among the buckets" became a scientific slogan and civilizational progress, while "do not follow whim lest it lead you astray" became a religious imitation and a superstitious saying.

As for their division of the course of human life into four eras, what we have of the history of religion and philosophy belies it. For the emergence of the religion of Abraham was only after the era of philosophy in India, Egypt, and Chaldea; and the religion of Jesus was after the philosophy of Greece; and likewise the religion of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny) — which is Islam — was after the philosophy of Greece and Alexandria. In short, the peak of philosophy's height was before religion reached its peak. And it has already passed that the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد) precedes in its era all other religions.

What the Quran approves of regarding the division of human history is its division into the era of simplicity (السذاجة) and the unity of nations, and the era of sense perception and matter. Its clarification will come in the discourse on His saying, the Exalted: كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللَّهُ النَّبِيِّينَ (Mankind were but one community; then Allah sent the prophets...) [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 213].

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُلُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لِلَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ (١٧٢)
O you who have faith! Eat of the good things We have provided you, and thank Allah, if it is Him that you worship. (172)
إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَيْتَةَ وَٱلدَّمَ وَلَحْمَ ٱلْخِنزِيرِ وَمَآ أُهِلَّ بِهِۦ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ بَاغٍ وَلَا عَادٍ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٧٣)
He has forbidden you only carrion, blood, the flesh of the swine, and that which has been offered to other than Allah. But should someone be compelled, without being rebellious or transgressive, there shall be no sin upon him. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (173)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْتُمُونَ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَيَشْتَرُونَ بِهِۦ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۙ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَا يَأْكُلُونَ فِى بُطُونِهِمْ إِلَّا ٱلنَّارَ وَلَا يُكَلِّمُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَلَا يُزَكِّيهِمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٧٤)
Indeed those who conceal what Allah has sent down of the Book and sell it for a paltry gain—they do not take in, into their bellies, [anything] except fire, and Allah shall not speak to them on the Day of Resurrection, nor shall He purify them, and there is a painful punishment for them. (174)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةَ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَٱلْعَذَابَ بِٱلْمَغْفِرَةِ ۚ فَمَآ أَصْبَرَهُمْ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ (١٧٥)
They are the ones who bought error for guidance, and punishment for pardon: how patient of them to face the Fire! (175)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ نَزَّلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَفِى شِقَاقٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (١٧٦)
That is so because Allah has sent down the Book with the truth, and those who differ about the Book are surely in extreme defiance. (176)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُلُوا مِن طَيِّبَاتِ مَا رَزَقْنَاكُمْ — "O you who have faith! Eat of the good things We have provided you" (2:172). This is a specific address to the believers (المؤمنين) following the previous general address to all people. It is of the nature of extracting a specific address from a general one, as if it were a turning away from addressing a group that does not accept advice or listen to speech, and a turning toward those who respond to the Caller due to their faith in Him. The disparity between the two addresses arises from the disparity between the addressees. Since the believers in Allah were expected to accept, the phrase "what is in the earth, lawful and good" was replaced with "the good things We have provided you." This served as a means to demand from them gratitude (الشكر) to Allah alone, as they are monotheists (موحدين) who worship none but Allah, the Glorified. For this very reason, He said, "We have provided you" (رزقناكم), and did not say, "you were provided" or "what is in the earth" and the like; this contains an allusion or indication of His being known to them, near to them, affectionate, and compassionate toward them. It appears that His saying, "of the good things We have provided you" (من طيبات ما رزقناكم), is a case of appending the attribute to the described object (إضافة الصفة إلى الموصوف) rather than the attribute standing in place of the described object. According to the first sense, the meaning is: "Eat of Our provision, all of which is good." This is consistent with the sense of seeking proximity and affection that radiates from the context. According to the second sense, the meaning would be: "Eat of the good provision and not the vile (خبيث) thereof." This is far-fetched and inconsistent with the context, which is one of lifting a prohibition and forbidding the abstention from some of what Allah, the Glorified, has provided them based on their own self-made legislation and speaking without knowledge.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاشْكُرُوا لِلَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ — "and give thanks to Allah, if it is Him that you worship" (2:172). He did not say, "and give thanks to Us," but rather "give thanks to Allah," so that it might be more indicative of the command toward monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. For this reason also, He said, "if it is Him that you worship" (إِن كُنتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ), indicating exclusivity and restriction (الحصر والقصر), and He did not say, "if you worship Him."

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَيْتَةَ وَالدَّمَ وَلَحْمَ الْخِنزِيرِ وَمَا أُهِلَّ بِهِ لِغَيْرِ اللَّهِ — "He has forbidden you only carrion, blood, the flesh of swine, and that which has been offered to other than Allah" (2:173). Offering to other than Allah (الإهلال لغير الله) refers to slaughtering for the sake of other than Him, such as idols.

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ بَاغٍ وَلَا عَادٍ — "But should someone be compelled, without being rebellious or aggressive" (2:173). That is, without being unjust (ظالم) or exceeding his limit (متجاوز حده). These two are circumstantial qualifiers (حالان) whose governing agent is the state of necessity (الاضطرار). Thus, the meaning is: whoever is compelled to eat something from the aforementioned forbidden things out of necessity, while in a state of not being rebellious and not being aggressive, then there is no sin upon him in eating. However, if he is compelled while in a state of rebellion or aggression—such that these two states were the cause of the necessity—then it is not permissible for him. His saying, the Exalted, إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ — "indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful" (2:173), is evidence that this allowance is a mitigation and a concession (رخصة) from Him, the Exalted, for the believers; otherwise, the basis for the prohibition exists in the state of necessity as well.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْتُمُونَ مَا أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ — "Indeed those who conceal what Allah has sent down of the Book" (2:174). This is an allusion to the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), as they possessed many good, lawful things that their elders and leaders had forbidden in matters of worship and otherwise—while they possessed the Book which did not decree their prohibition—and they only concealed what they concealed to preserve the provision that flowed to them from their leadership, the prestige of their position, status, and wealth.

In the verse, there is an unmistakable indication of the embodiment of deeds (تجسم الأعمال) and the realization of their consequences. He, the Exalted, first mentioned that their choosing a small price over what Allah sent down is the eating of fire into their bellies. Then, in the following verse, He replaced the choice of concealment and taking a price for explaining what Allah sent down with the choice of error over guidance (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — and then the choice of punishment over forgiveness. Finally, He concluded by saying: فَمَا أَصْبَرَهُمْ عَلَى النَّارِ — "How patient they are in the face of the Fire!" (2:175). What was apparent from them was the persistence in concealment and remaining upon it; so understand.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding the saying of the Exalted: فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ بَاغٍ وَلَا عَادٍ (But should someone be compelled, without being rebellious or aggressive). He said: الباغي باغي الصيد ، والعادي السارق ليس لهما أن يأكلا الميتة إذا اضطرا اليها ، هي حرام عليهما ليس هي عليهما كما هي على المسلمين وليس لهما أن يقصرا في الصلوة . "The rebellious (الباغي) is the one who seeks game for sport, and the aggressive (العادي) is the thief. It is not for them to eat carrion when they are compelled toward it; it remains forbidden for them and is not for them as it is for the Muslims, nor is it for them to shorten the prayer."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), he said: "The rebellious (الباغي) is the unjust (الظالم), and the aggressive (العادي) is the usurper (الغاصب)."

And from Hammad, from him (عليه السلام), he said: "The rebellious (الباغي) is the one who revolts against the Imam, and the aggressive (العادي) is the robber."

In al-Majma', narrated from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) and Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): "Without being rebellious (غير باغ) against the Imam of the Muslims, nor aggressive (ولا عادٍ) by sinning against the path of the righteous."

I say: All of these are by way of enumerating instances (المصاديق), and they support the meaning we derived from the apparent wording.

In al-Kafi and Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding the saying of the Exalted: فَمَا أَصْبَرَهُمْ عَلَى النَّارِ (How patient they are in the face of the Fire!). He said: ما أصبرهم على فعل ما يعلمون أنه يصيرهم إلى النار . "How patient they are in doing that which they know will lead them to the Fire."

In al-Majma', from Ali ibn Ibrahim, from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), he said: ما أجرأهم على النار . "How audacious they are toward the Fire."

And from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): ما أعملهم بأعمال أهل النار . "How diligent they are in the deeds of the people of the Fire."

I say: The narrations are close in meaning. In the first, "patience in the face of the Fire" is interpreted as patience in the face of the cause of the Fire. In the second, it is interpreted as audacity toward it, which is a necessary consequence of such patience. In the third, it is interpreted as performing the deeds of the people of the Fire, which refers back to the meaning of the first narration.

Ayah

۞ لَّيْسَ ٱلْبِرَّ أَن تُوَلُّوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ قِبَلَ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ وَٱلْمَغْرِبِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْبِرَّ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ وَءَاتَى ٱلْمَالَ عَلَىٰ حُبِّهِۦ ذَوِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينَ وَٱبْنَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَٱلسَّآئِلِينَ وَفِى ٱلرِّقَابِ وَأَقَامَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَى ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَٱلْمُوفُونَ بِعَهْدِهِمْ إِذَا عَـٰهَدُوا۟ ۖ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ فِى ٱلْبَأْسَآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ وَحِينَ ٱلْبَأْسِ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا۟ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ (١٧٧)
Piety is not to turn your faces to the east or the west; rather, piety is [personified by] those who have faith in Allah and the Last Day, the angels, the Book, and the prophets, and who give their wealth, for the love of Him, to relatives, orphans, the needy, the traveller and the beggar, and for [the freeing of] the slaves, and maintain the prayer and give the zakat, and those who fulfill their covenants, when they pledge themselves, and those who are patient in stress and distress, and in the heat of battle. They are the ones who are true [to their covenant], and it is they who are the Godwary. (177)

(Commentary)

It has been said that disputes and contentions increased among the people after the changing of the qiblah (القبلة) — the direction of prayer — from Jerusalem to the Kaaba, and the quarreling persisted until this verse was revealed.

His saying, the Exalted: ليس البرّ أن تولوا وجوهكم قبل المشرق والمغرب (Piety is not that you turn your faces toward the east or the west): Piety (البرّ) with a kasrah on the ba means an abundance of goodness and benevolence. Piety (البرّ) with a fathah is its assimilated adjective (صفة مشبهة). Direction (القبل) with a kasrah then a fathah means side or direction, from which the term qiblah (القبلة) is derived, referring to a specific type of direction. "Near relatives" (ذووا القربى) refers to kinsmen. "Orphans" (اليتامى) is the plural of yatim, which refers to one who has no father. "The needy" (المساكين) is the plural of miskin, who is in a worse state than the poor (الفقير). "The traveler" (ابن السبيل) is one cut off from his family. "Slaves" (الرقاب) is the plural of raqabah, referring to the neck of a slave. "Hardship" (البأساء) is a verbal noun like bu’s, meaning severity and poverty. "Affliction" (الضراء) is a verbal noun like durr, referring to a human being being harmed by illness, injury, loss of wealth, or the death of a child. "Battle" (البأس) refers to the intensity of war.

His saying, the Exalted: ولكن البر من آمن بالله (but piety is [personified by] those who have faith in Allah): He turned from defining piety (البرّ) as an abstract concept to defining it through the person (البرّ) who embodies it, so that it serves as an explanation and definition of the men themselves, while simultaneously describing their attributes. This hints at the fact that a concept (المفهوم) devoid of an actual instance (المصداق) has no effect and possesses no merit. This is the method of the Quran in all its explanations: it clarifies stations (المقامات) and describes states (الأحوال) by defining the men who occupy them, rather than sufficing with a mere conceptual definition.

In short, His saying, "but piety is [personified by] those who have faith in Allah and the Last Day," is a definition of the pious (الأبرار) and an explanation of the reality of their state. He defined them first across all three levels—creed, action, and character—by His saying: "those who have faith in Allah"; secondly by His saying: "it is they who are the truthful"; and thirdly by His saying: "and it is they who are the God-conscious."

As for the first definition, He began by saying: من آمن بالله واليوم الآخر والملائكة والكتاب والنبيين (those who have faith in Allah, the Last Day, the angels, the Book, and the prophets). This encompasses all the true knowledge (المعارف الحقة) that Allah, the Glorified, desires His servants to believe in. The intended faith (الإيمان) here is the complete faith from which its effects never fail to manifest—neither in the heart through the occurrence of doubt, agitation, objection, or resentment toward anything that befalls one which the soul dislikes, nor in character or action. The evidence that this is what is intended is His saying at the end of the verse: أُولئك الذين صدقوا (it is they who are the truthful). He expressed truthfulness (الصدق) in an absolute sense, without restricting it to any specific act of the heart or the limbs. Thus, they are the true believers, truthful in their faith, as the Exalted said: فَلَا وَرَبِّكَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّىٰ يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيمَا شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لَا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنفُسِهِمْ حَرَجًا مِّمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَيُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيمًا (But no, by your Lord! They will not believe until they make you a judge in their disputes, then do not find within their hearts any dissent to your verdict and submit in total submission) [4:65]. Consequently, their state corresponds to the fourth level of the levels of faith (مراتب الإيمان) which was previously explained under His saying: إِذْ قَالَ لَهُ رَبُّهُ أَسْلِمْ قَالَ أَسْلَمْتُ (When his Lord said to him, ‘Submit,’ he said, ‘I submit to the Lord of all the worlds.’) [2:131].

Then the Exalted mentioned a portion of their deeds, saying: وآتى المال على حبه ذوي القربى واليتامى والمساكين وابن السبيل والسائلين وفي الرقاب وأقام الصلوة وآتى الزكوة (and give their wealth, for the love of Him, to relatives, orphans, the needy, the traveller and the beggars, and for [the freeing of] slaves, and maintain the prayer and give the zakat). He mentioned prayer (الصلوة)—which is a devotional ruling (حكم عبادي)—and the Exalted has said: إِنَّ الصَّلَاةَ تَنْهَىٰ عَنِ الْفَحْشَاءِ وَالْمُنكَرِ (Indeed the prayer prevents indecencies and wrongs) [29:45], and said: وَأَقِمِ الصَّلَاةَ لِذِكْرِي (and maintain the prayer for My remembrance) [20:14]. He mentioned the zakat (الزكوة)—which is a financial ruling (حكم مالي) concerning the welfare of livelihood—and before these two, He mentioned the giving of wealth, which is the spreading of goodness and the distribution of non-obligatory benevolence to meet the needs of the destitute and to support them.
