Then the Glorified mentioned a portion of their general character, saying: والموفون بعهدهم إذا عاهدوا والصابرين في البأساء والضراء وحين البأس (and those who keep their pledges when they pledge, and those who are patient in adversity and hardship, and in the time of battle). A pledge (العهد) is a commitment to something and a binding contract (العقد) for it. The Exalted mentioned it in an absolute sense, yet it does not include faith and the commitment to its rulings—as some have imagined—due to His saying, "when they pledge," for the commitment to faith and its requirements does not accept being restricted to one time over another, as is evident. Rather, in its absolute sense, it includes every promise a person makes and every word spoken as a commitment, such as saying, "I will surely do such-and-all" or "I will surely refrain," and every contract entered into in transactions, social interactions, and the like. Patience (الصبر) is steadfastness (الثبات) amidst difficulties when calamities strike or when clashing with adversaries. Although these two traits do not exhaust all virtuous characters, if they are realized, that which is beneath them is also realized. Fulfilling pledges and patience during hardships are two traits: one relates to stillness and the other to movement, which is fulfillment. Mentioning these two descriptions among their attributes is as if to say: when they speak a word, they proceed with it and do not recoil from it by wavering.

As for the second definition, His saying: أُولئك الذين صدقوا (it is they who are the truthful), it is a comprehensive description of the sum of virtues in knowledge and action. Truthfulness (الصدق) is a trait that accompanies all virtues, such as chastity, courage, wisdom, justice, and their branches. For a human being possesses nothing but belief, speech, and action; when he is truthful, these three coincide. Thus, he does not do except what he says, and he does not say except what he believes. Man is created with a primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — to accept the truth and submit to it inwardly, even if he displays the opposite outwardly. So, when he yields to the truth and is truthful in it, he says what he believes and does what he says. At that point, pure faith, virtuous character, and righteous deeds are perfected for him. The Exalted said: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَكُونُوا مَعَ الصَّادِقِينَ (O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and be with the truthful) [9:119]. The restriction (الحصر) in His saying, "it is they who are the truthful," emphasizes the definition and the clarification of the boundary. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: if you seek those who are truthful, then it is they who are the pious.

As for the third definition, His saying: وأُولئك هم المتقون (and it is they who are the God-conscious), the restriction is to demonstrate perfection, for if piety (البر) and truthfulness (الصدق) were not complete, God-consciousness (التقوى) — the state of being wary of Allah's boundaries — would not be complete.

The attributes of the pious (الأبرار) that the Exalted has clarified in this verse are the same as those He mentioned elsewhere. The Exalted said: إِنَّ الْأَبْرَارَ يَشْرَبُونَ مِن كَأْسٍ كَانَ مِزَاجُهَا كَافُورًا . عَيْنًا يَشْرَبُ بِهَا عِبَادُ اللَّهِ يُفَجِّرُونَهَا تَفْجِيرًا يُوفُونَ بِالنَّذْرِ وَيَخَافُونَ يَوْمًا كَانَ شَرُّهُ مُسْتَطِيرًا . وَيُطْعِمُونَ الطَّعَامَ عَلَىٰ حُبِّهِ مِسْكِينًا وَيَتِيمًا وَأَسِيرًا . إِنَّمَا نُطْعِمُكُمْ لِوَجْهِ اللَّهِ — until He said — وَجَزَاهُم بِمَا صَبَرُوا جَنَّةً وَحَرِيرًا (Indeed the pious shall drink from a cup whose mixture is camphor, a spring whereat the servants of Allah drink, making it gush forth as they please. They fulfill their vows and fear a day whose ill is widespread. They give food, for the love of Him, to the needy, the orphan and the prisoner, [saying,] ‘We feed you only for the sake of Allah...’ And He will reward them for their patience with a garden and [garments of] silk) [76:5-12]. In these verses, He mentioned faith in Allah and the Last Day, spending for the sake of Allah, fulfilling pledges, and patience. The Exalted also said: كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْأَبْرَارِ لَفِي عِلِّيِّينَ وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا عِلِّيُّونَ كِتَابٌ مَّرْقُومٌ يَشْهَدُهُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ . إِنَّ الْأَبْرَارَ لَفِي نَعِيمٍ — until He said — يُسْقَوْنَ مِن رَّحِيقٍ مَّخْتُومٍ — until He said — عَيْنًا يَشْرَبُ بِهَا الْمُقَرَّبُونَ (No indeed! The record of the pious is in Illiyyun. And what will show you what is Illiyyun? It is a written record, witnessed by those brought near [to Allah]. Indeed the pious shall be in bliss... they will be given to drink a pure wine, sealed... a spring whereat those brought near [to Allah] drink) [83:18-28]. By comparing these verses with the preceding ones, the reality of their description and the ultimate end of their affair becomes clear if you contemplate them. These verses have described them as "the servants of Allah" (عباد الله) and as "those brought near" (المقربون). Allah, the Glorified, described His servants, among other things, by saying: إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ (As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them) [15:42], and He described those brought near by saying: وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ . أُولَٰئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ (And the Foremost Ones are the foremost ones: they are the ones brought near [to Allah], in the gardens of bliss) [56:10-12]. These are the ones who are foremost in this world toward their Lord, and foremost in the Hereafter toward His bliss. If you were to continue the investigation into their state as provided by the verses, you would find wonders.

It has become clear from what has passed that the pious (الأبرار) are the people of the high level of faith, which is the fourth level according to what was previously explained. The Exalted said: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمَانَهُم بِظُلْمٍ أُولَٰئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ (Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be safety, and they are the [rightly] guided) [6:82].

His saying, the Exalted: والصابرين في البأساء (and those who are patient in adversity): This is in the accusative case (منصوب) for the purpose of praise (المدح), to magnify the matter of patience. It has been said that when speech becomes lengthy by mentioning one attribute after another, it is the style of the Arabs to intervene between the attributes with praise or blame, and to vary the declension between the nominative and accusative cases.

(Narrative Discussion)

From the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: من عمل بهذه الآية فقد استكمل الإيمان — "Whoever acts upon this verse has perfected faith."

I say: The reason for this is clear from what we have explained. It has been narrated from al-Zajjaj and al-Farra' that they said: "The verse is specific to the infallible prophets, because no one can fulfill these things in their entirety according to their true obligation except the prophets." This [view] arises from a lack of contemplation on what the verses signify and a confusion between spiritual stations (المقامات المعنوية). The verses of Surah al-Dahr were revealed concerning the People of the House (أهل بيت) of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, and Allah named them therein as "the pious" (أبرار), yet they were not prophets.

Indeed, their status is great. Allah has described the state of the "possessors of intellect" (أولي الألباب) who remember Allah standing, sitting, and on their sides, and reflect upon the creation of the heavens and the earth; then He mentioned their plea that Allah join them with the pious, saying: وَتَوَفَّنَا مَعَ الْأَبْرَارِ (and make us die with the pious) [3:193].

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi extracted from Abu ‘Amir al-Ash‘ari, who said: "I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, what is the completion of piety?' He said: 'That you act in secret as you act in public.'"

In al-Majma‘, from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام: "Near relatives" (ذوي القربى) refers to the relatives of the Prophet.

I say: It is as if this is an instance of identifying the foremost example (المصداق) in light of the Verse of Kinship (آية القربى).

In al-Kafi, from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: "The poor (الفقير) is one who does not ask people, and the needy (المسكين) is in more strained circumstances than him, and the destitute (البائس) is the most strained of them."

In al-Majma‘, from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام: "The traveler" (ابن السبيل) is the one who is cut off [from resources while traveling].

In al-Tahdhib, from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: He was asked about a slave who had entered a contract of manumission (مكاتب) but was unable to complete the payments, having paid some of them. He عليه‌السلام said: "It should be paid on his behalf from the wealth of charity (الصدقة), for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: وفي الرقاب (and for [the freeing of] slaves)."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: والصابرين في البأساء والضراء (and those who are patient in adversity and hardship), he عليه‌السلام said: "In hunger, thirst, and fear." And regarding His saying: وحين البأس (and in the time of battle), he عليه‌السلام said: "During combat."

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْقِصَاصُ فِى ٱلْقَتْلَى ۖ ٱلْحُرُّ بِٱلْحُرِّ وَٱلْعَبْدُ بِٱلْعَبْدِ وَٱلْأُنثَىٰ بِٱلْأُنثَىٰ ۚ فَمَنْ عُفِىَ لَهُۥ مِنْ أَخِيهِ شَىْءٌ فَٱتِّبَاعٌۢ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَأَدَآءٌ إِلَيْهِ بِإِحْسَـٰنٍ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ تَخْفِيفٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَرَحْمَةٌ ۗ فَمَنِ ٱعْتَدَىٰ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فَلَهُۥ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٧٨)
O you who have faith! Retribution is prescribed for you regarding the slain: freeman for freeman, slave for slave, and female for female. But if one is granted any extenuation by his brother, let the follow up [for the blood-money] be honourable, and let the payment to him be with kindness. That is a remission from your Lord and a mercy; and should anyone transgress after that, there shall be a painful punishment for him. (178)
وَلَكُمْ فِى ٱلْقِصَاصِ حَيَوٰةٌ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (١٧٩)
There is life for you in retribution, O you who possess intellects! Maybe you will be Godwary! (179)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: يا أيها الذين آمنوا كتب عليكم القصاص في القتلى الحر بالحر (O you who have faith! Retribution is prescribed for you regarding the slain: the free for the free... [2:178]). Directing the address specifically to the believers (المؤمنين) indicates that this ruling is exclusive to Muslims. As for others, such as the protected people (أهل الذمة) and their like, the verse is silent regarding them.

The relationship of this verse to His saying, the Exalted: « أَنَّ النَّفْسَ بِالنَّفْسِ » (...that a soul is for a soul... [5:45]) is one of explanation (التفسير). Therefore, there is no merit to the claim sometimes made that this verse abrogates the former, such that a free person is not killed for a slave, nor a man for a woman.

In brief, retribution (القصاص) is a verbal noun (مصدر) from the verb qāṣṣa (قاصّ), meaning to follow someone's tracks (قص أثره). From this comes the term "storyteller" (القصاص) for one who relates traditions and tales, as if he is following the tracks of those who passed. Retribution is named qiṣāṣ (القصاص) because it involves following the criminal in his crime, whereby there is inflicted upon him the like of what he inflicted upon another.

His saying, the Exalted: فمن عفي له من أخيه شيء (...But if one is granted any remission by his brother... [2:178]). The relative pronoun "one" (من) refers to the killer. Remission (العفو) for the killer only occurs regarding the right of retribution; thus, "any [thing]" (شيء) refers to that right. Its use in the indefinite form (تنكير) generalizes the ruling—meaning any right whatsoever, whether it be the entire right or a portion of it. For instance, if there are multiple heirs of the blood (أولياء الدم) and some of them waive their right in favor of the killer, retribution is no longer applicable; rather, blood money (الدية) is due. Referring to the heir of the blood as a "brother" (الأخ) is intended to stir feelings of love and compassion, and it alludes to the fact that forgiveness is more beloved.

His saying, the Exalted: فاتباع بالمعروف وأداء إليه بإحسان (...then the [pursuit of the] follow-up should be honorable, and the payment to him with kindness... [2:178]). This is a subject whose predicate is omitted, meaning: "it is incumbent upon him [the heir] to follow up with the killer in demanding the blood money with honorable conduct (المعروف)." This is derived from "following up" (الاتباع). Similarly, it is incumbent upon the killer to pay the blood money to his brother, the heir of the blood, with kindness (الإحسان), without procrastination that causes him harm.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلك تخفيف من ربكم ورحمة (...This is a concession from your Lord, and a mercy... [2:178]). That is, the ruling of transitioning from retribution to blood money is a concession from your Lord, so it must not be altered. The heir of the blood has no right to seek retribution after granting remission, for that would be an act of transgression (اعتداء). Thus, whoever transgresses by seeking retribution after remission shall have a painful punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: ولكم في القصاص حياة يا اولي الألباب لعلكم تتقون (There is life for you in retribution, O you who possess intellects, so that you may be God-conscious. [2:179]). This is an indication of the wisdom behind the legislation (حكمة التشريع). It refutes the misconception that might arise from the legislation of remission and blood money, and clarifies the merit and benefit found in forgiveness—which is the spreading of mercy and the preference for compassion—and that forgiveness is closer to the public interest. The gist is that while forgiveness contains concession and mercy, the general welfare is sustained by retribution (القصاص). Indeed, life (الحيوة) is guaranteed by nothing but retribution—not by forgiveness, nor blood money, nor anything else. A human being judges this to be so if he is a person of intellect (ذا لب). His saying, "so that you may be God-conscious" (لعلكم تتقون), refers to avoiding murder, and it serves as the rationale for the legislation of retribution.

Scholars have noted that this sentence—His saying, the Exalted: "There is life for you in retribution..."—despite its brevity, conciseness, fewness of letters, smoothness of expression, and purity of composition, is among the most eloquent verses of the Quran in its clarity and the highest in its eloquence (البلاغة). It combines strength of argumentation with beauty and subtlety of meaning, and delicacy of indication with the clarity of what is indicated. Before this, the eloquent Arabs had phrases regarding killing and retribution whose eloquence and robust style they admired, such as: "Killing some is life for all," and "Increase killing so that killing decreases." Most admired among them was: "Killing is the most effective deterrent to killing." However, this verse eclipsed them all and rendered them obsolete: "There is life for you in retribution." This verse has fewer letters and is easier to pronounce. It uses the definite form for "retribution" (القصاص) and the indefinite for "life" (الحيوة) to indicate that the result is broader and greater than the act of retribution itself. It encompasses the statement of the result and the reality of the benefit, which is "life." It contains the reality of the meaning that serves the ultimate goal, for it is retribution that leads to life, not "killing" (القتل) in an absolute sense; for some killing occurs as an act of aggression and does not lead to life. Furthermore, it encompasses other matters besides killing that lead to life, namely the various types of retribution for injuries other than killing. It also carries an additional meaning: the sense of "following up" (المتابعة) indicated by the word qiṣāṣ (القصاص), unlike their saying "killing is the most effective deterrent to killing." Moreover, it contains an encouragement and inducement, for it points to a life stored for people of which they are heedless, yet they possess it; thus, they should seize it. This is similar to saying: "There is for you in such a place, or with such a person, wealth and fortune." It also indicates that the Speaker intends nothing by this statement except the preservation of their interests and the care for their welfare, without any benefit returning to Him, as He said: "for you" (ولكم).

These are some aspects of the subtleties contained in this verse. Others may have mentioned further points that a researcher might encounter. However, the more one increases in contemplation (تدبر) of the verse, the more it increases its manifestations of beauty and overwhelms you with the brilliance of its light—and the word of Allah is the highest.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: الحرّ بالحرِّ (the free for the free): لا يقتل الحر بالعبد ولكن يضرب ضرباً شديداً ويغرم دية العبد وإن قتل رجل امرأة فأراد أولياء المقتول أن يقتلوه أدّوا نصف ديته إلى أولياء الرجل — "A free man is not killed for a slave, but he is to be beaten severely and fined the blood money of the slave. If a man kills a woman and the heirs of the slain woman wish to kill him, they must pay half of his blood money to the heirs of the man."

In al-Kafi, from al-Halabi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: فمن تصدق به فهو كفارة له (...yet whoever remits it charitably, it shall be an atonement for him... [5:45]). He said: يكفر عنه من ذنوبه بقدر ما عفی — 'His sins are atoned for in proportion to what he forgave.' I also asked him about the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: فمن عفي له من أخيه شيء فاتباع بالمعروف وأداء اليه بإحسان (...But if one is granted any remission by his brother, then the [pursuit of the] follow-up should be honorable, and the payment to him with kindness... [2:178]). He said: ينبغي للذي له الحق أن لا يعسر أخاه إذا كان قد صالحه على دية وينبغي للذي عليه الحق أن لا يمطل أداه إذا قدر على ما يعطيه ويؤدي إليه بإحسان — 'It is fitting for the one who holds the right not to distress his brother if he has settled with him for blood money, and it is fitting for the one who owes the right not to procrastinate in its payment if he is able to give what he owes, and to pay it to him with kindness.' I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: فمن اعتدى بعد ذلك فله عذاب أليم (...Then whoever transgresses after that, there is a painful punishment for him. [2:178]). He said: هو الرجل يقبل الدية أو يعفو أو يصالح ثم يعتدي فيقتل كما قال الله عز وجل — 'It refers to a man who accepts the blood money, or forgives, or settles, and then transgresses by killing [the murderer], just as Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said.'"

I say: The narrations regarding these meanings are numerous.

(Scientific Discussion)

At the time of the revelation of the verse of retribution and before it, the Arabs believed in retribution for murder, but they did not limit it by any boundary. Rather, it followed the strength or weakness of the tribes. Sometimes a man was killed for a man and a woman for a woman, following the path of equality in killing. At other times, ten were killed for one, a free man for a slave, or a leader for a subordinate. Sometimes an entire tribe would annihilate another tribe for a single person killed from among them.

The Jews believed in retribution as mentioned in chapters twenty-one and twenty-two of Exodus and thirty-five of Numbers. The Quran has related this where the Exalted says: « وَكَتَبْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ فِيهَا أَنَّ النَّفْسَ بِالنَّفْسِ وَالْعَيْنَ بِالْعَيْنِ وَالْأَنفَ بِالْأَنفِ وَالْأُذُنَ بِالْأُذُنِ وَالسِّنَّ بِالسِّنِّ وَالْجُرُوحَ قِصَاصٌ » (In it We prescribed for them: a soul for a soul, an eye for an eye, a nose for a nose, an ear for an ear, a tooth for a tooth, and for wounds, retaliation... [5:45]).

The Christians, as is reported, recognized nothing in the case of murder except forgiveness and blood money. Other nations and peoples, despite their differing classes, were not devoid of retribution for murder in general, even if it was not governed by a complete standard until recent centuries.

Islam followed a middle path (مسلكاً وسطاً) in this regard between total abolition and absolute imposition. It affirmed retribution but abolished its necessity (تعينه); rather, it permitted forgiveness and blood money. Then, it balanced retribution by establishing equivalence between the killer and the slain: the free for the free, the slave for the slave, and the female for the female.

Objections have been raised against retribution in general, and retribution for murder in particular, claiming that the civil laws established by "advanced nations" do not see its permissibility or implementation among humans today.

They say: Killing for killing is something that a human finds repugnant, from which his nature recoils, and which his conscience forbids when presented to him as a matter of mercy and service to humanity. They say: If the first killing was the loss of one individual, the second killing is a loss upon a loss. They say: Retribution by killing stems from cruelty and a desire for revenge (الانتقام), a trait that must be removed from people through public education; the killer should instead be subjected to a reformative punishment, such as imprisonment or hard labor. They say: A criminal is only a criminal if he is mentally ill, so the duty is to place the criminal killer in psychiatric hospitals for treatment. They say: Civil laws follow the existing society, and since society is not fixed in one state, laws should be likewise; there is no reason for retribution to remain fixed for society forever, including today's advanced societies. It is necessary for society to benefit from the existence of its individuals as much as possible, and it is possible to punish the criminal with something less than death that equals it in fruit and result, such as life imprisonment or a term of years, thereby combining two rights: the right of society and the right of the heirs of the blood. These points are the pillars of what the deniers of the legislation of retribution for murder have mentioned.

The Quran has answered all these points with a single statement, which is the saying of the Exalted: « مَن قَتَلَ نَفْسًا بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ أَوْ فَسَادٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَكَأَنَّمَا قَتَلَ النَّاسَ جَمِيعًا وَمَنْ أَحْيَاهَا فَكَأَنَّمَا أَحْيَا النَّاسَ جَمِيعًا » (...whoever slays a soul, without [its being guilty of] manslaughter or corruption on the earth, is as though he had slain all mankind, and whoever saves a life is as though he had saved all mankind... [5:32]).

The explanation of this is as follows: Although the laws current among human individuals are conventional and social constructs (وضعية اعتبارية) that take into account the interests of human society, the underlying efficient cause (العلة العاملة) in them from their origin is the external human nature (الطبيعة الخارجية الإنسانية) which calls for completing its deficiency and fulfilling its ontological needs (حوائجها التكوينية). This external reality is not the numerical count of humans nor the single social form, for these are themselves products of the human existential being; rather, it is the human being and his nature. There is no difference between a single human and thousands of them gathered together in the fact that all are human; the weight of the individual and the collective is one from the perspective of existence (الوجود).

This existential nature is equipped within itself with powers and tools by which it wards off non-existence from itself, because it is fashioned upon the love of existence. It repels everything that strips away life by whatever means possible and to whatever end it reaches, even killing and execution. Therefore, you will not find a human whose primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — does not judge the permissibility of killing one who intends to kill him and will not desist except by it. These "advanced nations" themselves do not hesitate to go to war in defense of their independence, freedom, and nationality—so what of one who intends to kill their souls to the last one? They ward off the nullification of law by any means, even by killing, and they resort to preserving their interests through war if the disease cannot be treated otherwise—that war in which there is the destruction of the world and the ruin of crops and progeny. Nations continue to advance in armaments while others equip themselves to respond. All of this is nothing but a regard for the state of society and the preservation of its life. Society is but one of the products of nature; so why does nature permit massive killing, annihilation, and destruction to preserve one of its products—civil society—yet not permit it to preserve the life of nature itself? Why does it permit the killing of one who intends to kill but has not yet done so, but not permit it for one who intended and acted? Why does nature judge by reciprocity in historical events—such that he who does an atom's weight of good shall see it, and he who does an atom's weight of evil shall see it, and every action has a reaction in its law—yet it considers killing in the case of murder to be injustice and thus negates its own judgment?

Furthermore, Islam does not see any value in this world for a human by which he is established, nor a weight by which he is weighed, unless he follows the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. Thus, the weight of human society and the weight of a single monotheist are equal in its view; it is mandatory that their ruling be one. Whoever kills a believer is as if he has killed all of mankind from the perspective of his disparagement and violation of the sanctity of Truth, just as whoever kills a soul is as if he has killed all of mankind from the perspective of existential nature. As for the "civilized nations," they do not care for religion; if the nobility of religion held a value or weight in their eyes equal to—let alone exceeding—civil society in merit, they would have judged regarding it as they judged regarding the latter.

Moreover, Islam legislates for the world, not for a specific people or a particular nation. The "advanced nations" only judged as they did after they were convinced of the complete education (التربية) of their individuals, the good conduct of their governments, and the evidence of statistics in cases of crimes and atrocities showing that the existing education is effective and that the nation, as a result of its education, recoils from murder and atrocity. Thus, it only occurs among them as an anomaly, and when it does, they accept punishment with something less than death. Islam does not refuse to permit this education and its effect—which is forgiveness—while the principle of retribution (القصاص) remains standing.

This is alluded to by His saying, the Exalted, in the verse of retribution: "But if one is granted any remission by his brother, then the [pursuit of the] follow-up should be honorable, and the payment to him with kindness." The language is the language of education (التربية). If a people reach a point where they are convinced that public pride lies in forgiveness, they will not deviate from it toward the path of revenge.

As for nations other than these, the matter is quite the opposite. The proof for this is what we witness of the state of people and the perpetrators of atrocities and corruption. They are not deterred by imprisonment or hard labor, nor are they stopped by sermon or advice. They possess no ambition nor steadfastness upon any human right. The life prepared for them in prisons is more comfortable, higher, and more splendid than the wretched and miserable life they have for themselves. They are not frightened by blame or censure, nor are they startled by prison or beating. We also witness the increase in the number of atrocities in statistics day by day. Thus, the general ruling encompassing both groups—the majority of whom belong to the second—can only be retribution and the permissibility of forgiveness. If the nation becomes refined and is raised with a successful education, it will take to forgiveness (and Islam spares no effort in education); but if it only pursues decline, or is ungrateful for the blessings of its Lord and becomes rebellious, retribution is applied among them, and forgiveness remains permissible alongside it.

As for what they mentioned regarding the discourse of mercy and compassion for humanity, not every compassion is praiseworthy, nor is every mercy a virtue. To exercise mercy in the case of a hard-hearted criminal, a rebellious sinner, and a transgressor against life and honor is an act of cruelty toward righteous individuals. In its absolute application, there is the disruption of order, the destruction of humanity, and the nullification of virtue.

As for what they mentioned regarding it being a matter of cruelty and a desire for revenge, the response is the same as the previous one. Taking revenge for the oppressed from his oppressor to manifest justice and truth is not blameworthy or ugly, nor is the love of justice among the base traits. Furthermore, the legislation of retribution for murder is not purely for revenge; rather, it contains the criterion of public education and the closing of the door to corruption.

As for what they mentioned regarding the crime of murder being among the mental illnesses that must be treated in hospitals, this is among the excuses—and what an "excuse" it is!—that cause the spread of murder and indecency and the growth of crime in human society. If any person among us who loves killing and corruption knew that such an act would be considered a mental illness and a heard excuse, requiring the government to treat him with care and compassion, and that the ruling and executive power believed this about him, would he not proceed every day to kill?

As for what they mentioned regarding the necessity of benefiting from the existence of criminals through forced labor and the like while imprisoning them and preventing them from entering society: if this were a truth leaning upon reality, why do they not judge similarly in cases of legal execution that exist in all the laws currently circulating among nations? This is only because of the importance they see for execution in its proper places. It has already been stated that the individual and society, in the view of nature, are equal in terms of importance.

Ayahs

كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ إِن تَرَكَ خَيْرًا ٱلْوَصِيَّةُ لِلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبِينَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۖ حَقًّا عَلَى ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٨٠)
Prescribed for you, when death approaches any of you and he leaves behind any property, is that he make a bequest for his parents and relatives, in an honourable manner—an obligation on the Godwary. (180)
فَمَنۢ بَدَّلَهُۥ بَعْدَ مَا سَمِعَهُۥ فَإِنَّمَآ إِثْمُهُۥ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُبَدِّلُونَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (١٨١)
And should anyone alter it after hearing it, its sin shall indeed lie on those who alter it. Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (181)
فَمَنْ خَافَ مِن مُّوصٍ جَنَفًا أَوْ إِثْمًا فَأَصْلَحَ بَيْنَهُمْ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٨٢)
But should someone, fearing deviance or sin on the testator’s behalf, set things right between them, there is no sin upon him. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (182)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ إِن تَرَكَ خَيْرًا الْوَصِيَّةُ "Prescribed for you, when death approaches any of you and he leaves behind any property, is the making of a bequest" (2:180). The phrasing of the verse is that of obligation (الوجوب), for the term "prescribing" (الكتابة) is used in the Quran in contexts of decisiveness and necessity (اللزوم). This is further supported by His saying at the end of the verse, "as a duty" (حقاً), for a "duty" (الحق), like "prescribing," implies the meaning of necessity. However, the qualification of this duty with His saying, "upon the God-conscious" (على المتقين), weakens the indication of a strict obligation or a firm decree (العزيمة); it would have been more appropriate for an obligation to say, "a duty upon the believers." In any case, it has been said that the verse is abrogated (منسوخة) by the verse of inheritance. Even if that were so, what is abrogated is the mandatory nature (الفرض), not the recommendation (الندب) or the inherent desirability of the act. Perhaps the qualification of the duty to the "God-conscious" (المتقين) — those who possess godwariness (التقوى), the inner shield of consciousness that protects one from Divine displeasure — in the verse is intended to convey this purpose.

What is meant by "property" (الخير) is wealth (المال), and it appears to refer to substantial wealth that is of significant value, rather than a small amount that is considered negligible. What is meant by "honorable manner" (المعروف) is the recognized and customary practice of kindness and benevolence.

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَن بَدَّلَهُ بَعْدَ مَا سَمِعَهُ فَإِنَّمَا إِثْمُهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يُبَدِّلُونَهُ "Then whoever alters it after hearing it, its sin shall rest only on those who alter it" (2:181). The pronoun in "its sin" (إثمه) refers back to the act of "altering" (التبديل), while the remaining pronouns refer to the "bequest in an honorable manner" (الوصية بالمعروف). The latter is a verbal noun (مصدر) for which both grammatical possibilities are permissible. He said, "on those who alter it" (على الذين يبدلونه), rather than simply "on them," to indicate the cause of the sin, which is the alteration of the honorable bequest, and to allow the subsequent verse to follow logically from it.

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنْ خَافَ مِن مُّوصٍ جَنَفًا أَوْ إِثْمًا فَأَصْلَحَ بَيْنَهُمْ فَلَا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ "But should someone fear partiality or sin on the part of a testator, and settle the matter between them, there is no sin upon him" (2:182). "Partiality" (الجنف) is inclination and deviation. It is said that it refers to the outward turning of the feet, just as al-hanaf (الحنف) with the letter ḥā’ refers to their inward turning. In any case, what is meant here is an inclination toward sin, as evidenced by the context of "sin" (الإثم). The verse is a derivation from the preceding one. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is that the sin of alteration falls only upon those who alter an honorable bequest. From this it follows that if someone fears that the testator's bequest involves sin or an inclination toward it, and he rectifies the matter between the parties by returning it to that which contains no sin, then there is no sin upon him. This is because he has not altered an honorable bequest; rather, he has only altered that which contained sin or partiality.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, al-Tahdhib, and the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi—the wording being from the latter—it is narrated from Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: "I asked him whether a bequest is permissible for an heir. He replied: 'Yes,' then he recited this verse: إن ترك خيراً الوصية للوالدين والأقربين ('If he leaves behind property, the bequest is for parents and relatives')."

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq, from his father, from Imam Ali عليه‌السلام, who said: "Whoever does not make a bequest at the time of his death for those of his relatives who do not inherit has sealed his deeds with a sin."

Also in the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام regarding the verse, he said: "It is a right (حق) that Allah has placed in the wealth of the people for the Master of this Affair (صاحب هذا الأمر)." I said: "Is there a defined limit for that?" He said: "Yes." I asked: "How much?" He replied: "The minimum is a sixth and the maximum is a third."

I say: Al-Saduq also narrated this meaning in al-Faqih from him عليه‌السلام. This is a subtle derivation from the verse when joined with the saying of the Exalted: النَّبِيُّ أَوْلَىٰ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَأَزْوَاجُهُ أُمَّهَاتُهُمْ وَأُولُو الْأَرْحَامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُهَاجِرِينَ إِلَّا أَن تَفْعَلُوا إِلَىٰ أَوْلِيَائِكُم مَّعْرُوفًا كَانَ ذَٰلِكَ فِي الْكِتَابِ مَسْطُورًا "The Prophet is closer to the faithful than their own souls, and his wives are their mothers. The blood relatives are closer to each other in the Book of Allah than the [other] faithful and the Emigrants, unless you should do some kindness to your friends. That is written in the Book" (33:6). This verse is the one that abrogated the ruling of inheritance through brotherhood that existed at the beginning of Islam; it negated inheritance through brotherhood and established it for kinship. Then it excepted the "kindness" (معروف) done for the "guardians" (الأولياء). The Prophet has been counted as a guardian (ولي), and the pure ones from his progeny are guardians for them. This excepted "kindness" is the subject of His saying, the Exalted: "If he leaves behind property, the bequest..."—and they are the near of kin—so understand this.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from one of the two Imams (al-Baqir or al-Sadiq) عليهما السلام regarding the saying of the Exalted, "Prescribed for you, when death approaches..." the verse. He عليه‌السلام said: "It is abrogated; it was abrogated by the verse of fixed shares (آية الفرائض), which are the inheritances."

I say: The way to reconcile the previous narrations with this one is that what is abrogated from the verse is only the obligation (الوجوب), while the recommendation (الاستحباب) remains as it is.

In al-Majma', from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام regarding His saying, "But should someone fear partiality or sin on the part of a testator..." the verse. He said: "Partiality (الجنف) is when it is done by way of error, where he does not know what is permissible."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام said: "When a man makes his bequest, it is not permissible for the executor (الوصي) to change any bequest he makes; rather, he must carry it out as he bequeathed, unless he bequeaths something other than what Allah commanded, thereby sinning in the bequest and acting unjustly. In that case, it is permissible for the executor to return it to the truth. For example, a man who has heirs might leave all the wealth to some of his heirs and deprive others; the executor is permitted to return it to the truth, and that is His saying: 'partiality or sin' (جنفاً أو إثماً). Partiality (الجنف) is the inclination toward some heirs over others, and sin (الإثم) is to command the building of fire-temples or the production of intoxicants; it is permissible for the executor not to act upon any of that."

I say: From the meaning of "partiality" (الجنف) mentioned in the narration, the meaning of His saying, "and settle the matter between them" (فأصلح بينهم), becomes clear. What is meant is settling the matter between the heirs due to the occurrence of a dispute among them arising from the partiality of the testator.

In al-Kafi, from Muhammad ibn Suqah, who said: "I asked Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: 'Then whoever alters it after hearing it, its sin shall rest only on those who alter it.' He said: 'It was abrogated by the one after it, His saying: "But should someone fear partiality or sin on the part of a testator, and settle the matter between them, there is no sin upon him."' He said: 'It means the executor (الموصى إليه); if he fears partiality from the testator regarding his children in what he bequeathed to him—in that which does not please Allah and is contrary to the truth—then there is no sin upon him, meaning upon the executor, to alter it toward the truth and toward that which pleases Allah from the path of truth.'"

I say: This is an instance of explaining the verse by the verse (تفسير الآية بالآية). Applying the term "abrogation" (النسخ) to it is not according to the technical definition (الاصطلاح). It has been mentioned previously that in their speech, "abrogation" is sometimes applied to meanings other than those defined by the scholars of the principles of jurisprudence (الأصوليون).

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلصِّيَامُ كَمَا كُتِبَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (١٨٣)
O you who have faith! Prescribed for you is fasting as it was prescribed for those who were before you, so that you may be Godwary. (183)
أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ ۚ فَمَن كَانَ مِنكُم مَّرِيضًا أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِّنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُطِيقُونَهُۥ فِدْيَةٌ طَعَامُ مِسْكِينٍ ۖ فَمَن تَطَوَّعَ خَيْرًا فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّهُۥ ۚ وَأَن تَصُومُوا۟ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (١٨٤)
That for known days. But should any of you be sick or on a journey, let it be a [similar] number of other days. Those who find it straining shall be liable to atonement by feeding a needy person. Should anyone do good of his own accord, that is better for him, and to fast is better for you, should you know. (184)
شَهْرُ رَمَضَانَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ فِيهِ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ هُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ وَبَيِّنَـٰتٍ مِّنَ ٱلْهُدَىٰ وَٱلْفُرْقَانِ ۚ فَمَن شَهِدَ مِنكُمُ ٱلشَّهْرَ فَلْيَصُمْهُ ۖ وَمَن كَانَ مَرِيضًا أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِّنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ ۗ يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ بِكُمُ ٱلْيُسْرَ وَلَا يُرِيدُ بِكُمُ ٱلْعُسْرَ وَلِتُكْمِلُوا۟ ٱلْعِدَّةَ وَلِتُكَبِّرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ مَا هَدَىٰكُمْ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (١٨٥)
The month of Ramadan is one in which the Quran was sent down as guidance to mankind, with manifest proofs of guidance and the Criterion. So let those of you who witness it fast [in] it, and as for someone who is sick or on a journey, let it be a [similar] number of other days. Allah desires ease for you, and He does not desire hardship for you, and so that you may complete the number and magnify Allah for guiding you, and that you may give thanks. (185)

(Commentary)

The context of these three verses indicates, firstly, that they were all revealed together. His saying, the Exalted: "for a fixed number of days" (أياماً معدودات) at the beginning of the second verse is an adverbial phrase (ظرف) related to His saying: "fasting" (الصيام) in the first verse. His saying, the Exalted: "the month of Ramaḍān" (شهر رمضان) in the third verse is either the predicate (خبر) of an omitted subject (مبتدأ)—which is the pronoun referring back to His saying "for a fixed number of days," the implied meaning being: "they are the month of Ramaḍān"—or it is a subject for an omitted predicate, the implied meaning being: "the month of Ramaḍān is that whose fasting has been prescribed for you." Alternatively, it is an appositive (بدل) for "fasting" (الصيام) in His saying: "Prescribed for you is fasting" in the first verse. Regardless of the grammatical interpretation, it serves as an exposition and clarification (بيان وإيضاح) of the "fixed number of days" in which fasting was prescribed. Thus, the three verses together constitute a single discourse aimed at a single purpose: explaining the obligation of fasting the month of Ramaḍān.

Secondly, the context of the verses indicates that a portion of the speech placed in these three verses serves as a preamble and introduction (توطئة وتمهيد) for another portion. By this, I mean that the first two verses were composed to serve as a preface intended to calm the souls and secure their tranquility and stability against anxiety and agitation. This is done when the speaker's aim is to convey something for which non-compliance or refusal to accept is feared, because the forthcoming ruling or news is inherently heavy and difficult for the addressee. Therefore, you see that the first two verses are composed of sentences, none of which are devoid of guiding the mind of the addressee toward the legislation of the Ramaḍān fast with gentleness and suitability. This is achieved by mentioning that which removes estrangement and agitation, brings about the soul's contentment, and breaks the surge of defiance and arrogance, by pointing to types of mitigation and facilitation (تخفيف وتسهيل) observed in the legislation of this ruling, alongside the immediate and deferred good it contains.

Therefore, when He mentioned the prescription of fasting upon them by saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الصِّيَامُ "O you who have faith! Prescribed for you is fasting," He followed it with His saying: كَمَا كُتِبَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ "as it was prescribed for those who were before you." That is: you should not find it burdensome or feel estranged by its legislation for you and its prescription upon you, for this ruling is not restricted to you; rather, it is a ruling established for the nations prior to you, and you are not unique in this. Furthermore, in acting upon this ruling, there is the hope for what you seek and desire through your faith, which is godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah and guarding oneself against His displeasure — which is the best provision for one who believes in Allah and the Last Day, and you are the believers. This is His saying, the Exalted: لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ "so that you may be God-conscious." Moreover, this act, in which there is hope for godwariness for you and those before you, does not consume all your time, nor most of it; rather, it is only for a few, specific, numbered days. This is His saying, the Exalted: أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَاتٍ "for a fixed number of days." Indeed, the use of the indefinite form (تنكير) for "days" (أياماً) indicates minimization (تحقير), and describing them as "numbered" (معدودات) highlights the ease of the matter, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَشَرَوْهُ بِثَمَنٍ بَخْسٍ دَرَاهِمَ مَعْدُودَةٍ "And they sold him for a cheap price, a few dirhams" (Yusuf: 20). Furthermore, We have considered the side of those for whom the essence of fasting is difficult, such as those who can only fast with extreme hardship; such a person must replace it with a ransom (فدية) that is neither difficult nor burdensome for him, which is the feeding of a needy person. This is His saying, the Exalted: فَمَن كَانَ مِنكُم مَّرِيضًا أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ "So should any of you be sick or on a journey..." until His saying: فِدْيَةٌ طَعَامُ مِسْكِينٍ "...a redemption by feeding a needy person." If this act contains your own good and observes as much mitigation and facilitation for you as possible, then it is better for you to perform it with obedience and desire, without dislike, heaviness, or hesitation. For whoever does good of his own accord, it is better for him than performing it out of compulsion. This is His saying, the Exalted: فَمَن تَطَوَّعَ خَيْرًا فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّهُ "And whoever does good of his own accord, it is better for him." Thus, the discourse placed in the two verses, as you see, is a preamble and introduction for His saying, the Exalted, in the third verse: فَمَن شَهِدَ مِنكُمُ الشَّهْرَ فَلْيَصُمْهُ "So let those of you who witness it fast [in] it."

Accordingly, His saying, the Exalted, in the first verse: "Prescribed for you is fasting" (كتب عليكم الصيام) is an informative statement (إخبار) about the realization of the prescription, not the initial enactment (إنشاء) of the ruling, as in His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْقِصَاصُ فِي الْقَتْلَى "O you who have faith! Retribution is prescribed for you regarding the slain" (al-Baqarah: 178), and His saying, the Exalted: كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ إِن تَرَكَ خَيْرًا الْوَصِيَّةُ لِلْوَالِدَيْنِ وَالْأَقْرَبِينَ "Prescribed for you, when death approaches any of you and if he leaves any property, is that he make a bequest for his parents and near relatives" (al-Baqarah: 180). For there is a difference between retribution for the slain and bequests for parents and relatives, and fasting. Retribution for the slain is something that accords with the sense of revenge raging in the souls of the victims' guardians, and it suits the instinctive stinginess in human nature to see the killer alive, safe, and living without regard for the murder he committed. Similarly, the sense of compassion and mercy for kin calls the souls to show mercy to parents and relatives, especially at the time of death and permanent separation. Thus, these two—retribution and bequests—are rulings naturally accepted by human dispositions, agreeing with what they necessitate. Therefore, informing about them by enacting them does not require the preparation of a preface or the preamble of an exposition. This is in contrast to the ruling of fasting, for it necessitates depriving the souls of their greatest desires and most of what they incline toward, namely eating, drinking, and sexual intercourse. Therefore, it is heavy upon the nature and disliked by the soul. Directing its ruling to the addressees—who are the general mass of the legally responsible (المكلفين)—requires a preparation and preamble to gladden their souls and cause their natures to yearn for its acceptance and adoption. For this reason, His saying: "Retribution is prescribed for you..." and His saying: "Prescribed for you, when death approaches any of you..." were enactments (إنشاء) of the ruling without the need for a preparatory preface, unlike His saying: "Prescribed for you is fasting," which is an informative report about the ruling and a preparation for its enactment in His saying: "So let those of you who witness it fast [in] it," through the sum of the seven segments contained in the two verses.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أيها الذين آمنوا (O you who have faith!): Bringing this address is to remind them of an attribute within them, which is faith (الإيمان). When they attend to this attribute, they must accept the ruling their Lord presents to them, even if it is contrary to their desires and habits. The verse of retribution also began with this for the reason you have heard—that the Christians used to favor pardon over retribution, even though other religious and non-religious groups upheld retribution.

His saying, the Exalted: كتب عليكم الصيام كما كتب على الذين من قبلكم (Prescribed for you is fasting as it was prescribed for those who were before you): "Writing" (الكتابة) is well-known in meaning, and it is used as a metonym for obligation (الفرض), determination (العزيمة), and the final decree (القضاء الحتم), as in His saying: كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا وَرُسُلِي "Allah has ordained: ‘I shall surely prevail, I and My apostles’" (al-Mujadilah: 21), and His saying, the Exalted: وَنَكْتُبُ مَا قَدَّمُوا وَآثَارَهُمْ "and We write what they have sent ahead and their effects" (Ya Sin: 12), and His saying, the Exalted: وَكَتَبْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ فِيهَا أَنَّ النَّفْسَ بِالنَّفْسِ "And in it We prescribed for them: a life for a life" (al-Ma'idah: 45).

"Fasting" (الصيام) and (الصوم) are linguistically two verbal nouns meaning to abstain (الكف) from an action, such as abstaining from eating, drinking, sexual intercourse, speaking, walking, and so on. It is sometimes said that it is specifically abstaining from what the soul desires and yearns for. Then its usage became predominant in the Sacred Law (الشرع) for abstaining from specific things from the break of dawn until sunset with intention (النية).

The meaning of "those who were before you" (الذين من قبلكم) refers to the past nations among those who preceded the appearance of Islam from the nations of the prophets, such as the nations of Moses, Jesus, and others. This meaning is what is customary from the use of this phrase in the Quran wherever it appears. His saying: "as it was prescribed for those who were before you" is not in a position of absolute generality regarding individuals, nor is it for exact comparison (التنظير). Thus, it does not indicate that fasting was prescribed for all the nations of the prophets without exception, nor that the fasting prescribed for them was the same as the fasting prescribed for us in terms of time, specifics, and attributes. The comparison in the verse is only in terms of the essence of fasting and abstention, not in terms of its specifics.

What is meant by "those who were before you" are the previous nations of religious people (المليين) in general. The Quran did not specify who they were, except that the apparent meaning of His saying: "as it was prescribed" is that these were people of a religion (الملة) upon whom this was obligated. There is nothing in the Torah and Gospel currently in the hands of the Jews and Christians that indicates the obligation and prescription of fasting; rather, the two books only praise it and magnify its importance. However, they fast for a fixed number of days in the year until today in various forms: such as fasting from meat, fasting from milk, and fasting from eating and drinking. In the Quran, there is the story of Zechariah's fast from speaking, and likewise Mary's fast from speaking.

Rather, fasting is a worship (عبادة) — the act of humble submission and devotion to Allah — inherited from even non-religious peoples, as is reported from ancient Egypt, ancient Greece, and the Romans. The idolaters of India fast even today. Indeed, its being an act of worship that brings one closer (قربية) is among the things man is guided to by his primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — as will follow.

It is sometimes said that "those who were before you" refers to the Jews and Christians, or the previous prophets, based on narrations that are not free from weakness.

His saying, the Exalted: لعلكم تتقون (so that you may be God-conscious): The idolaters used to fast to please their gods or to extinguish the fire of their anger if they committed a crime or a sin, or when they wanted a need to be fulfilled. This makes fasting a transaction and an exchange whereby the servant gives the Lord's need so that He may fulfill the servant's need, or obtains His pleasure so that He may obtain the servant's pleasure. But Allah, the Glorified, is far too exalted for poverty, need, being affected, or harm to be conceived in His right. In short, He, the Glorified, is free from every deficiency. Thus, whatever beautiful effect worship gives—any worship and any effect—it only returns to the servant, not to the Lord, the Exalted and Sanctified. Just as sins are likewise; the Exalted said: إِنْ أَحْسَنتُمْ أَحْسَنتُمْ لِأَنفُسِكُمْ وَإِنْ أَسَأْتُمْ فَلَهَا "If you do good, you will do good to your own souls, and if you do evil, it will be [to their detriment]" (al-Isra: 7). This is what the Noble Quran points to in its teaching by returning the effects of acts of obedience and disobedience to the human being, whose only state is poverty and need. The Exalted said: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ أَنتُمُ الْفُقَرَاءُ إِلَى اللَّهِ وَاللَّهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ "O mankind! You are the ones who stand in need of Allah, and Allah—He is the All-sufficient" (Fatir: 15).

He points to this specifically regarding fasting with His saying: "so that you may be God-conscious" (لعلكم تتقون). That godwariness (التقوى) is expected to be attained through fasting is something beyond doubt. Every human being feels by his primordial nature (الفطرة) that whoever wishes to connect with the world of purity and loftiness, and to ascend to the level of perfection and spirituality, must first refrain from indulging in physical pleasures, restrain himself from the defiance of bodily desires, and sanctify himself from clinging to the earth. In short, he must guard against (يتقي) that which, by being occupied with it, distances him from the Lord, the Blessed and Exalted. This is a godwariness that is only attained through fasting and abstaining from desires. Closer than that, and more relevant to the state of the general mass of people—both those of this world and the hereafter—is to guard against the common trials of permissible desires like eating, drinking, and sexual intercourse, so that he may gain training in guarding against (اتقاء) prohibitions and avoiding them. Through this, his will is disciplined in abstaining from sins and drawing closer to Allah, the Glorified. For he who answers the caller of Allah regarding permissible desires and hears and obeys, will be even more hearing and obedient regarding the prohibitions and sins of Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: أياماً معدودات (for a fixed number of days): It is in the accusative case as an adverbial phrase (منصوب على الظرفية) with the implied preposition "in" (في), and it is related to His saying: "fasting" (الصيام). It has already been mentioned that the indefinite form of "days" and its description as "numbered" is to indicate the minimization of the task in terms of burden and hardship to encourage the legally responsible (المكلف). It has also been mentioned that His saying: "The month of Ramaḍān, in which the Quran was sent down..." is an exposition (بيان) of the "days," so the "fixed number of days" refers to the month of Ramaḍān.

Some exegetes have mentioned that the "fixed number of days" refers to three days of every month and the fast of the day of ʿĀshūrāʾ. Some said: the three days are the "white days" (الايام البيض) of every month and the fast of the day of ʿĀshūrāʾ; the Messenger of Allah and the Muslims used to fast them, then His saying, the Exalted: "The month of Ramaḍān, in which the Quran was sent down..." was revealed, so it abrogated that, and the obligation was settled on the fast of the month of Ramaḍān. They relied for this on many narrations from the paths of the Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jamāʿah which are not themselves free from discrepancy and contradiction.

What makes the falsehood of this view apparent is, firstly: that fasting, as has been said, is a general and comprehensive worship. Had the matter been as they say, history would have recorded it, and no one would have differed regarding its establishment and then its abrogation. Furthermore, the attachment of the day of ʿĀshūrāʾ to the three days of every month in the obligation or recommendation of fasting—like its being one of the Islamic festivals—is among the innovations of the Umayyads (may Allah curse them). They annihilated the progeny of the Messenger of Allah and his household (أهل بيته) by killing their men, taking their women and children captive, and plundering their wealth in the Event of al-Ṭaff. Then they sought blessings from that day, took it as a festival, and legislated its fasting to seek blessings through it. They fabricated virtues and blessings for it and inserted hadiths indicating that it was an Islamic festival, rather, one of the general festivals known to the Arabs of the Jāhiliyyah, the Jews, and the Christians since the mission of Moses and Jesus. None of that was true. The day has no religious significance to become a religious-national festival like Nowruz or Mihrajān among the Persians, nor did any event of conquest or victory occur on it to make it an Islamic day like the Day of the Mission (المبعث) or the Prophet's birthday, nor does it have a religious aspect to become a religious festival like ʿĪd al-Fiṭr or ʿĪd al-Aḍḥā. So why should it be cherished without cause?

Secondly: The third verse of the verses—meaning His saying: "The month of Ramaḍān..."—refuses by its context to have been revealed alone as an abrogator (ناسخ) for what preceded it. The apparent context is that "the month of Ramaḍān" is the predicate of an omitted subject or a subject for an omitted predicate, as previously mentioned. Thus, it is an exposition (بيان) for the "fixed number of days," and all three verses are a single discourse aimed at a single purpose: the prescription of the Ramaḍān fast. As for making His saying "the month of Ramaḍān" a subject whose predicate is "in which the Quran was sent down," while this would necessitate the independence of the verse and its suitability to be revealed alone, it would not then be suitable to be an abrogator for what preceded it, due to the lack of contradiction between it and its predecessor—whereas abrogation (النسخ) is conditional upon contradiction and disparity.

Even weaker than this view is the view of others—as appears from them—that the second verse, meaning His saying, the Exalted: "for a fixed number of days," is an abrogator for the first verse, meaning His saying, the Exalted: "Prescribed for you is fasting as it was prescribed for those who were before you." This is because fasting was prescribed for the Christians, then they added to it and subtracted from it after Jesus (peace be upon him) until it settled on fifty days. Then Allah legislated it for the Muslims through the first verse, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the people used to fast it in the early days of Islam until His saying, the Exalted: "for a fixed number of days" was revealed, so it abrogated the ruling and the ruling settled on something else.

This view is feebler than the previous one and its falsehood is more apparent. All the objections that apply to the previous view apply to it. That the second verse is a completion of the first verse is more apparent and clear. The narrations the proponent relied upon are more clearly in opposition to the apparent meaning of the Quran and the context of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: فمن كان منكم مريضاً أو على سفر فعدة من أيام أُخر (So should any of you be sick or on a journey, let it be a [fixed] number of other days): The "fa" (ف) is for derivation (التفريع), and the sentence is derived from His saying: "Prescribed for you" and His saying: "numbered." That is: fasting is prescribed and obligated, and the number is included in the obligation. Just as the hand is not removed from the essence of the obligation, it is not removed from the number. So if an occurrence arises that necessitates the removal of the obligatory ruling from the numbered days—which are the days of the month of Ramaḍān—such as the occurrence of sickness or travel, it does not remove the hand from fasting a number of other days outside the month of Ramaḍān equal in number to what the legally responsible person missed. This is what He, the Exalted, pointed to in the third verse with His saying: "and so that you may complete the number." Thus, His saying, the Exalted: "for a fixed number of days," just as it conveys the meaning of minimization as mentioned, conveys that the number is a pillar (ركن) included in the obligation and the ruling.

Furthermore, sickness (المرض) is the opposite of health, and travel (السفر) is taken from al-safar in the sense of uncovering (الكشف), as if the traveler is uncovered by his journey from his house where he takes shelter and is concealed. It is as if His saying, the Exalted: "or on a journey" (أو على سفر), rather than saying "a traveler" (مسافراً), is to point to the consideration of the actual engagement in the state at the moment, excluding the past and the future.

A group—the majority of the scholars of the Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jamāʿah—have said: What is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: "So should any of you be sick or on a journey, let it be a [fixed] number of other days" is a concession (الرخصة) rather than a determination (العزيمة). Thus, the sick person and the traveler have a choice between fasting and breaking the fast. You have already known that the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: "let it be a [fixed] number of other days" is the determination of breaking the fast, not a concession. This is what is narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt, and it is the school of a group of the Companions such as ʿAbd al-Raḥmān ibn ʿAwf, ʿUmar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb, ʿAbd Allāh ibn ʿUmar, Abū Hurayrah, and ʿUrwah ibn al-Zubayr. They are refuted by His saying, the Exalted: "let it be a [fixed] number of other days."

They have assumed an omission (تقدير) in the verse, saying: the implied meaning is "So should any of you be sick or on a journey and breaks his fast, then a number of other days."

This is refuted, firstly: because the omission, as they have stated, is contrary to the apparent meaning (خلاف الظاهر) and is not resorted to except with a contextual clue (قرينة), and there is no clue from the speech itself for it.

Secondly: The speech, even if the omission were granted, does not indicate a concession. For the context, as they mentioned, is a context of legislation (التشريع). Our saying: "So should any of you be sick or on a journey and breaks his fast," the most it indicates is that breaking the fast does not occur as a sin, but rather as permissible (جائزاً) in the broader sense of permissibility—encompassing obligation, recommendation, and mere permissibility. As for it being permissible in the sense of not being mandatory, there is absolutely no evidence for it from the speech; rather, the evidence is to the contrary. For the structure of speech in the context of legislation is based on not omitting what must be explained, which is unbecoming of the Wise Legislator, and this is apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: وعلى الذين يطيقونه فدية طعام مسكين (and for those who find it hard, a redemption by feeding a needy person): "Capability" (الاطاقة), as some have mentioned, is the expenditure of one's entire power in an action, and its necessity is that the action occurs with effort and hardship. The "ransom" (الفدية) is the substitute (البدل), and here it is a monetary substitute, which is the "feeding of a needy person" (طعام مسكين)—that is, food that satiates a hungry needy person from the average of what a person eats. The ruling of the ransom is also an obligation (فرض), like the ruling of making up the fast for the sick and the traveler, because of His saying: "and for those" (وعلى الذين), which is apparent in a specific obligation (الوجوب التعييني) rather than concession and choice.

Some have mentioned that the sentence conveys a concession and was then abrogated. Thus, He, the Glorified and Exalted, gave all those capable of fasting—meaning those able to fast—the choice between fasting and breaking the fast and expiating for every day by feeding a needy person, because the people at that time were not accustomed to fasting. Then that was abrogated by His saying: "So let those of you who witness it fast [in] it." Some of these have mentioned: it abrogated the ruling for those who are not incapacitated; as for the likes of the elderly man, the pregnant woman, and the nursing mother, they remained in their state of the permissibility of the ransom.

By my life, this is nothing but playing with the Quran and making its verses into fragments (عضين). If you reflect on the three verses, you will find them to be a discourse established for a single purpose, with a single context, consistent sentences, and elegant exposition. Then, if you apply this discourse—in its unity and consistency—to what this proponent sees, you will find its context disrupted, its sentences conflicting, some pushing others away, and its end contradicting its beginning. At one time it says: "Prescribed for you is fasting," at another it says: "It is permissible for the capable among you to break the fast and pay a ransom," and at another it says: "Fasting is obligatory upon all of you if you witness the month," thus abrogating the ruling of ransom for the capable while the ruling for the incapable remains as it was—yet there was no ruling for the incapable in the verse! Unless it is said: "they find it hard" (يطيقون) indicated capability before the abrogation, then it came to indicate the lack of capability after the abrogation. In short, according to this, His saying: "and for those who find it hard" in the middle of the verses must be an abrogator for His saying: "Prescribed for you is fasting" at their beginning due to the contradiction, and the question remains as to why it was restricted to "finding it hard" without an apparent reason. Then His saying: "So let those of you who witness it fast [in] it" at the end of the verses would be an abrogator for His saying: "and for those who find it hard" in the middle, and the question remains as to why it abrogated the ruling for those capable of fasting only, excluding the incapacitated, while the abrogator is absolute and inclusive of both the capable and the incapacitated, and the abrogated does not include the ruling of the incapacitated whose continuation is intended. This is among the most heinous of errors.

If you add to this abrogation after abrogation what they mentioned regarding the abrogation of His saying: "The month of Ramaḍān..." for His saying: "for a fixed number of days...", and the abrogation of His saying: "for a fixed number of days..." for His saying: "Prescribed for you is fasting...", and you reflect on the meaning of the verses, you will witness something astonishing.

His saying, the Exalted: فمن تطوع خيراً فهو خير له (And whoever does good of his own accord, it is better for him): "Doing of one's own accord" (التطوع) is the tafaʿʿul form from "obedience" (الطوع), the opposite of "compulsion" (الكره). It is performing the action with pleasure and desire. The meaning of the tafaʿʿul pattern is adoption and acceptance. Thus, the meaning of al-taṭawwuʿ is to engage in performing the action with pleasure and desire without dislike or feeling burdened, whether it is a mandatory action or a non-mandatory one. As for the restriction of al-taṭawwuʿ in usage to recommended and supererogatory acts (المستحبات والمندوبات), this is something that occurred among Muslims after the revelation of the Quran, with the notion that the action performed with pleasure is the recommendation, while the obligatory has a tinge of dislike due to the compulsion within it.

In short, al-taṭawwuʿ, as has been said, has no indication in its root or form toward recommendation. Accordingly, the "fa" (ف) is for derivation, and the sentence is derived from the sum of the previous meaning of the speech. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: Fasting is prescribed for you, observing your good and welfare, along with your stability in the ranks of the nations before you, and mitigation and facilitation for you. So perform it willingly, not reluctantly, for whoever performs good willingly, it is better for him than performing it reluctantly.

From here it becomes apparent that His saying: "And whoever does good of his own accord" is of the type of placing the cause in the place of the effect—that is, placing the fact that al-taṭawwuʿ in absolute good is good in the place of the fact that al-taṭawwuʿ in fasting is good. This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: قَدْ نَعْلَمُ إِنَّهُ لَيَحْزُنُكَ الَّذِي يَقُولُونَ فَإِنَّهُمْ لَا يُكَذِّبُونَكَ وَلَٰكِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ "We certainly know that what they say grieves you. Yet they do not call you a liar, but the wrongdoers deny the signs of Allah" (al-An'am: 33), meaning: so be patient and do not grieve, for they do not call you a liar.

It is sometimes said that the sentence—meaning His saying, the Exalted: "And whoever does good of his own accord, it is better for him"—is connected to the sentence that follows it, meaning His saying: "and for those who find it hard, a redemption by feeding a needy person." The meaning would be that whoever does good of his own accord regarding the ransom of feeding a needy person by giving more than the food for one needy person, such as the equivalent of two ransoms for two needy persons or for one, it is better for him.

This is refuted by: the lack of evidence for restricting al-taṭawwuʿ to recommendations as you have known, along with the obscurity of the point in the derivation. For no rational reason appears for the derivation of al-taṭawwuʿ by increase upon the ruling of the ransom. Moreover, His saying: "And whoever does good of his own accord" has no indication of al-taṭawwuʿ by increase, for doing good of one's own accord is different from doing an increase of one's own accord.

His saying, the Exalted: وأن تصوموا خير لكم إن كنتم تعلمون (and that you fast is better for you, should you know): A sentence completing its predecessor. The meaning according to the implied sense—as mentioned—is: perform the fasting prescribed for you willingly, for doing good willingly is good, and fasting is good for you, so doing it willingly is good upon good.

It is sometimes said that the sentence—meaning His saying: "and that you fast is better for you"—is an address to those with excuses, rather than the general body of believers addressed by the obligation and prescription. This is because its apparent meaning is the preference (رجحان) of the act of fasting, which does not prevent its omission, thus suiting recommendation (الاستحباب) rather than obligation. It is then interpreted as the preference and recommendation of fasting for the possessors of concession among the sick and the travelers; thus, it is recommended for them to choose fasting over breaking the fast and making it up.

This is refuted by: the lack of evidence for it, firstly. The difference between the two sentences—meaning His saying: "So should any of you..." and His saying: "and that you fast is better for you"—in terms of third-person and second-person address, secondly. That the first sentence is aimed at explaining concession and choice—rather, the apparent meaning of His saying: "let it be a [fixed] number of other days" is the specific determination of fasting in other days, as mentioned, thirdly. That the first sentence, assuming it came to explain concession for the excused, did not mention fasting and breaking the fast such that His saying: "and that you fast is better for you" would be an exposition for one of the two sides of the choice; rather, it only mentioned the fast of the month of Ramaḍān and the fast of a number of other days. In that case, there is no way to benefit the preference of the Ramaḍān fast over the fast of others from the mere saying: "and that you fast is better for you" without an apparent clue, fourthly. And that the context is not a context of explaining the ruling such that the appearance of preference would contradict the ruling being obligatory; rather, the context—as previously mentioned—is the context of the criterion of legislation (ملاك التشريع) and that the legislated ruling is not devoid of interest, good, and beauty, as in His saying: فَتُوبُوا إِلَىٰ بَارِئِكُمْ فَاقْتُلُوا أَنفُسَكُمْ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ "so turn penitenly to your Maker, and slay your own souls. That will be better for you" (al-Baqarah: 54), and His saying, the Exalted: فَاسْعَوْا إِلَىٰ ذِكْرِ اللَّهِ وَذَرُوا الْبَيْعَ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ "hasten to the remembrance of Allah and leave all business. That is better for you, should you know" (al-Jumu'ah: 9), and His saying, the Exalted: تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَتُجَاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ بِأَمْوَالِكُمْ وَأَنفُسِكُمْ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ "You have faith in Allah and His Apostle, and wage jihad in the way of Allah with your possessions and your persons. That is better for you, should you know" (al-Saff: 11). The verses in this regard are many, fifthly.

His saying, the Exalted: شهر رمضان الذي أُنزل فيه القرآن هدى (The month of Ramaḍān is every one in which the Quran was sent down as guidance): The month of Ramaḍān is the ninth month of the Arabic lunar months, between Shaʿbān and Shawwāl. No name of any of the months is mentioned in the Quran except the month of Ramaḍān.

"Descent" (النزول) is the arrival at a place from above. The difference between al-inzāl (الإِنزال) and al-tanzīl (التنزيل) is that al-inzāl is instantaneous (دفعي) while al-tanzīl is gradual (تدريجي). "The Quran" (القرآن) is the name for the Book revealed to His Prophet Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in consideration of its being recited, as He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّا جَعَلْنَاهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ "We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason" (al-Zukhruf: 3). It is applied to the whole of the Book and to its parts.

The verse indicates the descent of the Quran in the month of Ramaḍān. Yet He, the Exalted, has said: وَقُرْآنًا فَرَقْنَاهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلَىٰ مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْنَاهُ تَنزِيلًا "As a Quran We have unfolded it [gradually], that you may recite it to the people at intervals, and We have sent it down in stages" (al-Isra: 106). This is apparent in its gradual descent over the entire period of the mission, which is approximately twenty-three years. Mutawātir history indicates this. Therefore, an objection is sometimes raised regarding the contradiction between the two verses.

It is sometimes answered: that it descended all at once (دفعة) to the lowest heaven in the month of Ramaḍān, then it descended upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in installments and at intervals over the period of twenty-three years—the total duration of the mission. This is an answer taken from narrations, some of which we will mention in the discussion of narrations.

It has been objected to this: that following His saying, the Exalted: "in which the Quran was sent down" with His saying: "as guidance for mankind and [as] manifest proofs of guidance and the Criterion" does not support this, as there is no meaning for its remaining with the description of guidance and the Criterion in the heaven for years.

It was answered: that its being a guide means it is its nature to guide those who need its guidance from misguidance, and its being a criterion when truth is confused with falsehood does not contradict its remaining for a period in the state of potentiality (الشأنية) without actualization of effect (فعلية التأثير) until its appointed time arrives. There are parallels and examples for this in organized civil laws, which, whenever the time for one of their articles arrives, are implemented and move from potentiality to actuality.

The truth is that the ruling of laws and constitutions is different from the ruling of addresses (الخطابات), which cannot possibly precede the station of addressing even for a short time. In the Quran, there are many verses of this kind, such as His saying, the Exalted: قَدْ سَمِعَ اللَّهُ قَوْلَ الَّتِي تُجَادِلُكَ فِي زَوْجِهَا وَتَشْتَكِي إِلَى اللَّهِ وَاللَّهُ يَسْمَعُ تَحَاوُرَكُمَا "Allah has certainly heard the speech of her who pleads with you about her husband and complains to Allah, and Allah hears your conversation" (al-Mujadilah: 1), and His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا رَأَوْا تِجَارَةً أَوْ لَهْوًا انفَضُّوا إِلَيْهَا وَتَرَكُوكَ قَائِمًا "When they sight some merchandise or amusement, they scatter toward it and leave you standing" (al-Jumu'ah: 11), and His saying, the Exalted: رِجَالٌ صَدَقُوا مَا عَاهَدُوا اللَّهَ عَلَيْهِ فَمِنْهُم مَّن قَضَىٰ نَحْبَهُ وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَنتَظِرُ وَمَا بَدَّلُوا تَبْدِيلًا "Among the faithful are men who fulfill what they have pledged to Allah: there are some among them who have fulfilled their pledge, and some of them who still wait, and they have not changed in the least" (al-Ahzab: 23). Furthermore, in the Quran there is the abrogating (الناسخ) and the abrogated (المنسوخ), and there is no meaning for their meeting at one time in terms of descent.
