If he himself is a wrongdoer to others, he has called against himself with the very thing he called for himself. If he accepts vengeance against himself—and he will never accept it—he is punished with what he wants for others. And if he does not accept that, then supplication has not truly been realized from him. He, the Exalted, said: وَيَدْعُ الْإِنسَانُ بِالشَّرِّ دُعَاءَهُ بِالْخَيْرِ وَكَانَ الْإِنسَانُ عَجُولًا — "Man prays for evil as [vehemently as] he prays for good, and man is ever hasty" (17:11).

In ‘Uddat al-Da‘i: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said to Abu Dharr: "O Abu Dharr, shall I not teach you words by which Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, will benefit you?" I said: "Yes, O Messenger of Allah." He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said: "Be mindful of Allah, and Allah will protect you. Be mindful of Allah, and you will find Him before you. Make yourself known to Allah in prosperity, and He will know you in hardship. When you ask, ask Allah; and when you seek help, seek help from Allah. For the Pen has already run with what is to be until the Day of Resurrection; and if all of creation were to strive to benefit you with something that Allah has not written for you, they would not be able to do so."

I say: His saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "Make yourself known to Allah in prosperity, and He will know you in hardship," means: supplicate to Allah in prosperity and do not forget Him, so that He may answer your supplication in hardship and not forget you. This is because whoever forgets his Lord in prosperity has effectively acknowledged the independence of causes in prosperity. Then, when he calls his Lord in hardship, the meaning of his action is that he acknowledges Lordship (الربوبية) in the state of hardship and upon its condition. But He, the Exalted, is not characterized by this; rather, He is the Lord in every state and upon all conditions. Thus, he has not [truly] called his Lord. This meaning has been mentioned in some narrations in another way. In Makarim al-Akhlaq, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he (peace be upon him) said: "Whoever precedes in supplication is answered when calamity descends, and it is said: ‘A known voice,’ and it is not veiled from the sky. And whoever does not precede in supplication is not answered when calamity descends, and the angels say: ‘We do not know this voice’" (the hadith continues). This is what is understood from the absolute nature of His saying, the Exalted: نَسُوا اللَّهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ — "They forgot Allah, so He forgot them" (9:67). This does not contradict what has been narrated that supplication is not rejected when there is total detachment (الانقطاع), for absolute hardship is different from total detachment.

His saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "When you ask, ask Allah; and when you seek help, seek help from Allah," is an instruction to attach oneself to Allah in asking and seeking help in reality. For these conventional causes that are before us—their causality is limited to the limit Allah has ordained for them, not according to their apparent independence in influence. Rather, they possess nothing but the quality of being a path and an intermediary in delivering [the result], while the matter is in the hand of Allah, the Exalted. Therefore, it is mandatory for the servant to turn in his needs toward the Presence of Might and the Door of Grandeur, and not to rely on one cause after another, even though Allah has refused to let matters proceed except through their causes. This is an invitation to not rely on causes except through Allah who bestowed causality upon them; it is not a guidance to negate causes and seek from other than the cause, for that would be a desire for what cannot be desired. How could it be otherwise, when the caller wants what he asks for with the heart, asks for what he wants with the tongue, and seeks help for that with the pillars of his existence—and all of these are causes?

Consider this in the case of a human being, who does what he does with his bodily tools: he gives what he gives with his hand, sees what he sees with his eye, and hears what he hears with his ear. Whoever asks his Lord while negating the causes is like one who asks a person to hand him something without a hand, or to look at him without an eye, or to listen without an ear. And whoever relies on a cause besides Allah, the Glorified, is like one whose heart is attached to the hand of a person in its giving, or to his eye in its seeing, or to his ear in its hearing, while he is heedless and turning away from the person who is the actual agent through those tools. He is thus heedless and deluded. This is not a restriction of the infinite Divine Power, nor a stripping of the Mandatory Choice, just as the limitation we mentioned in the human being does not necessitate stripping him of power and choice; for the limitation in reality returns to the act, not to the agent. For it is necessary that the human is capable of handing, seeing, and hearing, but handing only occurs with the hand, and seeing and hearing are those which occur with the eye and ear, not absolutely. Similarly, the Mandatory One, the Exalted, is capable absolutely, but the specificity of the act depends on the mediation of causes. Zayd, for example—who is an act of Allah—is the human born to so-and-so and so-and-so at such a time and place, and upon the existence of such conditions and the removal of such obstacles. If one of these causes or conditions failed, he would not be himself. Thus, in his creation, he depends on the realization of all of them. The one who is dependent is the act, not the Agent; so understand that.

His saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "For the Pen has already run with what is to be until the Day of Resurrection," is a derivation from his saying: "When you ask, ask Allah," in the manner of following the effect with the cause. It is an explanation of the cause and reason for his saying: "When you ask." The meaning is that events are written and ordained by Allah, the Exalted; no cause among the causes has any real influence in them. Therefore, do not ask other than Him, the Exalted, and do not seek help from other than Him, the Exalted. As for Him, the Exalted: His authority is eternal, His kingdom is stable, His will is effective, and every day He is engaged in some work. For this reason, he followed the sentence with his saying: "And if all of creation were to strive..." etc.

Among the reports on supplication is what has been narrated from them extensively: "Supplication is part of the decree (القدر)."

I say: In this is the answer to the problem raised by the Jews and others regarding supplication: that the need supplicated for is either already decreed and ordained or it is not. If it is the former, it is necessary; if the latter, it is impossible. In either case, there is no meaning to the influence of supplication. The answer is: the assumption of the decree of a thing's existence does not necessitate its independence from the causes of its existence. Supplication is among the causes of a thing's existence. Thus, with supplication, a cause among the causes of existence is realized, and the effect is realized from its cause. This is the meaning of their saying: "Supplication is part of the decree." There are other narrations with this meaning.

In al-Bihar, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "Nothing repels the decree (القضاء) except supplication."

From Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Supplication repels the decree after it has been firmly established."

From Abu al-Hasan Musa (peace be upon him): "You must supplicate, for supplication and asking of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, repels calamity. It may have been ordained and decreed such that nothing remains but its execution; then if Allah is called and asked, He diverts the calamity completely."

From Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Indeed, supplication repels the firmly established decree—so increase in supplication, for it is the key to every mercy and the success of every need. What is with Allah is not attained except through supplication, for there is no door that is frequently knocked upon except that it is about to be opened for its seeker."

I say: In these is an indication toward persistence (الإصرار), which is among the factors that realize supplication; for the frequent repetition of the intention leads to its purity.

From Isma‘il ibn Hammam, from Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him): "A servant's secret supplication, a single call, is equal to seventy public calls."

I say: In this is an indication toward concealing the supplication and keeping it secret, for it better preserves the sincerity of the request.

In al-Makarim, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Supplication remains veiled until one sends blessings upon Muhammad and the family of Muhammad."

Also from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Whoever puts forward forty of the believers and then supplicates, he will be answered."

Also from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him)—when a man from his companions said to him: "I find two verses in the Book of Allah that I seek [the fulfillment of] but do not find it"—he said: "And what are they?" I said: "‘Call Me, and I will hear you,’ yet we call Him and do not see a response." He said: "Do you think Allah broke His promise?" I said: "No." He said: "Then why?" I said: "I do not know." He said: "But I will inform you: whoever obeys Allah in what He commanded him and then calls Him from the direction of supplication (جهة الدعاء), He answers him." I said: "And what is the direction of supplication?" He said: "You begin by praising Allah and glorifying Him, and you mention His blessings upon you and thank Him. Then you send blessings upon Muhammad and his family. Then you mention your sins and acknowledge them, then you seek forgiveness for them. This is the direction of supplication." Then he said: "And what is the other verse?" I said: "‘And whatever you spend, He will replace it’ (34:39), yet I see myself spending and do not see a replacement." He said: "Do you think Allah broke His promise?" I said: "No." He said: "Then why?" I said: "I do not know." He said: "If one of you were to earn wealth from its lawful source and spend it in its rightful place, he would not spend a single dirham except that Allah would replace it for him."

I say: The reason for these hadiths regarding the etiquette of supplication is clear, for they bring the servant closer to the reality of supplication and asking.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, from Ibn ‘Umar, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said: "Indeed, when Allah intends to answer a servant, He permits him to supplicate."

Also from Ibn ‘Umar, from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "Whoever among you has the door of supplication opened for him, the doors of mercy are opened for him." In another report: "Whoever among you is opened for in supplication, the doors of Paradise are opened for him."

I say: This meaning is also narrated through the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt: "Whoever is given supplication is given the response." Its meaning is clear from what has preceded.

Also in al-Durr al-Manthur, from Mu‘adh ibn Jabal, from the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "If you knew Allah with the knowledge He is truly entitled to, mountains would move by your supplication."

I say: This is because ignorance of the station of Truth and the authority of Lordship, and reliance on causes, necessitates the acknowledgment of real influence for the causes and the restriction of effects to their familiar causes and conventional means. This goes so far that even if a person ceases to acknowledge the real influence of causes, he remains acknowledging the necessity of the paths and the mediation of the intermediary causes. For we see that movement and travel necessitate approaching the destination. Then, if the belief in the real influence of travel in approaching ceases, we believe that travel is an intermediary and Allah, the Glorified, is the Influencer there; yet the belief remains in the necessity of the mediation—that were it not for the travel, there would be no closeness or approaching. In short, that effects do not fail to follow their causes, even if the causes possess nothing but mediation without influence. This is what knowledge of the station of Allah, the Glorified, does not validate, for it does not suit the absolute Divine Authority. This illusion is what necessitated our belief in the impossibility of effects failing to follow their conventional causes—such as weight and attraction from a body, closeness from movement, satiety from eating, quenching from drinking, and so on. It has already been mentioned in the discussion on miracles (الإعجاز) that the law of cause and effect—or in other words, the mediation of causes between Allah, the Glorified, and His effects—is a truth that cannot be avoided. However, it does not necessitate restricting events to their conventional causes. Rather, theoretical rational inquiry, the Book, and the Sunnah establish the principle of mediation and nullify the restriction. Yes, rational impossibilities (المحالات العقلية) are not to be hoped for.

Once you know this, you will know that knowledge of Allah necessitates the acknowledgment that whatever is not an essential impossibility (محال ذاتي) among all that habit deems impossible—supplication is answered regarding it, just as the core of the miracles of the Prophets returns to the answering of supplication.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: "So let them respond to Me, and let them believe in Me": "They should know that I am capable of giving them what they ask of Me."

In al-Majma‘, he said: It is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) that he said: "‘And let them believe in Me,’ meaning let them be certain that I am capable of giving them what they asked for. ‘So that they may be rightly guided,’ meaning so that they may hit upon the truth, i.e., be guided to it."

Ayah

أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ لَيْلَةَ ٱلصِّيَامِ ٱلرَّفَثُ إِلَىٰ نِسَآئِكُمْ ۚ هُنَّ لِبَاسٌ لَّكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لِبَاسٌ لَّهُنَّ ۗ عَلِمَ ٱللَّهُ أَنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ تَخْتَانُونَ أَنفُسَكُمْ فَتَابَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَعَفَا عَنكُمْ ۖ فَٱلْـَٔـٰنَ بَـٰشِرُوهُنَّ وَٱبْتَغُوا۟ مَا كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ۚ وَكُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَتَبَيَّنَ لَكُمُ ٱلْخَيْطُ ٱلْأَبْيَضُ مِنَ ٱلْخَيْطِ ٱلْأَسْوَدِ مِنَ ٱلْفَجْرِ ۖ ثُمَّ أَتِمُّوا۟ ٱلصِّيَامَ إِلَى ٱلَّيْلِ ۚ وَلَا تُبَـٰشِرُوهُنَّ وَأَنتُمْ عَـٰكِفُونَ فِى ٱلْمَسَـٰجِدِ ۗ تِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ فَلَا تَقْرَبُوهَا ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ (١٨٧)
You are permitted on the night of the fast to go into your wives: they are a garment for you, and you are a garment for them. Allah knew that you would betray yourselves, so He pardoned you and excused you. So now consort with them and seek what Allah has ordained for you, and eat and drink until the white streak becomes manifest to you from the dark streak at the crack of dawn. Then complete the fast until nightfall, and do not consort with them while you dwell in confinement in the mosques. These are Allah’s bounds, so do not approach them. Thus does Allah clarify His signs for mankind so that they may be Godwary. (187)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: أُحل لكم ليلة الصيام الرفث إلى نسائكم (Permitted to you on the night of the fast is goings-in to your wives): Permissibility (الإحلال) carries the meaning of authorization. Its root is from "untying" (الحل), which is the opposite of "binding" (العقد). "Goings-in" (الرفث) refers to the explicit mention of that which is usually referred to by allusion (التكنية) due to the perceived indecency of its mention—specifically those terms associated with sexual intimacy with women. Here, it is used as an allusion for the act of sexual intercourse (الجماع), which is part of the refined literary style (أدب) of the Noble Quran. Similarly, other terms used in the Quran, such as "consorting" (المباشرة), "entering" (الدخول), "touching" (المس), "feeling" (اللمس), "coming unto" (الإتيان), and "drawing near" (القرب), are all terms employed by way of allusion. Likewise, terms like "treading" (الوطء) and "intercourse" (الجماع) used outside the Quran are allusive terms, even if frequent usage has moved some of them from the boundary of allusion to explicit statement. This is similar to how the terms "private parts" (الفرج) and "excrement" (الغائط), in their modern known meanings, belong to this category. The use of the preposition "to" (إلى) with "goings-in" (الرفث) is because it incorporates the meaning of "reaching" or "attaining" (الإفضاء), as has been suggested.

His saying, the Exalted: هن لباس لكم وأنتم لباس لهن (They are a garment for you, and you are a garment for them): The apparent meaning of "garment" (اللباس) is its well-known sense: that which a person uses to cover their body. The two sentences are a form of metaphor (استعارة), for each spouse prevents the other from following lewdness and spreading it among the members of the species. Thus, each of them is a garment for the other, concealing their "shameful parts" (السوأة) and covering their "nakedness" (العورة).

This is a subtle metaphor, and its subtlety increases when joined with His saying: أُحل لكم ليلة الصيام الرفث إلى نسائكم (Permitted to you on the night of the fast is goings-in to your wives). For a person conceals their nakedness from others with a garment, but as for the garment itself, there is no concealment from it. Similarly, each spouse protects the other from "goings-in" (الرفث) toward anyone else, but as for "goings-in" toward the spouse, it is not prohibited, because the spouse is the garment attached to one's self and in direct contact with it.

His saying, the Exalted: علم الله أنكم كنتم تختانون أنفسكم فتاب عليكم وعفا عنكم (Allah knew that you used to betray yourselves, so He turned to you [clemently] and excused you): "Betrayal" (الاختيان) and "treachery" (الخيانة) carry the same meaning, and it contains the sense of "deficiency" (النقص), as has been said. In His saying: أنكم تختانون (that you used to betray), there is an indication of continuity (الاستمرار). Thus, the verse indicates that this betrayal was a recurring and continuous practice among the Muslims since the ruling of fasting was first legislated. They were secretly disobeying Allah the Exalted by betraying themselves. Had this betrayal not been a sin (معصية), He would not have sent down "repentance" (التوبة) and "pardon" (العفو). Although these two terms are not explicit in indicating a prior sin, they—especially when joined together—clearly suggest it.

Based on this, the verse indicates that the ruling of fasting before the revelation of this verse was the prohibition of sexual intercourse during the night of the fast. With its revelation, the verse legislated permissibility and abrogated (نسخت) the prohibition, as mentioned by a group of exegetes. This is sensed or indicated by His saying: أُحل لكم (Permitted to you), His saying: كنتم تختانون (you used to betray), His saying: فتاب عليكم وعفا عنكم (so He turned to you and excused you), and His saying: فالآن باشروهن (So now consort with them). For if there had been no prior prohibition, the appropriate phrasing would have been: "There is no blame upon you if you consort with them," or something conveying that meaning, which is evident.

It is sometimes said that the verse is not abrogating (ناسخة) because there is no prohibitory ruling in the verses of fasting regarding sexual intercourse or eating and drinking. Rather, as suggested by some narrations (روايات) transmitted through the paths of the Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama'ah, when the ruling of the obligation of fasting was revealed and the Muslims heard His saying: كتب عليكم الصيام كما كتب على الذين من قبلكم (Fasting has been prescribed for you as it was prescribed for those before you), they understood from it an equality in rulings in all aspects. The Christians, as it is said, used to marry, eat, and drink in the early part of the night, then abstain after that. The Muslims adopted this, but it became difficult for them. The young men among them would not refrain from marriage secretly, while viewing it as a sin and a betrayal of themselves. The elderly were perhaps exhausted by refraining from eating and drinking after sleeping; sometimes sleep would overcome one of them, and he would believe eating and drinking had become prohibited for him. Thus, the verse was revealed to clarify that marriage, eating, and drinking are not prohibited for them during the night in the month of Ramadan. It thereby became clear that the intent of the verse's comparison in His saying: كما كتب على الذين من قبلكم (as it was prescribed for those before you) was a comparison in the essence of the obligation of fasting, not in its specific details. As for His saying: أُحل لكم (Permitted to you), it does not indicate a prior prohibitory ruling, but merely the realization of permissibility, as in His saying: أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ صَيْدُ الْبَحْرِ (Permitted to you is the game of the sea) [5:96], for it is known that sea game was not prohibited for those in the state of ihram before the revelation of that verse. Similarly, His saying: عَلِمَ اللَّهُ أَنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ تَخْتَانُونَ أَنفُسَكُمْ (Allah knew that you used to betray yourselves) only means that they were betraying according to their own assumption and belief that it was betrayal and sin. Therefore, He said: تختانون أنفسكم (betray yourselves) and did not say: "betray Allah," as He said: لَا تَخُونُوا اللَّهَ وَالرَّسُولَ وَتَخُونُوا أَمَانَاتِكُمْ (do not betray Allah and the Apostle, and do not betray your trusts) [8:27]. Furthermore, it is possible that "betrayal" (الاختيان) means "deficiency" (النقص), meaning: Allah knew that you were depriving yourselves of their share of desires such as marriage and other things. Likewise, His saying: فتاب عليكم وعفا عنكم (so He turned to you and excused you) is not explicit in marriage being a prohibited sin.

However, there is a response to this: what you have seen is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse. For His saying: أُحل لكم (Permitted to you), His saying: كنتم تختانون أنفسكم (you used to betray yourselves), and His saying: فتاب عليكم وعفا عنكم (so He turned to you and excused you), even if not explicit in abrogation (النسخ), possess a complete appearance of indicating it. This is in addition to His saying: فالآن باشروهن (So now consort with them) etc., for if there had been nothing but a continuous permissibility before and after the revelation of the verse, there would be no apparent reason for this expression. As for the previous verses of fasting not containing the prohibitory ruling, this does not contradict the verse being abrogating. For those verses did not clarify the other rulings of fasting either, such as the prohibition of marriage, eating, and drinking during the day of the fast. It is known that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had clarified this to the Muslims before the revelation of this verse. Perhaps he had clarified this ruling among the rulings he explained, and the verse abrogates what the Messenger clarified, even if His (Allah's) speech did not contain it.

If you say: His saying: هن لباس لكم وأنتم لباس لهن (They are a garment for you, and you are a garment for them) indicates the reason for legislating the permissibility of "goings-in" (الرفث). Therefore, it must not encompass both the abrogating and the abrogated, for it would be unseemly to justify abrogation with something that applies to both the abrogating and the abrogated together—even if we say that these justifications occurring in the context of rulings are wisdoms (حكم) and interests (مصالح) rather than strict causes (علل), and it is not required for a "wisdom" to be all-inclusive and exclusive like "causes." If "goings-in" were prohibited before the revelation of the verse and then abrogated by the permitting verse, it would not be sound to justify the abrogation of the prohibition by saying that men are garments for women and they are garments for them.

I say: Firstly, this is refuted by the restriction of His saying: أُحل لكم (Permitted to you) with His saying: ليلة الصيام (on the night of the fast), despite the fact that the ruling of "garment" (اللباس) applies during the day just as it does at night, yet it is prohibited during the day. Secondly, the conditions taken in the verse from His saying: ليلة الصيام (on the night of the fast), His saying: هن لباس لكم (they are a garment for you), and His saying: أنكم كنتم تختانون أنفسكم (that you used to betray yourselves), indicate three ordered causes (علل) upon which the abrogated and abrogating rulings are based. The fact that one spouse is a garment for the other necessitates that "goings-in" (الرفث) between them be permitted absolutely. Then, the ruling of fasting indicated by His saying: ليلة الصيام (on the night of the fast)—and fasting (الصيام) is the refraining and abstaining from the desires of the soul such as eating, drinking, and marriage—necessitates restricting the permissibility of "goings-in" and diverting it away from the occasion of fasting. Then, the difficulty for them to restrain the soul from marriage for an entire month and their falling into continuous sin and ongoing betrayal necessitates a certain ease for them by granting a license for that at night. By this, the absolute nature of the ruling of "garment" (اللباس) restricted by fasting returns to some of its absolute application—namely, that it be acted upon at night and not during the day. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: the absolute nature of the ruling of "garment" which We restricted by fasting during the night and day and prohibited for you, We have now made permissible for you because We knew you were betraying yourselves therein, and We intended to lighten your burden out of kindness and mercy. We have restored the absolute nature of that ruling to it during the night of the fast and limited the ruling of fasting to the day; so complete the fast until the night.

In summary: His saying: هن لباس لكم وأنتم لباس لهن (They are a garment for you, and you are a garment for them), even if it is a cause (عللة) or wisdom (حكمة) for the permissibility of the essence of "goings-in" (الرفث), the purpose in the verse is not directed toward it alone. Rather, the purpose is to clarify the wisdom of the permissibility of "goings-in" on the night of the fast, which is the totality of His saying: هن لباس لكم (They are a garment for you) until His saying: وعفا عنكم (and excused you). This wisdom is limited to the abrogating ruling and certainly does not encompass the abrogated one.

His saying, the Exalted: فالآن باشروهن وابتغوا ما كتب الله لكم (So now consort with them and seek what Allah has ordained for you): This is a command occurring after a prohibition, thus indicating permissibility. It was preceded by His saying at the beginning of the verse: أُحل لكم (Permitted to you). The meaning is: from now on, consorting with them is permissible for you. "Seeking" (الابتغاء) is to search for or desire. What is meant by "seeking what Allah has ordained" is the seeking of offspring, which Allah the Glorified has ordained for the human species through the path of consorting. He fashioned their primordial nature (فطرهم) to seek it through the desire for marriage and consorting that He placed within them. He subjected them through this to this act; thus, they seek through it what Allah has ordained for them, even if they apparently only intend to satisfy desire and attain pleasure. This is just as He ordained for them the preservation of life and growth through eating and drinking, which is the primordial objective, even if they only intend by eating and drinking to obtain the pleasure of taste, satiety, and quenching thirst; for it is indeed a divine subjection (تسخير).

As for what has been said—that what is meant by "what Allah has ordained for them" is the permissibility and the license (الرخصة), for Allah loves that His licenses be taken just as He loves that His firm obligations (عزائمه) be taken—this is far-fetched because "ordaining" (الكتابة) in His speech is not customarily used in the context of permissibility and license.

His saying, the Exalted: وكلوا واشربوا حتى يتبين لكم الخيط الأبيض من الخيط الأسود من الفجر (and eat and drink until the white streak becomes manifest to you from the black streak at dawn): There are two dawns (الفجر). The first dawn is called the "false dawn" (الكاذب) because it vanishes after a short stay, and it is called the "wolf's tail" (ذنب السرحان) because of its resemblance to a wolf's tail when it is raised. It is a vertical column of light that appears at the end of the night in the eastern horizon when the sun's distance from the horizon reaches eighteen degrees below it. Then it vanishes by becoming horizontal, becoming a broad, elongated streak on the horizon like a white thread stretched over it; this is the second dawn. It is called the "true dawn" (الفجر الصادق) because of its truthfulness in what it reports and announces regarding the arrival of the day and its connection to the rising of the sun.

From this, it is known that what is meant by the "white streak" (الخيط الأبيض) is the true dawn, and that the word "from" (من) is explanatory (بيانية). His saying: حتى يتبين لكم الخيط الأسود (until the black streak becomes manifest to you) is a form of metaphor (استعارة) by likening the whiteness spreading across the horizon from the dawn, adjacent to the darkness of the night extending horizontally with it, to a white thread that becomes distinct from a black thread.

From this, it is also known that what is meant is the delimitation by the first moment of the rising of the true dawn, for the rising of the light of the day's whiteness nullifies the two "threads"; thus, there is no longer a white thread nor a black thread.

His saying, the Exalted: ثم أتموا الصيام إلى الليل (then complete the fast until the night): Since the delimitation by the dawn indicated the obligation of fasting until the night after it becomes manifest, He dispensed with mentioning its beginning, preferring brevity. Rather, He addressed its delimitation by "completing" (إتمام) it until the night. In His saying: أتموا (complete), there is an indication that it is a single, simple entity—a single, complete act of worship—rather than being composed of numerous matters, each of which is an independent act of worship. This is the difference between "completion" (التمام) and "perfection" (الكمال), where the former is the end of the existence of that which is not composed of parts having independent effects, and the latter is the end of the existence of that which each of its parts has an independent effect on its own. The Exalted said: الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي (Today I have perfected for you your religion, and completed My blessing upon you) [5:3]. For the religion (الدين) is the sum of prayer, fasting, pilgrimage, and others, each of which has an effect it is independent in, unlike the "blessing" (النعمة), as the explanation of this will come, Allah willing, in the discussion of that verse.

His saying, the Exalted: ولا تباشروهن وأنتم عاكفون في المساجد (and do not consort with them while you are in retreat in the mosques): "Staying" (العكوف) and "retreat" (الاعتكاف) mean remaining or adhering to something. Retreat in a place is to reside in it, adhering to it.

Retreat (الاعتكاف) is a specific act of worship, among whose rulings are remaining in the mosque and not leaving it except for an excuse, and fasting during it. Therefore, it was possible to imagine the permissibility of consorting with women during the nights of retreat in the mosque due to the legislation of the permissibility of "goings-in" (الرفث) on the night of the fast. Thus, He repelled this potential misunderstanding with His saying: ولا تباشروهن وأنتم عاكفون في المساجد (and do not consort with them while you are in retreat in the mosques).

His saying, the Exalted: تلك حدود الله فلا تقربوها (These are the bounds of Allah, so do not approach them): The root of "bound" (الحد) is "prevention" (المنع), and to this return all its usages and derivations, such as the "edge" (حد) of a sword, the "legal punishment" (حد) for lewdness, the "boundary" (حد) of a house, and "iron" (الحديد), among others. The prohibition against "approaching" (القرب) the bounds is an allusion (كناية) for not committing them or transgressing toward them. That is: do not commit these sins—which are eating, drinking, and consorting—or do not transgress these divine rulings and prohibitions which He has clarified for you, which are the rulings of fasting, by wasting them and abandoning God-consciousness (التقوى) regarding them.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: كان الاكل والنكاح محرمين في شهر رمضان بالليل بعد النوم يعني كل من صلى العشاء ونام ولم يفطر ثم انتبه حرم عليه الافطار ، وكان النكاح حراماً في الليل والنهار في شهر رمضان ، وكان رجل من أصحاب رسول الله يقال له خوّات بن جبير الانصاري اخو عبد الله بن جبير الذي كان رسول الله وكله بفم الشِعب يوم أُحد في خمسين من الرماة ففارقه أصحابه وبقي في اثنى عشر رجلاً فقتل على باب الشعب ، وكان اخوه هذا خوّات بن جبير شيخاً كبيراً ضعيفاً ، وكان صائماً مع رسول الله في الخندق ، فجاء إلى أهله حين أمسى فقال عندكم طعام ؟ فقالوا : لا تنم حتى نصنع لك طعاماً فأبطأت عليه اهله بالطعام فنام قبل ان يفطر ، فلما انتبه قال لأهله ، قد حرم على الاكل في هذه الليلة فلما اصبح حضر حفر الخندق فاغمى عليه فرآه رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم فرقّ له وكان قوم من الشبان ينكحون بالليل سراً في شهر رمضان فأنزل الله : أُحل لكم ليلة الصيام الرفث إِلى نسائكم الآية ، فأحل الله تبارك وتعالى النكاح بالليل من شهر رمضان ، والاكل بعد النوم إِلى طلوع الفجر لقوله : حتى يتبين لكم الخيط الابيض من الخيط الاسود من الفجر ، قال : هو بياض النهار من سواد الليل . — "Eating and marriage were prohibited in the month of Ramadan at night after sleeping. That is, whoever prayed Isha and slept without breaking his fast, then woke up, it was prohibited for him to break his fast. Marriage was prohibited during both the night and the day in the month of Ramadan. There was a man from the companions of the Messenger of Allah called Khawwat ibn Jubayr al-Ansari, the brother of Abdullah ibn Jubayr, whom the Messenger of Allah had entrusted with the mouth of the mountain pass on the day of Uhud among fifty archers; his companions left him and he remained with twelve men and was killed at the gate of the pass. This brother of his, Khawwat ibn Jubayr, was an old and weak man. He was fasting with the Messenger of Allah at the Trench (al-Khandaq). When evening came, he went to his family and said, 'Do you have food?' They said, 'Do not sleep until we prepare food for you.' His family was slow in bringing the food, and he fell asleep before breaking his fast. When he woke up, he said to his family, 'Eating has become prohibited for me this night.' When morning came, he attended the digging of the trench and fainted. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw him and felt pity for him. There were also some young men who were marrying [having intercourse] secretly at night in the month of Ramadan. So Allah revealed: 'Permitted to you on the night of the fast is goings-in to your wives...' the verse. Thus, Allah, Blessed and Exalted, permitted marriage during the night of the month of Ramadan, and eating after sleep until the rising of the dawn, due to His saying: 'until the white streak becomes manifest to you from the black streak at dawn.' He said: 'It is the whiteness of the day from the blackness of the night.'"

I say: The phrase "That is..." until "...There was a man" is from the speech of the narrator. This meaning is narrated in other reports transmitted by al-Kulayni, al-Ayyashi, and others. In all of them, the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) for His saying: وكلوا واشربوا (and eat and drink) etc., was the story of Khawwat ibn Jubayr al-Ansari, and the occasion of revelation for His saying: أُحل لكم (Permitted to you) etc., was what the young men among the Muslims were doing.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, narrated from several scholars of exegesis and tradition from al-Bara' ibn 'Azib, who said: كان أصحاب النبي صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم إِذا كان الرجل صائماً فحضر الافطار فنام قبل أن يفطر لم يأكل ليلته ولا يومه حتى يمسي وإن قيس بن صرمة الانصاري كان صائماً فكان يومه ذاك يعمل في ارضه فلما حضر الافطار اتى امرأته فقال : هل عندك طعام ؟ قالت : لا ولكن انطلق فأطلب لك فغلبته عينه فنام وجائت امرأته فلما رأته نائماً قالت : خيبة لك ، أنمت ؟ فلما انتصف النهار غشي عليه فذكر ذلك للنبي صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم فنزلت هذه الآية أُحل لكم ليلة الصيام الرفث ، إِلى قوله : من الفجر ففرحوا بها فرحاً شديداً . — "The companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to be such that if a man was fasting and the time for breaking the fast arrived, but he slept before breaking it, he would not eat that night nor the following day until evening. Qays ibn Sirmah al-Ansari was fasting and had been working in his land that day. When the time for breaking the fast arrived, he came to his wife and said, 'Do you have food?' She said, 'No, but I will go and seek some for you.' His eyes were overcome and he fell asleep. His wife came, and when she saw him sleeping, she said, 'Woe to you, did you sleep?' When midday came, he fainted. This was mentioned to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and this verse was revealed: 'Permitted to you on the night of the fast is goings-in...' until His saying: '...at dawn.' They rejoiced at it with a great joy."

I say: The story is narrated through other paths; in some, the name is Abu Qubays ibn Sirmah, and in others, Sirmah ibn Malik al-Ansari, with variations in the details of the story.

Also in al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn Abbas: ان المسلمين كانوا في شهر رمضان إذا صلوا العشاء حرم عليهم النساء والطعام إلى مثلها من القابلة ، ثم إِن ناساً من المسلمين أصابوا الطعام والنساء في رمضان بعد العشاء ، منهم عمر بن الخطاب فشكوا ذلك الى رسول الله فأنزل الله أُحل لكم ليلة الصيام ، الى قوله : فالآن باشروهن يعني انكحوهن . — "The Muslims in the month of Ramadan, when they prayed Isha, women and food became prohibited for them until the same time the following day. Then, some people among the Muslims took food and women in Ramadan after Isha, among them Umar ibn al-Khattab. They complained of that to the Messenger of Allah, so Allah revealed: 'Permitted to you on the night of the fast...' until His saying: '...So now consort with them,' meaning: have intercourse with them."

I say: The narrations from their paths regarding this meaning are numerous, and in most of them, the name of Umar is mentioned. They are united in that the ruling of marriage at night was like the ruling of eating and drinking: they were all permitted before sleep and prohibited after it. However, the apparent sense of the first narration we cited is that marriage was prohibited in the month of Ramadan during both the night and the day, unlike eating and drinking, which were permitted in the early part of the night before sleep and prohibited after it. The context of the verse supports this, for if marriage had been like eating and drinking—permitted before sleep and prohibited after it—it would have been necessary for the wording to be restricted by a limit (الغاية), as was done with His saying: كلوا واشربوا حتى يتبين لكم الخيط الأبيض (eat and drink until the white streak becomes manifest) etc. Yet He said: أُحل لكم ليلة الصيام الرفث (Permitted to you on the night of the fast is goings-in), and did not provide a restriction indicating a limit. Likewise, what is contained in some narrations—that the betrayal was not exclusive to marriage but that they were also betraying in eating and drinking—does not agree with what is suggested by the context of the verse in placing His saying: علم الله أنكم كنتم تختانون أنفسكم (Allah knew that you used to betray yourselves) etc., before His saying: وكلوا واشربوا (and eat and drink).

Also in al-Durr al-Manthur: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: الفجر فجران فأما الذي كأنه ذنب السرحان فإنه لا يحل شيئاً ولا يحرمه ، واما المستطيل الذي يأخذ الافق فإنه يحل الصلوة ويحرّم الطعام . — "The dawn is of two types: as for that which is like the wolf's tail, it neither permits anything nor prohibits it. As for the elongated one that takes the horizon, it permits the prayer and prohibits food."

I say: The narrations regarding this meaning are widely transmitted (مستفيضة) through both the general (Sunni) and specific (Shi'i) paths, as are the narrations regarding retreat (الاعتكاف) and the prohibition of sexual intercourse during it.

Ayah

وَلَا تَأْكُلُوٓا۟ أَمْوَٰلَكُم بَيْنَكُم بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ وَتُدْلُوا۟ بِهَآ إِلَى ٱلْحُكَّامِ لِتَأْكُلُوا۟ فَرِيقًا مِّنْ أَمْوَٰلِ ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْإِثْمِ وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (١٨٨)
Do not eat up your wealth among yourselves wrongfully, nor proffer it to the judges in order to eat up a part of the people’s wealth sinfully, while you know [that it is immoral to do so]. (188)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: ولا تأكلوا أموالكم بينكم بالباطل — "Do not eat your possessions among yourselves unrightfully" (2:188). What is intended by "eating" (الأكل) is seizing or any form of disposal (التصرف) in a metaphorical sense. The justification for this metaphorical usage is that eating is the most immediate and ancient of the natural acts that a human being needs to perform. When a human being first comes into existence, he perceives his need for nourishment, and from there, he moves to other natural needs such as clothing, shelter, marriage, and the like. Thus, it is the first act of disposal he senses within himself. For this reason, naming disposal and seizure—especially in the context of wealth—as "eating" (أكلاً) is not exclusive to the Arabic language but is common to all other languages.

Wealth (المال) refers to that which is desired among possessions (الملك); it is as if it is derived from "inclination" (الميل) because it is something toward which the heart inclines. "Among" (البين) refers to the gap or interval that is attributed to two or more things. "Falsehood" (الباطل) is the opposite of "truth" (الحق), which is the matter that is established (الثابت) with some form of ontological or legal stability.

In restricting the ruling—namely His saying: ولا تأكلوا أموالكم (do not eat your possessions)—with the phrase بينكم (among yourselves), there is an indication that all wealth belongs to all people collectively. Allah the Exalted has only divided it among them in a rightful division (تقسيماً حقاً) by establishing just laws that regulate ownership (الملك) in a rightful manner—a regulation that uproots the sources of corruption. No disposal by any agent may exceed these laws; otherwise, it would be considered "falsehood" (باطلاً). Thus, the verse serves as an explanation for the general scope of His saying, the Exalted: خلق لكم ما في الارض جميعاً — "It is He who created for you all that is in the earth" (2:29). In attributing "possessions" (الأموال) to the people, He has endorsed what has been established in the foundation of human society regarding the principle of ownership (أصل الملك) and its general respect since this species first settled upon the surface of the earth, as mentioned in reports and history. This principle has been mentioned in the Quran using the terms "ownership" (الملك), "wealth" (المال), the "lam" of possession (لام الملك), "succession" (الاستخلاف), and others in more than a hundred instances, which need not be cited here. Likewise, it is mentioned by way of implication in verses indicating the legislation of sale (البيع), trade (التجارة), and the like in several places, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَأَحَلَّ اللَّهُ الْبَيْعَ — "Allah has allowed trade" (2:275), and His saying, the Exalted: لَا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوَالَكُم بَيْنَكُم بِالْبَاطِلِ إِلَّا أَن تَكُونَ تِجَارَةً عَن تَرَاضٍ — "do not eat each other’s wealth unjustly, but let there be trade by mutual consent" (4:29), and His saying, the Exalted: تِجَارَةٌ تَخْشَوْنَ كَسَادَهَا — "a trade you fear will decline" (9:24), among others. The mutawatir Sunnah (السنة المتواترة) — the narrations transmitted by so many chains that their authenticity is certain — further supports this.

His saying, the Exalted: وتدلو بها الى الحكام لتأكلوا فريقاً — "nor offer them to the judges, so that you may eat a part [of the people’s possessions]" (2:188). "Lowering" (الإدلاء) refers to sending a bucket down into a well to draw water. It is used here as a metaphor for any act of bringing wealth near to judges so they may rule as the briber desires. This is a subtle metaphor suggesting the extraction of the desired judgment through bribery, likening it to the state of water in a well in relation to the one who seeks it. A "part" (الفريق) is a separated or isolated portion of something. The clause is conjoined to His saying: تأكلوا (that you eat), so the verb is in the jussive state due to the prohibition. It is also possible that the "waw" (و) means "along with" (مع), in which case the verb would be in the subjunctive state due to a hidden "an" (أن). The meaning would then be: "along with your eating." Thus, the entire verse would be a single discourse aimed at one purpose: the prohibition of the collusion between the briber and the bribed to consume the wealth of the people by placing it between them and dividing it for themselves—the judge taking what was "lowered" to him, and the briber taking another portion through sin, while both know that this is falsehood (باطل) and not truth (حق).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "A man used to gamble away his family and his wealth, so Allah prohibited them from that."

Also in al-Kafi, from Abu Basir, who said: "I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'The saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic, in His Book: ولا تأكلوا أموالكم بينكم بالباطل وتدلوا بها إلى الحكام (Do not eat your possessions among yourselves unrightfully, nor offer them to the judges).' He said: 'O Abu Basir, Allah, Mighty and Majestic, knew that there would be in the Ummah (الأمة) — the community of believers — judges who act unjustly. Indeed, He did not mean the judges of justice (حكام أهل العدل), but He meant the judges of injustice (حكام أهل الجور). O Abu Muhammad, if you had a right against a man and you called him to the judges of justice, but he refused and insisted on taking you to the judges of injustice so they might rule in his favor, he would be among those who seek judgment from the Taghut (الطاغوت) — any authority that transcends the limits of Allah's law. This is the meaning of the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: ألم تر إلى الذين يزعمون أنهم آمنوا بما أُنزل اليك وما أُنزل من قبلك يريدون ان يتحاكموا إِلى الطاغوت — "Have you not regarded those who claim that they believe in what has been sent down to you, and what was sent down before you, [yet] they desire to seek the judgment of the Taghut?" (4:60).'"

In al-Majma', it is reported from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "He means by 'falsehood' (الباطل) the false oath (اليمين الكاذبة) by which wealth is seized."

I say: These are specific instances (مصاديق), while the verse remains general (مطلقة) in its scope.

(Scientific and Social Discussion)

Every existing thing in the created world before us—including plants, animals, and humans—disposes of things outside the sphere of its own existence that it can utilize to maintain its existence and preserve its survival. There is no report in existence of an entity that is not active (فعال), nor of an act performed by its agent except for a benefit that returns to it. These various types of plants do what they do to benefit from it for their survival, growth, and reproduction. Likewise, the various types of animals and humans do what they do to benefit from it in some way, even if the benefit is imaginary (خيالي) or intellectual (عقلي). This is a matter beyond doubt.

These ontological agents (الفواعل التكوينية) perceive through natural instinct (الغريزة الطبيعية)—and in the case of animals and humans, through instinctive consciousness (الشعور الغريزي)—that the disposal of matter to fulfill a natural need and the utilization of it to preserve existence and survival cannot be achieved for an individual except through "exclusivity" (الاختصاص). This means that a single act cannot be performed by two agents simultaneously (this is the crux and criterion of the matter). Therefore, the human agent—or any agent whose criterion of action we perceive—prevents others from interfering in his affairs or disposing of what he intends to dispose of. This is the principle of exclusivity (أصل الاختصاص) which no human being hesitates to acknowledge. This is the meaning of the "lam" (ل) in our saying: "This is for me (لي) and that is for you (لك)," and "It is for me to do such-and-such and for you to do such-and-such."

This is evidenced by what we observe of animals contending over the nest (عش), shelter (كن), or burrow (وكر) they have acquired, or the prey they have caught or found for nourishment, or the mate they have grown accustomed to, and the like. We also observe children quarreling over the food and items they have seized; even a nursing infant will struggle with another over the breast. Furthermore, when a human being enters the arena of society by the decree of his primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — and the judgment of his instinct, he only reinforces what he had perceived through the principle of nature in general. This entry only necessitates the refinement of what he had initially established, and its organization and elevation into the form of prevailing social laws (النواميس الاجتماعية). At that point, the aforementioned general exclusivity branches into various types with different names: exclusivity regarding wealth is called "ownership" (الملك), while others are called "rights" (الحق), and so on.

Even though people may differ regarding the realization of ownership in terms of its causes—such as inheritance (الوراثة), buying and selling (البيع والشراء), or seizure by the power of a ruler (الغصب)—or in terms of the subject (الموضوع) who is the owner—such as whether the human is an adult (بالغ), a minor (صغير), sane (عاقل), or foolish (سفيه), or whether it is an individual or a group—and though they may increase in some aspects, decrease in others, affirm for some, and deny for others, the principle of ownership (أصل الملك) in general is something they cannot avoid acknowledging. For this reason, we see that those who oppose private ownership merely strip it from the individual and transfer it to the society or the state governing them. Even then, they are unable to strip it from the individual entirely, nor will they ever be able to do so, for the judgment is primordial (فطري). In the nullification of the primordial nature lies the annihilation of humanity.

We shall discuss what pertains to this established principle in terms of its causes—such as trade (التجارة), profit (الربح), inheritance (الارث), spoils (الغنيمة), and acquisition (الحيازة)—and in terms of the subject—such as the adult and the minor—in the appropriate places, Allah the Almighty willing.

Ayah

۞ يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْأَهِلَّةِ ۖ قُلْ هِىَ مَوَٰقِيتُ لِلنَّاسِ وَٱلْحَجِّ ۗ وَلَيْسَ ٱلْبِرُّ بِأَن تَأْتُوا۟ ٱلْبُيُوتَ مِن ظُهُورِهَا وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْبِرَّ مَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ ۗ وَأْتُوا۟ ٱلْبُيُوتَ مِنْ أَبْوَٰبِهَا ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (١٨٩)
They question you concerning the new moons. Say, ‘They are timekeeping signs for the people and [for the sake of] hajj.’ It is not piety that you enter houses from their rear; rather, piety is [personified by] one who is Godwary, and enter houses from their doors and be wary of Allah, so that you may be felicitous. (189)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: يسئلونك (They ask you) until His saying: والحج (and the Hajj). The term "crescents" (الاهلة) is the plural of "crescent" (هلال). The moon is called a crescent at the beginning of the lunar month when it emerges from beneath the sun's rays on the first and second nights, as has been said. Some have said it applies to the first three nights, while others say it is so called until it "becomes stony" (يتحجر). "Becoming stony" (التحجر) refers to when it forms a complete circle with a fine line. Others have said it is called a crescent until its light overpowers the darkness of the night, which occurs on the seventh night; thereafter it is called a "moon" (قمراً), and on the fourteenth night, it is called a "full moon" (بدراً). Its general name among the Arabs is al-Zibriqan (الزبرقان).

The word "crescent" (الهلال) is derived from the phrase "the infant raised a cry" (استهل الصبي) when it cries at birth or shouts. It is also related to their saying: "the people raised their voices for Hajj" (أهل القوم بالحج) when they raise their voices with the talbiyah (التلبية) — the ritual chant of 'Labbayk' performed by pilgrims. It was named thus because people raise their voices mentioning it when they sight it. "Appointed times" (المواقيت) is the plural of "appointed time" (ميقات), which is the time set for an action. It is also applied to a specific place for an action, such as the miqat of the people of Syria or the miqat of the people of Yemen, but the first meaning is intended here.

Regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَهِلَّةِ (They ask you about the new moons), although it does not explicitly state what the question regarding them was—whether it was about the reality of the moon and the cause of its varying phases as a crescent, a moon, and a full moon, as has been suggested; or about the reality of the crescent alone as it appears after the conjunction (المحاق) at the start of the lunar month, as mentioned by some; or about something else.

However, the use of "crescents" (الاهلة) in the question in the plural form, where it says: يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَهِلَّةِ (They ask you about the new moons), is evidence that the question was not about the essence (ماهية) of the moon and its varying phases. Had it been so, it would have been more appropriate to say: "They ask you about the moon" (عن القمر), not "about the crescents." Furthermore, if the question were about the reality of the crescent and the cause of its specific formation, it would have been more appropriate to say: "They ask you about the crescent" (عن الهلال), as there would be no purpose then for the plural. Thus, the use of "crescents" in the plural indicates that the question was about the reason or the benefit in the appearance of the moon as one crescent after another and its marking of the lunar months. It was expressed as "crescents" because they are what realize this function; thus, the answer was given regarding the benefit.

This is further supported by the specific nature of the response: قُلْ هِيَ مَوَاقِيتُ لِلنَّاسِ وَالْحَجِّ (Say, ‘They are markers of time for the people and [for the sake of] hajj’). For "appointed times" (المواقيت)—which are the periods set for actions and deeds—are the months themselves, rather than the crescents, which are not periods of time but rather shapes and forms of the moon.

In short, it is concluded that the purpose of the question pertained to the status of the lunar months in terms of their cause or benefit. The response was given by explaining the benefit: that they are times and periods set for people in the affairs of their worldly life and their hereafter. For man, by his very nature (الخلقة), must measure his actions and deeds—all of which are of the nature of motion—by time. This necessitates that the extended time (الزمان الممتد) upon which their affairs are mapped be divided into small and large segments, such as night and day, the day, the month, the seasons, and the years, through Divine care (العناية الالهية) which manages the affairs of His creation and guides them toward the rectitude of their lives. The most apparent division, from which the scholar, the ignorant, the nomad, and the city-dweller alike benefit, and which is easy for everyone to track, is the division of days into lunar months. This is perceived by every person of sound perception and straight senses, unlike the solar months, the significance of which man did not notice, nor did he attain their precise calculation, except after centuries and ages from the beginning of his life on earth; even then, it is not within the capacity of all people at all times.

Thus, the lunar months are set times designated for people in the affairs of their religion and their world, and for the Hajj specifically, as it consists of "known months." It is as if the specific mention of Hajj a second time serves as a prelude to what will be mentioned in the following verses regarding its restriction to certain months.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَيْسَ الْبِرُّ بِأَنْ تَأْتُوا الْبُيُوتَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهَا (It is not righteousness that you should come into houses from their backs) until His saying: مِنْ أَبْوَابِهَا (from their doors). It is established through transmission (النقل) that a group of the pre-Islamic Arabs, when they entered the state of ihram (الإحرام) — the sacred state of a pilgrim — for Hajj, would not enter their houses through the door when in need; instead, they would make a hole in the back of the house and enter through it. Islam forbade this and commanded them to enter houses through their doors. The revelation of the verse is consistent with this circumstance; thus, it is correct to rely on what has been transmitted regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) for this verse, as will be cited later.

Were it not for that, it would be possible to say that His saying: وَلَيْسَ الْبِرُّ (It is not righteousness...) to the end, is a metaphor (كناية) for the prohibition against complying with Divine commands and acting upon the legislated laws of the religion except in the manner in which they were legislated. Thus, Hajj is not permissible outside its months, nor is fasting outside the month of Ramadan, and so on. According to this view, the sentence would be a completion of the beginning of the verse, and the meaning would be: these months are set times for deeds legislated within them, and it is not permissible to transgress them to others, such as performing Hajj outside its months or fasting outside the month of Ramadan. Thus, the verse would contain the explanation of a single ruling.

According to the first interpretation, which is supported by transmission, the denial of "righteousness" (البر) for entering houses from their backs indicates that the mentioned act was not something endorsed by the religion; otherwise, there would be no meaning in denying its being "righteousness." Rather, it was a bad pre-Islamic custom, so Allah the Exalted denied its being part of righteousness and affirmed that righteousness is God-consciousness (التقوى) — the state of godwariness and protection against sin. It would have been expected to say: "but righteousness is God-consciousness," but the phrasing was changed to: وَلَكِنَّ الْبِرَّ مَنِ اتَّقَى (but righteousness is [the quality of] one who is God-conscious), to signal that perfection lies in being characterized by God-consciousness, which is the intended goal, rather than an empty concept, as seen in the parallel verse: لَيْسَ الْبِرَّ أَنْ تُوَلُّوا وُجُوهَكُمْ قِبَلَ الْمَشْرِقِ وَالْمَغْرِبِ وَلَكِنَّ الْبِرَّ مَنْ آمَنَ (Piety is not to turn your faces to the east or the west; rather, piety is [personified by] those who have faith...).

The command in His saying, the Exalted: وَأْتُوا الْبُيُوتَ مِنْ أَبْوَابِهَا (and come into houses from their doors) is not a masterly command (أمر مولوي) — a binding legal obligation — but rather an advisory guidance (إرشاد) toward the propriety of entering houses from their doors, as it aligns with the familiar and commendable custom that accords with the rational purpose (الغرض العقلائي) in building houses and placing doors as points of entry and exit. Since the speech occurs in the context of deterring a bad custom that has no basis other than violating the prevailing custom that accords with rational purpose, it does not indicate more than guidance toward the correct path without making it an obligation (إيجاب). However, entering from other than the door with the intention that it is part of the religion is a forbidden innovation (بدعة).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (and be God-conscious, so that you may be felicitous). You have learned at the beginning of the Surah that God-consciousness (التقوى) is among the attributes that encompass all levels of faith and stations of perfection. It is well known that not all stations necessitate felicity (الفلاح) and happiness (السعادة) in the same way as the final stations, which negate polytheism and deviation from their possessor; rather, they lead toward felicity and give tidings of happiness. Therefore, the Exalted said: وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (and be God-conscious, so that you may be felicitous), using the word of hope (الترجي). It is also possible that what is meant by God-consciousness here is the compliance with this specific command found in the verse and the abandonment of what He censured regarding entering houses from their backs.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The people asked the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم about the new moons, so this verse was revealed: يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَهِلَّةِ قُلْ هي مَوَاقِيتُ لِلنَّاسِ (They ask you about the new moons. Say, ‘They are markers of time for the people’), by which they know the term of their debts, the waiting period of their women, and the time of their Hajj."

I say: This meaning has been narrated therein through other chains from Abu al-Aliyah, Qatadah, and others. It is also narrated that some of them asked the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم about the different states of the moon, and so the verse was revealed. This is what we mentioned earlier as being contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse, so it is not to be relied upon.

Also in al-Durr al-Manthur: Waki', al-Bukhari, and Ibn Jarir narrated from al-Bara: "When they entered the state of ihram in the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية), they would enter the house from its back. So Allah revealed: وَلَيْسَ الْبِرُّ بِأَنْ تَأْتُوا الْبُيُوتَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهَا وَلَكِنَّ الْبِرَّ مَنِ اتَّقَى وَأْتُوا الْبُيُوتَ مِنْ أَبْوَابِهَا (It is not righteousness that you should come into houses from their backs, but righteousness is [the quality of] one who is God-conscious. And come into houses from their doors)."

Also in al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Abi Hatim and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated from Jabir, who said: "The Quraysh used to be called al-Hums (الحمس), and they would enter through the doors while in ihram. The Ansar and the rest of the Arabs would not enter through a door while in ihram. While the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم was in a garden, he exited through its door, and Qutbah ibn Amir al-Ansari exited with him. They said: 'O Messenger of Allah, Qutbah ibn Amir is a sinful man, for he exited with you through the door.' He said to him: 'What prompted you to do what you did?' He replied: 'I saw you do it, so I did as you did.' He said: 'I am a Humsi man.' He replied: 'Then my religion is your religion.' So Allah revealed: لَيْسَ الْبِرُّ بِأَنْ تَأْتُوا الْبُيُوتَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهَا (It is not righteousness that you should come into houses from their backs)."

I say: This meaning has been narrated through other chains as well. Al-Hums (الحمس) is the plural of ahmas (أحمس), like humr and ahmar, derived from al-hamasah (الحماسة), which means intensity or hardness. The Quraysh were named thus due to their intensity in the matter of their religion, or because of their toughness and great might.

The apparent sense of the narration is that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم had, prior to this incident, endorsed entering from the backs of houses for those other than the Quraysh; hence his questioning him: "What prompted you to do what you did?" etc. Based on this, the verse would be among the abrogating (الناسخ) verses, and it would be abrogating a legislated ruling that was not established by a verse. However, you have already seen that the verse contradicts this, as it says: لَيْسَ الْبِرُّ بِأَنْ تَأْتُوا (It is not righteousness that you should come...), and far be it from Allah, the Glorified, that He or His Messenger by His command should legislate a ruling and then censure it or deem it ugly and abrogate it thereafter; this is obvious.

In al-Mahasin of al-Barqi, narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَأْتُوا الْبُيُوتَ مِنْ أَبْوَابِهَا (and come into houses from their doors), he said: "It means to approach a matter from its proper direction, whatever matter it may be."

In al-Kafi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The Successors (الاوصياء) are the doors of Allah through which He is approached. Were it not for them, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, would not be known, and through them, Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, has established His proof over His creation."

I say: The narration is of the nature of "application" (الجرى) — the application of a verse to its most prominent instances — and an explanation of one of the instances of the verse according to the meaning explained in the first narration. There is no doubt that the verse, in terms of its meaning, is general, even if it is specific in terms of its occasion of revelation. His saying (peace be upon him), "Were it not for them, Allah would not be known," refers to the true explanation and the complete call (الدعوة التامة) that is with them. It has another, more subtle meaning that we may perhaps point to in what is to come, if Allah wills. Narrations carrying the meaning of these two are numerous.

Ayahs

وَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِينَ يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ وَلَا تَعْتَدُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ (١٩٠)
Fight in the way of Allah those who fight you, but do not transgress. Indeed Allah does not like transgressors. (190)
وَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ حَيْثُ ثَقِفْتُمُوهُمْ وَأَخْرِجُوهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ أَخْرَجُوكُمْ ۚ وَٱلْفِتْنَةُ أَشَدُّ مِنَ ٱلْقَتْلِ ۚ وَلَا تُقَـٰتِلُوهُمْ عِندَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ حَتَّىٰ يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ فِيهِ ۖ فَإِن قَـٰتَلُوكُمْ فَٱقْتُلُوهُمْ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٩١)
And kill them wherever you confront them, and expel them from where they expelled you, for persecution is graver than killing. But do not fight them near the Holy Mosque unless they fight you therein; but if they fight you, kill them; such is the requital of the faithless. (191)
فَإِنِ ٱنتَهَوْا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٩٢)
But if they desist, then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (192)
وَقَـٰتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّىٰ لَا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ وَيَكُونَ ٱلدِّينُ لِلَّهِ ۖ فَإِنِ ٱنتَهَوْا۟ فَلَا عُدْوَٰنَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٩٣)
Fight them until persecution is no more, and religion becomes [exclusively] for Allah. Then if they desist, there shall be no reprisal except against the wrongdoers. (193)
ٱلشَّهْرُ ٱلْحَرَامُ بِٱلشَّهْرِ ٱلْحَرَامِ وَٱلْحُرُمَـٰتُ قِصَاصٌ ۚ فَمَنِ ٱعْتَدَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَٱعْتَدُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ بِمِثْلِ مَا ٱعْتَدَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٩٤)
A sacred month for a sacred month, and all sanctities require retribution. So should anyone aggress against you, assail him in the manner he assailed you, and be wary of Allah, and know that Allah is with the Godwary. (194)
وَأَنفِقُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا تُلْقُوا۟ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ إِلَى ٱلتَّهْلُكَةِ ۛ وَأَحْسِنُوٓا۟ ۛ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٩٥)
Spend in the way of Allah, and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction; and be virtuous. Indeed Allah loves the virtuous. (195)

(Commentary)

The context of these noble verses indicates that they were revealed all at once. The discourse within them is directed toward a single purpose: the initial legislation of fighting against the polytheists of Mecca. They address the matter of expelling them from where they expelled the believers, as well as the issues of persecution (الفتنة), retaliation (القصاص), and the prohibition of fighting them near the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام) unless they initiate combat there. All these matters are tied to the polytheists of Mecca. Furthermore, the Exalted restricted the fighting to those who initiate it in His saying: "And fight in the way of Allah those who fight you." This does not imply a condition—meaning "fight them if they fight you"—as that is obvious; nor is it a restrictive qualification. Some have suggested it means "fight the men and not the women and children who do not fight you," but this is incorrect. There is no sense in "fighting" someone incapable of combat such that one would be prohibited from "fighting" them; rather, the correct expression would be a prohibition against "killing" them, not "fighting" them.

Rather, it is apparent that the verb—meaning "those who fight you" (يقاتلونكم)—denotes the present state and serves as a descriptive attribute for identification. It refers to those whose current state is one of combat against the believers, namely the polytheists of Mecca.

Thus, the course of these verses follows the course of His saying, the Exalted: أُذِنَ لِلَّذِينَ يُقَاتَلُونَ بِأَنَّهُمْ ظُلِمُوا وَإِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ نَصْرِهِمْ لَقَدِيرٌ الَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا مِن دِيَارِهِم بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ إِلَّا أَن يَقُولُوا رَبُّنَا اللَّهُ — "Permission [to fight] has been given to those who are being fought, because they have been wronged. Indeed Allah is capable of helping them—those who were expelled from their homes unjustly, only because they said, ‘Our Lord is Allah.’" (22:39-40). This is an initial permission for fighting against the combatant polytheists without condition.

Moreover, all five verses are concerned with explaining a single ruling with its boundaries, aspects, and implications. His saying, the Exalted: "And fight in the way of Allah," establishes the core ruling. His saying: "and do not transgress," defines its limits in terms of order. His saying: "And kill them [wherever you confront them]," defines it in terms of severity. His saying: "and do not fight them at the Sacred Mosque," defines it in terms of place. His saying: "And fight them until there is no more persecution," defines it in terms of duration and time. His saying: "The sacred month [for the sacred month]," explains that this ruling is a legislation for retaliation (القصاص) in combat and killing, and a reciprocal treatment of them. His saying: "And spend," makes mandatory its financial prerequisite, which is spending for equipment and preparation. Thus, it is likely that the entirety of the five verses was revealed for a single occasion without some abrogating (الناسخ) others, as some have conjectured, nor were they revealed for disparate matters as others have mentioned. Rather, their purpose is one: the legislation of fighting against the polytheists of Mecca who were fighting the believers.

His saying, the Exalted: وقاتلوا في سبيل الله الذين يقاتلونكم (And fight in the way of Allah those who fight you). Fighting (القتال) is a man's attempt to kill one who is attempting to kill him. Its being "in the way of Allah" is because the purpose behind it is the establishment of the religion (الدين) and the exaltation of the word of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. Thus, it is an act of worship (العبادة) intended for the sake of Allah the Exalted, not for seizing people's wealth or violating their honor. In Islam, it is a defense (دفاع) by which the legitimate rights of humanity are preserved according to the sound primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — as we shall explain. Defense is inherently limited, and transgression (التعدي) is a departure from that limit; therefore, He followed it with His saying: "and do not transgress; indeed Allah does not love the transgressors."

His saying, the Exalted: ولا تعتدوا (and do not transgress). Transgression (الاعتداء) is the departure from a limit. It is said "he crossed" (عدا) and "he transgressed" (اعتدى) when he exceeded his bound. The prohibition of transgression is absolute, intended to cover everything that qualifies as transgression, such as fighting before inviting to the truth, initiating combat, killing women and children, not ceasing when the enemy does, and other matters explained by the Prophetic Sunnah (السنة النبوية).

His saying, the Exalted: واقتلوهم حيث ثقفتموهم (And kill them wherever you confront them) until His saying: [persecution is worse] than killing. It is said "he found" (ثقف ثقافة) meaning he encountered or perceived. Thus, the meaning of the verse is the same as His saying: فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ — "then kill the polytheists wherever you find them" (9:5). Persecution (الفتنة) — the act of testing or trial — is that by which the state of a thing is tested. Therefore, it is applied to the trial and tribulation itself, and to what often accompanies it, such as hardship and torment, as well as what follows it, such as misguidance and polytheism (الشرك). It has been used in the Noble Quran in all these senses. What is intended in the verse is polytheism against Allah and His Messenger through coercion and torment, as the polytheists in Mecca used to do to the believers both before and after the migration of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم.

The meaning is: be as severe as possible against the polytheists in Mecca by killing them wherever they are found, until it leads to their departure from their homes and their exile from their land, just as they did that to you. And what they did was worse, for that was persecution (فتنة) on their part, and persecution is worse than killing. This is because in killing there is the cessation of the life of this world, while in persecution there is the cessation of both lives and the destruction of both abodes.

His saying, the Exalted: ولا تقاتلوهم عند المسجد الحرام حتى يقاتلوكم فيه (and do not fight them at the Sacred Mosque unless they fight you therein). This contains a prohibition against fighting near the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام) to preserve its sanctity so long as they preserve it. The pronoun in His saying "therein" (فيه) refers to the place indicated by His saying "at the Mosque."

His saying, the Exalted: فإِن انتهوا فإِن الله غفور رحيم (But if they relinquish, then Allah is indeed All-forgiving, All-merciful). Relinquishing (الانتهاء) is to refrain and desist. What is intended here is desisting from fighting near the Sacred Mosque specifically, not desisting from all fighting by obeying the religion and accepting Islam; for the latter is what is intended by His second saying: "then if they relinquish, there shall be no hostility." As for this relinquishing, it is a condition referring to the sentence closest to it, which is His saying: "and do not fight them at the Sacred Mosque." Accordingly, each of the two sentences—namely His saying: "But if they relinquish, then Allah..." and His saying: "then if they relinquish, there shall be no hostility"—is a condition for the speech connected to it, without repetition.

In His saying: "then Allah is indeed All-forgiving, All-merciful," the cause is placed in the position of the effect to provide the rationale for the ruling. The meaning is: if they desist, then Allah is All-forgiving and All-merciful [toward them].

His saying, the Exalted: وقاتلوهم حتى لا تكون فتنه ويكون الدين لله (Fight them until there is no more persecution, and religion belongs [entirely] to Allah). This defines the duration of the fighting, as previously mentioned. Persecution (الفتنة) in the language of these verses is polytheism (الشرك) through the taking of idols, as the polytheists in Mecca used to do and coerce others toward. This is indicated by His saying: "and religion belongs to Allah." The verse is similar to His saying, the Exalted: وَقَاتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّىٰ لَا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ وإِن تَوَلَّوْا فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ مُولَاكُمْ نِعْمَ الْمَوْلَىٰ وَنِعْمَ النَّصِيرُ — "Fight them until there is no [more] persecution, and religion becomes [exclusively] for Allah. But if they desist, then Allah is indeed watchful of what they do." (8:39). The verse contains an indication of the obligation of invitation (الدعوة) before fighting; if it is accepted, there is no fighting, and if it is rejected, then there is no guardianship except for Allah, and He is the best Guardian and the best Helper, helping His believing servants. It is well known that fighting is only so that religion belongs to Allah, and there is no sense in fighting of this nature and goal except after an invitation to the true religion—the religion that is established upon monotheism (التوحيد).

It appears from what we have mentioned that this verse is not abrogated by His saying, the Exalted: قَاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَلَا بِالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَلَا يُحَرِّمُونَ مَا حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَلَا يَدِينُونَ دِينَ الْحَقِّ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ حَتَّىٰ يُعْطُوا الْجِزْيَةَ عَن يَدٍ وَهُمْ صَاغِرُونَ — "Fight those who do not have faith in Allah nor [believe] in the Last Day, nor forbid what Allah and His Apostle have forbidden, nor practise the true religion, from among those who were given the Book, until they pay the tribute out of hand, being wretched." (9:29), based on the premise that their religion is for Allah, the Glorified. This is because the verse—meaning His saying: "And fight them until there is no more persecution"—is specific to the polytheists and does not include the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب). Thus, the meaning of "religion belonging to Allah" is that idols are not worshipped and monotheism is affirmed. The People of the Book affirm monotheism, even if that is technically disbelief (كفر) in Allah according to the reality, as He said: "they do not have faith in Allah nor in the Last Day... nor practise the true religion." However, Islam is content with them merely affirming monotheism, and only commanded fighting them until they pay the tribute (الجزية) to exalt the word of truth over theirs and to make Islam manifest over all religion.

His saying, the Exalted: فإِن انتهوا فلا عدوان إِلا على الظالمين (then if they relinquish, there shall be no hostility except against the wrongdoers). That is, if they desist from persecution and believe in what you believe in, then do not fight them, for there is no hostility except against the wrongdoers (الظالمين). This is another instance of placing the cause in the position of the effect, as seen previously in His saying: "But if they relinquish, then Allah is indeed All-forgiving, All-merciful." The verse is like His saying, the Exalted: فإِن تَابُوا وَأَقَامُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكَاةَ فَإِخْوَانُكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ — "Yet if they repent and maintain the prayer and give the zakat, then they are your brethren in faith." (9:11).

His saying, the Exalted: الشهر الحرام بالشهر الحرام والحرمات قصاص (The sacred month for the sacred month, and all sacred things are [subject to] retaliation).
