"Sacred things" (الحرمات) is the plural of "sanctity" (حرمة), which is that which is forbidden to violate and mandatory to venerate and respect. The sacred things are: the sanctity of the sacred month, the sanctity of the Sanctuary (الحرم), and the sanctity of the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام). The meaning is that if they were to violate the sanctity of the sacred month by fighting in it—and they did violate it when they barred the Prophet and his companions from the Pilgrimage in the year of al-Hudaybiyyah and pelted them with arrows and stones—it became permissible for the believers to fight them in it, and this is not a violation. For they are only striving in the way of Allah and obeying His command in exalting His word. And if they violated the sanctity of the Sanctuary and the Sacred Mosque by fighting in it or near it, it became permissible for the believers to treat them reciprocally. Thus, His saying: "The sacred month for the sacred month" is a specific explanation followed by a general explanation that includes all sacred things. Even more general than this general explanation is His saying immediately after: "So should anyone aggress against you, assail him in the manner he assailed you." The meaning is that Allah, the Glorified, only legislated retaliation (القصاص) in the sacred month because He legislated retaliation in all sacred things, and He only legislated retaliation in sacred things because He legislated the permissibility of reciprocal aggression.

Then He urged them to adhere to the path of caution (الاحتياط) in aggression, because it involves the use of severity, might, power, and other forces that invite tyranny (الطغيان) and deviation from the path of moderation, and Allah, the Glorified, does not love the transgressors. They are in greater need of Allah's love, guardianship, and help; so He said: "and be God-conscious, and know that Allah is with the God-conscious (المتقين)."

As for His command to "assail" (الاعتداء) despite the fact that He does not love the transgressors (المعتدين), aggression is blameworthy when it is not in response to aggression. But when it is in response to aggression, it is nothing but rising above the humiliation of lowliness and ascending from the depths of enslavement, injustice, and oppression—much like pride toward the proud, or the public utterance of evil by one who has been wronged.

His saying, the Exalted: وأنفقوا في سبيل الله ولا تلقوا بأيديكم إِلى التهلكة (Spend in the way of Allah, and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction). He commanded the spending of wealth to establish fighting in the way of Allah. The discourse in restricting spending here to being "in the way of Allah" is similar to the restriction of fighting at the beginning of the verses to being "in the way of Allah," as previously mentioned. The preposition "with" (بـ) in His saying "with your hands" (بأيديكم) is extra for emphasis. The meaning is: "do not cast your hands into destruction," which is a metaphor for the prohibition against nullifying one's strength, capability, and power, as the hand is the manifestation of these. It is also said that the "بـ" denotes causality and the object of "do not cast" is omitted; the meaning being: "do not cast yourselves by your own hands into destruction." Destruction (التهلكة) and ruin (الهلاك) are the same; it is for a human to reach a state where he does not know where he is. It is on the pattern of taph'ulah (تَفْعُلَة) with a damma on the 'ayn; there is no other infinitive (مصدر) in the language on this pattern.

The speech is absolute, intended as a prohibition against everything that leads to ruin, whether through excess (إفراط) or deficiency (تفريط). Just as stinginess and withholding wealth during combat leads to the nullification of strength and the loss of power—and therein lies the ruin of the group by the enemy's victory over them—so too does extravagance (التبذير) by spending all one's wealth lead to poverty and destitution, which lead to the degradation of life and the loss of dignity.

Then He, the Glorified, concluded the speech with excellence (الاحسان), saying: "and do good; indeed Allah loves the virtuous (المحسنين)." What is intended by excellence (الاحسان) is not refraining from fighting or showing pity in killing the enemies of the religion and the like. Rather, excellence is performing an act in a beautiful manner: by fighting in the place of fighting, refraining in the place of refraining, severity in the place of severity, and forgiveness in the place of forgiveness. Repelling the wrongdoer with what he deserves is an act of excellence toward humanity by fulfilling its legitimate right, and a defense of the religion that rectifies its affairs. Similarly, refraining from exceeding the limit in fulfilling a legitimate right is another act of excellence. The love of Allah, the Glorified, is the ultimate goal of religion, and it is the duty of every religious person to draw it from his Lord through following. He said: قُلْ إِن كُنتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللَّهَ فَاتَّبِعُونِي يُحْبِبْكُمُ اللَّهُ — "Say, ‘If you love Allah, then follow me; Allah will love you’" (3:31). These noble verses—which are the verses of fighting—began with the prohibition of transgression and that Allah does not love the transgressors, and concluded with the command for excellence and that Allah loves the virtuous. In this, there are aspects of beauty that are not hidden.

The Jihad Commanded by the Quran:

The Quran used to command the Muslims to refrain from fighting and to be patient with every injury in the way of Allah, the Glorified, as He said: قُلْ يَا أَيُّهَا الْكَافِرُونَ لَا أَعْبُدُ مَا تَعْبُدُونَ وَلَا أَنتُمْ عَابِدُونَ مَا أَعْبُدُ until His saying: لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَلِيَ دِينِ — "Say, ‘O faithless ones! I do not worship what you worship... To you your religion, and to me my religion.’" (109:1-6). And He said: وَاصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ — "and be patient with what they say" (73:10). And He said: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلى الَّذِينَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ كُفُّوا أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكَاةَ فَلَمَّا كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقِتَالُ — "Have you not regarded those who were told, ‘Keep your hands off [from fighting], and maintain the prayer, and give the zakat’? But when fighting was prescribed for them..." (4:77). It is as if this verse refers to His saying, the Glorified: وَدَّ كَثِيرٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ لَوْ يَرُدُّونَكُم مِّن بَعْدِ إِيمَانِكُمْ كُفَّارًا حَسَدًا مِّنْ عِندِ أَنفُسِهِم مِّن بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْحَقُّ فَاعْفُوا وَاصْفَحُوا حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَ اللَّهُ بِأَمْرِهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكَاةَ — "Many of the People of the Book desire to turn you back into faithless ones after your belief, out of envy from themselves, after the truth has become manifest to them. So excuse and forbear until Allah issues His edict. Indeed Allah has power over all things. And maintain the prayer and give the zakat." (2:109-110).

Then the verses of fighting were revealed. Among them are the verses of fighting specifically against the polytheists of Mecca and those with them, such as His saying: أُذِنَ لِلَّذِينَ يُقَاتَلُونَ بِأَنَّهُمْ ظُلِمُوا وإِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ نَصْرِهِمْ لَقَدِيرٌ الَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا مِن دِيَارِهِم بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ إِلَّا أَن يَقُولُوا رَبُّنَا اللَّهُ — "Permission [to fight] has been given to those who are being fought, because they have been wronged. Indeed Allah is capable of helping them—those who were expelled from their homes unjustly, only because they said, ‘Our Lord is Allah.’" (22:39-40). It is possible that this verse was revealed regarding the defense commanded at Badr and elsewhere. Likewise His saying: وَقَاتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّىٰ لَا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ وَيَكُونَ الدِّينُ كُلُّهُ لِلَّهِ فإِنِ انتَهَوْا فإِن اللَّهَ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ وإِن تَوَلَّوْا فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ مَوْلَاكُمْ نِعْمَ الْمَوْلَىٰ وَنِعْمَ النَّصِيرُ — "Fight them until there is no [more] persecution, and religion becomes exclusively for Allah. But if they desist, then Allah is indeed watchful of what they do. But if they turn away, then know that Allah is your guardian—an excellent guardian and an excellent helper!" (8:39-40). Likewise His saying: وَقَاتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ الَّذِينَ يُقَاتَلُونَكُمْ وَلَا تَعْتَدُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ الْمُعْتَدِينَ — "Fight in the way of Allah those who fight you, but do not transgress. Indeed Allah does not love transgressors." (2:190).

Among them are the verses of fighting against the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب). He said: قَاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَلَا بِالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَلَا يُحَرِّمُونَ مَا حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَلَا يَدِينُونَ دِينَ الْحَقِّ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ حَتَّىٰ يُعْطُوا الْجِزْيَةَ عَن يَدٍ وَهُمْ صَاغِرُونَ — "Fight those who do not have faith in Allah nor [believe] in the Last Day, nor forbid what Allah and His Apostle have forbidden, nor practise the true religion, from among those who were given the Book, until they pay the tribute out of hand, being wretched." (9:29).

Among them are the verses of fighting against the polytheists in general, who are not from the People of the Book, such as His saying: فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ — "then kill the polytheists wherever you find them" (9:5), and His saying: قَاتِلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ كَافَّةً كَمَا يُقَاتِلُونَكُمْ كَافَّةً — "and fight the polytheists all together just as they fight you all together" (9:36).

Among them are those that command fighting the disbelievers (الكفار) in general, such as His saying: قَاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ يَلُونَكُم مِّنَ الْكُفَّارِ وَلْيَجِدُوا فِيكُمْ غِلْظَةً — "fight those of the faithless who are in your vicinity, and let them find severity in you" (9:123).

The summary of the matter is that the Quran mentions that Islam and the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) are built upon the foundation of the primordial nature (الفطرة), and it is the guardian (القيم) over the rectification of humanity in its life, as He said: فأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللَّهِ ذَٰلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ — "So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind (there is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion, but most people do not know)" (30:30). Thus, establishing it and preserving it is the most important of legitimate human rights, as He said: شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ الدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِ نُوحًا وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ — "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus, declaring, ‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it.’" (42:13). Then He mentions that defense (الدفاع) of this legitimate natural right is another natural right. He said: وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ اللَّهِ النَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لَّهُدِّمَتْ صَوَامِعُ وَبِيَعٌ وَصَلَوَاتٌ وَمَسَاجِدُ يُذْكَرُ فِيهَا اسْمُ اللَّهِ كَثِيرًا وَلَيَنصُرَنَّ اللَّهُ مَن يَنصُرُهُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَقَوِيٌّ عَزِيزٌ — "Were it not for Allah’s repelling some people by means of others, monasteries, churches, synagogues and mosques where the name of Allah is much invoked would have been pulled down. Surely Allah will help those who help Him. Indeed Allah is all-strong, all-mighty." (22:40). Thus, He explained that the standing of the religion of monotheism and the life of its remembrance are contingent upon defense. Similar to this is His saying: وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ اللَّهِ النَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لَّفَسَدَتِ الْأَرْضُ — "Were it not for Allah’s repelling some people by means of others, the earth would surely have been corrupted" (2:251). He also said within the verses of fighting in Surah al-Anfal: لِيُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ وَيُبْطِلَ الْبَاطِلَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُجْرِمُونَ — "that He might confirm the truth and bring falsehood to naught, though the guilty should be averse" (8:8). Then He said after several verses: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعَاكُمْ لِمَا يُحْيِيكُمْ — "O you who have faith! Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life" (8:24). He named Jihad (الجهاد) and the fighting to which the believers are summoned as that which gives them life. This means that fighting—whether under the title of defending the Muslims or the core of Islam (بيضة الإسلام), or whether it was initial fighting—is all in reality a defense of the right of humanity to its life. For in polytheism (الشرك) against Allah, the Glorified, lies the destruction of humanity and the death of the primordial nature (الفطرة), and in fighting—which is a defense of its right—lies the restoration of its life and its revival after death.

From there, the intelligent and discerning person senses that Islam ought to have a defensive ruling regarding the cleansing of the earth from the filth of absolute polytheism and the purification of faith for Allah, the Glorified. For this fighting mentioned in the cited verses is either to kill the manifest polytheism of idolatry, or to exalt the word of truth over the word of the People of the Book by compelling them to pay the tribute (الجزية), even though the verse of fighting them includes that they do not believe in Allah and His Messenger nor practice the true religion. Thus, even if they are ostensibly on monotheism, they are polytheists in reality, harboring it within. Defense of the natural human right necessitates bringing them to the true religion.

Although the Quran does not contain an explicit command for this ruling, it openly promises a day for the believers over their enemies, the matter of which will not be completed except by fulfilling the command at this level of fighting—which is fighting to establish sincerity in monotheism (التوحيد). He said: هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ الْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُ عَلَى الدِّينِ كُلِّهِ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُشْرِكُونَ — "It is He who has sent His Apostle with guidance and the true religion that He may make it prevail over all religions, though the polytheists should be averse." (61:9). More manifest than this is His saying: وَلَقَدْ كَتَبْنَا فِي الزَّبُورِ مِن بَعْدِ الذِّكْرِ أنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُهَا عِبَادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ — "Certainly We wrote in the Psalms, after the Torah: ‘Indeed My righteous servants shall inherit the earth.’" (21:105). And more explicit than that is His saying: وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ كَمَا اسْتَخْلَفَ الَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ وَلَيُمَكِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ دِينَهُمُ الَّذِي ارْتَضَىٰ لَهُمْ وَلَيُبَدِّلَنَّهُم مِّنْ بَعْدِ خَوْفِهِمْ أَمْنًا يَعْبُدُونَنِي لَا يُشْرِكُونَ بِي شَيْئًا — "Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth, even as He made those who were before them successors, and He will surely establish for them their religion which He has approved for them, and that He will surely replace their fear with security, [for] they worship Me, not ascribing any partners to Me." (24:55). His saying "they worship Me" means the worship of sincerity with the reality of faith, by the context of His saying "not ascribing any partners to Me," even though He considers some faith to be polytheism (شرك), as He said: وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُم بِاللَّهِ إِلَّا وَهُم مُّشْرِكُونَ — "And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners to Him." (12:106). This is what He promised of purifying the earth and vacating it for the believers on a day when none but Allah is truly worshipped.

One might imagine that this is a promise of divine help through a hidden reformer without recourse to manifest causes. However, this is contradicted by His saying: "He will surely make them successors in the earth," for succession (الاستخلاف) only occurs by the departure of some and their removal from their place and the putting of others in their stead; thus, it contains an allusion to fighting.

Furthermore, His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَن يَرْتَدَّ مِنكُمْ عَن دِينِهِ فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِي اللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُ أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَعِزَّةٍ عَلَى الْكَافِرِينَ يُجَاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَلَا يَخَافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لَائِمٍ — "O you who have faith! Should any of you desert his religion, Allah will soon bring a people whom He loves and who love Him, [who will be] humble towards the faithful, dignified before the faithless, waging jihad in the way of Allah, not fearing the blame of any blamer." (5:54)—as will come in its place—points to a true call and a religious uprising that will occur by divine command. This supports the idea that this promised event will occur through a call to Jihad.

From the preceding explanation, the answer appears to what is sometimes raised against Islam regarding its legislation of Jihad: that it is a departure from the mode of religious movements narrated from previous prophets. It is claimed that their religions relied in their course and progress on invitation (الدعوة) and guidance (الهداية) alone, without coercion (الإكراه) into faith through fighting that entails killing, captivity, and raids. For this reason, some—such as Christian missionaries—have called it the "religion of the sword and blood," and others the "religion of compulsion and coercion."

This is because the Quran explains that Islam is built upon the decree of the human primordial nature (الفطرة), which should not be doubted that the perfection of man in his life is what it decrees, judges, and invites toward. It decrees that monotheism (التوحيد) is the foundation upon which individual and social laws must be built, and that defense of this principle by spreading it among people and preserving it from destruction and corruption is a legitimate human right that must be fulfilled by any possible means. Moderation has been observed in this: it began with pure invitation and patience with injury for the sake of Allah, then defense of the core of Islam and the lives, honor, and wealth of Muslims, then initial fighting which is a defense of the right of humanity and the word of monotheism. No fighting was initiated except after the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة) through beautiful invitation, as was the Prophetic Sunnah. He said: ادْعُ إِلَىٰ سَبِيلِ رَبِّكَ بِالْحِكْمَةِ وَالْمَوْعِظَةِ الْحَسَنَةِ وَجَادِلْهُم بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ — "Invite to the way of your Lord with wisdom and good advice and dispute with them in a manner that is best" (16:125). The verse is absolute. He also said: لِّيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَن بَيِّنَةٍ وَيَحْيَىٰ مَنْ حَيَّ عَن بَيِّنَةٍ — "so that he who perishes might perish by a clear proof, and he who lives might live by a clear proof" (8:42).

As for what they mentioned regarding it necessitating coercion (الإكراه) upon victory, there is no harm in it once the revival of humanity depends on imposing the legitimate truth upon a number of individuals after the explanation and the establishment of the conclusive proof (الحجة البالغة) against them. This is a method prevalent among nations and states; for the rebel who deviates from civil laws is invited to follow them, then they are imposed upon him by any means possible, even if it leads to fighting, until he obeys and submits, willingly or unwillingly.

Furthermore, coercion only lives and lasts in one generation; then religious education and training rectify the coming generations by raising them upon the natural religion and the word of monotheism willingly.

As for their claim that other prophets proceeded only with invitation and guidance, the existing history of their lives indicates that their scope was not wide enough to allow them to engage in fighting, such as Noah, Hud, and Salih (peace be upon them), for they were surrounded by coercion and sovereignty from every side. Similarly was Jesus (peace be upon him) during the days of his stay among the people and his engagement in invitation; his call only spread and his proof was only accepted at the time when abrogation (النسخ) was occurring to his law, which was during the rise of Islam.

Moreover, a group of prophets did fight in the way of Allah, as the Torah relates and the Quran mentions parts of. He said: وَكَأَيِّن مِّن نَّبِيٍّ قَاتَلَ مَعَهُ رِبِّيُّونَ كَثِيرٌ فَمَا وَهَنُوا لِمَا أَصَابَهُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَمَا ضَعُفُوا وَمَا اسْتَكَانُوا وَاللَّهُ يُحِبُّ الصَّابِرِينَ وَمَا كَانَ قَوْلَهُمْ إِلَّا أَن قَالُوا رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَا وَإِسْرَافَنَا فِي أَمْرِنَا وَثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَنَا وَانصُرْنَا عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْكَافِرِينَ — "How many a prophet there has been with whom a multitude of godly men fought. They did not falter for what befell them in the way of Allah, neither did they weaken, nor did they abase themselves; and Allah loves the steadfast. All that they said was, ‘Our Lord, forgive us our sins and our excesses in our affairs, make our feet steady, and help us against the faithless lot.’" (3:146-147). He also said—relating Moses' call to his people to fight the giants: وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ until He said: يَا قَوْمِ ادْخُلُوا الْأَرْضَ الْمُقَدَّسَةَ الَّتِي كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ وَلَا تَرْتَدُّوا عَلَىٰ أَدْبَارِكُمْ فَتَنقَلِبُوا خَاسِرِينَ until He said: قَالُوا يَا مُوسَىٰ إِنَّا لَن نَّدْخُلَهَا أَبَدًا مَّا دَامُوا فِيهَا فَاذْهَبْ أَنتَ وَرَبُّكَ فَقَاتِلَا إِنَّا هَاهُنَا قَاعِدُونَ — "And when Moses said... ‘O my people, enter the Holy Land which Allah has ordained for you, and do not turn your backs, or you will become losers.’ ... They said, ‘O Moses, we will never enter it so long as they are in it. Go ahead, you and your Lord, and fight! We will be sitting right here.’" (5:20-24). And He said: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلى الْمَلَإِ مِن بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ مِن بَعْدِ مُوسَىٰ إِذْ قَالُوا لِنَبِيٍّ لَّهُمُ ابْعَثْ لَنَا مَلِكًا نُّقَاتِلْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ — "Have you not regarded the elite of the Israelites after Moses, when they said to a prophet of theirs, ‘Appoint a king for us, that we may fight in the way of Allah’?" (2:246), until the end of the story of Saul (طالوت) and Goliath (جالوت).

He also said in the story of Solomon and the Queen of Sheba: أَلَّا تَعْلُوا عَلَيَّ وَأْتُونِي مُسْلِمِينَ until He said: ارْجِعْ إِلَيْهِمْ فَلَنَأْتِيَنَّهُم بِجُنُودٍ لَّا قِبَلَ لَهُم بها وَلَنُخْرِجَنَّهُم مِّنْهَا أَذِلَّةً وَهُمْ صَاغِرُونَ — "‘Do not defy me, and come to me in submission.’ ... ‘Go back to them, for we will come to them with hosts they cannot withstand, and we will expel them from it, abased and wretched.’" (27:31-37). This which he threatened them with—"we will come to them with hosts they cannot withstand"—was nothing but initial fighting following an initial invitation.

(Social Discussion)

There is no doubt that society, wherever it exists—such as the society of the human species and other various specific societies we observe in types of animals—is built upon the foundation of the natural need (الاحتياج الفطري) present within them, which is intended for the preservation of existence and survival.

Just as the primordial nature (الفطرة) and innate disposition (الجبلة) gave them the right of disposal (التصرف) over everything they benefit from in their lives for the preservation of existence and survival—as man disposes of minerals, plants, and animals, and even other humans by any possible means, seeing for himself a right in that even if it interferes with the rights of other animals or the perfection of plants and minerals—and as types of animals do in their disposals of others, acknowledging that they have a right in that; similarly, it gave them the right of defense (الدفاع) of their legitimate rights according to their nature. For the right of disposal is not complete without the right of defense. The world is an abode of interference (تزاحم), and the law is the law of the struggle for survival. Thus, every species that preserves its existence and survival through perception and movement sees for itself a natural right of defense of its rights and acknowledges that this is permissible for it, just as it acknowledges the permissibility of its aforementioned disposal. This is evidenced by what we observe in animal species: they resort during conflict to their bodily tools suitable for use in defense, such as horns, tusks, claws, hooves, thorns, beaks, and the like. Some that are not armed with any of these strong natural weapons resort to flight, hiding, or remaining still, like some prey, turtles, and certain insects. Some that are capable of employing ruses and schemes may take to them in defense, like monkeys, bears, foxes, and their like.

Man, among animals, is armed with intellectual perception (الشعور الفكري) by which he is able to employ others for the sake of defense, just as he is able to do so for the sake of disposal for benefit. He has a nature like other species, and his nature has a judgment and decree. Among its judgments is that man has a right to disposal, and a right to defend his natural right. This right, which man acknowledges by his nature, is what drives him toward fighting and combat in all instances where he is concerned with them in human society, rather than the law of employment (الاستخدام) which he judges as a primary natural judgment—whereby he employs everything he can in the path of his vital interests. For this judgment is moderated by society; when man senses his need to employ others of his kind, and then knows that other individuals of his kind are like him in need, he is forced into reconciliation and agreement upon civilization and social justice—that he serves others to the extent that he employs others, according to what need weighs with its scale and society moderates with its moderation.

From here it is known that man does not rely in any of his fightings upon the law of absolute employment and enslavement which he acknowledges at his first instance. For this is an absolute judgment that man himself abrogated upon his first entry into society, admitting that he should not dispose of the benefits of others except to the extent that he gives others of the benefits of himself. Rather, he only relies in that upon the right of defense of his rights in his benefits; he assumes a right for himself, then observes its loss, so he rises to defend it.

Thus, every fighting is a defense in reality. Even the conquering kings and the prevailing states assume for themselves a kind of right, such as the right of sovereignty and the fitness to command others, or a hardship in living, or a narrowness in land, or the like; they use these as excuses in their attacks on people, the shedding of blood, the corruption of the earth, and the destruction of crops and progeny.

It has been clarified that defense of human rights is a legitimate natural right, the fulfillment of which is permissible for man. However, since this is a right sought for something else and not for itself, it must be balanced against the importance of what belongs to others. One should not proceed with defense unless what man loses by defending is less in vital importance than the lost right being recovered. The Quran has established that the most important of human rights is monotheism (التوحيد) and the religious laws built upon it, just as the rational people of human society agree that the most important of its rights is the right to life under the laws governing human society that preserve the interests of individuals in their lives.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying, the Exalted: "And fight in the way of Allah..." the verse: This verse was revealed regarding the Peace of al-Hudaybiyyah. That was when the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم went out with his companions in the year they intended the Umrah—and they were one thousand four hundred. They traveled until they camped at al-Hudaybiyyah, and the polytheists barred them from the Sacred House. They slaughtered the sacrificial animals at al-Hudaybiyyah, then the polytheists made peace with them on the condition that he return that year and come back the following year, and Mecca would be vacated for him for three days to circumambulate the House and do as he wished. He returned to Medina immediately. When the following year came, the Prophet and his companions prepared for the Umrah of Redress (عمرة القضاء). They feared that Quraysh would not fulfill their promise and would bar them from the Sacred House and fight them. The Messenger of Allah disliked fighting them in the sacred month in the Sanctuary, so Allah revealed this verse.

I say: This meaning has been narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur through various chains from Ibn Abbas and others.

Also in al-Majma', from al-Rabi' ibn Anas and Abd al-Rahman ibn Zayd ibn Aslam: This is the first verse revealed regarding fighting. When it was revealed, the Messenger of Allah would fight those who fought him and refrain from those who refrained from him, until "kill the polytheists wherever you find them" was revealed, so it abrogated this verse.

I say: This is an exercise of personal judgment (اجتهاد) on their part. You have already learned that the verse does not abrogate the other; rather, it is a case of generalizing the ruling after its specification.

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: "And kill them wherever you confront them..." the verse: It was revealed concerning a man from the companions who killed a man from the disbelievers in the sacred month. The believers were criticized for that, so Allah, the Glorified, explained that persecution (الفتنة) in religion—which is polytheism—is greater than killing the polytheists in the sacred month, even if it is not permissible.

I say: You have already learned that the apparent unity of context in the noble verses indicates they were revealed all at once.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "And fight them until there is no more persecution..." the verse: through various chains from Qatadah, who said: "And fight them until there is no more persecution," meaning polytheism, "and religion belongs to Allah." He said: "Until it is said: 'There is no god but Allah.' Upon this the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم fought, and to this he invited." And it was mentioned to us that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم used to say: "Indeed Allah commanded me to fight the people until they say: 'There is no god but Allah.' Then if they relinquish, there shall be no hostility except against the wrongdoers." He said: "And the wrongdoer is he who refuses to say 'There is no god but Allah'; he is fought until he says: 'There is no god but Allah.'"

I say: His saying "And the wrongdoer..." is from the words of Qatadah; he derived that from the words of the Prophet, and it is a fine derivation. Something similar has been narrated from Ikrimah.

Also in al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bukhari, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn Umar: Two men came to him during the persecution (فتنة) of Ibn al-Zubayr and said: "The people have done what they have done, and you are the son of Umar and a companion of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, so what prevents you from coming out?" He said: "What prevents me is that Allah has forbidden the blood of my brother." They said: "Did not Allah say: 'And fight them until there is no more persecution'?" He said: "We fought until there was no more persecution and religion belonged to Allah, while you want to fight until there is persecution and religion belongs to other than Allah."

I say: He erred in the meaning of persecution (الفتنة), and the two questioners erred as well, as has been explained. Rather, the case is one of the instances of corruption in the earth or fighting out of rebellion (بغي), and it is not permissible for the believers to remain silent regarding it.

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: "And fight them until there is no more persecution..." the verse: He said, meaning polytheism. He said: And it is narrated from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "The sacred month for the sacred month," from al-Ala ibn al-Fudayl, who said: I asked him about the polytheists: do the Muslims initiate fighting against them in the sacred month? He said: If the polytheists initiate it by deeming it lawful, the Muslims may see fit to prevail over them in it. That is His saying: "The sacred month for the sacred month, and all sacred things are subject to retaliation."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, Ibn Jarir, and al-Nahhas in his al-Nasikh extracted from Jabir ibn Abdillah, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم would not raid in the sacred month until he was raided; and he would raid, but if it [the month] arrived, he would stay until it passed.

In al-Kafi, from Mu'awiyah ibn Ammar, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah about a man who killed a man in the non-sacred area (الحل) then entered the Sanctuary (الحرم). He عليه‌السلام said: "He is not killed, nor fed, nor given drink, nor traded with until he leaves the Sanctuary, then the legal punishment (الحد) is carried out upon him." I said: "What do you say about a man who killed in the Sanctuary or stole?" He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said: "The legal punishment is carried out upon him in the Sanctuary because he did not respect the sanctity of the Sanctuary. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has said: فَمَنِ اعْتَدَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَاعْتَدُوا عَلَيْهِ بِمِثْلِ مَا اعْتَدَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ — 'So should anyone aggress against you, assail him in the manner he assailed you' (2:194)." He said: "This is in the Sanctuary." He said: "There is no hostility except against the wrongdoers."

In al-Kafi, from al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام regarding His saying, the Exalted: "and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction," he said: "If a man were to spend all that is in his hands in the way of Allah, it would not be good nor would he be successful. Does not Allah say: 'and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction, and do good; indeed Allah loves the virtuous'—meaning the moderate (المقتصدين)!"

Al-Saduq narrated from Thabit ibn Anas, who said: The Messenger of Allah said: "Obedience to the ruler is obligatory, and whoever leaves obedience to the ruler has left obedience to Allah and entered into His prohibition. Allah says: 'and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, through many chains from Aslam Abu Imran, who said: We were at Constantinople. Over the people of Egypt was Uqbah ibn Amir, and over the people of Syria was Fadalah ibn Ubayd. A great rank of Romans came out, so we lined up for them. A man from the Muslims charged at the Roman rank until he entered among them. The people shouted and said: "Glory be to Allah! He is casting himself with his own hands into destruction." Abu Ayyub, the companion of the Messenger of Allah, stood up and said: "O people, you interpret this verse with this interpretation, but this verse was only revealed concerning us, the assembly of the Ansar. When Allah had strengthened His religion and its helpers became many, some of us said to others secretly, without the Messenger of Allah: 'Our wealth has been lost, and Allah has strengthened Islam and its helpers have become many. If only we were to stay with our wealth and rectify what was lost of it.' So Allah revealed to His Prophet, refuting what we said: 'And spend in the way of Allah, and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction.' Thus, the destruction was staying with the wealth and rectifying it, and leaving the raiding."

I say: The variation in the narrations, as you see, regarding the meaning of the verse supports what we mentioned: that the verse is absolute, including both sides of excess and deficiency in spending together; rather, it encompasses spending and other matters.

Ayahs

وَأَتِمُّوا۟ ٱلْحَجَّ وَٱلْعُمْرَةَ لِلَّهِ ۚ فَإِنْ أُحْصِرْتُمْ فَمَا ٱسْتَيْسَرَ مِنَ ٱلْهَدْىِ ۖ وَلَا تَحْلِقُوا۟ رُءُوسَكُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ ٱلْهَدْىُ مَحِلَّهُۥ ۚ فَمَن كَانَ مِنكُم مَّرِيضًا أَوْ بِهِۦٓ أَذًى مِّن رَّأْسِهِۦ فَفِدْيَةٌ مِّن صِيَامٍ أَوْ صَدَقَةٍ أَوْ نُسُكٍ ۚ فَإِذَآ أَمِنتُمْ فَمَن تَمَتَّعَ بِٱلْعُمْرَةِ إِلَى ٱلْحَجِّ فَمَا ٱسْتَيْسَرَ مِنَ ٱلْهَدْىِ ۚ فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ ثَلَـٰثَةِ أَيَّامٍ فِى ٱلْحَجِّ وَسَبْعَةٍ إِذَا رَجَعْتُمْ ۗ تِلْكَ عَشَرَةٌ كَامِلَةٌ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن لَّمْ يَكُنْ أَهْلُهُۥ حَاضِرِى ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (١٩٦)
Complete the hajj and the ‘umrah for Allah’s sake, and if you are prevented, then [make] such [sacrificial] offering as is feasible. And do not shave your heads until the offering reaches its [assigned] place. But should any of you be sick, or have a hurt in his head, let the atonement be by fasting, or charity, or sacrifice. And when you have security—for those who enjoy [release from the restrictions] by virtue of their ‘umrah until the hajj—let the offering be such as is feasible. As for someone who cannot afford [the offering], let him fast three days during the hajj and seven when you return; that is [a period of] ten complete [days]. That is for someone whose family does not dwell by the Holy Mosque. And be wary of Allah, and know that Allah is severe in retribution. (196)
ٱلْحَجُّ أَشْهُرٌ مَّعْلُومَـٰتٌ ۚ فَمَن فَرَضَ فِيهِنَّ ٱلْحَجَّ فَلَا رَفَثَ وَلَا فُسُوقَ وَلَا جِدَالَ فِى ٱلْحَجِّ ۗ وَمَا تَفْعَلُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ يَعْلَمْهُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَتَزَوَّدُوا۟ فَإِنَّ خَيْرَ ٱلزَّادِ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ ۚ وَٱتَّقُونِ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (١٩٧)
The hajj [season] is in months well-known; so whoever decides on hajj [pilgrimage] therein, [should know that] there is to be no sexual contact, vicious talk, or disputing during the hajj. And whatever good you do, Allah knows it. And take provision, for indeed the best provision is Godwariness. So be wary of Me, O you who possess intellects! (197)
لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَبْتَغُوا۟ فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۚ فَإِذَآ أَفَضْتُم مِّنْ عَرَفَـٰتٍ فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عِندَ ٱلْمَشْعَرِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۖ وَٱذْكُرُوهُ كَمَا هَدَىٰكُمْ وَإِن كُنتُم مِّن قَبْلِهِۦ لَمِنَ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (١٩٨)
There is no sin upon you in seeking your Lord’s bounty [during the hajj season]. Then when you stream out of ‘Arafat remember Allah at the Holy Mash‘ar, and remember Him as He has guided you, and earlier you were indeed among the astray. (198)
ثُمَّ أَفِيضُوا۟ مِنْ حَيْثُ أَفَاضَ ٱلنَّاسُ وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٩٩)
Then stream out from where the people stream out, and plead to Allah for forgiveness; indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (199)
فَإِذَا قَضَيْتُم مَّنَـٰسِكَكُمْ فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَذِكْرِكُمْ ءَابَآءَكُمْ أَوْ أَشَدَّ ذِكْرًا ۗ فَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَقُولُ رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِنَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَمَا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنْ خَلَـٰقٍ (٢٠٠)
And when you finish your rites, then remember Allah as you would remember your fathers, or with a more ardent remembrance. Among the people there are those who say, ‘Our Lord, give us in this world,’ but for such there is no share in the Hereafter. (200)
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَقُولُ رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِنَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ حَسَنَةً وَقِنَا عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ (٢٠١)
And among them there are those who say, ‘Our Lord, give us good in this world and good in the Hereafter, and save us from the punishment of the Fire.’ (201)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٢٠٢)
Such shall partake of what they have earned, and Allah is swift at reckoning. (202)
۞ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فِىٓ أَيَّامٍ مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ ۚ فَمَن تَعَجَّلَ فِى يَوْمَيْنِ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ وَمَن تَأَخَّرَ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ لِمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ ۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّكُمْ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (٢٠٣)
Remember Allah in the appointed days. Then whoever hastens off in a couple of days, there is no sin upon him, and whoever delays, there is no sin upon him—that for one who has been Godwary—and be wary of Allah and know that toward Him you will be mustered. (203)

(Commentary)

The verses were revealed during the Farewell Pilgrimage (حجة الوداع), the final pilgrimage performed by the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and they contain the legislation for the Tamattu‘ pilgrimage (حج التمتع).

His saying, the Exalted: وأتموا الحج والعمرة لله (And complete the Hajj and the ‘Umrah for Allah). The "completion" (تمام) of a thing is that part which, when joined to the other parts of the thing, makes the thing what it is, and upon which its desired effects depend. Thus, "completing" (الإتمام) is the act of joining the final part of a thing to it after having commenced some of its parts. "Perfection" (الكمال), on the other hand, is a state, attribute, or matter which, when found in a thing, results in an effect after its completion that would not have resulted without that perfection. For instance, the joining of the parts of a human being to one another is his completion (تمام), while his being a scholar, brave, or chaste is his perfection (كمال). Sometimes "completion" is used in place of "perfection" metaphorically, by claiming that an additional attribute is internal to the thing out of concern for its importance. What is intended by "completing the Hajj and the ‘Umrah" is the first, literal meaning. The evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted, immediately following it: فإِن أُحصرتم فما استيسر من الهدى (but if you are prevented, then [slaughter] whatever offering is easily available). This is a branching (تفريع) from the concept of completion in the sense of bringing the work to its final parts and joining them to the parts already performed after commencement. There is no meaning that would justify branching it from "completion" in the sense of "perfection," as is evident.

The Hajj (الحج) is the well-known act among Muslims that was legislated by Ibrahim the Intimate (إبراهيم الخليل) (عليه السلام). It continued among the Arabs after him, and then Allah, the Glorified, ratified it for this nation as a lasting law (شريعة) until the Day of Resurrection.

This act begins with the state of consecration (الإحرام) and the standing (الوقوف) at ‘Arafat, then the Sacred Monument (المشعر الحرام). It includes the sacrifice at Mina, the stoning of the three pillars (الجمرات), the circumambulation (الطواف) and its prayer, and the striving (السعي) between al-Safa and al-Marwah, along with other prescribed matters. It is of three types: the Ifrad pilgrimage (حج الإفراد), the Qiran pilgrimage (حج القران), and the Tamattu‘ pilgrimage (حج التمتع), which Allah legislated at the end of the era of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

The ‘Umrah (العمرة) is another act, which is the visitation of the House (زيارة البيت) by entering the state of consecration (الإحرام) from one of the designated stations (المواقيت), performing the circumambulation (الطواف) and its prayer, the striving (السعي) between al-Safa and al-Marwah, and the shortening of the hair (التقصير). Both of them—meaning the Hajj and the ‘Umrah—are acts of worship (عبادتان) that are not completed except for the sake of Allah, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وأتموا الحج والعمرة لله (And complete the Hajj and the ‘Umrah for Allah).

His saying, the Exalted: فإِن أُحصرتم فما استيسر من الهدى ولا تحلقوا رؤوسكم (but if you are prevented, then [slaughter] whatever offering is easily available. And do not shave your heads...) until the end of the verse. Prevention (الإحصار) is detention and hindrance. What is meant is being prevented from completion due to illness or an enemy after having commenced the state of consecration (الإحرام). Ease (الاستيسار) is for a thing to become easy (يسيراً) and not difficult, as if it brings ease to itself. The offering (الهدي) is the livestock (النعم) that a person presents to another or to a specific place to seek proximity [to Allah]. Its root is from hadiyyah (الهدية) meaning a gift, or from huda (الهدى) meaning guidance (الهداية), which is the leading toward a destination. Hady (الهدي) and hadiyyah (الهدية) are like tamr (التمر) and tamrah (التمرة) [collective and singular nouns]. What is meant here is the livestock that a person drives to be sacrificed during his pilgrimage.

His saying, the Exalted: فمن كان منكم مريضاً أو به أذى من رأسه (and if any of you is sick or has an ailment of the head...) until the end of the verse. The letter fa (الفاء) indicates branching (التفريع). The branching of this ruling from the prohibition of shaving the head indicates that what is meant by "sickness" is specifically that illness in which one is harmed by leaving the hair on the head without shaving. The use of the phrase "or has an ailment of the head" with the particle "or" (أو) indicating an alternative shows that "ailment" refers to something other than sickness, such as vermin; thus, it is an allusion (كناية) to being troubled by vermin like lice on the head. These two matters permit shaving provided a ransom (الفدية) is given from one of three options: fasting (الصيام), charity (الصدقة), or sacrifice (النسك).

The Sunnah (السنة) — the prophetic tradition and practice — has established that the fasting is for three days, the charity is feeding six needy people, and the sacrifice is a sheep.

His saying, the Exalted: فإِذا أمنتم فمن تمتع بالعمرة إِلى الحج (Then when you are secure, whoever enjoys the ‘Umrah until the Hajj). This is a branching from the state of being prevented (الإحصار); meaning, when you are secure from the hindrance of illness, an enemy, or otherwise, then whoever performs the Tamattu‘ (تمتع) by means of the ‘Umrah until the Hajj—meaning he enjoys (تمتع) the benefits made permissible by the ‘Umrah from the time of its completion and the exit from consecration (الإحلال) until the time of entering the state of consecration for Hajj (الإهلال بالحج)—then [he must provide] whatever offering is easily available. The letter ba (الباء) denotes causality (السببية). The ‘Umrah is the cause for the enjoyment (التمتع) of what was not permissible during the state of consecration, such as women, hunting, and the like, due to its completion by exiting the state of consecration.

His saying, the Exalted: فما استيسر من الهدى (then [slaughter] whatever offering is easily available). The apparent meaning of the verse is that this is a sacrifice (نسك), not a compensation (جبران) for what he missed of entering the state of consecration for Hajj (الإهلال بالحج) from the designated station (الميقات), for that is a matter that requires additional effort to understand from the verse, as is evident.

If it is said: The sequence of His saying: فما استيسر من الهدى (then [slaughter] whatever offering is easily available) following His saying: فمن تمتع (whoever enjoys) is the sequence of a recompense (الجزاء) following a condition (الشرط). Furthermore, the inclusion of the word "enjoyment" (التمتع) in the condition suggests that the offering is in exchange for the enjoyment, which is a type of facilitation legislated as a relief (تخفيفاً); thus, it is a compensation for that.

I say: This is refuted by His saying, the Exalted: بالعمرة (by the ‘Umrah), for that is consistent with permitting enjoyment (التمتع) in the midst of a single act. There is no meaning for "facilitation" where there is no state of consecration due to the completion of the ‘Umrah and the fact that the entry into Hajj has not yet occurred. Moreover, even if this suggestion were correct, it would only establish that the legislation of the offering is because of the legislation of the Tamattu‘ pilgrimage, not that the offering is a compensation for missing the entry into Hajj from the designated station (الميقات) instead of Mecca. The apparent meaning of the verse is that His saying: فمن تمتع بالعمرة إِلى الحج فما استيسر من الهدى (whoever enjoys the ‘Umrah until the Hajj, then [slaughter] whatever offering is easily available) is an informative statement (إخبار) about the legislation of Tamattu‘, not the initial enactment (إنشاء) of the legislation. This is because it treats the Tamattu‘ as a settled matter and then builds the legislation of the offering upon it. There is a difference between saying, "Whoever performs Tamattu‘ owes an offering," and saying, "Perform Tamattu‘ and drive the offering." As for the initial enactment of the legislation of Tamattu‘, it is contained in His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verse: ذلك لمن لم يكن أهله حاضري المسجد الحرام (That is for someone whose family does not dwell near the Holy Mosque).

His saying, the Exalted: فمن لم يجد فصيام ثلاثة أيام في الحج وسبعة إِذا رجعتم (But he who cannot afford [the offering] shall fast for three days during the Hajj and seven when you return). He made the Hajj the container (ظرفاً) for the fasting by considering it unified with the time and place of being occupied with it. Thus, the time that is considered the time of Hajj—which is from the time of entering the state of consecration for Hajj until the return—is the time for the three days of fasting. For this reason, narrations have come from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) that the time for fasting is before the Day of Sacrifice (يوم الأضحى) or after the Days of Tashriq (أيام التشريق) for one who was unable to fast before it; otherwise, it is upon returning to one's homeland. The container for the seven days is only after the return, as that is the apparent meaning of His saying: إِذا رجعتم (when you return). He did not say "at the time of return" (حين الرجوع). Furthermore, the shift (الالتفات) from the third person (غيبة) to the second person (حضور) in His saying "when you return" (إِذا رجعتم) is not without a suggestion of this.

His saying, the Exalted: تلك عشرة كاملة (these are ten complete [days]). Meaning, the three and the seven are ten complete days. In making the seven the "completer" (مكملة) of the ten rather than the "finisher" (متمة), there is an indication that each of the three and the seven has another independent ruling, based on what was mentioned regarding the meaning of "completion" (التمام) and "perfection" (الكمال) at the beginning of the verse. Thus, the three days are a complete (تام) act in themselves, and they only depend on the seven for their perfection (كمال), not their completion (تمام).

His saying, the Exalted: ذلك لمن لم يكن أهله حاضري المسجد الحرام (That is for someone whose family does not dwell near the Holy Mosque). Meaning, the aforementioned ruling—which is the Tamattu‘ pilgrimage—is for the non-resident. He is the one between whom and the Holy Mosque there is more than twelve miles, as explained by the Sunnah. A man's "family" (أهل) are his close relations: his wife and children. Expressing the state of being distant (النائي) by saying "his family does not dwell near the Holy Mosque" is among the most subtle of expressions. It contains an allusion to the wisdom of the legislation, which is relief (التخفيف) and facilitation (التسهيل). For the traveler from distant lands for Hajj—which is an act not free from toil, the endurance of fatigue, and the hardships of the road—is not free from the need for dwelling and rest. A person only finds dwelling and rest with his family, and the distant traveler has no family at the Holy Mosque. Therefore, Allah, the Glorified, substituted it with the Tamattu‘ pilgrimage and entering the state of consecration for Hajj from the Holy Mosque without having to travel a second time to the designated station (الميقات).

You have already seen that the sentence indicating the legislation of the Tamattu‘ is this sentence, namely His saying: ذلك لمن لم يكن (That is for someone who...), rather than His saying: فمن تمتع بالعمرة إِلى الحج (whoever enjoys the ‘Umrah until the Hajj), which is an absolute (مطلق) statement not restricted to a specific time, person, or state.

His saying, the Exalted: واتقوا الله واعلموا أن الله شديد العقاب (and be wary of Allah, and know that Allah is severe in retribution). The extreme emphasis in this concluding warning—even though the beginning of the discourse contained nothing more than the legislation of a ruling in Hajj—indicates that the expected state of the addressees was to deny the ruling or hesitate in accepting it. And so it was, for Hajj specifically, among the legislated rulings in the religion, existed among them since the era of Ibrahim the Intimate (عليه السلام). it was well-known to them and practiced among them; their souls had become accustomed to it and their hearts were familiar with it. Islam had ratified it as it was until nearly the end of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) era. Thus, changing its form was not a light matter or easily accepted by them. For this reason, they met it with denial, and it was not settled in the hearts of many of them, as appears from the narrations. Therefore, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was compelled to address them in a sermon, explaining that the ruling belongs to Allah—He judges as He wills—and that it is a general ruling from which no one is exempted, neither a prophet nor a nation. This is the reason for the emphasis at the end of the verse by commanding godwariness (التقوى) and warning against the retribution of Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: الحج أشهر معلومات فمن فرض فيهن الحج (The Hajj is in months well-known; so whoever undertakes the Hajj in them...) until His saying: في الحج (during the Hajj). Meaning, the time of Hajj is months well-known among the people, and the Sunnah has clarified them: Shawwal, Dhu al-Qa‘dah, and Dhu al-Hijjah.

The fact that the time of Hajj in Dhu al-Hijjah is only part of this month and not all of it does not prevent it from being counted as a month of Hajj. It is like saying, "The time of my coming to you is Friday," even though the coming only occurs during part of it, not all of it.

In the repetition of the word "Hajj" (الحج) three times in the verse—which is an instance of placing a manifest noun (الظاهر) in the place of a pronoun (المضمر)—there is a subtlety of conciseness. For what is meant by the first "Hajj" is the time of Hajj, by the second "Hajj" the act itself, and by the third its time and place. Had the manifest noun not been used, there would have been a need for unnecessary prolixity, as has been said.

"Undertaking" (فرض) the Hajj is making it obligatory upon oneself by commencing it, due to His saying, the Exalted: وأتموا الحج والعمرة لله (And complete the Hajj and the ‘Umrah for Allah). "Obscenity" (الرفث), as previously mentioned, is any explicit mention of that which is usually alluded to. "Transgression" (الفسوق) is departing from obedience. "Dispute" (الجدال) is wrangling in speech. However, the Sunnah has interpreted "obscenity" as sexual intercourse, "transgression" as lying, and "dispute" as saying "No, by Allah" and "Yes, by Allah."

His saying, the Exalted: وما تفعلوا من خير يعلمه الله (And whatever good you do, Allah knows it). This is a reminder that deeds are not hidden from Him, the Exalted, and a call to godwariness (التقوى) so that the one occupied with the obedience of Allah does not lose the spirit of presence and the meaning of the act. This is the method of the Quran: it explains the principles of knowledge (أُصول المعارف), relates stories, mentions laws, and accompanies the explanation in all of them with exhortation and counsel so that knowledge does not part from action. For knowledge without action has no value in Islam. Therefore, He concluded this call by saying: واتقوني يا أُولي الالباب (and be wary of Me, O you who possess intellects), shifting from the third person to the first person, which indicates complete concern, proximity, and specification.

His saying, the Exalted: ليس عليكم جناح ان تبتغوا فضلا من ربكم (There is no sin upon you in seeking bounty from your Lord). This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا نُودِيَ لِلصَّلَاةِ مِن يَوْمِ الْجُمُعَةِ فَاسْعَوْا إِلَىٰ ذِكْرِ اللَّهِ وَذَرُوا الْبَيْعَ — "O you who have faith! When the call is made for prayer on Friday, hurry toward the remembrance of Allah, and leave all business" — until He says: فَإِذَا قُضِيَتِ الصَّلَاةُ فَانتَشِرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَابْتَغُوا مِن فَضْلِ اللَّهِ — "Then, when the prayer is finished, disperse through the land and seek Allah’s grace" (62:9-10). He replaced "business" (البيع) with "seeking from the bounty of Allah," so they are the same. For this reason, the Sunnah has interpreted "seeking from the bounty" in this verse as business; thus, the verse indicates the permissibility of business during the Hajj.

His saying, the Exalted: فإِذا افضتم من عرفات فاذكروا الله عند المشعر الحرام (And when you stream forth from ‘Arafat, remember Allah at the Holy Monument). "Streaming forth" (الإفاضة) is the collective departure from a place. It indicates the standing at ‘Arafat just as it indicates the standing at the Holy Monument (المشعر الحرام), which is al-Muzdalifah.

His saying, the Exalted: واذكروه كما هديكم (and remember Him as He has guided you...) until the end of the verse. Meaning, remember Him with a remembrance that corresponds to His guidance of you, and [remember] that before His guidance, you were among those who were astray.

His saying, the Exalted: ثم افيضوا من حيث افاض الناس (Then stream forth from where the people stream forth). Its apparent meaning is the obligation of streaming forth according to the custom of the people and joining the addressees to them in this matter. This applies to what has been narrated that the Quraysh and their allies—the Hums—would not stand at ‘Arafat but at al-Muzdalifah, saying, "We are the people of Allah's Sanctuary (حرم الله); we do not leave the Sanctuary." So Allah, the Glorified, commanded them to stream forth from where the people stream forth, which is ‘Arafat.

Accordingly, the mention of this ruling after His saying: فإِذا أفضتم من عرفات (And when you stream forth from ‘Arafat) with the particle thumma (ثم) indicating delay is a consideration of the order of mention (الترتيب الذكري). The speech is like a rectification (استدراك). The meaning is that the rulings of Hajj are those that have been mentioned, except that it is obligatory upon you in the streaming forth to stream forth from ‘Arafat, not from al-Muzdalifah. It has also been said that there is a transposition (تقديم وتأخير) in the composition of the two verses, and the order is: "Then stream forth from where the people stream forth, and when you stream forth from ‘Arafat..."

His saying, the Exalted: فإِذا قضيتم مناسككم (And when you have finished your rites...) until His saying: ذكراً (remembrance). This is a call to the remembrance of Allah and to be thorough in it, such that the pilgrim remembers Him as he remembers his forefathers, or even more intensely. This is because His blessing upon him—which is the blessing of guidance, as He mentioned in His saying: وَاذْكُرُوهُ كَمَا هَدَاكُمْ (and remember Him as He has guided you)—is greater than the right of his forefathers over him. It has been said that the Arabs in the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية), when they finished the Hajj, would stay for a while in Mina and boast about their forefathers in poetry and prose. So Allah, the Exalted, replaced this with His remembrance like their remembrance [of their forefathers] or even more intense than their remembrance. The "or" (أو) in His saying "or more intense in remembrance" (أو أشد ذكراً) is for transition (إضراب), thus giving the meaning of "rather" (بل). Remembrance has been described as "intense" (الشدة), which is a matter that accepts intensity in quality just as it accepts abundance in quantity. He, the Exalted, said: اذْكُرُوا اللَّهَ ذِكْرًا كَثِيرًا — "remember Allah with much remembrance" (33:41), and He said: وَالذَّاكِرِينَ اللَّهَ كَثِيرًا — "and the men who remember Allah much" (33:35). Remembrance, in reality, is not limited to the word; rather, it is a matter related to the presence of the heart (الحضور القلبي), and the word is a narrator of it. Thus, it can be described by abundance (الكثرة) in terms of instances, by remembering Allah, the Glorified, in most states, as He, the Exalted, said: الَّذِينَ يَذْكُرُونَ اللَّهَ قِيَامًا وَقُعُودًا وَعَلَىٰ جُنُوبِهِمْ — "those who remember Allah standing, sitting, and lying on their sides" (3:191). It can also be described by intensity (الشدة) in a particular instance. Since the instance understood from His saying: فإِذا قضيتم مناسككم (And when you have finished your rites) is an instance that invites distraction from Him, the Exalted, and forgetting Him, it was more appropriate to describe the remembrance commanded therein as "intense" rather than "abundant," as is evident.

His saying, the Exalted: فمن الناس من يقول ربنا آتنا في الدنيا (Among the people there are those who say, ‘Our Lord, give us in this world’...) until the end of the verse. This is a branching from His saying, the Exalted: فاذكروا الله كذكركم آبائكم (remember Allah as you remember your forefathers). "The people" (الناس) is general, so it refers to human individuals, encompassing the disbeliever who remembers only his forefathers—meaning he seeks only worldly glories and asks only for the world, having no concern for the Hereafter—and the believer who desires only what is with Allah, the Glorified; even if he desires something from the world, he only desires what his Lord is pleased with for him. Accordingly, what is meant by "saying" and "supplication" is the request by the language of the state (لسان الحال) rather than the language of speech (لسان المقال). The meaning of the verse is that among the people are those who desire only the world and have no share in the Hereafter, and among them are those who desire only what their Lord is pleased with for them, whether in the world or the Hereafter, and for these there is a share in the Hereafter.

From here, the reason for mentioning "good" (الحسنة) in the speech of the people of the Hereafter but not the people of the world becomes clear. This is because the one who desires the world does not restrict it to being "good" in the sight of Allah, the Glorified; rather, the world and all that he enjoys in the earthly life is "good" to him, corresponding to the desire of his soul. This is in contrast to the one who desires what is with Allah, the Glorified; for him, what is in the world and what is in the Hereafter is divided into "good" and "evil," and he does not desire or ask his Lord for anything except the "good," not the "evil."

The contrast between His saying: وما له في الآخرة من خلاق (and he will have no share in the Hereafter) and His saying: أُولئك لهم نصيب مما كسبوا (Such shall have a share of what they have earned) shows that the deeds of the first group are vain and nullified (حابطة), unlike the second group, as He, the Exalted, said: وَقَدِمْنَا إِلَىٰ مَا عَمِلُوا مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْنَاهُ هَبَاءً مَّنثُورًا — "Then We shall attend to the works they have done and then render them as scattered dust" (25:23), and He said: وَيَوْم يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَلَى النَّارِ أَذْهَبْتُمْ طَيِّبَاتِكُمْ فِي حَيَاتِكُمُ الدُّنْيَا وَاسْتَمْتَعْتُم بِهَا — "The day when the faithless are exposed to the Fire, [they will be told,] ‘You exhausted your good things in your worldly life and enjoyed them’" (46:20), and He said: فَلَا نُقِيمُ لَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ وَزْنًا — "so We shall not set up any scale for them on the Day of Resurrection" (18:105).

His saying, the Exalted: والله سريع الحساب (and Allah is swift at reckoning). This is one of the Beautiful Names of Allah. Its absolute use indicates its inclusion of both the world and the Hereafter together. Thus, the reckoning is ongoing; whenever a servant performs any good deed or otherwise, Allah brings him the recompense, a fitting recompense.

The summary of the meaning of His saying: فمن الناس من يقول (Among the people there are those who say...) until the end of the verses, is: Remember Allah, the Exalted, for the people are of two groups: those who desire the world and do not remember anything else, and they have no share in the Hereafter; and those who desire what is with Allah of that which He is pleased with for them, and they have a share in the Hereafter. Allah is swift at reckoning; He hastens in the reckoning of what His servant desires and gives it to him as he desires. So be among the people of the "share" by remembering Allah, and do not be among those who have no "share" by abandoning the remembrance of their Lord, lest you be despondent and hopeless.

His saying, the Exalted: واذكروا الله في أيام معدودات (And remember Allah in the numbered days). The "numbered days" (الأيام المعدودات) are the Days of Tashriq (أيام التشريق), which are the eleventh, twelfth, and thirteenth days of Dhu al-Hijjah. The evidence that these days are after the ten [days] of Dhu al-Hijjah is the mention of the ruling after finishing the mention of the acts of Hajj. The evidence for them being three days is His saying, the Exalted: فمن تعجل في يومين (Then whoever hastens in two days...), for hastening in two days only occurs if the days are three: a day in which one departs, and two days in which one hastens; thus they are three. They have also been interpreted as such in the narrations.

His saying, the Exalted: فمن تعجل في يومين فلا إِثم عليه ومن تأخر فلا إِثم عليه لمن اتقى (Then whoever hastens in two days, there is no sin upon him, and whoever delays, there is no sin upon him—for him who is God-conscious). The "no" (لا) is for categorical negation (نافية للجنس). Thus, His saying "there is no sin upon him" in both places negates the category of sin from the pilgrim, and it is not restricted by anything at all. Had the meaning been "there is no sin upon him in hastening" or "in delaying," it would have been necessary to restrict it as such. Thus, the meaning is that whoever completes the act of Hajj is forgiven and has no sin, whether he hastens in two days or delays. From here, it becomes clear that the verse is not in the position of explaining the choice between delaying and hastening for the pilgrim; rather, the intent is to explain that sins are forgiven for the pilgrim in any case.

As for His saying: لمن اتقى (for him who is God-conscious), it is not an explanation of the hastening and delaying; otherwise, it would have been more appropriate to say "upon him who is God-conscious" (على من اتقى). Rather, it is apparent that His saying "for him who is God-conscious" is similar to His saying, the Exalted: ذلك لمن لم يكن اهله حاضري المسجد الحرام (That is for someone whose family does not dwell near the Holy Mosque). The meaning is that this ruling is for the one who is God-conscious (اتقى), while for the one who is not God-conscious, it is not for him. It is necessary that this godwariness (التقوى) be godwariness regarding what Allah, the Glorified, has prohibited during Hajj and specified for it. Thus, the meaning results in: the ruling is only for the one who was wary of the prohibitions of the state of consecration (تروك الإحرام) or some of them. As for the one who was not wary, he must remain in Mina and remember Allah during the numbered days. This meaning has been mentioned in some of what has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), as will come, if Allah wills.

His saying, the Exalted: واتقوا الله واعلموا انكم اليه تحشرون (and be wary of Allah, and know that you shall be mustered toward Him). This is a command for godwariness (التقوى) at the conclusion of the speech and a reminder of the Mustering (الحشر) and the Resurrection (البعث). For godwariness is not complete and sin is not avoided except with the remembrance of the Day of Recompense. He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِمَا نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسَابِ — "Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah—there is a severe punishment for them because they forgot the Day of Reckoning" (38:26).

In the choice of the word "Mustering" (الحشر) in His saying, the Exalted: "you shall be mustered toward Him," along with what is in the rites of Hajj of the mustering and gathering of people, there is a manifest subtlety. It is a suggestion that the pilgrim should be reminded by this gathering and streaming forth of a day when Allah, the Glorified, will muster the people, not leaving out a single one of them.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Tahdhib and Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: وأتموا الحج والعمرة لله (And complete the Hajj and the ‘Umrah for Allah), he said: "They are both obligatory (مفروضان)."

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq (عليهما السلام), they said: "We asked them both about His saying: واتموا الحج والعمرة لله. They said: 'The completion of the Hajj is that one does not use obscenity, nor transgress, nor dispute.'"

In al-Kafi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) in a tradition, he said: "He means by their completion their performance (أدائهما), and being wary of what the person in the state of consecration (المحرم) must avoid during them."

I say: The narrations are not inconsistent with what we previously mentioned regarding the meaning of "completion" (الإتمام), for their obligation and performance constitute their completion.

In al-Kafi, narrated from al-Halabi, from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), he said: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) performed the Pilgrimage of Islam (حجة الإسلام), he set out when four days remained of Dhu al-Qa‘dah until he reached al-Shajarah and prayed there. Then he led his camel until he reached al-Bayda’, where he entered the state of consecration (أحرم) and began the Hajj (أهل بالحج). He drove one hundred camels (بدنة). All the people entered the state of consecration for Hajj, not intending ‘Umrah and not knowing what Tamattu‘ was. When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) arrived in Mecca, he circumambulated the House, and the people circumambulated with him. Then he prayed two units (ركعتين) at the Station (المقام) and touched the Stone (الحجر). Then he said: 'I begin with what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, began with.' So he went to al-Safa and began with it, then he strove between al-Safa and al-Marwah seven times. When he finished his striving at al-Marwah, he stood as a speaker and commanded them to exit the state of consecration (يحلوا) and make it an ‘Umrah, which was something Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, had commanded. So the people exited the state of consecration. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Had I known before what I knew later, I would have done as I commanded you.' But he was unable to do so because of the offering (الهدي) that was with him, for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: ولا تحلقوا رؤسكم حتى يبلغ الهدي محله (And do not shave your heads until the offering reaches its destination). Suraqah ibn Ju‘tham al-Kinani said: 'Teach us our religion as if we were created today. Do you see this which you have commanded us—is it for our year [only] or for every year?' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'No, rather for eternity.' A man stood up and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, shall we go out as pilgrims while our heads are dripping from our women?' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'You will never believe in it.' He (عليه السلام) said: 'And ‘Ali (عليه السلام) arrived from Yemen until he reached the Hajj. He found Fatimah (عليها السلام) had exited the state of consecration and he found the scent of perfume. He went to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) seeking a ruling. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "O ‘Ali, with what did you enter the state of consecration?" He said: "With that which the Prophet entered with." He said: "Do not exit the state of consecration." So he shared the offering with him, assigning him thirty-seven. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sacrificed sixty-three; he sacrificed them with his own hand. Then he took a piece from every camel and put them in one pot, then he ordered it to be cooked. He ate from it and sipped from the broth. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "We have all eaten from it now." And Tamattu‘ is better than the Qarin who drives [the offering] and better than the Ifrad pilgrim.' He said: 'I asked him: Did the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) enter the state of consecration by night or by day?' He said: 'By day.' I said: 'At what hour?' He said: 'At the Dhuhr prayer.'"

I say: This meaning has also been narrated in al-Majma‘ and elsewhere.

In al-Tahdhib, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), he said: "The ‘Umrah has entered into the Hajj until the Day of Resurrection. So whoever performs Tamattu‘ by means of the ‘Umrah until the Hajj, then [he must provide] whatever offering is easily available. It is not for anyone except to perform Tamattu‘, because Allah revealed that in His Book and the Sunnah was established by the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

In al-Kafi also, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: فما استيسر من الهدي (whatever offering is easily available), he said: "A sheep."

In al-Kafi also, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding the Tamattu‘ pilgrim who cannot find a sheep, he said: "He fasts the day before the Day of Tarwiyah (يوم التروية), the Day of Tarwiyah, and the Day of ‘Arafah." It was said: "What if he arrives on the Day of Tarwiyah?" He said: "He fasts three days after the Tashriq." It was said: "What if his camel-driver does not wait for him?" He said: "He fasts on the Day of Hasbah (يوم الحصبة) and two days after it." It was said: "What is the Hasbah?" He said: "The day of his departure (يوم نفره)." It was said: "Does he fast while he is a traveler?" He said: "Yes, is he not a traveler on the Day of ‘Arafah? We, the Ahl al-Bayt, say that. Allah, the Exalted, says: فصيام ثلاثة ايام في الحج (fast for three days during the Hajj); He means in Dhu al-Hijjah."

Al-Shaykh narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), who said: "Whatever is within the designated stations (المواقيت) toward Mecca, he is a resident of the Holy Mosque (حاضري المسجد الحرام), and he has no Tamattu‘."

I say: He means that for one who dwells within the designated stations, it is true of him that he is among the residents of the Holy Mosque and he has no Tamattu‘. The narrations regarding these meanings are numerous through the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt.

In al-Kafi, narrated from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: الحج اشهر معلومات (The Hajj is in months well-known), he said: "The months of Hajj are well-known: Shawwal, Dhu al-Qa‘dah, and Dhu al-Hijjah. It is not for anyone to perform Hajj in other than them."

In it, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: فمن فرض فيهن (so whoever undertakes [the Hajj] in them...), he said: "Undertaking (الفرض) is the Talbiyah (التلبية), the marking of the animal (الإشعار), and the garlanding (التقليد). Whichever of these he does, he has undertaken the Hajj."

In al-Kafi, narrated from him (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: فلا رفث (there shall be no obscenity...), he said: "Obscenity (الرفث) is sexual intercourse, transgression (الفسوق) is lying and insulting, and dispute (الجدال) is a man's saying: 'No, by Allah' and 'Yes, by Allah.'"

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: لا جناح عليكم ان تبتغوا فضلاً من ربكم (There is no sin upon you in seeking bounty from your Lord), he said: "It means sustenance (الرزق). When a man exits his state of consecration and finishes, let him buy and sell during the season."

I say: It is said that they used to consider trading during Hajj a sin, so Allah removed that with the verse.

In al-Majma‘, it is said: Its meaning is "There is no sin upon you in seeking forgiveness from your Lord," narrated by Jabir from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام).

I say: This is an adherence to the absolute sense of "bounty" (الفضل) and applying it to the best of its instances.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: ثم افيضوا من حيث افاض الناس (Then stream forth from where the people stream forth), he said: "The people of the Sanctuary (أهل الحرم) used to stand at the Holy Monument (المشعر الحرام) while the people stood at ‘Arafah, and they would not stream forth until the people of ‘Arafah appeared to them. There was a man nicknamed Abu Sayyar who had a swift donkey and would precede the people of ‘Arafah. When he appeared to them, they would say: 'This is Abu Sayyar,' and then they would stream forth. So Allah commanded them to stand at ‘Arafat and to stream forth from there."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: ربنا آتنا في الدنيا حسنة وفي الآخرة حسنة (Our Lord, give us good in this world and good in the Hereafter), he said: "The pleasure (رضوان) of Allah and Paradise in the Hereafter, and abundance in sustenance and good character in the world."

From him (عليه السلام), he said: "The pleasure of Allah, expansion in livelihood, and good companionship; and in the Hereafter, Paradise."

From ‘Ali (عليه السلام): "In the world, a righteous wife; in the Hereafter, a houri; and the punishment of the Fire is an evil wife."

I say: The narrations are of the type of counting instances (المصداق), while the verse is absolute. Since the pleasure of Allah, the Exalted, is something whose attainment is possible in the world and whose complete manifestation is in the Hereafter, it was correct to count it among the good things of the world, as in the first narration, or the Hereafter, as in the second.

In al-Kafi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: وأذكروا الله في أيام معدودات (And remember Allah in the numbered days), he said: "They are the Days of Tashriq (أيام التشريق). When they stayed in Mina after the sacrifice, they would boast. A man among them would say: 'My father used to do such and such.' So Allah, majestic is His state, said: فإِذا قضيتم مناسككم فاذكروا الله كذكركم آبائكم أو أشد ذكراً (And when you have finished your rites, remember Allah as you remember your forefathers, or with a more intense remembrance). He said: 'The Takbir (التكبير) is: Allah is the Greatest, Allah is the Greatest, there is no god but Allah and Allah is the Greatest, and to Allah belongs all praise; Allah is the Greatest for what He has guided us to, Allah is the Greatest for what He has provided us of the livestock animals.'"

In it, from him (عليه السلام), he said: "The Takbir during the Days of Tashriq is from the Dhuhr prayer on the Day of Sacrifice until the Fajr prayer of the third day. In the cities, one says the Takbir after ten prayers."

In al-Faqih, regarding His saying, the Exalted: فمن تعجل في يومين فلا إِثم عليه ومن تأخر فلا إِثم عليه (Then whoever hastens in two days, there is no sin upon him, and whoever delays, there is no sin upon him), Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) was asked about this verse and said: "It is not that it is a matter of choice, that if he wishes he does this; rather, he returns forgiven, having no sin."
