In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from him (عليه السلام), he said: "He returns forgiven, having no sin—for him who is God-conscious (لمن اتقى)."

In al-Faqih, from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: لمن اتقى (for him who is God-conscious), he said: "He is wary of hunting until the people of Mina depart."

From Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام): "For him who is wary of obscenity, transgression, dispute, and what Allah has prohibited in his state of consecration."

From him also: "For him who is wary of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

From Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): "For him who is wary of the major sins (الكبائر)."

I say: You have already seen what the verse indicates. It is possible to adhere to the generality of godwariness (التقوى), as in the last two narrations.

(Another Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bukhari and al-Bayhaqi narrated from ibn ‘Abbas that he was asked about the Tamattu‘ pilgrimage. He said: "The Emigrants (المهاجرون), the Helpers (الأنصار), and the wives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) entered the state of consecration during the Farewell Pilgrimage, and we entered it too. When we arrived in Mecca, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Make your entry into Hajj an ‘Umrah, except for the one who has garlanded the offering (قلد الهدى).' So we circumambulated the House and al-Safa and al-Marwah, and we went to women and wore clothes. He said: 'Whoever has garlanded the offering, he shall not exit the state of consecration until the offering reaches [its destination].' Then he commanded us on the evening of Tarwiyah to enter the state of consecration for Hajj. When we finished the rites, we came and circumambulated the House and al-Safa and al-Marwah, and our Hajj was complete. We owed the offering as Allah said: فما استيسر من الهدى فمن لم يجد فصيام ثلاثة أيام في الحج وسبعة إِذا رجعتم (whatever offering is easily available. But he who cannot afford [the offering] shall fast for three days during the Hajj and seven when you return) to your cities. A sheep suffices. So they combined two rites in one year, between Hajj and ‘Umrah, for Allah revealed it in His Book and the Sunnah of His Prophet, and He made it permissible for the people other than the people of Mecca. Allah, the Exalted, said: ذلك لمن لم يكن أهله حاضري المسجد الحرام (That is for someone whose family does not dwell near the Holy Mosque). The months of Hajj which Allah mentioned are: Shawwal, Dhu al-Qa‘dah, and Dhu al-Hijjah. Whoever performs Tamattu‘ in these months owes a blood sacrifice or fasting. Obscenity (الرفث) is sexual intercourse, transgression (الفسوق) is acts of disobedience, and dispute (الجدال) is wrangling."

In al-Durr al-Manthur also, al-Bukhari and Muslim narrated from ibn ‘Umar, who said: "The Messenger of Allah performed Tamattu‘ during the Farewell Pilgrimage with ‘Umrah until Hajj. He offered a sacrifice and drove the offering with him from Dhu al-Hulayfah. The Messenger of Allah began by entering the state of consecration for ‘Umrah, then he entered it for Hajj. The people performed Tamattu‘ with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with ‘Umrah until Hajj. Among the people were those who offered a sacrifice and drove the offering, and among them were those who did not. When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) arrived in Mecca, he said to the people: 'Whoever among you has offered a sacrifice, he shall not exit the state of consecration for anything that was prohibited to him until his Hajj is completed. And whoever has not offered a sacrifice, let him circumambulate the House and al-Safa and al-Marwah, shorten his hair, and exit the state of consecration. Then let him enter the state of consecration for Hajj. He who cannot find an offering shall fast for three days during the Hajj and seven when he returns to his family.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur also, al-Hakim narrated and authenticated through the path of Mujahid and ‘Ata’ from Jabir, who said: "The talk among the people increased. We set out as pilgrims until, when there were only a few nights between us and exiting the state of consecration, we were commanded to exit it. We said: 'Shall one of us go to ‘Arafat while his private part is dripping with semen?' This reached the Messenger of Allah, so he stood as a speaker and said: 'Is it about Allah that you are teaching, O people? By Allah, I am the most knowledgeable of you about Allah and the most God-conscious of you toward Him. Had I known before what I knew later, I would not have driven an offering and I would have exited the state of consecration as they did. He who does not have an offering with him shall fast for three days during the Hajj and seven when he returns to his family. And he who finds an offering, let him sacrifice it.' We used to sacrifice a camel for seven." ‘Ata’ said: ibn ‘Abbas said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) distributed sheep among his companions that day, and Sa‘d ibn Abi Waqqas received a male goat, which he sacrificed for himself."

In al-Durr al-Manthur also, ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Bukhari, and Muslim narrated from ‘Imran ibn Husayn, who said: "The verse of Tamattu‘ was revealed in the Book of Allah, and we practiced it with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Then no verse was revealed to abrogate the verse of the Tamattu‘ pilgrimage, nor did he prohibit it until he died. A man said by his own opinion whatever he wished."

I say: The narration has been reported with other wordings close in meaning to what was quoted in al-Durr al-Manthur.

In Sahih Muslim, Musnad Ahmad, and Sunan al-Nasa’i, narrated from Mutarrif, who said: "‘Imran ibn Husayn sent for me during the illness in which he died and said: 'I was going to narrate to you some traditions so that perhaps Allah may benefit you by them after me. If I live, then keep them secret for me; and if I die, then narrate them from me. I have been greeted [by angels], and know that the Prophet of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) combined Hajj and ‘Umrah, then no [verse of the] Book of Allah was revealed regarding it [to abrogate it], nor did the Prophet of Allah prohibit it. A man said regarding it by his own opinion whatever he wished.'"

In Sahih al-Tirmidhi also, and Zad al-Ma‘ad by ibn al-Qayyim: ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Umar was asked about the Tamattu‘ pilgrimage. He said: "It is permissible (حلال)." The questioner said to him: "Your father prohibited it." He said: "Do you see—if my father prohibited it while the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) practiced it, is my father's command to be followed or the command of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)?" The man said: "Rather, the command of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)." He said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) practiced it."

In Sahih al-Tirmidhi, Sunan al-Nasa’i, Sunan al-Bayhaqi, Muwatta’ Malik, and Kitab al-Umm by al-Shafi‘i, narrated from Muhammad ibn ‘Abdullah, that he heard Sa‘d ibn Abi Waqqas and al-Dahhak ibn Qays during the year of Mu‘awiyah ibn Abi Sufyan's pilgrimage, while they were mentioning the Tamattu‘ pilgrimage. Al-Dahhak said: "No one does that except one who is ignorant of Allah's command." Sa‘d said: "Evil is what you have said, O son of my brother." Al-Dahhak said: "But ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab prohibited that." Sa‘d said: "The Messenger of Allah practiced it, and we practiced it with him."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bukhari, Muslim, and al-Nasa’i narrated from Abu Musa, who said: "I came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while he was at al-Batha’. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Have you entered the state of consecration?' I said: 'I entered it with the entry of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).' He said: 'Have you driven any offering?' I said: 'No.' He said: 'Circumambulate the House and al-Safa and al-Marwah, then exit the state of consecration.' So I circumambulated the House and al-Safa and al-Marwah. Then I came to a woman from my people who combed my head and washed my head. I used to give rulings to the people during the caliphate of Abu Bakr and the caliphate of ‘Umar. I was standing during the season when a man came to me and said: 'You do not know what the Commander of the Faithful has introduced regarding the rite.' I said: 'O people, whoever we gave a ruling to regarding anything, let him wait, for here is the Commander of the Faithful coming to you regarding it, so follow him.' When he arrived, I said: 'What is this that you have introduced regarding the rite?' He said: 'That we should take by the Book of Allah, for Allah said: وأتموا الحج والعمرة لله (And complete the Hajj and the ‘Umrah for Allah); and that we should take by the Sunnah of our Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), for he did not exit the state of consecration until he sacrificed the offering.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur also, Muslim narrated from Abu Nadrah, who said: "ibn ‘Abbas used to command Tamattu‘ while ibn al-Zubayr used to prohibit it. This was mentioned to Jabir ibn ‘Abdullah, and he said: 'Through me the tradition circulated. We performed Tamattu‘ with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). When ‘Umar rose [to power], he said: "Indeed, Allah used to make permissible for His Messenger whatever He willed of what He willed. And the Quran has been revealed in its stations. So complete the Hajj and the ‘Umrah as Allah commanded you, and separate your Hajj from your ‘Umrah, for that is more complete for your Hajj and more complete for your ‘Umrah."'"

In Musnad Ahmad, narrated from Abu Musa, that ‘Umar said: "It is the Sunnah of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)"—meaning Tamattu‘—"but I fear that they will lie with them [their wives] under the Arak trees and then set out as pilgrims."

In Jam‘ al-Jawami‘ by al-Suyuti, narrated from Sa‘id ibn al-Musayyib, that ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab prohibited Tamattu‘ during the months of Hajj and said: "I practiced it with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), but I prohibit it. That is because one of you comes from one of the horizons disheveled and exhausted, performing ‘Umrah in the months of Hajj. His dishevelment, exhaustion, and Talbiyah are only in his ‘Umrah. Then he arrives, circumambulates the House, exits the state of consecration, wears [clothes], perfumes himself, and lies with his wife if she is with him. Then when it is the Day of Tarwiyah, he enters the state of consecration for Hajj and goes out to Mina reciting the Talbiyah for a Hajj in which there is no dishevelment, no exhaustion, and no Talbiyah except for one day. And Hajj is better than ‘Umrah. If we left them to this, they would embrace them under the Arak trees, while the people of the House [the Meccans] have neither cattle nor crops; their springtime is only in those who come to them."

In Sunan al-Bayhaqi, from Muslim, from Abu Nadrah, from Jabir, who said: "I said: 'Indeed, ibn al-Zubayr prohibits Tamattu‘ while ibn ‘Abbas commands it.' He said: 'Through me the tradition occurred. We performed Tamattu‘ with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and with Abu Bakr. When ‘Umar took charge, he addressed the people and said: "Indeed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is this Messenger, and the Quran is this Quran. They were two Tamattu‘s during the era of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and I prohibit them both and will punish for them: one of them is the Tamattu‘ with women—and I will not catch a man who marries a woman for a term except that I will bury him with stones—and the other is the Tamattu‘ pilgrimage."'"

In Sunan al-Nasa’i, narrated from ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "I heard ‘Umar saying: 'By Allah, I prohibit you from Tamattu‘, and it is indeed in the Book of Allah, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) indeed practiced it'—meaning the ‘Umrah during Hajj."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Muslim narrated from ‘Abdullah ibn Shaqiq, who said: "‘Uthman used to prohibit Tamattu‘ while ‘Ali used to command it. ‘Uthman said a word to ‘Ali, and ‘Ali (عليه السلام) said: 'You have known that we performed Tamattu‘ with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).' He said: 'But we were afraid.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur also, ibn Abi Shaybah and Muslim narrated from Abu Dharr: "The Tamattu‘ pilgrimage was specifically for the companions of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur also, Muslim narrated from Abu Dharr, who said: "The two Tamattu‘s are not suitable except for us specifically"—meaning the Tamattu‘ with women and the Tamattu‘ pilgrimage.

I say: The narrations with this meaning are very numerous, but we have limited ourselves to those that have a bearing on our purpose, which is the exegetical (تفسيري) investigation of his prohibition. For this prohibition is sometimes investigated from the perspective of whether the one who prohibited it was correct or excused in it, or not; this is a theological (كلامي) discussion outside our purpose in this book.

Sometimes it is investigated from the perspective of the narrations' inclusion of arguments for this meaning based on what relates to the Book or the Sunnah; thus, they are linked to the indication of the manifest sense of the Book and the Sunnah, which is the nature of our investigation in this book.

We say: As for the argument for the prohibition of Tamattu‘ being that it is what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وأتموا الحج والعمرة لله (And complete the Hajj and the ‘Umrah for Allah), and that Tamattu‘ was among the things specific to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—as indicated by what is in the narration of Abu Nadrah from Jabir: "Indeed, Allah used to make permissible for His Messenger whatever He willed of what He willed. And the Quran has been revealed in its stations. So complete the Hajj and the ‘Umrah as Allah commanded you"—you have already seen that His saying, the Exalted: وأتموا الحج والعمرة لله (And complete the Hajj and the ‘Umrah for Allah) does not indicate more than the obligation of completing the Hajj and the ‘Umrah after undertaking them. The evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted: فإِن أُحصرتم (but if you are prevented...). As for the verse indicating "completion" in the sense of separating the ‘Umrah from the Hajj, and that the lack of separation was a matter specific to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) alone, or to him and those with him in that pilgrimage (the Farewell Pilgrimage), proving this is an impossible task.

Furthermore, it contains an admission that Tamattu‘ was the Sunnah of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), as in the narration of al-Nasa’i from ibn ‘Abbas: "By Allah, I prohibit you from Tamattu‘... and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) indeed practiced it."

As for the argument that in the prohibition there is an adherence to the Book or the Sunnah—as in the narration of Abu Musa: "That we should take by the Book of Allah, for Allah said: واتموا الحج والعمرة لله; and that we should take by the Sunnah of our Prophet, for he did not exit the state of consecration until he sacrificed the offering"—you have already seen that the Book indicates the opposite. As for the claim that abandoning Tamattu‘ is the Sunnah of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) because he did not exit the state of consecration until he sacrificed the offering, there are several points:

First: It contradicts what he himself explicitly stated, according to what other narrations establish, some of which were mentioned just now.

Second: The narrations explicitly state that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) practiced it, that he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) entered the state of consecration for ‘Umrah and then entered it a second time for Hajj, and that he addressed the people and said: "Is it about Allah that you are teaching, O people?" Among the strange claims in this regard is what ibn Taymiyyah claimed: that the Hajj of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was a Qiran pilgrimage and that Tamattu‘ was applied to the Qiran pilgrimage!

Third: Merely not shaving the head until the offering reaches its destination is not an entry into the state of consecration for Hajj, nor is it established by that. The verse also indicates that the one who drives the offering—whose ruling is not to shave—if his family is not resident at the Holy Mosque, he is inevitably a Tamattu‘ pilgrim.

Fourth: Suppose that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) performed other than Tamattu‘; however, he commanded all his companions and those with him to perform Tamattu‘. How can that which is of this nature be considered his Sunnah? Is it possible for a matter to be realized that the Messenger of Allah specified for himself, while commanding his nation with something else, and the Book was revealed with it, and then it becomes a followed Sunnah among the people?!

As for the argument that Tamattu‘ necessitates a state that does not suit the condition of a pilgrim and brings him into a state of pleasure by lying with women, using perfume, and wearing fine clothes—as indicated by what is in the narration of Ahmad from Abu Musa: "But I fear that they will lie with them under the Arak trees and then set out as pilgrims," and as in some narrations: "You have known that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) practiced it and his companions, but I disliked that they should lie with them in the Arak and then set out for Hajj with their heads dripping"—it is an instance of independent judgment (اجتهاد) in opposition to the text (النص). Allah and His Messenger have explicitly stated the ruling, and Allah and His Messenger know best that this ruling could lead to what he feared and disliked! Among the most strange things is that the verse which legislates this ruling uses the very meaning that he expressed fear and dislike of; for He, the Exalted, said: فمن تمتع بالعمرة إِلى الحج (whoever enjoys the ‘Umrah until the Hajj). Is Tamattu‘ anything other than the full seeking of enjoyment (المتاع) and pleasure in the good things of marriage, clothing, and others? It is exactly what he feared and disliked!

Even stranger is that the companions had objected to Allah and His Messenger when the verse was revealed! And the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) commanded Tamattu‘ with the very thing that was made a reason for the prohibition, when they said—as in the narration of al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Hakim from Jabir: "We said: 'Shall one of us go to ‘Arafat while his private part is dripping with semen?'" This reached the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), so he stood as a speaker, refuted their statement, and commanded them a second time with Tamattu‘ as he had prescribed it for them initially.

As for the argument that Tamattu‘ causes the suspension of the markets of Mecca—as in the narration of al-Suyuti from Sa‘id ibn al-Musayyib: "while the people of the House have neither cattle nor crops; their springtime is only in those who come to them"—it is also an instance of independent judgment in opposition to the text. Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, refutes him in a similar matter by His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّمَا الْمُشْرِكُونَ نَجَسٌ فَلَا يَقْرَبُوا الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرَامَ بَعْدَ عَامِهِمْ هَٰذَا وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ عَيْلَةً فَسَوْفَ يُغْنِيكُمُ اللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِ إِن شَاءَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ — "O you who have faith! Indeed the polytheists are unclean; so let them not approach the Holy Mosque after this year of theirs. And if you fear poverty, Allah will enrich you out of His grace, if He wishes. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise" (9:28).

As for the argument that the legislation of Tamattu‘ was due to fear, so there is no Tamattu‘ in other than a state of fear—as in the narration of al-Durr al-Manthur from Muslim from ‘Abdullah ibn Shaqiq, regarding ‘Uthman's word to ‘Ali (عليه السلام): "But we were afraid"—something similar has been narrated from ibn al-Zubayr, as reported in al-Durr al-Manthur. He said: ibn Abi Shaybah, ibn Jarir, and ibn al-Mundhir narrated from ibn al-Zubayr that he addressed the people and said: "O people, by Allah, Tamattu‘ with ‘Umrah until Hajj is not as you do. Indeed, Tamattu‘ is that a man enters the state of consecration for Hajj and then an enemy, illness, or a fracture confronts him, or a matter detains him until the days of Hajj pass. Then he arrives and makes it an ‘Umrah, thus enjoying (يتمتع) the exit from consecration until the following year, then he performs Hajj and offers an offering. This is the Tamattu‘ with ‘Umrah until Hajj..." until the end of the tradition.

Regarding this, it is said: The verse is absolute (مطلقة), encompassing both the fearful and others. You have already learned that the sentence indicating the legislation of the ruling of Tamattu‘ (التمتع) — the pilgrimage where the ‘Umrah is performed before the Hajj within the same journey — is His saying, the Exalted: ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن لَّمْ يَكُنْ أَهْلُهُ حَاضِرِي الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ "That is for him whose family do not reside by the Holy Mosque" [al-Baqarah: 196], rather than His saying, the Exalted: فَمَن تَمَتَّعَ بِالْعُمْرَةِ إِلَى الْحَجِّ "Whoever enjoys the ‘Umrah until the Hajj" [al-Baqarah: 196].

Furthermore, all the narrations explicitly state that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) performed his Hajj as Tamattu‘, and that he entered the state of Ihram (أهلّ) with two separate proclamations for the ‘Umrah and the Hajj.

As for the argument that Tamattu‘ was exclusive to the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), as found in the two narrations of al-Durr al-Manthur from Abu Dharr: if the intended meaning is the argument mentioned by ‘Uthman and Ibn al-Zubayr, its rebuttal has already been provided. If the intended meaning is that it was a specific ruling for the companions of the Prophet that does not extend to others, then this is refuted by the absolute nature (إطلاق) of His saying, the Exalted: ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن لَّمْ يَكُنْ أَهْلُهُ حَاضِرِي الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ "That is for him whose family do not reside by the Holy Mosque."

Moreover, the fact that some companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) denied that ruling and abandoned it—such as ‘Umar, ‘Uthman, Ibn al-Zubayr, Abu Musa, and Mu‘awiyah (and it is narrated that Abu Bakr was among them)—contradicts that claim!

As for the argument based on authority (الولاية) — the divinely sanctioned right of leadership and governance — claiming that he only prohibited it by virtue of his right as the Master of Affairs (ولي الأمر), and that Allah has enjoined obedience to the Masters of Affairs when He said: أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنكُمْ "Obey Allah and obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you" [al-Nisa: 59], the response is that the authority (الولاية) which the Quran has established for its rightful possessors does not encompass this case.

The clarification of this is as follows: The verses are numerous regarding the obligation of following what Allah has revealed to His Messenger, such as His saying, the Exalted: اتَّبِعُوا مَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ "Follow what has been sent down to you from your Lord" [al-A‘raf: 3], and what the Messenger of Allah explained of what he himself legislated by the permission of Allah the Exalted, as is apparent from His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا يُحَرِّمُونَ مَا حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ "and do not forbid what Allah and His Apostle have forbidden" [al-Tawbah: 29], and His saying, the Exalted: مَا آتَاكُمُ الرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَمَا نَهَاكُمْ عَنْهُ فَانتَهُوا "Take whatever the Apostle gives you, and relinquish whatever he forbids you" [al-Hashr: 7]. The meaning of "giving" (الإيتاء) here is "commanding," by the evidence of its juxtaposition with His saying: "whatever he forbids you." Thus, it is obligatory to obey Allah and His Messenger by complying with commands and refraining from prohibitions. The same applies to judgment (الحكم) and adjudication (القضاء), as the Exalted said: وَمَن لَّمْ يَحْكُم بِمَا أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ "Those who do not judge by what Allah has sent down—it is they who are the wrongdoers" [al-Ma'idah: 45], and in another place: فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْفَاسِقُونَ "it is they who are the transgressors" [al-Ma'idah: 47], and in another: فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْكَافِرُونَ "it is they who are the faithless" [al-Ma'idah: 44]. The Exalted also said: وَمَا كَانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ وَلَا مُؤْمِنَةٍ إِذَا قَضَى اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ أَمْرًا أَن يَكُونَ لَهُمُ الْخِيَرَةُ مِنْ أَمْرِهِمْ وَمَن يَعْصِ اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلَالًا مُّبِينًا "A believing man or a believing woman has no choice in their matter when Allah and His Apostle have ordained a matter; and whoever disobeys Allah and His Apostle has certainly strayed into manifest error" [al-Ahzab: 36]. And He said: وَرَبُّكَ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَاءُ وَيَخْتَارُ مَا كَانَ لَهُمُ الْخِيَرَةُ "Your Lord creates whatever He wishes and chooses: they have no choice" [al-Qasas: 68]. The meaning of "choice" (الاختيار) here is adjudication and legislation, or what encompasses both. The Quran has explicitly stated that it is a Book that is never abrogated (غير منسوخ) and that the rulings (الأحكام) remain as they are until the Day of Resurrection. The Exalted said: وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتَابٌ عَزِيزٌ لَّا يَأْتِيهِ الْبَاطِلُ مِن بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلَا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ تَنزِيلٌ مِّنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ "Indeed it is an august Book: falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it, [it is] a gradual sending down from One All-wise, All-laudable" [Fussilat: 41-42]. This verse is absolute (مطلقة), encompassing the abrogation of a ruling; therefore, whatever Allah and His Messenger have legislated or whatever Allah and His Messenger have ordained must be followed by the nation, from the Masters of Affairs (أولي الأمر) down to everyone else.

From here it becomes clear that His saying, the Exalted: أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنكُمْ "Obey Allah and obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you" only establishes for the Masters of Affairs the right of obedience in matters other than the established rulings (الأحكام). They and the rest of the nation are equal in the obligation to preserve the rulings of Allah and His Messenger; in fact, it is more obligatory upon them. That which requires obedience to the Masters of Affairs is only what they command or forbid regarding what they deem to be in the interest (صلاح) of the nation—in terms of action or omission—while preserving the ruling of Allah (حكم الله) in that specific instance.

Just as an individual has the right to eat on a certain day or not to eat, while it remains permissible for him to eat from his own wealth; and he has the right to buy and sell on a certain day or to refrain from it, while selling remains lawful (حلال); and he has the right to take a case to a judge if someone disputes his property, or he may choose to waive his defense while the right to litigate remains permissible—all of this if he sees his own interest in doing so, while the rulings remain as they are. However, he does not have the right to drink wine, nor to take usury, nor to usurp the property of another by nullifying their ownership, even if he sees his own interest in doing so, because all of that conflicts with the ruling of Allah the Exalted. All of this pertains to personal conduct. Similarly, the Master of Affairs (ولي الأمر) has the right to act in public matters according to the universal interests (المصالح الكلية) while preserving the divine rulings (الأحكام الإلهية) as they are. Thus, he may defend the frontiers of Islam at one time and refrain from it at another, according to the public interests he identifies; or he may order a general suspension of activities or a general expenditure on a certain day, and so on, according to the interest he perceives.

In short, everything that an individual Muslim has the right to act upon according to his personal interest while adhering to the ruling of Allah the Glorified in that instance, the Master of Affairs—on behalf of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—has the right to act upon according to the general interest pertaining to the state of the Muslims, while adhering to the ruling of Allah the Glorified in that instance.

If it were permissible for the Master of Affairs to interfere with a legislative ruling (الحكم التشريعي), whether it be a defining law (تكليفاً) or a declaratory law (وضعاً), according to what he perceives as the interest of the time, no ruling would remain standing, and the continuity of the Sharia (الشريعة) — the divine system of law and ethics — until the Day of Resurrection would have no meaning whatsoever. For what is the difference between someone saying: "The ruling of Tamattu‘ is among the pleasures of life and does not suit the state of asceticism and worship for the pilgrim, so it must be abandoned," and someone saying: "The permissibility of enslavement does not suit the current state of the world which dictates universal freedom, so it must be neglected," or "The implementation of the prescribed punishments (الحدود) is something that modern, advanced humanity cannot stomach, and the laws currently prevailing in the world do not accept it, so it must be suspended"?!

This meaning may be understood from some narrations on the subject, as in al-Durr al-Manthur: Ishaq ibn Rahwayh narrated in his Musnad, and Ahmad from al-Hasan: "That ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab intended to forbid the Tamattu‘ of Hajj, so Ubayy ibn Ka‘b stood up to him and said: 'That is not for you to do; the Book of Allah has revealed it and we performed ‘Umrah with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).' So ‘Umar desisted."

Ayahs

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يُعْجِبُكَ قَوْلُهُۥ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَيُشْهِدُ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ مَا فِى قَلْبِهِۦ وَهُوَ أَلَدُّ ٱلْخِصَامِ (٢٠٤)
Among the people is he whose talk about worldly life impresses you, and he holds Allah witness to what is in his heart, though he is the staunchest of enemies. (204)
وَإِذَا تَوَلَّىٰ سَعَىٰ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لِيُفْسِدَ فِيهَا وَيُهْلِكَ ٱلْحَرْثَ وَٱلنَّسْلَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْفَسَادَ (٢٠٥)
If he were to wield authority, he would try to cause corruption in the land and to ruin the crop and the stock, and Allah does not like corruption. (205)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُ ٱتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ أَخَذَتْهُ ٱلْعِزَّةُ بِٱلْإِثْمِ ۚ فَحَسْبُهُۥ جَهَنَّمُ ۚ وَلَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ (٢٠٦)
And when he is told, ‘Be wary of Allah,’ conceit seizes him sinfully; so let hell suffice him, and it is surely an evil resting place! (206)
وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَشْرِى نَفْسَهُ ٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ رَءُوفٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ (٢٠٧)
And among the people is he who sells his soul seeking the pleasure of Allah, and Allah is most kind to [His] servants. (207)

(Commentary)

The verses contain another classification of people based on the outcomes of their attributes, just as the preceding verses—namely His saying, the Exalted: فَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَن يَقُولُ رَبَّنَا آتِنَا فِي الدُّنْيَا "Among the people there are those who say, ‘Our Lord, give us in this world’" [2:200]—contained a classification. However, those verses categorized them in terms of seeking the world or the Hereafter, while these verses categorize them in terms of hypocrisy (النفاق) and sincerity in faith (الخلوص في الايمان). Thus, the connection of these verses to the verses regarding the Hajj al-Tamattu‘ is evident.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَن يُعْجِبُكَ قَوْلُهُ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا "Among the people is he whose speech concerning the life of this world amazes you" [2:204]. To be amazed (اعجبه الشيء) means to be pleased or delighted by something. His saying, "concerning the life of this world" (في الحياة الدنيا), is connected to "amazes you" (يعجبك); that is, the amazement occurs in this world because this life is a type of existence that only judges based on the outward (الظاهر). As for the inward (الباطن) and the secret conscience (السريرة), they remain under a veil and behind a curtain, unobserved by a human being who is attached to the life of this world, unless he uncovers something of the inward state through its effects. This is complemented by what follows in His saying, the Exalted: وَيُشْهِدُ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ مَا فِي قَلْبِهِ "and he calls Allah to witness as to what is in his heart" [2:204]. The meaning is that he speaks in a way that his speech amazes you, through which he alludes to observing the side of truth, showing concern for the welfare of the creation, and the advancement of the religion and the nation (الامة), while he is the most contentious of adversaries against the truth. The word aladd (ألد) is a superlative form from ladda (لدّ), meaning to be severe in contention. Khisaam (الخصام) is the plural of khasm (خصم) [adversary], similar to sa‘b (صعب) and si‘aab (صعاب). It is also said that khisaam is a verbal noun, meaning "the most severe in contention."

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا تَوَلَّىٰ سَعَىٰ فِي الْأَرْضِ لِيُفْسِدَ فِيهَا "If he comes to power, he hastens about the earth to cause corruption in it" [2:205]. Al-tawalli (التولي) means the assumption of authority (الولاية) and power (السلطان). This is supported by His saying, the Exalted, in the following verse: أَخَذَتْهُ الْعِزَّةُ بِالْإِثْمِ "pride carries him off to sin" [2:206], which indicates that he possesses a pride (عزة) acquired through the sin (الاثم) committed by his heart, which does not correspond to his tongue. Al-sa‘y (السعي) means action or hastening in movement. Thus, the meaning is that when this hypocrite, who is severe in contention, gains the capacity to act and is given power and assumes authority over the people's affairs, he hastens about the earth to cause corruption (الفساد) therein. It is also possible that al-tawalli means turning away from a conversation or confrontation; that is, when he leaves your presence, his absence contradicts his presence, and what he used to manifest of seeking reform and good changes into hastening about the earth for the sake of corruption and corrupting others.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيُهْلِكَ الْحَرْثَ وَالنَّسْلَ "and to destroy the tilth and the stock" [2:205]. Its apparent meaning is an explanation of His saying, the Exalted: "to cause corruption in it" (ليفسد فيها); that is, he causes corruption therein by destroying the tilth (الحرث) and the stock (النسل). Since the subsistence of the human species in terms of life and survival depends on nutrition and procreation, these are the two upright pillars that the species cannot do without in any state. As for procreation, it is obvious. As for nutrition, man relies therein upon animals and plants, and animals rely upon plants. Thus, plants are the foundation, and they are preserved through tilth (الحرث), which is the cultivation of plants. For this reason, corruption was linked to tilth and stock. The meaning is that he causes corruption in the earth by annihilating man and eradicating this species through the destruction of tilth and stock.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ الْفَسَادَ "and Allah does not like corruption" [2:205]. What is meant by corruption (الفساد) here is not that which is corruption in the cosmos and existence—ontological corruption (الفساد التكويني)—for this realm is the realm of generation and corruption (الكون والفساد), and the world of the struggle for survival. There is no generation (كون) except through corruption, and no life except through death; they are intertwined in this natural existence within the natural realm. Far be it from Allah, the Glorified, to hate what He has decreed and ordained.

Rather, it is the corruption related to legislation (التشريع). For Allah only legislated what He legislated of the religion (الدين) to rectify thereby the actions of His servants, thus rectifying their character and the dispositions of their souls (ملكات نفوسهم). Through this, the state of humanity and the human collective (الجامعة البشرية) becomes balanced. At that point, their life in this world and their life in the Hereafter become felicitous, as will be explained in His saying, the Exalted: كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً "The mankind were a single community" [2:213].

Therefore, this person whose inward heart contradicts his outward speech, when he hastens about the earth with corruption, he only corrupts through that which outwardly appears as reform—by perverting the word from its proper place, changing the judgment of Allah from what it is, and tampering with religious teachings in a way that leads to the corruption of character and the discord of the word. In that lies the death of religion, the annihilation of humanity, and the corruption of the world. These verses have been validated by what history has recorded regarding the rule of men who climbed onto the shoulders of this Islamic nation and tampered with the affairs of religion and the world in a way that resulted in nothing for the religion but calamity, for the Muslims but degradation, and for the nation but discord. It was not long before religion became a plaything for every player, and humanity became a prey for every predator. The result of this hastening is the corruption of the earth, and that is through the destruction of religion first, and the destruction of humanity second. For this reason, His saying, "and to destroy the tilth and the stock," has been interpreted in some narrations as the destruction of religion and humanity, as will come, Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ أَخَذَتْهُ الْعِزَّةُ بِالْإِثْمِ فَحَسْبُهُ جَهَنَّمُ وَلَبِئْسَ الْمِهَادُ "And when he is told, ‘Be wary of Allah,’ pride carries him off to sin. So hell shall suffice him, and it is surely an evil resting place" [2:206]. Al-‘izzah (العزة) [pride/might] is well-known, and al-mihaad (المهاد) is a bed or resting place. It appears that His saying, "to sin" (بالإثم), is connected to "pride" (العزة). The meaning is that when he is commanded with the godwariness (تقوى) of Allah, he is seized by the outward pride he acquired through the sin and hypocrisy (النفاق) concealed within his soul. This is because absolute pride (العزة) belongs only to Allah, the Glorified, as He said: تُعِزُّ مَن تَشَاءُ وَتُذِلُّ مَن تَشَاءُ "You exalt whomever You wish and abase whomever You wish" [3:26], and He said: وَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ "Yet all might belongs to Allah and His Apostle and the faithful" [63:8], and He said: أَيَبْتَغُونَ عِندَهُمُ الْعِزَّةَ فَإِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا "Do they seek might with them? Indeed all might belongs to Allah" [4:139].

Far be it from Him, the Exalted, to attribute something to Himself and reserve its bestowal for Himself, and then have it result in sin or evil. Thus, this pride is only a pride that the one ignorant of the reality of the matter perceives as pride according to the outward life of this world, not a true pride (عزة حقيقية) that Allah, the Glorified, has given to its possessor.

From here it becomes clear that His saying, "to sin" (بالإثم), is not connected to His saying, "carries him off" (أخذته), in the sense of the preposition ba (ب) being for transitivity—meaning that pride drove him to sin and to reject the command of godwariness, answering the commander with speech that grieves him—nor is the ba for causality—meaning that pride and resistance appeared in him because of the sin he acquired. This is because the absolute application of the term "pride" (العزة) to this psychological state and naming it "pride" would necessitate its endorsement and the confirmation from Him, the Exalted, that it is a true pride, which it is not; unlike if it were named "pride through sin" (عزة بالإثم).

As for His saying, the Exalted: بَلِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي عِزَّةٍ وَشِقَاقٍ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن قَرْنٍ فَنَادَوا وَّلَاتَ حِينَ مَنَاصٍ "Rather the faithless are in [the grip of] pride and defiance. How many a generation We have destroyed before them! Then they cried out [for help], but it was no longer the time for escape" [38:2-3], it is not of the category of naming and endorsement, because "pride" (عزة) is indefinite and followed by the verse: "How many a generation We have destroyed before them..." Thus, the pride there is a formal pride (عزة صورية), neither lasting nor authentic.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَن يَشْرِي نَفْسَهُ ابْتِغَاءَ مَرْضَاتِ اللَّهِ "And among the people is he who sells his soul seeking the pleasure of Allah" [2:207]. Its contrast with His saying, the Exalted: "And among the people is he whose speech amazes you..." [2:204] implies that the description is opposite to the description. That is, just as the purpose of His saying, "And among the people is he who amazes you," was to explain that there is a man proud of his sin, self-admiring, manifesting reform while concealing hypocrisy, from whom nothing returns to the state of religion and humanity except corruption and destruction; likewise, the purpose of His saying, "And among the people is he who sells his soul," is to explain that there is another man who has sold his soul to Allah, the Glorified, desiring nothing but what Allah desires. He has no personal whim, no pride except in his Lord, and no seeking except for the pleasure of Allah, the Exalted. Through him, the affairs of religion and the world are rectified, the truth is enlivened, the life of humanity becomes pleasant, and the abundance of Islam flows. By this, the connection of the conclusion to the beginning—namely His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ رَءُوفٌ بِالْعِبَادِ "and Allah is most kind to [His] servants" [2:207]—to what preceded it becomes clear. For the existence of a human being with this attribute is from the kindness (رأفة) of Allah, the Glorified, toward His servants. For if there were not men of these attributes among the people to counter other men whose attributes are the aforementioned hypocrisy and corruption, the pillars of religion would have collapsed, and not one brick of the structure of reform and guidance would have remained upon another. But Allah, the Glorified, continues to vanish that falsehood by this truth and to remedy the corruption of His enemies with the reform of His friends, as He said:

وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ اللَّهِ النَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لَّفَسَدَتِ الْأَرْضُ "Were it not for Allah’s repelling the people by means of one another, the earth would surely have been corrupted" [2:251], and He said:

وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ اللَّهِ النَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لَّهُدِّمَتْ صَوَامِعُ وَبِيَعٌ وَصَلَوَاتٌ وَمَسَاجِدُ يُذْكَرُ فِيهَا اسْمُ اللَّهِ كَثِيرًا "Were it not for Allah’s repelling the people by means of one another, there would surely have been demolished the cloisters, churches, synagogues and mosques in which Allah’s name is much mentioned" [22:40], and He said: فَإِن يَكْفُرْ بِهَا هَٰؤُلَاءِ فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنَا بِهَا قَوْمًا لَّيْسُوا بِهَا بِكَافِرِينَ "So if these [people] disbelieve in them, We have certainly entrusted them to a people who will not disbelieve in them" [6:89]. Thus, the corruption occurring to religion and the world from a group of those who have no whim except for themselves cannot have its breach closed except by the reform overflowing from others who have sold their souls to Allah, the Glorified, having no whim except in their Lord and the reform of the earth and those upon it. He has mentioned this profitable transaction with Allah by His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللَّهَ اشْتَرَىٰ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَأَمْوَالَهُم بِأَنَّ لَهُمُ الْجَنَّةَ يُقَاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ فَيَقْتُلُونَ وَيُقْتَلُونَ وَعْدًا عَلَيْهِ حَقًّا فِي التَّوْرَاةِ وَالْإِنجِيلِ وَالْقُرْآنِ وَمَنْ أَوْفَىٰ بِعَهْدِهِ مِنَ اللَّهِ فَاسْتَبْشِرُوا بِبَيْعِكُمُ الَّذِي بَايَعْتُم بِهِ "Indeed Allah has bought from the faithful their souls and their possessions for paradise being theirs: they fight in the way of Allah, so they slay and are slain. A promise binding upon Him in the Torah and the Evangel and the Quran. And who is truer to his covenant than Allah? So rejoice in the bargain you have made with Him" [9:111], among other verses.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, narrated from al-Suddi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَن يُعْجِبُكَ "Among the people is he whose speech amazes you" [2:204], that it was revealed concerning al-Akhnas ibn Shariq al-Thaqafi, an ally of the Banu Zuhrah. He came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم in Medina and said: "I have come wanting Islam, and Allah knows that I am truthful." This amazed the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم from him, and that is His saying, the Exalted: وَيُشْهِدُ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ مَا فِي قَلْبِهِ "and he calls Allah to witness as to what is in his heart." Then he left the presence of the Prophet and passed by a crop belonging to a group of Muslims and some donkeys; he burned the crop and hamstrung the donkeys. So Allah revealed: وَإِذَا تَوَلَّىٰ سَعَىٰ فِي الْأَرْضِ "If he comes to power, he hastens about the earth..." [2:205].

In al-Majma‘, narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas: These three verses were revealed concerning the hypocrite (المرائي) because he manifests the opposite of what he conceals. He said: And this is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام.

I say: But it does not align with the outward meaning of the verses. In some narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt, it is among the verses revealed concerning their enemies.

In al-Majma‘, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَيُهْلِكَ الْحَرْثَ وَالنَّسْلَ "and to destroy the tilth and the stock": that what is meant by tilth (الحرث) here is religion (الدين), and by stock (النسل) is humanity (الانسان).

I say: Its explanation has already passed. It has also been narrated that what is meant by tilth is progeny and crops, and the matter of application is easy.

In Amali al-Shaykh, narrated from Imam ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام: regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَن يَشْرِي نَفْسَهُ "And among the people is he who sells his soul" [2:207], he said: "It was revealed concerning ‘Ali عليه‌السلام when he spent the night on the bed of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم."

I say: The narrations from both schools (الفريقين) have become numerous that it was revealed regarding the night of the bed (laylat al-mabit). It was narrated in Tafsir al-Burhan through five chains from al-Tha‘labi and others.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Suhayb, who said: "When I intended to migrate from Mecca to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, the Quraysh said to me: 'O Suhayb, you came to us with no wealth, and now you leave with yourself and your wealth? By Allah, that shall never be.' I said to them: 'What if I give you my wealth, will you let me go?' They said: 'Yes.' So I gave them my wealth and they let me go. I left until I reached Medina. When that reached the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, he said: 'Suhayb has profited in the bargain twice.'"

I say: It was narrated through other chains, in some of which it says: and there was revealed: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَن يَشْرِي نَفْسَهُ "And among the people is he who sells his soul." In some, it says it was revealed concerning Suhayb and Abu Dharr for buying their souls with their wealth. It has already been mentioned that the verse does not harmonize with the meaning of "selling" being "buying" (الاشتراء).

In al-Majma‘, narrated from Imam ‘Ali عليه‌السلام: that what is meant by the verse is a man who is killed for commanding the right and forbidding the wrong.

I say: This is an explanation of the generality of the verse and does not contradict its revelation for a specific occasion.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ فِى ٱلسِّلْمِ كَآفَّةً وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ خُطُوَٰتِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (٢٠٨)
O you who have faith! Enter into submission, all together, and do not follow in Satan’s steps; he is indeed your manifest enemy. (208)
فَإِن زَلَلْتُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْكُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٢٠٩)
And should you stumble after the manifest proofs that have come to you, know that Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (209)
هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّآ أَن يَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِى ظُلَلٍ مِّنَ ٱلْغَمَامِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَقُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ (٢١٠)
Do they await anything but that Allah [’s command] should come to them in the shades of the clouds, with the angels, and the matter be decided [once for all]? To Allah all matters are returned. (210)

(Commentary)

These verses—from His saying: يا ايها الذين آمنوا (O you who have faith) until His saying: ألا إِن نصر الله قريب (Look! Allah’s help is indeed near)—comprise seven complete verses that clarify the method of preserving religious unity within the human collective. This method consists of entering into submission (السلم) and restricting oneself to what Allah has mentioned in speech and what He has shown as the path of action. It emphasizes that the unity of religion has never been severed, the felicity of the two abodes has never departed, and destruction has never descended upon a people except through exiting from submission (السلم) and interfering with the signs (آيات) of Allah the Exalted by altering them or placing them where they do not belong. This was witnessed in the Children of Israel and other past nations, and its likeness will occur in this nation; however, Allah promises them victory, saying: ألا إِن نصر الله قريب (Look! Allah’s help is indeed near).

His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ادْخُلُوا فِي السِّلْمِ كَافَّةً — O you who have faith! Enter into submission, all together (2:208). Submission (السلم), Islam (الإسلام), and surrender (التسليم) are one and the same. The word "all together" (كافة) is a term of emphasis meaning "entirely." Since the address is to the believers and they have been commanded to enter into submission (السلم) all together, it is a command pertaining to the collective as a whole and to every single individual within it. Thus, it is obligatory for every believer, and it is also obligatory for the collective not to differ therein and to surrender the matter to Allah and His Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). Furthermore, the address is specifically for the believers; therefore, the submission (السلم) they are called toward is surrender (التسليم) to Allah, the Glorified, after having believed in Him. It is mandatory for the believers to surrender the matter to Him, not to dictate for themselves what is beneficial through arbitrary opinion (استبداد من الرأي), and not to devise for themselves a path to follow from their own whims without it being clarified by Allah and His Messenger. For no people have perished except by following passion (الهوى) and speaking without knowledge (القول بغير العلم), and the right to life and the felicity of fortune have never been stripped from a people except due to discord (اختلاف).

From here it becomes clear that the meaning of "following the footsteps of Satan" (اتباع خطوات الشيطان) is not following him in every falsehood he calls toward, but rather following him in what he calls toward regarding religious matters—by beautifying some paths of falsehood with the ornament of truth and naming that which is not part of the religion as religion, such that a person adopts it without knowledge. The sign (علامة) of this is the absence of any mention of it by Allah and His Messenger within the religious teachings.

The specifics of the context and its constraints also indicate this: for "footsteps" (الخطوات) only exist within a traversed path. If the traveler is a believer and his path is the path of faith (الايمان), then this is a satanic path within faith. If the duty of the believer is to enter into submission (السلم), then the path he traverses without submission is among the footsteps of Satan, and following it is following the footsteps of Satan (اتباع خطوات الشيطان).

Thus, the verse is similar to His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ كُلُوا مِمَّا فِي الْأَرْضِ حَلَالًا طَيِّبًا وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا خُطُوَاتِ الشَّيْطَانِ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ إِنَّمَا يَأْمُرُكُم بِالسُّوءِ وَالْفَحْشَاءِ وَأَن تَقُولُوا عَلَى اللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ — O mankind! Eat of what is lawful and pure in the earth, and do not follow in the footsteps of Satan. Indeed he is your manifest enemy. He only commands you to [do] evil and indecency, and that you should attribute to Allah what you do not know (2:168-169). The discussion on this verse has already passed. And His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَتَّبِعُوا خُطُوَاتِ الشَّيْطَانِ وَمَن يَتَّبِعْ خُطُوَاتِ الشَّيْطَانِ فَإِنَّهُ يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشَاءِ وَالْمُنكَرِ — O you who have faith! Do not follow in the footsteps of Satan. Whoever follows in the footsteps of Satan [should know that] he indeed prompts indecency and wrong (24:21). And His saying, the Exalted: كُلُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللَّهُ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا خُطُوَاتِ الشَّيْطَانِ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ — Eat of what Allah has provided you and do not follow in the footsteps of Satan; indeed he is your manifest enemy (6:142).

The difference between this verse and those is that the call here is directed to the collective because of His saying, the Exalted: كافة (all together), whereas those verses are general. Thus, this verse carries the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: وَاعْتَصِمُوا بِحَبْلِ اللَّهِ جَمِيعًا وَلَا تَفَرَّقُوا — Hold fast, all together, to Allah’s cord, and do not be divided (3:103), and His saying, the Exalted: وأَنَّ هَٰذَا صِرَاطِي مُسْتَقِيمًا فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَن سَبِيلِهِ — ‘This is My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you from His way’ (6:153). It is inferred from the verse that Islam (الاسلام) is responsible for providing all the laws and knowledge that people need for their well-being.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِن زَلَلْتُم مِّن بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَتْكُمُ الْبَيِّنَاتُ — And if you should lapse after the manifest proofs have come to you (2:209). A "lapse" (الزلة) is a stumble. The meaning is: if you do not enter into submission (السلم) all together and you lapse—and the lapse is following the footsteps of Satan—then know that Allah is All-Mighty (عزيز), never overcome in His affair, and All-Wise (حكيم), never exceeding what His wisdom (حكمة) necessitates of decree (القضاء) regarding your state. He will decree for you what His wisdom requires and execute it upon you without any preventer being able to stop Him.

His saying, the Exalted: هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَن يَأْتِيَهُمُ اللَّهُ فِي ظُلَلٍ مِّنَ الْغَمَامِ — Do they await anything but that Allah should come to them in the shadows of the clouds... (2:210). Shadows (الظلل) is the plural of zullah (ظلة), which is that by which one seeks shade. The apparent meaning of the verse is that "the angels" (الملائكة) is conjoined to the Majestic Name (Allah). In the verse, there is a transition (التفات) from direct address to the third person, replacing the address to them with an address to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) by turning away from speaking to them. This indicates that their state is like that of one who awaits what We have promised them of the decree (القضاء) in accordance with what they have chosen of following the footsteps of Satan, discord, and fragmentation. This will occur when Allah comes to them in the shadows of the clouds and the angels, and the matter is decided (وقضي الأمر) from where they do not perceive, or in a manner that He pays no heed to them or the destruction they fall into. And to Allah all matters are returned (والى الله ترجع الامور); there is no escape from His judgment and decree. The context necessitates that His saying: هل ينظرون (Do they await) is the threat He warned them with in the previous verse: فاعلموا ان الله عزيز حكيم (then know that Allah is All-Mighty, All-Wise).

Furthermore, it is a necessary truth established by the Book and the Sunnah that Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, is not described with the attributes of bodies (الاجسام), nor is He characterized by the traits of contingent beings (الممكنات) which imply temporal origination (الحدوث) and necessitate poverty, need, and deficiency. For the Exalted has said: لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ — Nothing is like Him (42:11), and He said: وَاللَّهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ — and Allah is the All-sufficient (35:15), and He said: اللَّهُ خَالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — Allah is the creator of all things (39:62), among other verses. These are the clear verses (المحكمات) to which the ambiguous verses (المتشابهات) of the Quran must be referred. Whatever verses appear whose literal meaning attributes something of temporal attributes or actions to the Exalted should be referred back to these clear verses. They should be understood in a sense that does not contradict His sublime attributes and His beautiful names (الأسماء الحسنى), blessed and exalted is He.

Thus, verses containing the attribution of "coming" (المجيء) or "arriving" (الإتيان) to the Exalted—such as His saying: وَجَاءَ رَبُّكَ وَالْمَلَكُ صَفًّا صَفًّا — and your Lord comes with the angels in ranks (89:22), and His saying: فَأَتَاهُمُ اللَّهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَمْ يَحْتَسِبُوا — so Allah came at them from whence they did not expect (59:2), and His saying: فَأَتَى اللَّهُ بُنْيَانَهُم مِّنَ الْقَوَاعِدِ — so Allah razed their edifice from the foundations (16:26)—all of these are intended in a sense that befits the sanctuary of His holiness (ساحة قدسه), such as encompassing (الإحاطة) and the like, even if metaphorically. Accordingly, the meaning of "arriving" in His saying: أن يأتيهم الله (that Allah should come to them) is His encompassing them for the purpose of decreeing (القضاء) their fate.

Moreover, we find Him, the Glorified, in several instances of His speech, when He strips a relation or an action from the independence of causes and the mediation of intermediaries, He sometimes attributes it to Himself and sometimes to His command (أمره). For example, His saying: اللَّهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنفُسَ — Allah takes the souls (39:42), and His saying: يَتَوَفَّاكُم مَّلَكُ الْمَوْتِ — The Angel of Death... will take you (32:11), and His saying: تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنَا — Our messengers take him (6:61). He attributed the taking of souls (التوفي) once to Himself, once to the Angel of Death, and once to the messengers (angels). Then He said regarding the angels: بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ — they act by His command (21:27). Similarly, His saying: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ — Indeed your Lord will judge between them (10:93), and His saying: فَإِذَا جَاءَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ قُضِيَ بِالْحَقِّ — Then, when Allah’s edict comes, judgment will be made with justice (40:78). This is also like the present verse: ان يأتيهم اللَّهُ في ظلل من الغمام (that Allah should come to them in the shadows of the clouds), and His saying: هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَن تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ أَوْ يَأْتِيَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ — Do they await anything but that the angels should come to them, or your Lord’s edict should come? (16:33).

This necessitates the validity of assuming "the command" (الأمر) as an elided term in instances involving the attribution of matters to Him that do not befit the majesty of His essence (الذات), such as "your Lord comes" or "Allah comes to them." The implied meaning would be: "the command of your Lord comes" and "the command of Allah comes to them."

This is what a simple investigation into the meaning of these attributions necessitates, as held by the majority of commentators. However, deep reflection (التدبر) on His speech yields a meaning more subtle and refined than that. Verses such as His saying: وَاللَّهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ — and Allah is the All-sufficient (35:15), and His saying: الْعَزِيزِ الْوَهَّابِ — the All-mighty, the All-munificent (38:9), and His saying: أَعْطَىٰ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدَىٰ — He who gave everything its creation and then guided it (20:50), imply that He, the Exalted, possesses (واجد) whatever He bestows of creation, its affairs, and its modes. He is full of what He grants and gives generously, even if our understandings—due to their habituation to matter and its corporeal laws—find it difficult to conceive how He is characterized by some of the attributes He pours upon His creation and how they are attributed to Him. However, when these meanings are stripped of the constraints of matter and the attributes of temporal origination (الحدثان), there is no impediment to attributing them to Him. Deficiency and need are the criteria for stripping a meaning from Him. If the meaning is not accompanied by deficiency and need because it has been stripped of them, its attribution to Him is valid—rather, it is necessary—because everything to which the name of "thing" applies originates from Him in a manner befitting His majesty and greatness.

Thus, "coming" (المجيء) and "arriving" (الإتيان), which for us mean a body traversing a distance between itself and another body through motion and approaching it, when stripped of the particularity of matter, mean the attainment of proximity and the removal of the obstacle and barrier between two things from a certain aspect. In this case, its attribution to Him is valid in a literal sense (حقيقة) without metaphor (مجاز). His "coming" to them is the removal of barriers between them and His decree (القضاء) concerning them. This is one of the Quranic realities (الحقائق القرآنية) that demonstrative inquiries (الأبحاث البرهانية) did not succeed in reaching except after profound journeying, traversing every easy and difficult path, and establishing the concept of gradation (التشكيك) in the fundamental reality of existence (الحقيقة الوجودية الاصيلة).

In any case, this verse contains the threat indicated by His saying in the previous verse: إِن الله عزيز حكيم (Indeed Allah is All-Mighty, All-Wise). It is possible that it is a threat regarding what the people will face in the Hereafter on the Day of Resurrection, as is the apparent meaning of His saying in a similar verse: هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَن تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ أَوْ يَأْتِيَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ — Do they await anything but that the angels should come to them, or your Lord’s edict should come? (16:33). It is also possible that it is a threat of an event expected to occur in this world, as appears by referring to what is in Surah Yunus after His saying: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَّسُولٌ — There is a messenger for every nation (10:47), and what is in Surah al-Rum after His saying: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا — So set your heart on the religion as a hanif (30:30), and what is in Surah al-Anbiya and others. This is based on the fact that the Hereafter is the deferred end of this immediate world and the complete manifestation of what is in this world. It is also possible that it is a threat of what will occur in both this world and the Hereafter together. Whatever the case, His saying: في ظلل من الغمام (in the shadows of the clouds) contains a meaning that suits its context.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ وَإِلَى اللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ الْأُمُورُ — and the matter is decided, and to Allah all matters are returned (2:210). The silence regarding the agent of the decree—who is Allah, the Glorified, as indicated by His saying: والى الله ترجع الامور (and to Allah all matters are returned)—is to manifest majesty (إظهار الكبرياء), as is done by the great when reporting the occurrence of their judgments and the issuance of their commands; this is frequent in the Quran.

(Narrative Discussion)

Several narrations have already been mentioned under His saying: يا أيها الناس كلوا مما في الأرض حلالاً طيباً (O mankind! Eat of what is lawful and pure in the earth) which support what we mentioned regarding the meaning of "following the footsteps of Satan" (اتباع خطوات الشيطان), so refer back to them. In some narrations, it is stated that "submission" (السلم) is the Wilayah (الولاية) — the divinely appointed authority and guardianship; this is an instance of applying the general to a specific case (الجري), as has been mentioned repeatedly in similar instances.

In al-Tawhid and al-Ma'ani, narrated from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah the Exalted: هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَن يَأْتِيَهُمُ اللَّهُ فِي ظُلَلٍ مِّنَ الْغَمَامِ (Do they await anything but that Allah should come to them in the shadows of the clouds). He said: يقول : هل ينظرون إِلا أن يأتيهم الله بالملائكة في ظلل من الغمام وهكذا نزلت — "He says: Do they await anything but that Allah should come to them with the angels in the shadows of the clouds; and thus was it revealed." And regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَجَاءَ رَبُّكَ وَالْمَلَكُ صَفًّا صَفًّا (and your Lord comes with the angels in ranks). He said: ان الله عزّ وجل لا يوصف بالمجيء والذهاب ، تعالى عن الانتقال وانما يعني به وجاء امر ربك والملك صفاً صفاً — "Indeed Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is not described by coming and going; He is exalted above movement. Rather, it means: 'And the command of your Lord comes with the angels in ranks.'"

I say: His saying (peace be upon him) "He says: Do they await..." means "He intends: Do they await..."; thus, it is an explanation (تفسير) of the verse and not a variant reading (قرائة).

The meaning he mentioned is exactly what we proposed—that the intended meaning of the "coming" of the Exalted is the coming of His command. For the angels only do what they do and descend when they descend by the command. The Exalted said: بَلْ عِبَادٌ مُّكْرَمُونَ لَا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُم بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ — Rather they are [His] honored servants. They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command (21:26-27). And the Exalted said: يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلَائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ — He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command (16:2).

There is another meaning here that some have entertained: that the rhetorical question (الاستفهام الإنكاري) in His saying: هل ينظرون الا ان يأتيهم الله (Do they await anything but that Allah should come to them...) is to negate the entire sentence, not just the subject. The meaning would be: these people await nothing but an impossible matter, which is that Allah should come to them in the shadows of the clouds as a body comes to another body, and the angels come with Him to command and forbid them, which is impossible. Thus, it is an allusion (كناية) to the impossibility of rectifying them through these exhortations and reminders. However, this is problematic because it does not align with what was previously established: that the verses share a single context, which necessitates that the speech is directed toward the state of the believers. The state of the believers does not suit such a statement. Furthermore, if the speech were intended to convey that, it would not be devoid of a rebuttal against them, as is the custom of the Quran in such instances, like His saying: وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَاءَنَا لَوْلَا أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْمَلَائِكَةُ أَوْ نَرَىٰ رَبَّنَا لَقَدِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فِي أَنفُسِهِمْ وَعَتَوْا عُتُوًّا كَبِيرًا — Those who do not expect to encounter Us say, ‘Why are the angels not sent down to us?’ or ‘Why do we not see our Lord?’ Certainly they are too proud of themselves and have been excessively rebellious (24:21). And His saying: وَقَالُوا اتَّخَذَ الرَّحْمَٰنُ وَلَدًا سُبْحَانَهُ — They say, ‘The All-beneficent has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He! (21:26).

Moreover, there would then be no reason for His saying: في ظلل من الغمام (in the shadows of the clouds), nor any apparent point for the rest of the speech, as is obvious.

(Another Narrative Discussion)

Know that it has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) that the verse is interpreted as the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة), as in the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him). It is also interpreted as the Return (الرجعة) — the restoration to life of a group of people before the Day of Resurrection — as narrated by al-Saduq from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him). And it is interpreted as the appearance of the Mahdi (peace be upon him), as narrated by al-Ayyashi in his Tafsir from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) through two chains.

Similar instances are many. If you search, you will find a great number of verses whose interpretation has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt sometimes as the Resurrection, sometimes as the Return, and a third time as the Appearance (الظهور). This is only due to a unity and shared essence (سنخية) between these meanings. Because people have not investigated the reality of the Day of Resurrection and have not exhausted their efforts in uncovering what the Quran provides regarding the identity of this Great Day, they have become divided concerning these narrations. Some have discarded these narrations—which number in the hundreds, perhaps exceeding five hundred in various chapters—while others have interpreted them away despite their clarity and explicitness. Others—who follow the best path—transmit them and pause at them without further investigation.

Non-Shi'as, who are the majority of Muslims, although they acknowledge the appearance of the Mahdi and have narrated it through mutawatir (متواترة) — successive and widely transmitted — chains from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), they have denied the Return (الرجعة) and considered the belief in it to be one of the exclusive tenets of the Shi'a. In recent times, some who affiliate themselves with the Shi'a have followed them in this, considering it part of the interpolations (الدس) made by the Jews and some who feigned Islam, like Abdullah ibn Saba and his companions. Some have attempted to invalidate the Return using what they imagined to be a rational proof, stating in essence: "According to Divine Providence, death does not occur to a living being until it has completed the perfection of life and moved from potentiality (القوة) to actuality (الفعل) in all its perfections. Therefore, its return to the world after death would be a return to potentiality while it is already in actuality; this is impossible unless a truthful informant reports it, namely Allah the Glorified or one of His vicegerents, as reported in the stories of Moses, Jesus, Abraham (peace be upon them), and others. But nothing has been reported from Him or them regarding the Return, and what those who affirm it rely upon is incomplete." Then he proceeded to weaken the narrations, leaving neither a sound nor a weak one.

This poor soul did not realize that if his proof were valid as a rational proof, its beginning would invalidate its end. That which is essentially impossible (محال ذاتي) does not accept exceptions, nor does it become possible through the report of a truthful informant. An informant who reports the occurrence of the impossible cannot be truthful; even if his truthfulness were assumed, it would necessitate the interpretation (تأويل) of his speech into something possible—just as if he were to report that one is not half of two, or that every truthful person is identically a liar.

What he mentioned regarding the impossibility of that which has moved from potentiality to actuality returning to potentiality again is true, but the minor premise is denied. The mentioned impossibility only applies to the reviving of the dead and their return to the world after departing from it if that occurs after "natural death" (الموت الطبيعي) as they have defined it—which is the soul's separation from the body after it has moved from potentiality to actuality in a complete manner, followed by its separation from the body by its very nature. As for "accidental death" (الموت الاخترامي) which occurs due to an external force like killing or disease, it does not necessitate any impossibility for a return to the world after it. For it is possible that a human is prepared for a perfection that exists in a time after the time of his first worldly life; thus he dies and then is brought to life to acquire the perfection prepared for him in the second period. Or he may be prepared for a perfection conditioned by an intervening life in the intermediate realm (البرزخ), so he returns to the world after fulfilling the condition. Thus, based on one of the two hypotheses, the Return to the world is possible without the impediment of impossibility. The full discussion is deferred to another place.

As for his critique of every single narration, the response is that the narrations are mutawatir (متواترة) in meaning from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt, to the extent that the belief in the Return was considered by opponents to be an exclusive trait of the Shi'a and their Imams since the first century. Tawatur (التواتر) is not invalidated by the fact that individual narrations are subject to scrutiny or critique. Furthermore, several verses revealed concerning it and narrations reported about it are complete in their indication and worthy of reliance. They will be addressed in their appropriate places, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ فَوْجًا مِّمَّن يُكَذِّبُ بِآيَاتِنَا — The day We shall muster from every nation a group of those who deny Our signs (27:83), and other verses.

Moreover, the verses in a general sense indicate it, such as His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تَدْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِكُم مَّثَلُ الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِن قَبْلِكُم — Do you suppose that you will enter paradise though there has not yet come to you the like of [what befell] those who went before you? (2:214). Among the events that occurred before us was the reviving of the dead, as the Quran narrates in the stories of Abraham, Moses, Jesus, Ezra, Jeremiah, and others. The Messenger of Allah said in what has been narrated by both sects: "By Him in Whose hand is my soul, you shall surely follow the ways of those before you, shoe by shoe and feather by feather; you shall not miss their path, nor shall the ways of the Children of Israel miss you."

Furthermore, these matters that the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt informed us of are among the prophecies (الملاحم) related to the end of times. Transmitters and narrators have recorded them in books whose manuscripts are preserved with us, having been authored and written centuries and long periods before their occurrence. We witness every day the truth of a portion of them without addition or subtraction; thus, we should affirm the correctness of all of them and the truth of all their contents.

Let us return to the beginning of our discussion regarding the interpretation of a single verse sometimes as the Day of Resurrection and at other times as the Return or the Appearance. We say: what is gathered from His speech regarding the descriptions and traits of the Day of Resurrection is that it is a day in which no cause among causes and no preoccupation among preoccupations shall veil one from Him, the Glorified. In it, all illusions shall perish, and His signs shall appear with complete manifestation. This is a day whose existence and realization do not negate the realization and existence of this corporeal realm (النشأة الجسمانية); nothing in the Book or Sunnah indicates that—rather, the matter is to the contrary. However, what is apparent from the Book and Sunnah is that humanity—meaning this lineage that Allah the Glorified ended with Adam and his spouse—will become extinct from the world before this Day dawns for them.

There is no conflict between the two realms—the worldly realm (نشأة الدنيا) and the realm of resurrection (نشأة البعث)—such that one would repel the other, just as the realm of the intermediate state (النشأة البرزخية), which is currently established for the dead among us, does not repel the world, nor does the world repel it. The Exalted said: تَاللَّهِ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا إِلَىٰ أُمَمٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ أَعْمَالَهُمْ فَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُمُ الْيَوْمَ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ — By Allah, We certainly sent [messengers] to nations before you, but Satan made their deeds seem decorous to them. So he is their master today, and there is a painful punishment for them (16:63).

This is the reality of the Day of Resurrection: the day when mankind shall stand before the Lord of all the worlds, the day when they shall emerge, and nothing of them will be hidden from Allah. For this reason, the day of death is sometimes called "Resurrection" (القيامة) due to the lifting of the veils of causes from the illusion of the deceased. It is narrated from Ali (peace be upon him): "Whoever dies, his resurrection has begun." The explanation of all this will come, Allah willing.

The narrations affirming the Return (الرجعة), although they differ in individual details, are united in one meaning: that the course of the worldly system is heading toward a day in which the signs of Allah will appear with full manifestation. In that day, He, the Glorified and Exalted, will not be disobeyed; rather, He will be worshipped with pure worship, untainted by the passion of the soul and unaffected by the seduction of Satan. In it, some of the dead among the friends of Allah and His enemies will return to the world, and truth will be distinguished from falsehood.

This implies that the Day of the Return is one of the degrees (مراتب) of the Day of Resurrection, even if it is below it in manifestation due to the possibility of evil and corruption occurring in it to some extent, unlike the Day of Resurrection. For this reason, the day of the appearance of the Mahdi (peace be upon him) is sometimes joined to it as well, because of the complete manifestation of truth in it, even if it is also below the Return. It has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt: "The days of Allah are three: the Day of Appearance, the Day of the Return (الكرة), and the Day of Resurrection." In some narrations: "The days of Allah are three: the day of death, the Day of the Return, and the Day of Resurrection." This meaning—namely, unity in terms of reality and difference in terms of degrees—is what necessitates their (peace be upon them) interpretation of some verses as the Resurrection at one time, the Return at another, and the Appearance at a third. You have learned from the preceding discussion that this day is possible in itself—rather, it is a reality—and the denier has no proof to negate it.

Ayahs

سَلْ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ كَمْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍۭ بَيِّنَةٍ ۗ وَمَن يُبَدِّلْ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْهُ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٢١١)
Ask the Children of Israel how many a manifest sign We had given them. Whoever changes Allah’s blessing after it has come to him, indeed Allah is severe in retribution. (211)
زُيِّنَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَيَسْخَرُونَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ۘ وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (٢١٢)
Worldly life has been glamorized for the faithless, and they ridicule the faithful. But those who are Godwary shall be above them on the Day of Resurrection, and Allah provides for whomever He wishes without any reckoning. (212)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: سَلْ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ كَمْ آتَيْنَاهُم مِّنْ آيَةٍ بَيِّنَةٍ ۗ وَمَن يُبَدِّلْ نِعْمَةَ اللَّهِ مِن بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَتْهُ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقَابِ — "Ask the Children of Israel how many a manifest sign We had given them.
