Whoever changes Allah’s blessing after it has come to him, then Allah is indeed severe in retribution." (2:211). This is a confirmation and reinforcement of what was contained in His saying, the Exalted: "And if you should lapse after the manifest proofs have come to you, then know that Allah is All-mighty, All-wise" (2:209)—the verse containing the threat of seizing the dissenters with the seizing of One who is All-mighty and All-powerful.

He says: These are the Children of Israel before your very eyes; they are the nation to whom Allah gave the Book, the judgment, prophethood, and kingship, provided them with good things, and favored them over all the worlds (العالمين). Ask them: how many a manifest sign (آية بينة) did We give them? Look at their affair: where did they begin and what was their ultimate fate? They distorted the words from their contexts and set up matters of their own making in opposition to Allah, His Book, and His signs, out of rebellious envy (بغياً) after knowledge had come to them. Consequently, Allah punished them with the severest punishment due to what befell them of taking peers (أنداد) besides Him, falling into discord, the splintering of opinions, devouring one another, the loss of sovereignty, the vanishing of felicity, and the torment of humiliation and misery in this world—and the punishment of the Hereafter is more disgraceful, and they shall not be helped.

This is the established sunnah (السنة الجارية) — the habitual and consistent way of Allah’s acting in creation — of Allah, the Glorified: whoever changes a blessing (نعمة) and diverts it from its proper course, Allah punishes him, and Allah is severe in retribution (شديد العقاب). Accordingly, His saying: "Whoever changes Allah’s blessing..." until the end of the verse, is an instance of placing a universal (الكلي) principle in the stead of a particular (الجزئي) case to indicate that the ruling is an established sunnah.

His saying, the Exalted: زُيِّنَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا وَيَسْخَرُونَ مِنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ۘ وَالَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ ۗ وَاللَّهُ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ — "Worldly life has been glamorized for the faithless, and they ridicule the faithful. But those who are God-conscious shall be above them on the Day of Resurrection, and Allah provides for whomever He wishes without any reckoning." (2:212). This serves as the rationale (التعليل) for what preceded. The underlying factor in that [deviation] is the glamorization of the worldly life (تزين الحياة الدنيا) for them. For when life is glamorized for a human being, it invites him toward the whims of the soul (هوى النفس) and its desires, causing him to forget every truth and reality (حقيقة). Thus, the human being desires nothing but to attain it—whether through prestige, status, wealth, or finery. He soon begins to employ everything for its sake and in its path, including religion (الدين). He takes religion as a means (وسيلة) to achieve distinctions and social standing. Consequently, religion is transformed into a means for the distinction of leaders and chiefs and whatever suits their sovereignty and leadership, and a means for the commoners and blind followers (المقلدة) to seek proximity to their leaders and masters, as we witness in our nation today and as we witnessed in the Children of Israel before.

The apparent meaning of disbelief (الكفر) in the Quran is "concealing" (الستر), whether it be disbelief in the technical sense (كفراً اصطلاحياً) or absolute disbelief (كفراً مطلقاً) as opposed to absolute faith (الايمان المطلق). Thus, the glamorization of the worldly life is not restricted to disbelievers in the technical sense; rather, anyone who conceals a religious reality among the realities or changes a religious blessing is a disbeliever (كافر) for whom the worldly life has been glamorized—so let him prepare for a severe retribution.

His saying, the Exalted: "But those who are God-conscious (اتقوا) shall be above them on the Day of Resurrection..." The substitution of faith (الايمان) with godwariness (التقوى) — the protective state of being conscious of Allah's boundaries — in this sentence is because faith alone does not benefit without righteous deeds (العمل).

Ayah

كَانَ ٱلنَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ وَأَنزَلَ مَعَهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ فِيمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ ۚ وَمَا ٱخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ فَهَدَى ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لِمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٢١٣)
Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed, and none differed in it except those who had been given it, after the manifest proofs had come to them, out of envy among themselves. Then Allah guided those who had faith to the truth of what they differed in, by His will, and Allah guides whomever He wishes to a straight path. (213)

(Commentary)

The verse explains the reason for the legislation of the essence of religion (أصل الدين) and the charging of the human species with it, as well as the cause for the occurrence of disagreement (الاختلاف) therein. It clarifies that man—being a species naturally disposed toward sociality and cooperation (مفطور على الاجتماع والتعاون)—was, in his initial social state, a single community (أمة واحدة). Subsequently, there emerged within him, according to his primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — a disagreement regarding the acquisition of life's advantages. This necessitated the establishment of laws to resolve the incidental disagreements and disputes over the necessities of life. Thus, the established laws were clothed in the garment of religion and reinforced by glad tidings (التبشير) and warning (الانذار): through reward and punishment. They were further rectified by the recommended acts of worship (العبادات) through the raising of the prophets (النبيين) and the sending of the messengers (المرسلين).

Later, they disagreed regarding the knowledge of religion (معارف الدين) or matters of the Origin (المبدء) and the Return (المعاد), which disrupted the state of religious unity. Consequently, nations and factions emerged, and this disagreement was followed by discord in other matters. This second disagreement was nothing but a rebellion (بغياً) on the part of those who were given the Book, and an act of injustice and defiance by them after its principles and knowledge had become clear to them and the proof (الحجة) had been established against them.

Thus, there are two types of disagreement: a disagreement in the matter of religion, which is based on the rebellion of the transgressors rather than their primordial nature or instinct; and a disagreement in the matter of the world, which is natural (فطري) and the cause for the legislation of religion. Subsequently, Allah, the Glorified, guided the believers to the truth concerning that which was disagreed upon by His permission, and Allah guides whomsoever He wills to a straight path (صراط مستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah.

Therefore, divine religion is the sole cause for the happiness of this human species and the rectifier of its life affairs. It rectifies the primordial nature (الفطرة) by means of the primordial nature itself, balances its various powers when they transgress, and organizes for man the thread of his worldly and otherworldly life, both material and spiritual. This is a summary of the history of the life of this species (social and religious life) as provided by this noble verse. The verse has sufficed with this summary, leaving the details to what is provided by various other Quranic verses revealed concerning different matters.

(The Beginning of Human Creation)

The sum of what those verses clarify, despite their dispersion, is that the human species—and not just any human species, but this existing progeny of man—is not a species derived from another animal species or otherwise, transformed into it by a changing and evolving nature. Rather, it is a species that Allah, the Exalted, originated from the earth. There was the earth and what is upon it, and the heaven, and there was no man; then He created two mates from this species, and to them this existing progeny traces its end. The Exalted says: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَاكُم مِّن ذَكَرٍ وَأُنثَىٰ وَجَعَلْنَاكُمْ شُعُوبًا وَقَبَائِلَ لِتَعَارَفُوا — "O mankind! Indeed, We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify yourselves with one another." (49:13). And the Exalted says: هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَاحِدَةٍ وَجَعَلَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا — "It is He who created you from a single soul, and made from it its mate..." (7:189). And the Exalted says: إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسَىٰ عِندَ اللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِن تُرَابٍ — "Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust..." (3:59).

As for what natural scientists have hypothesized regarding the transformation of species—that man is derived from the ape, which is the axis of natural research today, or transformed from fish as some have conjectured—it is merely a hypothesis (فرضية). A hypothesis is not based on certain knowledge (العلم اليقيني); rather, it is posited to validate scientific explanations and accounts. Its consideration does not negate the consideration of certain truths, nor even mental possibilities, as it has no weight beyond explaining the effects and rulings related to the subject of research. We shall cover this research extensively, Allah willing, in Surah Al-Imran regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسَىٰ عِندَ اللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِن تُرَابٍ — "Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust..." (3:59).

(His Composition of Soul and Body)

Allah, the Glorified, produced this species, when He produced it, as a composite of two parts and an assembly of two substances: a bodily matter (مادة بدنية) and an immaterial substance (جوهر مجرد), which is the self (النفس) and the spirit (الروح). These two are inseparable and companions as long as worldly life lasts. Then the body dies and the living spirit departs from it, and then man returns to Allah, the Glorified. The Exalted says: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ مِن سُلَالَةٍ مِّن طِينٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَاهُ نُطْفَةً فِي قَرَارٍ مَّكِينٍ ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا النُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْمُضْغَةَ عِظَامًا فَكَسَوْنَا الْعِظَامَ لَحْمًا ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَاهُ خَلْقًا آخَرَ فَتَبَارَكَ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخَالِقِينَ ثُمَّ إِنَّكُم بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ لَمَيِّتُونَ ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ تُبْعَثُونَ — "Certainly We created man from an extract of clay. Then We made him a drop of [seminal] fluid [lodged] in a secure abode. Then We created the drop of fluid as a clinging mass. Then We created the clinging mass as a fleshy tissue. Then We created the fleshy tissue as bones. Then We clothed the bones with flesh. Then We produced him as [yet] another creature. So blessed is Allah, the best of creators! Then indeed you shall die after that. Then you shall indeed be resurrected on the Day of Resurrection." (23:12-16). (Note the position of His saying: "Then We produced him as [yet] another creature").

In this same vein is His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِي فَقَعُوا لَهُ سَاجِدِينَ — "So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit, then fall down before him in prostration." (38:72). Clearer than all is His saying, the Glorified: وَقَالُوا أَإِذَا ضَلَلْنَا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ بَلْ هُم بِلِقَاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كَافِرُونَ قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُم مَّلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ — "They say, ‘When we have been lost in the dust, shall we indeed be [created] anew?’ Indeed, they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord. Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take your souls fully, then you will be brought back to your Lord.’" (32:10-11). For He, the Exalted, answered their problem regarding the dispersion of limbs and parts and their consumption in the earth after death—rendering them unfit for resurrection—by stating that the Angel of Death takes them fully (يتوفيهم) and secures them, not leaving them behind. Thus, they are other than their bodies! Their bodies are lost in the earth, but they—meaning their souls—are neither lost, nor missing, nor consumed. The full research on what the Quran provides regarding the reality of the human spirit will come, Allah willing, in its appropriate place.

(His True Consciousness and Connection to Things)

Allah, the Glorified, created this species and deposited within it consciousness (الشعور), and composed within it hearing, sight, and the heart (الفؤاد). Thus, within him is the power of perception (الادراك) and thought (الفكر), through which he brings to presence the events manifest to him, what exists in the present, what was, and what will be, and that to which the matter of occurrence and happening will lead. Thus, he possesses a certain encompassment of all events. The Exalted says: عَلَّمَ الْإِنسَانَ مَا لَمْ يَعْلَمْ — "He taught man what he did not know." (96:5). And the Exalted says: وَاللَّهُ أَخْرَجَكُم مِّن بُطُونِ أُمَّهَاتِكُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ شَيْئًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصَارَ وَالْأَفْئِدَةَ — "Allah has brought you forth from your mothers’ bellies while you did not know anything. He made for you hearing, eyesight, and hearts..." (16:78). And the Exalted says: وَعَلَّمَ آدَمَ الْأَسْمَاءَ كُلَّهَا — "And He taught Adam the Names, all of them..." (2:31).

The Exalted has chosen for this species a mode of existence that accepts connection with everything and is capable of deriving benefit from every matter, whether through direct contact or by using it as a means to something else, making it a tool (آلة) and instrument (اداة) for benefiting from others, as we observe in his wondrous industrial ingenuity and his progression in intellectual paths. The Exalted says: هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ لَكُم مَّا فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا — "It is He who created for you all that is in the earth..." (2:29). And the Exalted says: وَسَخَّرَ لَكُم مَّا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا مِّنْهُ — "He has disposed for you[r benefit] whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth; all is from Him..." (45:13), among other verses stating that things are subjected (مسخرة) to man.

(His Practical Sciences)

These two favors—namely, the power of thought and perception, and the relationship of subjection (التسخير)—produced a third wondrous favor: that he prepares for himself sciences (علوم) and perceptions (إِدراكات) which he considers as conventions (يعتبرها اعتباراً) for entering the stage of disposing of things and the actuality of influence and action upon the existents external to him, in order to benefit from that in preserving his existence and survival.

To clarify: if you clear your mind and turn it toward man—this earthly existent who acts through thought and will—and consider yourself as if you were observing him for the first time, you would find that a single individual, in his vital actions, mediates vast and countless perceptions and thoughts. The intellect is almost bewildered by their quantity, the breadth of their scope, and the diversity of their directions. These are sciences for which the factors of acquisition, gathering, analysis, and synthesis were the external and internal senses of man, or the disposition of the intellectual power (القوة الفكرية) therein, whether as a primary disposition or a subsequent one. This is a clear matter that every person finds within himself and others, requiring nothing more than a reminder and awakening.

Then, if you repeat your gaze upon these sciences and perceptions, you will find a portion of them that is not fit to mediate between man and his voluntary actions, such as the concepts of earth, heaven, water, air, man, horse, and similar conceptions (تصورات); and such as the meanings of our saying: "four is even," "water is a fluid body," "the apple is a fruit," and other such assertions (تصديقات). These are sciences and perceptions that are realized in us from the action and passion (الفعل والانفعال) occurring between external matter and our senses and perceptual tools. Similar to them is our knowledge obtained from observing our own souls and their presence before us (that which we refer to with the word "I"), and other rational universals (الكليات). The acquisition of these sciences and perceptions does not necessitate the realization of a will or the issuance of an action; rather, they merely represent (تحكي) the external reality.

There is another portion, however, which is the opposite of the previous one, such as our saying: "there is beauty (حسن) and ugliness (قبح)," "what ought to be done," "what must be abandoned," "good must be observed," "justice is beautiful," and "injustice is ugly"; and like the concepts of leadership and subordination, and servitude and lordship. These are a series of thoughts and perceptions whose only purpose is for us to engage with and employ them. No voluntary action is completed except by mediating them and using them as a means to acquire perfection and possess the advantages of life.

Yet, they do not represent external matters fixed in reality independently of us and our understandings, as was the case in the first category. They are sciences and perceptions that do not exit the domain of action, nor are they produced in us by the influence of external factors. Rather, they are among what we have prepared and been inspired with by internal sensations (إِحساسات باطنية) that occurred in us due to the requirement of our active powers and our working apparatuses for deed and action. Our nutritive or reproductive powers, by their inclination toward action and their aversion to what does not suit them, cause the occurrence of forms of sensations: such as love and hate, longing and inclination, and desire. Then these sensational forms prompt us to formulate (اعتبار) these sciences and perceptions of the meaning of beauty and ugliness, "ought" and "ought not," "must" and "may," and so forth. Then, through their mediation between us and external matter and our action related to it, the matter is completed for us. Thus, it has become clear that we possess sciences and perceptions that have no value except for action (these are called the practical sciences (العلوم العملية)), and the full research concerning them belongs elsewhere.

Allah, the Glorified, inspired man with them to equip him for entering the stage of action and taking up the disposition of the universe, so that Allah might accomplish a matter that was to be done. The Exalted says: قَالَ رَبُّنَا الَّذِي أَعْطَىٰ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدَىٰ — "He said, ‘Our Lord is He who gave everything its creation and then guided it.’" (20:50). And the Exalted says: الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّىٰ وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدَىٰ — "Who created and proportioned, who determined and guided." (87:2-3). This is a general guidance (هداية عامة) for every created existent toward that which is the perfection of its existence, and a driving of it toward deed and action for the preservation of its existence and survival, whether it possesses consciousness or lacks it.

Regarding man specifically, the Exalted says: وَنَفْسٍ وَمَا سَوَّاهَا فَأَلْهَمَهَا فُجُورَهَا وَتَقْوَاهَا — "By the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its godwariness (تقواها)." (91:7-8). Thus, He informed that wickedness and godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah and guarding oneself against His displeasure — are known to man through a primordial inspiration (إِلهام فطري) from Him, the Exalted. They are what he ought to do or observe and what he ought not. These are the practical sciences which have no standing outside the human soul; perhaps this is what is alluded to by the attribution of wickedness and godwariness to the soul.

The Exalted says: وَمَا هَٰذِهِ الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوَانُ لَوْ كَانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ — "The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life (الحيوان), had they known." (24:64). For play (اللعب) has no reality except imagination (الخيال) alone. Likewise is the life of this world: status, wealth, precedence, delay, leadership, subordination, and so forth are merely imaginary matters (أُمور خيالية) that have no reality in the external world outside the mind of the thinker. This means that what exists in reality are only natural movements by which man disposes of matter, without any difference in that between individuals of man and their states.

Thus, what exists according to reality in "the leader man" is his humanity; as for his leadership, it is only in the imagination (الوهم). And in "the owned garment," it is the garment, for example; as for its being owned, it is an imaginary matter that does not exceed the limit of the mind. And so it follows.

(His Tendency Toward Using Others for Benefit)

This series of sciences and perceptions is what links man to action upon matter. Among these thoughts and assertions is man's conviction that he must employ (يستخدم) everything he can in the path of his perfection. In other words, his submission that he ought to benefit himself and sustain his life by whatever means possible. By this, he begins to dispose of matter and creates tools from matter with which he disposes of matter—such as using a knife for cutting, a needle for sewing, a vessel for holding liquids, and a ladder for climbing, among countless other things that are limitless in terms of composition, detail, and the types of industries and arts adopted to reach the intended goals and purposes.

By this, man also begins to dispose of plants in various ways, employing types of plants by disposing of them for food, clothing, housing, and so forth. By this, he also employs types of animals for the benefits of his life, benefiting from their meat, blood, skin, hair, wool, horns, dung, milk, offspring, and all their actions. He does not stop at other animals without also employing other individuals of his own species, the human beings. He employs them in every possible way and disposes of their existence and actions by whatever means are available to him; all of this is beyond doubt.

(His Being Social by Nature)

However, when man found other individuals of his species—who are his likes—wanting from him what he wants from them, he compromised with them and accepted that they benefit from him in proportion to what he benefits from them. This is his judgment regarding the necessity of adopting civilization (المدنية) and cooperative society (الاجتماع التعاوني). This necessitates the judgment of the necessity of stabilizing society in a way that every rightful person receives his due, and that proportions and relationships are balanced, which is social justice (العدل الاجتماعي).

Thus, this judgment—meaning his judgment for civil society and social justice—is a judgment prompted by necessity (الاضطرار). Had it not been for the aforementioned necessity, man would never have decreed it. This is the meaning of what is said: "Man is social by nature (مدني بالطبع)," and that he decrees social justice. For that is a matter generated by the aforementioned judgment of employment (الاستخدام) out of necessity, as previously explained. Therefore, whenever one man gains power over another, the judgment of cooperative society and the judgment of social justice weaken as an effect; thus, the strong does not observe it in the right of the weak. We observe what the weak nations suffer from the powerful nations, and history has proceeded upon this until this day, which is claimed to be the age of civilization and freedom.

This is what is understood from His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُ كَانَ ظَلُومًا جَهُولًا — "...Indeed he is most unjust and ignorant." (33:72), and His saying: إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ خُلِقَ هَلُوعًا — "Indeed man has been created covetous." (70:19), and His saying: إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ — "...Indeed man is most unjust and ungrateful." (14:34), and His saying: كَلَّا إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ لَيَطْغَىٰ أَن رَّآهُ اسْتَغْنَىٰ — "Indeed man becomes rebellious when he considers himself self-sufficient." (96:6-7).

If social justice were something required by man's nature as a primary requirement, then justice, the goodness of sharing efforts, and the observance of equality would be dominant in the affairs of societies. However, what is always witnessed is the opposite: the exercise of power and dominance, the strong and mighty imposing his demands on the weak, and the victor exploiting the vanquished and enslaving him in the path of his purposes and greeds.

(The Occurrence of Disagreement Among Human Individuals)

From here it is known that the instinct of employment (قريحة الاستخدام) in man, when joined with the necessary difference between individuals in terms of physical constitution, environment, habits, and morals based on that—and the production of necessary difference in terms of strength and weakness—leads to disagreement (الاختلاف) and deviation from what a sound society requires of social justice. Thus, the strong benefits from the weak more than he benefits him, and the victor profits from the vanquished without profiting him. The weak and vanquished counters this, as long as he remains weak and vanquished, with ruse, plot, and deception. When he becomes strong and victorious, he counters his oppressor with the severest vengeance. Thus, the emergence of disagreement leads to chaos and disorder, and calls for the destruction of humanity, the annihilation of the primordial nature, and the nullification of happiness.

To this, the Exalted alludes by His saying: وَمَا كَانَ النَّاسُ إِلَّا أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَاخْتَلَفُوا — "Mankind were but a single community; then they differed..." (10:19), and His saying: وَلَا يَزَالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ رَبُّكَ وَلِذَٰلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ — "...and they will not cease to differ, except those on whom your Lord has mercy—and for that He created them..." (11:118-119), and His saying in the verse under discussion: لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ — "...that He might judge between the people concerning that about which they differed."

This disagreement, as you have known, is necessary to occur among the individuals of human society due to the difference in constitution by the difference of materials, even if all are human according to the single human form (الصورة الانسانية). Unity in form requires unity in terms of thoughts and actions in one respect, while the difference of materials leads to the difference of sensations, perceptions, and states, even while they are united in another way. Their difference leads to the difference of goals, purposes, and hopes, and their difference leads to the difference of actions, which is what leads to the disruption of the social order.

The emergence of this disagreement is what necessitated legislation (التشريع), which is the establishment of universal laws (قوانين كلية) whose implementation necessitates the removal of disagreement and the attainment of every rightful person's due, and imposing them upon the people.

The path taken today to impose laws that rectify human society is one of two paths:

First: Compelling society to obey laws established to share the right to life among people and equalize them in rights, meaning that each individual attains what befits him of life's perfection, while discarding religious knowledge: such as monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and virtuous ethics. This is done by making monotheism discarded, neither looked upon nor observed, and making ethics subordinate to society and its transformation. Whatever ethics agree with the state of society are considered virtuous ethics: one day chastity, another day licentiousness; one day truthfulness, another day lying; one day honesty, another day betrayal, and so on.

Second: Compelling society to obey laws by cultivating the ethics that suit them and respecting them, while discarding religious knowledge in social cultivation.

These are two paths followed to remove social disagreements and unify the community assembled from man: one by coercive force and the dominating power of man alone, and the second by force and ethical cultivation. However, despite the corruptions that follow them, they are built on a foundation of ignorance. Therein is the ruin of this species and the destruction of human reality. For this man is an existent created by Allah, his existence linked to his Maker; he began from Him and will return to Him. He has a lasting life after departing from this worldly realm, a life of long duration, whose term is not cut off. It is arranged upon this worldly life and the manner of man's conduct therein, and his acquisition of states and traits suitable for monotheism, which is his being a servant of Allah, the Glorified, beginning from Him and returning to Him. If man builds his life in this world upon the forgetting of his monotheism and the veiling of the reality of the matter, he has destroyed himself and annihilated his reality.

The likeness of people in following these two paths is like a caravan that set out on a path to a distant land, having with it sufficient provisions and travel necessities. Then it stopped at one of the stations during the journey, and it was not long before they began to differ: through killing, hitting, violating honor, taking wealth, usurping places, and so forth. Then they gathered to consult among themselves on adopting a method to preserve themselves and their wealth.

One of them said: "You must share in the benefit of these goods and belongings, and enjoy them according to each person's social weight. There is nothing but this station, and whoever deviates from that shall be taken by force and policy."

Another said: "You ought to establish the law that rectifies this disagreement on the basis of the existing personalities with which you came from your land from which you departed. Thus, each shall be disciplined by the ethical personality he possesses, and shall take up mercy for his companions, and affection, magnanimity, and virtue. Then, along with that, you shall share in the benefit of these existing belongings, for they are only for you and for this station of yours."

Both speakers have erred, and have been heedless of the fact that the entire caravan is on the wing of travel. It is mandatory for the traveler to observe in all his states the condition of his homeland and the condition of the goal of his journey which he intends. If he forgets any of that, he will be met with nothing but misguidance, error, and destruction.

The correct speaker among them is the one who says: "Enjoy these belongings according to what suffices you for this night, and take from that provision for what is before you of the path, and what is required of you in your homeland, and what you intend for your destination."

(Removing Disagreement Through Religion)

Therefore, Allah, the Glorified, legislated what He legislated of laws and statutes, placing that on the foundation of monotheism, belief, ethics, and actions. In other words, the establishment of legislation is built on the foundation of teaching people and making them know the reality of their affair from their Origin to their Return, and that they must follow in this world a life that benefits them tomorrow, and work in the present for what they will live by in the future. Thus, religious legislation and divine lawmaking is that which is built on knowledge (العلم) alone, without anything else. The Exalted says: إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ أَمَرَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ ذَٰلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ — "...Sovereignty belongs only to Allah. He has commanded you to worship none except Him. That is the upright religion, but most people do not know." (12:40). And the Exalted says in this verse under discussion: فَبَعَثَ اللَّهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ وَأَنزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتَابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ — "...So Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed..." Thus, He paired the raising of the prophets with glad tidings and warning with the sending down of the Book containing the rulings and laws that remove their disagreement.

From this category is His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالُوا مَا هِيَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا الدُّنْيَا نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيَا وَمَا يُهْلِكُنَا إِلَّا الدَّهْرُ وَمَا لَهُم بِذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَظُنُّونَ — "They say, ‘There is nothing but the life of this world: we live and we die, and nothing consumes us but time.’ They have no knowledge of that; they only conjecture." (45:24). For they only insisted on that saying of theirs not merely to reject the saying regarding the Return, but because the saying regarding the Return and the call to it entailed applying worldly life to life in the manner of servitude and obedience to religious laws containing legislative articles and rulings: of worship, transactions, and policies.

In short, the saying regarding the Return necessitated practicing religion and following its rulings in life, and monitoring the Resurrection and the Return in all states and actions. So they rejected that by building social life on merely the worldly life without regard for what is beyond it.

Likewise is His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الظَّنَّ لَا يُغْنِي مِنَ الْحَقِّ شَيْئًا فَأَعْرِضْ عَن مَّن تَوَلَّىٰ عَن ذِكْرِنَا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحَيَاةَ الدُّنْيَا ذَٰلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُم مِّنَ الْعِلْمِ — "...Indeed conjecture is no substitute for the truth. So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge..." (53:28-30). Thus, the Exalted clarified that they build life on conjecture and ignorance, while Allah, the Glorified, calls to the Abode of Peace and builds His religion on truth and knowledge. The Messenger calls people to that which gives them life. The Exalted says: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذَا دَعَاكُمْ لِمَا يُحْيِيكُمْ — "O you who have faith! Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life..." (8:24). This life is that which is alluded to by His saying, the Exalted: أَوَمَن كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَاهُ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُ نُورًا يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَن مَّثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُمَاتِ لَيْسَ بِخَارِجٍ مِّنْهَا — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people, like one who is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?..." (6:122). And the Exalted says: أَفَمَن يَعْلَمُ أَنَّمَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ الْحَقُّ كَمَنْ هُوَ أَعْمَىٰ إِنَّمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ — "Is he who knows that what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth like one who is blind? Only those who possess intellect take admonition." (13:19). And the Exalted says: قُلْ هَٰذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُو إِلَى اللَّهِ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي وَسُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ وَمَا أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ — "Say, ‘This is my way. I summon to Allah with insight—I and he who follows me. Immaculate is Allah, and I am not one of the polytheists.’" (12:108). And the Exalted says: قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُونَ وَالَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ إِنَّمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ — "...Say, ‘Are those who know equal to those who do not know?’ Only those who possess intellect take admonition." (39:9). And the Exalted says: وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ — "...and teach them the Book and wisdom and purify them..." (2:129), among others. The Quran is replete with praise for knowledge, the call to it, and the urging toward it. It suffices you that it names the era prior to the emergence of Islam as the "Age of Ignorance" (الجاهلية), as has been said.

How far from fairness is the saying of one who says: "Religion is built on imitation (التقليد) and ignorance, and is contrary to science and defiant of it." These speakers are people who occupied themselves with natural and social sciences and did not find therein anything that proves anything beyond nature; so they thought the lack of proof was a proof of non-existence. They have erred in their thought and stumbled in their judgment. Then they looked at what was in the hands of their likes among the whimsical people of matters they call by the name of religion, which have no reality other than polytheism (الشرك), and Allah is clear of the polytheists, as is His Messenger. Then they looked at the religious call to devotion and obedience and reckoned it as imitation (تقليد). They have erred in their reckoning, for religion is too sublime in status to call to ignorance and imitation, and too fortified in its side to guide to an action without knowledge, or to direct to a saying without guidance or a luminous book. And who is more unjust than he who fabricates a lie against Allah or denies the truth when it has come to him?

(Disagreement Within Religion Itself)

In short, He, the Exalted, informs us that disagreement in livelihood and life affairs was removed, first and foremost, by religion. If there are non-religious laws, they are taken by imitation from religion.

Then He, the Exalted, informs us that disagreement arose among the species within religion itself, and it was only originated by the carriers of religion from among those who were given the Clear Book: from the scholars of the Book of Allah, out of rebellion (بغياً) among them, and injustice and defiance, after knowledge had come to them. The Exalted says: شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ الدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِ نُوحًا وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ — "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus, declaring, ‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it.’..." until He says: وَمَا تَفَرَّقُوا إِلَّا مِن بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْيًا بَيْنَهُمْ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ إِلَىٰ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى لَّقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ — "They did not divide [into sects] except after the knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves; and had it not been for a prior decree from your Lord [extending their respite] until a specified time, the matter would have been decided between them..." (42:13-14). And the Exalted says: وَمَا كَانَ النَّاسُ إِلَّا أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَاخْتَلَفُوا وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ فِيمَا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ — "Mankind were but a single community; then they differed; and had it not been for a prior decree from your Lord, the matter would have been decided between them concerning that about which they differ." (10:19). The "decree" (الكلمة) alluded to in the two verses is His saying, the Exalted: وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَاعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ — "...There will be for you on the earth an abode and a provision for a specified time." (7:24).

Thus, disagreement in religion is based on rebellion (البغي) rather than the primordial nature (الفطرة). For religion is natural, and that which is such does not lead the creation astray, nor does its ruling change, as the Exalted says: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللَّهِ ذَٰلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ — "So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature (فطرة) of Allah according to which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion..." (30:30). These are the summaries upon which the discourse in this noble verse is built.

(Man After the World)

Furthermore, He informs us that man will depart from the world in which his social life exists and will descend into another abode which He named the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — then another abode which He named the Hereafter (الآخرة). However, his life after this world is an individual life (حياة انفرادية). The meaning of life being individual is that it is not linked to cooperative society, sharing, and mutual support. Rather, the sovereignty there in all rulings of life belongs to the existence of his own self; the existence of others does not affect him through cooperation and mutual support at all. If that natural system witnessed in matter were present there, there would be no escape from the governance of cooperation and sharing. But man has left it behind his back and turned toward his Lord. All his practical sciences have been nullified for him; he no longer sees the necessity of employment, disposition, civilization, cooperative society, or any other rulings he used to decree in the world. He has nothing but the companionship of his deeds and the result of his good and evil actions. Nothing appears to him except the reality of the matter, and the Great News (النبأ العظيم) about which they differed becomes manifest to him. The Exalted says: وَنَرِثُهُ مَا يَقُولُ وَيَأْتِينَا فَرْدًا — "We shall inherit from him what he says, and he will come to Us alone." (19:80). And the Exalted says: وَلَقَدْ جِئْتُمُونَا فُرَادَىٰ كَمَا خَلَقْنَاكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَتَرَكْتُم مَّا خَوَّلْنَاكُمْ وَرَاءَ ظُهُورِكُمْ وَمَا نَرَىٰ مَعَكُمْ شُفَعَاءَكُمُ الَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ أَنَّهُمْ فِيكُمْ شُرَكَاءُ لَقَد تَّقَطَّعَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنكُم مَّا كُنتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ — "‘Certainly you have come to Us alone, just as We created you the first time, and you have left behind your backs what We had conferred on you. We do not see your intercessors with you—those whom you claimed to be [Our] partners in [deciding] your [fate]. Certainly [the link] between you has been cut, and what you used to claim has forsaken you.’" (6:94). And the Exalted says: هُنَالِكَ تَبْلُو كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا أَسْلَفَتْ وَرُدُّوا إِلَى اللَّهِ مَوْلَاهُمُ الْحَقِّ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ — "There every soul will examine what it has sent ahead, and they will be returned to Allah, their real Master, and what they used to fabricate will forsake them." (10:30). And the Exalted says: مَا لَكُمْ لَا تَنَاصَرُونَ بَلْ هُمُ الْيَوْمَ مُسْتَسْلِمُونَ — "‘What is the matter with you that you do not help one another?’ Indeed, today they are submissive." (37:25-26). And the Exalted says: يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ وَالسَّمَاوَاتُ وَبَرَزُوا لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ — "The day when the earth is transformed into another earth and the heavens [as well], and they emerge before Allah, the One, the All-paramount." (14:48). And the Exalted says: وَأَن لَّيْسَ لِلْإِنسَانِ إِلَّا مَا سَعَىٰ وَأَنَّ سَعْيَهُ سَوْفَ يُرَىٰ ثُمَّ يُجْزَاهُ الْجَزَاءَ الْأَوْفَىٰ — "and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for, and that he will soon be shown his endeavour, then he will be rewarded for it with the fullest reward." (53:39-41), among other verses. These verses, as you see, indicate that man changes the mode of his life after death; he does not live a social life built on cooperation and mutual support, nor does he employ what he originated in this life of practical sciences. He reaps nothing but the fruit of his deed and the result of his striving, which appears to him clearly, and he is rewarded for it.

His saying, the Exalted: كان الناس أمة واحدة (Mankind were but a single community). "The people" (الناس) is well-known, referring to the assembled individuals of man. "The community" (الامة) is the group of people. It is sometimes applied to a single person, as in His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ إِبْرَاهِيمَ كَانَ أُمَّةً قَانِتًا لِّلَّهِ — "Indeed Abraham was a nation (أمة) obedient to Allah..." (16:120). It is sometimes applied to a significant period of time, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَادَّكَرَ بَعْدَ أُمَّةٍ — "...and remembered after a time (أمة)..." (12:45), meaning after years; and His saying: وَلَئِنْ أَخَّرْنَا عَنْهُمُ الْعَذَابَ إِلَىٰ أُمَّةٍ مَّعْدُودَةٍ — "And if We defer their punishment until a reckoned time (أمة)..." (11:8). It is sometimes applied to the creed (الملة) and religion, as some have said regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ هَٰذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاتَّقُونِ — "Indeed this community (أمة) of yours is one community, and I am your Lord, so be wary of Me." (23:52), and in His saying: إِنَّ هَٰذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُونِ — "Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord, so worship Me." (21:92). The root of the word is from amma ya'ummu (أم يأم) when one intends (قصد); thus it was applied to the group, but not to every group, rather to a group that possessed a single purpose and a single desire which was the bond of unity between them. This is what justifies its application to the individual and to its other meanings when applied.

In any case, the apparent meaning of the verse indicates that this species passed through a time in their life when they were in unity and agreement, and in simplicity and naivety, with no disagreement among them through dispute and defense in life affairs, nor disagreement in doctrines and opinions. The proof for the negation of disagreement is His saying, the Exalted: فَبَعَثَ اللَّهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ وَأَنزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتَابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ — "...So Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed..." For He arranged the raising of the prophets and the judgment of the Book in the case of disagreement upon their being a single community. Thus, disagreement in life affairs arose after unity and oneness. The proof for the negation of the second disagreement is His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ بَغْيًا بَيْنَهُم — "...and none differed about it except those who had been given it, after the manifest proofs had come to them, out of envy among themselves..." Thus, disagreement in religion only arose from the carriers of the Book after its revelation, out of rebellion.

This is what rational consideration supports. For we observe the human species constantly rising in knowledge and thought, and advancing in the path of recognition and culture, year after year and generation after generation. By this, the pillars of its society are strengthened day after day, and it stands to remove the subtleties of need, resist the interferences of nature, and benefit from life's advantages. Whenever we go back in time, we find it less cognizant of life's symbols and nature's secrets. This progression leads us to the primitive man who possessed only a small amount of knowledge regarding life's affairs and the limits of living, as if they had nothing but self-evident truths and a few intellectual theories that prepared for them the means of survival in the simplest way, such as feeding on plants or some hunting, taking shelter in caves, and defending with stones and wood, and so forth. This was the state of man in his most ancient eras. It is known that a people whose state is such would not manifest disagreement in a significant way, nor would corruption appear in them in an influential manner, like a flock of sheep whose individuals have no concern but to be guided to some of what others have been guided to, and to gather in dwelling, pasture, and watering place.

However, because of the instinct of employment (قريحة الاستخدام) in man, as we alluded to previously, this forced sociality—in terms of cooperation to fulfill each other's needs—does not prevent him from disagreement, contention, and dominance. Every day he increases in knowledge and power regarding the ways of benefiting, becomes aware of new advantages, and awakens to subtle methods of profit. Among them are the strong, the powerful, and the possessors of might, and among them are the weak and those in their rank. This is the origin of the emergence of disagreement—the natural disagreement (الاختلاف الفطري) prompted by the instinct of employment, just as this very instinct prompted sociality and civilization.

There is no harm in the conflict of two natural judgments if there is a third above them that judges between them, balances their affair, and rectifies their condition. This is like man, whose powers race in their actions, leading to conflict; just as the nutritive attraction requires eating what the digestive system cannot bear to digest nor the stomach accommodate, yet there is an intellect (عقل) that balances between them, decrees for each what suits it, and determines the action of each of these active powers so that it does not interfere with the other in its action.

The contradiction between two natural judgments in our subject is of this kind. The progression of man's nature toward civilization and then its progression toward disagreement lead to contradiction. But Allah removes the contradiction by removing the existing disagreement through the raising of the prophets with glad tidings and warning, and the sending down of the Book that judges between people concerning that about which they differed.

By this explanation, the invalidity of what some have mentioned becomes clear: that the meaning of the verse is that people were a single community upon guidance (الهداية), because disagreement only appeared after the revelation of the Book out of rebellion among them, and the rebellion was from the carriers of the Book. This speaker has overlooked that the verse establishes two disagreements, not one, as previously explained; and that if people were upon guidance, it would be one without disagreement. So what would be the cause—rather, what would be the justification—for raising the prophets, sending down the Book, and leading them to rebellion through disagreement, spreading corruption, and stirring the instincts of disbelief, wickedness, and destructive morals while they were latent?

It also clarifies the invalidity of what others mentioned: that the meaning is that people were a single community upon misguidance (الضلالة), for without it there would be no reason for the sequence of His saying: "So Allah sent the prophets..." This speaker has overlooked that Allah, the Glorified, mentions that this misguidance he referred to—which is what He alluded to by His saying, the Glorified: فَهَدَى اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لِمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ مِنَ الْحَقِّ بِإِذْنِهِ — "...Then Allah guided those who had faith to the truth of what they differed about, by His leave..."—only arose from the evil inner state of the carriers of the Book and the scholars of religion after the revelation of the Book and the clarification of its verses to the people. If they were upon misguidance before the raising and revelation—which is the misguidance of disbelief, hypocrisy, wickedness, and sins—what would justify attributing that to the carriers of the Book and the scholars of religion?

It also clarifies what is in the saying of others: that the "people" (الناس) intended are the Children of Israel, since Allah mentions that they differed regarding the Book out of rebellion among them. The Exalted says: فَمَا اخْتَلَفُوا إِلَّا مِن بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْيًا بَيْنَهُمْ — "...and they did not differ except after the knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves..." (45:17). This is an interpretation without proof; the mere description of a people with a quality does not necessitate its restriction to them.

Even more invalid is the saying of one who said: the "people" intended in the verse is Adam (peace be upon him), and the meaning is that Adam (peace be upon him) was a single community upon guidance, then his progeny differed, so Allah raised the prophets... The verse in its entirety does not match this saying, neither in whole nor in part.

It also clarifies the invalidity of the saying of some: that "was" (كان) in the verse is stripped of its indication of time, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَكَانَ اللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا — "...and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise." (48:7); thus it indicates permanence. The meaning would be: people are a single community in terms of being social by nature (مدنيين طبعاً). For man is social by nature; the life of a single individual cannot be completed alone due to the abundance of his existential needs and the breadth of the circle of his life's necessities, such that perfection is not attained for him except by society and cooperation among individuals and the exchange of efforts. Thus, each takes from the results of his work what he deserves of this result and gives the rest to others, taking in exchange the remainder of what he needs and deserves in his existence. This is the state of man; he is never independent of society and cooperation at any time. This is indicated by what has reached us of the history of this social civil species, and his being social and civil has never ceased; thus it is the requirement of his nature and creation. However, that leads to disagreement and the disruption of the social order. So Allah, the Glorified, by His profound care, legislated laws that remove this disagreement and conveyed them to them by raising the prophets as bearers of good news and warners, and sending down the Book that judges with them to judge in cases of disagreement.

The sum of the meaning would be that people are a single community, social by nature, having no independence from society, which necessitates disagreement; therefore, Allah raised the prophets and sent down the Book.

Against this, it is objected firstly: that he took being social by nature as a primary nature for man, and society and sharing in life as an essential necessity for this species. You have known previously that the matter is not so, but rather a compromise out of necessity, and that the Quran also indicates the opposite.

Secondly: that the derivation of raising the prophets and sending down the Books from merely man being social by nature is not sound except after restricting this being social by nature to its leading to disagreement and the emergence of corruption. Thus, the discourse would require an implicit estimation (التقدير), which is contrary to the apparent meaning, and the speaker, moreover, does not accept the estimation of disagreement in the discourse.

Thirdly: that it is built on taking the disagreement mentioned and addressed by the verse as a single disagreement. Yet the verse is like an explicit text in being two disagreements, as it says: "and He sent down with them the Book... that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed." This is a disagreement prior to the Book, and those who differed with this disagreement are "the people." Then it says: "and none differed about it"—meaning about the Book—"except those who had been given it"—meaning those who knew the Book and carried it—"out of envy among themselves." This disagreement is subsequent to the Book and later than its revelation, and those who differed with this disagreement are the scholars of the Book and its carriers, rather than all people. Thus, one of the two disagreements is other than the other: one is a disagreement out of rebellion and knowledge, and the other is contrary to it.

His saying, the Exalted: فبعث الله النبيين مبشرين ومنذرين (So Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners...). The Exalted used "raising" (البعث) rather than "sending" (الارسال) and its like because this reported unity of the state of the primitive man is a state of dormancy and silence, which suits "raising," which is the standing up from sleep or dwelling and the like. This subtle point is perhaps what necessitated referring to these raised ones as "prophets" (النبيين) rather than "messengers" (المرسلين) or "envoys" (الرسل). Furthermore, raising and sending down the Book, as previously explained, have as their reality the clarification of the truth to people, awakening them to the reality of their existence and life, and informing them that they are created for their Lord—who is Allah, besides whom there is no god—and that they are travelers striving toward Allah, to be resurrected for a Great Day, standing in one of the stations of the journey which has no reality except play and delusion. Thus, they must observe that in this life and its actions, and keep before their eyes: from where, in where, to where. This meaning is more suitable to the word "prophet" (النبي), whose meaning is: one with whom the news (النبأ) has settled, rather than "messenger" (الرسول). Therefore, He used "prophets." In attributing the raising of the prophets to Allah, the Glorified, there is an indication of the infallibility (عصمة) of the prophets in their receiving of the revelation and their conveying of the message to the people; more clarification of this will come at the end of the commentary. As for glad tidings and warning—meaning the promise of Allah's mercy from His pleasure and Paradise for those who believe and are god-conscious, and the threat of Allah's punishment from His wrath and the Fire for those who deny and disobey—they are the levels of the call most relevant to the state of the average person, even if some of the righteous among His servants and His friends (أولياء) do not link their souls to anything other than their Lord, whether reward or punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: وأنزل معهم الكتاب ليحكم بين الناس فيما اختلفوا فيه (and He sent down with them the Book... that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed). "The Book" (الكتاب) is a noun meaning "that which is written." Although the common usage of the Book implies writing with a pen, since covenants and mandatory decrees are mostly solidified by writing, its usage became widespread for every mandatory ruling that must be followed, or every clarification—rather, every meaning that does not accept revocation in its solidification. Its usage in this sense is frequent in the Quran. In this sense, the Quran is called a "Book," and it is the speech of Allah. The Exalted says: كِتَابٌ أَنزَلْنَاهُ إِلَيْكَ مُبَارَكٌ — "[This is] a blessed Book that We have sent down to you..." (34:29). And the Exalted says: إِنَّ الصَّلَاةَ كَانَتْ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ كِتَابًا مَّوقُوتًا — "...Indeed prayer is a timed prescription (كتاباً) for the faithful." (4:103). In His saying: "concerning that about which they differed," there is an indication that the meaning is: "Mankind were but a single community; then they differed; so Allah sent..." as previously mentioned.

The definite article (اللام) in "the Book" (الكتاب) either denotes the genus (للجنس) or refers to a specific mental referent (للعهد الذهني). In the latter case, it refers to the Book of Noah (peace be upon him), based on His saying, the Exalted: شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ الدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِ نُوحًا وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ — "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus" (42:13). This verse is in the context of conferring a favor and clarifying that the law (الشريعة) revealed to this community (الأمة) encompasses the various elements of all previous laws revealed to the past prophets, along with what was specifically revealed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Thus, the law is specifically associated with these great prophets: Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

Since His saying, the Exalted: "and He sent down with them the Book that it might judge between the people concerning that about which they differed," indicates that the law (الشرع) was only established through the Book, the two verses combined indicate:

First: That Noah (peace be upon him) had a Book containing a law, and that it is what is intended by His saying, the Exalted: "and He sent down with them the Book that it might judge between the people concerning that about which they differed," whether it refers to it alone or along with other books, depending on whether the definite article is for a specific referent or the genus.

Second: That the Book of Noah was the first heavenly Book containing a law. Had there been a book before it, there would have been a governing law before it, and Allah, the Exalted, would have mentioned it in His saying: "He has prescribed for you..."

Third: That this period of conflict to which the Exalted refers in His saying: "Mankind were a single community..." occurred before the mission of Noah (peace be upon him), and his Book (peace be upon him) judged therein.

His saying, the Exalted: "And none differed about it except those who were given it, out of envy among themselves." It has already been mentioned that what is intended here is the difference occurring within the religion itself by its own bearers. Since religion is from the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا — "So set your heart on the religion as a hanif, with the nature of Allah which He has fashioned mankind on" (30:30), Allah, the Glorified, attributed the difference occurring within it to envy (البغي).

In His saying, the Exalted: "except those who were given it," there is an indication that the sentence is intended to point to the origin of the emergence of religious difference regarding the Book. It does not mean that everyone who deviates from the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — or follows a religion other than the true religion is necessarily an envious wrongdoer (باغياً), even if they are astray from the right path. For Allah, the Glorified, does not excuse the envious wrongdoer (البغي), yet He has excused those for whom the matter was confused, who found no way out and were not guided to a path. The Exalted said: إِنَّمَا السَّبِيلُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَظْلِمُونَ النَّاسَ وَيَبْغُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ أُولَٰئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ — "The ground for complaint is only against those who wrong the people and cause disruption in the earth unjustly; for such there is a painful punishment" (42:42). And the Exalted said: وَآخَرُونَ اعْتَرَفُوا بِذُنُوبِهِمْ خَلَطُوا عَمَلًا صَالِحًا وَآخَرَ سَيِّئًا عَسَى اللَّهُ إِن يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ — "And [there are] others who have confessed to their sins: they have mixed a righteous deed with another that was evil. Maybe Allah will accept their repentance. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful" (9:102), until He said: وَآخَرُونَ مُرْجَوْنَ لِأَمْرِ اللَّهِ إِمَّا يُعَذِّبُهُمْ وَإِمَّا يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ — "And [there are] others waiting for Allah’s decree: either He shall punish them, or He shall accept their repentance, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise" (9:106). And the Exalted said: إِلَّا الْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ الرِّجَالِ وَالنِّسَاءِ وَالْوِلْدَانِ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ حِيلَةً وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ سَبِيلًا فَأُولَٰئِكَ عَسَى اللَّهُ أَنْ يَعْفُوَ عَنْهُمْ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ عَفُوًّا غَفُورًا — "Except the oppressed among men, women and children, who have neither access to any means nor are guided to any way. Maybe Allah will excuse them, for Allah is all-excusing, all-forgiving" (4:98-99).

Furthermore, the primordial nature (الفطرة) does not preclude heedlessness (الغفلة) or doubt (الشبهة), but it does preclude intentionality (التعمد) and envy (البغي). For this reason, envy was specifically attributed to the scholars and those to whom the divine signs (الآيات الإلهية) had become clear. The Exalted said: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا أُولَٰئِكَ أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيهَا خَالِدُونَ — "And those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]" (2:39). The verses with this meaning are numerous, and in all of them, disbelief (الكفر) is conditioned upon the denial of Allah's signs, followed by the threat of punishment. In short, the meaning of the verse is that this difference traces back to the envy of the bearers of the Book after knowledge had come to them.

His saying, the Exalted: "Then Allah guided those who believed to the truth of what they differed about." This is an explanation of that which was differed upon, which is the truth (الحق) that the Book was revealed with, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: "and He sent down with them the Book with the truth." At that point, divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية) addressed both differences: the difference regarding the affairs of life, and the difference regarding the truth and divine knowledge (المعارف الإلهية), the primary cause of which was the envy of the bearers of the Book. In conditioning guidance with His saying, the Exalted: "by His leave" (بإِذنه), there is an indication that Allah's guidance of these believers was not a compulsion (إِلزاماً) from them, nor an obligation upon Allah to guide them because of their faith. For no judge rules over Allah, the Glorified, nor does any obligator impose upon Him, except what He has imposed upon Himself. Rather, the guidance was by His leave, and had He willed, He would not have given leave nor guided. Accordingly, His saying, the Exalted: "And Allah guides whomever He wishes to a straight path," serves as the rationale (التعليل) for His saying "by His leave." The meaning is that Allah only guided them by His leave because He has the right to guide them and is not forced or obligated to guide anyone; rather, He guides whomever He wishes, and He has wished to guide those who believe to a straight path (صراط مستقيم).

From the verse, several points become clear:

First: The definition and identifier of religion (الدين). It is a mode of conduct in the life of this world that encompasses the well-being of the world in accordance with otherworldly perfection (الكمال الاخروي) and the true, eternal life with Allah, the Glorified. Therefore, the law (الشريعة) must contain regulations that address the state of worldly livelihood (المعاش) to the extent of necessity.

Second: That religion first appeared to resolve the differences arising from the primordial nature (الفطرة), then it reached completion by resolving both natural and non-natural differences together.

Third: That religion continues to reach completion until its laws encompass all aspects of need in life. Once it encompasses them, it is sealed with a finality (ختماً), and there is no religion after it. Conversely, if a religion is the final one, it must be comprehensive in resolving all aspects of need. The Exalted said: مَّا كَانَ مُحَمَّدٌ أَبَا أَحَدٍ مِّن رِّجَالِكُمْ وَلَٰكِن رَّسُولَ اللَّهِ وَخَاتَمَ النَّبِيِّينَ — "Muhammad is not the father of any of your men, but he is the Apostle of Allah and the Seal of the Prophets" (33:40). And the Exalted said: وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ تِبْيَانًا لِّكُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "and We have sent down the Book to you as an exposition of all things" (16:89). And the Exalted said: وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتَابٌ عَزِيزٌ لَّا يَأْتِيهِ الْبَاطِلُ مِن بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلَا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ — "and indeed it is an august Book: falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it" (41:41-42).

Fourth: That every subsequent law (شريعة) is more complete than its predecessor.

Fifth: The reason for sending prophets and revealing books—or in other words, the cause (العلة) of the religious call. It is that man, by his nature and primordial disposition (الفطرة), moves toward difference just as he moves toward civil society (الاجتماع المدني). Since the primordial nature is what leads to difference, it cannot resolve that difference; for how can a thing repel what its own self attracts it toward? Thus, Allah, the Glorified, resolved this difference through prophethood (النبوة) and legislation (التشريع) by guiding the species toward the perfection (الكمال) appropriate to their state and corrective of their affairs. This perfection is a true perfection within the realm of creation and existence (الصنع والإيجاد), and so its prerequisites are likewise. The Exalted said: الَّذِي أَعْطَىٰ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدَىٰ — "He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (20:50). He clarified that it is of His station and command to guide everything to that which completes its creation. The completion of man's creation is that he be guided to the perfection of his existence in this world and the Hereafter. The Exalted also said: كُلًّا نُّمِدُّ هَٰؤُلَاءِ وَهَٰؤُلَاءِ مِنْ عَطَاءِ رَبِّكَ وَمَا كَانَ عَطَاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُورًا — "To these and to those—to all We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not confined" (17:20). This verse signifies that His station is to provide aid through giving (الامداد بالعطاء); He aids everyone who needs His support in the path of their life and existence, giving them what they deserve. Because His gift is neither restricted nor prohibited from His side, unless a refuser refuses it due to the misfortune of his own soul, coming from himself and not from Allah.

It is well known that man is unable to rectify this deficiency by himself, for his primordial nature is what leads to this deficiency; so how could he be capable of rectifying it and leveling the path of happiness and perfection in his social life?

If human nature is what leads to this difference that hinders man from reaching his worthy perfection, and it is incapable of redressing what it led to or reforming what it corrupted, then the reform (if it is to occur) must come from a source other than nature. This is the divine source, which is prophethood through revelation (الوحي). Therefore, the Exalted expressed the prophets' undertaking of this reform and the resolution of difference as "sending" (البعث), and in the entire Quran, He attributed it only to Himself, despite the fact that the prophets' undertaking, like all other matters, has connections to matter through temporal (زمانية) and spatial (مكانية) links.

Thus, prophethood is a divine state (or if you wish, say a state of the Unseen (غيبية)) whose relation to this general state of perception and action is like the relation of wakefulness to sleep. Through it, man perceives the knowledge by which difference and contradiction in human life are resolved. This perception and reception from the Unseen is what is called revelation (الوحي) in the language of the Quran, and the state that man adopts from it for himself is called prophethood (النبوة).

From this, it appears that this—namely, the primordial nature leading to civil society on one hand and to difference on the other, and His care for guiding toward the completion of creation—is the principle of the argument for the existence of prophethood, or in other words, the proof for general prophethood (النبوة العامة).

Its formulation: The human species is exploitative (مستخدم) by nature, and this natural exploitation leads him to civil society and to difference and corruption in all affairs of his life, which creation and existence (التكوين والايجاد) necessitate resolving. It cannot be resolved except through laws that reform social life by removing difference from it and guiding man to his perfection and happiness through one of two things: either through his primordial nature or through something beyond it. However, the primordial nature is insufficient, as it is what leads to the difference, so how could it resolve it? Therefore, it must be through guidance from a path other than the primordial nature and nature (الطبيعة), which is the non-natural divine instruction called prophethood and revelation. This argument is composed of premises explicitly stated in the Book of Allah, as you have seen previously. Each of these premises is empirical (تجربية), demonstrated by experience to man in the history of his life and his diverse societies that emerged and perished over the course of past accumulated centuries, back to the oldest ages of human life mentioned by history.

For man has never, at any point in his life, abandoned the rule of exploitation; nor has his exploitation failed to lead to society and instead dictated an individual life; nor has his constitutive society been free from difference; nor has difference been resolved without social laws; nor has his primordial nature and his intellect—which he considers a sound intellect—been able to establish laws that cut off the roots of difference and uproot the matter of corruption. Sufficient for you in this regard is what you witness of the course of social events, and what is before your eyes of the degradation of morals, the corruption of the human world, the wars destructive to crops and progeny, the massacres annihilating millions upon millions of people, and the reign of tyranny and the influence of enslavement over human souls, honors, and wealth in this century called the age of civilization, progress, culture, and science. So what would you think of the past centuries, the ages of ignorance and darkness?

As for the fact that creation and existence (الصنع والايجاد) drive every existent toward its appropriate perfection, this is a matter that applies to every existent according to experience and research. Likewise, the fact that if creation and constitution (التكوين) necessitate an effect, they do not necessitate its exact opposite, is an accepted matter established by experience and research. As for the fact that religious education and training originating from the source of prophethood and revelation are capable of repelling this difference and corruption, it is a matter confirmed by both research and experience. As for research: because religion calls to the truths of knowledge, the virtues of character, and the beauty of actions, the reform of the human world is presupposed within it. As for experience: Islam proved this in the brief period when religion was the ruler over the society of Muslims, and it proved it through the training of individuals whose souls were reformed and who reformed the souls of other people. Furthermore, the aspects of perfection and the pulsing veins in the structure of civil society today that guarantee the life of civilization and progress are indebted to Islamic advancement and its spread in the world, as analysis and decomposition undoubtedly show. We shall complete the discussion on this, Allah willing, in another more suitable place.

Sixth: That the religion which is the seal of religions necessitates the cessation of human completion (الاستكمال الانساني). The Quran's decree on the finality of prophethood, the non-abrogation of the religion, and the stability of the law necessitates that the individual and social completion of man is this measure which the Quran considered in its exposition and legislation.

This is among the prophecies (ملاحم) of the Quran confirmed by the course of human history from the revelation of the Quran until our day, in a period approaching fourteen centuries, during which the species has advanced in the natural aspects of its society with a brilliant advancement and covered a vast distance. However, in terms of its true knowledge and virtuous morals, it has remained where it was, not advancing even a single step, or rather, it has retreated many steps backward. Thus, it has not reached completion in its total perfection from a holistic perspective—meaning spiritual and physical perfection together.

The matter has become confused for those who say: since the establishment of general laws was for the well-being of mankind and the reform of his affairs, they must change with the change of social conditions themselves and their rise and ascent through the stages of perfection. There is no doubt that the ratio between us and the era of the Quran's revelation and the legislation of Islamic laws is much greater than the ratio between that era and the era of the mission of Jesus (peace be upon him) and Moses (peace be upon him). Thus, the difference in ratio between this era and the era of the Prophet necessitated the abrogation of Islamic laws and the establishment of other laws capable of being applied to the requirements of the present age.

The answer to this is: religion, as previously mentioned, did not consider in its legislation merely the material, natural perfection of man; rather, it considered the reality of human existence and built its foundation on spiritual and physical perfection together, seeking both material and spiritual happiness. The necessary consequence of this is that it considers the state of the social individual who is perfected through religious completion, rather than the social individual who is perfected through industry and politics. The matter has become confused for these researchers because, in their obsession with material social research (and matter is transforming and perfecting, like the society built upon it), they assumed that the society considered by religion is similar to the society they consider—a material, physical society. Thus, they judged it to be subject to change and abrogation according to the transformation of material society. You have already learned that religion does not build its legislation on the basis of the body alone, but on both the body and the soul together. Accordingly, one must posit a religious individual or a religious society that combines religious training with the material life permitted by today's world, and then see if there is any deficiency in need of completion or any weakness in need of strengthening.

Seventh: That the prophets (peace be upon them) are infallible (معصومون) from error.

(A Discussion on the Infallibility of the Prophets)

To clarify this conclusion: infallibility (العصمة) — the divine protection from error and sin — is of three types: infallibility from error in receiving the revelation, infallibility from error in delivery and the message, and infallibility from sin (المعصية), which is that which involves violating the sanctity of servitude and opposing the Master's command, ultimately tracing back to a word or deed that contradicts servitude in any way. By infallibility, we mean the existence of a factor in the infallible person that preserves him from falling into what is impermissible of error or sin.
