There is no doubt that these intellectual judgments are issued by the intellect with the conviction of reality (الحقيقة). Thus, the killer, for example, is held accountable for the crime of the killed, or the killed (or his heirs) is gifted with the good deed of the killer, and the like, based on the conviction that the crime is the very same crime and the good deed is the very same good deed, and so on.

This is the state of these rulings within the context of society, which is the domain of the judgments of the practical intellect (العقل العملي). As for any context other than this—namely, the domain of realities (الحقائق)—then all of them are figurative (مجازات) except by way of analysis. This means that since these very concepts are conventional concepts (مفاهيم اعتبارية) derived from realities taken by way of claim and comparison, they are all figurative when measured against those realities from which they were derived. So understand that.

Among the rulings of deeds: is that they are preserved, recorded, and embodied (متجسمة), as the Exalted said: يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُّحْضَرًا وَمَا عَمِلَتْ مِن سُوءٍ تَوَدُّ لَوْ أَنَّ بَيْنَهَا وَبَيْنَهُ أَمَدًا بَعِيدًا — "The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil, it has done, it will wish there were a wide gulf between it and that [day]" (3:30). And the Exalted said: وَكُلَّ إِنسَانٍ أَلْزَمْنَاهُ طَائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ كِتَابًا يَلْقَاهُ مَنشُورًا — "We have fastened every man’s omen to his neck, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall bring out for him a book which he will find spread open" (17:13). And the Exalted said: وَنَكْتُبُ مَا قَدَّمُوا وَآثَارَهُمْ وَكُلَّ شَيْءٍ أَحْصَيْنَاهُ فِي إِمَامٍ مُّبِين — "Indeed it is We who revive the dead and write what they have sent ahead and their effects [which they left behind], and We have figured everything in a manifest Imam" (36:12). And the Exalted said: لَّقَدْ كُنتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَٰذَا فَكَشَفْنَا عَنكَ غِطَاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ — "[He will be told,] ‘You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today’" (50:22). The discussion regarding the embodiment of deeds (تجسم الأعمال) has already passed.

Among the rulings of deeds: is that there is a connection between them and external events. By "deeds," we mean the good deeds (الحسنات) and evil deeds (السيئات) which are the titles of external movements, rather than the motions and rests that are the effects of natural bodies. For the Exalted said: وَمَا أَصَابَكُم مِّن مُّصِيبَةٍ فَبِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُو عَن كَثِيرٍ — "Whatever affliction that may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]" (42:30). And the Exalted said: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يُغَيِّرُ مَا بِقَوْمٍ حَتَّىٰ يُغَيِّرُوا مَا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ وَإِذَا أَرَادَ اللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ سُوءًا فَلَا مَرَدَّ لَهُ — "Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot unless they change what is in their souls. And when Allah wishes to visit ill on a people, there is nothing that can avert it" (13:11). And the Exalted said: ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّرًا نِّعْمَةً أَنْعَمَهَا عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ حَتَّىٰ يُغَيِّرُوا مَا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ — "That is because Allah never changes a blessing He has bestowed on a people unless they change what is in their own souls" (8:53). These verses are apparent in that there is a certain connection between deeds and events, whether evil or good.

The entirety of the matter is summarized by two verses from the Book of Allah, the Exalted: His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرَىٰ آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَرَكَاتٍ مِّنَ السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلَٰكِن كَذَّبُوا فَأَخَذْنَاهُم بِمَا كَانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ — "If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth. But they denied; so We seized them because of what they used to earn" (7:96); and His saying, the Exalted: ظَهَرَ الْفَسَادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ لِيُذِيقَهُم بَعْضَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ — "Corruption has appeared in land and sea because of what the hands of the people have wrought, that He may let them taste something of what they have done, so that they may come back" (30:41).

Thus, cosmic events follow deeds to some extent. If the human species proceeds upon obedience to Allah, the Glorified, and follows the path He approves, it entails the descent of good things and the opening of the doors of blessings. Conversely, the deviation of this species from the path of servitude (صراط العبودية), its persistence in transgression and misguidance, the corruption of intentions, and the vileness of deeds necessitate the appearance of corruption in land and sea. This leads to the destruction of nations through the spread of injustice, the loss of security, the emergence of wars, and other evils pertaining to man and his actions. It also leads to the appearance of afflictions and destructive cosmic events such as floods, earthquakes, lightning bolts, tempests, and the like. Allah, the Glorified, has counted the flood of the Arim (سيل العرم), the flood of Nuh, the lightning bolt of Thamud, and the roaring wind of ‘Ad as being of this nature.

Therefore, when a wicked nation (الأمة الطالحة) becomes submerged in vices and sins, Allah makes it taste the evil consequence of its affair, which leads to its destruction and annihilation. The Exalted said: أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ كَانُوا مِن قَبْلِهِمْ كَانُوا هُمْ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً وَآثَارًا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَأَخَذَهُمُ اللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَمَا كَانَ لَهُم مِّنَ اللَّهِ مِن وَاقٍ — "Have they not travelled over the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them? They were greater than them in might and with respect to the effects [they left] in the land. But Allah seized them for their sins, and they had no defender against Allah" (40:21). And the Exalted said: وَإِذَا أَرَدْنَا أَن نُّهْلِكَ قَرْيَةً أَمَرْنَا مُتْرَفِيهَا فَفَسَقُوا فِيهَا فَحَقَّ عَلَيْهَا الْقَوْلُ فَدَمَّرْنَاهَا تَدْمِيرًا — "And when We desire to destroy a town, We command its affluent ones [to obey Us]. But they commit transgression in it, and so the word becomes due against it, and We destroy it utterly" (17:16). And the Exalted said: ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلَنَا تَتْرَىٰ كُلَّ مَا جَاءَ أُمَّةً رَّسُولُهَا كَذَّبُوهُ فَأَتْبَعْنَا بَعْضَهُم بَعْضًا وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ أَحَادِيثَ فَبُعْدًا لِّقَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ — "Then We sent Our apostles successively. Whenever there came to a nation its apostle, they would impugn him, so We made them follow one another [to destruction] and We made them [the subject of] stories. So away with the people who do not have faith!" (23:44). All of this pertains to the wicked nation; the righteous nation (الأمة الصالحة) is the opposite of that.

The individual, like the nation, is taken to account for good and evil deeds, and for punishments and exemplary penalties (المثلات). However, an individual might be blessed with the blessings of his ancestors, just as he might be taken to account for the injustices of others, such as his fathers and grandfathers. The Exalted said, narrating from Yusuf (peace be upon him): قَالَ أَنَا يُوسُفُ وَهَٰذَا أَخِي قَدْ مَنَّ اللَّهُ عَلَيْنَا إِنَّهُ مَن يَتَّقِ وَيَصْبِرْ فإِن اللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ — "He said, ‘I am Joseph, and this is my brother. Allah has certainly shown us favour. Indeed if one is God-conscious and patient, Allah does not let the reward of the virtuous go to waste’" (12:90). What is meant here is the kingdom, honor, and other things Allah bestowed upon him. And the Exalted said: فَخَسَفْنَا بِهِ وَبِدَارِهِ الْأَرْضَ — "So We caused the earth to swallow him and his dwelling" (28:81). And the Exalted said: وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ عَلِيًّا — "and We assigned them a high and true renown" (19:50), which refers to righteous and blessed progeny, as the Exalted said: جَعَلَهَا كَلِمَةً بَاقِيَةً فِي عَقِبِهِ — "and he made it a lasting word among his posterity" (43:28). And the Exalted said: وَأَمَّا الْجِدَارُ فَكَانَ لِغُلَامَيْنِ يَتِيمَيْنِ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ وَكَانَ تَحْتَهُ كَنزٌ لَّهُمَا وَكَانَ أَبُوهُمَا صَالِحًا فَأَرَادَ رَبُّكَ إِن يَبْلُغَا أَشُدَّهُمَا وَيَسْتَخْرِجَا كَنزَهُمَا — "As for the wall, it belonged to two orphan boys in the city, and there was a treasure under it belonging to them. Their father had been a righteous man. So your Lord desired that they should come of age and take out their treasure" (18:82). And the Exalted said: وَلْيَخْشَ الَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعَافًا خَافُوا عَلَيْهِمْ — "Let those fear who, if they left behind them a helpless offspring, would be afraid on their account" (4:9). What is meant by this is the oppressed descendant who is afflicted by the injustice of his predecessor.

In short, when Allah pours a blessing upon a nation or an individual, if the recipient is righteous, it is a blessing He has bestowed upon him and a trial (امتحاناً) by which He tests him, as Allah the Exalted narrated from Sulayman when he said: قَالَ هَٰذَا مِن فَضْلِ رَبِّي لِيَبْلُوَنِي أَأَشْكُرُ أَمْ أَكْفُرُ وَمَن شَكَرَ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِ وَمَن كَفَرَ فإِنَّ رَبِّي غَنِيٌّ كَرِيمٌ — "He said, ‘This is by the grace of my Lord, to test me whether I will give thanks or be ungrateful. Whoever gives thanks, gives thanks only for his own sake. And whoever is ungrateful [should know that] my Lord is indeed all-sufficient, all-generous’" (27:40). And the Exalted said: لَئِن شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ وَلَئِن كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذَابِي لَشَدِيدٌ — "‘If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing], but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe’" (14:7). This verse, like the one before it, indicates that gratitude (الشكر) itself is among the righteous deeds that entail blessings.

If the recipient is wicked, the blessing is a ruse (مكراً) against him, a gradual leading to ruin (استدراجاً), and a granting of respite (إملائاً), as the Exalted said: وَيَمْكُرُونَ وَيَمْكُرُ اللَّهُ وَاللَّهُ خَيْرُ الْمَاكِرِينَ — "They were plotting and Allah was plotting, and Allah is the best of plotters" (8:30). And the Exalted said: سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ وَأُمْلِي لَهُمْ إِنَّ كَيْدِي مَتِينٌ — "We will draw them on [to ruin] degree by degree, in ways they do not know. I will grant them respite; indeed My stratagem is profound" (68:44-45). And the Exalted said: وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمَ فِرْعَوْنَ — "Certainly We tested the people of Pharaoh before them" (44:17).

When calamities descend and afflictions and trials strike a people or an individual, if the afflicted is righteous, it is a trial (فتنة) and an ordeal (محنة) by which Allah tests His servants to distinguish the corrupt from the good. His likeness with the affliction is like gold with the crucible and the touchstone. The Exalted said: أَحَسِبَ النَّاسُ أَن يُتْرَكُوا أَن يَقُولُوا آمَنَّا وَهُمْ لَا يُفْتَنُونَ وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا الَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ الْكَاذِبِينَ أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئَاتِ أَن يَسْبِقُونَا سَاءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ — "Do the people suppose that they will be let off because they say, ‘We believe,’ and they will not be tested? Certainly We tested those who were before them. So Allah shall surely ascertain those who are truthful, and He shall surely ascertain the liars. Do those who commit misdeeds suppose that they can outstrip Us? Evil is the judgment that they make!" (29:2-4). And the Exalted said: وَتِلْكَ الْأَيَّامُ نُدَاوِلُهَا بَيْنَ النَّاسِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَيَتَّخِذَ مِنكُمْ شُهَدَاءَ — "We make such days alternate among the people, so that Allah may ascertain those who have faith, and that He may take martyrs from among you" (3:140).

If the afflicted is wicked, it is a seizure by vengeance (أخذاً بالنقمة) and a punishment for deeds. The previous verses indicate this.

This is the ruling of the deed: it manifests in the cosmos and returns to its doer. As for His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْلَا أَن يَكُونَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً لَّجَعَلْنَا لِمَن يَكْفُرُ بِالرَّحْمَٰنِ لِبُيُوتِهِمْ سُقُفًا مِّن فِضَّةٍ وَمَعَارِجَ عَلَيْهَا يَظْهَرُونَ وَلِبُيُوتِهِمْ أَبْوَابًا وَسُرُرًا عَلَيْهَا يَتَّكِئُونَ وَزُخْرُفًا وإِن كُلُّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمَّا مَتَاعُ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةُ عِندَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ — "Were it not that mankind would have become a single community, We would have surely provided for those who disbelieve in the All-beneficent, silver roofs for their houses and [silver] stairs by which they ascend, and [silver] doors for their houses and [silver] couches on which they recline, and ornaments. Yet all that is nothing but the wares of the life of this world, and the Hereafter with your Lord is for the God-conscious" (43:33-35)—this does not look toward this door. Rather, what is meant (and Allah knows best) is the condemnation of the world and its wares, and that it and its wares have no value before Allah, the Glorified. For this reason, He favors the disbeliever with it. Indeed, value belongs to the Hereafter, and were it not that human individuals are similar and their efforts are uniform and alike, Allah would have restricted it to the disbeliever.

If it is said: General and specific events such as floods, earthquakes, contagious diseases, wars, and droughts have constant natural causes (علل طبيعية). When these causes are realized, their effects are realized, whether the souls are righteous or wicked. Therefore, there is no place for attributing them to good and evil deeds; rather, it is a religious hypothesis and a supposition that does not correspond to reality.

I say: This is a philosophical objection that does not contradict our current exegetical discussion regarding what is benefited from His speech, the Exalted. We shall address it in detail in a separate philosophical discussion in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرَىٰ آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَرَكَاتٍ مِّنَ السَّمَاءِ — "If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven" (7:96).

The summary of the matter is: the doubt arises from a misunderstanding and a failure to notice the objectives of the Quran and its people. They do not mean by their saying, "deeds, whether good or evil, entail corresponding events of good or evil," the nullification of natural causes or the denial of their influence, nor the partnership of deeds with material factors. Just as the monotheists (الإلهيين), by affirming the Maker (الصانع), do not mean to nullify the general law of cause and effect (العلية والمعلولية) or to affirm chance and haphazardness in existence, or to make the Maker a partner with natural causes such that some matters are attributed to Him and others to them. Rather, their intention is to affirm a cause in the longitudinal dimension (في طول) of another cause, and a spiritual factor above the material factors, and to attribute the influence to both causes, but in order: primary and secondary, similar to writing being attributed to both the human and his hand.

The core of the discourse is that the driver of creation (سائق التكوين) drives man toward his existential happiness (سعادته الوجودية) and vital perfection (كماله الحيوي), as discussed in the section on general prophethood (النبوة العامة). It is known that among the stages of this species in its journey toward happiness is the stage of deeds. Thus, if an obstacle arises in this journey that necessitates its cessation or brings its traveler to the brink of destruction and ruin, it is met with that which repels the mentioned obstacle or destroys the corrupt part. This is similar to the bodily temperament (المزاج البدني) opposing an ailment that afflicts the body or one of its organs; if it succeeds, it repairs the site, and if it fails, it leaves it paralyzed and useless.

Observation and experience have shown that creation and the cosmic process (الصنع والتكوين) have equipped every specific existent with that which repels the harms and corruptions directed toward it. There is no meaning in excluding man, in his species or as an individual, from this universality! They also show that creation opposes every specific existent with incompatible matters that call it to exercise its existential powers so that it may perfect its existence and reach its goal and happiness which was prepared for it. So why would man not be cared for in this regard?

This is what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَاعِبِينَ مَا خَلَقْنَاهُمَا إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ — "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. We did not create them except with consummate wisdom, but most of them do not know" (44:38-39); and His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا بَاطِلًا ذَٰلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا — "We did not create the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. That is the opinion of those who are faithless" (38:27). Just as a craftsman, if he makes something for play and without a goal, for example, the connection between him and his product is severed as soon as it is brought into existence, and he does not care what becomes of it or what corruption or harm meets it. But if he makes it for a goal, he remains watchful over its affair, a witness over it; if an accident occurs that hinders it from the goal for which he made it and assembled its parts to reach, he repairs its state and attends to its affair by addition, subtraction, or by nullifying it from the start, dissolving its structure, and returning to a new creation. Such is the case in the creation of the heavens and the earth and what is between them, including man. Allah, the Glorified, did not create what He created in vain, nor did He bring it into existence for nothing; rather, it is for the return to Him, as the Exalted said: أَفَحَسِبْتُمْ أَنَّمَا خَلَقْنَاكُمْ عَبَثًا وَأَنَّكُمْ إِلَيْنَا لَا تُرْجَعُونَ — "Did you suppose that We created you aimlessly, and that you will not be brought back to Us?" (23:115); and the Exalted said: وإِن إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ الْمُنتَهَىٰ — "and that the terminus is toward your Lord" (53:42). It is necessary then that the Divine care (العناية الربانية) pertains to bringing man, like all other creations, to his goal through invitation and guidance, then through trial and affliction, and then by destroying those for whom the goal of creation has become void and from whom guidance has fallen away. For in that is the perfection of craftsmanship in the individual and the species, the conclusion of the matter for a nation, and the relief of others. The Exalted said: وَرَبُّكَ الْغَنِيُّ ذُو الرَّحْمَةِ إِن يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَيَسْتَخْلِفْ مِن بَعْدِكُم مَّا يَشَاءُ كَمَا أَنشَأَكُم مِّن ذُرِّيَّةِ قَوْمٍ آخَرِينَ — "Your Lord is the All-sufficient, the Lord of mercy. If He wishes, He will take you away, and make whomever He wishes succeed you, just as He produced you from the descendants of another people" (6:133). (Observe the placement of His saying: "Your Lord is the All-sufficient, the Lord of mercy").

This Divine sunnah (السنة الربانية)—namely, the sunnah of trial and vengeance—is that which Allah informed us is a sunnah that is neither overcome nor subdued, but rather dominant and victorious, as the Exalted said: وَمَا أَصَابَكُم مِّن مُّصِيبَةٍ فَبِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُو عَن كَثِيرٍ وَمَا أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ مِن وَلِيٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ — "Whatever affliction that may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]. You cannot frustrate [Him] on the earth, and you do not have any guardian or helper besides Allah" (42:30-31); and the Exalted said: وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنَا لِعِبَادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنصُورُونَ وإِنَّ جُندَنَا لَهُمُ الْغَالِبُونَ — "Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles, that they will indeed be the ones to be helped, and that Our host will indeed be the victors" (37:171-173).

Among the rulings of deeds in terms of happiness (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاء): is that the category of happiness is superior to the category of wretchedness. Among the properties of the category of happiness is every beautiful attribute and characteristic, such as opening (الفتح), victory (الظفر), firmness (الثبات), stability (الاستقرار), security (الامن), rootedness (التأصل), and permanence (البقاء). Similarly, their opposites, such as vanishing (الزهاق), vanity (البطلان), instability (التزلزل), fear (الخوف), cessation (الزوال), and being overcome (المغلوبية), are among the properties of the category of wretchedness.

The Quranic verses in this sense are many and numerous. Sufficient in this regard is the parable Allah the Exalted struck: كَلِمَةً طَيِّبةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ أَصْلُهَا ثَابِتٌ وَفَرْعُهَا فِي السَّمَاءِ تُؤْتِي أُكُلَهَا كُلَّ حِينٍ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهَا وَيَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْأَمْثَالَ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ وَمَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ اجْتُثَّتْ مِن فَوْقِ الْأَرْضِ مَا لَهَا مِن قَرَارٍ يُثَبِّتُ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ وَيُضِلُّ اللَّهُ الظَّالِمِينَ وَيَفْعَلُ اللَّهُ مَا يَشَاءُ — "a good word is like a good tree: its roots are steady and its branches are in the sky. It gives its fruit every season by the leave of its Lord. Allah draws parables for mankind so that they may take admonition. And the parable of a bad word is that of a bad tree: uprooted from the ground, it has no stability. Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world and in the Hereafter, and Allah leads the wrongdoers astray, and Allah does whatever He wishes" (14:24-27). And His saying, the Exalted: لِيُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ وَيُبْطِلَ الْبَاطِلَ — "so that He might confirm the truth and confound falsehood" (8:8); and His saying, the Exalted: وَالْعَاقِبَةُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ — "and the outcome will be in favour of God-consciousness" (20:132); and His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنَا لِعِبَادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنصُورُونَ وإِنَّ جُندَنَا لَهُمُ الْغَالِبُونَ — "Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles, that they will indeed be the ones to be helped, and that Our host will indeed be the victors" (37:171-173); and His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ غَالِبٌ عَلَىٰ أَمْرِهِ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ — "Allah is dominant over His affairs, but most people do not know" (12:21), among other verses.

The concluding of the discourse in this last verse with His saying, "but most people do not know," suggests that this dominance (الغلبة) from Allah, the Glorified, is not such that all people perceive it; rather, most of them are ignorant of it. If it were the sensory dominance that everyone recognizes, the majority would not be ignorant of it. Indeed, those who were ignorant of it or denied it did so from two aspects:

First: Man is limited in his thought, restricted in his vision to what is before him of what he witnesses and what is not absent from him. He speaks of the present and is heedless of the future; he considers the state of a day to be the state [forever], counts the victory of an hour as victory, and takes his short life and meager wares as a measure by which he judges the whole of existence. But Allah, the Glorified—Who encompasses time and space, Who rules over the world and the Hereafter, and Who is the Sustainer (القيوم) over all things—when He judges, He judges decisively, and when He decrees, He decrees with truth. The first and the last are one to Him; He does not fear missing anything, nor does He hasten in any matter. Thus, it is possible (rather, it is the reality) that He decrees the corruption of a day as a prelude by which He reaches the rectification of an age, or the deprivation of an individual as a means to the success of a nation. The ignorant one then thinks that the matter has frustrated Him, the Exalted, and that Allah, the Glorified, has been preceded and overcome (evil is the judgment they make!). But Allah, the Glorified, sees the chain of time just as He sees a segment of it, and He judges over all His creation just as He judges over one of them; no affair distracts Him from another, nor does their preservation tire Him, and He is the All-Exalted, the All-Magnificent. The Exalted said: لَا يَغُرَّنَّكَ تَقَلُّبُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي الْبِلَادِ مَتَاعٌ قَلِيلٌ ثُمَّ مَأْوَاهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَبِئْسَ الْمِهَادُ — "Never be misled by the bustle of the faithless in the towns. It is a brief enjoyment; then their abode shall be hell, and it is an evil resting place" (3:196-197).

Second: The dominance of spiritualities (المعنويات) is different from the dominance of physicalities (الجسمانيات). The dominance and subduing of physicalities consist of gaining mastery over actions, making them submissive and obedient to the subduer who dominates them by stripping away the freedom of choice and spreading coercion and compulsion, as was the habit of the conquering despotic kings. They would kill one group, imprison others, and do as they pleased through arbitrary control and mockery. Experience and rational judgment have shown that coercion and compulsion do not last, and that the authority of foreigners does not settle upon living nations with an eternal stability; rather, it is a hostage of a few days.

As for the dominance of spiritualities, it is by finding hearts that internalize them and by raising individuals who believe in them and have faith in them. There is no degree above complete faith, nor any fortress like its fortification. If faith in a certain meaning becomes established, it will eventually manifest for an age, even if it remains hidden for a day or a period. Therefore, we find that the great states and living societies today pay more attention to the matter of propagation (التبليغ) than they do to the matter of equipment and force; for the weapon of meaning is more severe in might.

This pertains to the formal, imaginary spiritualities that exist among people in their social affairs, which do not exceed the limit of imagination and illusion. As for the true meaning (المعنى الحق) to which He, the Glorified, invites, its matter is clearer and more evident.

Truth (الحق), in itself, is not opposed except by error and falsehood (الباطل)—and what is there after truth except error? It is known that falsehood cannot resist truth; thus, dominance belongs to the proof of truth over falsehood.

Truth, in terms of its influence and its bringing to the goal, is also neither inconsistent nor failing. For if the believer overcomes the enemy of truth in the outward life, he is successful and rewarded. And if the enemy of truth overcomes him and forces him into what Allah, the Glorified, does not approve, his duty is to proceed upon coercion and necessity, and that accords with His pleasure, the Exalted. The Exalted said: إِلَّا إِن تَتَّقُوا مِنْهُمْ تُقَاةً — "unless you be on your guard against them" (3:28). And if he is killed, that is for him a pleasant life, not death. The Exalted said: وَلَا تَقُولُوا لِمَن يُقْتَلُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أَمْوَاتٌ بَلْ أَحْيَاءٌ وَلَٰكِن لَّا تَشْعُرُونَ — "Do not call those who were slain in Allah’s way ‘dead.’ No, they are living, but you are not aware" (2:154).

Thus, the believer is victorious and never overcome—either outwardly and inwardly, or inwardly only. The Exalted said: قُلْ هَلْ تَرَبَّصُونَ بِنَا إِلَّا إِحْدَى الْحُسْنَيَيْنِ — "Say, ‘Do you await for us anything except one of the two best outcomes?’" (9:52).

From here it becomes clear that truth is the victor in the world outwardly and inwardly together. As for outwardly: because the cosmos, as you have learned, guides the human species with a cosmic guidance (هداية تكوينية) toward truth and happiness, and it will surely reach its goal. The appearance seen from falsehood, round after round, is of no consequence; it is only a prelude to the appearance of truth, and to that which does not break the chain of time and does not perish with the age. The cosmic system is never overcome. As for inwardly: because of what you have learned, that dominance belongs to the proof of truth.

As for the fact that the truth of word and deed possesses every beautiful attribute such as firmness, permanence, and beauty, while the falsehood of word and deed possesses every blameworthy attribute such as instability, cessation, ugliness, and evil—the reason for this is what we pointed out in previous discussions: that what is benefited from His saying, the Exalted: ذَٰلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ خَالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "That is Allah, your Lord, the creator of all things" (40:62); and His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ — "[He] who perfected everything that He created" (32:7); and His saying, the Exalted: مَّا أَصَابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ اللَّهِ وَمَا أَصَابَكَ مِن سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِن نَّفْسِكَ — "Whatever good may happen to you is from Allah, and whatever evil may happen to you is from yourself" (4:79)—is that evil deeds are non-existences and vanities (أعدام وبطلانات) not attributed to Allah, the Glorified, Who is the Creator, the Originator, the Bestower of existence, unlike good deeds. Therefore, the good word and the good deed are the source of all beauty and goodness, and the fountain of all خير and happiness, such as firmness and permanence, blessing and benefit, unlike the evil of word and deed. The Exalted said: أَنزَلَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً فَسَالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِهَا فَاحْتَمَلَ السَّيْلُ زَبَدًا رَّابِيًا وَمِمَّا يُوقِدُونَ عَلَيْهِ فِي النَّارِ ابْتِغَاءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتَاعٍ زَبَدٌ مِّثْلُهُ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْحَقَّ وَالْبَاطِلَ فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفَاءً وَأَمَّا مَا يَنفَعُ النَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ — "He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to [the extent of] their capacity, and the flood carries a swelling scum. And from what they smelt in the fire for making ornaments or housewares, there is a similar scum. That is how Allah compares the truth and the falsehood. As for the scum, it leaves as dross, and as for what profits the people, it remains in the earth" (13:17).

Among the rulings of deeds: is that good deeds (الحسنات) of words and actions are in accordance with the judgment of the intellect (العقل), unlike evil deeds (السيئات) of actions and words. It has already passed that Allah, the Glorified, established what He explained to the people on the basis of the intellect (and we mean by the intellect that by which man perceives truth and falsehood, and distinguishes the beautiful from the ugly).

Therefore, He commanded its following and forbade everything that necessitates the disruption of its governance, such as drinking wine, gambling, idle play, cheating, and uncertainty (الغرر) in transactions. Similarly, He forbade lying, fabrication, slander, betrayal, assassination, and everything that necessitates the departure of the intellect from the soundness of judgment. For these actions and deeds cause the human intellect to stumble in its work, while human life is built upon the soundness of perception and thought in all affairs of individual and social life.

If you were to analyze social and individual corruptions, even the established corruptions that no denier denies, you would find that the basis for them is the deeds by which the governance of the intellect is nullified, and that the rest of the corruptions, however many and great they may be, are built upon them. To clarify the matter in this station, there is another place that will come, Allah willing.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "I was riding behind the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم and he said: 'O Ibn Abbas, be pleased with Allah regarding what He has decreed, even if it is contrary to your desire, for that is recorded in the Book of Allah.' I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, where? For I have read the Quran.' He said: 'Perhaps you hate a thing and it is good for you, and perhaps you love a thing and it is evil for you, and Allah knows and you do not know.'"

I say: In the narration, there is an indication that decree (التقدير) encompasses both legislation (التشريع) and creation (التكوين), and it only differs according to the perspective. As for "perhaps" (عسى) meaning obligation (الوجوب), there is no evidence for it. It has already passed that "perhaps" in the Quran is in its linguistic sense, which is hope (الترجي); so no weight is given to what was narrated from some exegetes: "Everything in the Quran that is 'perhaps' from Allah is an obligation!" Even more strange is what was narrated from others: "Everything in the Quran that is 'perhaps' is an obligation except for two instances: a letter in al-Tahrim: 'Perhaps his Lord, if he divorces you,' and in Bani Isra’il: 'Perhaps your Lord will have mercy on you.'"

Also in al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Jarir narrated by way of al-Suddi: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم sent a small expedition (سرية) containing seven people, led by Abdullah ibn Jahsh al-Asadi. Among them were Ammar ibn Yasir, Abu Hudhayfah ibn Utbah ibn Rabi'ah, Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, Utbah ibn Safwan al-Sulami (an ally of Banu Nawfal), Sahl ibn Bayda, Amir ibn Fuhayrah, and Waqid ibn Abdullah al-Yarbu'i (an ally of Umar ibn al-Khattab). He wrote a letter for Ibn Jahsh and commanded him not to read it until he reached Malal. When he reached the valley of Malal, he opened the letter and found in it: 'Travel until you reach the valley of Nakhlah.' He said to his companions: 'Whoever desires death, let him proceed and make his will, for I am making my will and proceeding according to the command of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم.' He traveled, but Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas and Utbah ibn Ghazwan stayed behind as they had lost a camel of theirs. Ibn Jahsh traveled and they encountered al-Hakam ibn Kaysan, Abdullah ibn al-Mughirah ibn Uthman, and Amr al-Hadrami. They fought and captured al-Hakam ibn Kaysan and Abdullah ibn al-Mughirah, while al-Mughirah escaped. Amr al-Hadrami was killed; Waqid ibn Abdullah killed him. This was the first booty taken by the companions of Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم. When they returned to Madinah with the two prisoners and the wealth they had gained, the polytheists said: 'Muhammad claims that he follows the obedience of Allah, yet he is the first to permit [fighting in] the sacred month.' So Allah sent down: يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الشَّهْرِ الْحَرَامِ قِتَالٍ فِيهِ قُلْ قِتَالٌ فِيهِ كَبِيرٌ — 'They ask you concerning warfare in the holy month. Say, "Warfare in it is a great sin..."' (2:217). And what you have done, O assembly of polytheists, is greater than killing in the sacred month, when you disbelieved in Allah and barred Muhammad from Him. And the trial (الفتنة)—which is polytheism (الشرك)—is greater before Allah than killing in the sacred month. That is His saying: 'and barring from the way of Allah and disbelief in Him.'"

I say: The narrations in this sense and what is close to it are many from their paths. This meaning was also narrated in al-Majma'. In some narrations, the expedition consisted of eight people, the ninth being their commander. Also in al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi narrated by way of Yazid ibn Ruman from Urwah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم sent Abdullah ibn Jahsh to Nakhlah and said to him: 'Stay there until you bring us news of the news of Quraysh,' and he did not command him to fight. That was in the sacred month. He wrote a letter for him before informing him that he would travel, saying: 'Go out, you and your companions, and when you have traveled for two days, open your letter and look into it. Whatever I have commanded you, proceed with it, and do not compel any of your companions to go with you.' When he had traveled for two days, he opened the letter and found in it: 'Proceed until you reach Nakhlah and bring us whatever news of Quraysh reaches you.' He said to his companions when he read the letter: 'Hearing and obeying. Whoever among you has a desire for martyrdom, let him set out with me, for I am proceeding according to the command of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم. And whoever among you dislikes that, let him return, for the Messenger of Allah has forbidden me from compelling any of you.' So the people proceeded with him. When they were at Najran, Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas and Utbah ibn Ghazwan lost a camel of theirs which they were riding in turns, so they stayed behind to search for it. The people proceeded until they reached Nakhlah. Amr ibn al-Hadrami, al-Hakam ibn Kaysan, Uthman, and al-Mughirah ibn Abdullah passed by them with trade they were bringing from Ta’if: leather and oil. When the people saw them, Waqid ibn Abdullah appeared to them, and he had shaved his head. When they saw him shaved, Ammar said: 'There is no harm to you from him.' The companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم consulted among themselves—and it was the last day of Jumada—saying: 'If you kill them, you will be killing them in the sacred month, and if you leave them, they will enter the sacred territory of Makkah tonight and will be protected from you.' So the people agreed to kill them. Waqid ibn Abdullah al-Tamimi shot Amr ibn al-Hadrami with an arrow and killed him. Uthman ibn Abdullah and al-Hakam ibn Kaysan were taken prisoner, while al-Mughirah fled and they could not catch him. They drove the caravan and brought it to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم. He said to them: 'By Allah, I did not command you to fight in the sacred month.' So the Messenger of Allah halted the two prisoners and the caravan and did not take anything from it. When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said to them what he said, they were filled with regret and thought they were ruined, and their brothers among the Muslims rebuked them. Quraysh said—when the news of these people reached them—: 'Muhammad has shed sacred blood, taken wealth, captured men, and permitted the sacred month.' So Allah sent down regarding that: يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الشَّهْرِ الْحَرَامِ قِتَالٍ فِيهِ the verse. When that was revealed, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم took the caravan and ransomed the two prisoners. The Muslims said: 'O Messenger of Allah! Do you hope that this will be a military expedition for us?' So Allah sent down: إِنَّ الذين آمنوا والذين هاجروا وجاهدوا في سبيل الله أُولئك يرجون رحمة الله — 'Indeed those who believe and those who migrate and wage jihad in the way of Allah, it is they who expect Allah’s mercy' (2:218). They were eight, and their commander was the ninth, Abdullah ibn Jahsh."

I say: Regarding the fact that His saying, the Exalted: إِن الذين آمنوا والذين هاجروا the verse, was revealed concerning the affair of the companions of Abdullah ibn Jahsh, there are other narrations. The verse indicates the excuse of one who performs an act seeking proximity [to Allah] but misses the reality; there is no sin with error. It also indicates the permissibility of forgiveness pertaining to cases other than sin.

In the narrations, there is an indication that the intended "questioners" in His saying, the Exalted, يَسْأَلُونَكَ (They ask you), are the believers rather than the polytheists who were criticizing the action of the believers. This is also supported by what passed from the narration of Ibn Abbas in the previous traditionary discourse: "I have not seen a people better than the companions of Muhammad; they did not ask him except about thirteen matters until he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم passed away, all of which are in the Quran: among them are 'They ask you about wine and gambling,' and 'They ask you about the sacred month'..." the narration. This is further supported by the fact that the address in the verse is to the believers, where the Exalted says: وَلَا يَزَالُونَ يُقَاتِلُونَكُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَرُدُّوكُمْ عَن دِينِكُمْ — "They will not cease fighting you until they turn you away from your religion" (2:217).

Ayahs

۞ يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْخَمْرِ وَٱلْمَيْسِرِ ۖ قُلْ فِيهِمَآ إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنَـٰفِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَإِثْمُهُمَآ أَكْبَرُ مِن نَّفْعِهِمَا ۗ وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ مَاذَا يُنفِقُونَ قُلِ ٱلْعَفْوَ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٢١٩)
They ask you concerning wine and gambling. Say, ‘There is a great sin in both of them, and some profits for the people, but their sinfulness outweighs their profit.’ And they ask you as to what they should spend. Say, ‘All that is surplus.’ Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may reflect (219)
فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۗ وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ ۖ قُلْ إِصْلَاحٌ لَّهُمْ خَيْرٌ ۖ وَإِن تُخَالِطُوهُمْ فَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ ٱلْمُفْسِدَ مِنَ ٱلْمُصْلِحِ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَأَعْنَتَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٢٢٠)
about the world and the Hereafter. And they ask you concerning the orphans. Say, ‘It is better to set right their affairs, and if you intermingle with them, they are of course your brothers: Allah knows those who cause corruption from those who set things right, and had Allah wished He would have put you to hardship.’ Indeed Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (220)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: يسألونك عن الخمر والميسر (They ask you about wine and gambling). Wine (الخمر), as understood from the language, refers to every liquid made for intoxication (السكر). Its linguistic root signifies "covering" or "veiling" (الستر). It is so named because it veils the intellect (العقل) and prevents it from distinguishing beauty from ugliness or good from evil. It is said that the head-covering of a woman is called a khimār (الخمار), and it is said: "I covered (خمرت) the vessel" when its top is covered. It is also said: "I leavened (أخمرت) the dough" when yeast (الخمير) is added to it. Yeast is called khamīrah because it is first kneaded and then covered to ferment. The Arabs only knew varieties of wine made from grapes, dates, and barley; subsequently, people gradually increased its varieties until today there are many types with varying levels according to their degree of intoxication, and all of them are wine (خمر).

Gambling (الميسر) linguistically refers to games of chance (القمار), and the gambler is called a yāsir (ياسر). Its root meaning is "ease" (السهولة); it is so named because of the ease of acquiring the wealth of others without the toil of earning or labor. Its most frequent use among the Arabs was for a specific type of gambling, which was the drawing of lots with arrows (القداح), also called azlām (الازلام) and aqlām (الاقلام).

As for its method, they would purchase a camel and slaughter it, then divide it into twenty-eight portions. They would then set ten arrows: al-fadhdh, al-taw’am, al-raqīb, al-ḥils, al-nāfis, al-musbil, al-mu‘allā, al-manīḥ, al-sanīḥ, and al-raghad. The fadhdh was entitled to one portion out of the twenty-eight; the taw’am to two portions; the raqīb to three; the ḥils to four; the nāfis to five; the musbil to six; and the mu‘allā to seven—this being the arrow with the largest share. As for the last three—al-manīḥ, al-sanīḥ, and al-raghad—they had no share. Whoever’s name was drawn with one of the seven arrows took his designated share of the portions, while the holders of the last three arrows were fined the price of the camel. This process was conducted among ten men by way of drawing lots for the shares and arrows.

His saying, the Exalted: قل فيهما إثم كبير (Say, ‘There is a great sin in both of them’). It has also been recited as "much sin" (إِثم كثير) with the letter thā’. Sin (الإثم) is close in meaning to "offense" (الذنب) and its likes; it is a state in a thing or in the intellect that slows a person down from attaining good things. Thus, it is the sin that entails wretchedness and deprivation in other matters and corrupts the happiness of life in its other aspects—and these two [wine and gambling] possess this quality.

As for the drinking of wine, its medical harms and its evil effects on the stomach, intestines, liver, lungs, nervous system, arteries, heart, and senses—such as sight and taste—have been the subject of works by skilled physicians, both ancient and modern. They have compiled remarkable statistics revealing the vast number of those afflicted with types of fatal diseases caused by this lethal poison.

As for its moral harms: the distortion of character and its leading the human being toward obscenity, harm, crimes, murder, the disclosure of secrets, and the violation of sanctities. It nullifies all human laws and norms (النواميس) upon which the foundation of a happy life is built, especially the norm of chastity (العفة) regarding honor, lives, and property. There is no protector against a drunkard who knows not what he says and is unaware of what he does. Rarely does any of these crimes—which have filled the world and embittered human life—occur without wine having a direct or indirect role in it.

As for its harm to perception (الادراك), its stripping away of the intellect, its disordered influence on human thoughts, and its alteration of the course of perception during intoxication and after sobering up, this is something no denier can deny. This is the greatest sin and corruption within it, and from it arise all other evils.

The Islamic Law (الشريعة), as previously indicated, established the foundation of its rulings upon the preservation of the sound intellect (العقل السليم). It prohibited with the utmost severity any act that nullifies the function of the intellect, such as wine, gambling, cheating, lying, and the like. Among actions, the drinking of wine is the most potent in nullifying the governance of the intellect over one's well-being, just as lying and false testimony are the most potent among speech.

These actions—meaning those that nullify the governance of the intellect, at the head of which are policies built upon intoxication and falsehood—are what threaten humanity and demolish the edifice of happiness. They yield no general fruit except that which is more bitter than its predecessor. Whenever the burden increases in weight and exhausts its carrier, more weight is added in the hope of capability; thus, the effort fails and the work is lost. Had this "Clear Path" (المحجة البيضاء) and "Radiant Law" (الشريعة الغراء) no other merit than its foundation upon the intellect and its prohibition of that which corrupts it through the following of whims (اتباع الهوى), it would suffice as a source of pride. There is a completion to this discussion which we shall address in Surah al-Ma'idah, if Allah wills.

Humanity, by its animalistic disposition (القريحة الحيوانية), has always inclined toward the pleasures of lust; thus, lustful actions spread among them faster than the spread of truth and reality. Habits became entrenched in their consumption, making it difficult to abandon them and follow the norms of human happiness. Therefore, Allah, the Glorified, legislated the rulings among them gradually (التدريج) and charged them with gentleness and respite.

Among those widespread evil habits was the drinking of wine. Its prohibition was undertaken gradually, as yielded by reflection on the verses related to it. It was revealed in four stages:

One of them: His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَوَاحِشَ مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا وَمَا بَطَنَ وَالْإِثْمَ وَالْبَغْيَ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ — "Say, ‘My Lord has only forbidden indecencies (الفواحش) — shameful acts that exceed the bounds of morality — whether open or hidden, and sin (الإثم) and undue aggression’" (7:33). This verse is Meccan, and in it, sin was explicitly forbidden. In wine there is sin, yet it was not clarified what that sin was, nor that there is a "great sin" in wine.

Perhaps that was a form of leniency and facilitation, as silence regarding clarification implies a degree of overlooking, as is also sensed in His saying, the Exalted: وَمِن ثَمَرَاتِ النَّخِيلِ وَالْأَعْنَابِ تَتَّخِذُونَ مِنْهُ سَكَرًا وَرِزْقًا حَسَنًا — "And of the fruits of the date palms and vines, you derive from them an intoxicant (سكرا) and a goodly provision" (16:67). This verse is also Meccan. It seems people were not fully alert to the great prohibition within it until His saying, the Exalted, was revealed: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَقْرَبُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَأَنتُمْ سُكَارَىٰ — "O you who have faith! Do not approach prayer when you are intoxicated" (4:43). This verse is Medinan, and it prevents people to some extent from drinking and intoxication during the best of states and in the best of places, which is prayer in the mosque.

Reason and the context of the noble verse refuse that it was revealed after the verse of al-Baqarah and the two verses of al-Ma'idah, for those two indicate an absolute prohibition (النهي المطلق). There is no meaning to a specific prohibition after the arrival of an absolute one; furthermore, it would contradict the gradualism (التدريج) understood from these verses, as gradualism is a movement from the easier to the more difficult, not the reverse.

Then the verse of al-Baqarah was revealed, namely His saying, the Exalted: يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ قُلْ فِيهِمَا إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنَافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَإِثْمُهُمَا أَكْبَرُ مِن نَّفْعِهِمَا — "They ask you concerning wine and gambling. Say, ‘There is a great sin in both of them, and some profits for mankind, but their sin is greater than their profit’" (2:219). This verse came after the verse of al-Nisa', as explained, and it contains the prohibition due to its definitive indication of sin in wine: "There is a great sin in both of them," and the prior revelation of the Meccan verse of al-A'raf which was explicit in prohibiting sin.

From here, the error of what some commentators have mentioned becomes apparent: that the verse of al-Baqarah was not explicit in prohibition, for His saying, the Exalted, "Say, ‘There is a great sin in both of them’" does not indicate more than that there is sin in them, and sin is harm (الضرر). They argue that the prohibition of everything harmful does not indicate the prohibition of that which contains harm from one aspect and benefit from another. Therefore, they claim this verse was a subject for the independent judgment (الاجتهاد) of the companions; some abandoned wine because of it, while others persisted in drinking it, as if they saw that they could benefit from it while avoiding its harm. This, they say, was a preparation for the finality of its prohibition, so His saying, the Exalted, was revealed: إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَالْمَيْسِرُ وَالْأَنصَابُ وَالْأَزْلَامُ رِجْسٌ مِّنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطَانِ — "Indeed wine, gambling, idols, and the divining arrows are abominations of Satan’s doing..." until His saying: فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّنتَهُونَ — "So will you desist?" (5:90-91).

The reason this view is erroneous is, firstly: because it takes "sin" (الإثم) to mean "harm" (الضرر) absolutely, whereas sin is not merely harm. Its mere juxtaposition with "benefit" (المنفعة) in the discourse does not necessitate that it means the harm that is the opposite of benefit. How can sin be taken to mean harm in His saying, the Exalted: وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ فَقَدِ افْتَرَىٰ إِثْمًا عَظِيمًا — "and whoever ascribes partners to Allah has indeed fabricated a tremendous sin" (4:48); and His saying: فَإِنَّهُ آثِمٌ قَلْبُهُ — "his heart is indeed sinful" (2:283); and His saying: إِن تَبُوءَ بِإِثْمِي وَإِثْمِكَ — "that you should bear my sin and your sin" (5:29); and His saying: لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِّنْهُم مَّا اكْتَسَبَ مِنَ الْإِثْمِ — "Every one of them shall bear [the punishment of] the sin that he has earned" (24:11); and His saying: وَمَن يَكْسِبْ إِثْمًا فَإِنَّمَا يَكْسِبُهُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ — "And whoever commits a sin, commits it only against himself" (4:111), among other verses?

Secondly: The verse did not justify the ruling by harm alone; even if that were granted, it justifies it by the preponderance of harm over benefit. Its wording is explicit in this, as He says: "and their sin is greater than their profit." To refer it, despite this, to independent judgment is to exercise judgment in the face of an explicit text (الاجتهاد في مقابل النص).

Thirdly: Suppose the verse itself were deficient in its indication of prohibition; it is nonetheless explicit in its indication of "sin," and it is a Medinan verse preceded in revelation by the verse of al-A'raf which explicitly prohibited sin. What is the excuse for one who heard the prohibition in a Meccan verse to then exercise independent judgment regarding a Medinan verse?

Furthermore, the verse of al-A'raf indicates the prohibition of sin in an absolute sense, while this verse qualified the sin with "greatness" (الكبر). With this, no doubt remains for any doubter that wine is a complete individual and a perfect instance (مصداق) of sin; it is not fitting to doubt its being among the prohibited sins. The Quran described murder, the concealment of testimony, and fabrication as "sin," yet it did not describe sin in any of those as "great" (الكبر) except in the case of wine and polytheism (الشرك), where He described the latter as "tremendous" (العظم) in His saying: وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ فَقَدِ افْتَرَىٰ إِثْمًا عَظِيمًا — "and whoever ascribes partners to Allah has indeed fabricated a tremendous sin" (4:48). In short, there is no doubt about the verse's indication of prohibition.

Then the two verses of al-Ma'idah were revealed: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَالْمَيْسِرُ وَالْأَنصَابُ وَالْأَزْلَامُ رِجْسٌ مِّنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطَانِ فَاجْتَنِبُوهُ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ الشَّيْطَانُ أَن يُوقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ الْعَدَاوَةَ وَالْبَغْضَاءَ فِي الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ وَيَصُدَّكُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ اللَّهِ وَعَنِ الصَّلَاةِ فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّنتَهُونَ — "O you who have faith! Indeed wine, gambling, idols, and the divining arrows are abominations of Satan’s doing, so avoid them, so that you may be felicitous. Satan only seeks to instigate enmity and hatred among you by means of wine and gambling, and to keep you from the remembrance of Allah and from prayer. So will you desist?" (5:90-91). The conclusion of the discourse indicates that the Muslims had not desisted from drinking wine after the revelation of the verse of al-Baqarah and had not completely uprooted it until this verse was revealed and it was said: "So will you desist?" This is all regarding wine.

As for gambling (الميسر): its social corruptions and its demolition of the edifice of life are matters witnessed and observed (معاين). Observation renders explanation unnecessary. We shall address its status in Surah al-Ma'idah, if Allah wills.

Let us return to our discussion on the vocabulary of the verse. His saying, the Exalted: قل فيهما إثم كبير ومنافع للناس (Say, ‘There is a great sin in both of them, and some profits for mankind’). The meaning of sin (الإثم) has already been discussed. As for greatness (الكبر), it is to volumes (الأحجام) what "muchness" (الكثرة) is to numbers. Greatness is the opposite of smallness (الصغر), just as muchness is the opposite of fewness (القلة). They are two relative attributes (وصفان اضافيان), meaning that a body or volume is great in relation to another smaller than it, while it is itself small in relation to another greater than it. Were it not for comparison and relation, there would be neither greatness nor smallness, just as there would be neither muchness nor fewness. It seems that the first thing people became aware of regarding the meaning of greatness was in volumes, which are among the continuous quantities (الكميات المتصلة) and are physical. Then they moved from forms to meanings, extending the sense of greatness and smallness to them. The Exalted said: إِنَّهَا لَإِحْدَى الْكُبَرِ — "Indeed it is one of the greatest [portents]" (74:35); and He said: كَبُرَتْ كَلِمَةً تَخْرُجُ مِنْ أَفْوَاهِهِمْ — "A monstrous word it is that comes out of their mouths" (18:5); and He said: كَبُرَ عَلَى الْمُشْرِكِينَ مَا تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَيْهِ — "Hard on the polytheists is that to which you summon them" (42:13). Magnitude (العظم) is similar in meaning to greatness, except that it appears "magnitude" (العظمة) is derived from "bone" (العظم), which is one of the parts of an animal's body. Since the greatness of an animal's body was traced back to the greatness of the bones composed and arranged within it, "bone" was borrowed for "greatness," then it became established and derivatives were formed from it like primary roots.

Profit (النفع) is the opposite of harm (الضرر). They are applied to things desired for other than themselves or disliked for other than themselves, just as good (الخير) and evil (الشر) are applied to things desired for their own sake or disliked for their own sake. What is meant by "profits" (المنافع) in both of them is what people intend by them of financial gains through buying, selling, labor, enjoyment, and amusement. When the second comparison was made between sin and profits using "greater" (أكبر), it necessitated singularizing "profits" and disregarding the aspect of "muchness" within them, for number has no effect on greatness; thus it was said: "and their sin is greater than their profit" (وإِثمهما أكبر من نفعهما) and it did not say "than their profits" (من منافعهما).

His saying, the Exalted: ويسئلونك ماذا ينفقون قل العفو (And they ask you what they should spend. Say, ‘The surplus’). Al-Raghib mentioned that al-‘afw (العفو) is to intend a thing in order to take it. Subsequently, various linguistic nuances caused it to be used for different meanings, such as al-‘afw in the sense of forgiveness (المغفرة), al-‘afw in the sense of erasing a trace (إِمحاء الاثر), and al-‘afw in the sense of moderation in spending (التوسط في الانفاق). This last one is what is intended in this context, and Allah knows best.

The discussion regarding the correspondence of the answer to the question in this verse is similar to what preceded in His saying, the Exalted: يسألونك ماذا ينفقون قل ما أنفقتم من خير فللوالدين والاقربين (They ask you as to what they should spend. Say, ‘Whatever good you spend, let it be for parents and relatives...’).

His saying, the Exalted: يبين الله لكم (Thus does Allah clarify for you...) until His saying: في الدنيا والآخرة (in this world and the Hereafter). The adverbial phrase (الظرف), namely His saying: في الدنيا والآخرة, is connected to His saying: تتفكرون (that you may reflect) and is not a container for it. The meaning is: so that you may reflect upon the matter of the two abodes and what concerns you of their reality; that the world is an abode Allah created for you to live in and to earn what benefits you in your permanent residence, which is the Hereafter, wherein you return to your Lord and He recompenses you for your deeds that you performed in the world.

In the verse, there is, firstly: an exhortation to investigate the realities of existence, the knowledge of the Origin (المبدء) and the Return (المعاد), and the secrets of nature; and to reflect upon the nature of society, the norms of ethics, and the laws of individual and social life—in short, all sciences that investigate the Origin and the Return and what lies between them that is connected to human happiness and wretchedness.

Secondly: Although the Quran calls for absolute obedience to Allah and His Messenger without any condition or restriction, it is not pleased that the rulings and knowledge it provides be taken with blindness and pure stagnation (الجمود المحض) without reflection and intellection (تعقل) that reveals the reality of the matter, and an enlightenment by which the path is illuminated in this journey and travel.

It seems that what is meant by "clarification" (التبيين) is the uncovering of the causes (علل) of rulings and laws, and the elucidation of the principles of knowledge and sciences.

His saying, the Exalted: ويسألونك عن اليتامى قل اصلاح لهم خير (They ask you concerning the orphans. Say, ‘To set right their affairs is better’). In the verse, there is an intimation, indeed an indication, of a type of mitigation and facilitation (التخفيف والتسهيل), as it permitted mixing with orphans. Then it was said: "And had Allah wished, He would have distressed you." This reveals a prior severity from Allah regarding the matter of orphans that caused anxiety and agitation in the hearts of the Muslims until it prompted them to ask about the orphans' affair. This was indeed the case, for there are verses with a severe tone regarding orphans, such as His saying: إِن الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوَالَ الْيَتَامَىٰ ظُلْمًا إِنَّمَا يَأْكُلُونَ فِي بُطُونِهِمْ نَارًا وَسَيَصْلَوْنَ سَعِيرًا — "Indeed those who consume the property of orphans wrongfully, only consume fire into their bellies, and they shall enter the Blaze" (4:10); and His saying: وَآتُوا الْيَتَامَىٰ أَمْوَالَهُمْ وَلَا تَتَبَدَّلُوا الْخَبِيثَ بِالطَّيِّبِ وَلَا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوَالَهُمْ إِلَىٰ أَمْوَالِكُمْ إِنَّهُ كَانَ حُوبًا كَبِيرًا — "Give the orphans their property, and do not replace the good [of theirs] with the bad [of yours], and do not consume their property [by mixing it] with your own property, for that is indeed a great sin" (4:2). Thus, it appears the verse was revealed after the verses of Surah al-Nisa'. This is supported by what we shall relate regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) in the traditional discussion. In His saying: قل إِصلاح لهم خير (Say, ‘To set right their affairs is better’), where "setting right" (إصلاح) is indefinite (نكر), there is an indication that what is pleasing to Allah, the Glorified, is a specific type of setting right, not just any setting right, even if it were only so in outward appearance. The indefiniteness in His saying "setting right" (إِصلاح) is to denote categorization (التنويع); what is meant is setting right according to reality, not according to form. This is intimated by His saying at the end: "And Allah knows the corrupter from the corrector."

His saying, the Exalted: وإِن تخالطوهم فإِخوانكم (And if you intermingle with them, they are your brethren). This is an allusion to the equality established among all believers by nullifying all distinguishing attributes that are the sources for the emergence of types of corruption among people in their society—such as enslavement, weakening, humiliation, arrogance, and types of aggression and injustice. Through this, balance is achieved between the weights of society, and parity between the weak orphan and the strong guardian, and between the wealthy rich and the destitute poor, and likewise every deficient and complete one. He has said: إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ — "The faithful are indeed brothers" (49:10).

Thus, what the verse permits regarding the guardian's intermingling with the orphan is that it be like the intermingling between two brothers who are equal in social rights among people; what is taken from his wealth is like what is given to him. The verse thus parallels His saying: وَآتُوا الْيَتَامَىٰ أَمْوَالَهُمْ وَلَا تَتَبَدَّلُوا الْخَبِيثَ بِالطَّيِّبِ وَلَا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوَالَهُمْ إِلَىٰ أَمْوَالِكُمْ إِنَّهُ كَانَ حُوبًا كَبِيرًا — "Give the orphans their property, and do not replace the good [of theirs] with the bad [of yours], and do not consume their property [by mixing it] with your own property, for that is indeed a great sin" (4:2). This parallelism is among the evidences that there is a type of mitigation and facilitation in the verse, as also indicated by its conclusion, and as indicated to some extent by His saying: "And Allah knows the corrupter from the corrector." The meaning is: if intermingling occurs (and this is the mitigation), let it be like the intermingling of two brothers, on the basis of equality in rights. There should be no fear or apprehension then, for if that is with the intention of setting things right in reality, not just in form, it is of the "better" (الخير). The reality of the matter is not hidden from Allah, the Glorified, such that He would take you to task for mere intermingling, for Allah, the Glorified, distinguishes the corrupter from the corrector.

His saying, the Exalted: والله يعلم المفسد من المصلح (And Allah knows the corrupter from the corrector) until the end of the verse. The transitivity of "knows" (يعلم) with "from" (من) is as if it is because it encompasses the meaning of "distinguishes" (يميز). Distress (العنت) is toil and hardship.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, narrated from ‘Ali ibn Yaqtin: He said that al-Mahdi asked Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him) about wine: "Is it prohibited in the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic? For people only know the prohibition (النهي) of it but do not know its forbiddenness (تحريم)." Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him) said to him: "Rather, it is forbidden." He asked: "In which place is it forbidden in the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, O Abu al-Hasan?" He replied: "The saying of Allah, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَوَاحِشَ مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا وَمَا بَطَنَ وَالْإِثْمَ وَالْبَغْيَ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ (until he said:) As for 'sin' (الإثم), it is wine itself. Allah, the Exalted, has said in another place: يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ قُلْ فِيهِمَا إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنَافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَإِثْمُهُمَا أَكْبَرُ مِن نَّفْعِهِمَا. Thus, 'sin' in the Book of Allah is wine and gambling, and their sin is greater than their profit, as Allah, the Exalted, said." Al-Mahdi said: "O ‘Ali ibn Yaqtin, this is a Hashemite decree (فتوى)." I said to him: "You have spoken the truth, O Commander of the Faithful. Praise be to Allah Who did not let this knowledge leave you, the People of the House (أهل البيت)." He said: "By Allah, al-Mahdi could not restrain himself from saying to me: 'You have spoken the truth, O Rafidite!'"

I say: What clarifies the meaning of this narration has already passed.

In al-Kafi also, from Abu Basir, from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them both): He said: "Indeed, Allah made a house for disobedience (المعصية), then He made a door for the house, then He made a lock for the door, then He made a key for the lock; and the key to disobedience is wine."

In it also, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): He said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Indeed, wine is the head of every sin (إِثم).'"

In it, from Isma‘il: He said: "Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) approached in the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام), and a group from Quraysh looked at him and said: 'This is the god of the people of Iraq.' Some of them said: 'Why don't you send one of you to him?' A young man from among them went to him and said: 'O uncle, what is the greatest of the major sins (الكبائر)?' He (peace be upon him) said: 'Drinking wine.'"

In it also, from Abu al-Bilad, from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them both): He said: "Allah has not been disobeyed with anything more severe than the drinking of an intoxicant (المسكر). Indeed, one of them leaves the obligatory prayer and leaps upon his mother, his daughter, and his sister while he does not understand."

In al-Ihtijaj: An atheist asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "Why did Allah forbid wine when there is no pleasure better than it?" He said: "He forbade it because it is the mother of all evils (ام الخبائث) and the head of every evil. A moment comes upon its drinker when his intellect is stripped away, so he knows not his Lord and leaves no disobedience but that he commits it..." (the hadith).

I say: The narrations explain one another, and experience and reason support them.

In al-Kafi, from Jabir, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): He said: "The Messenger of Allah cursed ten regarding wine: its planter, its guard, its presser, its drinker, its server, its carrier, the one to whom it is carried, its seller, its buyer, and the consumer of its price."

In al-Kafi and al-Mahasin, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): He said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Cursed, cursed is he who sits at a table (مائدة) upon which wine is drunk.'"

I say: The confirmation of these two narrations is His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَعَاوَنُوا عَلَى الْإِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوَانِ — "and do not cooperate in sin and aggression" (5:2).

In al-Khisal, with its chain of narration from Abu Umamah: He said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Four are they whom Allah will not look upon on the Day of Resurrection: one who is disobedient to parents (عاق), one who reminds others of his favors (منان), one who denies destiny (مكذب بالقدر), and a habitual drinker of wine (مدمن خمر).'"

In al-Amali of Ibn al-Shaykh, with its chain of narration from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): He said: "My Lord, Majestic is His Majesty, has sworn: 'No servant of Mine shall drink wine in the world but that I shall give him to drink on the Day of Resurrection the like of what he drank of scalding water (الحميم), whether he be punished thereafter or forgiven.' Then he said: 'Indeed, the drinker of wine will come on the Day of Resurrection with a blackened face, blue eyes, a twisted jaw, flowing saliva, and his tongue lolling from the back of his neck.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): He said: "It is a right upon Allah that He give those who drink wine to drink from what comes out of the private parts of harlots (المومسات). The harlots are the adulteresses; from their private parts comes out ṣadīd (صديد), and ṣadīd is pus and thick blood whose heat and stench torment the people of the Fire."

I say: These narrations are perhaps supported by His saying, the Exalted: إِن شَجَرَتَ الزَّقُّومِ طَعَامُ الْأَثِيمِ كَالْمُهْلِ يَغْلِي فِي الْبُطُونِ كَغَلْيِ الْحَمِيمِ خُذُوهُ فَاعْتِلُوهُ إِلَىٰ سَوَاءِ الْجَحِيمِ ثُمَّ صُبُّوا فَوْقَ رَأْسِهِ مِنْ عَذَابِ الْحَمِيمِ ذُقْ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْكَرِيمُ — "Indeed the tree of Zaqqum is the food of the sinful (الأثيم). Like molten copper, it shall boil in the bellies, like the boiling of scalding water. ‘Seize him and drag him into the midst of hell! Then pour over his head the punishment of scalding water! Taste this! Indeed you are the [self-styled] mighty and noble!’" (44:43-49). There are many narrations regarding all the preceding meanings.

In al-Kafi, from al-Washsha’, from Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him): He said: "I heard him saying: 'Gambling (الميسر) is games of chance (القمار).'"

I say: The narrations in this sense are many and beyond doubt.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: ويسألونك ماذا ينفقون (And they ask you what they should spend), from Ibn ‘Abbas: "A group of the companions, when they were commanded to spend in the way of Allah, came to the Prophet and said: 'We do not know what this spending is that we have been commanded regarding our wealth, so what should we spend from it?' So Allah revealed: وَيَسْأَلُونَكَ مَاذَا يُنفِقُونَ قُلِ الْعَفْوَ. Before that, a man would spend his wealth until he found nothing to give in charity, nor wealth to eat until he had given it in charity."

In al-Durr al-Manthur also, from Yahya: That it reached him that Mu‘adh ibn Jabal and Tha‘labah came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: "O Messenger of Allah, we have slaves and families, so what should we spend from our wealth?" So Allah revealed: وَيَسْأَلُونَكَ مَاذَا يُنفِقُونَ قُلِ الْعَفْوَ.

In al-Kafi and Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Al-‘Afw (العفو) is the middle course (الوسط)."

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (peace be upon them): "Sufficiency (الكفاف)." And in the narration of Abu Basir: "Moderation (القصد)."

In it also, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), regarding the verse: الَّذِينَ إِذَا أَنفَقُوا لَمْ يُسْرِفُوا وَلَمْ يَقْتُرُوا وَكَانَ بَيْنَ ذَلِكَ قَوَامًا — "Those who, when they spend, are neither wasteful nor tightfisted, and stay at a mean between these two" (25:67). He said: "This [verse] is after that [verse]; it is the middle course (الوسط)."

In al-Majma‘, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "Al-‘Afw is what remains beyond the year's sustenance."

I say: The narrations are in agreement, and the last one is by way of clarifying an instance (مصداق). The narrations regarding the merit of charity, its manner, its recipients, and its amount are beyond counting; some will be mentioned in appropriate places, if Allah wills.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: ويسألونك عن اليتامى (They ask you concerning the orphans), from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): He said: "When إِن الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوَالَ الْيَتَامَىٰ ظُلْمًا إِنَّمَا يَأْكُلُونَ فِي بُطُونِهِمْ نَارًا وَسَيَصْلَوْنَ سَعِيرًا was revealed, everyone who had an orphan with him turned them out, and they asked the Messenger of Allah about turning them out. So Allah revealed: يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْيَتَامَىٰ قُلْ إِصْلَاحٌ لَّهُمْ خَيْرٌ وَإِن تُخَالِطُوهُمْ فَإِخْوَانُكُمْ in religion, وَاللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ الْمُفْسِدَ مِنَ الْمُصْلِحِ."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, from Ibn ‘Abbas: He said: "When Allah revealed: وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا مَالَ الْيَتِيمِ إِلَّا بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ — 'Do not approach the orphan’s property except in the best manner' (6:152), and إِن الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوَالَ الْيَتَامَى (the verse), whoever had an orphan with him went and separated his food from his food and his drink from his drink. He would set aside something from his food, and it would be kept for him until he ate it or it spoiled and was thrown away. This became difficult for them, so they mentioned it to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and Allah revealed: وَيَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْيَتَامَىٰ قُلْ إِصْلَاحٌ لَّهُمْ خَيْرٌ وَإِن تُخَالِطُوهُمْ فَإِخْوَانُكُمْ. So they mixed their food with their food and their drink with their drink."

I say: This meaning has been narrated from Sa‘id ibn Jubayr, ‘Ata’, and Qatadah.

Ayah

وَلَا تَنكِحُوا۟ ٱلْمُشْرِكَـٰتِ حَتَّىٰ يُؤْمِنَّ ۚ وَلَأَمَةٌ مُّؤْمِنَةٌ خَيْرٌ مِّن مُّشْرِكَةٍ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ ۗ وَلَا تُنكِحُوا۟ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ حَتَّىٰ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ ۚ وَلَعَبْدٌ مُّؤْمِنٌ خَيْرٌ مِّن مُّشْرِكٍ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكُمْ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلْجَنَّةِ وَٱلْمَغْفِرَةِ بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۖ وَيُبَيِّنُ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٢٢١)
Do not marry idolatresses until they embrace faith. A faithful slave girl is better than an idolatress, though she should impress you. And do not marry [your daughters] to idolaters until they embrace faith. A faithful slave is better than an idolater, though he should impress you. Those invite [others] to the Fire, but Allah invites to paradise and pardon, by His will, and He clarifies His signs for the people so that they may take admonition. (221)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: ولا تنكحوا المشركات حتى يؤمن — Do not marry idolatresses until they embrace faith. (2:221) Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "The origin of al-nikah (النكاح) — marriage or sexual intercourse — is for the contract (العقد), then it was used metaphorically for intercourse. It is impossible for it to have originally meant intercourse and then been used metaphorically for the contract, because all names for intercourse are euphemisms (كنايات), as they [the Arabs] found mentioning it repugnant, just as they found its practice [publicly] repugnant. It is impossible for one who does not intend obscenity to borrow the name of something they find loathsome for something they find commendable." End quote. This is a sound observation, except that "the contract" (العقد) must be understood as the marital bond (علقة الزوجية) rather than the specific verbal contract (العقد اللفظي) commonly known.

"Idolatresses" (المشركات) is the active participle of al-ishrak (الإشراك) — polytheism or associating partners with Allah — in the sense of taking a partner for Allah, the Glorified. It is well known that this has various levels in terms of manifestness (الظهور) and hiddenness (الخفاء), similar to disbelief (الكفر) and faith (الإيمان). Thus, the assertion of a plurality of gods and the taking of idols and intercessors is manifest polytheism (شرك ظاهر). More hidden than that is the state of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) regarding their disbelief in prophethood—specifically, their saying: "Ezra is the son of Allah" or "the Messiah is the son of Allah," and their saying: "We are the children of Allah and His beloved ones"; this is also polytheism (شرك). Even more hidden is the belief in the independence of causes (استقلال الأسباب) and relying upon them, which is polytheism (شرك), until it reaches that from which none are saved except the sincere ones (المخلصون) — those whose devotion is purified for Allah alone — which is heedlessness (الغفلة) of Allah and turning one's attention toward other than Allah, exalted is His Presence. All of this is part of polytheism (الشرك). However, the application of the verb (إطلاق الفعل) is different from the application of the description (إطلاق الوصف) and naming (التسمية) someone by it. Just as a believer who abandons one of the obligatory duties (الفرائض) has committed disbelief (كفر) regarding it, yet he is not named a "disbeliever" (كافر). The Exalted said: وَلِلَّهِ عَلَى النَّاسِ حِجُّ الْبَيْتِ (until He said) وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَنِيٌّ عَنِ الْعَالَمِينَ — ...Piligrimmage to the House is a duty prescribed by Allah for all mankind... and should anyone disbelieve, Allah is indeed independent of all the worlds. (3:97). One who abandons the Hajj is not a disbeliever (كافر) in the absolute sense; rather, he is a transgressor (فاسق) who has disbelieved in a single obligation. If the term "disbeliever" were applied to him, it would be said he is a "disbeliever in the Hajj." The same applies to other attributes used in the Quran, such as the righteous (الصالحين), the obedient (القانتين), the thankful (الشاكرين), and the purified (المتطهرين), as well as the transgressors (الفاسقين) and the wrongdoers (الظالمين), and so on; they do not equate to the verbs that share their root material. This is evident; for description (التوصيف) and naming (التسمية) have one rule, while the attribution of the verb (إسناد الفعل) has another.

Furthermore, the term "polytheists" (المشركين) in the Quran is not clearly applied to the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), unlike the term "disbelievers" (الكافرين). Rather, it is applied—where its referent is known—to other than them among the disbelievers, as in His saying, the Exalted: لَمْ يَكُنِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ وَالْمُشْرِكِينَ مُنفَكِّينَ حَتَّىٰ تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْبَيِّنَةُ — The faithless from among the People of the Book and the polytheists were not going to leave [their ways] until there should come to them a clear proof. (98:1), and His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا الْمُشْرِكُونَ نَجَسٌ فَلَا يَقْرَبُوا الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرَامَ — Indeed the polytheists are impure. So let them not approach the Holy Mosque... (9:28), and His saying, the Exalted: كَيْفَ يَكُونُ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ عَهْدٌ — How can the polytheists have a treaty...
