(9:7), and His saying, the Exalted: وَقَاتِلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ كَافَّةً — ...and fight the polytheists all together... (9:36), and His saying, the Exalted: فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ — ...then kill the polytheists wherever you find them... (9:5), among other instances.

As for His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالُوا كُونُوا هُودًا أَوْ نَصَارَىٰ تَهْتَدُوا قُلْ بَلْ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ حَنِيفًا وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ — And they say, ‘Be Jews or Christians and you will be guided.’ Say, ‘Rather [we will follow] the creed of Abraham, a Hanif, and he was not one of the polytheists.’ (2:135), the intended meaning of "polytheists" in this verse is not the Jews and Christians such that it would be an allusion (تعريض) to them. Rather, it is apparent that they are others, as evidenced by His saying, the Exalted: مَا كَانَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ يَهُودِيًّا وَلَا نَصْرَانِيًّا وَلَٰكِن كَانَ حَنِيفًا مُّسْلِمًا وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ — Abraham was neither a Jew nor a Christian, but he was a Hanif, a Muslim, and he was not one of the polytheists. (3:67). In establishing "uprightness" (الحنف) for him (peace be upon him), there is an allusion to the People of the Book and a vindication of Abraham from inclining away from the exact center toward the pure materialism (مادية) of the Jews or the pure spiritualism (معنوية) of the Christians. Rather, he (peace be upon him) was neither a Jew nor a Christian, but a Muslim to Allah, not taking any partner for Him, unlike the polytheists (المشركين) who were idol-worshippers (عبدة الأوثان).

Similarly, His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُم بِاللَّهِ إِلَّا وَهُم مُّشْرِكُونَ — And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners [to Him]. (12:106), and His saying, the Exalted: وَوَيْلٌ لِّلْمُشْرِكِينَ الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْتُونَ الزَّكَاةَ — ...and woe to the polytheists, those who do not pay the zakat... (41:6-7), and His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا سُلْطَانُهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُ وَالَّذِينَ هُم بِهِ مُشْرِكُونَ — His authority is only over those who befriend him and those who make him a partner [of Allah]. (16:100). These verses are not in the context of naming (التسمية) such that the instance to which the description of polytheism applies would be considered a "polytheist" (مشرك) who is not a believer. The proof for this is its application to some classes of believers—indeed, to all of them except for the rare and exceptional few, who are the near friends (الأولياء المقربون) among the righteous servants of Allah.

It has become clear from this explanation, despite its length, that the apparent meaning of the verse—namely His saying, the Exalted: ولا تنكحوا المشركات (Do not marry idolatresses)—limits the prohibition to the polytheistic women and men from the idolaters, excluding the People of the Book.

From here, the invalidity of the claim that this verse abrogates (ناسخة) the verse in al-Ma'idah becomes apparent, which is His saying, the Exalted: الْيَوْمَ أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ الطَّيِّبَاتُ وَطَعَامُ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ حِلٌّ لَّكُمْ وَطَعَامُكُمْ حِلٌّ لَّهُمْ وَالْمُحْصَنَاتُ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنَاتِ وَالْمُحْصَنَاتُ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ — Today all the good things have been made lawful to you. The food of those who were given the Book is lawful to you, and your food is lawful to them. [Lawful to you are] chaste women from among the faithful and chaste women from among those who were given the Book before you... (5:5).

Nor is it correct to say that this verse—namely His saying: ولا تنكحوا المشركات—and the verse in al-Mumtahanah, namely His saying: وَلَا تُمْسِكُوا بِعِصَمِ الْكَوَافِرِ — ...and do not hold on to the ties of the faithless women... (60:10), are both abrogators of the verse in al-Ma'idah. Likewise, the claim that the verse in al-Ma'idah abrogates the two verses in al-Baqarah and al-Mumtahanah is also invalid.

The reason for this invalidity is that this verse—the verse of al-Baqarah—by its apparent meaning does not include the People of the Book, while the verse of al-Ma'idah only includes the woman from the People of the Book (الكتابية). Thus, there is no relationship of contradiction (تنافي) between the two verses such that the verse of al-Baqarah would be an abrogator (ناسخة) of the verse of al-Ma'idah or abrogated (منسوخة) by it. The same applies to the verse of al-Mumtahanah, even though the term "faithless women" (الكوافر) is used therein—which is more general than "idolatresses" (المشركات) and includes the People of the Book. For while it is apparent that the application of "disbeliever" (كافر) includes the person from the People of the Book (الكتابي) in terms of naming (التسمية), such that its application to him necessitates the negation of the application of "believer" (مؤمن) to him—as evidenced by His saying, the Exalted: مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِّلَّهِ وَمَلَائِكَتِهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَجِبْرِيلَ وَمِيكَالَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ عَدُوٌّ لِّلْكَافِرِينَ — [Say,] ‘Whoever is an enemy of Allah, His angels and His apostles, and Gabriel and Michael, [let him know that] Allah is indeed the enemy of the faithless.’ (2:98)—nevertheless, the apparent meaning of the verse [in al-Mumtahanah], as will come if Allah the Almighty wills, is the establishment (إنشاء) of a rule that if a man embraces faith while having a disbelieving wife, it is forbidden for him to "hold on to her ties" (الإمساك بعصمتها), meaning to maintain the previous marriage, unless she also embraces faith. Thus, it carries no indication regarding the initial marriage (النكاح الابتدائي) to a woman from the People of the Book.

Even if we were to concede that the two verses—the verse of al-Baqarah and the verse of al-Mumtahanah—indicate the prohibition of initially marrying a woman from the People of the Book, they would not, according to the context (السياق), be abrogators of the verse in al-Ma'idah. This is because the verse in al-Ma'idah was revealed in a context of favor (الامتنان) and alleviation (التخفيف), as reflection upon its context reveals. Thus, it is resistant to being abrogated; rather, the alleviation understood from it governs (حاكم) the stringency understood from the verse of al-Baqarah. If one were to assume abrogation, the verse of al-Ma'idah would be the abrogator.

Furthermore, Surat al-Baqarah was the first surah revealed in Medina after the Migration (الهجرة), and Surat al-Mumtahanah was revealed in Medina before the Conquest of Mecca, while Surat al-Ma'idah was the last surah revealed to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, being an abrogator and not abrogated. There is no sense in the earlier abrogating the later.

His saying, the Exalted: ولأمة مؤمنة خير من مشركة ولو أعجبتكم — ...A faithful slave girl is better than an idolatress, though she should please you. (2:221). It is apparent that the "faithful slave girl" (الأمة المؤمنة) refers to the bondwoman (المملوكة) who is contrasted with the free woman (الحرة). People used to look down upon slave girls and reproach those who married them. Thus, qualifying the slave girl as being "faithful" and leaving "idolatress" (المشركة) absolute—despite the people's habit of belittling slave girls and avoiding marriage to them—indicates that the meaning is that a faithful woman, even if she is a slave girl, is better than an idolatress, even if she is a free woman of noble lineage, status, and wealth that would normally please a person.

It has been said that "slave girl" (الأمة), like "slave" (العبد) in the following sentence, refers to "the female servant of Allah" and "the male servant of Allah" [in a general sense], but this is far-fetched.

His saying, the Exalted: ولا تنكحوا المشركين حتى يؤمنوا ولعبد مؤمن — And do not marry [your women] to idolaters until they embrace faith. A faithful slave is better... (2:221). The discussion regarding this is the same as the discussion regarding the previous sentence.

His saying, the Exalted: اولئك يدعون الى النار والله يدعوا الى الجنة والمغفرة بإِذنه — They invite to the Fire, and Allah invites to paradise and forgiveness by His will. (2:221). This is an indication of the wisdom (حكمة) behind the ruling of prohibition. It is that the polytheists, due to their belief in falsehood and their following the path of misguidance, have ingrained within them base dispositions (الملكات الرذيلة) that beautify disbelief and transgression and blind the eyes from the path of truth and reality. Thus, the invitation to polytheism and the guidance toward ruin and the eventual path to the Fire have become fixed in their speech and actions. So they invite to the Fire. The believers, by contrast, through their following the path of faith and their donning the garment of God-consciousness (التقوى), invite by their speech and actions to Paradise and forgiveness by the permission of Allah, as He has permitted their invitation to faith and their being guided to success and righteousness that leads to Paradise and forgiveness.

The discourse might have been expected to say: "And these [the believers] invite to Paradise..."; however, there is a substitution (استخلاف) here on behalf of the believers. This indicates that the believers, in their invitation—indeed, in all their existential affairs (شؤونهم الوجودية) toward their Lord—are not independent in any matter apart from their Lord, blessed and exalted is He, and He is their Guardian (الولي), as the Exalted said: وَاللَّهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — ...and Allah is the Guardian of the faithful. (3:68).

There is another perspective on the verse: that the "invitation to Paradise and forgiveness" refers to the legislated ruling (الحكم المشرع) at the beginning of the verse in His saying: ولا تنكحوا المشركات حتى يؤمن (Do not marry idolatresses until they embrace faith). For establishing the ruling for the purpose of deterring the believers from mixing in social life (العشرة) with those whose proximity and intimacy only increase one's distance from Allah, the Glorified, and urging them to associate with those in whose company is proximity to Allah, the remembrance of His signs, and the observance of His commands and prohibitions, is an invitation from Allah to Paradise. This view is supported by the conclusion of this sentence with His saying: ويبين آياته للناس لعلهم يتذكرون — ...and He clarifies His signs for mankind so that they may take admonition. (2:221). It is possible that the "invitation" (الدعوة) is intended to be broader than both views, and the context in that case is not without a certain subtlety; so understand.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', regarding the verse: It was revealed concerning Marthad ibn Abi Marthad al-Ghanawi. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم sent him to Mecca to bring out some Muslims from there. He was a strong and brave man. A woman called 'Anaq invited him to herself, but he refused. There had been a friendship between them in the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية). She said: "Would you like to marry me?" He replied: "Not until I seek permission from the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم." When he returned, he sought permission to marry her.

I say: Al-Suyuti narrated this meaning in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Abbas.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Al-Wahidi narrated via al-Suddi from Abu Malik from Ibn Abbas regarding this verse: ولأمة مؤمنة خير من مشركة (A faithful slave girl is better than an idolatress). He said: It was revealed concerning Abdullah ibn Rawahah. He had a black slave girl and he became angry with her and slapped her. Then he became frightened and went to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم and informed him of what happened. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said to him: "What is she, O Abdullah?" He replied: "She fasts, prays, performs ablution well, and bears witness that there is no god but Allah and that you are His Messenger." He said: "O Abdullah, she is a believer." Abdullah said: "By the One Who sent you with the truth, I shall set her free and marry her." He did so, and some of the Muslims criticized him, saying: "He married a slave girl." They wanted to marry into the polytheists and marry [their women] to them out of desire for their noble lineage. So Allah revealed concerning them: ولأمة مؤمنة خير من مشركة (A faithful slave girl is better than an idolatress).

Also therein, from Muqatil regarding the verse ولأمة مؤمنة (A faithful slave girl): He said: It has reached us that she was a slave girl belonging to Hudhayfah; he emancipated her and Hudhayfah married her.

I say: There is no contradiction between these narrations regarding the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول), as it is possible for several incidents to occur, after which a verse is revealed that encompasses the ruling for all of them. There are conflicting narrations reported regarding whether His saying, the Exalted: ولا تنكحوا المشركات حتى يؤمن (Do not marry idolatresses until they embrace faith) is an abrogator of His saying: والمحصنات من الذين أُوتوا الكتاب من قبلكم (and chaste women from among those who were given the Book before you) or is abrogated by it; these will be presented to you in the commentary on the verse from Surat al-Ma'idah.

Ayahs

وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْمَحِيضِ ۖ قُلْ هُوَ أَذًى فَٱعْتَزِلُوا۟ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فِى ٱلْمَحِيضِ ۖ وَلَا تَقْرَبُوهُنَّ حَتَّىٰ يَطْهُرْنَ ۖ فَإِذَا تَطَهَّرْنَ فَأْتُوهُنَّ مِنْ حَيْثُ أَمَرَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلتَّوَّٰبِينَ وَيُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَطَهِّرِينَ (٢٢٢)
They ask you concerning [intercourse during] menses. Say, ‘It is hurtful.’ So keep away from wives during the menses, and do not approach them till they are clean. And when they become clean, go into them as Allah has commanded you. Indeed Allah loves the penitent and He loves those who keep clean. (222)
نِسَآؤُكُمْ حَرْثٌ لَّكُمْ فَأْتُوا۟ حَرْثَكُمْ أَنَّىٰ شِئْتُمْ ۖ وَقَدِّمُوا۟ لِأَنفُسِكُمْ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّكُم مُّلَـٰقُوهُ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٢٢٣)
Your women are a tillage for you, so come to your tillage whenever you like, and send ahead for your souls, and be Godwary, and know that you will encounter Him; and give good news to the faithful. (223)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: ويسألونك عن المحيض قل هو أذى (They ask you concerning menstruation. Say, ‘It is a hurt.’) [2:222] etc. The term al-maḥīḍ (المحيض) is a verbal noun like al-ḥayḍ (الحيض). It is said: "The woman menstruated (حاضت), she menstruates (تحيض), with a menstruation (حيضاً ومحيضاً)" when her nature discharges the well-known blood possessing the familiar characteristics specific to women. For this reason, it is said: "She is menstruating (حائض)," just as it is said: "She is pregnant (حامل)."

Hurt (الأذى) is said to be harm (الضرر), but this is not without some scrutiny. If it were identical to harm, it would be correct to contrast it with benefit (النفع), just as harm is the opposite of benefit, but this is not the case. One says: "A harmful (مضر) or injurious (ضار) medicine," but if one said: "A hurting (موذ) medicine," it would convey a different meaning. Furthermore, the Exalted says: لَن يَضُرُّوكُمْ إِلَّا أَذًى (They will never harm you, except for a [passing] hurt) [3:111]; if one were to say: "They will never harm you except for a harm," the speech would be corrupted. Also, its being synonymous with harm is not apparent in examples such as His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ (Indeed those who offend Allah and His Apostle...) [33:57], and His saying, the Exalted: لِمَ تُؤْذُونَنِي وَقَد تَّعْلَمُونَ أَنِّي رَسُولُ اللَّهِ إِلَيْكُمْ (Why do you offend me, when you certainly know that I am Allah’s apostle to you?) [61:5]. Thus, it appears that hurt (الأذى) is that which happens to a thing that is incompatible with its nature (طبعه); in this way, the meaning of harm (الضرر) may apply to it in a certain respect.

The naming of menstruation as a "hurt" (أذى) according to this meaning is because this blood, attributed to the habit of women, results from a specific function of her nature that affects the temperament of the natural blood produced by the digestive system. It corrupts a portion of it from its natural state and discharges it into the womb for purification, or for the nourishment of the fetus, or to prepare milk for breastfeeding. As for those who say that hurt (الأذى) means harm (الضرر), it has been said that what is meant by menstruation (المحيض) here is approaching women during the state of menses; thus the meaning would be: "They ask you about approaching them in this state."

The response was that it is a harm (ضرر), and indeed it is so. Physicians have mentioned that during the period of menses, nature is occupied with purifying the womb and preparing it for pregnancy. Sexual intercourse disrupts the order of this process and thus harms the results of this natural function, such as pregnancy and other matters.

His saying, the Exalted: فاعتزلوا النساء في المحيض ولا تقربوهن (So keep away from women during their monthly courses, and do not approach them...) [2:222]. Withdrawal (الاعتزال) is to take seclusion (العزلة) and to avoid social mixing and cohabitation. It is said: "I set aside (عزلت) his portion" when you distinguish it and place it to one side by separating it from the other portions. Proximity (القرب) is the opposite of distance (البعد). What is meant by withdrawal (الاعتزال) here is the cessation of intercourse in the site of the blood, as we shall explain.

People held various views regarding the matter of menstruation. The Jews were severe in this regard; they would separate from women during menstruation in matters of food, drink, sitting, and sleeping. In the Torah, there are severe rulings regarding them during menses, and regarding anyone who approaches them in sitting, sleeping, touching, and so forth. As for the Christians, they had nothing that prevented gathering with them or approaching them in any way. As for the polytheists among the Arabs, they had no such restrictions, except that the Arabs residing in Medina and its surroundings were influenced by some of the Jewish customs regarding menstruation and the severity of cohabitation in this state. Others perhaps preferred approaching women during menstruation, believing that a child conceived then would become a shedder of blood and fond of slaughter—qualities that were considered commendable among the Bedouin tribes.

In any case, His saying, the Exalted: فاعتزلوا النساء في المحيض (So keep away from women during their monthly courses), even if its literal sense suggests a command for absolute withdrawal as the Jews practiced—reinforced by His saying a second time: ولا تقربوهن (and do not approach them)—nevertheless, His saying at the end: فأتوهن من حيث امركم الله (then come to them as Allah has enjoined you)—and it is known that this refers to the site of the blood—serves as a contextual clue (قرينة) that His saying: "withdraw" and "do not approach" are used as metonyms (كناية) rather than explicit literal commands. What is meant is only the cessation of intercourse in the site of the blood, not absolute social withdrawal, nor the absolute prohibition of all forms of enjoyment and pleasure.

Thus, Islam has taken a middle path (طريقاً وسطاً) in the matter of menstruation between the total severity of the Jews and the absolute negligence of the Christians. It is the prohibition of intercourse in the site of the blood while permitting what is besides that. In His saying: في المحيض (during their monthly courses), the explicit noun was placed where a pronoun would normally be; the literal expectation was to say: "withdraw from women in it (فيه)." The reason for this is that the first mention of menstruation (المحيض) referred to the verbal noun (the state), while the second refers to the time of menstruation; thus the second is different from the first, and replacing it with a pronoun referring to a different meaning would not convey the intended sense.

His saying, the Exalted: حتى يطهرن فإِذا تطهرن فأتوهن من حيث أمركم الله (...until they become clean. But when they become clean, come to them as Allah has enjoined you.) [2:222]. Purity (الطهارة) and its opposite, impurity (النجاسة), are among the concepts circulating within the religion of Islam, possessing established rulings and properties that encompass a vast portion of religious issues. Through frequent usage, these two terms have become legal realities (حقائق شرعية) or conventional religious terms (متشرعة) according to the terminology used in the discipline of the principles of jurisprudence (أصول الفقه).

The origin of purity (الطهارة) according to its meaning is something known to people across their various tongues and languages. From this, it is known that it is one of the concepts that man recognizes throughout his life, not being specific to one people over another or one era over another.

The foundation of life is built upon the utilization of material things to reach the goals of life and benefit from them for the needs of living. Man naturally seeks everything for the benefit, property, and utility it contains, and desires it for that reason. The broadest of these benefits are those related to nutrition and reproduction.

Sometimes, an accidental factor (عارض) occurs to a thing that necessitates a change in the qualities that made it desirable to one's nature—primarily taste, smell, and color. This causes the nature to feel aversion and lose its desire for it. This is what is called impurity (النجاسة), and by it, man deems a thing filthy (يستقذر) and avoids it. Its opposite—the thing remaining in its original state of benefit and utility by which the nature desires it—is purity (الطهارة). Thus, purity and impurity are two existential attributes (وصفان وجوديان) in things insofar as they possess a quality that necessitates desire for them or a quality that necessitates dislike and aversion.

Man first became aware of these two concepts in sensory matters (المحسوسات), then began to generalize them to non-sensory, intellectual matters (الأمور المعقولة) due to the existence of the underlying meaning of desire and aversion in them, such as lineages, actions, ethics, beliefs, and speech.

This is a summary of the meaning of purity and impurity among people. As for cleanliness (النظافة), integrity (النزاهة), sanctity (القدس), and glorification (السبحان), they are terms close in meaning to purity (الطهارة). However, cleanliness (النظافة) is the purity that returns to a thing after a prior filthiness, and its usage is specific to sensory things. Integrity (النزاهة) originally means distance (البعد), and its application to purity is metaphorical (استعارة). Sanctity (القدس) and glorification (السبحان) are specific to intellectual and spiritual matters. As for filth (القذارة) and abomination (الرجس), they are terms close in meaning to impurity (النجاسة). However, the root of filth (القذارة) is the meaning of distance; it is said: "A distant she-camel (ناقة قذرور)" when she leaves the side of the camels and stays away, and "A filthy man (رجل قاذورة)" who does not mix with people due to his bad character. A man is called "shunned (مقذر)" when people avoid him. It is said: "I loathed (قذرت) the thing" when you disliked it. Accordingly, the original use of filth (القذارة) in the sense of impurity is metaphorical, because the impurity of a thing necessitates man's distancing himself from it. Similarly, abomination (الرجس) and defilement (الرجز)—with a kasrah on the rā’—originally seem to mean horror and loneliness; thus, their indication of impurity is metaphorical.

Islam has considered the meaning of purity and impurity and generalized them in both the sensory and the intellectual realms, applying them to universal doctrines (المعارف الكلية) and established laws. The Exalted says: ولا تقربوهن حتى يطهرن (...and do not approach them until they become clean), which refers to becoming clear of menstruation and the cessation of blood. The Exalted says: وَثِيَابَكَ فَطَهِّرْ (and purify your clothes) [74:4]. The Exalted says: وَلَٰكِن يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ (...but He desires to purify you) [5:6]. The Exalted says: أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَمْ يُرِدِ اللَّهُ أَن يُطَهِّرَ قُلُوبَهُمْ (They are the ones whose hearts Allah did not desire to purify) [5:41]. The Exalted says: لَّا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ (None shall touch it except the purified) [56:79].

The Islamic law (الشريعة) has counted several things as impure, such as blood, urine, feces, and semen from humans and certain animals, as well as carrion and swine, as essential impurities (أعياناً نجسة). It has ruled that it is obligatory to avoid them in prayer, eating, and drinking. It has also counted as purity matters such as the removal of external impurity (الطهارة الخبثية) resulting from contact with essentially impure things, and the removal of ritual impurity (الطهارة الحدثية) through ablution (الوضوء) and the ritual bath (الغسل) according to the methods established in the law and explained in the books of jurisprudence (الفقه).

It has already been explained that Islam is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts; thus, it refers the branches (الفروع) back to a single root, which is monotheism, and spreads that single root into its branches.

From here it becomes clear: that the root of monotheism is the "Great Purity" (الطهارة الكبرى) before Allah, the Glorified. After this purity, the rest of the universal doctrines are purities for man; after that come the roots of virtuous ethics, and after that the rulings established for the welfare of this world and the Hereafter. Upon this principle, the previously mentioned verses apply, such as His saying: يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ (...He desires to purify you) [5:6], and His saying: وَيُطَهِّرَكُمْ تَطْهِيرًا (...and purify you with a thorough purification) [33:33], among other verses concerning the meaning of purity.

Let us return to our subject. His saying, the Exalted: حتى يطهرن (...until they become clean) means until the blood ceases for them, which is the state of purity (الطهر) after menstruation. His saying: فإِذا تطهرن (But when they become clean) means when they wash the site of the blood or perform the ritual bath (الغسل). His saying: فأتوهن من حيث امركم الله (...come to them as Allah has enjoined you) is a command that indicates permissibility (الجواز) because it occurs after a prohibition. It is a metonym (كناية) for the command of sexual intercourse, expressed with the sublime divine etiquette (الأدب الإلهي) befitting the Noble Quran. The restriction of the command to "come to them" with the phrase "as Allah has enjoined you" completes this etiquette. For sexual intercourse might, at first glance, be considered a vain or idle play (لغواً ولهواً); thus He restricted it to being something Allah has commanded with a creative command (امراً تكوينياً) to indicate that it is something by which the order of the human species is completed in its life and survival. Therefore, it should not be counted as vanity or play, but rather as one of the fundamental creative laws (النواميس التكوينية).

This verse—namely His saying: فأتوهن من حيث امركم الله—is similar to His saying: فَالْآنَ بَاشِرُوهُنَّ وَابْتَغُوا مَا كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ (...So now consort with them and seek what Allah has ordained for you) [2:187], and His saying: فَأْتُوا حَرْثَكُمْ أَنَّىٰ شِئْتُمْ وَقَدِّمُوا لِأَنفُسِكُمْ (...so come to your tilth however you wish, and send ahead for yourselves) [2:223], in terms of context. Thus, it appears that what is meant by the command to "come to them" in the verse is the creative command (الأمر التكويني) indicated by the equipping of man with organs and faculties that lead to reproduction, just as what is meant by "ordained" (كتابة) in His saying: "and seek what Allah has ordained for you" is also that, which is apparent. It is also possible that what is meant by the command is the collective obligation (الإيجاب الكفائي) related to marriage and procreation, similar to other collective obligations without which the life of the species cannot be completed, but this is remote.

Some commentators have used this verse to argue for the prohibition of anal intercourse with women, but this is among the weakest and poorest of arguments. It is based either on arguing from the "implication of the title" (مفهوم اللقب) of His saying: فأتوهن (come to them), the non-authority of which is certain, or on the argument that a command implies a prohibition of its specific opposite (الضد الخاص), which is also certainly weak.

Furthermore, if the argument were based on the command in His saying: فأتوهن (come to them), it occurs after a prohibition and thus does not indicate obligation (الوجوب). If it were based on the command in His saying: من حيث امركم الله (as Allah has enjoined you), then if it is a creative command, it falls outside the scope of linguistic indication (الدلالة اللفظية); and if it is a legislative command, it would be for a collective obligation. The indication of a prohibition of the opposite—even if granted—only applies to a personal, mandatory, sovereign command (الأمر الإيجابي العيني المولوي).

His saying, the Exalted: إِن الله يحب التوابين ويحب المتطهرين (Indeed Allah loves the penitent and He loves those who keep clean.) [2:222]. Repentance (التوبة) is the return to Allah, the Glorified. To keep clean (التطهر) is to adopt purity and accept it; thus, it is an uprooting of filth and a return to the root, which is purity. The two meanings coincide in the context of Allah's commands and prohibitions, especially in the context of purity and impurity. Complying with one of His commands and abstaining from all He has forbidden is a purification (تطهر) from the filth of opposition and corruption, and a repentance (توبة) and return to Him, exalted is His state. Because of this suitability, He justified the ruling He mentioned by saying: "Indeed Allah loves the penitent and He loves those who keep clean," for it is necessary that the stated reason applies to all the rulings mentioned—namely His saying: "So keep away from women during their monthly courses" and His saying: "then come to them as Allah has enjoined you." The verse—namely His saying: "Indeed Allah loves the penitent and He loves those who keep clean"—is absolute (مطلقة) and unrestricted, thus encompassing all levels of repentance and purity as previously explained. It is not unlikely that the intensive form (المبالغة) is intended in His saying: المتطهرين (those who keep clean), just as the intensive form was used in: التوابين (the penitent). This results in the indication of frequency in repentance and purity, both in terms of type and number. That is: Allah loves all types of repentance, whether through seeking forgiveness, or by complying with every command and prohibition of His obligations, or by adopting every true belief; and He loves all types of purification, whether through the ritual bath (الغسل), ablution (الوضوء), or purification through righteous deeds and true sciences; and He loves the repetition of repentance and the repetition of purification.

His saying, the Exalted: نسائكم حرث لكم فأتوا حرثكم أنى شئتم (Your women are a tilth for you, so come to your tilth however you wish...) [2:223]. Tilth (الحرث) is a verbal noun meaning cultivation, and like cultivation, it is applied to the land in which plowing and sowing are performed. Annā (أنى) is one of the conditional nouns used for time, like "when" (متى), and it is sometimes used for place as well. The Exalted says: يَا مَرْيَمُ أَنَّىٰ لَكِ هَٰذَا قَالَتْ هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ اللَّهِ (O Mary, from where has this come to you? She said, ‘It is from Allah.’) [3:37]. If it means place, the meaning would be: "from whatever place you wish"; if it means time, the meaning would be: "at whatever time you wish." In any case, it conveys absolute application (الإطلاق) according to its meaning, especially since it is restricted by His saying: شئتم (you wish). This is what prevents the command—namely His saying: فأتوا حرثكم (so come to your tilth)—from indicating obligation (الوجوب), as there is no meaning in obligating an act while referring it to the choice and will of the subject.

Furthermore, the placement of His saying: نساءكم حرث لكم (Your women are a tilth for you) before this ruling, as well as the second reference to women as "tilth," is not without indication that what is meant is the latitude in approaching women in terms of the place or time from which they are sought, rather than the place that is sought from them. If the absolute application is in terms of place, the verse does not address temporal application, and it does not conflict with His saying in the previous verse: "So keep away from women during their monthly courses and do not approach them until they become clean." If it is in terms of time, it is restricted by the verse of menstruation. The proof for this is that the verse of menstruation contains that which refuses to be abrogated by the verse of tilth—namely, the indication in the verse of menstruation that menstruation is a "hurt" (أذى) and that this is the reason for legislating the prohibition of approaching them during it, and menstruation is always a hurt. Also, its indication that the prohibition of intercourse during menstruation is a type of purification from filth, and Allah, the Glorified, always loves purification and favors His servants by purifying them, as He says: مَا يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيَجْعَلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْ حَرَجٍ وَلَٰكِن يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ وَلِيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكُمْ (Allah does not desire to put you to hardship, but He desires to purify you and to complete His blessing upon you...) [5:6].

It is well known that this style of speech does not accept restriction by something like His saying: "Your women are a tilth for you, so come to your tilth however you wish," which contains, firstly, a latitude—a reason that was present along with the reason for prohibition at the time of its legislation and had no effect; thus its effect cannot be imagined after the legislation has been established. Secondly, it contains a concluding remark (تذييل) such as His saying: "and send ahead for yourselves, and be God-conscious, and know that you will encounter Him, and give good news to the believers." From this explanation, it appears that the verse of tilth is not suitable to abrogate the verse of menstruation, whether it was revealed before it or after it.

The summary of the verse's meaning is: the relationship of women to human society is like the relationship of tilth to man. Just as tilth is needed to preserve seeds and obtain the nourishment required to maintain and preserve life, so too are women needed by the species for the preservation of progeny and the continuation of the species. This is because Allah, the Glorified, has placed the formation of man and the shaping of his matter into his form within the nature of their wombs. Then He made the nature of men—who carry some of the original matter—inclined and leaning toward them, and placed between the two groups affection and mercy. Since this is the case, the creative purpose (الغرض التكويني) of this arrangement is to provide the means for the survival of the species. Thus, there is no meaning in restricting this act to one time over another, or one place over another, if it leads to that purpose and does not conflict with another matter that is obligatory in itself and cannot be neglected. By what we have mentioned, the meaning of His saying: وقدموا لانفسكم (and send ahead for yourselves) becomes clear.

Among the strange interpretations is the use of His saying: "Your women are a tilth for you..." as evidence for the permissibility of coitus interruptus (العزل) during intercourse; the verse does not look toward this type of absolute application. Similar to this is the interpretation of His saying: "and send ahead for yourselves" as the mention of Allah's name (التسمية) before intercourse.

His saying, the Exalted: وقدموا لانفسكم واتقوا الله واعلموا أنكم ملاقوه وبشر المؤمنين (...and send ahead for yourselves, and be God-conscious, and know that you will encounter Him, and give good news to the believers.) [2:223]. It has become clear that the meaning of His saying: "send ahead for yourselves"—addressing men or the collective of men and women—is the urging toward the preservation of the species through marriage and procreation. Allah, the Glorified, desires nothing from the human species and its survival except the life of His religion and the manifestation of His monotheism and worship through their general godwariness. He says: وَمَا خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ (I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me.) [51:56]. Thus, if He commands them with something related to their life and survival, He only intends for them to reach the worship of their Lord through it, not for them to cling to the earth, immerse themselves in the lusts of the belly and the private parts, and wander in the valleys of error and heedlessness.

So the meaning of His saying: "send ahead for yourselves," even if it refers to procreation and the sending ahead of new individuals into human society—which constantly loses individuals to death and annihilation and decreases in number over time—is not for their own sake, but rather to reach the preservation of the remembrance of Allah, the Glorified, through the survival of the progeny and the emergence of righteous individuals with righteous deeds whose rewards and goodness return to themselves and to their righteous fathers who were the cause of their existence, as He says: وَنَكْتُبُ مَا قَدَّمُوا وَآثَارَهُمْ (...and We write what they have sent ahead and their footsteps) [34:12].

By what we have mentioned, it is supported that what is meant by "sending ahead for themselves" is the sending ahead of righteous deeds for the Day of Resurrection, as He says: يَوْمَ يَنظُرُ الْمَرْءُ مَا قَدَّمَتْ يَدَاهُ (the day when a man will look at what his hands have sent ahead...) [78:40], and He also says: وَمَا تُقَدِّمُوا لِأَنفُسِكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ تَجِدُوهُ عِندَ اللَّهِ هُوَ خَيْرًا وَأَعْظَمَ أَجْرًا (...Whatever good you send ahead for yourselves you will find it with Allah [to be] better and greater in reward.) [73:20]. Thus, His saying: "and send ahead for yourselves, and be God-conscious, and know that you will encounter Him..." is similar in context to His saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَلْتَنظُرْ نَفْسٌ مَّا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ خَبِيرٌ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (O you who have faith! Be God-conscious, and let every soul consider what it sends ahead for tomorrow, and be God-conscious. Indeed Allah is well aware of what you do.) [59:18].

What is meant (and Allah knows best) by His saying: "and send ahead for yourselves" is the sending ahead of righteous deeds, including the sending ahead of children in the hope of their righteousness for society. By His saying: "and be God-conscious (واتقوا الله)," it means godwariness through righteous deeds in approaching the tilth, not transgressing the limits of Allah, not being negligent toward Allah, and not violating the sanctities of Allah. By His saying: "and know that you will encounter Him (واعلموا أنكم ملاقوه)..." it is a command for godwariness in the sense of fearing the Day of Encounter and the evil of the Reckoning, just as what is meant by His saying in the verse of al-Ḥashr: "and be God-conscious. Indeed Allah is well aware of what you do" is godwariness in the sense of fear. The absolute use of the command to "know" while intending its necessary consequence—which is vigilance, self-preservation, and godwariness—is common in speech. He says: وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَحُولُ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَقَلْبِهِ (...and know that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart) [8:24], meaning: be wary of His intervention between you and your hearts. Since righteous deeds and the fear of the Day of Reckoning are among the specific requirements of faith, He concluded His speech by saying: "and give good news to the believers," just as He began the verse of al-Ḥashr by saying: "O you who have faith."

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthūr: It is narrated by Ahmad, ‘Abd ibn Ḥumayd, al-Dārimī, Muslim, Abū Dāwūd, al-Tirmidhī, al-Nasā’ī, Ibn Mājah, Abū Ya‘lā, Ibn al-Mundhir, Abū Ḥātim, al-Naḥḥās in his Nāsikh, Abū Ḥayyān, and al-Bayhaqī in his Sunan, from Anas: "When a woman among the Jews menstruated, they would turn her out of the house, and they would not eat with her, nor drink with her, nor cohabit with them in the houses. So the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم was asked about that, and Allah revealed: ويسألونك عن المحيض قل هو أذى فاعتزلوا النساء في المحيض (They ask you concerning menstruation. Say, ‘It is a hurt.’ So keep away from women during their monthly courses...) [2:222]. Then the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said: 'Cohabit with them in the houses and do everything except sexual intercourse (النكاح).' This reached the Jews, and they said: 'This man does not want to leave anything of our matter except that he opposes us in it.' Then Usayd ibn Ḥuḍayr and ‘Abbād ibn Bishr came and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, the Jews said such and such; shall we not then have sexual intercourse with them?' The face of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم changed until we thought he was angry with them. They left, and they were met by a gift of milk for the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, so he sent for them and gave them to drink, and they knew he was not angry with them."

In al-Durr al-Manthūr, from al-Suddī regarding His saying: ويسألونك عن المحيض (They ask you concerning menstruation), he said: "The one who asked about that was Thābit ibn al-Daḥdāḥ."

I say: The like of it has been narrated from Muqātil as well.

In al-Tahdhīb, from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) in a narration regarding His saying, the Exalted: فأتوهن من حيث أمركم الله (...come to them as Allah has enjoined you), he (peace be upon him) said: "This is regarding the seeking of a child; so seek the child from where Allah has enjoined you."

In al-Kāfī: Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) was asked: "What is allowed for the husband of a menstruating woman from her?" He (peace be upon him) said: "Everything except the vagina (القبل) itself."

Also in it, from him (peace be upon him): regarding a woman whose menstrual blood ceases in her final days, he (peace be upon him) said: "If her husband is overcome by lust, let him command her to wash her private parts, then he may touch her if he wishes, before she performs the ritual bath (تغتسل)." In another narration: "And the ritual bath (الغسل) is more beloved to me."

I say: The narrations regarding these meanings are very numerous, and they support the reading of yaṭhurna (يطهرن) with the lightened pronunciation (تخفيف), which refers to the cessation of blood. As it has been said: the difference between yaṭhurna (يطهرن) and yataṭahharna (يتطهرن) is that the second implies the acceptance of purity, which contains the meaning of choice and thus suits the ritual bath (الاغتسال), unlike the first, which is the attainment of purity and does not contain the meaning of choice, thus suiting the purity through the cessation of blood. If what is meant by al-taṭahhur (التطهر) is washing (الغسل) — with a fatḥah on the ghayn — it indicates the recommendation of that; and if it is the ritual bath (الغسل) — with a ḍammah on the ghayn — it indicates the recommendation of intercourse after the ritual bath, as he (peace be upon him) indicated by saying: "And the ritual bath is more beloved to me," not the prohibition of intercourse before it—meaning in the interval between purity (الطهارة) and the ritual bath (التطهر)—because that would contradict yaṭhurna being the limit set for the prohibition. Understand this.

Also in al-Kāfī, from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِن الله يحب التوابين ويحب المتطهرين (Indeed Allah loves the penitent and He loves those who keep clean), he said: "People used to clean themselves with cotton and stones, then the ritual ablution (الوضوء) was introduced, and it is a noble character. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم commanded it and performed it, so Allah revealed in His Book: 'Indeed Allah loves the penitent and He loves those who keep clean.'"

I say: The reports regarding this meaning are numerous. In some of them: "The first to clean himself with water was Barā’ ibn ‘Āzib, so the verse was revealed and the Sunnah was established by it."

In it, from Sallām ibn al-Mustanīr, who said: "I was with Abū Ja‘far (peace be upon him) when Ḥumrān ibn A‘yan entered and asked him about several things. When Ḥumrān intended to stand up, he said to Abū Ja‘far (peace be upon him): 'I inform you—may Allah prolong your life and grant us enjoyment of you—that we come to you and do not leave your presence until our hearts soften and our souls become detached from the world, and what is in the hands of people of these riches becomes insignificant to us. Then we leave your presence, and when we are with people and merchants, we love the world.' He said: Abū Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: 'It is only the hearts; sometimes they are hard and sometimes they are soft.' Then Abū Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: 'Indeed, the companions of Muhammad said: "O Messenger of Allah, we fear hypocrisy (النفاق) for ourselves." He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said: "And why do you fear that?" They said: "When we are with you, and you remind us and urge us, we become fearful and forget the world and become ascetic until it is as if we are witnessing the Hereafter, Paradise, and the Fire while we are with you. But when we leave your presence and enter these houses, and smell the children and see the family and the wife, we nearly turn away from the state we were in with you, until it is as if we were not upon anything. Do you fear for us that this might be hypocrisy?" The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said to them: "Not at all; these are the steps of Satan who makes you desire the world. By Allah, if you were to remain in the state you described yourselves to be in, the angels would shake hands with you and you would walk upon water. And were it not that you sin and then seek forgiveness from Allah the Exalted, Allah would have created a creation that would sin and then seek forgiveness from Allah the Exalted, and He would forgive them. Indeed, the believer is one who is tested and penitent (مفتن تواب). Have you not heard the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: 'Indeed Allah loves the penitent and He loves those who keep clean,' and the Exalted says: 'Seek forgiveness from your Lord, then repent to Him'?"'"

I say: al-‘Ayyāshī narrated the like of it in his Tafsir. His saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "if you were to remain in the state..." is an allusion to the station of guardianship (مقام الولاية) — the state of turning away from the world and overseeing what is with Allah, the Glorified. A portion of the discussion regarding this has already passed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ إِذا أَصَابَتْهُم مُّصِيبَةٌ (those who, when an affliction visits them...) [2:156].

His saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: "were it not that you sin..." is an allusion to the secret of destiny (سر القدر) — the extension of the ruling of His names, the Exalted, to the level of actions and particular events according to the requirements of the concepts of the names. The discussion on this will come under His saying, the Exalted: وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ (There is not a thing but that its treasuries are with Us, and We do not send it down except by a known measure) [15:21], and other verses of destiny. His saying: "Have you not heard the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: 'Indeed Allah loves the penitent'..." is from the speech of Abū Ja‘far (peace be upon him), and the address is to Ḥumrān. In it is the interpretation of repentance and purification as returning to Allah the Exalted from sins and removing the filth of sins from the soul and their rust from the heart. This is an instance of deriving the levels of a ruling from the ruling of some of its levels, similar to what has been narrated regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَّا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ (None shall touch it except the purified) [56:79], using it as evidence that the knowledge of the Book is with the purified ones from the People of the House (أهل البيت), and using it as evidence for the prohibition of touching the script of the Quran without purity.

Just as creation descends from the treasuries that are with Allah the Exalted until it reaches the end of the world of measures (عالم المقادير) according to His saying: "There is not a thing but that its treasuries are with Us...", so too the rulings of measures do not descend except by passing through the stations of realities (منازل الحقائق). Understand this, and further clarification will come in the discussion of His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ مِنْهُ آيَاتٌ مُّحْكَمَاتٌ (It is He who has sent down to you the Book. Parts of it are definitive verses...) [3:7].

From here, one can find support for what was previously alluded to: that what is meant by repentance and purification in the verse according to the literal revelation (ظاهر التنزيل) is washing with water; thus, it is the returning of the body to Allah the Exalted by removing filth from it.

It also becomes clear the meaning of what was previously quoted from the Tafsir of al-Qummī from his (peace be upon him) saying: "Allah revealed to Abraham (peace be upon him) the Ḥanīfiyyah (الحنيفية) — the primordial upright religion — which is purity (الطهارة). It consists of ten things: five in the head and five in the body. As for those in the head: trimming the mustache, letting the beard grow, cutting the hair, using the tooth-stick (السواك), and cleaning between the teeth (الخلال). As for those in the body: removing hair from the body, circumcision (الختان), clipping the nails, the ritual bath from major impurity (الجنابة), and purification with water. This is the pure Ḥanīfiyyah brought by Abraham; it has not been abrogated and will not be abrogated until the Day of Resurrection..." the tradition. The reports stating that these matters are part of purity are numerous, and among them is that the depilatory agent (النورة) is a purifier.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyāshī: regarding His saying, the Exalted: نسائكم حرث لكم (Your women are a tilth for you...), from Mu‘ammar ibn Khallād from Imam al-Riḍā (peace be upon him) that he said: "What do you say regarding approaching women in their posteriors (أعجازهن)?" I said: "It has reached me that the people of Medina see no harm in it." He (peace be upon him) said: "The Jews used to say that if a man approaches her from behind, his child will be born cross-eyed. So Allah revealed: 'Your women are a tilth for you, so come to your tilth however you wish,' meaning from behind or from the front, contrary to the saying of the Jews, in their vaginas."

In it, from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he (peace be upon him) said: "From her front and from her back, in the vagina (القبل)."

In it, from Abū Baṣīr from Abū ‘Abdillāh (peace be upon him), he said: "I asked him about a man approaching his wife in her anus (دبرها), and he disliked that and said: 'Beware of the posteriors (محاش) of women.' And he said: 'The meaning of "Your women are a tilth for you, so come to your tilth however you wish" is: at whatever hour you wish.'"

In it, from al-Fatḥ ibn Yazīd al-Jurjānī, who said: "I wrote to al-Riḍā (peace be upon him) regarding the like of this, and the reply came: 'You asked about one who approaches a girl in her anus; the woman is a plaything, she is not to be harmed, and she is a tilth as Allah said.'"

I say: The narrations regarding these meanings from the Imams of the People of the House are numerous, narrated in al-Kāfī, al-Tahdhīb, and the Tafsirs of al-‘Ayyāshī and al-Qummī. They all indicate that the verse does not indicate more than approaching them from their front. Upon this, the saying of Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) in the narration of al-‘Ayyāshī from ‘Abdullāh ibn Abī Ya‘fūr can be understood, who said: "I asked Abū ‘Abdillāh (peace be upon him) about approaching women in their posteriors (أعجازهن). He said: 'There is no harm,' then he recited this verse: 'Your women are a tilth for you, so come to your tilth however you wish.'"

I say: It appears that what is meant by approaching them in their posteriors is approaching from behind into the vagina, and the use of the verse as evidence for that is as testified by the previously mentioned report of Mu‘ammar ibn Khallād.

In al-Durr al-Manthūr: Ibn ‘Asākir narrated from Jābir ibn ‘Abdillāh, who said: "The Anṣār used to approach their women lying on their sides, while the Quraysh used to practice many positions. A man from the Quraysh married a woman from the Anṣār and wanted to approach her [in his way], but she said: 'No, except as it is done.' This was reported to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, so he revealed: 'so come to your tilth however you wish,' meaning standing, sitting, or lying down, as long as it is in one passage (صمام واحد)."

I say: This meaning has been narrated through several chains from the companions regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) of the verse, and the narration regarding it from Imam al-Riḍā (peace be upon him) has already passed.

His saying: "in one passage (صمام واحد)"—meaning in one path—is a metonym for the intercourse being in the vagina only. For the narrations are abundant from their chains regarding the prohibition of approaching the posteriors of women; they narrated them through many chains from several companions from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم. Although the view of the Imams of the People of the House is permissibility with severe dislike—according to what our companions narrated through their connected chains to them (peace be upon them)—they (peace be upon them) did not rely for this on His saying: "Your women are a tilth for you, so come to your tilth however you wish," as previously explained. Rather, they used as evidence His saying, recounting from Lot: قَالَ هَٰؤُلَاءِ بَنَاتِي إِن كُنتُمْ فَاعِلِينَ (He said, ‘These are my daughters, if you should do [anything].’) [15:71], where he (peace be upon him) offered them his daughters while knowing they did not desire the vaginas, and the ruling was not abrogated by anything in the Quran.

The ruling, nonetheless, is not agreed upon in what they narrated from the companions. It has been narrated from ‘Abdullāh ibn ‘Umar, Mālik ibn Anas, Abū Sa‘īd al-Khudrī, and others that they saw no harm in it, and they used to argue for its permissibility using His saying: "Your women are a tilth for you..." until it was reported from Ibn ‘Umar that the verse was revealed specifically to clarify its permissibility.

In al-Durr al-Manthūr, from al-Dāraquṭnī in Gharā’ib Mālik, with a chain to Nāfi‘, who said: "Ibn ‘Umar said to me: 'Hold the Muṣḥaf for me, O Nāfi‘!' He read until he reached: 'Your women are a tilth for you, so come to your tilth however you wish.' He said to me: 'Do you know, O Nāfi‘, regarding whom this verse was revealed?' I said: 'No.' He said: 'It was revealed regarding a man from the Anṣār who approached his wife in her anus, and people found that to be a grave matter. So Allah revealed: "Your women are a tilth for you, so come to your tilth however you wish."' I said to him: 'From her back into her vagina?' He said: 'No, except in her anus.'"

I say: This meaning has been narrated from Ibn ‘Umar through many chains. He said: Ibn ‘Abd al-Barr said: "The narration with this meaning from Ibn ‘Umar is authentic, well-known from him, and famous."

Also in al-Durr al-Manthūr: Ibn Rāhwayh, Abū Ya‘lā, Ibn Jarīr, al-Ṭaḥāwī in Mushkil al-Āthār, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated with a good chain from Abū Sa‘īd al-Khudrī: "A man approached his wife in her anus, and people denounced him for that, so 'Your women are a tilth for you, so come to your tilth however you wish' was revealed."

In it also: al-Khaṭīb narrated in Ruwāt Mālik from Abū Sulaymān al-Jūzajānī, who said: "I asked Mālik ibn Anas about intercourse with wives in the anus. He said to me: 'I have just washed my head from it (performed the ritual bath) this moment.'"

In it also: al-Ṭaḥāwī narrated by way of Aṣbagh ibn al-Faraj from ‘Abdullāh ibn al-Qāsim, who said: "I have not met anyone I take as a model in my religion who doubts that it is permissible—meaning intercourse with a woman in her anus—then he recited: 'Your women are a tilth for you,' then said: 'What could be clearer than this?'"

In the Sunan of Abū Dāwūd, from Ibn ‘Abbās, who said: "Indeed Ibn ‘Umar—may Allah forgive him—was mistaken. The matter was that this tribe of the Anṣār were people of idols along with this tribe of Jews who were People of the Book, and they saw them as having a superiority over them in knowledge, so they followed much of their practice. It was the practice of the People of the Book not to approach women except on one side (على حرف), as that is most concealing for the woman. This tribe of the Anṣār had adopted that from their practice. This tribe of the Quraysh used to position women in many ways, enjoying them from the front, from the back, and lying flat. When the Emigrants (المهاجرون) arrived in Medina, a man from them married a woman from the Anṣār and went to do that with her, but she denounced it, saying: 'We were only approached on one side; do that or else stay away from me.' Their matter spread until it reached the Messenger of Allah, so Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, revealed: 'Your women are a tilth for you, so come to your tilth however you wish,' meaning from the front, from the back, and lying flat, meaning by that the place of the child."

I say: al-Suyūṭī narrated it in al-Durr al-Manthūr through other chains as well from Mujāhid, from Ibn ‘Abbās.

In it also: Ibn ‘Abd al-Ḥakam narrated: "al-Shāfi‘ī debated Muhammad ibn al-Ḥasan regarding that. Ibn al-Ḥasan argued that tilth (الحرث) only occurs in the vagina. al-Shāfi‘ī said to him: 'Then would everything besides the vagina be prohibited?' He committed to that. al-Shāfi‘ī said: 'What if he approached her between her legs or in the folds of her skin (الأعكان)? Is there tilth in that?' He said: 'No.' al-Shāfi‘ī said: 'Is it then prohibited?' He said: 'No.' al-Shāfi‘ī said: 'Then how do you argue with what you yourselves do not say?'"

In it also: Ibn Jarīr and Ibn Abī Ḥātim narrated from Sa‘īd ibn Jubayr, who said: "While Mujāhid and I were sitting with Ibn ‘Abbās, a man came to him and said: 'Will you not satisfy me regarding the verse of menstruation?' He said: 'Yes,' and he read: 'They ask you concerning menstruation...' until His saying: 'come to them as Allah has enjoined you.' Ibn ‘Abbās said: 'From where the blood comes, from there you were commanded to come.' The man said: 'What about the verse: "Your women are a tilth for you, so come to your tilth however you wish"?' He said: 'Woe to you! Is there any tilth in the anus? If what you say were true, the verse of menstruation would be abrogated; if she were occupied from here, you would come from here! Rather, "however you wish" means by night or by day.'"

I say: His argument, as you can see, is flawed. For the verse of menstruation does not indicate more than the prohibition of intercourse in the site of the blood during menstruation. If the verse of tilth indicated the permissibility of approaching the posteriors, there would be no relationship of contradiction between them at all such that it would necessitate the abrogation of the ruling of the verse of menstruation. Furthermore, you have already known that the verse of tilth also does not indicate what they intended regarding the permissibility of approaching the posteriors. Yes, there exists in some narrations from Ibn ‘Abbās the use of the command in His saying: "come to them as Allah has enjoined you" as evidence for the prohibition of approaching their posteriors. You have already known from the previous explanation that this is among the most flawed of arguments, and that the verse only indicates the prohibition of intercourse in the site of the blood as long as they have not become clean, while it is silent regarding what is besides that; and that the verse of tilth also only indicates latitude in terms of the tilth. The issue is a jurisprudential one; we have only engaged in discussing it to the extent that it relates to the indication of the verses.

Ayahs

وَلَا تَجْعَلُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عُرْضَةً لِّأَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ أَن تَبَرُّوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ وَتُصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٢٤)
Do not make Allah an obstacle, through your oaths, to being pious and Godwary, and to bringing about concord between people. And Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (224)
لَّا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِٱللَّغْوِ فِىٓ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا كَسَبَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ (٢٢٥)
Allah will not take you to task for what is unconsidered in your oaths, but He will take you to task for what your hearts have incurred, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-forbearing. (225)
لِّلَّذِينَ يُؤْلُونَ مِن نِّسَآئِهِمْ تَرَبُّصُ أَرْبَعَةِ أَشْهُرٍ ۖ فَإِن فَآءُو فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٢٢٦)
For those who forswear their wives shall be a waiting [period]of four months. And if they recant, Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (226)
وَإِنْ عَزَمُوا۟ ٱلطَّلَـٰقَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٢٧)
But if they resolve on divorce, Allah is indeed all-hearing, all-knowing. (227)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: ولا تجعلوا الله عرضة لايمانكم ان تبروا (Do not make Allah an obstacle to your oaths, [against] being pious...) until the end of the verse. The word urḍah (عرضة) is derived from ʿarḍ (العرض), which refers to presenting one thing to another to see its suitability for a specific purpose or intent—such as presenting goods for sale, a house for lodging, or food for eating. From this, a target is called urḍah for arrows, a woman suitable for marriage is called urḍah for wedlock, and a beast prepared for travel is called urḍah for travel. This is the primary linguistic root of the word. As for urḍah meaning an obstacle (المانع) placed in a path, or that which is set up to be exposed to successive occurrences—like a target for arrows—to signify a frequency of happenings, these are secondary meanings derived from specific contexts of usage and are not intrinsic to the primary root.

The word aymān (الايمان) is the plural of yamīn (يمين), meaning an oath (الحلف). It is derived from yamīn meaning the right hand (الجارحة), because people used to strike their right hands together when making an oath, a covenant, or a pledge of allegiance. Thus, the name of the instrument was used for the action itself due to their constant association, just as the name of an action is sometimes used for its instrument—such as calling the finger used for pointing in reproach the sabbābah (السبابة).

The meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: Do not make Allah an obstacle (عرضة) to which your oaths are attached—those oaths you have bound yourselves by—such that you refrain from being pious (تبروا), being God-conscious (تتقوا), and setting things right (تصلحوا) between people. Indeed, Allah, the Glorified, does not approve of His Name being used as a pretext (ذريعة) to abstain from what He has commanded regarding piety, godwariness, and reconciliation. This meaning is supported by the reports concerning the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) for this verse, which we shall mention in the "Discussion on Traditions," Allah willing.

Based on this, His saying, the Exalted: أن تبروا (that you be pious...) implies a suppressed "no" (لا), meaning "that you do not be pious." This is common when using the particle an (أن) as a gerundive, as in His saying: يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ أَن تَضِلُّوا — "Allah clarifies for you lest you should go astray" (4:176), meaning "lest you go astray" or "out of dislike that you should go astray." Alternatively, it is possible that there is no suppressed "no," and the phrase أن تبروا is connected to the prohibition implied by ولا تجعلوا (do not make). In this case, the meaning would be: Allah forbids you from such oaths, or He clarifies their ruling for you, so that you may be pious, God-conscious, and set things right between people.

It is also possible that urḍah (عرضة) means that which is frequently exposed to something. In this sense, it would be a prohibition against swearing by Allah, the Glorified, excessively. The meaning would then be: Do not swear by Allah too much, for if you do, it will lead you to not being pious, not being God-conscious, and not reconciling between people. This is because a person who swears oaths (الحلاف) excessively does not hold the One by whom he swears in high esteem; rather, the matter of the oath becomes trivial to him due to its frequent use. Consequently, he becomes indifferent to lying and does so frequently within himself. Likewise, his status diminishes and his worth falls in the eyes of people, as they sense that he does not see himself as having a footing of truthfulness (قدم صدق) among them, believing that they will not believe what he says, nor does he honor himself by relying on his own word. This would be similar to His saying: وَلَا تُطِعْ كُلَّ حَلَّافٍ مَّهِينٍ — "And do not obey any vile swearer" (68:10). According to this interpretation, it is also more appropriate not to assume a suppressed "no" in the text; rather, the phrase أن تبروا would be in the accusative case due to the removal of a preposition (منصوب بنزع الخافض) or as an object of purpose (مفعول له) for what the prohibition indicates, as previously mentioned.

In His saying, the Exalted: والله سميع عليم (And Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing), there is a type of threat (تهديد) applicable to all the meanings, though the first meaning is the most apparent, as is not hidden.

His saying, the Exalted: لا يؤاخذكم الله باللغو في أيمانكم (Allah will not take you to task for what is vain in your oaths...) until the end of the verse. Vain (اللغو) refers to those actions that do not entail any consequence (أثر). The consequence of a thing varies according to its different aspects and attachments. An oath has a consequence insofar as it is a word, a consequence insofar as it emphasizes speech, a consequence insofar as it is a binding contract (عقد), and a consequence insofar as its breach (حنث) and the violation of its content, and so on. However, the contrast in the verse between the lack of accountability for vain oaths and the accountability for what the hearts have earned (ما كسبته القلوب)—specifically regarding oaths—indicates that "vain oaths" refers to those that do not stem from the intention (قصد) of the swearer. This refers to oaths where the person does not intend to bind themselves to anything, such as saying "No, by Allah" or "Yes, by Allah" out of habit.

Earning (الكسب) is the acquisition of benefits through action, such as a craft, a profession, or the like. Its origin lies in the acquisition of things that fulfill a person's material needs. It was then used metaphorically for everything a person acquires through any of their actions, whether good or evil—such as earning praise, pride, and a good reputation through good character and social service; or earning a virtuous character, beneficial knowledge, and excellence through appropriate deeds; or earning blame, censure, curses, criticism, sins, and transgressions through actions that entail such consequences. This is the meaning of kasb (الكسب) and iktisāb (الاكتساب). It has been said that the difference between them is that iktisāb is a person's acquisition of benefit for themselves, while kasb is more general, whether for oneself or for another—such as a slave's earning for his master or a guardian's earning for his ward.

In any case, the one who earns (الكاسب) or acquires (المكتسب) is the human being and none other.

(A Discourse on the Meaning of the Heart in the Quran)

This is among the evidences that what is meant by the heart (القلب) is the human being in the sense of the soul (النفس) and the spirit (الروح). While intellect (التعقل), reflection (التفكر), love (الحب), hatred (البغض), fear (الخوف), and the like can be attributed to the heart based on the belief that it is the perceiving organ in the body—as the common folk might believe, just as hearing is attributed to the ear, sight to the eye, and taste to the tongue—earning (الكسب) and acquisition (الاكتساب) are things that can absolutely only be attributed to the human being.

Similar to this verse is His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنَّهُ آثِمٌ قَلْبُهُ — "his heart is indeed sinful" (2:283), and His saying: وَجَاءَ بِقَلْبٍ مُّنِيبٍ — "and comes with a penitent heart" (50:33).

It appears that when man observed himself and other species of animals and reflected upon them, he saw that consciousness (الشعور) and perception (الادراك) might cease or vanish from the animal due to fainting, epilepsy, or the like, while life—indicated by the movement and beating of the heart—remains. Conversely, if the heart stops, life ceases. Thus, he concluded that the principle of life (مبدء الحياة) is the heart; that is, the spirit (الروح) which he believes exists in the animal first attaches itself to the heart, even if it then spreads from it to all the organs of life. He concluded that spiritual effects and properties—such as intuitive sensations like consciousness, will, love, hatred, hope, fear, and the like—all belong to the heart by virtue of it being the primary attachment of the spirit. This does not contradict the fact that each organ is the principle for its own specific action—such as the brain for thought, the eye for sight, the ear for hearing, and the lungs for breathing. For all of these are like instruments (الآلات) through which actions requiring an intermediary instrument are performed.

This view may be supported by scientific experiments which found that birds do not die upon the loss of the brain, though they lose perception and become unconscious of anything, remaining in that state until they die from lack of nutrition and the heart stopping its beat.

It may also be supported by the fact that natural scientific research has not succeeded to this day in identifying the source from which bodily judgments originate—that is, the throne of commands (عرش الاوامر) obeyed by the active organs in the human body. For there is no doubt that these organs, despite their inherent diversity and the separation of their functions, are gathered under one banner and led by a single commander with a true unity (وحدة حقيقية).

One should not imagine that this [attribution to the heart] arose from ignorance regarding the brain and its specific role in perception. Indeed, man has been aware of the importance of the head since the most ancient times. The evidence for this is what we see in all nations and peoples, despite their different languages, in naming the principle of judgment and command "the head" (الرأس), and the derivation of various terms from it—such as "head" (الرأس), "chief" (الرئيس), "leadership" (الرئاسة), the "head" of a thread, the "head" of a period, the "head" of a distance, the "head" of a speech, the "head" of a mountain, a "head" of cattle, and the "head" (hilt) of a sword.

This—as it appears—is the reason for their attributing perception, consciousness, and things not devoid of a trace of perception—such as love, hatred, hope, fear, intent, envy, chastity, courage, boldness, and the like—to the heart. By "heart," they mean the spirit (الروح) attached to the body or flowing through it by means of the heart. Thus, they attribute these things to it just as they attribute them to the spirit and to themselves. It is said: "I loved him," "my spirit loved him," "my soul loved him," and "my heart loved him." Eventually, this metaphorical usage became established, and "heart" was used to mean the soul (النفس) metaphorically. They even extended this to the "chest" (الصدر), making it—because it contains the heart—the locus for various types of perception and spiritual actions and attributes.

In the Quran, there is much of this nature. The Exalted said: يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلَامِ — "He opens his breast to Islam" (6:125); and He said: أَنَّكَ يَضِيقُ صَدْرُكَ — "that your breast is straitened" (18:97); and He said: وَبَلَغَتِ الْقُلُوبُ الْحَنَاجِرَ — "and the hearts reached the throats" (33:10), which is a metaphor for the constriction of the chest. He also said: إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ بِذَاتِ الصُّدُورِ — "Indeed Allah knows best what is in the breasts" (5:7). It is not unlikely that these usages in His Book, the Exalted, are an indication of the reality of this view, even if it has not yet become entirely clear.

Sheikh Abu Ali ibn Sina (Avicenna) preferred the view that perception belongs to the heart, in the sense that the brain's involvement in it is that of an instrument; thus, the heart possesses perception while the brain serves as the intermediary.

Let us return to the verse. His saying, the Exalted: ولكن يؤاخذكم بما كسبت قلوبكم (but He will take you to task for what your hearts have earned) is not devoid of a rational metaphor (مجاز عقلي). The apparent shift from the lack of accountability for one type of oath—the vain—to another type would imply accountability for the oath itself. However, this was diverted to its consequence, which is the sin (الاثم) resulting from it upon breach (حنث). Thus, there is a rational metaphor and a shift within a shift to indicate that Allah, the Glorified, is concerned only with the heart, as He said: وَإِن تُبْدُوا مَا فِي أَنفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحَاسِبْكُم بِهِ اللَّهُ — "Whether you disclose what is in your souls or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it" (2:284), and He said: وَلَٰكِن يَنَالُهُ التَّقْوَىٰ مِنكُمْ — "but it is your God-consciousness that reaches Him" (22:37).

In His saying, the Exalted: والله غفور حليم (And Allah is All-forgiving, All-forbearing), there is an indication of the dislike of vain oaths, for they are not something that should proceed from a believer. The Exalted has said: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ — "Certainly the faithful have attained salvation" (23:1) until He said: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنِ اللَّغْوِ مُعْرِضُونَ — "and who avoid vain talk" (23:3).

His saying, the Exalted: للذين يؤلون من نسائهم (For those who forswear their wives...) until the end of the verse. Īlāʾ (الايلاء) is from aliyyah (الالية), meaning an oath. In the Sharīʿah, it became predominantly used for a husband's oath not to approach his wife out of anger or to cause harm, and this is what is intended in the verse. Tarabbuṣ (التربص) is waiting (الانتظار), and fayʾ (الفىء) is returning (الرجوع).

It appears that īlāʾ is made transitive with the preposition min (من) because it encompasses the meaning of distancing (الابتعاد) and the like, thus signifying that the oath is taken to avoid sexual intercourse. This is suggested by the limitation of the waiting period to four months, as this is the maximum period set for mandatory intercourse in the Sharīʿah. From this, it is known that "resolving on divorce" (العزم على الطلاق) means the resolve accompanied by its execution. This is also suggested by the concluding phrase: فإِن الله سميع عليم (for Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing), as hearing (السمع) relates to the divorce that has occurred, not merely the resolve for it.

In His saying, the Exalted: فإِن الله غفور رحيم (then Allah is All-forgiving, All-merciful), there is evidence that there is no punishment for īlāʾ if one returns (الفيىء). As for the expiation (الكفارة), it is a legal ruling (حكم شرعي) that is not subject to forgiveness. The Exalted said: لَا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ اللَّهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمَانِكُمْ وَلَٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا عَقَّدتُّمُ الْأَيْمَانَ فَكَفَّارَتُهُ إِطْعَامُ عَشَرَةِ مَسَاكِينَ — "Allah will not take you to task for what is vain in your oaths, but He will take you to task for the oaths you have bound. Its expiation is feeding ten needy persons..." (5:89).

The meaning is that for one who forswears his wife, the judge allows him a period of four months. If he returns to the marital right—which is intercourse—and expiates and has intercourse, there is no punishment upon him. If he resolves on divorce and executes it, that is the other way out. And Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsīr al-ʿAyyāshī, narrated from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: ولا تجعلوا الله عرضة لايمانكم (Do not make Allah an obstacle to your oaths...), he (peace be upon him) said: "It is a man's saying: 'No, by Allah' and 'Yes, by Allah'."

Also in it, from Imam al-Bāqir and Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon them) regarding the verse: "It means a man who swears that he will not speak to his brother and the like, or that he will not speak to his mother."

In al-Kāfī, from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: "When you are called to reconcile between two people, do not say: 'I have an oath not to do so'."

I say: The first narration, as you see, interprets the verse according to one of the two meanings, while the second and third interpret it according to the other meaning. Close to these two is what is also in Tafsīr al-ʿAyyāshī from Imam al-Bāqir and Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon them), who said: "It is a man who reconciles between two men and bears the sin that was between them..." (the tradition). It is as if the meaning is that he should not swear [to refrain], but rather reconcile and bear the sin, and Allah will forgive him; thus, he would be an instance of one who acts upon the verse.

In al-Kāfī, from Masʿadah, from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: لا يؤاخذكم الله باللغو في ايمانكم (Allah will not take you to task for what is vain in your oaths...), he said: "Vain (اللغو) is a man's saying: 'No, by Allah' and 'Yes, by Allah' without binding himself to anything."

I say: This meaning is narrated in al-Kāfī from him (peace be upon him) through another chain, and in al-Majmaʿ from him and from Imam al-Bāqir (peace be upon him).

Also in al-Kāfī, from Imam al-Bāqir and Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon them), they said: "When a man forswears (آلى) approaching his wife, she has no say or right during the four months, and there is no sin upon him for refraining from her during the four months. If the four months pass before he touches her, then if she remains silent and is content, he is in a state of ease and latitude. But if she takes her case [to the judge], he is told: 'Either you return (تفيء) and touch her, or you divorce.' And the resolve on divorce (عزم الطلاق) is that he leaves her alone; then when she menstruates and becomes pure, he divorces her. He has more right to take her back as long as three menstrual cycles (قروء) have not passed. This is the īlāʾ which Allah sent down in His Book and which the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) established as a Sunnah."

Also in it, from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) in a tradition: "And īlāʾ is that he says: 'By Allah, I will not have intercourse with you for such and such [a time],' or he says: 'By Allah, I will surely vex you,' and then he vexes her..." (the tradition).
