I say: Regarding the specific details of īlāʾ and some matters related to it, there is disagreement between the Sunnis and the Shi'a, and the discussion is a juristic one mentioned in the books of jurisprudence (الفقه).

Ayahs

وَٱلْمُطَلَّقَـٰتُ يَتَرَبَّصْنَ بِأَنفُسِهِنَّ ثَلَـٰثَةَ قُرُوٓءٍ ۚ وَلَا يَحِلُّ لَهُنَّ أَن يَكْتُمْنَ مَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ فِىٓ أَرْحَامِهِنَّ إِن كُنَّ يُؤْمِنَّ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۚ وَبُعُولَتُهُنَّ أَحَقُّ بِرَدِّهِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ إِنْ أَرَادُوٓا۟ إِصْلَـٰحًا ۚ وَلَهُنَّ مِثْلُ ٱلَّذِى عَلَيْهِنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ وَلِلرِّجَالِ عَلَيْهِنَّ دَرَجَةٌ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٢٢٨)
Divorced women shall wait by themselves for three periods of purity [after menses], and it is not lawful for them to conceal what Allah has created in their wombs if they believe in Allah and the Last Day; and their husbands have a greater right to restore them during this [duration], if they desire reconcilement. The wives have rights similar to the obligations upon them, in accordance with honourable norms; and men have a degree above them, and Allah is all-mighty and all-wise. (228)
ٱلطَّلَـٰقُ مَرَّتَانِ ۖ فَإِمْسَاكٌۢ بِمَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ تَسْرِيحٌۢ بِإِحْسَـٰنٍ ۗ وَلَا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَن تَأْخُذُوا۟ مِمَّآ ءَاتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ شَيْـًٔا إِلَّآ أَن يَخَافَآ أَلَّا يُقِيمَا حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ أَلَّا يُقِيمَا حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِمَا فِيمَا ٱفْتَدَتْ بِهِۦ ۗ تِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ فَلَا تَعْتَدُوهَا ۚ وَمَن يَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٢٢٩)
[Revocable] divorce may be only twice; then [let there be] either an honourable retention, or a kindly release. It is not lawful for you to take back anything from what you have given them, unless the couple fear that they may not maintain Allah’s bounds; so if you fear they would not maintain Allah’s bounds, there is no sin upon them in what she may give to secure her own release. These are Allah’s bounds, so do not transgress them, and whoever transgresses the bounds of Allah—it is they who are the wrongdoers. (229)
فَإِن طَلَّقَهَا فَلَا تَحِلُّ لَهُۥ مِنۢ بَعْدُ حَتَّىٰ تَنكِحَ زَوْجًا غَيْرَهُۥ ۗ فَإِن طَلَّقَهَا فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِمَآ أَن يَتَرَاجَعَآ إِن ظَنَّآ أَن يُقِيمَا حُدُودَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ يُبَيِّنُهَا لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (٢٣٠)
And if he divorces her, she will not be lawful for him thereafter until she marries a husband other than him, and if he divorces her, there is no sin upon them to remarry if they think that they can maintain Allah’s bounds. These are Allah’s bounds, which He clarifies for a people who have knowledge. (230)
وَإِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فَبَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ سَرِّحُوهُنَّ بِمَعْرُوفٍ ۚ وَلَا تُمْسِكُوهُنَّ ضِرَارًا لِّتَعْتَدُوا۟ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ فَقَدْ ظَلَمَ نَفْسَهُۥ ۚ وَلَا تَتَّخِذُوٓا۟ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ هُزُوًا ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَمَآ أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَٱلْحِكْمَةِ يَعِظُكُم بِهِۦ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٢٣١)
When you divorce women and they complete their term [of waiting], then either retain them honourably or release them honourably, and do not retain them maliciously in order that you may transgress; and whoever does that certainly wrongs himself. Do not take the signs of Allah in derision, and remember Allah’s blessing upon you, and what He has sent down to you of the Book and wisdom, to advise you therewith. Be wary of Allah and know that Allah has knowledge of all things. (231)
وَإِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فَبَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَلَا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ أَن يَنكِحْنَ أَزْوَٰجَهُنَّ إِذَا تَرَٰضَوْا۟ بَيْنَهُم بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ يُوعَظُ بِهِۦ مَن كَانَ مِنكُمْ يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۗ ذَٰلِكُمْ أَزْكَىٰ لَكُمْ وَأَطْهَرُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٢٣٢)
When you divorce women and they complete their term, do not hinder them from [re]marrying their husbands, when they honourably reach mutual consent. Herewith are advised those of you who believe in Allah and the Last Day. That will be more decent and purer for you, and Allah knows and you do not know. (232)
۞ وَٱلْوَٰلِدَٰتُ يُرْضِعْنَ أَوْلَـٰدَهُنَّ حَوْلَيْنِ كَامِلَيْنِ ۖ لِمَنْ أَرَادَ أَن يُتِمَّ ٱلرَّضَاعَةَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱلْمَوْلُودِ لَهُۥ رِزْقُهُنَّ وَكِسْوَتُهُنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ لَا تُكَلَّفُ نَفْسٌ إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا ۚ لَا تُضَآرَّ وَٰلِدَةٌۢ بِوَلَدِهَا وَلَا مَوْلُودٌ لَّهُۥ بِوَلَدِهِۦ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱلْوَارِثِ مِثْلُ ذَٰلِكَ ۗ فَإِنْ أَرَادَا فِصَالًا عَن تَرَاضٍ مِّنْهُمَا وَتَشَاوُرٍ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِمَا ۗ وَإِنْ أَرَدتُّمْ أَن تَسْتَرْضِعُوٓا۟ أَوْلَـٰدَكُمْ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذَا سَلَّمْتُم مَّآ ءَاتَيْتُم بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٢٣٣)
Mothers shall suckle their children for two full years—that for such as desire to complete the suckling—and on the father shall be their maintenance and clothing, in accordance with honourable norms. No soul is to be tasked except according to its capacity: neither the mother shall be made to suffer harm on her child’s account, nor the father on account of his child, and on the [father’s] heir devolve [duties and rights] similar to that. And if the couple desire to wean with mutual consent and consultation, there will be no sin upon them. And if you want to have your children wet-nursed, there will be no sin upon you so long as you pay what you give in accordance with honourable norms, and be wary of Allah and know that Allah watches what you do. (233)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُتَوَفَّوْنَ مِنكُمْ وَيَذَرُونَ أَزْوَٰجًا يَتَرَبَّصْنَ بِأَنفُسِهِنَّ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ وَعَشْرًا ۖ فَإِذَا بَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيمَا فَعَلْنَ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (٢٣٤)
As for those of you who die leaving wives, they shall wait by themselves four months and ten days, and when they complete their term, there will be no sin upon you in respect of what they may do with themselves in accordance with honourable norms. And Allah is well aware of what you do. (234)
وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيمَا عَرَّضْتُم بِهِۦ مِنْ خِطْبَةِ ٱلنِّسَآءِ أَوْ أَكْنَنتُمْ فِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ ۚ عَلِمَ ٱللَّهُ أَنَّكُمْ سَتَذْكُرُونَهُنَّ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تُوَاعِدُوهُنَّ سِرًّا إِلَّآ أَن تَقُولُوا۟ قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا ۚ وَلَا تَعْزِمُوا۟ عُقْدَةَ ٱلنِّكَاحِ حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ أَجَلَهُۥ ۚ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ فَٱحْذَرُوهُ ۚ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ (٢٣٥)
There is no sin upon you in what you may hint in proposing to [recently widowed] women, or what you may secretly cherish within your hearts. Allah knows that you will be thinking of them, but do not make troth with them secretly, unless you say honourable words, and do not resolve on a marriage tie until the prescribed term is complete. Know that Allah knows what is in your hearts, so beware of Him; and know that Allah is all-forgiving, all-forbearing. (235)
لَّا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِن طَلَّقْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ مَا لَمْ تَمَسُّوهُنَّ أَوْ تَفْرِضُوا۟ لَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً ۚ وَمَتِّعُوهُنَّ عَلَى ٱلْمُوسِعِ قَدَرُهُۥ وَعَلَى ٱلْمُقْتِرِ قَدَرُهُۥ مَتَـٰعًۢا بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۖ حَقًّا عَلَى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٢٣٦)
There is no sin upon you if you divorce women while you have not yet touched them or settled a dowry for them. Yet provide for them—the well-off according to his capacity, and the poorly-off according to his capacity—with a sustenance that is honourable, an obligation on the virtuous. (236)
وَإِن طَلَّقْتُمُوهُنَّ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَمَسُّوهُنَّ وَقَدْ فَرَضْتُمْ لَهُنَّ فَرِيضَةً فَنِصْفُ مَا فَرَضْتُمْ إِلَّآ أَن يَعْفُونَ أَوْ يَعْفُوَا۟ ٱلَّذِى بِيَدِهِۦ عُقْدَةُ ٱلنِّكَاحِ ۚ وَأَن تَعْفُوٓا۟ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ ۚ وَلَا تَنسَوُا۟ ٱلْفَضْلَ بَيْنَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٢٣٧)
And if you divorce them before you touch them, and you have already settled a dowry for them, then [pay them] half of what you have settled, unless they forgo it, or someone in whose hand is the marriage tie forgoes it. And to forgo is nearer to Godwariness; so do not forget graciousness among yourselves. Indeed Allah watches what you do. (237)
حَـٰفِظُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلصَّلَوَٰتِ وَٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ٱلْوُسْطَىٰ وَقُومُوا۟ لِلَّهِ قَـٰنِتِينَ (٢٣٨)
Be watchful of your prayers, and [especially] the middle prayer, and stand in obedience to Allah; (238)
فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ فَرِجَالًا أَوْ رُكْبَانًا ۖ فَإِذَآ أَمِنتُمْ فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَمَا عَلَّمَكُم مَّا لَمْ تَكُونُوا۟ تَعْلَمُونَ (٢٣٩)
and should you fear [a danger], then [pray] on foot or mounted, and when you are safe remember Allah, as He taught you what you did not know. (239)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُتَوَفَّوْنَ مِنكُمْ وَيَذَرُونَ أَزْوَٰجًا وَصِيَّةً لِّأَزْوَٰجِهِم مَّتَـٰعًا إِلَى ٱلْحَوْلِ غَيْرَ إِخْرَاجٍ ۚ فَإِنْ خَرَجْنَ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِى مَا فَعَلْنَ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِنَّ مِن مَّعْرُوفٍ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٢٤٠)
Those of you who die leaving wives shall bequeath for their wives providing for a year, without turning them out; but if they leave, there is no sin upon you in respect of what they may do with themselves in accordance with honourable norms. And Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (240)
وَلِلْمُطَلَّقَـٰتِ مَتَـٰعٌۢ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۖ حَقًّا عَلَى ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٢٤١)
For the divorced women there shall be a provision, in accordance with honourable norms—an obligation on the Godwary. (241)
كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٢٤٢)
Thus does Allah clarify His signs to you so that you may exercise your reason. (242)

(Commentary)

These verses concern the rulings of divorce (الطلاق), the waiting period (العدة), and the nursing of a child by a divorced mother; interspersed within them are some rulings regarding prayer (الصلاة).

His saying, the Exalted: المطلقات يتربصن بأنفسهن ثلاثة قروء — Divorced women shall wait by themselves for three periods of purity (قروء) [2:228]. The linguistic root of divorce (الطلاق) is to release from a bond or restriction; it was subsequently used metaphorically for releasing a woman from the bond of marriage (حبالة النكاح) and the shackle of matrimony, eventually becoming a literal term for this through frequent usage.

Waiting (التربص) means to expect and to hold back. It is restricted by His saying, the Exalted: بأنفسهن (by themselves), to indicate the meaning of refraining from allowing men access to them, thereby conveying the sense of the waiting period (العدة) — the period a woman must wait after divorce or death before remarrying — specifically the waiting period of divorce. This is the woman’s act of withholding herself from marriage to guard against the mixing of paternity (اختلاط المياه). Furthermore, this phrasing points toward the wisdom of the legislation (حكمة التشريع), which is the preservation against the mixing of paternity and the corruption of lineages (فساد الانساب). It is not necessary for this wisdom to be present in every individual case, for laws and rulings revolve around predominant interests and wisdoms (المصالح والحكم الغالبة) rather than universal ones. Thus, His saying, the Exalted: يتربصن بأنفسهن (they shall wait by themselves) is equivalent to saying: "they shall observe a waiting period to prevent the mixing of paternity and the corruption of progeny by allowing men access to themselves." The sentence is a declarative statement (خبر) intended as a command (إنشاء) for the sake of emphasis.

The term quru’ (القروء) is the plural of qur’ (القرء), a word applied to both the state of purity (الطهر) and menstruation (الحيض). It is said to be among the words with opposite meanings (الأضداد). However, the linguistic root of the matter q-r-’ is "gathering" (الجمع), but not just any gathering; rather, it is a gathering followed by discharge or transformation. Based on this, the most apparent meaning is the state of purity (الطهر), as it is the state in which blood gathers, and it was then used for menstruation (الحيض) because it is the state of its expulsion after gathering. By this same association, it was applied to the gathering of letters to indicate the meaning of recitation (القراءة). Lexicographers have explicitly stated its meaning as "gathering." That the root of q-r-’ is gathering is suggested by His saying, the Exalted: لَا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِ لِسَانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِ إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا جَمْعَهُ وَقُرْآنَهُ فَإِذَا قَرَأْنَاهُ فَاتَّبِعْ قُرْآنَهُ — Do not move your tongue with it to hasten it. Indeed, its gathering and its recitation (قُرْآنَهُ) are Our charge. So when We have recited it, follow its recitation [75:16-18], and His saying: وَقُرْآنًا فَرَقْنَاهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلَىٰ مُكْثٍ — We have sent the Quran (قُرْآنًا) in fragments so that you may read it to the people at intervals [17:106]. In both verses, the Exalted used the term Quran (القرآن) rather than "the Book" (الكتاب) or "the Criterion" (الفرقان) or similar terms; it is for this reason the Quran was named Quran.

Al-Raghib said in his Mufradat: "The qur’ (القرء) is actually a name for the transition into menstruation from a state of purity. Since it is a name encompassing both matters—purity and the menstruation that follows it—it is applied to each individually. This is because any name coined for two meanings together can be applied to each when separate, like ma’idah (المائدة) for both the table and the food. Thus, each may be called by that name individually. Qur’ is not a name for purity alone, nor for menstruation alone; the proof is that a pure woman who has never seen a trace of blood is not said to be 'possessing a qur’' (ذات قرء), nor is a menstruating woman whose bleeding is continuous said to be so." End quote.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا يَحِلُّ لَهُنَّ أَن يَكْتُمْنَ مَا خَلَقَ اللَّهُ فِي أَرْحَامِهِنَّ إِن كُنَّ يُؤْمِنَّ بِاللَّهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ — It is not lawful for them to conceal what Allah has created in their wombs, if they believe in Allah and the Last Day [2:228]. This refers to the prohibition of a divorced woman concealing blood or a child to hasten the end of the waiting period, or to harm the husband regarding his right of return (الرجوع), and so on. Restricting this with the condition إِن كُنَّ يُؤْمِنَّ بِاللَّهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ (if they believe in Allah and the Last Day)—even though the ruling itself does not depend on faith—is a form of encouragement and urging toward compliance and steadfastness in the ruling. This restriction indicates that this ruling is a necessary consequence of faith in Allah and the Last Day, upon which the Islamic law (الشريعة الاسلامية) is built; thus, there is no dispensing with this ruling in Islam. This is similar to saying: "Treat people well if you desire good," or saying to a sick person: "You must adhere to the diet if you want healing and recovery."

His saying, the Exalted: وبعولتهن أحق بردهن في ذلك ان ارادوا اصلاحاً — And their husbands have a greater right to restore them during this, if they desire reconciliation [2:228]. Bu’ulah (البعولة) is the plural of ba’l (البعل), which refers to the male of a couple as long as they are married. The term carries a sense of superiority, strength, and steadfastness in hardships, as the man stands in such a relation to the woman. It then became a root from which terms with this meaning are derived; thus, the rider of a beast is called its ba’l, elevated land is called ba’l, an idol is called ba’l, and a palm tree when it grows large is called ba’l, and so forth.

The pronoun in بعولتهن (their husbands) refers to the divorced women, yet the ruling is specific to revocable divorces (الرجعيات) rather than all divorced women, which would include those irrevocably divorced (البائنات). The demonstrative ذلك (this) refers to the waiting (التربص), which carries the meaning of the waiting period (العدة). The restriction ان ارادوا اصلاحاً (if they desire reconciliation) indicates that the return must be for the purpose of reform, not for the purpose of causing harm, which is later prohibited by His saying: ولا تمسكوهن ضراراً لتعتدوا — and do not retain them to cause them harm, so that you may transgress [2:231].

The word ahaqq (أحق) — having a greater right — is a superlative (اسم تفضيل) whose meaning usually requires a comparison. It is as if the first husband has a right to the divorced woman and other suitors also have a right, but the first husband has a greater right to her due to the prior marriage. However, the "restoration" (الرد) mentioned here can only be realized by the first husband.

From this, it becomes clear that there is a subtle implicit meaning (تقدير) in the verse: "And their husbands have a greater right to them than others." This is achieved through restoration and return during the days of the waiting period. This "greater right" is only realized in revocable divorces, not in irrevocable ones where there is no right of return. This is the contextual clue (القرينة) that the ruling is specific to revocable divorces, rather than the pronoun in "their husbands" referring to only some divorced women through syllepsis (الاستخدام) or the like. The verse is specific to the ruling of women with whom marriage has been consummated (المدخول بهن), who menstruate, and are not pregnant. As for those with whom marriage has not been consummated, the young, the menopausal, and the pregnant, there are other verses for their rulings.

His saying, the Exalted: ولهن مثل الذي عليهن بالمعروف وللرجال عليهن درجة — And they have [rights] similar to those [duties] they have, in a settled manner (بالمعروف), and men have a degree [of advantage] over them [2:228]. The "settled manner" (المعروف) — that which is recognized as good and equitable by social custom — is what people recognize through the taste acquired from the common social life prevalent among them. The Glorified has repeated the term al-ma’ruf in these verses, mentioning it in twelve places out of concern that this process—namely divorce and its consequences—should proceed according to the path of primordial nature (الفطرة) and soundness. Thus, al-ma’ruf encompasses the guidance of reason, the ruling of the law, the virtue of good character, and the norms of etiquette.

Since Islam built its law upon the foundation of primordial nature and creation, the "settled manner" (المعروف) according to it is what people recognize when they follow the path of primordial nature and do not exceed the bounds of creation. Among the rulings of a society built on the foundation of primordial nature is that its members and parts should be equal in ruling, such that what is due from them is like what is due to them. However, this equality is maintained while preserving the weight each individual has in society, their influence, and their perfection in the affairs of life. Thus, the ruler is preserved in his governance, the subject in his status as a subject, the scholar in his knowledge, the ignorant in his state, the strong in his capacity for action, and the weak in his weakness. Then, equality is spread among them by giving each their due right. Upon this, Islam proceeded in the rulings established for and against women; it granted her rights similar to her duties while preserving her weight in social life in her union with man for marriage and procreation. In this regard, Islam views that للرجال عليهن درجة (men have a degree over them), and a "degree" (الدرجة) means a rank or station.

From here it appears that His saying, the Exalted: وللرجال عليهن درجة (and men have a degree over them) is a complementary restriction to the preceding sentence. The intended meaning of the whole is a single concept: that Allah has made women—or divorced women—equal to men while preserving the degree men have over them. Thus, He established for them rulings similar to the duties they owe. We shall return to this issue with further clarification in a specific scholarly discussion dedicated to it.

His saying, the Exalted: الطلاق مرتان فإِمكساك بمعروف أو تسريح بإِحسان — Divorce [may be pronounced] twice: then [there must be] either a retaining in a settled manner (بالمعروف) or a release with kindness (بإِحسان) [2:229]. A "time" (المرة) means an instance or occurrence, derived from "passing" (المرور) to indicate a single act.

"Release" (التسريح) originally means letting livestock out to pasture, derived from "I pastured the camels" (سرحت الابل), which is to let them graze on sarh (السرح) — a type of tree with fruit that camels graze upon. It is used metaphorically in the verse for releasing a divorced woman by not returning to her during the waiting period and leaving her until her waiting period expires, as will be explained.

The "divorce" intended in His saying, the Exalted: الطلاق مرتان (Divorce is twice) is the divorce in which return (الرجعة) is permissible; hence He followed it with His saying: فإِمكساك (then a retaining), etc. As for the third, it is the divorce indicated by His saying, the Exalted: فإِن طلقها فلا تحل له من بعد حتى تنكح زوجاً غيره — If he divorces her, she will not be lawful for him thereafter until she marries another husband [2:230].

The "release with kindness" (تسريح بإِحسان) apparently refers to allowing the separation (البينونة) to occur and leaving her after each of the first two divorces until she becomes separated by the expiration of the waiting period. However, it is more apparent that it refers to the third divorce, as suggested by the literal implication of the branching of His saying: فإِمكساك (then a retaining), etc. Based on this, His saying later: فإِن طلقها (If he divorces her), etc., would be a detailed explanation of the "release" after the general mention.

In restricting "retaining" to the "settled manner" (المعروف) and "release" to "kindness" (الإحسان), there is a subtle care that is not hidden. Retaining and returning to the bond of marriage might sometimes be done to harm her, which is a "reprehensible" (منكر) act, not "settled" (معروف). An example is one who divorces his wife, then leaves her until she nears the end of her term, then returns to her, then divorces her again, then returns likewise, intending thereby to annoy and harm her. This is a reprehensible harm not recognized in this law and is prohibited. Rather, the retaining permitted by the law is to return to her with a form of harmony (الالتيام), whereby intimacy and the tranquility of the soul (سكون النفس)—which Allah placed between man and woman—are completed.

Similarly, release might occur in a reprehensible manner where factors of discontent and anger operate, taking the form of revenge. What this law permits is a release in a manner recognized by people and not rejected by the law; that is "release in a settled manner" (التسريح بالمعروف), as He said in the following verse: فأمسكوهن بمعروف أو سرحوهن بمعروف — then retain them in a settled manner or release them in a settled manner [2:231]. This expression is the primary way to convey the required meaning we mentioned. As for what is in this verse—أو تسريح بإِحسان (or a release with kindness)—where release is restricted by "kindness" (الإحسان), which is a meaning additional to "settled manner," that is because the sentence is followed by something that necessitates it, namely His saying, the Exalted: ولا يحل لكم ان تأخذوا مما آتيتموهن شيئاً — And it is not lawful for you to take back anything from what you have given them [2:229].

The explanation is: the restriction by "settled manner" and "kindness" is to negate what would cause the corruption of the intended legislated ruling. What is required by restricting "retaining" to the "settled manner" is to negate retaining done for the purpose of harm, as He said: ولا تمسكوهن ضراراً لتعتدوا — and do not retain them to cause them harm, so that you may transgress. What is required in the case of "release" is to negate the husband taking back some of the dower (المهر) he gave the wife. Restricting "release" to the "settled manner" alone—as was done in the subsequent verse—is not sufficient here, for a husband's demand and taking of some of what he gave his wife might not be rejected by the common customs prevailing among people. Thus, the restriction was increased to "kindness" in this verse, unlike the subsequent one, so that His saying: ولا يحل لكم ان تاخذوا مما آتيتموهن شيئاً (And it is not lawful for you to take back anything from what you have given them) would be consistent, and to compensate thereby for the advantage of married life and marital harmony that the woman loses. Had it been said: "or a release in a settled manner, and it is not lawful for you...", the subtle point would have been lost.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلا ان يخافا الا يقيما حدود الله — unless they both fear that they may not maintain Allah’s bounds [2:229]. "Fear" (الخوف) here means the predominance of their assumption that they will not maintain Allah’s bounds (حدود الله) — His commands and prohibitions regarding the obligations and forbidden acts in religion. This only occurs due to the divergence of their characters, the demands of their needs, and the mutual hatred generated between them from that.

His saying, the Exalted: فإِن خفتم ألا يقيما حدود الله فلا جناح عليهما فيما افتدت به — So if you fear that they may not maintain Allah’s bounds, there is no sin upon them in what she gives to ransom herself [2:229]. The shift from the dual to the plural in His saying: خفتم (you [plural] fear) is as if to indicate that the fear must be a fear recognized by custom and habit, not what might arise from caprice, amusement, or obsessive doubt (الوسوسة) and the like. For this reason, He also shifted from using a pronoun to explicit naming: ألا يقيما حدود الله (that they may not maintain Allah’s bounds), and did not say "if you fear that" to avoid ambiguity.

As for negating "sin" (الجناح) from both of them—even though the prohibition in His saying: ولا يحل لكم ان تأخذوا (it is not lawful for you to take) was directed at the husband—it is because the prohibition of taking for the husband necessitates the prohibition of giving for the wife, as a matter of "assisting in sin and transgression" (الإعانة على الاثم والعدوان). Except in the case of khul’ divorce (طلاق الخلع) — a divorce initiated by the wife in exchange for a financial compensation — where it is permissible for them to agree on divorce with a ransom (الفدية). Thus, there is no sin upon the husband to take the ransom, and no sin upon the wife to give the ransom and assist in the taking; hence, "there is no sin upon them in what she gives to ransom herself."

His saying, the Exalted: تلك حدود الله فلا تعتدوها ومن يتعد حدود الله — These are Allah’s bounds, so do not transgress them, and whoever transgresses Allah’s bounds... [2:229]. The demonstrative refers to the knowledge mentioned in the two verses, which are legal rulings (احكام فقهية) mixed with ethical issues and other scholarly matters built upon fundamental knowledge. "Transgression" (الاعتداء والتعدي) means exceeding the limits.

One might sense from the verse the impermissibility of separating legal rulings from ethical principles, or sufficing in practice with mere legal rulings, being rigid upon outward forms and asceticism in them. For in that is the nullification of the interests of legislation and the killing of the purpose of religion and the happiness of human life. For Islam, as has been mentioned repeatedly, is a religion of action, not just speech, and a law of practice, not just theory. The Muslims have not reached the decline and fall they have reached except by sufficing with the "bodies" of the rulings and turning away from their "spirit" and inner reality. This is indicated by what follows in His saying: وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ فَقَدْ ظَلَمَ نَفْسَهُ — and whoever does that has certainly wronged himself [2:231].

In the verse, there is a transition (التفات) from the plural address in ولا يحل لكم (it is not lawful for you) and فإِن خفتم (if you fear) to the singular address in تلك حدود الله (these are Allah’s bounds), then back to the plural in فلا تعتدوها (do not transgress them), then to the singular in فاولئك هم الظالمون (those are the wrongdoers). This serves to stimulate the mind of the addressee, alert them to wakefulness, and remove lethargy in listening.

His saying, the Exalted: فإِن طلقها فلا تحل له من بعد حتى تنكح زوجاً غيره — If he divorces her, she will not be lawful for him thereafter until she marries another husband [2:230] to the end of the verse. This is an explanation of the ruling of the third divorce, which is prohibition until she marries another husband. He negated "lawfulness" (الحل) from the wife herself—even though what is prohibited is the contract or intercourse—to indicate that the prohibition attaches to both. His saying: حتى تنكح زوجاً غيره (until she marries another husband) suggests both the contract and intercourse together. If the second husband divorces her, there is no sin upon them—meaning the woman and the first husband—to return to the state of marriage through a contract by mutual agreement from both sides; this is "returning to each other" (التراجع), and it is not the "return" (الرجوع) which was a right for the husband in the first two divorces. This is "if they think they will maintain Allah’s bounds."

The explicit noun was placed in the position of a pronoun in His saying: وتلك حدود الله (and these are Allah’s bounds), because the intended "bounds" are different from the previous "bounds."

In the verse, there is a wondrous conciseness (إيجاز) that baffles the intellect. For the speech, despite its brevity, contains fourteen pronouns with differing and intermingled referents, without causing any complexity in the speech or obscurity in understanding.

This verse and the one before it contain a large number of indefinite nouns and metonymies (كنايات) without any flaw in the flow, such as His saying: فإِمساك بمعروف أو تسريح بإِحسان (then a retaining in a settled manner or a release with kindness), four indefinite nouns; and His saying: مما آتيتموهن شيئاً (anything from what you have given them), a metonymy for the dower; and His saying: فإِن خفتم (if you fear), a metonymy for the necessity of the fear being according to recognized custom; and His saying: فيما افتدت به (in what she gives to ransom herself), a metonymy for the wealth of khul’; and His saying: فإِن طلقها (If he divorces her), meaning the third divorce; and His saying: فلا تحل له (she will not be lawful for him), meaning the prohibition of the contract and intercourse; and His saying: حتى تنكح زوجاً غيره (until she marries another husband), meaning both the contract and intercourse together as a polite metonymy; and His saying: ان يتراجعا (to return to each other), a metonymy for the contract.

In the two verses, there is a beautiful correspondence (مقابلة) between "retaining" and "release," and between His saying "if they both fear that they may not maintain Allah’s bounds" and His saying "if they think they will maintain Allah’s bounds," and the variation in expression in His saying فلا تعتدوها (do not transgress them) and ومن يتعد (and whoever transgresses).

His saying, the Exalted: إِذا طلقتم النساء فبلغن أجلهن — When you divorce women and they reach their term [2:231] to His saying: لتعتدوا (so that you may transgress). "Reaching the term" (بلوغ الاجل) means being on the verge of the expiration of the waiting period, for "reaching" (البلوغ) is used for arriving at the end as well as for approaching it. The proof that this is intended is His saying: فأمسكوهن بمعروف أو سرحوهن بمعروف (then retain them in a settled manner or release them in a settled manner), as there is no meaning to retaining or releasing after the expiration of the waiting period. In His saying: ولا تمسكوهن ضراراً لتعتدوا (and do not retain them to cause them harm, so that you may transgress), He prohibited returning with the intention of causing harm, just as He prohibited release by taking from the dower except in khul’.

His saying, the Exalted: ومن يفعل ذلك فقد ظلم نفسه — and whoever does that has certainly wronged himself [2:231] to the end of the verse. This is an indication of the wisdom behind the prohibition of retaining for harm. Marriage is for the completion of the happiness of life, and that is not achieved except by the tranquility of each spouse in the other and their mutual assistance in fulfilling instinctive needs. "Retaining" (الإمساك) specifically is a return to connection and union after separation and parting; it is the gathering of the family after its dispersal. How far is that from a return with the intention of harm?

So whoever does that—meaning he retains for harm—has wronged himself by forcing it to deviate from the path to which his human primordial nature (فطرته الانسانية) guides him.

Furthermore, he has taken the signs of Allah in mockery (هزواً). For Allah, the Glorified, did not legislate the rulings He legislated for them as a rigid legislation restricted to the "bodies" of acts—taking, giving, retaining, releasing, and so on. Rather, He built them upon general interests by which the corruption of society is rectified and the happiness of human life is completed. He mixed them with noble ethics by which souls are disciplined, spirits are purified, and high knowledge is clarified: from monotheism (التوحيد), guardianship (الولاية), and other pure beliefs. Whoever restricts his religion to the outward forms of the rulings and casts everything else behind his back has taken the signs of Allah in mockery.

The "blessing" (النعمة) in His saying: واذكروا نعمة الله عليكم (and remember Allah’s blessing upon you) refers to the blessing of religion or the reality of religion, which is the happiness attained by acting according to the laws of religion, such as the happiness of life specific to the harmony of spouses. For Allah has called religious happiness a "blessing" (نعمة), as in His saying: وَأَتْمَمْتُ إِلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي — and I have completed My blessing upon you [5:3], and His saying: وَلِيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكُمْ — and that He may complete His blessing upon you [5:6], and His saying: فَأَصْبَحْتُم بِنِعْمَتِهِ إِخْوَانًا — and by His blessing you became brothers [3:103].

Based on this, His saying afterward: وما أُنزل عليكم من الكتاب والحكمة يعظكم به — and what He has sent down to you of the Book and wisdom, to exhort you with it [2:231] is like an explanation of this blessing. The "Book and wisdom" (الكتاب والحكمة) would mean the outward aspect of the law and its inward aspect—that is, its rulings and its wisdoms.

It is also possible that "blessing" refers to all divine blessings, existential (تكوينية) and otherwise. The meaning would then be: remember the reality of the meaning of your lives, especially the advantages and beauties of harmony and tranquility between spouses, and what Allah has explained to you through exhortation regarding the knowledge related to them in the outward rulings and their wisdom. For if you reflect upon that, you will likely adhere to the path of happiness and not corrupt the perfection of your lives and the blessing of your existence. And be conscious of Allah, and let your souls be directed to the fact that Allah is All-Knowing of all things, so that your outward does not contradict your inward, and you do not dare against Allah by destroying the inward of religion in the guise of building its outward.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذا طلقتم النساء فبلغن أجلهن فلا تعضلوهن أن ينكحن أزواجهن إِذا تراضوا بينهم بالمعروف — When you divorce women and they reach their term, do not prevent them from marrying their husbands when they have agreed among themselves in a settled manner [2:232]. "Prevention" (العضل) means to hinder. It appears that the address in فلا تعضلوهن (do not prevent them) is to their guardians (اولياء) and those like them whom the women cannot easily oppose. "Their husbands" (أزواجهن) refers to the husbands before the divorce. The verse indicates a prohibition for guardians and their like from preventing a woman from remarrying her husband after the expiration of the waiting period out of anger or stubbornness, as often happens. There is no indication in this that the contract is only valid with a guardian.

Firstly: Because His saying فلا تعضلوهن (do not prevent them), if it does not indicate the lack of effect of guardianship in this, it does not indicate its effect either.

Secondly: Because there is no evidence for restricting the address to guardians only; rather, it appears to be more general than them, and the prohibition is an advisory prohibition (نهي إِرشادي) toward the interests and benefits resulting from this return, as He said: ذلكم ازكى لكم وأطهر — That is more decent for you and purer [2:232].

It has been said that the address is to the husbands, following the flow of His saying: إِذا طلقتم النساء (When you divorce women). The meaning would be: "When you divorce women, O husbands, and their waiting period expires, do not prevent them from marrying husbands who would be their husbands," by concealing the divorce from them so they are harmed by the length of the waiting period and the like.

This view does not suit His saying: أزواجهن (their husbands), for the appropriate expression for this meaning would be "that they marry" or "that they marry husbands," as is apparent.

The meaning of His saying: فبلغن أجلهن (and they reach their term) is the expiration of the waiting period. For if the waiting period had not expired, no one—guardians or otherwise—would have the right to prevent that, as "their husbands have a greater right to restore them during this." Furthermore, His saying أن ينكحن (that they marry), rather than "that they return" or similar, contradicts that view.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلك يوعظ به من كان منكم يؤمن بالله واليوم الآخر — That is an exhortation for him among you who believes in Allah and the Last Day [2:232]. This is like His previous saying: ولا يحل لهن ان يكتمن ما خلق الله في أرحامهن إِن كن يؤمن بالله واليوم الآخر (It is not lawful for them to conceal what Allah has created in their wombs, if they believe in Allah and the Last Day). These two instances were specifically restricted by faith in Allah and the Last Day—which is monotheism (التوحيد)—because the religion of monotheism calls for union rather than separation, and dictates connection rather than severance.

In His saying: ذلك يوعظ به من كان منكم (That is an exhortation for him among you), there is a transition (التفات) to the singular address from the plural, then a transition from the singular to the plural. The original basis of this speech is an address to the collective—meaning the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and his entire community. However, sometimes the address shifts to the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) alone in matters other than the legal rulings, such as His saying: تلك حدود الله فلا تعتدوها (These are Allah’s bounds, so do not transgress them), and His saying فاولئك هم الظالمون (those are the wrongdoers), and His saying: وبعولتهن احق بردهن في ذلك (And their husbands have a greater right to restore them during this), and His saying: ذلك يوعظ به من كان يؤمن بالله (That is an exhortation for him who believes in Allah), to preserve the structure of the address and out of regard for the one who is the pillar of this communication, namely the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), for he is the one addressed by the speech without intermediary, while others are addressed through his mediation. As for the addresses containing rulings, they are all directed toward the collective. The reality of this type of linguistic transition returns to expanding the address after narrowing it, and narrowing it after expanding it; so let one reflect upon it.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلكم ازكى لكم واطهر — That is more decent (ازكى) for you and purer (واطهر) [2:232]. Zakah (الزكاة) is wholesome and good growth. The meaning of purity (الطهارة) has been discussed previously. The demonstrative ذلكم (that) refers to not preventing them from returning to their husbands, or the return itself; the outcome is the same. This is because it involves a return from fracture and separation to harmony and connection, and a strengthening of the instinct of monotheism in the souls, upon which all religious virtues grow. It also involves cultivating the trait of modesty and shame in them, which is more concealing for them and purer for their souls. From another perspective, it involves guarding their hearts from falling for strangers if they are prevented from marrying their husbands.

Islam is the religion of growth (الزكاة), purity (الطهارة), and knowledge (العلم). The Exalted said: وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ — and purifies them and teaches them the Book and wisdom [3:164], and He said: وَلَٰكِن يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ — but He desires to purify you [5:6].

His saying, the Exalted: والله يعلم وانتم لا تعلمون — and Allah knows and you do not know [2:232], meaning except what He teaches you, as He said: وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ (and teaches them the Book and wisdom), and He said: وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَيْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِ إِلَّا بِمَا شَاءَ — and they do not comprehend anything of His knowledge except what He wishes [2:255]. Thus, there is no contradiction between this verse and His saying: وتلك حدود الله يبينها لقوم يعلمون — and these are Allah’s bounds which He clarifies for a people who have knowledge [2:230], meaning they know through Allah’s teaching.

His saying, the Exalted: والوالدات يرضعن اولادهن حولين كاملين لمن اراد ان يتم الرضاعه — Mothers shall suckle their children for two full years—that for such as desire to complete the suckling [2:233]. Walidat (الوالدات) are mothers. The term was shifted from "mothers" (الامهات) to walidat because "mother" is more general than walidah (biological mother), just as "father" (الاب) is more general than walid (biological father) and "son" (الابن) is more general than walad (child). The ruling in the verse is legislated specifically for the case of the biological mother, the child, and the one to whom the child is born (المولود له). As for replacing "father" with "the one to whom the child is born" (المولود له), it contains an indication of the wisdom of the legislation. Since the child is born to the father and is attached to him in most rulings of life—though not all, as will be explained in the verse of prohibition in Surah al-Nisa, Allah willing—it is his duty to provide for the child's life interests and the necessities of his upbringing. Among these are the clothing and maintenance of the mother who suckles him. It is also the mother's duty not to harm the father, because the child is born to him.

Among the most strange statements is what some commentators mentioned: that it was said "the one to whom the child is born" rather than "the father" to let it be known that mothers only give birth for the fathers, because children belong to the fathers and are thus attributed to them, not to the mothers. Al-Ma’mun ibn al-Rashid recited:

Indeed, the mothers of people are but vessels Entrusted [with them], and to the fathers belong the sons

This is the summary of his point. It is as if he was oblivious to the beginning and end of the verse where the Exalted says: أولادهن (their children) and says: بولدها (with her child). As for the poetry of al-Ma’mun he recited, it and its like are of too low a status for the speech of Allah, the Exalted and Sanctified, to be supported by it.

Many scholars of literature have confused linguistic matters with legislative ones, and social rulings with existential realities. They sometimes cite language as evidence for a social ruling or an existential truth.

The crux of the matter regarding the child is that existence (التكوين) attaches him to both parents together, as his existence depends on both of them. Social consideration (الاعتبار الاجتماعي) regarding this differs among nations: some nations attach him to the mother, and some to the father. The verse affirms the view of the latter and points to it by His saying: المولود له (the one to whom the child is born), as mentioned. "Suckling" (الإرضاع) is the causative form of radha’ah (الرضاعة), which is sucking the breast to drink milk from it. A "year" (الحول) is called hawl because it "turns" (يحول). It was described as "full" (كاملين) because a year, having many parts, might be spoken of loosely, being applied to a deficient year as well as a full one. It is often said: "I stayed there for a year or two" when one stayed for a period slightly less than that.

In His saying, the Exalted: لمن اراد ان يتم الرضاعة (for such as desire to complete the suckling), there is evidence that custody (الحضانة) and suckling are a right of the divorced mother, entrusted to her choice. Reaching the end of the period is also her right; if she wishes to suckle for two full years, she may, and if she does not wish to complete it, she may. As for the husband, he has no right in this unless the wife agrees to it by mutual consent, as indicated by His saying: فإِن ارادا فصالاً (if they both desire weaning), etc.

His saying, the Exalted: وعلى المولود له رزقهن وكسوتهن بالمعروف لا تكلف نفس الا وسعها — and their maintenance and their clothing, in a settled manner, shall be the charge of the one to whom the child is born. No soul shall be tasked beyond its capacity [2:233]. "The one to whom the child is born" is the father, as mentioned. "Maintenance and clothing" (الرزق والكسوة) are food and dress. Allah has based them upon the "settled manner" (المعروف), which is what is customary for their situation. He justified this with another general ruling that removes hardship (الحرج): لا تكلف نفس الا وسعها (No soul shall be tasked beyond its capacity). From this, He branched two other rulings: First: the right of custody and suckling belonging to the wife and similar rights; thus, the husband has no right to come between the mother and her child by preventing her from custody, seeing him, or the like, for that is harm and hardship upon her. Second: negating the wife's harming of the husband regarding his child by preventing him from seeing him and the like. That is His saying: لا تضار والدة بولدها ولا مولود له بولده — A mother shall not be harmed because of her child, nor a father because of his child [2:233]. The subtle point in using the explicit noun instead of a pronoun—saying "because of his child" rather than "because of him"—is to remove any imagined contradiction. For if it were said "nor a father because of him," the pronoun would refer back to "her child," and the literal meaning would be: "nor a father because of the woman's child," which would suggest a contradiction because attributing birth to the man contradicts attributing it to the woman. Thus, the sentence observes both the legislative and existential rulings together: the child belongs to both of them existentially—he is his child and her child—and belongs to him alone legislatively because he is "born to him."

His saying, the Exalted: وعلى الوارث مثل ذلك — and the same [duties] shall rest upon the heir [2:233]. The apparent meaning of the verse is that what was placed upon the father regarding clothing and maintenance is also placed upon his heir if he dies. Other things have been said regarding the meaning of the verse that do not agree with its apparent sense; we have omitted them as they are more relevant to legal discussion (البحث الفقهي) and should be sought there. What we have mentioned is what accords with the school of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) in the narrations reported from them, and it also accords with the apparent sense of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: فإِن أرادا فصالاً عن تراض منهما وتشاور — If they both desire weaning by mutual consent and consultation [2:233] to the end of the verse. Fisal (الفصال) is weaning. Tashawur (التشاور) is meeting for consultation. The speech is a branching from the right granted to the wife and the negation of hardship between them. Custody and suckling are not an obligation upon her that is unchangeable; rather, it is a right she may waive.

Thus, it is permissible for them to agree through consultation on weaning the child without any sin or blame upon them. Likewise, it is permissible for the husband to seek a wet-nurse for his child from someone other than the biological mother if she returns the child to him by refusing to suckle, or for another reason such as the cessation of milk or illness, provided he hands over to her what she is entitled to in a settled manner, such that her right is not encroached upon in all of this. This is His saying: وإِن أردتم أن تسترضعوا اولادكم فلا جناح عليكم إِذا سلمتم ما آتيتم بالمعروف — And if you desire to have your children suckled [by a wet-nurse], there is no sin upon you, provided you pay what you give in a settled manner [2:233].

His saying, the Exalted: واتقوا الله واعلموا ان الله بما تعملون بصير — and be conscious of Allah, and know that Allah sees best what you do [2:233]. He commanded godwariness (التقوى) and that this godwariness be through rectifying the form of these actions, for they are matters related to the outward form. Therefore, He said: واعلموا ان الله بما تعملون بصير (and know that Allah sees best what you do). This is unlike what was at the end of the previous verse: إِذا طلقتم النساء فبلغن اجلهن (When you divorce women and they reach their term), where He said: واتقوا الله واعلموا ان الله بكل شيء عليم (and be conscious of Allah, and know that Allah is All-Knowing of all things). That verse contained His saying: ولا تمسكوهن ضراراً لتعتدوا (and do not retain them to cause them harm, so that you may transgress), and harming might return to the intention without appearing in the form of the action except by its later effect.

His saying, the Exalted: والذين يتوفون منكم ويذرون ازواجاً يتربصن بأنفسهن اربعة اشهر وعشراً — As for those of you who die leaving wives, they shall wait by themselves for four months and ten [days] [2:234]. Tawaffi (التوفي) is causing death. It is said: "Allah took him" (توفاه الله) when He caused him to die; thus he is mutawaffa (متوفى) in the passive participle form. Yadharun (يذرون) is like yada’un (يدعون), meaning "they leave," and they have no past tense from their root. "Ten" (العشر) refers to days, omitted because the speech indicates it.

His saying, the Exalted: فإِذا بلغن اجلهن فلا جناح عليكم فيما فعلن في أنفسهن بالمعروف — And when they have reached their term, there is no sin upon you in what they may do with themselves in a settled manner [2:234]. "Reaching the term" means the expiration of the waiting period. His saying فلا جناح (there is no sin), etc., is a metonymy for granting them choice in their actions. If they choose marriage for themselves, they may do so. It is not for the relatives of the deceased to prevent them from any of that based on some customs built on ignorance, blindness, stinginess, or envy. For they have a right in that which is recognized in the law, and no one may forbid what is "settled" (المعروف).

Nations have had various inclinations regarding the widow: some ruled for burning the living wife with her dead husband or burying her with him; some dictated the impermissibility of her remarrying for the rest of her life, like the Christians; some required her to withdraw from men for a year from the time of death, like the pre-Islamic Arabs, or close to a year like nine months, as is the case with some advanced nations; and some believed that the deceased husband has a right over the wife to refrain from marriage for a time without specifying the duration. All of this is because they find within themselves that marriage is for partnership in life and blending within it, built on the foundation of intimacy and affection, and love has a sanctity that must be observed. Although this is a meaning existing in both parties and related to both husband and wife—such that whichever of them is taken by death, the other must observe this sanctity after their companion—this observance is more binding and necessary for the woman. This is due to the requirement upon her to observe modesty, seclusion, and chastity. It is not appropriate for her to be cheapened like a common commodity passing from hand to hand. This is the reason for what these various peoples ruled regarding the widow. Islam has specified this waiting period as approximately one-third of a year, namely four months and ten days.

His saying, the Exalted: والله بما تعملون خبير — and Allah is aware of what you do [2:234]. Since the speech included the legislation of the waiting period for death and the legislation of their right to marriage after it—and all of that is a specification of actions based on divine expertise—it was most appropriate to justify it by stating that Allah is aware of actions, distinguishing the prohibited from the permissible. Thus, they must wait in one instance and choose what they wish for themselves in another. Therefore, He concluded the speech with الله بما تعملون خبير (Allah is aware of what you do).

His saying, the Exalted: لا جناح عليكم فيما عرضتم به من خطبة النساء أو أكننتم في انفسكم — There is no sin upon you in what you may hint at in proposing to [these] women, or what you may conceal in your hearts [2:235]. "Hinting" (التعريض) is to incline the speech to a side so the addressee understands a matter intended by the speaker without him wishing to state it explicitly; it is from ’ardh (العرض) meaning "side," thus it is the opposite of explicit statement. The difference between hinting and metonymy (الكناية) is that the speech containing a hint has an intended meaning other than what was alluded to, such as a suitor saying to a woman: "I am of good character and I love women," meaning "if you married me, you would be happy with a good life and become beloved." This is unlike metonymy, where nothing other than the thing alluded to is intended, such as saying "so-and-so has much ash," meaning he is generous.

"Proposal" (الخطبة) with a kasrah on the kha’ is from khatb (الخطب) meaning speaking and mutual discussion. It is said: "he proposed to the woman" (خطب المرأة خطبة) with a kasrah when he spoke to her regarding marriage; he is then a khatib (خاطب) [suitor], and is not called khatib (خطيب) [orator]. It is said "he addressed the people" (خطب القوم خطبة) with a dhammah on the kha’ when he spoke to them, especially in exhortation; he is then a khatib (خاطب) from khitab (خطاب) and a khatib (خطيب) from the khutaba’ (خطباء).

Iknan (الاكنان) is from kinn (الكن) meaning "covering," but iknan is specific to what is concealed in the soul, as He said: أو أكننتم في انفسكم (or what you may conceal in your hearts). Kinn is what is covered by a physical object like a case, a garment, or a house. The Exalted said: كَأَنَّهُنَّ بَيْضٌ مَّكْنُونٌ — as if they were hidden eggs [37:49], and He said: كَأَمْثَالِ اللُّؤْلُؤِ الْمَكْنُونِ — like hidden pearls [56:23]. The verse intends to negate blame for hinting at a proposal or concealing matters in the heart regarding it.

His saying, the Exalted: علم الله انكم ستذكرونهن — Allah knows that you will be thinking of them [2:235], in the context of justifying the negation of sin for the proposal and hinting at it. The meaning is: your thinking of them is a matter ingrained in your natures, and Allah does not prohibit a matter dictated by your primordial instinct and the nature of your creation; rather, He permits it. This is among the clear instances showing that the religion of Islam is built upon the foundation of primordial nature (الفطرة).

His saying, the Exalted: ولا تعزموا عقدة النكاح حتى يبلغ الكتاب أجله — But do not resolve on the marriage tie until the prescribed term has come to its end [2:235]. "Resolving" (العزم) is the heart's determination upon an act and the firming of a judgment such that no weakness remains in its effect unless it is nullified from the start. "Tie" (العقدة) is from ’aqd (العقد) meaning "binding." In the speech, there is a comparison of the marital bond to a knot by which one string is tied to another such that they become one through connection, as if the bond of marriage makes the spouses one connected entity. Then, in attaching the "marriage tie" to "resolving"—which is a matter of the heart—there is an indication that the nature of this tie and bond is a matter existing in intention and belief. For it is among the rational constructs (الاعتبارات العقلائية) that have no locus except the vessel of belief and perception, similar to ownership and other social rational rights, as explained previously under His saying: كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً — Mankind were a single community [2:213]. Thus, in the verse, there is a metaphor and metonymy. "The Book" (الكتاب) refers to what is written, i.e., the prescribed ruling, which is the waiting period Allah prescribed for women in their waiting periods.

The meaning of the verse is: do not conduct the marriage contract until their waiting period has expired. This verse reveals that the discussion in it and the verse preceding it—namely His saying: لا جناح عليكم فيما عرضتم به من خطبة النساء (There is no sin upon you in what you may hint at in proposing to women)—is specifically about proposing to women in their waiting periods and their marriage contracts. Based on this, the "al-" in النساء (the women) is for specific reference (العهد), not for the genus or otherwise.

His saying, the Exalted: واعلموا أن الله يعلم ما في أنفسكم — And know that Allah knows what is in your hearts [2:235] to the end. Mentioning these attributes of His—namely knowledge, forgiveness, and forbearance—in the verse indicates that the matters mentioned in the two verses—proposing to women in their waiting periods, hinting to them, and promising them secretly—are among the instances of peril that Allah, the Glorified, does not entirely approve of, even if He permitted what He permitted of them.

His saying, the Exalted: لا جناح عليكم إِن طلقتم النساء ما لم تمسوهن أو تفرضوا لهن فريضه — There is no sin upon you if you divorce women while you have not yet touched them or settled a dowry for them [2:236]. "Touching" (المس) is a metonymy for intercourse. "Settling a dowry" (فرض الفريضة) means naming the dower (المهر). The meaning is: the lack of touching the wife does not prevent the validity of the divorce, nor does the lack of mentioning the dower.

His saying, the Exalted: ومتعوهن على الموسع قدره وعلى المقتر قدره متاعاً بالمعروف — and provide for them—the well-to-do according to his capacity, and the ill-to-do according to his capacity—with a provision in a settled manner [2:236]. "Providing" (التمتيع) is giving that which is enjoyed. Mata’ (المتاع) and mut’ah (المتعة) are that which is enjoyed. Mata’an (متاعاً) is a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق) for His saying ومتعوهن (provide for them); between them intervened His saying: على الموسع قدره وعلى المقتر قدره (the well-to-do according to his capacity, and the ill-to-do according to his capacity). Al-musi’ (الموسع) is the active participle of awsa’a when one is in a state of financial ease; it is as if it is among the transitive verbs whose object was frequently omitted for brevity until it came to indicate the root meaning and became intransitive. Al-muqtir (المقتر) is the active participle of aqtara when one is in a state of financial hardship. Qadar (القدر) with a fathah or sukun on the dal has the same meaning.

The meaning of the verse is: you must provide for divorced women for whom no dowry was settled a provision in a settled manner. This provision is mandatory upon the well-to-do according to his capacity—i.e., what suits his state and by which his situation is measured—and upon the ill-to-do according to his capacity. This is specific to the divorced woman for whom no dowry was settled and whose dower was not named. The proof that this mentioned provision is specific to her and does not encompass the divorced woman for whom a dowry was settled but with whom marriage was not consummated is what is in the following verse explaining her ruling.

His saying, the Exalted: حقاً على المحسنين — a duty upon the virtuous [2:236], meaning the ruling is established as a duty upon the virtuous. Although the apparent sense of the sentence is that the attribute—namely virtue (الإحسان)—is involved in the ruling, and since virtue is not mandatory, it would imply the ruling is recommended (استحبابي) rather than obligatory (وجوبي). However, the texts from the paths of the Ahl al-Bayt interpret the ruling as obligatory. Perhaps the reason for this is what was mentioned previously in His saying: الطلاق مرتان فامساك بمعروف او تسريح باحسان (Divorce is twice: then either a retaining in a settled manner or a release with kindness); thus He made kindness (الإحسان) mandatory upon those who release, who are the divorcers. They are thus "the virtuous" (المحسنين), and the ruling has been established as a duty in this verse upon the virtuous, who are the divorcers. Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: وان طلقتموهن من قبل ان تمسوهن — And if you divorce them before you have touched them [2:237] to the end. That is, if you enact divorce before consummation and you have settled a dowry for them and named the dower, you must pay half of what you settled of the dower, unless these divorced women forgo it, or he in whose hand is the marriage tie—their guardian—forgoes it, in which case the mentioned half also falls away. Or the husband forgoes it—for the marriage tie is also in his hand—so the divorced wife is not required to return the half of the dower she took. Forgoing (العفو) in any case is closer to godwariness (التقوى), because one who turns away from his legally established right is stronger and more capable of turning away from what is not his right among the prohibitions of Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: ولا تنسوا الفضل بينكم — and do not forget graciousness (الفضل) among yourselves [2:237]. Fadhl (الفضل) is "excess" like fudhul, except that fadhl is excess in noble and praiseworthy deeds, while fudhul is unpraiseworthy excess, as has been said. In the speech, there is a mention of the graciousness that a person should prefer in the society of life, by which some excel over others. The intent is to encourage kindness and graciousness by forgoing rights and being easy and lenient, from the husband to the wife and vice versa. The subtle point in His saying: إِن الله على كل شيء بصير (Indeed Allah sees best all things) is like the point mentioned previously at the end of His saying: والوالدات يرضعن أولادهن (Mothers shall suckle their children).

His saying, the Exalted: حافظوا على الصلوات — Be watchful of the prayers [2:238] to the end of the verse. To "watch" (حفظ) something is to guard it; in meanings, it most often refers to the soul's guarding of what it brings to mind or perceives. Wusta (الوسطى) is the feminine of awsat (middle). The "middle prayer" (الصلاة الوسطى) is the one situated in the middle. It is not apparent from His speech, the Exalted, what is intended by the middle prayer; rather, its explanation is in the Sunnah (السنة) — the practice and teachings of the Prophet. The narrations regarding its identification will follow.

The lam in His saying: قوموا لله (stand for Allah) is for purpose (للغاية). "Standing" (القيام) for a matter is a metonymy for undertaking it and engaging in its performance. Qunut (القنوت) is humility in obedience. The Exalted said: كُلٌّ لَّهُ قَانِتُونَ — all are obedient to Him [2:116], and He said: وَمَن يَقْنُتْ مِنكُنَّ لِلَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ — and whoever of you is obedient to Allah and His Apostle [33:31]. The sum of the meaning is: engage in the obedience of Allah, the Glorified, with humility, being sincere to Him and for His sake.

His saying, the Exalted: فإِن خفتم فرجالاً أو ركباناً — And if you are in fear, then [pray] on foot or riding [2:239] to the end of the verse. Branching the condition upon the preceding sentence indicates an implicit omitted condition: "be watchful if you are not in fear." And if you are in fear, then measure the watchfulness according to what is possible of the prayer, on foot—standing or walking—or riding. Rijal (الرجال) is the plural of rajil (on foot) and rukban (الركبان) is the plural of rakib (riding). This is the "prayer of fear" (صلاة الخوف).

The fa’ in His saying: فإِذا أمنتم (And when you are secure) is for branching (للتفريع), meaning that being watchful of the prayer is a matter that does not fall away entirely. Rather, if you do not fear anything and it is possible for you, it is mandatory upon you; and if it is difficult for you, then measure it according to what is possible for you. And if the fear departs from you by the renewal of security, the obligation returns and you must remember Allah, the Glorified.

The kaf in His saying: كما علمكم (as He has taught you) is for comparison (للتشبيه). His saying ما لم تكونوا تعلمون (what you did not know) is of the type of placing the general in the position of the specific to indicate gratitude for the breadth of the blessing and the teaching. The meaning based on this is: remember Allah with a remembrance that resembles what He taught you of the prescribed, written prayer in the state of security, within what He taught you of the laws of religion.

His saying, the Exalted: والذين يتوفون منكم ويذرون أزواجاً وصية لأزواجهم — As for those of you who die leaving wives, [let them leave] a bequest for their wives [2:240]. "Bequest" (وصية) is a cognate accusative for an omitted verb; the implicit meaning is: let them bequeath a bequest by which their wives benefit and enjoy a provision for a year after the death.

Defining "the year" (الحول) with the "al-" is not without indication that the verse was revealed before the legislation of the waiting period for death, namely the four months and ten days. For the women of the pre-Islamic Arabs used to stay for a full year after the death of their husbands. The verse recommends that husbands bequeath for them wealth to enjoy until the completion of the year without being expelled from their homes. However, since this was a right for them and a right may be waived, they were permitted to demand it or waive it. If they left, there was no sin upon the heirs and those like them in what they did with themselves in a settled manner. This is similar to what Allah recommended for one who is facing death: to bequeath to parents and relatives in a settled manner. The Exalted said: كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ إِن تَرَكَ خَيْرًا الْوَصِيَّةُ لِلْوَالِدَيْنِ وَالْأَقْرَبِينَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ حَقًّا عَلَى الْمُتَّقِينَ — Prescribed for you, when death approaches any of you and he leaves behind any property, is that he make a bequest for his parents and relatives in a settled manner, a duty on the God-conscious [2:180].

From what we have mentioned, it appears that the verse is abrogated (منسوخة) by the verse of the waiting period for death and the verse of inheritance with the fourth and the eighth.

His saying, the Exalted: وللمطلقات متاع بالمعروف حقاً على المتقين — For the divorced women there shall be a provision in a settled manner, a duty on the God-conscious [2:241]. The verse concerns the right of all divorced women. Attaching the establishment of the ruling to the attribute of godwariness (التقوى) suggests recommendation (الاستحباب).

His saying, the Exalted: كذلك يبين الله لكم آياته لعلكم تعقلون — Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may exercise your intellect [2:242]. The root meaning of "intellect" (العقل) is to bind and to withhold. By this, the human perception was named "intellect" (عقلاً) as it is a perception upon which one binds oneself. What one perceives is called "intellectually perceived," and the faculty that is claimed to be one of the faculties by which man acts, distinguishing between good and evil and truth and falsehood, is called "intellect." Its opposites are madness, folly, stupidity, and ignorance, according to different considerations.

The terms used in the Noble Quran for the various types of perception (الإدراك) are numerous, perhaps reaching twenty, such as: conjecture (الظن), calculation (الحسبان), sensing (الشعور), remembrance (الذكر), recognition (العرفان), understanding (الفهم), comprehension (الفقه), discernment (الدراية), certainty (اليقين), reflection (الفكر), opinion (الرأي), assertion (الزعم), preservation (الحفظ), wisdom (الحكمة), expertise (الخبرة), witnessing (الشهادة), and intellect (العقل). Related to these are terms like: saying (القول), formal legal opinion (الفتوى), insight (البصيرة), and the like.

Conjecture (الظن) — the predominant belief (التصديق الراجح) even if it does not reach the level of absolute conviction and certainty (القطع); and the same applies to calculation (الحسبان).

However, the use of calculation (الحسبان) for conjectural perception seems to be a metaphorical usage (استعمال استعاري), similar to "counting" (العد) in the sense of conjecture. Its origin is from our saying: "He counted Zayd among the heroes" or "He calculated him among them," meaning he included him with them in the count and reckoning.

Sensing (الشعور) — the subtle perception (الإدراك الدقيق); derived from "hair" (الشعر) due to its fineness. Its usage is predominantly for the sensible (المحسوس) rather than the intelligible (المعقول), and from this comes the term "senses" (المشاعر) for the sensory faculties.

Remembrance (الذكر) — the summoning of a form stored in the mind after its absence from perception, or preserving it from becoming absent from perception.

Recognition and knowledge (العرفان والمعرفة) — the application of a form obtained in the perceiving faculty to what is already stored in the mind; hence it is said that it is perception after prior knowledge.

Understanding (الفهم) — a type of mental affection (انفعال) from the external world through the impression of a form within the mind.

Comprehension (الفقه) — the firm establishment (التثبت) of this impressed form and stability in belief (التصديق).

Discernment (الدراية) — the deep penetration (التوغل) into that establishment and stability until the specificities, hidden aspects, and merits of the known object are perceived. Therefore, it is used in contexts of magnifying and aggrandizing a matter. The Exalted says: الْحَاقَّةُ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ — "The Besieger! What is the Besieger? And what will show you what the Besieger is?" (69:1-3), and the Exalted says: إِنَّا أَنزَلْنَاهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ — "Indeed We sent it down on the Night of Ordainment. And what will show you what the Night of Ordainment is?" (97:1-2).

Certainty (اليقين) — the intensification of mental perception such that it does not accept cessation or weakness.

Reflection (الفكر) — a kind of movement and passage over existing, present information to acquire the unknown things that necessarily follow from them.

Opinion (الرأي) — the belief (التصديق) resulting from reflection and deliberation. However, its usage is predominant in the practical sciences (العلوم العملية) regarding what ought to be done and what ought not to be done, rather than the theoretical sciences (العلوم النظرية) pertaining to ontological matters (الأمور التكوينية). Close to it are insight (البصيرة), issuing a legal opinion (الإفتاء), and saying (القول). However, the use of "saying" (القول) is as if it were a metaphorical usage, placing the necessary consequence (اللازم) in place of the necessitated (الملزوم), because saying something necessitates belief in what it indicates.

Assertion (الزعم) — belief (التصديق) insofar as it is a form in the mind, whether it is a predominant belief or a definitive, certain one.

Knowledge (العلم), as previously mentioned — the perception that precludes its contradiction (النقيض).

Preservation (الحفظ) — the retention of the known form such that change and cessation do not find a way to it.

Wisdom (الحكمة) — the cognitive form (الصورة العلمية) insofar as it is firm and perfected.

Expertise (الخبرة) — the manifestation of the cognitive form such that it is not hidden from the knower how any result follows from its premises.

Witnessing (الشهادة) — the direct attainment (نيل) of the thing itself and its essence, either through the external senses as in sensible things, or internally as in subjective experiences (الوجدانيات) such as knowledge, will, love, hatred, and the like.

The preceding terms, based on the meanings we have identified, are not free from association with matter (المادة), motion (الحركة), and change (التغير). Therefore, none of them are used in reference to the Exalted except for the last five: knowledge (العلم), preservation (الحفظ), wisdom (الحكمة), expertise (الخبرة), and witnessing (الشهادة). Thus, it is not said of the Exalted that He "conjectures," "calculates," "asserts," "understands," or "comprehends," and so on.

As for the last five terms, because they do not entail deficiency or lack, they are used in reference to the Exalted. He, the Glorified, says: وَاللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ — "and Allah has knowledge of all things" (4:176); and the Exalted says: وَرَبُّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ حَفِيظٌ — "and your Lord is watchful over all things" (34:21); and the Exalted says: وَاللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ — "and Allah is well aware of what you do" (2:234); and the Exalted says: هُوَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ — "He is the All-knowing, the All-wise" (12:83); and the Exalted says: أَنَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ — "that He is witness to all things?" (41:53).

Let us return to our subject and say: the term intellect (العقل), as you have learned, is applied to perception insofar as it involves the "binding" (عقد) of the heart through belief (التصديق), based on what Allah, the Glorified, has naturally disposed (جبل) man upon: perceiving truth and falsehood in theoretical matters, and good and evil, benefits and harms in practical matters. For Allah, the Glorified, created him with a constitution (خلقة) such that he perceives himself at the beginning of his existence, then He equipped him with external senses through which he perceives the outward aspects of things, and with internal senses through which he perceives spiritual meanings (معاني روحية) by which his soul connects with things external to it—such as will, love, hatred, hope, fear, and the like. Then he operates upon these through arrangement, detail, specification, and generalization. Thus, he issues a theoretical judgment (قضاء نظري) regarding theoretical matters and things outside the stage of action, and a practical judgment (قضاء عملي) regarding practical matters and things related to action—all of this proceeding along the course identified for him by his original primordial nature (فطرته الاصلية). This is the intellect (العقل).

However, some faculties may dominate man by overcoming other faculties—such as lust (الشهوة) and anger (الغضب)—thereby nullifying the judgment of the rest or weakening it. Through this, man deviates from the path of moderation (صراط الاعتدال) into the valleys of excess and deficiency. Consequently, this intellectual agent does not function within him in its sound state, much like a judge who decides based on false, deviant, or distorted evidence or testimonies; he swerves in his judgment from the truth even if he judges without intending falsehood. He is a judge, yet not a judge. Similarly, man judges in the realms of false information as he does; and although his action is called "intellect" (عقلاً) by way of some indulgence (مسامحة), it is not intellect in reality, because man has at that point departed from the soundness of the primordial nature (سلامة الفطرة) and the ways of correctness.

Upon this basis, His speech, the Exalted, proceeds. For He defines the intellect (العقل) as that which benefits man in his religion and by which he mounts his guidance toward the realities of knowledge and righteous deeds. If it does not proceed along this course, it is not called intellect, even if it operates in worldly good and evil only. The Exalted says: وَقَالُوا لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ مَا كُنَّا فِي أَصْحَابِ السَّعِيرِ — "And they will say, ‘Had we listened or applied reason, we would not have been among the inmates of the Blaze.’" (67:10).

And the Exalted says: أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَتَكُونَ لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ يَعْقِلُونَ بِهَا أَوْ آذَانٌ يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَا فَإِنَّهَا لَا تَعْمَى الْأَبْصَارُ وَلَٰكِن تَعْمَى الْقُلُوبُ الَّتِي فِي الصُّدُورِ — "Have they not travelled through the land so that they may have hearts by which to exercise their reason, or ears by which to hear? Indeed, it is not the eyes that turn blind, but it is the hearts, which are in the breasts, that turn blind." (22:46). The verses, as you see, use "intellect" (العقل) for the knowledge that man independently establishes by himself, and "hearing" (السمع) for the perception in which he seeks the aid of another, provided the primordial nature (الفطرة) remains sound in all of that. The Exalted says: وَمَن يَرْغَبُ عَن مِّلَّةِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ إِلَّا مَن سَفِهَ نَفْسَهُ — "And who will turn away from the creed of Abraham except one who befools himself?" (2:130). It has already been mentioned that the verse is like the contrapositive (عكس النقيض) of his saying (peace be upon him): "The intellect is that by which the All-Beneficent is worshipped..." to the end of the tradition.

It has become clear from all we have mentioned that what is meant by "intellect" (العقل) in His speech, the Exalted, is the perception that is completed for man along with the soundness of his primordial nature. Through this, the meaning of His saying, the Glorified, becomes apparent: "Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may exercise your reason." For through clarification (البيان), knowledge (العلم) is completed, and knowledge is a premise for the intellect and a means to it, as the Exalted says: وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثَالُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ وَمَا يَعْقِلُهَا إِلَّا الْعَالِمُونَ — "We draw these parables for mankind; but no one grasps them except those who have knowledge." (29:43).

(Commentary)

In the Sunan of Abu Dawud, narrated from Asma’ bint Yazid ibn al-Sakan al-Ansariyyah, she said: "I was divorced during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and there was no waiting period (عدة) for a divorced woman. So when I was divorced, the waiting period for divorce was sent down: وَالْمُطَلَّقَاتُ يَتَرَبَّصْنَ بِأَنفُسِهِنَّ ثَلَاثَةَ قُرُوءٍ — 'Divorced women shall wait by themselves for three periods of purity [or monthly periods]' (2:228). Thus, she was the first for whom the waiting period for divorce was revealed."

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالْمُطَلَّقَاتُ يَتَرَبَّصْنَ بِأَنفُسِهِنَّ ثَلَاثَةَ قُرُوءٍ, narrated from Zurarah, who said: "I heard Rabi‘at al-Ra’y saying: 'In my opinion, the periods (الأقراء) that Allah named in the Quran are only the state of purity (الطهر) between two menstruations, and not the menstruation itself.'" He said: "So I went to Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) and told him what Rabi‘ah said. He said: 'He did not say it by his own opinion; rather, it reached him from Ali (peace be upon him).'" I said: "May Allah set you right, did Ali (peace be upon him) use to say that?" He said: "Yes, he used to say: 'The period (القرء) is the state of purity; the blood gathers (تقرأ) in it and collects, and when it [the menstruation] comes, it expels it.'" I said: "May Allah set you right, what about a man who divorces his wife while she is in a state of purity without having had intercourse, with the testimony of two just witnesses?"
