He said: "When she enters the third menstruation, her waiting period has ended and she is lawful for [other] husbands," to the end of the tradition.

I say: This meaning is narrated through several chains from him (peace be upon him). His saying: "I said: May Allah set you right, did Ali (peace be upon him) use to say that?"—he only inquired about this after his saying (peace be upon him): "It reached him from Ali," because it was famous among the general public (العامة) that Ali used to say that the periods (القروء) in the verse are the menstruations (الحيض) rather than the states of purity (الأطهار), as found in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Shafi‘i, Abd al-Razzaq, Abd ibn Humayd, and al-Bayhaqi from Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him), who said: "The right of return (الرجعة) for her husband remains until she washes from the third menstruation, then she is lawful for [other] husbands." However, the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt deny this and attribute to him (peace be upon him) that the periods are the states of purity rather than the menstruations, as seen in the previous narration. They have also attributed this view to several other companions besides him (peace be upon him), such as Zayd ibn Thabit, Abdullah ibn Umar, and Aisha, and narrated it from them.

In al-Majma‘, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا يَحُلُّ لَهُنَّ أَن يَكْتُمْنَ مَا خَلَقَ اللَّهُ فِي أَرْحَامِهِنَّ — "and it is not lawful for them to conceal what Allah has created in their wombs" (2:228): "Pregnancy (الحبل) and menstruation (الحيض)."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: "Allah has delegated three things to women: purity, menstruation, and pregnancy."

Also in the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلرِّجَالِ عَلَيْهِنَّ دَرَجةٌ — "and men have a degree [of advantage] over them" (2:228), he said: "He (peace be upon him) said: 'The right of men over women is greater than the right of women over men.'"

I say: This does not contradict equality in terms of the establishment of rights, as previously mentioned.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: الطَّلَاقُ مَرَّتَانِ فَإِمْسَاكٌ بِمَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ تَسْرِيحٌ بِإِحْسَانٍ — "Divorce [of the sort that allows a return] is twice; then [there must be] either an honorable retention or a kindly release" (2:229), from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "Allah says: 'Divorce is twice; then an honorable retention or a kindly release,' and the 'kindly release' is the third divorce."

In al-Tahdhib, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "The divorce of the Sunnah (طلاق السنة) is that he divorces her once—meaning in a state of purity without intercourse, with the testimony of two witnesses—then he leaves her until her periods pass. When her periods have passed, she is separated (بانت) from him, and he becomes one of the suitors: if she wishes, she marries him, and if she wishes, she does not. If he wants to take her back (يراجعها), he must have witnesses for his taking her back before her periods pass, and she remains with him based on the previous divorce," to the end of the tradition.

In al-Faqih, from al-Hasan ibn Faddal, who said: "I asked al-Rida about the reason why a woman divorced for the [third] time is not lawful for her husband until she marries a husband other than him. He (peace be upon him) said: 'Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, only permitted divorce twice, saying: الطَّلَاقُ مَرَّتَانِ فَإِمْسَاكٌ بِمَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ تَسْرِيحٌ بِإِحْسَانٍ, meaning in the third divorce. And because of his entering into what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, dislikes of divorce—which He made forbidden to him—she is not lawful for him until she marries a husband other than him, so that people do not treat divorce lightly and so that women are not harmed,'" to the end of the tradition.

I say: The school of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt is that divorce with a single pronouncement or in a single sitting counts only as one divorce, even if he says "I divorce you thrice," according to what the Shi‘a narrate. As for the Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama‘ah, their narrations regarding this vary: some indicate it counts as one divorce, and some indicate that three occur. They sometimes narrate this from Ali and Ja‘far ibn Muhammad (peace be upon them). However, it appears from some of their narrations recorded by the authors of the Sahih collections, such as Muslim, al-Nasa’i, Abu Dawud, and others, that the occurrence of three with a single pronouncement was something Umar permitted after two or three years of his caliphate. In al-Durr al-Manthur: "Abd al-Razzaq, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Nasa’i, al-Hakim, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: 'Divorce during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), Abu Bakr, and two years of the caliphate of Umar, was such that the triple divorce counted as one. Then Umar ibn al-Khattab said: "Indeed, people have become hasty in a matter in which they had patience; if only we were to enforce it upon them." So he enforced it upon them.'"

In the Sunan of Abu Dawud, from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Abd Yazid, the father of Rukanah, divorced the mother of Rukanah and married a woman from Muzaynah. She came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and said: 'He is of no use to me except as this hair is of use to a hair'—taking a hair from her head—'so separate between me and him.' The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) was moved by protective zeal (حمية) and called for Rukanah and his brothers. Then he said to those sitting with him: 'Do you see that so-and-so resembles him in such-and-such, and so-and-so in such-and-such?' They said: 'Yes.' The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said to Abd Yazid: 'Divorce her,' and he did. He said: 'Take back your wife, the mother of Rukanah.' He said: 'I have divorced her thrice, O Messenger of Allah.' He said: 'I know; take her back,' and he recited: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ فَطَلِّقُوهُنَّ لِعِدَّتِهِنَّ — 'O Prophet! When you divorce women, divorce them at [the onset of] their waiting period' (65:1)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, from al-Bayhaqi from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Rukanah divorced a woman thrice in one sitting, then he grieved for her intensely. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) asked him: 'How did you divorce her?' He said: 'I divorced her thrice in one sitting.' He said: 'Yes, that is only one, so take her back if you wish.' So he took her back. Ibn Abbas used to hold that divorce should be at every state of purity, for that is the Sunnah that Allah commanded: 'Divorce them at their waiting period.'"

I say: This meaning is also narrated in other traditions, and the discussion regarding this permission is similar to the previous discussion regarding the Mut‘ah of Hajj.

The non-occurrence of three with a single pronouncement has been argued using His saying, the Exalted: الطَّلَاقُ مَرَّتَانِ (Divorce is twice), for "twice" and "thrice" do not apply to what is initiated with a single pronouncement, as is the case in the matter of Li‘an (mutual imprecation) by the consensus of all.

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ تَسْرِيحٌ بِإِحْسَانٍ (or a kindly release), he said: "There are two views regarding it. One: that it is the third divorce. The second: that he leaves the woman in her waiting period until she is separated by the end of the waiting period. This is from al-Suddi and al-Dahhak, and it is what is narrated from Abu Ja‘far and Abu Abdullah (peace be upon them)."

I say: The reports, as you see, differ regarding the meaning of His saying: "or a kindly release."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا يَحُلُّ لَكُمْ أَن تَأْخُذُوا مِمَّا آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ شَيْئًا إِلَّا أَن يَخَافَا أَلَّا يُقِيمَا حُدُودَ اللَّهِ — "And it is not lawful for you to take back anything from what you have given them, unless the couple fears that they may not maintain Allah’s bounds" (2:229), from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "The Khul‘ (divorce at the wife's request) does not occur unless the woman says to her husband: 'I will not fulfill an oath for you, I will surely go out without your permission, I will surely allow others to tread upon your bed, and I will not wash from ritual impurity for you,' or she says: 'I will not obey any command of yours, so divorce me.' When she says that, it becomes lawful for him to take from her all that he gave her and everything he can of what she gives him from her wealth. When they agree upon that, he divorces her in a state of purity with witnesses, and she is separated from him by one [divorce], and he becomes one of the suitors. If she wishes, she marries him, and if she wishes, she does not. If he marries her, she is with him with two remaining [divorces]. He should make a condition with her as the practitioner of Mubarat (mutual release) does: 'If you take back anything of what you gave me, then I possess your person (بضعك).'" He (peace be upon him) said: "There is no Khul‘, nor Mubarat, nor Takhyir (giving the choice) except in a state of purity without intercourse, with the testimony of two just witnesses. When the woman who has undergone Khul‘ marries another husband and then he divorces her, it is lawful for the first to marry her." He said: "The husband has no right of return over the woman of Khul‘ nor the woman of Mubarat unless the woman changes her mind, in which case he returns to her what he took from her."

In al-Faqih, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him), who said: "If the woman says to her husband the phrase: 'I will not obey any command of yours,' whether explained or unexplained, it is lawful for him to take from her, and he has no right of return over her."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: "Ahmad narrated from Sahl ibn Abi Hathmah, who said: 'Habibah bint Sahl was under Thabit ibn Qays ibn Shammas, but she disliked him, as he was an ugly man. She came and said: "O Messenger of Allah, I truly do not see him [with favor]; were it not for the fear of Allah, I would have spat in his face." He said to her: "Will you return to him his garden that he gave as your dowry?" She said: "Yes." So she returned his garden to him and he separated them. That was the first Khul‘ in Islam.'"

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Blessed and Exalted: وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللَّهِ فَلَا تَعْتَدُوهَا — "These are Allah’s bounds, so do not transgress them" (2:229). He said: "Indeed, Allah was angry with the adulterer and prescribed for him a hundred lashes. Whoever is angry with him and adds [to that], then I am free from him before Allah. That is His saying, the Exalted: 'These are Allah’s bounds, so do not transgress them.'"

In al-Kafi, from Abu Basir, who said: "The woman who is not lawful for her husband until she marries a husband other than him—he said: 'She is the one who is divorced, then taken back, then divorced a third time; she is the one who is not lawful for her husband until she marries a husband other than him and he tastes her sweetness (عسيلتها).'"

I say: Al-Usaylah (sweetness) refers to intercourse. It is said in al-Sihah: "In intercourse, it is called al-usaylah, likening that pleasure to honey (al-asal). It is made diminutive with the letter ha because honey is predominantly feminine. It is also said: it is made feminine because what is meant is al-asalah, which is a piece of it, just as a piece of gold is called dhahabah." End quote.

His saying (peace be upon him): "and he tastes her sweetness," is like an adaptation from the word of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم): "No, until you taste his sweetness and he tastes your sweetness," in the story of Rifa‘ah.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: "From al-Bazzar, al-Tabarani, and al-Bayhaqi: Rifa‘ah ibn Samu’al divorced his wife, so she came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, Abd al-Rahman has married me, and he has nothing with him except like this,' and she pointed to the fringe of her garment. The Messenger of Allah began to turn away from her speech, then he said to her: 'Do you wish to return to Rifa‘ah? No, until you taste his sweetness and he tastes your sweetness.'"

I say: The narration is among the famous ones, narrated by a large group of narrators from the authors of the Sahih collections and others from the paths of the Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama‘ah and some of the elite (الخاصة). Although the wordings of the narrations vary, most of them include this term.

In al-Tahdhib, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding whether Mut‘ah marriage (تزويج المتعة) makes [the woman] lawful [for the first husband]? He said: "No, because Allah says: فَإِن طَلَّقَهَا فَلَا تَحُلُّ لَهُ مِن بَعْدُ حَتَّىٰ تَنكِحَ زَوْجًا غَيْرَهُ فَإِن طَلَّقَهَا فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِمَا أَن يَتَرَاجَعَا — 'And if he divorces her, she will not be lawful for him thereafter until she marries a husband other than him. Then if he divorces her, there is no sin on them in returning to each other' (2:230), and in Mut‘ah there is no divorce."

Also in it, from Muhammad ibn Mudarib, who said: "I asked al-Rida (peace be upon him) whether a eunuch (الخصي) makes [the woman] lawful? He said: 'He does not make [her] lawful.'"

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ فَبَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ to His saying وَلَا تُمْسِكُوهُنَّ ضِرَارًا لِّتَعْتَدُوا — "And when you divorce women and they complete their term... and do not retain them to harm them, so that you transgress" (2:231). He said: "He (peace be upon him) said: 'When he divorces her, it is not permissible for him to take her back if he does not [truly] want her.'"

In al-Faqih, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "It is not proper for a man to divorce his wife, then take her back when he has no need for her, then divorce her. This is the 'harm' (الضرار) that Allah forbade, unless he divorces then takes back while intending to retain [her]."

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَتَّخِذُوا آيَاتِ اللَّهِ هُزُوًا — "and do not take the signs of Allah in derision" (2:231), from Umar ibn al-Jami‘, raising it to the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he said: "And whoever among this Ummah recites the Quran then enters the Fire, he is among those who took the signs of Allah in derision," to the end of the tradition.

In Sahih al-Bukhari, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ فَبَلَغْنَ أَجَلَهُنَّ, the verse: "The sister of Ma‘qil ibn Yasar was divorced by her husband, who then left her until her waiting period ended. Then he proposed to her, but Ma‘qil refused. So there was revealed: فَلَا تَعْضُلُوهُنَّ أَن يَنكِحْنَ أَزْوَاجَهُنَّ — 'do not prevent them from marrying their husbands' (2:232)."

I say: This meaning was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from him and from several authors of the Sahih collections such as al-Nasa’i, Ibn Majah, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Dawud, and others.

Also in al-Durr al-Manthur, from al-Suddi, who said: "This verse was revealed regarding Jabir ibn Abdullah al-Ansari. He had a female cousin whose husband divorced her once, and her waiting period ended. He then wanted to take her back, but Jabir refused, saying: 'You divorced our cousin and now you want to marry her a second time?' The woman wanted her husband, so Allah revealed: وَإِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ, the verse."

I say: There is no guardianship (ولاية) for the brother or the cousin according to the school of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt. Even if one of the two narrations were accepted, the prohibition in the verse is not aimed at defining guardianship, nor at establishing a legal status (حكم وضعي), but rather at guiding toward the repulsiveness of coming between a husband and wife, or for a reprehensibility (كراهة) or a moral prohibition (حرمة تكليفية) pertaining to anyone who prevents them from marriage, and nothing else.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالْوَالِدَاتُ يُرْضِعْنَ أَوْلَادَهُنَّ — "Mothers shall suckle their children" (2:233), from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "'Mothers shall suckle their children for two full years.' He said: 'As long as the child is being suckled, he is between the parents equally. When he is weaned, the father has more right to him than the agnates (العصبة). If the father finds someone to suckle him for four dirhams, and the mother says: "I will not suckle him except for five dirhams," then he has the right to take him from her, except that it is more restorative for him, more primary, and more gentle for him to be left with his mother.'"

Also in it, from him regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَا تُضَارَّ وَالِدَةٌ — "No mother shall be made to suffer" (2:233). He (peace be upon him) said: "The woman used to raise her hand against the man when he wanted to have intercourse with her, saying: 'I will not let you; I fear that I will become pregnant with my child.' And the man would say to the woman: 'I will not have intercourse with you; I fear that you will conceive and I will kill my child.' So Allah forbade the man from harming the woman and the woman from harming the man."

Also in it, from one of them (peace be upon them) regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَعَلَى الْوَارِثِ مِثْلُ ذَٰلِكَ — "and the same [duty] rests upon the heir" (2:233). He said: "It is regarding maintenance (النفقة): upon the heir is the same as what is upon the father."

Also in it, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: "It is also not proper for the heir to harm the woman by saying: 'I will not let her child come to her,' and to harm her child if they have something with him; nor should he be stingy toward him."

Also in it, from Hammad from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "There is no suckling after weaning." He said: "I said to him: 'May I be your ransom, what is weaning?' He said: 'The two years that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, mentioned.'"

I say: His saying: "The two years," is a citation of what is in the wording of the verse, which is why he (peace be upon him) described it by saying: "that Allah mentioned."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: "Abd al-Razzaq in al-Musannaf and Ibn Adi narrated from Jabir ibn Abdullah, who said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: "There is no orphanhood after puberty (حلم), no suckling after weaning (فصال), no silence for a day until night, no continuous fasting (وصال), no vow in disobedience, no spending in disobedience, no oath in the severing of kinship, no returning to nomadism after migration (تعرّب بعد الهجرة), no migration after the Conquest [of Mecca], no oath for a wife with her husband, no oath for a child with a parent, no oath for a slave with his master, no divorce before marriage, and no manumission before ownership."'"

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Abu Bakr al-Hadrami from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "When this verse was revealed: وَالَّذِينَ يُتَوَفَّوْنَ مِنكُمْ وَيَذَرُونَ أَزْوَاجًا يَتَرَبَّصْنَ بِأَنفُسِهِنَّ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ وَعَشْرًا — 'As for those of you who die leaving wives, they shall wait by themselves for four months and ten days' (2:234), women came to dispute with the Messenger of Allah and said: 'We cannot endure [this].' The Messenger of Allah said to them: 'One of you, when her husband died, used to take a piece of dung and throw it behind her back in her chamber, then she would sit [there]. When the same day of the following year came, she would take it and break it, then use it as kohl, then she would marry. Allah has removed eight months from you.'"

In al-Tahdhib, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "In every marriage, when the husband dies, the woman—whether free or slave, and in whatever form the marriage was, whether Mut‘ah, permanent marriage, or ownership—must observe a waiting period of four months and ten days."

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "I said to him: 'May I be your ransom, how did the waiting period of the divorced woman become three menstruations or three months, while the waiting period of the one whose husband died became four months and ten days?' He said: 'As for the waiting period of the divorced woman being three periods, it is for the purpose of ensuring the womb is free of a child. As for the waiting period of the one whose husband died, Allah made a condition for women and a condition upon them. As for what He conditioned for them, it is in Ila’ (vow of abstinence) for four months, as He says: لِّلَّذِينَ يُؤْلُونَ مِن نِّسَائِهِمْ تَرَبُّصُ أَرْبَعَةِ أَشْهُرٍ — "For those who vow to abstain from their wives, there shall be a waiting period of four months" (2:226); thus it is not permissible for anyone to exceed four months, because of His knowledge, Blessed and Exalted is He, that it is the limit of a woman's patience for a man. As for what He conditioned upon them, He commanded her to observe a waiting period when her husband dies for four months and ten days; thus He took from her for him at his death what He took for her from him during his life.'"

I say: This meaning is also narrated from al-Rida and al-Hadi (peace be upon them) through other chains.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيمَا عَرَّضْتُم بِهِ مِنْ خِطْبَةِ النِّسَاءِ — "There is no sin on you in what you may hint at in proposing to women" (2:235). "The woman in her waiting period—you say to her a beautiful word to make her desire you, and you do not say: 'I will do such and such' or 'I will do such and such'—the repulsive matter regarding the person (البضع) and every repulsive matter. In another narration: you say to her while she is in her waiting period: 'O lady, I love only what pleases you, and if your waiting period were to pass, do not let me miss out, if Allah wills, and do not hold back from yourself.' All of this is without them resolving on the knot of marriage."

I say: There are other narrations from them (peace be upon them) regarding this meaning.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَّا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِن طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ — "There is no sin on you if you divorce women" (2:236), from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "If a man divorces his wife before he has entered into her, she is entitled to half of her dowry. If he had not specified a dowry for her, then a provision (متاع) in an honorable manner—upon the wealthy according to his means and upon the poor according to his means. She has no waiting period and may marry whomever she wishes immediately."

In al-Kafi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding a man who divorces his wife before he has entered into her, he said: "He owes half the dowry if he had fixed something for her. If he had not fixed anything for her, let him provide for her (فليمتعها) in the manner that women like her are provided for."

I say: In it is the explanation of "provision in an honorable manner" (المتاع بالمعروف).

In al-Kafi, al-Tahdhib, the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, and others, from al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (peace be upon them) regarding His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ عُقْدَةُ النِّكَاحِ — "he in whose hand is the marriage tie" (2:237), they both said: "He is the guardian (الولي)."

I say: The narrations regarding this are numerous. It has been reported in some narrations from the paths of the Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama‘ah from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and Ali (peace be upon him) that "he in whose hand is the marriage tie" is the husband.

In al-Kafi, al-Faqih, and the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi and al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: حَافِظُوا عَلَى الصَّلَوَاتِ وَالصَّلَاةِ الْوُسْطَىٰ — "Be watchful of your prayers, and [especially] the middle prayer" (2:238), through many chains from al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (peace be upon them): "The middle prayer is the Noon prayer (الظهر)."

I say: This is what is transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt in the narrations reported from them with a single voice.

Yes, in some of them it is the Friday prayer (الجمعة), except that what is understood from them is that they considered the Noon and Friday prayers as one type, not two distinct types, as narrated in al-Kafi and the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi from Zurarah from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him)—and the wording is from al-Kafi: "Allah, the Exalted, said: 'Be watchful of your prayers, and the middle prayer,' and it is the Noon prayer, the first prayer the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) prayed, and it is in the middle of the day, and in the middle of two prayers during the day: the Morning prayer and the Afternoon prayer. He said: 'And this verse was revealed while the Messenger of Allah was on a journey, so the Messenger of Allah performed the Qunut (supplication) in it and left it as it was in journey and at home. He added two units (ركعتين) for the resident. The two units that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) added on Friday for the resident were only established in place of the two sermons with the Imam. So whoever prays on Friday without a congregation, let him pray it as four units like the Noon prayer on other days,'" to the end of the tradition. The narration, as you see, counts the Noon and Friday prayers as one prayer and judges that it is the middle prayer. However, most of the narrations are disconnected (مقطوعة), and those that are supported by a chain (مسندة) have texts that are not free from confusion, such as the narration of al-Kafi. Moreover, its application to the verse is not entirely clear. And Allah knows best.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: "Ahmad, Ibn al-Mani‘, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Jarir, al-Shashi, and al-Diya’ narrated through the path of al-Zibarqan: 'A group from the Quraysh were passed by Zayd ibn Thabit while they were gathered. They sent two of their youths to him to ask him about the middle prayer. He said: "It is the Noon prayer." Then they went to Usamah ibn Zayd and asked him, and he said: "It is the Noon prayer. Indeed, the Messenger of Allah used to pray the Noon prayer in the midday heat (بالهجير) such that there would be only one or two rows behind him, while people were in their midday nap or their trade. So Allah revealed: 'Be watchful of your prayers, and the middle prayer, and stand before Allah in obedience.' Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) said: 'Either men will stop [staying away] or I will surely burn their houses.'"'

I say: This occasion [of revelation] was narrated from Zayd ibn Thabit and others through other paths.

Know that the opinions regarding the interpretation of the middle prayer are diverse, most of them arising from the differences in their narrations. It was said it is the Morning prayer, narrated from Ali (peace be upon him) and some companions. It was said it is the Noon prayer, narrated from the Prophet and several companions. It was said it is the Afternoon prayer, narrated from the Prophet and several companions—al-Suyuti narrated over fifty traditions regarding this in al-Durr al-Manthur. It was said it is the Sunset prayer. It was said it is hidden among the prayers like the Night of Ordainment among the nights; narrations from the companions were reported regarding both. It was said it is the Night prayer, and it was said it is the Friday prayer.

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَقُومُوا لِلَّهِ قَانِتِينَ — "and stand before Allah in obedience" (2:238), he said: "It is the supplication (الدعاء) in the prayer while standing, and it is what is narrated from Abu Ja‘far and Abu Abdullah (peace be upon them)."

I say: That was also narrated from some of the companions.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "A man's attentiveness to his prayer and his watchfulness over its time so that nothing distracts him or occupies him from it."

I say: There is no contradiction between the two narrations, as is apparent.

In al-Kafi, from al-Sadiq regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ فَرِجَالًا أَوْ رُكْبَانًا — "And if you are in fear, then [pray] on foot or riding" (2:239): "If he fears a predatory beast or a thief, he says the Takbir and gestures with signs."

In al-Faqih, from him (peace be upon him) regarding the prayer of the thick of battle (صلاة الزحف), he said: "Takbir and Tahlil," then he recited the verse.

In it, from him (peace be upon him): "If you are in a fearful land and you fear a thief or a predatory beast, then pray the obligatory prayer while you are on your mount."

In it, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "He who fears thieves prays by gesturing on his mount."

I say: The narrations regarding these meanings are numerous.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Abu Basir, who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah: وَالَّذِينَ يُتَوَفَّوْنَ مِنكُمْ وَيَذَرُونَ أَزْوَاجًا وَصِيَّةً لِّأَزْوَاجِهِم مَّتَاعًا إِلَى الْحَوْلِ غَيْرَ إِخْرَاجٍ — 'Those of you who die leaving wives shall bequeath for their wives a year’s maintenance without expulsion' (2:240). He (peace be upon him) said: 'It is abrogated (منسوخة).' I said: 'How was it?' He said: 'When a man died, his wife was maintained from the core of the estate for a year, then she was sent out without inheritance. Then the verse of the quarter and the eighth [shares] abrogated it, so the woman is maintained from her own share.'"

In it, from Mu‘awiyah ibn Ammar, who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah: 'Those of you who die...' etc. He said: 'It is abrogated; the verse "they shall wait by themselves for four months and ten days" abrogated it, and the verse of inheritance abrogated it.'"

In al-Kafi and the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi: al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) was asked about a man who divorces his wife—should he provide for her (يمتعها)? He said: "Yes. Does he not love to be among the virtuous (المحسين)? Does he not love to be among the God-conscious (المتقين)?"

(Scientific Discussion)

It is well known that Islam—the laws of which were legislated by Allah, exalted is His Name—did not build its laws upon the principle of experimentation (التجارب) as other laws have been built. However, in the judgment of the intellect regarding its laws, we may need to reflect upon the rulings, laws, and customs prevalent among contemporary nations and past centuries, then investigate human happiness (السعادة الإنسانية) and apply the result to what has been gathered from their schools and paths, so that we may weigh its status and theirs, and distinguish its living, perceiving spirit from their spirits. This is what necessitates referring to the histories of nations and their biographies, and summoning the characteristics and paths of life found among those currently existing.

Therefore, we need, in investigating what Islam views and believes regarding:

  1. The identity of woman and the comparison between her identity and that of man.
  2. Her weight in society, so that the extent of her influence on the life of the human world may be known.
  3. Her rights and the rulings legislated for her sake.
  4. The foundation upon which the rulings related to her were built.

To summon what history has recorded of her life before the dawn of Islam and how non-Muslim nations—civilized and otherwise—treated her until today. Although an exhaustive investigation of this is beyond the scope of this book, we shall mention a portion of it:

(The Life of Woman in Uncivilized Nations)

The life of women in primitive nations and tribes—such as those inhabiting Africa, Australia, the islands of Oceania, ancient America, and others—was, in relation to the life of men, like the life of domestic animals (الحيوانات الأهلية) such as cattle and others in relation to the life of man.

For just as man, due to his innate disposition for exploitation (قريحة الاستخدام), sees for himself a right to possess cattle and other domestic animals and to dispose of them however he wishes and for any of his needs—benefiting from their hair, wool, meat, bones, blood, skin, milk, their protection and guarding, their mating, offspring, and growth, in carrying heavy loads, in plowing, in hunting, and other countless purposes.

And these mute animals have no goals in life or hopes of the heart regarding food, drink, shelter, mating, and rest except what is approved by the man who possesses them; and he will only approve of what does not contradict his purposes in subjugating them and in which there is benefit for his life. This often led to strange ironies and bizarre arbitrary acts from the perspective of the exploited animal, were it to look into its own affairs: some are oppressed without having committed any crime, crying out with no one to aid them; some are oppressors with no one to stop them; some are happy without merit, like the breeding stallion living in the most luxurious and pleasurable life; and some are wretched without merit, like the pack donkey or the mill horse.

They have no rights in life except what the man who owns them sees as a right for himself. Whoever transgresses against them is only held accountable because he transgressed against their owner in his property, not against the animal in itself. All of this is because man sees their existence as subordinate to his own existence, their life as a branch of his life, and their status as that of a parasite (الطفيلي).

Similarly, the life of women among the men of these nations and tribes was a subordinate life. Women were created, in their view, "for men" in an absolute sense: women were subordinate in existence and life to them, without independence in life or in right. Their fathers were their absolute guardians as long as they were not married, and their husbands after marriage.

A man had the right to sell the woman to whomever he wished; he had the right to gift her to another; he had the right to lend her to one who requested her for the bed, for procreation, for service, or otherwise. He had the right to discipline her even unto death. He had the right to abandon her, whether she lived or died. He had the right to kill her and feed on her meat like a beast, especially during famine and at banquets. He possessed whatever wealth or rights the woman had, especially in terms of conducting transactions such as buying, selling, taking, and giving.

It was the duty of the woman to obey the man—her father or her husband—in whatever he commanded, whether willingly or unwillingly. She was not to be independent of him in any matter pertaining to him or her. She was responsible for the affairs of the house, the children, and everything the man's life required therein. She had to endure the most arduous of tasks, such as carrying heavy loads, working with clay, and the like, and the most menial and trivial of crafts and industries. This reached such a strange extent that a pregnant woman in some tribes, upon giving birth, would immediately rise to attend to the needs of the house, while the man would lie on her bed for days, acting sick and treating himself. These are the generalities of what was for him and upon her; and every one of these primitive generations has characteristics and specificities of national customs and traditions, varying according to their inherited habits in their regions of life and surrounding environments, which can be discovered by those who refer to books authored on these matters.

(The Life of Woman in Civilized Nations)

Before Islam

By these, we mean the nations that lived under national traditions preserved by inherited customs without reliance on a book or law, such as ancient China, India, Egypt, Iran, and the like.

All these nations shared the fact that woman among them possessed no independence or freedom, neither in her will nor in her actions. Rather, she was under guardianship (الولاية) and custodianship (القيمومة). She could not accomplish anything on her own, nor did she have the right to interfere in social affairs such as government, judiciary, or others.

It was her duty to participate with the man in all works of life, such as earning and so on.

It was her duty to be restricted to the affairs of the house and children, and she had to obey the man in everything he commanded and desired of her.

Woman among these was in a more comfortable state compared to her state in uncivilized nations. She was not killed and her meat was not eaten, nor was she entirely deprived of possessing wealth; rather, she possessed it in general through inheritance, marriage, or otherwise, even if she did not have the right to dispose of it independently. A man had the right to take multiple wives without limit, and he had the right to divorce whomever he wished of them. A husband could marry after the death of his wife, but the reverse was generally not the case. She was generally forbidden from socializing outside the house.

Each of these nations had specificities according to the requirements of regions and conditions: just as the distinction of classes in Iran sometimes necessitated a distinction for women of the high classes in interfering in the kingdom and government, or attaining sovereignty and the like, or marriage with close relatives (المحارم) such as mother, daughter, sister, or others.

In China, marriage to a woman was a kind of purchasing her person and her being enslaved. she was barred from inheritance and from sharing food with men, even her own sons. Men could share—more than one of them—in marriage to a single woman, sharing in the enjoyment of her and benefiting from her labor, and the children were generally attributed to the strongest of the husbands.

In India, women were among the subordinates of their husbands; it was never permissible for them to marry after the death of their husbands. Rather, they would either be burned with fire along with the bodies of their husbands or live humiliated. During their menstrual days, they were considered impure and filthy (أنجاس خبيثات), requiring avoidance, as were their clothes and everything they touched with their skin.

Her status in these nations can be summarized as follows: she was like an intermediate realm (برزخ) between animal and human. She was utilized as a weak, average human who had no right except to assist the average human in the affairs of his life, like a small child in relation to his guardian, except that she remained under guardianship and custodianship permanently.

(And here are other nations)

The aforementioned nations were those whose specific customs and traditions mostly proceeded on the basis of regional requirements and inherited habits, without apparently relying on a book or law. However, there were other nations that lived under the control of law or a book, such as the Chaldeans, the Romans, and the Greeks.

As for the Chaldeans and Assyrians, the law of "Hammurabi" ruled among them with the subordination of the woman to her husband and the loss of her independence in will and action. So much so that if the wife did not obey her husband in any matter of cohabitation or acted independently in it, he had the right to expel her from his house, or marry another over her and treat her thereafter purely as a slave. If she erred in managing the house through extravagance or wastefulness, he had the right to bring her case to the judge and then drown her in water after proving the crime.

As for the Romans, they are also among the oldest nations in establishing civil laws. The law was first established there around four hundred years before Christ, then they began to complete it gradually. It granted the household a kind of independence in executing orders specific to it. The head of the household (رب البيت)—who was the husband of the woman and the father of her children—possessed a kind of lordship (ربوبية); for that reason, the members of the household worshipped him, just as he worshipped his ancestors who preceded him in founding the household. He had absolute choice and an effective will in everything he desired and commanded of the household members—wife and children—even death if he saw benefit in it, and no one would oppose him in that. The women of the household, such as the wife, daughter, and sister, were in a worse state than the men, even the sons who were purely subordinate to the head of the household. For they were not parts of the civil society; thus, no complaint of theirs was heard, no transaction of theirs was effective, and no interference in social affairs was valid for them. However, the men—meaning the brothers and the males among the children, even the adopted ones (for adoption and attributing a child to other than his father was a common practice among them, as well as in Greece, Iran, and the Arabs)—it was permissible for the head of the household to grant them independence in the affairs of life absolutely for themselves.

They [women] were not original parts of the household; rather, the members of the household were the men, while the women were subordinates. Thus, the official social kinship (القرابة الاجتماعية الرسمية) effective in inheritance and the like was restricted to what was between men. As for women, there was no kinship between themselves, such as the mother with the daughter or the sister with the sister, nor between them and the men, such as the spouses, or the mother with the son, or the sister with the brother, or the daughter with the father. There was no inheritance where there was no official kinship. Yes, natural kinship (القرابة الطبيعية)—which is necessitated by connection in birth—existed among them, and its effect would sometimes appear in matters like marriage with close relatives, and the guardianship of the head and lord of the household over her.

In short, woman among them was a parasitic existence (طفيلية الوجود), subordinate in life within society (both civil and domestic society). The reins of her life and will were in the hand of the head of the household—her father if she was in the father's house, or her husband if she was in the husband's house, or others. Her lord would do with her as he wished and judge regarding her as he desired. He might sell her, gift her, or lend her for enjoyment. He might give her in a right to be fulfilled from him, such as a debt or tax and the like. He might discipline her with death, beating, or otherwise. In his hand was the management of her wealth if she owned anything through marriage or earning with the permission of her guardian—not through inheritance, for she was deprived of it. In the hand of her father or one of the nobles of her people was her marriage, and in the hand of her husband was her divorce.

As for the Greeks, the matter among them regarding the formation of households and the lordship of their heads within them was close in status to the Roman situation.

Civil society, as well as domestic society, was constituted by men, and women were subordinate to them. Therefore, she had no independence in will or action except under the guardianship of men. However, they all contradicted themselves in reality regarding that; for their established laws would judge against them [women] with independence, but would not judge for them except by subordination when it agreed with the benefit of men. Thus, the woman among them was punished for all her crimes independently, but was not rewarded for her good deeds, nor was her side considered except by subordination and under the guardianship of the man.

This itself is among the evidences indicating that all these laws did not view her as a weak part of human society possessing a subordinate personality; rather, they estimated that she was like harmful germs (الجراثيم المضرة), corrupting the temperament of society and harmful to its health, except that society had a necessary need for her in terms of the survival of the species. Therefore, her affairs had to be attended to, and she had to taste the consequence of her matter if she offended or committed a crime, while men would milk her benefit if she did good or was useful. She was not to be left to her own will, to guard against her evil, like a powerful enemy who is overcome and taken as a captive slave, living his whole life under coercion: if he does evil, he is held accountable for it, and if he does good, he is not thanked for it.

This which you have heard—that social assembly was, in their view, sustained by men—is what compelled them to believe that true children are only the males, and that the survival of the lineage is through their survival. This was the origin of the practice of adoption (التبني) and affiliation (الإلحاق) among them. For a household whose master had no male child was deemed ruined, and its lineage was marked for extinction and demise. Thus, they were forced to adopt sons to safeguard against extinction and the death of the male line. They called those who were not the biological offspring of their loins "sons" for themselves; these became sons by formal convention (رسماً), inheriting and being inherited from, and the legal effects of biological sons were applied to them. If a man among them believed himself to be sterile and unable to sire a child, he would turn to one of his relatives, such as his brother or nephew, and bring him to his wife’s bed so she might conceive from him; she would then bear a child whom he would claim as his own to maintain the continuity of his house.

The matter of marriage and divorce in Greece was similar to that of Rome. Polygyny was permissible among them, except that if a man had more than one wife, only one was the official wife (زوجة رسمية) while the others were unofficial.

(The Condition of Women Among the Arabs and Their Living Environment)

The Environment of the Quran’s Revelation

The Arabs inhabited the Peninsula, a hot region with barren land. The majority of their nation consisted of nomadic tribes far removed from civilization and urbanity, living by launching raids. They were connected to Persia on one side, Rome on another, and the lands of Abyssinia and Sudan on yet another.

Consequently, the bulk of their customs were those of savagery (التوحش), though occasionally one might find among them elements of Roman or Persian customs, and sometimes even ancient Indian or Egyptian traditions.

The Arabs did not recognize any independence in life, sanctity, or nobility for the woman, except for the sanctity and nobility of the household. They did not allow women to inherit. They permitted polygyny without a specific limit on number, similar to the Jews, and the same applied to divorce. They practiced female infanticide (تئد البنات); this was initiated by the Banu Tamim due to a battle they had with al-Nu'man ibn al-Mundhir, in which several of their daughters were taken captive. The story is well-known: this enraged them, so they began the practice, which then spread as a disposition (السجية) to others. The Arabs would feel an ill omen if a daughter was born to a man; he would consider it a disgrace to himself and would hide from the people because of the "evil" news he had received. However, he would be delighted by a son, no matter how many, even through adoption or affiliation. They would even adopt a child resulting from adultery (زنا) committed by a married woman, and sometimes their powerful and influential men would dispute over a child, each claiming it for himself.

Occasionally, in some households, a degree of independence appeared for their women, especially for daughters in the matter of marriage, where the woman’s consent and choice were considered. This resembled the practice of the nobility in Persia, which was based on class distinction.

In any case, their treatment of women was a composite of the treatment by civilized peoples like the Romans and Persians—such as prohibiting independence in rights and participation in general social affairs like governance, war, and marriage (except by exception)—and the treatment by savage and barbaric peoples. Their deprivation of women was not based on the sanctification or worship of the heads of households, but rather on the principle of the strong prevailing over and exploiting the weak.

As for worship (العبادة), they all (men and women) worshipped idols whose nature resembled the idols of the Sabians, the followers of the stars and the lords of species (ارباب الانواع). Their idols were distinguished according to the distinctions of the tribes and their varying whims. They worshipped stars and angels (whom they claimed were the daughters of Allah, the Glorified) and fashioned them into forms depicted by their imaginations, using various materials like stone and wood. Their whims in this regard reached such a point that it is narrated of the Banu Hanifa that they fashioned an idol out of hays (a mixture of dates, clarified butter, and flour) and worshipped it for a long time; then a famine struck them, so they ate it. It was said of them:

Hanifa ate its lord In the time of hardship and famine They did not fear from their lord The evil consequences and retribution

Sometimes they would worship a stone until they found a better one, at which point they would cast away the first and take the second. If they found nothing, they would gather a handful of soil, bring a sheep and milk it over the soil, then circumambulate it in worship.

This deprivation and misery instilled in the souls of women a weakness of thought (ضعفاً في الفكرة) that conjured strange illusions and superstitions regarding various events and occurrences, which have been recorded in books of biography and history.

These are summaries of the condition of women in human society across its various stages before Islam and at the time of its emergence, in which we have preferred total brevity. From all of this, several conclusions can be drawn: First: They viewed her as a human being on the horizon of a non-rational animal (الحيوان العجم), or a human of diminished humanity and debased status whose evil and corruption could not be trusted if she were released from the bond of dependency and gained freedom in her life. The first view is more suited to the conduct of savage nations, and the second to others. Second: They viewed her social weight (وزنها الاجتماعي) as being outside the structure of the composite society, not included within it; rather, she was considered one of its indispensable conditions, like a dwelling that one must resort to, or like an enslaved captive who is an appendage of the victorious society, whose labor is utilized but whose guile is not trusted, across the two different approaches. Third: They viewed her as deprived of all general rights from which she could benefit, except to the extent that her benefit returned to the benefit of the men in charge of her affairs. Fourth: The basis of their treatment of her was the dominance of the strong over the weak—in other words, the instinct of exploitation (قريحة الاستخدام). This was the case in uncivilized nations. As for civilized nations, they added to this their belief regarding her: that she is a human of weak constitution (ضعيف الخلقة), incapable of independence in her affairs, and whose evil is not to be trusted. Sometimes these matters became intertwined due to the differences between nations and generations.

(What Islam Innovated Regarding Her Affair)

All [these societies] continued to view the matter of women as we have recounted to you, confining her in the prison of humiliation and lowliness until weakness and smallness became a second nature (طبيعة ثانية) for her, upon which her flesh and bone grew, and upon which she lived and died. The terms "woman," "weakness," and "humiliation" became like synonymous words after having been distinct—not only among men but even among women, which is truly astonishing. You will not find a nation, whether savage or civilized, but that they have proverbs regarding her weakness and the lowliness of her affair. In their languages, despite their different origins, contexts, and tones, there are types of metaphor, metonymy, and simile linked to this word ("woman") used to taunt the coward, rebuke the weak, and blame the forsaken, the despised, and the oppressed. One finds verses like:

I know not—and would that I knew— Are the folk of Hisn a tribe of men or women?

There are hundreds and thousands of such examples in poetry and prose in every language.

This in itself is sufficient for a researcher to understand what the human collective (الجامعة الإنسانية) believed regarding the matter of women, even if the doctrines of nations and religions recorded in history books were not available. For the spiritual traits and existential aspects of every nation are manifested in its language and literature.

Nothing was inherited from the predecessors that showed significant concern for her or prioritized her affair, except for some of what is in the Torah and what Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon them both) enjoined regarding the necessity of easing her burden and treating her with kindness.

As for Islam—meaning the primordial religion (الدين الحنيف) revealed in the Quran—it innovated regarding her right something the world had not known since its inhabitants first dwelt therein. It opposed them all by building a structure based on the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — which the world had demolished from the first day and whose traces it had erased. It abolished what the world believed regarding her identity (الهوية) as a matter of belief, and how it treated her as a matter of practice.

As for her identity: It clarified that the woman, like the man, is a human being, and that every human, male or female, is a human being in whose matter and element two humans—a male and a female—participate. No one has superiority over another except through godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah and guarding oneself against His displeasure. The Exalted said: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَاكُم مِّن ذَكَرٍ وَأُنثَىٰ وَجَعَلْنَاكُمْ شُعُوبًا وَقَبَائِلَ لِتَعَارَفُوا إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِندَ اللَّهِ أَتْقَاكُمْ — "O mankind! Indeed, We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify with one another. Indeed the noblest of you in the sight of Allah is the most Godwary among you" (49:13). Thus, the Exalted made every human being derived and composed from two humans, a male and a female, who together and in equal proportion are the matter of his being and existence. Whether male or female, the person is the sum of the matter taken from both. The Exalted did not say as the poet said:

The mothers of people are but vessels

Nor did He say as another said:

Our sons are the sons of our sons; as for our daughters, Their sons are the sons of distant men.

Rather, the Exalted made everyone created and composed from everyone. Thus, all became equals (أمثالاً), and no explanation is more complete or eloquent than this. Then He placed superiority in godwariness.

The Exalted also said: أَنِّي لَا أُضِيعُ عَمَلَ عَامِلٍ مِّنكُم مِّن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ بَعْضُكُم مِّن بَعْضٍ — "I do not waste the work of any worker among you, whether male or female; you are all [sprung] from one another" (3:195). He explicitly stated that effort is not in vain and work is not wasted before Allah, and He justified this by saying: "you are all [sprung] from one another." He expressed explicitly what is the result of His saying in the previous verse: "Indeed, We created you from a male and a female," which is that man and woman are both of one species (نوع واحد) without any difference in origin or essence (السنخ).

He then clarified through this that the work of each of these two categories is not wasted before Allah; it does not become void in itself, nor does it pass from the worker to another. "Every soul is a hostage to what it has earned." It is not as people used to say: that women bear their own sins, while men receive the rewards of their existence. A further explanation of this will follow.

Since each of them has what they have wrought, and there is no nobility except through godwariness—and among godwariness are the virtuous traits such as faith (الإيمان) in its various degrees, beneficial knowledge, sound intellect, good character, patience, and forbearance—then a believing woman with degrees of faith, or one filled with knowledge, or sound in intellect, or good in character, is nobler in essence and higher in degree than any man in Islam who does not equal her in those traits, whoever he may be. For there is no nobility except for godwariness and virtue.

In the same vein as the previous verse, and even clearer, is the saying of the Exalted: مَنْ عَمِلَ صَالِحًا مِّن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَيَاةً طَيِّبَةً وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْرَهُم بِأَحْسَنِ مَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — "Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall surely revive him with a good life and pay them their reward by the best of what they used to do" (16:97). And His saying: وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صَالِحًا مِّن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولَٰئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ يُرْزَقُونَ فِيهَا بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ — "Whoever acts righteously, whether male or female, should he be faithful—such shall enter paradise, and they will be provided therein without any reckoning" (40:40). And His saying: وَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِنَ الصَّالِحَاتِ مِن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولَٰئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ نَقِيرًا — "And whoever does righteous deeds, whether male or female, should he be faithful—such shall enter paradise and they will not be wronged [so much as] the speck on a date-stone" (4:124).

Allah, the Glorified, has condemned the disparagement of daughters with verses such as His saying, which is among the most eloquent of condemnations: وَإِذَا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُم بِالْأُنثَىٰ ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُ مُسْوَدًّا وَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ يَتَوارَىٰ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ مِن سُوءِ مَا بُشِّرَ بِهِ أَيُمْسِكُهُ عَلَىٰ هُونٍ أَمْ يَدُسُّهُ فِي التُّرَابِ أَلَا سَاءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ — "When one of them is brought the news of a female [newborn], his face turns black and he chokes with suppressed agony. He hides from the people out of the evil of the news he has been given: shall he keep her in humiliation, or bury her in the ground? Behold! Evil is the judgment that they make" (16:58-59). Their hiding was only because they considered her birth a disgrace to the father. The primary reason for this was their perception that she would grow up to become a plaything for another who would enjoy her; this was seen as a form of dominance by the husband over her in a matter deemed loathsome, the disgrace of which would return to her household and her father. For this reason, they practiced female infanticide. You have already heard the first reason for this. Allah, the Glorified, went to great lengths in emphasizing its gravity when He said: وَإِذَا الْمَوْءُودَةُ سُئِلَتْ بِأَيِّ ذَنبٍ قُتِلَتْ — "when the girl buried-alive is asked for what sin she was killed" (81:8-9).

Remnants of these superstitions have remained among Muslims, inherited from their ancestors, and educators have not washed their stain (الرين) from their hearts. Thus, you see them considering adultery (الزنا) an indelible disgrace upon the woman and her household even if she repents, but not upon the male adulterer even if he persists—whereas Islam has concentrated all disgrace and ugliness in the sin itself, and the male and female adulterer are equal in it.

As for her social weight: Islam has equated her with the man in terms of managing the affairs of life through will (الإرادة) and action (العمل). They are equal in terms of the will's attachment to what the human constitution needs—such as food, drink, and other necessities of survival. The Exalted has said: "you are all [sprung] from one another" (3:195). Thus, she has the right to be independent in will and independent in action, and to possess the fruits of both, just as the man does, without any difference: "For her is what she has earned, and against her is what she has committed."

They are equal in what Islam envisions and what the Quran validates—and Allah validates the truth by His words. However, He has ordained in her two characteristics by which Divine Craftsmanship (الصنع الالهي) has distinguished her: One of them: She is in the position of the tilth (الحرث) in the formation and growth of the species. The species relies upon her for its survival, so she is specifically assigned laws similar to those assigned to tilth, and she is distinguished from the man by this. The second: Her existence is built upon a delicate constitution (لطافة البنية) and refined sentiment (رقة الشعور). This also has an effect on her states and the social functions assigned to her.

This is her social weight, and through it, the weight of the man in society becomes clear. To this are reduced all the laws shared between them and those specific to one of them in Islam. The Exalted said: وَلَا تَتَمَنَّوْا مَا فَضَّلَ اللَّهُ بِهِ بَعْضَكُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ لِّلرِّجَالِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا اكْتَسَبُوا وَلِلنِّسَاءِ نَصِيبٌ مِّمَّا اكْتَسَبْنَ وَاسْأَلُوا اللَّهَ مِن فَضْلِهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمًا — "Do not covet the advantage which Allah has given some of you over others. To men belongs a share of what they have earned, and to women a share of what they have earned. And ask Allah for His bounty. Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things" (4:32). He intends that the actions each group offers to society are the criterion for the specific excellence (الفضل) assigned to them. Some of this excellence is determined to belong to some rather than others—such as the man's advantage over the woman in the share of inheritance, and the woman's advantage over the man in being relieved of the obligation of maintenance (النفقة). Thus, no one should covet it. Other forms of excellence are not determined except by the action of the worker, whoever they may be—such as the excellence of faith, knowledge, intellect, godwariness, and other virtues approved by the religion. That is the bounty of Allah which He gives to whom He wills; "And ask Allah for His bounty." The evidence for what we have mentioned is the saying of the Exalted immediately following it: "Men are the maintainers..." as will be explained later.

As for the shared and specific laws: She shares with the man in all devotional laws (الأحكام العبادية) and social rights. She has the right to be independent in what the man is independent in, without any difference in inheritance, earning, transactions, education, acquisition of rights, defense of rights, and so on—except in cases where her nature necessitates otherwise.

The primary instances of these cases are: she does not assume the roles of government (الحكومة) or judgeship (القضاء), nor does she engage in combat in the sense of direct clashing (المقارعة)—not absolute presence and assistance in the matter, such as treating the wounded. She has half the share of the man in inheritance. Upon her is the veil (الحجاب) and the covering of the places of adornment. Upon her is to obey her husband in what pertains to his enjoyment of her. What she misses [in terms of financial independence] is compensated for by making her maintenance in life the responsibility of the man: the father or the husband. He must protect her to the utmost of his ability. She has the right to the upbringing and custody (حضانة) of the child.

Allah has eased matters for her in that she is protected in person and honor, even from ill mention. Devotional worship is lifted from her during the days of her menses and post-natal bleeding. She is to be treated with kindness (الارفاق) in all circumstances.

The conclusion of all this is: in the realm of knowledge, nothing is obligatory upon her except knowledge of the fundamental doctrines (أصول المعارف) and knowledge of the religious branches (the laws of worship and the regulations governing society). As for the realm of action, it is the laws of religion and obedience to the husband in his enjoyment of her. As for organizing individual life through work or earning by a craft or industry—and likewise entering into what sustains the household, and intervening in what benefits the general society, such as learning sciences and adopting industries and crafts useful to the public and beneficial in social gatherings, while maintaining the established boundaries—none of that is obligatory upon her. The consequence of this is that entering into all these areas—whether knowledge, earning, occupation, or upbringing—are all considered virtues (فضلاً) for her by which she may excel, and a source of pride (فخراً) for her. Islam has permitted, indeed encouraged, mutual pride among women, whereas men are forbidden from pride except in the state of war.

The Prophetic Sunnah (السنة النبوية) supports what we have mentioned. Were it not for the length of this discourse exceeding the capacity of this place, we would have mentioned aspects of the life of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with his wife Khadija, with his daughter the Mistress of Women Fatima (peace be upon her), with his wives, and with the women of his people; and what he enjoined regarding the matter of women; and what is narrated of the way of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt and their women, such as Zaynab daughter of Ali, and Fatima and Sukayna daughters of al-Husayn, and others (peace be upon them all), and their testaments regarding women. Perhaps we will be successful in conveying a portion of this in the traditional discussions (الأبحاث الروائية) related to the verses concerning women; let the seeker refer to them there.

As for the foundation upon which these laws and rights are built, it is the primordial nature (الفطرة). The manner of this construction has been understood from the discussion on her social weight, and we shall further clarify it here by saying:

The researcher into social laws and related scientific inquiries should not doubt that social functions and the conventional obligations (التكاليف الاعتبارية) branching from them must ultimately trace back to nature (الطبيعة). The specific characteristic of the natural human constitution is what guided man to this species-wide social assembly (الاجتماع النوعي) which the species is almost never found without at any time—even if it is possible for this assembly, which is based on the requirement of nature, to be subjected to what takes it from the course of health to the course of corruption, just as it is possible for the natural body to be subjected to what takes it from its natural perfection to a defect in creation, or from its natural health to sickness and malady.

Thus, social assembly in all its affairs and aspects—whether it be a virtuous assembly or a corrupt one—ultimately traces back to nature, though the two types differ in that the corrupt assembly encounters along the path of its tracing back that which corrupts its effects, unlike the virtuous assembly.

This is a reality, and researchers in these fields have pointed to it explicitly or implicitly. The Divine Book preceded them in its explanation, clarifying it with the most exquisite exposition. The Exalted said: الَّذِي أَعْطَىٰ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدَىٰ — "He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (20:50). And the Exalted said: الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّىٰ وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدَىٰ — "who created and proportioned, who determined and guided" (87:2-3). And the Exalted said: وَنَفْسٍ وَمَا سَوَّاهَا فَأَلْهَمَهَا فُجُورَهَا وَتَقْوَاهَا — "and the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its Godwariness" (91:7-8)—among other verses concerning destiny (القدر).

Thus, things—including man—are only guided in their existence and life toward that for which they were created and equipped with what suffices and suits them in terms of creation. The life that is upright (القيمة) with human happiness is that whose actions correspond perfectly to creation and the primordial nature, and whose functions and obligations trace back correctly to nature. This is what is pointed to by the saying of the Exalted: فأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللَّهِ ذَٰلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ — "So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion" (30:30).

What the primordial nature requires regarding social functions and rights among individuals—given that all are human beings possessing a human nature—is to equate between them in rights and functions without favoring some and oppressing others by nullifying their rights. However, the requirement of this equality (التسوية) which social justice (العدل الاجتماعي) dictates is not to grant every social position to every individual of the society. For instance, the child, despite his childhood, or the fool, despite his folly, should not assume what the rational, experienced person assumes; nor should the weak and incapable person take on the affairs and ranks that the strong and capable person takes on. For equating the state of the fit and the unfit is a corruption of the state of both.

Rather, what social justice requires, and how the meaning of equality is interpreted, is that every possessor of a right be given his right and placed in his proper position. Equality between individuals and classes is only in each possessor of a right attaining his specific right without one right encroaching upon another, or a right being neglected or nullified through transgression or arbitrariness. This is what is pointed to by the saying of the Exalted: "And women have rights similar to those [of men] over them in a courteous manner, and men have a degree over them," as previously explained. For the verse explicitly states equality while simultaneously affirming the difference between them and men.

Furthermore, the participation of both groups—men and women—in the foundations of existential gifts, namely thought (الفكر) and will (الإرادة) which generate choice (الاختيار), necessitates their participation with the man in freedom of thought and will—that is, choice. She has the right to independence in managing all affairs of her individual and social life, except where a barrier prevents it. Islam has given her this independence and freedom in the most complete manner, as you have heard. Thus, by the grace of Allah, she became independent in herself, her will and action separated from men and their guardianship (ولاية) and maintenance (قيمومة), possessing what the world did not grant her in all its eras and what was absent from the pages of the history of her existence. The Exalted said: فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِيمَا فَعَلْنَ فِي أَنفُسِهِنَّ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ — "there is no sin upon you in what they may do with themselves in a courteous manner" (2:234).

However, despite the presence of the aforementioned shared factors in her existence, she differs from men in another respect. The average woman lags behind the average man in the perfecting characteristics (الخصوصيات الكمالية) of her constitution—such as the brain, heart, arteries, nerves, stature, and weight—as explained by the science of physiology. This necessitated that her body be more delicate and soft, just as the man's body is coarser and harder. Delicate feelings (الإحساسات اللطيفة) such as love, tenderness of heart, and the inclination toward beauty and adornment are more dominant in her than in the man, just as rationality (التعقل) is more dominant in him than in the woman. Her life is a sentimental life (حياة إِحساسية), just as the man's life is a rational life (حياة تعقلية).

Therefore, Islam differentiated between them in the general social functions and obligations whose stability is linked to one of the two matters—rationality or sentiment. It assigned roles such as governance, judgeship, and combat to men because of their dire need for rationality, and the rational life is for the man rather than the woman. It assigned roles such as the custody and upbringing of children and the management of the home to the woman. It made her maintenance the responsibility of the man, and compensated him for this with two shares in inheritance. (In reality, this is as if they divide the inheritance into two halves, then the woman gives a third of her share to the man in exchange for her maintenance—that is, for the benefit of half of what is in his hand. Thus, it returns in reality to two-thirds of the wealth in the world belonging to men in ownership and essence (عيناً), and two-thirds to women in benefit (انتفاعاً). Thus, the dominant management is for men due to the dominance of their rationality, and the dominant benefit and enjoyment is for women due to the dominance of their sentiment. We shall further clarify this in the discussion on the verses of inheritance, if Allah wills). Then He completed this with easements and mitigations for the woman, as previously indicated.

If you say: What has been mentioned of the extreme kindness shown to the woman in Islam necessitates her idleness in work. For the removal of the dire need for the necessities of life by her seclusion, and the sufficiency of her provision by making maintenance obligatory upon the man, leads to her neglect, laziness, and reluctance to bear the hardships of work and occupations. Thus, she grows in a poor manner and develops into an unhealthy plant unfit for the perfection of society, as experience has confirmed.

I say: Enacting laws that rectify the condition of humanity is one thing, and implementing them through righteous conduct and good upbringing—which causes the human to grow as a good plant—is another. What Islam has been afflicted with during its past course is the loss of righteous guardians and striving maintainers. Consequently, the breath of the laws was stifled, upbringing halted, and then it regressed. Among the clearest things established by definitive experience is that mere theory and belief do not produce their effect unless they are established in the soul through righteous propagation and upbringing. Muslims, except for a very brief period, did not benefit from the guardians who manifested their authority and were in charge of their affairs a righteous upbringing that combines knowledge and action. Here is Mu'awiya, saying on the pulpit of Iraq when he gained control over the Caliphate, the gist of which is: "I did not fight you so that you would pray or fast—that is up to you—but I fought you so that I might rule over you, and I have done so." And such were others from the Umayyads, the Abbasids, and those after them. Were it not for this religion being illuminated by the light of Allah which is never extinguished—and Allah will complete His light even if the faithless loathe it—it would have been finished long ago.

(The Freedom of Women in Western Civilization)

There is no doubt that Islam has the brilliant precedence in releasing her from the bond of captivity and giving her independence in will and action. The nations of the West, in what they have fashioned regarding her affair, have only imitated Islam—even if they performed the imitation and emulation poorly. For the conduct of Islam is a prominent and most influential link in the chain of social progression, being an intermediate and intervening one. It is impossible for the end of the chain to connect to its beginning without it.

In short, these people have built upon total equality between man and woman in rights in these times, after striving for that for years, despite the woman's lag in perfecting characteristics relative to the man, as you have heard in summary.

The general opinion among them is approximately that the woman's lag in perfection and virtue is due to the poor upbringing that has persisted for her and lasted for centuries—perhaps equaling the age of the world—despite her nature being equal to the man's nature.

The response to this is: Society, since the earliest eras of its formation, has dictated her lag behind the man in general. If the two natures were equal, the opposite would have appeared at least sometimes, and the creation of her primary organs and others would have changed to be like those in the man.

This is supported by the fact that Western civilization, despite its utmost care in advancing the woman, has not yet been able to create equality between them. Statistics in all areas where Islam prioritized the man over the woman—such as governance, judgeship, and combat—still place men ahead and women behind. As for what this equality has bequeathed to the structure of present society, we shall explain what is possible for us in its proper place, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

(Another Scientific Inquiry)

The act of marriage (النكاح) is among the foundational social acts. Humanity, from its first formation and multiplication until today, has never been without this social act. You have already learned that these acts must have a natural basis to which they return, initially or ultimately.

Islam placed this act, when legislating it, upon the basis of the creation of maleness and femaleness. For it is clear that this reciprocal equipment (التجهيز المتقابل) found in the man and the woman—which is a precise equipment encompassing the entire bodies of males and females—was not placed in vain. It is clear to anyone who reflects deeply that the nature of the human male, in its equipment, desires only the female, and vice versa; and that this equipment has no goal other than the generation of the like and the preservation of the species thereby. The act of marriage is built upon this reality, and all laws related to it revolve around it. Therefore, legislation was placed upon this—that is, upon the private parts (البضع)—and upon it were placed the laws of chastity (العفة), intercourse, the exclusivity of the wife to the husband, and the laws of divorce, the waiting period (العدة), children, inheritance, and the like.

As for other contemporary laws, they have placed the basis of marriage upon the spouses sharing their efforts in life. Thus, marriage is a type of partnership in living, which is a narrower circle than municipal society and the like. Therefore, you do not see contemporary laws addressing any of what Islam addressed regarding the laws of chastity and so on.

This construction—along with the various problems and social hazards that branch from it, as we shall explain if Allah, the Mighty, wills—does not correspond to the basis of creation and the primordial nature at all. For the utmost we find in man of the natural motive toward society and the sharing of efforts is that his constitution, for the happiness of his life, needs many matters and various works that he cannot perform alone except through assembly and cooperation. Thus, the whole is sustained by the whole, and the specific desires—each attached to one of the occupations and types of work—are distributed among individuals; from their sum, the sum of occupations and works is achieved.

This motive only calls for assembly and cooperation between one individual and another, whoever they may be. As for the assembly consisting of a man and a woman, there is no call from this motive specifically for it. Thus, building marriage upon the basis of vital cooperation is a deviation from the path of the natural requirement for procreation and reproduction toward something else for which there is no call from nature and the primordial nature.
