If the matter were so—meaning the placement of marriage upon the basis of cooperation and partnership in life—it would be necessary that the matter of marriage not be distinguished by any social laws at all except the general laws placed for absolute partnership and cooperation. In that would be the total nullification of the virtue of chastity, and the nullification of the laws of lineage (الأنساب) and inheritance, as Communism has committed to. In that would be the nullification of all the natural instincts with which the males and females of humanity have been equipped. We shall further clarify this in a suitable place, if Allah wills. This is a summary of the discourse on marriage. As for divorce, it is among the prides of this Islamic Sharia. Its permissibility was placed upon the primordial nature, as there is no evidence from nature that indicates its prohibition. As for the specifics of the restrictions taken in its legislation, the discussion on them will come in Surah al-Talaq, if Allah, the Mighty and Dear, wills.

The great nations today have been forced to introduce it into their civil laws after it was not there.

Ayah

۞ أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ خَرَجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ وَهُمْ أُلُوفٌ حَذَرَ ٱلْمَوْتِ فَقَالَ لَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مُوتُوا۟ ثُمَّ أَحْيَـٰهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٢٤٣)
Have you not regarded those who left their homes in thousands, apprehensive of death, whereupon Allah said to them, ‘Die,’ then He revived them? Indeed Allah is gracious to mankind, but most people do not give thanks. (243)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ خَرَجُوا مِن دِيَارِهِمْ وَهُمْ أُلُوفٌ حَذَرَ الْمَوْتِ "Have you not regarded those who left their homes in thousands, precarious of death?" Seeing (الرؤية) here carries the meaning of knowledge (العلم). This expression is employed to claim that the matter is so manifest that knowledge of it is regarded as seeing it. It is similar to His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ "Have you not regarded that Allah created the heavens and the earth with the truth?" (Ibrahim: 19), and His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَوْا كَيْفَ خَلَقَ اللَّهُ سَبْعَ سَمَاوَاتٍ طِبَاقًا "Have you not regarded how Allah created the seven heavens in layers?" (Nuh: 15).

Al-Zamakhshari mentioned that the phrase "Have you not regarded" (ألم تر) functions like a proverb; it is invoked in contexts of wonderment. Thus, our saying "Have you not seen such and such" means "Are you not amazed by such and such?" The phrase "precarious of death" (حذر الموت) is an object of purpose (مفعول له), though it is also possible for it to be an absolute object (مفعول مطلق), with the implied meaning being "they feared death with a great fearing."

His saying, the Exalted: فَقَالَ لَهُمُ اللَّهُ مُوتُوا ثُمَّ أَحْيَاهُمْ "whereupon Allah said to them, ‘Die!’ Then He revived them." The command (الأمر) here is existential (تكويني) — pertaining to the divine decree that brings things into being — and it does not contradict the fact that their death occurred through a natural course, as mentioned in the narrations that it was by means of a plague. The use of the command, rather than saying "Allah caused them to die and then revived them," is intended to be more indicative of the effective penetration of Divine Power and the dominance of His command. Indeed, expression through the creative imperative (الإنشاء) in existential matters (التكوينيات) is more potent and emphatic than expression through reporting (الإخبار). Conversely, expression in the form of reporting to indicate occurrence in legislative matters (التشريعيات) is more potent and emphatic than the imperative. Furthermore, His saying, the Exalted, "Then He revived them" (ثم أحياهم) is not devoid of the indication that Allah revived them so that they might live; thus, they lived on after their revival. Had their revival been merely for a lesson to be learned by others, or to complete a proof, or to clarify a reality, it would have been mentioned as such, according to the custom of the Qur'an in its eloquence, as seen in the story of the People of the Cave. Moreover, His saying, the Exalted, immediately after: "Indeed Allah is gracious to mankind," also intimates this.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ "but most people do not give thanks." The use of the explicit noun in place of a pronoun—namely, the repetition of the word "mankind" (الناس) for the second time—is due to its indication of the low level of their intellects. Furthermore, those upon whom Allah bestowed the favor of revival were a specific group, and the meaning is not that the majority of that specific group were ungrateful, but rather the majority of all mankind. This verse is not without some relevance to the verses that follow it concerning the obligation of fighting (القتال), as there is in struggle (الجهاد) a revival of the nation (الملة) after its death.

Some commentators have mentioned that the verse is a parable (مثل) struck by Allah for the state of a nation in its decline and death through the humiliation imposed by foreigners who spread their authority over it, and then its life through its uprising and defense of its vital rights and independence in self-governance.

He said, in summary: If the verse were intended to relate a story from the stories of the Children of Israel—as most narrations indicate—or others, it would have been necessary to point out that they were from the Children of Israel and to mention the prophet who revived them, as is the custom of the Qur'an in its other stories. Yet the verse is devoid of that. Furthermore, the Torah does not mention this in the stories of the Prophet Ezekiel (peace be upon him and upon our Prophet and his Progeny). Thus, the narrations are nothing but Isra'iliyyat (إسرائيليات) — narratives of Judeo-Christian origin — which the Jews interpolated. Moreover, worldly death and life are but one death and one life, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: لَا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا الْمَوْتَ إِلَّا الْمَوْتَةَ الْأُولَىٰ "They will not taste death therein except for the first death" (Ad-Dukhan: 56), and His saying, the Exalted: وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ "and You have given us life twice" (Al-Mu'min: 11). Therefore, there is no meaning for two lives in this world. Thus, the verse is presented as a parable, and what is meant by it is a people who were attacked by those of power and strength among their enemies, who sought to humiliate and subjugate them, spreading authority over them and ruling them. They did not defend their independence and left their homes while they were thousands—possessing numbers and resolve—precarious of death. So Allah said to them, "Die the death of disgrace and ignorance," for ignorance and lethargy are death, just as knowledge and the refusal of injustice are life. The Exalted said: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذَا دَعَاكُمْ لِمَا يُحْيِيكُمْ "O you who have faith! Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life" (Al-Anfal: 24), and the Exalted said: أَوَمَن كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَاهُ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُ نُورًا يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَن مَّثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُمَاتِ لَيْسَ بِخَارِجٍ مِّنْهَا "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people, like one whose description is that he is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (Al-An'am: 122).

In short, these people die through disgrace and the empowerment of enemies over them, and they remain dead. Then Allah revived them by casting into them the spirit of uprising and the defense of truth. Thus, they stood up for their rights and became independent in their affairs. These people whom Allah revived, even if they are different individuals from those whom Allah caused to die, the whole is one nation that died at one time and lived at another time later on. Allah has regarded a people as one despite the difference in individuals, as in His saying to the Children of Israel: أَنجَيْنَاكُم مِّنْ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ "We delivered you from Pharaoh’s clan" (Al-A'raf: 141), and His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ بَعَثْنَاكُم مِّن بَعْدِ مَوْتِكُمْ "Then We raised you up after your death" (Al-Baqarah: 56). Were it not for what we mentioned regarding the verse being presented as a parable, the connection of the verse to the verses of fighting that follow it would not be sound, and this is evident. This is the summary of what he mentioned.

This discourse, as you see, is based firstly on the denial of miracles (المعجزات) and the breaking of natural norms (خوارق العادات) — supernatural events that transcend the regular laws of nature — or some of them, such as the revival of the dead. The proof for these has already passed. Furthermore, the manifest sense of the Qur'an in establishing the breaking of norms through the revival of the dead and the like is something that cannot be denied, even if we were unable to prove its possibility through the way of reason (العقل).

Secondly: It is based on the claim that the Qur'an indicates the impossibility of more than one life in this world, as he argued with verses like His saying, the Exalted: لَا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا الْمَوْتَ إِلَّا الْمَوْتَةَ الْأُولَىٰ "They will not taste death therein except for the first death" (Ad-Dukhan: 56), and His saying, the Exalted: وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ "and You have given us life twice" (Al-Mu'min: 11).

The response to this is that all the verses indicating the revival of the dead—as in the stories of Ibrahim, Musa, 'Isa, and 'Uzayr—in a manner whose indication cannot be repelled, are sufficient to refute what he mentioned. Moreover, worldly life does not become two lives by the intervention of death, as is most clearly understood from the story of 'Uzayr, where he did not perceive his prolonged death. What is meant by the verses he cited is the type of life.

Thirdly: It is based on the idea that if the verse were intended to relate a story, it would have identified their people and specified the prophet who revived them.

You know that the methods of eloquence (البلاغة) are diverse and varied. Just as speech may follow the course of prolixity (الإطناب), it may also follow the course of brevity (الإيجاز). The verse has parallels in the Qur'an, such as His saying, the Exalted: قُتِلَ أَصْحَابُ الْأُخْدُودِ النَّارِ ذَاتِ الْوَقُودِ إِذْ هُمْ عَلَيْهَا قُعُودٌ وَهُمْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَفْعَلُونَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ شُهُودٌ "Perish the Men of the Ditch! The fire abounding in fuel, as they sat by it, and were witnesses to what they did to the faithful" (Al-Buruj: 4-7), and His saying, the Exalted: وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنَا أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ "Among those We have created are a nation who guide by the truth and do justice thereby" (Al-A'raf: 181).

Fourthly: It is based on the claim that if the verse is not taken as a parable, it would not be connected in meaning to the verses following it. You know that the gradual revelation of the Qur'an (نجوماً) renders unnecessary any forced attempt to link verses to one another, except for those whose connection is manifest, being joined according to the requirements of eloquent speech.

The truth is that the verse, as is its manifest sense, is intended to relate a story. I wonder, what eloquence is there in Allah, the Glorified, delivering to people a speech in which most observers see nothing but a story from the stories of the past, while it is in reality a parable based on imagination (التخييل) without reality?

This is especially so when the custom of His speech, the Exalted, is to distinguish a parable from other things in all the parables placed within it, through phrases such as: مَثَلُهُمْ كَمَثَلِ الَّذِي "Their parable is that of one who..." (Al-Baqarah: 17), and His saying: إِنَّمَا مَثَلُ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا "The parable of the life of this world is only..." (Yunus: 24), and His saying: مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ حُمِّلُوا "The parable of those who were charged..." (Al-Jumu'ah: 5), and others.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Ihtijaj, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he (peace be upon him) said: أحيى الله قوماً خرجوا من اوطانهم هاربين من الطاعون ، لا يحصى عددهم ، فأماتهم الله دهراً طويلاً حتى بليت عظامهم ، وتقطعت أوصالهم ، وصاروا تراباً ، فبعث الله في وقت احب ان يرى خلقه نبياً يقال له : حزقيل ، فدعاهم فاجتمعت ابدانهم ، ورجعت فيها ارواحهم ، وقاموا كهيئة يوم ماتوا ، لا يفتقدون في اعدادهم رجلاً فعاشوا بعد ذلك دهراً طويلاً . — "Allah revived a people who left their homelands fleeing from the plague; their number could not be counted. So Allah caused them to die for a long period until their bones decayed, their joints separated, and they became dust. Then, at a time when He wished to show His creation, Allah sent a prophet called Ezekiel (حزقيل). He called them, and their bodies gathered, their spirits returned to them, and they stood up in the same state as the day they died, not missing a single man from their number. They lived after that for a long time."

I say: This meaning was narrated by al-Kulayni and al-'Ayyashi in a more detailed manner, and at its end: "And concerning them, this verse was revealed."

Ayahs

وَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٤٤)
Fight in the way of Allah, and know that Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (244)
مَّن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يُقْرِضُ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا فَيُضَـٰعِفَهُۥ لَهُۥٓ أَضْعَافًا كَثِيرَةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَقْبِضُ وَيَبْصُۜطُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٢٤٥)
Who is it that will lend Allah a good loan that He may multiply it for him severalfold? Allah tightens and expands [the means of life], and to Him you shall be brought back. (245)
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلْمَلَإِ مِنۢ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مُوسَىٰٓ إِذْ قَالُوا۟ لِنَبِىٍّ لَّهُمُ ٱبْعَثْ لَنَا مَلِكًا نُّقَـٰتِلْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ هَلْ عَسَيْتُمْ إِن كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْقِتَالُ أَلَّا تُقَـٰتِلُوا۟ ۖ قَالُوا۟ وَمَا لَنَآ أَلَّا نُقَـٰتِلَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَقَدْ أُخْرِجْنَا مِن دِيَـٰرِنَا وَأَبْنَآئِنَا ۖ فَلَمَّا كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقِتَالُ تَوَلَّوْا۟ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّنْهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٢٤٦)
Have you not regarded the elite of the Israelites after Moses, when they said to their prophet, ‘Appoint for us a king that we may fight in the way of Allah.’ He said, ‘May it not be that you will not fight if fighting were prescribed for you?’ They said, ‘Why should we not fight in the way of Allah, when we have been expelled from our homes and [separated from] our children?’ So when fighting was prescribed for them, they turned back except a few of them, and Allah knows well the wrongdoers. (246)
وَقَالَ لَهُمْ نَبِيُّهُمْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَدْ بَعَثَ لَكُمْ طَالُوتَ مَلِكًا ۚ قَالُوٓا۟ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لَهُ ٱلْمُلْكُ عَلَيْنَا وَنَحْنُ أَحَقُّ بِٱلْمُلْكِ مِنْهُ وَلَمْ يُؤْتَ سَعَةً مِّنَ ٱلْمَالِ ۚ قَالَ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰهُ عَلَيْكُمْ وَزَادَهُۥ بَسْطَةً فِى ٱلْعِلْمِ وَٱلْجِسْمِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يُؤْتِى مُلْكَهُۥ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٤٧)
Their prophet said to them, ‘Allah has appointed Saul as king for you.’ They said, ‘How can he have kingship over us, when we have a greater right to kingship than him, as he has not been given ample wealth?’ He said, ‘Indeed Allah has chosen him over you, and enhanced him vastly in knowledge and physique, and Allah gives His kingdom to whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing.’ (247)
وَقَالَ لَهُمْ نَبِيُّهُمْ إِنَّ ءَايَةَ مُلْكِهِۦٓ أَن يَأْتِيَكُمُ ٱلتَّابُوتُ فِيهِ سَكِينَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَبَقِيَّةٌ مِّمَّا تَرَكَ ءَالُ مُوسَىٰ وَءَالُ هَـٰرُونَ تَحْمِلُهُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٢٤٨)
Their prophet said to them, ‘Indeed the sign of his kingship shall be that the Ark will come to you, bearing tranquillity from your Lord and the relics left behind by the House of Moses and the House of Aaron, borne by the angels. There is indeed a sign in that for you, should you be faithful.’ (248)
فَلَمَّا فَصَلَ طَالُوتُ بِٱلْجُنُودِ قَالَ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مُبْتَلِيكُم بِنَهَرٍ فَمَن شَرِبَ مِنْهُ فَلَيْسَ مِنِّى وَمَن لَّمْ يَطْعَمْهُ فَإِنَّهُۥ مِنِّىٓ إِلَّا مَنِ ٱغْتَرَفَ غُرْفَةًۢ بِيَدِهِۦ ۚ فَشَرِبُوا۟ مِنْهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّنْهُمْ ۚ فَلَمَّا جَاوَزَهُۥ هُوَ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ قَالُوا۟ لَا طَاقَةَ لَنَا ٱلْيَوْمَ بِجَالُوتَ وَجُنُودِهِۦ ۚ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَظُنُّونَ أَنَّهُم مُّلَـٰقُوا۟ ٱللَّهِ كَم مِّن فِئَةٍ قَلِيلَةٍ غَلَبَتْ فِئَةً كَثِيرَةًۢ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (٢٤٩)
As Saul set out with the troops, he said, ‘Allah will test you with a stream: anyone who drinks from it will not belong to me, but those who do not drink from it will belong to me, barring someone who draws a scoop with his hand.’ But they drank from it, [all] except a few of them. So when he crossed it along with the faithful who were with him, they said, ‘We have no strength today against Goliath and his troops.’ Those who were certain that they would encounter Allah said, ‘How many a small party has overcome a larger party by Allah’s will! And Allah is with the patient.’ (249)
وَلَمَّا بَرَزُوا۟ لِجَالُوتَ وَجُنُودِهِۦ قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ أَفْرِغْ عَلَيْنَا صَبْرًا وَثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَنَا وَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٢٥٠)
So when they marched out for [encounter with] Goliath and his troops, they said, ‘Our Lord, pour patience upon us, make our feet steady, and assist us against the faithless lot.’ (250)
فَهَزَمُوهُم بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ وَقَتَلَ دَاوُۥدُ جَالُوتَ وَءَاتَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُلْكَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَعَلَّمَهُۥ مِمَّا يَشَآءُ ۗ وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ ٱللَّهِ ٱلنَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لَّفَسَدَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٥١)
Thus they routed them with Allah’s will, and David killed Goliath, and Allah gave him kingdom and wisdom and taught him whatever He liked. Were it not for Allah’s repelling the people by means of one another, the earth would surely have been corrupted; but Allah is gracious to the world’s people. (251)
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ نَتْلُوهَا عَلَيْكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ وَإِنَّكَ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٢٥٢)
These are the signs of Allah, which We recite for you in truth, and you are indeed one of the apostles. (252)

(Commentary)

The manifest connection between these verses—namely, the apparent link between the obligation of fighting, the encouragement toward a "goodly loan," and the collective meaning derived from the story of Saul (طالوت), David (داود), and Goliath (جالوت)—indicates that these verses were revealed all at once. The objective is to clarify the vital role of fighting in the affairs of life and the spirit through which a nation advances in its religious and worldly existence and its true felicity. In these verses, the Glorified One clarifies the obligation of jihad (الجهاد) — the struggle in the way of Allah — and calls for spending and sacrifice in equipping the believers and preparing strength and provisions. He termed this "lending to Allah" because it is in His way, and to emphasize the perfection of devotion and the intimacy of proximity. Then, He relates the story of Saul, Goliath, and David so that these believers, commanded to fight the enemies of religion, may take heed. They are to learn that sovereignty and victory belong to faith (الإيمان) and godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah's presence and avoiding His displeasure — even if their adherents are few; while ignominy and annihilation are the lot of hypocrisy (النفاق) and transgression (الفسق), even if their assembly is numerous. Indeed, the Children of Israel, who are the subjects of this story, were humiliated and disgraced as long as they remained in a state of lethargy, laziness, and procrastination. However, when they rose for Allah, fought in His way, and sought support through the word of Truth—even though those among them who were sincere in their word were few, and most of them turned away first when fighting was mandated, secondly by objecting to Saul, thirdly by drinking from the river, and fourthly by saying, "We have no strength against Goliath and his hosts"—Allah the Exalted granted them victory over their enemy. They defeated them by Allah’s leave, David killed Goliath, sovereignty was established among them, life returned to them, and their leadership and strength were restored. All of this occurred only because of a word that faith and godwariness brought to their tongues when they went forth against Goliath and his hosts, which was their saying: "Our Lord, pour patience upon us and grant us victory over the faithless people." Thus, it is fitting for the believers to follow the conduct of the righteous among those who passed before; for they are the ones who shall prevail if they are truly believers.

His saying, the Exalted: وقاتلوا في سبيل الله (Fight in the way of Allah) [2:244] to the end of the verse, is a prescription and an obligation for jihad. Allah the Exalted has restricted it here, as in all other places in His speech, to being "in the way of Allah" so that it does not occur to one's mind, nor settle in the imagination, that this important religious duty is for the sake of establishing dry worldly authority or expanding a formal kingdom—as some contemporary social scientists and others have imagined regarding Islamic progress. Rather, it is for the expansion of the authority of religion, wherein lies the welfare of people in their worldly life and the Hereafter.

In His saying, the Exalted: واعلموا أن الله سميع عليم (and know that Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing) [2:244], there is a warning to the believers in this path of theirs not to oppose by word when Allah and His Messenger command something, nor to harbor hypocrisy as was the case with the Children of Israel when they spoke regarding the matter of Saul, saying: "How can he have kingdom over us..." etc., and when they said: "We have no strength today against Goliath and his hosts," and when they failed and turned away when fighting was prescribed for them, and when they drank from the river after Saul had forbidden them from drinking it.

His saying, the Exalted: من ذا الذي يقرض الله قرضاً حسناً (Who is it that will lend Allah a goodly loan) [2:245] until His saying: أضعافاً كثيرة (many times over). A loan (القرض) is well-known, and Allah the Glorified has counted what they spend in His way as a loan to Himself for the reasons mentioned: to encourage them, because it is spending in His way, and because it is something that will be returned to them manifold.

The style of address has shifted from command to interrogation; thus, after His saying: "Fight in the way of Allah," it was said: "Who is it that will lend Allah a goodly loan?" He did not say: "Fight in the way of Allah and lend," in order to stimulate the mind of the addressee by moving from the sphere of command—which is not free from the burden of obligation—to the sphere of invitation and recommendation, thereby providing a sense of ease and arousal of interest.

His saying, the Exalted: والله يقبض ويبصط وإليه ترجعون (It is Allah who withholds and outspreads, and to Him you shall be returned) [2:245]. Withholding (القبض) is taking a thing toward oneself, and its opposite is outspreading (البسط). The word (البصط) is the same as (البسط), where the letter sin (س) is changed to sad (ص) due to its proximity to the letter of velarization and emphasis, which is the ta (ط).

The mention of His three attributes—namely, His being the Withholder (القابض), the Outspreader (الباسط), and the Return (المرجع) to Whom they shall go back—is to intimate that what they spend by lending to the Exalted will not go to waste, nor is it far-fetched that it be multiplied many times over. For Allah is the Withholder and the Outspreader; He decreases what He wills and increases what He wills, and to Him they shall return, whereupon He will repay them what they lent with the most perfect fulfillment.

His saying, the Exalted: ألم تر إلى الملأ من بني إسرائيل (Have you not regarded the elite of the Children of Israel) [2:246] until His saying: في سبيل الله (in the way of Allah). The elite (الملأ), as has been said, refers to a group of people united upon a single opinion; they are called (الملأ) because their grandeur and prestige "fill the eyes" (تملاء العيون).

Their saying to their prophet, "Appoint for us a king that we may fight in the way of Allah," as indicated by the context, shows that the king named Goliath had subjugated them and treated them in a way that caused them to lose all aspects of their independent life, including their homes and children. This was after Allah had saved them from the clan of Pharaoh, who had been visiting them with terrible punishment, through the mission of Moses and his guardianship, and the guardianship of his successors after him. The matter became so severe for them that it awakened their dormant inner powers and restored their lost and weakened sense of collective zeal. At that point, their elite asked their prophet to appoint a king for them, so that the discord among them might be removed, their scattered and ineffective forces might be gathered, and they might fight under his command in the way of Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: قال هل عسيتم إن كتب عليكم القتال أن لا تقاتلوا (He said, ‘May it not be that if fighting were prescribed for you, you would not fight?’) [2:246]. The Children of Israel had asked their prophet to appoint a king for them to fight with in the way of Allah, but that matter does not belong to the prophet; rather, the matter belongs to Allah the Glorified. Therefore, their prophet referred the matter of fighting and the appointment of the king to Allah the Exalted. He did not explicitly mention His Name out of veneration, because what he was addressing them with was an inquiry about their potential disobedience—which was expected of them and apparent from their condition through His revelation. Thus, he kept His Name from being explicitly mentioned in this context, merely indicating that the matter is from Him and belongs to Him by saying: "if it were prescribed" (إن كتب), for prescription (الكتابة) — the divine decreeing of an obligation — only comes from Allah the Exalted.

The disobedience and turning away from fighting were expected from them, but he presented it in the form of an interrogation to complete the argument against them through the denial they would offer in their response: "And why should we not fight in the way of Allah?"

His saying, the Exalted: قالوا وما لنا أن لا نقاتل في سبيل الله وقد أخرجنا (They said, ‘And why should we not fight in the way of Allah, when we have been expelled...’) [2:246]. Since being expelled from one's lands is inseparable from being separated from one's familiar homelands and being prevented from managing and enjoying them, it was used as a metonym for the absolute loss of management and enjoyment. For this reason, the expulsion was attributed to the children as well as to the lands.

His saying, the Exalted: فلما كتب عليهم القتال تولوا إلا قليلاً منهم والله عليم بالظالمين (But when fighting was prescribed for them, they turned back, except a few of them, and Allah knows best the wrongdoers) [2:246]. This is a consequence of their prophet's saying: "May it not be..." and their saying: "And why should we not fight." In His saying, the Exalted: "and Allah knows best the wrongdoers," there is an indication that their prophet's saying to them: "May it not be that if fighting were prescribed for you, you would not fight," was due to a revelation from Allah the Glorified that they would indeed turn away from fighting.

His saying, the Exalted: وقال لهم نبيهم إن الله قد بعث (And their prophet said to them, ‘Indeed Allah has appointed...’) [2:247] until His saying: من المال (of wealth). In his response (peace be upon him) here, by attributing the appointment of the king to Allah, there is a reminder of what they had missed when they said to their prophet: "Appoint for us a king that we may fight," and did not say: "Ask Allah to appoint for us a king and prescribe fighting for us."

In short, the explicit mention of Saul's name is what triggered their objection to his kingship. This was due to the presence of two characteristics in him that, in their view, were incompatible with kingship. These are what Allah the Exalted related of their saying: "How can he have kingdom over us when we are more deserving of kingdom than him?" It is evident that this saying of theirs to their prophet—without providing evidence for why they were more deserving—indicates that their reasoning was a manifest matter that required no mention. This was nothing other than the fact that the house of prophethood and the house of kingship among the Children of Israel—two houses honored with the gifts of prophethood and kingship—were other than the house from which Saul came. In other words, Saul was neither from the house of kingship nor from the house of prophethood. Therefore, they objected to his kingship, arguing that "we"—the people of the house of kingship, or both kingship and prophethood—"are more deserving of kingdom than him because Allah placed the kingdom in us, so how can it be transferred to another?" This statement of theirs is a branch of their belief in the denial of bada’ (البداء) — the possibility of a change in the apparent divine decree — and the impermissibility of abrogation and change, as they said: "The hand of Allah is tied; tied be their hands!" Their prophet answered this by saying: "Indeed Allah has chosen him over you." This was one of the two characteristics they deemed incompatible with kingship. The second characteristic is found in their saying: "and he has not been given an amplitude of wealth," for Saul was poor. Their prophet answered this by saying: "and He has increased him abundantly in knowledge and stature..." etc.

His saying, the Exalted: قال إن الله اصطفاه عليكم وزاده بسطة في العلم والجسم (He said, ‘Indeed Allah has chosen him over you, and He has increased him abundantly in knowledge and stature’) [2:247]. Selection (الاصطفاء) and choosing (الاستصفاء) mean picking out, and its root is purity (الصفو). Abundance (البسطة) means amplitude and capability. These are two answers to their objections.

As for their objection that they were more deserving of kingship than Saul due to the nobility of their house, the answer is: this was a merit that Allah the Glorified had specifically granted to their house, but when He chooses another over them, that person becomes more deserving of kingship than they are. Nobility and precedence then belong to his house over theirs and to his person over theirs, for excellence follows His preference, the Exalted.

As for their objection that he was not given an amplitude of wealth, the answer is: kingship—which is the establishment of authority over a society of people—has as its sole purpose the harmonization of the scattered wills of people so they gather under a single will, and the unification of the reins of power by connecting them to a single rein. Thus, every individual in society travels the path of their appropriate perfection without one individual encroaching upon another, without anyone advancing without right, and without anyone falling behind without right. In short, the purpose of kingship is for its holder to manage society in a way that brings every individual to their appropriate perfection and repels everything that hinders that. What is necessary for achieving this objective are two things: first, knowledge (العلم) of all the interests and harms of human life; and second, physical capability (القدرة الجسمية) to implement what he sees as the interests of the kingdom. These are what His saying, the Exalted, refers to: "and He has increased him abundantly in knowledge and stature." As for amplitude of wealth, counting it as a constituent of kingship is a result of ignorance.

Then He gathered all these points under a single argument, mentioned in His saying, the Exalted: والله يؤتي ملكه من يشاء (and Allah gives His kingdom to whomever He wishes) [2:247]. This means that sovereignty belongs to Allah alone; no one has a share in it except what Allah the Glorified grants them. Even then, it remains Allah's, as indicated by the possessive construction in His saying, the Exalted: "gives His kingdom." Since this is the case, He, the Exalted, has the right to dispose of His kingdom however He wills and desires. No one has the right to say "why" or "by what" (meaning to ask about the cause of the disposal, because Allah the Exalted is the Absolute Cause; nor about the completion of causality or the instrument of action, because Allah the Exalted is Perfect and does not need a completer). Thus, it is not fitting to question the transfer of kingship from one house to another, or its conferral upon someone who lacks the apparent means of assembly and wealth.

Divine granting (الإيتاء) and emanation (الإفاضة), although they occur as He wills and to whom He wills, do not occur haphazardly, devoid of wisdom and purpose. For the meaning of our saying: "He, the Exalted, does what He wills," and "He gives kingship to whom He wills," and similar expressions, is not that Allah the Glorified does not observe the aspect of interest in His action, or that He performs an action and if it happens to coincide with an interest, it does, and if not, it becomes haphazard—and that there is no problem because the kingdom is His, so He may do as He wills. This is something that is invalidated by both religious outward meanings and rational proofs.

Rather, the meaning is: since every creation and command terminates in Allah the Glorified, interests and aspects of good, like all other things, are created by Him, the Exalted. Since this is the case, Allah the Glorified is not compelled in His action by any interest, nor governed by its rule, as we are in our actions. When He, the Exalted, performs an act or creates a creature—and He performs only what is beautiful and creates only what is good—His act possesses an interest wherein the welfare of the servants is observed, yet He, the Exalted, is neither governed nor compelled by that interest.

From here, it is correct for this reasoning to be joined with what preceded it—namely, the joining of His saying, the Exalted: "and Allah gives His kingdom to whomever He wishes," with His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed Allah has chosen him over you and increased him abundantly in knowledge and stature." For the first argument includes reasoning based on interests and causes, while the second argument is based on the absolute nature of the Sovereignty that does what it wills. Were it not that the absolute nature of Sovereignty and His doing what He wills do not contradict His actions being accompanied by interests and wisdom, it would not have been correct to join the two statements, let alone for one to support or complete the other.

This meaning has been clarified most excellently by the conclusion of the verse in His saying, the Exalted: والله واسع عليم (and Allah is All-bounteous, All-knowing) [2:247]. For "the All-bounteous" (الواسع) indicates the absence of any prevention of His action or granting whatsoever, and "the All-knowing" (العليم) indicates that the action of the Exalted is an action that proceeds from established knowledge that does not err. Thus, He, the Glorified, does all that He wills, and He does only that which possesses an interest.

Amplitude (الوسعة) and bounteousness (السعة) originally refer to a state in a body by which it accepts other things in terms of capacity, like the capacity of a vessel for what is poured into it, a chest for what is placed in it, or a house for those who dwell in it. Then it was used metaphorically for wealth (الغنى), but not just any wealth from every aspect; rather, from the aspect of the possibility of spending from it, as if the wealth "has room" (يسع) for the spending of what is intended to be spent. In this sense, it is applied to Him, the Glorified; thus, He, the Glorified, is "All-bounteous" (واسع), meaning Self-sufficient (غني), such that the spending of what He intends to spend does not exhaust Him; rather, He is capable of that.

His saying, the Exalted: وقال لهم نبيهم إن آية ملكه أن يأتيكم التابوت فيه سكينة من ربكم (And their prophet said to them, ‘Indeed the sign of his kingdom is that the Ark will come to you, in which there is peace from your Lord’) [2:248]. The Ark (التابوت) is the chest. It is said to be of the form fa‘alut from tawb, meaning "return," because a person returns to the chest time after time.

( A Discourse on the Meaning of Tranquility )

Tranquility (السكينة) is from stillness (السكون), the opposite of motion. It is used to refer to the stillness of the heart (سكون القلب) — the stability of a person and the absence of inner agitation in the determination of one's will, which is the state of the wise person (in the terminology of ethics) who possesses resolve in their actions. Allah the Glorified has made it one of the characteristics of faith (الإيمان) at its level of perfection and counted it among His sublime gifts.

To clarify: a human being, by their primordial instinct, issues their actions from intellect (التعقل), which is the organization of intellectual premises containing the interests of actions and their effect on their felicity in life and the good required in their social existence, followed by the conclusion of what ought to be done and what ought to be left.

This intellectual process, if the human being follows the method of their primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — and intends only what truly benefits them in their felicity, proceeds with a settled soul and a still mind, without agitation or wavering. However, if the human being clings to the earth in their life and follows caprice (الهوى), the matter becomes confused for them. Imagination (الخيال), with its embellishments and decorations, enters into their thoughts and resolves, causing them to deviate from the path of correctness at times, and to hesitate and be agitated in their resolve and the determination of their will when facing hardships and upheavals at other times.

The believer, through their faith in Allah the Exalted, relies on a support that does not move and a pillar that cannot be demolished. They build their affairs upon true gnosis that accepts no doubt or suspicion, proceeding in their actions from a divine obligation in which they do not waver. Nothing of the matter belongs to them such that they should fear its loss, grieve for its absence, or be agitated in distinguishing its good from its evil.

As for the non-believer, they have no guardian (ولي) to take charge of their affairs; rather, their good and evil return to their own self. Thus, they fall into the darknesses of these thoughts that assault them from every side through the path of caprice, imagination, and ill-omened sensations. The Exalted said: والله ولي المؤمنين — "and Allah is the Guardian of the faithful" (3:68). And He said: ذلك بأن الله مولى الذين آمنوا وأن الكافرين لا مولى لهم — "That is because Allah is the Master of the faithful, and because the faithless have no master" (47:11). And He said: الله ولي الذين آمنوا يخرجهم من الظلمات إلى النور والذين كفروا أولياؤهم الطاغوت يخرجونهم من النور إلى الظلمات — "Allah is the Guardian of the faithful: He brings them out of darkness into light. As for the faithless, their guardians are the Rebels, who take them out of light into darkness" (2:257). And He said: إنا جعلنا الشياطين أولياء للذين لا يؤمنون — "Indeed We have made the devils guardians of those who do not have faith" (7:27). And He said: ذلكم الشيطان يخوف أولياءه — "It is only the Devil who frightens his allies" (3:175). And He said: الشيطان يعدكم الفقر ويأمركم بالفحشاء والله يعدكم مغفرة — "The Devil threatens you with poverty and bids you to indecency, but Allah promises you His forgiveness" (2:268). And He said: ومن يتخذ الشيطان ولياً من دون الله فقد خسر خسراناً مبيناً يعدهم ويمنيهم وما يعدهم الشيطان إلا غروراً — "Whoever takes Satan for a guardian instead of Allah has certainly suffered a manifest loss. He makes them promises and gives them [false] hopes, and the Devil does not promise them anything but delusion" — until He said — وعد الله حقاً ومن أصدق من الله قيلاً — "Allah’s promise is true, and who is truer in speech than Allah?" (4:119-122). And He said: ألا إن أولياء الله لا خوف عليهم ولا هم يحزنون — "Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve" (10:62). These verses, as you see, place all fear, grief, agitation, and delusion on the side of faithlessness (الكفر), and the opposite qualities on the side of faith.

He has explained the matter even more clearly in His saying, the Exalted: أومن كان ميتاً فأحييناه وجعلنا له نوراً يمشي به في الناس كمن مثله في الظلمات ليس بخارج منها — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people, like one who is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (6:122). This indicates that the stumbling of the disbeliever in their walk is because they are situated in darknesses and see nothing; but the believer has a divine light by which they see their path and perceive their good and evil. This is because Allah has emanated upon them a new life added to the life they share with the disbeliever, and that life is what entails this light by which they are illuminated. In this sense is His saying, the Exalted: يا أيها الذين آمنوا اتقوا الله وآمنوا برسوله يؤتكم كفلين من رحمته ويجعل لكم نوراً تمشون به ويغفر لكم — "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and have faith in His Apostle. He will grant you a double share of His mercy and give you a light to walk by, and forgive you" (57:28).

Then He, the Exalted, said: لا تجد قوماً يؤمنون بالله واليوم الآخر يوادون من حاد الله ورسوله ولو كانوا آباءهم أو أبناءهم أو إخوانهم أو عشيرتهم أولئك كتب في قلوبهم الإيمان وأيدهم بروح منه — "You will not find a people believing in Allah and the Last Day love those who oppose Allah and His Apostle, even though they be their fathers, or their sons, or their brothers, or their clansmen. They are the ones in whose hearts He has inscribed faith and whom He has helped with a spirit from Him" (58:22). This informs us that this life is only through a spirit from Him, and it is inseparable from the establishment and stability of faith in the heart. Thus, these believers are supported by a spirit from Allah that entails the stability of faith in their hearts, the new life in their forms, and the light shining before them.

This verse, as you see, is closely applicable to His saying, the Exalted: هو الذي أنزل السكينة في قلوب المؤمنين ليزدادوا إيماناً مع إيمانهم ولله جنود السماوات والأرض وكان الله عليماً حكيماً — "It is He who sent down tranquility into the hearts of the faithful that they might add faith to their faith. To Allah belong the hosts of the heavens and the earth, and Allah is All-knowing, All-wise" (48:4). Tranquility (السكينة) in this verse corresponds to the spirit (الروح) in the previous verse, and the increase of faith upon faith in this one corresponds to the inscription of faith in that one. This application is supported by His saying at the end of the verse: "To Allah belong the hosts of the heavens and the earth," for the Quran applies the term "host" (الجند) to things like the angels and the spirit.

Similar in context to this verse is His saying, the Exalted: فأنزل الله سكينته على رسوله وعلى المؤمنين وألزمهم كلمة التقوى وكانوا أحق بها وأهلها — "Then Allah sent down His tranquility upon His Apostle and upon the faithful, and made them abide by the word of godwariness, and they were worthier of it and deserved it" (48:26), and likewise His saying, the Exalted: أنزل الله سكينته عليه وأيده بجنود لم تروها — "Allah sent down His tranquility upon him and fortified him with hosts you did not see" (9:40).

It has become clear from what has passed that it can be inferred from His speech, the Exalted, that tranquility is a divine spirit (روح إلهي) or necessitates a divine spirit from the command of Allah the Exalted that causes the stillness of the heart, the stability of the soul, and the strengthening of the heart. It is evident that this does not cause the speech to depart from its apparent meaning, nor the use of tranquility—which means the stillness of the heart and the absence of its agitation—to refer to the divine spirit. In this sense, the narrations that will follow should be interpreted.

His saying, the Exalted: وبقية مما ترك آل موسى وآل هارون تحمله الملائكة (and a relic of what the house of Moses and the house of Aaron left, carried by the angels) [2:248] to the end of the verse. The "house" (آل) of a man refers to his specific family members, and it includes himself when used generally. Thus, the house of Moses and the house of Aaron are Moses, Aaron, and their specific family members. His saying: "carried by the angels," is a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to the Ark. In His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed there is a sign in that for you, should you be believers," as in the context of the beginning of the verse, there is an indication that they had asked their prophet for a sign of the truth of what he had informed them: that Allah had appointed Saul for them as a king.

His saying, the Exalted: فلما فصل طالوت بالجنود قال إن الله مبتليكم بنهر (As Saul set out with the hosts, he said, ‘Indeed Allah will test you with a river’) [2:249] until His saying: منهم (of them). Setting out (الفصل) here means departing from a place, as in His saying, the Exalted: ولما فصلت العير — "As the caravan set out" (12:94). It is sometimes used in the sense of cutting, which is creating a separation between two things, as He, the Exalted, said: وهو خير الفاصلين — "and He is the best of judges" (6:57). Thus, the word can be both transitive and intransitive.

A host (الجند) is a dense gathering of anything; an army is called a host because of the accumulation of individuals in it and their density. The use of the plural "hosts" (الجنود) in the speech indicates that they were of such a large number as to be significant, especially considering the fewness of the believers after crossing the river and the dispersal of the people. A similar point exists in His saying, the Exalted: "As Saul set out with the hosts."

In the totality of the speech, there is an indication of the reality of the matter regarding the Children of Israel and their fulfillment of Allah's covenant. They had all asked for the appointment of a king and had affirmed the covenant. They were so numerous that when "most of them turned back" from fighting, the remaining few were still considered "hosts." These hosts also did not suffice them; rather, they fell behind by drinking from the river, and there remained only a few of the few, with a taint of failure and hypocrisy among them from the side of those who scooped a handful. Despite this, victory belonged to those who believed and were patient, notwithstanding the initial large number of Saul's hosts.

Testing (الابتلاء) is trial (الامتحان). A river (النهر) is a course of flowing water. Scooping (الاغتراف) and (الغرف) is lifting a thing and taking it; it is said: he scooped the water (غرف الماء غرفة) and (اغترفه غرفة) when he lifted it to take it and drink it.

In the exception of His saying, the Exalted: "except him who scoops a handful with his hand," from the general drinking, there is an indication that what was forbidden was drinking in a specific manner. It would have been expected to say: "so whoever drinks from it is not of me, except him who scoops a handful with his hand." However, the placement of His saying: "and whoever does not taste it is indeed of me," in the speech, while replacing "drinking" with "tasting" (الطعم) — the act of sensing flavor — necessitated a shift in the speech from the perspective of meaning. For if the second sentence were not added, the import of the speech would be that all the hosts were with Saul, and drinking causes the separation of a group from him, while scooping causes the separation from the separated—that is, reconnection. But if the second sentence—namely, His saying: "and whoever does not taste it is indeed of me"—is added to the first sentence, the import of the speech is that the matter is not truly settled except in appearance. The hosts are outwardly with Saul, but it has not yet been realized who among them is truly with Saul. Then, the river with which Allah will test them will realize both groups and distinguish them: it will identify those who are not of him, namely those who drink from the river; and it will identify those who are of him, namely those who do not taste it. If this is the import of the speech, then His saying in the exception: "except him who scoops a handful with his hand," does not imply that the scoopers are of Saul, because that would only be the import if only the first sentence were mentioned. But with the presence of both sentences, two groups are identified: those who are not of him, who are the drinkers; and those who are of him, who are the non-tasters. It is evident that exclusion from the first group only necessitates leaving it, not entering the second. The consequence of this is that the speech necessitates the existence of three groups: those who are not of him, those who are of him, and the scoopers. Accordingly, those remaining with him after crossing are two groups: those who are of him, and those who are not among those who left. Thus, it was possible for their state to differ in patience and anxiety, in reliance upon Allah and in worry and agitation.

His saying, the Exalted: فلما جاوزه هو والذين آمنوا معه (So when he crossed it, along with the faithful who were with him) [2:249] until the end of the verse. A "fئة" is a group of people. Reflection upon the verses suggests that those who said: "We have no strength," were the scoopers, and those who answered them were those who did not taste it at all. "Certainty of encountering Allah" (الظن بلقاء الله) is either in the sense of conviction (اليقين) in it, or a metonym for humility (الخشوع).

They did not say: "It is possible for a small group to overcome a large group by Allah's leave"; rather, they said: كم من فئة (How many a small group...) [2:249], taking from reality in their argument by showing an instance, so as to be more convincing to the opponent.

His saying, the Exalted: ولما برزوا لجالوت وجنوده (And when they went forth against Goliath and his hosts) [2:250] to the end. Going forth (البروز) is appearing; from it comes (البراز), which is appearing for war. Pouring (الإفراغ) is the pouring of a liquid-like substance into a mold. What is meant is Allah the Glorified's emanation of patience upon them according to their capacity; it is a subtle metaphor by way of metonymy. Likewise, "making the feet firm" is a metonym for steadfastness and not fleeing.

His saying, the Exalted: فهزموهم بإذن الله (Thus they routed them by Allah’s leave) [2:251]. Routing (الهزم) is repelling.

His saying, the Exalted: ولولا دفع الله الناس بعضهم ببعض (Were it not for Allah’s repelling the people by means of one another) [2:251] until the end of the verse. It is evident that what is meant by the "corruption of the earth" is the corruption of those upon the earth—that is, the corruption of human society. If the corruption of society entails corruption on the surface of the earth, it is included in the objective secondarily, not primarily. This is one of the scientific truths to which the Quran draws attention.

To clarify: the felicity of this species is not completed except through society and cooperation. It is evident that this matter is not completed except with the attainment of a certain unity in the structure of society, through which the members and parts of society are united with one another such that the whole becomes like a single individual, acting and being acted upon from a single soul and a single body. The social unity and its composite—which is the gathering of individuals of the species—are similar in state to the cosmic unity and its composite—which is the gathering of the parts of this witnessed world. It is evident that the unity of this system—namely, the system of creation (نظام التكوين)—is the result of the interaction (التأثير والتأثر) existing between the parts of the world. Were it not for the competition between the creative causes, the overcoming of some over others, and the repelling of others from them and their being overcome by them, the parts of the system would not be linked to one another; rather, each would remain in its own actuality. At that point, motions would cease, and the world of existence would cease.

Likewise, the system of human society: if it were not established on the basis of interaction, repelling, and overcoming, the parts of the system would not be linked to one another, no system would be realized, and the felicity of the species would cease. For if we were to assume the removal of "repelling" in this sense—which is overcoming and the imposition of will—from between them, every individual in society would perform an act that contradicts the interests of another, whether their legitimate interests or otherwise, and the other would have no way to return them to what accords with and suits their interests, and so on. By this, the unity between the parts would be severed and society would cease. This is the research we discussed previously: that the first primordial principle of the human being that forms society is "utilization" (الاستخدام), while cooperation and civilization are derived from it and are a secondary principle. The detailed discussion of this passed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: كان الناس أمة واحدة — "Mankind were but one community" (2:213).

In reality, the meaning of repelling and overcoming is a general meaning that permeates all affairs of human society. Its reality is the compelling of another, by whatever means possible, toward what the human being desires, and repelling them from what interferes with and hinders them. This is a general meaning present in both war and peace, in hardship and ease, in comfort and toil, and between all individuals in all nations of society. Yes, the human being only becomes aware of it upon the appearance of opposition and the interference of some individuals with others in the rights of life or in desires and inclinations and the like. Then the human being begins to repel the interfering human being who hinders them from their right or their desire. It is known that this exists in weak and strong degrees, and fighting and war are one of its degrees.

You know that this reality—namely, that repelling and overcoming are among the primordial principles in the human being—is a primordial principle more general than this repelling being a repelling in justice for a legitimate right or otherwise. For if there were not in the primordial nature of the human being an accepted principle of this sort, no defense—whether a legitimate defense of truth or otherwise—would be realized from them. For the actions of the human being are based on their primordial nature, as explained previously. Were it not for the sharing of the primordial nature between the believer and the disbeliever, it would not be possible for the believer to be distinguished by a primordial nature upon which they build their actions.

This primordial principle is utilized by the human being in establishing the basis of society, as per the previous explanation. Then they utilize it in imposing their will upon others and possessing what is in their hands through overcoming and transgression; and they utilize it in repelling them and reclaiming what they possessed through overcoming and transgression; and they utilize it in reviving the truth after its death through ignorance among people and imposing their felicity upon them. Thus, it is a primordial principle from which the human being benefits more than they are harmed.

This which we have mentioned is "perhaps" what is meant by His saying, the Exalted: "Were it not for Allah’s repelling the people by means of one another, the earth would surely have been corrupted." This is supported by its conclusion in His saying, the Exalted: ولكن الله ذو فضل على العالمين — "but Allah is gracious to all the worlds" (2:251).

Some commentators have mentioned that what is meant by "repelling" in the verse is Allah's repelling the disbelievers by means of the believers, as the specific instance is also like that. This is perhaps also supported by His saying, the Exalted: ولولا دفع الله الناس بعضهم ببعض لهدمت صوامع وبيع وصلوات ومساجد يذكر فيها اسم الله — "Were it not for Allah’s repelling the people by means of one another, monasteries and churches, synagogues and mosques—wherein the name of Allah is much mentioned—would have been destroyed" (22:40).

Against this: it is in itself a correct meaning, but the apparent meaning of the verse is that what is meant by the "welfare of the earth" is absolute, permanent welfare that preserves society, rather than the specific welfare present in rare times, such as the story of Saul and other few, numbered stories.

Others have mentioned: that what is meant by it is Allah's repelling of punishment and destruction from the profligate because of the righteous. Narrations have been reported regarding this through both Sunni and Shi'a chains. As in al-Majma‘ and al-Durr al-Manthur from Jabir, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Indeed, Allah rectifies, through the righteousness of a Muslim man, his child, his grandchild, the people of his small house, and the small houses around him; and they remain in the protection of Allah as long as he is among them." And in al-Kafi and the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), who said: "Indeed, Allah repels, by means of those of our Shi'a who pray, from those of our Shi'a who do not pray; and if they were to agree upon abandoning prayer, they would perish. And indeed, Allah repels, by means of those of our Shi'a who pay zakat, from those of our Shi'a who do not pay zakat; and if they were to agree upon abandoning zakat, they would perish. And indeed, Allah repels, by means of those of our Shi'a who perform hajj, from those of our Shi'a who do not perform hajj; and if they were to agree upon abandoning hajj, they would perish..." the hadith, and others like them.

Against this: the lack of correspondence of the two verses to the meaning of the two hadiths is not hidden, unless they correspond to them from the aspect that their subject is also among the instances of "repelling people."

Some have mentioned: that what is meant is Allah's repelling of the wrongdoers by means of the wrongdoers, which is as you see.

His saying, the Exalted: تلك آيات الله (These are the signs of Allah) [2:252] to the end, is like a conclusion with which the speech and the story are ended. However, the end of the verse: "and you are indeed one of the apostles," is not without a connection to the following verse.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Abd al-Razzaq and Ibn Jarir narrated from Zayd ibn Aslam, who said: "When 'Who is it that will lend Allah a goodly loan' was revealed, Abu al-Dahdah came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'O Prophet of Allah, do I not see our Lord asking us for a loan from what He gave us for ourselves? Indeed, I have two lands: one in the upper part [of the city] and the other in the lower part. I have made the better of them a charity.' And the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to say: 'How many a dangling cluster of dates belongs to Abu al-Dahdah in Paradise!'"

I say: The narration is reported through many chains.

In al-Ma‘ani from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): "When this verse was revealed: 'Whoever brings a good deed shall have [a reward] better than it' (27:89), the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'O Allah, increase for me.' So Allah revealed: 'Whoever brings a good deed shall have ten times its like' (6:160). The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'O Allah, increase for me.' So Allah revealed: 'Who is it that will lend Allah a goodly loan, that He may multiply it for him many times over' (2:245). Then the Messenger of Allah knew that 'many' from Allah cannot be counted and has no end."

I say: Al-Tabarsi in al-Majma‘ and al-Ayyashi in his Tafsir narrated its like, and something close to it was narrated through Sunni chains as well. His saying (عليه السلام): "Then the Messenger of Allah knew," is intimated by the end of the verse: "and it is Allah who withholds and outspreads," since there is no limit that bounds the granting of the Exalted. He has said: وما كان عطاء ربك محظوراً — "and the bounty of your Lord is not confined" (17:20).

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi from Abu al-Hasan (عليه السلام) regarding the verse, he said: "It is the gift to the Imam."

I say: The like of it was narrated in al-Kafi from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), and it is by way of mentioning an instance.

In al-Majma‘ regarding His saying, the Exalted: "when they said to a prophet of theirs," he is Ishmu'il, which in Arabic is Isma'il.

I say: This is narrated through Sunni chains as well. Ishmu'il is the one found in the two Testaments with the word Samuel.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, from his father, from al-Nadr ibn Suwayd, from Yahya al-Halabi, from Harun ibn Kharijah, from Abu Basir, from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام): "Indeed, the Children of Israel, after the death of Moses, committed acts of disobedience, changed the religion of Allah, and rebelled against the command of their Lord. There was a prophet among them who would command them and forbid them, but they did not obey him." And it is narrated that he was the prophet Jeremiah (peace be upon our Prophet and his Progeny and upon him). "So Allah gave Goliath power over them, and he was from the Copts. He humiliated them, killed their men, expelled them from their lands and their wealth, and enslaved their women. So they fled to their prophet and said: 'Ask Allah to appoint for us a king that we may fight in the way of Allah.' Prophethood among the Children of Israel was in one house, and kingship and authority were in another house; Allah had not gathered prophethood and kingship in a single house. For that reason, they said to a prophet of theirs: 'Appoint for us a king that we may fight in the way of Allah.' Their prophet said to them: 'May it not be that if fighting were prescribed for you, you would not fight?' They said: 'And why should we not fight in the way of Allah, when we have been expelled from our dwellings and our children?' It was as Allah said: 'But when fighting was prescribed for them, they turned back, except a few of them, and Allah knows best the wrongdoers.' Their prophet said to them: 'Indeed Allah has appointed Saul for you as a king.' They were angry at that and said: 'How can he have kingdom over us, when we are more deserving of kingdom than him and he has not been given an amplitude of wealth?' Prophethood was in the house of Levi, and kingship was in the house of Joseph. Saul was from the descendants of Benjamin, the brother of Joseph from his mother and father; he was neither from the house of prophethood nor from the house of kingship. Their prophet said to them: 'Indeed Allah has chosen him over you, and He has increased him abundantly in knowledge and stature, and Allah gives His kingdom to whomever He wishes, and Allah is All-bounteous, All-knowing.' He was the greatest of them in stature and was strong, and he was the most knowledgeable of them, except that he was poor. So they criticized him for poverty and said: 'he has not been given an amplitude of wealth.' Their prophet said to them: 'Indeed the sign of his kingdom is that the Ark will come to you, in which there is peace from your Lord and a relic of what the house of Moses and the house of Aaron left, carried by the angels.' The Ark was that which Allah sent down to Moses, and his mother placed him in it and cast him into the river. It remained among the Children of Israel, and they would seek blessings through it. When death approached Moses, he placed the Tablets, his coat of mail, and the signs of prophethood he had in it, and entrusted it to Joshua, his successor. The Ark remained among them until they made light of it, and children would play with it in the streets. The Children of Israel remained in glory and honor as long as the Ark was with them. When they committed acts of disobedience and made light of the Ark, Allah took it away from them. When they asked the prophet, Allah appointed Saul for them as a king, and he fought with them, and Allah returned the Ark to them as He said: 'Indeed the sign of his kingdom is that the Ark will come to you, in which there is peace from your Lord and a relic of what the house of Moses and the house of Aaron left, carried by the angels.' He said: 'The relic (البقية) is the progeny of the prophets.'"

I say: His saying: "And it is narrated that he was the prophet Jeremiah," is an intercalated narration within a narration. His saying (عليه السلام): "It was as Allah said..." etc., means that the many turned away and none remained in submission to the command of fighting except a few of them. In some reports, these few were sixty thousand; al-Qummi narrated that in his Tafsir from his father, from al-Husayn ibn Khalid, from Imam al-Rida (عليه السلام), and al-Ayyashi narrated it from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام). His saying: "Prophethood was in the house of Levi, and kingship was in the house of Joseph." It has been said: "Indeed, kingship was in the house of Judah," but this has been objected to on the grounds that there was no king among them before Saul, David, and Solomon such that it could be in the house of Judah. This supports what has been reported in the hadiths of the Imams of the People of the House (أهل البيت) that kingship was in the house of Joseph, for Joseph's being a king is something that cannot be denied.

His saying: "He said: 'The relic is the progeny of the prophets,'" is from the narrator. He (عليه السلام) only interpreted His saying: "the house of Moses and the house of Aaron" with his saying: "the progeny of the prophets." What we have mentioned is supported by what is in the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): that he was asked about the saying of Allah: "and a relic of what the house of Moses and the house of Aaron left, carried by the angels," and he said: "The progeny of the prophets."

In al-Kafi from Muhammad ibn Yahya, from Muhammad ibn Ahmad, from Muhammad ibn Khalid and al-Husayn ibn Sa'id, from al-Nadr ibn Suwayd, from Yahya al-Halabi, from Harun ibn Kharijah, from Abu Basir, from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) in a hadith: "And Allah said: 'Indeed Allah will test you with a river; so whoever drinks from it is not of me, and whoever does not taste it is indeed of me.' They drank from it except for three hundred and thirteen men; among them were those who scooped, and among them were those who did not drink. When they went forth against Goliath, those who scooped said: 'We have no strength today against Goliath and his hosts,' and those who did not scoop said: 'How many a small group has overcome a large group by Allah’s leave, and Allah is with the patient.'"

I say: As for the remaining ones with Saul being three hundred and thirteen men, the number of the people of Badr, the narrations regarding this are numerous through both Shi'a and Sunni chains. As for those who said: "We have no strength," being the scoopers, and those who said: "How many a small group..." being those who did not drink at all, this can be inferred from the manner of the exception in the verse according to what we explained regarding the meaning of the exception.

In al-Kafi with its chain from Ahmad ibn Muhammad, from al-Husayn ibn Sa'id, from Fadalah ibn Ayyub, from Yahya al-Halabi, from Abd Allah ibn Sulayman, from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed the sign of his kingdom..." until His saying: "carried by the angels," he said: "They were carrying it in the form of a cow."

Know that the reason for mentioning the chain of this hadith—even though it is not the custom of this book—is because the omission of chains in it is only in cases of agreement with the Quran, in which case there is no need to mention the chain of the hadith. However, in what does not consistently show agreement and where application is not possible, the chain must be mentioned. Nevertheless, we choose to report narrations with authentic chains or those supported by circumstantial evidence.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi from Muhammad al-Halabi from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), he said: "David had four brothers, and with them was their father, an old man. David stayed behind with his father's sheep. Saul set out with the hosts, and David's father called him—he was the youngest of them—and said: 'O my son, go to your brothers with this that we have made for them, so they may strengthen themselves against their enemy.' He was a short man, blue-eyed, with little hair, and pure of heart. He went out when the people had drawn near to one another." It is mentioned from Abu Basir, who said: I heard him saying: "David passed by a stone, and the stone said: 'O David, take me and kill Goliath with me, for I was only created for his killing.' So he took it and placed it in his bag in which were his stones that he would throw from his sling at his sheep. When he entered the camp, he heard them magnifying the matter of Goliath. David said to them: 'What do you magnify of his matter? By Allah, if I see him, I will surely kill him.' They spoke of his news until he was brought before Saul. He said: 'O youth, what strength do you have? And what have you tested of yourself?' He said: 'A lion would run at a sheep from my flock, and I would catch it, take it by its head, pry its jaws apart from it, and take it from its mouth.' He said: 'Bring me a full coat of mail.' A coat of mail was brought, and he threw it over his neck, and it filled out from him until it startled Saul and those of the Children of Israel who were present. Saul said: 'By Allah, perhaps Allah will kill him through him.' He said: 'When morning came and they returned to Saul and the people met, David said: "Show me Goliath." When he saw him, he took the stone, placed it in his sling, and threw it. He struck him between his eyes and crushed his brain, and he fell from his beast. The people said: "David has killed Goliath," and the people made him king until Saul's name was no longer heard. The Children of Israel gathered around David, and Allah sent down the Psalms (الزبور) upon him, taught him the craft of iron and made it soft for him, and commanded the mountains and the birds to glorify with him.' He said: 'No one was given the like of his voice.' David remained among the Children of Israel in seclusion, and he was given strength in his worship."

I say: The (المقذاف) is the sling that shepherds have, with which they throw stones. The tongues of reports from both groups have agreed that David killed Goliath with a stone.

In al-Majma‘, he said: "Indeed, the tranquility that was in it was a gentle wind from Paradise that had a face like the face of a human being," narrated from Ali (عليه السلام).

I say: This meaning was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Sufyan ibn Uyaynah and Ibn Jarir through the chain of Salamah ibn Kuhayl from Ali (عليه السلام); and likewise from Abd al-Razzaq, Abu Ubayd, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Hakim—who authenticated it—and Ibn Asakir and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il through the chain of Abu al-Ahwas from Ali (عليه السلام) the like of it.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi from his father, from Ali ibn al-Husayn ibn Khalid, from Imam al-Rida (عليه السلام): "Tranquility is a wind from Paradise that has a face like the face of a human being."

I say: Al-Saduq in al-Ma‘ani and al-Ayyashi in his Tafsir also narrated this meaning from Imam al-Rida (عليه السلام). These reports regarding the meaning of tranquility, even if they are solitary reports (آحاد), are capable of interpretation and approximation to the meaning of the verse. For what is meant by them—on the assumption of their authenticity—is that tranquility is a level among the levels of the soul in perfection that necessitates the soul's stillness and its composure toward the command of Allah. Such expressions containing representation (التمثيل) are numerous in the speech of the Imams. It then corresponds to the spirit of faith (روح الإيمان), and you have learned in the previous commentary that tranquility corresponds to the spirit of faith.

Upon this meaning should be carried what is in al-Ma‘ani from Abu al-Hasan (عليه السلام) regarding tranquility, where he (عليه السلام) said: "A spirit of Allah that speaks; when they differed in anything, it would speak to them and inform them," the hadith. For it is only the spirit of faith that guides the believer to the truth regarding that which is differed upon.

( Scientific and Social Discourse )

Natural scientists have noted that scientific experiments indicate that these natural beings—disposed by their very nature to preserve their existence and survival, and active through their inherent powers that necessitate actions suitable to them—contend with one another for survival. Since this contention arises from the expansion of influence over others and the reciprocal being-influenced by others, victory belongs to the stronger and more perfect in existence (الأكمل وجوداً). From this, it is concluded that nature continually selects from among the individuals of one or two species the most perfect and exemplary (الأمثل) to be singled out for survival, while the remaining individuals perish and gradually become extinct. Thus, there are two natural principles: the first is the struggle for survival (تنازع البقاء), and the second is natural selection (الانتخاب الطبيعي) and the survival of the fittest (بقاء الأمثل).

Since society relies for its existence upon nature, a similar pair of laws operates within it: namely, the law of the struggle for survival and the law of selection and survival of the fittest.

Thus, a perfect society—which is a society built upon the foundation of complete and firm unity wherein the individual and social rights of its members are observed—is more entitled to survival, while others are more entitled to perishing and extinction. Experience judges that living nations (الامم الحية) that are mindful of their social duties and preserve their social path will survive, while nations will go extinct due to the division of hearts, the spread of hypocrisy, the prevalence of injustice and corruption, the luxury-induced decadence of the elite, and the collapse of the foundation of earnestness among them. In this regard, society mimics nature as mentioned. Research into terrestrial remains leads us to the existence of animal species from the early terrestrial eras that are today extinct, such as the animal called the Brontosaurus, or those of which only a few specimens remain, like the crocodile and the frog. Nothing brought about their destruction and extinction except the struggle for survival, natural selection, and the survival of the fittest. Likewise, the species existing today continue to change under the factors of struggle and selection, and only the fittest and strongest in existence are fit to survive. Then, the law of heredity (حكم الوراثة) leads to the continuity of existence and the survival of the species. In this same manner were the species and compositions in the origin of their formation; for they are but parts of matter scattered in the atmosphere that, through their accumulation and gathering, formed the spheres and the species arising within them. Whatever among them was fit for survival remained and then inherited existence, and whatever was unfit for that due to the contention of what was stronger than it, corrupted and went extinct. This is what natural and social scientists have mentioned.

However, later scholars have countered this with the existence of many species weak in existence (الضعيفة الوجود) that remain among other species to this day, and with many varieties of plant and animal species. For the occurrence of breeding through the domestication of many types of plants and animals—taking them out of the wild and savage state and advancing them through breeding toward the excellence of the genus and the perfection of the species, while the wild and savage versions remain in their poor quality and move toward weakness day by day, with heredity settling in them upon that trait—all of this dictates that the two principles, namely the struggle for survival and natural selection, are not universal (عدم اطراد القاعدتين).

For this reason, some have explained this characteristic found among natural things with another hypothesis, which is environmental adaptation (تبعية المحيط). The existing environment—which is the sum of natural factors under specific temporal (زمانية) and spatial (مكانية) conditions—demands that the existing being follow it in the aspects of its existence. Likewise, the nature present in the individual necessitates the adaptation of its existence to the specificities present in the environment of its life. Therefore, every species living on land or in the sea, or in different terrestrial regions like the polar or equatorial zones and others, possesses organs, tools, and powers suitable for its region of life and livelihood. Thus, it is the environment of life that necessitates survival when the existence of the being conforms to its requirements, and disappearance and perishing when it does not conform to its requirements. Therefore, the two principles ought to be derived from this law; meaning that the root of the laws of the struggle for survival and natural selection is environmental adaptation. In cases where the two principles are not universal, there is no influential environment necessitating an effect. However, the principle of environmental adaptation faces exceptions to its universality similar to those faced by the two aforementioned principles, and scholars have detailed this in its proper places.

If environmental adaptation were complete in its influence and universal in its rule, it would be necessary that no species or individual exist that is not subordinate to it, nor that an environment should change in itself. Similarly, if the two principles were complete and universal in their rule, it would be necessary that nothing of the beings weak in existence remain alongside the strong ones, nor that the law of heredity operate in the inferior varieties of plants and animals.

The truth, as scientific research has perhaps admitted, is that these principles, despite the degree of correctness they contain in general (في الجملة), are not universal (غير مطردة).

The universal philosophical view (النظر الفلسفي الكلي) in this chapter is as follows: the matter of the occurrence of material events—whether in terms of the origin of their existence or the transformations and changes occurring in the peripheries of their existence—revolves around the law of cause and effect (قانون العلية والمعلولية).
