Every being among material beings, by virtue of its active form (الصورة الفعالة) for the benefit of its own existence, directs its influence toward others to produce in them a form suitable to its own form. This is a reality that one cannot escape admitting when contemplating the state of beings with one another. This necessitates that each takes from the other for the benefit of its own existence, attaching what it has taken to its own existence in some way. The necessary consequence of this is that every being is active in preserving its own existence and life. Based on this, it is correct to say that there is a struggle for survival among beings. Likewise, it is a necessary consequence of causal influence (التأثير العلي) that the stronger disposes of the weaker by destroying it for its own benefit or by changing it in a way that it benefits from it for itself. By this, the two laws—namely natural selection and environmental adaptation—can be explained. For when a species is under the influence of opposing factors, it can only resist them if it is strong in existence (قوي الوجود) and capable of defending itself. The same applies to individuals of a single species; only those whose existence is strong against the incompatibilities and opposites directed toward them are fit for survival. This is natural selection and the survival of the fittest. Similarly, when a large number of factors gather and most of them unite or converge in terms of action, the being that is caught between them must be influenced by the effect that suits their action. This is environmental adaptation.

Among the things that must be known is that such laws—namely environmental adaptation and others—only influence what is susceptible to influence, specifically the accidents of a thing's existence (عوارض وجود الشيء) and its appendages. As for the essence (الذات) itself, such that it becomes another species, then no. However, since those people do not believe in the existence of a substantial essence (الذات الجوهري) but rather build their research on the premise that every being is a collection of accidental qualities (العوارض) occurring upon matter—and by this, one species is distinguished from another—in reality, there is no substantial species that differs from another substantial species. Rather, all species are analyzed back into a single matter in terms of species, differing only according to various compositions. From here, you see them judging the transformation of species (تبدل الانواع) and environmental adaptation or the influence of other natural factors, and they do not mind the transformation of the essence therein. This discussion has a long extension, the details of which will come to you, Allah willing.

Returning to the beginning of the discourse, we say: some commentators have mentioned that His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ اللَّهِ النَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لَّفَسَدَتِ الْأَرْضُ وَلَٰكِنَّ اللَّهَ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ — "Were it not for Allah’s repelling the people by means of one another, the earth would surely have been corrupted; but Allah is gracious to the world's creatures" (2:251), is an allusion to the two laws of the struggle for survival and natural selection.

He said: This is confirmed by His saying, the Exalted: أُذِنَ لِلَّذِينَ يُقَاتَلُونَ بِأَنَّهُمْ ظُلِمُوا وَإِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ نَصْرِهِمْ لَقَدِيرٌ الَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا مِن دِيَارِهِم بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ إِلَّا أَن يَقُولُوا رَبُّنَا اللَّهُ وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ اللَّهِ النَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لَّهُدِّمَتْ صَوَامِعُ وَبِيَعٌ وَصَلَوَاتٌ وَمَسَاجِدُ يُذْكَرُ فِيهَا اسْمُ اللَّهِ كَثِيرًا وَلَيَنصُرَنَّ اللَّهُ مَن يَنصُرُهُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَقَوِيٌّ عَزِيزٌ الَّذِينَ إِن مَّكَّنَّاهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَقَامُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَآتَوُا الزَّكَاةَ وَأَمَرُوا بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَنَهَوْا عَنِ الْمُنكَرِ وَلِلَّهِ عَاقِبَةُ الْأُمُورِ — "Those who are fought against are permitted [to fight] because they have been wronged, and Allah is indeed able to help them —those who were expelled from their homes unjustly, only because they said, ‘Our Lord is Allah.’ Had not Allah repelled some people by means of others, monasteries, churches, synagogues and mosques—where the name of Allah is much mentioned—would have been demolished. Allah will surely help those who help Him. Indeed Allah is all-strong, all-mighty. Those who, if We granted them power in the land, will maintain the prayer, give the zakat, bid what is right and forbid what is wrong. And with Allah rests the outcome of all matters" (22:39-41). This is an allusion to the struggle for survival and defense of the truth, and that it ends with the survival of the fittest and the preservation of the best.

Among the things that indicate this principle from the Mighty Quran is His saying, the Exalted: أَنزَلَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً فَسَالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِهَا فَاحْتَمَلَ السَّيْلُ زَبَدًا رَّابِيًا وَمِمَّا يُوقِدُونَ عَلَيْهِ فِي النَّارِ ابْتِغَاءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتَاعٍ زَبَدٌ مِّثْلُهُ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْحَقَّ وَالْبَاطِلَ فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفَاءً وَأَمَّا مَا يَنفَعُ النَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْأَمْثَالَ — "He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to [the limit of] their capacity, and the flood carries a swelling scum. And from what they smelt in the fire for making ornaments or housewares [there is] a similar scum. Thus does Allah draw the parable of truth and falsehood. As for the scum, it leaves as dross, and as for what profits the people, it remains in the earth. Thus does Allah draw parables" (13:17). This implies that the floods of events and the scale of contention cast away the scum of falsehood that is harmful to society and repel it, while the silt (الابليز) of the beneficial truth remains, in which civilization grows, and the pure gold (الابريز) of interest remains, with which man is adorned. [End of his words].

I say: As for the principle of the struggle for survival and likewise the principle of natural selection (in the sense previously explained) being true in general, and that the Quran pays attention to them, there is no dispute in that. However, these two categories of verses that he cited are not directed toward explaining either of the two principles. The first category of verses is directed toward explaining that Allah, the Glorified, is never overcome in His will, and that the Truth—which is what Allah approves of religious knowledge—is never overcome, and that its bearer, when he carries it with truth and sincerity, is never overcome at all. This is indicated by His saying, the Exalted, first: "because they have been wronged, and Allah is indeed able to help them," and His saying, the Exalted, second: "those who were expelled from their homes unjustly, only because they said, ‘Our Lord is Allah.’" For these two sentences are in the position of explaining that the believers will overcome their enemies, not because of contention and the survival of the fittest and strongest—for the fittest and strongest according to nature is the individual strong in his natural equipment, not the one strong in terms of truth and the fittest in terms of meaning—rather, they will overcome because they are wronged for speaking the truth, and Allah, the Glorified, is Truth and He helps the truth in itself, meaning that falsehood is unable to refute the proof of truth when they meet, and He helps the bearer of truth if he is sincere in carrying it, as Allah mentioned in His saying: "Allah will surely help those who help Him. Indeed Allah is all-strong, all-mighty. Those who, if We granted them power in the land, will maintain the prayer..." etc. That is, they are sincere in their saying of the truth and their carrying of it. Then He concluded the speech with His saying, the Exalted: "And with Allah rests the outcome of all matters," referring by it to several verses implying that the universe moves in the path of its perfection toward truth, sincerity, and true happiness. There is also no doubt in the Quran's indication that victory belongs to Allah and His host absolutely, as indicated by His saying: كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا وَرُسُلِي — "Allah has ordained: ‘I shall surely prevail, I and My apostles’" (58:21), and His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنَا لِعِبَادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنصُورُونَ وَإِنَّ جُندَنَا لَهُمُ الْغَالِبُونَ — "Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles, that they will indeed be the ones to be helped, and that Our host will indeed be the victors" (37:171-173), and His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ غَالِبٌ عَلَىٰ أَمْرِهِ — "Allah has full control over His affairs" (12:21).

Likewise, the second verse he cited, namely His saying, the Exalted: "He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to [the limit of] their capacity..." etc., is directed toward explaining the survival of truth and the vanishing of falsehood, whether it be in the manner of contention—as in the truth and falsehood that are both of the genus of material things, where survival between them is by way of contention—or not in the manner of contention and opposition, as in the truth and falsehood that are between material and spiritual things. For meaning—and we mean by it the being immaterial to matter—precedes matter and is never overcome in any state at all. Thus, precedence and survival belong to the meaning over the form without contention. This is also like the truth and falsehood that are both of the genus of spiritual and immaterial things. The Exalted has said: وَعَنَتِ الْوُجُوهُ لِلْحَيِّ الْقَيُّومِ — "All faces shall be humbled before the Living One, the All-sustainer" (20:111), and He said: لَّهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ كُلٌّ لَّهُ قَانِتُونَ — "To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth; all are obedient to Him" (2:116), and He said: وَأَنَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ الْمُنتَهَىٰ — "and that the terminus is toward your Lord" (53:42). Thus He, the Exalted, is victorious over everything, and He is the One, the All-paramount.

As for the verse we are considering, namely His saying, the Exalted: "Were it not for Allah’s repelling the people by means of one another, the earth would surely have been corrupted," you have already learned that it is in the position of referring to a reality upon which human society relies, by which the earth is cultivated, and by the disruption of which civilization is disrupted and the earth is corrupted. This is the instinct of exploitation (غريزة الاستخدام) upon which man is disposed, and its leading to the reconciliation of interests, meaning civilization and cooperative society. This meaning, even if some of its roots and origins are the struggle for survival and natural selection, is nevertheless the proximate cause upon which the cultivation of the earth and its protection from corruption are based. Therefore, the verse that intends to give the reason for the absence of corruption occurring on the earth should be interpreted according to this, not according to the two aforementioned principles.

To put it more clearly: the two principles, namely the struggle for survival and natural selection, necessitate the dissolution of multiplicity and its return to unity. For each of the contenders intends by the struggle to destroy the other and attach the other's existence and advantages to himself. Nature, through selection, intends that the one who remains of the two be the stronger and the fitter. Thus, the result of the operation of the two principles is the corruption of multiplicity, its nullification, and its transformation into a single fittest entity. This is something that contradicts society, cooperation, and the sharing of life that man seeks by his primordial nature (الفطرة) and to which he is guided by his instinct, and by which the earth is cultivated by this species—not the extinction of one group by another, and the devouring of one another. The repelling (الدفع) by which the earth is cultivated and protected from corruption is the repelling that calls to society and unity established upon multiplicity and the group, not the repelling that calls to the nullification of society and the creation of a unity that destroys multiplicity. Thus, fighting is a cause for the cultivation of the earth and the absence of its corruption from the aspect that through it, vital social rights are revived for the consumed and the humiliated, not from the aspect that through it the assembly is scattered, the essence is destroyed, and the trace is erased. So understand this.

(A Discussion on History and What the Quran Attends to Therein)

Narrative history (التاريخ النقلي)—by which we mean the recording of universal and particular events through transmission and report—is something that man, from the earliest eras of his life and times of his existence on earth, has never ceased to be concerned with. In every age, as far as we know, there have been a number of its memorizers, writers, and authors, while others frequent what those have recorded and take what they have gifted them. Man benefits from it in various aspects of his life, such as society, taking heed (الاعتبار), storytelling, conversation, entertainment, and other political, economic, or industrial matters, and so forth.

Despite its nobility and numerous benefits, two factors of corruption have never ceased, and will never cease, to operate within it, causing it to deviate from the correctness of nature and the truth of expression toward falsehood and lies:

The first: It is always, in every age, subject to the current government that holds power and strength. It tends toward manifesting what benefits the government and overlooks what harms it or spoils its affairs. This is nothing other than what we do not doubt: that powerful governments in every age are concerned with spreading the truths they benefit from and concealing those they are harmed by, or dressing them in a garment they benefit from, or depicting falsehood and lies in the image of truth and sincerity. For the individual human and the human society are naturally disposed toward seeking benefit and repelling harm in whatever way possible. This is a matter that no one with the slightest perception of the general current conditions in the time of his life, and who contemplates the history of past and distant nations, can doubt.

The second: Those who carry reports, those who transmit them, and those who author works on them are all not free from the influence of internal feelings and ethnic prejudices (العصبيات القومية) in what they carry or judge. For the carriers of reports in the past—when the government in their eras was the government of religion—were adherents of sects and followers of various religions. Religious feelings in them were strong and ethnic prejudices were intense. Inevitably, these would interfere with historical reports in terms of their inclusion of judgments and decrees. Similarly, materialist prejudice and the strong feelings today for liberty over religion and for desire over reason necessitate interferences from the people of reports today similar to the interferences of the ancients in what they recorded or transmitted. Hence, you do not see a person of a religion or sect, in what he authored or collected of reports, depositing anything that contradicts his school of thought; rather, what the people of every school recorded is in accordance with the principles of their school. Likewise is the matter in transmission today; you do not see a historical word produced by their hands except that it contains some support for the materialist school.

Furthermore, there are other factors that necessitate the corruption of history, such as the lack of means for recording, taking, carrying, transmitting, authoring, and preserving from change and loss in the past. Although this deficiency has been removed today by the proximity of countries, the accumulation of means of communication, and the ease of transmitting reports and moving, the calamity has become general from another side: namely, that politics has interfered in all affairs of man in his life. The world today revolves around technical politics, and according to its transformation, reports transform from one state to another. This is what necessitates a bad opinion of history, to the point that it has almost brought it to the point of collapse. The existence of these deficiencies or nullifiers in narrative history is the reason, or the main reason, for the turning away of scholars today from it toward establishing historical propositions on the basis of terrestrial remains (الآثار الارضية). Although this is safe from some of the aforementioned problems—like the first, for example—it is not free from the rest, the main one being the interference of the historian with the feelings and prejudices he possesses in his judgments, and the manipulation of politics therein through disclosure, concealment, change, and substitution. This, then, is the state of history and the aspects of corruption accompanying it that can never be reformed.

From here it becomes clear: the Noble Quran is not contradicted in its stories by history if it differs from it. For it is a divine revelation (وحي إِلهي) purified from error and free from lies. Thus, it is not contradicted by history, which has no guarantor to secure it from lies and error. Most Quranic stories (like this very story, the story of Talut) differ from what is found in the books of the Two Testaments. There is no harm in this, for the books of the Two Testaments are no more than the common histories in which you have learned how hands have played with them. Furthermore, the author of this story—which is the story of Samuel and Saul in the language of the Two Testaments—is a person whose identity is not known at all. In any case, we do not mind the Quran's disagreement with what is found to be contrary to it in histories, especially in the books of the Two Testaments. The Quran is the word of truth from the Truth, exalted is His Name.

Moreover, the Quran is not a book of history, nor does it intend in its stories to present history in the manner intended by a history book. Rather, it is speech cast in the mold of revelation by which Allah guides those who follow His pleasure to the ways of peace. For this reason, you do not see it narrating a story with all its sides and aspects of occurrence; rather, it takes from the story scattered points, the scrutiny and contemplation of which necessitate the attainment of the goal—be it a lesson (عبرة), wisdom (حكمة), an exhortation (موعظة), or otherwise. This is witnessed in this story, the story of Talut and Jalut, where He, the Exalted, says: "Have you not regarded the elite of the Israelites," then He says: "Their prophet said to them, ‘Allah has appointed Talut as king for you’" etc., then He says: "Their prophet said to them, ‘Indeed the sign of his kingship...’", then He says: "So when Talut set out..." etc., then He says: "So when they went forth against Jalut." It is well known that the connection of these sentences to one another in the complete discourse requires a long story. We have alerted you to the like of this in what passed of the story of the Cow, and it is universal in all the stories narrated in the Quran; nothing is mentioned from them except the points of need: for a lesson, an exhortation, wisdom, or a divine sunnah (سنة إِلهية) in the days of old and the nations of the past. He, the Exalted, said: لَقَدْ كَانَ فِي قَصَصِهِمْ عِبْرَةٌ لِّأُولِي الْأَلْبَابِ — "There is certainly a moral in their stories for those who possess intellect" (12:111), and He, the Exalted, said: يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ وَيَهْدِيَكُمْ سُنَنَ الَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ — "Allah desires to explain to you and to guide you to the customs of those who were before you" (4:26), and He, the Exalted, said: قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ سُنَنٌ فَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانظُرُوا كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ هَٰذَا بَيَانٌ لِّلنَّاسِ وَهُدًى وَمَوْعِظَةٌ لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ — "Certain customs have passed before you; so roam the earth and see how was the fate of the deniers. This is an exposition for the people, and a guidance and advice for the God-conscious" (3:137-138), among other verses.

Ayahs

۞ تِلْكَ ٱلرُّسُلُ فَضَّلْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۘ مِّنْهُم مَّن كَلَّمَ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَرَفَعَ بَعْضَهُمْ دَرَجَـٰتٍ ۚ وَءَاتَيْنَا عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَأَيَّدْنَـٰهُ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ ۗ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَا ٱقْتَتَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ وَلَـٰكِنِ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ ءَامَنَ وَمِنْهُم مَّن كَفَرَ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَا ٱقْتَتَلُوا۟ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَفْعَلُ مَا يُرِيدُ (٢٥٣)
These are the apostles, some of whom We gave an advantage over others: of them are those to whom Allah spoke and some of them He raised in rank, and We gave Jesus, son of Mary, manifest proofs and strengthened him with the Holy Spirit. Had Allah wished, those who succeeded them would not have fought one another after the manifest proofs had come to them. But they differed. So there were among them those who had faith and there were among them those who were faithless, and had Allah wished, they would not have fought one another; but Allah does whatever He desires. (253)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ يَوْمٌ لَّا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ وَلَا خُلَّةٌ وَلَا شَفَـٰعَةٌ ۗ وَٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٢٥٤)
O you who have faith! Spend out of what We have provided you before there comes a day on which there will be no bargaining, neither friendship, nor intercession. And the faithless—they are the wrongdoers. (254)

(Commentary)

The context of these two verses is not entirely distant from the context of the preceding verses, which were commanding struggle (الجهاد) and exhorting toward spending (الانفاق), then narrating the story of the fighting of Ṭālūt so that the believers might take heed. That story concluded with His saying, the Exalted: "And indeed you are one of the apostles" (2:252). These two verses begin with His saying: "These are the apostles; We have preferred some of them over others," then return to the matter of the fighting of the nations of the prophets after them. In the previous story—meaning the story of Ṭālūt—He said: "Have you not regarded the elite of the Israelites after Moses?" (2:246), bringing the phrase "after Moses" as a qualification. Then He returns to the call to spend before a day comes [in which there is no bargaining]. All of this supports the view that these two verses are a continuation of the preceding ones, and that they were all revealed together.

In short, the verse is in the position of dispelling what might be imagined: that the messengership (الرسالة)—especially since it is accompanied by manifest proofs (الآيات البينات) indicating the truth of the message—ought to have put an end to the calamity of fighting. This could be from the perspective that since Allah, the Glorified, intended to guide people to their worldly and otherworldly felicity by sending messengers and giving manifest proofs, it would have been appropriate to divert them from fighting thereafter and unite them upon guidance. So, what is the cause of these wars and disputes among the nations of the prophets after them, especially after the spread of the call of Islam, which considers unity and agreement among the pillars of its rulings and the foundations of its laws? Or it could be from the perspective that sending messengers and giving manifest proofs to call to the truth is for the purpose of obtaining the faith of the hearts (ايمان القلوب), and faith (الايمان) is one of the qualities of the heart that cannot be produced in the heart by force or compulsion; so what benefit does fighting provide after the establishment of prophethood? This is the problem whose formulation and answer preceded in the discussion on the verses of fighting.

That which the Exalted answers with is: that fighting is an effect (معلول) of the difference (الاختلاف) that exists between nations, for were it not for the existence of difference, the affair of the community would not lead to mutual killing. Thus, the cause (علة) of mutual killing is the difference occurring between them. Had Allah willed, no difference would have existed, and thus no mutual killing would have occurred at all. And had He willed, He could have rendered this cause sterile after its existence, but Allah, the Glorified, does what He intends (يفعل ما يريد). He has intended for affairs to proceed according to the sunnah of causes (سنة الأسباب); thus, difference existed, and consequently, fighting existed. This is the summary of what the verse provides.

His saying, the Exalted: تلك الرسل فضلنا بعضهم على بعض — "These are the apostles; We have preferred some of them over others" (2:253). This is an allusion to the magnificence of the messengers' affair and the loftiness of their station. Therefore, the demonstrative pronoun "those" (تلك) was used, which indicates an allusion to the distant. In it is an indication of the divine preference (التفضيل الإلهي) occurring between the prophets (peace be upon them); among them is he who is preferred, and among them is he who is preferred over. Yet all possess merit (فضل), for messengership in itself is a virtue (فضيلة) shared by all. Thus, among the messengers themselves, there is also a difference in stations (المقامات) and a variation in degrees (الدرجات), just as there is a difference among those after them, as indicated by the end of the verse. However, there is a distinction between the two types of difference. The difference between the prophets is a difference in stations and a preference in degrees despite their unity in the origin of merit—which is messengership—and their gathering in the assembly of perfection—which is monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. This is unlike the difference existing between the nations of the prophets after them, for that is a difference of faith (الايمان) and faithlessness (الكفر), of negation and affirmation. It is well known that there is no common ground in this type of difference. Therefore, the Exalted distinguished between them in terms of expression: He called what pertains to the prophets "preference" (تفضيل) and attributed it to Himself, while He called what pertains to the people "difference" (اختلاف) and attributed it to them. He said regarding the messengers, "We preferred" (فضلنا), and regarding their nations, "they differed" (اختلفوا).

Since the end of the verse addresses the issue of fighting and is linked to it, and the verses preceding this verse also pertain to fighting by commanding it and narrating accounts of it, it was inevitable that this segment of the speech—meaning His saying, the Exalted: "These are the apostles; We have preferred..." until His saying "...with the Holy Spirit"—serves as a preamble to clarify what is in the end of the verse: "Had Allah willed, those who succeeded them would not have fought one another..." until His saying, the Exalted: "...but Allah does whatever He desires."

Accordingly, the beginning of the verse is to clarify that the station of messengership, despite being shared among the messengers (peace be upon them), is a station in which goodness and blessings grow, and from which perfection, felicity, and degrees of proximity and nearness spring—such as the divine speaking (التكليم الالهي), the giving of manifest proofs (إِيتاء البينات), and the support with the Holy Spirit (التأييد بروح القدس). This station, despite the goodness and perfection it contains, did not necessitate the cessation of fighting, because fighting is based on the difference of the people themselves.

In other words, the sum of the verse's meaning is that messengership, with all its virtue, is a station in which goodness grows; whenever you turn to one side of it, you find a new merit, and whenever you incline toward one of its aspects, you find it lush and fresh. Yet this station, despite its splendor, radiance, and the bringing of manifest proofs, does not suffice to remove the difference between people regarding faithlessness and faith, for this difference is based only on themselves. They themselves originated this difference, as the Exalted said in another place: إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِندَ اللَّهِ الْإِسْلَامُ وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ إِلَّا مِن بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْيًا بَيْنَهُمْ — "Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam, and those who were given the Book did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves" (3:19). The explanation of this has already passed regarding His saying, the Exalted: كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً — "Mankind were but a single community" (2:213). Had Allah willed, He would have prevented this fighting occurring after them through an existential (تكوينياً) prevention, but they differed among themselves out of envy. Allah has made the sunnah of creation (سنة الايجاد) proceed according to causality (سببية ومسببية) between things, and difference is among the causes of conflict. Had Allah, the Exalted, willed, He could have prevented this fighting through a legislative (تشريعياً) prevention or by not commanding it. However, He, the Exalted, commanded it and intended by His command trial and examination (البلاء والامتحان), so that Allah might distinguish the corrupt from the good, and so that Allah might know those who believe and know the liars.

In short, fighting between the nations of the prophets after them is inevitable due to the difference arising from envy. Messengership and its manifest proofs only refute falsehood and remove doubt. As for envy, stubbornness, and similar vices, there is no way to purge the earth of them and reform the human species except through fighting. Experience shows that the proof (الحجة) alone has never succeeded unless accompanied by the sword. Therefore, whenever the interest (المصلحة) necessitated it, Allah, the Glorified, commanded standing for the truth and struggle in the way of Allah, as in the era of Abraham and the Israelites, and after the mission of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). Some discussion on this meaning has passed in the commentary on the verses of fighting previously.

His saying, the Exalted: منهم من كلم الله ورفع بعضهم درجات — "Some of them were those to whom Allah spoke, and some of them He raised in rank" (2:253). In these two sentences, there is a transition (التفات) from the second person (presence) to the third person (absence). The reason for this—and Allah knows best—is that virtuous attributes are of two types: some are virtuous according to the very meaning of the name, such as manifest proofs and support with the Holy Spirit, as mentioned for Jesus (peace be upon him), for these traits are themselves precious and lofty. Others are not so, but only indicate virtue and entail merit by means of attribution (الاضافة), such as speaking (التكليم); it is not considered a merit or virtue in itself unless it is attributed to something from which it acquires splendor and merit, such as its attribution to Allah, exalted is His Name. Likewise, the raising of degrees (رفع الدرجات) contains no virtue in itself unless it is said: "Allah raised the degrees," for example, thus attributing the raising to Allah. Once you know this, you will realize that this is the reason for the transition from presence to absence in two of the three sentences, where the Exalted said: "Some of them were those to whom Allah spoke, and some of them He raised in rank," and "We gave Jesus, son of Mary, manifest proofs." He turned the direction of the speech from the first person (speaking) to the third person (absence) in the first two sentences until the purpose was fulfilled, then returned to the first direction of speech, which is the first person, saying: "And We gave Jesus, son of Mary..."

The commentators have differed regarding who is intended by these two sentences. It was said that the one to whom Allah spoke refers to Moses (peace be upon him), due to His saying, the Exalted: وَكَلَّمَ اللَّهُ مُوسَىٰ تَكْلِيمًا — "and Allah spoke to Moses with [direct] speech" (4:164), and other verses. It was also said that it refers to the Messenger of Allah, Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), because Allah, the Exalted, spoke to him on the night of the Ascension (المعراج) when He brought him so near that all intermediaries were removed, and He spoke to him through revelation without an intermediary. The Exalted said: ثُمَّ دَنَا فَتَدَلَّىٰ فَكَانَ قَابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنَىٰ فَأَوْحَىٰ إِلَىٰ عَبْدِهِ مَا أَوْحَىٰ — "Then he drew near and hovered, until he was within two bows’ length or even nearer, whereat He revealed to His servant whatever He revealed" (53:8-10). It was also said that it refers to revelation (الوحي) — the divine communication to a prophet — in general, because revelation is a hidden speaking, and Allah, the Exalted, has called it speaking where He said: وَمَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَن يُكَلِّمَهُ اللَّهُ إِلَّا وَحْيًا أَوْ مِن وَرَاءِ حِجَابٍ — "It is not [fitting] for any human that Allah should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a curtain..." (42:51). This view does not harmonize with the partitive "some" (من) in His saying, the Exalted: "Some of them were those to whom Allah spoke."

The most appropriate view for the context is that it refers to Moses (peace be upon him), because his being spoken to is what is well-known from His speech, the Exalted, revealed before this Medinan Surah. The Exalted said: وَلَمَّا جَاءَ مُوسَىٰ لِمِيقَاتِنَا وَكَلَّمَهُ رَبُّهُ — "When Moses arrived at Our tryst and his Lord spoke to him..." (7:143), until He said: قَالَ يَا مُوسَىٰ إِنِّي اصْطَفَيْتُكَ عَلَى النَّاسِ بِرِسَالَاتِي وَبِكَلَامِي — "He said, ‘O Moses, I have chosen you over the people with My messages and My speaking’" (7:144). This is a Meccan verse, so Moses being the one spoken to was well-known at the time of the revelation of this verse.

Likewise, regarding His saying: ورفع بعضهم درجات — "and some of them He raised in rank," it was said that it refers to Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), because Allah raised his degree by preferring him over all messengers through his mission to all of creation, as the Exalted said: وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَاكَ إِلَّا كَافَّةً لِّلنَّاسِ — "We did not send you except as a bearer of good news and warner to all mankind" (34:28); and by making him a mercy to all the worlds, as the Exalted said: وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَاكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِّلْعَالَمِينَ — "We did not send you but as a mercy to all the worlds" (21:107); and by making him the Seal of Prophethood, as the Exalted said: وَلَٰكِن رَّسُولَ اللَّهِ وَخَاتَمَ النَّبِيِّينَ — "but [he is] the Apostle of Allah and the Seal of the Prophets" (33:40); and by giving him a Quran that is a guardian (مهيمناً) over all books, a clarification for all things, preserved from the corruption of the falsifiers, and a miracle remaining as long as the world remains, as the Exalted said: وَأَنزَلْنَا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ وَمُهَيْمِنًا عَلَيْهِ — "We have sent down to you the Book with the truth, confirming what was before it of the Book and as a guardian over it" (5:48); and the Exalted said: وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ تِبْيَانًا لِّكُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "We have sent down the Book to you as a clarification of all things" (16:89); and the Exalted said: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحَافِظُونَ — "Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it" (15:9); and the Exalted said: قُل لَّئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنسُ وَالْجِنُّ عَلَىٰ أَن يَأْتُوا بِمِثْلِ هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنِ لَا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ وَلَوْ كَانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيرًا — "Say, ‘Should entire mankind and the jinn get together to produce the like of this Quran, they will not produce the like of it, even if they were to help one another’" (17:88); and by singling him out with a right religion (دين قيم) that addresses all interests of this world and the Hereafter, as the Exalted said: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ الْقَيِّمِ — "So set your face toward the upright religion" (30:43). It was also said that it refers to the degrees Allah raised for more than one of the prophets, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted, regarding Noah: سَلَامٌ عَلَىٰ نُوحٍ فِي الْعَالَمِينَ — "Peace be to Noah throughout the worlds!" (37:79); and His saying, the Exalted, regarding Abraham (peace be upon him): وَإِذِ ابْتَلَىٰ إِبْرَاهِيمَ رَبُّهُ بِكَلِمَاتٍ فَأَتَمَّهنَّ قَالَ إِنِّي جَاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِمَامًا — "And when his Lord tested Abraham with certain words, and he fulfilled them, He said, ‘I am making you the Imam of mankind’" (2:124); and His saying, the Exalted, regarding him: وَاجْعَل لِّي لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ فِي الْآخِرِينَ — "and confer on me a worthy repute among the posterity" (26:84); and His saying, the Exalted, regarding Idris (peace be upon him): وَرَفَعْنَاهُ مَكَانًا عَلِيًّا — "and We raised him to a station sublime" (19:57); and His saying, the Exalted, regarding Joseph: نَرْفَعُ دَرَجَاتٍ مَّن نَّشَاءُ — "We raise in rank whomever We wish" (12:76); and His saying regarding David (peace be upon him): وَآتَيْنَا دَاوُودَ زَبُورًا — "and We gave David the Psalms" (4:163), among other specificities of the prophets.

Similarly, it was said: that the intended "messengers" in the verse are those specifically mentioned in Surah al-Baqarah, such as Abraham, Moses, Jesus, Ezra, Jeremiah, Samuel, David, and Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). Moses and Jesus were mentioned among them, and the rest remained; thus, the "some" whose degrees were raised is Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) in relation to the rest. It was also said: since the intended messengers in the verse are those whom Allah mentioned shortly before the verse in the story—namely Moses, David, Samuel, and Muhammad—and He mentioned the speaking specific to Moses, then He mentioned the raising of degrees, and it belongs to none but Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). The explicit mention of Jesus's name according to this view can be justified by saying: the reason for it is that he (peace be upon him) had not been previously mentioned among the prophets mentioned in these verses.

What ought to be said is: there is no doubt that the degrees by which Allah raised the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) are intended in the verse, yet there is no reason to restrict the verse to him, nor to those mentioned in these verses—namely Jeremiah, Samuel, David, and Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم)—nor to those mentioned in this Surah among the prophets. All of that would be an arbitrary restriction without an apparent reason. Rather, what is apparent from the absolute nature of the verse is the inclusion of all messengers (peace be upon them) and the inclusion of everyone upon whom Allah bestowed the raising of degrees in His saying, the Exalted: "and some of them He raised in rank."

As for what was said: that the style necessitates that the intended one is Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) because the context is in the clarification of the lesson for the nations that fight after their messengers despite their religion being one, and those present among them are the Jews, the Christians, and the Muslims, so it is appropriate to specify their messengers for mention; and since Moses and Jesus were mentioned in detail in the verse, it is determined that the remaining "some" is Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم).

To this it is replied: the Quran dictates that all messengers are messengers to all people; the Exalted said: لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُمْ — "We make no distinction between any of them" (2:136). Thus, the bringing of manifest proofs by all messengers should have cut off the root of corruption and fighting among those after them, but they differed out of envy among themselves. This became a root upon which fighting hastened; so Allah, the Exalted, commanded it when interest necessitated it, so that He might verify the truth by His words and cut off the root of the falsifiers. Thus, the generality (العموم) is well-founded in the verse.

(A Discourse on Speech)

Furthermore, His saying, the Exalted: "Some of them were those to whom Allah spoke," indicates the occurrence of speaking (التكليم) from Him to some people in general—that is, it indicates the occurrence of a real matter without metaphor (مجاز) or allegory (تمثيل), and Allah has called it "speech" (الكلام) in His Book, whether this usage is literal (حقيقي) or metaphorical. The discussion in this station is from two perspectives:

The First Perspective: That His speech, the Exalted, indicates that what Allah specifically bestowed upon His prophets and messengers of blessings that are hidden from the perception of other people—such as revelation (الوحي), speaking (التكليم), the descent of the Spirit (الروح) and the angels (الملائكة), and the witnessing of the greatest divine signs; or what He informed them of, such as the King, the Devil, the Tablet (اللوح), the Pen (القلم), and other signs hidden from human senses—all of these are real, factual matters (أُمور حقيقية واقعية) without metaphor in their claims. It is not as if they called the rational faculties that call to good "angels," and what these faculties cast into human perception "revelation," and the high rank of these faculties from which pure thoughts for the reform of human society emanate "the Holy Spirit" and "the Trustworthy Spirit"; or the appetitive and irascible psychic faculties that call to evil and corruption "devils" and "jinn," and the bad thoughts that corrupt righteous society or cause evil deeds "whispering" (الوسوسة) and "prompting" (النزعة), and so on.

For the Quranic verses, as well as what has been transmitted to us from the explanations of the past prophets, are apparent in that they did not intend by them metaphor and allegory, such that none doubts it except a stubborn person who is unreasonable, and we have no discourse with such a person. If it were permissible to interpret these explanations into such metaphors, it would be permissible to interpret all the divine truths they reported, without exception, into pure materialism that negates everything beyond matter. Some discussion on this station has passed in the discourse on miracles (الاعجاز). In the case of divine speaking, there is inevitably a real, realized matter upon which follow the same effects that follow the speeches existing among us.

To clarify this: He, the Glorified, expressed some of His acts as "speech" (الكلام) and "speaking" (التكليم), such as His saying, the Exalted: وَكَلَّمَ اللَّهُ مُوسَىٰ تَكْلِيمًا — "and Allah spoke to Moses with [direct] speech" (4:164), and His saying, the Exalted: "Some of them were those to whom Allah spoke." He, the Exalted, explained this vague usage in these two verses and their like with His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَن يُكَلِّمَهُ اللَّهُ إِلَّا وَحْيًا أَوْ مِن وَرَاءِ حِجَابٍ أَوْ يُرْسِلَ رَسُولًا فَيُوحِيَ بِإِذْنِهِ مَا يَشَاءُ — "It is not [fitting] for any human that Allah should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a curtain, or He sends a messenger who reveals by His permission whatever He wishes" (42:51). The exception in His saying, the Exalted, "except by revelation..." is not valid unless the speaking indicated by His saying "that Allah should speak to him" is a real speaking. Thus, the speaking of Allah, the Exalted, to humans is speaking, but in a specific manner; so the definition of the essence of speaking is not negated from it in reality.

That which we hold regarding the reality of speech is: that man, due to his need for society and civilization, needs by primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — everything that this cooperative society requires, among which is speaking. Primordial nature has compelled man to seek a way to indicate what is in the mind through sound (الصوت) based on the points of articulation (مخارج الحروف) from the mouth, and to make composed and mixed sounds signs (إمارات) indicating the meanings hidden in the mind, to which there is no path except through conventional, stipulated signs (العلائم الاعتبارية الوضعية). Thus, man needs speaking because he has no way to convey and receive understanding except by making words and composed sounds conventional signs and stipulated marks. Therefore, languages in their breadth revolve around the existing needs—meaning the needs that man has become aware of in his present life. For this reason also, languages continue to increase and expand according to the progress of society on its path and the multiplication of human needs in social life.

From here it appears: that speech—meaning the conveying of what is in the mind through composed sounds indicating it by convention and stipulation—is only completed in man when he is situated in the context of society. Perhaps it is attached to some types of animals that have a form of society and something of the genus of sound (as we suppose). As for man outside the context of cooperative society, there is no realization of speech for him. If there were a single human being without any assumed society, the need for speaking would certainly not arise, due to the lack of need for conveying and receiving understanding. Likewise, other than man, among those who do not need social cooperation and civil life for their existence, such as the angel and the devil, for example.

Thus, speech does not issue from Him, the Exalted, in the same way speech issues from us—meaning by way of sound exiting the throat and being based on the segments of breath from the mouth joined to it, with conventional, stipulated indication. For He, the Exalted, is too majestic in station and too hallowed in presence to be equipped with corporeal equipment, or to be perfected by illusory, conventional claims; and He, the Exalted, has said: لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ — "Nothing is like Him" (42:11).

However, He, the Glorified, in what has passed of His saying: "It is not [fitting] for any human that Allah should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a curtain," affirms for His aforementioned affair and act the reality of speaking, even if He negates from it the ordinary meaning well-known among people. Thus, speech in its well-known conventional definition is negated from the divine speech, but it is affirmed for it in its properties (خواصه) and effects (آثاره). With the preservation of the effect and the goal, the defined thing remains in the conventional matters circulating in human society, similar to the cubit, the balance, the measure, the lamp, the weapon, and the like, as has been previously explained.

It has thus become clear: that what Allah, the Glorified, discloses of a meaning intended to be understood by the prophet is speech in reality. Although He has explained to us in summary that it is speech in reality, not according to the quality we consider of the speech we use, He, the Exalted, has not explained to us—nor have we perceived from His speech—what the reality of this which He calls speech, with which He speaks to His prophets, is, or how it is realized. Nevertheless, in any case, the properties of the speech well-known among us are not negated from it, and its effects are affirmed for it, which are the conveying of intended meanings and casting them into the mind of the listener.

Accordingly, speech from Him, the Exalted—like giving life, causing death, providing sustenance, guidance, and acceptance of repentance—is an act (فعل) among His acts, the Exalted, which requires for its realization the completeness of the Essence (الذات) before it. It is not like knowledge (العلم), power (القدرة), and life (الحياة), among the attributes that are identical to the Essence (عين الذات), without which the Necessary Essence has no completeness. Indeed, there is no difference between it and His other acts that issue from Him after the assumption of the completeness of the Essence! Perhaps it even accepts application to time; the Exalted said: وَلَمَّا جَاءَ مُوسَىٰ لِمِيقَاتِنَا وَكَلَّمَهُ رَبُّهُ قَالَ رَبِّ أَرِنِي أَنظُرْ إِلَيْكَ قَالَ لَن تَرَانِي — "When Moses arrived at Our tryst and his Lord spoke to him, he said, ‘My Lord, show [Yourself] to me, that I may look at You.’ He said, ‘You shall not see Me’" (7:143); and the Exalted said: وَقَدْ خَلَقْتُكَ مِن قَبْلُ وَلَمْ تَكُ شَيْئًا — "when I created you before, when you were nothing" (19:9); and the Exalted said: فَقَالَ لَهُمُ اللَّهُ مُوتُوا ثُمَّ أَحْيَاهُمْ — "So Allah said to them, ‘Die!’ Then He gave them life" (2:243); and the Exalted said: نَّحْنُ نَرْزُقُكُمْ وَإِيَّاهُمْ — "We provide for you and for them" (6:151); and the Exalted said: الَّذِي أَعْطَىٰ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدَىٰ — "He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (20:50); and the Exalted said: ثُمَّ تَابَ عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَتُوبُوا — "Then He turned to them [clemently] so that they might repent" (9:118). The verses, as you see, indicate the temporality (زمانية) of speech just as they indicate the temporality of other acts like creation, causing death, giving life, providing sustenance, guidance, and acceptance of repentance, in the same manner.

This is what reflection upon His speech, the Exalted, and the exegetical research restricted to the Quranic verses provide regarding the meaning of speech. As for what theological research (البحث الكلامي) necessitates—according to what the predecessors among the theologians engaged in—or philosophical research, its report will come to you.

Know: that speech (الكلام) or speaking (التكليم) is something He, the Exalted, has not used except in the case of humans. Yes, "word" (الكلمة) or "words" (الكلمات) have been used in cases other than his. The Exalted said: وَكَلِمَتُهُ أَلْقَاهَا إِلَىٰ مَرْيَمَ — "and His Word that He cast toward Mary" (4:171), by which the human soul itself was intended. The Exalted said: وَكَلِمَةُ اللَّهِ هِيَ الْعُلْيَا — "and Allah’s word is the highest" (9:40); and the Exalted said: وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ صِدْقًا وَعَدْلًا — "The word of your Lord has been completed in truth and justice" (6:115); and the Exalted said: مَّا نَفِدَتْ كَلِمَاتُ اللَّهِ — "Allah’s words would not be exhausted" (31:27). By these, the decree (القضاء) or a type of creation (الخلق) was intended, as will be indicated.

As for the term "saying" (القول), it has been general in His speech, the Exalted, for humans and others. The Exalted said regarding humans: فَقُلْنَا يَا آدَمُ إِنَّ هَٰذَا عَدُوٌّ لَّكَ وَلِزَوْجِكَ — "So We said, ‘O Adam! This is indeed an enemy to you and your wife’" (20:117). The Exalted said regarding the angels: وَإِذ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً — "When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth’" (2:30); and He also said: إِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ إِنِّي خَالِقٌ بَشَرًا مِّن طِينٍ — "When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am about to create a human being out of clay’" (38:71). He said regarding Iblis: قَالَ يَا إِبْلِيسُ مَا مَنَعَكَ أَن تَسْجُدَ لِمَا خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَيَّ — "He said, ‘O Iblis! What kept you from prostrating before that which I have created with My [own] two hands?’" (38:75). The Exalted said regarding those other than the possessors of intellect: ثُمَّ اسْتَوَىٰ إِلَى السَّمَاءِ وَهِيَ دُخَانٌ فَقَالَ لَهَا وَلِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيَا طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا قَالَتَا أَتَيْنَا طَائِعِينَ — "Then He turned to the heaven, and it was smoke, and He said to it and to the earth, ‘Come! willingly or unwillingly.’ They said, ‘We come willingly’" (41:11). The Exalted said: قُلْنَا يَا نَارُ كُونِي بَرْدًا وَسَلَامًا عَلَىٰ إِبْرَاهِيمَ — "We said, ‘O fire! Be cool and safe for Abraham!’" (21:69). The Exalted said: وَقِيلَ يَا أَرْضُ ابْلَعِي مَاءَكِ وَيَا سَمَاءُ أَقْلِعِي — "And it was said, ‘O earth, swallow your water! O sky, leave off!’" (11:44). All of these, despite the multiplicity and dispersion of their instances, are gathered by His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَن يَقُولَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ — "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is" (36:82); and His saying, the Exalted: إِذا قَضَىٰ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ — "when He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is" (19:35).

That which reflection upon His speech, the Exalted, provides—where He uses "saying" (القول) in the mentioned instances for that which has hearing and perception in the sense well-known among us, like man, and for that whose way is existential creation (التكوين) and has no hearing or perception in the sense well-known among us, like the earth and the heaven, and since the last two verses are in the position of an explanation for the verses preceding them—is that "saying" from Him, the Exalted, is the origination (ايجاد) of a matter that indicates the intended meaning.

In existential matters (التكوينيات), the very thing that He, the Exalted, originated and created is a created, existing thing, and it is itself a "saying" for Him, the Exalted, because of its indication by its existence of His specific will, the Glorified. For it is well-known that when He wills a thing and says to it "Be" and it is, there is no intermediate word between Him, the Exalted, and the thing, and there is nothing other than the very existence of the thing. Thus, it is itself created and it is itself His saying, "Be." So His saying in existential matters is the act itself, which is origination (الإيجاد), and it is existence (الوجود), and it is the thing itself.

As for matters other than existential ones—such as the case of man, for example—it is by His origination of a matter that necessitates an inner knowledge in the human being that such-and-such is the case. This is either by originating a sound near one of the bodies, or in another manner that we do not perceive, or we do not perceive the quality of its effect on the soul of the prophet such that knowledge is produced in his soul that such-and-such is the case, according to what has passed regarding speech.

Likewise is the "saying" in His speech to the angels or the devil. However, these two types and their like—if they have a like—are characterized by a specificity: that the speech and saying well-known among us is only through the use of sound or gesture joined with conventional stipulation (الاعتبار الوضعي) necessitated in us by our social animal nature. It is well-known—according to what His speech, the Exalted, provides—that the angel and the devil, their existence is not of the same genus as our social animal existence, and there is not in their existence this gradual, scientific perfection that requires the establishment of conventional matters.

It appears from this: that there is not among the angels, nor among the devils, this type of mental conveying and receiving of understanding in which linguistic convention and composed sounds stipulated for meanings are used. Accordingly, the realization of "saying" among themselves is not like its realization among us human individuals—by the issuance of a sound composed in a linguistic, conventional composition from a split mouth joined by organs active for sound from one, and the being affected by that through the sensation of a split ear joined by organs receiving the struck sound from another. This is apparent. However, the reality of "saying" exists between their two types such that the effect of saying and its property follow from it, which is the understanding and perception of the intended meaning. Thus, between the angels or the devils there is "saying," but not like our saying; and likewise between Allah, the Glorified, and them there is "saying," but not by way of originating sound and stipulated words and making them hear them as you have heard.

Likewise is the "saying" in what is attributed to the types of mute animals of "saying" in the Holy Quran, such as His saying, the Exalted: قَالَتْ نَمْلَةٌ يَا أَيُّهَا النَّمْلُ ادْخُلُوا مَسَاكِنَكُمْ — "an ant said, ‘O ants, enter your dwellings’" (27:18); and the Exalted said: فَقَالَ أَحَطتُ بِمَا لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِ وَجِئْتُكَ مِن سَبَإٍ بِنَبَإٍ يَقِينٍ — "and said, ‘I have come to know what you do not know, and I have brought you a certain report from Sheba’" (27:22). Likewise is what is mentioned therein of the saying of Allah, the Exalted, and His revelation to them, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَأَوْحَىٰ رَبُّكَ إِلَى النَّحْلِ أَنِ اتَّخِذِي مِنَ الْجِبَالِ بُيُوتًا وَمِنَ الشَّجَرِ وَمِمَّا يَعْرِشُونَ — "And your Lord revealed to the bee: ‘Make homes in the mountains, and in the trees and in what they erect’" (16:68).

There are other terms that are perhaps used in the sense of "saying" and "speech" or what is close to their meaning, such as revelation (الوحي); the Exalted said: إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ كَمَا أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَىٰ نُوحٍ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ مِن بَعْدِهِ — "We have indeed revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him" (4:163); and inspiration (الالهام); the Exalted said: وَنَفْسٍ وَمَا سَوَّاهَا فَأَلْهَمَهَا فُجُورَهَا وَتَقْوَاهَا — "and the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its godwariness" (91:7-8); and report (النبأ); the Exalted said: قَالَ نَبَّأَنِيَ الْعَلِيمُ الْخَبِيرُ — "He said, ‘The All-knowing and the All-aware informed me’" (66:3); and narration (القص); the Exalted said: يَقُصُّ الْحَقَّ — "He narrates the truth" (6:57). The "saying" in all these terms, in terms of the reality of the meaning, is what we said at the beginning of the discourse: the necessity of the realization of a real matter with it upon which follows the effect of saying and its property, whether we know the reality of this real matter realized by necessity or we do not know its reality in detail. Regarding revelation specifically, there is a discourse that will be addressed in Surah al-Shura, if Allah wills.

As for the specificity of some instances with some of these terms—despite the mentioned shared meaning being present in all—such as calling some of them "speech," some "saying," and some "revelation," for example, and not otherwise, it revolves around the appearance of the application of the linguistic consideration (العناية اللفظية) to the instance. Thus, "saying" is called "speech" (كلاماً) in view of the cause that provides the occurrence of the meaning in the mind; therefore, this divine act was called "speech" in the instance of clarifying the preference of the prophets and their honoring, because the consideration there is only for the address and the speaking. It is called "saying" (قولاً) in view of the meaning intended to be cast and understood; therefore, this divine affair was called "saying" in the instance of decree, predestination, judgment, legislation, and the like, such as His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ فَالْحَقُّ وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ — "He said, ‘The truth is—and the truth I say—that I will surely fill...’" (38:84-85). It is called "revelation" (وحياً) with the consideration of its being hidden from those other than the prophets; therefore, it was expressed in their case (peace be upon them) as revelation, such as His saying: "We have indeed revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him..." (4:163).

The Second Perspective: Which is the research from the perspective of the quality of usage. You have learned that the vocabulary of language was only transferred by man to its meanings, and he established words corresponding to them and used them therein, in the sensibles (المحسوسات) among corporeal matters initially, then he gradually transferred to the intelligibles (المعنويات). This, although it necessitated that the usage of a word established for a sensible meaning in an intelligible meaning be a metaphorical usage initially, it would return to being literal (حقيقة) after the stabilization of usage and the attainment of precedence in the mind (التبادر). Likewise, the progress of society and the advancement of man in civilization and culture necessitate change in the means that fulfill his vital needs, and the constant substitution therein while the names remain. Thus, names continue to have the instances (مصاديق) of their meanings substituted while the intended purposes remain. This is just as the lamp (السراج), when man first became aware of the possibility of fulfilling some needs by it, was, for example, something of oil or fatty substances with a wick connected to them in a vessel that preserved them, so the wick would burn for illumination at night. Industry fashioned it upon this form first, and man called it a "lamp." Then it did not cease to transform stage after stage, and be fashioned layer upon layer, until it ended at these electric lamps in which there is not found, nor with them, any of the parts of the lamp fashioned first—corresponding to which the word "lamp" was established—of oil, wick, and a clay or metal bowl. Nevertheless, we apply the word "lamp" to them and to all types of lamps equally, and without special consideration. This is only because the goal and purpose of the lamp—meaning the intended effect from it following from what was fashioned first—follows identically from what was fashioned last without difference, which is illumination. We do not intend any of the means of life, nor do we know them, except by their goal in life and their following effect. Thus, the reality of the lamp is that by whose light one is illuminated at night; and with the preservation of this property and effect, the reality of the lamp remains, and the name of the lamp remains for the reality of its meaning without change or substitution, even if the form changes sometimes, or the quality, or the quantity, or the very parts of the essence, as you learned in the example. Accordingly, the criterion in the preservation of the literal meaning and its non-preservation is the preservation of the desired effect from the thing as it was without change. Rarely is there found today among fashioned things and means of life—which are thousands upon thousands—anything whose essence has not changed from what it was first originated upon; yet the preservation of the effect and the property preserved for each of them its first name that was established for it. In languages, there is much of the first type—which is the word transferred from a sensible meaning to an intelligible meaning—that the insightful researcher encounters.

It has thus been obtained that the usage of "speech" and "saying" in what has passed, with the assumption of the preservation of the effect and the property, is a literal usage, not metaphorical.

It has become clear from all that we have explained: that the application of "speech" and "saying" in His case, the Exalted, reports a real, factual matter, and that it is among the degrees of the literal meaning of these two words, even if it differs in terms of the instance (المصداق) from the instance of speech we have, just as other words shared in usage between us and Him, the Exalted—such as life, knowledge, will, and giving—are likewise.

Know: that the discourse on the meaning of "raising degrees" in His saying, the Exalted: "and some of them He raised in rank," in terms of its containing a real, factual matter that is not conventional (اعتباري), is exactly like the discourse on the meaning of speech. Most researchers in religious knowledge have imagined: that what these explanations contain are conventional matters and illusory meanings similar to what exists among us, the community of social human beings, of stations of leadership, authority, virtue, precedence, presiding, and the like. Consequently, they were forced to make the truths associated with them—such as the stations of the Hereafter of Paradise, Fire, questioning, and others—associated and following in a manner similar to the following of external effects upon these social conventional stations. That is, the link between the mentioned spiritual stations and the results following from them is a link of convention and stipulation. They were forced—by necessity—to consider the originator of these links, who is Allah, the Exalted and Hallowed, as being governed by conventional opinions and prompted by illusory consciousness like a human being situated in the world of matter and descending in the abode of movement and perfection. Therefore, you see them recoiling from affirming for the near ones among His prophets and friends the real spiritual perfections that the outward meanings of the Book and the Sunnah affirm for them, unless they are stripped of their reality and returned to a form of conventionalities.

His saying, the Exalted: وآتينا عيسى بن مريم البينات وأيدناه برروح القدس — "And We gave Jesus, son of Mary, manifest proofs and confirmed him with the Holy Spirit" (2:253). This is a return to the original context, which is the first person (speaking) rather than the third person (absence), as passed.

The reason for the explicit mention of Jesus's name while not mentioning other messengers in the verse is: that what He mentioned for him (peace be upon him) of aspects of preference—which is the giving of manifest proofs and the support with the Holy Spirit—is shared among all messengers and is not something specific to some of them to the exclusion of others. The Exalted said: لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلَنَا بِالْبَيِّنَاتِ — "Certainly We sent Our apostles with manifest proofs" (57:25); and the Exalted said: يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلَائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ أَنْ أَنذِرُوا — "He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command to whomever He wishes of His servants: ‘Warn...’" (16:2). However, they were in Jesus in a specific manner; for all his signs—such as giving life to the dead, creating the bird by breathing, and healing the blind and the leper, and informing of the unseen—were matters leaning upon life, emanating from the Spirit. Therefore, He attributed them to Jesus (peace be upon him) and explicitly mentioned his name, for had there been no explicit mention, it would not have indicated its being a specific virtue, as if it were said: "And We gave some of them manifest proofs and supported him with the Holy Spirit," since manifest proofs and the Holy Spirit, as you have learned, are shared and not specific. Thus, it is not sound to attribute them to "some" by way of specificity except with the explicit mention of his name, so that it may be known that they are in him in a specific, non-shared manner, approximately. Furthermore, in the name of Jesus (peace be upon him) specifically is another property and a manifest sign, which is that he is the son of Mary with no father. The Exalted said: وَجَعَلْنَاهَا وَابْنَهَا آيَةً لِّلْعَالَمِينَ — "and We made her and her son a sign for all the worlds" (21:91). Thus, the combination of the son and the mother is a manifest divine sign and another specific virtue.

His saying, the Exalted: ولو شاء الله ما اقتتل الذين من بعدهم من بعدما جائتهم البينات — "Had Allah willed, those who succeeded them would not have fought one another after the manifest proofs had come to them" (2:253). The shift to the third person (absence) again is because the station is a station of manifesting that the Lordly volition and will are not overcome, and power is not nullified. Thus, all events, on both sides of their affirmation and negation, are not outside the divine sovereignty. In short, the attribute of Divinity (الالوهية) is that which negates the restriction of power and necessitates the absolute nature of its attachment to both sides of affirmation and negation. Thus, the need of the station required the manifestation of this transcendent attribute—meaning Divinity—for mention; so it was said: "Had Allah willed, they would not have fought," and it was not said: "Had We willed, they would not have fought." This is also the reason for His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verse: "Had Allah willed, they would not have fought," and His saying: "but Allah does whatever He desires." It is also the reason for the shift from the pronoun to the explicit noun.

His saying, the Exalted: ولكن اختلفوا فمنهم من آمن ومنهم من كفر — "but they differed; so there were some of them who believed and some of them who were faithless" (2:253). He attributed the difference to them, not to Himself, because He, the Exalted, mentioned in places of His speech: that the difference regarding faith, faithlessness, and other original knowledge clarified in the Books of Allah revealed to His prophets only occurred among people out of envy (البغى). Far be it that envy or injustice be attributed to Him, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: ولو شاء ما اقتتلوا ولكن الله يفعل ما يريد — "Had He willed, they would not have fought one another, but Allah does whatever He desires" (2:253). That is, had Allah willed, the difference would not have been effective in necessitating fighting, but Allah does what He intends, and He has intended that this difference be effective in driving people to mutual killing, proceeding according to the sunnah of causes.

The sum of the verse's meaning—and Allah knows best—is: that the messengers who were sent to the people are servants of Allah, near to their Lord, their horizon raised above the people, and they are preferred some over others despite their having one origin and a shared station. This is the state of the messengers, and they brought to the people manifest proofs by which they manifested the truth with all manifestation and clarified the path of guidance with the most complete clarification. Its necessity was that people after them should not be driven except toward unity, intimacy, and love in the religion of Allah without difference and fighting. However, there was another cause that rendered this cause sterile, which is the difference arising from envy among them and their branching into believer and disbeliever, then the separation after that in all affairs of life and felicity. Had Allah willed, He would have rendered this cause—meaning difference—sterile, so it would not have necessitated mutual killing and they would not have fought. But He did not so will, and He made this cause proceed like other causes and reasons according to the sunnah of causes that Allah intended in the world of fashioning and origination; and Allah does what He intends.

His saying, the Exalted: يا ايها الذين آمنوا انفقوا — "O you who have faith! Spend..." (2:254). Its meaning is clear, and at the end of the verse is an indication that recoiling from spending is faithlessness (كفر) and wrongdoing (ظلم).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kāfī, narrated from Imam al-Bāqir (peace be upon him): regarding His saying, the Exalted: "These are the apostles; We have preferred..." He said: "In this is that by which it is proven that the companions of Muhammad differed after him; so among them were those who believed and among them were those who were faithless."

In the Tafsir of al-ʿAyyāshī, narrated from Aṣbagh ibn Nubātah, who said: "I was standing with the Commander of the Faithful, Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him), on the Day of the Camel, when a man came and stood before him and said: 'O Commander of the Faithful, the people [the opposing side] have proclaimed the greatness of Allah (كبروا) and we have proclaimed His greatness; the people have proclaimed His oneness (هللوا) and we have proclaimed His oneness; the people have prayed and we have prayed; so upon what basis do we fight them?!' He (peace be upon him) replied: 'Upon this verse: تِلْكَ الرُّسُلُ فَضَّلْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ مِّنْهُم مَّن كَلَّمَ اللَّهُ وَرَفَعَ بَعْضَهُمْ دَرَجَاتٍ وَآتَيْنَا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ الْبَيِّنَاتِ وَأَيَّدْنَاهُ بِرروحِ الْقُدُسِ وَلَوْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ مَا اقْتَتَلَ الَّذِينَ مِن بَعْدِهِم مِّن بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّنَاتُ وَلَٰكِنِ اخْتَلَفُوا فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ آمَنَ وَمِنْهُم مَّن كَفَرَ وَلَوْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ مَا اقْتَتَلُوا وَلَٰكِنَّ اللَّهَ يَفْعَلُ مَا يُرِيدُ (These are the apostles; We have preferred some of them over others. Some of them were those to whom Allah spoke, and some of them He raised in rank. And We gave Jesus, son of Mary, manifest proofs and confirmed him with the Holy Spirit. Had Allah willed, those who succeeded them would not have fought one another after the manifest proofs had come to them. But they differed; so there were some of them who believed and some of them who were faithless. Had Allah willed, they would not have fought one another, but Allah does whatever He desires). We are those who believed and they are those who were faithless.' The man said: 'The people have become faithless, by the Lord of the Kaaba!' Then he charged and fought until he was killed—may Allah have mercy on him."

I say: This story was narrated by al-Mufīd and al-Shaykh [al-Ṭūsī] in their Amālī, and al-Qummī in his Tafsir. The narration indicates that he (peace be upon him) took "faithlessness" (الكفر) in the verse in the more general sense, rather than the specific technical faithlessness that has specific rulings in the religion. For the widely transmitted reports, as well as history, testify that he (peace be upon him) did not treat his opponents from the People of the Camel, the People of Siffin, and the Kharijites with the treatment of disbelievers from other than the People of the Book, nor the treatment of the People of the Book, nor the treatment of the people of apostasy (أهل الردة) from the religion. It is only that he counted them as faithless in the inward (الباطن) rather than the outward (الظاهر). He (peace be upon him) used to say: "I fight them upon the interpretation (التأويل) rather than the revelation (التنزيل)."

The outward meaning of the verse supports this sense, for it indicates that the manifest proofs brought by the messengers did not avail in removing mutual killing from those after them due to the difference based on themselves. Thus, the occurrence of difference is something in which the manifest proofs from the messengers do not avail; rather, it is something to which human society leads, which is not free from envy and injustice. Thus, the verse is in the course of His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا كَانَ النَّاسُ إِلَّا أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَاخْتَلَفُوا وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ فِيمَا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ — "Mankind were but a single community; then they differed; and were it not for a prior word from your Lord, decision would have been made between them concerning that about which they differ" (10:19); and His saying, the Exalted: كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً — until He said — وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ مِن بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّنَاتُ بَغْيًا بَيْنَهُمْ فَهَدَى اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لِمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ مِنَ الْحَقِّ بِإِذْنِهِ — "and none differed about it except those who had been given it, after the manifest proofs had come to them, out of envy among themselves. Then Allah guided those who believed to the truth of what they differed about by His leave" (2:213); and His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا يَزَالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ رَبُّكَ — "and they will not cease to differ, except those on whom your Lord has mercy" (11:118-119). All of this indicates that difference regarding the Book—which is difference in religion—among the followers of the prophets after them is inevitable. He, the Exalted, said regarding this specific nation: أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تَدْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِكُم مَّثَلُ الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِن قَبْلِكُم — "Do you suppose that you shall enter paradise though there has not yet come to you the like of [what befell] those who went before you?" (2:214). And He, the Exalted, said, narrating from His Messenger for the Day of Resurrection: وَقَالَ الرَّسُولُ يَا رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِي اتَّخَذُوا هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنَ مَهْجُورًا — "And the Apostle will say, ‘O my Lord! Indeed my people consigned this Quran to oblivion’" (25:30). Within the verses are explicit statements and allusions to that.

As for the fact that the tail of this difference extends to the time of the companions after the departure [of the Prophet], the reliable history and the widely transmitted or consecutively reported (المتواتر) reports indicate that the companions themselves treated one another in the tribulations and differences occurring after the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) with this treatment, without exempting themselves from that based on infallibility, glad tidings, independent judgment (اجتهاد), or an exemption from Allah and His Messenger beyond this amount. Research beyond this measure does not suit the nature of this book.

In the Amālī of al-Mufīd, narrated from Abū Baṣīr, who said: "I heard Abū ʿAbdillāh (peace be upon him) saying: 'Allah, majestic is His Name, has never ceased to be knowing by His Essence when there was no object of knowledge; and He has never ceased to be powerful by His Essence when there was no object of power.' I said: 'May I be your ransom, has He never ceased to be speaking (متكلماً)?' He replied: 'Speech (الكلام) is originated (محدث). Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, was, and He was not speaking, then He originated speech.'"

In al-Ihtijaj, it is narrated from Safwan ibn Yahya that he said: Abu Qurrah, the traditionist (المحدث), questioned Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him), saying: "Inform me—may I be your ransom—concerning Allah’s speaking to Moses." He (peace be upon him) replied: "Allah knows best in which tongue He spoke to him, whether in Syriac or Hebrew." Abu Qurrah then held his own tongue and said: "I am only asking you about this tongue." Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him) replied: "Glory be to Allah (سبحان الله) above what you say! I seek refuge in Allah (معاذ الله) from Him resembling His creation or speaking in the manner they speak. Rather, glorified is He, there is nothing like Him (ليس كمثله شيء), nor is there any speaker or actor like Him." He asked: "How so?" The Imam replied:

كلام الخالق لمخلوق ليس ككلام المخلوق لمخلوق ، ولا يلفظ بشق فم ولسان ، ولكن يقول له كن فكان بمشيته ما خاطب به موسى من الامر والنهي من غير تردد في نفس — "The speech of the Creator to a creature is not like the speech of a creature to a creature. It is not articulated by the opening of a mouth or a tongue; rather, He says to it 'Be' and it is (كن فكان) by His will (المشيئة). That which He addressed to Moses of command and prohibition was without any hesitation in a mind." — the narration.

In Nahj al-Balaghah, in one of his sermons (peace be upon him): "He is a speaker (متكلم) but not through reflection (روية), a willer (مريد) but not through aspiration (همة)..." — the sermon.

Also in Nahj al-Balaghah, in another of his sermons (peace be upon him): "He who spoke to Moses directly (تكليماً) and showed him of His great signs, without limbs (جوارح), nor instruments (أدوات), nor speech (نطق), nor uvulae (لهوات)..." — the sermon.

I say: The narrations (الأخبار) reported from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt regarding this meaning are numerous. they are in unanimous agreement that the speech (الكلام) of the Exalted—which the Book and the Sunnah term 'speech'—is an attribute of action (صفة فعل), not an attribute of essence (صفة ذات).

( Philosophical Discussion )
