By reflecting on the context of the verse and the way matters have historically proceeded among people—and continue to proceed—the meaning of this argument mentioned by Allah, the Exalted, in this verse and the subject upon which their argument occurred becomes clear.

To explain: Man remains, by his primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — submissive to the powers that dominate him and influence him. This is something that no researcher into the history of past nations or observer of the current state of various groups can doubt, as we have explained in previous discussions. By his primordial nature (الفطرة), man affirms for the world a Creator (صانعاً) who influences it in terms of creation (التكوين) and governance (التدبير), which has also been previously explained. This is a matter in which the judgment of man does not differ, whether he professes monotheism (التوحيد)—upon which the religion of the Prophets is built and their call relies—or adopts polytheism (تعدد الآلهة), as is the case with idolaters, or denies the Creator, as is the case with materialists (الدهريون) and atheists. For the primordial nature (الفطرة) does not accept invalidation as long as man remains man, even if it is susceptible to heedlessness and distraction.

However, primitive, simple man, because he used to measure things against himself, saw that his various actions were attributed to his various powers and limbs. Similarly, various social actions were attributed to different individuals in society, and various events to different proximate causes, even if all reins ultimately meet at the Creator to whom the entirety of the world of existence is attributed. Consequently, he inevitably affirmed different lords (أرباباً) for different types of events instead of Allah, the Glorified. Sometimes he affirmed this in the name of the "Lords of Species" (أرباب الأنواع), such as the Lord of the Earth, the Lord of the Seas, the Lord of Fire, the Lord of Air and Winds, and so on. At other times, he affirmed it in the name of the stars, especially the planets (السيارات), to which he attributed—despite their differences—various influences in the world of elements and generated beings, as is reported regarding the Sabaeans. Then, he would make images and statues for those lords and worship them so they might serve as a means of intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — with the master of the idol, and the master of the idol would be an intercessor for him with Allah the Great, the Glorified, thereby attaining happiness in life and death.

For this reason, idols differed according to the differences of nations and generations, because opinions varied in identifying the different species and imagining the forms of the "Lords of Species" represented by their idols; perhaps other desires and whims also became attached to this. Gradually, the matter led to clinging to the idols and forgetting their lords, and even the Lord of Lords, because the senses and imagination beautified what they perceived of them, remembering them while forgetting what lay beyond them. This caused their side to prevail over the side of Allah, the Glorified. All of this occurred because they saw these lords as having an influence on the affairs of their lives, such that their will overcame human will, and their governance (تدبيرها) dominated human governance.

Perhaps some of those possessing power, might, and authority among the tyrannical kings took advantage of this belief and the influence of their command over various affairs of life. Thus, such a king would covet the station and claim divinity (الالوهية), as is reported of Pharaoh, Nimrod, and others. He would place himself in the rank of the lords, even if he himself worshipped idols as they did. Although this was the pattern at the outset, the manifestation of his influence and the dominance of his command over the senses necessitated his precedence in the eyes of his worshippers over other lords and the triumph of his side over theirs. This was alluded to earlier, as Allah, the Exalted, recounts Pharaoh's saying to his people: أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلَىٰ — "I am your exalted lord" (An-Nazi'at: 24). He claimed to be the highest of the lords despite being among those who took lords, as the Exalted said: وَيَذَرَكَ وَآلِهَتَكَ — "and [to] abandon you and your gods" (Al-A'raf: 127). Similarly, Nimrod made such a claim, as inferred from his saying: "I give life and make to die," in this verse, as we shall explain.

Through this explanation, the meaning of this argument occurring between Abraham (peace be upon him) and Nimrod is revealed. Nimrod acknowledged divinity (الوهية) for Allah, the Glorified; were it not so, he would not have conceded to Abraham (peace be upon him) his saying: "Indeed Allah brings the sun from the east, so bring it from the west," nor would he have been confounded (بهت) by it. Rather, he could have said: "I bring it from the east, not the one you claim," or "some other gods bring it from the east." He believed there were other gods besides Allah, the Glorified, and his people believed the same, as indicated by all the stories of Abraham (peace be upon him), such as the story of the star, the moon, and the sun, his dialogue with his father regarding the idols, his address to his people, his breaking the idols into pieces except for their chief, and so on. He acknowledged divinity for Allah, the Exalted, and that there were other gods with Him, but he also saw divinity for himself and that he was the highest of the gods. Therefore, he argued for his own lordship (ربوبيته) when he disputed with Abraham (peace be upon him) regarding his Lord, and he did not mention anything regarding the other gods.

From here, it is concluded that the argument that took place between him and Abraham (peace be upon him) was that Abraham (peace be upon him) claimed his Lord was Allah and none other, while Nimrod claimed he was the lord of Abraham and others. Therefore, when Abraham (peace be upon him) argued for his claim by saying: "My Lord is He who gives life and makes to die," he said: "I give life and make to die." He claimed to be characterized by what Abraham described his Lord with; thus, he was the lord to whom Abraham must submit and occupy himself with worshipping, instead of Allah, the Glorified, and instead of the idols. He did not say: "And I give life and make to die," because the use of the conjunction "and" would imply sharing lordship with Allah, and that was not his objective; rather, his objective was singular superiority, as you have seen. Nor did he say: "And the gods give life and make to die."

He did not oppose Abraham (peace be upon him) with truth, but rather with deception, fallacy (المغالطة), and by obfuscating the matter for those present. For Abraham (peace be upon him) only intended by his saying: "My Lord is He who gives life and makes to die," the life and death witnessed in these living, sentient, and volitional beings. This life, whose essence is unknown, can only be originated by one who possesses it; it cannot be attributed to inanimate nature which lacks it, nor to any of these living beings, for their life is their existence and their death is their non-existence, and a thing has no power to originate itself nor to annihilate itself. Had Nimrod taken this speech in its true sense, he could not have opposed it with anything. However, he committed a fallacy and took life and death in their metaphorical sense, or a sense broader than the real and metaphorical. For "giving life" (الاحياء) is said of bestowing life upon something like a fetus when life is breathed into it, and it is also said of rescuing a person from the brink of destruction. Similarly, "making to die" (الاماتة) is applied to the taking of the soul (التوفي), which is the act of Allah, and to something like killing with a lethal instrument. At that point, he ordered two men to be brought from prison; he ordered one to be killed and the other to be released. When this one was killed and that one released, he said: "I give life and make to die." He obfuscated the matter for the onlookers, and they believed him. Abraham (peace be upon him) could not, at that moment, explain the nature of the fallacy—that he did not mean life and death in this metaphorical sense, and that an argument does not oppose an argument. Had it been within his power (peace be upon him) to do so, he would have explained it. This was only because he witnessed Nimrod's state in his deception and the state of the onlookers in their blind acceptance of his false claim. He realized that if he explained the nature of the fallacy, no one would believe him. Thus, he turned to another argument that a stubborn person could not oppose. Abraham (peace be upon him) said: "Indeed Allah brings the sun from the east, so bring it from the west." This is because the sun, even if it was among the gods for them or some of them—as appears from what relates to the star and the moon in his story (peace be upon him)—it and the acts associated with its existence, like rising and setting, are ultimately attributed to Allah, whom they saw as the Lord of Lords. A volitional agent (الفاعل الارادي), when he chooses an act by will, has the power to choose its opposite, just as he chose the act itself, for the matter revolves around the will. In short, when Abraham said that, Nimrod was confounded (بهت), as he could not say that this continuous matter proceeding in a single pattern—its constant rising from the east—is a coincidental matter requiring no cause. Nor could he say it was an act attributed to the sun itself and not to Allah, for he already conceded the opposite. Nor could he say: "I am the one who brings it from the east," for he would then be demanded to bring it from the west. Thus, Allah silenced him and he was confounded; and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people.

His saying, the Exalted: أَنْ آتَاهُ اللَّهُ الْمُلْكَ (because Allah had given him the kingdom) [Qarai 2:258]. The apparent context is that it is like someone saying: "So-and-so mistreated me because I treated him well," meaning: "My good treatment of him necessitated that he treat me well, but he replaced good treatment with mistreatment, so he mistreated me." And their saying: "Beware the evil of him to whom you have done good." The poet said:

His sons rewarded Abu al-Ghilan for old age And good deeds, as Sinimmar was rewarded.

The sentence—meaning His saying: أَنْ آتَاهُ اللَّهُ الْمُلْكَ (because Allah had given him the kingdom)—presupposes the "lam" of causality (لام التعليل). It is of the category of placing a thing in the position of its opposite for the purpose of complaint, seeking redress, and the like. For Nimrod's enmity and transgression in this argument should have been attributed to the opposite of Allah's blessing of kingship upon him. However, since nothing was realized from Allah toward him except benevolence and the granting of kingship, it was placed in the position of the cause, thereby indicating his ingratitude for Allah's blessing. In a way, it is like His saying, the Exalted: فَالْتَقَطَهُ آلُ فِرْعَوْنَ لِيَكُونَ لَهُمْ عَدُوًّا وَحَزَنًا — "Then Pharaoh’s family picked him up, that he might become an enemy and a cause of grief for them" (Al-Qasas: 8). This is one subtle point (نكتة) in mentioning the granting of the kingdom.

There is another subtle point: indicating the wretchedness of his claim from the very start. This is because he only made this claim due to a kingdom that Allah, the Exalted, gave him without him possessing it for himself. He was only Nimrod the King, the one with authority and power, by a blessing from his Lord. As for himself, he was but one of the common people, with no known description or distinguished attribute. For this reason, his name was not mentioned, and he was referred to as: "him who argued with Abraham about his Lord," to indicate the insignificance of his person and the meanness of his affair.

As for attributing his kingdom to Allah's granting, it has been mentioned in previous discussions that there is no problem in this. For kingship (الملك)—which is a type of authority spread over a nation like all other types of authority and power—is a blessing from Allah and a favor He grants to whom He wills. He has deposited the knowledge of it and the desire for it in the primordial nature (الفطرة) of man. If it is placed in its proper place, it is a blessing and happiness. The Exalted said: وَابْتَغِ فِيمَا آتَاكَ اللَّهُ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ — "By means of what Allah has given you, seek the abode of the Hereafter" (Al-Qasas: 77). But if it exceeds its bounds and deviates from the path, it becomes for him a punishment and ruin. The Exalted said: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ كُفْرًا وَأَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دَارَ الْبَوارِ — "Have you not regarded those who exchanged Allah’s blessing for ungratefulness, and landed their people in the house of ruin?" (Ibrahim: 28). It has already been explained that everything has an attribution to Him, the Exalted, in a manner befitting His holy presence, from the aspect of the beauty within it, not from the aspect of ugliness or evil.

From here, the invalidity of what some commentators have mentioned becomes clear: that the pronoun in His saying: أَنْ آتَاهُ اللَّهُ الْمُلْكَ (because Allah had given him the kingdom) refers to Abraham (peace be upon him), and the intended kingdom is Abraham's kingdom, as the Exalted said: أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ النَّاسَ عَلَىٰ مَا آتَاهُمُ اللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِ فَقَدْ آتَيْنَا آلَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَآتَيْنَاهُم مُّلْكًا عَظِيمًا — "Do they envy the people for what Allah has given them out of His bounty? We have certainly given the progeny of Abraham the Book and wisdom, and We have given them a great kingdom" (An-Nisa: 54), and not Nimrod's kingdom, because it was a kingdom of oppression and disobedience which cannot be attributed to Allah, the Glorified.

This is refuted firstly by the fact that the Quran frequently attributes such kingship and its like to Him, the Exalted, such as His saying recounting the believer from the family of Pharaoh: يَا قَوْمِ لَكُمُ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ ظَاهِرِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ — "O my people! Today the kingdom is yours, as you are dominant in the land" (Al-Mu'min: 29), and His saying, the Exalted, recounting Pharaoh—and He affirmed it by recounting it: يَا قَوْمِ أَلَيْسَ لِي مُلْكُ مِصْرَ — "O my people! Does not the kingdom of Egypt belong to me?" (Az-Zukhruf: 51). Indeed, the Exalted said: لَهُ الْمُلْكُ — "To Him belongs the kingdom" (At-Taghabun: 1), thus restricting all kingship to Himself; so there is no kingship except that it is from Him, the Exalted. The Exalted also said recounting Moses (peace be upon him): رَبَّنَا إِنَّكَ آتَيْتَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَأَهُ زِينَةً — "Our Lord! You have given Pharaoh and his elite trappings" (Yunus: 88). The Exalted said regarding Korah: وَآتَيْنَاهُ مِنَ الْكُنُوزِ مَا إِنَّ مَفَاتِحَهُ لَتَنُوءُ بِالْعُصْبَةِ أُولِي الْقُوَّةِ — "We gave him such treasures that their keys would weigh down a group of stalwart men" (Al-Qasas: 76). The Exalted said addressing His Prophet: ذَرْنِي وَمَنْ خَلَقْتُ وَحِيدًا وَجَعَلْتُ لَهُ مَالًا مَّمْدُودًا — "Leave Me to [deal with] him whom I created alone, and then I gave him extensive wealth" — until He said — وَمَهَّدتُّ لَهُ تَمْهِيدًا ثُمَّ يَطْمَعُ أَنْ أَزِيدَ — "and smoothed [the way] for him [in every way]. Then he covets that I should give more" (Al-Muddaththir: 11-15), among other verses.

Secondly: This does not align with the apparent meaning of the verse, for its apparent meaning is that Nimrod was disputing with Abraham regarding his monotheism (توحيده) and faith (إِيمانه), not that he was disputing and arguing with him regarding his kingdom. For the outward kingdom belonged to Nimrod, and he did not see any kingdom for Abraham such that he would quarrel with him over it.

Thirdly: Everything has an attribution to Allah, the Glorified, and kingship is among those things; there is no problem in attributing it to Him, the Exalted, as has been explained in detail.

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ رَبِّيَ الَّذِي يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ (Abraham said, ‘My Lord is He who gives life and makes to die’) [Qarai 2:258]. Life and death, although they exist in things other than the animal genus as well, such as plants—and the Quran has affirmed this as explained in the commentary on the Verse of the Throne (آية الكرسي)—his (peace be upon him) intention by them was either specifically animal life and death, or the broader sense encompassing both due to the generality of the term. The evidence for this is Nimrod's saying: "I give life and make to die," for this which he claimed for himself was not like giving life to plants through plowing and planting, for example, nor giving life to animals through mating and procreation, for that and its like were not exclusive to him but occurred in other human individuals. This supports what has been reported in narrations: that he ordered two men from his prison to be brought, released one and killed the other, and said at that moment: "I give life and make to die."

He (peace be upon him) chose giving life (الاحياء) and making to die (الاماتة) in his argument because they are two matters in which nature, lacking life, has no role—especially that which is in animals, as it entails consciousness (الشعور) and will (الارادة), which are definitely non-material matters. The same applies to death, which is their opposite. Yet the argument, despite its brilliance and clarity, did not succeed with them, because their intellectual degradation and confusion in reasoning were beyond what he (peace be upon him) had expected of them. They understood nothing of giving life and making to die except the metaphorical meaning encompassing things like release and killing. Thus Nimrod said: "I give life and make to die," and those present believed him. From the context of this argument, the observer can guess the extent of intellectual degradation reached at that time in knowledge and spiritual matters. This does not contradict the civilizational rise and material progress indicated by the remaining ruins and relics of Chaldean Babylon, Pharaonic Egypt, and others. For material civilization is one thing, and progress in spiritual knowledge is another. In the rise of the present world in its civilization and its degradation in ethics and spiritual knowledge, there is that which dismisses this doubt.

From here, the reason becomes clear why he (peace be upon him) did not use in his argument the issue of the entire world's need for the Creator, the Originator (الفاطر) of the heavens and the earth, as he had used for his own insight at the beginning of his affair, as Allah recounts of him: إِنِّي وَجَّهْتُ وَجْهِيَ لِلَّذِي فَطَرَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ حَنِيفًا وَمَا أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ — "Indeed I have turned my face toward Him who originated the heavens and the earth, as a hanif, and I am not one of the polytheists" (Al-An'am: 79). For the people, despite their general acknowledgment of that by their primordial nature (الفطرة), were at a lower level than to conceive it as it ought to be conceived such that his argument would succeed and his intention (peace be upon him) would be clear. Sufficient for you in this regard is what they understood from his saying: "My Lord is He who gives life and makes to die."

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ أَنَا أُحْيِي وَأُمِيتُ (He said, ‘I [too] give life and make to die’) [Qarai 2:258]. Meaning: "Then I am your Lord whom you described as giving life and making to die."

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ يَأْتِي بِالشَّمْسِ مِنَ الْمَشْرِقِ فَأْتِ بِهَا مِنَ الْمَغْرِبِ فَبُهِتَ الَّذِي كَفَرَ (Abraham said, ‘Indeed Allah brings the sun from the east; now you bring it from the west.’ Thereat the faithless one was confounded) [Qarai 2:258]. When he (peace be upon him) despaired of the success of his argument that his Lord is He who gives life and makes to die—due to the opponent's poor understanding and his deception and obfuscation of the matter for those present—he turned from explaining his intention regarding life and death to another argument. However, he built this second argument upon the opponent's claim in the first argument, as indicated by the use of the prefix "fa" (فـ) in his saying: فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ (Indeed Allah...). The meaning is: "If the matter is as you say—that you are my lord, and it is the role of the lord to manage the affairs of this cosmic system—then Allah manages the sun by bringing it from the east; so you manage it by bringing it from the west, so that it becomes clear that you are a lord just as Allah is the Lord of everything, or that you are the Lord above the lords." Thereat the faithless one was confounded (فبهت). He branched the argument upon what preceded it so it would not be thought that the first argument was completed in Nimrod's favor and yielded what he claimed. For this reason also, he said: "Indeed Allah," and did not say: "Indeed my Lord," because the opponent had misused his saying "my Lord" and applied it to himself through fallacy; so he (peace be upon him) used the Majestic Name (لفظة الجلالة) the second time to be protected from such a previous application. It has already been explained that Nimrod could not utter anything in the face of this argument except to be confounded and fall silent.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ (and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people) [Qarai 2:258]. The apparent context is that it is a justification for His saying: "Thereat the faithless one was confounded." His being confounded was due to Allah's not guiding him, not due to his disbelief. In other words, the meaning is that Allah did not guide him, so he was confounded for that reason; had He guided him, he would have overcome Abraham in the argument. It does not mean He did not guide him so he disbelieved for that reason, because the concern in this context is directed toward his argument with Abraham (peace be upon him), not toward his disbelief, and this is apparent.

From here, it appears that the description carries an indication of causality, meaning: the reason for Allah's not guiding the wrongdoers is their wrongdoing (ظلمهم), as is the case in other instances of this sentence in His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying: وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرَىٰ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ وَهُوَ يُدْعَىٰ إِلَى الْإِسْلَامِ وَاللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah while he is being invited to Islam? And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" (As-Saff: 7), and His saying: مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ حُمِّلُوا التَّوْرَاةَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَحْمِلُوهَا كَمَثَلِ الْحِمَارِ يَحْمِلُ أَسْفَارًا بِئْسَ مَثَلُ الْقَوْمِ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ وَاللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "The example of those who were charged with the Torah, then failed to carry it, is that of an ass carrying books. Evil is the example of the people who deny Allah’s signs, and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" (Al-Jumu'ah: 5). Similar to wrongdoing (الظلم) is transgression (الفسق) in His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا زَاغُوا أَزَاغَ اللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ وَاللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفَاسِقِينَ — "So when they swerved, Allah made their hearts swerve, and Allah does not guide the transgressing people" (As-Saff: 5).

In short, wrongdoing (الظلم)—which is deviation from the path of justice and turning away from what is appropriate action to what is inappropriate—necessitates not being guided to the intended goal and leads to failure and loss in the end. This is one of the clear truths mentioned by the Noble Quran and emphasized in many verses.

(A Discourse on Benevolence and its Guidance, and Wrongdoing and its Misguidance)

This is a fixed truth explained by the Holy Quran as we mentioned just now. It is a universal principle that accepts no exception, and He has mentioned it in various ways and built many truths of His knowledge upon it. The Exalted said: الَّذِي أَعْطَىٰ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدَىٰ — "He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (Taha: 50). This indicates that everything, after the completion of its creation, is guided by guidance from Allah, the Glorified, toward the objectives of its existence and the perfections of its essence. This is only through its connection with other things and its benefit from them through action and passion, gathering and separation, connection and disconnection, proximity and distance, taking and leaving, and the like. It is well known that ontological matters (الامور التكوينية) do not err in their effects, and real intentions do not miss or stumble in identifying their ends and objectives. Fire, for instance, in touching wood while it is hot, does not intend to cool it; and a growing thing like a plant, while it is growing, only intends increase in size, not decrease, and so on. The Exalted has said: إِنَّ رَبِّي عَلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ — "Indeed my Lord is on a straight path" (Hud: 56); thus, there is no failure or discrepancy in existence.

The necessity of these two premises—namely, the universality of guidance and the absence of error in creation (التكوين)—is that everything has real links with others, and that between everything and the effects and ends intended for it, there is a specific path or paths which are traversed to reach its end and the specific effect intended from it. Similarly, existential ends and objectives are only attained if they are approached through the specific paths belonging to them and the ways leading to them. A seed only grows the tree that it has the potential to grow when the path leading to it is traversed through its specific causes and conditions. Likewise, a tree only bears the fruit that it is its nature to bear. Not every cause leads to every effect. The Exalted said: وَالْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَبَاتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ وَالَّذِي خَبُثَ لَا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِدًا — "As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the leave of its Lord, and as for that which is bad, it does not come out except scantily" (Al-A'raf: 58). Reason and sense witness to this, otherwise the general law of causality would be disrupted.

If this is so, then fashioning and origination guide everything to a specific end and do not guide it to another; and they guide to every end through a specific path and do not guide to it through another. "The handiwork of Allah who has perfected everything." Every chain of these existential chains leading to an end and an effect, if we assume one of its links is changed, it will inevitably necessitate a change in its effect. This is in ontological matters.

Non-ontological matters, such as social conventions and others, are also of this description insofar as they are results of the primordial nature (الفطرة) which relies on creation (التكوين). Social affairs, the stations within them, and the actions proceeding from them are each linked to effects and ends; only those effects and ends are generated from them, and they are only generated from them. Thus, righteous upbringing (التربية الصالحة) is only realized from a righteous educator, and a corrupt educator's upbringing results in nothing but a corrupt effect (that corruption hidden within himself), even if he feigns righteousness and adheres to the straight path in his upbringing, and covers the corruption folded within himself with a hundred veils and hides behind a thousand curtains. Similarly, the usurping ruler in his rule, the judge who leaps onto the seat of judgment without merit in his judgment, and everyone who assumes a social position through other than its legitimate path. Likewise, every action that is void (باطل) in any way, when it mimics the truth (الحق) and thereby takes the place of the true action; and the false word when it is placed in the position of the true word—like betrayal in the place of trust, mistreatment in the place of benevolence, deception in the place of sincere advice, and lying in the place of truth. All of this will eventually manifest its effect and its root will be cut off, even if its matter is confused for a time and it occasionally wears the garment of truth and sincerity. This is the sunnah (سنة) — the established way or law — of Allah that has proceeded in His creation, and you will never find any change in the sunnah of Allah, nor will you find any deviation in the sunnah of Allah.

For the truth (الحق) does not die and its effect does not falter, even if it is hidden from the perception of perceivers for some moments. And falsehood (الباطل) does not stand and its effect does not remain, even if its matter and its evil consequence are sometimes confused. The Exalted said: لِيُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ وَيُبْطِلَ الْبَاطِلَ — "that He might confirm the truth and bring falsehood to naught" (Al-Anfal: 8). Among the confirmation of the truth is the stabilization of its effect, and among the nullification of falsehood is the manifestation of its corruption and the stripping away of the garment of truth it wore through mimicry and deception. The Exalted said: أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ ضَرَبَ اللَّهُ مَثَلًا كَلِمَةً طَيِّبَةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ أَصْلُهَا ثَابِتٌ وَفَرْعُهَا فِي السَّمَاءِ تُؤْتِي أُكُلَهَا كُلَّ حِينٍ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهَا وَيَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْأَمْثَالَ لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ وَمَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ اجْتُثَّتْ مِن فَوْقِ الْأَرْضِ مَا لَهَا مِن قَرَارٍ يُثَبِّتُ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ وَيُضِلُّ اللَّهُ الظَّالِمِينَ وَيَفْعَلُ اللَّهُ مَا يَشَاءُ — "Have you not regarded how Allah draws a parable? A good word is like a good tree: its roots are steady and its branches are in the sky. It gives its fruit every season by the leave of its Lord. Allah draws parables for mankind so that they may take admonition. And the parable of a bad word is that of a bad tree: uprooted from the ground, it has no stability. Allah fortifies those who have faith with a steady word in the life of this world and in the Hereafter, and Allah leads the wrongdoers astray, and Allah does what He wills" (Ibrahim: 24-27). He mentioned "the wrongdoers" (الظالمين) generally, so Allah misguides them in their affairs; and they have no affair except that they want the effects of truth through other than its path—meaning, through the path of falsehood. As the Exalted said—recounting Joseph the Truthful: قَالَ مَعَاذَ اللَّهِ إِنَّهُ رَبِّي أَحْسَنَ مَثْوَايَ إِنَّهُ لَا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ — "He said, ‘I seek Allah’s refuge! He is indeed my lord; He has given me a good abode. Indeed the wrongdoers are not felicitous’" (Yusuf: 23). So the wrongdoer does not succeed in his wrongdoing, nor does his wrongdoing guide him to that which the benevolent is guided to by his benevolence and the God-conscious by his godwariness. The Exalted said: وَالَّذِينَ جَاهَدُوا فِينَا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنَا وَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لَمَعَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ — "As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them in Our ways, and Allah is indeed with the virtuous" (Al-Ankabut: 69). And the Exalted said: وَالْعَاقِبَةُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ — "and the outcome will be in favour of Godwariness" (Taha: 132).

The Quranic verses on these meanings are many, despite their varying specific contents. Among the most comprehensive and complete in explanation is His saying, the Exalted: أَنزَلَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً فَسَالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِهَا فَاحْتَمَلَ السَّيْلُ زَبَدًا رَّابِيًا وَمِمَّا يُوقِدُونَ عَلَيْهِ فِي النَّارِ ابْتِغَاءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتَاعٍ زَبَدٌ مِّثْلُهُ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْحَقَّ وَالْبَاطِلَ فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفَاءً وَأَمَّا مَا يَنفَعُ النَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْأَمْثَالَ — "He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to their capacity, and the flood carries a swelling scum. And from what they smelt in the fire for making ornaments or housewares, there is a similar scum. That is how Allah draws the parable of truth and falsehood. As for the scum, it leaves as dross, and as for what profits the people, it remains in the earth. That is how Allah draws the parables" (Ar-Ra'd: 17).

It has already been alluded to that reason supports this, for it is a necessity of the universality of the law of cause and effect (العلية والمعلولية) proceeding among the parts of the world. Definitive experience resulting from repeated sensory perception also witnesses to it; for there is none among us but that in his memory are preserved reports of the end of the wrongdoers' affair and the cutting off of their roots.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ كَالَّذِي مَرَّ عَلَىٰ قَرْيَةٍ وَهِيَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا (Or him who carried past a town that had fallen on its trellises) [Qarai 2:259]. Khawiyah (الخاوية) means empty; it is said: "the house became empty" (خوت الدار) when it is vacated. ‘Urush (العروش) is the plural of ‘arsh (العرش), which is what is made like a ceiling for a vineyard, standing on pillars. The Exalted said: جَنَّاتٍ مَّعْرُوشَاتٍ وَغَيْرَ مَعْرُوشَاتٍ — "gardens, trellised and untrellised" (Al-An'am: 141). From here, the ceiling of a house was called ‘arsh, but there is a difference between them: the ceiling (السقف) is what stands as the roof upon the walls, while the ‘arsh is the ceiling along with the pillars upon which it relies, like the form of a vineyard trellis. Therefore, it is correct to say of houses that they are "empty upon their trellises" (خالية على عروشها), but it is not correct to say "empty upon their ceiling."

The commentators have mentioned several views regarding the justification of the conjunction in His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ كَالَّذِي (Or him who...). It was said: it is conjoined to His saying in the previous verse: "him who argued with Abraham," and the kaf is a noun, meaning: "Or have you seen the like of him who passed by a town..." This kaf was brought to draw attention to the multiplicity of witnesses. It was also said: rather, the kaf is redundant, and the meaning is: "Have you not regarded him who argued with Abraham or him who passed by a town..." It was also said: it is a conjunction based on the meaning, and the meaning is: "Have you not regarded [one] like him who argued with Abraham or like him who passed by a town." It was also said: it is from the speech of Abraham in response to the opponent's claim that he gives life and makes to die, and the estimation is: "And if you give life, then give life like the giving of life to him who passed by a town..." These are views they mentioned regarding the verse to justify the conjunction, but all of them are as you see.

I think—and Allah knows best—that the conjunction is based on the meaning as in the third view, but the estimation is different. To clarify: when Allah, the Glorified, mentioned His saying: "Allah is the Wali of those who believe; He brings them out of darkness into light. And those who disbelieve, their walis are the Taghut; they bring them out of light into darkness," it resulted from this that He guides the believers to the truth and does not guide the disbeliever in his disbelief, but rather his walis whom he took besides Allah misguide him. Then He mentioned for that three witnesses by which He explains the types of His guidance, which are three sequential ranks:

The first: Guidance to the truth through proof (البرهان) and deduction (الاستدلال), as in the story of him who argued with Abraham regarding his Lord, where He guided Abraham to the truth of the word, and did not guide the one who argued with him, but rather confounded him, and his disbelief misguided him. He did not explicitly mention Abraham's guidance but placed the core of the speech on the matter of his opponent to indicate a new benefit shown by His saying: "And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people."

The second: Guidance to the truth through demonstration (الارائة) and witnessing (الاشهاد), as in the story of him who passed by a town that had fallen on its trellises; for He explained to him what was problematic for him regarding the matter of giving life by making him die and then giving him life, and the rest of what He mentioned in the verse—all of that through demonstration and witnessing.

The third: Guidance to the truth and explanation of the event by witnessing the reality and the cause from which the event emanates; in other words, by demonstrating the cause and the effect together. This is the strongest rank of guidance and explanation, the highest and most sublime. Just as one who has never seen cheese, for example, and doubts its nature, his doubt is removed sometimes by the testimony of one who has seen it, eaten it, and tasted its flavor; sometimes by showing him a piece of cheese and letting him taste its flavor; and sometimes by bringing milk and rennet extract and mixing a portion of it with it until it solidifies, then letting him taste some of it—and this is the most effective rank for removing doubt.

If you understand what we have mentioned, you will know that the context in the three verses—which is the context of witnessing—allows for all three modes of delivering the intended meaning to the addressee: by saying that Allah guides the believers to the truth: "Have you not regarded the story of Abraham and Nimrod," or "Have you not regarded the story of him who passed by a town," or "Have you not regarded the story of Abraham and the birds." Or it is said: Allah guides the believers to the truth: either as He guided Abraham in the story of the argument, which is one type of guidance; or like him who passed by a town, which is another type; or as in the story of Abraham and the birds, which is yet another type. Or it is said: Allah guides the believers to the truth, and I remind you of what witnesses to that: so remember the story of the argument, and remember him who passed by a town, and remember when Abraham said, "My Lord, show me."

This is what the three verses accept in terms of context according to the situation, except that Allah, the Glorified, adopted variation (التفنن) in the explanation and specified each of the three verses with one of the three contexts to stimulate the mind of the addressee and to fulfill all possible contextual benefits.

From here, it appears that His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ كَالَّذِي (Or him who...) is conjoined to an estimated [phrase] indicated by the previous verse, and the estimation is: "Either like him who argued with Abraham or like him who passed by a town." It also appears that His saying in the following verse: وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَاهِيمُ (And when Abraham said...) is conjoined to an estimated [phrase] indicated by the previous verse, and the estimation is: "Remember the story of the argument and the story of him who passed by a town, and remember when Abraham said, ‘My Lord, show me...’"

Allah, the Glorified, has kept vague the name of this person who passed by the town, the name of the town, the people who used to inhabit it, and the people for whom this passerby was sent as a sign, as indicated by His saying: "and that We may make you a sign for mankind." This is despite the fact that in the context of witnessing, it would be more appropriate to mention their names to better remove doubt.

However, the sign (الآية)—which is life after death—as well as the matter of guidance through this type of fashioning, since it was a momentous matter and had occurred in a position of being deemed far-fetched and immense, the requirement of eloquence (البلاغة) was that the Wise and Powerful Speaker express it in a tone of disparagement and belittlement to break the force of the addressee's and listeners' deeming it far-fetched. Just as great men speak of great men and great matters with diminutives and belittlement to magnify their own station. For this reason, many aspects of the story were kept vague in the verse—those which do not constitute its essence—to indicate the ease of its matter for Allah. For this reason also, Abraham's opponent was kept vague in the previous verse, and aspects of the story like the names of the birds, the names of the mountains, the number of parts, and others were kept vague in the subsequent verse.

As for the explicit mention of Abraham's (peace be upon him) name, the Quran has a concern for honoring him (peace be upon him). The Exalted said: وَتِلْكَ حُجَّتُنَا آتَيْنَاهَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِ — "This was Our argument, which We gave to Abraham against his people" (Al-An'am: 83). And the Exalted said: وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُرِي إِبْرَاهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ — "Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who have certitude" (Al-An'am: 75). So in mentioning him (peace be upon him) by name, there is a special concern.

Because of the subtle point we mentioned, you see that He, the Exalted, mentions the matter of giving life and making to die in most instances of His speech in a way that is not devoid of disparagement and belittlement. The Exalted said: وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَبْدَأُ الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ وَهُوَ أَهْوَنُ عَلَيْهِ وَلَهُ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلَىٰ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ — "It is He who originates the creation, and then He will bring it back—and that is more simple for Him. To Him belongs the highest metaphor in the heavens and the earth, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise" (Ar-Rum: 27). And the Exalted said: "He said, ‘My Lord, how shall I have a son...’" — until His saying — قَالَ رَبُّكَ هُوَ عَلَيَّ هَيِّنٌ وَقَدْ خَلَقْتُكَ مِن قَبْلُ وَلَمْ تَكُ شَيْئًا — "He said, ‘So shall it be. Your Lord says, ‘‘It is simple for Me, and certainly I created you before when you were nothing’’’’" (Maryam: 9).

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ أَنَّىٰ يُحْيِي هَٰذِهِ اللَّهُ (He said, ‘How will Allah revive this [town]...’) [Qarai 2:259]. Meaning: "How will Allah revive the people of this town?" So there is a metaphor (مجاز) here, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَاسْأَلِ الْقَرْيَةَ — "and ask the town" (Yusuf: 82).

He only said this out of magnifying the matter and the power of Allah, the Glorified, without any deeming it far-fetched that leads to denial or originates from it. The evidence for this is his saying, as Allah, the Exalted, recounts of him at the end of the story: "I know that Allah has power over all things," and he did not say: "Now [I know]," as in the similar instance of His saying, the Exalted, recounting the wife of the Governor: الْآنَ حَصْحَصَ الْحَقُّ — "Now the truth has become known" (Yusuf: 51). The clarification of this will follow shortly.

Furthermore, the man was a Prophet addressed by revelation and a sign sent to the people; and the Prophets are infallible (معصومون), far be it from them to have doubt or hesitation regarding the Resurrection (البعث), which is one of the foundations of religion.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَمَاتَهُ اللَّهُ مِائَةَ عَامٍ ثُمَّ بَعَثَهُ (So Allah made him die for a hundred years, then He revived him) [Qarai 2:259]. Its apparent meaning is taking his soul (توفيه) by seizing it and keeping him in this state for a hundred years, then reviving him by returning his soul to him.

Some commentators have mentioned that the intended "death" is the state called "coma" (السبات) by physicians, which is when a living being loses sense and consciousness while the root of life remains for a period of time—days, months, or years. This is what is apparent from the story of the People of the Cave and their sleep for three hundred and nine years, then their being raised from that sleep, and His, the Exalted, arguing with it for the Resurrection; so the story resembles the story.

He said: that which has been found of its occurrences does not exceed a few years, so a coma of a hundred years is an unfamiliar matter and a breaking of the norm (خارق للعادة). But He who is capable of taking a person in a coma for a period like several years is capable of casting a coma for a hundred years. We do not stipulate for submission to what has been mutawatirly reported in the text of Allah's signs and taking them at their apparent meaning except that they be among the possibilities, not the impossibilities. Allah argued with this coma and the return of sense and consciousness to him a second time after its removal for a hundred years for the possibility of life returning to the dead after its removal from them for thousands of years. This is the summary of what he mentioned.

I wonder how it can be correct to judge the death mentioned in the verse as being of the type of coma, based on the story of the People of the Cave being of the type of coma—assuming it is conceded—merely due to the resemblance between the two stories, despite the clarity of His saying, the Exalted: فَأَمَاتَهُ اللَّهُ (So Allah made him die) in the sense of the well-known death, not the coma he invented for the verse? Is this anything but analogy (قياس) in a matter where no one has permitted analogy—namely, the matter of linguistic indication (الدلالة)? If it is permissible for Allah to cast a coma upon a man for a hundred years despite it being a breaking of the norm, then let it be permissible for Him to make him die for a hundred years and then revive him. For there is no difference for Him, the Exalted, between one miracle and another, except that this speaker considers the revival of the dead in this world impossible without any evidence indicating it. For this reason, he also interpreted His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verse: "and look at the bones, how We muster them and then clothe them with flesh," and we shall address this.

In short, the indication of His saying, the Exalted: فَأَمَاتَهُ اللَّهُ مِائَةَ عَامٍ (So Allah made him die for a hundred years), in terms of the literal appearance of the word and in view of His saying before it: "How will Allah revive this [town]," and His saying after it: "but look at your food and drink—they have not spoiled; and look at your ass," and His saying: "and look at the bones," is something about which there is no doubt.

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ كَمْ لَبِثْتَ قَالَ لَبِثْتُ يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ قَالَ بَل لَّبِثْتَ مِائَةَ عَامٍ (He said, ‘How long have you remained?’ He said, ‘I have remained a day or part of a day.’ He said, ‘No, you have remained a hundred years’) [Qarai 2:259]. Labth (اللبث) is staying. The hesitation of the answer between a day and part of a day indicates the difference in the time of his death and his revival, such as the beginning of the day and its end. Thus, he reckoned the death and life as sleep and waking, then he witnessed the difference in their two times, so he hesitated whether a night had intervened between the two times or not. He said "a day" (if the night had intervened) "or part of a day" (if it had not). He said: "No, you have remained a hundred years."

His saying, the Exalted: فَانظُرْ إِلَىٰ طَعَامِكَ وَشَرَابِكَ لَمْ يَتَسَنَّهْ (but look at your food and drink—they have not spoiled) — until His saying — لَحْمًا (flesh) [Qarai 2:259]. The context of these sentences in his affair is wondrous; for He repeated in them His saying: "look" (انظر) three times, whereas it seemed apparent that one would suffice. He mentioned the matter of the food, the drink, and the ass, whereas the first thought to come to mind is that there was no need to mention them. His saying: "and that We may make you [a sign]" was brought as an interpolation in the speech, whereas it seemed apparent that it should follow the sentence: "and look at the bones." Furthermore, the explanation of what this passerby by the town deemed immense—namely, the revival of the dead after a long duration and the occurrence of every change upon them—had already been achieved by his own revival after death, so what necessitated his being ordered a second time to look at the bones? However, reflection on the aspects of the noble verse clarifies the specifics of the story in a way that resolves the knot and clears the aforementioned doubt.

(The Story)

Reflection on the verse yields that the man was among the righteous servants of Allah, knowledgeable of his Lord's station, observant of His command, and indeed a Prophet addressed by revelation. For the apparent meaning of his saying: "I know that Allah..." is that after the matter was clarified to him, he returned to what he had always known of Allah's absolute power. The apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: "then He revived him; He said, ‘How long have you remained?’" is that he was accustomed to revelation and address, and that this was not the first revelation revealed to him; otherwise, the proper speech would have been: "So when He revived him, He said..." or its like, such as His saying, the Exalted, regarding Moses (peace be upon him): فَلَمَّا أَتَاهَا نُودِيَ يَا مُوسَىٰ إِنِّي أَنَا رَبُّكَ — "When he came to it, he was called: ‘O Moses! Indeed I am your Lord’" (Taha: 11-12), and His saying, the Exalted, regarding him also: فَلَمَّا أَتَاهَا نُودِيَ مِن شَاطِئِ الْوَادِ الْأَيْمَنِ — "When he came to it, he was called from the right bank of the valley" (Al-Qasas: 30).

In any case, he (peace be upon him) had set out from his house intending a place far from his town where he resided. The evidence for this is his setting out with an ass he was riding, and his carrying food and drink to nourish himself with. When he traveled to what he intended, he passed by the town which Allah, the Exalted, mentioned was "fallen on its trellises." He did not intend the town itself, but rather passed by it, then stopped to take a lesson from what he witnessed of the ruined town whose people had been destroyed, overtaken by the calamity of death, their decayed bones within his sight and view (peace be upon him). For he refers to the dead by his saying: "How will Allah revive this [town]," and had his intention been the reconstruction of the town itself after its ruin and the reference to the town itself, the proper speech would have been: "How will Allah reconstruct this." Furthermore, a ruined town is not expected to be reconstructed after its ruin, nor is its reconstruction after ruin something usually deemed immense. Had the dead referred to been buried and he had taken a lesson from their graves, it would have been necessary to mention it and turn away from mentioning the town itself, as befits the most eloquent speech.

Then he deepened his reflection, and he was awestruck by what he witnessed of it. He deemed immense the length of its duration along with what accompanies it of transformation from state to state and evolution from form to form, such that the original becomes a thing forgotten. At that point, he said: "How will Allah revive this [town]." This speech resolved into two aspects: the first: deeming immense the length of the duration and the revival despite that; and the second: deeming immense the return of the parts to their first vanished form after the occurrence of these innumerable changes. So Allah explained the matter to him from both aspects together: as for the first aspect, by making him die then reviving him and questioning him; and as for the second aspect, by reviving the bones within his sight and view.

So Allah made him die for a hundred years then revived him. The death and revival were at two different times of the day as mentioned. He said: "How long have you remained?" He said: "I have remained a day or part of a day," looking at the difference between the two times. His death had been in the earlier part of the day and his revival in the later part of it; had it been the opposite, he would have said: "I have remained a day," without hesitation. So Allah, the Glorified, replied to him and said: "No, you have remained a hundred years." Thus he saw from himself that he witnessed a hundred years as a day or part of a day; so in it was what he had deemed immense regarding the length of the stay.

Then He, the Exalted, sought evidence for His saying: "No, you have remained a hundred years," by His saying: "but look at your food and drink—they have not spoiled; and look at your ass!" This is because his saying: "I have remained a day or part of a day" indicates that he did not perceive anything of the length or shortness of the duration; he only deduced what he mentioned from what he witnessed of the state of the day, such as sun or shade and the like. When he was answered with His saying, the Exalted: "No, you have remained a hundred years," the answer was in a position where he might doubt it from the aspect of what he witnessed of himself, as nothing of his body's form had changed. When a person dies and a hundred years pass over him, his body inevitably changes from its freshness and tenderness and becomes dust and decayed bones. So Allah, the Exalted, repelled that which could occur to his mind by ordering him to look at his food and drink—that nothing of them had changed from what they were—and to look at the ass, which had become decayed bones. Thus the state of the ass indicates the length of the stay, and the state of the food and drink indicates the possibility of remaining for this long duration in one state without anything of its form changing.

From here, it appears that the ass also had been made to die and was decayed; it is as if the silence regarding the mention of its being made to die along with him was due to the exquisite etiquette (الأدب البارع) of the Quran.

In summary, the divine explanation was completed at that point: that his regarding the duration as immense was misplaced, as Allah extracted from him the admission that a hundred years—the period of his stay—was like a day or part of a day, just as He extracts the admission of the people of the Gathering on the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) regarding the like of what he admitted. Thus, He clarified to him that the intervention of time between death and revival, whether long or short, does not affect His power (قدرته), which governs all things. For His is not a material, temporal power (قدرة مادية زمانية) such that its state would vary by the occurrence of fewer or more changes in the subject; thus, reviving those long dead would be more difficult for Him than reviving the recently deceased. Rather, the distant is to Him like the near without distinction, as the Exalted said: إِنَّهُمْ يَرَوْنَهُ بَعِيدًا وَنَرَاهُ قَرِيبًا — "Indeed they see it to be far off, and We see it to be near" (70:7), and the Exalted said: وَمَا أَمْرُ السَّاعَةِ إِلَّا كَلَمْحِ الْبَصَرِ — "The matter of the Hour is just like the twinkling of an eye" (16:77).

Then the Exalted said: "and that We may make you a sign for mankind." The conjunction of the purpose (عطف الغاية) indicates that there are other purposes besides it. The meaning is: We did to you what We did to clarify such-and-half to you and that We may make you a sign (آية) for mankind. Thus, He clarified that the divine purpose in this was not restricted to clarifying the matter to him personally, but there was another goal, which was making him a sign for the people. Therefore, the purpose of His saying: "And look at the bones..." etc., was to clarify the matter to him only, while the purpose of his death and revival was both to clarify the matter to him and to make him a sign for the people. For this reason, He prioritized His saying: "and that We may make you..." over His saying: "And look at the bones..."

From what we have explained, the reason for the repetition of His saying "Look" (انظر) three times in the verse becomes apparent; for each of the three instances has a specific purpose unique to it.

In his death and revival, there was a clarification for him of what the deceased experiences within himself when Allah revives him for the Day of Resurrection (يوم البعث), as the Exalted said: وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يُقْسِمُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ مَا لَبِثُوا غَيْرَ سَاعَةٍ كَذَٰلِكَ كَانُوا يُؤْفَكُونَ وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ وَالْإِيمَانَ لَقَدْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الْبَعْثِ فَهَٰذَا يَوْمُ الْبَعْثِ وَلَٰكِنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ — "And on the day when the Hour sets in, the guilty will swear that they did not tarry but for an hour: thus were they used to being deluded. But those who were given knowledge and faith will say, ‘Certainly you have tarried in Allah’s Book until the Day of Resurrection. So this is the Day of Resurrection, but you did not know’" (30:55-56).

Then Allah clarified to him the second aspect contained in his saying: "How will Allah revive this...?" which is: how do the parts return to their form after all these changes and transformations that have occurred to them? He drew his attention to the bones, saying: "And look at the bones, how We raise them (ننشزها)." Raising (الإنشاز) means growth (الإنماء). The apparent meaning of the verse is that the bones intended are the bones of the donkey, for if they were the bones of the people of the town, the sign would not have been restricted to him—as is apparent from His saying: "and that We may make you a sign"—but rather the deceased whom Allah revived would have shared in it!

It is strange what some commentators have mentioned: that the bones intended are the bones within living bodies, for in their growth and being clothed with flesh, they are among the signs of the Resurrection; for He who gave them maturity and growth through life is the Reviver of the dead, and He has power over all things. They argue that Allah has used similar things as evidence for the Resurrection, such as the dead earth which Allah revives through vegetation. This, as you can see, is an unnecessary affectation (تكلف).

It has become clear from all that has passed that everything the verse contains, from His saying: "So Allah caused him to die..." until the end of the verse, is a single, non-repetitive answer to his saying: "How will Allah revive this...?"

His saying, the Exalted: "So when it became clear to him, he said, 'I know that Allah has power over all things.'" This is a return on his part, after the clarification, to the knowledge he possessed before the clarification. It is as if he (peace be upon him), when the thought mentioned in his saying "How will Allah revive this...?" occurred to his mind, he satisfied himself with the knowledge he had of absolute power (القدرة المطلقة). Then, when Allah clarified the matter to him through witnessing (إشهاد) and direct vision (عيان), he returned to his soul and affirmed the knowledge he had relied upon, saying: "You have always advised me and never betrayed me in your guidance and rectification; and that which my soul continues to rely upon—that power is absolute—is not ignorance, but a knowledge worthy of being relied upon."

This is a matter with many parallels; often a person has knowledge of something, then a thought occurs to him and stirs in his soul that contradicts it—not due to doubt or the invalidity of knowledge, but due to other causes and factors. So he satisfies himself until the ambiguity is removed, then he returns and says: "I know that such-and-half is so, and not otherwise," thereby affirming his knowledge and putting his soul at ease!

The meaning of the speech is not that when the matter became clear to him, knowledge was produced for him while he had been a doubter before that, such that he said "I know..." as previously alluded to. This is because the man was a prophet (نبي) spoken to by Allah, and the station of the prophets is transcendent above ignorance of Allah, especially regarding an attribute like power (القدرة), which is among the attributes of the Essence (صفات الذات) firstly; and because the proper phrasing then would have been "I have learned" (علمت) or its equivalent secondly; and because the attainment of knowledge regarding the connection of power to reviving the dead does not necessitate the attainment of knowledge regarding its connection to everything, yet he said: "I know that Allah has power over all things." Yes, an intuition (الحدس) might occur in some souls, like one who deems the matter of revival as immense for power, so when he witnesses what he witnessed, and his soul becomes oblivious to all other matters, he judges that He who revives the dead is capable of everything He wills or is requested of Him. However, this is an intuitive belief (اعتقاد حدسي) caused by the aforementioned psychological awe and magnification, which vanishes when they vanish and is not found in one who did not witness that. In any case, it does not deserve to be depended upon or relied upon, and far be it from the Divine Speech to consider such a belief and statement as a good and praised result of a divine clarification, as is apparent from the Exalted's saying after narrating the story: "So when it became clear to him, he said: I know that Allah has power over all things." Furthermore, it would be an error in speech not befitting the station of the prophets thirdly.

His saying, the Exalted: "And when Abraham said, 'My Lord! Show me how You revive the dead.'" It has already passed that this is conjoined to something omitted, the estimation being: "And mention when..." etc., and it is the operator (العامل) for the adverbial particle (الظرف). Some have entertained the possibility that the operator for the adverb is His saying: "He said, 'Do you not believe?'" and the arrangement of the speech being: "Do you not believe when Abraham said, 'My Lord! Show me...'" but this is of no consequence.

In His saying: "Show me how You revive the dead," there is an indication:

Firstly: That he (peace be upon him) only asked for vision (الرؤية) rather than a demonstrative explanation (البيان الاستدلالي). For the prophets, and especially a majestic prophet like Abraham the Friend (إبراهيم الخليل), are of a status too high to believe in the Resurrection without having a proof for it. Theoretical belief (الاعتقاد النظري) without proof is either a conformist belief (اعتقاد تقليدي) or one arising from intellectual deficiency, and neither applies to Abraham (peace be upon him). Furthermore, he (peace be upon him) only asked what he asked using the word "how" (كيف), and "how" is only used to inquire about the modality of a thing's existence (خصوصية وجود الشيء), not about the origin of its existence. For if you say: "Did you see Zayd?" it is an inquiry into the realization of the act of seeing itself. But if you say: "How did you see Zayd?" the origin of the seeing is taken for granted, and the inquiry is only about the circumstances of the seeing. Thus, it becomes clear that he (peace be upon him) only asked for clarification through showing and witnessing (الإرائة والاشهاد), not through argumentation and demonstration (الاحتجاج والاستدلال).

Secondly: That Abraham (peace be upon him) only asked to witness the modality of revival (كيفية الاحياء), not the fact of revival itself, as is apparent from his saying: "how You revive the dead." This question can be conceived in two ways:

The first way: That it be a question about the modality of the material parts' acceptance of life, their gathering after dispersal and scattering, and their taking the form of a living being. The sum of this returns to the connection of power to revival after death and annihilation.

The second way: That it be about the modality of Allah's bestowing life upon the dead and His action upon their parts by which they are clothed in life. The sum of this returns to a question about the cause (السبب) and the modality of its influence. This is, in a sense, what Allah the Glorified calls the "dominion of things" (ملكوت الاشياء) in the saying of the Mighty Speaker: إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَن يَقُولَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ فَسُبْحَانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is. So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things" (36:82-83).

Abraham (peace be upon him) only asked about the modality in the second sense, not the first. Firstly: Because he said: "how You revive (تُحيي) the dead," with the damma on the ta (from ihya'), thus asking about the modality of the act of reviving, which is an attribute describing Allah the Exalted and is the cause of the living being's life by His command. He did not say: "how the dead come to life (تَحيى)" with the fatha on the ta (from hayat), which would have been a question about the modality of the parts gathering, returning to their first form, and accepting life. Had the question been about the modality in the second sense, it would have necessarily followed the second form. Secondly: Because if his question had been about the modality of the parts' acceptance of life, there would have been no reason for the matter to be carried out by Abraham's hand; it would have sufficed for Allah to will the revival of some animal after its death. Thirdly: Because it would then have been necessary to conclude the speech with something like: "And know that Allah has power over all things," not with His saying: "And know that Allah is All-mighty, All-wise," according to the established custom of the Noble Quran. For what is appropriate to the aforementioned question is the attribute of power (القدرة), not the attributes of might (العزة) and wisdom (الحكمة). For might and wisdom—which are the Essence's possession of all that things lack and deserve, and His perfecting of His command—are related to the bestowal of life, not the reception of it by matter. Understand this well.

From what we have mentioned, the invalidity of what some commentators have stated becomes apparent: that Abraham (peace be upon him) only asked by his saying "My Lord, show me" for the attainment of knowledge regarding the modality of how revival occurs, not for the witnessing of the modality of revival itself, and that what he was answered with in the verse does not indicate more than that. He said, in summary: that there is nothing in the speech indicating that Allah the Glorified commanded him to revive, nor that Abraham (peace be upon him) did what He commanded him to do; for not every command is intended for compliance. For a report (الخبر) may come in the form of an imperative (الإنشاء), as when a questioner asks you: "How is ink made, for example?" and you say: "Take such-and-half and do such-and-half with it, and it becomes ink," intending that this is its modality, not intending to command him to actually manufacture ink.

He said: In the Quran, there is much that has come as a report in the form of a command, and the speech here is a parable for the revival of the dead. Its meaning is: Take four birds, draw them to you and domesticate them until they become familiar and such that they answer your call when you call them—for birds are among the animals most disposed to that—then place each one of them on a mountain, then call them, and they will hasten to you despite the dispersal and distance of their locations. Likewise is the command of your Lord: when He wills the revival of the dead, He calls them with the word of creation (كلمة التكوين): "Be alive," and they become alive, just as was His state at the beginning of creation; that was when He said to the heavens and the earth: "Come, willingly or unwillingly," and they said: "We come willingly."

He said: The evidence for this from the verse is His saying: "and incline them (فصرهن)," for its meaning is "make them lean," i.e., create their inclination toward you and their familiarity with you. This is attested by its being made transitive with "to" (إلى), for sara when made transitive with ila means "to incline." As for what the commentators mentioned regarding it meaning "to cut," i.e., cut them into pieces after slaughtering, its being made transitive with ila does not support this. As for what was said: that His saying "to you" (إليك) is connected to His saying "take" (فخذ) rather than His saying "incline them" (فصرهن), and the meaning is: "Take to yourself four birds and cut them," this is contrary to the apparent meaning of the speech.

Secondly: That it is apparent that the pronouns in "incline them" (فصرهن), "from them" (منهن), "call them" (ادعهن), and "they will come to you" (يأتينك) all refer to the birds. It follows from their view—that the intention is cutting them, dispersing their parts, placing each part on a mountain, then calling them—that a distinction be made between the pronouns, such that the first two refer to the birds, and the third and fourth to the parts, which is contrary to the apparent meaning.

Some who agreed with him on the meaning of the verse added other points, which we follow here:

Thirdly: That showing the modality of creation, if it means witnessing the modality of the parts gathering and their forms changing back to the first living form, is something that would not be achieved according to what they mentioned of his cutting the parts, mixing them, and placing them on a distant mountain—a portion of them—so how can the witnessing of the various movements and diverse changes occurring to the atoms of the parts be conceived in this way? And if the intention was showing the modality of revival in the sense of encompassing the essence of the word of creation (كلمة التكوين)—which is the Divine Will (الإرادة الإلهية) connected to the existence of a thing and the reality of its speaking to things—then the apparent meaning of the Quran, and what the Muslims are upon, is that this is impossible for a human being, for the attributes of Allah are transcendent above modality (الكيفية).

Fourthly: That His saying: "then place" (ثم اجعل) indicates a delay (التراخي), which is appropriate for the meaning of domestication, as is His saying: "and incline them" (فصرهن), unlike what they mentioned of the meaning of slaughtering and cutting.

Fifthly: That if it were as they say, it would have been more appropriate to conclude the verse with the name "the All-powerful" (القدير) rather than the two names: "the All-mighty, the All-wise" (العزيز الحكيم), for the All-mighty is the Victorious who cannot be reached. This is what they mentioned.

By reflecting on the explanation we have provided, you will recognize the collapse of what they mentioned. For the verse's inclusion of the request with the word "Show me" and His saying "how You revive," and the execution of the matter by Abraham's hand as previously explained—all of this contradicts this meaning. Furthermore, the "part" (الجزء) in the Exalted's saying: "then place on every mountain a part (جزئاً) of them" apparently means a part of the bird, not one of the birds.

As for the points they mentioned, the answer to the first is: that the meaning of surhunna is "cut them," and its being made transitive with ila is due to its implying the meaning of "inclining," as in the Exalted's saying: الرَّفَثُ إِلَىٰ نِسَائِكُمْ — "go into your women" (2:187), where it implies the meaning of "reaching" (الإفضاء).

To the second: that all four pronouns refer to the birds. The reason for the pronouns in "call them" and "they will come to you" referring to them while they do not exist in their parts and forms, but rather exist in their parts only, is the same reason for the pronoun referring to the heaven despite its non-existence except in its matter in the Exalted's saying: ثُمَّ اسْتَوَىٰ إِلَى السَّمَاءِ وَهِيَ دُخَانٌ فَقَالَ لَهَا وَلِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيَا طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا قَالَتَا أَتَيْنَا طَائِعِينَ — "Then He settled to the heaven when it was smoke, and said to it and to the earth, ‘Come! willingly or unwillingly.’ They said, ‘We come willingly’" (41:11), and the Exalted's saying: إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَن يَقُولَ لَهُ كُن — "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be’" (36:82). The reality of the matter is that verbal address (الخطاب اللفظي) is a branch of the addressee's existence before the address, whereas in the creative address (الخطاب التكويني), the matter is the opposite: the addressee is a branch of the address. For the address in it is the act of bringing into existence (الإيجاد), and it is known that existence (الوجود) is a branch of the act of bringing into existence, as indicated by the Exalted's saying: "that We say to it 'Be,' and it is." The saying "and it is" (فيكون) is an indication of the thing's existence which branches from His saying "Be" (كن), which is the command of address.

To the third: We choose the second alternative, that the question is about the modality of Allah the Glorified's action and His reviving, not about the modality of matter's reception and its life. As for his saying that a human cannot reach the essence of the Divine Will—which is among His attributes—as the apparent meaning of the Quran indicates and the Muslims are upon:

We say: Will (الإرادة) is among the attributes of action (صفات الفعل) abstracted from it, like creation (الخلق) and revival (الاحياء) and the like. That which there is no path to is the Transcendent Essence (الذات المتعالية), as the Exalted said: وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِهِ عِلْمًا — "and they do not comprehend Him in [their] knowledge" (20:110).

Thus, Will is abstracted from the action, which is the act of bringing into existence (الإيجاد) that is identical with the existence of the thing, and it is the word "Be" (كن) in the Exalted's saying: "that We say to it 'Be,' and it is." Allah has mentioned in the following verse that this word—the word "Be"—is the dominion (ملكوت) of everything, when He said: "So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things." Allah the Exalted has mentioned that He showed Abraham the dominion of His creation when He said: وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُرِي إِبْرَاهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ — "Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certitude" (6:75). Among the dominion is the revival of the birds mentioned in the verse.

The source of this ambiguity and its likes among these researchers is their thinking that Abraham's (peace be upon him) calling of the birds for their revival, and Jesus's (peace be upon him) saying to a dead person during his revival: "Rise by the permission of Allah," and the wind's flowing by Solomon's command, and other things contained in the Book and the Sunnah, are only due to an effect Allah placed in their words composed of the letters of the alphabet, or in their imaginative perception indicated by their words—similar to the relationship between our ordinary words and their meanings. It has been hidden from them that this is only due to an inner connection (اتصال باطني) with a divine power (قوة الهية) that is not overcome and an infinite power (قدرة غير متناهية) which is the true effective agent.

To the fourth: The delay (التراخي) indicated by the word "then" (ثم), just as it suits the meaning of nurturing and domestication as they mentioned, also suits the meaning of cutting, dispersing the parts, and placing them on the mountains, as is apparent.

To the fifth: The problem is reversed upon them. For what they mentioned is that Allah only clarified the modality of revival to Abraham through theoretical, scientific explanation (البيان العلمي النظري) rather than through witnessing (الشهودي). Thus, the objection returns to them that it would then have been appropriate to conclude the verse with the attribute of power (القدرة) rather than the attributes of might and wisdom. You have already learned from what we presented that the most appropriate, according to the meaning of the verse we explained, is the conclusion with the two names: the All-mighty, the All-wise, as in the verse.

From what we have mentioned, the invalidity of what some other commentators have stated also becomes apparent: that the intention of the question in the verse is only the request for witnessing the modality of revival in the sense of how the parts accept the form of life.

He said, in summary: that the question was not in a religious matter—Allah forbid—but it was a question about the modality of revival to encompass knowledge of it; and encompassing the form of the modality of revival is not a condition for faith (الايمان). Thus, Abraham (peace be upon him) requested a knowledge upon which faith does not depend. This is indicated by the question coming in the form of "how" (كيف), and its subject is the inquiry about the state (الحال). A parallel to this is when one says: "How does Zayd judge among the people?" for he does not doubt that he judges among them, but he asks about the modality of his judgment whose occurrence is known. Had he been asking about the occurrence of that, he would have said: "Does Zayd judge among the people?" The confirmation—namely His saying: "Do you not believe?"—only came after it because that form, although used apparently to ask about modality as you have learned, may also be used to express the seeking of proof of inability (الاستعجاز), as when a claimant claims that he can carry a certain weight and you know his inability to carry it, so you say to him: "Show me how you carry this," intending that you are unable to carry it. When Allah the Glorified knew Abraham's (peace be upon him) innocence of hovering around this sanctuary, He wanted to make him speak in the answer with that which would repel that linguistic possibility in the first expression—since his faith was sincere—with an expression that explicitly states it such that everyone who hears it understands it with an understanding in which no doubt interferes. The meaning of tranquility (الطمانينة) then is the soul's stillness from wandering among the possible modalities of revival through the appearance of the witnessed form; and the non-attainment of this tranquility before does not contradict the attainment of faith in the power to revive in the most perfect manner. Seeing the modality did not add anything to the faith required of him; it only provided a matter that faith in it is not obligatory.

Then he said after a long discourse: The verse indicates the merit of Abraham (peace be upon him) in that Allah the Glorified showed him what he asked for immediately in the easiest of ways, while He showed Uzayr what He showed him after He caused him to die for a hundred years.

By reflecting on the verse and considering the explanation we have provided, you will recognize the collapse of what he mentioned. For the question occurred regarding the modality of His revival, the Exalted, not about the modality of the parts' acceptance of life, secondly; for it was said: "how You revive" (تُحيي) with the damma on the ta, not with the fatha. Furthermore, the execution of the matter by Abraham's (peace be upon him) hand indicates that. Had the question been about the modality of acceptance, it would have sufficed to show some of the dead whom Allah revives, as in the story of the one who passed by the ruined town in the previous verse where the Exalted said: "And look at the bones, how We raise them, then We clothe them with flesh," and there would have been no need for the revival to be carried out by Abraham's hand. This is what we alluded to just now: that they measure the souls of the prophets in their reception of divine gnosis (المعارف الالهية) and their being the source of miraculous matters by their own ordinary souls.
