This results, for example, in there being no difference between life being formed by Abraham's hand and its being formed externally with respect to Abraham's state. This is a matter that does not occur to the mind of one who searches for realities and is expert in them; but these people, due to their neglect of the matter of realities, fell into the corruption they fell into, and the more they delved into the research, the further they moved from the truth. Do you not see that he interpreted tranquility as the removal of thoughts regarding the possible forms in the formation and the conceived shapes, while this intellectual hesitation is among the vanities that have no path to the sanctuary of one like Abraham (peace be upon him)? Moreover, the answer narrated in the verse did not provide anything in that regard; for Abraham (peace be upon him) said: "how You revive the dead?" using the term "the dead" (الموتى) generally, intending the human dead or what is more general than that, yet Allah the Glorified only showed him the formation of life in four birds.

Then he mentioned the merit of Abraham (peace be upon him) over Uzayr (intending the protagonist of the story in the previous verse) by what he mentioned, thus taking the story in the two verses as being of one type—namely the question about modality which he interpreted as he did, and the answer to it. Thus, the meaning of the two verses together became confused for him, even though both verses—despite the gems of explanation and subtleties of meaning they contain—are foreign to modality in the sense he mentioned. All of this is clear by referring to what has passed regarding them.

Furthermore, what is appropriate for the clarification of modality is concluding the verse with the attribute of power (القدرة), not with the attributes of might and wisdom, as in the Exalted's saying: وَمِنْ آيَاتِهِ أَنَّكَ تَرَى الْأَرْضَ خَاشِعَةً فَإِذَا أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهَا الْمَاءَ اهْتَزَّتْ وَرَبَتْ إِنَّ الَّذِي أَحْيَاهَا لَمُحْيِي الْمَوْتَىٰ إِنَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ — "And among His signs is that you see the earth desolate, but when We send down water upon it, it stirs and swells. Indeed He who revives it is the reviver of the dead. Indeed He has power over all things" (41:39). The verse, as you see, is in the context of clarifying the modality and has been concluded with the attribute of absolute power. Similar to it is the Exalted's saying: وَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّ اللَّهَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلَمْ يَعْيَ بِخَلْقِهِنَّ بِقَادِرٍ عَلَىٰ أَن يُحْيِيَ الْمَوْتَىٰ بَلَىٰ إِنَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ — "Do they not see that Allah, who created the heavens and the earth and was not exhausted by their creation, is able to revive the dead? Yes, indeed He has power over all things" (46:33). In it also is a clarification of the modality by showing examples, then the speech is concluded with the attribute of power.

His saying, the Exalted: "He said, 'Do you not believe?' He said, 'Yes, but that my heart may be at rest.'" Bala (بلى) is a word used to rebut a negation, and therefore the negation is turned into an affirmation by it, as in the Exalted's saying: أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ قَالُوا بَلَىٰ — "‘Am I not your Lord?’ They said, ‘Yes’" (7:172). Had they said na'am (نعم), it would have been disbelief. Tranquility (الطمانينة) and being at rest (الاطمينان) is the soul's stillness after its agitation and disturbance. It is derived from their saying: "the land is tranquil" (اطمأنت الارض) and "a tranquil land" (ارض مطمئنة) when it has a depression in which water settles when it flows to it and a stone when it descends to it.

The Exalted said: "Do you not believe?" (أولم تؤمن) and did not say: "Do you not believe?" (ألم تؤمن) to signal that there is a place for the question and request, but it should not be accompanied by a lack of faith in the revival. If it were said: "Do you not believe?" (ألم تؤمن), it would indicate that the speaker received the question as arising from a lack of faith, so it would be a reproach and a deterrent from such a question. This is because the waw (و) is for conjunction, so the interrogation with it was an inquiry into whether this question is accompanied by a lack of faith, not an inquiry into the reason for the question such that it would result in reproach and deterrence.

Faith (الايمان) is absolute in His speech, the Exalted, and in it is an indication that faith in Allah the Glorified is not realized with doubt in the matter of revival and resurrection. This is not contradicted by the specific instance being about revival, because the instance does not necessitate the specification of the word's generality nor the restriction of its absolute sense.

Likewise, His saying, the Exalted, narrating from him (peace be upon him): "that my heart may be at rest," is absolute, indicating that his (peace be upon him) objective from this question was the attainment of absolute tranquility and the cutting off of the sources of every heart-felt thought and their roots. For the estimation (الوهم) in its particular perceptions and its judgments, since it is devoted to the door of the senses and most of its judgments and affirmations are in the percepts it receives through the senses, it shrinks from complying with what the intellect (العقل) affirms, even if the soul is believing and certain of it. This is like the true universal intellectual judgments (الاحكام الكلية العقلية) regarding matters outside of matter and hidden from the senses; for it [the estimation] refuses to accept them even if it submits to the premises that produce them, so it brings to mind judgments that contradict them, then it stirs the psychological states appropriate to its refusal, so it becomes strong and supported thereby in its opposing influence, even if the soul, from the side of its intellect, is certain of the judgment and believes in the matter—it does not harm it except as an annoyance. Just as one who spends the night in a dark house in which there is a dead body knows that the dead person is an inanimate object without perception or will, so it does not harm anything; yet the estimation refuses this result and demands from the imagination (المتخيلة) to depict for the soul terrifying and lonely images of the dead person's matter, then the attribute of fear is stirred and dominates the soul, and it may reach a point where the intellect vanishes or the soul departs.

It has thus become clear that the existence of thoughts (الخطورات) contrary to certain beliefs (العقائد اليقينية) does not always contradict faith and affirmation, except that they annoy the soul and strip stillness and قرار from it. The existence of these thoughts is not removed except by sense or witnessing. For this reason, it was said: "Indeed, for direct observation (المعاينة) there is an effect that is not found with knowledge." Allah the Exalted informed Moses at the appointed time of his people's straying through the worship of the calf, yet that did not necessitate the appearance of his anger until he came to them and witnessed them and observed their matter directly; then he became angry, threw down the tablets, and took his brother by the head, pulling him toward himself.

It has become clear from here and from what preceded that Abraham (peace be upon him) was not asking for witnessing through the sense that relates to the material parts' acceptance of life after losing it; rather, he was only asking to witness the action of Allah the Glorified and His command in reviving the dead. This is not something sensed, even if it is inseparable from the sensed matter which is the material parts' acceptance of life through gathering and taking the form of a living being. Thus, he (peace be upon him) was only asking for the "truth of certitude" (حق اليقين).

His saying, the Exalted: "He said, 'Then take four birds and incline them (فصرهن) to you, then place on every mountain a part of them, then call them; they will come to you in haste.'" Surhunna (صرهن) with a damma on the sad according to one of the two readings is from sara yasuru when he cuts or inclines; or with a kasra on the sad according to the other reading from sara yasiru with one of the two meanings. The context of the speech indicates the intention of the meaning of cutting, and its being made transitive with "to" (إلى) indicates the implication of the meaning of inclining. So the meaning is: cut them while inclining them to you, or incline them to you while cutting them, depending on the disagreement regarding the implication in terms of estimation.

In any case, His saying, the Exalted: "Take four birds..." etc., is an answer to what Abraham (peace be upon him) asked by his saying: "My Lord! Show me how You revive the dead." It is known that the answer must correspond to the question; the eloquence of speech and the wisdom of the speaker prevent the speech from containing what is vain and redundant, from whose existence no benefit returns to the intended purpose of the speech—especially the Quran, which is the best speech delivered by the best speaker to the best attentive listener. The story is not of that simplicity that appears from it at first glance. Had it been so, the answer would have been completed by the revival of any dead thing in any manner, and the excess over that would have been vain and dispensable. But it is not so. Constraints and additional specificities have been taken in it beyond the original meaning: it was required that what was to be revived be a bird, that it be alive, that it be of the number four, that it be killed, mixed, and its parts blended, that the mixed parts be dispersed into portions, then each portion be placed in a location distant from the other like the peak of this mountain and that mountain, and that the revival be by Abraham's hand (the questioner himself) through his calling them, and that they all gather with him.

These, as you see, are additional specificities in the story that are undoubtedly involved in the meaning intended to be conveyed. They have mentioned for them aspects of subtleties that only increase the researcher's wonder (the correctness of what we mentioned is known by referring to the detailed commentaries).

In any case, these specificities must be connected to the question. What is found in the question—which is his saying: "My Lord! Show me how You revive the dead"—are two matters.

One of them: What is contained in His saying "You revive" (تحيي), which is that the request was to witness the revival insofar as it is an attribute of Allah the Glorified, not insofar as it is an attribute of the parts of matter carrying life.

The second of them: What is contained in the word "the dead" (الموتى) of the meaning of plurality, for it is an additional specificity.

As for the first: There is connected to it in the answer the execution of this matter by Abraham's own hand where He says: "Then take," "and incline them," "then place," in the imperative form, and He says: "then call them; they will come to you," for the Exalted made their coming in haste—which is life—connected to and branching from the call. This call is the cause from which the life of what was intended to be revived overflows. There is no revival except by Allah's command; thus, Abraham's calling them by Allah's command was connected with a certain connection to Allah's command from which the life of the living trickles. At that point, Abraham witnessed it and saw the modality of the overflow of the command with life. Had Abraham's calling them not been connected to Allah's command—which is to say to a thing He willed: "Be," and it is—like our words which are not connected except by imagination, he would also have been like us: if we said to a thing "Be," it would not be. For there is no haphazard influence in existence.

As for the second: His saying "how You revive the dead" indicates that the plurality of the dead and their multiplicity has an involvement in the question. This is only because bodies, by their death, the dispersal of their parts, the change of their forms, and the transformation of their states, lose the state of distinction and connection that was between them, so they become lost in the darkness of annihilation and ruin, and become like forgotten tales of which there is no news in reality or mind; so how does the reviving power encompass them when there is nothing encompassed in reality?

This is what Pharaoh raised to Moses (peace be upon him), and Moses answered him with knowledge as Allah narrated by His saying: قَالَ فَمَا بَالُ الْقُرُونِ الْأُولَىٰ قَالَ عِلْمُهَا عِندَ رَبِّي فِي كِتَابٍ لَّا يَضِلُّ رَبِّي وَلَا يَنسَى — "He said, ‘What about the former generations?’ He said, ‘Their knowledge is with my Lord in a Book. My Lord neither errs nor forgets’" (20:51-52).

In summary, Allah answered him by commanding him to take four birds (and perhaps the choice of birds was because this act is easier and takes less time in them) so he could witness their life and see the difference of their individuals and forms, and know them with a complete knowledge first; then kill them and mix their parts with a fine mixing, then make that into portions, and place each portion of them on a mountain so that distinction and identification are lost, and knowledge vanishes; then call them, and they will come to him in haste. For he witnesses then that distinction and taking the form of life—all of that is following the call which relates to their souls (أنفسها), i.e., that their bodies follow their souls and not the reverse. For the body is a branch following the spirit (الروح), not the reverse; rather, the relationship of the body to the spirit is, in a way, the relationship of the shadow to the object. When the object exists, its existence is followed by the existence of the shadow, and to whatever state the object or its parts transform, the shadow follows it in that, until if it is annihilated, it follows it in annihilation. When Allah the Glorified brings a living being into existence, or returns life to parts previously possessed of life, His bringing into existence only relates to the spirit possessing life first, then the parts of matter follow it through links preserved with Allah the Glorified which we do not encompass in knowledge; thus the body is determined by the determination of the spirit without separation or obstacle. By this, His saying, the Exalted, signals: "then call them; they will come to you in haste (سعياً)," i.e., speeding and hurrying.

This is what is benefited from the Exalted's saying: وَقَالُوا أَإِذَا ضَلَلْنَا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ بَلْ هُم بِلِقَاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كَافِرُونَ قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُم مَّلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ — "They say, ‘When we have been lost in the earth, shall we indeed be created anew?’ Rather they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord. Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you fully, then you will be brought back to your Lord’" (32:10-11). Some discussion of the verse has already passed in the research on the immateriality of the soul (تجرد النفس), and the detailed discussion will come in its place, if Allah wills.

His saying, the Exalted: "Then take four birds," he was only commanded that so he would know them and thus not doubt them when life is returned to them nor deny them, and so he would see what they are upon of difference and distinction first and their disappearance second. His saying: "and incline them to you, then place on every mountain a part of them," i.e., slaughter them, scatter their parts, and mix them, then disperse them on the mountains present there so that the parts become distant while being indistinct. This is among the evidences that the story only occurred after Abraham's migration from the land of Babylon to Syria, for the land of Babylon has no mountains. His saying: "then call them," i.e., call the birds: "O peacock," "O so-and-so," and "O so-and-so." This can be benefited, in addition to the indication of the pronoun "them" (هن) referring to the birds, from His saying: "call them" (ادعهن); for if the call were for the parts of the birds rather than their souls, it would have been more appropriate to say: "then hail them" (نادهن), for they were on mountains distant from his (peace be upon him) position, and the word used specifically for the distant is "hailing" (النداء) rather than "calling" (الدعاء). His saying: "they will come to you in haste," i.e., they became embodied and were described with coming and speeding to you.

His saying, the Exalted: "And know that Allah is All-mighty, All-wise," i.e., All-mighty (عزيز) who loses nothing by its departure from Him, All-wise (حكيم) who does nothing except through its appropriate path; thus He brings bodies into existence by summoning the spirits and bringing them into existence, not the reverse.

In His saying, the Exalted: "And know that..." rather than saying "Indeed Allah is...", there is an indication that the heart-felt thought from whose side Abraham was asking his Lord for witnessing so that his heart might be at rest was returning to the reality of the meaning of the two names: the All-mighty, the All-wise. Thus, Allah the Glorified provided him with this answer the knowledge of their reality.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding the Exalted's saying: "Have you not regarded him who argued with Abraham about his Lord..." the verse: al-Tayalisi and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) who said: "The one who argued with Abraham about his Lord was Nimrod ibn Canaan."

In Tafsir al-Burhan: Abu Ali al-Tabarsi said: There is disagreement regarding the time of this argument. It was said it was at the breaking of the idols before he was cast into the fire, narrated from Muqatil; and it was said it was after he was cast into the fire and it was made for him cool and safe, narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

I say: Although the verse does not address whether it was before or after, consideration supports its being after the casting into the fire. For his stories mentioned in the Quran at the beginning of his affair—from his arguing with his father and his people and his breaking the idols—convey that the first time Abraham (peace be upon him) met Nimrod was when his case was raised to him in the matter of breaking the idols as a criminal among them. So he judged him to be burned, and the judgment upon him for his crime occupied them from speaking to him about the matter of his Lord: is He Allah or Nimrod? Had Nimrod argued with him then, he would have argued with him about the matter of Allah and the matter of the idols, not the matter of Allah and the matter of himself!

In several narrations narrated by both the Sunnis and the Shi'a regarding the Exalted's saying: "Or him who passed by a town which had fallen on its trellises..." the verse: that the protagonist of the story was Jeremiah the Prophet; and in several of them: that it was Uzayr. However, they are isolated reports (آحاد) not mandatory to accept, and in their chains of transmission (أسانيد) there is some weakness, and they have no witness from the apparent meaning of the verses. The story is not mentioned in the Torah, and what is in the narrations of the story is long and contains some variation, but it is outside our purpose; whoever wants it should refer to its sources.

In al-Ma'ani, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the Exalted's saying: "And when Abraham said, 'My Lord! Show me how You revive the dead...'" the verse: in a hadith, he (peace be upon him) said: "This is an allegorical verse (آية متشابهة), and its meaning is that he asked about the modality (الكيفية), and modality is from the action of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. Whenever the scholar does not know it, no blame attaches to him, nor does any deficiency occur in his monotheism (توحيده)." The hadith.

I say: The meaning of the hadith has become clear from what has passed.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Ali ibn Asbat: that Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him) was asked about the saying of Allah: "He said, 'Yes, but that my heart may be at rest'": Was there doubt in his heart? He said: "No, but he wanted an increase from Allah." The hadith.

I say: This meaning was narrated in al-Kafi from al-Sadiq and from the Righteous Servant (peace be upon them both), and its explanation has passed.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from his father, from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Abu Ayyub, from Abu Basir, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "Abraham looked at a carcass on the seashore being eaten by the beasts of the sea, then the beasts would leap upon one another and eat each other. Abraham wondered and said: 'O Lord! Show me how You revive the dead?' Allah said: 'Do you not believe?' He said: 'Yes, but that my heart may be at rest.' He said: 'Then take four birds and incline them to you, then place on every mountain a part of them, then call them; they will come to you in haste, and know that Allah is All-mighty, All-wise.' So Abraham took the peacock, the rooster, the pigeon, and the crow. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: 'Incline them to you,' meaning cut them, then mix their flesh and disperse them on ten mountains. Then he called them and said: 'Come to life by the permission of Allah.' So they began to gather and join, flesh to flesh and bone to bone, until each bird came to its head, and they flew to Abraham. At that point, Abraham said: 'Indeed Allah is All-mighty, All-wise.'"

I say: Al-Ayyashi narrated this meaning in his Tafsir from Abu Basir from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), and it was narrated through Sunni paths from Ibn Abbas.

His saying: "Abraham looked at a carcass..." until his saying "so he said: O Lord! Show me..." etc., is an explanation of the ambiguity that led him to the question, which is the dispersal of the body's parts after death—a dispersal leading to their change and transition to different places and diverse states such that nothing of the original remains.

If you say: The apparent meaning of the narration is that the ambiguity was the "problem of the eater and the eaten" (شبهة الآكل والمأكول), as it included the leaping of some and the eating of some by others, then branched Abraham's wonder and question upon that.

I say: The ambiguity is two ambiguities: one of them is the dispersal of the body's parts and the annihilation of their original forms and accidents, and in summary, their non-persistence such that they could be distinguished and life could be composed in them; and the second of them is the parts of some animals becoming part of the body of another, leading to the impossibility of reviving both animals with their complete bodies together, because the premise is that a part of the body of one of them is identically a part of the body of the other; so if each one of them were returned complete, the other would remain deficient and not accept restoration. This is the problem of the eater and the eaten.

What Allah the Glorified answered with—which is the body's following of the spirit—even if it is sufficient to repel both ambiguities together, yet what Abraham was commanded with according to what the verse narrates does not contain the material for the problem of the eater and the eaten, which is the eating of some animals by others; rather, it only contains the dispersal of parts, their mixing, and the change of their forms and states. This is the material for the first ambiguity. Thus, the verse only addresses its repulsion, even if both ambiguities share in being repelled by what was answered in the verse as previously mentioned. What the narration contained regarding the eating of some by others is not intended in the interpretation of the verse.

His saying (peace be upon him): "So Abraham took the peacock, the rooster, the pigeon, and the crow." In some narrations, the birds were the vulture, the duck, the peacock, and the rooster; narrated by al-Saduq in al-Uyun from al-Rida (peace be upon him) and quoted from Mujahid, Ibn Jurayj, Ata, and Ibn Zayd. In some of them: they were the hoopoe, the shrike, the peacock, and the crow; narrated by al-Ayyashi from Salih ibn Sahl from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him). In some of them: they were the ostrich, the peacock, the goose, and the rooster; narrated by al-Ayyashi from Ma'ruf ibn Kharrabudh from al-Baqir (peace be upon him) and quoted from Ibn Abbas. It was also narrated through Sunni paths from Ibn Abbas: they were the crane, the peacock, the rooster, and the pigeon. That which all narrations and opinions share is: the peacock.

His saying (peace be upon him): "And disperse them on ten mountains." The mountains being ten is what the reports transmitted from the Imams of the People of the House (أئمة اهل البيت) agreed upon. It was said they were four, and it was said seven.

In al-Uyun, with a chain of transmission from Ali ibn Muhammad ibn al-Jahm, who said: "I attended the assembly of al-Ma'mun while al-Rida Ali ibn Musa was with him. Al-Ma'mun said to him: 'O son of the Messenger of Allah! Is it not your view that the prophets are infallible (معصومون)?' He said: 'Yes.' So he asked him about verses from the Quran. Among what he asked him was that he said: 'Then inform me about the saying of Allah: "My Lord! Show me how You revive the dead. He said, 'Do you not believe?' He said, 'Yes, but that my heart may be at rest.'"' Al-Rida said: 'Indeed Allah, Blessed and Exalted, had revealed to Abraham: "I am taking from My servants a friend (خليلا); if he asks Me to revive the dead, I will answer him." It occurred in Abraham's heart that he was that friend, so he said: "My Lord! Show me how You revive the dead?" He said: "Do you not believe?" He said: "Yes, but that my heart may be at rest regarding the friendship (الخلة)."'" The hadith.

I say: A discussion regarding Ali ibn Muhammad ibn al-Jahm and this narration which he narrated from al-Rida (peace be upon him) has already preceded in the reports of Adam's Garden, so refer back to it.

Know that the narration is not devoid of some indication that the station of friendship (مقام الخلة) necessitates the answering of prayer (استجابة الدعاء). The wording supports this, for friendship (الخلة) is need (الحاجة), and the friend (الخليل) is only called a friend because when friendship is complete, the friend raises his needs to his friend, and there is no meaning to raising them without sufficiency and fulfillment.

Ayahs

مَّثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ حَبَّةٍ أَنۢبَتَتْ سَبْعَ سَنَابِلَ فِى كُلِّ سُنۢبُلَةٍ مِّا۟ئَةُ حَبَّةٍ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُضَـٰعِفُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٦١)
The parable of those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah is that of a grain which grows seven ears, in every ear a hundred grains. Allah enhances severalfold whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing. (261)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ لَا يُتْبِعُونَ مَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ مَنًّا وَلَآ أَذًى ۙ لَّهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٢٦٢)
Those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah and then do not follow up what they have spent with reproaches and affronts, they shall have their reward near their Lord, and they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (262)
۞ قَوْلٌ مَّعْرُوفٌ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ خَيْرٌ مِّن صَدَقَةٍ يَتْبَعُهَآ أَذًى ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَنِىٌّ حَلِيمٌ (٢٦٣)
An honourable reply [in response to the needy] and forgiving [their annoyance] is better than a charity followed by affront. Allah is all-sufficient, most forbearing. (263)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُبْطِلُوا۟ صَدَقَـٰتِكُم بِٱلْمَنِّ وَٱلْأَذَىٰ كَٱلَّذِى يُنفِقُ مَالَهُۥ رِئَآءَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَا يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ ۖ فَمَثَلُهُۥ كَمَثَلِ صَفْوَانٍ عَلَيْهِ تُرَابٌ فَأَصَابَهُۥ وَابِلٌ فَتَرَكَهُۥ صَلْدًا ۖ لَّا يَقْدِرُونَ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ مِّمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٢٦٤)
O you who have faith! Do not render your charities void by reproaches and affronts, like those who spend their wealth to be seen by people and have no faith in Allah and the Last Day. Their parable is that of a rock covered with soil: a downpour strikes it, leaving it bare. They have no power over anything of what they have earned, and Allah does not guide the faithless lot. (264)
وَمَثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمُ ٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِ ٱللَّهِ وَتَثْبِيتًا مِّنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ كَمَثَلِ جَنَّةٍۭ بِرَبْوَةٍ أَصَابَهَا وَابِلٌ فَـَٔاتَتْ أُكُلَهَا ضِعْفَيْنِ فَإِن لَّمْ يُصِبْهَا وَابِلٌ فَطَلٌّ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٢٦٥)
The parable of those who spend their wealth seeking Allah’s pleasure and to confirm themselves [in their faith], is that of a garden on a hillside: the downpour strikes it, whereupon it brings forth its fruit twofold; and if it is not a downpour that strikes it, then a shower, and Allah watches what you do. (265)
أَيَوَدُّ أَحَدُكُمْ أَن تَكُونَ لَهُۥ جَنَّةٌ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَأَعْنَابٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ لَهُۥ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ وَأَصَابَهُ ٱلْكِبَرُ وَلَهُۥ ذُرِّيَّةٌ ضُعَفَآءُ فَأَصَابَهَآ إِعْصَارٌ فِيهِ نَارٌ فَٱحْتَرَقَتْ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٢٦٦)
Would any of you like to have a garden of palm trees and vines, with streams running in it, with all kinds of fruit for him therein, and old age were to strike him while he has weakly offspring; whereupon a fiery hurricane were to hit it, whereat it lies burnt? Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may reflect. (266)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَنفِقُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا كَسَبْتُمْ وَمِمَّآ أَخْرَجْنَا لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَلَا تَيَمَّمُوا۟ ٱلْخَبِيثَ مِنْهُ تُنفِقُونَ وَلَسْتُم بِـَٔاخِذِيهِ إِلَّآ أَن تُغْمِضُوا۟ فِيهِ ۚ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ (٢٦٧)
O you who have faith! Spend of the good things you have earned [through trade and the like] and of what We bring forth for you from the earth, and do not be of the mind to give the bad part of it, for you yourselves would not take it, unless you ignore. And know that Allah is all-sufficient, all-laudable. (267)
ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ يَعِدُكُمُ ٱلْفَقْرَ وَيَأْمُرُكُم بِٱلْفَحْشَآءِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَعِدُكُم مَّغْفِرَةً مِّنْهُ وَفَضْلًا ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٦٨)
Satan frightens you of poverty and prompts you to [commit] indecent acts. But Allah promises you His forgiveness and bounty, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing. (268)
يُؤْتِى ٱلْحِكْمَةَ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَن يُؤْتَ ٱلْحِكْمَةَ فَقَدْ أُوتِىَ خَيْرًا كَثِيرًا ۗ وَمَا يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّآ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (٢٦٩)
He grants wisdom to whomever He wishes, and he, who is given wisdom, is certainly given an abundant good, and none takes admonition except those who possess intellect. (269)
وَمَآ أَنفَقْتُم مِّن نَّفَقَةٍ أَوْ نَذَرْتُم مِّن نَّذْرٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُهُۥ ۗ وَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍ (٢٧٠)
Allah indeed knows whatever charity you may give, or vows that you may vow, and the wrongdoers have no helpers. (270)
إِن تُبْدُوا۟ ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ فَنِعِمَّا هِىَ ۖ وَإِن تُخْفُوهَا وَتُؤْتُوهَا ٱلْفُقَرَآءَ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۚ وَيُكَفِّرُ عَنكُم مِّن سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (٢٧١)
If you disclose your charities, that is well, but if you hide them and give them to the poor, that is better for you, and it will atone for some of your misdeeds, and Allah is well aware of what you do. (271)
۞ لَّيْسَ عَلَيْكَ هُدَىٰهُمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَلِأَنفُسِكُمْ ۚ وَمَا تُنفِقُونَ إِلَّا ٱبْتِغَآءَ وَجْهِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ يُوَفَّ إِلَيْكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تُظْلَمُونَ (٢٧٢)
It is not up to you to guide them; rather, it is Allah who guides whomever He wishes. Whatever wealth you spend, it is for your own benefit, as you do not spend but to seek Allah’s pleasure, and whatever wealth you spend will be repaid to you in full and you will not be wronged. (272)
لِلْفُقَرَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ أُحْصِرُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ ضَرْبًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَحْسَبُهُمُ ٱلْجَاهِلُ أَغْنِيَآءَ مِنَ ٱلتَّعَفُّفِ تَعْرِفُهُم بِسِيمَـٰهُمْ لَا يَسْـَٔلُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ إِلْحَافًا ۗ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِهِۦ عَلِيمٌ (٢٧٣)
[The charities are] for the poor who are straitened in the way of Allah, not capable of moving about in the land [for trade]. The unaware suppose them to be well-off because of their reserve. You recognize them by their mark; they do not ask the people importunately. And whatever wealth you may spend, Allah indeed knows it. (273)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُم بِٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ سِرًّا وَعَلَانِيَةً فَلَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٢٧٤)
Those who give their wealth by night and day, secretly and openly, they shall have their reward near their Lord, and they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (274)

(Commentary)

The context of the verses, in terms of their unity in explaining the matter of spending (الانفاق) — the act of giving wealth for the sake of Allah — and the way their contents and objectives relate to one another, indicates that they were revealed all at once. They urge the believers to spend in the way of Allah (سبيل الله). First, they strike a parable for its increase and growth with Allah, the Glorified: one to seven hundred, and perhaps He increases beyond that by the permission of Allah. Second, they strike a parable for the fact that it never fails its purpose under any circumstance. They forbid showing off (الرياء) — performing deeds to be seen by people rather than for Allah — in spending, striking a parable for spending out of ostentation and not for the sake of Allah, showing that it neither grows nor yields any fruit. They also forbid spending followed by reminders of generosity (المن) — the act of mentioning one's favors to the recipient to make them feel indebted — and injury (الأذى) — causing emotional or physical harm to the recipient, as these nullify its effect and ruin its great reward. Then, they command that spending be from the good (طيب) of one's wealth, not from the vile (خبيث) out of stinginess and greed. Subsequently, they specify the recipient where this good deed should be placed, namely the poor who are constrained in the way of Allah. Finally, they mention the great reward that this spending holds with Allah.

In summary, the verses call toward spending and explain: first, its direction and purpose, which is that it be for Allah and not for people; second, the form and manner of the act, which is that it not be followed by reminders of generosity or injury; third, the description of the wealth spent, which is that it be good and not vile; fourth, the attribute of the recipient of the spending, which is that they be poor and constrained in the way of Allah; and fifth, the great reward it carries in this world and the hereafter.

(A Discourse on Spending)

Spending (الانفاق) is among the greatest matters emphasized by Islam in one of its two pillars, namely the rights of people (حقوق الناس). Islam has utilized every means to encourage it, both as an obligation and a recommendation, through the channels of the alms-tax (الزكاة), the one-fifth tax (الخمس), financial expiations (الكفارات المالية), various types of ransom (الفدية), obligatory maintenance (الانفاقات الواجبة), and recommended charities (الصدقات المندوبة), as well as through endowments (الوقف), granting the use of a house (السكنى), life-tenancy (العمرى), bequests (الوصايا), gifts (الهبة), and other means.

By this, Islam intends to raise the living standard of the lower class who cannot meet the necessities of life without financial aid from others, so that their horizon may approach the horizon of the people of luxury and wealth. On the other hand, it has prohibited the upper class from flaunting beauty and adornment in the manifestations of life in a way that does not conform to the common good (المعروف) and is beyond the reach of the middle class, by forbidding extravagance (الاسراف) and wastefulness (التبذير) and the like.

The purpose of all this was to create a balanced communal life with closely integrated parts and similar dimensions, which revives the law of unity and mutual assistance (المعاضدة) and puts to death conflicting wills, heart-felt grudges, and the breeding grounds of hatred. Indeed, the Quran views the function of the true religion (الدين الحق) as the organization of life in all its affairs, arranging it in a manner that ensures the happiness of man in the immediate and the ultimate, whereby man lives with true knowledge (معارف حقة), virtuous character (اخلاق فاضلة), and a goodly life (عيشة طيبة) in which he enjoys the blessings Allah has bestowed upon him in this world, while warding off misfortunes, calamities, and material deficiencies.

This cannot be achieved except through a goodly communal life (الحياة الطيبة النوعية) that is uniform in its goodness and purity. This, in turn, cannot happen except by reforming the state of the species by fulfilling its life necessities. This cannot be perfected except through financial means, wealth, and possessions (القنية). The way to achieve this is for individuals to spend from what they have acquired by the labor of their right hands and the sweat of their brows; for indeed, the believers are brothers, the earth belongs to Allah, and wealth is His wealth.

This is a reality whose correctness, stability, growth, and results were proven by the Prophetic biography (السيرة النبوية) — the life and conduct of the Prophet — (upon its subject be the best of salutations) during a period of time, namely the time of his (peace be upon him and his progeny) life and the influence of his command.

It is this reality that the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), laments and whose deviation he complains of when he says: "You have entered a time in which good only increases in its retreat, and evil only increases in its approach, and Satan only increases in his greed for the destruction of people. This is a time when his equipment has strengthened, his plot has become widespread, and his prey has become accessible. Cast your glance wherever you wish: do you see anything but a poor man struggling with poverty? Or a rich man who has exchanged Allah's blessing for disbelief? Or a miser who has turned his stinginess regarding Allah's right into a means of increasing wealth? Or a rebel as if there were a deafness (وقراً) in his ears against hearing admonitions?" (Nahj al-Balagha).

The passing of days has revealed the truth of the Quran in this theory—namely, narrowing the gap between classes by aiding the lower through spending and preventing the upper from luxury and flaunting beauty. For after the emergence of Western civilization, people surrendered themselves to the earth and went to extremes in pursuing animalistic desires and satisfying psychological whims. They prepared for this whatever power they could, which led to wealth and the finest pleasures of life being confined to the doors of the powerful and the wealthy. Nothing remained in the hands of the lower class but deprivation. The upper class continued to devour one another until the happiness of material life was monopolized by a tiny few, and the right to life was stripped from the majority, who are the masses of the people. This stirred up all moral vices from both sides, each acting according to his own disposition, sparing nothing. This resulted in the confrontation between the two groups, the entanglement of conflict and combat between the two parties, and the mutual destruction between the rich and the poor, the blessed and the deprived, the haves and the have-nots. The Great World War broke out, Communism emerged, truth and virtue were abandoned, and tranquility (السكن), peace of mind (الطمانينة), and the goodness of life departed from the species. This is what we witness today of the corruption of the human world, and what threatens the species in the future is even greater and more horrific.

Among the greatest factors in this corruption is the closing of the door of spending and the opening of the doors of usury (الربا) — the practice of charging interest on loans — whose horrific nature Allah the Exalted will explain in the seven verses following these verses, I mean the verses of spending. He mentions that in its prevalence lies the corruption of the world, which is one of the prophecies of the Noble Quran; it was an embryo during the days of the Quran's revelation, and the pregnant world has given birth to it in these days.

If you wish to verify what we have mentioned, then contemplate what He, the Glorified, mentioned in Surah al-Rum when He said: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللَّهِ ذَٰلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ مُنِيبِينَ إِلَيْهِ وَاتَّقُوهُ وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَلَا تَكُونُوا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ مِنَ الَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا دِينَهُمْ وَكَانُوا شِيَعًا كُلُّ حِزْبٍ بِمَا لَدَيْهِمْ فَرِحُونَ وَإِذَا مَسَّ النَّاسَ ضُرٌّ دَعَوْا رَبَّهُم مُّنِيبِينَ إِلَيْهِ ثُمَّ إِذَا أَذَاقَهُم مِّنْهُ رَحْمَةً إِذَا فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُم بِرَبِّهِمْ يُشْرِكُونَ لِيَكْفُرُوا بِمَا آتَيْنَاهُمْ فَتَمَتَّعُوا فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (So set your face on the religion as a Hanif, with the nature of Allah which He has created in mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion, but most people do not know. Turning to Him [in penitence]; and be wary of Him, and maintain the prayer, and do not be among the polytheists—of those who split up their religion and became sects, each party exulting in what it had. When distress befalls people, they supplicate their Lord, turning to Him in penitence. Then, when He lets them taste a mercy from Him, behold, a part of them ascribe partners to their Lord, so as to be ungrateful for what We have given them. So enjoy yourselves; soon you will know.) until He said: فَآتِ ذَا الْقُرْبَىٰ حَقَّهُ وَالْمِسْكِينَ وَابْنَ السَّبِيلِ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ اللَّهِ وَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ وَمَا آتَيْتُم مِّن رِّبًا لِّيَرْبُوَ فِي أَمْوَالِ النَّاسِ فَلَا يَرْبُو عِندَ اللَّهِ وَمَا آتَيْتُم مِّن زَكَاةٍ تُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ اللَّهِ فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْمُضْعِفُونَ (So give the kinsman his due, and the needy and the wayfarer. That is better for those who seek Allah’s pleasure, and it is they who are the felicitous. What you give as usury in order that it may increase people’s wealth does not increase with Allah. But what you give as zakat, seeking Allah’s pleasure—it is they who will receive a manifold increase.) until He said: ظَهَرَ الْفَسَادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ (Corruption has appeared in land and sea because of what the hands of the people have earned) until He said: لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانظُرُوا كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلُ كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّشْرِكِينَ فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ الْقَيِّمِ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِيَ يَوْمٌ لَّا مَدَدَّ لَهُ مِنَ اللَّهِ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصَّدَّعُونَ (that He may let them taste [the consequence of] some of what they have done, so that they may come back. Say, ‘Travel over the land and observe how was the fate of those who were before [you]. Most of them were polytheists.’ So set your face toward the upright religion, before there comes a day from Allah which cannot be averted. On that day they will be split [into two groups].) (Surah al-Rum 30–43). There are similar verses in Surahs Hud, Yunus, al-Isra, al-Anbiya, and others that inform of this matter, the explanation of which will come, Allah willing.

In short, this is the reason for what is seen in these verses—I mean the verses of spending—of intense urging and extreme emphasis on its matter.

His saying, the Exalted: مَّثَلُ الَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَالَهُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ حَبَّةٍ (The parable of those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah is that of a grain...) What is meant by "the way of Allah" (سبيل الله) is every matter that leads to His pleasure, the Glorified, for a religious purpose for which the act was performed. For the word in the verse is absolute, even if the verse was preceded by verses mentioning fighting in the way of Allah, and even if the phrase "in the way of Allah" is associated with Jihad in many verses; for that does not necessitate restriction (التخصيص), as is evident.

They have mentioned that His saying, the Exalted: كَمَثَلِ حَبَّةٍ أَنبَتَتْ (is that of a grain that sends up...) is based on the estimation of our saying: "is like the parable of one who sowed a grain that sent up...", for the grain that sends up seven ears is the parable for the wealth spent in the way of Allah, not the parable for the one who spends, as is evident.

While this statement is plausible in itself, contemplation suggests otherwise. For most of the parables struck in the Quran are of this nature; it is a common literary device (صناعة شائعة) in the Quran, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَمَثَلُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا كَمَثَلِ الَّذِي يَنْعِقُ بِمَا لَا يَسْمَعُ إِلَّا دُعَاءً وَنِدَاءً (The parable of the faithless is that of one who shouts to that which hears nothing but a call and a cry) (al-Baqarah: 171), for it is the parable of the one who calls the disbelievers, not the parable of the disbelievers themselves. And His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا مَثَلُ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا كَمَاءٍ أَنزَلْنَاهُ (The parable of the life of this world is only like the water We send down...) (Yunus: 24), and His saying, the Exalted: مَثَلُ نُورِهِ كَمِشْكَاةٍ (The parable of His Light is a niche...) (al-Nur: 35), and His saying, the Exalted, in the verses following this one: فَمَثَلُهُ كَمَثَلِ صَفْوانٍ (his parable is that of a smooth rock...), and His saying, the Exalted: وَمَثَلُ الَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَالَهُمُ ابْتِغَاءَ مَرْضَاتِ اللَّهِ وَتَثْبِيتًا مِّنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ كَمَثَلِ جَنَّةٍ بِرَبْوَةٍ (The parable of those who spend their wealth seeking Allah’s pleasure and to confirm themselves is that of a garden on a high ground...), among many other instances.

These parables struck in the verses all share the characteristic that they are restricted to the material of the representation (مادة التمثيل) upon which the parable is based, while disregarding the remaining parts of the speech for the sake of conciseness (الإيجاز).

To clarify: A parable (المثل) is actually a story, whether real or hypothetical, that resembles another in its aspects, brought forth so that the mind of the listener may move from its conception to the complete conception of the thing represented (الممثل). Examples include their saying: "I have neither a she-camel nor a male camel in this," and their saying: "In the summer you wasted the milk," which are parables that have real stories, and the representation is intended to remind the listener of them and apply them to the subject of the speech for clarification; hence it is said: "Parables do not change." Another example is our saying: "The parable of those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah is like the parable of one who sowed a grain that sent up seven ears, in every ear a hundred grains," which is a hypothetical, imaginary story.

The meaning contained in the parable, which serves as the scale by which the state of the thing represented is weighed, may be the entire story that constitutes the parable, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَمَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ (And the parable of a corrupt word is that of a corrupt tree...) (Ibrahim: 26), and His saying, the Exalted: مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ حُمِّلُوا التَّوْرَاةَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَحْمِلُوهَا كَمَثَلِ الْحِمَارِ يَحْمِلُ أَسْفَارًا (The example of those who were charged with the Torah, then failed to carry it, is that of the ass carrying books) (al-Jumu'ah: 5). At other times, it may be a part of the story that fulfills the purpose of the representation, which we call the "material of the representation" (مادة التمثيل), while the other part is brought only to complete the story, as in the last example (the parable of spending and the grain); for the material of the representation is only the grain that sends up seven hundred grains, and we only added "the one who sowed" to complete the story.

In those Quranic parables where the material of the representation is the entire parable, it is presented as it is. But in those where the material of the representation is only part of the story, it is restricted to the material of the representation, which is placed in the position of the entire story because the purpose of the representation is achieved thereby. This also serves to stimulate the mind of the listener by the absence of one element and the finding of another in its place that fulfills the purpose; thus, it is the thing in one respect and not it in another. This is a form of conciseness by inversion (الإيجاز بالقلب) in a subtle manner employed by the Quran.

His saying, the Exalted: أَنبَتَتْ سَبْعَ سَنَابِلَ فِي كُلِّ سُنبُلَةٍ مِّائَةُ حَبَّةٍ (...that sends up seven ears, in every ear a hundred grains). The ear (السنبل) is well-known. It is said that the root meaning of its material is "to cover" (الستر); it was named so because it covers the grains it contains within husks.

Among the most absurd objections raised against the verse is that it is a representation of something that has no reality in the external world, namely an ear containing a hundred grains. The response is that a parable, as you have learned, does not require its content to be realized in the external world; imaginary parables are too many to count. Furthermore, an ear containing a hundred grains and a single grain producing seven hundred grains is not a rare occurrence.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ يُضَاعِفُ لِمَن يَشَاءُ وَاللَّهُ وَاسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (And Allah enhances severalfold whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing). That is, He increases beyond seven hundred for whomever He wishes, for He is the All-Bounteous (الواسع); there is no barrier to His generosity and no limit to His grace, as He said: مَّن ذَا الَّذِي يُقْرِضُ اللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا فَيُضَاعِفَهُ لَهُ أَضْعَافًا كَثِيرةً (Who is it that will lend Allah a good loan, that He may multiply it for him with severalfold increase?) (al-Baqarah: 245), where He left "increase" absolute and did not restrict it to a specific number.

It has been said that its meaning is that Allah multiplies this specific multiplication (up to seven hundred) for whomever He wishes, so the multiplication to seven hundredfold is the maximum indicated by the verse. The flaw in this is that the sentence would then occupy the position of a justification (التعليل), and the proper way for the speech then would be to begin with "Indeed" (إِن), as in His saying: اللَّهُ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ لِتَسْكُنُوا فِيهِ وَالنَّهَارَ مُبْصِرًا إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى النَّاسِ (It is Allah who made the night for you to rest in it, and the day to provide light. Indeed Allah is gracious to mankind...) (al-Mu'min: 61), and similar instances.

He did not restrict the parable He struck to the hereafter; rather, the speech is absolute, encompassing this world as well as the hereafter. This is indeed the case, and reason supports it. For the one who spends something of his wealth, even if it initially occurs to his mind that the wealth is gone and has left no replacement, if he reflects a little, he will find that human society is like a single person with different organs in terms of names and forms, but they are all united in the purpose of life, linked in terms of effect and benefit. If one of them loses the blessing of health and integrity, and becomes sluggish in its function, this causes the sluggishness of all in their functions and their loss in their objectives.

The eye and the hand, though they are two organs in terms of name and outward action, the creation only equipped man with sight to distinguish things by light, color, proximity, and distance, so that the hand may grasp what man must bring to himself and ward off what he must repel from himself. If the hand ceases to function, man must compensate for all its lost benefits using his other organs; he will first endure a labor and fatigue that is not normally borne, and the actions of the other organs will decrease by the amount they are used in place of the non-functioning organ. However, if he repairs the state of his damaged hand with the surplus he had stored for some other organs, that would be a reform for the state of all, and there would return to him of real benefit many times what he lost of the surplus—benefits that may exceed hundreds and thousands through the resulting reform of the state of others, the repelling of vices that poverty and need can instill in the soul, the creation of love in the heart, good mention on the tongue, and vigor in work. Society links all of this and inevitably returns it to the spender, especially if the spending is to meet communal needs like education, upbringing, and the like. This is the state of spending.

When spending is in the way of Allah and seeking the pleasure of Allah, growth and increase are among its inseparable consequences without fail. For if spending were not for the sake of Allah, it would only be for a benefit returning to the spender himself, such as the rich spending on the poor to ward off his evil, or the wealthy spending on the destitute to meet his need so that the state of society becomes balanced and life becomes pure for the wealthy spender. This is a type of exploitation of the poor and an investment from him for his own benefit, which may leave a bad impression in the soul of the poor man; these effects may accumulate and manifest as a trial. But spending that is intended for nothing but the sake of Allah and seeks nothing but His pleasure is free from these deficiencies; it produces nothing but beauty and is followed by nothing but good.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَالَهُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ ثُمَّ لَا يُتْبِعُونَ مَا أَنفَقُوا مَنًّا وَلَا أَذًى (Those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah and then do not follow up what they have spent with reminders of their generosity or with injury...) Following up (الاتباع) means joining or attaching. He said: فَأَتْبَعُوهُم مُّشْرِقِينَ (So they pursued them at sunrise) (al-Shu'ara: 60), meaning they caught up with them. And He said: وَأَتْبَعْنَاهُمْ فِي هَٰذِهِ الدُّنْيَا لَعْنَةً (And We made a curse to follow them in this world) (al-Qasas: 42), meaning We attached it to them. Reminding of generosity (المن) is the mention of that which spoils the favor, such as the benefactor saying to the beneficiary: "I bestowed such and such upon you," and the like. The root of its meaning, as is said, is "to cut" (القطع); from it is His saying: لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ (...they will have a reward that is never-failing) (Fussilat: 8), meaning not cut off. Injury (الأذى) is immediate harm or slight harm. Fear (الخوف) is the expectation of harm, and grief (الحزن) is the sorrow that weighs heavily on the soul due to a disliked event that has occurred or is as if it has occurred.

His saying, the Exalted: قَوْلٌ مَّعْرُوفٌ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ خَيْرٌ مِّن صَدَقَةٍ (A worthy word and forgiveness are better than a charity...) A worthy word (قول معروف) is speech that people do not find objectionable according to custom; it varies according to the situation. The root meaning of forgiveness (المغفرة) is "to cover" (الستر). Wealth (الغنى) is the opposite of need and poverty. Forbearance (الحلم) is remaining silent in the face of something disliked in word or deed.

The preference of a worthy word and forgiveness over a charity followed by injury, followed by the contrast, testifies that what is meant by "worthy word" is a prayer or another beautiful expression when turning away a beggar if one does not speak what offends the one asked. And "covering" and "overlooking" when the beggar accompanies his request with something offensive—these two are better than a charity followed by injury. For the injury caused by the spender to the recipient indicates the greatness of his spending and the wealth he spent in his own eyes, and his being affected by what offends him in the request. These are two causes that must be removed from the soul of the believer, for the believer is characterized by the character of Allah, and Allah, the Glorified, is All-Sufficient (غني); what He bestows and gives generously does not seem great to Him. He is All-Forbearing (حليم); He does not hasten to punish for an evil deed, nor does He become angry at every act of ignorance. This is the meaning of concluding the verse with His saying: وَاللَّهُ غَنِيٌّ حَلِيمٌ (and Allah is all-sufficient, all-forbearing).

His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تُبْطِلُوا صَدَقَاتِكُم (O you who have faith! Do not render your charities void...) The verse indicates the nullification (حبط) of charity by the attachment of reminders of generosity and injury. It is sometimes used to argue for the nullification of every sin, or specifically major sins, for the acts of obedience that preceded them. However, there is no indication in the verse for anything other than reminders of generosity and injury in relation to charity. We have already provided an extensive discussion on nullification (الحبط).

His saying, the Exalted: كَالَّذِي يُنفِقُ مَالَهُ رِئَاءَ النَّاسِ وَلَا يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ (...like him who spends his wealth to be seen by people and does not believe in Allah and the Last Day). Since the address is to the believers, and the ostentatious person (المرائي) is not a believer as Allah mentioned—because he does not intend the sake of Allah with his deeds—He did not attach the prohibition to ostentation as He attached it to reminders of generosity and injury. Rather, He only likened the giver of charity who follows his charity with reminders and injury to the ostentatious person in the voiding of the charity, even though the deed of the ostentatious person is void from the start, while the deed of the one who reminds and injures was initially valid and then was overtaken by voidness.

The unity in the tense of the verbs in His saying: يُنفِقُ مَالَهُ (spends his wealth) and His saying: وَلَا يُؤْمِنُ (and does not believe), without saying "and did not believe," indicates that what is meant by the ostentatious person's lack of belief in Allah and the Last Day in his spending is his lack of belief in the call to spend which Allah urges toward and for which He promises a great reward. For if he believed in the Caller in this call of His, and in the Day of Resurrection in which the recompense is manifest, he would have intended the sake of Allah in his act, and would have loved and chosen the great reward, and would not have intended thereby to be seen by people. Thus, what is meant by the ostentatious person's lack of belief is not a total lack of belief in Allah, the Glorified, from the outset.

It appears from the verse that ostentation (الرياء) in an act necessitates a lack of belief in Allah and the Last Day regarding that act.

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَثَلُهُ كَمَثَلِ صَفْوَانٍ عَلَيْهِ تُرَابٌ (his parable is that of a smooth rock with earth upon it...) to the end of the verse. The pronoun in His saying: فَمَثَلُهُ (his parable) refers to the one who spends his wealth to be seen by people, and the parable is for him. Safwan (صفوان) and Safa (صفا) mean a smooth stone, as does Sald (صلد). Wabil (وابل) is heavy, intense rain.

The pronoun in His saying: لَّا يَقْدِرُونَ (they shall not be able) refers to the one who spends ostentatiously because it carries the meaning of a plural. The sentence explains the point of resemblance (وجه الشبه), which is the commonality between the thing likened (المشبه) and the thing likened to (المشبه به). His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكَافِرِينَ (and Allah does not guide the faithless lot) is an explanation of the ruling in a general way, which is that the ostentatious person in his ostentation is among the instances of the disbeliever, and Allah does not guide the faithless lot; therefore, it conveys the meaning of justification.

The summary of the parable's meaning: The state of the ostentatious person in his ostentation and in the resulting reward is like the state of a smooth stone with some earth on it when heavy rain falls upon it. Rain, especially heavy rain, is the prominent cause for the life of the earth, its greenness, and its adornment with plants. However, when earth is on a hard, smooth rock, it does not remain in its place when the heavy rain falls; rather, the rain washes it away, leaving the hard rock which does not absorb water, nor can any seed for a plant be nurtured in it. Thus, although the heavy rain is among the most manifest causes of life and growth, as is the earth, the fact that the location is hard rock nullifies the action of these two causes, without the deficiency or shortcoming being on their part. This is the state of the hard rock.

This is also the state of the ostentatious person: since he did not intend the sake of Allah by his deed, no reward results from his deed, even if the deed—like spending in the way of Allah—is among the prominent causes for the resulting reward. For he is stripped of receptivity; his heart does not accept mercy and honor.

It has become clear from the verse that the acceptance of a deed requires the intention of sincerity (الاخلاص) and intending the sake of Allah. Both groups (Sunni and Shia) have narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he said: "Deeds are only by intentions."

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَثَلُ الَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَالَهُمُ ابْتِغَاءَ مَرْضَاتِ اللَّهِ وَتَثْبِيتًا مِّنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ (The parable of those who spend their wealth seeking Allah’s pleasure and to confirm themselves...) Seeking pleasure (ابتغاء المرضات) is the seeking of approval, and it returns to the intention for the sake of Allah. For the "face" (وجه) of a thing is that with which it faces and meets you. His "face," the Exalted, in relation to His servant whom He has commanded with something and desired from him, is His pleasure with his act and his compliance. For the Commander first meets the one commanded with the command, and when he complies, He meets him with pleasure. Thus, Allah's pleasure with the servant who is charged with a duty is His "face" toward him. Therefore, seeking Allah's pleasure is intending His face, the Mighty and Majestic.

As for His saying: وَتَثْبِيتًا مِّنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ (and to confirm themselves), it has been said that what is meant is affirmation and certainty. It has also been said that it is deliberation (التثبت), meaning they deliberate where they place their wealth. It has been said it is deliberation in spending: if it is for Allah, they proceed; if it is mixed with something of ostentation, they refrain. It has been said "confirmation" is the settling of the soul upon obedience to Allah the Exalted. It has been said it is the strengthening of the soul in the stations of faith by habituating it to giving wealth for the sake of Allah. You are well aware that none of these statements applies to the verse except with forced interpretation.

What ought to be said—and Allah knows best—in this regard is: When Allah the Exalted first spoke generally in praise of spending in the way of Allah and that it has a great reward with Allah, He followed it by excepting two types of spending that Allah the Exalted does not approve of and for which no reward results: spending ostentatiously, which causes the act to be invalid from the start, and spending followed by reminders or injury, for it is nullified by them even if it was initially valid. Voidness only occurs to these two types due to the lack of seeking Allah's pleasure in them from the start, or due to the soul's departure from this intention—I mean seeking pleasure—later after it had it initially. Thus, He intended in this verse to explain the state of the elite among the people of sincere spending, after excepting the ostentatious and the people of reminders and injury; they are those who spend their wealth seeking the face of Allah and then maintain their souls in steadfastness upon this pure, growing intention without following it with that which nullifies and corrupts the deed.

From here it becomes clear that what is meant by "seeking Allah's pleasure" is not intending ostentation and the like which makes the intention not sincere for the sake of Allah. And by His saying "to confirm themselves" is the person's confirming himself upon what he intended of sincere intention, which is a confirmation arising from the soul and acting upon the soul. Thus, His saying "confirmation" (تثبيتاً) is a specification (تمييز), and the word "from" (من) is causal (نشوية), and His saying "themselves" (أنفسهم) is in the meaning of the subject, while that which is in the meaning of the object is omitted. The estimation is: "confirming from their souls for their souls," or it is a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق) for a verb from its root.

His saying, the Exalted: كَمَثَلِ جَنَّةٍ بِرَبْوَةٍ أَصَابَهَا وَابِلٌ (...is that of a garden on a high ground: a heavy rain falls upon it...) The root of the material raba (ربا) is "increase." Rabwah (ربوة), with the three vowels on the ra, is good land that increases and rises in its growth. Ukul (أُكل), with two dammahs, is what is eaten of a thing, and the singular is uklah. Tall (طل) is the weakest of rain with little effect.

The purpose of the parable is that spending intended for the sake of Allah never fails to produce its good effect, for divine care is placed upon it and attached to it because of the preservation of its connection to Allah, the Glorified, even if the levels of care differ due to the different degrees of sincerity in intention and the different weight of deeds according to them. Just as the garden on high ground, when rain falls upon it, never fails to yield its fruit in a goodly manner, even if its yielding differs in quality according to the rain falling upon it, whether heavy rain or a drizzle.

Due to the existence of this difference, He concluded the speech with His saying: وَاللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (and Allah sees best what you do), meaning the matter of reward is not obscured from Him, nor does the reward of different deeds become confused for Him such that He gives the reward of this for that and the reward of that for this.

His saying, the Exalted: أَيَوَدُّ أَحَدُكُمْ أَن تَكُونَ لَهُ جَنَّةٌ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَأَعْنَابٍ (Would any of you like to have a garden of palm trees and vines...) Love (الود) is affection and contains the meaning of wishing. Jannah (الجنة) is many intertwined trees like a garden; it was named so because it covers (تجن) the ground, hides it, and protects it from sunlight and the like. Therefore, it is correct to say: "rivers flowing from beneath it." If it were the land itself with its trees, for example, that would not be correct as it would convey the opposite of the intended meaning. Thus, Allah said regarding the like of the rabwah, which is cultivated land: رَبْوَةٍ ذَاتِ قَرَارٍ وَمَعِينٍ (...a high ground, level and with flowing water) (al-Mu'minun: 50), and He repeated in His speech His saying: "gardens from beneath which rivers flow," thus making the flowing water (المعين) in them, not flowing beneath them.

The "from" (من) in His saying: مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَأَعْنَابٍ (of palm trees and vines) is for clarification (للتبيين) and conveys the meaning of predominance rather than exhaustiveness. For a jannah, a garden, and what is of this sort is only attributed to the predominant genus; thus it is said "a garden of grapes" or "a garden of vines" if the predominant part is the vineyard, while it is not devoid of various other trees. Therefore, Allah said secondly: لَهُ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ الثَّمَرَاتِ (...with all kinds of fruit in it for him).

Old age (الكبر) is advanced age, which is senility. Offspring (الذرية) are children, and the weak (الضعفاء) is the plural of weak. Allah has combined in the parable the onset of old age and the presence of weak offspring to establish the absolute, desperate need for the aforementioned garden, along with the loss of all other means that are reached for preserving the happiness of life and securing a livelihood. For if the owner of the garden were assumed to be a strong youth, he could rely on the strength of his right hand if his garden were struck by a calamity. If he were assumed to be a decrepit old man without weak offspring, his state would not be so bad because he would see for himself only a few days whose passing and end would not be slow for him. If he were assumed to be old but had strong children capable of working and earning a living, they could sustain themselves with what they earn and be independent of it in some way! But when old age and weak offspring are combined there, and the garden is burned, the means are cut off from them at that point. Neither can the owner of the garden restore youth and strength to himself or the bygone days so that he may prepare for himself the like of what he had prepared, nor do his offspring have the strength for that, nor do they have hope that the garden will return after the burning to its former state of freshness and fruitfulness.

A whirlwind (الاعصار) is dust that twists upon itself between the sky and the earth, just as a garment twists upon itself when wrung (العصر).

This is a parable Allah struck for those who spend their wealth and then follow it with reminders and injury, thus nullifying their deed, with no way for them to restore the voided deed to its state of validity and integrity. The application of the parable to the thing represented is manifest. He hoped for reflection (التفكر) from them because such actions that corrupt deeds only arise from people when they have psychological states like love of wealth and status, pride, vanity, and stinginess, which do not leave room for the person to deliberate, reflect, and distinguish the beneficial from the harmful. If they reflected, they would see clearly.

His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنفِقُوا مِن طَيِّبَاتِ مَا كَسَبْتُمْ (O you who have faith! Spend of the good things you have earned...) Tayammum (التيمم) is to intend and aim. Vile (الخبيث) is the opposite of good (الطيب). His saying: "from it" (منه) is related to "vile." His saying: "you spend" (تنفقون) is a circumstantial clause (حال) from the subject of "do not aim." His saying: "while you would not take it" (ولستم بآخذيه) is a circumstantial clause from the subject of "you spend," and its operator is the verb. His saying: "unless you were to overlook it" (أن تغمضوا فيه) is in the interpretation of a verbal noun (مصدر). The "lam" is estimated, as is said, and the estimation is: "except for your overlooking it," or the estimated part is the "ba" of accompaniment, and the estimation is: "except with the accompaniment of overlooking."

The meaning of the verse is manifest. Allah has only explained the quality of the wealth for spending, and that it ought to be from the good of the wealth, not from the vile which the spender would not take except by overlooking. For it is not characterized by the attribute of generosity and bounty; rather, it is conceived in the form of riddance. Thus, it does not produce love for the good deed and favor, nor perfection for the soul. Therefore, He concluded it with His saying: وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ غَنِيٌّ حَمِيدٌ (and know that Allah is all-sufficient, all-laudable), meaning observe in your spending His self-sufficiency and His praise; for He, in the very essence of His self-sufficiency, praises your good spending, so spend from the good of the wealth. Or, He is All-Sufficient and All-Laudable; it is not fitting that you meet Him with what is not worthy of His majesty, exalted be His majesty.

His saying, the Exalted: الشَّيْطَانُ يَعِدُكُمُ الْفَقْرَ وَيَأْمُرُكُم بِالْفَحْشَاءِ (Satan frightens you of poverty and prompts you to indecency...) This is an establishment of the proof that choosing the vile wealth for spending is not good for the spenders, unlike choosing the good, which is good for them. Thus, in the prohibition lies the benefit of their affair, just as in the prohibited act lies their corruption. Their withholding from spending the good of the wealth and giving it is only because they see it as influential in the sustenance of wealth and riches; thus their souls shrink from proceeding to give it, unlike the vile, for it has no value they care about, so there is no harm in spending it. This is from the embellishment of Satan, who frightens his allies with poverty, even though giving, the departure of wealth, and spending in the way of Allah and seeking His pleasure—like giving in transactions—is not devoid of compensation and profit as mentioned before. Furthermore, the One who enriches and gives possessions is Allah the Glorified, not wealth. He said: وَأَنَّهُ هُوَ أَغْنَىٰ وَأَقْنَىٰ (and that it is He who enriches and gives possessions) (al-Najm: 48).

In short, since their withholding from giving the good of the wealth out of fear of poverty was an error, He pointed it out by His saying: "Satan frightens you of poverty." However, He placed the cause in the position of the effect; I mean He placed the promise of Satan in the position of the fear of their souls to indicate that it is a fear harmful to them, for Satan only commands falsehood and misguidance, either initially and without intermediary, or ultimately and through what appears to be truth.

Since it was possible to imagine that this fear is true even if it is from the side of Satan, He repelled that by following His saying: "Satan frightens you of poverty" with His saying: "and prompts you to indecency" firstly. For this withholding and sluggishness on their part prepares in their souls the faculty of withholding and the disposition of stinginess, leading to the rejection of Allah's commands related to their wealth, which is disbelief in Allah the Great. It also leads to casting the people of need into the destruction of hardship, poverty, and destitution, in which lies the loss of souls, the violation of honor, and every crime and indecency. He said: وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ عَاهَدَ اللَّهَ لَئِنْ آتَانَا مِن فَضْلِهِ لَنَصَّدَّقَنَّ وَلَنكُونَنَّ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ فَلَمَّا آتَاهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِ بَخِلُوا بِهِ وَتَوَلَّوا وَّهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ فَأَعْقَبَهُمْ نِفَاقًا فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يَلْقَوْنَهُ بِمَا أَخْلَفُوا اللَّهَ مَا وَعَدُوهُ وَبِمَا كَانُوا يَكْذِبُونَ (Among them are those who made a pledge with Allah: ‘If He gives us out of His grace, we will surely give the zakat and we will surely be among the righteous.’ But when He gave them out of His grace, they were stingy with it and they turned away, being averse. So He caused hypocrisy to ensue in their hearts until the day they will encounter Him, because they failed Allah in what they had promised Him and because they used to lie.) until He said: الَّذِينَ يَلْمِزُونَ الْمُطَّوِّعِينَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ فِي الصَّدَقَاتِ وَالَّذِينَ لَا يَجِدُونَ إِلَّا جُهْدَهُمْ فَيَسْخَرُونَ مِنْهُمْ سَخِرَ اللَّهُ مِنْهُمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (Those who blame the voluntary givers among the faithful regarding the charities, and those who find nothing [to give] but [the fruit of] their endeavor, and then they ridicule them—Allah shall ridicule them, and there is a painful punishment for them.) (al-Tawbah: 75–79).

Then, by following it with His saying: وَاللَّهُ يَعِدُكُم مَّغْفِرَةً مِّنْهُ وَفَضْلًا وَاللَّهُ وَاسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (while Allah promises you His forgiveness and grace; and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing) secondly. For Allah has explained to the believers that there is truth and misguidance with no third option, and that the truth—which is the straight path—is from Allah the Glorified, and that misguidance is from Satan. He said: فَمَاذَا بَعْدَ الْحَقِّ إِلَّا الضَّلَالُ (So what is there after the truth except error?) (Yunus: 32). And He said: قُلِ اللَّهُ يَهْدِي لِلْحَقِّ (Say, ‘Allah guides to the truth.’) (Yunus: 35). And He said regarding Satan: إِنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ مُّضِلٌّ مُّبِينٌ (Indeed he is a manifest, misleading enemy.) (al-Qasas: 15). All these verses are Meccan. In short, He pointed out by His saying: "and Allah promises you" that this thought that occurs to your mind from the side of fear is a misguidance of thought, for Allah's forgiveness and the increase He mentioned in the previous verses are only in giving from the good things of wealth.

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: "and Allah promises you..." is like His saying: "Satan frightens you...", of the type of placing the cause in the position of the effect. In it is the presentation of the contrast between the promise of Allah, the All-Bounteous, All-Knowing, and the promise of Satan, so that the spenders may look into the matter of the two promises and choose what is more beneficial for their state from them.

The summary of the verse's proof: Your choosing the vile over the good is only due to the fear of poverty and ignorance of what this spending entails. As for the fear of poverty, it is a Satanic suggestion, and Satan only wants for you misguidance and indecency, so it is not permissible for you to follow him. As for what this spending entails, it is the increase and forgiveness that were mentioned to you in the previous verses, and it is an entailment by truth because the One who promises you the entailment of spending with this forgiveness and increase is Allah the Glorified, and His promise is true. He is All-Bounteous; it is within His capacity to give what He promised of forgiveness and increase. And He is All-Knowing; He is not ignorant of anything or any state of a thing, so His promise is a promise based on knowledge.

His saying, the Exalted: يُؤْتِي الْحِكْمَةَ مَن يَشَاءُ (He gives wisdom to whomever He wishes). Ita (الإيتاء) is giving. Wisdom (الحكمة), with a kasrah on the ha, is on the pattern of fi'lah, a structure indicating the type of meaning; thus its meaning is the type of precision and perfection (الإحكام والإتقان), or a type of precise and perfected matter in which no breach or weakness is found. Its usage has become predominant in true, sincere rational knowledge that accepts neither voidness nor falsehood at all.

The sentence indicates that the explanation by which Allah explained the state of spending—by gathering its causes, its reasons, and the righteous effect it entails in the reality of human life—is from wisdom. Thus, wisdom consists of true propositions corresponding to reality in terms of their encompassing, in some way, the happiness of man, such as the true divine knowledge regarding the Origin and the Return, and the knowledge that explains the realities of the natural world in terms of its contact with human happiness, such as the primordial realities (الحقائق الفطرية) which are the basis of religious legislations.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَن يُؤْتَ الْحِكْمَةَ فَقَدْ أُوتِيَ خَيْرًا كَثِيرًا (and he who is given wisdom, is certainly given an abundant good). The meaning is manifest.
