For we only intend by our actions their goals and results whose realization is predominant, and we create, when intending them, their causes from which their effects are not separated in most cases, though not always. We treat the rare exception as non-existent. As for the complete causes (العلل التامة) from which their effects cannot possibly be separated in existence, they are specific to the realm of creation (التكوين) and are dealt with by the real sciences (العلوم الحقيقية) that research external realities.

Contemplation of the verses of judgments (آيات الاحكام) in which the benefits and harms of deeds and actions are mentioned—leading to felicity or wretchedness—shows that the Quran, in building the effects of deeds upon the deeds and building the deeds upon their causes, follows this path and places the predominant in the position of the constant, as is the practice of the rational.

Second: That society is like the individual, and the social matter is like the individual matter; they are similar in states according to what suits each in terms of existence. Just as the individual has a life, a lifespan, a fixed death, and actions and effects, so too does society have its life, death, lifespan, actions, and effects. The Quran speaks of this, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا وَلَهَا كِتَابٌ مَّعْلُومٌ مَّا تَسْبِقُ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلَهَا وَمَا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ — "We have not destroyed any town but that it had a known term. No community can advance its term, nor can they defer it" (15:4-5).

Accordingly, if the description of a matter changes from individual to social, the manner of its persistence, disappearance, and effect changes. Individual chastity and individual profligacy, while they are individual, have a type of influence on life; for committing indecency, for example, causes people to be repelled by the person, to avoid marrying him or sitting with him, and leads to the loss of trust in his honesty—this is when it is an individual matter and society is contrary to it. But when it becomes social and well-known among the public, those deterrents disappear because they were consequences of public disapproval and general loathing of the act, which circulation and prevalence have removed. However, the natural corruptions—such as the cessation of lineage, venereal diseases, and other social corruptions that nature does not accept, like the nullification and disorder of lineages and the corruption of communal branches and the social benefits resulting from them—are inevitably resulting from it. Similarly, the appearance of effects in the individual when it was individual differs from their appearance in society when it is social in terms of speed and slowness.

Once you know this, you will know that His annihilation of usury in contrast to His increasing of charities inevitably differs between when the act is an individual act—like usury practiced by a person, for it usually destroys its practitioner, and rarely does a usurer escape it due to the presence of specific causes and factors that ward off extinction and humiliation from the arena of his life—and when it is a social act, like the usury circulating today which nations and states recognize officially, for which laws have been established, and upon which banks have been founded. For it loses some of its individual characteristics due to the society's acceptance of what has become prevalent and customary among them, and the turning of souls away from thinking about its defects. However, its necessary effects—such as the accumulation and concentration of public wealth on one side, and the occurrence of public poverty and deprivation on the other, and the appearance of complete separation and gap between the two groups: the wealthy and the destitute—are inseparable from this usury and will surely produce their evil, ill-omened effect. This type of appearance and manifestation, although we may perceive it as slow from an individual perspective and perhaps disregard it by treating it as non-existent due to the length of time, is immediate from a social perspective; for the social lifespan is not like the individual lifespan, and a social day may equal an age in the view of an individual. The Exalted said: وَتِلْكَ الْأَيَّامُ نُدَاوِلُهَا بَيْنَ النَّاسِ — "We make such days alternate among mankind" (3:140). This "day" refers to the era in which some people prevail over others, one group over another, one government over another, and one nation over another. It is clear that just as the felicity of man must be cared for in terms of the individual, it must be cared for in terms of the social species.

The Quran does not speak only of the individual nor only in the individual, though it is not silent about him. Rather, it is a Book revealed by Allah the Exalted as a guardian over the felicity of man: his species and his individual, and as a witness over the felicity of the world: its present and its past.

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: "Allah brings usury to naught and gives increase to charities" explains the state of usury and charity in their effects, whether they are social or individual. Annihilation is among the necessary consequences of usury, inseparable from it, just as increase is among the necessary consequences of charity, inseparable from it. Thus, usury is annihilated even if it is called "increase" (رباً), and charity is a truly increasing usury even if it is not called usury. To this He, the Exalted, points: "Allah brings usury to naught and gives increase to charities" by giving the description of usury to charities of all kinds, and describing usury with a description that contradicts its name in terms of meaning, which is annihilation.

From the preceding explanation, the weakness of what some mentioned becomes clear: that the annihilation of usury does not mean the nullification of effort and the loss of work through the disappearance of usurious wealth, for observation and sight refute this; rather, what is meant by annihilation is the nullification of effort in terms of the goals intended by this type of transaction. For the usurer intends by collecting wealth in this way the pleasure of ease, the goodness of life, and the enjoyment of living, but he is distracted from that by the obsession with collecting wealth and placing dirham upon dirham, and the struggle against those who intend evil for him, his wealth, or his profits, and the anxieties attacking his soul from the enmity of people and the hatred of the needy for him. The reason for its weakness is clear.

Similarly, what others mentioned: that what is meant by it is the annihilation of the Hereafter and the reward of deeds from which the usurer turns away by his preoccupation with usury, or which the use of usurious wealth nullifies, such as types of worship. The reason for its weakness is that there is no doubt that what they mentioned is part of the annihilation, but there is no evidence for its restriction to that.

Similarly, the weakness of what the Mu'tazilites used as an argument for the eternal punishment of the committer of a major sin in the Fire by His saying: "and whoever resumes...", and what has passed clarifies both the establishment of the argument and its refutation.

His saying, the Exalted: إِن الله لا يحب كل كفار أثيم — "Allah does not like any sinful ingrate" (2:276). This is an explanation (تعليل) for the annihilation of usury in a universal way. The meaning is that the devourer of usury is a great ingrate (كفار) due to his ingratitude for many of Allah's blessings by his veiling the natural paths in human life—which are the paths of natural transactions—and his disbelief in many judgments regarding the prescribed acts of worship and transactions. For by spending usurious wealth on his food, drink, clothing, and dwelling, he nullifies many of his acts of worship due to the loss of conditions required in them. By using the usurious wealth in his hand, he nullifies many of his transactions, becomes liable for others, and usurps the wealth of others in many instances. By employing greed and avarice regarding people's wealth, and harshness and cruelty in exacting what he considers his right, he corrupts many of the foundations of ethics and virtues and their branches. He is "sinful" (أثيم)—one in whose soul sin has settled. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, does not love him because Allah does not love any sinful ingrate.

His saying, the Exalted: إِن الذين آمنوا وعملوا الصالحات — "Indeed, those who have faith and do righteous deeds..." (2:277). This is an explanation by which the reward of the charitable and those who desist from what Allah has forbidden of devouring usury is clarified in a general way that applies to the case perfectly.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أيها الذين آمنوا اتقوا الله وذروا ما بقي من الربا ان كنتم مؤمنين — "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and abandon what remains of usury, if you are believers" (2:278). This is an address to the believers and a command to them to be wary of Allah, which is a preamble to what follows of the command: "and abandon what remains of usury." It indicates that among the believers at the time of the revelation of the verses were those who took usury and had remnants of it owed by people; thus, they were commanded to abandon them, and were threatened regarding that by what follows of His saying: "But if you do not, then be informed of a war from Allah and His Apostle."

This supports what we will narrate of the narration regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) of the verse in the following tradition-based discussion.

In restricting the discourse with His saying: "if you are believers," there is an indication that abandoning it is among the requirements of faith, and a confirmation of what preceded of His saying: "and whoever resumes...", and His saying: "Allah does not like any sinful ingrate."

His saying, the Exalted: وإِن لم تفعلوا فأذنوا بحرب من الله ورسوله — "But if you do not, then be informed of a war from Allah and His Apostle" (2:279). The word "اذن" (be informed) is like "علم" (know) in weight and meaning. It has been recited as "فآذنوا" as a command from "ايذان" (to inform). The "ب" in His saying "بحرب" (of a war) is to imply the meaning of certainty and the like. The meaning is: be certain of a war, or inform yourselves with certainty of a war from Allah and His Apostle. The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "war" is to indicate its greatness or its variety. The attribution of the war to Allah and His Apostle is because it is connected to the judgment in which Allah the Glorified has a share through establishment and legislation, and His Apostle has a share through transmission. If it were from Allah alone, it would be a creative (تكويني) matter; as for His Apostle, he does not act independently in any matter apart from Allah the Glorified. The Exalted said: لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ شَيْءٌ — "You have no hand in the matter" (3:128).

War from Allah and His Apostle regarding one of the judgments against one who does not submit to it is the enforcement of the judgment upon the one who rejects it among the Muslims through fighting, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: فَقَاتِلُوا الَّتِي تَبْغِي حَتَّىٰ تَفِيءَ إِلَىٰ أَمْرِ اللَّهِ — "then fight the one that is rebellious until it reverts to Allah’s command" (49:9). Furthermore, Allah the Exalted has another way of defending His judgment, which is His warring against them through the path of nature—by inciting the general nature against them, which cuts off their breaths, destroys their homes, and erases their traces. The Exalted said: وَإِذَا أَرَدْنَا أَن نُّهْلِكَ قَرْيَةً أَمَرْنَا مُتْرَفِيهَا فَفَسَقُوا فِيهَا فَحقَّ عَلَيْهَا الْقَوْلُ فَدَمَّرْنَاهَا تَدْمِيرًا — "When We desire to destroy a town, We command its affluent ones [to obey Us]. But they commit transgression in it, and so the word becomes due against it, and We destroy it utterly" (17:16).

His saying, the Exalted: وإِن تبتم فلكم رؤس أموالكم لا تظلمون ولا تظلمون — "And if you repent, then you shall have your principal, neither tyrannizing nor being tyrannized" (2:279). The word "And if you repent" supports what has passed—that the address in the verse is to some of the believers who were taking usury and had remnants owed by their debtors and clients. His saying: "then you shall have your principal" (رؤس أموالكم) means the original amounts of your wealth free from usury. "Neither tyrannizing" by taking usury, "nor being tyrannized" by encroachment upon your principal. In the verse, there is an indication of the confirmation of the origin of ownership (الملك) first; and that taking usury is an injustice (ظلم) as previously mentioned second; and the confirmation of types of transactions where He expressed it as "your principal" (رؤس أموالكم), for wealth is only called "principal" (رأس مال) when it is spent in the ways of transactions and types of earning third.

His saying, the Exalted: وإِن كان ذو عسرة فنظرة إِلى ميسرة — "And if [the debtor] is in financial difficulty, let there be a respite until a time of ease" (2:280). The word "كان" is complete (تامة), meaning: if there exists one in difficulty. "النظرة" is the respite (المهلة). "الميسرة" is ease (اليسار) and ability, the opposite of difficulty. That is: if a debtor (غريم) among your debtors is found who is unable to pay his due debt, then give him respite and delay him until he is able and possesses ease, then he may pay his debt.

The verse, although absolute and not restricted, applies to the case of usury. For they used to, when the term of the debt arrived, demand it from the debtor. The debtor would say to his creditor: "Increase my term by such a duration, and I will increase the price for you by such a ratio." The verse forbids this usurious increase and commands respite.

His saying, the Exalted: وان تصدقوا خير لكم إِن كنتم تعلمون — "And that you should give it as charity is better for you, if you knew" (2:280). That is: if you remit the debt for the one in difficulty and give it as charity to him, it is better for you if you knew; for you have then replaced what you intended of increase through the path of annihilated usury with the increase through the path of truly increasing charity.

His saying, the Exalted: واتقوا يوما ترجعون فيه إِلى الله — "And beware of a day in which you will be brought back to Allah" (2:281). In this is a concluding remark (تذييل) for the verses of usury, including the judgment and the recompense, by a general reminder of the Day of Resurrection with some of its descriptions suited to the context. Its mention prepares the souls for the godwariness (تقوى) of Allah and the refraining from His prohibitions regarding the rights of people upon which life relies. It is that before you is a day in which you will be brought back to Allah, then every soul will be paid in full what it has earned, and they will not be wronged.

As for the meaning of this "return" despite our not being absent from Allah, and the meaning of this "paying in full," the discussion on it will come in the commentary on Surah Al-An'am, Allah the Exalted willing.

It has been said that this verse: "And beware of a day in which you will be brought back to Allah, then every soul will be paid in full what it has earned, and they will not be wronged," is the last verse revealed to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). What indicates this from the narrations will come in the following tradition-based discussion.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Those who devour usury...", narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'When I was taken on the Night Journey (أُسري بي) to the heavens, I saw a people, one of whom would want to rise but could not rise due to the greatness of his belly. I said: Who are these, O Gabriel? He said: These are those who devour usury; they shall not rise except like one whom Satan has confounded by his touch. And behold, they are on the path of the people of Pharaoh: they are exposed to the Fire morning and evening, and they say: Our Lord, when will the Hour arrive?'"

I say: This is a barzakh-related (برزخي) — pertaining to the intermediate realm between death and resurrection — example and a confirmation of his saying (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "As you live, you shall die; and as you die, you shall be resurrected."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Asbahani narrated in his Targhib from Anas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'The devourer of usury will come on the Day of Resurrection as a madman (مختبلاً) dragging his sides.' Then he recited: 'they shall not rise except like one whom Satan has confounded by his touch.'"

I say: Many narrations have been reported regarding the punishment for usury through the paths of the Shi'a and the Sunnis. In some of them, it is stated that it is equivalent to seventy acts of adultery committed by the usurer with his mother.

In al-Tahdhib, through his chain of narrators from 'Umar ibn Yazid, the seller of Sabiri-cloth, who said: "I said to Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'May I be your ransom! People claim that profit (الربح) made from a person in distress (المضطر) is forbidden (حرام).'" He replied: "Have you ever seen anyone, whether rich or poor, buy anything except out of necessity (ضرورة)? O 'Umar, Allah has made trade lawful and forbidden usury; so take a profit, but do not practice usury." I asked: "And what is usury (الربا)?" He said: "Dirhams for dirhams, two for one; and wheat for wheat, two for one."

In al-Faqih, through his chain of narrators from 'Ubayd ibn Zurarah, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Usury (الربا) only occurs in that which is measured by volume (يكال) or weighed (يوزن)."

I say: There has been disagreement regarding the items in which usury occurs, resulting in various opinions. The doctrine of the People of the House (peace be upon them) is that it only occurs in the two currencies [gold and silver] and in what is measured or weighed. This is a matter of jurisprudence (فقهية), and our purpose here only concerns it to this extent.

In al-Kafi, from one of the two [Imams al-Baqir or al-Sadiq], and in the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: فَمَن جَاءَهُ مَوْعِظَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِ فَانتَهَى — "So whoever receives an admonition from his Lord and desists" (2:275), he said: "The admonition (الموعظة) is repentance (التوبة)."

In al-Tahdhib, from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: "A man from the people of Khurasan came to Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him). He had dealt in usury until his wealth became vast. He then asked the jurists (الفقهاء), and they said: 'Nothing will be accepted from you until you return it to its owners.' He then came to Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) and related his story to him. Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: 'Your way out is from the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: فَمَن جَاءَهُ مَوْعِظَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِ فَانتَهَى فَلَهُ مَا سَلَفَ وَأَمْرُهُ إِلَى اللَّهِ — "So whoever receives an admonition from his Lord and desists, he shall keep what is past, and his case will rest with Allah." He said: 'The admonition is repentance.'"

In al-Kafi and al-Faqih, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Every instance of usury that people consumed out of ignorance (جهالة) and then repented, it shall be accepted from them once their repentance (التوبة) is known." He also said: "If a man inherits wealth from his father and knows that there is usury in that wealth, but it has been mixed with other funds in trade, then it is lawful (حلال) for him and he may consume it. But if he recognizes a specific, known portion of it, he should take his principal (رأس ماله) and return the excess (الزيادة)."

In al-Faqih and al-'Uyun, from al-Rida (peace be upon him): "It is a major sin (كبيرة) after the clarification (البيان)." He said: "And to treat that with light regard is an entry into disbelief (الكفر)."

In al-Kafi: He was asked about a man who consumes usury while believing it to be lawful. He said: "It does not harm him until he does it intentionally (متعمداً). Once he does it intentionally, he is in the position described by Allah, the Mighty and Majestic."

In al-Kafi and al-Faqih, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), having been asked about the saying of the Exalted: يَمْحَقُ اللَّهُ الرِّبَا وَيُرْبِي الصَّدَقَاتِ — "Allah brings usury to naught and He makes charities flourish" (2:276). It was said to him: "I see one who consumes usury and his wealth increases (يربو)?" He replied: "What 'bringing to naught' (محق) is more destructive than a dirham of usury? It brings the religion (الدين) to naught, and even if he repents, his wealth departs and he becomes impoverished."

I say: The narration, as you see, interprets the legislative nullification (المحق التشريعي) — meaning the lack of recognition of ownership and the prohibition — and charity stands in contrast to it in this regard. This does not contradict what was previously mentioned regarding the universality of this nullification.

In al-Majma', from 'Ali (peace be upon him): "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) cursed five types of people regarding usury: the one who consumes it, the one who pays it, its two witnesses, and its scribe."

I say: This meaning has been narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur through various chains from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم).

In the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him), who said: "Allah the Exalted said: 'I am the Creator of everything, and I have entrusted things to others besides Me, except for charity (الصدقة); for I receive it with My own hand. Even if a man or woman gives half a date in charity, I nurture it (أربيها) for them just as one of you nurtures his weaned foal (فصيله) or young colt (فلوه), until I return it to him on the Day of Resurrection greater than Mount Uhud.'"

In the same source, from 'Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon them), from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), who said: "Indeed, Allah nurtures charity for one of you just as one of you nurtures his child, until he meets Him on the Day of Resurrection and it is like Uhud."

I say: This meaning has been narrated through Sunni chains from several companions, such as Abu Hurayrah, 'Aishah, Ibn 'Umar, and Abu Barzah al-Aslami, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: When Allah revealed: الَّذِينَ يَأْكُلُونَ الرِّبَا — "Those who exact usury..." (2:275), Khalid ibn al-Walid stood before the Messenger of Allah and said: "O Messenger of Allah, my father's usury is with the tribe of Thaqif, and he charged me at his death to collect it." Then Allah revealed: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَذَرُوا مَا بَقِيَ مِنَ الرِّبَا — "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and abandon [your claims to] what remains of usury..." (2:278).

I say: A similar account is narrated in al-Majma' from al-Baqir (peace be upon him).

In al-Majma' also, from al-Suddi and 'Ikrimah, they said: "It was revealed concerning a remainder of usury belonging to al-'Abbas and Khalid ibn al-Walid. They were partners during the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية), lending with usury to the Banu 'Amr ibn 'Umayr, a group from Thaqif. When Islam came, they had vast amounts of wealth in usury. So Allah revealed this verse, and the Prophet said: 'Behold! Every instance of usury from the Age of Ignorance is abolished (موضوع), and the first usury I abolish is the usury of al-'Abbas ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib. And every blood-feud from the Age of Ignorance is abolished, and the first blood-feud I abolish is the blood of Rabi'ah ibn al-Harith ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib, who was being nursed among the Banu Layth and was killed by Hudhayl.'"

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim, from al-Suddi, except that it says: "It was revealed concerning al-'Abbas ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib and a man from the Banu al-Mughirah."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it authentic), al-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan narrated from 'Amr ibn al-Ahwas: "That he attended the Farewell Pilgrimage with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), who said: 'Behold! Every instance of usury from the Age of Ignorance is abolished. You shall have your principal wealth (رؤوس أموالكم); you shall neither wrong nor be wronged.'"

I say: The narrations regarding these meanings are numerous. What is gathered from the narrations of both the elite (الخاصة) [Shi'a] and the general (العامة) [Sunni] traditions is that the verse was revealed concerning usurious wealth owed to the Banu al-Mughirah by the Thaqif. They used to practice usury with them during the Age of Ignorance. When Islam came, they demanded the remainders owed to them, but the others refused to pay because Islam had abolished it. The matter was raised to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), and the verse was revealed.

This supports what we previously stated in the Commentary: that usury was forbidden in Islam before the revelation of these verses and had been clarified to the people, and that these verses only emphasize and affirm the prohibition. Therefore, no weight is given to some reports suggesting that the prohibition of usury was only revealed at the end of the Messenger of Allah's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) life and that he passed away without clarifying the matter of usury to the people—as found in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Jarir and Ibn Mardawayh from 'Umar ibn al-Khattab: "That he gave a sermon and said: 'Among the last of the Qur'an to be revealed was the verse of usury, and the Messenger of Allah died without clarifying it to us; so leave what makes you doubt for what does not make you doubt.'"

Furthermore, it is the doctrine of the Imams of the People of the House (peace be upon them) that Allah the Exalted did not take His Prophet until He had legislated everything the people needed regarding their religion, and His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) had clarified that to the people.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, through numerous chains from Ibn 'Abbas, al-Suddi, 'Atiyyah al-'Awfi, Abu Salih, and Sa'id ibn Jubayr: "The last verse revealed of the Qur'an was the saying of the Exalted: وَاتَّقُوا يَوْمًا تُرْجَعُونَ فِيهِ إِلَى اللَّهِ — 'And beware of a day in which you will be brought back to Allah...'" (2:281).

In al-Majma', from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The prohibition of usury was made so severe only so that people would not refrain from performing acts of kindness (اصطناع المعروف) through lending (قرضاً) or assistance (رفداً)."

In al-Majma' also, from 'Ali (peace be upon him): "When Allah intends the destruction of a town, usury becomes manifest among them."

I say: The meaning of these narrations was clarified in the preceding Commentary.

In the same source, regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَإِن كَانَ ذُو عُسْرَةٍ فَنَظِرَةٌ إِلَى مَيْسَرَةٍ — "And if [the debtor] is in financial difficulty, let there be a respite until a time of ease" (2:280), he said: "There is disagreement regarding the limit of insolvency (الإعسار). It is narrated from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) that he said: 'It is when he is unable to provide what exceeds his own sustenance and the sustenance of his family with moderation (الاقتصاد).'"

In the same source: "Granting respite to the insolvent (المعسر) is obligatory (واجب) for every debt." This is from Ibn 'Abbas, al-Dahhak, and al-Hasan, and it is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon them).

In the same source: Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: "إِلَى مَيْسَرَةٍ (until a time of ease) means when news of him reaches the Imam, who then pays on his behalf from the share of the debtors (سهم الغارمين), provided he spent it on what is right (المعروف)."

In al-Kafi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah ascended the pulpit one day, praised and glorified Allah, invoked blessings upon His prophets, and then said: 'O people! Let those present among you inform those who are absent. Behold! Whoever grants respite to an insolvent person, he shall have from Allah every day a charity (صدقة) equal to his wealth until he recovers it.' Then Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: 'وَإِن كَانَ ذُو عُسْرَةٍ فَنَظِرَةٌ إِلَى مَيْسَرَةٍ وَأَن تَصَدَّقُوا خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ — "And if [the debtor] is in financial difficulty, let there be a respite until a time of ease. And that you remit it as charity is better for you, should you know" (2:280)—if you know he is insolvent, then remit your wealth to him as charity, for that is better for you.'"

I say: The narration contains an interpretation of His saying: إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (should you know). Another meaning for it has already been mentioned. The narrations regarding these meanings and what pertains to them are numerous, and the reference for them is the "Book of Debt" (كتاب الدين) in jurisprudence.

( A Scientific Discussion )

It has been mentioned repeatedly in previous discussions that man has no concern in his life except to perform those actions that acquire for him his existential perfections (كمالاته الوجودية); in other words, to fulfill his material needs. Thus, he performs an action related to matter in some way, through which he fulfills a vital need. He is the owner (مالك) of his labor and what he has produced—labor (العمل) in this context is broader than active doing or passive undergoing; it is a relation (نسبة) and a link (رابطة) upon which social effects are based among people. That is, he reserves the material he has worked upon for himself and considers it property (ملكاً) in which he is permitted to dispose as an individual. The rational people (العقلاء) of society permit him this; so understand.

However, since he cannot fulfill all his needs through his own labor alone, this led to cooperative society (الاجتماع التعاوني) and to each person benefiting from the labor of others and what others have acquired and owned through their labor. This led to exchange (المعاوضة) between them. This became established by man working in one field or a limited number of fields of labor, thereby owning things, then taking the amount that fulfills his need and exchanging the surplus for what he lacks from the property of others. This is the origin of transaction (المعاملة) and exchange.

However, the total disparity between goods and commodities in terms of type, the intensity or weakness of the need for them, and their abundance or scarcity, created difficulties in exchange. For instance, fruit is for eating, a donkey for carrying, water for drinking, and a precious gem for adornment. These have different weights and values in the needs of life, and different ratios to one another.

Thus, the need arose to establish value (القيمة) by instituting coins (الفلوس), dirhams, and dinars. The basis for their institution was that they took something from the commodities that were rare, such as gold, and made it a standard (أصل) to which all other commodities and goods were referred. It became like a complete unit of a species used as a measure for the rest of its members, like weights (المثاقيل) and measures (المكائيل). Thus, a single unit of currency was used to estimate general value, and every commodity was priced by it. Through this, the ratio of each commodity to the currency, and the ratio of commodities to one another, was determined.

Then, to generalize the benefit, they established units of measurement for things, such as the unit of length like the cubit (الذراع), the unit of volume like the kayl (الكيل), and the unit of weight like the mann (المن). At that point, ratios were determined, ambiguity was removed, and it became clear, for example, that a carat of diamond equals four dinars, and a mann of wheat flour equals one-tenth of a dinar. Thus, it became clear that a carat of diamond equals forty manns of wheat flour, and so on.

They then expanded by instituting other currencies from various materials, whether precious or cheap, for ease and expansion, such as silver, copper, bronze, and paper money (الورق والنوط), as explained in books of economics.

Subsequently, the door of earning (الكسب) and trade (التجارة) was opened after buying and selling became widespread, by certain individuals specializing their labor and occupation in exchange—exchanging one type of commodity for another seeking profit (الربح), which is a type of increase in what one receives in exchange for the commodity one gives.

These are actions man has put forward to fulfill his needs in life. Eventually, the matter settled such that public need seemed to revolve around the door of the dirham and dinar. The medium of value became as if it were the entirety of wealth, and as if it were every commodity man needs, because by obtaining it, man is able to obtain everything he desires and needs for enjoyment in life. Sometimes it was treated as a commodity itself and traded for profit, as one trades in other goods; this is money-changing (الصرف).

From what has preceded, it is clear that the origin of transaction and exchange was established on the basis of exchanging one commodity for another different (مغاير) from it, due to the pressing need for the substitute, as in the origin of exchange; or due to the need for profit, which is an increase in the value of the substitute. This—meaning disparity (المغايرة)—is the principle upon which the life of society relies. As for a transaction involving the exchange of a commodity for its like in kind or its equivalent: if it is without increase, such as a loan (قرض) of like for like, the rational people may recognize it due to the need for it; it is something that sustains society and fulfills the need of the destitute, and no corruption results from it. But if it is with an increase in the substitute, which is profit, then that is usury (الربا). So let us look at the result of usury.

Usury—by which we mean the exchange of like for like with an increase, such as giving ten for a term, or giving a commodity worth ten for a term and taking twelve when the term expires, and the like—only occurs when the buyer or borrower is compelled (اضطرار) to take it due to insolvency (الإعسار) and want (الإعواز), such that the extent of his need exceeds the amount of wealth he earns. For example, he earns ten for his day in an average state, but he needs twenty; so he borrows the remaining ten for twelve to be paid tomorrow. This necessitates that tomorrow he will have eight while needing twenty. From there, the average of his livelihood and life begins to vanish (الانمحاق) and diminish. It will not be long before the entirety of what he earns is exhausted and only the entirety of what he borrows remains. He is then demanded to pay twenty while he has nothing (20 - 0 = Wealth). This is destruction and the annihilation of one's effort in life.

As for the usurer (المرابي), the ten that was his own and the ten that belonged to the borrower accumulate with him, totaling twenty. Thus, both sums of wealth accumulate on one side, while the other side is emptied of wealth. This is solely because the increase was taken without any financial counter-value (عوض مالي). Thus, usury leads to the annihilation of the class of the insolvent and the dragging of wealth toward the class of the wealthy. This leads to the rule of the affluent usurers and their domination over people's wealth, honor, and lives in pursuit of all they desire and crave, due to the human instinct for superiority (التعالي) and exploitation (الاستخدام). It also leads to those exploited and humiliated defending themselves against the bitterness of life they have fallen into by every means of defense and revenge they can muster. This is chaos (الهرج والمرج) and the corruption of the system (فساد النظام), in which lies the destruction of humanity and the annihilation of civilization.

This is in addition to what frequently happens regarding the total loss of usurious wealth; for not every debtor upon whom debts have accumulated is able or willing to pay his debts.

This is regarding usury practiced between the rich and the people of hardship. As for that which occurs between others, such as commercial usury (الربا التجاري) upon which the affairs of banks and others are conducted—like usury on loans for trade—the least of its harms is that it causes wealth to be gradually dragged toward the capital set aside for usury on one hand, and causes the increase of commercial capitals and their power beyond what they actually are in reality. This leads to aggression between them, where they consume one another, some are absorbed into others, and everything is annihilated into that which is stronger than it. Thus, it continues to increase the number of those in need through insolvency, and wealth accumulates by being restricted to the few. The same peril we mentioned earlier returns.

No researcher in the fields of economics doubts that the sole reason for the spread of Communism (الشيوعية) and the advancement of the Socialist (الاشتراك) aim is the obscene accumulation of wealth among individuals and their prominent advancement in the advantages of life, while others—who are the majority—are deprived of their most essential necessities. The powerful class had deceived these weak ones with the words they struck their ears with, such as "civilization," "justice," "freedom," and "equality in human rights." They were saying with their mouths what was not in their hearts, intending by them meanings that are, in reality, the opposites of their meanings. They thought these would aid them in their desired luxury, the humiliation of the lower class, superiority over them, and absolute control as they pleased. They thought it was the only means for their happiness in life, but it was not long before what they thought was for them turned against them. Their plot and deception returned upon themselves; "they plotted, and Allah plotted, and Allah is the best of plotters." Allah the Glorified knows best what this human state will become in its future days. Among the ominous corruptions of usury is its facilitating the way to the hoarding (كنز) of wealth, and the imprisonment of thousands and millions in bank vaults away from circulation in buying and selling, and the sitting of a group on the throne of idleness and luxury, while others are deprived of the project to which the primordial nature (الفطرة) guides—which is man's reliance in his life upon labor (العمل). Thus, some do not live by labor due to their luxury, and others do not live by it due to their deprivation.

( Another Scientific Discussion )

Al-Ghazali said in the "Book of Gratitude" from al-Ihya': "Among the blessings of Allah the Exalted is the creation of dirhams and dinars, by which the world is sustained. They are two stones with no utility in their physical essences (أعيانهما), but creation is compelled to use them because every human is in need of many specific items for his food, clothing, and other needs. He may lack what he needs while possessing what he can do without, such as one who possesses saffron but needs a camel to ride, while the one who possesses the camel may not need it but needs saffron. Thus, there must be an exchange between them, and there must be an estimation of the amount of the substitute. For the owner of the camel will not give his camel for just any amount of saffron, and there is no inherent relationship (مناسبة) between saffron and a camel such that it could be said: 'Give its equivalent in weight or form.' Likewise, for one who buys a house with clothes, or a slave with a shoe, or flour with a donkey—there is no inherent relationship between these things. Thus, it is not known how much the camel is worth in saffron, making transactions extremely difficult. Therefore, these disparate and distant items required a mediator (متوسط) between them to judge with a just judgment, so that the rank and status of each could be known. Once the ranks are established and the levels ordered, the equal can be known from the unequal. So Allah the Exalted created dinars and dirhams as two judges and mediators between properties so that properties could be estimated by them. It is said: 'This camel is worth a hundred dinars, and this amount of saffron is worth a hundred.' Thus, insofar as they are equal to one thing, they are equal to each other. Balancing was only possible through the two currencies because there is no purpose in their physical essences. If there were a purpose in their essences, that specific purpose might necessitate a preference for the one who has that purpose, but not for the one who does not, and the system would not be orderly. Therefore, Allah the Exalted created them to be circulated by hands and to be just judges between properties.

And for another wisdom: which is using them as a means to all other things, because they are precious (عزيزان) in themselves, yet there is no purpose in their essences. Their relationship to all other properties is the same. So whoever owns them, it is as if he owns everything—unlike one who owns a garment, for he only owns the garment. If he needs food, the owner of the food might not desire the garment because his purpose is, for example, an animal. Thus, there was a need for something else which, in its form, is as if it were nothing, but in its meaning, is as if it were all things. A thing's relationship to different things is only equal when it has no specific form of its own that provides a specific benefit, like a mirror which has no color but reflects every color. Likewise, currency has no purpose in itself but is a means to every purpose. It is like a particle (الحرف) which has no meaning in itself but through which meanings appear in others. This is the second wisdom. And in them are also other wisdoms whose mention would be lengthy."

He then said, in summary: "Since they were from Allah the Exalted for the sake of these wisdoms based upon them, whoever uses them in a way that contradicts the intended wisdoms has been ungrateful (كفر) for the blessing of Allah."

He derived from this the prohibition of hoarding (كنز) them, for it is an injustice (ظلم) and an invalidation of their wisdom. Hoarding them is like imprisoning the judge of the people in a jail, preventing him from judging between the people and casting chaos among them without the presence of one to whom they can return for justice.

He also derived from it the prohibition of using gold and silver vessels, for in that is the intention of them for their own sake (بالاستقلال), whereas they are intended for other than themselves. That is an injustice, like one who employs the judge of the city in weaving, tax-collecting, and the works performed by the lowest of people.

He also derived from it the prohibition of usurious transactions (معاملة الربا) with dirhams and dinars, for it is ingratitude for the blessing and an injustice. For they were created for other than themselves, not for themselves, as no purpose pertains to their physical essences.

The matter was confused for him regarding the consideration of their origin and the branches he derived from it:

Firstly: He mentioned that no purpose pertains to them in themselves. If that were so, it would not be possible for them to estimate other commodities and needs. How can one thing estimate another by something it does not possess? Can a cubit measure the length of something except by the length it possesses? Or can a mann estimate the weight of something except by the weight within it?

Furthermore, his admission that they are "precious in themselves" is inconsistent except with their being intended for themselves. How can "preciousness" and "honor" be conceived without being desired?

Moreover, if they were only intended for other than themselves by creation, there would be no difference in consideration between the dinar and the dirham—meaning gold and silver—yet reality belies this. All types of currency would have equal values, and consideration would not have fallen upon other commodities like leather, salt, and others.

Secondly: Because the wisdom necessitating the prohibition of hoarding is not the granting of independent purpose to them. Rather, what appears from the saying of the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يَكْنِزُونَ الذَّهَبَ وَالْفِضَّةَ وَلَا يُنفِقُونَهَا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ — "As for those who treasure up gold and silver and do not spend them in the way of Allah..." (9:34), is the deprivation of the poor from sustaining themselves through them, while the need for labor and exchange is constant, as the explanation of this will come in the commentary of that verse.

Thirdly: Because the reason he mentioned for the prohibition of using gold and silver vessels—it being an injustice and ingratitude—is present in the use of jewelry made from them, as well as in money-changing (بيع الصرف), yet these are not considered in the Law (الشرع) as injustice, ingratitude, or forbidden.

Fourthly: Because the corruption he mentioned, if it were the cause of the injustice and ingratitude he described, would apply to money-changing in general, just as it applies to usurious transactions involving credit (النسيئة) and loans. Yet it would not apply to usury in measured and weighed goods, even though the ruling is the same. Thus, what he mentioned is incomplete, neither inclusive nor exclusive.

What the Exalted mentioned regarding the wisdom of the prohibition corresponds to what we have put forward: taking an increase without a counter-value. The Exalted said: وَمَا آتَيْتُم مِّن رِّبًا لِّيَرْبُوَ فِي أَمْوَالِ النَّاسِ فَلَا يَرْبُو عِندَ اللَّهِ وَمَا آتَيْتُم مِّن زَكَاةٍ تُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَ اللَّهِ فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْمُضْعِفُونَ — "What you give by way of usury, so that it may increase in people’s wealth, does not increase with Allah. But what you give by way of zakat, seeking Allah’s pleasure—it is they who will receive a manifold increase" (30:39). He made usury something that "increases" (رابياً) in people's wealth; that is because it grows by attaching parts of people's wealth to itself, just as the seed of a plant grows by feeding from the earth and attaching its parts to itself. Thus, usury continues to grow and increase itself while diminishing people's wealth until it reaches the end of it. This is what we mentioned previously. By this, it becomes clear that the meaning of the saying of the Exalted: وَإِن تُبْتُمْ فَلَكُمْ رُؤُوسُ أَمْوَالِكُمْ لَا تَظْلِمُونَ وَلَا تُظْلَمُونَ — "And if you repent, then you shall have your principal, neither wronging nor being wronged" (2:279), means you shall not wrong the people, nor shall you be wronged by them or by Allah the Glorified. Thus, usury is an injustice (ظلم) against the people.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا تَدَايَنتُم بِدَيْنٍ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى فَٱكْتُبُوهُ ۚ وَلْيَكْتُب بَّيْنَكُمْ كَاتِبٌۢ بِٱلْعَدْلِ ۚ وَلَا يَأْبَ كَاتِبٌ أَن يَكْتُبَ كَمَا عَلَّمَهُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ فَلْيَكْتُبْ وَلْيُمْلِلِ ٱلَّذِى عَلَيْهِ ٱلْحَقُّ وَلْيَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّهُۥ وَلَا يَبْخَسْ مِنْهُ شَيْـًٔا ۚ فَإِن كَانَ ٱلَّذِى عَلَيْهِ ٱلْحَقُّ سَفِيهًا أَوْ ضَعِيفًا أَوْ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُ أَن يُمِلَّ هُوَ فَلْيُمْلِلْ وَلِيُّهُۥ بِٱلْعَدْلِ ۚ وَٱسْتَشْهِدُوا۟ شَهِيدَيْنِ مِن رِّجَالِكُمْ ۖ فَإِن لَّمْ يَكُونَا رَجُلَيْنِ فَرَجُلٌ وَٱمْرَأَتَانِ مِمَّن تَرْضَوْنَ مِنَ ٱلشُّهَدَآءِ أَن تَضِلَّ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا فَتُذَكِّرَ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا ٱلْأُخْرَىٰ ۚ وَلَا يَأْبَ ٱلشُّهَدَآءُ إِذَا مَا دُعُوا۟ ۚ وَلَا تَسْـَٔمُوٓا۟ أَن تَكْتُبُوهُ صَغِيرًا أَوْ كَبِيرًا إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ أَقْسَطُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَأَقْوَمُ لِلشَّهَـٰدَةِ وَأَدْنَىٰٓ أَلَّا تَرْتَابُوٓا۟ ۖ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَ تِجَـٰرَةً حَاضِرَةً تُدِيرُونَهَا بَيْنَكُمْ فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَلَّا تَكْتُبُوهَا ۗ وَأَشْهِدُوٓا۟ إِذَا تَبَايَعْتُمْ ۚ وَلَا يُضَآرَّ كَاتِبٌ وَلَا شَهِيدٌ ۚ وَإِن تَفْعَلُوا۟ فَإِنَّهُۥ فُسُوقٌۢ بِكُمْ ۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ وَيُعَلِّمُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٢٨٢)
O you who have faith! When you contract a loan for a specified term, write it down. Let a writer write with honesty between you, and let not the writer refuse to write as Allah has taught him. So let him write, and let the one who incurs the debt dictate, and let him be wary of Allah, his Lord, and not diminish anything from it. But if the debtor be feeble-minded, or weak, or incapable of dictating himself, then let his guardian dictate with honesty, and take as witness two witnesses from your men, and if there are not two men, then a man and two women—from those whom you approve as witnesses—so that if one of the two defaults the other will remind her. The witnesses must not refuse when they are called, and do not consider it wearisome to write it down, whether it be a big or small sum, [as a loan lent] until its term. That is more just with Allah and more upright in respect to testimony, and the likeliest way to avoid doubt, unless it is an on-the-spot deal you transact between yourselves, in which case there is no sin upon you not to write it. Take witnesses when you make a deal, and let no harm be done to the writer or witness, and if you did that, it would be sinful of you. Be wary of Allah and Allah will teach you, and Allah has knowledge of all things. (282)
۞ وَإِن كُنتُمْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ وَلَمْ تَجِدُوا۟ كَاتِبًا فَرِهَـٰنٌ مَّقْبُوضَةٌ ۖ فَإِنْ أَمِنَ بَعْضُكُم بَعْضًا فَلْيُؤَدِّ ٱلَّذِى ٱؤْتُمِنَ أَمَـٰنَتَهُۥ وَلْيَتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّهُۥ ۗ وَلَا تَكْتُمُوا۟ ٱلشَّهَـٰدَةَ ۚ وَمَن يَكْتُمْهَا فَإِنَّهُۥٓ ءَاثِمٌ قَلْبُهُۥ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ (٢٨٣)
If you are on a journey and cannot find a writer, then a retained pledge [shall suffice]. And if one of you entrusts [an asset] to another, let him who is trusted deliver his trust, and let him be wary of Allah, his Lord. And do not conceal your testimony; anyone who conceals it, his heart will indeed be sinful, and Allah knows well what you do. (283)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: إِذا تداينتم (When you contract a debt) etc. Mutual debt (التداين) refers to the act of people transacting credit with one another. Dictation (الإملال) and (الإملاء) both refer to a person reciting to a scribe what is to be written. Defrauding (البخس) is to diminish or act unjustly. Weariness (السأمة) is boredom or fatigue. Harming (المضارة) is a reciprocal form (مفاعلة) derived from harm (الضرر), used for actions between two parties or otherwise. Transgression (الفسوق) is the departure from obedience (الطاعة). Pledges (الرهان)—also recited as (رُهُن) with two dammahs—are both plurals of pledge (الرهن), meaning the mortgaged property (المرهون).

The use of an explicit noun where a pronoun would suffice in His saying, the Exalted: فإِن كان الذي عليه الحق (but if he who owes the right), is to remove ambiguity that would arise if the pronoun referred back to the previously mentioned scribe.

The prominent pronoun in His saying: أن يمل هو فليملل وليه (to dictate, then let his guardian dictate), serves the purpose of associating the one who owes the right with his guardian. This case differs from the first two cases in that the guardian in the first two is the one solely responsible for the command. In contrast, in this case, the one who owes the right participates with the guardian in the action. It is as if it were said: whatever part of the task he is capable of, he must do, and what he is incapable of, his guardian must do.

His saying: أن تضل إِحديهما (lest one of them should err), is based on the implied meaning of "for fear that one of them should err." In His saying: إِحديهما الاخرى (one of them... the other), an explicit noun is placed where a pronoun could be. The subtle point (النكتة) here is the difference in the meaning of the word in the two instances: the first refers to either one of them indefinitely, while the second refers to the one remaining after the other has erred. Thus, the two meanings are distinct.

His saying: واتقوا (And be God-conscious), is a command for godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being consciously protective against disobeying Allah — regarding the commands and prohibitions Allah has set forth for them in this verse. As for His saying: ويعلمكم الله والله بكل شيء عليم (and Allah teaches you, and Allah has knowledge of all things), it is a newly commenced sentence (كلام مستأنف) intended to express divine favor (الامتنان), similar to His saying, the Exalted, in the verse of inheritance: يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ أَن تَضِلُّوا — "Allah clarifies for you lest you should go astray" (An-Nisa: 176). Thus, the intent is to express favor through the teaching of the laws of religion (شرائع الدين) and matters of the lawful (الحلال) and the prohibited (الحرام).

Regarding what has been said—that His saying: واتقوا الله ويعلمكم الله (And be God-conscious of Allah, and Allah teaches you) indicates that godwariness (التقوى) is a cause for divine teaching—it must be noted that while this is a truth supported by the Book and the Sunnah, this particular verse does not indicate it due to the presence of the coordinating conjunction "and" (واو العطف). Furthermore, such a meaning does not suit the context (سياق) of the verse or the connection of its conclusion to its beginning.

What we have mentioned is supported by the repetition of the Majestic Name (لفظ الجلالة) a second time. Had His saying ويعلمكم الله (and Allah teaches you) not been a newly commenced sentence, the context would have required saying "He teaches you" (يعلمكم) by using a hidden pronoun for the subject. In His saying, the Exalted: واتقوا الله ويعلمكم الله ان الله بكل شيء عليم (And be God-conscious of Allah, and Allah teaches you, and indeed Allah has knowledge of all things), the Name was made explicit the first and second time because they occur in two independent sentences, and it was made explicit a third time to indicate causality (التعليل), as if it were said: He has knowledge of all things because He is Allah.

Know that these two verses indicate approximately twenty rulings from the fundamental laws of religion (أُصول أحكام الدين), pledges (الرهن), and others. The narrations (الاخبار) regarding them and related matters are numerous, but their investigation belongs to jurisprudence (الفقه). For this reason, we have chosen to overlook them; whoever desires to investigate them should consult the appropriate sources in jurisprudence.

Ayah

لِّلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَإِن تُبْدُوا۟ مَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحَاسِبْكُم بِهِ ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَيَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٢٨٤)
To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. Whether you disclose what is in your hearts or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it. Then He will forgive whomever He wishes and punish whomever He wishes, and Allah has power over all things. (284)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: لِلَّهِ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ — To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth (2:284). This is a discourse indicating His ownership, the Exalted, of the created world (عالم الخلق) consisting of what is in the heavens and the earth. It serves as a preamble to His subsequent saying: وَإِن تُبْدُوا مَا فِي أَنفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحَاسِبْكُم بِهِ اللَّهُ — Whether you disclose what is in your hearts or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it (2:284). That is to say: since to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth—and you and your deeds and what your souls have earned are part of that—He encompasses you and is dominant (مهيمن) over your actions. It makes no difference to Him whether your deeds are manifest and apparent, or hidden and concealed; He will bring you to account for them.

It may be inferred from the verse that the "heaven" (السماء) is ontologically congruent (مسانخاً) with the actions of the hearts and the attributes of the soul; thus, what is in the souls is part of what is in the heavens, and to Allah belongs what is in the heavens. Similarly, when what is in the souls is manifested through the actions of the limbs, it becomes part of what is in the earth, and to Allah belongs what is in the earth. Therefore, whatever is enfolded within the souls, whether disclosed or hidden, is owned by Allah and encompassed by Him, and He will deal with it through accounting (المحاسبة).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِن تُبْدُوا مَا فِي أَنفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحَاسِبْكُم بِهِ اللَّهُ — Whether you disclose what is in your hearts or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it. Disclosure (الإبداء) is manifestation as opposed to concealment (الإخفاء). The meaning of "what is in your souls" (ما في أنفسكم) refers to what has become established (استقر) in your souls, according to the understanding of the people of convention and language. Nothing is established in the soul except traits (الملكات) and attributes (الصفات) consisting of virtues and vices, such as faith (الإيمان) and disbelief (الكفر), love and hate, resolve (العزم), and the like; for these are what accept manifestation and concealment. As for their manifestation, it is achieved through appropriate actions issuing from the limbs, which the senses perceive and the intellect judges to be the result of those congruent psychological sources. For were it not for those psychological attributes and traits—such as will, aversion, faith, disbelief, love, hate, and so on—these actions would not have been issued. Thus, by the issuance of actions, the existence of their origin becomes manifest to the intellect. As for their concealment, it is by refraining from any action that indicates their presence in the soul.

In short, the apparent meaning of His saying, "what is in your souls," implies fixity and establishment (الاستقرار) in the soul. This establishment does not necessarily mean being so deeply rooted in the soul that removal is impossible—like ingrained traits (الملكات الراسخة)—but rather a complete presence (ثبوتاً تاماً) that is significant enough to result in the issuance of an action. This is suggested by His saying, "whether you disclose" and "or hide it," for these two descriptions indicate that what is in the soul is such that it can be a source for manifestation or not, which is concealment. These attributes can be such whether they are passing states (أحوالاً) or established traits (ملكات). As for the passing thoughts (الخطورات) and psychological whispers (الهواجس) that strike the soul without a person's will, as well as simple conceptions (التصورات الساذجة) unaccompanied by assent (تصديق)—such as conceiving the image of a sin without inclination or resolve—the wording of the verse does not include them at all. This is because, as you have learned, they are not established in the soul, nor are they the source for the issuance of actions.

Thus, it is concluded that the verse only indicates the psychological states and traits that are the sources of actions, whether acts of obedience or sins, and that Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, holds man accountable for them. Thus, the verse follows the same course as His saying, the Exalted: لَّا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ اللَّهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمَانِكُمْ وَلَٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا كَسَبَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ — Allah will not take you to task for what is soul-less in your oaths, but He will take you to task for what your hearts have earned (2:225); and His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنَّهُ آثِمٌ قَلْبُهُ — his heart is indeed sinful (2:283); and His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ السَّمْعَ وَالْبَصَرَ وَالْفُؤَادَ كُلُّ أُولَٰئِكَ كَانَ عَنْهُ مَسْئُولًا — Indeed the hearing, the eyesight, and the heart—all of these are accountable (17:36). All these verses indicate that hearts—which are the souls—have states and attributes for which man is held accountable. Likewise is His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُحِبُّونَ أَن تَشِيعَ الْفَاحِشَةُ فِي الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ فِي الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةِ — Indeed those who want indecency to spread among the faithful—for them is a painful punishment in the world and the Hereafter (24:19); for it is apparent that the punishment is for the "wanting" (الحب), which is an internal matter of the heart.

This is the apparent meaning of the verse. It must be known that the verse only indicates the accounting (المحاسبة) for what is in the souls, whether manifested or hidden. As for whether the recompense (الجزاء) in the cases of concealment and manifestation is identical—in other words, whether the recompense revolves around the resolve (العزم) regardless of whether the action was performed or not, and whether the action coincided with the intended reality or not (as in the case of audacity (التجري), for example)—the verse does not address that.

The commentators have taken various paths regarding the meaning of the verse because they imagined it indicates being taken to task for every passing thought (خاطر نفساني), whether established in the soul or otherwise, which would be nothing but a duty beyond one's capacity (تكليفاً بما لا يطاق). Thus, some committed themselves to this view, while others sought an interpretation (مؤول) to escape it.

Among them are those who said: The verse indicates accounting for everything that enters the heart, which is a duty beyond capacity; however, the verse is abrogated (منسوخة) by what follows it in His saying, the Exalted: لَا يُكَلِّفُ اللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا — Allah does not task any soul beyond its capacity (2:286).

The critique of this is that the verse does not apparently carry such generality, as previously mentioned. Furthermore, imposing a duty beyond capacity is undoubtedly impermissible. Moreover, He, the Exalted, informs us in His saying: وَمَا جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ — and He has not placed upon you any hardship in religion (22:78), that He does not legislate in religion what is beyond capacity.

Among them are those who said: The verse is specific to the concealment of testimony (كتمان الشهادة) and is linked to the preceding verse of debt (آية الدين) mentioned therein. This is refuted by the absolute nature (إطلاق) of the verse, as is the claim of those who said it is specific to the disbelievers.

Among them are those who said: The meaning is: "If you disclose through your actions the evil that is in your souls by being blatant and public with the deed, or hide it by performing the deed secretly, Allah will bring you to account for it."

Among them are those who said: What is meant by the verse is passing thoughts (الخواطر) in general, but "accounting" (المحاسبة) means "informing" (الإخبار). That is, everything that occurs to your minds, whether you manifest it or hide it, Allah will inform you of it on the Day of Resurrection. Thus, it follows the course of His saying, the Exalted: فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — then He will inform you of what you used to do (5:105). This and the preceding view are refuted by their opposition to the apparent meaning of the verse, as previously explained.

His saying, the Exalted: فَيَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَاءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَاءُ وَاللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ — Then He forgives whomever He wishes, and punishes whomever He wishes, and Allah has power over all things (2:284). The alternation in the branching statement between forgiveness (المغفرة) and punishment (العذاب) is not devoid of an indication that what is meant by "what is in the souls" are the evil psychological attributes and states. Although forgiveness is sometimes used in the Quran in contexts other than sins, such usage is rare (كالنادر) and requires the support of specific contextual clues (قرائن خاصة). His saying, "Indeed Allah..." is a justification (تعليل) referring back to the content of the final sentence, or to the significance of the verse in its entirety.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Sahih Muslim, narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "When there was revealed to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: لِلَّهِ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَإِن تُبْدُوا مَا فِي أَنفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحَاسِبْكُم بِهِ اللَّهُ, it weighed heavily upon the companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم. They came to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم and knelt on their knees, saying: 'O Messenger of Allah, we were tasked with deeds that we are capable of: prayer, fasting, jihad, and charity. But Allah has revealed this verse, and we cannot bear it.' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said: 'Do you wish to say as the People of the Book said before you: "We hear and we disobey"? Rather, say: "We hear and we obey. Our Lord, [we seek] Your forgiveness, and toward You is the return."' When the people recited it and their tongues became humbled by it, Allah revealed immediately after it: آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ بِمَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِن رَّبِّهِ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ (The Apostle has faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord, and all the faithful [also have faith]...). When they did that, Allah the Exalted abrogated it and revealed: لَا يُكَلِّفُ اللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا (Allah does not task any soul beyond its capacity...) to the end of the chapter."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ahmad, Muslim, Abu Dawud in his Nasikh, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim from Abu Hurayrah. Something similar was narrated through several chains from Ibn Abbas. Abrogation (النسخ) was also narrated through several chains from others, such as Ibn Mas'ud and Aisha.

It is narrated from al-Rabi' ibn Anas: "The verse is decisive (محكمة), not abrogated; what is meant by 'accounting' is what Allah informs the servant of on the Day of Resurrection regarding the deeds he performed in the world."

It is narrated from Ibn Abbas through several chains: "The verse is specific to the concealment and performance of testimony. Thus, it is decisive, not abrogated."

It is also narrated from Aisha: "What is meant by 'accounting' is the grief and sorrow that afflicts a man when he intends a sin but does not perform it. Thus, the verse is also decisive, not abrogated."

It is narrated through the chain of Ali from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: وَإِن تُبْدُوا مَا فِي أَنفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ: "That refers to your secrets and your public affairs; Allah will bring you to account for them. It has not been abrogated, but when Allah gathers the creation on the Day of Resurrection, He will say: 'I shall inform you of what you hid in your souls which my angels did not see.' As for the believers, He informs them and forgives them for what they whispered to themselves—and that is His saying: يُحَاسِبْكُم بِهِ اللَّهُ, meaning He informs you. As for the people of doubt and suspicion, He informs them of the denial (التكذيب) they hid—and that is His saying: وَلَٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا كَسَبَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ (but He will take you to task for what your hearts have earned)."

I say: These narrations, despite the differences in their contents, share the commonality of being contrary to the apparent meaning of the Quran as previously explained. The apparent meaning of the verse is that accounting only occurs for what the hearts have earned (كسبته القلوب), whether within themselves or through the limbs. There is no "earning" (كسب) in a passing thought (الخطور النفساني). In this regard, there is no difference between testimony and other matters, nor between a believer and a disbeliever. The apparent meaning of "accounting" (المحاسبة) is accounting for recompense (الجزاء), not merely informing of passing thoughts and psychological intentions. This is what the verse indicates, and it is supported by other verses as previously mentioned.

As for the report of abrogation (النسخ) specifically, it contains several flaws that necessitate its dismissal from being authoritative (الحجية).

First: Its contradiction with the apparent meaning of the Book, as previously explained.

Second: Its implication of the permissibility of tasking with what is beyond capacity (تكليف ما لا يطاق), the falsehood of which the intellect does not doubt, especially from Him, the Exalted. Abrogation does not help in this regard, as is not hidden; rather, it may increase problem upon problem. For the apparent meaning of the saying in the narration, "When the people recited it..." is that the abrogation occurred before the action was even performed, which is problematic.

Third: You will see in the discussion of the following two verses that His saying: لَا يُكَلِّفُ اللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا (Allah does not task any soul beyond its capacity), is not suitable to be an abrogator (ناسخاً) for anything. Rather, it indicates that every soul will only face what it has earned, whether that is difficult for it or easy. If it were burdened with what it cannot bear, or if a burden (إصر) were placed upon it as was placed upon those before us, it would only be a matter the soul earned through its own evil choice; so let it blame none but itself. Thus, the sentence—meaning His saying: لَا يُكَلِّفُ اللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا—is like a parenthetical statement (المعترضة) to repel an objection.

Fourth: It will also be shown that the direction of the discourse in the two verses is not toward the matter of passing thoughts at all, and the direct confrontation between the abrogator (الناسخ) and the abrogated (المنسوخ) is a necessity in the chapter of abrogation.

Rather, His saying, the Exalted: آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ (The Apostle has faith...) to the end of the two verses, is aimed at explaining a purpose other than the purpose for which His saying, the Exalted: لِلَّهِ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ to the end of the verse, was aimed, as will come, Allah willing.

Ayahs

ءَامَنَ ٱلرَّسُولُ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِن رَّبِّهِۦ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ۚ كُلٌّ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتِهِۦ وَكُتُبِهِۦ وَرُسُلِهِۦ لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّن رُّسُلِهِۦ ۚ وَقَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا ۖ غُفْرَانَكَ رَبَّنَا وَإِلَيْكَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٢٨٥)
The Apostle and the faithful have faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord. Each [of them] has faith in Allah, His angels, His scriptures and His apostles. [They declare,] ‘We make no distinction between any of His apostles.’ And they say, ‘We hear and obey. Our Lord, forgive us, and toward You is the return.’ (285)
لَا يُكَلِّفُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا ۚ لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْهَا مَا ٱكْتَسَبَتْ ۗ رَبَّنَا لَا تُؤَاخِذْنَآ إِن نَّسِينَآ أَوْ أَخْطَأْنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا وَلَا تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْنَآ إِصْرًا كَمَا حَمَلْتَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا وَلَا تُحَمِّلْنَا مَا لَا طَاقَةَ لَنَا بِهِۦ ۖ وَٱعْفُ عَنَّا وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَٱرْحَمْنَآ ۚ أَنتَ مَوْلَىٰنَا فَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٢٨٦)
Allah does not task any soul beyond its capacity. Whatever [good] it earns is to its own benefit, and whatever [evil] it incurs is to its own harm. ‘Our Lord! Take us not to task if we forget or make mistakes! Our Lord! Do not place upon us a burden as You placed on those who were before us! Our Lord! Do not lay upon us what we have no strength to bear! Excuse us, forgive us, and be merciful to us! You are our Master, so help us against the faithless lot!’ (286)

(Commentary)

The discourse in these two verses serves as a summation (الفذلكة) that encapsulates the details contained within the surah which clarify its primary objective. It has been previously mentioned that the objective of the surah is to explain that the right of worship (العبادة) due to Allah, the Exalted, is to believe in everything He has revealed to His servants through the tongues of His messengers, without making any distinction between His messengers. This is precisely what the first verse contains, from His saying: آمن الرسول (The Apostle has faith...) to His saying: من رسله (...of His apostles).

Furthermore, the surah contains narratives recounting the various blessings Allah bestowed upon the Children of Israel—such as the Book, prophethood, and kingship—and the disobedience, rebellion, breach of covenants, and disbelief with which they responded. This is what is alluded to, along with the seeking of refuge in Allah to avoid such a fate, at the end of the first verse and throughout the second verse. Thus, the two verses return the end of the surah's discourse to its beginning and its conclusion to its commencement.

From here, the particularity of the station of exposition in these two verses becomes apparent. To clarify: Allah, the Glorified, opened this surah with the description that the God-conscious (المتقون) — those who possess godwariness (التقوى), the innate and acquired protective shield of the soul against divine displeasure — must possess; namely, what is incumbent upon the servant in fulfilling the right of Lordship (الربوبية). He mentioned that the God-conscious among His servants believe in the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — maintain the prayer, spend from the provision Allah has given them, believe in what Allah revealed to His Messenger and to the messengers before him, and possess certainty regarding the Hereafter. Consequently, Allah blessed them with the guidance of the Quran and, by contrast, explained the state of the disbelievers and the hypocrites.

Then, He detailed the matter of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), specifically the Jews, mentioning that He favored them with the subtleties of guidance and honored them with various blessings and magnificent gifts. Yet, they met this with nothing but arrogance, disobedience to His command, ingratitude for His blessings, rejection of Allah and His messengers, enmity toward His angels, and the differentiation between Allah’s messengers and His books. Consequently, Allah met them by imposing upon them the heavy burden (الإصر) of difficult laws—such as the command to kill themselves—and by burdening them with that which they had no strength to bear, such as transformation (المسخ), the falling of the thunderbolt, and the plague (الرجز) from the sky.

Then, at the conclusion of the exposition, He returned to describing the state of the Messenger and those believers who followed him. He mentioned that they, unlike the People of the Book, met their Lord’s blessings of guidance and direction with nothing but the best of acceptance, hearing, and obedience. They are believers in Allah, His angels, His books, and His messengers, not making a distinction between any of His messengers. In doing so, they preserve the requirement of their station, which is encompassed by the humility of servitude (العبودية) and the majesty of Lordship (الربوبية); for despite their absolute response to the Caller of Truth, they confessed their inability to fully discharge the right of that response.

This is because their existence is built upon weakness and ignorance; thus, they might fall short in maintaining the duties of vigilance (المراقبة) due to forgetfulness (نسيان) or error (خطأ), or they might fail in performing the duty of servitude, whereby their souls betray them by committing a sin that brings them to the place of wrath and censure, just as it brought the People of the Book before them. Therefore, they sought refuge in the Presence of Might and the Fountainhead of Mercy, asking that He not take them to task if they forget or err, nor lay upon them a heavy burden (إصراً), nor burden them with what they have no strength to bear, and that He pardon them, forgive them, and help them against the faithless people.

This is the station upon which the exposition in these two noble verses relies, and it is consistent, as you see, with the objective derived from the surah. It is not as some have mentioned: that the content of these two verses is connected to His saying in the previous verse: إِن تُبْدُوا مَا فِي أَنفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحَاسِبْكُم بِهِ اللَّهُ (Whether you disclose what is in your hearts or hide it, Allah will bring you to account of it), which they claim indicates a duty (التكليف) beyond one's capacity, and that the first verse: آمن الرسول بما أُنزل اليه من ربه والمؤمنون (The Apostle has faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord, and all the faithful...) is a narration of the companions' acceptance of a duty beyond their capacity, while the second verse abrogates (ناسخة) that!

What we have mentioned is also consistent with what has been narrated regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول): that al-Baqarah was the first surah revealed in Medina. The migration of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم to Medina and his settlement there coincided with the complete reception of the divine religion by the believers of the Ansar and their standing to support the Messenger of Allah with their wealth and lives. Furthermore, the believers among the Muhajirun left behind families, children, wealth, and homelands for the sake of Allah to join His Messenger. This was the appropriate occasion for praise from Allah, the Glorified, for their response to His Prophet’s call with hearing and acceptance, and for His gratitude toward them. This is partially indicated by the end of the verse: أنت مولانا فانصرنا على القوم الكافرين (You are our Master, so help us against the faithless people), as the sentence intimates that this supplication of theirs occurred in the early stages of the emergence of Islam.

In these verses, there are wonders of brevity and detail, conciseness followed by elaboration, the etiquette of servitude, and the gathering of the foundations of perfection and felicity.

His saying, the Exalted: آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ بِمَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِن رَّبِّهِ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ — The Apostle has faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord, and all the faithful [have faith]. (2:285). This is an attestation to the faith of the Messenger and the believers. The Messenger of Allah was mentioned individually regarding faith in what was revealed to him from his Lord, and then the believers were joined to him as a mark of honor (تشريفاً) for him. This is the custom of the Quran in instances where honoring is appropriate: to ennoble the Prophet by mentioning him individually and first, and then following that with the mention of the believers, such as His saying, the Exalted: فَأَنزَلَ اللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — ...then Allah sent down His composure upon His Apostle and upon the faithful... (48:26), and His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ لَا يُخْزِي اللَّهُ النَّبِيَّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ — ...the day Allah will not let the Prophet down and those who have faith along with him. (66:8).

His saying, the Exalted: كُلٌّ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ وَمَلَائِكَتِهِ وَكُتُبِهِ وَرُسُلِهِ — ...each [of them] has faith in Allah, His angels, His scriptures and His apostles. (2:285). This is a detailing of the general statement indicated by the preceding sentence. For that which was revealed to the Messenger of Allah calls for faith and the attestation to the truth of the books, the messengers, and the angels, who are honored servants. Thus, whoever believes in what was revealed to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم has believed in all of that, each in the manner appropriate to it.

His saying, the Exalted: لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّن رُّسُلِهِ — [saying,] ‘We make no distinction between any of His apostles.’ (2:285). This is a narration of their speech without the mediation of the word "saying" (قول).
