The general point regarding this type of narration was mentioned under His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْرَاهِيمُ الْقَوَاعِدَ مِنَ الْبَيْتِ وَإِسْمَاعِيلُ رَبَّنَا تَقَبَّلْ مِنَّا إِنَّكَ أَنتَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ — As Abraham raised the foundations of the House with Ishmael, [they prayed]: ‘Our Lord, accept it from us! Indeed You are the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (2:127). It is among the most beautiful of Quranic styles. The specific point here, in addition to it being a representation of their state and their speech, is that this discourse is extracted from the very nature of their state in believing in what Allah, the Exalted, revealed. Thus, they did not say it except through the "tongue of their state" (لسان حالهم); and if they did say it verbally, each said it individually within himself. As for their speaking it as one voice, that is only through the "tongue of the state."

Among the remarkable aspects of the style in this verse is how it combines two narrated statements from them while differentiating in the mode of narration. I refer to His saying, the Exalted: لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّن رُّسُلِهِ (...We make no distinction between any of His apostles) and وَقَالُوا سَمْعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا (...And they say, ‘We hear and obey’). He narrated the first without the mediation of "saying" and the second with it, yet both are the speech of the believers in responding to the call of the Caller.

The reason for this differentiation is that their saying "We make no distinction..." is said by them through the "tongue of their state," unlike their saying "We hear and obey."

Furthermore, the Exalted began by informing about the state of each one of them in the singular form, saying: كُلٌّ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ (each has faith in Allah), then shifted to the plural, saying: لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ (We make no distinction...) until the end of the two verses. This is because what occurred regarding these matters among the People of the Book was in the form of a collective (نعت الجمع); for the Jews distinguished between Moses and between Jesus and Muhammad, and the Christians distinguished between Moses and Jesus and between Muhammad. Thus, they split into sects and formed factions, whereas Allah, the Exalted, had created them as a single community upon the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being. Similarly, the censure, the burden, and the imposition that befell them occurred to their collective. Likewise, what occurs at the end of the verse regarding the request for help against the disbelievers is a matter linked to the collective rather than the individual, unlike faith (الايمان), which is a matter that truly subsists in the individual.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالُوا سَمْعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا غُفْرَانَكَ رَبَّنَا وَإِلَيْكَ الْمَصِيرُ — ...And they say, ‘We hear and obey. Our Lord, [we crave] Your forgiveness, and toward You is the return.’ (2:285). Their saying "We hear and obey" is an origination (إنشاء) and not a report (إخبار). It is an allusion (كناية) to the response through faith in the heart and action with the limbs; for "hearing" (السمع) is used linguistically to allude to acceptance and submission, and "obedience" (الاطاعة) is used for compliance through action. Thus, the combination of hearing and obedience completes the matter of faith.

Their saying "We hear and obey" is the fulfillment of the entirety of what is due from the servant regarding the right of Lordship in its call. This is the complete right that Allah, the Glorified, has established for Himself over His servant: that he should hear in order to obey, which is worship (العبادة), as He, the Exalted, said: وَمَا خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ مَا أُرِيدُ مِنْهُم مِّن رِّزْقٍ وَمَا أُرِيدُ أَن يُطْعِمُونِ — I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me. I do not desire any provision from them, nor do I desire that they should feed Me. (51:56-57), and He, the Exalted, said: أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يَا بَنِي آدَمَ أَن لَّا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطَانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي — Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, “Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. Worship Me...” (36:60-61).

In exchange for this right that He established for Himself over His servant, the Glorified has established another right for His servant upon Himself, which is forgiveness (المغفرة) — the divine act of pardoning sins and shielding the servant from their consequences — without which no one can achieve the felicity of their soul, neither the prophets and messengers nor those below them. He promised them that He would forgive them if they obeyed Him through servitude, as He mentioned at the very beginning of legislating the law for Adam and his children, saying: قُلْنَا اهْبِطُوا مِنْهَا جَمِيعًا فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى فَمَن تَبِعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ — We said, ‘Get down from it, all of you! Yet if there comes to you a guidance from Me, those who follow My guidance shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve.’ (2:38). This "lack of fear and grief" is nothing other than forgiveness.

When the people said "We hear and obey"—which is a response with absolute hearing and obedience without restriction—they fulfilled the right of Lordship. Consequently, they asked Him, the Exalted, for their right which He established for them, which is forgiveness. Thus, they said immediately following their statement "We hear and obey": غُفْرَانَكَ رَبَّنَا وَإِلَيْكَ الْمَصِيرُ (Your forgiveness, our Lord, and toward You is the return). Forgiveness (المغفرة) and pardon (الغفران) mean "veiling" (الستر). His forgiveness, the Exalted, refers back to the averting of punishment, which is a veiling of the deficiencies in the stage of servitude. This becomes manifest when the servant returns to his Lord; therefore, they followed their saying "Your forgiveness, our Lord" with their saying "and toward You is the return."

His saying, the Exalted: لَا يُكَلِّفُ اللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْهَا مَا اكْتَسَبَتْ — Allah does not task any soul beyond its capacity. To its merit is what it has earned, and within its debt is what it has amassed. (2:286). Capacity (الوسع) is ability and power. The root of wus‘ is spatial amplitude (السعة المكانية); then, the human power is imagined as a sort of container for the voluntary actions (الأفعال الاختيارية) that proceed from it. Thus, the actions a human is capable of are as if his power "contains" them, and what he is incapable of, it does not "contain." Thus, the meaning of power (الطاقة) was applied to it. Subsequently, power was called wus‘, and it was said: "the wus‘ of a human," meaning his power and the capacity of his ability.

You have already learned that the complete right of Allah, the Exalted, over His servant is that he hears and obeys. It is evident that a human only says "I hear" regarding that which his soul can accept through understanding; as for that which does not accept understanding, there is no meaning to responding to it with hearing and acceptance. It is also evident that a human only says "I obey" regarding that which the limbs and tools of action can comply with. For obedience (الاطاعة) is the compliance (مطاوعة) of the human and the being-affected of his powers and limbs by the influence of the one commanding. As for that which does not accept compliance—such as a human being commanded to hear with his eyes, or for his body to occupy more than one place, or to be born from his parents a second time—it does not accept obedience, and no masterly command (تكليف مولوي) attaches to it. Thus, responding to the Caller of Truth with hearing and obedience is only realized in that which is voluntary (اختياري) for the human and to which his power attaches. This is what the human earns (يكسب) for himself, whether it benefits or harms him. Thus, "earning" (الكسب) is the best evidence that what the human has earned, he only found and became clothed with through the path of capacity and power.

It has become clear from what we mentioned that His saying: لَا يُكَلِّفُ اللَّهُ (Allah does not task...) is a discourse following the established way (سنة) of Allah among His servants: that He does not task them with what is not within their capacity, such as faith in what is beyond their understanding or obedience to what is beyond the power of their faculties. This is also the established way among the rational and sentient beings of His creation. It is a discourse whose meaning corresponds exactly to what is contained in His saying narrating from the Messenger and the believers: "We hear and obey," without addition or deficiency.

The sentence, namely His saying: لَا يُكَلِّفُ اللَّهُ نَفْسًا (Allah does not task any soul...), is connected in content to what precedes it and what follows it among the sentences listed in the two verses.

As for what precedes it, it conveys that Allah does not task His servants with more than what they can hear and obey, which is what is within their capacity to perform.

As for what follows it, it conveys that what the Prophet and the believers asked for—regarding not being taken to task for error and forgetfulness, not having a heavy burden (إصر) laid upon them, and not being burdened with what they have no strength to bear—all of these, though they may be difficult matters (أُموراً حرجية), are not from the category of "tasking with what is not within capacity" (التكليف بما ليس في الوسع). For that which can be laid upon them of what they have no strength to bear is not of the nature of a primary task (تكليف), but rather of the nature of a recompense for rebellion and disobedience. As for being taken to task for error and forgetfulness, although they are not voluntary in themselves, they are voluntary through their preliminaries (مقدماتها). It is possible for a preventer to prevent them by prohibiting their preliminaries or by making vigilance against them obligatory, especially if the human's affliction with them is based on an evil choice (سوء الاختيار). The same applies to the laying of a heavy burden (إصر); if it is based on a tightening (التشديد) upon the human as a recompense for his rebellion against easy tasks by replacing them with what is difficult and distressing for him, then that is not from the category of the tasking denied of Him, the Exalted, which is impermissible according to reason, because it is something the human chose for himself through his evil choice; thus, there is no problem in directing it toward him.

His saying, the Exalted: رَبَّنَا لَا تُؤَاخِذْنَا إِن نَّسِينَا أَوْ أَخْطَأْنَا — ‘Our Lord, do not take us to task if we forget or err!’ (2:286). When they said in the station of responding to the call "We hear and obey"—which is a statement informing of an absolute response without restriction—they then turned to the weakness and frailty of their existence, and they also turned to what the matter of those before them had become, who were nations like them. They sought mercy from their Lord and asked Him not to treat them as He treated those before them regarding censure, the burden, and the imposition, because they knew, through what Allah had taught them, that there is no power or strength except by Allah, and that there is no protector from Allah except His mercy.

The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, although he is infallible (معصوم) from error and forgetfulness, only possesses infallibility through the protection of Allah and is preserved by Him, the Exalted. Thus, it was correct for him to ask his Lord for that which he is not secure from within himself, and to include himself for that reason in the group of the believers.

His saying, the Exalted: رَبَّنَا وَلَا تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْنَا إِصْرًا كَمَا حَمَلْتَهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِنَا — ‘Our Lord, do not lay upon us a burden as You laid it upon those who were before us!’ (2:286). The heavy burden (الإصر) is weight, as has been said; and it is said to be the confinement of a thing by force, which is close to the first meaning, for in confinement there is the imposition of something upon that which it dislikes and finds heavy.

The meaning of "those who were before us" refers to the People of the Book, especially the Jews, as the surah alludes to many of their stories, and as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَيَضَعُ عَنْهُمْ إِصْرَهُمْ وَالْأَغْلَالَ الَّتِي كَانَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ — ...and relieves them of their burden and the shackles that were upon them... (7:157).

His saying, the Exalted: رَبَّنَا وَلَا تُحَمِّلْنَا مَا لَا طَاقَةَ لَنَا بِهِ — ‘Our Lord, do not overburden us with what we have no strength to bear!’ (2:286). What is meant by "what we have no strength to bear" is not the initial tasking (التكليف الابتدائي) with the impossible, for you have already learned that reason never permits this, and that His discourse—namely what He narrated by His saying: "And they say, ‘We hear and obey’"—indicates the opposite. Rather, what is meant by it is the recompense for evil deeds that reaches them in the form of a difficult task that is not normally bearable, or a descending punishment, or an afflicting plague such as transformation and the like.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاعْفُ عَنَّا وَاغْفِرْ لَنَا وَارْحَمْنَا — ‘...and pardon us, and forgive us, and have mercy upon us!’ (2:286). Pardon (العفو) is the erasing of the trace of a thing; forgiveness (المغفرة) is its veiling; and mercy (الرحمة) is well-known. As for the referent (المصداق), considering the linguistic meanings necessitates that the sequence of the three sentences be of the nature of a progression from the branch to the root, or in other words, from the more specific benefit to the more general. Accordingly, pardon from Him, the Exalted, is the removal and erasure of the trace of the sin, such as the punishment written against the sinner. Forgiveness is the removal of the form of the sin within the soul and its veiling. Mercy is the divine gift that veils the sin and its form.

The conjunction of these three—namely His saying: "and pardon us, and forgive us, and have mercy upon us"—to His saying: "Our Lord, do not take us to task if we forget or err," given the context and order of the whole, intimates that what is meant by pardon, forgiveness, and mercy is that which relates to their sins from the perspective of error, forgetfulness, and the like. From this, it becomes clear that the forgiveness sought here is other than the pardon (الغفران) mentioned in His saying: "Your forgiveness, our Lord" (غفرانك ربنا); for that is an absolute forgiveness in response to the absolute response as previously mentioned, while this is a specific forgiveness in response to sin arising from forgetfulness or error. Thus, the request for forgiveness is not redundant.

The word "Lord" (الرب) has been repeated in these supplications four times to evoke the attribute of mercy by intimating and alluding to the attribute of servitude; for the mention of Lordship brings to mind the attribute of servitude and humility.

His saying, the Exalted: أَنتَ مَوْلَانَا فَانصُرْنَا عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْكَافِرِينَ — ‘...You are our Master, so help us against the faithless people.’ (2:286). This is a resumption and an independent supplication. The Master (المولى) is the Helper, but not just any helper; rather, the Helper who takes charge of the affairs of the one helped, for it is derived from guardianship (الولاية) in the sense of taking charge of an affair. Since He, the Exalted, is the Guardian (ولي) of the believers, He is their Master (مولى) in what they need of His help. He, the Exalted, said: وَاللَّهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — ...and Allah is the Guardian of the faithful. (3:68), and He, the Exalted, said: ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ مَوْلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَأَنَّ الْكَافِرِينَ لَا مَوْلَىٰ لَهُمْ — That is because Allah is the Master of the faithful, and because the faithless have no master. (47:11).

This supplication from them indicates that after hearing and obeying the foundation of the religion, they had no concern except for its establishment, its dissemination, and the struggle (الجهاد) to make the word of truth manifest and to achieve the consensus of the nations upon it. He, the Exalted, said: قُلْ هَٰذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُو إِلَى اللَّهِ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي وَسُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ وَمَا أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ — Say, ‘This is my way: I summon to Allah with insight—I and he who follows me. Immaculate is Allah, and I am not one of the polytheists.’ (12:108). Thus, the call to the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) is the way of the religion, and it is that which is followed by struggle, fighting, commanding the right and forbidding the wrong, and all other types of calling and warning—all of this to sever the root of discord from among this species. His saying, the Exalted, alludes to the importance of this in the view of the Legislator of the religion: شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ الدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِ نُوحًا وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ — He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus, declaring, ‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it.’ (42:13). Thus, their saying "You are our Master, so help us" indicates that they made the general call to the religion the first thing to occur to their minds after binding the heart to hearing and obedience. And Allah knows best.

Praise be to Allah.
